title: Vulnerability Practices among Professionals in Multidisciplinary Settings
methods: Inductive qualitative case study of a weekly multidisciplinary meeting in an academic primary-care clinic. Insider–outsider team combined 20 recorded weekly meetings (~93 hours of audio-video), 19 semi-structured interviews, and 7 participatory group discussions; 27 episodes of vulnerability practices were isolated and coded across six analytic phases using grounded-theory-style open coding, role analysis, chronological sequencing, and theoretical model building.
industry: Primary health care (academic family medicine clinic)
time_period: 10 months of observation (conducted online during COVID-19 lockdown)
units: A weekly multidisciplinary team meeting assembling a senior psychologist, senior physicians, medical residents, psychology interns, social workers, a nurse, and patient partners; 27 episodes of vulnerability practices
n_sample: 20 meetings observed; 27 episodes analyzed; 19 interviews; 7 group discussions; typically 8–10 participants per meeting
authors: Bouchard, M.; Chreim, S.; Langley, A.; Boivin, A.
theories: holding environments (Kahn, 2001; Winnicott, 1965); practice theory; professional work and jurisdictional authority (Abbott, 1988); theory of ritual (Bell, 1992)
keywords: holding environment; multidisciplinary settings; power dynamics; professional work; ritual; social support; theorizing; vulnerability practices
research_question: How and with what perceived effects do professionals in multidisciplinary settings engage in practices that enable the sharing of doubts and insecurities experienced in the performance of their work roles?
mechanism_summary: Professionals operate under institutional norms that equate competence with invulnerability, leaving role-based doubts and insecurities unvoiced. In a dedicated, bounded setting, a ritualized sequence of moves — an invitation to "take the mic," incremental questioning that moves the storyteller from facts to emotions, and multifaceted theorizing that abstracts from the case — legitimates disclosure and activates validation, empathy, and advice from colleagues. Theorizing is pivotal: it enacts the interpretive function of a holding environment, contains difficult emotions, and turns a single disclosure into collective learning. Positive appraisals of past episodes feed back into willingness to disclose in future episodes, while under-validation or power imbalances can derail the ritual and undermine the holding environment.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces "vulnerability practices" as a novel concept naming the ritualized sequence of moves through which group members display, lead others to display, and address work-role doubts and insecurities, and derives an inductive process model linking individual disclosure to communal reflection via theorizing. It reconceptualizes holding environments not as fixed social containers but as precarious, processual accomplishments sustained (or not) episode-by-episode through a tension between social pressure and social support. It also theorizes how power and authority dynamics — especially those tied to multidisciplinarity — seep into vulnerability practices and shape who can be "held" and how.
practical_implication: The authors argue that recognizing sustained engagement in vulnerability practices as "real work" can help practitioners (including academics) legitimize dedicated settings that function as holding environments (or "lifeboats") for coping with pervasive doubts and insecurities at work. For organizations designing such settings in multidisciplinary contexts, attention to ritual predictability, incremental facilitation from facts to emotions, multifaceted theorizing resources, and the mitigation of power asymmetries is essential to avoid under-validation and unintended harm.
limitations: The study relies on a single case — a weekly multidisciplinary meeting in one Quebec primary-care clinic — explicitly designed around vulnerability sharing, and was conducted entirely online during the COVID-19 lockdown, which limits the generalizability of the process model to other settings, formats, and professional configurations.
future_research: Future work could adopt multi-case designs to compare vulnerability practices across contexts and processes, examine online versus in-person dynamics, and explore other groups mixing disciplinary and seniority statuses to understand how to "level the playing field." The authors also suggest investigating how rotating the facilitator role across disciplinary statuses, or convening groups whose members do not otherwise work together, might reshape power dynamics and participants' appraisals of the benefits and risks of vulnerability practices.
abstract: Professional workers are expected to display confidence and mastery in their work roles, and may be hesitant to reveal their doubts and insecurities, especially in multidisciplinary settings characterized by status differences. However, scholars have recognized the benefits of sharing vulnerabilities to create opportunities for social support. We contribute to the literature by revealing how this might be achieved through a ritualized sequence of “vulnerability practices” in a dedicated setting. Based on a qualitative study of a weekly meeting where a multidisciplinary group of professionals shares experiences, we develop a process model that explains how vulnerability practices evolve through phases of individual disclosure and communal reflection bridged by theorizing from experiences. We reveal how a delicate tension between social pressure (to display vulnerabilities) and social support (to contain them) underpins these practices, and how this tension is influenced by power relations associated with multidisciplinarity. We show how participants’ appraisals of past episodes shape their willingness to engage in subsequent episodes, reinforcing or undermining the capacity of the setting to serve as a “holding environment” offering interpretation and containment. We thus reveal holding environments at work to be precarious accomplishments, created and maintained through ritualized vulnerability practices, and imbued by power relations.
apa_citation: Bouchard, M., Chreim, S., Langley, A., & Boivin, A. (2026). Vulnerability practices among professionals in multidisciplinary settings. *Academy of Management Journal*, 69(1), 46-75. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0645

title: Not So Fast? Rapid Response to Voice Leads to Perceived Inauthenticity
methods: Mixed-method, preregistered multi-study design combining qualitative content analysis and between-subjects experiments. Study 1 uses a qualitative-plus-experimental design with doctoral students recalling their advisors; Studies 2 and 3 use online vignette experiments with working adults. Analyses include ANOVA, regression, and moderated mediation (indirect effects via authenticity and responsiveness, with change difficulty as a moderator).
industry: Multiple (academia in Study 1; general part-time/full-time workforce via Prolific Academic in Studies 2 and 3)
units: Individual followers (doctoral students in Study 1; employees in Studies 2 and 3) evaluating leaders
n_sample: Total n = 3,056 across three studies (Study 1: 190 doctoral students from R1 U.S. universities; Study 2: 1,676 employees; Study 3: 710 employees, final sample)
authors: Chon, D.; Sezer, O.; Flynn, F. J.
theories: attribution theory; authenticity in leadership; lay theories of behavior change; person perception
keywords: behavior change; lay beliefs; leader-centric voice; perceived authenticity; rate of change; responsiveness; subsequent voice
research_question: How does the rate at which a leader changes their behavior in response to leader-centric voice shape followers' evaluations of the leader's authenticity, and how do those evaluations in turn affect followers' willingness to voice concerns in the future?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on theories of person perception and attribution in leader-follower relationships, the authors argue that followers hold lay beliefs that true, authentic behavior change takes time and effort because it requires a corresponding shift in the actor's core sense of self. When leaders improve rapidly in response to voice, this violates those lay expectations and triggers an attributional search that leads followers to see the change as spurious rather than reflecting an underlying transformation. The resulting authenticity penalty is amplified when the requested change is difficult (because rapid difficult change contradicts lay beliefs most sharply) and attenuated when the change is easy. Because followers evaluate leaders on authenticity as well as responsiveness, rapid change produces offsetting effects that, on net, reduce followers' willingness to voice future concerns—generating a recursive dynamic in voice processes.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to voice research by identifying the rate of leader response as a previously overlooked factor shaping leader evaluations, challenging the prevailing assumption that faster change is unambiguously better. It introduces perceived authenticity as a second, overlooked basis on which followers judge leader responses to voice (alongside utility and responsiveness) and shows how the interplay between authenticity and responsiveness, moderated by change difficulty, shapes cyclical voice dynamics. It also answers Cha and colleagues' (2019) call to address the temporal dimensions of perceived authenticity by theorizing that authenticity judgments depend on followers' comparison of leaders' past and present selves.
practical_implication: Leaders who aim to demonstrate responsiveness by changing rapidly in response to employee voice should be aware that swift transformations can backfire by eliciting perceptions of inauthenticity, especially when the requested change is difficult, which can in turn discourage subsequent voice. The authors do not simply recommend slowing down, because rapid change also signals responsiveness; instead they suggest leaders weigh these trade-offs and can mitigate the authenticity penalty by proactively communicating about why a change is more or less challenging than followers assume.
limitations: The studies are largely hypothetical vignette-based designs and focus narrowly on leader-centric voice (discretionary feedback about the leader's own behavior) rather than other forms of voice such as concerns about routine business operations. The research does not capture how followers perceive leader changes at the precise moment they occur in ongoing relationships, and it relies primarily on Western-oriented theory and samples, leaving cultural variation in conceptions of the "true self" and authenticity unaddressed.
future_research: Future work could conduct longitudinal or experience-sampling investigations of ongoing leader-follower relationships to examine how change-rate evaluations evolve over multiple timepoints and whether sub-variants of change rates (e.g., constant vs. slow-start-then-flurry) moderate the effect. Researchers could also explore possible asymmetries between judgments of authenticity and inauthenticity, test whether different types of leader behaviors (e.g., interpersonal vs. task-oriented) strengthen or attenuate the authenticity penalty, and replicate the findings using non-Western theoretical perspectives and populations with different conceptions of self.
abstract: Employees voice their concerns in the hope that leaders will change their behavior. But how do employees evaluate these changes? In the present research, we highlight the rate of behavior change as a key factor in determining leader evaluations: Leaders are seen as less authentic when they change rapidly in response to employee voice because people believe that true change takes time. We investigate this claim with three studies that adopt a mixed-method approach (n 5 3,056). In Study 1, PhD students describe rapid improvement in their advisor’s behavior as less authentic than gradual improvement— even when these changes are desired. In Study 2, we employ a stimulus sampling design to test the robustness of this effect. Followers see rapid improvement as less authentic than gradual improvement across a wide array of leader behaviors. In Study 3, we highlight change difficulty as a moderator and subsequent voice as a downstream consequence: When change is hard, rapid improvement elicits a greater authenticity penalty, which undermines followers’ willingness to voice their concerns in the future.
apa_citation: Chon, D., Sezer, O., & Flynn, F. J. (2026). Not so fast? Rapid response to voice leads to perceived inauthenticity. *Academy of Management Journal*, 69(1), 123–148. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.1044

title: Under Pressure to Be Perfect: How Dehumanizing and Rehumanizing Social Cues Lead to Maladaptive and Adaptive Perfectionism in Professional Ballet
methods: Inductive, qualitative grounded-theory study. Data from 19 hours of participant observation at a large U.S. professional ballet company plus 88 semi-structured interviews with 61 professional and semi-professional ballet dancers from 24 companies (in-person and Zoom). Iterative coding moved from open coding to theoretical dimensions and a process model integrating dehumanization and perfectionism.
industry: Professional ballet (performing arts)
country: Primarily United States, with some international dancers
units: Professional and semi-professional ballet dancers across 24 companies of varying size
n_sample: 61 dancers (76% female; ages 17-40; across corps, soloist, and principal ranks)
authors: Goodwin, R. D.; Garrett, L. E.; Block, A. P.
theories: organizational dehumanization; perfectionism theory; social information processing theory
keywords: adaptive perfectionism; maladaptive perfectionism; organizational dehumanization; performance pressure; professional ballet; rehumanization; self-compassion; social information processing; workplace perfectionism
research_question: How do social cues within high-pressure work environments influence the experience of different forms of perfectionism?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on social information processing theory (Salancik & Pfeffer, 1978), the authors theorize that, under high performance pressure, organizational cues that deny workers' fallibility, agency, subjectivity, and individuality are internalized as organizational dehumanization, activating perfectionistic concerns and self-destructive behaviors in a self-reinforcing maladaptive cycle that culminates in breakdowns. Breakdowns open workers to rehumanizing cues from peers and supportive others, which reaffirm their humanity and facilitate internal shifts toward perfectionistic strivings paired with self-compassionate behaviors, constituting an adaptive perfectionism cycle. Because dehumanizing cues persist in the environment, dancers can relapse, but accumulated experience helps them stay adaptive more of the time. Dehumanization and rehumanization thus function as contextual mechanisms that translate the same performance pressure into qualitatively different perfectionism experiences.
theoretical_contribution: The study identifies organizational dehumanization and rehumanization as underlying contextual mechanisms that separate maladaptive from adaptive perfectionism, shifting the perfectionism literature from a predominantly trait-based view toward a situated, social-information-processing account of how workplace cues activate different forms of perfectionism. It contributes to the dehumanization literature by proposing denial of fallibility as a novel form of dehumanization alongside denial of agency, subjectivity, and individuality, and by showing how workers can internalize dehumanizing cues into self-destructive behavior rather than only rejecting them. It also advances theory on performance pressure by positioning dehumanization (vs. rehumanization) as a key moderator that channels pressure into maladaptive versus adaptive responses and by articulating perfectionism as a dynamic, cyclical process fueled by everyday social cues.
practical_implication: Leaders and practitioners in high-pressure workplaces should recognize that perfectionism is shaped by environmental cues, not merely individual disposition, and take responsibility for affirming workers' fallibility, agency, voice, and individuality. Training managers and peers to detect signs of maladaptive perfectionism (e.g., harsh self-criticism, self-harming behaviors) and to deliver rehumanizing cues such as supportive remarks, constructive feedback, and validation of emotional and physical limits can help workers shift toward adaptive perfectionism. The findings are especially relevant for entry-level professionals in competitive industries such as the arts, academia, healthcare, law, elite sport, and tech, where "produce or be replaced" norms cue maladaptive patterns.
limitations: The study may not be readily transferable to all competitive organizational settings: the sample is 76-78% female, which may inflate the salience of dehumanization given gendered dynamics of replaceability and objectification, and professional ballet uniquely emphasizes the physical body, so perfectionism is more embodied than in most workplaces. The inductive qualitative design is not intended to isolate causal effects of dehumanization or rehumanization on perfectionism, and qualitative interviews are subject to biased recall and self-protective framing, making it hard to know whether dancers truly shifted to adaptive perfectionism or reframed experiences more positively.
future_research: Future deductive and experimental studies should test the moderating effects of dehumanization and rehumanization on perfectionism while controlling for trait perfectionism, conscientiousness, and gender, and should explore gendered dynamics in how dehumanization and perfectionism co-develop. Scholars should extend the model to less physically demanding work, investigate other maladaptive responses (e.g., unethical behavior) triggered by internalized dehumanization, and examine self-compassion as an environmentally induced response rather than a purely individual trait. Additional work is encouraged on organizational-level outcomes such as retention and performance tied to adaptive vs. maladaptive perfectionism, and on the nature of performance pressure (including time-vs-precision tensions) across competitive industries.
abstract: Research on workplace perfectionism has focused on harmful consequences of workers’ perfectionism. However, perfectionism is not a uniform experience. Maladaptive and adaptive perfectionism have different outcomes for worker performance and well-being. While perfectionism appears especially prevalent in high performance pressure environments, minimal research has examined how social cues shape workers’ experience of perfectionism. Through an inductive study of professional ballet dancers facing extreme performance pressure, we discovered that social cues influence whether people experience maladaptive or adaptive perfectionism. We found that organizational dehumanizing cues elicited perfectionistic concerns and increased self-destructive behaviors, leading to a vicious cycle of maladaptive perfectionism and an increased likelihood of breakdowns at work. However, rehumanizing cues precipitated a shift toward adaptive perfectionism characterized by perfectionistic strivings and self-compassionate behaviors. We also show how workers can shift back and forth between maladaptive and adaptive perfectionism cycles throughout their careers. We reveal new insights at the intersection of high performance pressure, dehumanization, and perfectionism, offering dehumanization and rehumanization as key mechanisms linking the environment and perfectionism. We also discuss how our findings, though in an extreme context, apply to other high-pressure workplace environments, and open new opportunities to explore the processes of maladaptive and adaptive perfectionism in other domains.
apa_citation: Goodwin, R. D., Garrett, L. E., & Block, A. P. (2026). Under pressure to be perfect: How dehumanizing and rehumanizing social cues lead to maladaptive and adaptive perfectionism in professional ballet. *Academy of Management Journal*, 69(1), 6-45. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0187

title: The Dualities of Multiple Team Membership: Implications for Team Performance and Adaptability
methods: Archival, multilevel quantitative study of sell-side financial analyst teams. The authors extracted authorship data from J. P. Morgan U.S. research reports via ProQuest (2011-2019) and linked reports to I/B/E/S one-year-ahead EPS forecasts to measure team performance (forecast accuracy) and team adaptability (number of forecast updates within one month of the focal report). Team-level MTM, external network heterogeneity (Hoberg-Phillips product cosine similarity across external members' covered firms), external role heterogeneity (Blau index over authorship-order distributions), and internal informational diversity were computed per team-report episode. Hypotheses were tested with 1-1-1 unconflated multilevel modeling (Preacher, Zyphur, & Zhang 2010), with random intercepts for teams, group-mean-centered Level 1 predictors, and grand-mean-centered Level 2 means. Conditional indirect effects for moderated mediation were assessed following Edwards and Lambert (2007) with 95% biased-corrected confidence intervals from 10,000 Monte Carlo simulations (Preacher & Hayes, 2008). Supplementary non-linear analyses, alternative MTM operationalizations (page-adjusted, team-size-adjusted), and robustness checks were conducted. Nine exploratory interviews with sell-side analysts validated role-flexibility assumptions.
industry: Financial services (sell-side equity research / investment banking)
country: United States (J. P. Morgan U.S. firm research reports)
units: Team-report performance episodes nested within analyst teams
n_sample: 20,867 team performance episode observations nested within 473 analyst teams at J. P. Morgan
authors: Lang, T.; de Vries, T. A.; Li, H.; Shaw, J. D.
theories: attention-based view; categorization-elaboration model; social network perspective; team information processing framework
keywords: attention-based perspective; external network heterogeneity; external role heterogeneity; internal informational diversity; multiple team membership; social network perspective; team adaptability; team information processing; team performance
research_question: Why and when does team-level multiple team membership (MTM) positively or negatively impact team effectiveness? Specifically, through which mediating mechanisms does team-level MTM simultaneously benefit team performance and undermine team adaptability, and how does teams' internal informational diversity modulate these indirect relationships?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the social network perspective, higher team-level MTM expands team members' connections to nonredundant external contacts with varied expertise, increasing external network heterogeneity and providing resources that enable efficient, high-quality task execution (i.e., team performance). Drawing on the attention-based view, higher MTM simultaneously induces switching costs that lead members to adopt focused coping strategies and converge on similar external roles, reducing external role heterogeneity and thereby limiting the broad cognitive constructs needed for team adaptability. Integrating these via the team information processing framework, internal informational diversity enables members to combine and integrate externally accessed knowledge (accentuating the performance benefits) and to compensate for missing heterogeneous role experiences through interpersonal elaboration (attenuating the adaptability costs).
theoretical_contribution: The paper reconciles divergent findings in MTM research by identifying two countervailing mediating mechanisms (external network heterogeneity and external role heterogeneity) that jointly explain MTM's curvilinear team-level effects, and by distinguishing team performance from team adaptability as two effectiveness dimensions differentially affected by these mechanisms. It integrates the social network and attention-based perspectives through the team information processing framework, positioning internal informational diversity as a common boundary condition. In doing so, it extends seminal MTM theory (O'Leary et al., 2011), the categorization-elaboration model, team boundary-spanning research, and the diversity-bandwidth trade-off literature by adopting an open-systems view of internal and external team diversity.
practical_implication: Team leaders can monitor external network and role heterogeneity as intermediate outcomes of MTM and intervene (e.g., through role rotation schemes and deliberate composition of external ties) to preserve performance benefits while protecting adaptability. Team members can anticipate the consequences of joining additional parallel teams based on the composition of those teams, and higher-level administrators can decide when to deploy MTM structures: favoring MTM for high-performance, knowledge-intensive projects while considering alternative or hybrid structures when rapid adaptation is essential, especially by building internally informationally diverse teams.
limitations: The model was tested in a single organization (J. P. Morgan) with comparable financial analyst teams, limiting generalizability to other industries and geographies. The study relies on archival proxies (authorship orders for roles; Hoberg-Phillips similarity for network/role/information diversity), which may not fully capture the underlying constructs. Team adaptability was conceptualized broadly as the rate of forecast updates, without distinguishing types of adaptation, and team performance and adaptability showed an unexpected negative relationship warranting further investigation.
future_research: Future work should conduct replication studies in other industries (e.g., manufacturing) and regions (e.g., Africa, Europe, Asia), operationalize external network and role mechanisms in alternative ways, and integrate structural adaptation theory to examine which types of adaptations are hindered or enabled by MTM. Scholars should extend the model to team viability and turnover, investigate whether autonomy over role choice shapes MTM effects in contexts where roles are organizationally assigned, and differentiate task-related from demographic diversity in moderating MTM's team-level consequences.
abstract: Researchers recognize that multiple team membership (MTM) represents a doubleedged sword for teams, but the precise mechanisms that explain the countervailing effects and contingencies that modulate these relationships remain underexplored. We argue that team-level MTM allows team members to access heterogeneous external resources through more diverse external-network ties, benefiting team performance. In contrast, we advance ideas that team-level MTM can negatively impact team adaptability by diminishing the heterogeneity of team members’ external roles. To reconcile these divergent arguments, we introduce an informational perspective to suggest that teams can strengthen the benefits and mitigate the downsides of MTM when they have high levels of internal informational diversity. Using data from financial forecast reports published by 473 analyst teams in J. P. Morgan from 2011 to 2019, we find support for our integrated theoretical framework. MTM indeed appears to be beneficial for team performance through increased external network heterogeneity, but detrimental for team adaptability through reduced external role heterogeneity. Teams’ internal informational diversity was furthermore found to accentuate these indirect benefits of MTM for team performance, while simultaneously attenuating its indirect detriments for team adaptability.
apa_citation: Lang, T., de Vries, T. A., Li, H., & Shaw, J. D. (2026). The dualities of multiple team membership: Implications for team performance and adaptability. *Academy of Management Journal*, 69(1), 176-203. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2024.0176

title: When Dirty Work Inspires: The Joint Effect of Work Dirtiness and Perceived Stigma on Pro-Organizational Behavior
methods: Multi-study quantitative design combining three field surveys and one scenario-based experiment. Study 1: cross-sectional survey of 179 Chinese at-home care nurses from 27 hospitals, analyzed with path modeling accounting for hospital-level clustering. Study 2: three-wave time-lagged online survey of 302 U.S. employees across diverse occupations/industries with temporally separated predictors and outcomes. Study 3: scenario-based experiment manipulating high vs. low work dirtiness in a fitness-trainer ('Iron House') context with U.S. full-time workers, testing interaction with measured occupational stigma. Study 4: two-week experience-sampling study with daily surveys of 194 Chinese employees (1,798 person-day observations) analyzed with multilevel structural equation modeling. All four studies used Johnson-Neyman analyses to probe the interaction between work dirtiness and perceived occupational stigma on OCB, with moderated-mediation tests via organizational identification.
industry: Study 1: healthcare (at-home nursing). Study 2: mixed (technology, manufacturing, public sector, healthcare, retail, service). Study 3: fitness training (experimental scenario) with full-time U.S. workers. Study 4: mixed (information/software/technology, manufacturing, finance, hospitality, public hygiene and social services, retail, construction, real estate, transportation, public administration, entertainment, others).
country: Study 1: China; Study 2: United States; Study 3: United States; Study 4: China
units: Individual employees; Study 4 also uses daily (person-day) observations
n_sample: Study 1: 179 nurses from 27 hospitals; Study 2: 302 employees (three-wave); Study 3: U.S. full-time workers in a scenario experiment; Study 4: 194 employees with 1,798 daily observations; plus two supplementary studies (168 restaurant servers and 128 U.S. security guards) testing identity accessibility
authors: Peng, A. C.; Kim, D.; Schaubroeck, J. M.; Zeng, W.
theories: self-categorization theory; social identity theory
keywords: dirty work; normative fit; organizational citizenship behavior; organizational identification; perceived occupational stigma; pro-organizational behavior; self-categorization theory; social identity salience; work dirtiness
research_question: Why do many individuals performing dirty work engage positively with their jobs and organizations whereas others become disengaged? Specifically, how do the experience of performing dirty tasks (work dirtiness) and the perception of being stigmatized by the public (perceived occupational stigma) jointly shape pro-organizational behavior through processes of social-identity salience.
mechanism_summary: Drawing on self-categorization theory, the authors argue that perceived occupational stigma renders a stigmatized work-related social category cognitively accessible, and that engaging in dirty tasks supplies the "normative fit" that activates this social identity by matching behavior to societal stereotypes about the job. When both work dirtiness and perceived stigma are high, the work-related social identity becomes salient, heightening organizational identification and motivating employees to support coworkers, customers, and the organization through OCBs. When perceived stigma is low, high work dirtiness no longer creates normative fit; instead, dirty tasks are appraised as a threat to personal identity, prompting employees to psychologically distance themselves from the organization and reducing pro-organizational behavior.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends dirty-work research by providing a theoretical account of why individuals engaged in dirty work develop a salient work-related social identity rather than merely describing the tactics dirty workers use once that identity is presumably already salient. It conceptually and empirically disentangles "work dirtiness" (the direct experience of degrading tasks) from "perceived occupational stigma" (beliefs about societal views of the occupation), showing that the two are distinct constructs that operate jointly via normative fit to determine identity salience and pro-organizational behavior. In doing so, the authors reconcile contradictory prior findings showing both positive and negative responses to dirty work and broaden attention from occupational identity to organizational identity as a locus of social categorization.
practical_implication: Managers of jobs involving dirty tasks should recognize that employees who do not perceive strong occupational stigma are more likely to interpret work dirtiness as a personal-identity threat and disengage, so these employees — not only those who already hold strong stigma beliefs — warrant proactive communication framing dirty tasks as meaningful and professionally skilled work. Symbolic management efforts (stories, rituals, language, status symbols) are more effective when they explicitly acknowledge the unfavorable societal stereotypes about the job rather than ignoring them, because highlighting those stereotypes reinforces normative fit and strengthens the work-related social identity that drives pro-organizational behavior.
limitations: Three of the four studies relied on self-reports, which may introduce common method bias, though the authors mitigated this through temporal separation, experimental manipulation, and daily repeated measures, and note that interaction effects cannot be explained by common method bias. Data come only from the United States and China, limiting generalizability to other cultural contexts, and occupational stigma was conceptualized and measured at the individual level rather than as a shared group perception. The theory and tests focused on between-person processes; the authors examined but did not find support for a within-person daily process linking fluctuations in work dirtiness to identity salience and OCB.
future_research: Future work should test the theory using externally rated pro-organizational behavior, examine whether group-level shared perceptions of stigma add explanatory power beyond individual perceptions, and explore "incongruent" cases such as workers in tainted occupations performing ordinary tasks or workers in low-stigma occupations performing highly dirty tasks. Researchers could also investigate pivotal or crucial moments (e.g., a police officer using force) that temporarily heighten work dirtiness and activate social identity, and consider how comparative fit (alongside normative fit) and other forms of societal stigma (e.g., discrimination, social exclusion) guide identity processes and workplace behavior.
abstract: Research reveals that many employees derive esteem and pride from their work despite indignities attributed to their occupations. Whereas studies have identified what these “dirty workers” think and do to maintain a positive work identity, it remains unclear why they do so whereas others tend to disengage from work. We draw from selfcategorization theory to develop a model that conceptualizes how the salience of one’s social identity is determined jointly by engaging in dirty tasks (i.e., work dirtiness) and perceived occupational stigma. When perceiving that others in society stigmatize one’s occupation, high work dirtiness creates a match between one’s behavior and societal expectations linked to one’s job. Based on this mechanism of normative fit, work-related social identity is salient, encouraging pro-organizational behavior. Conversely, we argue that when stigma is perceived as low, individuals experiencing high dirtiness at work become more concerned about their personal identity, reducing engagement with their organization. Results from four studies using survey and experimental designs largely support our hypotheses. This research reconciles seemingly contradictory results concerning employee responses toward dirty work and provides a theoretical explanation for why people with dirty jobs engage positively with their work and organizations.
apa_citation: Peng, A. C., Kim, D., Schaubroeck, J. M., & Zeng, W. (2026). When dirty work inspires: The joint effect of work dirtiness and perceived stigma on pro-organizational behavior. *Academy of Management Journal*, 69(1), 149-175. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2024.0108

title: On the Move: The Impact of Internal Mobility and Internal Comobility on Unit- and Organization-Level Outcomes
methods: Longitudinal panel study using monthly store-level data from a Fortune 500 U.S. retail firm (2016-2018); two-way fixed-effects regressions (store and month fixed effects) for established stores and separate cross-sectional OLS for new stores; rolling six-month measures of transfers in/out decomposed into comobile, multisource, and multi-destination categories; focal DV is monthly retail sales
industry: Retail (single Fortune 500 U.S. retail firm)
units: Retail stores (establishment-months); 884 established stores and 259 new stores
n_sample: 26,910 store-month observations for established stores; 259 new stores for the new-unit comparison
authors: Ray, C.; Nyberg, A. J.; Maltarich, M. A.; Reilly, G.
theories: human capital resources (HCR) theory; human capital theory; social capital theory
keywords: comobile transfers; firm-specific human capital; human capital resources; internal comobility; internal mobility; multisource transfers; social capital; transfers in; transfers out; unit performance
research_question: Whether, when, and why does internal mobility (transfers into and out of a unit) affect unit- and organization-level performance, and how do the attributes of transfers—specifically comobile versus multisource transfers in, and comobile versus multi-destination transfers out—shape those effects?
mechanism_summary: The authors integrate human capital resources (HCR) theory with the internal mobility literature to argue that internal transfers reconfigure a unit's HCR in two distinct ways: by importing/exporting firm-specific human capital (knowledge, skills, and abilities honed to the organization) and by moving intact social structures when employees travel together. Transfers in should raise receiving-unit performance because firm-specific human capital aligns decisions with organizational routines and raises productivity; multisource transfers in add breadth of unit-specific knowledge while retaining enough firm-wide overlap to communicate, yielding larger gains than comobile transfers in (whose value rests on pre-existing social ties that are redundant with firm-specific familiarity). Transfers out should depress supplying-unit performance by removing firm-specific HCR, and comobile transfers out should amplify that loss by dissolving intact social structures. These amplified effects should be stronger in new units, where path-dependent history and established routines do not yet buffer HCR change.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reframes internal mobility from an individual-level phenomenon into a collective-level mechanism for HCR reallocation across units, introducing new constructs (comobile vs. multisource transfers in; comobile vs. multi-destination transfers out) and showing that their effects on unit and organizational outcomes diverge. By theorizing and testing the net organizational effect—gains in receiving units net of losses in supplying units—it answers Dalton's (1997) long-standing "domino effect" question and shows that reallocating firm-specific HCR is value-creating for the firm overall only when transfers in are broadly sourced. It also contributes to the macro resource-reallocation literature by treating human capital as a strategically allocated, nonfinancial resource distinct from financial capital.
practical_implication: Managers should actively reallocate internal talent rather than minimize disruption, but they should source transfers from as many different units as possible rather than moving intact groups. In practical terms, sourcing one transfer each from several supplying units yields substantially larger net gains than moving a comobile cluster from a single unit; transferring more than one worker from any given supplying unit brings diminishing benefit and may be outweighed by the cost to that unit. The benefits are largest when populating new units or stores, where firm-specific knowledge from multisource movers has the strongest effect on early performance.
limitations: The study uses data from a single U.S. Fortune 500 retail firm, so generalizability to other industries, countries, and organizational forms is untested. HCR is theorized as the underlying mechanism but is not measured directly — the authors do not observe the quality of transferred human capital, the content of relationships, or diversity of knowledge structures within units. All transferred employees are treated as equivalent even though they occupied different roles (e.g., managers vs. frontline workers), and the analyses cannot separate managerial from non-managerial transfer effects.
future_research: Future research should test boundary conditions across industries and settings, examine the duration and decay of transfer effects on HCR over longer horizons, and directly measure the quality of transferred human capital, the content of pre-existing relationships, and within-unit knowledge diversity to test the proposed mechanism more cleanly. Scholars should also model the causes of transfer decisions (e.g., employee self-selection into higher-performing units), explore more complex HCR configurations such as fault lines and subgroup dynamics among transfers, and untangle role-specific effects (e.g., manager versus frontline transfers) on unit outcomes.
abstract: Internal mobility is commonly used to reallocate talent within organizations. Despite its prevalence and potential implications for organizational performance, most prior work has focused on the individual-level outcomes associated with internal mobility. That is, there has been relatively limited theoretical and empirical attention to the mechanisms that connect internal mobility to unitand organization-level outcomes, making it difficult to predict when or why internal mobility is beneficial to organizations. To address this oversight, we draw from human and social capital literatures to build theory to predict when and why internal mobility will have unit-level implications. We test this by examining a period of growth in a Fortune 500 company and evaluate gains and losses associated with moving employees within firms, including distinguishing between two types of internal movers—those who come from different units in the organization (solo movers) and those who come from the same unit in the organization (comobile movers). In doing so, we contribute to both internal mobility and strategic human capital resource literatures and provide actionable insights regarding when to source internal movers, and where from.
apa_citation: Ray, C., Nyberg, A. J., Maltarich, M. A., & Reilly, G. (2026). On the move: The impact of internal mobility and internal comobility on unit- and organization-level outcomes. *Academy of Management Journal*, 69(1), 76-98. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0311

title: Stewardship at Scale: A New Editorial Term at AMJ
methods: Editorial (From the Editor); reflective and programmatic argument that synthesizes prior AMJ editorials and articulates an agenda for the 24th editorial term
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Roberson, Q.
theories: contributive justice; organizational justice; stewardship theory
keywords: contributive justice; developmental reviewing; editorial infrastructure; editorial stewardship; generative AI in scholarly publishing; inclusion; knowledge translation; peer review; reviewer development; scale and complexity
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary: Roberson advances a normative, programmatic argument rather than a causal model. She contends that AMJ's very success — roughly 2,000 annual submissions, a 13.8 five-year impact factor, and a global author base — has intensified demands on editorial, reviewing, and developmental systems in ways that threaten the journal's core commitments to intellectual pluralism and developmental reviewing. Building on the "stewardship" orientations of prior editors Beyer, Tsui, and Rynes, she argues that sustaining excellence at scale requires editorial infrastructures intentionally oriented toward contributive justice and inclusion: systems that broaden meaningful participation in authorship, reviewing, and editorial roles while preserving the standards that define the journal. The mechanism is institutional rather than individual — values endure only to the extent that the systems enacting them can adapt.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial frames editorial leadership as "inclusive stewardship" and advances contributive justice and inclusion as central editorial principles for the 24th term, extending prior editors' stewardship orientation by explicitly foregrounding individuals' belonging within an institutional context and their access to meaningful opportunities to participate in and be recognized for core scholarly activities. It reframes AMJ not simply as a publication outlet but as a field-shaping developmental institution whose continued excellence depends on the intentional evolution of editorial systems — aligning developmental effort with scholarly value creation, investing in reviewer development as field-building work, and cultivating a more inclusive, globally connected pipeline of contributors.
practical_implication: Authors, reviewers, and editors are called to approach submission, review, and editorial service as a collective enterprise. The 24th editorial team will streamline workflows, publish synthesized guidance from prior "From the Editors" essays to support manuscript readiness, run review development workshops and Community Accelerator outreach, expand knowledge-translation efforts (e.g., The Lit Review podcast; an "AMJ Behind the Scenes" feature), and ensure transparent, ethical use of generative AI consistent with AOM's AI policy. Success will be monitored through mixed-methods indicators — including topical and methodological diversity, review timeliness, and qualitative feedback on clarity, developmental value, and perceived fairness — treated as tools for responsible oversight rather than targets.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Roberson sets a forward agenda for the 24th editorial term: developing pre-submission guidance that synthesizes accumulated wisdom from prior "From the Editors" essays; aligning developmental initiatives with AOM Divisions, Interest Groups, and PDWs; investing in reviewer development workshops and Community Accelerator outreach to broaden access to AMJ's editorial processes; and expanding knowledge-translation efforts such as The Lit Review podcast and an "AMJ Behind the Scenes" feature. She also calls for continued, transparent engagement with generative AI in authoring and reviewing, and for mixed-methods monitoring of whether editorial systems enact the journal's pluralistic and developmental commitments at scale.
abstract: FROM THE EDITOR STEWARDSHIP AT SCALE: A NEW EDITORIAL TERM AT AMJ
apa_citation: Roberson, Q. (2026). Stewardship at scale: A new editorial term at AMJ. *Academy of Management Journal*, 69(1), 1-5. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2026.4001

title: Anticipatory Impression Management for Potential Adverse Events: Positive Framing in the Wake of Short Seller Attacks on a Competitor
methods: Longitudinal panel analysis of 11,824 firm-quarter observations (1,989 firms with a direct competitor attacked by a short seller, 2007-2022), using content analysis of CEO earnings-call transcripts with Loughran-McDonald sentiment dictionaries; fixed-effects regression with firm and year controls; TNIC-3 text-based competitor identification; robustness checks including alternative estimation windows and unscaled dependent variable.
industry: Publicly traded firms across multiple industries listed on NASDAQ or NYSE
units: firm-quarters (CEO earnings conference calls at firms whose TNIC-3 competitors were attacked by short sellers)
n_sample: 1,989 firms; 344 unique short attacks; 11,824 firm-quarter observations
authors: Song, R.; Connelly, B. L.; Ketchen, D. J.; Shi, W.
theories: anticipatory impression management; awareness-motivation-capability (AMC) perspective; impression management theory
keywords: CEO communication; anticipatory impression management; awareness-motivation-capability; competitor spillover; earnings conference calls; positive framing; short seller attacks; theory elaboration
research_question: Under what conditions do firms engage in anticipatory impression management (AIM) — specifically, CEO positive framing in earnings conference calls — following a short seller attack on a competitor, and how do awareness, motivation, and capability shape this response?
mechanism_summary: When a short seller attacks a competitor, focal-firm CEOs anticipate that they could be the next target and use positive language in earnings calls to persuade short sellers to look elsewhere. Drawing on Chen's (1996) awareness-motivation-capability logic, the authors argue the AIM response is amplified when the focal firm is more aware the threat applies to them (high product-market overlap with the attacked firm), more motivated to act (the attacked competitor suffered a severe stock-price decline), and more capable of credibly projecting positivity (strong recent performance makes the framing believable). Positive framing functions as a deterrent signal that raises short sellers' expected cost of attacking the focal firm.
theoretical_contribution: The study elaborates AIM theory by extending it from known upcoming events with uncertain consequences to potential upcoming events with known adverse consequences, shifting uncertainty from the response to the event itself. By importing Chen's awareness-motivation-capability framework, it specifies the boundary conditions under which anticipation of a potential adverse event triggers AIM and redirects AIM from an investor audience toward an adversarial audience (short sellers). Secondarily, it contributes to the short-selling literature by demonstrating a previously overlooked spillover effect of attacks on non-targeted competitors and showing that CEO language can operate as an ex ante attack-prevention tool.
practical_implication: CEOs should treat a short seller attack on a competitor as a signal to proactively shape firm narrative in earnings calls, since positive framing is associated with reduced short interest and can deter attacks before they happen. Boards can encourage CEOs to use AIM selectively, drawing executives' attention to product-market similarity, the severity of the competitor's stock-price decline, and the firm's own credibility to perform the tactic. However, firms with weak recent performance should be cautious: overly positive language without supporting fundamentals is not credible and can backfire by raising investor expectations.
limitations: The authors could not fully account for why particular firms are susceptible to short seller attacks, and their archival data cannot directly observe the CEO decision-making process that leads to AIM. The goodness-of-fit improvement of the full model over the baseline was only marginal, possibly reflecting nonlinearities or an excess of nonsignificant controls. The study also focuses on one class of potential adverse event (short seller attacks), leaving open how AIM operates against other anticipated threats.
future_research: Scholars could test these ideas experimentally (e.g., policy-capturing studies with executives) to unpack decision-making; explore other forms of awareness such as shared buyers/suppliers or socially interconnected firms; integrate AIM with protection motivation theory, threat rigidity theory, signaling theory, and the attention-based view to build a multiplex theory of proactive responses; and examine other potential adverse events (hostile takeovers, market entry, labor disputes, regulatory changes) where CEO language may serve as a first line of defense.
abstract: Anticipatory impression management (AIM) provides a theoretical foundation for understanding the tactics organizations use to mitigate the potential adverse consequences of known upcoming events. We extend this theorizing to encompass potential upcoming events with known adverse consequences. We posit that a potential event is more likely to induce AIM to the extent that the firm is aware the event might occur, motivated to mitigate its potential consequences, and capable of engaging in tactics that could reduce the event’s likelihood. We test our theorizing in the scenario wherein a competitor has been attacked by a short seller, which raises the specter of a short seller attack on the focal firm. We contend that, following an attack on a competitor, chief executive officers use positive framing in earnings conference calls as an AIM tactic to deter short sellers. Examination of 1,989 firms with a direct competitor attacked by a short seller confirms our ideas. Our study thus extends the boundaries of theory on AIM and broadens research on short selling to encompass principles of attack prevention.
apa_citation: Song, R., Connelly, B. L., Ketchen, D. J., & Shi, W. (2026). Anticipatory impression management for potential adverse events: Positive framing in the wake of short seller attacks on a competitor. *Academy of Management Journal*, 69(1), 99-122. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0914

title: More-than-capitalist economies: Insights from community supported agriculture, tiny houses and hitchhiking in Denmark
methods: Qualitative focus group study. Seven focus groups (6-9 participants each, 52 in total) conducted Fall/Winter 2023-24 with professional stakeholders mixed across consumption domains and organizational sectors, recruited by first mapping the institutional and organizational landscape of food, housing and mobility in Denmark (public, civil, private). Groups were loosely moderated with open-ended questions on work practices, sustainability approaches, and relationships to consumers, plus a future-imaginaries visioning exercise. Analysis maps mainstream vs. alternative consumption-production relationships and applies Gibson-Graham's 'reading for economic difference' to three in-depth grassroots cases (CSA, tiny house project, hitchhiking).
industry: Food, housing and mobility (three high-emission consumption domains), spanning mainstream actors (supermarkets, food production companies, pension-fund property investors, public transport, private-car lobby) and grassroots actors (food cooperatives, regenerative farms, tiny-house projects, non-profit housing, ridesharing, hitchhiking)
units: Professional stakeholders / organizations across food, housing and mobility
n_sample: 52 participants across seven focus groups
authors: Aagaard, L. K.
theories: capitalocentrism critique; diverse economies framework (Gibson-Graham, 2006b, 2020); reading for economic difference; sufficiency
keywords: Community supported agriculture; Consumption-production relationships; Diverse economies; Hitchhiking; More-than-capitalist; Sufficiency; Tiny house
research_question: How can reading for economic difference across the domains of food, housing and mobility reveal the way more-than-capitalist economic activities can show a path towards more sustainable, sufficiency-oriented consumption-production relationships? Concretely: how do grassroots organizations offer alternative approaches to sustainable consumption that exist alongside, and challenge, dominant capitalist models?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: capitalocentrism vs. diverse / more-than-capitalist economies; efficiency-oriented (profit-driven) vs. sufficiency-oriented (collective) economic models; mainstream vs. grassroots actors
- Method-as-lens: "reading for economic difference" — making subordinated, less-visible economic activities visible and granting them independent agency (the iceberg metaphor)
- Cases (reading for difference across three domains): community supported agriculture (food), a tiny house project (housing), hitchhiking (mobility)
- Cross-cutting dimensions deduced from the cases: 1) spatial (local proximity, direct relationship to land/community over global supply-chain distance); 2) temporal (long-term, flexible engagement with time over immediate, predictable market exchange); 3) relational (face-to-face interaction, interdependency, trust and community over individualism and profit maximization)
- Boundary conditions: the Danish welfare-state context means these cases pursue ecological/relational aims rather than urgent responses to unmet needs (unlike U.S. solidarity economies); none of the cases is fully independent of capitalist structures (each still relies on money and resources)

The paper argues that economic systems are performative — how the economy is conceptualized and enacted shapes which sustainability strategies become possible. Efficiency-oriented sustainability reproduces profit-driven, market-based relationships, whereas sufficiency-oriented practices align with collective, more-than-capitalist forms of organizing. Using Gibson-Graham's diverse economies lens, the three grassroots cases are shown to share spatial, temporal and relational characteristics that decommodify consumption-production relationships and foster ethical and ecological bonds, illustrating a sufficiency-oriented pathway out of the dominant capitalist paradigm.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to diverse economies scholarship by bringing the framework into sustainable consumption research and, rather than studying single sectors or organizations in isolation, reading for economic difference across multiple high-emission domains simultaneously. Its central contribution is a crosscutting framework of three dimensions — spatial, temporal and relational — that travel across food, housing and mobility and serve as anchor points for identifying and framing more-than-capitalist economies as a lens for sustainable, sufficiency-oriented consumption-production relationships.
practical_implication: For more-than-capitalist economies to proliferate from the margins, the paper argues for alternative, collective governance that integrates municipalities, funding bodies and regulatory agencies across public and private sectors. Public funding and subsidies, accessible public land and facilities, and public procurement policies that prioritize cooperatives, CSAs and other sufficiency-oriented enterprises can foster these models, alongside knowledge-sharing networks, hybrid public-grassroots partnerships, and education that brings these economic concepts into public discourse.
limitations: The study is situated in a Danish welfare-state context with relatively low poverty, so the cases pursue slowing societal metabolism and reducing ecological harm rather than responding to injustices of unmet food, housing or mobility needs, limiting transferability to contexts where diverse economies arise as resistance to social, economic and racial exclusion. The paper also frames itself as one initial, exploratory step at the intersection of sufficiency and more-than-capitalist economies, and the long-term social and ecological implications of the cases are not assessed.
future_research: The paper calls for further empirical investigation into the long-term social and ecological implications of more-than-capitalist initiatives, and for assessing the effectiveness of policy interventions that support them so as to refine future regulatory and funding strategies. It also invites continued attention to spatial proximity, long-term engagement and community-building in developing consumption-production relationships for a sustainable future.
abstract: Furthermore, more-than-capitalist economic activities in this landscape are explored. By zooming in on three cases, community supported agriculture, tiny houses and hitchhiking, I draw attention to diverse economies as a lens to reframe consumption-production relationships in a sufficiency-oriented direction. I contribute to this field by proposing three key dimensions in which more-than-capitalist actors share characteristics across food, housing and mobility: 1) spatial; 2) temporal; and 3) relational. Highlighting these, the paper calls for further research and attention to spatial proximity, long-term engagement and community-building in developing consumption-production relationships for a sustainable future.
apa_citation: Aagaard, L. K. (2026). More-than-capitalist economies: Insights from community supported agriculture, tiny houses and hitchhiking in Denmark. *Ecological Economics*, 240, 108831. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108831

title: The Real Effects of Protecting Biodiversity
methods: Continuous-treatment difference-in-differences (DiD) and stacked event-study DiD on a 1990-2021 panel of US production establishments; OLS and PPML estimators with establishment, industry-year, and state-year fixed effects; establishment-clustered standard errors.
industry: US production establishments reporting toxic releases (EPA TRI), linked to publicly listed parent firms (Compustat/CRSP)
units: Production establishments and their parent firms
n_sample: 18,341 establishments linked to 2,574 parent firms (165,500 establishment-year observations)
authors: Akbari, Amir; Ng, Lilian; Pham, Man Duy (Marty); Yu, Jing
theories: biodiversity finance; spatial finance frameworks (Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures, 2022)
keywords: biodiversity; industrial pollution; protected areas; real effects; regulatory risk; spatial finance
research_question: How do designations of nearby protected areas influence the operational, environmental, and financial outcomes of proximate firms, and through what adaptive strategies and channels (e.g., regulatory oversight) do firms respond to the stricter governance requirements these designations impose?
mechanism_summary: Proximity to newly designated protected areas raises compliance costs, permitting complexity, and reputational and regulatory scrutiny for nearby establishments. Firms respond primarily by scaling down production (lower sales, productivity, and employment) rather than investing in cleaner abatement technologies, yielding a roughly 6 percent reduction in on-site toxic emissions for an interquartile increase in CBE. A falsification test on off-site releases and a PADDD (downgrading/downsizing/degazettement) test support a localized, enduring regulatory channel. These establishment-level disruptions aggregate to lower parent-firm profitability, productivity, and market valuation, indicating that biodiversity-related regulatory exposure is financially material.
theoretical_contribution: The study contributes to the emerging field of biodiversity finance by introducing CBE, a transparent, scalable, spatially explicit measure of establishment-level biodiversity-regulatory exposure, and by documenting a regulatory channel through which conservation mandates shape corporate environmental behavior and firm value. It distinguishes spatially targeted, enduring biodiversity shocks from transitory, diffuse climate-related risks, and aligns with emerging spatial finance frameworks that incorporate geospatial environmental data into financial analysis.
practical_implication: For policymakers, the "30 by 30" conservation agenda delivers environmental benefits but imposes material costs on pollution-intensive firms, implying a need for transitional support and regulatory clarity. For firms and investors, proximity to protected areas is a salient, financially material risk that should be incorporated into location decisions, strategic planning, valuation, risk management, and capital allocation.
limitations: The authors caution that protected-area designations may not be random (site selection correlates with local economic conditions), so despite extensive fixed effects some selection bias may remain and several findings (e.g., enforcement effects) are presented as suggestive rather than definitive causal estimates. CBE captures only localized on-site exposure and does not reflect supply-chain dependencies or ecological risks in unprotected areas, and high-frequency return tests are impractical given heterogeneous, multi-facility designation timing.
future_research: The paper implies opportunities to extend the CBE framework to other countries and to supply-chain or value-chain biodiversity dependencies beyond localized on-site exposure, to further disentangle the regulatory enforcement versus reputational channels, and to examine firm adaptation strategies such as cross-border outsourcing in response to biodiversity-related regulatory pressure.
abstract: Protected areas are a central instrument in biodiversity conservation. However, their broader implica­ tions for corporate behavior and financial performance remain underexplored. This study examines how proximity to newly designated protected areas shapes establishment-level environmental, opera­ tional, and financial outcomes from 1990 to 2021. We find that nearby establishments significantly reduce toxic emissions, driven not by cleaner technologies but by production cutbacks and workforce contraction. Exploratory evidence suggests that heightened regulatory oversight may be one channel that constrains operations and is associated with lower profitability and market value. These results un­ derscore the environmental benefits of conservation but also reveal the material costs imposed on affected firms. As global conservation initiatives expand, our findings show that biodiversity-related regulatory exposure constitutes a material financial risk that should be incorporated into policy design, corporate strategy, and capital allocation decisions.
apa_citation: Akbari, A., Ng, L., Pham, M. D. (M.), & Yu, J. (2026). The real effects of protecting biodiversity. *Review of Finance*, 30(1), 193-230. https://doi.org/10.1093/rof/rfaf055

title: Do Investors care about the Rainforest? Evidence from Voluntary Carbon Offsets around the World
methods: Event study using the Fama-French Three-Factor Model (and Constant Mean Return Model) to compute cumulative average abnormal returns (CAARs) over multiple event windows around carbon-credit retirement announcements; significance tested with a non-parametric Wilcoxon signed-rank test and a CAAR t-test; cross-sectional OLS regressions of CARs on a CCB dummy, size, and market-to-book; sample partitioned before/after the January 2023 Guardian article shock.
industry: Publicly listed firms across five most-active NACE sectors: Mining and Quarrying, Manufacturing, Motor Vehicles, Transportation and Storage, and Financial and Insurance Activities
country: Global (firms in Asia, North America, Oceania, South and Central America, and Western Europe); forestry credits predominantly from rainforest projects
units: Publicly listed companies retiring forestry-related voluntary carbon credits (retirement announcements as events)
n_sample: 781 listed firms; 3,072 forestry-credit retirements (1,051 retirements by 325 CCB-credit firms), representing 116 million tons of CO2
authors: Allen, Franklin; Behr, Patrick; Cosenza, Riccardo; Nowak, Eric
theories: biodiversity finance (Flammer et al., 2025; Giglio et al., 2024); event study methodology (MacKinlay, 1997); signaling theory / greenwashing
keywords: biodiversity; carbon offsets; carbon pricing; climate change; climate finance; rainforest; voluntary carbon markets
research_question: How do investors react to publicly listed firms' announcements of voluntary forestry-related carbon-credit retirements, and does the biodiversity impact of those credits (as signaled by CCB certification) and the January 2023 Guardian "worthless offsets" article shape the market reaction?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Firm size, market-to-book ratio

Carbon-credit retirements are publicly observable signals of corporate climate action, but their credibility is contested. The paper argues investors reprice firms based on the perceived environmental integrity of the offsets they retire: after a quasi-exogenous shock to public trust (the Guardian's claim that >90% of rainforest offsets are "worthless"), retirements of standard non-CCB forestry credits earn significantly negative abnormal returns, consistent with greenwashing concerns. CCB certification acts as a credible biodiversity signal that mitigates (but does not eliminate) this penalty, leaving CCB-credit retirements with neutral, statistically insignificant reactions.
theoretical_contribution: The study is among the first to examine investor reactions to voluntary carbon-credit retirements and their biodiversity impact, extending the green-finance event-study literature (e.g., green bonds, eco-friendly behavior) into the Voluntary Carbon Market. It shows that markets distinguish offsets by perceived environmental credibility, treating biodiversity co-benefit certification (CCB) as a signal that softens reputational/greenwashing penalties, thereby linking biodiversity finance to firm value.
practical_implication: Results underscore the need for enhanced disclosure standards, independent auditing, and stricter certification criteria in voluntary carbon markets to restore investor confidence and prevent misleading net-zero claims. Firms that integrate credible, high-quality offsets with certified biodiversity impact are likely to benefit from stronger investor trust and reduced reputational risk.
limitations: Most CAAR differences between CCB and non-CCB credits are economically but not statistically significant at conventional levels; the authors attribute this to limited statistical power in a highly heterogeneous sample. Voluntary-market offset prices are OTC, opaque, and not standardized, so a reliable time series of offset prices could not be constructed. CCB certification is used as a proxy for biodiversity impact because stand-alone biodiversity credits are too scarce to measure directly.
future_research: Constructing a comprehensive, reliable time series of voluntary offset prices is flagged as a challenge beyond the scope of this article. The puzzle of why firms voluntarily engage in costly climate-mitigation and biodiversity activities that are at best neutral (and potentially harmful) to shareholder value invites further investigation, as does tracking the developing market for stand-alone biodiversity credits.
abstract: We explore how investors react to firms’ biodiversity-focused activities in the Voluntary Carbon Market.
apa_citation: Allen, F., Behr, P., Cosenza, R., & Nowak, E. (2026). Do investors care about the rainforest? Evidence from voluntary carbon offsets around the world. *Review of Finance*, 30(1), 321–349. https://doi.org/10.1093/rof/rfaf059

title: Practice Paper—AI-Driven Behavioral Nudges for Organizations: An Integrative System for Sustainable Resource Management
methods: Multiphase longitudinal field study with three phases; neural-network time-series (NNTS) forecasting benchmarked against tree-based, ARIMA, regression-with-ARIMA-errors, gradient-boosting, and LSTM models; between-firm field experiment of planning-prompt curtailment emails (control vs. experimental) analyzed with linear mixed-effects models (firm random effects, fixed-effect controls, ARMA error structure); plus a supplementary online survey.
industry: Large industrial energy users spanning nine industries (e.g., manufacturing, food and beverage, logistics, education) under critical peak pricing
time_period: January 1, 2021 to August 29, 2022 (firm-level); provincial data August 5, 2020 to September 25, 2022
units: Firms (and their constituent locations); curtailment-window observations
n_sample: 25 organizations across 39 locations; field experiment: 12 control firms (16 locations) vs. 13 experimental firms (23 locations); 351 curtailment-window observations
authors: Amaral, Christopher; Kolsarici, Ceren; Ikonen, Iina; Robitaille, Nicole
theories: dynamic capabilities (Teece et al., 1997; Sirmon et al., 2007); sociotechnical systems (STS) theory (Cherns, 1987; Clegg, 2000); systems thinking / resource orchestration (Ostrom, 2009; Barney et al., 2010)
keywords: artificial intelligence; behavioral nudging; critical peak pricing; dynamic pricing; energy consumption; sustainability
research_question: How can artificial-intelligence-driven demand forecasting be integrated with behaviorally informed communications (planning prompts) to help large organizations optimize energy consumption under critical peak pricing (CPP) schemes — reducing costs and emissions — and do such behavioral effects persist across repeated curtailment events?
mechanism_summary: The paper combines two streams. First, an NNTS model forecasts provincial peak demand up to 24 hours ahead, improving peak-identification accuracy while minimizing false alarms, so firms know *when* to curtail. Second, planning-prompt emails clarify *how* to act, embedding behavioral science (Rogers et al., 2015) to convert abstract goals into concrete steps. Grounded in dynamic capabilities, sociotechnical systems theory, and systems-thinking/resource-orchestration, the authors argue that effective demand response is a coordination problem requiring alignment of digital tools, stakeholder incentives, and organizational behavior — not merely an optimization problem. The intervention reduced peak demand by roughly 2,949 kWh per organization, yielding average net annual savings of ~$232,843, with nudge effects stable across nine repeated curtailment events.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends behavioral and resource-management theory to the underresearched organizational (rather than household) context, showing that planning-prompt nudges can shift energy use in large, highly motivated firms and persist across repeated decisions. It reframes energy curtailment as a coordination problem and demonstrates how AI forecasting and behavioral nudging operate in tandem, advancing the practical application of dynamic capabilities and sociotechnical systems theory in sustainability contexts.
practical_implication: System operators, who hold real-time demand data, are best positioned to forecast peaks; local distribution companies should communicate both when and how to act, using simple, timely messages with concrete planning prompts, while energy consultants translate signals into operational guidance. Successful implementation requires integration across regulators, system operators, distribution companies, consultants, and commercial users.
limitations: Participating firms were large industrial users who voluntarily subscribed to the advisory service, limiting generalizability; the authors lack data on intra-organizational communication chains. The bundled field intervention (revised subject line, visual salience, planning-prompt content) prevents isolating the precise causal mechanism, so field findings are suggestive rather than definitive. The small number of generator-equipped firms limits subgroup precision, and durability under different market conditions or personnel changes remains untested.
future_research: Multiarm field experiments could disentangle the independent and interactive contributions of the bundled behavioral design elements. Researchers should vary advance notification lead times, examine longer-term load-shifting behaviors, and test whether the integrative approach scales to other uneven-demand domains such as water or traffic to assess generalizability across industries, organizations, and customers.
abstract: The results demonstrate significant financial savings and reduced environmental impact, benefiting organizations, regulators, service providers, and society. We contribute to research on resource management, systems thinking, and nudging in an organizational context by aligning technological tools with human processes.
apa_citation: Amaral, C., Kolsarici, C., Ikonen, I., & Robitaille, N. (2026). Practice paper—AI-driven behavioral nudges for organizations: An integrative system for sustainable resource management. *Marketing Science*, 45(3), 675–695. https://doi.org/10.1287/mksc.2024.1154
