title: Siloed Sustainability: How Paradox Management Unravels in Integrative Practice Implementation
methods: Qualitative inductive single-case ethnography of LatinCar; 480 hours of observation (Oct 2018–Jan 2019), 73 semi-structured interviews across 26 departments, and 1,600+ pages of internal documents (2014–2023); NVivo open coding (130+ codes) plus temporal bracketing process analysis into three stages.
industry: Automotive manufacturing — Brazilian subsidiary of a multinational carmaker (advanced manufacturing, ~15,000 workers)
country: Brazil (a Latin American country); subsidiary of a multinational with HQ-set targets
time_period: Documents and interviews spanning 2014–2023; on-site ethnographic observation October 2018–January 2019
units: Single revelatory negative case (LatinCar) and its sustainability department / process integration office, tracked across departments
n_sample: 480 hours of observation; 73 interviews across 26 departments; 1,600+ pages of documents
authors: Amaral, Lucas; Reinecke, Juliane; Etter, Michael
theories: integration–differentiation / organizational design (Lawrence & Lorsch, 1967); paradox theory (Smith & Lewis, 2011); practice implementation studies (Ansari et al., 2010; Nutt, 1986)
keywords: integration–differentiation paradox; integrative practices; organizational silos; paradox management; sustainability implementation
research_question: How do efforts to work through paradoxes unravel in the implementation of sustainability practices? The authors ask why organizations fail to embed sustainability "in meaningful and lasting ways" even under seemingly favorable conditions of leadership commitment and resources.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: An underlying "integration–differentiation paradox" (the simultaneous, contradictory demands for strategic integration and specialized differentiation) plus three ensuing secondary tensions — jurisdictional, outcome, and attributional.
- Practice characteristics: "Integrative practices" defined by three properties — indivisibility (cannot be split across teams without undermining coherence), complexity (hard to grasp cognitively and operationally), and non-codifiability (context-dependent, reliant on tacit knowledge). Sustainability sits at the high end of all three.
- Process / phases: Stage 1 — "centering practices" (creating a sustainability department) provoke jurisdictional tensions; Stage 2 — "mandate enlargement" (a process integration office to break silos) surfaces complexity and outcome tensions / goal displacement; Stage 3 — "focus on visibility" (short-term symbolic wins) triggers attribution tensions over credit.
- Emergent outcome: A self-reinforcing, recursive cycle in which each well-intentioned attempt to work through the paradox generates new tensions that compound, narrow actors' courses of action, and derail implementation.

Through temporal bracketing of a failed (negative) case, the authors show that paradox management unravels not because actors fail to recognize the paradox but because the indivisible, complex, and non-codifiable nature of sustainability practices structurally constrains their responses. Each move to balance integration and differentiation surfaces deeper structural and political fault lines, so the original reflective effort is progressively displaced by reactive "containment of tension," producing a downward spiral of constrained action and organizational fatigue.
theoretical_contribution: The study makes three contributions: (1) to paradox theory, by illuminating the "dark side" of paradox management — showing how and why deliberate, well-intentioned attempts to work through paradoxes can inadvertently escalate tensions and hinder implementation; (2) to sustainability research, by uncovering intraorganizational constraints on embeddedness beyond the traditional focus on social/environmental/economic goal tensions; and (3) to practice theory, by introducing "integrative practices" as a novel lens (indivisibility, complexity, non-codifiability) for understanding overlooked complexities of practice implementation.
practical_implication: Conventional integration fixes — central coordination, mandate expansion, and role flexibility — backfire when highly integrative practices meet entrenched silos. Organizations should instead reconfigure how authority, accountability, and recognition are distributed: define clear roles for indivisible practices before implementation, appoint "sustainability integrators," align reward structures with processes (not just outcomes), and create "safe spaces" that let actors pause, reassess evolving tensions, and learn from failure.
limitations: The theorizing is bounded: organizations lacking commitment may respond by decoupling rather than attempting paradox management; the jurisdictional tensions observed may be less salient in organizations structured for cross-functional teamwork with shared goals; and the process model is most relevant to early stages of implementing new, immature sustainability practices, before they become institutionalized, codified, and divisible.
future_research: A key avenue is to examine how organizations transition from fragile, early-stage enactment to mature, institutionalized integrative practices — investigating to what extent inherent practice features delay institutionalization and which factors mitigate them over time. Scholars could ask whether integrative practices can become more divisible, codifiable, or less complex through learning, emerging standards, or peer exchange, and study the material configurations, social practices, and institutional arrangements that entrench paradoxes.
abstract: Organizations often struggle to implement sustainability practices, even when such efforts are strategically prioritized. While paradox studies have highlighted successful cases in which tensions between economic, environmental, and social goals are constructively balanced, less attention has been paid to how the characteristics of the practices themselves complicate paradox management. Through an ethnographic study of LatinCar, the Brazilian subsidiary of a multinational carmaker, we introduce the concept of "integrative practices," patterns of activity marked by indivisibility, complexity, and non-codifiability, to explain why certain sustainability practices require extensive cross-functional coordination and generate persistent contradictions. In siloed organizations, these integrative practices trigger an integration–differentiation paradox that becomes increasingly difficult to manage. Our process analysis reveals how efforts to work through this paradox inadvertently surface new tensions—jurisdictional, outcome, and attributional—that accumulate and derail implementation. We contribute to paradox theory by illuminating the dark side of paradox management, showing how practice characteristics can undermine actors' ability to balance competing demands. We also advance sustainability and practice implementation literatures by theorizing the distinctive intraorganizational challenges posed by integrative practices.
apa_citation: Amaral, L., Reinecke, J., & Etter, M. (2026). Siloed sustainability: How paradox management unravels in integrative practice implementation. *Academy of Management Journal, 69*(2), 299–327. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0548

title: Reaffirming and Renewing Our Declaration of Interdependence
methods: Inaugural co-editors' editorial; non-empirical agenda-setting essay outlining the journal's status, six features of the institutional environment, and a three-year vision.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Arora, Punit; Gardberg, Naomi A.; Louche, Céline
theories: institutional contestation / institutional contradictions (Friedland & Alford, 1991)
keywords: business and society; declaration of interdependence; institutional contestation; polycrisis; safety; transparency; trust
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: As a polycrisis "shaped by ecological, economic, political, social, and technological trends" reshapes the institutional environment, the journal *Business & Society* has an even more critical agenda-setting role to play at the business-society interface.
- Six interrelated features of the societal environment: (1) contested and conflictual institutional environments; (2) climate change, biodiversity, and ecosystem collapse; (3) Diversity, Equity, and Inclusion (DEI) contestation; (4) autocratization and democratic backsliding (including techno-feudalism); (5) social inequalities and inequities; (6) immigration and the movement of people / "tribalistic" identities.
- Three guiding principles for the response: transparency (to foster informed, deliberative engagement), safety (to report unvarnished and uncomfortable findings), and trust (to build an interdependent community).
- Planned initiatives: data-sharing and generative-AI policies, temporary author pseudonyms, a strategic advisory board, and new "Perspectives" and "conversations" article formats.

The editorial frames the incoming co-editors' three-year agenda as a response to interlocking grand challenges that simultaneously threaten society and the academic freedom on which business-and-society scholarship depends. The process is normative rather than causal: by anchoring the journal on transparency, safety, and trust, the editors aim to keep BAS a venue where difficult, controversial, and rigorous work can be published and debated, thereby renewing the founding "declaration of interdependence" between business and society.
theoretical_contribution: As an editorial, its contribution is agenda-setting rather than theory-testing: it synthesizes six features of a contested institutional environment (drawing on the language of institutional contestation and contradictory institutional demands) and articulates transparency, safety, and trust as guiding principles for the field of business and society. It positions the journal in direct dialogue with mainstream management journals while preserving its boundary-pushing, agenda-setting identity.
practical_implication: The editorial announces concrete journal practices: required data statements and encouraged data/code deposition (e.g., via OSF), a forthcoming generative-AI use policy, optional temporary author pseudonymity for at-risk scholars, a new strategic advisory board, and new "Perspectives" and "conversations" formats — all intended to support scholars working on increasingly controversial topics such as climate change, DEI, and human rights.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editors identify research avenues tied to each of the six environmental features — institutional contestation and the "post-truth" era, holistic business-nature relations, the path forward for equitable opportunity amid DEI backlash, the links between business and democratic backsliding, social and environmental inequality, and the consequences of restricted human mobility — and invite agenda-setting work on these grand challenges.
abstract: Not reported in paper
apa_citation: Arora, P., Gardberg, N. A., & Louche, C. (2026). Reaffirming and renewing our declaration of interdependence. *Business & Society*, 65(1), 3–24. https://doi.org/10.1177/00076503251403345

title: Ethical trade-offs in fast fashion: Exploring social, environmental, and health dimensions in clothing consumption
methods: Stated-preference discrete choice experiment (D-efficient design) on T-shirts with three non-monetary attributes (working conditions, health protection, environmental protection) plus price; split-sample (General vs. Parents). Latent class logit models (2 classes) with stated attribute non-attendance, estimated in Nlogit 6; marginal willingness to pay computed by the Delta method.
industry: Clothing / textiles (T-shirt purchases)
units: Individual Swedish consumers (web panel respondents); General Sample buying for themselves and Parents Sample buying for their oldest child under 18
n_sample: 1721 respondents (1283 General Sample, 438 Parents Sample)
authors: Boman, Anders; Kataria, Mitesh; Lampi, Elina; Slunge, Daniel
theories: latent class modeling (Boxall & Adamowicz, 2002); random utility theory (McFadden, 1974)
keywords: Choice experiment; Environmental impacts; Hazardous chemicals; Health impacts; Parents; Working conditions
research_question: How much are consumers willing to pay for improvements in the social (working conditions), environmental, and health attributes of clothing, and how do these ethical trade-offs differ between consumers buying for themselves and parents buying for their children?
mechanism_summary: Utility is modeled in the random utility framework (McFadden, 1974) and estimated with a two-class latent class logit that segments respondents by preference heterogeneity, incorporating stated attribute non-attendance so that ignored attributes carry zero weight. Across both samples, health protection is valued most and environmental protection least, and MWTP is consistently larger for avoiding the worst (‘Very Poor’) level than for reaching the best (‘Good’) level — reflecting the intentionally non-linear attribute scale in which ‘Fairly Good’ corresponds to compliance with existing EU regulation. Parents display the same ranking but lower overall MWTP than the General Sample.
theoretical_contribution: The study provides a comprehensive, integrated analysis of consumer preferences across social, environmental, and health dimensions of clothing sustainability within a single choice experiment, whereas prior research typically examined only one or two dimensions. It documents that willingness to pay to avoid the worst practices substantially exceeds willingness to pay to reach the highest standards, linking this asymmetry to risk aversion / negative-label effects and to a non-linear, regulation-anchored attribute design.
practical_implication: There is substantial consumer demand for more sustainable clothing, especially for avoiding the worst practices, suggesting that sustainability labeling (e.g., under the EU Ecodesign for Sustainable Products Regulation) emphasizing avoidance of the most harmful practices would resonate with consumers. Producers could pass on a portion of cost increases for moderate sustainability improvements if these are clearly communicated, and a subset of consumers forms a target market for premium high-sustainability products.
limitations: The hypothetical nature of the choice experiment means hypothetical bias cannot be ruled out despite oath-script and budget-reminder mitigations. Respondents were given clear sustainability information that is rarely available in real purchasing situations, and the latent class model did not converge for the Parents Sample when interacting attributes with socio-economic variables, limiting heterogeneity analysis in that sample.
future_research: The authors note that their experimental design does not allow direct examination of compensatory decision-making or moral-licensing heuristics across sustainability attributes, pointing to further work on how consumers trade off multiple sustainability dimensions. Estimating producer-side costs of sustainability improvements is left beyond the scope of this study.
abstract: followed by working conditions and the environmental attribute. While the ranking of the attributes is consistent between the two samples, parents exhibited a lower overall MWTP. We also observe a general pattern of higher willingness to pay to avoid the lowest level (‘Very Poor’) of each attribute and achieve the intermediate level (‘Fairly Good’) than for further improvements to the highest level (‘Good’). This pattern, consistent with how the levels were designed, holds across all subsamples and attributes. Thus, we find substantial demand for more sustainable clothing, particularly for avoiding the worst practices. Our results also suggest that producers could pass on a portion of their increases in costs if sustainability improvements are effectively communicated. While most consumers are not willing to pay more to reach the highest level of an attribute, there are groups of consumers who are willing to pay a premium for high sustainability standards.
apa_citation: Boman, A., Kataria, M., Lampi, E., & Slunge, D. (2026). Ethical trade-offs in fast fashion: Exploring social, environmental, and health dimensions in clothing consumption. *Ecological Economics*, 242, 108878. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108878

title: Seeking Greener Pastures: Employee Turnover Following Corporate Stakeholder Violations
methods: Panel data analysis of a firm-year dataset; Poisson pseudo maximum likelihood (PPML) regressions with high-dimensional firm and year fixed effects and firm-clustered robust standard errors, predicting employee departure counts.
industry: Cross-industry: largest U.S. publicly traded firms spanning many sectors, penalized for violating a range of stakeholder interests (environmental, health/safety, employment discrimination, customer harm)
units: Firm-years (large U.S. public companies)
n_sample: 735 firms (5,590 firm-years)
authors: Briscoe, Forrest; DesJardine, Mark R.
theories: attribution theory (Wong & Weiner, 1981); event-based information processing / event attribute theory (Morgeson, Mitchell & Liu, 2015); organizational stigma / reputation spillover (Zhang, Wang, Toubiana & Greenwood, 2021); unfolding model of turnover (Lee & Mitchell, 1994)
keywords: corporate misconduct; employee turnover; interorganizational mobility; reputational risk; stakeholder violations; values-based sorting
research_question: Are employees actually willing to leave their jobs and find new employment in response to episodes of corporate wrongdoing—specifically, regulator-sanctioned stakeholder violations—and how do features of those violation events shape that turnover response?
mechanism_summary: Using an event-based information processing lens, the authors argue that the intensity of a firm's stakeholder violations functions as a salient negative shock that triggers reputationally concerned employees to engage in deliberate attributional reasoning about whether the misconduct is isolated or systemic. When violations are broader in reach, occur in closer succession, or deviate more from historical patterns, employees are more likely to infer systemic dysfunction—heightening the perceived reputational risk of continued affiliation and motivating exit. Aggregated to the organization level, higher intensity (amplified by breadth, shorter intervals, and novelty) raises the number of departures, and departing employees sort toward firms and industries with cleaner violation records.
theoretical_contribution: The study introduces a new theoretical framework that treats stakeholder violations as multidimensional organizational events—distinct from, and not the mere inverse of, CSR—whose intensity signals reputational risk that propagates to ordinary employees via reputation/stigma spillover. By integrating the psychology of event interpretation (breadth, interval, novelty) with the unfolding model of turnover and reputation-spillover theory, it advances the microfoundations of human capital responses to negative organizational events and shows that violations catalyze directional, values-based sorting of talent away from high-violation employers and industries.
practical_implication: Because stakeholder violations can drive substantial, costly turnover that extends well beyond executives across seniority, education, income, and geography, organizations have a retention-based incentive to maintain systems and procedures that guard against socially irresponsible conduct. The authors estimate that for a representative firm a year of 5% violation intensity can imply up to roughly $10M in direct replacement costs, especially when violations are broad, frequent, and novel.
limitations: The data lack detail on the development and timing of the problems that led to each violation, and employees who depart before a fine is announced are missed, implying the estimates are conservative and the true effect may be larger. The analysis is confined to large U.S. public firms and uses aggregate firm-level departure counts rather than micro-level motivational data, so it cannot directly observe individual employees' reasons for leaving.
future_research: Future work could model the staged, dynamic unfolding of the violation process and departure effects, and examine cross-national and cross-organizational-type variation (e.g., privately held, smaller, social-purpose, and nonprofit organizations, or violations harming stakeholders in other countries). Researchers could also collect micro-level motivational data via surveys or exit interviews and study alternative employee responses such as voice and activism.
abstract: Are employees willing to leave when their employer violates societal norms? We develop an event-based framework for understanding employee turnover in response to stakeholder violations—publicly announced, regulator-sanctioned harms to stakeholders. We theorize that intense violations prompt some employees to reassess their affiliation due to reputational concerns, especially when violations are broad in scope, occur in rapid succession, or diverge from past patterns. These features increase the likelihood that observers, including peers and future employers, will view the misconduct as systemic, elevating reputational risk for those who remain. Using eight years of job history data from 735 large U.S. public companies, sourced from LinkedIn by Revelio Labs, we find that violations amounting to 5% of a company's annual revenues translate into a 3.93% increase in employee turnover. Turnover rates rise further when violations are broad, rapid, or historically atypical. Departing employees tend to join firms with fewer prior violations, indicating a form of values-based sorting. We also estimate the financial toll of violation-induced turnover for representative firms in our sample. Our findings offer a new lens on stakeholder violations as organizational events that drive interorganizational migration.
apa_citation: Briscoe, F., & DesJardine, M. R. (2026). Seeking greener pastures: Employee turnover following corporate stakeholder violations. *Academy of Management Journal*. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2024.0243

title: Pollution havens versus gates of hell: Environmental pollution and multinationals' foreign investment
methods: Panel data; firm-country fixed-effects OLS and two-stage least squares (2SLS) regression instrumenting host-country pollution with the country's share of coal electricity production; regressors lagged 1 year; robustness via negative binomial, conditional logit, alternative IV/DV measures.
industry: US multinational corporations across industries (two-digit SIC codes), including labor-intensive and consumer goods industries
units: Firm-country-year observations of US MNCs
n_sample: 113,069 firm-country-year observations of 1269 MNCs in 53 host countries
authors: Bu, Juan; Genin, Aurora
theories: institutional arbitrage (Ahuja & Yayavaram, 2011; Oliver, 1991); natural-resource-based view (Hart & Dowell, 2011); pollution haven effect (Copeland & Taylor, 1994; Rugman & Verbeke, 1998)
keywords: corporate sustainability; foreign investment; natural environment; pollution havens; pollution-induced operational cost
research_question: How do polluting environmental conditions alter firms' cost structures and, in turn, shape their global strategies? Specifically, how does a host country's environmental pollution affect multinational corporations' foreign investment in that country?
mechanism_summary: The central mechanism is the pollution-induced operational cost: environmental pollution in a host country raises MNCs' labor costs (by thinning the skilled-labor supply and reducing workforce productivity) and tightens material supply constraints (by contaminating or depleting input materials and disrupting transportation networks). These rising operational costs erode profit margins and diminish the investment attractiveness of pollution havens, so when compliance pressure is held constant, more-polluted host countries draw less MNC investment. The effect is buffered where countries offer deep skilled-labor markets or strong rail networks and is intensified for labor-intensive and consumer goods industries that are most exposed to these inputs.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances three literatures. It extends pollution haven research beyond compliance-cost arbitrage by showing that environmental degradation imposes direct operational costs that can push foreign investment out rather than pull it in. It complements the corporate sustainability literature's emphasis on intangible reputational loss by documenting the tangible operational costs—elevated labor and material expenses and supply-chain disruption—of operating in degraded environments. And it advances MNC foreign-investment research by establishing environmental pollution, a direct outcome of weak regulation, as an overlooked determinant of location decisions, with contingencies tied to host-country resource availability and firms' resource dependence.
practical_implication: For managers, the findings show that investing in pollution havens creates both compliance-arbitrage opportunities and rising operational costs, with labor-intensive and consumer goods firms most exposed; these costs are attenuated where skilled labor and advanced transport networks are available. For policymakers—particularly in high-pollution developing countries—relaxing environmental regulation to attract FDI may yield unsustainable growth, since unchecked degradation eventually inflicts operational costs that erode the very business opportunities sought, so reducing pollution supports long-term economic growth and sustainability.
limitations: The study focuses only on the negative effect of host-country pollution on foreign investment and does not examine other strategic decisions (e.g., R&D investment, new product development) that pollution might drive or hinder. It isolates operational costs from labor and supply constraints but does not capture other costs of operating in pollution havens, such as climate-risk exposure or the consequences of environmental violations. The sample is limited to MNCs headquartered in the United States.
future_research: Future work could examine whether environmental pollution shapes other MNC strategic activities, including R&D investment, clean-technology development, and global sourcing from polluted countries. Scholars could explore additional financial and nonfinancial costs of exploiting pollution havens, including climate risks such as extreme weather and rising sea levels. Researchers could also test whether the findings generalize to MNCs from non-US countries, especially developing-country firms operating in different institutional contexts.
abstract: We find evidence in a panel of US multinational corporations from 2007 to 2020. A firm's investment in a host country is decreasing in the host country's environmental pollution, especially if the firm faces heightened constraints in finding alternative labor inputs and material supplies or is highly dependent on these resources.
apa_citation: Bu, J., & Genin, A. (2026). Pollution havens versus gates of hell: Environmental pollution and multinationals' foreign investment. *Strategic Management Journal*, 47(4), 1009–1038. https://doi.org/10.1002/smj.70039

title: Biodiversity Entrepreneurship
methods: Quantitative empirical study of private-market venture financing. A fine-tuned LLM (Llama-3.1-8B, LoRA) trained on 173 manually verified Crunchbase biodiversity startups (plus matched sustainability and generic startups) classifies 630 biodiversity startups in PitchBook; propensity-score matching pairs them with generic startups; binomial/multinomial logit, OLS on log deal size, Poisson count models, and a Twitter character-limit difference-in-differences, with industry/year/company fixed effects and clustered standard errors.
industry: Biodiversity-focused private startups/ventures (marine, forest, agricultural, animal, and multifunctional biodiversity), benchmarked against matched generic startups in the PitchBook VC universe
country: Cross-national (global PitchBook VC universe; matched on region and industry)
time_period: Startups founded 2010–2023; PitchBook VC data as of August 2024; tweet counts through December 2024
units: Private startups (firm-level) and their financing deals (deal-level)
n_sample: 630 biodiversity startups identified; 605 matched to generic counterparts (1,210 firms); 2,483 deal-level observations
authors: Cao, Sean Shun; Karolyi, G. Andrew; Xiong, William W.; Xu, Hui
theories: blended/concessionary capital in biodiversity finance (Flammer, Giroux, and Heal, 2025a); social media and information asymmetry in entrepreneurial finance (Wang, Wu, and Hitt, 2024); values-driven (values vs. value) investors framework (Starks, 2023)
keywords: Twitter; biodiversity finance; entrepreneurship; fine-tuning; large language model; social media; sustainable investing; venture capital
research_question: The study focuses on three questions: (1) how to best classify, or create a taxonomy of, biodiversity startups and their investors; (2) what types of VC investors match with which kind of biodiversity startups; and (3) what strategies can improve the funding outcomes among VC investors for biodiversity startups.
mechanism_summary: Biodiversity startups raise systematically smaller deals (roughly half the size of matched generic startups) yet attract a broader, hybrid coalition of capital. Relative to matched generics they have about 2.20 times the odds of securing impact-investor funding and 1.26 times the odds of attracting public investors. "Values" investors (mission-aligned impact funds and public institutions) supply incremental rather than substitutive capital, but funding gaps persist. Twitter presence is associated with larger total fundraising (~36 percent) and more deals; the proposed process is that social media reduces information asymmetry and connects ventures to "minds-unalike" value (for-profit) investors who would not otherwise engage.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces "biodiversity startups" as a distinct, understudied object in entrepreneurial and sustainable finance, providing the first taxonomy of such ventures and of their investors. It links the sustainable-finance and entrepreneurial-finance literatures, extends Starks's (2023) values-versus-value investor distinction into private biodiversity markets, and demonstrates how alternative data and a fine-tuned LLM can identify and study an emerging phenomenon that lacks structured data.
practical_implication: For policy and practice, the findings emphasize mobilizing private capital toward biodiversity preservation through hybrid and blended financing models that pair impact investors and public-sector backers with traditional VC, and through strategic communication: biodiversity ventures can use social media to raise visibility, signal credibility, and broaden their investor base beyond mission-aligned funders.
limitations: The data are limited to proprietary private-market databases (PitchBook/Crunchbase) with possible reporting lags (recent founding years and deals may be underreported), and detailed project-level capital-needs data are unavailable, so observed funding differences are interpreted as a potential financing gap rather than a clean causal effect. The decision to open a Twitter account is endogenous and may reflect unobserved startup characteristics, so the authors caution against causal interpretation despite the DID design.
future_research: The authors point to deeper examination of investor coalitions, the long-term performance and survival of biodiversity startups, and the broader societal impact of their nature-positive innovations. A more systematic study of biodiversity startup founders and their interdisciplinary backgrounds is also flagged as worthwhile.
abstract: We study an emerging class of startup organizations focused on biodiversity conservation and the challenges they face in financing these ventures. By fine-tuning a large language model, we identify 630 biodiversity-linked startups in PitchBook and compare their financing dynamics with those of other ventures.
apa_citation: Cao, S. S., Karolyi, G. A., Xiong, W. W., & Xu, H. (2026). Biodiversity entrepreneurship. *Review of Finance*, 30(1), 43–86. https://doi.org/10.1093/rof/rfaf062

title: When and how simplified nutrition labels improve fast-food choices
journal: Journal of the Academy of Marketing Science
methods: Three studies: two pre-registered between-subjects online experiments (Study 1, N=812; Study 3, nationally representative N=929) with OLS regressions and Process Model 4 parallel-mediation analyses, plus a 16-week field quasi-experiment (Study 2) on 200,039 fast-food meals analyzed with a difference-in-differences design and restaurant-clustered robust standard errors.
industry: Fast-food / quick-service restaurants (Pomme de Pain French sandwich chain)
time_period: Field study: baseline 24 May–29 Aug 2021 and post-intervention 1 Sep–24 Dec 2021
units: Individual meal choices (online experiment participants) and individual fast-food transactions (field study)
n_sample: 812 (Study 1), 200,039 meals (Study 2 field), 929 (Study 3)
authors: Chandon, Pierre; Indaburu, Andde
theories: health nudges and choice architecture (Krefeld-Schwalb et al., 2024; Mrkva et al., 2021); lay beliefs / unhealthy = tasty intuition (Raghunathan et al., 2006); theory of reasoned action / deliberate decision making (Sheppard et al., 1988)
keywords: Fast-food restaurants; Food; Inequality; Nutrition; Nutrition labeling; Socioeconomic differences
research_question: Whether, when, and how adding the Nutri-Score simplified nutrition label leads fast-food customers to choose meals of higher nutritional quality, including the distinct psychological mechanisms underlying its effects and how those effects vary across socioeconomic status and place of residence.
mechanism_summary: Nutri-Score improves fast-food nutritional quality by facilitating the evaluation of nutritional quality — making it easier to identify the healthiest options — rather than by shifting eating goals, risk attitudes, or regulatory focus. Because the effect operates through making healthiness legible, it only changes behavior for consumers who already attach importance to nutrition. Since nutrition importance is lower among low-SES consumers and those living in provincial towns, the label benefits middle-class metropolitan consumers but has no effect (or backfires) for lower-status provincial consumers, thereby widening pre-existing nutrition inequalities.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to the health-nudge literature by documenting consumer heterogeneity along socioeconomic and geographic lines in response to behavioral interventions and by showing that nudges can increase nutrition inequalities. It advances food-labeling research by demonstrating that simplified labels work by facilitating evaluation of nutritional quality rather than by changing eating goals, and by isolating nutrition importance as the mechanism that both moderates label effectiveness and explains socioeconomic and geographic differences in fast-food choices.
practical_implication: For fast-food chains, adopting Nutri-Score was inexpensive, did not create negative inferences about taste or other attributes, and did not hurt (and sometimes increased) revenue, making it attractive for restaurants serving healthier-profile foods such as sandwiches. For policymakers, because labels help only consumers who already prioritize nutrition, complementary strategies (e.g., displaying portion weight to counter the belief that healthy items are less filling) may be needed to avoid widening the socioeconomic diet gap.
limitations: The studies were conducted with a single French sandwich chain (Pomme de Pain) and with the official French label, limiting generalizability to other cuisines, chains, and countries. The online experiments measured consumption intentions rather than actual choices, which can inflate socially desirable responding, and in the field study Nutri-Score recall was imperfect, so label visibility and attention could not be cleanly measured.
future_research: Future work should use eye-tracking to measure the role of label visibility and attention in real-world settings, examine whether socioeconomic differences exist in how consumers process simplified labels, and probe why provincial residents attach less importance to nutrition. Researchers should also study how labeling affects prices, product reformulation and degree of processing, competition across distribution channels, and the influence of other individual characteristics such as political ideology.
abstract: Simplified nutrition labels, such as Nutri-Score, are increasingly used in grocery stores, but their effects on out-of-home consumption, their psychological mechanisms, and potential moderators remain poorly understood. An initial online experiment showed that Nutri-Score leads fast-food customers to select meals (and especially desserts) of higher nutritional quality by helping them identify the healthiest options, ruling out three other theory-driven potential mechanisms. A field study involving 200,039 fast-food meals replicated the positive effects of Nutri-Score in a middle-class metropolitan area but found that it backfired in a less affluent provincial town. A second online experiment, which recruited a nationally representative sample, replicated the nutritional improvement for middle-class people living in metropolitan areas, but found no effect among those with a lower socioeconomic status living in provincial towns. Nutrition importance was the only one of the nine factors tested that moderated the effects of Nutri-Score and explained dietary differences across these groups.
apa_citation: Chandon, P., & Indaburu, A. (2026). When and how simplified nutrition labels improve fast-food choices. *Journal of the Academy of Marketing Science*, 54(2), 490–512. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11747-025-01126-0

title: The Aesthetics of Impact: Making Impact Beautiful, Not Just Useful
methods: Conceptual commentary; develops an aesthetic reframing of social impact through theoretical synthesis (Deweyan aesthetics) and proposes a framework of aesthetic inquiry — structured attunement, participatory aesthetic evaluation, and compositional case-making — illustrated with a river-restoration vignette.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Chen, Suwen
theories: Deweyan aesthetics (Dewey, 1934); aesthetic theory of impact / art as experience
keywords: aesthetics; evaluation; experiential knowledge; relationality; social impact
research_question: How can social-impact scholarship move beyond metric- and output-oriented framings to recognize forms of significance that are lived, affective, and experiential — and what conceptual and methodological apparatus would such an aesthetic reframing of impact require?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: The dominant ontological assumption — that impact is an output awaiting demonstration — fundamentally restricts what impact is allowed to be; an aesthetic lens widens the evidentiary frame to include how individuals and communities experience change.
- Key constructs: impact as lived/affective/experiential; resonance, atmosphere, rhythm, and felt significance; the experiential registration of change.
- Framework (three practices): (1) structured aesthetic attunement — disciplined documenting of how people perceive and feel change (sensory fieldnotes, reflexive logs); (2) participatory aesthetic evaluation — those affected become co-interpretive partners, including anti-narrative attention to silences; (3) compositional case-making — assembling sensory traces and emotional turning points into an analytically interpretable account, with renewal as an aesthetic-temporal modality.
- Boundary conditions: aesthetic judgment is not arbitrary (business already relies on it in brand, architecture, ritual); metrics are not abandoned but paired with aesthetic evidence.

Drawing on Dewey's (1934) notion of art as experience, the commentary argues that organizational impact resembles artistic encounter — unfolding through form, rhythm, sensory engagement, and emotional meaning, and often felt before it is proven. The reframing shifts the field ontologically (impact as lived experience), epistemically (from "impact must be proven" to "impact is known through resonance"), and methodologically (toward sensory ethnography, atmospheric and anti-narrative inquiry, and participatory meaning-making). The researcher becomes a composer who curates and arranges diverse evidentiary registers rather than a detached verifier of predefined outcomes.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances three contributions to Business and Society scholarship: ontologically, it redirects attention from impact as a measurable outcome to impact as lived experience; epistemically, it shifts inquiry from representational logic to experiential logic; and methodologically, it expands the repertoire of impact inquiry by incorporating sensory ethnography, atmospheric analysis, (anti)narrative inquiry, and participatory meaning-making. In doing so it positions aesthetics as a distinct register for theorizing and evidencing impact beyond demonstration and measurement.
practical_implication: For evaluators and organizations, the commentary offers feasible alternatives to metric-only impact assessment — structured sensory fieldnotes and observation templates, participatory and place-based testimony, and compositional case-making — that render experiential significance analytically visible without reducing it to metric proxies. It reframes the researcher/evaluator role as an active mediator who curates evidence and attends to whose voices and silences are centered.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: By placing experiential significance at the center of analysis, the commentary invites scholars to study atmospheres as well as outcomes, resonance as well as scale, and experience as well as evidence, arguing that increasingly complex social and environmental challenges require richer conceptual and methodological imaginations. It positions structured attunement, participatory aesthetic evaluation, and compositional case-making as an agenda for re-aestheticizing impact research.
abstract: This commentary introduces an aesthetic reframing of social impact, offering a conceptual and methodological shift beyond prevailing metric- and output-oriented approaches. While recent scholarship has begun to view impact as a process, such work still relies on representational logics that privilege what can be demonstrated. I advance a distinct contribution by conceptualizing impact as lived, affective, and experiential—emerging through resonance, atmosphere, rhythm, and relational renewal, often before it becomes measurable. Drawing on Deweyan aesthetics, I propose a feasible framework of aesthetic inquiry that includes structured attunement, participatory aesthetic evaluation, and compositional case-making. This aesthetic turn expands the ontology, epistemology, and methodology of impact scholarship, enabling business and society research to recognize forms of significance that standard frameworks overlook.
apa_citation: Chen, S. (2026). The aesthetics of impact: Making impact beautiful, not just useful. *Business & Society*, 65(6), 1434–1439. https://doi.org/10.1177/00076503251413065

title: Fixed capital and growth imperatives: Is commercial aviation trapped in a treadmill?
methods: Mixed analytical-empirical study. The analytical component builds a transdisciplinary institutionalist-sociometabolic model of fixed-capital lock-in; the empirical component aggregates financial data (capital expenditures, gross property/plant/equipment, EBITDA) from WRDS Compustat (North America and Global) and material-throughput data from UN Comtrade, all converted to 2024 constant US dollars via the Producer Price Index, for a sample of commercial-aviation firms across five sub-sectors over 1988-2023.
industry: Commercial aviation (aircraft manufacturers, suppliers, maintenance providers, airlines, airport operators)
country: Global (Compustat North America covering U.S./Canadian firms plus Compustat Global covering firms outside North America)
time_period: 1988-2023 (financial variables run 1989-2023, 1990-2023, and 1994-2023 depending on the series)
units: Publicly traded firms across five aviation sub-sectors
n_sample: 29 companies (n = 29); single-variable analyses span 27-28 firms, 30 distinct organizations combined
authors: Chevalier, Henri; Pineault, Eric
theories: capital lock-in theory; growth imperatives (Richters and Siemoneit, 2019); post-Keynesian and Kaleckian theory of the firm (Lavoie, 2022); sociometabolic nexus / social ecology of stocks and flows (Pineault, 2023; Haberl et al., 2021); treadmill of production (Schnaiberg, 1980)
keywords: Capital expenditures; Fixed capital; Flows; Growth imperative; Lock-in; Social metabolism; Stocks; Theory of the firm
research_question: The paper examines the supply-side lock-ins linked to growth imperatives that can limit demand-side decarbonization strategies and create inertia in commercial aviation, asking whether the industry is structurally trapped in a growth "treadmill" that makes net-zero unattainable under its current capitalist institutional structure.
mechanism_summary:
- Core construct: Capital lock-in — investments in large-scale, long-lived fixed assets create structural inertia, with existing material stocks commanding future financial and metabolic flows.
- Four lock-in effects: two financial (the yield constraint *q − c*; the constraint of expanding maximum production capacity) and two physical (minimum operational utilization, e.g., refinery turndown rates; minimum circulating capital for maintenance/repair).
- Defensive investment: firms reinvest to avoid social devaluation of fixed capital from competitive pressure, which converts the yield constraint into a microeconomic growth imperative.
- Emergent outcome: rising organizational and material complexity raises the minimum financial and metabolic throughput thresholds, driving ever-higher production, passenger volumes, resource use, and GHG emissions (a socio-metabolic growth treadmill).

The process integrates an institutionalist Kaleckian model of accumulation (Crotty, 1993; Eichner, 1976) with the sociometabolic nexus framework (Haberl et al., 2021; Pineault, 2023): fixed capital is theorized as a coupling point between the socio-economic accumulation treadmill and its sustaining biophysical throughput. Because each oligopolistic firm is coerced into reinvesting by competitors, micro-, meso-, and macro-level drivers reinforce one another into a structural growth imperative that anchors aviation in a fossil-fuel-based socio-metabolic regime and narrows the "socio-metabolic corridors" available for an ecological transition.
theoretical_contribution: The paper integrates institutionalist post-Keynesian/Kaleckian theories of the firm with the social-ecological theory of material stocks and flows to model fixed capital as the mechanism linking capital accumulation to a self-reinforcing growth imperative. It extends the carbon lock-in and treadmill-of-production literatures by specifying four micro-level financial and physical lock-in effects, and offers a framework transposable to other capital-intensive industries and to the macroeconomic level.
practical_implication: Demand-reduction measures (flight limits, behavioral shifts such as flygskam, efficiency, fuel substitution, offsets) cannot decarbonize aviation while the supply-side growth imperatives of its capitalist organization remain intact. The authors argue decarbonization requires structural transformation of production and ownership institutions — postcapitalist, degrowth-oriented solutions including a planned wind-down and closure of capacity.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors call for research on how ownership and governance of aviation assets could be restructured to escape the growth imperative, what regulatory frameworks could cap or reduce aviation growth while ensuring just transitions for workers and regions, and through what democratic, multi-level governance processes societies could define "essential" versus "non-essential" air travel without reproducing existing power imbalances.
abstract: Roadmaps to make commercial aviation net-zero face major challenges, including the lack of time needed to mass-produce sustainable aviation fuels, the growing risks of biomass appropriation, and the persistent inability to decouple sectoral growth from greenhouse gas emissions. Consequently, demand-reduction strategies for aviation emissions will remain ineffective unless the supply-side growth imperatives embedded in the capitalist organization of the industry are addressed. The proposed framework also offers broader insights into the inertia and self-reinforcing metabolic dynamics of capitalist economies.
apa_citation: Chevalier, H., & Pineault, E. (2026). Fixed capital and growth imperatives: Is commercial aviation trapped in a treadmill? *Ecological Economics, 240*, 108842. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108842

title: “Local Heroes”: Construction firms pioneering circular innovation
methods: Cross-sectional logit estimation (average marginal effects) on an original 2022 regional survey of construction firms; one aggregate CEI adoption model plus six practice-specific models, with robustness checks (traditional-innovation proxy, continuous local-orientation index).
industry: Construction firms (buildings 34.8%, civil engineering 9.8%, specialized construction activities 55.3%); manufacturers of construction products excluded
units: Construction firms
n_sample: 376 firms (final sample after excluding construction-product manufacturers; initial 408 respondents)
authors: Chioatto, Elisa; Montanaro, Alessandro; Mazzanti, Massimiliano
theories: circular economy / eco-innovation literature; innovation determinants in construction (Nam and Tatum, 1988; Reichstein et al., 2008; Barata and Fontainha, 2017); proximity-based local networks (Bourdin and Torre, 2024)
keywords: Circular economy; Construction and demolition sector; Innovation; Local networks
research_question: Why do some construction firms adopt Circular Economy Innovations (CEI) while others do not, and how do regulation, R&D expenditure, firm size, and local market orientation shape that adoption? A second question asks whether local market orientation — contrary to its documented negative effect on conventional innovation — positively drives CEI adoption.
mechanism_summary: R&D investment and firm size are strong, significant predictors of aggregate CEI adoption, with R&D showing the larger marginal effect (0.239) over size (0.126). Local market orientation is positive and highly significant: firms anchored in local markets are more likely to adopt circular practices, especially selective demolition and waste transfer — practices that hinge on inter-firm exchange of secondary materials. The proposed process is that proximity-based local networks lower transaction costs, mitigate quality risks of secondary materials, and ease knowledge exchange through short, close-knit supply chains, so that localization becomes an enabler of (rather than a brake on) circular innovation. Regulation is insignificant in the aggregate but becomes decisive for specific practices (sustainable inputs, selective demolition, decontamination, waste transfer), reflecting the targeted design of Italian green public-procurement rules.
theoretical_contribution: The study provides what the authors describe as the first firm-level investigation of CEI adoption determinants in the construction sector, extending general construction-innovation theory to circular-specific practices. Its most original claim is that circular innovation follows different adoption dynamics than conventional innovation: local market orientation — associated with lower innovativeness in prior construction-innovation work — is here a positive driver of CEI, because circular material flows and knowledge exchange are best organized through proximity relationships and short supply chains.
practical_implication: Policymakers should expand public support for R&D, particularly in smaller firms, through funding programs, tax incentives, or grants targeting resource-intensive CEIs such as selective demolition and decontamination. Regulatory frameworks should reflect the multidimensional nature of CEIs with targeted (not one-size-fits-all) incentives, and should reinforce the creation of inter-firm networks, collaborations, or clusters to leverage local markets, reduce transaction costs, and improve secondary-material quality control.
limitations: The dataset, though rich and original, covers only a single period, constraining the ability to capture the dynamic impact of circular-innovation determinants over time. The evidence is also limited to Emilia-Romagna construction firms, restricting the generalizability of the findings to other territorial contexts.
future_research: Future research should expand both the temporal and geographical scope — moving beyond a single cross-section and a single region — to provide a more comprehensive and robust understanding of the drivers of CEI adoption across different territorial spaces.
abstract: points, while larger firm size raises it by 13 points. Local market orientation exerts a strong positive effect: firms generating at least 50 % of their turnover locally are 15 percentage points more likely to adopt a circular innovation. Regulatory factors are not significant in aggregate, but become decisive for specific circular practices such as selective demolition and the use of sustainable inputs. Theoretically, the study demonstrates that circular innovations follow different adoption dynamics than traditional innovations in the construction industry, with proximity-based networks and local material flows playing a central role. Policy-wise, findings highlight the need to support R&D, especially in smaller firms, strengthen targeted regulatory incentives, and foster local inter-firm collaborations to reduce transaction costs and overcome secondary material quality risks.
apa_citation: Chioatto, E., Montanaro, A., & Mazzanti, M. (2026). “Local heroes”: Construction firms pioneering circular innovation. *Ecological Economics*, *241*, 108862. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108862

title: Human Capital Disclosure and Labor Market Outcomes: Evidence from Regulation S‐K
methods: Difference-in-differences design centered on the 2020 amendment of Regulation S-K; treatment = U.S. public firms, control = matched large U.S. private firms; OLS with firm-by-job-type and month fixed effects, entropy balancing on sales, standard errors clustered at firm-job-type level; DEI disclosure in job postings measured via RavenPack NLP (NER) and a Generative LLM; cross-sectional and dynamic event-time tests.
industry: All-industry cross-section of U.S. public and large private firms (job postings classified by 6-digit O*NET occupation codes); DEI in job postings
time_period: Job postings within a [-5, +5] month window around August 2020 (short term); extended to 24 months post-regulation (long term)
units: Firm-job-type-month observations (job postings, workforce diversity, recruitment outcomes)
n_sample: 5.2 million job postings matched to 1,891 public and 347 large private firms
authors: Choi, Jung Ho; Li, Dan; Macciocchi, Daniele
theories: disclosure theory / signaling (Milgrom, 1981); real effects of disclosure regulation
keywords: Regulation S-K; diversity, equity, and inclusion; human capital disclosure; job postings; labor market outcomes; securities regulation; workforce diversity
research_question: What are the labor market consequences of the 2020 Regulation S-K, which mandates human capital disclosure (and, prominently, DEI information) in 10K filings — specifically, does the regulation change firms' DEI disclosure in job postings and their realized workforce diversity?
mechanism_summary: Because including DEI information in 10Ks is credible and salient, firms facing external pressure are incentivized to raise actual workforce diversity; to do so they first signal commitment by disclosing more DEI information in job postings to attract diverse candidates (a signaling logic in which forthcoming vs. omitted disclosure reveals firm type, per Milgrom [1981]). However, short-term inelastic labor supply of diverse candidates lengthens recruitment, so disclosure rises immediately while measurable diversity gains emerge only about four quarters later and concentrate among firms with credible internal commitment.
theoretical_contribution: The study documents that securities regulations can reach beyond capital markets to shape labor market outcomes through disclosure, contributing to the literature on the real effects of disclosure regulation. It extends work on labor-market disclosure and on firm responses to diversity issues by isolating the role of mandated securities disclosure (rather than social movements such as George Floyd's death or #MeToo) in altering firms' hiring and recruiting practices, while underscoring the structural constraints (inelastic labor supply) that limit short-term diversity change.
practical_implication: Principles-based human capital disclosure mandates can prompt firms to change labor-market disclosure and, eventually, workforce composition, but meaningful diversity gains take time and depend on credible internal commitment (e.g., a DEI officer). Regulators and practitioners should anticipate lagged, heterogeneous effects and recognize the supply-side limits of using disclosure rules to shape workforce diversity.
limitations: The authors observe only one state of the world in which a regulatory change and strong social movements occurred within a few months of each other, complicating clean attribution; they mitigate this with a narrow event window, pre-trend tests, and a #MeToo falsification test. Workforce demographics from Revelio Labs are inferred via machine learning rather than self-reported, introducing potential measurement error in diversity measures.
future_research: Future studies could investigate whether Regulation S-K leads to further changes in HR management beyond DEI — such as employee turnover or workplace safety — given that Safety & Health and Employee Development are other value-relevant human capital topics in 10Ks. Such work can inform practitioners and regulators on the consequences of a more prescriptive, rule-based human capital disclosure mandate.
abstract: We examine the labor market consequences of the 2020 Regulation S-K requiring human capital disclosure in 10K filings. Using large-sample joblevel data and a Generative Large Language Model (GLLM), we observe that public firms subject to the regulation increase their disclosure of diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) information in job postings relative to a matched sample of large private firms. The increase in job-posting disclosure is more pronounced among firms facing greater external pressure to increase their workforce diversity. These findings suggest a shift in demand for diverse candidates by public firms following the regulation. Yet, consistent with
apa_citation: Choi, J. H., Li, D., & Macciocchi, D. (2026). Human capital disclosure and labor market outcomes: Evidence from Regulation S-K. *Journal of Accounting Research*. https://doi.org/10.1111/1475-679x.70036

title: The Unintended Carbon Impacts of Large-Scale Electricity Storage
methods: Analytical optimization model of an auction-based wholesale electricity market with a profit-maximizing storage agent; derives tight worst-case emission bounds and a minimum sufficient carbon levy via theorems, validated with numerical case studies on Dutch electricity-market data for 2019 and 2022.
industry: Wholesale electricity market (large-scale energy storage, conventional generators, renewables)
time_period: Numerical validation on two years: 2019 and 2022
units: Storage transactions (buy-sell operation pairs) in a competitive electricity market
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Dupont, Clarisse; Heijmans-Kuryatnikova, Olga; Ghiassi-Farrokhfal, Yashar; Bunn, Derek
theories: analytical model of profit-maximizing arbitrage in a competitive electricity market; carbon pricing / carbon levy as policy instrument
keywords: CO2; carbon pricing; decarbonization; electricity market; energy storage; renewable energy
research_question: What are the primary drivers of the unintended carbon emissions induced by large-scale electricity storage, under what market conditions does storage increase or decrease emissions, and how can regulators mitigate these emissions while still enabling storage agents to maximize profits?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Although storage operations are directly carbon-neutral (scope 1 ≈ 0), profit-maximizing price arbitrage shifts the dispatch of conventional generators, producing non-zero indirect (scope 2) market-level emissions that can be positive or negative.
- Key constructs: round-trip efficiency (η); the cost gap between cleaner and more-polluting generators; the transaction (buy-sell operation pair) as the unit of emission accounting; the minimum sufficient carbon levy.
- Boundary conditions: results derived as tight worst-case lower/upper emission bounds robust to uncertainty in demand and renewable generation; technical constraints (start-up costs, minimum load, ramping) examined separately and shown not to overturn the core conclusions.

The paper formalizes, in a multiperiod auction-based market model, how a storage agent's arbitrage alters which generators are scheduled and thus how much CO2 the system emits. It proves that a "perfect" (η = 1) storage does not raise emissions only when more-polluting generators also have higher marginal costs; with η below one or when dirty generators are cheaper, even efficient storage can raise emissions. Counterintuitively, higher round-trip efficiency can increase system emissions (by unlocking more polluting transactions), so a steeper marginal-emission-cost curve — and a higher carbon levy — may be required to keep emissions below a target, and a higher levy does not always lower observed emissions.
theoretical_contribution: The paper is the first to extend the prior two-period analysis of storage-induced CO2 emissions to a multiperiod setting and to analyze emissions at the transaction level, deriving tight analytical bounds on the marginal emission rate of storage that depend on the generation mix and storage type. It formalizes analytically the minimum carbon levy sufficient to prevent a storage agent from increasing emissions beyond a target, where prior work only showed levy effects numerically, and it shows that a higher levy can paradoxically increase storage-induced emissions.
practical_implication: The framework lets market participants and regulators assess, interpret, and potentially control the unintended carbon impacts of storage operations. It implies that carbon levies for storage may need to be substantially higher than typical cap-and-trade prices, that there is no monotonic relationship between round-trip efficiency and worst-case emission impact, and that indirect emissions depend on whether solar or wind dominates the renewable mix.
limitations: Real-world factors such as network congestion and multimarket participation are excluded, a simplification the authors note facilitates clear, generalizable insights but limits realism. The robustness of the bounds to uncertainty introduces conservatism, and the analysis focuses on worst-case indirect emissions of storage.
future_research: The authors suggest relaxing the conservatism of the worst-case robust bounds through probabilistic approaches better suited to regulators wishing to target more flexible standards, and extending the framework to incorporate excluded real-world features such as network congestion and multimarket participation. They also point to developing mechanisms that manage emissions at finer temporal resolution.
abstract: Methodology/results: We develop a model to analyze the emission impact of profitmaximizing large-scale storage agents in a competitive electricity market.
apa_citation: Dupont, C., Heijmans-Kuryatnikova, O., Ghiassi-Farrokhfal, Y., & Bunn, D. (2026). The unintended carbon impacts of large-scale electricity storage. *Manufacturing & Service Operations Management*, 28(2), 663–685. https://doi.org/10.1287/msom.2024.1411

title: Political Segregation in the Labor Market and the Persistence of Pay Gaps
methods: Archival, large-scale quantitative study; OLS regression with fixed effects (state, industry, occupation, survey year) on GSS, CPS, and supplementary datasets; interaction terms between gender/race and industry/occupational liberalism; ICC2 reliability and validation against Verdant Labs FEC data.
industry: U.S. industries and occupations across the labor market (250 industries, 394 occupations measured for ideology); firms (Russell 1000 / JUST Capital) and nonprofit organizations
units: Individual employees nested in industries and occupations (fields)
n_sample: GSS analysis n=14,696; CPS analysis n=1,458,666 individuals; plus three supplementary studies (cohort, large firms, nonprofits)
authors: Fang, Ray Tsai; Klein, Felice B.; Nguyen, Chi; Lingwall, Jeff; Lewis, Alexander
theories: field theory (Bourdieu, 1972; Fligstein & McAdam, 2012); logics and norms (Scott, 2013); organizational ideology
keywords: field theory; organizational ideology; pay gaps; political polarization; political segregation; pro-equality practices
research_question: Why have gender and racial pay gaps persisted despite increasingly common organizational equality initiatives? The paper asks whether the political composition (the ratio of liberals to conservatives) of an industry or occupation shapes whether pro-equality logics and norms emerge, and thereby whether equality initiatives are genuinely pursued and pay gaps narrow.
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Personal liberalism, age, gender, race, education, region/state fixed effects, industry/occupational polarization, work-related variables; industry and occupational fixed effects

Drawing on field theory, the authors argue that industries and occupations function as fields whose ideological composition is reproduced and reinforced through interorganizational "hubs" (trade associations, conferences, unions, professional groups). A higher ratio of liberals lets pro-equality logics—shared understandings that gender and racial equality are valued and expected—emerge, spread, and become legitimate, so members support pro-equality practices and pay gaps narrow. In conservative fields, equality-supporting members encounter fewer like-minded peers and efforts challenge a meritocratic status quo, so pro-equality logics emerge weakly or not at all and gaps persist.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends research on pay gaps, organizational ideology, and political polarization by theorizing ideology as a field-level (industry/occupational) norm rather than only an attribute of individuals, teams, or firms. It shows that shared ideological logics can emerge even in loosely connected settings through informational hubs, and it identifies political segregation in the labor market as an overlooked driver of societal polarization, reframing slow equality progress as the uneven distribution of pro-equality fields rather than uniformly symbolic effort.
practical_implication: Initiatives to reduce pay gaps should be more effective in liberal fields (where equality is more widely valued) and face greater barriers—but greater room for change—in conservative fields. Efforts are likely more effective when directed toward influential hubs such as industry associations, professional conferences, training centers, and accreditation agencies that can normalize fair-pay and objective-hiring practices across a field.
limitations: The authors could not directly measure whether promoting equality is a logic or norm for each field, so they cannot fully confirm that differing liberal/conservative logics explain the findings. The data lack information on individuals' specific job levels, limiting analysis of pay gaps among similar roles at different hierarchical levels, and other omitted controls may refine the understanding of pay inequality. Data are limited to the United States.
future_research: Future work could examine how actors embedded in multiple intersecting fields (e.g., an HR director in the energy sector) navigate conflicting ideological pressures, and whether powerful actors (executives, boards) exert disproportionate influence over a field's ideological leaning. Researchers could measure pro-equality norms directly, incorporate finer-grained job-level indicators, and conduct cross-cultural studies to test whether the patterns hold where institutional support for equality and political polarization differ.
abstract: Many scholars assume that initiatives to promote gender and racial pay equality are largely performative, helping firms appear socially responsible while making little progress. However, the extent to which such initiatives are genuinely supported may depend on the ideological leaning of the field in which they occur. We propose that the higher the ratio of liberals in an industry or occupation, the more its members will be guided by logics that support the equal distribution of resources between gender and racial groups. Using large-scale archival samples of U.S. employees and recent data on U.S. firms and nonprofit organizations, we find support for our theory. The results indicate that membership in liberal-leaning industries and occupations is associated with stronger support for the use of pro-equality practices. Liberal-leaning industries and occupations also showed greater progress toward gender and racial pay equality over time. Notably, we controlled for various alternative explanations, such as demographic and geographic differences between fields. Thus, this paper suggests that the political composition of a field may give rise to logics and norms that shape how efforts to promote group equality are understood and pursued.
apa_citation: Fang, R. T., Klein, F. B., Nguyen, C., Lingwall, J., & Lewis, A. (2026). Political segregation in the labor market and the persistence of pay gaps. *Academy of Management Journal*. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0371

title: Linking Multisite Sex Ad Data at the Individual Level to Aid Counter-Trafficking Efforts
methods: Design-science / analytics pipeline. End-to-end ad-linking process using a graph representation of sex ad data, a giant-component filtering procedure, an ASW-tailored sentence transformer (BERT variant) with cosine similarity, and a logistic-regression binary classifier; benchmarked against a betweenness-centrality method on an open multisite dataset.
industry: Online commercial sex / adult service websites (ASWs)
country: United States (ASWs hosting US-targeted ads; some sites hosted outside U.S. jurisdiction)
units: Sex ads (and the artifacts within them: post text, images/pHashes, contact info) linked to individuals or unique posting entities
n_sample: More than 10 million ads collected from nine ASWs (open dataset)
authors: Freeman, Nickolas; Bott, Greg; Keskin, Burcu; Parton, Jason; Cochran, James
theories: network science (giant-component theory); transformer/sentence-embedding models (Vaswani et al., 2017; Reimers and Gurevych, 2019)
keywords: anomaly detection; artificial intelligence; network science; sex trafficking
research_question: How can the high volumes of disparate, noisy data from multiple adult service websites be collected, linked at the level of an individual or unique posting entity, and filtered of erroneous links to produce actionable intelligence for counter-trafficking operations?
mechanism_summary: The process builds artifacts (post text, perceptual image hashes, contact info) into an undirected graph in which a giant component emerges from generic and misappropriated data. By recognizing this giant component as a known network-science phenomenon, the authors project it to post-text vertices and iterate over edges, using a sentence transformer fine-tuned on ASW posts to score same-user probability and remove low-probability (erroneous) edges without deleting data. This decomposes the giant component into many smaller components, each likely corresponding to one individual or posting entity, yielding more actionable leads than a betweenness-centrality method while running far faster.
theoretical_contribution: The paper's central contribution is recognizing the emergence of a giant component in the graph representation of sex ad data, establishing a connection between counter-trafficking analytics and network science. It introduces a data-driven, language-model-based edge-filtering procedure that decomposes the giant component without data loss and a novel scheme for generating training labels from nongiant components in the absence of ground truth.
practical_implication: The process transforms large volumes of disparate ASW data into summary and detail outputs that law enforcement and nonprofit partners can use directly to initiate contact with potential victims, minimizing volunteers' exposure to explicit content. Refined over years of practitioner collaboration, it has already supported operations identifying more than 60 potential trafficking victims and can process millions of ads in under an hour for daily updates.
limitations: Not all ads on ASWs are associated with trafficking victims, and the process does not attempt to predict whether any individual is being trafficked. ASWs are only one channel (others include social media and the Dark Web). Because the procedure avoids data loss, scam ads are allowed to persist, and a residual (smaller) giant component often remains whose composition is not fully understood.
future_research: A better understanding of how scam ads manifest in the graph representation is needed. The authors also call for improved tools to discover emerging ASWs and shifts in volume within a highly fluid ecosystem, and for techniques that exploit images to detect erroneous links despite the low-resolution imagery prevalent on these sites.
abstract: Methodology/results: Our process leverages concepts and techniques from network science, information systems, and artificial intelligence to link ads across sites at the level of an individual or unique posting entity.
apa_citation: Freeman, N., Bott, G., Keskin, B., Parton, J., & Cochran, J. (2026). Linking multisite sex ad data at the individual level to aid counter-trafficking efforts. *Manufacturing & Service Operations Management*, 28(1), 133–152. https://doi.org/10.1287/msom.2024.0816
