title: Recent global value chain reconfiguration: drivers and consequences on EU carbon footprint
methods: Environmentally extended multiregional input-output (MRIO) model using OECD ICIO tables; additive structural decomposition analysis (SDA) isolating eight elements; relocation typology (reshoring/offshoring/reoffshoring) following Gao et al. (2022).
industry: All sectors economy-wide; results aggregated to 16 sectors with focus on emission-intensive industries (mining, basic metals, electricity and gas, transport)
country: Global, with six aggregated regions (EU, USMCA, BRIIAT, East Asia, China, RoW) and particular focus on the European Union
time_period: 1995 to 2018, split into benchmark (1995-2008) and reporting (2008-2018) periods
units: Country-sector pairs (regions and industries) in the input-output system
n_sample: OECD ICIO tables covering 67 regions and 45 industries; CO2 satellite account for 85 regions and 71 industries
authors: García-Alaminos, Ángela; Cadarso, María-Ángeles; López, Luis A.; Tobarra, María-Ángeles
theories: Consumption-based carbon accounting; Environmentally extended multiregional input-output (MRIO) analysis; Structural decomposition analysis (SDA)
keywords: Carbon footprint; International trade; Multiregional input-output model; Reshoring
research_question: To what extent the recent evolution of trade flows and the GVC supplying the EU are geared towards decreasing carbon emissions, as well as the role played by reshoring strategies so far.
mechanism_summary: The paper computes carbon multipliers from an environmentally extended MRIO model and applies an additive structural decomposition (averaging two polar decompositions) to attribute changes in the EU's carbon footprint to specific drivers. By comparing a benchmark period (1995-2008) against a reporting period (2008-2018), it isolates relocation patterns and shows that reshoring and reoffshoring embody less carbon than offshoring, while geographical supplier shifts toward dirtier economies raise emissions in both periods. EU footprint reductions after 2008 stem mainly from lower emission intensities and cleaner intermediate-input technical change.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends the GVC-reconfiguration literature, which is dominated by sustainability-as-driver framings, by treating sustainability as an outcome of relocation and quantifying the carbon content of distinct relocation patterns. It widens the temporal coverage with more recent data, complements prior GVC-reconfiguration analyses with the environmental dimension, and isolates the effect of technical change on GVC restructuring within a complete additive decomposition.
practical_implication: The growing relevance of imported emissions supports the adequacy of the EU's planned Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism. Because offshoring shifts toward dirtier economies persist, trade policy and alliances (e.g., Free Trade Agreements, reshoring/nearshoring/friendshoring) should embed sustainability and emissions objectives, accompanied by low-carbon technology transfers and binding responsible-sourcing standards to exploit unrealized synergies between resilience and emission-reduction goals.
limitations: The analysis relies on the 2021 OECD ICIO release ending in 2018, so it cannot capture the carbon consequences of the COVID-19 pandemic or the Ukraine-Russia war. The reshoring definition is strict (requiring prior offshoring in the benchmark period), excluding cases where domestic supply rises without prior offshoring. Monetary deflation assumes similar price evolution across industries within a country.
future_research: Continued monitoring of GVC trends and their carbon content is needed, including the years after 2018 not in the database, to assess the impact of the pandemic and the Ukraine-Russia war. Further work should examine how environmental issues can play a more prominent role in firms' supply and location decisions and GVC reconfiguration.
abstract: Our results show a change in global emissions evolution from 2008 onwards, with emerging trends of global value chains reconfiguration showing a more vital role of environmental concerns. We also find that reshoring and reoffshoring are less emission-intensive than offshoring. In the EU's context, its footprint has been reduced since the 2008 crisis, mainly due to lower emissions intensities and technical changes towards cleaner ways of production. In contrast, the geographical shift of suppliers contributes to the growth of emissions in the whole period.
apa_citation: García-Alaminos, Á., Cadarso, M.-Á., López, L. A., & Tobarra, M.-Á. (2026). Recent global value chain reconfiguration: Drivers and consequences on EU carbon footprint. *Ecological Economics*, 240, 108828. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108828

title: Evaluating Organizations in an Age of Absurdity: How Fake News Disrupts the Relationship Between Business and Society
methods: Editorial introduction to a five-article special issue; reviews the literature on social evaluations, summarizes and synthesizes the special-issue studies, and develops a revised conceptual perspective plus a future-research agenda across stakeholder, social-evaluation, and societal levels.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Gardberg, Naomi A.; Colleoni, Elanor; Barnett, Michael L.; Rehbein, Kathleen
theories: information asymmetry theory (Stiglitz, 2000); signaling theory (Spence, 1974; Bergh et al., 2014); social evaluations / legitimacy and reputation theory (Bitektine & Haack, 2015; Pollock et al., 2019); stakeholder theory (Freeman, 1984)
keywords: fake news; information asymmetry; legitimacy; reputation; social evaluations; stigma
research_question: As information about firms becomes decreasingly credible, can stakeholders still make effective use of their influence to drive firms toward socially responsible behaviors—and to what extent does the rise of fake news compromise the social evaluations (reputation, legitimacy, status, stigma, celebrity) that underpin stakeholder cognition and the use of social control to align business with society?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Social evaluations function as a mechanism of social control over business only if three interrelated conditions hold—credible, verifiable signals from accountable intermediaries (antecedent), judgment aggregation toward consensus (formation), and predictable market reward/punishment (outcome).
- Disruption: Fake news laden with emotional appeals corrodes all three conditions—lay experts produce near-costless signals with no reputational stake, signal convergence fails amid algorithmic echo chambers, and ideological/identity-based prior beliefs override organization-specific evaluations.
- Emergent dynamics: Individual entrenchment plus collective fragmentation make evaluations polarized and unstable; emotionality outperforms facts, a strong prior reputation becomes a liability rather than a buffer, and a negative "illusory" residue persists even after fake news is debunked.
- Boundary conditions: Vulnerability likely varies by stakeholders' information asymmetry, direct experience with the firm, valence of that experience, and whether a firm's offerings are search versus experience goods.

The editorial synthesizes five studies to argue that fake news shifts social-evaluation management from substantive to symbolic, crisis-driven impression management. Because emotionally resonant narratives can sustain stakeholder support despite objectively problematic firm conduct—and because moral- and emotion-based evaluations (stigma, celebrity) resist rational correction more than rational ones (reputation)—the disciplinary function of stakeholder disapproval weakens, loosening the tie between firm behavior and social outcomes.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial advances a revised perspective on social evaluations that integrates the uncertainty introduced by fake news, specifying how the antecedent, formation, and outcome conditions underpinning social evaluations break down in a compromised information environment. It reframes signaling, stakeholder, and information-asymmetry accounts by distinguishing organization-specific prior beliefs from broader ideological/identity-based priors, and theorizes the conditions under which different social evaluations (reputation vs. stigma vs. celebrity) diverge in their susceptibility to, and recoverability from, fake news.
practical_implication: Firms can no longer rely on a strong prior reputation as a buffer; countering fake news requires moving beyond fact-checking and debunking to manage the emotional intensity and legitimacy crisis that fake news creates, combining verifiable disclosure with emotionally resonant responses while recognizing that defensive narrative management may crowd out substantive reputation-building.
limitations: As a conceptual editorial introducing a special issue, the claims rest on synthesis of five studies rather than new systematic evidence. The contributing papers focus mainly on micro-level evaluators, and differences across stakeholder groups in exposure and vulnerability to fake news remain largely unexplored.
future_research: The authors propose questions across three levels: stakeholder-level (who is vulnerable, how information asymmetry, direct experience, and search vs. experience goods moderate fake-news effects), social-evaluation-level (how fake news differentially affects reputation, stigma, and celebrity given their rational, moral, and emotional bases), and societal-level (which mechanisms can restore the integrity of evaluation processes and whether some evaluations are more resilient or correctable than others).
abstract: Fake news—false or misleading information presented as news—now abounds. As information about firms becomes decreasingly credible, can stakeholders make effective use of their influence to drive firms to engage in socially responsible behaviors? We assembled this special issue to explore the extent to which the social evaluations that underpin stakeholder cognition and action have been compromised and to assess how this affects firm behavior in this era of fake news. The five articles comprising this issue elucidate the strategies used to spread fake news and show how it influences stakeholders. They find that emotionality proves effective in spreading and countering fake news. However, even when fake news is successfully countered, a negative residue remains. Fake news is particularly insidious because even when stakeholders know it to be false, they act in ways consonant with it being true. Overall, these articles provide evidence
apa_citation: Gardberg, N. A., Colleoni, E., Barnett, M. L., & Rehbein, K. (2026). Evaluating organizations in an age of absurdity: How fake news disrupts the relationship between business and society. *Business & Society*, 65(2), 279–301. https://doi.org/10.1177/00076503251412639

title: Firm-Level Nature Dependence
methods: Constructs firm-level NatureDep scores by combining ENCORE Dependency Materiality Ratings with revenue-segment data; panel OLS / linear probability regressions with industry-by-country-by-time fixed effects, clustered standard errors; validation against BlackRock engagements, downside-risk (VaR, LPM), and RepRisk incidents.
industry: Listed firms across all sectors (most nature-dependent: Pharmaceutical, Biotechnology & Life Sciences; Food, Beverage & Tobacco; Household & Personal Products)
country: 117 countries (global; Compustat North America and Compustat Global universe matched to Refinitiv)
units: Firm-year observations (248,394 firm-years in baseline descriptive sample)
n_sample: 31,772 listed firms
authors: Garel, Alexandre; Romec, Arthur; Sautner, Zacharias; Wagner, Alexander
theories: double materiality of nature loss; downside / tail risk pricing (Hoepner et al., 2024; Ilhan, Vilkov, and Sautner, 2021); ecosystem services / natural capital (Dasgupta, 2021)
keywords: biodiversity risk; nature dependence; nature risk; physical risk; shareholder engagement
research_question: How can a firm's dependence on ecosystem services be quantified at scale, and do these dependencies have financially material consequences for investors and firms? The paper builds new firm-level NatureDep scores and asks whether they are reflected in corporate action, disclosure, investor engagement, stock-market downside risk, and real (incident) outcomes.
mechanism_summary: NatureDep scores translate global ENCORE Dependency Materiality Ratings into firm-level exposure to physical nature risk via revenue-segment weighting. Because the loss of a critical ecosystem service is hard to substitute, strong reliance on a few services (NatureDepHigh) generates non-linear tail risk: it predicts downside risk (driven by water-related dependencies) but not average returns. High dependence also raises the likelihood of damaging nature, producing ESG incidents that link physical and transition risks and concentrate downside outcomes in incident years.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to the nascent biodiversity and nature-finance literature by introducing novel, scalable firm-level measures of nature dependence and demonstrating a "double materiality" of nature loss — firms with greater impact on nature also tend to depend on it more. It shows that nature dependence is financially material through tail-risk and incident channels rather than average returns, and that investors (e.g., BlackRock) have begun pricing this exposure while corporate action and disclosure lag.
practical_implication: Investors can use NatureDep scores to identify firms most exposed to physical nature risks and address them via divestment (exit) or engagement (voice). Firms that mitigate or hedge nature dependencies may see financial-market benefits as investor awareness grows, and the metrics can help firms meet upcoming nature-related disclosure requirements.
limitations: The measures capture exposure to nature risks rather than risk realizations: they reflect global dependence assessments and do not account for local ecosystem deterioration, the likelihood of disruption, or firms' adaptive capacity, because asset-location data are not yet available at scale. Downside-risk evidence is indirect since the authors cannot condition on specific risk realizations, and ENCORE's annual frequency imperfectly captures the dynamics of living organisms.
future_research: The authors anticipate refinement of the measures, particularly by linking geospatial firm asset-location data with localized ecosystem-service conditions. Further work could examine how nature-related shocks propagate through supply chains, how firms adapt to dependencies, sharpen tests of financial implications with more granular data, and develop easy-to-use dependence metrics for corporate disclosure.
abstract: We employ the scores in two applications. First, the scores relate positively to measures of downside risk, with effects stemming mostly from high dependencies on water-related ecosystem services. Second, the scores predict nature-related incidents that arise when high nature dependence leads firms to damage nature, overuse resources, or trigger disputes with local communities.
apa_citation: Garel, A., Romec, A., Sautner, Z., & Wagner, A. (2026). Firm-level nature dependence. *Review of Finance*, 30(1), 231–272. https://doi.org/10.1093/rof/rfaf069

title: Corporate Nature Risk Perceptions
methods: Global cross-sectional survey of investee companies of a large asset owner (385 respondents); descriptive tabulations, chi-square goodness-of-fit tests against the population, and OLS regressions with size/industry/region/respondent-function/distribution fixed effects and robust standard errors.
industry: Listed equity portfolio companies across all sectors (e.g., financials 16%, real estate 13%, technology 12%, industrials 11%)
country: Global; headquarters mostly Europe (52%), Asia (22%), North America (16%)
time_period: Survey distributed February 3, 2025 (portfolio as of June 2024)
units: Firms (corporate respondents, mostly sustainability and investor-relations officers)
n_sample: 385 respondent companies (374 investor channel, 11 UZH channel)
authors: Gjerde, Snorre; Sautner, Zacharias; Wagner, Alexander F.; Wegerich, Alexis
theories: feedback effects of stock prices (Bond, Edmans, & Goldstein, 2012); financial materiality (IFRS, 2024); value versus values motives (Starks, 2023)
keywords: biodiversity risk; natural capital; nature risk; shareholder engagement
research_question: How do companies perceive nature-related financial risks (their exposure, management, and disclosure), and how do they perceive and respond to investor interest and engagement on these emerging risks?
mechanism_summary: The paper treats nature risks through the lens of financial materiality (IFRS, 2024): risks matter when they substantially affect a firm's financial position in ways that influence investor decisions. Survey responses show that perceived material exposure is strongly associated with undertaking assessment, mitigation, and disclosure actions, and that investor engagement is associated with higher propensities to act on dependency assessments, risk management, and disclosure—evidence the authors read as supporting causal effects of ESG engagement that are hard to document with archival data. Drawing on feedback-effects reasoning (Bond, Edmans, & Goldstein, 2012), the authors argue a perceived horizon gap and a perceived disconnect between investor attention and cashflow/cost-of-capital analysis could impede efficient incorporation of nature risks into prices.
theoretical_contribution: The study contributes early, survey-based evidence to the nascent finance literature on biodiversity and nature risks, documenting corporate (rather than investor or academic) perceptions of an emerging risk type. It surfaces a "fuzziness of the objective function" problem—companies perceive investor attention to nature risks that does not translate into traditional valuation channels (cashflows, discount rates)—posing a challenge for theorists modelling nature risk in corporate finance and asset pricing.
practical_implication: Because financial effects originate from dispersed sources across the entire value chain, no single one-size-fits-all metric (unlike carbon emissions for climate) can capture nature-risk exposure, so risk-management and reporting frameworks must accommodate sectoral and regional heterogeneity. Investor engagement on nature appears value-generating and behavior-changing for many firms, and because climate and nature are widely seen as intertwined, the authors suggest integrating rather than sequencing climate and nature strategies.
limitations: The sample is likely tilted toward companies with higher awareness of and stronger credentials in nature risks, since such firms are more disposed to participate, and the overall response rate is below 6 percent. Because the surveying asset owner is also a major shareholder engaging on nature, responses may be subject to acquiescence bias; the authors run several tests (state/sovereign-fund top-5 indicator, cross-channel t-tests, personal-experience correlations) that show little systematic bias but cannot fully rule it out.
future_research: The authors call for theoretical work on the financial (cashflow and risk) and non-financial channels through which nature risks affect firms, development and integration of metrics capturing nature-risk complexity into risk management and reporting standards, and incorporation of nature-risk features into asset-pricing and valuation models. More empirical work is also needed on how investor engagement contributes to better identification, assessment, and management of financially material nature risks.
abstract: We survey portfolio companies of a large asset owner to explore the evolving landscape of nature risks. Nearly half of all companies (48 percent) view nature risks as financially material, and 43 percent of those perceive nature-related physical risks, and 27 percent transition risks, as having financial effects already today. Three-quarters of companies experiencing nature-related investor engagement view these interactions as value-generating.
apa_citation: Gjerde, S., Sautner, Z., Wagner, A. F., & Wegerich, A. (2026). Corporate nature risk perceptions. *Review of Finance*, 30(1), 11–42. https://doi.org/10.1093/rof/rfaf050

title: The pricing of biodiversity risk in commodity markets
methods: Characteristic-based asset pricing on a panel of internationally traded commodities; panel regressions of monthly spot/futures excess returns on the intensity of species loss with month-year and commodity fixed effects, standard errors double-clustered at month-year and commodity level; risk-adjusted returns from a seven-factor commodity model; an event study around the Kunming Declaration (with a Paris Agreement placebo); and beta-sorted tercile portfolios on exposure to an aggregate biodiversity loss index (PC of LPI and RLI).
industry: Globally traded agricultural commodities (baseline 23 spot, 10 futures, e.g., cocoa, coffee, palm oil, sugar), expanded to 56 commodities including non-agricultural in the aggregate analysis
units: Commodity-month observations
n_sample: 23 agricultural commodities (spot) and 10 (futures) in baseline; 56 commodities in the aggregate-shock analysis
authors: Guidolin, Massimo; Pedio, Manuela
theories: characteristic-based asset pricing (Bolton & Kacperczyk, 2021, 2023; Brennan, Chordia & Subrahmanyam, 1998); commodity production / convenience-yield model (Gorton, Hayashi & Rouwenhorst, 2012; Yang, 2013); transition risk vs. physical risk framework
keywords: biodiversity premium; biodiversity risk; commodity returns; transition risk
research_question: The paper examines whether commodities with higher biodiversity footprints — reflected in species loss intensity and land-use impact — earn systematically different returns, consistent with investors pricing in the financial implications of nature-related regulatory and societal responses (i.e., biodiversity transition risk).
mechanism_summary: Higher biodiversity-footprint commodities face elevated transition risk because regulatory shifts, import bans, due-diligence rules, and changing investor/consumer preferences can penalize biodiversity-harming production; rational investors therefore demand a risk premium, so prices are bid down on adverse news to allow higher subsequent expected returns. The baseline panel finds a premium of roughly 20-60 basis points per month per ten additional species lost per 10,000 km², concentrated after 2015 and robust to emissions and deforestation controls. The Kunming-Declaration event study and the BLI beta-sorted long-short portfolio (annualized alpha ~5.4%) corroborate that this transition-risk premium operates at both the individual-commodity and aggregate-market levels; a Section 5 production model shows why the premium appears in spot but is washed out of futures returns via a risk-driven convenience-yield adjustment.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends the emerging literature on the financial materiality of biodiversity loss from equities to commodity markets, a domain where nature-related risks are immediate, physical, and directly measurable, and shows that investors price biodiversity transition risk at the asset level even absent firm-level disclosure. It develops a commodity production model (extending Gorton, Hayashi & Rouwenhorst, 2012 and Yang, 2013) in which expected spot returns decompose into a basis premium, a storage-cost volatility premium, and a biodiversity-intensity premium, and explains why the biodiversity premium does not survive in futures returns.
practical_implication: The findings imply that investors can use commodities' intensity-of-species-loss footprints to anticipate biodiversity-related repricing, and that a high-beta-minus-low-beta commodity portfolio can hedge short-run aggregate biodiversity shocks. For policymakers, intensity-based species-loss metrics (rather than raw deforestation or emissions) better identify the commodities most exposed to the regulatory measures that constitute transition risk.
limitations: The intensity-of-species-loss measure, being the output of a cSAR land-use model, captures habitat degradation from anthropogenic land use but not the effects of chemicals, invasive species, water consumption, habitat fragmentation, or species endemism, and is subject to measurement error (attenuation bias). The futures-market null result rests on a small sample of futures with correspondingly low test power, and the Commodity Footprints data cover only agricultural commodities.
future_research: Future work could extend the analysis to non-agricultural commodities (metals, fossil fuels) as broader biodiversity data become available; enrich the footprint measure with multi-dimensional biodiversity data capturing taxonomic, functional, and geographic variation; and test whether markets differentiate among sub-components of biodiversity loss (species abundance, genetic diversity, ecosystem degradation) using emerging sources and taxonomies such as ENCORE, the IUCN Red List, and NatureDep.
abstract: This article provides empirical evidence that biodiversity-related transition risk is priced in global commodity markets, with particular emphasis on agricultural commodities. Using intensity-based metrics of species loss per harvested land unit, we obtain empirical evidence that commodities with higher biodiversity footprints earn significant risk premia, after controlling for commodity-specific factors. An event study around the Kunming Declaration further shows that commodities associated with greater biodiversity risk experienced negative abnormal returns following the declaration. In an aggregate-level analysis, we additionally find that commodities with higher sensitivity (beta) to biodiversity shocks earn significantly higher excess returns, reinforcing the presence of a biodiversity­related risk premium across global commodity markets. Our findings suggest that investors are increasingly internalizing the biodiversity-related risks at the commodity-asset level. The findings can be rationalized by a commodity production model, which we outline in Section 5.
apa_citation: Guidolin, M., & Pedio, M. (2026). The pricing of biodiversity risk in commodity markets. *Review of Finance*, 30(1), 351-389. https://doi.org/10.1093/rof/rfaf068

title: Introduction to the special issue: ‘Biodiversity and finance: Risk, disclosure and double materiality’
methods: Editorial introduction to a special issue; no empirical design. Frames the disconnect between accelerating biodiversity loss and financial-sector responsiveness, motivating an emerging field of ecological finance.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Guo, Chunying; Agarwala, Matthew; Freeman, Mark; Groom, Ben; McKenzie, Emily
theories: ecological economics (Boulding, 1966; Daly, 1993; Victor, 2010)
keywords: biodiversity; biodiversity-related risk; double materiality; ecological finance; ecosystem services; finance
research_question: How can biodiversity be integrated into financial logic, given that the financial sector has remained largely unresponsive to accelerating biodiversity loss despite the two-way relationship between biodiversity-related risk and the performance of financial assets?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: The financial sector channels capital through lending, investment, insurance, and asset management, yet has remained largely unresponsive to biodiversity loss.
- Key construct — biodiversity-related risk: Biodiversity loss poses both physical risks (degraded ecosystems reducing returns on nature-dependent sectors) and transition risks (regulatory, legal, or market changes).
- Two-way relationship: Financial flows can themselves exacerbate biodiversity loss (e.g., financing deforestation-linked infrastructure, insurance underwriting for high-risk extractive industries).
- Structural barriers: The difficulty of monetizing ecosystem services, the public-goods nature of biodiversity, and misaligned fiduciary incentives keep biodiversity marginal in financial decision-making.
- Proposed reframing — ecological finance: Treating economies as open subsystems of the biosphere, subject to biophysical constraints, limits to substitution between capitals, and ecological timescales.

The introduction motivates the special issue by arguing that biodiversity remains marginal in financial decision-making despite a documented two-way relationship: biodiversity loss undermines the long-term viability of underlying economic activities and thus the value of financial assets, while financial flows can directly and indirectly accelerate that loss. Drawing on ecological economics, which views the economy as fundamentally embedded in nature rather than external to it, the editors call for moving beyond narrow risk framing toward an "ecological finance" that confronts the systemic challenge of integrating biodiversity into financial logic, while acknowledging the public-goods obstacle that makes private financial returns from biodiversity conservation difficult to capture.
theoretical_contribution: The piece advances the framing of an emergent field of "ecological finance" grounded in ecological economics, extending the view of economies as open subsystems of the closed biosphere to the financial system, and positing that if global financial returns are subject to biophysical constraints, the financial system would have an interest in conserving nature.
practical_implication: It signals to investors, insurers, and policymakers that biodiversity-related physical and transition risks materially affect financial assets, and that policy efforts to "crowd-in" private finance for biodiversity conservation must contend with the public-goods nature of biodiversity's economic benefits.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Not reported in paper
abstract: Not reported in paper

title: Do sustainability reports contain financially material information?
methods: Event study following Beaver (1968): three-day cumulative abnormal returns, abnormal return volatility, and abnormal trading volume around sustainability-report announcement dates, benchmarked against quarterly earnings announcements via two-sample t-tests; OLS cross-sectional regressions on report characteristics; supplemental materiality analysis of SASB legal-loss metrics using the Violation Tracker database, plus document-type and website-positioning audits.
industry: US firms across Fama–French 48 industries, weighted toward high-impact / high-visibility sectors (petroleum and natural gas, utilities, chemicals, food products, trading/investment managers)
time_period: Sustainability reports announced 2019–2021 (drawn from a SASB list of reports issued 2015–2021); financials 2017–2021
units: Firm-year sustainability-report announcements containing SASB metrics
n_sample: 295 sustainability-report announcements (247 unique firms); from an initial list of 865 US SASB reports
authors: Haley, Suzanne; Shaffer, Matthew; Sloan, Richard
theories: financial materiality / decision-usefulness framework (SASB 2020); information content / event-study paradigm (Beaver, 1968); voluntary disclosure theory
keywords: Disclosure; Materiality; Reporting; Sustainability
research_question: Do corporate sustainability reports that incorporate SASB metrics — designed to be financially material and decision-useful — actually convey new, financially material information to investors, as measured by stock-price and trading-volume reactions to their announcement?
mechanism_summary: Adapting Beaver's (1968) information-content paradigm, the authors test whether the release of SASB-aligned sustainability information moves prices or volume. Abnormal Volatility (1.046) and Abnormal Volume (0.069) are statistically indistinguishable from their no-information benchmarks, whereas the matched earnings announcements show large, highly significant reactions (Abnormal Volatility 3.757; Abnormal Volume 1.328). Supplemental tests trace the null to two mechanisms: the metrics (e.g., monetary losses from legal proceedings) are typically financially immaterial, and in the rare material cases the information is preempted by mandatory Form 8-K/10-K disclosures. Document-type and website-positioning analyses show most firms target the reports at a broad stakeholder audience rather than investors.
theoretical_contribution: The paper provides the first direct event-study evidence on the information content of periodic corporate sustainability reports containing SASB metrics, reaching the opposite conclusion to Beaver's (1968) seminal finding for earnings. It reconciles prior mixed value-relevance results by showing that associations documented elsewhere need not reflect incremental decision-usefulness, and it reframes sustainability reporting as primarily addressed to impact-materiality concerns of broad stakeholders rather than to financial materiality for investors.
practical_implication: Because US SASB-aligned sustainability reports do not appear to deliver significant new financially material information, standard setters (SASB, TCFD, ISSB) and securities regulators cannot justify mandatory sustainability disclosure on financial-materiality grounds alone; an impact-materiality focus (as adopted by the GRI and the EU CSRD) may better serve actual users. Investors and reporting firms should reassess the costs and benefits of producing and analyzing such reports.
limitations: Results are specific to the sample of US sustainability reports examined and may not generalize. Firms' decisions about whether, where, and how (e.g., with a press release) to disclose SASB metrics are endogenous, and the sample-selection requirements may bias toward reports containing relatively less information. SASB standards and the financial consequences of the underlying impacts are nascent and evolving.
future_research: The financial materiality of sustainability metrics may rise as standards improve and as governments impose greater financial penalties (e.g., carbon taxes) for negative impacts, warranting re-examination over time. The evidence speaks only to financial materiality and is silent on impact materiality, leaving open the assessment of sustainability reports against impact-materiality objectives and broader stakeholder uses.
abstract: Recent years have witnessed significant growth in corporate sustainability reporting. Yet existing research provides mixed evidence on the information content of these reports for investors. We examine the stock market reaction to the announcement of a sample of US corporate sustainability reports incorporating Sustainability Accounting Standards Board metrics that are intended to provide financially material information to investors. Using standard measures of information content, we cannot find compelling evidence that these reports provide a significant amount of new information to investors. Further analysis of a subset of common metrics indicates that they are either financially immaterial or preempted by traditional financial disclosures. Finally, we show that most firms target their sustainability reports at a broad set of sustainability-oriented stakeholders rather than a narrow set of financially oriented investors.
apa_citation: Haley, S., Shaffer, M., & Sloan, R. (2026). Do sustainability reports contain financially material information? *Review of Accounting Studies*, 31(1), 1–36. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11142-025-09912-5

title: Reimagining Growth Futures: Overcoming the False Binary between Green Growth and Degrowth
methods: Conceptual analysis conducted as an integrative literature review; inductively maps green growth and degrowth standpoints along nine dimensions grouped into national institutions, world order, and scientific cosmology, drawing on existing reviews plus keyword searches and gray literature.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Scholarly and gray-literature positions within the green growth and degrowth debate (conceptual, no empirical sample)
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Hasselbalch, Jacob; Larsen, Mathias
theories: orientational paradigms (Hoyningen-Huene & Kincaid, 2023; Engler et al., 2025); scientific cosmology (Allan, 2018)
keywords: Degrowth; Green Growth; Imaginaries; Paradigms; Post-Growth
research_question: The paper asks whether green growth and degrowth are genuinely mutually exclusive, internally coherent, competing paradigms, or whether the debate is "caught in a false binary" that obscures substantial intra-paradigm disagreements and cross-paradigm overlaps in how a green transition can be imagined.
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Green growth and degrowth are better understood as "orientational paradigms" (a thinner version of Kuhnian paradigms) rather than distinct, internally consistent scientific paradigms or opposed ecopolitical projects.
- Key constructs / dimensions: Nine inductively derived dimensions of variation, grouped into three meta-categories — national institutions (agents of change, mechanisms of change, economic system, political institutions), world order (international economic integration, global applicability, international redistribution), and scientific cosmology (technological progress, understanding of history).
- Process / method: An integrative literature review maps each position along a continuum (extreme A to opposite B) within both green growth and degrowth, surfacing internal inconsistencies and mutual overlaps.
- Emergent outcome: A shift from a two-pole dichotomy to a multidimensional landscape of possible growth futures, revealing common ground (e.g., shared appeals to stronger global governance, technologically neutral Green New Deal thinking).

By demonstrating that each paradigm spans wide internal variation (e.g., green growth running from neoliberal carbon pricing to green industrial policy; degrowth running from local self-sufficiency to digitally enabled planning), the authors show the binary is "false." The straw-manning by which each side mischaracterizes the other reproduces an illusory paradigm; re-reading both as overlapping orientational paradigms within ecological economics builds the case for pluralizing the debate.
theoretical_contribution: The paper re-conceptualizes green growth and degrowth as overlapping "orientational paradigms" rather than competing, internally coherent ecopolitical projects, and offers a nine-dimensional analytical framework (spanning national institutions, world order, and scientific cosmology) for mapping the range of standpoints. This moves theorizing of growth futures from a restrictive dichotomy toward a multidimensional landscape, and contributes to the disciplinary distinction between ecological and mainstream economics.
practical_implication: The authors hope the framework can inspire policy proposals and reforms that combine elements previously assigned to opposing camps — for example, pairing planning with carbon pricing and industrial policy to move from green capitalism toward green mixed economies or ecosocialism — to deliver a rapid and just green transition.
limitations: The nine dimensions cover only identified actual disagreements, not all possible ones, and the authors explicitly do not consider them the final or ultimate framework for understanding sustainability and growth. The mapping is illustrative rather than a comprehensive or representative review of the literature, and examples are offered to support points rather than as exhaustive evidence.
future_research: Future scholarship can use the proposed dimensions to avoid assuming homogeneity within, and difference between, the two positions, and can extend or revise the framework with entirely different dimensions and categorizations. Researchers could also probe more deeply the repercussions of treating green growth and degrowth as orientational paradigms for ecological economics, and further establish how this reframing affects the relationship between ecological and mainstream economics.
abstract: We find that there are substantial disagreements within as well as agreements between green growth and degrowth. In consequence, we argue that the literature is caught in a false binary. To constructively move the debate forward, we propose giving up the paradigmatic and polarized approach and instead embracing a multidimensional plurality of imagined growth futures.
apa_citation: Hasselbalch, J., & Larsen, M. (2026). Reimagining growth futures: Overcoming the false binary between green growth and degrowth. *Ecological Economics*, *240*, 108823. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108823

title: Can objective information update subjective beliefs on sustainable farming? Evidence from a randomized experiment with Japanese rice farmers
methods: Online randomized information-provision experiment (three arms) with pre/post belief elicitation; OLS belief-update regressions (ANCOVA with treatment-by-perception-gap interaction); discrete-choice experiment estimated by mixed logit; CPT risk-preference elicitation via multiple price lists; policy simulation.
industry: Crop farming — Japanese rice farmers currently practicing conventional farming (not organic / not half-chemical-reduction)
units: Individual farmers
n_sample: 283 (final analytic sample; 318 responses collected)
authors: Inoue, Ryosuke; Kawasaki, Kentaro
theories: Cumulative Prospect Theory (Tversky & Kahneman, 1992; Tanaka et al., 2010); belief updating / Bayesian-style learning rate (Haaland et al., 2023); information avoidance / asymmetric (good-news bad-news) updating (Golman et al., 2017; Eil & Rao, 2011)
keywords: Environmentally sustainable farming; Information provision; Randomized experiment; Subjective belief
research_question: Can providing objective information about the income changes experienced by current adopters update conventional rice farmers' subjective beliefs (mean and variability) about the profitability of environmentally sustainable practices, and do these beliefs in turn shape willingness to adopt?
mechanism_summary: The mechanism is partial Bayesian-style belief updating: farmers shift their prior subjective distribution toward the objective information at a learning rate θ that depends on the prior-information gap, but updating is incomplete (θ = 0.268 for the OF mean, 0.123 for the HCR standard deviation) because the population-average information is not seen as fully applicable to a farmer's own circumstances. Updating is asymmetric — it occurs only for favorable information (higher OF profitability, lower HCR variability) and not for unfavorable information, consistent with optimism bias and information-avoidance accounts. Because higher subjective means raise and higher subjective variances lower adoption intentions, information that revises beliefs upward translates into greater willingness to adopt, with simulations showing information provision is roughly equivalent to a 4,000 JPY/10a subsidy.
theoretical_contribution: The paper is the first to elicit farmers' subjective beliefs about the opportunity costs (income changes) of adopting sustainable practices and to show experimentally that such beliefs can be updated by objective information, extending the literature that treats perceptions as fixed barriers to adoption. It documents asymmetric ("good-news/bad-news") belief updating among farmers in the agri-environmental domain and links updated beliefs about both the mean and the variance of income to adoption decisions, integrating belief-updating and risk-preference (Cumulative Prospect Theory) perspectives.
practical_implication: Information campaigns that credibly convey the realized profitability of current adopters can cost-effectively raise adoption of sustainable practices, with an estimated policy effect equivalent to a 4,000 JPY/10a subsidy. Because updating is asymmetric and credibility-dependent, policymakers should pair information with peer-to-peer learning, early-adopter "reference points," and demonstration projects, and design transparent, contextually appropriate messaging.
limitations: Subjective beliefs were elicited about an inherently unobservable outcome, so incentive-compatible mechanisms were infeasible and some measurement error may remain. The choice experiment was hypothetical (mitigated only by a cheap-talk script), and the online-panel sample is younger and more male than the national rice-farmer population, limiting external validity. Post-treatment beliefs were measured immediately, so persistence is unknown.
future_research: Field experiments and incentive-compatible designs could test belief updating and actual adoption behavior in real-world settings. Studies should examine other farmer populations, crops, and countries and additional sources of heterogeneity (education, crop type, prior familiarity, regional conditions) to assess generalizability. Longitudinal designs with short- and long-term follow-ups are needed to learn whether belief changes persist and translate into adoption.
abstract: Participants are randomly assigned to one of three groups: a control group receiving only qualitative information (C), a treatment group receiving average income change information about OF and HCR (T1), and a treatment group receiving distributional income change information about OF and HCR (T2). We estimate the effect of the information by eliciting farmers’ subjective beliefs about income changes associated with adopting OF and HCR both before and after the treatment. The results suggest that subjective beliefs can be updated by objective information, particularly when farmers receive favorable information. Additionally, the findings from a choice experiment indicate that both the subjective mean and subjective standard deviation contribute to farmers’ willingness to adopt sustainable farming practices. These findings show the potential of offering objective information to revise subjective beliefs as an effective strategy for promoting the adoption of sustainable farming practices.
apa_citation: Inoue, R., & Kawasaki, K. (2026). Can objective information update subjective beliefs on sustainable farming? Evidence from a randomized experiment with Japanese rice farmers. *Ecological Economics*, 240, 108837. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108837

title: Bottom‐up effects of female strategic leadership: Firm performance effects through employees
methods: Longitudinal panel; generalized estimating equations (GEE) with exchangeable correlation structure and robust SEs; bootstrapped (moderated) mediation; instrumental-variables regression and coarsened exact matching as robustness checks.
industry: For-profit South Korean business organizations across industries (1-digit KSIC), drawn from the Human Capital Corporate Panel (HCCP)
units: Firm-year observations (firms matched with aggregated employee survey responses)
n_sample: 1920 firm-year observations from 744 unique firms (analytic sample); 42,953 employee responses aggregated
authors: Jeong, Seung-Hwan; Kang, Saehee; Byron, Kris
theories: autonomous strategic processes / strategy process (Burgelman, 1983a); microfoundations (Foss & Pedersen, 2016); upper echelons theory (Hambrick & Mason, 1984; Hambrick, 2007)
keywords: diversity; employee participation; gender; top management teams; upper echelons
research_question: How — not whether — does female representation in top management influence firm performance? The authors ask whether female top managers improve firm performance through a bottom-up pathway in which they foster employee participation, complementing prior top-down and perception-based explanations.
mechanism_summary: Drawing on upper echelons theory and strategy-process research, the authors argue that female top managers' more democratic, participative leadership style raises employee participation, which activates bottom-up autonomous strategic processes (Burgelman, 1983a). These processes enhance firm performance by enabling exploration of new competencies and by fostering employee "buy-in" and commitment. Consistent with microfoundations theorizing, employee participation mediates the macro-level link between female top-management representation and firm performance (indirect-only mediation), and the participation–performance link is amplified where firms emphasize innovation or are labor-intensive.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends upper echelons theory by opening the "relational black box" between top managers and employees, showing that TMT composition shapes not only top-down decision processes but also bottom-up autonomous strategic processes operating through employees. It contributes employee-level microfoundations to research on female strategic leadership and firm performance, and argues the "business case" and "social justice case" for gender diversity are more compatible than previously thought, since female leaders broaden employee voice (social justice) which in turn benefits performance (business case).
practical_implication: Firms can realize performance benefits from female strategic leadership by empowering and soliciting voice from all employees, thereby activating bottom-up autonomous strategic processes. Because the business and social-justice rationales for gender diversity are complementary, organizations need not choose between "what women can do for firms" and "what firms can do for women and all employees."
limitations: The theory is tested only with South Korean data, where female representation in top management is very low and gender equality ranks comparatively low, so generalizability to other contexts is untested (likely a conservative setting). Despite GEE, instrumental-variables regression, and coarsened exact matching, endogeneity is not fully eliminated; the authors interpret results as robust associations rather than causal evidence.
future_research: Future studies should test the bottom-up model in varied national and institutional contexts to assess generalizability, and should adopt experimental methods and other robust designs for triangulation to further address endogeneity. The authors also encourage broader adoption of multi-wave, multi-source datasets like the HCCP to examine cross-level macro–micro mechanisms.
abstract: We propose a complementary, bottom-up view: female top managers foster employee participation, which is positively associated with firm performance. Using a unique panel dataset from South Korea, we find that female representation in top management is linked to higher employee participation, which mediates its relationship with firm performance. The benefits of employee participation are especially pronounced in firms with innovation-oriented or labor-intensive strategies. By integrating micro-level employee behaviors with macro-level firm outcomes, our study shows how female strategic leaders contribute to firm performance through a top-enabled, bottom-up process.
apa_citation: Jeong, S.-H., Kang, S., & Byron, K. (2026). Bottom-up effects of female strategic leadership: Firm performance effects through employees. *Strategic Management Journal*, 47(4), 947–973. https://doi.org/10.1002/smj.70038

title: Alienable or inalienable, and how? Individual property rights in commons governance
methods: Single-community longitudinal case study on the Qinghai-Tibetan Plateau (Village X) over ~2009-2022, with five rounds of fieldwork (participant observation, in-depth interviews, stratified household surveys) plus two neighboring comparison villages; ecological outcomes via livestock-quantity counts and remote-sensing NDVI (Mann-Kendall trend test, threshold detection), social outcomes via Student's t-tests and Fisher's exact test, and a qualitative comparison of transaction costs across institutional stages.
industry: Pastoral animal husbandry (yak, Tibetan sheep, horses) on alpine-meadow and wetland rangeland under a common property regime
country: China (Village X, Zoige County, Aba Tibetan Autonomous Prefecture, Sichuan Province, eastern Qinghai-Tibetan Plateau)
time_period: Field data ~2009-2022 (fieldwork rounds in 2012, 2014, 2018, 2020, 2022); NDVI series 2000-2023
units: One focal pastoral community (Village X) plus two neighboring comparison villages; herder households as survey units
n_sample: Village X: 150 households / 850 people; surveyed household samples ranged 30-60 per round (sample ratios 21-40%)
authors: Jia, Xiangyu; Wei, Yiran; Zhang, Zhiqi; Zeren, Gongbu; Pérez-Ibarra, Irene; Villamayor-Tomas, Sergio; Li, Wenjun
theories: Calabresi & Melamed framework (property rules, liability rules, inalienability) (Calabresi & Melamed, 1972); common property / commons governance (Ostrom, 1990); transaction cost economics (Coase, 1960)
keywords: Alienability; Calabresi & Melamed framework; Commons; Community-based environmental markets; Grazing quota; Qinghai-Tibetan Plateau
research_question: How do community institutional arrangements evolve to integrate or resist market mechanisms within commons and support sustainable and equitable resource governance? Specifically, how should individual grazing rights be made alienable (and through which legal protection — property rules, liability rules, or inalienability) within a common property regime?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / entitlement types: inalienability; property rules (voluntary transfer at consented price); liability rules (transfer compensated at a price set by an authoritative third party — here the village collective).
- Process / phases: Stage 1 (2009-2013) inalienable quotas with informal "iron yak" reciprocal lending; Stage 2 (2013-2018) alienable quotas under property rules with bilaterally negotiated prices; Stage 3 (since 2018) alienable quotas under liability rules via collective-monitored compensation.
- Triggering conditions: pre-2009 overgrazing/"internal open access" prompts the quota system; "poor sells cheap" inequities and low/zero negotiated prices under property rules trigger the shift to liability rules.
- Mediating mechanism: transaction costs (information, negotiation, enforcement) — collective monopolization of trading lowers search/negotiation costs while keeping monitoring costs low.
- Emergent outcomes: alienability does not raise grazing intensity; liability rules improve distributive equity and satisfaction (66.7% → 88.1%) and reduce conflict.
- Boundary conditions: efficacy of liability rules is not inherent but depends on strong collective organization, broad participation of poorer households, low monitoring costs, and local norms (oath-keeping, fairness).

The paper traces an evolutionary process in which a Tibetan pastoral community sequentially redesigns the legal protection of individual grazing quotas. Drawing on the Calabresi & Melamed framework and Coasean transaction-cost reasoning, it shows that substituting collective third-party compensation (liability rules) for bilateral bargaining (property rules) lowers negotiation and enforcement costs and counters the "poor sells cheap" problem, so that alienable rights can be integrated into the commons without ecological harm or distributive loss.
theoretical_contribution: The study complements the common property rights framework by foregrounding the alienability dimension of individual rights nested within commons, and demonstrates the enduring viability of the Calabresi & Melamed framework as an analytical tool for commons research. It provides empirical evidence that liability rules — rather than the more-studied property rules — can be a feasible governance pathway, and theorizes the commons as a "dual-structured" arrangement in which legally recognized private use rights and informally negotiated communal contracts are interdependent.
practical_implication: Communities facing marketization pressure can retain quota exchangeability through a collectively managed compensation mechanism (liability rules) rather than fully privatizing or rejecting markets, thereby protecting alienable individual rights while safeguarding distributive equity and ecological sustainability. The approach may inform governance of other measurable, relatively homogeneous resources (e.g., grazing quotas, carbon-emission allowances, single-species fisheries) and community-based water markets that use clearinghouses to mediate pricing.
limitations: The qualitative comparison of transaction costs, though grounded in long-term observation, remains somewhat subjective, and data-availability constraints prevent the authors from systematically quantifying these measurements. The findings derive from a single community in one ecological and cultural context, limiting generalizability.
future_research: Future work incorporating a broader range of geographical areas and ecological contexts can address the study's contextual limitations, and more longitudinal, interdisciplinary studies would improve understanding of institutional change and governance adaptation. The authors propose for further inquiry the hypothesis that quota-based, nested property arrangements are most applicable to resource systems whose extraction units are measurable, relatively homogeneous, and directly tied to the underlying system's sustainability.
abstract: By assessing social-ecological performance and conducting a qualitative analysis of transaction costs, we analyze how these three institutions evolved and what outcomes they produced. We find that quota alienability doesn't increase grazing intensity.
apa_citation: Jia, X., Wei, Y., Zhang, Z., Zeren, G., Pérez-Ibarra, I., Villamayor-Tomas, S., & Li, W. (2026). Alienable or inalienable, and how? Individual property rights in commons governance. *Ecological Economics*, *242*, 108903. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108903

title: Does an exclusive relationship with government banks matter during a climate shock?
methods: Triple difference-in-differences exploiting exogenous variation in the timing and location of monsoon rainfall shocks across Indian meteorological subdivisions interacted with firms' exclusive GOB banking relationships; firm, banking-relationship-type, and subdivision × industry × year fixed effects, with robust standard errors clustered at the subdivision level.
industry: Nonfinancial, nongovernment listed and unlisted firms (Prowess/CMIE), excluding financial firms
units: firm–year observations
n_sample: 31,899 firm–year observations
authors: Kamal, Harish; Paul, Samit; Bansal, Avijit
theories: hold-up problem (Sharpe, 1990; Rajan, 1992); relationship banking / bank insurance channel (Hoshi, Kashyap and Scharfstein, 1990; Bolton et al., 2016); social vs. political views of government-owned banks (Stiglitz, 1993; La Porta, Lopez-de-Silanes and Shleifer, 2002)
keywords: climate change; climate finance; firm–bank relationships; government banks; relationship lending
research_question: Does maintaining an exclusive banking relationship with government-owned banks (GOBs) help firms secure credit, invest, and remain profitable during a climate-related shock when explicit government disaster aid is unavailable?
mechanism_summary: When relief funds are absent, rainfall acts as an exogenous local credit-demand shock. GOBs, created to address market failures and possessing proprietary information about their relationship firms, extend countercyclical credit only to firms exclusively tied to them—consistent with the relationship-banking insurance channel—while private/foreign banks hold up and multi-bank firms suffer coordination failure. Because GOBs absorb the credit-risk increase without raising interest rates and lend to financially constrained but healthier firms (no crowding out, no politically directed lending), GOB firms can invest in risky-but-profitable projects and outperform comparable firms during abnormal rainfall.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to the climate-finance literature by showing that exclusive GOB relationships help firms access credit during climate shocks in a setting where government aid is unavailable—contrasting with prior evidence that GOBs ration credit during such shocks. It advances the "social view" of government banking by documenting that GOBs extend countercyclical support to constrained-but-healthy relationship firms without political direction, and it extends relationship-banking theory by showing that the bank-insurance channel (not hold-up) operates for GOBs, whereas private/foreign banks hold up and multiple transactional relationships become disadvantageous during distress.
practical_implication: Maintaining a close, exclusive relationship with government banks is a potential solution for firms seeking finance during climate shocks, especially in economies where GOBs are prevalent and disaster aid is unavailable. The findings offer policymakers in similar developing economies guidance for designing climate-related policies and supporting banks to ameliorate the effects of climate change.
limitations: The dataset does not allow direct observation of loan interest rates, so total interest expense scaled by total debt is used as a proxy. The empirical design is associational/quasi-experimental rather than a controlled experiment, relying on the parallel-trends assumption and the exogeneity of rainfall shocks. Data are licensed (Prowess/CMIE), constraining replication, and several supporting analyses are reported only in the Supplementary Appendix.
future_research: Not reported in paper
abstract: We provide novel evidence on the role of firms’ banking relationships with government banks (GOBs) during a climate-related shock when relief funds are unavailable. Using variation in the locations of rainfall shocks and firms’ banking relationships, we find that firms maintaining exclusive banking relationships with GOBs (GOB firms) secure more debt relative to other firms during rainfall shocks. We do not find such effects for firms that maintain exclusive relationships with private banks, foreign banks, or maintain multiple banking relationships. We also find that GOB relationships are particularly beneficial for firms that are more vulnerable to rainfall shocks, have long-term relationships with GOBs, and are, at the same time, healthier compared to other firms. With regard to real effects, GOB firms invest more and remain profitable than other firms during rainfall shocks. Overall, our results highlight the benefits of GOB relationships for firms during climate shocks.
apa_citation: Kamal, H., Paul, S., & Bansal, A. (2026). Does an exclusive relationship with government banks matter during a climate shock? *Review of Finance*, 30(3), 949–994. https://doi.org/10.1093/rof/rfaf073

title: From wells to windmills: Resource redeployment and new technology investment in the energy sector
methods: Quantitative archival study using OLS fixed-effects regressions (firm, year, continent, segment FE) on firm-year-continent-segment and firm-year-windfarm panels, structured as a repeated cross-section difference-in-differences around the 2014 oil price shock, with coarsened exact matching (CEM) on pre-shock size and profitability, plus instrumental-variable and proximity-distance specifications; corroborated by industry interviews.
industry: Energy sector — oil and gas exploration and production (E&P) and wind power industries
country: Global; oil and gas data span Africa, Asia, Europe, Middle East, North and South Americas, and Oceania; windfarms across 107 countries and regions
time_period: 2010–2019 (10-year period spanning the late-2014 oil price crash)
units: Firm-year-continent-segment oil and gas expenditure observations; firm-year-windfarm and windfarm-level wind power observations
n_sample: 66,625 oil and gas expenditure observations (1,731 firms in fixed-effects models); 143,001 wind power firm-year-windfarm observations; 14,905 windfarm-level observations
authors: Kapacinskaite, Aldona
theories: Penrosian theory of firm growth / unused productive services (Penrose, 1959); asset complementarity and dynamic capability accumulation (Helfat, 1997); inter-temporal economies of scope and resource relatedness (Sakhartov & Folta, 2014; Sakhartov, 2018); resource redeployment (Helfat & Eisenhardt, 2004; Lieberman et al., 2017)
keywords: co-location; energy sector; industry downturn; new technology investment; resource redeployment
research_question: How do multi-business firms redeploy non-scale-free (financial) resources across industries following a negative industry shock, and under what conditions does such inter-industry redeployment stimulate investment in new technology in the recipient industry? The study examines oil and gas firms diversified into wind power after the 2014 oil price crash.
mechanism_summary: Following the exogenous 2014 oil price collapse, a subset of wind-diversified oil and gas firms cut oil and gas spending—especially on complex offshore contracts—and redirected financial resources into wind power, consistent with diverting "unused productive services" (Penrose, 1959). These redeploying firms invested more than wind-industry rivals, and adopted newer, more powerful turbines, but only where windfarms were co-located with existing offshore oil and gas assets, indicating that the ability to leverage complementary, geographically proximate capabilities governs both whether redeployment occurs and whether it pushes the technological frontier. Stronger employment-protection laws dampened, but did not offset, the redeployment effect.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends theory on resource redeployment by linking inter-industry redeployment to technology choice in the recipient industry, showing that a negative shock (exogenously low prices) induces firms to reallocate resources to the closest marginal use—selecting challenging, higher-return projects—rather than to invest across the board, in contrast to settings where a positive shock raises cash flows (Helfat, 1997). It provides granular, large-sample empirical evidence of redeployment, foregrounds asset co-location as a driver of new-technology investment, and develops the notion of inter-temporal appropriability whereby firms capture value from new uses of existing resources.
practical_implication: Only about 2.5% of firms redeployed resources from oil and gas to wind power even after a major shock, suggesting that market-based inducements alone are unlikely to drive meaningful industrial change or accelerate the energy transition. Firms are more likely to transition where they can co-locate new renewable projects with existing offshore assets; policymakers should recognize that incentives interact with firm-level capabilities, asset proximity, and labor-market institutions.
limitations: The study is based on a specific two-industry setting in the energy sector, limiting generalizability. The author cannot precisely measure the specific human capital and machinery redeployed from oil and gas, and the analysis captures only one possible recipient industry (wind power), likely underestimating total redeployment.
future_research: Future work could examine the competitive and technological implications of resource redeployment for rivals in the recipient industry, measuring their outcomes more directly. It could also further investigate the role of oil price fluctuations in the broader energy transition from hydrocarbons to renewables.
abstract: increasing investment in wind power. Importantly, firms were more likely to invest in newer, highercapacity wind technologies when they could co-locate these with existing offshore oil and gas assets. These findings suggest that firms facing industry shocks can redeploy resources into more promising sectors, but their propensity to do so may depend on the possibility of leveraging existing assets across domains.
apa_citation: Kapacinskaite, A. (2026). From wells to windmills: Resource redeployment and new technology investment in the energy sector. *Strategic Management Journal*, 47(2), 616–642. https://doi.org/10.1002/smj.70028

title: Waste delinking: A pathway to degrowth?
methods: Conceptual survey article; develops the concept of 'waste delinking' through theoretical synthesis of Amin's delinking and dependency theory with degrowth scholarship, illustrated with secondary evidence on plastic, textile, and e-waste trade flows. No primary data.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Karimzadeh, Sara
theories: degrowth; delinking theory (Amin, 1987, 1990); dependency theory (Dos Santos, 1970; Amin, 1976); ecologically unequal exchange (Dorninger et al., 2021)
keywords: Degrowth; Ecological Sovereignty; Transformation; Unequal Exchange; Waste Colonialism
research_question: Can a targeted practice of "waste delinking" — strategically disengaging high-income regions from transnational waste trade — serve not as a by-product of degrowth but as a concrete policy lever that triggers degrowth-oriented transformation of production–consumption systems?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Cross-border waste trade lets the Global North externalize the ecological and social costs of overproduction/overconsumption onto the Global South, sustaining unsustainable growth; closing this "offshore escape valve" re-imposes material limits.
- Key constructs: waste delinking (an upstream strategy internalizing environmental/social costs where waste is generated); waste colonialism; ecologically unequal exchange; ecological sovereignty.
- Deterrent mechanisms (activated when waste trade is constrained): (1) source-reduction pressure within domestic treatment capacity; (2) rising marginal costs and siting conflicts for incineration/landfill; (3) accelerating investment in prevention, reuse, repair, and safer design; (4) rising political opposition to local pollution.
- Boundary conditions / enablers: stricter enforcement of international agreements (Basel, Bamako), national bans on waste exports, and redesign of domestic waste management.

Drawing on Samir Amin's delinking and dependency theory, the paper argues that the waste trade is infrastructural to global capitalism's metabolism, allowing affluent territories to maintain a "fantasy of cleanliness" while displacing harms. Because recycling and circular-economy rhetoric grant a moral license that dulls pressure to curb throughput, downstream fixes cannot break the overproduction cycle. Removing the export option forces upstream prevention and redesign, making perpetual throughput growth physically and politically untenable and thereby rendering degrowth structurally realizable rather than merely compatible.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes two contributions: first, it repositions delinking not as a by-product of degrowth but as a practical mechanism that can actively trigger degrowth by closing avenues for externalization; second, it extends Amin's political-economic notion of delinking into the ecological realm, showing how territorial responsibility for waste can dismantle the colonial pattern of burden-shifting documented in global waste studies.
practical_implication: Enacting waste delinking requires high-income countries to reconfigure production and consumption around material sufficiency via export bans, extended-producer-responsibility regimes, and material-input caps, while enabling lower-income countries to reject the historical role of sink for externalized residuals.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Not reported in paper
abstract: Using recent evidence on plastic, textile, and electronic waste, the article shows how closing export outlets constrains growth and can catalyze systemic transformation.
apa_citation: Karimzadeh, S. (2026). Waste delinking: A pathway to degrowth? *Ecological Economics*, 241, 108869. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108869

title: Changes in the Salience of Collective Reputations: Information Disclosure and Shareholder Reactions
methods: Event study of the June 19, 1989 first release of U.S. EPA Toxic Release Inventory (TRI) data, treating the release as an exogenous shock; cumulative abnormal returns (CAR) computed against a market-model estimation window. Difference-in-difference random-effects regressions of conditional mean and squared-residual variance, plus multiplicative heteroskedastic linear regression (Harvey, 1976) estimating mean and variance equations jointly via maximum likelihood. Salience proxied by News (1984-1989 pollution newspaper coverage by industry); robustness across varying event windows and SIC-code interactions.
industry: Manufacturing firms (SIC codes 20-39) listed in the TRI, spanning 22 industries; chemicals (SIC 28) most salient, transportation equipment (SIC 37) least
time_period: Event window around June 19, 1989 TRI release; media salience measured Jan 1984-June 1989
units: Publicly traded firms (parent-company level)
n_sample: 570 firms across 22 industries (1,066 firm-period observations)
authors: Kim, Seonghoon; Potoski, Matthew
theories: collective reputation theory (Tirole, 1996; Barnett & King, 2008); signaling theory (Spence, 2002; Connelly et al., 2011); transaction cost economics (Williamson, 1981)
keywords: collective reputation; environmental performance; financial performance; information disclosure; shareholder reaction
research_question: Can the influence of a collective reputation vary across contexts, and if so, what changes that influence? Specifically, does the mandatory disclosure of firm-specific information reduce the salience of an industry's collective reputation, leading shareholders to evaluate peer firms more heterogeneously?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on transaction cost economics, the authors argue a collective reputation acts as a perceptual heuristic that shareholders rely on when firm-specific information is scarce and the reputed quality dimension is valued—the condition of high "salience." Mandatory disclosure (the TRI release) supplies low-cost, reliable individual-firm information that decouples members' true performance from the group stereotype, lowering the reputation's salience and allowing more individualized, heterogeneous assessments. Salience thus depends on an exogenous factor (the existence of reliable information) independent of the strength of the collective reputation itself. The effect is amplified for firms whose individual performance (emissions) deviates substantially from the group, which—from a signaling view—serves as a more diagnostic cue.
theoretical_contribution: Introduces the salience of a collective reputation as a new construct, showing that even a "strong" collective reputation's influence is contingent on exogenous reliable information rather than fixed once established—filling a gap in a literature that had focused on the drivers and consequences of collective reputation but treated its influence as constant. It is, to the authors' knowledge, the first study to empirically demonstrate the influence of collective reputation on firms' financial performance using stock returns, and it introduces multiplicative heteroskedastic regression as a novel methodological lens for studying evaluation uncertainty under information disclosure.
practical_implication: Managers can treat information disclosure as a strategy for managing collective-reputation salience: high-performing firms in a stigmatized group may lobby for or support disclosure programs to lower the group reputation's salience and surface their individual quality (as Du Pont did for TRI legislation). Because shareholders' interpretations of disclosed information are heterogeneous and hard to predict, however, such political activities should be approached with caution and firms should carefully estimate how their own stakeholders would react.
limitations: The collective-reputation measure (newspaper coverage by SIC-coded industry) may not match how shareholders actually group firms, so it may mismeasure salience. The empirical test focuses on a single stakeholder group—shareholders—whose beliefs about pollution may differ from those of NGOs or regulators. The study examines only industry-based reputation in one historical disclosure event.
future_research: Examine more diverse stakeholder groups (e.g., NGOs, government regulators) with measures suited to their differing evaluations. Test alternative group categorizations beyond industry, such as geographical location or political-party affiliation, to improve generalizability. Extend the analysis to more recent TRI disclosures or equivalent environmental reports, and leverage later TRI updates and corrections for additional analytic insight.
abstract: New information about these attributes can reduce the salience of the collective reputation, helping shareholders to make more individual and accurate assessments. In 1989, the U.S. EPA published the Toxic Release Inventory (TRI), providing for the first time detailed pollution emissions data for large manufacturing facilities. We use several analytic approaches, including multiplicative heteroskedastic linear regression models, and data on 570 firms across 22 industries, to examine how the TRI data release affected shareholder evaluations of firms.
apa_citation: Kim, S., & Potoski, M. (2026). Changes in the salience of collective reputations: Information disclosure and shareholder reactions. *Business & Society*, 65(5), 1375-1417. https://doi.org/10.1177/00076503251383838
