title: Who’s Afraid of the Minimum Wage? Measuring the Impacts on Independent Businesses Using Matched U.S. Tax Returns
methods: Stacked panel difference-in-differences event study exploiting 19 state-level minimum wage increases (2013-2016) as identifying variation, comparing treated to clean never-treated control states; linear probability models for worker-level outcomes; estimated on matched firm-worker-owner tax-record panels with firm-cohort and cohort-by-year fixed effects, standard errors clustered at the state-by-cohort level.
industry: Independent (pass-through) businesses in highly exposed four-digit industries (each employing >=1% of minimum wage workers), led by restaurants and other food services; placebos use non-highly-exposed industries
units: Pass-through firms (S-corporations, partnerships, LLCs) linked to their workers and owners; plus individual panels of low-earning workers and young people (ages 15-26)
n_sample: ~271,000 highly exposed firms per year in the full sample (134,974 in the balanced panel); ~1,386,000 non-highly-exposed firms/year for placebos; 2% individual samples of low-earning and young workers
authors: Rao, Nirupama L.; Risch, Max
theories: imperfect product market competition with fixed costs (Cournot framework; Besley, 1989); monopsony in labor markets (Bhaskar & To, 1999; Dustmann et al., 2022)
keywords: employment; firm entry and exit; incidence; independent businesses; minimum wage; pass-through firms; productivity; worker reallocation
research_question: How do independent (predominantly small and medium-size, pass-through) U.S. firms accommodate minimum wage increases along product- and labor-market margins, who ultimately bears the economic burden of higher wage floors, and how are potentially vulnerable low-earning and young workers affected?
mechanism_summary: Incumbent firms absorb the labor-cost shock not by laying off workers (they only trim very part-time, mostly teenage hiring) but by financing higher wage bills with new revenue, leaving owner profits intact—so consumers, not owners, bear essentially 100% of the incidence. At the extensive margin, higher wage floors deter entry (~2% fewer firms), and the entrants who remain are positively selected on productivity and cost efficiency; incumbents also become leaner and more productive. The result is an industry-level reshaping toward fewer but more productive firms in which workers receive a larger slice of a larger revenue pie, while worker reallocation toward C-corporations buffers the modest hiring reductions so individual employment stays flat and earnings rise.
theoretical_contribution: The study provides the first comprehensive, multi-margin documentation of minimum wage incidence for a large set of U.S. firms—jointly tracing product-market, labor-market, and productivity responses using administrative tax data—and shows that minimum wages mainly redistribute from customers to workers rather than from owners to workers. By identifying off clean never-treated control states (rather than baseline exposure), it estimates novel extensive-margin effects, showing how reduced entry and selection among entrants shift the productivity distribution of exposed industries, akin to how new technology, trade, or recessions reallocate activity toward more productive firms.
practical_implication: The findings undercut the common rationale for small-business exemptions from minimum wage hikes: even small and medium-size independent firms accommodate phased-in increases without layoffs or profit losses, financing higher pay through added revenue. Policymakers can expect higher wage floors to raise low earners' incomes (~18-22%) with negligible disemployment, while the principal real cost is somewhat dampened new-firm entry, partly borne by would-be entrepreneurs.
limitations: The estimates capture short- to medium-run effects (four years out) of phased-in increases; longer-run responses may differ if entrants or incumbents reconfigure away from low-wage labor. Because the tax data do not separately report prices and quantities, the authors cannot empirically distinguish price pass-through from demand reallocation as the source of revenue gains, nor can they observe hours, so reductions in employment relationships cannot be fully translated into changes in jobs.
future_research: Future work could assess the longer-run effects of minimum wage increases as firms adapt production technologies and input mixes, and examine margins not estimated here—such as reduced workplace amenities or deferred equipment maintenance. Separating price from quantity responses and estimating effects on hours and tasks would sharpen the interpretation of how independent firms and their workers absorb wage-floor policies.
abstract: A common concern surrounding minimum wage policies is their impact on independent businesses, which are often feared to be less able to bear or pass on cost increases. We examine how these typically small and medium-size firms accommodate minimum wage increases along product and labor market margins using a matched owner-firm-worker panel data set drawn from the universe of U.S. tax records over a 10-year period, and using state minimum wage changes as identifying variation. We find that on average, firms in highly exposed industries do not substantially reduce employment—they do not lay off workers but moderately reduce part-time hiring. Instead, these firms are able to fully finance the new labor costs with new revenues, leaving average owner profits unchanged. Higher wage floors, however, forestall entry, particularly for less productive firms, reducing the number of independent firms operating in these industries by roughly 2%. Yet these industries do not shrink; instead, incumbent responses and strong positive selection among entrants reshape industries that rely heavily on low-wage workers, yielding fewer but more productive firms after the cost shock.
apa_citation: Rao, N. L., & Risch, M. (2026). Who’s afraid of the minimum wage? Measuring the impacts on independent businesses using matched U.S. tax returns. *The Quarterly Journal of Economics*, 141(1), 373–427. https://doi.org/10.1093/qje/qjaf053

title: Local development based on non-timber forest products: Revisiting the case of São Francisco do Iratapuru in the Brazilian Amazon
methods: In-depth longitudinal single-case study reconstructing results chains; five-step data collection (academic literature review, public databases, 12 key-informant interviews, a systematic 50% household survey, and a women's focus group), triangulated against neighboring-municipality counterfactuals.
industry: Non-timber forest product (NTFP) extraction and processing — primarily Brazil nuts and Brazil nut oil — from natural Amazon rainforest
country: Brazil (São Francisco do Iratapuru village, Eastern Amazon, state of Amapá)
time_period: Roughly 1992–2023 (more than three decades of interventions; field visit July 2023)
units: A single traditional riverine community and its cooperative/associations, plus three principal facilitators (state government, federal Ministry of Environment via PPG7, and the cosmetics firm Natura)
n_sample: 25 of the 50 village households surveyed (systematic 50% sample); 12 key informants; 7 women in a focus group
authors: Rosenfeld, Tomas; Pokorny, Benno; Marcovitch, Jacques; Poschen, Peter
theories: non-timber forest product (NTFP) based development; results chains / impact pathways; social-ecological systems / resource system framework (Ostrom, 2009)
keywords: Amazon; Brazil; Non-timber forest products; bioeconomy; deforestation
research_question: To better understand whether and how strategies for NTFPs can be effective in achieving the dual goals of local economic development and forest conservation, the study analyses in-depth the long-term economic, social, and environmental impacts of NTFP-based development interventions in the case of São Francisco do Iratapuru.
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering conditions: chronic land disputes, dependence on barter middlemen, lack of marketing and education access, prompting 22 families to found the village (1992) and the Comaru cooperative.
- Intervention strategies (facilitators): public procurement of Brazil nut biscuits (Amapá State), infrastructure investment plus protected-area creation (federal PPG7), and a guaranteed-purchase company-community partnership (Natura), with an incidental hydroelectric-dam intervention (CEJA).
- Key constructs / tensions: local institutions (cooperative/associations) vs. external interventions; value-added processing vs. raw-product sale; income growth vs. income inequality and elite capture; productive vs. community infrastructure.
- Process / phases: timeline reconstruction (1992–2023) of initiatives related to selected economic, social, and environmental indicators, then probing causal results chains via triangulated qualitative and secondary data.
- Emergent outcomes: ~three-fold real-income increase, reduced inequality, near-universal access to goods/services (except electricity), and zero deforestation in the reserve.
- Boundary conditions: low external deforestation pressure, low-perishability/hard-to-substitute NTFPs, well-defined property rights, women's involvement, and pre-existing local organizations.

The central process is that durable, locally-rooted organizations channel and sustain external interventions: investment in productive infrastructure with technical assistance and guaranteed NTFP purchases raised value-added and unit prices (harvesters received ~187% more than middlemen prices), tripling real income without higher extraction rates and thereby strengthening residents' incentive and capacity to conserve the forest. The authors argue that external interventions were necessary but not sufficient — local social cohesion (e.g., Comaru predating any state intervention) was the decisive condition enabling the interventions' viability, longevity, and recovery from shocks such as the 2003 factory fire.
theoretical_contribution: The study supplies rare long-term, multi-dimensional proof of concept that NTFP-based development can reconcile socio-economic improvement with forest conservation in the Brazilian Amazon. Against a literature that attributes NTFP success chiefly to outside intervention, it foregrounds the centrality of locally created and managed institutions, and surfaces a substantive mismatch between researcher-defined indicators and the development dimensions (e.g., community infrastructure, education, oil-extraction productivity) that local resource users themselves value.
practical_implication: NTFP-based development should be promoted where specialised extraction, capable local organizations, women's involvement, low-perishability/hard-to-substitute products, secure property rights, and controlled deforestation pressure coincide. Financing mechanisms (e.g., the Tropical Forest Forever Facility) should channel funds directly to local communities, and public investment must complement production by securing water, electricity, and other services and by effectively protecting the forest base.
limitations: The analysis is limited by the scope of available indicators — for example, the environmental indicator relies on forest cover rather than forest quality or extraction sustainability. The findings rest on a single, exceptionally well-documented case situated in an area where external pressure to deforest remains low, constraining direct generalization.
future_research: Whether and to what extent NTFP extraction is a viable economic option for sustainable development of Amazonian communities, including under higher deforestation pressure, requires further long-term scientific study. The authors call for robust databases with panels of communities and interventions monitored over time, and for defining and measuring criteria and indicators jointly with local communities, including via local "citizen scientists."
abstract: Using information from former studies as well as own empirical data from interviews, a representative household survey and focus groups, we show that the quality of life in the village has improved markedly over the last decades while the forest has been conserved. We observed an increase in real income, a reduction in income inequality and better access of households to marketed as well as public goods and services, except eletricity.
apa_citation: Rosenfeld, T., Pokorny, B., Marcovitch, J., & Poschen, P. (2026). Local development based on non-timber forest products: Revisiting the case of São Francisco do Iratapuru in the Brazilian Amazon. *Ecological Economics*, 242, 108894. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108894

title: No News About Climate Action is Good News for Low-Polluting Firms
methods: Panel longitudinal time-series analysis of US public firms; machine-learning text-mining vocabulary to measure climate-action 'News Spotlight'; two-stage least squares (2SLS) IV regression using county-level 2000 presidential Democratic-voting ratio as the instrument; firm/year/industry/state fixed effects; causal mediation (PROCESS Model 4) through COGS and SG&A; FinBERT sentiment split; moderation via interaction terms.
industry: Public US firms across diverse industries (mining, transportation/utilities, manufacturing, finance, retail, services, etc.); pollution-status partitioned via two-digit SIC codes
units: Firm-year observations
n_sample: 452 firms; up to 12,977 firm-year observations (News Spotlight); 316,692 news articles in corpus
authors: Safaei, Nima; Pant, Gautam
theories: agenda-setting; media reputation as a strategic resource (Deephouse, 2000); mimetic and coercive isomorphism / institutional pressures (DiMaggio and Powell, 1983)
keywords: Corporate Climate Action; Firm Financial Performance; Media Spotlight; Operational Costs; Text Analytics
research_question: Whether and how climate action-related news spotlight affects firms' financial performance, and how variations in firms' environmental performance (and related firm/industry attributes) influence this relationship.
mechanism_summary: Increased media attention on a firm's climate actions raises environmental expectations and stakeholder scrutiny, compelling firms to undertake costly operational adjustments (supplier vetting, sustainable transitions, intensified marketing, reporting, and audits) that inflate COGS and SG&A and thereby depress financial performance. Mediation analysis shows COGS and SG&A together fully mediate the spotlight-to-ROA effect (25% and 75%, respectively). The effect is heterogeneous: low-polluting firms incur net financial harm because additional environmental investment yields marginal gains at substantial cost, whereas high-polluting firms (and firms in "dirty" industries) — starting from a low reputational baseline with cheaper improvement opportunities — can see the financial sign "flip" to positive.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes a novel, automated, interpretable machine-learning climate-action vocabulary as an information artifact for measuring overall media spotlight on firms' climate actions, moving beyond the prior literature's reliance on isolated environmental events and proprietary datasets. Substantively, it isolates the long-term financial impact of the climate-action news spotlight itself (while controlling for actual environmental performance via CO2 emissions) and documents counterintuitive, heterogeneous effects across pollution levels and firm types, advancing research on automated content analysis, firm environmental practices, media coverage, and public relations in operations management.
practical_implication: Firms should tailor their media strategy to their environmental profile: low-polluting (cleaner) firms should be cautious about seeking climate-action attention given its associated costs, while high-polluting firms may derive financial benefits and use the spotlight as a driver for cost-effective improvement. Managers should shift from superficial virtue signaling toward genuine environmental action, and from a societal standpoint media attention is better directed at high-polluting firms with greater potential for environmental strides.
limitations: The findings primarily address firms' financial performance and do not capture intangible, long-term benefits of media spotlight on climate actions that financial measures may miss.
future_research: Future work may further explore the complex dynamics between media spotlight and financial performance amid evolving global sustainability priorities to better understand managerial and operational responses to media scrutiny. Another direction is to examine climate-action-related spotlight in social media and its effect on firm performance.
abstract: Media plays a crucial role in shaping public perception of firms, significantly impacting their operations and performance. Firms often attempt to influence media channels to showcase their climate action initiatives, aiming to enhance their public image, reputation, and stakeholder trust. While firms cannot directly control their media coverage, intentional or unintentional dissemination of their climate action efforts can thrust them into the media spotlight. The implications of this media spotlight remain uncertain. While firms may anticipate positive public relations benefits from such exposure, it may also raise environmental expectations for firms and risk highlighting less favorable aspects of their environmental practices. Despite previous research on the impact of media exposure on firms' financial outcomes, a notable gap exists in understanding how the media spotlight on firms' climate actions affects their operational and financial performance. A clear hurdle is the lack of a systematic and objective measurement of such a spotlight. Our study addresses this research gap by developing a machine learning-based framework to derive a climate action vocabulary as a novel information artifact. The vocabulary is subsequently used to measure the intensity of media attention on a firm's climate actions. Our research reveals that an increased media spotlight on climate actions, regardless of their sentiment, has an adverse effect on firms' financial performance, primarily due to the rise in operational costs. Furthermore, we find that the impact of media spotlight is heterogeneous. Low-polluting firms experience negative financial consequences as the downside of the heightened media spotlight. In contrast, high-polluting firms and those operating in polluting industries may witness an overall positive financial impact. We also find that firms' market orientation (business-to-business vs. business-to-consumer), durability classification, index constituency (i.e., S&P 500), and greenwashing status significantly moderate the relationship. Our research highlights key considerations for corporate leaders with respect to the drawbacks for low-polluting firms in seeking media attention for their climate actions. Given that the media captures society's limited attention, our research suggests that firms should refrain from promoting superficial narratives about climate change that distract society from more impactful sustainability conversations.
apa_citation: Safaei, N., & Pant, G. (2026). No news about climate action is good news for low-polluting firms. *Production and Operations Management*, 35(5), 1668-1687. https://doi.org/10.1177/10591478251387803

title: Socio-Ecological Inequalities in Housing Consumption: How Income, Urban Form, and Tenure Drive Carbon Footprints
methods: Quantitative register-based footprint accounting. Dutch CBS System of Social Statistical Datasets register microdata (2019, 2023) linked to the ODIN national mobility survey, combined with life cycle inventory data (MPG, BONSAI, Ecoinvent) to compute consumption-based GHG carbon footprints for 6000/1800 household profiles. Drivers analyzed via multiple linear regression (OLS) and XGBoost gradient boosting with SHAP attribution.
industry: Residential housing sector (housing provision and operation: construction materials, building energy use, and housing-related mobility)
units: Household profiles (aggregated from full-population register data), stratified by income decile, tenure, building typology, construction period, and urban density
n_sample: 6000 household profiles by income (plus 600 top-percentile profiles) and 1800 profiles by tenure; ODIN mobility survey of 60,000 respondents
authors: Savini, Federico; Kopp, Mira; Hochstenbach, Cody; Cohen, Leshem; Berrill, Peter; Merciai, Stefano
theories: degrowth; environmental justice; political ecology of housing consumption
keywords: Environmental justice; Greenhouse gas emissions; Homeownership; Household income; Housing; Mobility; Social inequality
research_question: How and to what extent do income, housing tenure, building type, and urban form impact per capita carbon footprints of housing? The paper develops a multidimensional model to assess socio-ecological inequalities in housing consumption — the overall use of material and energy in the provision and operation of housing.
mechanism_summary: Housing footprints in the Netherlands already exceed the per capita Paris-aligned emissions ceiling across all income and tenure groups, with the budget projected to be exhausted by 2033. Inequalities between income groups are driven mainly by car use — when car use is excluded, footprints are remarkably similar across social groups except the top 1%. Higher-income households suburbanize into low-density locations that lock in car dependency, while building-related emissions of the poorest 30% resemble those of the top 10% because of poor insulation and fossil-fuel dependence in older rental stock. Tenure structures who can invest in efficiency: private renters face weak landlord incentives, so suburbanization offsets emissions-saving investments concentrated in owner-occupied stock.
theoretical_contribution: The paper integrates social and environmental critiques of housing by linking quantitative analyses of environmental footprints to socio-spatial dynamics, opening a niche that treats housing as a key site of socio-ecological inequality. It advances a political-ecology and degrowth-oriented framing of housing consumption, showing that footprint assessments based on household budget surveys and monetary proxies miss the material differences (dwelling size, gas use, kilometers traveled) and tenure dynamics that structure who appropriates housing resources and who is responsible for emissions.
practical_implication: Without a multidimensional understanding of housing's socio-ecological inequalities, some sustainability measures (e.g., carbon pricing) will be socially regressive and must be coupled with support mechanisms (e.g., an ETS2 Social Climate Fund). The authors call for densification, reductions in per capita floor area, progressive income- or asset-based property taxation, substantial investment in social-rental stock and public transport, and land-use planning that curbs suburban sprawl and car dependency.
limitations: The model does not account for the specific car fleet (size/weight) of each housing profile, likely underestimating inequalities since richer households own larger, heavier cars. It lacks information on the retrofitting and energy efficiency of buildings, so residents' energy-use norms cannot be cleanly isolated from building efficiency. Part of the population was excluded due to missing data or suspected dwelling-sharing registration errors, which may overestimate private-rental footprints; income and tenure were analyzed separately despite their interrelation.
future_research: The authors call for further interdisciplinary research into ways to provide socially just and environmentally sustainable housing. They specifically suggest studying interactions between income and tenure, which were analyzed separately here, and extending the model — adjustable to land and material footprints — to compare other affluent countries with more sprawling urban forms or residualized social-housing stock.
abstract: We draw on a unique combination of sources—register data about the Dutch population and housing stock, a mobility survey, and life cycle inventory data—to reveal that a) the carbon footprint per person differs starkly between richest and poorest groups; b) the low-density locations and income of richer households drive mobility emissions; c) rental units show larger carbon footprints related to lower quality housing stock
apa_citation: Savini, F., Kopp, M., Hochstenbach, C., Cohen, L., Berrill, P., & Merciai, S. (2026). Socio-ecological inequalities in housing consumption: How income, urban form, and tenure drive carbon footprints. *Ecological Economics*, 242, 108896. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108896

title: Climate change mitigation and green energy investment: A stock-flow consistent model
methods: Theoretical two-sector ecological stock-flow consistent (SFC) business-cycle model; parameters calibrated to US data (1992Q1-2019Q4) rather than estimated; numerical simulation in Dynare generating impulse response functions over 50 periods, plus a Sobol-based global sensitivity analysis via Latin Hypercube Sampling.
industry: Two-sector closed national economy: an energy sector (transitioning from brown to green technologies) and a non-energy production sector
units: A single closed macroeconomy of worker and rentier households, energy and non-energy firms, a central bank, and commercial banks
n_sample: Simulated model economy (not a sampled dataset; no empirical data used)
authors: Serra, Gustavo Pereira; Gallo, Ettore
theories: Goodwin-type conflicting-claims distribution dynamics; ecological stock-flow consistent (SFC) modeling; post-Keynesian macroeconomics (Kalecki, 1971; Steindl, 1979; Minsky, 1982)
keywords: Business cycles; Climate change; Mitigation policies; Stock-flow consistency
research_question: The paper addresses a research gap concerning the interactions between demand-side dynamics and environmental factors, investigating how demand-driven dynamics shape both economic and environmental outcomes during the green energy transition, and how the speed of that transition influences economic and environmental variables. Its central hypothesis is that a combination of targeted public policies and progressive taxation can achieve the low-carbon transition without triggering degrowth or exacerbating income inequality.
mechanism_summary: The model couples Kalecki-Steindl-Minsky investment dynamics and Goodwin-type conflicting-claims distribution with an ecological dimension: as energy firms raise their green investment share, emissions decline toward a net-zero steady state, while emission flows feed back into investment. A negative shock to the green investment share slows the transition and depresses aggregate demand; progressive taxation (taxing rentier rather than wage income) funds mitigation spending that sustains demand and employment while still reducing emissions, so decarbonization need not require degrowth.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to demand-led (post-Keynesian) climate macroeconomics by building a tractable, medium-scale two-sector ecological SFC model that integrates aggregate demand, income distribution, and ecological constraints, rather than the supply-side neoclassical growth frameworks that have traditionally dominated climate economics. It advances the literature by endogenizing environmental conditions in investment decisions and by making the speed of the low-carbon transition responsive to fiscal policy, capturing feedback effects between distribution, demand, accumulation, and emissions that standard models omit.
practical_implication: Government mitigation spending and the speed of public response are crucial for reaching net-zero emissions and avoiding degrowth; delayed or weakened mitigation effort produces fossil-fuel lock-in and persistent emission overshooting that propagate through the investment channel. Financing mitigation through progressive taxation — taxing rentier income more heavily than wages — can reduce emissions while sustaining wages and employment, so the cost of the transition need not fall on workers.
limitations: The model incorporates only a single energy-transition policy instrument — a balanced-budget government expenditure on transition-accelerating technology — and abstracts from alternatives such as emission limits and carbon taxes. It is a short-run business-cycle model that abstracts from long-run growth determinants, physical/biophysical constraints, and relative-price and cost differentials between green and brown energy; parameters are calibrated for illustrative purposes rather than estimated, so results indicate the direction of impacts rather than precise magnitudes.
future_research: Future work could explore alternative policy instruments such as emission limits and carbon taxes, whose implications lie beyond this paper's scope. It could also extend the framework by endogenizing the long-run growth trend and incorporating alternative transition pathways as well as innovation dynamics.
abstract: only slows the low-carbon transition but also depresses aggregate demand, wages, and employment. The paper examines possibilities for reducing the carbon intensity of production and provides insights into the interactions between ecological, macroeconomic, and distributive factors during the transition to a green economy.
apa_citation: Serra, G. P., & Gallo, E. (2026). Climate change mitigation and green energy investment: A stock-flow consistent model. *Ecological Economics*, *241*, 108863. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108863

title: Agent-based insight into eco-choices: Simulating the fast fashion shift
methods: Agent-based simulation in NetLogo 6.3.0; agents calibrated with a linear regression decision model estimated on survey data; scenario experiments vary peer-communication, social-media exposure/bias, and government-campaign parameters across non-polarized and polarized populations.
industry: Fast fashion / apparel consumption
units: Simulated consumer agents (calibrated on Spanish survey respondents)
n_sample: 1050 agents (calibrated on 1067 Spanish survey respondents)
authors: Soboleva, Daria; Sánchez, Angel
theories: Bounded Confidence Model (Hegselmann and Ulrich, 2022); opinion dynamics (DeGroot, 1974; Friedkin–Johnsen)
keywords: Agent-based modeling; Consumer behavior; Demand; Environment; Fast fashion; Working conditions
research_question: How do awareness and education about sustainability shape individuals' decision-making regarding fast-fashion purchases, and how can peer pressure, social media, and government interventions shift public opinion toward more sustainable garment choices?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: agents' probability to purchase fast fashion, environmental concerns, working-conditions awareness, education on sustainable fashion, normative expectations, trust, access, shopping frequency.
- Decision model: a linear regression (R² = 0.37) on Spanish survey data calibrates each agent's purchase probability; environmental concerns and normative expectations are negatively but weakly associated, while shopping frequency dominates.
- Influence channels: peer interaction (DeGroot/Friedkin–Johnsen opinion dynamics; tolerance threshold for polarized agents), social-media feedback loops with bias, and "smart" re-election-seeking government campaigns with campaign-fatigue decay.
- Triggering conditions / moderators: population polarization level, communication (sharing) threshold δ, tolerance τ, social-media exposure σ and bias β, and government stance ζ.
- Emergent outcomes: population-level shifts in average concerns and fast-fashion purchase probability.

Individual micro-level agents update opinions through repeated weighted averaging over a small-world clique network, and these micro interactions aggregate to macro-level shifts in public opinion. Government campaigns set the overall tone for sustainable change, but their effectiveness is conditioned by initial polarization and prevailing social-media biases; beyond a point, additional intervention yields diminishing returns, and a slightly polarized population best sustains campaign effects after they are halted.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends agent-based and opinion-dynamics modeling (DeGroot and Friedkin–Johnsen frameworks, with a bounded-confidence-style tolerance threshold) to the domain of sustainable fashion consumption, integrating peer, media, and state influence in one calibrated model. It theorizes that the well-documented attitude–behavior ("ethical purchasing") gap reflects an absence of societal pressure rather than insufficient concern, and that collective behavioral change depends on the broader population's receptiveness, not on highly concerned individuals or social media alone.
practical_implication: Policymakers can shift consumption patterns through moderate, well-timed pro-sustainability campaigns rather than extreme or indefinite intervention, since excessive campaigning yields diminishing returns. Campaign design should account for population polarization and social-media bias; a slightly polarized population sustains effects longest, while educational efforts are needed to close the gap between concern and action.
limitations: The model rests on simplifying assumptions: agents form ties randomly and cannot dissolve them, interactions are unilateral and equally weighted, and agents address one topic per time step, so model time does not map cleanly to real time and the lack of longitudinal data prevents validation. Susceptibilities are static and assigned independently, the government is assumed to know the population's average opinion, and the decision model is calibrated specifically to the Spanish market, limiting generalizability.
future_research: Future work could let agents create and dissolve ties based on opinion differences to produce more realistic homophilous group formation, model bilateral and unequally weighted interactions, and allow susceptibility to change dynamically with usage and political alignment. Interconnecting the three susceptibility types and recalibrating the decision model with local data for other countries would extend applicability beyond Spain.
abstract: the demand for fast fashion, with a focus on Spain. We explore the individual decision-making process involved in choosing to buy fast fashion and the role of awareness regarding working conditions, environmental consequences, and education on sustainable fashion in influencing consumer behavior. By employing Agent-Based Modeling, we investigate the factors influencing garment consumption patterns and how shifts in public opinion can be achieved through peer pressure, social media influence, and government interventions. Our study revealed that government interventions are pivotal, with the state's campaigns setting the overall tone for progress, although its success is conditioned by social media and polarization levels of the population. Importantly, the state does not need to adopt an extremely proactive stance or continue the campaigns indefinitely to achieve optimal results, as excessive interventions yield diminishing returns.
apa_citation: Soboleva, D., & Sánchez, A. (2026). Agent-based insight into eco-choices: Simulating the fast fashion shift. *Ecological Economics*, 240, 108824. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108824

title: Post-growth meets polycentric governance: Toward an interdisciplinary research program
methods: Conceptual / theoretical synthesis integrating two literatures (post-growth and polycentric governance); a threefold analytical procedure — (1) assess the consistency of the integration across positive grounds, normative underpinnings, and boundary conditions; (2) develop the concept of polycentric articulation and its rearrangement processes; (3) map five analytic components into a conceptual matrix that delineates a future research program. No empirical data.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Soto-Oñate, David; Petrovics, Daniel; Bauwens, Thomas
theories: commons / institutional analysis (E. Ostrom, 1990); polycentric governance / polycentricity (Bloomington School of Political Economy; V. Ostrom, 1972; E. Ostrom, 2010a); post-growth / degrowth scholarship (Latouche, 2009; Jackson, 2017; Hickel and Kallis, 2020)
keywords: Commons; Degrowth; Ecological Economics; Governance; Polycentricity; Post-Growth
research_question: How can societies adjust their economic scale to ecological boundaries without sacrificing democracy, and specifically, how can a polycentric approach to governance be built, maintained, and operationalized to coordinate post-growth transformations while preserving democracy and self-governance at lower political scales? The article asks whether post-growth and polycentricity cohere, and what an integrated research program connecting them should contain.
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: post-growth scholarship (which holds that ecosystem limits entail general limits to growth) and polycentric governance (multiple, formally independent decision units nested within an overarching institutional system) share coherent positive grounds, normative underpinnings, and boundary conditions, making their integration into "polycentric post-growth" theoretically consistent.
- Key construct — "polycentric articulation": the process by which rights and responsibilities are polycentrically rearranged to align governance with socio-ecological problems (matching the "shape" of the governance system to the "shape" of the problem, i.e., institutional fit).
- Transitional processes (four rearrangement pathways, distinguished by vertical direction and locus of sovereignty): pool, disaggregation, cession, and appropriation.
- Boundary conditions: both frameworks are circumscribed to societal solutions to collective problems; post-growth is more restrictive on societal goals while polycentricity is more precise on governance.
- Five analytic components mapped into a conceptual matrix: post-growth concerns; autonomous actors; coordination mechanisms; overarching institutional framework; and political-economy dynamics.

The central process is the articulation and disarticulation of jurisdictions: autonomous lower-scale actors and overarching institutions rearrange rights and responsibilities (bottom-up or top-down) to build governance systems whose scope matches the socio-ecological problem, while retaining residual sovereignty at lower scales under the subsidiarity principle. By breaking polycentric reality into its components and the dynamic processes that connect them, the framework yields a transdisciplinary "map" that identifies research avenues, testable hypotheses, and design guidance for governing beyond growth.
theoretical_contribution: The paper integrates two previously disconnected scholarships — post-growth/degrowth and Bloomington-School polycentricity — into a coherent research program, demonstrating their consistency and formulating the novel concept of "polycentric articulation of post-growth aspirations." It supplies a typology of four (dis)articulation processes and a five-component conceptual matrix that together delineate the domain, research avenues, and testable hypotheses for governing post-growth transitions, addressing the gap whereby degrowth authors invoke local autonomy yet default to top-down, nation-state-centered policies.
practical_implication: The conceptual matrix is intended to guide practitioners designing post-growth interventions by clarifying how the structure of a socio-ecological problem (its scale, rights/responsibilities, and complexity) should shape the corresponding governance architecture and the direction of jurisdictional transfer (pooling, cession, disaggregation, appropriation). It signals where coordination must be built across scales (e.g., for global commons) while preserving democratic self-governance and autonomy at lower scales.
limitations: As an explicitly conceptual, agenda-setting article, it develops no empirical test and acknowledges that central aspects (e.g., how autonomy is granted, supported, and protected; redistributive polycentric architectures; conflict dynamics beyond mutual gain) are only scratched at the surface and remain underexplored. The authors note that complexities emerging specifically from integrating post-growth and polycentricity are still underdeveloped and that, following Elinor Ostrom, "there are no panaceas."
future_research: The article proposes a research program organized around its five components: characterizing post-growth concerns through a polycentric lens; the features and systems formed by autonomous actors; deliberate coordination mechanisms and institutional-design principles; the role of the overarching institutional framework and the state; and the political economy of transition (pathways, conflict, evolutionary dynamics). It calls for work on the enablers, obstacles, and conditions for the emergence of functional polycentric systems that can politically address post-growth concerns.
abstract: the paper produces a conceptual matrix that reveals research avenues, identifies testable hypotheses, and guides practitioners designing post-growth interventions. The result is a coherent roadmap for empirical and theoretical work on governing beyond growth.
apa_citation: Soto-Oñate, D., Petrovics, D., & Bauwens, T. (2026). Post-growth meets polycentric governance: Toward an interdisciplinary research program. *Ecological Economics*, *242*, 108891. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108891

title: How Employees Who Have Made Upward Social Class Transitions Get Heard in the Workplace
methods: Mixed-methods convergent triangulation. Study 1: multisource cross-sectional field survey (employees nested within managers) analyzed with cluster-robust regression in Mplus (TYPE=COMPLEX) and moderated indirect-effects analysis. Studies 2A/2B/2C: preregistered quasi-experimental-causal-chain design (online samples; coded chat conversations, self-report scales, and an audio-clip experiment manipulating voice crafting and manager power). Study 3: qualitative semi-structured interviews with coded categorization of voice strategies.
industry: Study 1: shared services division of a multinational engineering company (HR, procurement, finance/accounting, legal, IT support functions). Studies 2A-2C: working adults and undergraduate students across occupations. Study 3: salaried professionals across varied fields.
country: Study 1: India. Studies 2A, 2B, 2C: United States. Study 3: largely United States and Canada.
units: Individual employees / working adults / interview participants (and their managers in Study 1)
n_sample: Study 1: 218 employees nested in 32 managers (166 matched); Study 2A: 359; Study 2B: 335; Study 2C: 338; Study 3: 42 (22 upward transitioners, 20 higher-class non-transitioners)
authors: Spriha, Yasha; Tangirala, Subrahmaniam; Shu, Rui; Ekkirala, Srinivas
theories: cross-cultural contact / adaptation theory (Adler, 1975); situated focus theory of power (Guinote, 2008); theories of social class transitions
keywords: current social class; employee voice; manager power; open-mindedness; social class origins; upward social class transitions; voice crafting; voice implementation
research_question: How and when do the strengths of upward social class transitioners translate into real workplace benefits? Specifically, do upward transitions foster a distinct open-minded communication style (voice crafting), and does that style help such employees get their voice implemented, especially when interacting with highly powerful managers?
mechanism_summary: Cultural retooling during an upward transition forces individuals to question taken-for-granted "doxa," cultivating humility and a learning orientation that together constitute open-mindedness; this is behaviorally expressed as voice crafting—speaking up while acknowledging one's fallibility and inviting managerial input. Drawing on the situated focus theory of power, the authors argue that highly powerful managers are more sensitive to how voice is presented and, via a reciprocal open-mindedness effect and a participation effect, respond more strongly (implementing ideas) when voice is crafted. Thus upward transitions exert a positive indirect effect on voice implementation through voice crafting that is amplified under high manager power.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends social class research by identifying open-mindedness/voice crafting as a strength that is neither inherited from origins nor destinations but emerges from the transition experience itself, and it shows this strength yields tangible workplace influence. It introduces the construct of voice crafting to the voice literature, reframing voice as a two-way co-construction of ideas rather than a one-way persuasion attempt. It also nuances power research by showing that high manager power does not uniformly increase voice implementation but heightens managers' sensitivity to how voice is delivered.
practical_implication: Managers should deliberately create opportunities for upward transitioners to speak up and should recognize and recruit for the value these employees bring. Upward transitioners should recognize that their open-minded conversational style resonates especially well with powerful managers and engage leaders rather than hesitating out of an ingrained reluctance toward authority figures. More broadly, voice crafting can foster mutual open-mindedness that improves problem-solving and helps bridge divides in polarized settings.
limitations: Upward transitions were measured rather than experimentally manipulated, so unobserved characteristics driving mobility cannot be fully ruled out. The studies did not measure the proposed reciprocal open-mindedness mechanism or alternative explanations such as threat reduction, and Study 2's effective samples were reduced by preregistered data-quality exclusions. The qualitative study (Study 3) lacked downward transitioners and lower-class non-transitioners and relied on retrospective accounts.
future_research: Panel studies could track how changes in social class over time affect voice crafting, and research could examine how voice crafting unfolds when subjective and objective class indicators diverge. Scholars could test moderators of the transition–voice crafting link (e.g., just-world beliefs, time spent in the new class, psychological adjustment) and probe whether voice crafting is a habit versus a strategy by examining learning versus instrumental motives. Additional outcomes—informal leadership, creativity, and potential emotional exhaustion from continuously absorbing input—also warrant study.
abstract: Individuals can move from lower social class origins to higher social class through education and entry into prestigious or well-paying jobs. These individuals, known as upward transitioners, have several strengths. Yet, research suggests that their strengths often go unrecognized in the workplace. We highlight a particular strength that does translate into tangible benefits for upward transitioners. Drawing on theories of social class, cross-cultural adaptation, and power, we propose that upward transitions foster a distinct open-minded communication style in individuals, which we term voice crafting. This style involves acknowledging the fallibility of one's viewpoints and staying receptive to managerial input when speaking up about work issues. It emerges from upward transitioners' experiences navigating unfamiliar higher-class cultural contexts, which build humility and a learning orientation. We argue that voice crafting helps upward transitioners get their ideas and concerns implemented, especially when they are engaging with powerful managers who expect interactive conversations. We find support for our theory across survey, experimental, and qualitative interview studies with diverse samples. In the process, we highlight how upward transitioners, because of their inclination to voice craft, are in unique positions to exercise influence within organizations.
apa_citation: Spriha, Y., Tangirala, S., Shu, R., & Ekkirala, S. (2026). How employees who have made upward social class transitions get heard in the workplace. *Academy of Management Journal*, 69(2), 243–274. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0042

title: The information value of energy labels: Evidence from the Dutch residential housing market
methods: Quasi-experimental observational study using administrative transaction microdata; hedonic pricing OLS regressions with neighborhood (4-digit postcode) and time fixed effects, a sharp Regression Discontinuity Design on the Energy Index cutoffs (non-parametric local/quadratic polynomials, Calonico et al. bandwidths), 'artificial label' pre-treatment premium tests, density-manipulation tests, and propensity-score matching.
industry: Privately-owned residential dwellings (residential housing market)
units: Individual housing transactions (dwellings) merged with energy-label records
n_sample: Voluntary system 613,352 transactions (39,563 labeled); mandatory system 390,962 transactions (350,886 labeled)
authors: Stangenberg, Lennart; van Wickeren, Sjors; Zhang, Lu
theories: Hedonic price theory (Rosen, 1974); Information value / information asymmetry of quality disclosure
keywords: Energy labels; Hedonic pricing models; House prices; Information value; Regression discontinuity design
research_question: Do energy labels provide additional information not readily available to the market, and is this information reflected in housing prices? Does the voluntary or mandatory nature of energy labels affect their information value?
mechanism_summary: The authors define a label's "information value" as the extent to which information conveyed by the label *beyond* directly observable features is capitalized into prices. Conventional hedonic models show large premiums for better labels, but these conflate the label with collinear, observable energy efficiency. To isolate any incremental label effect, the sharp RDD compares dwellings just above versus just below the Energy Index thresholds that mechanically determine the discrete label, where dwellings are otherwise similar. For the mandatory system (which lacks the underlying Energy Index), the authors instead use "artificial labels"—checking whether dwellings later labeled already commanded premiums before obtaining a label. Finding no premium at the RDD margins, and substantial pre-label premiums, implies the observed premiums largely reflect unobserved-by-researcher dwelling characteristics that buyers can perceive, so the labels' marginal information value is limited.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to the literature on the market capitalization of energy efficiency by separating the price premium attributable to a label from the premium attributable to readily observable energy efficiency, and by comparing a complex-voluntary against a simple-mandatory disclosure design within one market. It shows that hedonic-pricing premiums for labels are largely an artifact of collinearity and inadequate locational controls, and that better-labeled dwellings already attracted premiums before being labeled—evidence that the introduction of labels per se need not be what drives observed price differences.
practical_implication: Policymakers should not treat label price premiums as proof that labels themselves add information, nor rely on labeling as the sole instrument for informing the public about energy efficiency; labeling should be one part of a larger information system. Disclosure designs with a more thorough, expert-based evaluation process have greater potential to convey additional information to homebuyers, so the latest (2021) Dutch reform holds promise for improving labels' information function.
limitations: The information value is defined only via transaction prices, so effects on insulation investment or buyer welfare are not assessed, and the asymmetric-information dynamics of the rental market are not examined. The RDD requires the continuous Energy Index, which the mandatory system does not produce, so the mandatory-system conclusions are less direct; the data span 2000–2017, a period of relatively stable Dutch energy prices. The underlying transaction data are confidential.
future_research: Future work could examine the broader information value of energy labels—including effects on insulation investments and the value buyers create—and the role of labels in the rental market and incentive compatibility between landlords and tenants. Researchers could also compare outcomes between periods of stable versus sharply rising energy prices, given heightened post-2022 attention to energy efficiency in the Netherlands.
abstract: This paper examines the information value of energy labels using administrative data on all transactions in the

title: Decommodifying Cacao: Matchmaking between Producers and Buyers of Fine Flavour Cacao from Peru
methods: Cross-sectional empirical study using structured interviews and household surveys; Multiple Correspondence Analysis (MCA) with Hierarchical Clustering on Principal Components (HCPC) for the buyer typology, Principal Coordinate Analysis (PCoA) with partitioning-around-medoids (PAM) clustering for the producer typology, and a weighted attribute-importance/attribute-matching scoring scheme to quantify producer-buyer matchmaking.
industry: Fine flavour cacao (FFC) value chain — cacao farming households and FFC buyers (chocolate makers, intermediaries, exporters)
country: Peru (departments of Cusco and Piura); buyers also from Europe, North America, Asia, Central and South America
units: FFC buyers and cacao farmer households
n_sample: 76 FFC buyers and 337 cacao farmer households from 114 communities
authors: Thomas, Evert; Villar, Gesabel; Zavaleta, Diego; Ceccarelli, Viviana; Yovera, Fredy; Blare, Trent; Ramirez, Marleni; Oberlack, Christoph; Atkinson, Rachel
theories: Decommodification (Vail, 2010; Gerber & Gerber, 2017); Telecoupled resource systems / polycentric governance (Liu et al., 2013; Oberlack et al., 2018)
keywords: Blanco de Piura cacao; Cadmium; Chuncho cacao; Clustering mixed data; Comparative analysis; MCA; PCOA; Typology
research_question: The study examines the alignment between fine flavour cacao (FFC) buyers and producers in Peru — focusing on Piura (Blanco de Piura) and Cusco (Chuncho cacao) — assessing the extent to which different producer types match the requirements and interests of different buyer types, and how the matchmaking could be improved so that smallholders benefit from cacao decommodification.
mechanism_summary: Buyers were grouped via MCA + HCPC into four types and producers via PCoA + PAM into distinct types per region; weighted attribute-importance scores per buyer type were combined with producer compliance proportions to compute matching scores. The mechanism is that decommodification through FFC shifts trade terms favourably but imposes more stringent quality, ecological, and socio-economic requirements, so benefits accrue selectively to producers able to meet them — potentially reinforcing exclusion of less-equipped smallholders unless institutions and trade relationships close the gaps.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends the concept of decommodification beyond its conventional association with common-property regimes and collective action, showing that in a telecoupled resource system like cacao — where long distances separate production and consumption — trade and market exchange remain central, so specialization and value-chain trade are the likely institutional outcome rather than ideal-typic common-property arrangements. It identifies three insights (institutional interplay, the continued role of trade, and patterns of inclusion/exclusion) characterizing decommodification in historical commodity sectors.
practical_implication: The matchmaking methodology lets public authorities, NGOs, farmer organizations, and buyers identify concrete actions (e.g., diversifying buyers, formalizing contracts, enabling credit access, acquiring certifications, increasing wet-cacao marketing, making women's value-chain contributions visible) that specific producer types can take to expand and stabilize their market opportunities and access higher FFC prices.
limitations: Applying the matchmaking methodology at the producer- and buyer-type level risks oversimplifying reality because these groups are not always homogeneous, and connecting specific buyers and producers may require more tailored interventions. North American and Asian buyers may also be underrepresented because buyer interviews were conducted at European-centred trade fairs.
future_research: Future research is needed to test whether different matching scores account for differences in the socio-economic and ecological outcomes of FFC production.
abstract: If decommodification of cacao is to benefit vulnerable smallholder producers, institutions and trade relationships are needed that allow more producers to meet the more stringent requirements of a diversified, high-quality cacao sector.
apa_citation: Thomas, E., Villar, G., Zavaleta, D., Ceccarelli, V., Yovera, F., Blare, T., Ramirez, M., Oberlack, C., & Atkinson, R. (2026). Decommodifying cacao: Matchmaking between producers and buyers of fine flavour cacao from Peru. *Ecological Economics*, 241, 108850. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108850

title: “Taking Wendake Beyond Wendake”: Economic Development and Legal Tenacity in the Wendat Community
methods: Ethnographic case study of a single Indigenous community (participant observation across five fieldwork stays); qualitative analysis combining thematic analysis of 25 interviews with 17 community members, triangulation with documentary sources, and validation of findings with the community.
industry: Indigenous community economic development (financial institutions, tourism, crafts); SOCCA credit corporation, Hôtel-Musée, Pow-Wow
units: Wendat community of Wendake and its institutions/members
n_sample: 25 interviews with 17 people
authors: Viale, Thierry; Gendron, Yves
theories: Honneth's theory of the struggle for recognition (Honneth, 1992)
keywords: Axel Honneth; Indigenous emancipation; Indigenous identity; Wendat people; recognition
research_question: In a broader context of neocolonial economic development, how does an Indigenous community pursue peaceful struggles for the resurgence of its culture and the recognition of its Indigenous identity?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: inherited capital of recognition (accumulated ancestral self-esteem); Honneth's three spheres of recognition — love, legal rights, social esteem; tangibility of achievements
- Triggering condition: the Indian Act of 1876 denying Indigenous dignity and declaring populations legally irresponsible to own their land, superimposed on historic treaties
- Practices / peaceful struggles: (a) legal struggles to re-establish old treaties (e.g., the 1990 "Sioui case" recognizing the 1760 Murray Treaty; framework self-determination agreement); (b) economic development by creating an Indigenous-adapted financial apparatus (SOCCA, founded 1992) and a tourism/cultural-identity project (Hôtel-Musée, Pow-Wow, off-reserve exhibitions)
- Emergent outcome: strengthened legal rights and social esteem, consolidating the Nation's overall capital of recognition

The paper argues that because ancestral cultural specificities were recognized and experienced as rewarding, the Wendats built self-esteem that became an inherited capital of recognition — the foundation for contemporary, non-violent yet assertive struggles. Rather than radical rupture (assertive recognition) or passive dependence (reconciliation), the community pursued a "third way," asserting treaty rights through the courts and building a financial system adapted to the unseizability of reserve land. Tangible legal and economic achievements furnished recognition through the gaze of the non-Indigenous "other," reinforcing self-respect and social esteem without dissolving Indigenous identity into the market economy.
theoretical_contribution: The paper's main contribution is the concept of inherited capital of recognition — historically accumulated self-esteem (conveyed through historical books and oral narratives) on which contemporary emancipatory struggles are grounded and which, when struggles succeed, strengthens the community's overall capital of recognition. It extends Honneth's theory of recognition to an Indigenous community navigating neither rupture with nor passive subjection to the state, and underlines the role of tangibility in establishing recognition. The authors also frame their work as a pragmatic (not merely theoretical) contribution that could serve the agenda of Indigenous revitalization.
practical_implication: The authors suggest that Indigenous communities look to their pre-colonial history to identify cultural markers and restore self-confidence as a basis for recognition struggles, and that awareness, reappropriation, and diffusion of these markers can be facilitated through training programs. They argue legal struggle and economic development—pursued peacefully—can restore dignity and self-respect even where the prospects of full victory remain uncertain.
limitations: The authors acknowledge their analytic journey is incomplete and not without limitations: proximity to cities may significantly affect development potential, and the Wendat case is not mechanically transferable to communities (e.g., the Sioux) whose conditions make restoring traditional ways of life more complex. As non-Indigenous researchers with initially low understanding of First Nations' quests, they note their study required intensive reflexivity, co-construction of the object of study with the community, and vigilance against the threat of neo-colonialism; fieldwork was also fragmented across stays.
future_research: Future research could further investigate the potential for resurgence and identity recognition in conjunction with work on communities in South America, Australia, and New Zealand—including more precise identification of sources of cultural self-esteem rooted in a community's history, arguments for the legal consolidation of Indigenous rights, and economic strategies closely linked to cultural markers. The authors also point to open questions about which cultural representations are chosen for tourist consumption and which "accommodations" are acceptable in hybridizing tradition with modernity.
abstract: The economic self-sufficiency of Indigenous communities has become a widely recognized debate in Canada. This article analyzes how the Indigenous community of Wendake, located in Québec, is working toward the cultural resurgence and recognition of its identity—in order to combat the negative consequences ensuing from the Indian Act of 1876. The latter resulted in Indigenous populations declared legally irresponsible to own their land. Drawing on Axel Honneth’s concept of the struggle for recognition, we show how the Wendat community relied on what we call its inherited capital of recognition (notably its ancestral political and economic dispositions) to peacefully assert itself in two key areas. First, the legal right to have treaties signed with colonial powers from past centuries recognized; and second, economic development through the creation of a financial system adapted to Indigenous specificities. Our analysis points to a process of peaceful struggle for the resurgence and recognition of Indigenous identity in what at first glance appears to be a process of neocolonial subjugation to the market economy.
apa_citation: Viale, T., & Gendron, Y. (2026). “Taking Wendake beyond Wendake”: Economic development and legal tenacity in the Wendat community. *Business & Society*, 65(4), 837–871. https://doi.org/10.1177/00076503251376902

title: Resource regimes and the success of local renewable energy projects
methods: Qualitative comparative analysis (QCA, crisp-set conditions, fuzzy-set outcome, intermediate solution in R) combined with case-based process tracing across 12 renewable energy project cases; 55 semi-structured interviews plus document content analysis.
industry: Local renewable energy projects (solar, wind, biomass, geothermal)
units: Renewable energy projects (cases)
n_sample: 12 cases (1 Canada, 4 France, 7 Switzerland); 55 semi-structured interviews
authors: Viallon, F.-X.; Sarti, F.; Bombenger, P.-H.; Varone, F.; Trein, J. P.; Kodjovi, M.-J.; Dubois, J.; Fournis, Y.; Pineau, P.-O.
theories: institutional resource regime (IRR) approach (Gerber et al., 2009); law-and-economics / property rights (Coase, 1960; Ostrom, 1990); policy integration
keywords: Environmental protection; Planning; Property rights; Public policies; Renewable energy projects; Resource regimes
research_question: Under what configurations do property rights and public policies jointly explain the success or failure of local renewable energy projects (REPs)? The study tests whether four conditions — public landownership, revenue sharing, strategic planning, and environmental legislation — are necessary and/or sufficient for REP success.
mechanism_summary:
- Key conditions (property rights): public landownership (PUBLIC) within the REP area; revenue sharing (RESOURCE) with local public and/or private actors.
- Key conditions (public policies): strategic planning (PLAN) prior to local development plans; environmental and landscape legislation (ENV), where the absence of use restrictions favors success.
- Outcome: degree of REP success, fuzzy-set coded (0 = abandoned, 0.3 = on hold due to contestation, 0.7 = ongoing administrative/judicial procedure, 1 = operating).
- Causal logic: configurational (multiple conjunctural causation, equifinality, asymmetry); four sufficient paths identified via Boolean minimization (intermediate solution).

Drawing on the institutional resource regime (IRR) approach, the paper argues that ownership and policy regulation are interdependent in shaping resource use. Public landownership lets authorities simultaneously control planning and land, resolving "complexity traps," while revenue sharing incentivizes local actors to support a project. Public landowners (e.g., communes, cantons) prove more involved in REP initiation and more resilient against political contestation than private landowners, who may judge contested projects too risky and withdraw support.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the institutional resource regime approach by explicitly bringing property rights into the analysis of REP implementation, which prior literature on local acceptance and policy integration treats only through proxies. It shows that public policies alone are insufficient and that property-rights conditions — especially public landownership and revenue sharing — are central to explaining REP success, contributing to debates on energy democracy and energy citizenship.
practical_implication: Because landownership matters, policymakers should review means of securing public land control (expropriation, pre-emption, emption rights) in areas targeted for REPs, and consider redistributing project revenues locally to build support. Authorities may also use land-policy instruments to support private landowners, including co-landownership arrangements with private investors.
limitations: The number of cases (twelve) is limited, so replication with additional cases is needed to refine the results. Scope conditions apply: the cases are located in wealthy, liberal Western democracies, and institutional differences (legal systems, levels of government) are not explicitly modeled, which may limit generalizability.
future_research: Ongoing cases should be reconsidered later in light of legislative, judicial, economic, and technological changes. Further work could study the governance arrangements of REPs — involved actors, ownership, management, and decision rules — and how restructuring the distribution of property rights (e.g., delegating disposition and use rights over energy land to sub-national authorities) might accelerate the energy transition.
abstract: More specifically, public landownership and revenue sharing increase the chances of project success, as this entails the involvement of local democratic institutions and supports the resilience of project promotors against political contestation.
apa_citation: Viallon, F.-X., Sarti, F., Bombenger, P.-H., Varone, F., Trein, J. P., Kodjovi, M.-J., Dubois, J., Fournis, Y., & Pineau, P.-O. (2026). Resource regimes and the success of local renewable energy projects. *Ecological Economics*, *240*, 108843. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108843

title: The Grey Shades of Green Jobs: Unpacking the Occupational Approach to Green Employment
methods: Conceptual and methodological critique of the O*NET occupational approach combined with an empirical validation: green-employment shares computed from Labour Force Survey microdata for 24 European countries and 21 (1-digit NACE) industries, 2011-2022, using binary and Vona et al. (2018) greenness-index operationalisations; correlations and country-and-year fixed-effects panel regressions of green-job shares (levels, lags, first differences) on green/environmental performance indicators (GHG per capita, EGSS output).
industry: All industries (21 1-digit NACE sectors); sectoral analyses focus on energy and waste management/recycling
country: 24 European countries (Labour Force Survey); O*NET-SOC source classification is US-based
units: Country-year observations of green-employment shares (and country-industry-year for sectoral correlations)
n_sample: 24 European countries over 2011-2022 (panel; econometric models with up to 288 country-year observations)
authors: Villani, Davide; Fernández-Macías, Enrique; González-Vázquez, Ignacio; Oshafi, Vesna
theories: measurement validity critique (construct mismatch, type I/II errors); output- and process-based conceptualisation of green employment; task-based / occupational approach to green employment (Vona et al., 2018; Vona, 2021)
keywords: Green employment; Green jobs; Green occupations; Green tasks; Green transition
research_question: Is the occupational (task-based) approach — the dominant framework used by academics and institutions to measure green employment — theoretically and methodologically valid for capturing the labour-market impact of the green transition, and does the green employment it estimates actually track environmental outcomes?
mechanism_summary: The occupational approach infers a job's environmental contribution from its occupational title or task description rather than from what the worker or firm actually produces. This construct mismatch generates type I errors (workers in "green" occupations producing non-green outputs, e.g., robotics technicians in oil refining) and type II errors (essential contributors such as accountants or pesticide handlers in green firms going uncounted), and is compounded by arbitrary, outdated, US-anchored classifications requiring lossy aggregation. Because occupational structures are stable, the measure shows essentially flat green employment in Europe (2011-2022) and no robust correlation with environmental indicators — reflecting the framework's flaws rather than a failure of the green transition itself.
theoretical_contribution: The paper provides the first systematic scrutiny of the occupational approach to green employment that runs from its theoretical foundations through to empirical quantification, showing that the framework is misaligned with the very concept of green employment (which depends on outputs and processes, not occupational titles). It demonstrates that this construct-validity problem, not measurement noise alone, makes occupational measures unsuitable for benchmarking the green transition, and argues for output- and process- (organisation-) based alternatives.
practical_implication: Because accurate green-employment measures are central to reskilling, employment support, and regional industrial policy, statistical bodies such as Eurostat, Cedefop, and national institutes should develop EU-specific, regularly updated, output/process-based definitions rather than relying on the US-anchored O*NET occupational approach, which risks misguiding workforce policy and eroding confidence in green policies precisely when robust evidence is most needed.
limitations: The empirical validation deliberately implements the (flawed) occupational approach itself, so its flat trends and null correlations illustrate rather than test alternative measures. The environmental and policy indicators used as benchmarks have their own limitations — greenwashing in firm-level targets, the composite/indirect nature of indexes such as the EPI, and structural changes unrelated to green-job creation — which the authors acknowledge.
future_research: Future work should pursue output- and organisation-based frameworks that identify sectors generating green outputs and trace back all contributing workers (including supporting roles), drawing on granular firm-level and administrative datasets to map green value chains. The task-based approach retains value for mapping the tasks and skills of green new and emerging (GNE) occupations to inform targeted training and curricula.
abstract: Second, we discuss the methodological problems of this approach, including inconsistent categorisations, outdated classifications, and the assumption that the content of occupations remains constant across time and countries.
apa_citation: Villani, D., Fernández-Macías, E., González-Vázquez, I., & Oshafi, V. (2026). The grey shades of green jobs: Unpacking the occupational approach to green employment. *Ecological Economics*, 240, 108833. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108833
