text_path: library/NBS/2025-12/text/Villani 2025 The grey shades of green jobs Unpacking the occupational approach to green employment.txt

title: From Grand Challenges to Bold Solutions: Investor Perspectives on Financing Urban Climate Change Adaptation
methods: Qualitative comparative case study of three cities; 51 semi-structured interviews with investors plus focus group workshops (64 participants) and documentary policy analysis; data triangulation, timeline development, and coding of transcripts mapped onto a conceptual interventions matrix.
industry: Private finance and investment actors (banks, institutional/pension investors, asset managers, finance consultants, property developers, philanthropic organisations, family trusts) plus climate/green finance experts
time_period: Interviews and workshops conducted 2020–2023 (case timelines covering 2008–2022)
units: Investor key informants and finance-regime interventions across three city cases
n_sample: 51 semi-structured interviews; 64 focus group/workshop participants; 43 adaptation-specific interventions mapped
authors: Whittaker, Stella; Johnson, Craig; Ranger, Nicola; Pedersen, Esben Rahbek Gjerdrum
theories: strategic niche management (SNM) (Smith and Raven, 2012; Kemp et al., 1998); sustainability transitions (ST) (Geels and Schot, 2007; Schot and Geels, 2008)
keywords: Adaptation finance instruments; Adaptation finance needs; Cities; Climate change adaptation; Finance; Investors; Sustainability transition; Transform
research_question: 'What are the perspectives of investors regarding the challenges and opportunities for transforming the current finance regime for urban climate change adaptation?' The paper examines how private investors perceive challenges and opportunities for financing city-level innovations in urban climate change adaptation (UCCA), and how they interact with niche innovations and the broader finance regime.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: niche innovations versus the prevailing finance "regime"; "conforming" (fitting within existing finance structures) versus "stretching" (transforming the regime) interventions, with a hybrid "adjusting" category; "soft" (behavioural — education, networks, advice) versus "hard" (structural — policies, laws, taxes) interventions.
- Regime attributes analysed: Industry Structure, Policy, Market/Finance, Knowledge, and Technology — forming the "selection environment".
- Triggering conditions / barriers: three root market imperfections — incomplete/asymmetric information, positive and negative externalities, and imperfect markets (short-termism, market power) — plus path-dependent practices, mitigation bias, and risk-averse investor beliefs.
- Process / phases: (I) constructing case timelines (2008–2022); (II) interviews and focus groups with investors; (III) mapping 42–43 interventions across regime attributes into an interventions matrix to classify their transformative potential.
- Emergent outcomes: across all three cities, "conforming" and "adjusting" interventions dominate while "stretching" interventions are rare, signalling a finance regime poorly equipped to mobilise capital for UCCA.

Drawing on sustainability transitions (ST) and strategic niche management (SNM), the paper argues that finance regimes change when niche innovations reshape the perceptions of influential actors (investors, associations, regulators) through nurturing, empowerment, narrative-building, and political negotiation. Mobilising adaptation finance depends on whether innovations conform to existing finance structures or push institutions to "stretch" — addressing market imperfections, internalising externalities, and instilling a "sense of urgency" and industry leadership. Because investors treat UCCA as a contested public good with unattractive risk/return profiles, regime "stretching" requires regulatory, fiscal, and market-rule changes that codify responsibilities and de-risk investment.
theoretical_contribution: The study develops a new conceptual framework (an interventions matrix) extending Smith and Raven's (2012) and Geddes and Schmidt's (2020) work to categorise "conforming," "adjusting," and "stretching" interventions for financing UCCA, foregrounding the neglected role of finance in sustainability transitions. It empirically documents investor perspectives across three capital markets and offers a scholarly critique of overly optimistic views about private-sector investment's transformative role absent structural changes to address market imperfections.
practical_implication: Mobilising private finance for UCCA requires "stretching" interventions: new regulations and fiscal instruments (e.g., resilience pricing, tax exemptions, adaptation subsidies), de-risking via blended finance and public guarantees, standardised disclosure and impact-measurement tools, and stronger industry leadership from actors such as State Investment Banks. Shifting the spotlight from governments to finance bodies, the authors stress codifying investor responsibilities and building track record, trust, and co-investment to close the gap between cities' adaptation plans and available private finance.
limitations: The three exemplar cases (large, low-lying coastal capital markets) and the unique configuration of conditions in each city make generalisation a challenge. The secondary data was guided by interviewees and provides a snapshot rather than a comprehensive dataset, and government-representative interview data was collected but not analysed. Interventions in the future matrix (Fig. 6) are placed by independent features without a systems analysis of their interrelationships.
future_research: Future research could expand the framework to additional cities in both developed and emerging economies, allowing greater cross-case learning and refinement of the conceptual model. Further investigation is needed to determine the interrelationships among interventions more precisely, and to examine connections (excluded here) between sustainability-transitions and adaptation scholars' perspectives on transformation, as well as decentralised, community-driven, and alternative financing mechanisms.
abstract: The analysis is based on 51 semi-structured interviews as well as secondary information on UCCA policies and finance documents. The interview respondents indicate that market imperfections, including incomplete and asymmetric information, positive and negative externalities and imperfect finance markets, as well as path-dependent practices and views, hinder investment. Overall, the study contributes to an emerging body of scholarship that seeks to understand the conditions under which public and private actors may collaborate in generating enabling conditions for financing UCCA.
apa_citation: Whittaker, S., Johnson, C., Ranger, N., & Pedersen, E. R. G. (2026). From grand challenges to bold solutions: Investor perspectives on financing urban climate change adaptation. *Ecological Economics*, 242, 108879. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108879

title: Biodiversity finance: What has been achieved and where is it heading
methods: Conceptual essay / argumentative review; synthesizes policy reports, framework publications (TNFD, GBF, OECD, UNEP FI), and market data to diagnose why capital markets under-finance nature and to propose an academic agenda.
industry: Financial / capital markets (asset managers, banks, insurers, pension funds) and nature/biodiversity finance
country: Global (with US, EU, and UK regulatory examples)
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Willis, John
theories: double materiality; externalities / market failure (Dasgupta, 2021)
keywords: Biodiversity; Capital markets; Finance; Natural capital; Nature
research_question: Why are financial flows into nature-based instruments well behind targets despite a clear case for protecting nature, and how can the academic and scientific community help direct the necessary financial flows into nature recovery solutions?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: nature and biodiversity are materially significant to the economy, yet capital flowing into nature-based solutions is well below required levels — the paper diagnoses why and prescribes a remedy.
- Eight obstacles to financing nature: setting priorities; aligning time scales; educational shortfall; providing the framework; measurement of biodiversity; relying on technology and innovation; political considerations; and externality mentality.
- Key constructs: double materiality (impacts on a company and a company's impacts on nature/climate); the nature financing gap; nature-negative subsidies/outflows that dwarf nature-based-solution inflows; and unpriced externalities.
- Proposed lever: the academic and scientific community accelerating market urgency by working on the intersection of nature-related risks/opportunities and financial markets, building benchmarks and data, and helping reduce nature-negative subsidies.

The paper argues that biodiversity loss is a market failure rooted in unpriced externalities and a perceived absence of frameworks, data, and benchmarks. It contends that the necessary resources, instruments, and roadmaps already largely exist, so the binding constraints are prioritization, timescale mismatch, education, measurement, and politics rather than scarcity. Academics can be instrumental by reframing nature as risk-and-opportunity for investors, bridging short-term capital horizons with nature's long-term recovery dynamics, and informing policymakers and accounting bodies to internalize externalities.
theoretical_contribution: The paper frames under-investment in nature as a financial-markets coordination and materiality problem rather than a resource-availability problem, organizing the obstacles into eight analytically distinct mechanisms (priorities, timescales, education, frameworks, measurement, technology reliance, politics, externalities). It extends the "double materiality" concept from climate to biodiversity finance and articulates a normative agenda positioning the academic community as an instrumental actor in redirecting capital toward nature-positive outcomes.
practical_implication: Financial institutions should treat nature as a risk-and-opportunity issue warranting higher priority, exploit existing instruments, roadmaps, and data tools (e.g., TNFD disclosures, ENCORE, WWF Risk Filter Suite, BIOFIN catalogue) rather than waiting for a single benchmark, and design dual-purpose instruments that align returns with biodiversity's rebound. Academics can deliver near-term value by helping reduce nature-negative subsidies and clarifying externalities for policymakers.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The author calls for academics to analyze the timescale mismatch between short-term capital markets and long-term natural systems, to help shape more accurately dual-purpose nature-based financial instruments, to align new studies with financial preferences and available data sources, and to work with accounting bodies and regulators on incorporating nature and reducing nature-negative subsidies.
abstract: capiatl markets may be resistant to financing nature. Financial markets are great innovators, evidenced by the number of nature-related instruments available, showing that intellectual capital is being applied to this issue. We propose ways in which the academic community can be instrumental in directing the necessary financial flows into nature recovery solutions.
apa_citation: Willis, J. (2026). Biodiversity finance: What has been achieved and where is it heading. *Ecological Economics*, 240, 108713. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108713

title: Activism risk and corporate self‐regulation: Investigating how anti‐ SLAPP laws impact firms' institutional corporate social performance
methods: Quasi-experimental difference-in-differences (DiD) exploiting staggered state-level enactment of anti-SLAPP laws (1991–2018); two-way (firm, state, year) fixed-effects panel regressions on US public firms (MSCI/KLD + Compustat); standard errors clustered at firm level; event-study, permutation (500 draws), placebo, and block-bootstrap robustness checks.
industry: US public firms across a broad industrial spectrum (MSCI/KLD coverage, excluding the Corporate Governance category)
time_period: 1991–2018 (moderation analysis with RepRisk CSI coverage confined to 2007–2018)
units: firm-year observations
n_sample: 3488 firms (28,353 firm-year obs.) for H1; 3005 firms (19,363 firm-year obs.) for H2
authors: Wu, Zhiyan; Bruton, Garry; Krause, Ryan
theories: activism opportunity structures (Briscoe & Gupta, 2016; Walker et al., 2008); corporate preemptive self-regulation (Baron, 2001; King & Lenox, 2000; Maxwell et al., 2000); institutional determinants of CSP (Ioannou & Serafeim, 2012, 2022); moral capital / normative legitimacy (Godfrey, 2005; Suchman, 1995)
keywords: corporate social performance; difference in differences; nonmarket strategy; self-regulation; stakeholder activism
research_question: Whether and how do firms preemptively self-regulate—by enhancing their institutional corporate social performance—in anticipation of (rather than in reaction to) stakeholder activism, when the institutional risk of such activism rises following the enactment of anti-SLAPP laws?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls/FE: firm size, ROA, leverage, industry competition (HHI), state GDP, state liberalism, state disasters; firm, state, and year fixed effects

Anti-SLAPP laws strip firms of litigation as a tool to silence critics, lowering activists' perceived legal risk and creating an environment-level opportunity structure that raises firms' ex-ante activism risk. Anticipating heightened targeting, firms self-regulate by improving institutional CSP—performance beyond their formal boundaries that builds moral capital and normative legitimacy, making it harder for activists to frame them as morally culpable "safe bets." This preemptive response intensifies for firms whose firm-level vulnerability is amplified by media coverage of CSI, where environment- and firm-level opportunity structures interact to create layered vulnerability. Empirically, enactment raises institutional CSP by about 0.22 CSP strengths (~0.21 SD), with the effect stronger under high CSI coverage and emerging ~1 year post-enactment, persisting up to six years.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances scholarship on corporate strategies to preempt activism risk, shifting from the dominant backward-looking (reactive) view to a forward-looking one and generalizing preemptive self-regulation beyond location-sensitive sectors to institutional CSP as a broadly relevant tool. It theorizes the interdependence of multi-level activism opportunity structures, showing how environment-level (anti-SLAPP laws) and firm-level (CSI media coverage) conditions interact to shape firm adaptation. It also extends research on how institutions shape CSP from cross-national comparison toward subnational (state-level) institutional variation, heeding Ioannou and Serafeim's call to understand why institutions affect some CSR actions more than others.
practical_implication: For policymakers, activist-friendly legal reforms such as anti-SLAPP laws can drive meaningful improvements in firms' institutional CSP—not by mandate, but by altering the stakeholder environment firms operate in. Safeguarding civic freedoms and protecting public dissent thus carries an additional payoff: when firms lose the ability to suppress critics through litigation, they become more likely to improve institutional CSP to avoid becoming activist targets, generating broader societal benefits.
limitations: The analysis does not capture variability in how stakeholders actually receive these self-regulatory efforts, leaving stakeholder receptivity outside the model. It also cannot observe firms' nuanced internal decision-making processes, as executive interviews would be compromised by social desirability bias (executives framing CSP improvement as altruistic rather than strategic). Finally, institutional CSP is unlikely to be the only preemptive tactic firms use against would-be activism.
future_research: Future studies could integrate stakeholder receptivity into models of preemptive strategy and develop indirect methods that navigate social desirability bias to recover the true strategic motivations behind CSP improvement. Researchers could also examine other preemptive tactics firms deploy against would-be activism and the effects of other activism-friendly legislation (e.g., whistleblower protection laws) on firms' stakeholder-oriented adaptation.
abstract: By analyzing how US firms respond to the enactment of laws that prevent Strategic Lawsuits Against Public Participation (anti-SLAPP laws), we find that in anticipation of activist attacks, firms enhance their institutional corporate social performance (institutional CSP). This proactive response is more evident in firms facing a heightened risk of activism, such as those with substantial media exposure for socially irresponsible outcomes. We suggest the looming institutional risk of activism prompts firms to engage in self-regulatory adaptation in enhancing their institutional CSP, hopefully mitigating the risk of being targeted by stakeholder activists.
apa_citation: Wu, Z., Bruton, G., & Krause, R. (2026). Activism risk and corporate self-regulation: Investigating how anti-SLAPP laws impact firms' institutional corporate social performance. *Strategic Management Journal*, 47(3), 831–859. https://doi.org/10.1002/smj.70031

title: Nudging grid-friendly electric vehicle charging: Different shades of social framing and the power of individual factors
methods: Online vignette (between-subjects) experiment with four randomly assigned treatment groups; binary logit regression analysis reporting coefficients, odds ratios, z-values, and 95% CIs; Kruskal-Wallis tests for between-group balance; analyzed in Stata 18.
industry: Electric vehicle drivers / electric mobility (consumer behavior); recruited via the Norstat consumer panel
units: Individual EV (battery electric vehicle, BEV) drivers
n_sample: 1178 (of 1318 contacted); control n=286, social framing w/o transparency n=300, with transparency n=297, with donation-based gamification n=295
authors: Zatsarnaja, Junianna; Reiter, Katharina; Mohnen, Alwine; Nordfjærn, Trond
theories: free-riding and public goods; nudge theory (Thaler & Sunstein, 2009); social norms / social comparison
keywords: Behavioral intervention; Charging; Choice architecture; Electric mobility; Grid optimization
research_question: How does nudging through social framing—with or without social transparency of one's own decision, and with or without a donation-based gamification opportunity—affect EV drivers' willingness to charge during grid-friendly times (RQ1, RQ2), and how do grid-friendly charging adoption factors (RQ3) and individual characteristics (RQ4) relate to that willingness?
mechanism_summary:
- Adoption factors (covariates): Prior knowledge of grid-friendly charging; flexibility to integrate grid-friendly charging into routine; importance of economic benefit
- Individual characteristics (covariates): Biospheric values; sensitivity to social norms; morality toward grid-friendly charging; gender; age

Social framing was expected to recruit social-norm and reciprocity mechanisms—adding visibility of individual contributions to counter free-riding on the shared grid, and adding donation-based gamification to combine reward anticipation with prosocial generosity. Empirically, none of the three social-framing variants increased grid-friendly charging relative to control; donation-based gamification actually reduced willingness relative to the other framing conditions, consistent with a dilution effect from addressing too many motives at once and a weakened behavioral-reward link given an abstract, delayed donation. Willingness was instead driven by routine flexibility, stronger moral values, valuing economic benefit, and being female.
theoretical_contribution: The study shows that social framing—even when augmented with social transparency or donation-based gamification—does not reliably promote grid-friendly EV charging, contradicting prior findings that removing behavioral anonymity or adding gamification motivates pro-environmental behavior. It foregrounds intrinsic moral values and perceived routine flexibility, rather than externally imposed social-norm signals, as the psychological mechanisms underlying EV drivers' grid-conscious charging, and surfaces a potential detrimental ("dilution") effect of combining donation-based gamification with social framing.
practical_implication: For charging service providers and policymakers, social framing techniques that highlight collective impact or expose individual users' actions appear ineffective, and adding donation-based gamification to such framing may backfire. Because willingness was tied to moral values and routine flexibility, providers should instead enhance temporal flexibility (e.g., convenient off-peak charging at workplaces and destinations), design more tailored and interactive gamification, deploy smart-charging/V2G infrastructure, and run educational campaigns that cultivate personal responsibility.
limitations: The online experiment and survey create a potential intention-behavior gap, so stated intentions may not translate into real-world charging. Reliance on a single vignette limits ecological validity and the ability to examine contextual trade-offs, and the text-based nudges with restricted donation options were not very engaging. The Norwegian setting—with its unique energy landscape and highly developed electric mobility—limits generalizability to other contexts.
future_research: Future work should validate the findings through multiple-treatment field studies and observational data, and apply multi-criteria scenarios varying charging urgency, opportunities, and incentive forms. Researchers could test more engaging and personalized gamification and non-monetary strategies (e.g., mobile apps with rewards and challenges, community feedback, real-time grid-condition messaging), investigate smart-charging and V2G technologies, and assess policy instruments such as time-of-use rates and dynamic tariffs.
abstract: Moreover, higher flexibility in daily routines, as well as individual characteristics such as being female and holding stronger moral values, are positively associated with grid-friendly charging. These findings provide insights into factors that are related to grid-conscious charging behavior, contributing to power system stability, renewable energy integration, and a successful transition to sustainable electric mobility.
apa_citation: Zatsarnaja, J., Reiter, K., Mohnen, A., & Nordfjærn, T. (2026). Nudging grid-friendly electric vehicle charging: Different shades of social framing and the power of individual factors. *Ecological Economics*, 240, 108805. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108805

title: Transparency and divestment: the impact of a public database about insurers’ carbon-intensive investments on their portfolio choices
methods: Difference-in-differences design comparing treatment (California-licensed) and control (non-California-licensed) insurers around the 2016 CRCI database introduction, with insurer and year fixed effects, standard errors clustered at the insurer level; supplemented by parallel-trends, regression-discontinuity, entropy-balancing, and propensity-score-matching robustness tests, plus cross-sectional and investee-level DiD analyses.
industry: Insurance (life/health and property/casualty insurers) and their carbon-intensive bond/stock investees
units: Insurer-year observations (treatment vs. control insurers); investee-year observations in externality tests
n_sample: 12,605 insurer-year observations from 1,287 unique insurers (514 California-licensed, 773 not)
authors: Zhang, Xiao
theories: disclosure processing costs (Blankespoor et al., 2020); rational inattention / information capacity constraints (Sims, 2003, 2010; Peng, 2005); reputational and stakeholder pressure / public shaming (Dyreng et al., 2016)
keywords: Carbon-intensive investments; Climate risk information; Information processing costs; Insurance industry; Portfolio choices; Transparency
research_question: Does enhancing transparency about insurers’ environmentally irresponsible (carbon-intensive) investments — by reducing the processing costs of identifying them through a public database — influence insurers’ portfolio choices and, in turn, the financing and investment decisions of their carbon-intensive investees?
mechanism_summary: By centralizing processed summary statistics of insurers’ carbon-intensive holdings, the CRCI database reduces the awareness and acquisition costs that previously made these positions hard to infer from complex statutory filings. Lower processing costs let climate-conscious stakeholders and regulators identify environmentally irresponsible insurers and pressure them via boycotts, divestment campaigns, reputational damage, and regulatory scrutiny. To the extent this anticipated pressure imposes economic costs, treatment insurers shift away from carbon-intensive assets (a ~14% relative reduction, ~$10 million per insurer). Because bonds make up ~90% of these holdings, the resulting demand shock propagates to investees, who reduce bond issuances and capital expenditures.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the insurer-investment literature by being the first to show that enhanced transparency (specifically, reduced processing costs) of insurers’ carbon-intensive holdings can drive portfolio decarbonization, complementing work on mandatory qualitative climate disclosures and responding to Blankespoor et al.’s (2020) call for research on the broader implications of processing costs. It also contributes to the debate on the efficacy of investor exit/divestment strategies by demonstrating that bond investors’ divestitures influence targeted firms’ financing (lower bond issuances) and real investment (lower capital expenditures).
practical_implication: The findings suggest that policymakers and regulators seeking to redirect capital away from the carbon economy can use low-cost transparency interventions — publicizing processed data on institutions’ carbon-intensive holdings — to prompt portfolio decarbonization across the broader financial sector, not only among insurers.
limitations: The documented decline in carbon-intensive investments could partly reflect contemporaneous decarbonization pressure from California insurance regulators, which is difficult to fully disentangle because the database may itself amplify such pressure by increasing regulatory awareness. Given California's leadership in promoting portfolio decarbonization, the author cannot rule out that other concurrent California regulatory efforts partially contribute to the observed divestment.
future_research: Not reported in paper
abstract: In 2016, the California Department of Insurance introduced a public database of carbon-intensive holdings of certain California-licensed insurers. The database lowers the cost to external stakeholders of assessing insurers’ carbon investments by removing the need to analyze complex statutory filings. Using a difference-in-differences design, I find that, after the introduction of the database, affected insurers (i.e., those included in the database) reduce their carbon-intensive holdings by approximately 14% relative to unaffected insurers. The reduction is more pronounced for insurers with harder-to-process statutory filings, greater public visibility, or operations in states with more climate-conscious stakeholders. In terms of insurers’ divestment consequences, I find that affected portfolio firms reduce bond offerings and capital expenditures following the database’s introduction. Overall, my findings suggest that enhancing transparency on institutions’ carbon-intensive investments helps redirect capital away from the carbon economy.
apa_citation: Zhang, X. (2026). Transparency and divestment: the impact of a public database about insurers’ carbon-intensive investments on their portfolio choices. *Review of Accounting Studies*, 31(1), 37–73. https://doi.org/10.1007/s11142-025-09928-x

title: Toxic neighbors: E-waste dumps and the decline of social capital
methods: Quasi-experimental difference-in-differences (DID) on pooled cross-sections, using geodesic distance to the nearest e-waste site as continuous treatment intensity interacted with a post-establishment indicator; dumping-site, survey-year, and region-by-year fixed effects, plus individual and PSU controls; complemented by an event-study and a mediation-style mechanism analysis.
industry: Communities living near electronic waste (e-waste) dumping sites (Agbogbloshie, Ghana; Soluos, Nigeria)
units: Individual survey respondents residing within 20 km of an e-waste dumping site
n_sample: 2,886 respondents in the baseline social capital sample (2,413 in the preferred fully-controlled specification)
authors: Zhu, Jialiang; Appiah-Kubi, Michael Akwasi; Hu, Runzhe
theories: Social capital theory (Coleman, 1988; Ostrom & Ahn, 2009; Pretty & Ward, 2001)
keywords: Dumping sites; E-waste; Social capital; West Africa
research_question: How does living near electronic waste dumping sites affect the multidimensional social capital of nearby communities, and through what mechanisms does this environmental exposure erode community cohesion?
mechanism_summary: The paper argues that e-waste exposure corrodes social capital through two complementary, layered channels. First, environmental contamination and health risks degrade material living conditions, depressing subjective well-being, encouraging social withdrawal, and weakening cooperative relationships. Second, persistent hazards under weak enforcement are read as evidence of governance failure and official–polluter collusion, raising perceived corruption, damaging institutional trust, and discouraging civic engagement. Both mediators are positively associated with social capital, and entering each into the outcome equation attenuates (but does not eliminate) the proximity coefficient, consistent with partial mediation through material well-being and institutional trust respectively.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the e-waste literature beyond its well-documented environmental and health dimensions by empirically identifying its adverse effects on social capital, and it illuminates the mechanisms — deteriorating living conditions and weakened institutional trust — through which environmental degradation jointly erodes social cohesion. It connects e-waste exposure to the circular-economy policy agenda, bridging environmental economics, political science, and development studies to inform socially grounded environmental governance.
practical_implication: Appraisal, permitting, and monitoring of waste infrastructure should internalize social costs by tracking social cohesion, trust, and civic participation alongside emissions and health indicators. Extended Producer Responsibility (EPR), with legally binding collection and recovery targets, earmarked revenues for clean recycling and community programs, and integration of informal workers, is a central lever to repair the social fabric while reducing the physical externalities of waste.
limitations: Afrobarometer randomly displaces field coordinates for confidentiality, which likely attenuates the distance gradient toward zero, so estimated effects may be conservative. The mediation analysis is a simple mediation-style strategy rather than a formal causal mediation design, and the "Member/attends local development association" item was not fielded in Rounds 8-9, creating missingness in the social insurance dimension.
future_research: Not reported in paper
abstract: We study how living near electronic waste dumping sites shapes the social fabric of communities in Ghana and Nigeria.
apa_citation: Zhu, J., Appiah-Kubi, M. A., & Hu, R. (2026). Toxic neighbors: E-waste dumps and the decline of social capital. *Ecological Economics*, 242, 108897. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ecolecon.2025.108897

title: From Stigma to Support: “Black-Owned” Labels and Expertise Stereotypes in Cannabis and Psychedelics Markets
methods: Mixed-methods, predominantly experimental. Study 1: survey of Black drugs-industry professionals with qualitative coding of open-ended responses (within-subject, repeated-measures t-tests, chi-square). Study 2: within-subject online experiment measuring expertise-stereotype scores (single-mean t-tests vs. 0). Study 3: 2 (label) × 2 (product) between-subjects online experiment; three-way ANOVA and PROCESS Model 12 moderated mediation (5,000 bootstraps). Study 4: two field A/B advertising tests on Meta/Facebook; logistic regressions of click-through on ownership label with age/gender controls.
industry: Legalized 'drugs' (cannabis derivatives such as CBD/THC and psychedelics such as psilocybin) versus non-drug consumer products (candy, dietary supplements); psychedelic-medicine advertising
country: United States (Study 1 also included Canada-based professionals)
time_period: Data collected 2023 (Studies 2-4 online and field) and 2024 (Study 1 survey)
units: Individual consumers and Black drugs-industry professionals/entrepreneurs; ad impressions in field tests
n_sample: Study 1 N=37 Black professionals; Study 2 N=223; Study 3 N=429; Study 4 N=42,994 ad impressions
authors: Achar, Chethana; Agrawal, Nidhi; Lock, Keyaira
theories: expertise stereotype framework (Herling, 2000; Gürhan-Canli & Maheswaran, 2000; Maheswaran, 1994); in-group support / social identity (Brewer, 1999; Babar et al., 2023); stereotype activation and application (Devine, 1989; Kunda & Spencer, 2003); stereotype threat (Steele, 1997; Spencer et al., 2016)
keywords: cannabis; expertise stereotypes; field studies; ownership labeling; psychedelics; social identity branding
research_question: What are the drivers of consumer responses to Black business owners and marketers in the legalized drugs category, and can Black entrepreneurs realize the intended benefits of equity-focused policies by signaling their racial identity through "Black-owned" labeling? The paper asks whether such labeling helps or hurts, and how responses differ between in-group (Black) and out-group (White) consumers.
mechanism_summary: A "Black-owned" label spontaneously activates domain-specific expertise stereotypes about the owner's social group. Because drugs are a high-consumption-risk category, out-group (White) consumers apply favorable expertise stereotypes—via quality inferences—and increase intentions for "Black-owned" drug products. In-group (Black) consumers, however, do not extend support to the stigmatized drugs category—plausibly due to stereotype threat, distancing from stigmatizing associations, and self-enhancement goals—even though they hold the same positive cannabis expertise stereotype; their in-group support appears only for low-risk, low-stigma products like candy.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces an expertise-stereotype mechanism to the ownership-labeling literature, presenting a phenomenon in which stereotypes can yield a positive response among out-group members—diverging from previously documented in-group support, social justice, and racial discrimination mechanisms. It documents (a) race-related product expertise stereotypes (previously unestablished, alongside gender and country-of-origin) and (b) the bounds of expertise-stereotype application in consumption decisions, showing product category and stigma as critical moderators.
practical_implication: In contexts like legalized drugs with a predominantly White (out-group) audience, "Black-owned" labeling can benefit Black entrepreneurs by leveraging favorable expertise stereotypes. In low-risk categories such as candy, "Black-owned" labeling is beneficial mainly when Black customers are the key target segment. Notably, Black professionals' own expectations (anticipating mostly in-group support) are inconsistent with realized consumer responses in the drugs category.
limitations: The studies do not involve actual purchase or consumption of cannabis or psychedelics; ad click-through is used as a behavioral proxy, which is many steps removed from purchase and post-consumption responses. Participants' drug-use history and domain knowledge were not measured due to participant-risk considerations. Field tests (Study 4) rely on aggregate, individual-unidentifiable data and cannot confirm random assignment given platform ad-delivery optimization. The work emphasizes the positive aspects of expertise stereotypes without fully examining their potential negative consequences.
future_research: Future studies could ethically collect consumption and post-consumption data, examine consumer expertise/knowledge as a moderator of reliance on stereotype cues, and explore whether "Black-owned" versus broader "Minority-owned" labels are preferable. Researchers should investigate how shifting societal attitudes toward drugs may change the documented mechanisms over time, extend to other social identities and labeling types (Table 4), and critically examine how expertise stereotypes in stigmatized domains can have adverse effects.
abstract: We examine the effect of “Black-owned” labeling on cannabis and psychedelic brands, in context of the stigmatized and risky nature of the drugs category. Building on prior studies examining social justice or discrimination, we introduce an expertise stereotype framework.
apa_citation: Achar, C., Agrawal, N., & Lock, K. (2026). From stigma to support: “Black-owned” labels and expertise stereotypes in cannabis and psychedelics markets. *Journal of Consumer Research*, 52(5), 1000–1021. https://doi.org/10.1093/jcr/ucaf022

title: An Economic View of Corporate Social Impact
methods: Structural empirical economics: a microfounded static partial-equilibrium welfare model in which a firm's social impact is the welfare loss from its exit; demand estimated via a random-coefficient logit discrete-choice system fit to a new stated-preference survey, marginal costs inferred from Nash-Bertrand first-order conditions, counterfactual prices simulated, and labor supply slopes estimated from survey responses; firm and industry impacts computed (industry impact apportioned by Shapley value) and benchmarked against ESG/social-impact ratings.
industry: 74 firms across 12 industries: upstream oil plus 11 differentiated-product markets (automobiles, airlines, six CPG categories — beer, cereal, cigarettes, soda, toothpaste, yogurt — grocery retail, smartphones, chain restaurants)
time_period: Survey fielded in two rounds, July and November 2021; firm revenues and employment based on 2019 data
units: Firms (defined at stock-ticker or holding-company level), plus a 3,500-person consumer/worker survey
n_sample: 74 firms; ~3,500 survey respondents fielded, 3,544 valid responses after screening
authors: Allcott, Hunt; Montanari, Giovanni; Ozaltun, Bora; Tan, Brandon
theories: Shapley value (Shapley 1953); discrete choice demand (random coefficient logit; Berry, Levinsohn, and Pakes 1995); partial equilibrium consumer and producer theory; welfare economics / welfarist framework
keywords: ESG ratings; consumer surplus; corporate social impact; externalities; impact investing; social welfare; worker surplus
research_question: How can a company's social impact be defined and measured in a theoretically grounded, economically consistent way — and how do such measures compare with existing ESG and social-impact ratings? The paper answers by conceptualizing a firm's social impact as the social welfare loss that its exit would cause.
mechanism_summary:
- Core construct: corporate social impact defined as the welfare loss from a firm's exit (individual impact) and the firm's Shapley-value share of the welfare loss from the whole industry's exit (share of industry impact).
- Components of total surplus: consumer surplus, worker surplus, firm profits, and externalities/internalities (e.g., CO2, smoking harms), aggregated as a Pareto-weighted sum of utility with social marginal welfare weights inversely proportional to income.
- Key drivers (IV-like): consumers' and workers' willingness to substitute away (own-price and labor-supply elasticities), consumer/worker income levels, and the magnitude of negative externalities/internalities.
- Outcome (DV): dollar-valued social welfare loss from exit, by firm and by industry.
- Identification: a 3,500-person stated-preference survey supplies firm-level price-response and labor-supply-response shares, feeding a random-coefficient logit demand system, Nash-Bertrand markup recovery, and integration under residual labor supply.

A firm has larger social impact when its consumers and workers are less willing to substitute away, when it serves lower-income consumers or employs lower-ability workers (who carry higher welfare weights), and when its products generate fewer negative externalities and internalities. Profits contribute little because most are business-stealing and accrue to high-income owners with low welfare weights. Empirically, consumer surplus dominates; the largest positive impact is Walmart's grocery business (~$151B/year) and the most negative is Philip Morris (~-$17B/year).
theoretical_contribution: The paper provides an economically grounded, welfarist definition of corporate social impact — the causal welfare effect of a firm's exit — and shows that the standard toolkit of welfare economics (consumer/worker surplus, externalities, Shapley-value apportionment) can be assembled into an internally consistent, dollar-denominated measure. It reframes "impact" as a partial-equilibrium counterfactual and demonstrates that consumer surplus, not profit, is the primary component.
practical_implication: Impact investors and rating agencies should give more attention to consumer surplus, especially for low-income consumers, rather than focusing narrowly on profits or conventional ESG dimensions. Because prominent ESG and social-impact ratings are essentially uncorrelated with the economically grounded measure, the framework offers firms, investors, workers, and consumers a transparent way to quantify and compare social impact.
limitations: The welfarist moral philosophy and the conceptualization of impact as the effect of exit may omit characteristics stakeholders value (e.g., diversity and inclusion, governance, lobbying). The empirical implementation relies on restrictive static partial-equilibrium assumptions (ignoring supplier surplus, innovation spillovers, and competitor entry), uses hypothetical stated-preference surveys to identify elasticities, and requires strong functional-form and contested externality/internality assumptions; estimates are a proof of concept, not final measures.
future_research: Dynamic product-entry models could capture the time path of exit effects that the static framework approximates only roughly. The approach could be extended to many more markets and used to address limitations in existing corporate social impact and ESG measurement systems, and to refine the (normative) distributional weights and externality magnitudes used in the quantification.
abstract: Growing discussions of impact investing and stakeholder capitalism have increased interest in measuring companies’ social impact. We conceptualize corporate social impact as the welfare loss that would be caused by a firm’s exit. To illustrate, we quantify the social impacts of 74 firms in 12 industries using a new survey measuring consumer and worker substitution patterns combined with models of product and labor markets. We find that consumer surplus is the primary component of social impact, suggesting that consumer impacts deserve more attention from impact investors. Existing environmental, social, and governance (ESG) and social impact ratings are essentially unrelated to our economically grounded measures.
apa_citation: Allcott, H., Montanari, G., Ozaltun, B., & Tan, B. (2026). An economic view of corporate social impact. *The Journal of Finance*, 81(1), 285–328. https://doi.org/10.1111/jofi.70004

title: Thinking-Being With a Garden: Developing Ecocentric Ethics for Sustainable Organising
methods: Conceptual theory development through two autoethnographic vignettes of the authors' own allotment gardening, read through a Baradian 'agential cut' lens, combined with a cross-disciplinary review of gardening, business-ethics, and organisation-studies literatures.
industry: Allotment gardening (non-commercial, urban allotment plots)
time_period: Vignettes span roughly five years (e.g., a cherry tree pruned and harvested for nearly 10 years; the plot in the authors' care since October 2021)
units: Two authors' allotment gardens, presented as two autoethnographic vignettes
n_sample: 2 autoethnographic vignettes (two gardens)
authors: Allen, Stephen; Strauß, Anke
theories: Baradian agential realism / ethico-onto-epistemology (Barad, 2003, 2007, 2010, 2014, 2016); ecocentrism (Heikkurinen et al., 2016; Allen et al., 2019); relational posthuman ethics / ethics of care (Phillips, 2015, 2019; Cozza & Gherardi, 2023)
keywords: Ethico-onto-epistemology; Garden; Organising; Posthuman; Sustainability
research_question: In the context of considering sustainable ways of organising businesses, the paper pursues the question: "How do we develop the contours of an ecocentric ethics that is rooted in living socio-material entanglements?" The aim is to move beyond human-centred, anthropocentric framings of nature toward an embodied, relational ethics of "being with" earth-others.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: agential cut / "cutting-together apart"; "ethico-onto-epistemology"; "naturecultures"; "earth-others"; habitat vs. boundary; "ecology-in-place"; knowing vs. unknowing.
- Process / phases: mapping the cross-disciplinary garden(ing) literature → introducing Barad's ethico-onto-epistemology and the agential cut → weaving two autoethnographic vignettes (Sheffield allotment; Berlin cherry tree) → moving from perceiving individual species toward perceiving living assemblages and habitats.
- Practices: organic/allotment gardening, zoning, digging a pond, pruning, wildlife-camera observation — read as material-discursive boundary-making practices.
- Emergent outcomes: recognition that humans can be peripheral or decentred participants in webs of life; a shift from intimate "knowing" relations to an ethics of "unknowing"; sensitivity to planetary rhythms and temporalities.

Through a Baradian lens, the authors argue that gardens come into being as phenomena through intra-active "agential cuts" that materially and discursively produce distinctions (e.g., garden vs. wild, plant vs. soil, present vs. absent species). By tracing how these cuts both separate and connect humans and earth-others across seasonal and planetary rhythms, they develop an ecocentric ethics in which responsibility is reframed as "response-ability," human involvement is sometimes peripheral, and "unknowing" becomes a valued epistemic dimension of sustainable organising.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends relational posthuman and ecocentric ethics in business ethics / MOS by grounding them in concrete, located naturecultures (gardens) rather than abstract or single-species framings, thereby answering critiques that ecocentric theorising stays too general and must be rooted in an "ecology-in-place." It develops Barad's "ethico-onto-epistemology" and the agential cut to show how cutting-together-apart produces species and habitats as co-emerging, and contributes the insight that humans are not always active participants in the entanglements that sustain earth-others — decentring the human further than prior ethics-of-care accounts.
practical_implication: For sustainable business practice, the authors sketch propositions translating ecocentric ethics into reflexive questions managers might ask — e.g., "How do we understand that our business activities are participating in ecosystems?", "Who else's thriving do we make possible?", and how organizing practices attend to planetary rhythms — positioning an "ecology-related unknowingness" as a source of livelihood (re)creation rather than something to be eliminated through control-oriented data and compliance (e.g., EU CSRDDD/non-financial reporting).
limitations: The authors note they have "not (yet) outlined possible consequences for business ethics," offering only sketched propositions in their closing remarks. The account is built from the authors' own two allotment gardens (Sheffield and Berlin) and their individual experiences of particular naturecultures, so its claims are situated and experiential rather than generalisable, and the move from intimate gardening relations to organisational/business contexts is acknowledged as highly challenging.
future_research: The paper invites further engagement with the "rich socio-materialities and embodied multispecies practices that gardens offer" and with developing an ecocentric *business* ethics that takes planetary boundaries and Anthropocene volatilities seriously. It proposes pursuing reflexive questions about how organising practices cut earth-others' webs of life together and apart, and about how a practice of "unknowing" and embodied listening can reshape sustainable organising.
abstract: In this paper, we turn to gardens to offer a new ethics of being with the world that can inform sustainable organising. For this, we develop an ecocentric ethics perspective informed by the writing of Karen Barad, in particular her notion of ‘agential cuts’. By reviewing gardening literatures, and exploring our embodied gardening practices, we attempt to ‘step out’ of institutionalised ways of thinking and working to reach for different embodied and relational ways of being with earth-others. We contribute to debates by: (a) tracing multispecies mutualities of ‘cutting-together apart’ within the gardens we tend, to show how such cutting can develop understandings of an entangled ethics in which human involvement can at times be peripheral; and, (b) showing how garden(ing)s can help enrich understandings of ‘ethico-onto-epistemologies’ by situating embodied practices of organising within planetary processes and rhythms which are variously consequential for sustaining forms of life. These contributions stemming from garden(ing)s can help to inform ecocentric thinking-being in business organisations by developing possibilities for (re)conceptualising: how businesses practices, and connected imaginaries, are entangled in creating conditions that can diminish and enhance earth-others existences; and, how the fluidities and temporalities of specifically located webs of life in which businesses activities are part necessitate sensitivities to working with unknowing.
apa_citation: Allen, S., & Strauß, A. (2026). Thinking-being with a garden: Developing ecocentric ethics for sustainable organising. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-026-06255-2

title: Digital Resilience for the Climate Crisis: A Multi-Perspective Analysis
methods: Conceptual research commentary structured as a metasynthesis weaving together five peer-reviewed scholarly contributions; one contribution (Section 2) draws on field research and site visits in Brazil and Indonesia
industry: Information systems / digital technologies applied to climate resilience (disaster monitoring, global supply chains / circular economy, consumer carbon-incentive programs)
country: Global, with focal field cases in Brazil and Indonesia (Global South); illustrations from China, Singapore, the EU, and others
time_period: Commentary developed from a 2022 MIS Quarterly call; not an empirical time series
units: Five scholarly teams' perspectives / facets of digital resilience for the climate crisis (DRCC)
n_sample: Five condensed contributions selected from 79 initial submissions (six contributions later condensed into five sections)
authors: Boh, W. F.; Melville, N. P.; Baptista, J.; Chasin, F.; Horita, F.; Ixmeier, A.; Johnson, S. L.; Ketter, W.; Kranz, J.; Miranda, S.; Nan, N.; Pentland, B. T.; Recker, J.; Sadeghi, S.; Sarker, Saonee; Sarker, Suprateek; Sutanto, J.; Wang, P.; Wilopo, W.
theories: general systems theory (holons; information ecology theory); neoclassical economics / change management; resilience theory (engineering vs. ecological resilience); sociotechnical theory / eco-sociotechnical perspective
keywords: Digital resilience; adaptation; climate crisis; complex system; ecology; mitigation; nature; sociotechnical systems
research_question: How can the information systems field identify effective approaches to achieving digital resilience for the climate crisis (DRCC) — that is, how can digital technologies be used to absorb, adapt to, transform after, and proactively mitigate the root causes of climate-induced shocks across multiple entities, scales, and time horizons?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Digital resilience for the climate crisis (DRCC) must move beyond a reactive, "crisis-as-event" view of digital technologies absorbing discrete shocks toward an expanded, proactive conceptualization that integrates nature, spans micro and macro scales, combines adaptation with mitigation, and treats the climate crisis as an ongoing process.
- Key constructs: eco-sociotechnical ecosystems composed of social, technical, and natural "holons" interacting across hierarchical scales; cycle time and scale of holons; engineering resilience (control to increase scale difference) versus ecological resilience (adapt while retaining function); an "ecosystem-wide information system" (EWIS).
- Process / framework: a two-dimensional framework classifying DRCC actions by (1) adaptation vs. mitigation and (2) reactive vs. proactive orientation, illustrated through digital circular-economy supply chains and consumer carbon-incentive (CCI) programs with cross-scale "reform" and "remember" processes.
- Boundary conditions / tensions: tensions between centralized data analytics and local/indigenous knowledge; trade-offs across scales (Global North mitigation harming Global South); rebound effects and resource intensity of the digital technologies themselves.

Across five contributions, the commentary develops the process by which digital technologies can build resilience: natural, social, and technical holons operating at disparate spatial and temporal scales interact within loosely coupled ecosystems, and digital technologies that fit the scale and cycle time of the holons involved can enable either engineering resilience (mitigation that separates climate and human activity scales) or ecological resilience (adaptation that aligns ecosystem and climate scales). Because micro-level resilience actions do not automatically aggregate into macro-level sustainability, the central argument is that DRCC requires aligning micro actions with systemic macro objectives, integrating nature into sociotechnical theory, and combining reactive adaptation with proactive mitigation of root causes under a crisis-as-process perspective.
theoretical_contribution: The commentary expands the conceptualization of digital resilience by integrating nature into sociotechnical thinking through an eco-sociotechnical perspective built on holons, scales, and an adapted information ecology theory, and by distinguishing engineering from ecological resilience as scale-management strategies. It contributes a framework that organizes DRCC actions along adaptation/mitigation and reactive/proactive dimensions, and it reframes the climate crisis from a series of exogenous events to an ongoing anthropogenic process, surfacing four transcending themes (integrate nature, examine micro and macro actions, include reactive and proactive strategies, and view crisis as process) intended to seed a cumulative IS research agenda.
practical_implication: For those designing, implementing, and regulating climate-change solutions, the reconceptualization of ecosystems highlights the need to assess each mitigation and adaptation solution at multiple scales and to match technologies to the type of resilience being pursued (strong programming functions for engineering resilience; robust communication functions for ecological resilience). The framework points to concrete applications such as digital twins and federated open-data spaces for circular supply chains and consumer carbon-incentive programs that translate micro-level low-carbon choices into macro-level mitigation.
limitations: The authors acknowledge that the metasynthesis embraces breadth over depth and does not claim to be exhaustive, declining to engage deeply with any single theoretical tradition; important vantage points such as critical social science and historical perspectives remain underexplored. The Brazil and Indonesia cases focus primarily on adaptation rather than the root causes of the climate crisis, and the commentary notes that digital technologies themselves are resource-intensive and can reinforce prevailing ideologies or exacerbate inequalities.
future_research: The authors call for new methodologies and metrics that quantify how micro-level actions aggregate into macro-level sustainability outcomes, longitudinal studies that capture the crisis-as-process and long-term temporal dynamics, and integrated assessment models capturing interdependencies among human, technological, and environmental systems. They invite IS scholars to develop and validate integrative theories that incorporate natural ecosystems into sociotechnical studies, to investigate digital circular-economy and secondary-market governance, and to examine the second-order and cross-level effects of consumer carbon-incentive programs.
abstract: This commentary explores multiple perspectives on the potential use of digital technologies to improve organizational resilience in the context of climate change. Such an approach is needed to address this complex problem space, especially since it encompasses a wide variety of phenomena, including floods and landslides, disruptions to global supply chains, heat waves, biodiversity loss, greenhouse gas emissions, and food insecurity. We assembled a diverse set of five scholarly teams specializing in multiple problem topics, research approaches, and theoretical perspectives on this project. Each team identified and problematized a specific facet of digital resilience for the climate crisis. The perspectives cover a range of rich narratives, including digital resilience in the context of floods and landslides in Brazil and Indonesia, conceptual development efforts incorporating the natural environment with people and technology, reconceptualization of the problem space in terms of time and type, and two applications of digital resilience in the domains of global supply chains and carbon emissions tracking. This research commentary thus presents a multiperspective examination and interrogation of digital resilience for addressing the climate crisis, out of which four transcending themes emerge: the need to integrate nature into sociotechnical thinking, the need to examine actions at both micro and macro levels, the need to include both reactive and proactive strategies, and the need to view climate crisis as a process rather than a series of events. This commentary aims to motivate other scholars who take diverse theoretical perspectives to join us in developing fundamental knowledge and practical solutions needed to achieve digital resilience for the climate crisis.
apa_citation: Boh, W. F., Melville, N. P., Baptista, J., Chasin, F., Horita, F., Ixmeier, A., Johnson, S. L., Ketter, W., Kranz, J., Miranda, S., Nan, N., Pentland, B. T., Recker, J., Sadeghi, S., Sarker, Saonee, Sarker, Suprateek, Sutanto, J., Wang, P., & Wilopo, W. (2026). Digital resilience for the climate crisis: A multi-perspective analysis. *MIS Quarterly*, 50(1), 1-34. https://doi.org/10.25300/misq/2025/18779

title: Business and management research on societal wellbeing: a historic, multi-journal and large language model analysis of the UN’s Sustainable Development Goals
methods: Large-scale AI-assisted bibliometric mapping. An ensemble of four AI models (GPT 4.1, DeepSeek, Claude Sonnet 3.7, and the BERT-based RSM SDG Mapper) scored each article's relevance to the 17 SDGs on a 1–10 scale; a majority-voting rule (≥3 of 4 models above a threshold of 7/10) classified articles as SDG-related. Robustness checks used all four-model combinations drawn from a six-model pool; topic modeling (LDA) extracted themes for the six most-studied SDGs.
industry: Business and management scholarly research across five disciplines (accounting, finance, management, marketing, operations)
country: Multi-journal / global (18 leading business and management journals, including JIBS)
units: Peer-reviewed journal articles, classified by SDG and aggregated by journal and decade
n_sample: 47,197 articles (from 53,459 retrieved after merging two independent collections and cleaning)
authors: Boroomand, Farzam; Tsui, Anne S.; Djabbarov, Ibrat; van Witteloostuijn, Arjen; Mijnhardt, Wilfred
theories: Grand challenges in management research (George et al., 2016, 2024); Responsible Research in Business and Management (RRBM) movement (Co-founders of RRBM, 2017, 2020); UN Sustainable Development Goals as a categorizing framework (United Nations, 2016, 2023a, b)
keywords: Artificial intelligence; Large language models; Responsible research; Stakeholders; Sustainable Development Goals; Wellbeing
research_question: To what extent has the increasing awareness of the need to refocus business and management research on society's "grand challenges" actually changed what is published in leading journals, and specifically how does the Journal of International Business Studies compare to other premier journals in publishing SDG-related research over 1970–2024?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: The 17 UN SDGs serve as an "analytical anchor" — a comprehensive, well-established yardstick — for categorizing five-plus decades of business and management scholarship by its relevance to societal wellbeing.
- Key constructs: the "SDG ratio" (share of SDG-related articles in a year, decade, or journal); SDGs 8, 9, 16, and 17 framed as cross-cutting "enabler" goals; complementarities versus trade-offs across goals.
- Method as mechanism: an ensemble of four AI models with a majority-voting rule converts ~50,000 abstracts into reliable SDG classifications that no human coding effort could feasibly produce (802,349 article-by-SDG assessments).
- Boundary conditions: a deliberately conservative 7/10 threshold and 3-of-4 consensus rule set a high bar, potentially underestimating coverage; several environmental and social SDGs fall outside the natural domain of business research.

The paper documents an accelerating but uneven engagement: the SDG ratio more than tripled from ~8% (pre-1992) to ~31% (2015–2024), tracking the timeline of UN sustainability agendas (Development Decades, Agenda 21, MDGs, Agenda 2030), yet remains heavily concentrated in economically oriented goals (SDGs 8, 9, 16 = 78% of aligned articles). JIBS ranks among the leaders in total SDG coverage (~24% of its articles) and in treating SDGs 8 and 9 as enablers of other goals.
theoretical_contribution: By longitudinally mapping the evolution of SDG-related research across 18 flagship journals, the essay contributes to ongoing discussions about the purpose of business and management scholarship and its role in advancing societal wellbeing by addressing systemic grand challenges. It positions the flagship journals as legitimating venues able to push wellbeing topics from the "periphery" to the "mainstream," and corroborates prior findings (Walsh et al., 2003; Tsui & Jia, 2013, 2024) that welfare-oriented research has hovered around 20% historically with a modest recent increase.
practical_implication: IB research, given its intrinsically cross-disciplinary and international nature and its focus on internationally operating firms, is argued to be especially well-positioned to lead cross-goal inquiry. The authors urge scholars and editors to broaden attention to under-studied SDGs (inequality, health and wellbeing, responsible consumption/production), to model inter-goal complementarities and trade-offs, and to pursue geographically nuanced, interdisciplinary research designs that translate into education, managerial practice, and policy.
limitations: SDG research in the 18 journals is sharply skewed toward SDGs 8, 9, and 16 (over 78% of aligned articles), with environmental, social-inclusion, and gender goals receiving little attention. Examination of the enabling dynamics among SDGs 8, 9, 16, and 17 is very limited (only 646 unique articles, ~1.4%, show co-occurrence). The conservative 7/10 cutoff and broad goal definitions (e.g., SDG 8) may both over- and under-state genuine engagement with stakeholder wellbeing beyond firm performance, and the analysis is restricted to a US-biased set of top journals with JIBS as the sole IB outlet.
future_research: Future work should explore the under-studied SDGs and theorize their relevance for international firms; map both synergies and trade-offs among goals (e.g., desalination's water–marine tension, cap-and-trade's climate–toxics tension); conduct interdisciplinary, cross-goal "cascade" studies of how economic growth, innovation, institutional quality, and partnerships spill into other SDGs; and pursue comparative work across developed and emerging markets, North and South, and subnational regions, including a finer-grained analysis of a wider set of IB journals.
abstract: The United Nations’ 17 Sustainable Development Goals provide a framework for categorizing research that addresses global challenges and advances societal wellbeing. Using multiple large language models, this study analyzed 47,197 articles published in 18 business and management journals, including the Journal of International Business Studies, representing five business and management disciplines over 55 years (1970–2024). The objective was to assess the extent to which scholars, including those in IB, are tackling issues related to grand challenges as captured by the United Nation’s 17 Sustainable Development Goals. Findings show that among the 47,197 articles, 17.4% relate to one or more SDGs. This percentage increased over time, from 8% before the introduction of UN Agenda 21 in 1992 to 31% after the launch of UN Agenda 2030 in 2015. SDGs 8 (Decent Work and Economic Growth), 9 (Industry, Innovation, and Infrastructure), and 16 (Peace, Justice, and Strong Institutions) accounted for 78% of the SDG-aligned articles. The JIBS is among the leading journals contributing to these SDGs. We offer suggestions on how IB research can contribute to societal wellbeing by paying more attention to complementarities among goals and exploration of trade-offs where progress on one goal may impede advancement on another.
apa_citation: Boroomand, F., Tsui, A. S., Djabbarov, I., van Witteloostuijn, A., & Mijnhardt, W. (2026). title: Sustainability-Oriented Institutions and the Success of Green Reward-Based Crowdfunding Campaigns
methods: Replication brief; Random Forest machine-learning classifier to label campaigns green/non-green, then Logit models of campaign success with clustered standard errors (geographical level) and category/year/country fixed effects; post-hoc moderation, mediation (KHB and Buis decompositions), and robustness checks on U.S. campaigns.
industry: Reward-based crowdfunding campaigns on Kickstarter (cross-category)
country: European countries (main analysis); U.S. campaigns in additional analyses
units: Crowdfunding campaigns
n_sample: 8,912 European Kickstarter campaigns (main); 46,756 U.S. campaigns in additional analyses
authors: Butticè, Vincenzo; Colombo, Massimo G.; Orsenigo, Carlotta
theories: crowding-out / adverse selection in entrepreneurial finance; institutional theory (North, 1990; W. R. Scott, 2005)
keywords: crowdfunding; green ventures; institutions; kickstarter; random forest
research_question: How does the environmental orientation of country-level institutions moderate the relationship between a crowdfunding campaign's greenness and its success, and can this institutional heterogeneity explain the mixed findings in prior research on green reward-based crowdfunding?
mechanism_summary: Country-level environmental institutions exert two opposing forces on green campaign success. Stronger institutions embed green ventures in a knowledge-rich, legitimate ecosystem (proxied by green patents) that should raise perceived credibility, but they also expand alternative public and private funding (proxied by green VC), leading prospective backers to perceive their individual contributions as less needed. This crowding-out effect dominates, so green campaigns are less likely to succeed—and the gap widens—in high-EPI countries, operating principally through reduced engagement of domestic backers.
theoretical_contribution: The paper addresses an empirical puzzle in the green-crowdfunding literature by showing that overlooked cross-country institutional heterogeneity, and a related omitted-variable bias around information disclosure, can account for divergent prior results. It contributes to work on institutions and entrepreneurial finance by uncovering an unintended detrimental consequence: strong institutional support for sustainability can crowd out bottom-up crowdfunding participation, implying crowdfunding is more effective when it fills a perceived institutional void.
practical_implication: Green entrepreneurs in countries with well-developed institutional ecosystems should carefully assess whether crowdfunding is the most effective financing channel, since backers there may be less inclined to contribute. Platforms could emphasize funding gaps and the additive value of backer participation, and policymakers should promote crowdfunding as a green-finance tool especially in countries with less developed green ecosystems where contributions are perceived as more essential.
limitations: The analysis is correlational and does not establish causality between campaign greenness and success. The data are limited to reward-based campaigns on Kickstarter, so generalizability to other platforms and crowdfunding types (e.g., equity crowdfunding) is uncertain. The sample covers only Europe and the United States, leaving contexts with lower institutional support for sustainability untested.
future_research: Future studies could adopt experimental or other identification strategies to establish causality. Extending the analysis to other platforms and crowdfunding types would enhance generalizability. Examining contexts with weaker institutional support for sustainability would test whether the findings hold more broadly.
abstract: Previous research has produced mixed evidence on the success of green crowdfunding campaigns. We investigate whether country-level environmental institutions help explain these inconsistencies by moderating backers’ responses. While such institutions may enhance campaign legitimacy, they can also reduce the perceived urgency to contribute by offering alternative support channels. Analyzing a large Kickstarter dataset with machine learning, we find that green campaigns are less likely to succeed, particularly in countries with stronger environmental institutions. Post-hoc analyses point to a crowding-out effect of institutional support, which dampens individual backing, and suggest that omitted variable bias may partly account for prior conflicting results.
apa_citation: Butticè, V., Colombo, M. G., & Orsenigo, C. (2026). Sustainability-oriented institutions and the success of green reward-based crowdfunding campaigns. *Entrepreneurship Theory and Practice*. https://doi.org/10.1177/10422587261415928

title: The politics of impact: How political ideology shapes perceptions of the environmental impact of individual actions
methods: Seven preregistered focal studies (plus supplementary follow-ups): correlational, field, and between-subjects experimental designs with online panels (MTurk, CloudResearch Connect, Prolific) and an in-person mall field study. Analyses use OLS and logistic regression, one-way ANOVA, and PROCESS mediation/moderation/serial-mediation models (Hayes, 2017; Models 1, 4, 6, 8) with 5000 bootstrap resamples.
industry: General consumers (no specific industry; sustainable consumption contexts)
country: United States (Studies 1A-3, 4A) and UK/international Prolific panels (Studies 4B, 5)
time_period: Data collection circa 2023-2025 (manuscript received 14 November 2023; accepted 30 November 2025); specific collection windows not reported except a 3-day mall field study (Study 1B)
units: Individual consumers / survey participants
n_sample: Seven focal studies; per-study completed N ranges from 107 (Study 1B field) to 3023 (Study 4B recruitment), e.g., 402 (1A), 401 (2), 396 (3), 797 (4A), 599 (5)
authors: Cakanlar, Aylin; White, Katherine; Trudel, Remi
theories: availability heuristic (Tversky & Kahneman, 1973); informational vs. normative social influence (Deutsch & Gerard, 1955; Kelman, 1958); perceived impact / response efficacy (Cojuharenco et al., 2016; Bandura, 1977)
keywords: perceived impact; perceived prevalence; political ideology; sustainable behavior
research_question: Do conservatives and liberals construe the environmental impact of identical sustainable behaviors differently, and if so, what mechanism explains this ideological gap and how can it be attenuated? The paper proposes that perceived prevalence of the behavior within one's political ingroup drives ideological differences in perceived impact and, in turn, sustainable behavior.
mechanism_summary: Because objectively estimating environmental impact is difficult, consumers rely on readily available social cues—chiefly the perceived prevalence of a behavior within their ingroup—to infer how impactful it is (an availability-heuristic logic). Conservatives observe fewer ingroup members engaging in sustainable behaviors, infer lower prevalence, and therefore perceive lower impact, which depresses engagement. The effect reflects informational (not merely normative) influence: low ingroup prevalence is internalized as valid information about a behavior's true impact. Attenuating the prevalence gap (health-domain framing, ingroup-prevalence messaging) or bypassing it (explicit impact information) eliminates the ideological difference, and the prevalence-to-impact pathway outperforms alternatives such as collective efficacy, system justification, fair-market ideology, and normative conformity.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces perceived environmental impact as a distinct, ideology-sensitive driver of sustainable intentions that operates over and above climate-change beliefs, moral values, and personality traits. It identifies a novel perceived-prevalence-to-impact pathway grounded in informational social influence (Deutsch & Gerard, 1955; Kelman, 1958), showing that conservatives use the lower prevalence of sustainable behavior among their ingroup as diagnostic information about impact. It thereby extends literatures on political ideology, accuracy of environmental perceptions, and social influence by distinguishing consumer segments rather than treating perceptions as uniform.
practical_implication: Policymakers and marketers can raise conservatives' engagement by (a) reframing behaviors into non-environmental domains where they appear more prevalent among the ingroup (e.g., health), (b) using messaging that highlights ingroup prevalence of sustainable actions, and (c) communicating the positive environmental impact of actions explicitly. Value-aligned appeals alone are insufficient; messages must also make impact salient.
limitations: Liberals' impact estimates happened to align closely with actual impact for the studied behaviors, but the authors caution they would be surprised if liberals are consistently accurate, since both groups mis-estimated certain behaviors (e.g., air travel, recycling). Multiple factors plausibly contribute to ideological differences, and the studies rule out several—but not all—alternative explanations; rationalization and other constructs may still play some role.
future_research: Future work could examine how perceived prevalence varies across behavioral domains and replicate the effect in other low-ingroup-prevalence contexts such as vaccinations. Researchers could study how prevalence messaging interacts with internal beliefs and values where it may provoke psychological reactance (e.g., free-speech, political-expression domains), and how combining value-based appeals with impact-focused messaging affects engagement across ideological groups.
abstract: of that impact are subjective and systematically shaped by political ideology. Seven
