title: European regions transitioning to green markets: the role of related capabilities and public procurement policies
methods: Linear Probability Model (entry models) estimating the probability of regional entry into new green market specializations, with region, Nice class, and time fixed effects; standardized lagged predictors; robustness checks include persistence models, alternative RMA thresholds, neighbor spillover controls, logit specifications with average marginal effects, and additional controls.
industry: Green markets (green trademark classes) and green technologies (green patents), measured across all Nice market classes and CPC technology classes
country: 287 NUTS-2 regions across 27 EU countries plus EEA states (Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway), Switzerland, and the UK
units: Region-time-market (NUTS-2 region x green Nice subclass x 5-year time-window) observations
n_sample: 108,938 observations for the green market entry models
authors: Castaldi, Carolina; Abbasiharofteh, Milad; Petralia, Sergio
theories: evolutionary economic geography; geography of transitions; principle of relatedness (Hidalgo et al., 2018)
keywords: Green markets; Patents; Public procurement; Regions; Relatedness; Sustainability; Trademarks
research_question: How and whether do European regions transition into new green market specializations, and what roles do prior related regional capabilities (path-dependence) and green public procurement (path-creation) play in shaping these transitions?
mechanism_summary: The paper integrates evolutionary economic geography (the principle of relatedness) with the geography of transitions to argue that new green market specializations emerge along two logics: a path-dependent capability-development process, in which regions branch into green markets that are related to their existing market and technological capabilities, and a path-creation process, in which deliberate green public procurement articulates regional demand and legitimizes green alternatives during early market formation. Trademark-based metrics capture downstream market specializations while patents capture upstream technological relatedness, so cross-relatedness captures synergies between the two. Empirically, cross-relatedness shows the strongest association with green market entry, followed by market relatedness and then green public procurement.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the evolutionary economic geography approach to environmental innovation beyond technological relatedness by incorporating market relatedness and tech-market cross-relatedness as drivers of regional green market (not just technology) specialization. It contributes a systematic, quantitative comparative analysis to the largely qualitative geography-of-transitions literature, and demonstrates trademark data as a methodological device for capturing green market formation and its linkages to technologies at the regional level.
practical_implication: Regional and EU policymakers should treat market leadership, not only technological leadership, as a target of sustainability and competitiveness policy, recognizing that some regions can lead in green markets without leading in green technologies. Green public procurement is a viable demand-pull lever for path creation, and smart specialization policies (with an added sustainability "S4") can combine "smart" and "sustainable" path development when regions intelligently leverage related existing capabilities.
limitations: The analysis cannot fully control for endogeneity, so the evidence captures associations rather than identified causal effects, and quasi-experimental designs would be needed for cleaner identification of policy effects. Green procurement contracts were selected via pre-defined project tags, potentially missing relevant unlabeled procurements, and the study shares the usual caveat that patents and trademarks do not capture all technological and market activity.
future_research: Future work could link green regional capabilities to the underlying availability of green skills and skill mismatch, and broaden the analysis beyond public procurement to a wider range of policy interventions and mixes. Researchers could also move beyond average patterns to examine how the identified drivers differ across region types (e.g., peripheral vs. core, urban vs. rural) and refine policy classification using deductive coding assisted by large language models.
abstract: To do so, we investigate two key sets of factors. First, we consider the evolutionary capability development process whereby new specializations emerge from existing related regional capabilities, in a path-dependent way. Second, we account for green public procurement initiatives to capture path-creation efforts in the form of deliberate regional policy directed towards green market formation.
apa_citation: Castaldi, C., Abbasiharofteh, M., & Petralia, S. (2026). European regions transitioning to green markets: the role of related capabilities and public procurement policies. *Research Policy*, 55(2), 105374. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.respol.2025.105374

title: Can Purpose and Professional Advancement Coexist? Review of Make It Meaningful: How to Find Purpose in Life and Work by Debbie Haski-Leventhal
methods: Book review (critical evaluation). Chavan reviews Haski-Leventhal's Make It Meaningful, summarizing its argument that purpose is an ethical imperative, situating the book within business ethics scholarship on meaningful work, and appraising its integrative memoir-plus-research approach and the 'Purpose Compass' framework.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Chavan, Meena
theories: business ethics scholarship on meaningful work; ikigai; stakeholder theory (Freeman et al., 2007)
keywords: Purpose Compass; business ethics; ethical ambition; meaningful work; purpose
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Purpose is framed not merely as a route to personal flourishing but as a moral imperative for ethical engagement at work, such that organizations bear a responsibility to enable employees to experience meaningful work.
- Key construct ("ethical ambition" / purpose-in-action): Career advancement and leadership need not require sacrificing one's core purpose or values but can be an expression of them — reframed as especially empowering for women uneasy about self-promotion.
- Practical framework (the "Purpose Compass"): An ikigai-inspired model organized around four questions — what do I love, what am I good at, what does the world need, and what can I be paid for — operationalized through reflective exercises.
- Contextual lens: Meaningful work as fundamentally relational, with particular resonance for female academics facing gendered expectations, structural barriers, and tensions between caregiving values and traditional metrics of success.

The review traces how Haski-Leventhal grounds the importance of purpose in ethical rather than purely instrumental terms — connecting individual meaning to stakeholder responsibility, conscious capitalism, and the avoidance of "moral disjunction" between one's values and one's role. Chavan reads the book as bridging scholarly research on meaningful work and accessible personal-development guidance, arguing that its central normative claim — individuals have a duty to seek purpose and organizations a duty to support it — is both inspirational and normatively robust.
theoretical_contribution: Not reported in paper
practical_implication: The review distills the book's call to action for leaders to design work environments that honor the human search for meaning — articulating clear organizational values, connecting employees' roles to a broader mission, and fostering a culture of respect and growth — and encourages professionals, especially women in academia, to reframe advancement as a platform to amplify impact rather than a compromise of their ethos.
limitations: Chavan notes that the book is light on theoretical nuance and systematic empirical detail, leaning on moving anecdotes where a researcher might crave more data, and that the Purpose Compass simplifies complex constructs for a general audience — characterizing this as a deliberate trade-off of depth for reach rather than a flaw.
future_research: Not reported in paper
abstract: meaningful work is both a personal quest and a collective
apa_citation: Chavan, M. (2026). Can purpose and professional advancement coexist? Review of Make It Meaningful: How to find purpose in life and work by Debbie Haski-Leventhal. *Journal of Business Ethics*, 206(1), 207-212. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06222-3

title: Corporate Social Responsibility and Employee Information Sharing
methods: OLS regressions of absolute abnormal returns (Fama–French three-factor plus momentum) on lagged MSCI ESG social/environmental scores, with firm and quarter fixed effects, firm-clustered SEs; Heckman selection correction, propensity score matching, structural equation (mediation) channel analyses, and textual analysis of review comments.
industry: U.S. publicly-listed firms across Fama–French 12 industries (e.g., Business Equipment, Finance, Shops)
country: United States (with an alternative validation sample of Chinese publicly-listed firms)
units: Glassdoor employee reviews (firm-quarter linked)
n_sample: 30,675 reviews
authors: Chen, Yifei; Palmon, Dan; Shan, Chenyu; Zhang, Qi
theories: agency-cost and risk-management perspectives of CSR (McWilliams et al., 2006); socially responsible culture / corporate culture (Gao et al., 2014; Hoi et al., 2013; Kreps, 1990); stakeholder theory (Donaldson & Preston, 1995)
keywords: Corporate culture; Corporate social responsibility (CSR); Glassdoor employee reviews; Information environment
research_question: Is a firm's CSR performance associated with the informativeness of information shared by its rank-and-file employees, and if so, through what mechanisms (information volume and perceived credibility) does a socially responsible culture shape that informativeness?
mechanism_summary: Strong CSR performance is theorized to proxy for a socially responsible corporate culture marked by stakeholder concern, trust, honesty, and a moral duty to "do the right thing." Such a culture deepens employee insight, fosters psychological safety and identification, reduces managerial pressure to distort disclosures, and strengthens employees' willingness to voice concerns despite retaliation risk. These forces raise both the volume and the credibility of employee-shared information, producing stronger and more accurate stock-price reactions to reviews of CSR firms relative to non-CSR firms.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the CSR–information-environment literature from top-management disclosures to bottom-up, crowdsourced, employee-generated information, identifying socially responsible corporate culture as an underexplored institutional factor shaping the value of employee disclosures. It also uncovers an unexplored mechanism linking CSR to firm value—employee-shared information that elicits greater stock-price responses—and contributes to research on the determinants of rank-and-file employee disclosure informativeness.
practical_implication: Managers should recognize an unintended economic benefit of a socially responsible culture: it enhances the informativeness of employee communications and reduces information asymmetry with the capital market. Investors should closely monitor employee voices for CSR firms on social-media platforms as early signals of firm fundamentals and future operating outcomes that may not surface through formal financial disclosures or managerial communications.
limitations: CSR represents only one element of a much larger set of determinants of employee-disclosure informativeness; factors such as employee-specific characteristics, workplace dynamics, institutional conditions, and broader industry and regulatory environments also matter, so the analysis captures only part of the picture. The reliance on voluntarily posted reviews is a limitation inherent in voluntary-disclosure research.
future_research: Future research could identify additional determinants of review informativeness to provide a more comprehensive understanding of the mechanisms through which employee voices contribute to information transparency and market efficiency.
abstract: This study investigates the association between corporate social responsibility (CSR) and the informativeness of employeeshared information. Focusing on employee reviews for U.S. publicly-listed firms from Glassdoor.com, we find a significantly positive association between firms’ CSR performance and the magnitude of market reactions to these reviews. The results hold across various robustness tests. Channel analyses demonstrate that this positive association is due to reviews for firms with stronger CSR performance providing a greater volume of information and being perceived as more credible. Moreover, employee ratings for firms with stronger CSR performance exert greater influence on stock prices, are less likely to be overly optimistic, decline more sharply following irresponsible social practices, and better predict future operating performance. Review sentiment for these firms also exerts a stronger impact on stock prices. Textual analyses of employee reviews further indicate that a socially responsible culture is considered important in firms with strong CSR performance and is positively associated with the magnitude of market reactions to those reviews. The main results hold using an alternative sample of employee reviews for Chinese publicly-listed firms. Collectively, these findings suggest that strong CSR performance reflects a socially responsible culture that fosters more informative disclosures by employees.
apa_citation: Chen, Y., Palmon, D., Shan, C., & Zhang, Q. (2026). Corporate social responsibility and employee information sharing. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-026-06266-z

title: Marginalized Stakeholder Theory: A Normative Foundation for Decolonization
methods: Conceptual / normative theory development. The author builds 'marginalized stakeholder theory' (MST) by combining postcolonial theory with stakeholder management, and reconceptualizes three engagement approaches (proactive, reactive, defensive) using illustrative cases (e.g., the Treatment Action Campaign in South Africa, Tata in Singur, Asia Energy's Phulbari mine, Shell in Nigeria).
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Chowdhury, Rashedur
theories: integrative social contracts theory (Donaldson & Dunfee, 1994); marginalized stakeholder theory (proposed); postcolonial / decolonial theory (Spivak, 1988, 1994; Mignolo, 2009; Said, 1978); stakeholder theory (Freeman, 1994; Mitchell et al., 1997; Donaldson & Preston, 1995)
keywords: Business ethics; Corporate responsibility; Decolonization; Epistemic justice; Marginalized stakeholder theory; Stakeholder theory
research_question: How can firms engage with marginalized stakeholders (MSs) in a normatively decolonial manner that transforms corporate actions toward these stakeholders from the outset, rather than treating them instrumentally or as peripheral, vulnerable parties?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Traditional stakeholder theory privileges powerful stakeholders (via salience attributes of power, legitimacy, urgency) and is built on normative foundations entangled with colonial legacies; this perpetuates "epistemic obfuscation" that overlooks or co-opts work on marginalized stakeholders.
- Key constructs: marginalized stakeholders (MSs); epistemic obfuscation and the "meta-fallacy"; moral courage, moral imagination, and "perfect/imperfect obligations"; decolonial social contracts; third-party (NGO/state) interference and misrepresentation.
- Reconceptualized engagement approaches: (1) **proactive** — MS capacity auditing and "proactive deep dialog" before firm activities begin; (2) **reactive** — "reactive deep dialog" and compromise once grievances or political opportunities emerge; (3) **defensive** — protecting MS moral and representational rights under state repression, exiting non-violently rather than supporting coercion.
- Boundary conditions: contexts vary (democratic/autocratic, postcolonial/neocolonial); marginalization can be studied beyond postcolonial settings, but MST centers colonial legacies and epistemic injustice and excludes "fringe" actors who espouse regressive worldviews.

The central process is normative rather than causal: by recognizing colonial legacies and securing MS consent and self-representation from the outset, firms revise the underlying normative assumptions of instrumental engagement, redistribute profits equitably, and reduce third-party misrepresentation, thereby enabling structural and agentic transformation toward decolonization.
theoretical_contribution: The paper proposes marginalized stakeholder theory as a normative foundation for decolonizing corporate activity, making three contributions: (1) it shows how traditional stakeholder management perpetuates epistemic obfuscation by incentivizing attention to powerful stakeholders at the expense of MSs, and argues firms need moral courage and moral imagination to break free of (neo)colonial instrumental logics; (2) it repositions MSs as a central lens (a "dialectical" framework) for decolonizing firm-level activities and re-reading misrepresentations; and (3) it calls for breaking free of the (neo)colonial narrative of profit maximization disguised as instrumentality, reciprocity, and trust, urging scholars to revisit, redefine, or abandon concepts such as CSR, ESG, and base-of-the-pyramid.
practical_implication: Firms should conduct "MS capacity auditing," establish robust social contracts and dignity criteria with marginalized stakeholders, and obtain freely given (non-coerced) consent before pursuing activities that affect them. They should reduce reliance on NGOs and government intermediaries who may misrepresent MSs, be willing to accept profit trade-offs (including leaving sites or forgoing extraction), and treat not-exploiting as a legitimate way of creating collective value and wellbeing.
limitations: The author notes the viability of MST is itself constrained by entrenched neocolonial logics of profit maximization and by education systems and business schools that perpetuate epistemic obfuscation. The illustrative contexts are not representative of all political or economic systems, and MST predominantly focuses on marginalization rooted in colonial legacies and epistemic injustice rather than organizational adjustment, requiring clear scope boundaries.
future_research: The author calls for further disruption of epistemic injustice so that more scholars recognize epistemic obfuscation in their own work, and for exploring additional engagement situations and approaches beyond the three reconceptualized here. He also invites work on how marginalization can be studied across diverse (not only postcolonial) contexts while maintaining MST's premises, and cautions scholars to exercise sustained care when evaluating claims of marginalization.
abstract: Whether they address issues of marginalization or study organizations in general, traditional management and business ethics scholars predominantly focus on firms' engagement with powerful stakeholders. This represents a severe problem globally, manifesting acutely in postcolonial contexts, as firms' engagement with marginalized stakeholders is either neglected or ignored. This neglect also highlights the problem of epistemic obfuscation in management and business ethics scholarship, through which existing works on decolonization and marginalized stakeholders are either overlooked or co-opted by the mainstream. To advance decolonial organizational scholarship and counter epistemic obfuscation, I propose marginalized stakeholder theory. This theory establishes new pathways for firms to revise their normative principles and decolonize their corporate activities. It enables firms to overcome colonial legacies, reject neocolonial logics embedded in instrumental approaches, and develop decolonial engagement with marginalized stakeholders. To this end, I reconceptualize three engagement approaches—proactive, reactive, and defensive—which are generally used by firms for instrumental purposes. I argue that this reconceptualization provides a starting point for the necessary structural and agentic transformation toward decolonization in epistemic and organizational practices.
apa_citation: Chowdhury, R. (2026). Marginalized stakeholder theory: A normative foundation for decolonization. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-026-06269-w

title: Careless or Clueless? the Lost Promise of Facebook
methods: Book review essay engaging Wynn-Williams' memoir Careless People as a case study in business ethics and leadership
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Ciulla, J. B.
theories: complicity vs. standard theory of whistleblowing (Davis, 1996); corporate citizenship (Matten et al., 2003); ethical leadership (Ciulla, 2020); political corporate social responsibility (Scherer et al., 2016)
keywords: Facebook; corporate citizenship; ethical leadership; free speech; political CSR; social media companies; technology ethics; whistleblowing
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary: Not reported in paper
theoretical_contribution: Reviewing Sarah Wynn-Williams' memoir as a business-ethics case study, Ciulla argues the book's most significant contribution is to the literature on political corporate social responsibility (PCSR) in the technology sector: drawing on Scherer et al. (2016) and Matten et al. (2003), she reframes Facebook/Meta as a "political actor" that began as a public good and then positioned itself at the center of political activity, jettisoning its own Community Guidelines when ethical standards conflicted with profits. The review also advances theory on ethical leadership (Ciulla, 2020), contending that technical expertise without ethical competence produces leaders like Zuckerberg who appear "more clueless than careless," and on whistleblowing, classifying Wynn-Williams under Davis's (1996) complicity theory (reporting on harms she participated in) versus Haugen's standard-theory whistleblowing.
practical_implication: The review warns against allowing technology companies to design and enforce the ethical standards for their own products, since firms "excel at designing ethical safeguards, but struggle to adhere to them when they conflict with their own interests and profits" — evidenced by Microsoft, X, and Google downsizing or eliminating ethics teams. Ciulla underscores the importance of political CSR and of corporations collaborating with governments to establish responsible guidelines, while noting that all-or-nothing regulation (e.g., the U.S. State Department's stance that Facebook should accept being shut down rather than censor on Russia's behalf) is a hard pill for firms and politicians to swallow.
limitations: The review notes the obvious limitation of the memoir under review: it offers only one woman's (Wynn-Williams') perspective on Facebook and Zuckerberg, a bias the author concedes by calling it a memoir, and one possibly colored by her having been fired after filing a sexual-harassment complaint against Joel Kaplan. Ciulla also observes that Wynn-Williams discloses her harassment complaint only briefly and at the end, raising questions about non-disclosure agreements and whether a settlement shaped the account.
future_research: Ciulla frames the governance of ethical standards for social media and AI — and the question of who should develop those standards, firms or governments — as "one of the most pressing issues in business ethics today," leaving open whether government regulation would do a better job. She points to the unresolved challenge of reconciling the conflict between user safety and profits in an industry "operating in a regulatory wasteland, where rules are made and broken as they go along."
abstract: serves as a cautionary tale about the moral challenges that social media companies face in a complex and rapidly evolving environment and the importance of political CSR.
apa_citation: Ciulla, J. B. (2026). Careless or clueless? The lost promise of Facebook. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-026-06263-2

title: Innovation under dual policies: The impact of R&D subsidies and emissions trading on green patenting in Sweden
methods: Staggered difference-in-differences (Callaway & Sant'Anna, 2021) with augmented inverse-probability-weighting ATET estimation, combined with propensity-score / nearest-neighbor matching to address non-random subsidy and EU ETS participation; firm-level panel.
industry: Industrial firms in manufacturing, energy, waste and water sectors (Swedish Industrial Classification SNI codes 05-39), incl. pulp/paper, petroleum refining, chemicals, metals, machinery and transport
time_period: Firm-level data 2000-2018; subsidy data 2007-2018; EU ETS coverage from 2005
units: Firm-year observations across four policy-mix groups (ETS-and-subsidy, subsidy-only, ETS-only, neither)
n_sample: 806,668 firm-year observations (incl. 294 EU ETS firms)
authors: Coria, Jessica; Jaraitė, Jūratė
theories: induced/directed technical change (Acemoglu et al., 2012); market failures in green R&D; policy mix / policy instrument interaction
keywords: EU ETS; Emissions trading; Environmental policy; Green patents; Innovation subsidies; Micro data; Sweden
research_question: Do public innovation subsidies and the EU Emissions Trading Scheme (EU ETS) interact synergistically to enhance green innovation at the firm level — i.e., do firms subject to both policies patent more green technologies than those affected by only one — and what are the separate causal effects of each policy on Swedish industrial firms' green patenting?
mechanism_summary: The paper theorizes that combining innovation subsidies with emissions pricing should address two joint market failures (insufficient private R&D incentives plus weak regulatory demand), so a policy mix could direct technical change toward green innovation at lower cost than either instrument alone. A firm-level synergy, however, requires that the *same* firms respond to both policies. Empirically the authors find no synergistic effect: high-emitting ETS firms — often locked into carbon-intensive, path-dependent technologies — are largely not the firms receiving innovation subsidies or producing green patents, so the two instruments act on different firm populations. Subsidies (especially large ones, and for larger firms) are independently and positively associated with the quantity, quality, and scope of green patenting, while EU ETS participation shows no measurable effect on patentable green innovation.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the policy-mix literature by isolating, at the firm level, the interaction between a specific emissions-pricing instrument (the EU ETS) and direct R&D subsidies — a test prior aggregate or cross-country work could not perform. It shows that firm-level complementarity between emissions pricing and innovation subsidies cannot be assumed from aggregate complementarity: when the policies' target populations barely overlap, no synergy emerges, and demand-side environmental regulation must be paired with strong supply-side financial support to drive green technological development.
practical_implication: Because emissions pricing alone did not stimulate patentable green innovation in Sweden, policymakers should not rely on carbon markets to generate green R&D; substantial subsidy support remains essential to overcome market failures. The absence of firm-level synergy suggests prioritizing broad measures such as technology transfer and adoption incentives for high-polluting firms, while channeling sizable subsidies — which disproportionately benefit larger firms with greater absorptive and administrative capacity — to firms positioned to develop new green technologies.
limitations: Due to data limitations the authors cannot control for firms' energy use or actual CO2 emissions, so they measure EU ETS participation but not firm-specific compliance costs, limiting assessment of whether the emissions-pricing/subsidy interaction differs for high- versus low-compliance-cost firms. They also cannot assess heterogeneity in EU ETS effects. Outcomes rely solely on patents, which capture only part of firms' innovation responses, and cumulative DID outcomes must be interpreted cautiously given selection and attribution concerns.
future_research: Future work should revisit the interaction in more recent years as free emissions-allowance allocation is phased out and compliance costs rise. Researchers could use alternative innovation indicators (e.g., R&D expenditures) beyond patents, examine both green and non-green patents to capture bidirectional spillovers, and test whether firms under more stringent environmental regulation generate more green patents or instead redirect innovation — potentially shaped by state dependency — toward other technological domains.
abstract: are large. These findings imply that, while environmental policies like the EU ETS might create demand for green innovation, substantial subsidy support is essential to overcome market failures and promote the

title: Political costs and strategic corporate communication
methods: Difference-in-differences-style OLS regression of county-level industry advertising spending on a lagged political-attention indicator, with industry-county and year fixed effects and standard errors clustered at the congressional-district level; identification exploits geographic variation in which politicians (committees) oversee an industry's hearings; cross-sectional interaction tests on hearing acrimony, testimony characteristics, political pressure, and citizen engagement.
industry: 99 Borrell industry classifications; most common in testimony are Manufacturing & Other, Computer-related Services, and Electric Power Utilities
time_period: 2015-2019 (advertising); congressional hearings collected 2014-2018 given the one-year lag
units: industry-county-year observations
n_sample: 1,489,450 industry-county-year observations (99 industries across 3,101 counties); 356 hearings with corporate testimony, 321 meeting the 3+ hearing threshold
authors: Cuny, Christine; Kim, Jungbae; Mehta, Mihir N.
theories: political cost hypothesis (Watts and Zimmerman, 1978)
keywords: Congress; Image building; Information; Political cost hypothesis
research_question: When subject to public scrutiny, do firms strategically use advertising to reduce expected political costs? Specifically, do scrutinized industries direct disproportionate advertising spending toward the electorates of the politicians who oversee the congressional hearings that generate that scrutiny?
mechanism_summary:
- Complements/substitutes: income-decreasing discretionary accruals (complement); lobbying expenditures (substitute)

Industries under expected political costs raise advertising in the electorates of overseeing politicians to ease constituents' concerns and relieve the pressure on those politicians to legislate harshly against the industry. Because only the subset of politicians on the relevant committees can impose costs, the incremental advertising in "strategically important electorates" (ComRep) over baseline electorates (NoComRep) isolates the strategic response from general reputation-building. The effect strengthens monotonically in the number of hearings and is concentrated where the political incentive to improve image is greatest (acrimonious hearings, CEO testimony, election years, senior members, engaged or media-exposed constituencies).
theoretical_contribution: The study provides some of the first evidence on the third Watts-and-Zimmerman (1978) channel for managing political costs — strategic communication via advertising — which had been largely undocumented relative to the well-studied lobbying and income-decreasing-accounting channels. It shows that the firm's choice among political-cost-management tools varies with the target audience, with advertising complementing income-decreasing accruals (both aimed at voters and politicians) while substituting for lobbying (aimed at politicians alone), and that advertising can curb rather than incite political activism.
practical_implication: The findings document a previously unexamined channel of corporate intervention in the political process, informing the ongoing policy debate about money and corporate influence in politics: firms can quietly redirect advertising toward an overseeing politician's constituents to shape the demand for legislation, a behavior that is harder to observe than lobbying or contributions yet economically meaningful (about $60,000 of incremental advertising per strategically important industry-county-year).
limitations: The authors cannot observe advertisement content, so they cannot directly verify that the advertising is intended to improve public image and must instead rely on cross-sectional variation in hearing and electoral characteristics. Selection remains a possible concern — politicians may choose to serve on committees relevant to economically important firms in their electorates — though results are robust to excluding counties/states tied to testifying firms' headquarters or operations and to controlling for a political-risk measure.
future_research: Examine the content of advertisements to better understand how and when firms use strategic advertising as a commitment mechanism. Investigate how firms choose among tools (e.g., earnings management, lobbying, advertising) and the trade-offs they face in response to congressional or regulatory scrutiny, and assess the efficacy of these efforts in limiting political costs, perhaps using single-industry settings.

title: When are Customer Boycotts Permissible?
methods: Conceptual / normative philosophical analysis; develops a new framework for boycott permissibility through argument, contrast with prior accounts (SCEC, just-war/liability), and illustrative cases.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Donaldson, Thomas
theories: all-things-considered ought (Horty, 2012; Mullins, 2020); permissibility / deontic logic (Scanlon, 2008; McNamara & Putte, 2022); threshold deontology (Kagan, 1989)
keywords: Boycotts; Consumer ethics; Consumer freedom
research_question: When is an individual's decision to engage in an ethical customer boycott—made by someone who believes the boycott is morally right—morally permissible (as distinct from objectively good or bad), and when, if ever, is the default commercial freedom to refrain from buying defeated?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: An "ethical customer boycott" should be assessed through *moral permissibility* (a required/permissible/impermissible triad), not through whether the boycott is objectively good or bad; conflating the two distorts the boycott debate.
- Key constructs: commercial freedom as a default right to refrain from exchange; the distinction between "objective ought" and permissibility; an "all-things-considered permission" that holds when no all-things-considered ought conflicts with the boycott.
- Boundary conditions (four reasons the impermissibility threshold is high): (1) any impermissibility claim must overcome the default presumption of commercial freedom; (2) impermissibility implies an unusual "hard duty" to refrain-from-refraining (i.e., to buy); (3) a boycotter's true intent is opaque and contested; (4) boycotts serve as nonviolent alternatives to violence and social reform.
- Decision rule: a boycott is impermissible "only when it is likely to cause catastrophic harm or demonstrates flagrant disregard for the human dignity of an impacted stakeholder."

The paper builds a normative framework grounded in the default permissibility of market exchange: because refusing to buy is an omission protected by commercial freedom, and because defeating that right would impose a rare "hard duty" to purchase, the threshold for declaring an individual boycott impermissible is extraordinarily high. Drawing on Scanlon's permissibility framing and Mullins/Horty's "all-things-considered ought," it argues that only catastrophic-harm or dignity-violating cases (e.g., the 1920s boycott of Jewish merchants) override the default right, so even objectively wrong boycotts are usually permissible.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reframes the ethics of consumer boycotts around a customer's *commercial freedom* and the deontic concept of permissibility, rather than around democratic values (the SCEC framework of Hussain and Berkey), intentional harm and just-war/liability logic (Betz), or absolutist libertarianism (Rothbard). It offers a "cosmopolitan" account that is neither democracy-centric nor Western-centric and that accommodates clean-hands, expressive, and direct boycotts excluded by prior frameworks, including boycotts arising in non-democratic settings.
practical_implication: The high impermissibility threshold has implications for lawmakers: laws that directly conflict with an individual's moral right to boycott amount to prohibiting morally permissible action, and indirect mechanisms such as loyalty oaths or anti-boycott certifications become morally suspect. Because boycotts function as nonviolent alternatives to conflict, caution is warranted when designing anti-boycott legislation.
limitations: The framework's scope is deliberately limited: it does not address whether any given boycott decision is objectively morally good or bad, only whether it is permissible. It also brackets boycotts undertaken by groups, corporations, influencers, and organizers, and does not attempt a full analysis of anti-boycott laws, which would require engaging jurisprudential and political-theory questions left outside the paper.
future_research: The paper points toward unresolved questions it sets aside: the moral status of boycotts by groups, corporations, and informal collectives whose distinct agency yields different rights and duties; the permissibility of influencer and organizer conduct (framed under free speech and assembly rather than commercial exchange); and a comprehensive moral and jurisprudential evaluation of anti-boycott laws.
abstract: This paper examines ethical customer boycotts, instances where individuals choose to boycott for ethical reasons. By focusing on the commercial freedom of the customer, it offers a new framework for distinguishing between permissible and impermissible boycotts. It concludes that individual ethical boycotts are almost never morally impermissible. The scope of the paper is limited. It does not attempt to answer the important question of when an individual boycott decision is objectively good or bad. Rather, it attempts to answer the prior question of when a decision to boycott—made by someone who believes their decision is morally right—is doing something that is morally permissible. In defending this position, the paper distinguishes between “objective ought” and moral permissibility. The paper argues that focusing on the right of a consumer to choose is the best way to approach the question, “May I boycott this product?” The approach shifts the emphasis in the boycott debate from prevailing frameworks based on democratic values, intentional harm, and libertarianism, toward a customer’s right to choose. It proposes a cosmopolitan interpretation that relies on the default right of market participants to refrain from commercial exchanges except under extraordinary circumstances. Among other benefits, this approach addresses gaps in alternative accounts that overlook a significant portion of customer boycott decision-making. The paper explains why the threshold for boycott impermissibility is very high: (1) because any claim of impermissibility must overcome the default presumption of freedom in commercial exchange; (2) because an unusual “hard duty” follows from a determination of boycott impermissibility; (3) because a boycotter’s true intent is difficult to discern; and (4) because boycotts serve as alternatives to violence. The account offered here implies that even decisions to boycott that are objectively wrong may be morally permissible. Finally, the paper shows why certain kinds of anti-boycott laws threaten an individual’s moral right to boycott.
apa_citation: Donaldson, T. (2026). When are customer boycotts permissible? *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-026-06261-4

title: The effect of extreme heat on economic growth: Evidence from Latin America
journal: Journal of Environmental Economics and Management
methods: Panel regressions on an unbalanced region-year panel (15 Latin American countries, 1970-2010) with region and year fixed effects; separate regressions for hot-day and heat-wave counts at varying intensity/duration thresholds, controlling for seasonal mean temperatures, monthly precipitation, and lagged GRP growth; standard errors clustered by region; back-of-the-envelope mid-century projections.
industry: Whole-economy gross regional product (agriculture examined as a channel)
country: 15 Latin American countries (Argentina, Bolivia, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, Ecuador, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, Mexico, Nicaragua, Panama, Peru, Uruguay, Venezuela)
units: Sub-national regions (region-year observations)
n_sample: 274 regions; 7,498 region-year observations in main specifications
authors: Dueñas, Juliana; Goytia, Alejandra; Hoffmann, Bridget
theories: Climate-economy / temperature-growth literature (Dell et al., 2012; Burke et al., 2015)
keywords: Economic growth; Extreme heat; Heat waves; Latin America
research_question: Does extreme heat affect economic growth at the sub-national level in Latin America beyond the indirect effect operating through higher seasonal mean temperatures, and how does that effect vary with the intensity and duration of heat?
mechanism_summary: Defining extreme heat relative to each region-month's moving historical distribution lets the measures capture unusually hot days for a given climate while accounting for acclimation to the season and local climate. By controlling for seasonal mean temperatures, the authors isolate the direct effect of extreme heat from its indirect effect through rising seasonal averages. Plausibly exogenous year-to-year, region-to-region variation in hot-day and heat-wave counts (net of region and year fixed effects) identifies the effect on GRP growth, with reduced agricultural productivity proposed as one transmission channel.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes two main contributions to the literature on the effects of extreme heat on aggregate economic activity. First, it shows that the negative effect of extreme heat on aggregate economic growth increases with both the intensity and duration of heat, using easy-to-interpret measures that allow comparisons across heat waves. Second, it provides evidence that the negative economic impacts of rising temperature will be driven primarily by extreme events rather than gradual increases in local mean temperatures, suggesting extreme heat is one channel behind documented non-linearities in temperature-growth relationships.
practical_implication: Because extreme heat affects growth beyond average seasonal temperatures and accounts for 34%–68% of the total projected mid-century temperature impact, integrated assessment models that focus on mean temperature underestimate the economic costs of climate change. Adapting economic activity to extreme heat is important but challenging, since heat waves are relatively infrequent, intense, and difficult to predict in advance.
limitations: The back-of-the-envelope mid-century projections must be interpreted with caution: they assume the impacts of extreme heat and seasonal mean temperatures on growth do not change over time or vary across geographies, and they ignore uncertainty about the estimated coefficients. The agricultural channel is established only as suggestive evidence via employment-share splits, and the heat-wave definitions used in the climate projections (Feron et al., 2019) differ slightly from those used in the historical analysis.
future_research: Not reported in paper
abstract: Extreme heat could account for 34 %–68 % of the total projected reduction in the annual economic growth rate at mid-century due to temperature change. Our results suggest that extreme heat is one potential channel for the documented non-linearity in the impacts of rising mean temperature.
apa_citation: Dueñas, J., Goytia, A., & Hoffmann, B. (2026). The effect of extreme heat on economic growth: Evidence from Latin America. *Journal of Environmental Economics and Management*, *135*, 103241. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jeem.2025.103241

title: Mandatory Carbon Disclosure and New Business Creation
methods: Difference-in-differences (differential-exposure design) on an industry-county-year panel; OLS with county, industry, and year fixed effects; binary and continuous treatment, triple-interaction cross-sectional tests, parallel-trends, pseudo-event, and randomization-inference falsification tests; standard errors clustered by state-industry.
industry: All NAICS two-digit industries (treated = GHGRP-covered industries such as manufacturing, mining, transportation; controls = arts/entertainment, retail, etc.), excluding financial, utility, and waste-management industries
time_period: 2009-2014 (three years before to three years after the 2012 GHGRP public disclosure)
units: Industry-county-year observations
n_sample: 275,293 industry-county-year observations
authors: Duguay, Raphael; Li, Chenchen; Zhang, Frank
theories: proprietary information / entry deterrence (Darrough & Stoughton, 1990); real effects of disclosure; stakeholder and public pressure
keywords: Disclosure; Entrepreneurship; Greenhouse gas; Real effects
research_question: How does mandatory carbon disclosure affect the birth of new businesses? The paper examines whether the U.S. Greenhouse Gas Reporting Program (GHGRP) stimulates new business formation in affected industries relative to unaffected controls, and through what channels.
mechanism_summary: Using a differential-exposure difference-in-differences design, the authors find business births increase roughly 3-4% in GHGRP-affected industries after the 2012 public release of emissions data. Two mechanisms operate: mandatory disclosure pressures incumbents (via stakeholder, investor, and public scrutiny) to curb production and reallocate toward innovation, eroding their competitive position and opening market space; and the granular proprietary data disclosed (production volumes, technical inputs, cost structures) help prospective entrants identify profitable opportunities. Entry effects are stronger where incumbents actually cut emissions, where firms face greater scrutiny, and where industries raised proprietary-cost objections.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends the real-effects-of-disclosure literature by shifting attention from incumbents to new business formation, documenting an unintended consequence of carbon-disclosure mandates: increased entry whose emissions may partially offset incumbents' reductions, implying that incumbent-only analyses overstate the net environmental benefit. It deepens understanding of the relation between incumbents' disclosure and new entry (extending work on financial-disclosure spillovers to GHG disclosure), and adds to the pollution-displacement literature by showing new entrants—not just incumbents—drive displacement, especially under exemption thresholds for small emitters.
practical_implication: As governments expand climate-related disclosure regimes, policymakers should account for secondary effects on market structure and entrepreneurial activity: emissions-reduction credit attributed to disclosure mandates may be overestimated when new (sub-threshold) entrants are ignored, and the proprietary content of mandated disclosures can reshape competition. Designing effective, balanced disclosure policy requires attention to entry incentives and threshold exemptions for small emitters.
limitations: The authors caution that evidence on the underlying channels should be interpreted as descriptive rather than causal, and that endogeneity cannot be fully ruled out because GHGRP implementation was not entirely exogenous. The analysis focuses on short-run frictions that create competitive openings and does not speak to longer-run efficiency gains from subsequent innovation. The industry-versus-local information comparison is acknowledged as conceptually and empirically difficult and should be read with caution.
future_research: Future work could investigate the extent to which specific GHGRP disclosure components (e.g., inputs to emission equations) impose proprietary costs and influence competition, and could examine the longer-run efficiency and innovation consequences of disclosure-induced entry that the short-run design does not capture.
abstract: This paper studies how mandatory greenhouse gas disclosure affects new business formation.
apa_citation: Duguay, R., Li, C., & Zhang, F. (2026). Mandatory carbon disclosure and new business creation. *Journal of Accounting and Economics*, 101864. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jacceco.2026.101864

title: Reframing Sustainable Consumption: Toward an Ethical Dilemma Perspective
methods: Conceptual study. Adapts the organizational triple bottom line (TBL) framework to the individual consumer level, deriving a typology of nine ethical-dilemma types through theoretical synthesis and illustrating each with concrete decision scenarios; no primary data collected.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Elgaaied-Gambier, Leila; Ladli, Yacine; Reniou, Fanny
theories: ethical dilemma perspective; framing theory (Goffman, 1974); triple bottom line (Elkington, 1997, 1998)
keywords: Decision-making process; Ethical dilemmas; Sustainable consumption; Triple bottom line
research_question: How can sustainable consumption be reconceptualized once consumers' self-interest and personal well-being are admitted as a legitimate, co-equal concern alongside social and environmental concerns, and what ethical dilemmas does this consumer-level triple bottom line generate when those three dimensions conflict?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Traditional definitions frame sustainable consumption as purely altruistic and self-transcendent, treating restraint as individual sacrifice; this omission of personal benefit makes sustainability seem burdensome and helps explain the attitude–behavior "green gap."
- Key constructs (consumer-level TBL): three dimensions — planet (environmental harm reduction), people (social imperatives, rights, equality), and self-profit (the consumer's self-interest and personal well-being, extending the original "profit" pillar beyond economics).
- Boundary conditions: situations qualify as ethical dilemmas only when consumers are ethically aware, value the relevant concerns, and are not indifferent; the framing also presumes an atomistic, individual-level decision paradigm.
- Emergent outcomes: nine dilemma types arising from conflicts among (and within) the three dimensions, going beyond Elkington's three "shear zones" (e.g., Planet vs. self-profit, People vs. self-profit, Planet vs. Planet, Self-profit vs. self-profit), each illustrated with a binary-choice scenario.

By adapting the triple bottom line from the organizational to the individual level and adding self-profit as a third pillar, the paper reframes sustainable consumption as an ethical decision-making process in which planet, people, and self-profit can collide. Drawing on framing theory, the authors argue that how sustainability is defined and framed shapes consumer perceptions: surfacing personal benefits makes sustainable behavior more appealing and durable. A dynamic cognition–affect interplay (guilt, pride, empathy, anticipated regret) governs how consumers perceive and resolve these dilemmas.
theoretical_contribution: The study proposes a paradigm shift that incorporates consumers' self-interest and well-being as integral components of sustainable consumption rather than treating sustainability as strictly altruistic. It contributes to the sustainable-consumption literature by broadening its definition and underlying dimensions (planet, people, self-profit), and to transformative consumer research by foregrounding individual well-being. Reframing the phenomenon through ethical dilemmas yields a nine-type typology that complements, rather than replaces, attitudinal and affective accounts of the intention–behavior gap.
practical_implication: Marketers and policymakers should identify the dilemmas their products or services trigger and adjust product attributes (e.g., bundling, flexible pricing, "green but affordable" options) to minimize or resolve them; surveys, focus groups, and analytics can reveal which dilemmas matter most and support segmentation. Because definitions shape evaluation metrics and advocacy, communication that highlights personal benefits and deconstructs the "sacrifice" framing can make sustainable consumption resonate with a broader audience.
limitations: The framework adopts an individual-level, somewhat atomistic and rational perspective that may underweight social, cultural, emotional, and relational dynamics of consumption, and framing dilemmas through personal motivation risks downplaying the need for broader structural change toward sufficiency or degrowth. It also inherits well-known criticisms of the TBL (measurement difficulty, weak pillar integration, use as a compliance tool), and its scenarios apply best to high-involvement, slow decisions; concretizing environmental–social–individual attributes for low-involvement, signal-based choices is difficult.
future_research: The authors outline five avenues: (1) assess individuals' propensity to experience dilemmas and build a consumer typology via scenario-based surveys; (2) explore consumers' underlying moral reasoning using think-aloud protocols; (3) assess downstream consequences such as dissonance, confidence, and longer-term behavior; (4) identify intervention strategies (communication, empowerment) for firms and policymakers, attending to institutional and market context; and (5) advance methodologies, including qualitative and neurocognitive/biometric approaches for greater ecological validity.
abstract: This conceptual study explores consumers’ ethical dilemmas inherent in the domain of sustainable consumption. The traditional approach to sustainable consumption tends to exclude individual interests and consumer well-being, implicitly assuming that sustainable consumption should be entirely altruistic and devoid of personal benefits. Using the triple bottom line framework adapted to the consumer level, we argue that, in addition to addressing social and environmental concerns, sustainable offerings should integrate self-profit as an equally important concern. From this perspective, sustainable consumption inherently entails dilemmas, as social, environmental, and individual benefits may conflict with one another. In this research, we propose examining sustainable consumption through the lens of ethical dilemmas, to shed light on the tensions involved in navigating these multifaceted issues. We identify nine types of ethical dilemmas related to sustainable consumption and illustrate them with concrete scenarios to demonstrate the applicability of our proposed classification. This study sets the stage for future research by highlighting key areas in which additional investigations are required to deepen understanding of ethical dilemmas and develop practical solutions for fostering sustainable consumption.
apa_citation: Elgaaied-Gambier, L., Ladli, Y., & Reniou, F. (2026). Reframing sustainable consumption: Toward an ethical dilemma perspective. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06226-z

title: Fair Fares for Vehicle Sharing Systems
methods: Analytical operations-research study: a stylized two-node queueing-network pricing model (linear and exponential demand, with extensions for nonzero travel time, repositioning, and multiple units) yielding closed-form solutions and propositions; a convex-relaxation heuristic with provable approximation bounds for the general N-node network; and a real-data case study fitting logistic demand and solving the fair-pricing program with Ipopt/Pyomo.
industry: Vehicle sharing / ride-hailing transportation (Yellow Taxi, Green Taxi, and High-Volume For-Hire Vehicles such as Uber and Lyft)
time_period: Trips with pick-up times 7 a.m.–9 a.m. on March 3, 2023 (a 6 p.m.–8 p.m. window analyzed in the Online Appendix)
units: Trips between Manhattan neighborhoods (63 taxi zones aggregated into N = 10 neighborhoods)
n_sample: K = 1548 vehicles (estimated); market sizes per node-pair from total Yellow/Green/Uber/Lyft trips in the window
authors: Elmachtoub, Adam N.; Kim, Hyemi
theories: price fairness and access fairness (Cohen, Elmachtoub, & Lei, 2022); queueing-network pricing models for shared vehicle systems (Waserhole & Jost, 2016; Banerjee et al., 2022; Benjaafar & Shen, 2023)
keywords: fairness; price discrimination; vehicle sharing systems
research_question: How does imposing price fairness or access fairness on a revenue-maximizing vehicle-sharing platform affect platform revenue, consumer surplus, and social welfare across locations, and how should platforms and regulators set fair fares to minimize the inequalities users experience by location?
mechanism_summary:
- Decision variable (platform): origin–destination prices `p = {p_ij}` set to maximize steady-state revenue `R(p)` over a connected network of `N` nodes and `K` vehicles, subject to fairness constraints parameterized by `α ∈ [0,1]`.
- Fairness constraints (IV/treatment): *price fairness* (bounding the gap between max and min normalized prices) and *access fairness* (bounding the gap between max and min fractions of fulfilled requests, where access = availability `Π_i` × affordability `q_ij`), each in trip-based and origin-based forms.
- Outcomes (DVs): platform revenue, normalized consumer surplus `S(p)`, and social welfare `W(p) = R(p) + S(p)`.
- Key operational mechanism: vehicle availability is endogenous to prices through a steady-state flow-balance condition, so fair prices must also keep flow balanced across nodes.

The central mechanism is that prices in a shared-vehicle system govern not only affordability but also the spatial availability of vehicles via flow balance. A little price fairness can be win-win-win, reallocating a vehicle toward the lower-valuation node and raising surplus at both locations and overall welfare under some market scenarios. Access fairness, by contrast, forces availability at both nodes to move together and can only be satisfied by raising prices everywhere, so it always reduces consumer surplus at both locations and social welfare—perfect access fairness shuts the system down entirely. Repositioning can partly relax this "everyone worse-off" tendency by letting one location gain while another loses.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends fairness-constrained pricing (Cohen et al., 2022) from an infinite-supply monopolist to a finite-supply network where availability is endogenous, showing that the operational coupling between prices and vehicle flow produces regimes—and a uniformly harmful access-fairness result—that do not arise under unlimited resources. It formalizes price and access fairness for shared-vehicle networks, proves an impossibility of simultaneous perfect price and access fairness, and supplies a convex relaxation for access fairness with an asymptotically optimal approximation guarantee.
practical_implication: Platforms and regulators are advised to favor price fairness over access fairness: a small amount of price fairness can raise welfare and surplus, whereas access fairness, though well intentioned, makes all parties worse off. The Manhattan case study shows how to quantify, by neighborhood, which areas gain or lose and how much revenue must be sacrificed for a given fairness level (e.g., origin-based access fairness retains ~80% of revenue and ~56% of surplus at full fairness).
limitations: The theoretical results rely on a stylized two-node, single-unit model with specific demand families, and several general-network claims are obtained via a convex relaxation rather than exact solution. The case study cannot directly observe valuations or rejected trips, so demand functions are approximated from observed prices via a random-utility argument, and the analysis covers limited time windows in a single city.
future_research: The authors suggest studying platforms that maximize social welfare rather than revenue, exploring alternative rider-side fairness notions (e.g., surplus-based) and driver-side fairness criteria, and investigating the legal and ethical implications of the proposed fairness measures.
abstract: To address the more general network pricing problem, we employ a convex relaxation technique that is asymptotically optimal with access fairness constraints. Lastly, we conduct a case study using real-world data of a vehicle sharing system operating in New York City. We analyze the tradeoff between fairness and various metrics, including revenue, consumer surplus, and social welfare, based on the fairness criteria we define.
apa_citation: Elmachtoub, A. N., & Kim, H. (2026). Fair fares for vehicle sharing systems. *Operations Research*. https://doi.org/10.1287/opre.2024.1043

title: Do sufficiency consumption changes drive emissions down? A production network approach
journal: Journal of Environmental Economics and Management
methods: Static general-equilibrium macroeconomic model with disaggregated CES production-network structure; analytical comparative statics deriving demand, price, and substitution channels; numerical calibration on multi-regional environmentally-extended input–output (GLORIA) tables for a two-region (EU/RoW) economy, solved via exact-hat algebra across four scenarios (Input–Output, Demand, Cobb-Douglas, CES) and two preference shocks (Food, Energy).
industry: Cross-sector economy-wide (120 sectors spanning agriculture, energy, manufacturing, and services)
country: European Union and Rest of the World (two-region open economy)
time_period: GLORIA input–output tables for base year 2018
units: Economic sectors / production network
n_sample: 120 sectors; two regions (EU, RoW)
authors: Escribe, Célia; Quirion, Philippe
theories: macroeconomics of production networks (Carvalho and Tahbaz-Salehi, 2019; Baqaee and Farhi, 2019b); rebound effect theory (Sorrell, 2007; Gillingham et al., 2016); sufficiency rebound effect (Alcott, 2008; Sorrell et al., 2020)
keywords: Production networks; Rebound effect; Sufficiency
research_question: What are the economy-wide emissions consequences of sufficiency-driven consumption changes once macroeconomic feedback effects are accounted for, and through which propagation channels in the production network do reductions in demand for emission-intensive goods actually translate into emissions reductions?
mechanism_summary: A reduction in relative preference for an emission-intensive good lowers its relative demand and, when primary factors are sector-specific, its relative price; the price drop propagates downstream and can raise production of the very emission-intensive sectors that were targeted, generating a rebound, while substitution patterns (governed by elasticities below or above one) either magnify or dampen it. Because energy sectors occupy central, upstream network positions, a fall in energy prices spreads to all producers and drives positive carbon leakage abroad, whereas freed-up income under the Food shock is re-spent on other carbon-intensive goods (income rebound). The net emissions outcome thus depends jointly on the network structure, the carbon intensity of affected sectors, and the elasticities of substitution.
theoretical_contribution: The paper provides what the authors describe as the first theoretical and numerical analysis of sufficiency-related consumption changes within a macroeconomic framework that retains a detailed microeconomic production structure. Its core contribution is disentangling three network-based propagation channels for preference shocks — a demand effect (consistent with existing input–output work) plus novel price and substitution effects derived analytically — and showing that these feedbacks, absent from standard LCA and IO studies, can be quantitatively large.
practical_implication: Demand-side and integrated-assessment models that represent sufficiency as an exogenous reduction in baseline energy demand risk overstating its mitigation potential, because they omit price and substitution rebound effects that can reach 38% of domestic and 60% of global avoided emissions. Modelers and policymakers should endogenize these macroeconomic feedbacks and attend to elasticities of substitution when evaluating low-demand decarbonization pathways and designing complementary policy mixes.
limitations: The framework does not specify the origin of the preference shocks (e.g., whether driven by nudges, information campaigns, or structural infrastructure change), limiting policy-specific conclusions. It is static and flow-based, abstracting from capital accumulation, savings, and investment dynamics, and it focuses on demand-side changes while disregarding endogenous production-side technological change. CES preferences with uniform income elasticity and historically calibrated, symmetric substitution elasticities are acknowledged as strong simplifications.
future_research: The authors call for endogenizing a wider set of feedback effects within integrated assessment and macroeconomic models, incorporating more sophisticated (e.g., nonhomothetic CES) household-preference structures, and translating interdisciplinary evidence on low-carbon lifestyles into empirically grounded behavioral parameters. They also flag dynamic, stock-based extensions, endogenous production-technique change, a leisure-based reconceptualization of sufficiency, and — as a key step — investigating the policy drivers behind the behavioral changes modeled here.
abstract: Our findings reveal significant rebound effects, up to 38 % for domestic emissions and 60 % for global emissions (accounting for carbon leakage), compared to estimates excluding behavioral aspects. Rebound effects from sufficiency strategies are smaller than those from energy efficiency improvements.
apa_citation: Escribe, C., & Quirion, P. (2026). Do sufficiency consumption changes drive emissions down? A production network approach. *Journal of Environmental Economics and Management*, 136, 103258. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jeem.2025.103258

title: Good Data, Good Questions: Leveraging Comprehensive, Direct-Observation Data Sets for Impactful Research on Organizations and the Environment
methods: Conceptual/agenda-setting article; surveys five direct-observation natural-science data sets and assesses each against the four-dimension Wang & Strong (1996) data-quality framework (accessibility, intrinsic quality, contextual relevance, interpretability), with illustrative quasi-experimental research designs.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Etzion, Dror; Baudoin, Lucie; Pongeluppe, Leandro S.; Callery, Patrick J.; Lancaster, Cady A.; Panwar, Rajat
theories: data quality assessment framework (Wang & Strong, 1996); planetary boundaries (Richardson et al., 2023)
keywords: corporate environmental impacts; data quality; interdisciplinary research; research design; sustainability data sets
research_question: How can comprehensive, direct-observation data sets originating in the natural sciences be identified, assessed for quality, and harnessed by organizations-and-the-natural-environment (ONE) scholars to study the actual environmental impacts of corporate actions on local and planetary biophysical systems?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: ONE research has drifted toward economic performance and intermediated ESG ratings, yielding "partial and imprecise information about the direct impact organizations have on biophysical systems"; direct-observation data sets can re-anchor the field on impact.
- Key constructs / data sources: five exemplar data sets spanning water (HydroBASINS, Aqueduct, French Naïades/Hydro), forests and fire (INPE DETER/Queimadas), ocean salinity (NASA Aquarius), GHG emissions (Climate TRACE), and timber-trade provenance (ILAT, InsideWood/POWO/Tree of Life).
- Assessment framework: each data set is evaluated on accessibility, intrinsic quality, contextual relevance, and interpretability.
- Boundary conditions: attribution/attributability of biophysical change to specific firms, plus contextual relevance and interpretability, are the recurring hurdles; longitudinal and quasi-experimental designs, narrowed settings, and facility-to-parent aggregation are the proposed remedies.

By organizing natural-science data along organizational units and merging it geographically with firm activity, ONE scholars can shift the dependent variables they study from disclosed intent to observed environmental impact. The article frames this not merely as methodological innovation but as an "epistemological turn toward materiality" that lets the field test theories of organizational adaptation, ecological feedback, and system resilience grounded in biophysical properties.
theoretical_contribution: The article argues that integrating comprehensive direct-observation biophysical data can transform the field's central constructs—corporate environmental performance, stakeholder salience, sustainability strategy—"from interpretive estimates to empirically grounded measures," redefining the theories the field can build "from those centered on disclosure and intent to those centered on impact and causality." It positions data work as an entry point to radical interdisciplinarity and re-anchors ONE in its founding ambition to study organizations as part of, not apart from, biophysical systems.
practical_implication: ONE researchers are urged to invest effort in discovering, understanding, and merging openly available natural-science data sets, to collaborate with domain experts (e.g., environmental scientists) to specify control variables and attribution, and to favor longitudinal or quasi-experimental designs and tightly circumscribed settings where impacts are easier to attribute to specific firms.
limitations: The article is an agenda-setting overview rather than an empirical study; its set of data sets and research questions is explicitly "far from exhaustive." It also cautions that alternative natural-science data sets are not a panacea and can themselves suffer from faulty observations, inconsistency over time, and poor weighting, and that several (e.g., Climate TRACE) capture only direct Scope 1 emissions or single points in time.
future_research: Table 2 offers sample research questions—on regulatory effects, corporate target setting, disclosure veracity, and supply chains—that the highlighted databases could answer. The authors point to emerging databases (biodiversity, soil health, biogeochemical flows, air pollution, food security, ocean plastics, methane) and to machine-learning-enabled predictive modeling, facility-to-parent aggregation, and "planet-centered" analyses as promising directions.
abstract: Much research on organizations and the natural environment relies on self-reported corporate data of dubious reliability and accuracy. A new wave of data, originating in satellite observations, forensic genetics, hydrological maps and other sources, can help overcome these shortcomings. We identify several data sets from the natural sciences that are easily accessible and highly accurate, and provide roadmaps for management researchers to utilize them. We further explore the types of research questions these data sets enable, extending beyond the confines of environmental-financial performance relationships that have dominated previous research. By leveraging these and similar data sets, corporate sustainability researchers can obtain clearer insights into corporate environmental impact, opening doors to new avenues of inquiry.
apa_citation: Etzion, D., Baudoin, L., Pongeluppe, L. S., Callery, P. J., Lancaster, C. A., & Panwar, R. (2026). Good data, good questions: Leveraging comprehensive, direct-observation data sets for impactful research on organizations and the environment. *Organization & Environment*, 39(1), 112–139. https://doi.org/10.1177/10860266251403388

title: Competing With Smart Machines: The dark side of ‘conjoined agency’ in contemporary organizations
methods: Inductive grounded-theory study; 46 semi-structured interviews with UK passenger train drivers and managers over three rounds plus a one-day observation/simulator visit; four-phase coding building to theory.
industry: Passenger rail (UK train operating franchises, drivers and managers)
units: Individual train drivers and rail managers
n_sample: 46 interviews
authors: Fisher, Daniel; Fleming, Peter
theories: automation-augmentation paradox (Raisch & Krakowski, 2021); conjoined agency (Murray, Rhymer & Sirmon, 2021); labour process / ideology of automation
keywords: artificial intelligence; automation; conjoined agency; management by algorithm; technological unemployment
research_question: How do conjoined agency, management by algorithm, and symbolic impression management play out among frontline workers when the threat of full automation is salient? The paper asks why UK passenger train drivers covertly urinate in bottles despite contractual rest breaks, using this puzzle to surface a "dark side" of human-machine collaboration.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: conjoined agency (shared human-machine intentionality); the "ideology of automation" (managerial threats of future technological unemployment that shape present power relations); human robotic mimesis (workers performing like robots).
- Triggering conditions: semi-automated cabin technologies (DSDs, "downloads," algorithmically calculated "diagrams"); combative industrial relations and managerial invocation of imminent driverless trains.
- Process / phases: first-order mimesis (robotic behaviour intrinsic to the labour process), second-order mimesis (self-imposed bodily bioregulation of food, drink, and toilet needs), third-order mimesis (symbolic signalling to a managerial audience that humans can match ATO).
- Emergent outcomes: bodily strain, fatigue, and covert workarounds (e.g., urinating in bottles) that sustain a symbolic impression of machine-like efficiency.

Drivers, faced with an antagonistic ideology of automation, treat their own bodies as machines to signal that they can perform as well as or better than the robots management claims will replace them. Because human bodies have biological limits, drivers conceal those limits through backstage "body work" rather than concede the superiority of driverless trains, exposing how power relations and ideology — not just the technology itself — shape the labour/technology interface.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends the conjoined-agency framework by revealing a previously unexamined dark side: under asymmetrical power relations, semi-automation can produce roboticization of human labour rather than enrichment. It introduces "human robotic mimesis" (in three orders) and the "ideology of automation" as a mode of power, and foregrounds the embodied, visceral dimension ("cyber corporeality" / "virtual viscerality") largely absent from the disembodied management-by-algorithm literature.
practical_implication: The study suggests managers and policymakers should recognize that invoking imminent full automation as a disciplinary threat imposes hidden bodily and psychological costs on skilled workers, degrading dignity, security, and safety (e.g., drivers making unsafe decisions to avoid stops). It implies that surveillance algorithms and unrealistic efficiency "diagrams" should account for embodied human needs.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors propose examining how semi-automation reshapes workers' subjective and cognitive qualities (political awareness, identity, ethical deliberation, intuition) even when jobs are not eliminated; how the ideology of automation functions as a mode of power and how workers resist it across occupations; and pursuing more critical, ethical, and speculative inquiry into who wins and loses as the "fourth industrial revolution" unfolds.
abstract: While many are concerned about artificial intelligence (AI) enabled automation and the threat of mass technological unemployment, this paper contributes to a stream of organizational research that suggests semi-automation is a more probable scenario. A growing subtheme in this stream emphasizes ‘conjoined agency’, where employees collaborate with smart machines rather than be subordinated by them. In this paper, we argue that conjoined agency has a hitherto unexamined dark side. Building on an in-depth qualitative study of passenger train drivers and their managers in the United Kingdom, we demonstrate how drivers require taxing levels of stamina to successfully work with algorithms and semi-automated train systems. This is exacerbated by what we call an ideology of automation, notably threats from management that driverless trains are imminent. Drivers feel compelled to compete with these prospective robots. We term this human robotic mimesis, where workers attempt (but often fail) to perform like robots. By exploring this dark side of conjoined agency, we propose that it isn’t only the practical application of new automating technologies that is shaping organizational life but their ideological evocation within specific power relationships.
apa_citation: Fisher, D., & Fleming, P. (2026). Competing with smart machines: The dark side of ‘conjoined agency’ in contemporary organizations. *Organization Studies*, 47(5), 713–737. https://doi.org/10.1177/01708406251391972
