title: Entrepreneurship and Gentrification
methods: Panel regression on neighborhood (ward) x year data for Greater London; OLS with neighborhood and year fixed effects, neighborhood-specific fixed trends, and a shift-share instrumental-variable design exploiting the 2010 reform of the UK Enterprise Investment Scheme (EIS); standard errors clustered at the neighborhood level.
industry: Growth-oriented startups in high-tech and tradable services (financial services, e-commerce, IT, app developers, data/analytics, AI), drawn from Crunchbase, PitchBook, and VentureXpert
time_period: Firms founded 2001-2016; neighborhood outcomes observed 2001-2016 (Census 2001 and 2011)
units: Census wards (neighborhoods) within Greater London
n_sample: 6,419 startups; 633 of 649 wards used for estimation (10,128 ward-year observations)
authors: Gagliardi, Luisa; Sorenson, Olav
theories: agglomeration economics (Marshall, 1922; Glaeser et al., 2001); gentrification theory (Glass, 1964; Smith, 1996); organizational legitimacy and category labels (Romanelli & Khessina, 2005; Hsu & Hannan, 2005)
keywords: communities; entrepreneurship; gentrification; inequality; real estate
research_question: How does growth-oriented entrepreneurship influence the neighborhoods in which startups locate — specifically, does startup entry initiate and propel the demographic, organizational, and real-estate-price changes characteristic of gentrification, and who captures the resulting value?
mechanism_summary: Startups transform neighborhoods through three reinforcing channels: they create local jobs that draw younger, more educated, higher-income people; this influx raises demand for upscale amenities (trendier, more diverse restaurants and retail); and clusters of startups act as a signal and confer legitimacy and place labels (e.g., "Silicon Roundabout") that attract further entrepreneurs, businesses, and residents. These dynamics form a self-reinforcing cycle of renewal that bids up residential real estate prices, especially in neighborhoods that began with low prices — but because appreciation concentrates in high-renting areas, the gains accrue largely to landlords rather than to displaced long-term residents.
theoretical_contribution: The paper identifies growth-oriented entrepreneurship as a distinct, more emergent and less centrally planned form of gentrification, contrasting with the capital-infusion and municipal-policy accounts that dominate the gentrification literature. By documenting microgeographic links between startup entry and neighborhood change, it extends work on the local economic and social consequences of entrepreneurship, surfacing an additional channel through which entrepreneurship generates a tension between economic growth and economic inequality via spatial sorting within cities.
practical_implication: Although the job creation and price appreciation associated with startup clusters may tempt politicians and urban planners to promote growth-oriented entrepreneurship, these transformations impose uneven costs: rising rents displace longstanding residents and older businesses, and renters (the majority in the most-affected areas) do not share in the capital gains. Policymakers should weigh distributional consequences and the long-run affordability and sustainability of entrepreneurial ecosystems.
limitations: London represents but a single case with its own idiosyncrasies, limiting generalizability. The data are at the neighborhood (ward) level and do not allow household-level measurement of home ownership or identification of who fills the new jobs or where displaced residents go. Crunchbase data are crowdsourced with uncertain provenance (partly mitigated by a VentureXpert-based measure), and the price measure does not adjust for year-to-year quality variation in the properties sold.
future_research: The authors call for individual-level data to determine who benefits from these revitalizations and where displaced residents relocate, and for cross-city designs comparing places with varying degrees of entrepreneurship-driven gentrification to assess net city-wide effects. They also urge further study of the property-price/entrepreneurship relationship, including whether rising prices eventually undermine competitiveness and deter the next generation of entrepreneurs ("Silicon Valley syndrome").
abstract: experiencing startup entry calls for deeper reflection on the role of entrepreneurship in inequality and the sustainability of entrepreneurial ecosystems.
apa_citation: Gagliardi, L., & Sorenson, O. (2026). Entrepreneurship and gentrification. *Organization Science*, 37(1), 1-16. https://doi.org/10.1287/orsc.2024.18920

title: “If I Accomplish Nothing Else but Hope, That Will be Enough”: The Ethics and Morality of Market-Based Hope in Illness Entrepreneurship
methods: Qualitative interpretive study; 12 narrative interviews with 11 ME/CFS recovery coaches (one interviewed twice) embedded in a broader project of 46 narrative interviews, triangulated with extensive secondary digital data (books, documentaries, media articles, newsletters, blogs, podcasts, YouTube channels); emergent, iterative thematic analysis via 'close readings' and 'moments of resonance' between concepts and data.
industry: Recovery coaching / illness entrepreneurship for chronic energy-limiting illnesses (ME/CFS and Long Covid)
country: International; coaches based primarily in the US and UK (also Spain, Canada, Germany), with international online client bases
time_period: Data collection through 2024 (study received 27 January 2025); newsletters cited up to 2024
units: Recovery coaches (illness entrepreneurs) who are former ME/CFS sufferers
n_sample: 11 recovery coaches across 12 interviews (one interviewed twice); part of a broader study of 46 interviewees
authors: Geiger, Susi; Loza, Olya
theories: ethical agency; ethics of care (Tronto, 2013; Johansson & Wickström, 2023); ethics of hope; moral markets (Geiger & Gross, 2018)
keywords: Chronic illness; Ethics of care; Ethics of hope; Illness entrepreneurship; ME/CFS; Markets for hope; Moral markets
research_question: How do entrepreneurs embody and enact hope as a motor for ethical agency—agency that builds one's own and others' capacities and capabilities to overcome a painful present? The authors further ask whether marketized hope can foster people's agency rather than trapping them in dependency, and whether the individual moral struggles of illness entrepreneurs can collectively lead to a more 'moral' market.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: hope (embodied, affective, future-oriented), care ('caring with' others), and ethical agency (building one's own and others' capacities to overcome a painful present); the central tension between self-agency and other-agency.
- Triggering conditions: biographical disruption from chronic illness (ME/CFS / Long Covid) and the perceived failure, carelessness, and false hope of mainstream medical markets, prompting former sufferers to become 'accidental' recovery-coach entrepreneurs.
- Process / phases: leaving a painful present behind → embodying hope to open spaces of possibility for others → enacting hope through self-agency (pursuing 'alignment' and a livelihood) → balancing self- and other-agency → moral struggles around marketizing and pricing hope.
- Practices: 'dosing' hope to avoid feeding 'cruel optimism' or magical thinking; flexible/sliding-scale pricing as a duty of care; bricolage of coaching, group programs, and digital content; insisting clients pay 'something' to stay 'invested.'
- Emergent outcomes: a precarious, individualized 'moral market' / 'ecology of hope' that fosters ethical agency yet remains unlikely to produce systemic change.

Through repeated moral struggles, recovery coaches realign their own hopes for a new life with their care for fellow sufferers, using hope as a 'guardrail' that gives care the impetus to move people toward new spaces of possibility. The authors theorize four interacting 'building blocks' of an architecture of hope—pain/vulnerability, care, ethical agency, and spaces of possibility—arguing that ethical agency is the 'fuel' that turns hope into forward movement, and that care and hope are most powerful when co-existent and aligned.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances two conceptual contributions: first, a new way of conceptualizing 'morality' in markets—particularly markets where shared pain, suffering, or vulnerability provide the foundation for market encounters—by arguing that the morality of marketized care services ought to include ethical agency. Second, it traces the first contours of an 'ethics of hope,' analyzing how care, hope, and ethical agency work together to move people out of painful presents toward new spaces of possibility. It extends Johansson and Wickström's (2023) definition of ethical agency from 'care for others' to a continuous balancing act between self- and other-agency, contributing to recent calls to assemble an 'architecture of hope' (Hudson et al., 2025) in organization and entrepreneurship studies.
practical_implication: The findings illuminate how entrepreneurs operating in care markets (wellness, lifestyle, craft, and medical adjacencies) can 'dose' hope honestly—opening pathways while being transparent about their uncertainty—to avoid the 'cruel optimism' and magical thinking that exploit vulnerable buyers. They highlight fair-pricing and access practices (sliding scales, scholarships, group formats) as expressions of an ethics of care, and surface the limits of individualized market solutions, suggesting that genuine systemic relief for chronic-illness sufferers may require structural supports (reimbursement, social welfare) rather than markets alone.
limitations: The authors caution against romanticizing the market's practices: they cannot exclude that recovered sufferers may become 'cut-throat and selfish' rather than caring entrepreneurs, and they have no way to determine whether the hope generated was not also 'cruel' or whether coaching helped people more than other 'merchants of hope.' Hope in recovery is highly personal and often spiritual—hope, belief, and magical thinking are frequently inseparable—and its marketization almost always targets the already-vulnerable, complicating any claim about the market's morality.
future_research: More research is needed in other settings of markets of hope to put their morality to the test, and to study entrepreneurs motivated by very different conceptions of the spaces of possibility their entrepreneurship opens up (across illness, wellness, and lifestyle entrepreneurship). Future studies should continue building out the 'architecture of hope' in other cases and communities, delineate the relationship between an ethics of care and an ethics of hope, and be especially sensitive to the embodied and affective nature of hope—asking what a body can do when it becomes hopeful.
abstract: How do entrepreneurs embody and enact hope as a motor for ethical agency—agency that builds one's own and others' capacities and capabilities to overcome a painful present? This study puts hope at the center of theorizing around the activities of entrepreneurs who leverage it both as a way to sketch a new life for themselves and to sell it to others. Through the case of illness entrepreneurs—recovery coaches for those suffering from chronic illnesses—we observe the moral struggles involved when hope becomes framed within the logic of the market. Recovery coaches at once embody hope (as formerly sick people who have recovered), neatly package and sell it (as entrepreneurs), and manifest it (opening up new spaces of possibility for themselves). The work involved in balancing these threads requires constant realignment of the entrepreneurs' own hopes for a new life and their care for others. We show how, through these moral struggles, hope opens up new spaces of possibility and imbues people—both entrepreneurs and their clients—with the agency to move toward those new horizons. Conceptualizing the complex entanglements of hope, care, and ethical agency, our study represents a foray into recent calls for assembling and theorizing the 'architecture of hope' in organization and entrepreneurship studies. We also discuss whether this entrepreneurship, balancing hope and care to enable ethical agency, opens up new ways of understanding morality and agency in market contexts.
apa_citation: Geiger, S., & Loza, O. (2026). “If I accomplish nothing else but hope, that will be enough”: The ethics and morality of market-based hope in illness entrepreneurship. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06230-3

title: Nourishing sustainability innovation: Scientific trajectories in industrial protein research
methods: Two-step text-analytic design: POS-enhanced LDA topic modeling of 32,042 food-science articles to identify four scientific areas, then dictionary-based classification of USPTO A23J patent texts; Pseudo Poisson maximum likelihood (PPML) regressions of forward citations on trajectory dummies with CPC and filing-year fixed effects, robust standard errors clustered at the firm (assignee) level.
industry: Industrial protein research for foodstuffs (USPTO sub-technology class A23J); patents and food-science academic publications
country: United States (USPTO patents); top-34 Q1 Web of Science Food Science and Technology journals for the article corpus
time_period: Patents filed 1990–2015; academic articles 2000–2020; forward citations counted in a 5-year window after grant
units: Granted patents (patent-level analysis)
n_sample: 1561 patents; 32,042 articles searched (28,081 uniquely classified)
authors: Giarratana, Marco S.; Pasquini, Martina; Simeth, Markus
theories: absorptive capacity (Cohen and Levinthal, 1990; Zahra and George, 2002); science as a map (Fleming and Sorenson, 2004); sustainability innovation (van der Have and Rubalcaba, 2016); technological paradigms and trajectories (Dosi, 1982)
keywords: Academic articles; Food and proteins; Machine learning; Patents; Scientific trajectory; Sustainability innovation
research_question: Which scientific trajectories in protein research are most likely to shape follow-on innovation, and to what extent are they associated with sustainability-oriented outcomes? The paper asks how upstream science structures the technological search space for industrial R&D in the alternative-protein sector.
mechanism_summary: The paper theorizes science as a cognitive map (Fleming and Sorenson, 2004) layered onto Dosi's (1982) paradigm–trajectory distinction: trajectories grounded in codified, abstract, modular knowledge (Trajectory B, science-push) generalize and recombine across domains, producing broad and SDG-aligned spillovers, whereas trajectories rooted in tacit, context- and firm-specific knowledge (Trajectory C, demand-pull) remain locked into narrow use cases with weak diffusion. Higher codification raises third parties' absorptive capacity, enabling cumulative learning and systemic recombination, while application-bound knowledge confines learning within firm or sectoral boundaries. PPML estimates confirm B_CELLULAR is positively and C_CHEMISTRY negatively associated with forward citations, with magnitudes larger for SDG-related citations and effects that shift across early and late periods.
theoretical_contribution: The paper refines the sustainability-innovation literature by showing that scientific trajectories differ substantially in their contribution to technological impact and SDG-aligned outcomes, challenging the assumption that scientific knowledge uniformly enhances impact (Fleming and Sorenson, 2004; Arora and Gambardella, 1994). It reframes science not as a monolithic enabler or mere source of citations but as an epistemic infrastructure whose influence depends on how codified knowledge is structured and embedded across domains, clarifying the link between scientific paradigms and technological trajectories (Dosi, 1982). Methodologically, it advances text-based innovation research with a hybrid approach combining unsupervised topic modeling of publications with supervised classification of patent texts.
practical_implication: For innovation policy, the evaluation of public science should move beyond traditional academic-output metrics to consider science's capacity to shape industrial innovation paths, which is especially crucial in sustainability-intensive sectors where alignment between scientific abstraction and industrial application can determine the success of long-term transitions. The science-based-dictionary classification offers firms, investors, and policymakers a toolkit to visualize where innovation is scientifically grounded and where strategic recombination may unlock additional value.
limitations: The study focuses exclusively on USPTO data, which may not fully capture global patterns of sustainability innovation or regional differences in regulatory pressures. The patent-level focus improves consistency but abstracts from firm-level strategies and organizational routines that may further shape trajectory evolution. Finally, the keyword-matching classification of SDG-related patents, while validated, may not fully capture the complexity of sustainability intent.
future_research: Future work should expand the framework across sectors and countries to test the generalizability of trajectory-based patterns, including comparative studies in sustainability-critical domains such as renewable energy, circular materials, or decarbonized mobility. Fieldwork involving interviews or ethnographic studies with inventors could illuminate how firms interpret and operationalize scientific knowledge, and introducing country-level regulatory contexts would reveal the institutional contingencies of scientific trajectories. Linking trajectory patterns to firm-level outcomes such as profitability or ESG performance could bridge epistemic structure with organizational impact.
abstract: These findings contribute to a view of science as a cognitive map that structures industrial research by making more explicit the underlying causal mechanisms that govern each trajectory, thereby shaping which domains are more likely to yield impactful and sustainabilityaligned innovations.
apa_citation: Giarratana, M. S., Pasquini, M., & Simeth, M. (2026). Nourishing sustainability innovation: Scientific trajectories in industrial protein research. *Research Policy, 55*(2), 105375. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.respol.2025.105375

title: The living lab and the cursed catalyst: Navigating the legitimacy challenges of innovation intermediaries for sustainable innovation
methods: Inductive single-case study (the MOSIS water-management project) using the Gioia method; data from ~23 hours of semi-structured interviews with all stakeholders plus ~140 pages of internal/external documents, triangulated over an 18-month period with a posteriori ex-post validation interviews.
industry: Sustainability-oriented innovation / water resource management using satellite Earth Observation (remote sensing); living lab as innovation intermediary
country: France (living lab E2L, Toulouse) and Senegal River basin (Guinea, Mali, Mauritania, Senegal)
time_period: MOSIS project began November 2017; data collected over an 18-month period; ex-post validation interviews in 2025
units: Single project (MOSIS) and its focal innovation intermediary, the E2L living lab, plus its consortium stakeholders
n_sample: 1 case (MOSIS project); 25 interviews across all stakeholder organisations plus 140 pages of documents
authors: Gibert, Cylien; Plantec, Quentin
theories: organisational legitimacy theory (Suchman, 1995; Tost, 2011; Bitektine & Haack, 2015; Bitektine et al., 2025)
keywords: Innovation intermediaries; Living labs; Organisational legitimacy; Sustainable innovation
research_question: "How do the roles assumed by living labs intersect with organisational legitimacy in sustainability projects involving frontier technologies?" The paper foregrounds legitimacy as an under-examined factor in how innovation intermediaries reconcile competing stakeholder expectations and sustainability goals.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: organisational legitimacy decomposed into instrumental, relational, and moral (active) judgments versus cognitive (passive, taken-for-granted) legitimacy; categorical identity / categorical cues.
- Roles (practices): technological expert (delivering technological advisory; designing rapidly implementable solutions); stakeholder orchestrator (engaging diverse stakeholders; harmonising goals; employing facilitation methods); end-user empowerer (building user competencies; integrating user needs; ensuring local compatibility).
- Synergies: each pair of roles is mutually reinforcing, accelerating experimentation, aligning heterogeneous interests, and strengthening trust — together producing strong active legitimacy and project success.
- Dis-synergies: performing all three roles simultaneously draws on the same scarce resources and blurs the lab's categorical identity, weakening cognitive legitimacy.
- Emergent outcome: the "Cursed Catalyst" — an imbalanced legitimacy portfolio in which active legitimacy accumulates while passive cognitive legitimacy stagnates, ultimately leading to the lab's exclusion from later project phases.

The case shows that the same role pluralism that lets an intermediary catalyse collective innovation prevents it from being recognised as a clearly bounded organisation. Because cognitive legitimacy depends on shared categorical mental shortcuts about "what the entity is," excelling across multiple roles paradoxically renders the lab's contribution invisible and its identity ambiguous. Drawing on Tost's (2011) active-versus-passive legitimacy logics and Bitektine et al.'s (2025) functional theory, the authors argue legitimacy is not a cumulative, sequential ladder but a delicate equilibrium whose dimensions can evolve in divergent—even opposing—directions, so functional effectiveness is necessary but not sufficient for survival.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances organisational legitimacy theory by showing that legitimacy dimensions can move in divergent or opposing directions, challenging the classical view of legitimacy as a cumulative, sequential ladder culminating in taken-for-grantedness. It contributes the "Cursed Catalyst" concept to the living labs and innovation-intermediary literatures, repositioning legitimacy—rather than functional prowess or structural design—as the pivotal challenge intermediaries face, and answering calls (e.g., Kivimaa et al., 2019) for a less idealised account of intermediaries in sustainability transitions.
practical_implication: Living-lab managers should guard against the Cursed Catalyst by deliberately foregrounding or staging the pursuit of cognitive legitimacy: maintaining a clear, shared understanding of the lab's roles, negotiating longer-term or framework contracts, embedding explicit role descriptions and milestones, and issuing regular dashboards/briefs that make multi-role contributions visible. Curatively, managers need mid-term legitimacy "health checks" and reflective debriefs to detect under-recognition even when projects appear successful. Partners and policymakers should structure contracts and funding across the full project life-cycle and institutionalise living labs (shared guidelines, labelling/accreditation) to reduce category ambiguity.
limitations: The findings rest on a single case study, limiting generalisability; comparative case studies and configurational methods (e.g., fsQCA) are needed to test the conditions that trigger the phenomenon. The MOSIS project was shaped by developed–developing-country collaboration dynamics that exacerbate power asymmetries and raise questions about whether a France-based living lab can fully understand local constraints and empower African end-users. The qualitative design also lacks the more objective, quantitative effectiveness measures that surveys or performance metrics could provide.
future_research: Future work should examine how legitimacy components interact dynamically across project phases, especially through comparative studies and methods capable of identifying micro-level legitimacy processes and their evolution. Researchers should test how the Cursed Catalyst phenomenon manifests in other types of innovation intermediaries and identify the boundary conditions under which intermediaries can reconcile catalytic effectiveness with the cognitive anchoring needed for long-term survival, complementing qualitative insight with quantitative assessment.
abstract: We identify three roles—technological expert, stakeholder orchestrator, and end-user empowerer—each corresponding to one dimension of legitimacy (instrumental, relational, and moral). When combined, these roles create synergies that endow the living lab with strong active legitimacy, but also trigger dis-synergies that obscure the categorical cues required for passive cognitive legitimacy.
apa_citation: Gibert, C., & Plantec, Q. (2026). The living lab and the cursed catalyst: Navigating the legitimacy challenges of innovation intermediaries for sustainable innovation. *Research Policy*, 55(3), 105403. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.respol.2025.105403

title: Carbon accounting quality: Measurement and the role of assurance
methods: Archival panel; OLS regressions of carbon accounting quality (abnormal emissions, manager uncertainty, filing lag) on a scope-specific assurance indicator with industry and year (or firm) fixed effects, standard errors clustered by firm; cross-sectional partitioned regressions and Wald tests; staggered and NFRD difference-in-differences designs.
industry: U.S. public firms across industries (Trucost industry classifications) disclosing Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions; excludes regulated utilities and banks in robustness tests
country: United States (main sample); 42 non-U.S. countries plus E.U. (France, Italy, Spain) for the NFRD analysis
time_period: 2010 to 2020 (main panel); 2015-2019 event window for the NFRD difference-in-differences
units: Firm-year-scope observations
n_sample: 998 U.S. firms (5,395 Scope 1 and 5,202 Scope 2 firm-year observations)
authors: Gipper, Brandon; Sequeira, Fiona; Shi, Shawn X.
theories: Earnings-quality framework (Dechow, Ge, & Schrand, 2010); Economics of auditing / verification (Watts & Zimmerman, 1983)
keywords: Carbon accounting; ESG assurance; Sustainability
research_question: Does external, third-party assurance improve the quality of firms’ carbon accounting — i.e., the extent to which reported emissions reflect a firm’s actual emissions — and through what mechanism?
mechanism_summary: The paper adapts Dechow et al.’s (2010) earnings-quality framework to carbon reporting, arguing that reported emissions deviate from actual emissions because of measurement-standard flaws and implementation issues; because the GHG Protocol is near-universal, the authors focus on implementation issues. Third-party assurance is theorized to improve quality primarily by identifying implementation issues (e.g., omitted sources, incorrect conversion factors) that firms then remediate, yielding fewer omissions and more revisions of prior disclosures rather than by addressing managerial misreporting incentives. The effect is predicted to be larger where pre-existing systems are weak and where assurance is more thorough.
theoretical_contribution: The study contributes to the ESG-reporting-quality literature by introducing a novel, vendor-independent, scope-specific measure of emissions reporting quality (abnormal emissions) that evaluates the accuracy of reported emissions rather than the breadth of disclosure. It extends the literature on the voluntary adoption of verification services by providing direct evidence that assurance — even predominantly “limited” assurance — shapes reporting quality, and it informs the broader audit literature by examining the reporting consequences of audit/assurance adoption rather than variation within mandatory regimes.
practical_implication: For investors, regulators, and other stakeholders, the abnormal-emissions measure offers a tool to assess carbon disclosure quality independent of data-vendor coverage. The findings suggest that requiring or encouraging emissions assurance — even limited assurance — can improve carbon accounting quality, which is directly relevant to looming mandates such as California SB 253 and the E.U. Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive.
limitations: The regression-based abnormal-emissions measure is subject to model misspecification and measurement error, and the authors explicitly do not claim it is a definitive measure of carbon accounting quality. The study does not establish causality for the voluntary-assurance results; although supplementary and NFRD difference-in-differences tests support a causal interpretation, endogeneity concerns cannot be fully ruled out. The emissions model relies on financial-statement data, so differences between carbon-reporting and financial-consolidation boundaries may add noise.
future_research: Future work could build on or develop alternative measures of carbon accounting quality to corroborate and compare inferences across measures. Researchers could explore whether managerial incentives drive manipulated emissions reporting and whether assurance effects are partly driven by such incentives, and could revisit the effects of assurance mandates (e.g., California SB 253, the E.U. CSRD) as carbon reporting and assurance practices mature.
abstract: We examine whether external assurance improves the quality of firms’ carbon accounting.
apa_citation: Gipper, B., Sequeira, F., & Shi, S. X. (2026). Carbon accounting quality: Measurement and the role of assurance. *Journal of Accounting and Economics*, 81(2), 101849. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jacceco.2025.101849

title: Whose Justice? A Turn to Relational Equality from the Experiences of Ethno-Racially Marginalised Women in Australian Workplaces
methods: Qualitative-dominant multi-method design: an online survey of 366 women leaders/emerging leaders, six two-hour focus groups with 54 ethno-racially marginalised women, and 15 in-depth interviews with senior racialised women leaders; thematic analysis in NVivo, triangulated across data sources and read through an intersectional, relational-equality lens (survey percentages reported descriptively).
industry: Australian subsidiaries of large MNCs across multiple sectors, including engineering, professional services firms, and finance and banking
country: Australia (focus groups in Melbourne and Sydney)
time_period: Data collected between 2017 and 2018; project reference group met fortnightly over a nine-month period
units: Women leaders and emerging leaders (survey respondents; focus group participants; senior leader interviewees), self-classified as ethno-racially diverse or non-ethno-racially diverse
n_sample: 366 survey respondents (96 only non-main-English-speaking + 67 both = 163 classified ethno-racially diverse); 54 focus-group participants; 15 interviewees
authors: Groutsis, Dimitria; Han, Christine; O'Leary, Jane; D'Almada Remedios, Rose; Kaabel, Annika
theories: business-case rationale for diversity (van Dijk et al., 2012; Noon, 2007); distributive vs. relational justice; epistemic neocolonialism (Chowdhury, 2023); intersectionality (Crenshaw, 1991; Collins, 2019); relational equality (Anderson, 1999)
keywords: Diversity; Ethics of inclusion; Ethno-racial; Relational equality; Women
research_question: Within the context of Australian organisations, how do ethno-racially marginalised professional women experience their workplace? What do their accounts reveal about the relational conditions that shape inclusion and recognition in organisational life?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: distributive justice and business-case logics vs. relational equality; access/representation vs. equal standing; symbolic vs. structural inclusion; single-axis (gender-only or race-only) frameworks vs. intersectional marginalisation; the "two levers" of hard (legal/compliance) and soft (voluntary, business-case) diversity measures.
- Structural barriers (themes): organisational norms privileging Anglo/Western and masculine leadership models; institutional invisibility and underrepresentation; "epistemic neocolonialism" (co-option of marginalised knowledge; tokenism).
- Lived experience (themes): dual marginalisation / "double jeopardy" (race × gender); simultaneous invisibility and hypervisibility; stereotyping, accent- and appearance-based judgments, and microaggressions framed as humour; being treated as a "risky appointment".
- Relational capital (themes): exclusion from informal "closed shop" networks; scarce sponsorship/mentorship, especially across ethno-racial lines.
- Emergent outcomes: misrecognition and conditional standing; pressure to assimilate to dominant norms ("be a chameleon"); scaling back ambition or exiting; calls for accountability, targets, and reporting.

The paper's process logic is interpretive rather than causal: drawing on Elizabeth Anderson's (1999) relational equality, it argues that DEI initiatives grounded in distributive justice and the business case redirect attention to "what people have" (representation, pay equity, compliance) while leaving untouched the relational structures through which standing, credibility, and authority are negotiated. Because these foundational logics treat ERM women's difference as a liability to be managed or a value to be extracted, inclusion becomes a "relational burden" that requires marginalised women to adapt, advocate, and prove themselves within norms they had no part in shaping—reproducing the very exclusions it claims to remedy.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes two contributions to scholarship on business ethics and intersectionality. Empirically, it centres the underexamined experiences of ERM women in Australian workplaces, showing how inclusion efforts that claim to promote justice fail to secure equal standing or meaningful participation. Theoretically, it develops a relational egalitarian framework—drawing on Anderson's account of equality as equal standing and interpersonal justification—that reframes inclusion as a matter of relational structure rather than managerial strategy, shifting the analytic focus from access to standing, from presence to participation, and from outcomes to moral accountability.
practical_implication: The authors call for organisations to move beyond symbolic gestures and representational targets to interrogate how everyday norms around feedback, credibility, trust, sponsorship, and belonging are structured by unequal distributions of moral and epistemic authority. Concretely, respondents recommended holding leaders accountable for delivering on DEI policies, setting targets as an enabler of mindset change, and establishing mechanisms to measure, monitor, and report on the career progress of ethno-racially diverse women—while recognising ERM women as equal participants in reshaping leadership norms.
limitations: The study notes a notable over-reliance on approaches that treat ethno-racial diversity as a discrete, fixed demographic characteristic, and reports Australia-wide constraints of limited, inconsistent, and patchy data collection on ethno-racial representation. Fine-grained regional analysis (e.g., distinguishing Asian, South Asian, or South East Asian groups) was not possible due to sample-size constraints, and the data are not publicly available due to ethical restrictions.
future_research: Future research should continue to centre the perspectives of those most often excluded from organisational norm-setting—not as recipients of inclusion policies but as agents of epistemic and institutional transformation—and explore how inclusion frameworks can be reconstituted through relationally just practices (e.g., rethinking how feedback is given, how credibility is conferred, and how leadership is recognised). Further studies might examine other intersectional configurations such as migration status, linguistic background, or class position, where formal access to opportunity does not translate into relational equality.
abstract: This paper examines how ethno-racially marginalised women in Australian workplaces continue to be constrained by enduring organisational norms that privilege whiteness, masculinity, and Western/Anglo-cultural modes of participation. Further, we show how mainstream diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) initiatives reproduce exclusion and marginalisation in unintended and new ways by misrecognising or sidelining intersectional experiences. Through an intersectional reading of survey and interview data, we show how DEI initiatives frequently reflect narrow settings for equity that privilege dominant norms while rendering others invisible or “risky”. We engage with Elizabeth Anderson’s theory of relational equality to show that the shortcomings of these initiatives result not from idiosyncratic error or oversight but from the foundational logics that underpin them, including a business-case rationale and a default to distributive justice logics. We suggest that DEI should be reimagined as a practice of enabling equal participation, mutual recognition, and interpersonal justifications within organisational life. In doing so, the paper contributes both empirically and theoretically to current debates on workplace justice, intersectional equality, and the ethics of inclusion.
apa_citation: Groutsis, D., Han, C., O'Leary, J., D'Almada Remedios, R., & Kaabel, A. (2026). Whose justice? A turn to relational equality from the experiences of ethno-racially marginalised women in Australian workplaces. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06239-8

title: Green industrial policy and latecomer catch-up: A missed green window of opportunity for domestic solar PV module manufacturers in Indonesia
methods: Qualitative, longitudinal single-case study of the Indonesian solar PV module-manufacturing industry (2008-2023). Twenty semi-structured interviews (nine domestic manufacturers, government agencies, industry informants, international manufacturers), site visits, and triangulation with policy documents/reports; firm biographies and a coding matrix based on the conceptual framework, analyzed in NVivo across three industry-lifecycle phases.
industry: Solar PV module manufacturing (renewable energy)
units: Domestic solar PV module-manufacturing firms, government agencies, and industry informants
n_sample: 20 interviews (incl. 9 domestic manufacturing firms)
authors: Hansen, Ulrich Elmer; Nygaard, Ivan; Kadarusman, Yohanes Berenika
theories: firm-level catch-up of latecomer firms (Lee & Malerba, 2017; Bell & Figueiredo, 2012); green industrial policy (Altenburg & Rodrik, 2017; Rodrik, 2014); policy mixes in sustainability transitions (Rogge & Reichardt, 2016)
keywords: Catch-up; Domestic manufacturing; Green industrial policies; Industrial policy; Latecomer firms; Solar photovoltaics
research_question: How have industrial policies and sector-specific energy policies interacted over time to influence the initial entry and early-stage catch-up of domestic solar PV module manufacturers in Indonesia, and what firm-level inhibiting and encouraging factors shaped their catch-up trajectories?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / analytical elements: industrial-policy attributes (commitment, embeddedness, discipline) and sector-specific energy-policy attributes (ambition, temporal consistency, market formation), plus their alignment.
- Firm-level conditions: firm-specific attributes and decisions (size, age, ownership, prior competences, foreign linkages, managerial strategy) that differentiate intra-industry catch-up.
- Process / phases: Phase I 'Era of ferment' (2008-2012); Phase II 'Growth' (2013-2018); Phase III 'Shakeout' (2019-2023).
- Emergent outcomes: catch-up trajectories ranging from "coexistence" (viable firms alongside incumbents) to "aborted" catch-up (stagnation, exit, diversification).
- Boundary conditions: external factors (global Chinese cost dominance, import tariffs, anti-dumping rules) that the GIP framework cannot reduce to single causal factors.

The framework treats GIP as the configuration of, and interaction between, industrial and sector-specific policies that condition whether latecomer firms catch up. In Indonesia, a 'green window of opportunity' (low entry barriers around 2008) initially encouraged domestic entry, but inconsistent energy policy, weak market formation, local-content rules undercut by permitted imports, and overwhelming Chinese price competition eroded demand, producing an aborted catch-up by Phase III. A recent export-oriented strategy (Chinese-linked production on Batam Island) may seed a new coexistence trajectory, though its developmental linkages remain uncertain.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances the GIP literature by shifting from the prevailing meso-level (industry/country) lens toward a firm-oriented, micro-level perspective that surfaces intra-industry heterogeneity and firm agency. Its novelty lies in integrating three previously unconnected strands -- development-studies work on industrial policy, policy-mix/sustainability-transition research, and firm-level catch-up of latecomer firms -- into a single processual framework that links GIP to the early-stage catch-up of developing-country firms, countering the literature's empirical bias toward the exceptional case of China.
practical_implication: For policymakers in developing economies, the case shows that seizing a green window of opportunity requires sustained commitment, embedded coordination with firms, disciplined enforcement, and consistent, ambitious sector-specific policies that build genuine domestic market demand -- not local-content rules that can be circumvented by cheaper imports. It also highlights trade-offs between accelerating technology diffusion and nurturing domestic manufacturing, and warns that export/FDI-oriented strategies risk becoming isolated enclaves without linkages to the surrounding economy.
limitations: As a single-country, qualitative case study focused on the early "entry and initial growth" stage of catch-up, the findings are context-specific and not intended for statistical generalization; the authors instead pursue analytical generalization and theoretical development. Company names and interviewees were anonymized, and twelve of the contacted firms did not respond, so the firm-level evidence rests on the nine participating manufacturers.
future_research: The authors call for comparative applications of the framework to the same sector across countries (e.g., comparing Indonesia, Malaysia, and Vietnam) to verify its applicability, and for extensions to other sectors by combining industrial-policy analysis with the relevant adjacent policy domains. They also urge greater attention to the influence of external factors -- such as global production-network shifts, import tariffs, and anti-dumping regulation -- on latecomer catch-up under GIPs.
abstract: We apply the framework to study how industrial policies and energy policies have interacted and influenced the initial entry and early-stage catch-up of domestic solar PV module manufacturers in Indonesia in the period 2008–2023. Empirically, we draw on semi-structured interviews with representatives of domestic solar PV module manufacturers, industry informants and relevant government agencies. Based on the conceptual framework, we identify the inhibiting and encouraging factors influencing firm-level catch-up trajectories within three distinctive phases. We find that, while the catch-up trajectory of domestic solar PV module manufacturers resembled a path toward coexistence during the first and second phases, the third phase involved an aborted catch-up trajectory. However, a new catch-up trajectory toward coexistence may be emerging in relation to the recent establishment of export-oriented solar PV module production. Conceptually, the paper contributes to advancing the literature on GIPs by adopting a firm-oriented perspective and by seeking closer integration with research in development studies on the catching-up of latecomer firms.
apa_citation: Hansen, U. E., Nygaard, I., & Kadarusman, Y. B. (2026). Green industrial policy and latecomer catch-up: A missed green window of opportunity for domestic solar PV module manufacturers in Indonesia. *Research Policy*, 55(2), 105369. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.respol.2025.105369

title: Knowledge ecosystem emergence: Organizing participation, identity, and actorhood to bridge the governance void
methods: Single, in-depth longitudinal field case study of the Swedish High-Capacity Transport (HCT) knowledge ecosystem, analyzed through a moderate process research approach (Langley et al., 2013; Langley, 1999) and the Gioia methodology. Multi-method qualitative design triangulating 51 selected semi-structured interviews (from 65 conducted across three rounds, 45–120 min), 350+ hours of participant and non-participant observation (50 observations), and 112 archival items (5,100+ pages); data coded in NVivo via open/first-order codes, second-order themes, and aggregate dimensions (Grodal et al., 2021), with three temporal shifts (T1, T2, T3) traced to theoretical saturation.
industry: Cross-sectoral knowledge ecosystem spanning Sweden's transportation, forest, and logistics industries (sustainable high-capacity freight transport), with government agencies, vehicle OEMs, logistics operators, and universities
time_period: Phenomenon traced 2010–2023 (a dataset ranging over 10 years); interviews and observations conducted 2015–2023
units: One knowledge ecosystem (the HCT initiative), comprising 7 initial to 50+ participant organizations across public and private sectors
n_sample: Single case; 51 interviews selected for analysis (of 65 conducted), 50 observations (350+ hrs.), 112 archival items
authors: Hetemi, Ermal; Ritala, Paavo; Jerbrant, Anna
theories: discursive and performative practices (Thomas & Ritala, 2022); governance void (Jarvenpaa & Välikangas, 2016); meta-organization theory (Ahrne & Brunsson, 2005, 2011); organizationality (Dobusch & Schoeneborn, 2015; Schoeneborn et al., 2019); process ontology / process view (Langley et al., 2013)
keywords: Case study; Collective action; Knowledge ecosystem; Knowledge search and creation; Meta-organization; Organizationality; Process view; governance void
research_question: How do actors develop the necessary organizing elements to overcome initial governance voids in the knowledge ecosystem emergence phase? The study examines how a broad university-industry-academia collaboration in Sweden gradually developed an organizational structure while pursuing innovation in High-Capacity Transport.
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: the *governance void* — the lack of constituent elements of network governance (authority, conflict resolution, resource allocation) that loosely coupled, cross-sectoral knowledge ecosystems face at emergence.
- Three governance-void challenges (sequentially salient): resourcing, belonging, and collective action.
- Three emergent organizational elements (process phases): participation (T1, 2012–2016), identity (T2, 2016–2019), and actorhood (T3, 2019–2023).
- Meta-organizational practices (the means): discursive practices (contextualizing attention, discursive framing, goal consolidation) and performative practices (open-ended prospection, experimental prospection, influence structuring).
- Emergent outcomes: three knowledge search modes — problem search, integrative search, and solution search — that co-evolve with the organizing elements.
- Boundary condition: organization is never 'complete'; the governance void is only partially resolved given the underdefined nature of new knowledge as the shared goal.

The process model explains how an informal, loosely coupled group self-organizes "from the ground up": in each phase, participants jointly enact discursive and performative practices that act as "semi-structures" to bridge the focal challenge, build the corresponding organizational element, and unlock the next knowledge search mode. Participation establishes a "minimum viable" structure that mobilizes voluntary, in-kind resources and enables problem search; identity-building forges a shared purpose that enables integrative search; and actorhood consolidates goals and structures influence so the collective can act as a unified entity and pursue solution search. The ecosystem thereby graduates into a meta-organization that mitigates—but never fully closes—the initial governance void.
theoretical_contribution: The study develops a process model of knowledge ecosystem emergence and organizationality, showing how loosely coupled actors progressively construct the "minimum criteria" of an organization and thereby extend Dobusch and Schoeneborn's (2015) notion of organizationality to the meta-organizational level. It enriches meta-organization theory by characterizing how a meta-organization can bootstrap through informal social commitment (resourcing without a central budget) and by identifying belonging and collective-action challenges alongside resourcing. It also advances knowledge ecosystem scholarship by sequencing three knowledge search modes—introducing "integrative search" as an in-between mode that extends Järvi et al.'s (2018) two-step problem/solution logic.
practical_implication: Practitioners orchestrating cross-sector collaborations should build collective mobilization early—using open participation, asset-mapping canvases, and open-ended prospection to unlock in-kind resources without dedicated funding—then purposefully shape a shared identity through joint visioning and consistent communication. Policymakers should tailor interventions to the ecosystem's developmental phase: stable long-term commitments and low-commitment signals during the participation stage, then streamlined permits, agile/matching funding, regulatory sandboxes, and inter-agency coordination as identity and actorhood emerge, especially where public funding is contingent on demonstrable progress.
limitations: Reliance on a single-case study limits statistical generalizability; the emergent patterns and process model would benefit from validation across diverse innovation processes, and the proposed participation–identity–actorhood sequence reflects this case and should not be treated as deterministic. The analysis emphasizes collective dynamics and discursive/performative practices but offers limited insight into the micro-level behaviors of individual participants. The study context is shaped by a robust innovation policy and high public-private collaboration, so findings may not capture ecosystems in regions with weaker institutional support.
future_research: Future studies could examine how the participation–identity–actorhood trajectory varies across innovation contexts differing in institutional or regulatory dynamics and resource environments, and how variations in institutional maturity and political economy shape meta-organizational governance choices. Research could investigate how actors build effective "innovation-discourse infrastructure," incorporate the micro-level behaviors of participants, and—given the role of coopetition—give more explicit consideration to power imbalances and competing institutional logics during knowledge ecosystem emergence.
abstract: To address this gap, we theorize knowledge ecosystems as meta-organizations, examining how they gradually develop organizing elements that bridge the initial governance void. Empirically, we draw on an in-depth field case study of the High-Capacity Transport ecosystem in Sweden, demonstrating how three interrelated organizational elements—participation, identity, and actorhood—emerge through an iterative, yet broadly sequential process to resolve governance void challenges in resourcing, belonging, and collective action. Furthermore, we identify discursive and performative meta-organizational practices that enable the actors to collectively enact the aforementioned organizational elements and to engage in knowledge search. We further demonstrate how the organization of knowledge ecosystems is never ‘complete’ and the governance void remains only partially resolved, given the underdefined nature of new knowledge as the ecosystem's shared goal. Overall, our process model contributes to the theory, practice, and policy of knowledge ecosystem emergence and organizing.
apa_citation: Hetemi, E., Ritala, P., & Jerbrant, A. (2026). Knowledge ecosystem emergence: Organizing participation, identity, and actorhood to bridge the governance void. *Research Policy*, 55(1), 105365. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.respol.2025.105365

title: Trademarks and the innovativeness of social enterprises
methods: Multimethod exploratory design triangulating (1) a text-based website analysis of 706 German social enterprises (keyword web crawler building two innovativeness indices), analyzed with seemingly unrelated regressions, and (2) a survey of 108 SEs analyzed with logistic regression plus descriptive analysis of trademarking motives and illustrative case vignettes.
industry: Social enterprises (market-driven, mission-oriented organizations spanning for-profit and non-profit legal forms, across NACE-coded industries)
time_period: Website crawl July/August 2021; survey conducted April-June 2022 (trademarks registered up to 15 years before crawling)
units: Social enterprises (organizations)
n_sample: 706 SEs in the text-based website analysis; 108 SEs completed the survey (15.3% response rate)
authors: Hirschmann, Mirko; Block, Joern H.; Krlev, Gorgi
theories: organizational hybridity / dual-objective organizations (Fosfuri et al., 2016; Santos et al., 2015); trademarks as indicators of organizational innovativeness (Block et al., 2015; Flikkema et al., 2019; Mendonça et al., 2004)
keywords: Innovativeness; Measurement; Social enterprise; Survey; Text analysis; Trademarks
research_question: The paper poses two questions: (1) "To what extent do trademarks, if used at all, relate to the innovativeness of social enterprises?" and (2) "What are the motives of SEs to register (or not to register) trademarks?"
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Metropolis location, company age, number of employees (size categories), social impact certification, legal form (non-profit), number of patent filings, website size (log), 12 industry dummies, 17 SDG dummies
- Survey constructs: 14 motives to register trademarks and 11 motives not to register, rated on 1-5 scales

The paper argues that because SEs frequently innovate in organizational processes, business models, and services rather than in science-based, patentable artifacts, traditional indicators (patents, R&D) fail to capture their innovativeness; trademarks—being apt at signaling creative and service innovation—can fill this gap. Empirically, more registered trademarks correlate with higher website-displayed innovativeness and with innovative main products/services; each additional trademark raises the odds of being innovative by a factor of 3.6. The survey reveals SEs register trademarks chiefly to protect innovations and brands, to pursue a particular form of market orientation, and—distinctively—to protect and scale their social impact, while a minority deliberately forgo trademarks to invite imitation and scale impact indirectly.
theoretical_contribution: The study introduces trademarks as a new, activity-based, readily available IP indicator for identifying innovative SEs within the heterogeneous SE population, advancing social-innovation measurement at the organizational level rather than the macro level. It shows that the trademark-innovativeness link holds across legal forms and firm ages (challenging legal form and startup status as differentiators) and that SEs combine innovation-protection with innovation-promotion and impact-scaling motives—a combination new to the trademark and hybridity literatures—thereby demonstrating that market orientation and social mission need not be opposed.
practical_implication: The findings give SEs a tool to benchmark their innovativeness against peers in their industry and clarify why and how other SEs use trademarks strategically. For policymakers and impact investors, trademarks offer an accessible signal to detect innovative, scalable SEs during screening, and the results inform the broader policy debate on how intellectual property rights can amplify social benefit.
limitations: Validating the trademark measure against website content is imperfect because websites are communicative devices that may present organizations as innovative for branding reasons, and because temporal discrepancies between trademark registrations and website snapshots cannot be fully ruled out despite robustness tests. The study is also confined to German SEs and to the SE population, limiting generalizability across countries and across other impact-oriented organizational forms.
future_research: The authors call for applying the website-based approach in other countries and for cross-country comparisons using international trademark filings; for longitudinal designs that track trademark-innovativeness associations over time; for deeper investigation of SEs' trademarking motives and links to specific innovation stages; and for comparative studies contrasting SEs with foundations, cooperatives, and mainstream firms to test how trademark use relates to innovativeness across organizational forms and goals.
abstract: Additionally, we investigate the underlying reasons SEs choose to register (or not register) trademarks, recognizing that trademarks can serve various purposes. Our findings reveal that innovative SEs are more likely to file trademarks, primarily to protect their innovations and associated brands, pursuing market orientation, and scaling their social impact. These insights deepen our understanding of the heterogeneity among SEs and contribute to the growing conversation on the role of intellectual property rights in creating positive effects for society.
apa_citation: Hirschmann, M., Block, J. H., & Krlev, G. (2026). Trademarks and the innovativeness of social enterprises. *Research Policy*, 55(1), 105342. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.respol.2025.105342

title: Rewarding Ethical Leadership: Corporate LGBTQ+ Friendliness and CEO Compensation
methods: Panel OLS regressions of CEO total compensation on firm-level LGBTQ+ friendliness (HRC CEI score) with a lead-lag specification and fixed effects; identification via propensity score matching, entropy balancing, instrumental-variable (2SLS) estimation using state-level cultural tightness, and a triple-difference quasi-natural experiment exploiting staggered state non-discrimination acts.
industry: Publicly traded U.S. firms across industries (Compustat/ExecuComp universe; S&P 500 and Fortune 500 coverage of HRC Corporate Equality Index)
units: Firm-year observations (609 unique firms)
n_sample: 4,648 firm-year observations
authors: Hossain, Ashrafee; Masum, Abdullah-Al; Saadi, Samir
theories: agency theory (Jensen & Meckling, 1976); compensating wage differential theory (Rosen, 1986); signaling theory (Spence, 1973); stakeholder theory (Freeman, 1984); stewardship theory (Donaldson & Davis, 1991)
keywords: CEO compensation; Corporate governance; Ethical leadership; LGBTQ+ friendliness
research_question: Do firms reward ethical leadership by linking corporate LGBTQ+ friendliness to higher CEO compensation, and does this relation reflect a genuine ethical commitment to integrity and transparency rather than managerial rent extraction?
mechanism_summary: The paper argues that boards reward CEOs who advance LGBTQ+ inclusiveness because such leadership signals ethical and strategic value that enhances stakeholder confidence and long-term firm value. Drawing on stakeholder and stewardship theories (supplemented by compensating wage differential, signaling, and agency perspectives), inclusiveness is framed as a costly, responsibility-laden behavior that boards compensate as recognition rather than as the product of managerial entrenchment. The relation strengthens where ethical foundations are already institutionalized and where generationally diverse boards are more attuned to ethical leadership, while CEO-power proxies do not drive the effect.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the CSR-executive pay literature by isolating LGBTQ+ inclusiveness, an underexplored CSR dimension, and showing it commands a CEO pay premium that reflects ethical recognition rather than opportunistic rent seeking. It advances ethical-governance research by demonstrating a spillover effect whereby firms with established ethical foundations are more likely to reward inclusive leadership, and by showing that board generational diversity reinforces corporate accountability and inclusivity through executive pay incentives.
practical_implication: For firms, the results emphasize the strategic value of promoting LGBTQ+ inclusiveness as a factor that shapes executive incentives, not only employee engagement and reputation. For policymakers and investors, the findings inform debates over DEI initiatives and corporate governance, suggesting regulatory frameworks can encourage transparent reporting on diversity metrics and executive compensation, and aligning with the UN SDGs (notably Goal 10 and Goal 5).
limitations: The measurement of LGBTQ+ friendliness, while robust, inevitably simplifies a multidimensional construct. The focus on U.S. firms may restrict the generalizability of the results to other institutional and cultural contexts.
future_research: Future research could enrich this line of inquiry by conducting cross-country analyses and investigating additional mechanisms through which inclusivity shapes governance, managerial incentives, and firm performance.
abstract: We examine whether firms reward ethical leadership by linking corporate LGBTQ+ friendliness to CEO compensation. Using comprehensive data from publicly traded firms from the United States, we find a strong positive relation, indicating that boards actively incentivize leaders who advance inclusivity. The effect is concentrated in firms with high integrity, transparent reporting, and responsible environmental conduct, consistent with stakeholder and stewardship theories and inconsistent with rent-extraction motive. Moreover, boards with younger-generation directors amplify this relation, reflecting evolving governance norms that value ethical leadership. Overall, our evidence positions workplace inclusivity as a strategic asset that shapes executive pay design and reinforces responsible corporate governance.
apa_citation: Hossain, A., Masum, A.-A., & Saadi, S. (2026). Rewarding ethical leadership: Corporate LGBTQ+ friendliness and CEO compensation. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-026-06250-7

title: Problematizing the role of artificial intelligence in hiring and organizational inequalities: A multidisciplinary review
methods: Hybrid review combining a scoping methodology (Arksey & O'Malley, 2005) and a problematizing review (Alvesson & Sandberg, 2020). An initial search across Google Scholar, ProQuest, Sociological Abstracts, SSCI, Sage Journals, and SSRN returned 389 publications; blind screening (Rayyan) yielded 212 highly relevant items mapped across four disciplinary clusters (computing science; HRMOS; social sciences; law), from which a 'supercorpus' of 97 readings was deeply, reflexively re-read to problematize underlying assumptions.
industry: Multidisciplinary scholarship on AI in hiring and recruitment (cross-disciplinary; not industry-specific), spanning computing and data sciences, human resource/management/organization studies, social sciences, and law
country: Predominantly Global North (US- and EU-focused scholarship); the review notes its own Global North bias and the exclusion of Global South perspectives
time_period: Contemporary algorithmic-hiring scholarship; engages regulatory developments through the EU's 2024 AI Act and a 2025 proposed US state-level AI-regulation moratorium
units: Academic publications (journal articles, conference proceedings, books) on the intersection of AI, hiring, and inequality
n_sample: 212 highly relevant publications screened; final problematizing 'supercorpus' of 97 readings
authors: Hughes, Karen D.; Konnikov, Alla; Denier, Nicole; Hu, Yang
theories: black box society / opacity (Pasquale, 2015); inequality regimes (Acker, 2006); surveillance capitalism (Zuboff, 2019)
keywords: employment law; human resource management; inequality; new technology; personnel selection; recruitment
research_question: What are the implications of the growing use of AI in recruitment and hiring for organizational inequalities? Specifically, the review asks (RQ1) what key questions and concerns are being examined across disciplines about the role of AI in organizational hiring, and (RQ2) what underlying assumptions and approaches guide each discipline's treatment of algorithmic hiring and inequality.
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: AI in hiring both echoes and extends Acker's (2006) "inequality regimes"; whereas inequality-producing mechanisms historically operate invisibly until made visible and de-legitimated, AI moves in the opposite direction — gaining near-ubiquitous legitimacy while becoming more invisible as an opaque "black box."
- Key constructs: an "algorithmically-mediated inequality regime" characterized by heightened algorithmic invisibility and pervasive, unchallenged algorithmic legitimacy; a "chain of knowledge" (multidisciplinary expertise) and a "chain of responsibility" (multi-stakeholder accountability).
- Disciplinary clusters problematized: computing science (CS) frames inequality as a technical "bias" problem; HRMOS frames AI as a solution to human bias and theorizes human-AI collaboration; social sciences (SS) treat inequality as systemic and contextual; legal scholarship (LS) debates the adequacy of existing law and proactive regulation.
- Boundary conditions: regulatory responses lag and are contested, varying across global, national, regional, and local contexts (e.g., the US market-/complaint-based model versus the EU rights-/preventative-based model).

The review argues that the dominance of CS and its language of "bias"/"debiasing" diffuses a "technosolution" paradigm that narrows attention to individual decision-makers and technical fixes, obscuring the structural pervasiveness of inequality. By extending Acker's framework into the digital era, the authors show how AI's opacity and growing legitimacy conceal and reproduce inequalities within routine hiring — a foundational gatekeeping process — while deflecting accountability across employers, vendors, developers, and platforms.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends Acker's (2006) framework of "inequality regimes" into the digital era by conceptualizing AI-transformed organizations as sites of "algorithmically-mediated inequality regimes," in which inequality is concealed further through algorithmic invisibility and the growing legitimacy of AI solutions. It reframes AI not as a neutral tool or a solution to human bias but as a structural mechanism that participates in and transforms organizational inequality, and it introduces the paired notions of an interdisciplinary "chain of knowledge" and a multi-stakeholder "chain of responsibility."
practical_implication: The authors advocate operationalizing the "chain of responsibility" across employers, algorithm designers, unions, regulators, and policy makers, since no single stakeholder can address AI-mediated hiring inequality alone. Employers can pursue participatory design and voluntary/independent audits, build cross-functional capacity (HR, designers, regulators), and embed fairness insights into hiring practices, while legal practitioners and civil-society groups push for context-tailored regulation and public accountability (e.g., using resources such as The AI Policy Sourcebook).
limitations: The review is limited to English-language publications, reflecting a Global North bias and under-representing Global South perspectives. Its hybrid scoping-plus-problematizing methodology does not include explicit quality assessment, and publication practices vary across fields (e.g., CS often publishes in peer-reviewed conference proceedings rather than indexed journals). The analysis also focuses on hiring rather than other AI-altered HR processes.
future_research: Future work should continue theorizing AI's role in perpetuating inequality (including whether AI is a distinct structural mechanism or embeds in existing ones), prioritize comparative work across diverse jurisdictions and the Global South, and adopt systematic reviews with explicit quality measures. The authors call for qualitative and ethnographic studies of AI's creation, use, and regulation; multi-level (micro-meso-macro) and contextualized analyses; and research on the governance of algorithmic hiring and other AI-altered HR decisions (e.g., promotions, compensation).
abstract: What are the implications of the growing use of artificial intelligence (AI) in recruitment and hiring for organizational inequalities? While advocates suggest that AI is a groundbreaking tool that can enhance hiring precision, efficiency, diversity and fit, critics raise serious concerns around bias, fairness, and privacy. This review article critically advances this debate by drawing on diverse scholarship across computing and data sciences; human resource, management, and organization studies; social sciences; and law. Using a hybrid review approach that combines scoping and problematizing review methods, we examine the implications of algorithmic hiring for organizational inequalities. Our review identifies a multidisciplinary discussion marked by asymmetries in how key concerns are conceptualized; a clear and heightened potential for AI to conceal
apa_citation: Hughes, K. D., Konnikov, A., Denier, N., & Hu, Y. (2026). Problematizing the role of artificial intelligence in hiring and organizational inequalities: A multidisciplinary review. *Human Relations*, 79(2), 246-278. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187267251403902

title: When Family Firm Reputation Backfires: The Role of Controllability in Consumer Responses to Negative CSR Incidents
methods: Two randomized, longitudinal, between-subjects online experiments (Prolific). Study 1 (N=288) tests mediation by perceived controllability via Baron-Kenny and PROCESS bootstrapping; Study 2 (N=246) uses a 2x2 design testing controllability as a moderator with z-standardized OLS regressions and simple-slope analyses.
industry: Stimulus was a fictitious U.S.-based tire manufacturer ('Tiroxo'); participants were general U.S. consumers.
units: Individual consumers (online experiment participants)
n_sample: 288 (Study 1); 246 (Study 2)
authors: Jaufenthaler, Philipp; Schroll, Roland; Kellermanns, Franz W.
theories: attribution theory (Folkes, 1984; Weiner, 2000)
keywords: attribution theory; consumer perceptions; corporate social responsibility (CSR); family firms; reputation
research_question: How do negative CSR incidents shape consumer attitudes toward family firms (vs. non-family firms), and what role does incident controllability play in this relationship?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on attribution theory, the authors argue that consumer responses to a negative CSR incident hinge on the controllability attributed to it. When controllability is unknown, the heightened trustworthiness and moral responsibility consumers ascribe to family firms lead them to infer lower controllability — a "benefit of the doubt" effect — preserving more favorable attitudes than for non-family firms (Study 1: full mediation through perceived controllability). When controllability is known, low controllability neutralizes this advantage (attitudes converge), while high controllability reverses it: deliberate misconduct sharply violates the elevated moral expectations and humanized perceptions attached to family firms, amplifying condemnation and producing more negative attitudes toward family firms than non-family firms (Study 2: significant ownership × controllability interaction).
theoretical_contribution: The paper is the first to document a reversal effect in which family ownership status turns into a liability, yielding significantly more negative consumer attitudes toward family firms than non-family firms after controllable negative CSR incidents. It identifies controllability attribution as the mechanism explaining why and when attitude differences persist, converge, or reverse, and extends the application of attribution theory in family business research by showing that perceived family/non-family distinctions shape both the inferences and the consequences of controllability attributions.
practical_implication: Family firms should treat their status as both an asset and a liability in CSR crises. When controllability is unknown, emphasizing family ownership can support an accidental-incident framing and preserve favorable attitudes; when low controllability is evident, doing so adds little value. When an incident is controllable, firms should avoid emphasizing family ownership at any stage and instead invest in robust internal controls, ethical accountability, and transparent post-incident communication to avoid disproportionate backlash.
limitations: The studies used specific, internally caused negative CSR scenarios (a minimum-wage breach) with a single fictitious firm, so the controllability dynamics may differ for other incident types (e.g., corruption vs. environmental harm) that vary in perceived controllability. The research focused only on the controllability dimension of attribution and on a single Western (U.S.) context, leaving stability, locus, cross-cultural variation, and richer forms of family involvement unexamined.
future_research: Future work could examine other incident types and audiences, additional attributional dimensions (stability, locus), and how repeated incidents affect the benefit-of-the-doubt effect over time. Researchers could study downstream reactions (accountability, anger, moral hypocrisy), antecedents of controllability perceptions, alternative outcomes (purchase intention, willingness to pay, brand loyalty), varying forms of family involvement and cultural contexts, and recovery and apology strategies for rebuilding trust after controllable incidents.
abstract: We apply attribution theory to examine how family ownership status shapes consumer attitudes toward companies after negative corporate social responsibility incidents, focusing on incident controllability. Two longitudinal experiments reveal that, when controllability is unknown, consumers infer lower controllability for family firms and thus hold more favorable attitudes toward them than non-family firms. However, when controllability is known, low-controllability incidents lead to similar attitudes across firm types, whereas high-controllability incidents reverse the effect, resulting in more negative attitudes toward family firms than non-family firms. These findings challenge the prevailing assumption that family firms consistently benefit from their reputation and inform stakeholder communication strategies.
apa_citation: Jaufenthaler, P., Schroll, R., & Kellermanns, F. W. (2026). When family firm reputation backfires: The role of controllability in consumer responses to negative CSR incidents. *Entrepreneurship Theory and Practice*. https://doi.org/10.1177/10422587251406322
