title: Carbon Emissions Reduction or ‘Relocation’? A Study on the Corporate Response to Climate Change and the Role of Environmental Law
methods: Panel regression on a balanced firm-year panel; firm-level 'abnormal carbon emissions reduction' (ACER) measured as the residual of an Asset4 emissions-score model; generalised estimating equations (GEE) with Huber-White robust standard errors; robustness via pooled regression, lead-lag, Granger causality, and Heckman two-stage selection.
industry: Publicly listed U.S. firms across nine GICS sectors (energy, materials, industrials, consumer, health care, IT, communication services, real estate), excluding financial-sector firms
units: Firms (firm-year observations)
n_sample: 360 firms; 2,495 firm-year observations
authors: Pan, Lipeng; Li, Yongqing; Fan, Hanlu; Tang, Qingliang
theories: legitimacy cost management framework (Jeong & Kim, 2019); legitimacy theory (Suchman, 1995)
keywords: abnormal carbon emissions reduction; corporate carbon emissions; environmental law
research_question: How do external pressures—specifically environmental law enforcement—influence firms' carbon relocation (leakage) behaviour, and what role does a firm's level of CSR play in driving or mitigating that behaviour?
mechanism_summary: Framed by legitimacy theory and the legitimacy cost management framework of Jeong and Kim (2019), firms under stringent environmental enforcement face a cost-benefit choice among doing nothing, costly green innovation, or low-cost carbon relocation. Because relocation minimises legitimacy costs while preserving the appearance of compliance, rational managers favour it over expensive technological upgrades. Higher-CSR firms, being more sensitive to legitimacy and reputational risk, are even more inclined to substitute symbolic relocation for substantive reduction—so CSR steepens rather than dampens the enforcement-to-relocation relationship.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends legitimacy theory to corporate carbon-management decisions, showing how firms trade off 'social legitimacy' against 'economic efficiency' and may choose carbon transfer over genuine emission reductions under external pressure. It reveals a dual-edged effect of CSR—challenging the view of CSR as a purely positive environmental force by exposing its strategic use in legitimacy management. It also introduces and validates the firm-level ACER indicator, filling a methodological gap in the micro-level quantification of corporate carbon leakage.
practical_implication: Policymakers should build more comprehensive, technology-driven carbon-footprint tracking tools covering global value chains, expand the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism through multilateral cooperation, harmonise international environmental regulations, and pair punitive measures with economic incentives and technical support for green transformation. Managers should align CSR with substantive environmental performance and strengthen internal governance so regulatory pressure becomes a driver of genuine innovation rather than symbolic compliance.
limitations: The sample comprises large, highly internationalised, well-resourced publicly listed U.S. firms, so conclusions may not generalise to SMEs or firms in emerging economies, or across institutional and industry contexts. The multiyear data still struggle to fully capture the dynamic process by which firms adjust carbon strategies as policies evolve. The study focuses on the ELP–CSR interaction without examining other mechanisms such as corporate governance structures, managerial cognition, or stakeholder engagement.
future_research: Future work could extend the analysis to SMEs and emerging-economy firms and use cross-country or cross-industry comparisons to test robustness across institutional conditions. Longitudinal tracking or mixed-method designs combining panel data with in-depth case studies could capture firms' adaptive evolution under varying regulatory pressures. Researchers could also incorporate governance, cognition, and stakeholder mechanisms, and examine cross-regional policy coordination (e.g., CBAM, regional carbon markets) and the effect of policy uncertainty on long-term strategic adjustments.
abstract: Reducing the carbon footprint left by humans is a vital challenge for companies in our fight to combat climate change. However, concerns persist over an alarming trend where companies appear to be merely ‘shifting’ their carbon emissions from domestic markets to other jurisdictions. Consequently, it is ‘leaking’ carbon rather than genuinely reducing emissions. Through an analysis of over 360 U.S. firms and framed by legitimacy theory, this study finds that companies facing increasingly intense pressure to legitimise their operations under stricter environmental regulations are more likely to opt for ‘relocating’ their carbon emissions instead of implementing tangible reductions. Intriguingly, corporate social responsibility (CSR) fails to curtail this practice; instead, it amplifies it. These results underscore the importance for environmental policymakers and academia to recognise that, as environmental regulations become more stringent, relocating carbon emissions may become increasingly common unless companies are incentivised economically to genuinely reduce their CO2 output.
apa_citation: Pan, L., Li, Y., Fan, H., & Tang, Q. (2026). Carbon emissions reduction or ‘relocation’? A study on the corporate response to climate change and the role of environmental law. *Organization & Environment*, 39(1), 79–111. https://doi.org/10.1177/10860266251405064

title: Datafied Selves at Work: Ethical Boundaries of Surveillance in People Analytics
methods: Conceptual, normative theory-building paper; constructs a four-pillar ethical framework through theoretical synthesis of deontological ethics, virtue ethics, and stakeholder theory, supported by illustrative tables, a RACI matrix, and a visual framework. No empirical data collected or analyzed.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Pawirosumarto, Suharno; Kurniawan, Buyung
theories: deliberative / discursive ethics (García-Marzá & Calvo, 2024); deontological ethics (Kant, 1996); stakeholder theory (Freeman, 2010); virtue ethics / phronesis (Aristotelian tradition; MacIntyre, 2013)
keywords: Algorithmic ethics; Algorithmic governance; Datafied self; Ethical governance; Human resource management; People Analytics; Workplace surveillance
research_question: What are the ethical boundaries of algorithmic surveillance in People Analytics, and how can a normative framework reposition HR as a moral and deliberative agent that upholds dignity, justice, and trust in datafied workplaces? The paper asks not "what works" but "what is right" in the design and governance of HR analytics.
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: People Analytics is not a neutral instrumental tool but a moral practice; any responsible use must uphold boundary conditions grounded in justice, dignity, and human agency, shifting HR from "what can be measured" to "what ought to be measured."
- Key constructs / pillars: Data Sovereignty (individual control rights over personal data), Algorithmic Fairness (non-discrimination, bias auditing), Informed Consent (renewable, dialogical participation), and Ethical Accountability (shared, traceable governance). The pillars are presented as mutually reinforcing moral boundaries.
- Theoretical anchors: Kantian deontology grounds Data Sovereignty and Informed Consent (humanity as an end, never merely a means); Aristotelian virtue ethics / phronesis grounds Ethical Accountability (HR leaders as moral exemplars); stakeholder theory grounds Algorithmic Fairness (fairness as participatory dialogue among affected parties).
- Boundary zones: consent, purpose limitation / function creep, emotional and relational labor, and accountability gaps / algorithmic opacity—pressure points where ethical failure is most likely, with salience varying by industry and governance setting.
- Emergent harms addressed: "datafied selves," epistemic injustice (testimonial and hermeneutical), power asymmetries, and purpose drift.

The paper develops its normative model by mapping general AI-ethics principles (beneficence, justice, autonomy, accountability) onto context-specific HR pillars, then layering a discursive/deliberative-ethics requirement (García-Marzá & Calvo, 2024) that consent and fairness be sustained through ongoing communicative processes in which employees can question, contest, or withdraw. Operationalization is proposed through ethics-by-design, HR data charters, inclusive governance, ethics boards, and a RACI accountability matrix across HR, IT, Legal, and Vendors.
theoretical_contribution: The paper synthesizes three non-interchangeable moral-philosophical traditions—Kantian deontology, Aristotelian virtue ethics, and stakeholder theory—into an integrated four-pillar normative framework that fills an identified gap: the absence of a robust ethical theory of surveillance and consent in digital HR environments. It reframes People Analytics as a moral practice and HR as a deliberative moral agent, advancing business ethics scholarship from descriptive utility toward a critical, dignity-centered account of algorithmic people management.
practical_implication: HR leaders, scholars, and policymakers should treat ethics as the guiding architecture (not an afterthought) of People Analytics by implementing the four pillars through concrete mechanisms: ethics-by-design, renewable and dialogical consent, purpose-specific protocols against function creep, HR Data Charters, inclusive/participatory governance, People Analytics ethics boards, and a RACI accountability matrix clarifying HR–IT–Legal–Vendor responsibilities.
limitations: The authors explicitly state the work is conceptual and does not involve empirical data collection or analysis, so the normative model is proposed but not yet empirically validated or refined. The paper itself frames empirical validation across contexts as outstanding and notes that ethical frameworks risk remaining aspirational and detached from operational realities absent coordinated implementation.
future_research: The paper calls for empirical work examining how the four pillars manifest across organizational contexts, industries, and cultural settings (via qualitative case studies, comparative policy analyses, and new measurement instruments for ethical maturity); longitudinal study of whether ethics-by-design and participatory governance yield outcomes such as increased trust and perceived justice; interrogation of the unintended consequences of ethical frameworks; and critical scholarship engaging deliberative ethics and collective data rights, questioning the epistemological limits of datafication in HRM.
abstract: In the age of algorithmic decision-making, People Analytics has emerged as a powerful tool for enhancing efficiency and strategic alignment in human resource management (HRM). Yet, its growing influence also introduces profound ethical concerns—particularly regarding surveillance, consent, fairness, and employee autonomy. This paper offers a normative framework that critically examines the ethical boundaries of People Analytics by integrating four foundational pillars: data sovereignty, algorithmic fairness, informed consent, and ethical accountability. Drawing from deontological ethics, virtue ethics, and stakeholder theory, the study constructs a conceptual model that positions HR not merely as a technological function, but as a moral actor responsible for upholding dignity, justice, and trust in datafied workplaces. The paper further explores how People Analytics reshapes employees into "datafied selves," enabling new forms of epistemic injustice and organizational control. A visual framework and practical tables support the translation of ethical theory into applied HR governance. Through this lens, the paper identifies four critical boundary zones—consent, purpose limitation, emotional labor, and accountability gaps—where ethical failure is most likely to occur. Finally, the study provides strategic recommendations, including ethics-by-design principles, inclusive governance, and data charters, while proposing a research agenda to empirically validate and refine the normative model. This contribution urges HR leaders, scholars, and policymakers to treat ethics not as an afterthought but as the guiding architecture of algorithmic people management.
apa_citation: Pawirosumarto, S., & Kurniawan, B. (2026). Datafied selves at work: Ethical boundaries of surveillance in people analytics. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-026-06272-1

title: Carbon Pricing versus Green Finance
methods: Theoretical paper: a unified dynamic general-equilibrium model of carbon pricing and green finance, with calibrations using firm-level emissions (Trucost) and market-value (CRSP/Compustat) data for 2021 to illustrate magnitudes.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Pedersen, Lasse Heje
theories: Pigouvian externality taxation; general-equilibrium asset pricing; social cost of carbon (Nordhaus, 2019)
keywords: ESG investing; carbon pricing; cost of capital; green finance; social cost of carbon; stranded assets; sustainable discount rate
research_question: Can green finance accomplish the same as a carbon tax, and if so, how does a social cost of carbon (e.g., 43$/tCO2) translate into a change in firms' cost of capital, which is measured in different units?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Carbon pricing targets the cost of emissions while green finance targets the cost of capital of emitting firms; a unified general-equilibrium model translates between the two.
- Key construct — sustainable discount rate: the "normal" cost of capital r plus an externality (carbon burden) rate equal to the "missing" carbon tax (St+1 − τit+1) times the firm's emission-to-value ratio.
- Boundary conditions: green finance can implement the social optimum only when the cost of capital can be controlled, firms can commit to their future emissions, and stranded assets are not a severe problem.
- Implementations: emission-based capital taxes, or a voluntary system in which investors internalize emissions as a "negative dividend"; scope 1+2 variants, cash subsidies, and subsidized discount rates for green electricity address stranded assets.

When the carbon price is below the social cost of carbon, the social optimum can still be reached by setting each firm's discount rate to its sustainable discount rate, which makes the firm bear a capital cost equivalent to an implicit carbon tax of St+1 − τit+1; adding the explicit tax τit+1, the firm faces a total carbon tax equal to the social cost St+1. This works because the construction forces market value to equal social value in equilibrium, so a value-maximizing firm chooses the socially optimal investment and pollution policy. The same discount rate also characterizes the return investors require when they internalize untaxed externalities, whether motivated by ESG preferences or emission-based capital taxes.
theoretical_contribution: The paper unifies two previously distinct literatures—the economics literature on carbon pricing and the finance literature on ESG investing and sustainable finance regulation—into a single general-equilibrium framework. Its central new result is the sustainable (externality-adjusted) discount rate, which gives a tractable translation between the cost of pollution and the cost of capital and shows precisely when and how green finance can or cannot reverse-engineer a Pigouvian carbon tax.
practical_implication: A 43$/tCO2 carbon tax corresponds to only a 0.19 pp increase in the value-weighted average firm's cost of capital but a 3.2 pp increase for the average U.S. brown electricity firm, providing a calibrated benchmark for evaluating green-finance policy. Because discount rates are hard to shift voluntarily and face distributional and stranded-asset problems, regions able to impose a carbon tax (e.g., Europe) have a clearer path to a green transition than those relying on burdensome sustainable-finance regulation.
limitations: Implementing the sustainable discount rate requires firms and investors to use it as a forward-looking incentive scheme, which is challenging because the cost of capital depends on the collective actions of many investors with limited regulatory control, and firms must credibly commit to future emissions. The voluntary green-finance channel is undone in part by purely financial investors, cannot sustain the infinite discount rates implied by stranded assets, and is susceptible to greenwashing and corporate restructuring that games sustainability scores.
future_research: The author notes extensions to long-term sustainable discount rates for a green transition, to uncertainty (state-dependent emission commitments), to voluntary carbon-offset and carbon-allowance markets, and to firms' green-versus-brown technology choices, and points to related work (Chittaro et al., 2025; Pedersen, 2026) generalizing the framework with richer input-output structure, short-sale constraints, and multiple externalities.
abstract: Green finance—including environmental, social, and governance investing and sustainable finance regulations—is widespread, but can it substitute for carbon pricing in fighting climate change? In a unified model, I show that (i) when carbon prices reflect the social cost of carbon, green finance should not be used; (ii) when carbon prices are too low, green finance can implement the social optimum if each firm's cost of capital can be set to its sustainable discount rate, which increases with the ratio of carbon emissions to firm value. I provide calibrations, analyze stranded assets, and present implementations through subsidies or preferential financing for green firms.
apa_citation: Pedersen, L. H. (2026). Carbon pricing versus green finance. *The Journal of Finance*, *81*(2), 561–602. https://doi.org/10.1111/jofi.70022

title: The non-green effects of going green: Local environmental and economic consequences of lithium extraction in Chile
journal: Journal of Environmental Economics and Management
methods: Quasi-experimental causal inference. Difference-in-differences on groundwater wells (2014 cutoff) plus a continuous exposure design (EV-sales shift / inverse distance to extraction wells) for satellite-measured NDVI, nighttime-light radiance, and human-settlement density; pixel and locality-by-year fixed effects; Conley-Hsiang spatial-and-serial-correlation-adjusted p-values; placebo salt flat and falsification-shift tests.
industry: Lithium (brine) mining around the Atacama Salt Flat, Chile
time_period: 2010-2020 (groundwater 2010-2019; vegetation and nighttime lights 2013-2020; human settlements 2010-2020)
units: 300m x 300m / 500m x 500m / 100m x 100m satellite pixels across 14 localities; SQM and DGA monitoring wells
n_sample: Up to 125,892 pixel-year observations (human settlements); 117,205 (NDVI); 196 SQM + 109 DGA wells
authors: Peñaloza-Pacheco, Leonardo; Triantafyllou, Vaios; Martínez, Gonzalo
theories: Roy-Borjas migration framework (Roy, 1951; Borjas, 1987); consumer-producer household model (Aragón and Rud, 2016)
keywords: Chile; Environment; Lithium; Vegetation; Water
research_question: What are the local environmental and economic effects of lithium extraction in the Atacama Salt Flat (ASF) in Chile, driven by the global push to adopt electric vehicles? The paper asks whether and how lithium extraction affects water availability, vegetation, economic activity, and population dynamics in surrounding communities.
mechanism_summary: The paper argues that the water-intensive nature of lithium brine extraction depletes groundwater around the ASF, lowering the total factor productivity of subsistence agriculture on which local (largely indigenous) communities depend. Within a Roy-Borjas-type consumer-producer household framework, this reduces the utility of staying relative to migrating, so falling agricultural output translates into reduced economic activity and population outflow. A one-SD increase in exposure reduces NDVI by 0.4%, nighttime lights by 2.6%, and human settlements by 1.5%, while groundwater fell up to 15% post-2014.
theoretical_contribution: The paper provides, for the first time, causal (rather than purely correlational/observational) evidence on the environmental and socioeconomic effects of lithium extraction, isolating extraction from confounding climate phenomena via an exogenous EV-demand shift and granular fixed effects. It shows that adverse local consequences of resource extraction can arise through water depletion rather than the pollution channel emphasized by prior work (Aragón and Rud, 2016), and contributes to the literature on "North-South displacement" externalities of developed-country environmental regulation onto developing economies.
practical_implication: Cost-benefit and supply-chain sustainability assessments of EV adoption should internalize the local water, vegetation, and livelihood costs borne by rural and indigenous communities around extraction sites. Policymakers in the Lithium Triangle (Chile, Argentina, Bolivia) should anticipate and compensate these communities, recognizing indigenous peoples' special legal status and the ecological value of the salt flat.
limitations: The analysis relies on remote-sensing proxies (NDVI for agricultural activity, nighttime lights for economic activity) rather than direct granular agricultural or income data, and the mechanism evidence on agricultural decline is descriptive and suggestive. The estimates capture net effects, including any company/government transfers and community-engagement efforts, so individual channels cannot be fully separated, and a full life-cycle cost-benefit analysis (incorporating lithium's export revenues and climate benefits) is left outside the paper's scope.
future_research: A life-cycle cost-benefit analysis weighing lithium's economic benefits (export revenue, GDP contribution) and its role in the global energy transition against the local environmental and socioeconomic costs would complement the findings. The implications also warrant extension to the other Lithium Triangle countries, Bolivia and Argentina.
abstract: was concentrated in human settlements as opposed to natural reserves, suggesting a reduction
apa_citation: Peñaloza-Pacheco, L., Triantafyllou, V., & Martínez, G. (2026). The non-green effects of going green: Local environmental and economic consequences of lithium extraction in Chile. *Journal of Environmental Economics and Management*, 136, 103257. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jeem.2025.103257

title: Rethinking the Third Mission: Organizing dissonance in transformative universities
methods: Conceptual framework development; the authors build a typology of equivalence- and dissonance-oriented organizational forms from Convention Theory and illustrate it with secondary cases from the literature (e.g., Rust2Green, South Africa Food Lab, EUREF Campus).
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Pflitsch, Gesa; Menzel, Max-Peter
theories: Convention Theory (Boltanski & Thévenot, 2006; Diaz-Bone, 2018); dissonance (Stark, 2009); sustainability transitions / transformative innovation policy (Schot & Steinmueller, 2018; Smith & Raven, 2012)
keywords: Convention theory; Entrepreneurial university; Sustainability transitions; Third mission; Transformative innovation policy; Transformative universities
research_question: The paper is guided by two questions: (1) What are the qualities of knowledge processes in the context of TM that contribute to transformation? and (2) How can universities organize a TM that contributes to transformation?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Value collisions ("dissonance") between competing value systems are not byproducts of transformation but its primary drivers; a transformative Third Mission (TM) must therefore deliberately organize and productively mobilize dissonance.
- Key constructs: Drawing on Convention Theory, the paper distinguishes *equivalence-oriented* knowledge processes (shared values, smooth coordination, efficient problem-solving) from *dissonance-oriented* processes (divergent values, problem interpretation, novel solutions), bridged by provisional "settlements."
- Organizational forms: A continuum from problem-solving and matching organizations (TTOs, incubators — minimize dissonance) to transformative organizations (living labs, real-world labs, transdisciplinary initiatives — cultivate dissonance).
- Governance / boundary conditions: Heterarchy (coexisting evaluative principles, situationally shifting authority) and an "intermediate space" between academia and society sustain dissonance; productive use further depends on actors' reflexive capacity, deep dialogue, time, and "multiple insiders."

The framework reframes transformation as the reconfiguration of systems of worth rather than the protection or upscaling of niches. Universities contribute to sustainability transitions when they generate dissonance (a necessary condition) and then mobilize it productively in a "stretch-and-transform" mode (the sufficient condition), challenging and reshaping dominant value systems rather than reducing friction for the sake of efficiency.
theoretical_contribution: The paper's central contribution is to reposition dissonance — the collision of competing value systems — as the defining analytical core of a transformative Third Mission, distinguishing the transformative university from the entrepreneurial and engaged models not by extension but by its treatment of value conflict. By importing Convention Theory and Stark's concept of dissonance into the literature on universities and sustainability transitions, it shifts attention from institutional rigidities to concrete situations, separates the necessary condition (creating dissonance) from the sufficient condition (using it productively), and introduces heterarchy and an intermediate space between academia and society as the organizational and governance arrangements that sustain dissonance.
practical_implication: Universities and policymakers should design organizational forms and governance (heterarchical, decentralized, intermediate spaces) that deliberately sustain rather than suppress value conflicts, and should resist output-oriented efficiency logics that prematurely force consensus. Because transformative TM cannot be carried by universities alone, support must be embedded in policy mixes, incentive systems, and impact-assessment frameworks that reward value plurality and longer-term outcomes over immediate deliverables.
limitations: The argument is primarily conceptual and therefore subject to limitations, though it is informed by empirical insights from adjacent literatures on the generative potential of dissonance. A further limitation is the paper's implicit focus on universities in the Global North; universities in the Global South often have deeper experience navigating dissonance through traditions of social engagement and regional embeddedness.
future_research: The authors flag four directions: empirically grounding the "intermediate space" (its actors, boundaries, and how universities mobilize dissonance within it); disentangling different types of conflict (institutional, value-based, identity-based) in transformative TM; identifying what organizational conditions enable *productive* dissonance beyond individual actors and methods; and examining how heterarchical coordination and policy mixes / micro-missions can support transformative TM.
abstract: Using this perspective, we assess which organizational forms and governance arrangements enable transformation by sustaining dissonance, identifying heterarchy and an intermediate space between academia and society as central. This delineation sets transformative universities apart from other models and yields far-reaching implications for TM-related policy mixes and impact assessment.
apa_citation: Pflitsch, G., & Menzel, M.-P. (2026). Rethinking the Third Mission: Organizing dissonance in transformative universities. *Research Policy*, 55(2), 105394. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.respol.2025.105394

title: Subtle Discrimination
methods: Conceptual paper; formal game-theoretic model. A two-agent promotion tournament with a subtly biased principal is solved for equilibrium human-capital investments, optimal compensation contracts, and endogenous firm bias, plus stylized extensions to analysts, lending, fund flows, and entrepreneurial finance.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Pikulina, Elena S.; Ferreira, Daniel
theories: statistical discrimination (Phelps, 1972; Arrow, 1973); taste-based discrimination (Becker, 1957); tournament/contest theory of promotions (Lazear & Rosen, 1990)
keywords: discouragement effect; human capital; overcompensation effect; overt discrimination; promotions; subtle discrimination
research_question: How does "subtle" discrimination—biased acts that cannot be objectively ascertained as discriminatory because the decision-maker can resort to plausible deniability—affect workers' human-capital investment, firms' promotion gaps and profits, and firms' choices of antidiscrimination policy, and how do these consequences differ from those of overt discrimination?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: A principal with a small, unobservable "tie-breaking" bias toward Blue over Red sets up a competitive promotion tournament in which two opposing forces shape the unfavored (Red) agent's effort—a *discouragement effect* (low promotion odds deter investment) and an *overcompensation effect* (investing to separate from the favored agent). The premium-cost ratio σ (the "stakes") determines which dominates.
- Key constructs: subtle vs. overt bias (distinguished by whether an act is objectively ascertainable as discriminatory); achievement gap vs. favoritism gap (the two components of the promotion gap); productivity-cost ratio θ as a proxy for firm quality.
- Boundary conditions: results hinge on discrimination being subtle rather than overt (overt bias moderates/eliminates the overcompensation effect); the central comparative static (Red invests more when stakes are low, less when high) holds for quadratic and other convex costs and survives cost heterogeneity between groups.

When stakes are low the overcompensation effect dominates and the unfavored agent overinvests, so subtle discrimination *raises* profits in low-productivity (low-θ) firms; when stakes are high the discouragement effect dominates and the unfavored agent underinvests, *lowering* profits in high-productivity firms. When firms can choose their bias at no cost, they polarize to corner solutions—high-θ firms become "progressive" (β = 0, more top diversity) while low-θ firms become "conservative" (β = 0.5)—so even negligible productivity differences generate large differences in corporate culture and top-level diversity that market forces cannot erase.
theoretical_contribution: The paper is the first to formalize the concept of subtle discrimination, proposing a novel two-category taxonomy of discriminatory acts (subtle and overt) and demonstrating that the two have markedly different empirical consequences. By embedding subtle bias in a competitive promotion model, it shows that small biases can be either amplified or attenuated depending on the stakes, yielding a rich set of predictions linking firm characteristics to group performance, top-management diversity, and antidiscrimination policy—and offering a unifying explanation for otherwise puzzling evidence in labor, banking, analyst, lending, and fund-flow settings.
practical_implication: The model implies that investing in a credible reputation for nondiscrimination pays off primarily for high-return-on-human-capital firms, where eliminating subtle bias encourages discriminated workers to invest in skills; low-return firms have less to gain. It also cautions that subtle discrimination is hard to detect with standard outcome (Becker marginal-outcome) tests and that hard quotas achieve equal outcomes only when bias is high and may still leave protected workers worse off in expected utility because firms lower promotion stakes.
limitations: The analysis is a deliberately simple, stylized formalization that "necessarily leaves out many nuances in real life," relying on assumptions (binary firm-specific skills, exact ties, a monopsonist principal, quadratic costs, ω → 0) adopted for tractability. The applications to analysts, lending, fund flows, and entrepreneurial finance are illustrative examples rather than tested empirical analyses, and the model abstracts from many features of real promotion and hiring processes.
future_research: The authors argue subtle discrimination should feature alongside statistical discrimination as a null hypothesis in Becker outcome tests, and they outline testable predictions—e.g., that unanticipated bias shocks should move promotion gaps without changing short-run firm performance, that observable skills should matter more for unfavored groups, and that promotion gaps should grow at the top of hierarchies. They note the framework generates "new predictions for this rapidly growing empirical literature" on founders competing for capital and on academic and banking careers.
abstract: We introduce the concept of subtle discrimination—biased acts that cannot be objectively ascertained as discriminatory. When candidates compete for promotions by investing in skills, firms’ subtle biases induce discriminated candidates to overinvest when promotions are low-stakes (to distinguish themselves from favored candidates) but underinvest in high-stakes settings (anticipating low promotion probabilities). This asymmetry implies that subtle discrimination raises profits in low-productivity firms but lowers them in high-productivity firms. Although subtle biases are small, they generate large gaps in skills and promotion outcomes. We derive further predictions in contexts such as equity analysis, lending, fund flows, banking careers, and entrepreneurial finance.
apa_citation: Pikulina, E. S., & Ferreira, D. (2026). Subtle discrimination. *The Journal of Finance*, 81(1), 329–369. https://doi.org/10.1111/jofi.13506

title: Sustaining Unsustainability: The Legitimation Paradox of Unsustainable Companies
methods: Qualitative single-case study of H&M using the Gioia methodology; inductive and abductive coding of sustainability reports, press releases, and news-article quotes to build a grounded model of legitimation under paradoxical conditions.
industry: Fast fashion (apparel retail)
units: Corporate sustainability communication: 742 report pages, 42 press releases, and 231 news articles
n_sample: Single case (H&M); 18 first-order concepts reduced to 10 legitimation strategies
authors: Pollach, Irene; Høvring, Christiane Marie; Thomsen, Christa; Nielsen, Anne Ellerup
theories: legitimation theory (van Leeuwen & Wodak, 1999; van Leeuwen, 2007); paradox theory (Smith & Lewis, 2011; Berti & Cunha, 2023)
keywords: communication; legitimation; paradox; sustainability; unsustainability
research_question: How does an inherently unsustainable company legitimize its business model in view of the legitimation paradox it faces?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: The legitimation paradox — visible, inherently unsustainable companies must present themselves as sustainable to retain their license to operate while profiting from fundamentally unsustainable practices; nested within a structural and a strategic sustainability paradox.
- Sources of legitimation: Authority, morality, and rationality (after van Leeuwen, 2007), each operationalized through specific discursive strategies.
- Traditional strategies: Endorsing, Re-valencing, Democratizing, and Technologizing.
- Paradoxical strategy pairs: Blurring vs. Forming Boundaries (authority), Delegating vs. Accepting Moral Agency (morality), and Infinitizing vs. Finitizing Processes (rationality).
- Emergent tensions: Boundary, agency, and temporality tensions, in which the firm adopts two interrelated yet contradictory positions on its sustainability.

The paper theorizes that paradoxical legitimation strategies form pairs of interrelated yet contradictory claims that work in tandem to simultaneously defend unsustainable practices and claim sustainability. Individually contestable, together these paired strategies oscillate between contradictory positions in a mutually reinforcing way that renders them uncontestable, allowing the company to sustain its unsustainability through an ongoing oscillation between claiming sustainability and defending unsustainability while deflecting attention from growth and overproduction as the root drivers of its unsustainable business model.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends legitimation research by showing that legitimation strategies do not merely defend organizations against episodic or continuous legitimacy threats but can themselves take paradoxical forms. It develops a model of legitimation under paradoxical conditions that goes beyond an enumeration of strategies to offer a systematic account of how companies construct legitimacy through paradoxical reasoning that embraces contradictory positions in a dual both-and perspective, arguing that considering legitimation strategies in isolation is insufficient to understand responses to the legitimation paradox.
practical_implication: Stakeholders who scrutinize, rely on, assess, or regulate corporate sustainability communication (e.g., NGOs, activists, journalists, ethical investors, ESG rating bodies, and regulators) need to understand how paradoxical legitimation constructs unsustainability as legitimate so they can decode and challenge such narratives rather than amplify them. The findings should alert policymakers to the need for more robust regulation extending beyond product-label claims (e.g., the EU Green Claims Directive) to all forms of direct and indirect corporate sustainability communication.
limitations: The analysis is an extreme single-case study of one highly visible, inherently unsustainable fast fashion company (H&M), conducted at the micro-level of corporate sustainability communication, which constrains generalizability even as the authors argue the paradoxical-legitimation logic applies more broadly. The study does not approach corporate legitimations from a greenwashing perspective and therefore cannot assess whether paradoxical legitimation qualifies as greenwashing.
future_research: Future research could explore the connection between paradoxical legitimation and greenwashing, examining the perceptions of circularity experts, greenwashing activists, and policymakers as to whether such legitimations constitute a potentially new form of greenwashing. This may require expanding traditional conceptualizations of greenwashing beyond false or evidently misleading information to include claims that render the unsustainable status quo as legitimate or attribute sustainability to inherently unsustainable operations.
abstract: Companies with inherently unsustainable business models and high public salience face a paradox, as they need to construct themselves as desirable and appropriate organizations to maintain their license to operate, while at the same time profiting from practices that are fundamentally unsustainable. This paper advances our understanding of legitimation under paradoxical conditions by examining how a highly visible and inherently unsustainable company legitimizes its unsustainability. Based on a micro-level analysis of corporate sustainability reports, press releases, and company quotes in news articles, we identify both traditional legitimation strategies and paradoxical legitimation strategies, the latter of which conflict with other legitimation strategies through tensions related to boundaries, agency, and temporality. Our findings show how paradoxical legitimation strategies enable companies to sustain their unsustainability by engaging in an ongoing oscillation between claiming sustainability and defending unsustainability.
apa_citation: Pollach, I., Høvring, C. M., Thomsen, C., & Nielsen, A. E. (2026). Sustaining unsustainability: The legitimation paradox of unsustainable companies. *Organization & Environment*, 39(2), 161–180. https://doi.org/10.1177/10860266251389784

title: The data-based power of big-tech multinational enterprises
methods: Conceptual perspective piece; develops a power-mutuality research framework from power-dependence theory and proposes a research agenda. No data are analyzed or generated.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Rong, Ke; Parente, Ronaldo; Deng, Ziliang; Kang, Zhengyao; Mudambi, Ram; Newburry, William
theories: power-dependence theory (Emerson, 1962); resource dependence theory (Pfeffer & Salancik, 1978)
keywords: Artificial intelligence; Big-tech multinational enterprise; Data ecosystem; Data-based power; Digital globalization; Power-dependence theory
research_question: How is power generated from the ubiquity of global data for big-tech MNEs, and how do various stakeholders—such as foreign users and governments—respond to and counterbalance this power across global data value chains (GDVCs)?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Big-tech MNEs derive a new source of power from harnessing data ubiquity across global data value chains (GDVCs), comprising the interplay of three interconnected processes—data access, data analysis, and data application.
- Key constructs: power mutuality (power generation vs. power counterbalance); the three attributes of data (virtuality, non-rivalry, externalities); the data-AI feedback loop / data network effects.
- Power generation: data-harnessing capabilities create dependency of users, businesses, and governments on big-tech MNEs, which—per the power-dependence logic—grants the MNEs market, information, and sociopolitical power.
- Power counterbalance: this data-based power is reciprocally dependent on every process and participant in the GDVC, so users (societal resistance), firms (supply limitation, alternative platforms), and governments (regulation, data localization, antitrust) counter it.

Drawing on power-dependence theory (Emerson, 1962) and resource dependence theory (Pfeffer & Salancik, 1978), the paper develops a mutuality logic in which power is a relational property: A's power over B equals B's dependence on A. Applied to GDVCs, big-tech MNEs generate power by creating dependence through data access, analysis, and application, yet remain simultaneously dependent on the consent, cooperation, and regulatory tolerance of the very stakeholders they dominate—producing a dynamic interplay of power generation and counterbalance rather than unilateral dominance.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends power-dependence theory into the digital, data-driven IB context by articulating a "power mutuality" logic that explains how big-tech MNEs both generate and are counterbalanced in their data-based power across GDVCs. It defines big-tech MNEs as enterprises whose value propositions are fundamentally based on data-based power on a global scale, and reframes the liability of foreignness, internationalization, and global governance around the unique features of data (virtuality, non-rivalry, externalities).
practical_implication: Big-tech MNEs must adapt their data strategies to divergent and conflicting national data laws (e.g., data localization, opt-in vs. opt-out consent), balancing analytical efficiency against regulatory and national-security constraints. Conventional ("brick-and-mortar") MNEs are increasingly subject to the same data-based power dynamics and should reevaluate data as a strategic resource in cross-border strategy.
limitations: The authors deliberately focus on big-tech MNEs as the most novel and powerful form of MNE and do not intend to provide an entirely comprehensive perspective covering all related issues; the boundary between big-tech and brick-and-mortar MNEs is acknowledged to be blurring. The paper is conceptual and analyzes no data, leaving the proposed agenda open for empirical development.
future_research: The paper proposes a multi-theme agenda: MNEs' data strategies (optimal data acquisition, internalization, data-center location, security-seeking FDI, global virtual work); foreignness and liabilities of data (data nationality, "state-MNE-data compounds," pendulum-like internationalization); orchestration and power asymmetries in GDVCs (data monopolies, core-periphery and "data divide" dynamics); and data fragmentation and global data governance (comparative regulation, multilateral standard-setting, grand challenges).
abstract: Despite the increasing recognition of digital globalization in international business (IB) literature, there is a more pressing need for a comprehensive understanding of the unprecedented power generated by big-tech multinational enterprises (MNEs). This paper suggests that harnessing data ubiquity globally, instantaneously, and continuously represents a defining feature and a revolutionary power source for big-tech MNEs. By leveraging global data value chains through a mutually reinforcing data-AI feedback loop of data access, analysis, and application, big-tech MNEs can reshape IB practices and challenge global regulatory frameworks. Drawing on power-dependence theory, our research framework highlights a logic of power mutuality characterized by the interplay between power generation and power counterbalance. These two forces provide a holistic lens for examining how big-tech MNEs create data-based power, but are simultaneously countered by the economic and sociopolitical stakeholders of big-tech MNEs. We propose promising avenues for future research on MNEs' data strategies, global data value chains, foreignness and liabilities of data, and global data governance.
apa_citation: Rong, K., Parente, R., Deng, Z., Kang, Z., Mudambi, R., & Newburry, W. (2026). The data-based power of big-tech multinational enterprises. *Journal of International Business Studies*, 57(2), 220–235. https://doi.org/10.1057/s41267-025-00828-5

title: Reimagining ESG: A Tetralemma Approach to Multifunctional Sustainability Reporting
methods: Conceptual article; develops its argument through theoretical synthesis, combining Luhmann's theory of functional differentiation with the tetralemma concept from Indian logic, illustrated with a multifunctional tetralemma matrix across societal function systems (Figure 1).
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Roth, Steffen; Valentinov, Vladislav
theories: Luhmann's social systems theory / functional differentiation (Luhmann, 1982, 2006, 2013, 2018a); commensuration theory (Espeland & Stevens, 1998; Espeland & Yung, 2019); paradox theory (Smith & Lewis, 2011; Schad et al., 2016); tetralemma (Indian logic) (Kleve et al., 2020; Roth et al., 2023)
keywords: ESG reporting; commensuration; corporate accountability; social systems theory; sustainability metrics; tetralemma logic
research_question: How can sustainability reporting better account for the coexistence and incommensurability of multiple moral and functional logics in society? The paper asks the deeper diagnostic question of which dimensions of social life get counted and which remain invisible in existing ESG frameworks.
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: By collapsing functionally differentiated societal subsystems (science, politics, economy, law, religion, art, education, etc.) into three macro-categories (E, S, G), conventional ESG frameworks commensurate incommensurable logics and standardise moral imagination, obscuring ethical concerns that do not fit the predefined boxes.
- Key constructs: functional differentiation (each function system observes its environment through its own incommensurable code); commensuration as complexity reduction; the focus-shaping power of metrics; quantification as a technology of governance and power.
- Proposed remedy (two-fold): (1) a multifunctional approach that explicitly maps how each subsystem evaluates a corporate action rather than merging logics into a single score; (2) the tetralemma — "this", "not this", "both this and not this", "neither this nor not this" (and a fifth transcending position) — as a structured grammar for navigating paradoxes without prematurely collapsing them.
- Boundary conditions: critique is directed primarily at rating and integration frameworks, where pressures of quantification and commensuration are most pronounced; the tetralemma is not a panacea and may be exploited to evade accountability.

Drawing on Luhmann's functional differentiation, the authors argue that modern society comprises multiple subsystems with logically dissimilar codes (e.g., payment/non-payment, true/false, power/opposition) that resist seamless translation; any attempt to weight them under one ESG metric inevitably reflects observer biases that privilege scientific, political, and economic codes while marginalising religion, art, and education. The tetralemma extends paradox theory's "both–and" framing by legitimising "neither–nor" and "none of the above" positions, opening discursive space for reframing purportedly insoluble sustainability dilemmas rather than forcing binary trade-offs. Together, the multifunctional approach plus tetralemma logic reframe ESG as a flexible, context-sensitive practice that surfaces cross-system conflicts instead of compressing them into headline scores.
theoretical_contribution: The article contributes to the sustainability-reporting and commensuration literatures by showing that functional differentiation fundamentally complicates commensuration: because Luhmann's theory treats social, economic, and political domains as dissimilar systems, attempts to unify them under a single metric inevitably suffer from biases. By combining paradox theory with the tetralemma concept, it demonstrates how an expanded logic of decision-making lets organisations face moral conflicts without artificially reducing them, and it extends established social and environmental accounting critiques by offering a systems-theoretical explanation for why these problems persist.
practical_implication: Managers can diversify their metrics — incorporating cultural or local-level concerns that escape standard E–S–G classification — to reduce legitimacy gaps and social frictions, while policymakers might revisit disclosure mandates to encourage a more pluralist set of recognised domains. Financial intermediaries and rating agencies could normalise multifunctional metrics by permitting "qualitative expansions" and context-specific data (e.g., art-based or religiously oriented initiatives) as complementary evidence of sustainable operations.
limitations: The arguments advanced here remain primarily conceptual, underscoring the need for empirical research that tests the viability and outcomes of a multifunctional, tetralemma-based approach to ESG. The authors also caution that the openness of the "neither–nor" stance could be exploited as a rhetorical device for stalling or diluting meaningful ESG commitments.
future_research: Scholars might investigate how specific companies (large multinationals or local social enterprises) apply or reject a broader set of indicators reflecting religion, health, or art, using comparative case studies and quantitative projects linking multifunctional reporting to market reactions and investor confidence. Further work could examine how technological advances in data analytics (machine learning, big data) might aid or complicate efforts to track multiple function systems, and what new governance structures algorithmic rating would require.
abstract: ESG frameworks have become a central tool for aligning corporate strategies with sustainability imperatives. Yet, by reducing environmental, social, and governance dimensions to standardised metrics, conventional ESG framework risk obscuring the complex ethical commitments that arise from incommensurable societal subsystems. Drawing on Niklas Luhmann’s social systems theory, this paper critiques ESG’s tendency to privilege specific function systems – science, politics, and economics – while marginalising others such as religion, art, and education. We further introduce the tetralemma, a concept from Indian logic, to illuminate novel ways of reconciling or transcending the paradoxes inherent in integrating these domains. By combining a multifunctional approach with tetralemma thinking, we demonstrate how organisations can expand their reporting scope beyond narrow ESG categories, foster inclusive stakeholder engagement, and respond more effectively to morally fraught sustainability dilemmas. This integration ultimately reframes ESG as a flexible, context-sensitive practice that embraces societal complexity and offers a holistic route to accountability.
apa_citation: Roth, S., & Valentinov, V. (2026). Reimagining ESG: A tetralemma approach to multifunctional sustainability reporting. *Organization & Environment*, 39(2), 181-200. https://doi.org/10.1177/10860266251410473

title: Ethical Decision-Making in Organizations in Times of Conflict: The Role of Personal Values and Collective Victimhood
methods: Two-wave field survey (vignette-based); EGA and CFA to validate scales; mediation analysis via PROCESS (Model 4) and structural equation modeling (SEM), controlling for actual harm and social desirability.
industry: Employed adults across organizations (including business, educational, and academic institutions), with focus on ethnically diverse organizations
time_period: January–February 2024 (five months after the October 7, 2023 escalation of the Israeli–Hamas conflict)
units: Employed adult individuals
n_sample: 272 respondents (from 300 initial participants; Mage = 35.26, 53.67% female, 51.1% managers)
authors: Rubel-Lifschitz, Tammy; Moscovich, Inbal; Bucevschi, Alexandru D.; Oliver, Amalya L.
theories: Collective victimhood / competitive victimhood (Noor et al., 2017; Bar-Tal et al., 2009); Schwartz theory of basic human values (Schwartz, 1992, 2012, 2016)
keywords: Collective victimhood; Ethical decision-making; Intergroup conflict; Personal values
research_question: How are personal values associated with general ethical decision-making (EDM) versus intergroup-related ethical decision-making (IG-EDM) in organizations during escalating intergroup conflict, and does a subjective sense of collective victimhood mediate these associations?
mechanism_summary: The paper argues that the value structure organizes EDM along two distinct motivational principles. General EDM tracks the social-versus-individual focus axis (self-transcendence and conservation positive; self-enhancement and openness to change negative). Under intergroup conflict, however, IG-EDM tracks the growth-versus-self-protection (anxiety-regulation) axis: self-protection values (self-enhancement, conservation) elevate a sense of collective victimhood, which in turn rationalizes prioritizing ingroup security over outgroup fairness and lowers ethical choices; growth values (self-transcendence, openness to change) reduce collective victimhood and sustain ethical conduct across group lines. Collective victimhood mediated all four values' effects on IG-EDM (indirect effects accounting for 38–61% of total effects) but had no role for general EDM, and objective harm showed no significant association with either outcome.
theoretical_contribution: The study demonstrates that the well-established values–EDM associations are not invariant: in conditions of active intergroup conflict, the motivational tension underlying ethical decisions shifts from a social–personal axis (for general EDM) to a growth–self-protection axis (for IG-EDM), reversing the roles of conservation and openness to change. It introduces and validates a novel measure of intergroup ethical decision-making (IG-EDM) grounded in real organizational decisions, and establishes collective victimhood as a subjective psychological mechanism that mediates values–IG-EDM links independently of objective harm.
practical_implication: For organizations operating in diverse and conflict-prone environments, fostering ethical decision-making toward outgroup members requires addressing not only individual values but also the broader social and psychological forces—particularly a collective-victimhood mindset—that shape ethical perceptions. The findings suggest cultivating values (self-transcendence, openness to change) that discourage a collective-victim mindset to help sustain fair, inclusive decisions during crises.
limitations: The correlational, self-report design limits causal inference; although a two-wave structure reduced common method bias, it did not establish temporal precedence. Evidence derives from a single field survey within one intergroup conflict, limiting generalizability across cultures and conflict types. The study focused on the majority group within Israeli society, so it does not capture the experiences or ethical challenges of minority or outgroup members.
future_research: Future studies should employ experimental or longitudinal designs to better examine directionality and mechanisms, and should test the findings across diverse intergroup-conflict settings and cultural contexts (e.g., Ukraine–Russia, India–Pakistan). Researchers could also develop a broadly applicable, rigorously validated IG-EDM scale and compare perspectives across conflict groups, including minority and outgroup members, to build a more balanced understanding.
abstract: This study examined the links between values and ethical decision-making in organizational contexts during a time of escalatory intergroup conflict, when social tensions can spill over into organizational life. Specifically, we compared how personal values are associated with general ethical decision-making and intergroup-related ethical decision-making and tested whether a subjective sense of collective victimhood mediates these associations. Using vignette-based measures of general and intergroup ethical decision-making, we surveyed 272 employed adults in Israel during a major escalation of the Israeli–Palestinian conflict. While general ethical decision-making was positively related to social values (self-transcendence and conservation) and negatively related to individual values (self-enhancement and openness to change), intergroup ethical decision-making was positively related to growth values (self-transcendence and openness to change) and negatively related to self-protection values (self-enhancement and conservation). Collective victimhood mediated associations between personal values and intergroup ethical decisions, but did not mediate associations between personal values and other ethical decisions. Overall, findings suggest that in the context of intergroup conflict, distinct patterns of association between values, collective victimhood, and ethical choices appear across decision types. The paper concludes with implications for organizations operating under intergroup tension and notes the need for strategies that temper collective-victimhood dynamics while fostering ethical decision-making that transcends group boundaries.
apa_citation: Rubel-Lifschitz, T., Moscovich, I., Bucevschi, A. D., & Oliver, A. L. (2026). Ethical decision-making in organizations in times of conflict: The role of personal values and collective victimhood. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06231-2

title: Certified Sustainability: Third-Party Certification of Sustainable Ventures and its Relationship to Venture Funding
methods: Quasi-experimental observational study using propensity score matching (nearest-neighbor, no replacement, caliper 0.2) on Crunchbase and B Corp Impact data; generalized linear model with negative binomial distribution and logarithmic link; robustness via Heckman two-stage selection model and 2SLS instrumental variables.
industry: Sustainable ventures across SIC Level 1 industries (B Corp-certified ventures, e.g., consumer products, services)
country: Global, aggregated by continent (Africa, Asia, Europe, North America, Oceania, South America); regional hot spots in the US, Italy, and the UK
time_period: Founding dates 2011-2021; Crunchbase collected April 18, 2024 and B Corp Impact data April 15, 2024; three-year funding observation window per venture
units: Sustainable ventures (374 B Corp-certified paired with 374 non-certified via PSM)
n_sample: 748 sustainable ventures (374 certified, 374 non-certified)
authors: Schmelter, Dominik; Schmidt, Corinna Vera Hedwig; Strese, Steffen
theories: instrumental societal logic of business ethics (Hockerts & Searcy, 2023); signaling theory (Spence, 1973; Connelly et al., 2011; Bafera & Kleinert, 2023)
keywords: Sustainability certification; Venture financing; Venture stage
research_question: Do third-party sustainability certifications enhance the funding outcome of certified sustainable ventures compared to non-certified sustainable ventures, and how does the venture stage at the time of certification moderate this relationship?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on signaling theory, the authors argue that a B Corp sustainability certification is a costly, credible, and observable third-party signal that reduces the severe information asymmetry between sustainable ventures and investors, signaling the materiality of a venture's sustainability commitment beyond mere discourse or greenwashing. Because the certification demonstrates genuine, hard-to-imitate commitment, investors allocate more funding to certified than to comparable non-certified ventures (H1). As ventures mature, however, the certification signal becomes less costly, less credible, and less observable—more financial slack lowers its cost, redundant performance information lowers its credibility, and signal noise lowers its observability—so the positive certification-funding relationship weakens at later stages (H2).
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the instrumental societal logic of business ethics by showing that third-party-certified ethical standards positively influence venture funding, helping alleviate the founder's tension between investing scarce resources in certification versus building the venture. It advances the sustainable-finance literature by isolating the funding effect of certification within a sample restricted exclusively to sustainable ventures, and by introducing signal timing—the venture stage at certification—as a boundary condition that conditions when sustainability signals pay off across the venture life cycle.
practical_implication: Sustainable ventures should obtain third-party certification, ideally at early stages, to secure funding that finances their ethical mission, and can strategically combine internal (self-proclaimed) and external (certified) sustainability signals. Investors are advised to incentivize early-stage certification to attract syndicate co-investors, and policymakers can encourage certification through tax benefits, government procurement, or support networks and workshops that ease the certification process.
limitations: Although PSM and the Heckman/2SLS corrections address observed selection and endogeneity, unobserved variables (e.g., financial slack, founder networks, other certifications) may still bias the likelihood of certification and funding. The authors theorize but do not empirically test why certification signals materiality—they cannot explain the underlying mechanism—and they study only the B Corp certification, which holistically assesses ventures and may differ from product-based or regional certifications.
future_research: Future studies should use (quasi-)experimental designs with real investors and mixed-method/interview approaches to uncover the mechanisms by which certification increases funding. Scholars could examine other certifications (e.g., Green Seal, ISO 26000, Climate Neutral, EU Ecolabel), split sustainability into distinct impact areas, analyze venture scores below 80, and conduct more granular analyses of diverse funding sources and investor types.
abstract: This study explores how sustainability certifications help attract investor funding to sustainable ventures, depending on the venture's stage at the time of certification. Sustainable ventures secure financing at every stage to build viable businesses and achieve ethical missions, such as addressing climate change and social inequalities. To signal their genuine commitment to sustainability efforts, these ventures seek third-party certifications. Research has investigated third-party certification as a means to reduce information asymmetries between ventures and investors. However, it has yielded ambiguous results regarding the financial implications of such certifications for sustainable ventures. In our study, we use propensity score matching on a sample of 748 certified and comparable non-certified sustainable ventures. We examine the role of sustainability certifications in attracting investor funding and the moderating role of venture stage at certification. Drawing on signaling theory, we show that the positive relationship between sustainability certification and funding weakens at later venture stages. Our findings highlight third-party sustainability certification as a key signal in venture financing, enabling sustainable ventures to prove the materiality of their sustainability efforts and increase funding. We thus advance the literature on business ethics and sustainable finance.
apa_citation: Schmelter, D., Schmidt, C. V. H., & Strese, S. (2026). Certified sustainability: Third-party certification of sustainable ventures and its relationship to venture funding. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06241-0

title: Managing multi-stakeholder co-creation to address grand challenges: The role of paradox management capabilities
methods: Inductive, qualitative multiple-case study of 30 COVID-19 co-creation initiatives; 90-minute semi-structured interviews with initiative leaders using a standardized template; within- and cross-case thematic analysis with first-order, second-order, and aggregate themes, independently coded by three researchers.
industry: Cross-sectoral COVID-19 co-creation initiatives spanning academia, government, industry, and civil society (e.g., drug discovery, supercomputing, data platforms, ventilators); 17 projects and 13 programmes
time_period: COVID-19 pandemic period (initiatives developed from 2020 onward)
units: Multi-stakeholder co-creation initiatives (projects and programmes)
n_sample: 30 co-creation initiatives; 30 in-depth interviews with initiative leaders
authors: De Silva, Muthu; Rossi, Federica; Paunov, Caroline
theories: dynamic capabilities (Teece et al., 1997); effectuation/causation (Sarasvathy, 2001); paradox theory (Smith and Lewis, 2011; Cunha and Putnam, 2019); transformative innovation policy (Haddad et al., 2022)
keywords: Co-creation; Covid-19; Disruptive sustainability; Grand challenges; Multi-stakeholder networks; Paradox theory
research_question: What are the paradox management capabilities that enable multi-stakeholder co-creation networks — set up in the context of initiatives directed at addressing Grand Challenges — to swiftly and successfully achieve their objectives?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: Organizational paradoxes (performing, organizing, belonging, learning) arising in multi-stakeholder co-creation networks, mapped onto the defining features of Grand Challenges (complexity, uncertainty, large scale, urgency).
- Levels of analysis: Two distinct levels of transformative innovation — programme co-creation (orchestrating a portfolio of interrelated projects) and project co-creation (time-bound collaboration on a defined deliverable).
- Higher-order capabilities (programme level): macro- and meso-level; top-down and bottom-up; cross-departmental paradox management capabilities.
- Higher-order capabilities (project level): social and business; effectuation and causation; short-term and long-term paradox management capabilities.
- Routine capabilities: Each higher-order capability decomposes into three operational routine capabilities (e.g., integrating a portfolio of projects, horizontal coordination, repurposing assets, flexible fast-tracked decision-making, swift digital trust building).
- Boundary conditions: Networks established under pressing conditions with little room to design optimal structures ex ante; capabilities partners bring matter more than network design.

The paper develops a capability-based account of how partners manage the contradictory-yet-interdependent demands of Grand Challenge co-creation. Rather than relying on optimal incentives or partnership structures designed in advance, successful networks deploy higher-order paradox-management capabilities — and their underlying routine capabilities — to reconcile competing logics (social vs. business, short- vs. long-term, exploration vs. exploitation, control vs. autonomy). The analysis links the empirically derived capabilities back to Smith and Lewis's (2011) paradox taxonomy, showing that belonging and organizing paradoxes are more salient at the programme level while performing and learning paradoxes dominate at the project level.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances paradox theory by identifying the specific capabilities that underpin the management of paradoxes across organizational boundaries, extending Jarzabkowski et al.'s (2019) work with a granular, capability-based and multi-level framework. It contributes to the transformative innovation policy literature by specifying the concrete paradox-management capabilities (higher-order and routine) that public-sector actors and network participants need, addressing the noted gap that this literature does not engage with what such capabilities entail. It also enriches the inter-organizational and interdisciplinary collaboration literature by showing that the success of co-creation networks hinges on the capabilities partners bring rather than on ex ante network design.
practical_implication: Policymakers should integrate paradox-management skills into funding criteria and evaluation frameworks, design support structures (training, facilitation, peer learning) to build these capabilities, and encourage iterative learning and adaptation across both project and programme levels. Participants in co-creation networks should anticipate the paradoxical tensions most likely to emerge at their level and develop the corresponding higher-order and routine capabilities to manage them.
limitations: The study relies on 30 cases of multi-stakeholder co-creation networks addressing COVID-19, and as a qualitative study its aim was to identify (not statistically generalize) paradox-management capabilities. COVID-19 may have specificities (e.g., its scale and urgency) not shared by all Grand Challenges. Although cases varied in scale, the capabilities were consistent across them, so a more nuanced understanding of how scale shapes paradox management was beyond the study's scope.
future_research: Similar analyses of other cases — and quantitative studies — could test the robustness and generalizability of the findings. The framework should be extended to multi-stakeholder co-creation networks developed in the context of other Grand Challenges beyond COVID-19. A dedicated study focused specifically on how scale influences the management of paradoxes would also be a valuable avenue.
abstract: Our findings advance paradox theory by articulating six higher-order paradox-management capabilities needed to manage paradoxes relating to two levels of transformative innovation, project co-creation and programme co-creation. For each of these higher-order capabilities, we identify more specific routine capabilities that allow partners to manage these paradoxes to achieve swift and successful co-creation for disruptive sustainability. We provide implications for policy and practice.
apa_citation: De Silva, M., Rossi, F., & Paunov, C. (2026). Managing multi-stakeholder co-creation to address grand challenges: The role of paradox management capabilities. *Research Policy*, 55(2), 105408. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.respol.2025.105408

title: Corporate ESG Profiles and Investor Horizons
methods: Panel regressions of investor- and firm-level ESG scores on investor horizon (turnover/churn ratios), with two-way clustered standard errors; 2SLS using Sustainalytics/Refinitiv scores as instruments for MSCI; difference-in-differences and IV designs exploiting the 2004 SEC quarterly-disclosure shock and the 2016 Morningstar globe roll-out.
industry: Actively managed U.S. domestic equity mutual funds and 13f-filing institutional investors (excluding ETFs, index funds, and explicit ESG/SRI funds); cross section of publicly listed firms
units: Mutual fund-years, 13f institution-years, and firm-years
n_sample: 98,252 mutual fund-years; 166,185 institution-year observations; 26,217 firm-years
authors: Starks, Laura T.; Venkat, Parth; Zhu, Qifei
theories: clientele-catering / nonpecuniary preferences (Heinkel, Kraus, and Zechner, 2001); information economics / specialization in information processing (Froot, Perold, and Stein, 1992); limits-to-arbitrage (Shleifer and Vishny, 1997)
keywords: ESG profiles; institutional investors; investor horizon; limits-to-arbitrage; mutual funds; portfolio tilt
research_question: Do longer-term institutional investors have stronger revealed preferences for higher-ESG stocks than shorter-term investors, and if so, which mechanism—differential information processing, limits-to-arbitrage, or clientele-catering—explains this association between investor horizon and ESG portfolio composition?
mechanism_summary: Long-horizon investors systematically overweight high-ESG stocks. The information channel is supported: long-term funds are patient through poor earnings announcements but disproportionately sell stocks hit by severe ES incidents, consistent with longand short-term investors specializing in different information. The limits-to-arbitrage channel is supported: low flow-performance-sensitivity (more arbitrage-resilient) funds tilt more toward ESG, and a 2004 SEC disclosure shock that shortened horizons causally weakened the ESG tilt. The clientele-catering channel finds no support—flow responses to Morningstar globes and the ESG tilt's variation with climate news do not differ by horizon.
theoretical_contribution: The paper moves beyond aggregate institutional-ownership studies by exploiting the cross section of investors along the horizon dimension, showing that investor horizon is a meaningful determinant of revealed ESG preferences. It adjudicates among three competing theoretical accounts of why long-term investors prefer high-ESG firms, providing evidence consistent with information-based and limits-to-arbitrage explanations rather than clientele-catering, and links these revealed preferences to long-standing debates on long-termism versus short-termism in financial markets.
practical_implication: For asset owners and regulators, the findings suggest that policies shortening institutional investors' horizons (e.g., more frequent mandatory disclosure) can blunt the flow of capital toward high-ESG firms, since horizon itself shapes ESG demand. The results also indicate that long-term investors' attention to severe ES incidents makes ESG profiles materially relevant to a firm's shareholder base and trading dynamics.
limitations: Investor horizon is measured indirectly through turnover and churn ratios rather than observed intentions, and the ESG preferences of fund end-investors cannot be directly observed. The authors note that, given the difficulty of measuring clientele catering, they cannot definitively rule out that the catering channel matters; they simply find no evidence consistent with it.
future_research: The paper points to a potentially interesting feedback loop between long-term investors' portfolio decisions and their voting behavior as ES proposals gain traction over time, inviting further study of how horizon shapes engagement and voice alongside portfolio tilting. Further work could refine horizon and clientele-preference measures to better separate the information and catering channels.
abstract: We find that long-term institutional investors tilt their portfolios toward firms with better Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) profiles, in the cross sections of both institutional investor portfolios and the ownership of firms. We test whether several theoretically motivated mechanisms can explain this relationship. Our results that long-term investors exhibit patience with firms around poor earnings announcements, but quickly sell portfolio firms after negative ES incidents, support the view that longand short-term investors evaluate information differently. Our evidence shows that limits-to-arbitrage play a role, as we find that investors' ESG tilt weakens following regulatory shocks that shorten their horizon.
apa_citation: Starks, L. T., Venkat, P., & Zhu, Q. (2026). Corporate ESG profiles and investor horizons. *The Journal of Finance*, 81(2), 603–642. https://doi.org/10.1111/jofi.70008
