title: Political Tension and MNEs’ Disclosure of Host-Country ESG Activities
methods: Heckman two-stage selection design; first-stage Probit (with two-way clustering) of ESG report issuance using Required disclosure as an instrument; second-stage complementary log-log (cloglog) regression of host-country ESG disclosure with inverse Mills ratio and country/year/industry fixed effects; content analysis of Chinese-language ESG reports; robustness via firm fixed-effects, probit/logit, PSM, and difference-in-differences.
industry: Publicly listed Chinese MNEs across industries (firm-, industry-, province-, and country-level controls)
country: China (home country); subsidiaries in 12 host countries: Australia, France, Germany, India, Indonesia, Japan, Pakistan, Russia, South Korea, United Kingdom, United States, Vietnam
units: Firm-country-year observations (MNE host-country subsidiary disclosures)
n_sample: Unbalanced panel of 3,641 firm-country-year observations covering 376 MNEs (7,601 obs in the first-stage selection model)
authors: Xiang, Yi; Zhang, Ying; Wang, Chenjing; Du, Yongzhi
theories: deontological logic and consequentialist logic (business ethics); institutional theory / legitimacy (Suchman, 1995; Palazzo & Scherer, 2006); negative national sentiment
keywords: ESG disclosure; Ethics of transparency; MNEs; Political tension
research_question: When and why do MNEs choose to selectively disclose — or strategically withhold — their host-country ESG activities from home-country stakeholders under political tension between the home and host countries, and which firm-level conditions amplify this effect?
mechanism_summary: Under political tension, deteriorating bilateral relations fuel negative national sentiment toward the hostile host country, so home-country stakeholders may reframe favorable treatment of host-country stakeholders — including ESG activities — as benefiting an enemy and diverting domestic resources, threatening home-country legitimacy. To avoid this legitimacy loss, MNEs depart from a deontological duty of transparency and adopt a consequentialist logic of selective disclosure, withholding host-country ESG activities from domestic reports. This withholding is stronger when firms face greater home-country scrutiny (political connections), greater consumer-animosity exposure (marketing dependence), or deeper institutionalized skepticism toward foreigners (colonial legacy). All four hypotheses are supported (e.g., H1 β = -0.62, p < 0.001; interaction terms negative and significant in Table 9).
theoretical_contribution: First, the paper extends ESG-disclosure research by theorizing the conditions under which firms deviate from a deontological duty of transparency and instead adopt a consequentialist logic of selective disclosure, foregrounding how home-country stakeholder pressures (not just host-country audiences) shape what MNEs report. Second, it contributes to the political-tension literature by identifying a home-country pathway — negative national sentiment — through which political tension shapes ESG disclosure, reframing ESG from a purely legitimacy-enhancing practice abroad to one that can simultaneously entail legitimacy loss at home.
practical_implication: Managers should exercise heightened sensitivity to political tension and adopt a contextually nuanced ESG-disclosure strategy, taking particular care when the firm is heavily reliant on home-country stakeholders or located in a city with a colonial legacy, to mitigate political and reputational costs while ethically aligning with both home- and host-country expectations. Policymakers can help by fostering a standardized, internationally recognized ESG reporting framework that lets MNEs disclose with greater transparency and integrity without incurring undue political backlash.
limitations: The sample captures only political relations between China and a set of major countries, so generalizability to other dyads may be limited. Despite the authors' efforts, the media data used to validate host-country ESG activity may not be comprehensive, and the proposed negative-national-sentiment mechanism could not be directly measured because of data-availability constraints. The study also treats legitimacy as a binary construct rather than capturing its pragmatic, moral, and cognitive dimensions.
future_research: Future work could employ alternative or qualitative data to capture firms' actual host-country ESG performance, and use experimental or other methods to directly test the underlying sentiment mechanism. Researchers could also investigate non-sentiment pathways (e.g., political risk and operational threats) through which political dynamics shape MNE strategy, and adopt a more nuanced, multidimensional operationalization of legitimacy (pragmatic, moral, cognitive) under varying forms and intensities of political tension.
abstract: Scholars have explored the ways in which multinational enterprises (MNEs) react to host-country stakeholders amid the political tension between the host country and home country. However, scant attention has been paid to understanding how MNEs respond to home-country stakeholders in the context of political tension. Our study formulates a theoretical framework to investigate the impact of political tension on MNEs’ decisions about disclosing their host-country ESG activities. Our analysis reveals that an MNE is less likely to disclose host-country ESG activities in its home-country ESG report when political tension between its home country and host country is stronger. We further find that this negative relationship is more pronounced for MNEs with higher domestic political connections, larger domestic marketing dependence, and located in cities with a colonial legacy. These findings raise important ethical considerations about transparency in multinational contexts and offer a new perspective to interpret MNEs’ ESG disclosure decisions.
apa_citation: Xiang, Y., Zhang, Y., Wang, C., & Du, Y. (2026). Political tension and MNEs’ disclosure of host-country ESG activities. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-026-06256-1

title: Water stress and industrial firm productivity
journal: Journal of Environmental Economics and Management
methods: Quasi-experimental panel fixed-effects regression. Firm-level TFP (Olley-Pakes with ACF correction) regressed on county-level runoff shocks defined relative to long-run mean/SD, with firm, industry-by-year, and province-by-year fixed effects; weather and firm controls; county-by-year clustered and Conley spatial HAC standard errors; horserace, LP-DiD dynamic, and channel analyses.
industry: Formal industrial firms across 40 industries (manufacturing, mining, utilities), producing ~90% of China's total industrial output
units: Industrial firms (firm-year observations)
n_sample: 423,237 firms / 1.82 million firm-year observations
authors: Yu, Xiaojun; Smyth, Russell; Yao, Yao; Zhang, Quanda
theories: asymmetric (Leontief-type) production technology; climate economics / environmental shocks on production
keywords: China; Firm performance; Industrial survey; Panel model; Water runoff; Water stress
research_question: What is the causal effect of water stress — measured by local water runoff rather than precipitation — on the total factor productivity of industrial firms in China, and through which channels does it operate?
mechanism_summary: Runoff shocks affect productivity asymmetrically because water often serves as a binding input under Leontief-type production technology: when water availability falls below a critical threshold, production cannot be sustained, whereas additional water above the threshold yields diminishing marginal returns. A moderate negative shock lowers TFP by 1.93% and a large negative shock by 5.40%, while positive shocks are insignificant. The losses are transmitted primarily through constrained water inputs, reduced (especially thermal and hydro) power generation, and tighter cash flow, with SOEs and groundwater-rich or coastal counties more insulated.
theoretical_contribution: The paper argues that local water runoff is a more appropriate proxy of water availability for industrial firms than the precipitation measure dominant in prior work, reconciling the literature's mixed findings on water stress. It documents an asymmetric, threshold-type production response to water availability and shows that water stress can have an adverse effect on industrial productivity that is equal to or greater than heat stress, linking climate-driven hydrological variability to economic development.
practical_implication: Water stress imposes meaningful, threshold-driven productivity penalties on industrial firms, implying aggregate GDP-growth costs that intensify as climate change amplifies hydrological variability. Government support and water infrastructure matter: SOEs' preferential resource access mitigates losses, and water-supply/flood-control dams and denser urban drainage networks attenuate the adverse effects of negative runoff shocks, suggesting targets for adaptation policy.
limitations: Several channel and adaptation analyses rely on substantially smaller matched samples (e.g., shrinking from 1.81 million to as few as 0.34–0.64 million observations) and on model-predicted or machine-learning-derived water-quality measures rather than direct observations, making some mechanism results suggestive rather than causal. The matching and instrumental-variable strategies for dams and drainage cannot fully rule out unobserved confounders, and the political pecking-order (SOE) result is specific to China.
future_research: The authors note that water-quality channels concentrated in ultrapure-water industries warrant further study using direct in-situ measurements with broader geographic coverage, and that the suggestive evidence on adaptation through dams and drainage networks could be strengthened. Extending the analysis beyond China and beyond the 1998-2007 window would help assess external validity of how water stress impedes economic development.
abstract: We combine data on over half a million industrial firms with fine-scale hydrological data to estimate the causal effect of water stress on firm productivity in China. We find that runoff shocks exert asymmetric effects on firm-level TFP.
apa_citation: Yu, X., Smyth, R., Yao, Y., & Zhang, Q. (2026). Water stress and industrial firm productivity. *Journal of Environmental Economics and Management*, 138, 103309. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jeem.2026.103309

title: Artificial Intelligence and Disability Entrepreneurship: Moving the Field Forward
methods: Conceptual theory-building paper; develops a preliminary theoretical model and nine propositions through synthesis of the AI and disability-entrepreneurship literatures, organizing AI's influence into four dimensions (body, psychology, AI-as-extension, external environment) and contrasting virtuous and vicious feedback loops.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Zhang, Mengjie; Li, Weiwen
theories: AI-as-extension-of-the-body / human-AI symbiosis; entrepreneurial agency / self-efficacy; ideology of ableism (Campbell, 2009)
keywords: AI; Disability entrepreneurship; The ideology of ableism
research_question: The paper addresses three questions: (1) How does AI uniquely influence disabled entrepreneurs? (2) What opportunities and risks does this bring to disabled entrepreneurs? and (3) How can future research build on these transformations to advance new lines of inquiry?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: AI is theorized as distinctly reshaping disability entrepreneurship along four dimensions — effects on the body and bodily functions (substitution and activation), psychological experiences (empowerment and vulnerability), AI as an external extension of the body (cognitive/operational augmentation), and transformation of the external environment (algorithmic evaluation, shifting legitimacy and expectations).
- Key constructs: entrepreneurial agency, self-efficacy, entrepreneurial identity, legitimacy, the ideology of ableism, external attribution, AI inclusivity.
- Feedback loops: a virtuous (positive) loop in which functional empowerment strengthens self-efficacy and identity, normalizes AI as a capability resource, and elicits favorable stakeholder attribution; a vicious (negative) loop in which dependence, uneven empowerment across disability types, low AI inclusivity (opacity), and misattribution of outcomes to the machine intensify ableist bias.
- Tipping-point conditions: AI technology, individual traits, and external attribution determine which loop dominates (Propositions 1-9; Fig. 1 balance-scale model).

The model reframes ethical dilemmas not as simple benefit-versus-exclusion trade-offs but as inherent outcomes of the entanglement among disabled entrepreneurs, AI technologies, and external environments: the same technologies that broaden functional capacity and reduce visible disadvantage can simultaneously produce psychological dependency, exclusionary stratification within and across disability categories, and new forms of "algorithmic ableism." Relative dominance of the positive versus negative cycle is primarily shaped by whether entrepreneurial outcomes are attributed to disabled entrepreneurs or to the machine, as conditioned by individual traits and AI design/inclusivity.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes three contributions: it establishes disability entrepreneurship as a theoretically significant domain in the AI era by identifying how AI distinctly reshapes disabled entrepreneurs and entrepreneurial processes (repositioning the field from a study of demographic variance to a lens on human-technology interaction, embodiment, and inclusion); it advances ethics scholarship by theorizing how AI generates ethical paradoxes (not simple trade-offs) for a marginalized group through coexisting positive and negative feedback loops; and it offers a systematic framework, organized around the ideology of ableism, for structuring future theoretical and empirical research at the intersection of AI and disability entrepreneurship.
practical_implication: The framework is intended to guide inclusive technology design and evidence-based policymaking by clarifying when AI empowers rather than excludes disabled entrepreneurs. It highlights the need to attend to AI inclusivity and transparency, to how stakeholders (investors, partners, regulators) attribute success between entrepreneur and machine, and to governance mechanisms that protect sensitive disability-related data while preserving the empowering potential of AI.
limitations: The paper is explicitly conceptual and preliminary: it proposes a theoretical model and propositions rather than testing them empirically. It deliberately narrows scope to how AI influences entrepreneurs with disabilities and their interactions with the external environment, so broader ethical issues that arise when enterprises rely on AI fall outside its scope and the propositions await empirical validation.
future_research: The authors propose a structured research agenda mirroring the model's dimensions: using AI as an exogenous shock to study how lifting bodily/sensory constraints affects opportunity recognition and venture creation; examining the psychological effects of AI (which may operate even without adoption) on cognition, motivation, and self-efficacy; investigating the inclusiveness of AI as an extension of the body across heterogeneous impairment types; and studying how AI-mediated external evaluation (e.g., algorithmic screening, raised performance baselines, homogenized creativity, privacy of intimate data) reshapes legitimacy and resource access for disabled entrepreneurs.
abstract: AI has already generated transformative effects across domains of business and everyday life. However, the integration of AI into the scholarship of disability entrepreneurship remains nascent. One critical challenge is the difficulty to identify what constitutes a theoretically distinct effect of AI on disabled entrepreneurs and corresponding entrepreneurial processes, as opposed to merely exploring its use among a marginalized group. To address this issue, the present study systematically analyzes how AI uniquely affects disability entrepreneurs. On this basis, the paper proposes a preliminary theoretical model of disability entrepreneurship in the AI era that examines both the positive and negative impacts of AI on disabled entrepreneurs, as well as the ethical dilemmas that arise from these dynamics. It then outlines a set of theoretically meaningful and practically relevant research opportunities that bridge the literatures on AI and disability entrepreneurship. By doing so, this paper positions disability entrepreneurship as a theoretically significant research domain in the AI era, advances discussions of ethical dilemmas related to AI, and offers a systematic framework for guiding future theoretical and empirical research.
apa_citation: Zhang, M., & Li, W. (2026). Artificial intelligence and disability entrepreneurship: Moving the field forward. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-026-06249-0

title: Climate Beliefs and Attitudes and Corporate Tax Savings
methods: Archival panel study. Baseline firm-year OLS regressions of industry-size-adjusted effective tax rate on county-level CBA (Yale Climate Opinion Maps, PCA index) with firm/county controls and industry and year fixed effects, standard errors clustered by firm. Channel tests use a Zero-Inflated Poisson model (green patents) and CSR Escore/Sscore regressions. Identification via difference-in-differences (Hurricanes Harvey/Irma, staggered climate adaptation plans, headquarters relocations with PSM), conditional-mean analysis, a placebo/falsification test, and a Sobel path analysis.
industry: U.S. public firms across two-digit SIC industries, excluding financial and utility firms
units: firm-year observations (4,387 distinct firms)
authors: Zhang, Lei; Kanagaretnam, Kiridaran
theories: legitimacy theory; social norm theory; stakeholder theory
keywords: Climate beliefs and attitudes; Climate change; Corporate social responsibility; Corporate tax savings; Green innovation
research_question: Whether and to what extent do county-level climate beliefs and attitudes (CBA) surrounding a firm's headquarters influence corporate tax savings, and through what channels does CBA operate?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on social norm theory, the paper argues that managers of firms in high-CBA counties conform to a nascent, increasingly prevalent climate social norm even absent personal belief, because stakeholder pressure and reputational risk push them toward sustainable practices. Firms respond by investing in climate-related R&D and pro-environmental initiatives, which generate investment-related tax credits and thereby lower effective tax rates. The DiD, relocation, conditional-mean, and placebo tests are used to argue that it is CBA, rather than concurrent climate risk, that drives the lower tax payments.
theoretical_contribution: The study documents a counterintuitive positive link between CBA (and, by extension, CSR) and corporate tax avoidance, contrasting with the dominant negative CSR–tax-avoidance finding and with the "organized hypocrisy" view of CSR-as-tax-camouflage. It surfaces a "business ethics paradox" in which high-CBA firms simultaneously advance and undermine climate goals, contributing to the social-norms, climate-awareness, and taxes-and-sustainability literatures by identifying a previously undocumented nontax benefit of tax planning and raising distributive-justice and intergenerational-equity concerns.
practical_implication: Managers can align tax strategy with sustainability goals by channeling climate-related tax savings into verifiable green investments, demonstrating responsible corporate citizenship rather than financial arbitrage. Policymakers could institutionalize this alignment through regulatory safeguards—e.g., mandating that a defined portion of tax savings be allocated to carbon offsets or green R&D—so that private financial incentives serve collective decarbonization goals.
limitations: Data limitations prevent the authors from distinguishing conventional "dirty" R&D from climate-related R&D, and reported R&D expenses may not accurately reflect actual R&D expenditures. The CBA measure is a county-level proxy for managers' own beliefs rather than a direct measure, and headquarters relocation decisions are potentially endogenous to state tax policy (partially addressed with controls and an instrumental-variable approach).
future_research: The authors call for deeper investigation of how managers incorporate tax considerations into broader ESG initiatives, given the absence of an established theory or framework linking tax planning to ESG strategy. Further work is invited on the unintended consequences and ethical considerations of using climate-related tax incentives, particularly amid the growing popularity of policies such as the Inflation Reduction Act.
abstract: This study examines the relationship between climate beliefs and attitudes (CBA) and corporate tax savings. We document that firms headquartered in counties with high CBA are more likely to utilize climate change-related tax incentives to reduce their tax payments. Our evidence suggests that CBA’s influence manifests as increased climate-related R&D investments and corporate environmental commitments, which give rise to various investment-related tax credits, resulting in lower tax payments. Further analyses indicate that the positive relationship between CBA and corporate tax savings is more pronounced for firms in climate-vulnerable industries and those in states with climate action plans in place. Using attention-grabbing disaster events, the implementation of climate adaptation plans, and corporate headquarters relocation events, we document that heightened CBA incentivizes firms to realize greater tax benefits. Our findings present an important business ethics paradox: while firms in high-CBA counties genuinely invest in sustainability, they simultaneously engage in tax planning that reduces public revenue available for broader climate initiatives. This creates a tension between shareholder value maximization and responsible corporate citizenship, raising critical concerns about distributive justice and the alignment of private financial incentives with public environmental goals.
apa_citation: Zhang, L., & Kanagaretnam, K. (2026). Climate beliefs and attitudes and corporate tax savings. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-026-06254-3

title: Short-term fit, long-term trap: The career development lock of low-skilled gig workers
methods: Grounded theory (Strauss & Corbin, 1990) using open, axial, and selective coding in NVivo 11.0; 70 semi-structured interviews, 30 hours of field immersion, 8 months of online community observation, and media data.
industry: Platform gig work: crowdsourced food delivery riders and ride-hailing drivers
country: China (two cities with high concentration of delivery riders)
time_period: Field immersion (5 days/30 hours) plus 8 months of online observation; retrospective worker accounts
units: Low-skilled, self-employed (crowdsourced) gig workers
n_sample: 70 interviews with 42 food delivery riders and 14 ride-hailing drivers (141.2 hours), plus 820 observed riders
authors: Zheng, Xueni; Du, Jing; Xiang, Yuan; Long, Lirong; Xie, Huanyan
theories: labour process theory and algorithmic management (Gandini, 2019); person–environment fit theory (Edwards, 2008; Vleugels et al., 2023); subjective time / temporal sensemaking (Shipp & Jansen, 2021)
keywords: algorithmic management; career development lock; grounded theory; low-skilled gig workers; person–career fit
research_question: (1) What does the career development lock look like for low-skilled gig workers? (2) What is the role of algorithmic management in low-skilled gig workers' career development lock?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: Career development lock (four forms — lock-out, lock-in, lock-up, lock-down); short-term person–career fit vs. anticipated long-term person–career misfit; continuous temporal narrative (workers) vs. fragmented temporal logic (platform algorithms).
- Triggering conditions: Algorithmic management operating through agency suppression, resource deprivation, and normative framing (entrepreneurial/"freedom" discourse).
- Process / phases: Workers enter gig work as a strategic, temporary transition; algorithmic governance commodifies labour and dynamically intensifies discipline (declining pay, tightening control); this produces superficial short-term fit but growing long-term misfit.
- Practices: Adaptive self-exploitation — cycles of compensatory effort (longer hours, faster pace, greater risk) and "deferred futurity" that prioritize immediate survival over long-term development.
- Emergent outcomes: Voluntary retention and deepening entrapment; job-level constraints (lock-out, lock-in, lock-up) drive role-level lock-down, which feeds back to reinforce lock-out.

Career development lock arises from an irreconcilable conflict between workers' long-term aspirations (grounded in a continuous temporal narrative) and platform algorithms' need for flexible, on-demand labour (a fragmented temporal logic). Under algorithmic constraints, workers engage in adaptive self-exploitation that magnifies short-term fit while obscuring long-term misfit, reframing the erosion of labour protections as voluntary freedom and ultimately locking workers into the gig economy.
theoretical_contribution: The study introduces career development lock as a multidimensional, multidirectional, and dynamic form of entrapment, extending non-linear career concepts such as workplace "locked-in" (Stengård et al., 2016) and career inaction (Verbruggen & De Vos, 2020) by shifting the explanation from individual decision failure to exogenous structural forces. It recasts algorithmic management as a long-term career-structuring force—not merely a day-to-day control mechanism—that induces hidden adaptive self-exploitation, and it advances person–environment fit research by showing that fit and misfit coexist under different subjective time frames.
practical_implication: Platforms should adopt moderated algorithmic designs (per-worker assignment caps, dynamic time buffers, limits on surge allocation) and mandatory algorithmic transparency, complemented by portable benefits to buffer income volatility. Governments, unions, and non-profits should provide embedded vocational training, skills-upgrade subsidies, and community support centres, while platforms implement skill-certification and promotion systems to dismantle the mobility ceiling of flat job designs.
limitations: Findings are based on delivery riders and ride-hailing drivers, whose algorithmic oversight is more intensive than in other crowdwork, so generalizability to other low-skilled gig workers is uncertain. The study also relies on workers' retrospective accounts rather than longitudinal data tracking how lock unfolds over time.
future_research: Future work should compare career development lock across platform governance models (e.g., centralized vs. decentralized) and adopt longitudinal approaches such as event-history analysis to capture how workers move across the four lock dimensions over time. It could also examine worker agency through algorithmic activism—how gig workers individually or collectively resist career development lock.
abstract: Why do low-skilled gig workers remain stuck in work they originally intended as temporary? Although gig work is widely portrayed as flexible and temporary, our study shows that platforms can gradually trap workers in place. Drawing on grounded theory and fieldwork—including 70 interviews with 42 food delivery riders and 14 ride-hailing drivers, 30 hours of firsthand riding experience, and observations of online communities totalling 820 riders—this study develops a conceptual framework of career development lock, identifying four interrelated forms: lock-out, lock-in, lock-up, and lock-down. We find that career development lock stems from a structural conflict

title: The Ethics of Artificial Intelligence: Principles, Challenges, and Opportunities, by Luciano Floridi. Oxford University Press, 2023. 272 pp.
methods: Book review; critical essay engaging Floridi's normative framework for AI ethics
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Acosta-Navas, D.
theories: informational ethics; principlism (bioethics)
keywords: AI ethics; AI governance; engineering approach; ethics of artificial intelligence; good AI society; productive AI; sociotechnical systems; value tradeoffs
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary: Not reported in paper
theoretical_contribution: Not reported in paper
practical_implication: The reviewer argues that Floridi's normative frameworks and taxonomies offer compelling resources to motivate deeper philosophical engagement and to guide ethically informed practical discussions in AI ethics. The book is positioned as rich input for business ethics researchers interested in the responsibilities of organizations that develop, adopt, deploy, or regulate AI, and for understanding the role of frontier technologies in channeling large-scale societal transformation. Business ethicists are uniquely situated to contribute frameworks for the responsibilities of private actors in the AI ecosystem.
limitations: The reviewer identifies three limitations of Floridi's framework. First, despite its global aspirations, the book tends toward Eurocentrism in policy recommendations, emphasizing bureaucratic regulatory bodies that may be difficult to realize in countries with fewer technical and financial resources. Second, the book overlooks the problem of tradeoffs, assuming that optimal balances can be attained between conflicting societal values (e.g., open source vs. safety, privacy vs. fairness in facial recognition) rather than acknowledging that tragic choices may be unavoidable. Third, Floridi's "engineering approach" refuses to define AI and dismisses productive AI's capabilities based on a decade-old example (Watson), leaving conceptual ambiguities that powerful actors could exploit and failing to account for recent advances in agentic and reasoning systems.
future_research: The reviewer identifies three ripe areas for future research. First, developing policy recommendations beyond Eurocentric bureaucratic models, expanding on international cooperation, industry-wide standards, accountability, and scalable education for the public, government workers, and technologists. Second, investigating how to address value tradeoffs responsibly, including identifying procedural constraints such as participatory frameworks for reaching acceptable compromises. Third, examining the sociotechnical systems and meso-level institutions (research labs, corporations, professional associations) in which AI is developed and deployed, including the conditions for legitimate decision making, operational constraints, and relationships with governments. The reviewer also calls for a fully-fledged theory of agency and intelligence to ground AI regulation.
abstract: fter the public launch of ChatGPT, the Future of Life Institute published an open letter calling for a moratorium on the development of frontier artificial intelligence (AI) systems. It was signed by over thirty thousand scholars, industry leaders, and public intellectuals. Judging by media headlines at the time, the signatories were not alone. Open AI’s launch created widespread concern regarding the rapid pace of AI’s development, and the risks it posed for humanity. Luciano Floridi seems to disagree with the reasoning behind this initiative. In his book The Ethics of Artificial Intelligence, he states: “It is because we lack a clear sense of socio-political direction that we are worried by the speed of our technological travel. We should be. Yet the solution is not to slow down, but to decide together where we want to go” (79). With adequate normative frameworks for AI, he says, we can simultaneously mitigate its worst risks and reap its greatest benefits. The book sets out to provide systematic and comprehensive guidelines for what he calls a “good AI society.”
apa_citation: Acosta-Navas, D. (2026). The ethics of artificial intelligence: Principles, challenges, and opportunities, by Luciano Floridi. Oxford University Press, 2023. 272 pp. *Business Ethics Quarterly*, 36(1), 179-183. https://doi.org/10.1017/beq.2026.10108

title: Is academia addressing relevant questions to support a circularity transition in Latin America and the Caribbean?
methods: Convergent mixed-methods design combining 19 semi-structured expert interviews (generating 51 priority stakeholder questions), an updated PRISMA systematic literature review (335 peer-reviewed records, 405 research objectives), and thematic analysis following Braun and Clarke (2006); alignment between interview and literature datasets assessed with a Research Alignment Ratio (RAR) computed over 27 sub-themes within four overarching categories
industry: Cross-sectoral (waste management, manufacturing, agriculture/forestry/livestock, energy, construction)
country: Latin America and the Caribbean (11 countries plus 3 regional experts; literature dominated by Brazil 49%, Mexico 12%, Chile 8%, Colombia 6%)
time_period: Interviews March-November 2024; literature review 1997-April 2025 (updating Gallego-Schmid et al., 2024)
units: CE expert interviews and peer-reviewed scientific publications on CE in LAC
n_sample: 19 interviews (51 stakeholder questions); 335 publications (405 research objectives)
authors: Aguilar-Hernandez, G. A.; Guerrero, A. B.; Vásquez-Ibarra, L.; Rebolledo-Leiva, R.; Gallego-Schmid, A.
theories: stakeholder engagement; transdisciplinary research
keywords: Circular economy; Global South; Latin America and the Caribbean; Research gaps; Stakeholder engagement; Thematic analysis
research_question: Is academia addressing relevant questions to support the Circular Economy transition in Latin America and the Caribbean? Specifically, the paper determines (1) which stakeholder questions are being directly or indirectly addressed in current literature, (2) which areas remain underexplored or overlooked, and (3) how research agendas can be better aligned to support CE implementation.
mechanism_summary:
- Two datasets compared: 51 priority questions from 19 CE expert interviews and 405 research objectives from 335 peer-reviewed publications on CE in LAC
- Four overarching thematic categories (with 27 sub-themes): (1) technology and data, (2) culture, education and society, (3) economy and market, (4) policy
- Research Alignment Ratio (RAR): per-sub-theme ratio of interview frequency to literature frequency (centered on 1), classified as High, Medium, or Low alignment
- Three key factors driving misalignment between academic research and stakeholder needs: (1) science communication, (2) the process of setting research priorities and defining expected roles, and (3) the level of transdisciplinarity within CE research in LAC

The paper characterizes alignment as a descriptive, thematic comparison rather than a causal test: thematic overlaps at the category level coexist with considerable sub-theme misalignments, particularly in socio-cultural, economic, and policy dimensions. Drawing on the discussion, the authors argue that misalignment is sustained where science communication is fragmented and one-way rather than dialogue-based, where research priorities are set without participatory engagement with non-academic actors (leaving sub-themes such as citizen engagement, SME economic impacts, or monitoring of public policy underexplored), and where CE research remains disciplinarily siloed rather than transdisciplinary. Pragmatic stakeholder engagement, boundary organizations, stronger national-level financial instruments, and transdisciplinary research processes that bridge academic fields while actively involving practitioners and policymakers are offered as enabling conditions for closer alignment.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances the debate on the societal role of academic research in sustainability transitions by providing an empirically grounded, LAC-specific assessment of alignment between stakeholder priorities and academic CE research. It contributes a Research Alignment Ratio methodology for systematically comparing thematic frequencies across stakeholder and literature datasets, and identifies three key drivers of misalignment (science communication, priority setting, and transdisciplinarity). It positions LAC as a Global South case study that invites the global CE research community to reflect on whether current research agendas adequately address pressing stakeholder needs while preserving academia's critical independence.
practical_implication: Policymakers, businesses, and practitioners gain a clearer view of where existing CE research provides an actionable knowledge base and where gaps remain—especially in culture/education/society, economy/market, and policy sub-themes such as cross-sectoral interaction, citizen engagement, SME economic impacts, policy instruments, and monitoring of public policy. For academic researchers and funders, the study calls for strengthening participatory research designs, dialogue-based science communication, national-level financial instruments for CE research, and transdisciplinary collaborations that actively involve practitioners and policymakers to translate scientific insights into CE implementation in LAC.
limitations: The interview sample size (19 experts, 35% response rate) may limit full thematic saturation, reflecting the comparatively small pool of CE experts in LAC versus regions such as Europe. There is a mismatch between interviewees' country expertise and the literature's concentration on Brazil (only 21% of interviewees specialized in Brazil while ~49% of reviewed studies focus on it), which may introduce bias. The degree of coordination and collaboration among CE researchers themselves across LAC was not directly measured.
future_research: Further country-level research—particularly in Brazil, Mexico, Chile, and Colombia—should refine understanding of research alignment in specific national contexts. Future work should investigate the degree of connection and collaboration among CE researchers across LAC, the role of gender dynamics given that ~70% of interviewees were women and CE initiatives in the region appear often driven by women, and how participatory and pragmatic stakeholder engagement approaches can be applied to LAC's cultural and institutional context to co-develop research agendas. Additional empirical work is needed on the informal sector's CE practices beyond waste management.
abstract: Latin America and the Caribbean (LAC) is advancing toward a Circular Economy (CE), supported by expanding policy initiatives and a growing body of academic research. Over the past decade, CE academic studies in the region have examined developments at multiple sectors, scales and circular strategies, thus, contributing to CE research in the region. Yet, it remains uncertain whether academic research addresses the critical questions most relevant for non-academic stakeholders—including policymakers, practitioners, and businesses—to support the CE transition in LAC. To assess this alignment, we compared 51 priority questions identified by 19 CE experts in LAC countries with the research objectives of 335 scientific publications on CE in the region. The analysis reveals the extent to which stakeholder priorities are reflected in academic literature and highlights persistent gaps where further research is needed. Our findings stress the importance of fostering more collaborative, transdisciplinary, and stakeholder-oriented research to accelerate a just and effective CE transition in LAC.
apa_citation: Aguilar-Hernandez, G. A., Guerrero, A. B., Vásquez-Ibarra, L., Rebolledo-Leiva, R., & Gallego-Schmid, A. (2026). Is academia addressing relevant questions to support a circularity transition in Latin America and the Caribbean? *Journal of Industrial Ecology*, 30(2), 555–569. https://doi.org/10.1007/s44498-026-00038-8

title: Fair Market Value of Used Capacity Assets: Forecasts for Repurposed Electric Vehicle Batteries
methods: Analytical valuation model extended from prior replacement-value and user-cost-of-capital frameworks, combined with empirical calibration using battery cost and degradation data for LFP and NCX chemistries in the U.S. and China
industry: Lithium-ion battery and stationary energy storage
time_period: Forecasts over the coming decade (through approximately 2031)
units: Battery chemistries (LFP and NCX) and their fair market values for second-life applications
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Bach, A.; Onori, S.; Reichelstein, S.; Zhuang, J.
theories: no-arbitrage pricing; replacement value theory; user cost of capital
keywords: capacity assets; fair market valuation; recycling; repurposing; second life application; used EV batteries
research_question: How should the fair market value of used capacity assets be determined when owners face a choice between immediate recycling and repurposing for a second-life application, and what does this imply for the economic viability of repurposing retired lithium-ion EV batteries (LFP vs. NCX) in the U.S. and China over the coming decade?
mechanism_summary: The authors extend replacement-value and user-cost-of-capital models to assets that can be recycled or repurposed. Under no-arbitrage pricing for energy storage services, the fair market value of a used asset equals the discounted cash flows from its remaining capacity valued at the user cost of capital, with two new elements: a terminal recycling payoff and a repurposing cost. The effective FMV at the end of first life is the better of immediate recycling versus costly repurposing followed by second-life deployment and delayed recycling. Because LFP and NCX chemistries compete in the same capacity market, NCX values are bounded by the lower user cost of capital set by LFP, so NCX batteries with high raw-material recycling value are pushed toward immediate recycling while LFP batteries remain attractive for repurposing.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends prior accounting and economic studies on the replacement value of operating assets (Arrow 1964; Rogerson 2008; Rajan and Reichelstein 2009) by incorporating two new elements into the user-cost-of-capital framework: a terminal recycling cash flow and an explicit repurposing cost that gates entry into a second-life application. The authors develop a no-arbitrage valuation framework in which multiple competing asset technologies determine the user cost of capital for capacity services, yielding a general model of how technological progress, repurposing costs, and raw-material values jointly shape repurpose-versus-recycle decisions. The framework generalizes beyond EV batteries to other second-life assets such as consumer electronics, data-center hardware, industrial equipment, and renewable-power components.
practical_implication: For LFP batteries, repurposing into stationary energy storage is economically viable in both the U.S. and China over the coming decade, supporting business cases and investment in second-life deployment. For NCX batteries, owners should generally favor immediate recycling, especially in China and for batteries below roughly 80 percent state of health, with recycling becoming preferred in the U.S. even at 90 percent SOH by around 2031. Managers and investors can use the framework to guide corporate investment toward recycling versus repurposing infrastructure, and automakers may redesign vehicles (e.g., cell-to-body structural batteries for NCX, repurposing-friendly designs for LFP) and contractual arrangements (e.g., retained battery ownership rights) around these asset economics.
limitations: The model assumes competitive and frictionless markets for new and used assets and treats key parameters (such as degradation pathways and raw-material values) as known; introducing uncertainty, volatility, and real-option effects is acknowledged as a complication. The empirical application is restricted to two battery chemistries (LFP and NCX) and two markets (U.S. and China), and does not incorporate temporary recycling or repurposing capacity constraints or risk aversion on the part of decision-makers.
future_research: Future work could examine the sensitivity of fair market value estimates to market frictions such as limited recycling or repurposing capacity, and extend the framework to emerging battery chemistries such as sodium-ion and solid-state batteries, whose declining costs would further reduce the user cost of capital and the fair market value of incumbent chemistries. Researchers could also explore how vehicle owners will capture the residual value of used battery packs, including contractual models in which manufacturers retain ownership rights over the battery in exchange for lower upfront vehicle prices.
abstract: In response to growing economic and environmental concerns, companies in a range of industries seek to repurpose products (assets) that retain functional capacity beyond their initial first life. This paper examines a generic valuation model for used capacity assets that can either be recycled immediately or repurposed for a second life application. We apply our model framework to lithium-ion batteries retired from electric vehicles, as these assets typically retain substantial energy storage capacity at the end of their first life. Our analysis focuses on two battery chemistries: lithium-iron-phosphate (LFP) and nickel-cobalt-X (NCX) based. We project their future fair market values in the United States and China. Our findings indicate that repurposing LFP batteries will be economically viable in both countries for the coming decade. In contrast, for most NCX batteries immediate recycling will soon be preferable due to their more valuable raw material content and shorter usable lives.
apa_citation: Bach, A., Onori, S., Reichelstein, S., & Zhuang, J. (2026). Fair market value of used capacity assets: Forecasts for repurposed electric vehicle batteries. *The Accounting Review*, 101(2), 33-55. https://doi.org/10.2308/TAR-2025-0024

title: Memento Mori: mortality salience, legacy and time perspective in sustainable consumption
methods: Cross-sectional online survey; moderated mediation analysis using Hayes' PROCESS macro Model 9 with bootstrap confidence intervals (5,000 resamples)
industry: General consumers (online survey panel)
units: Adults aged over 25
authors: Bae, H.; Jo, S. H.
theories: socioemotional selectivity theory; terror management theory
keywords: Future time perspective; Mortality legacy; Mortality salience; Socioemotional selectivity theory; Sustainable consumption; Terror management theory
research_question: How does mortality salience influence sustainable consumption behavior, and do mortality legacy (as mediator) and limited future time perspective and chronological age (as moderators) shape this relationship, drawing on terror management theory and socioemotional selectivity theory?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on terror management theory, the authors argue that awareness of death prompts individuals to manage existential anxiety through distal defenses such as upholding cultural worldviews and boosting self-esteem. In contemporary society, sustainability functions as a dominant moral worldview, so mortality salience heightens consumers' desire to leave a mortality legacy — a lasting positive contribution that transcends physical death — which in turn channels behavior into sustainable consumption as a form of symbolic immortality. Integrating socioemotional selectivity theory, the authors further theorize that consumers with a limited future time perspective perceive time as scarce and therefore become more motivated to engage in generative, legacy-oriented behaviors, strengthening the mediated path from mortality salience to sustainable consumption via mortality legacy. Chronological age was hypothesized to operate similarly but was not supported empirically.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends terror management theory by demonstrating a novel, constructive distal defense: people adopt sustainable consumption behavior, via the desire for a mortality legacy, as a way to manage death-related anxiety and achieve symbolic immortality. It contributes to the literature on antecedents of sustainable consumption by identifying mortality salience and legacy motivation as psychological drivers, and it advances socioemotional selectivity theory by showing that limited future time perspective — but not chronological age — moderates the indirect effect, suggesting that psychological rather than chronological markers of time horizon matter most in mortality-driven sustainable consumption.
practical_implication: Marketers and policymakers can promote sustainable consumption by designing communications that subtly elicit consumers' awareness of mortality and foster a desire to leave a lasting legacy, framing sustainability around "protecting what matters for future generations" or "leaving a meaningful mark" rather than using explicit fear-based appeals. Because age did not moderate the effect, such interventions can be deployed broadly without age-specific tailoring, though they should be implemented ethically and transparently. Managers should also consider consumers' future time perspective when deploying mortality salience appeals, since legacy motivations are strongest among those who perceive their time as limited.
limitations: The survey data were collected only in the USA, which may limit generalizability, especially given that consumer responses to mortality salience, mortality legacy, and sustainable consumption may differ between Asian and Western cultures. The study did not empirically compare the relative strength of opposing psychological mechanisms — helplessness or avoidance versus legacy motives — that mortality salience can elicit. The lack of a significant age moderation effect also invites reconsideration of how chronological age operates within these processes.
future_research: Future research should examine whether mortality salience similarly affects sustainable consumption behavior in Asian cultural contexts (e.g., South Korea, China, Japan) where religious traditions such as Buddhism may shape death-related cognition. Researchers should explore alternative moderating variables such as cultural values, personal environmental attitudes, and socioeconomic status, and investigate psychological markers of age (e.g., subjective, felt, or cognitive age) rather than chronological age. Further work should also examine boundary conditions under which helplessness versus legacy motives dominate as responses to mortality salience.
abstract: Purpose – The purpose of this study is to advance the understanding of the interplay between mortality salience, mortality legacy and sustainable consumption behavior. Design/methodology/approach – Data were collected through an online questionnaire from 1,000 consumers aged over 25 in the USA. Findings – The results show that mortality salience induces sustainable behavior and that mortality legacy mediates this relationship. Moreover, a limited future time perspective is found to moderate the mediation process in the path from mortality salience to mortality legacy. Furthermore, age did not emerge as a significant moderator of the indirect effect of mortality salience on sustainable consumption behavior through mortality legacy. Originality/value – The findings connect the terror management theory with the socioemotional selectivity theory. They also reveal that marketers can nudge consumers’ sustainable consumption behavior by incorporating mortality salience and legacy appeals into their marketing communications.
apa_citation: Bae, H., & Jo, S. H. (2026). Memento mori: Mortality salience, legacy and time perspective in sustainable consumption. *Journal of Consumer Marketing*, 1-12. https://doi.org/10.1108/JCM-09-2025-8257

title: 2025 Presidential Address: Activism, Impact, and Science: Management Scholarship in a Challenging Era
methods: Editorial essay (AOM Presidential Address); reflective argumentation with illustrative references to prior research and AOM initiatives
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Bamberger, P.
theories: engaged scholarship
keywords: academic activism; academic freedom; business school accreditation; engaged scholarship; global inclusion; open science; rigor and relevance; scholar-practitioner gap; scholarly impact; scientific integrity
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary: Bamberger advances a normative argument rather than a causal model. He contends that management scholarship can have impact only if it is relevant, but that relevance must be pursued without sacrificing rigor or scientific credibility. He argues that the payoff structures governing scholarship (citation-based metrics, high-impact journal counts, and ranking systems) incentivize questionable research practices and crowd out socially useful work; that academic activism, while understandable, can undermine open science and fuel anti-intellectualism when advocacy shapes design and execution; and that engaged scholarship — agenda-setting, globally inclusive, and communicated accessibly to practitioners and publics — is the route by which management science can simultaneously enhance rigor, relevance, and credibility.
theoretical_contribution: The address synthesizes three AOM-led initiatives into a programmatic vision for the field: (a) reforming the definition and measurement of scholarly impact through an AACSB-led coalition aimed at updating accreditation and ranking criteria to reward evidence-based real-world impact; (b) reaffirming academic freedom alongside open science and scientific integrity, with explicit warnings about how advocacy-driven research erodes credibility; and (c) building bridges for global relevance by extending AOM's infrastructure to underrepresented regions and by translating research for practitioner and public audiences. Bamberger frames these as components of Van de Ven's engaged scholarship, arguing that this stance is how the field can "set the agenda" on issues such as human-capital sustainability, business as a vehicle for peace and development, and redesigning educational organizations.
practical_implication: Business school deans, journal editors, publishers, and tenure committees should adjust incentive structures so that evidence-based, real-world impact and diverse forms of contribution — not just theoretical novelty or high-impact-journal placement — are recognized and rewarded. Scholars studying politically loaded topics (e.g., DEI, pay transparency, inequality) should keep advocacy out of research design and execution and report findings fully, including inconvenient ones. The field should invest in globally inclusive infrastructure (memberships, workshops, open-access outlets, rotating meetings) and in practitioner-accessible communication channels such as AOM Today so that research can reach and inform decision-makers.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Bamberger calls on the field to move from reactive to agenda-setting research, including work that: (a) broadens sustainability research to encompass human-capital sustainability and work-based interventions; (b) examines how business organizations can serve as vehicles for sustainable economic development and regional conflict resolution; and (c) develops and tests new models of educational organizations to replace industrial-scale public schooling grounded in scientific management.

title: Supporting repairability analysis through graph-based visualisation
methods: Three-stage methodology combining (I) input data structuring via an EN 45554-based Repair Matrix, (II) graph visualisation in Gephi using the ForceAtlas2 layout and (III) analytical output based on network metrics (degree, modularity, eccentricity, betweenness centrality) plus a novel graph-based Repair Index; demonstrated through an in-depth single-product case study.
industry: Small household appliances (capsule coffee machines)
units: Product (capsule coffee machine) modelled as a graph of components and connections
n_sample: 1 product case study
authors: Blanco-Espeleta, E.; Pérez-Belis, V.; Bovea, M. D.
theories: graph theory; network analysis
keywords: Circular economy; EN 45554; Graph-based modelling; Industrial ecology; Network metrics; Repair Index
research_question: The main objective of the study is to assess the potential of graph-based modelling and network metrics as tools for evaluating product repairability, including identifying the required input data, the most relevant graph tools and metrics, appropriate visualisation strategies, and a new graph-based metric to quantify repairability at the network level.
mechanism_summary: The authors argue that repairability is driven not only by component-level attributes but by the structural configuration of the product's architecture. By encoding EN 45554-based Repair Matrix scores as node and edge attributes in a graph and applying the ForceAtlas2 layout in Gephi, the methodology makes visible how connectivity, accessibility and sequencing shape repair effort. Network metrics such as degree, modularity, eccentricity and betweenness centrality then translate this structure into quantitative indicators of complexity, accessibility constraints and critical components, which are aggregated into a novel graph-based Repair Index that summarises system-level repairability.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances repairability assessment by integrating analysis, visualisation and parametrisation of repair-related information within a single graph-theoretic framework, addressing the gap left by conventional scoring matrices and static diagrams that do not preserve architectural logic. It introduces a novel graph-based Repair Index tailored to repairability, combining EN 45554-based parameters with network metrics so that accessibility, sequencing and part centrality are directly embedded in the assessment. In doing so, it contributes a conceptually scalable and interpretable methodology that links product architecture to circular-economy objectives of repairability and sustainability.
practical_implication: Designers, policymakers and manufacturers can use the graph-based methodology as a decision-support tool to identify high-priority parts that should be redesigned to reduce dependency, improve accessibility or enhance modularity. By combining macro-level graph insights with micro-level node analysis and visual ranking, the approach provides actionable guidance for design-for-repair strategies and supports compliance with ecodesign and Right-to-Repair regulations such as Regulation (EU) 2024/1781.
limitations: The work is an exploratory investigation tested through an in-depth case study on a single product configuration (a capsule coffee machine), so the immediate generalisation of the results is limited. The current implementation relies on expert-based scoring from the EN 45554-based Repair Matrix, whose weights may vary depending on the experts consulted, and Gephi is a general-purpose tool that does not interpret product structures or handle hierarchical assemblies, with modelling and data entry still performed manually.
future_research: Future work will apply the approach to additional product models and categories to assess its versatility and generalisability, and will conduct systematic sensitivity and uncertainty analyses to examine how alternative weighting schemes influence the indicators. Empirical validation in collaboration with repair centres and manufacturers is planned to compare model-derived indicators (disassembly paths, repair effort, graph-based RI) with real-world repair times, frequencies and outcomes. The authors also plan to automate data integration and graph generation by linking the EN 45554-based Repair Matrix and the graph-based model directly to CAD environments, using machine learning to assist in the automatic identification and classification of components and connections.
abstract: Repairability is a key aspect of circular economy strategies, contributing to product longevity and resource efficiency. However, existing assessment methods often provide limited insight into product architecture and rarely combine visual and quantitative analysis. This study proposes a graph-based modelling approach for repairability assessment that integrates network metrics with structural visualisation. Building on EN 45554, an EN 45554-based Repair Matrix (RM) is used to assign repair-related attributes to product components and their connections. These attributes are represented as nodes and edges within a network, enabling both visual exploration and quantitative analysis of product architecture. A three-stage methodology is developed: (i) input data structuring, (ii) graph visualisation using Gephi software and (iii) analytical output. In addition, a novel graph-based Repair Index (RI) is introduced to quantify repairability at the system level. The approach is demonstrated through an in-depth case study of a capsule coffee machine. The results show that graph metrics such as degree, modularity, eccentricity and betweenness centrality effectively reveal structural complexity, accessibility constraints and critical components. The proposed method offers a conceptually scalable and interpretable framework that supports designfor-repair strategies by highlighting component dependencies and optimising disassembly logic. Overall, this work contributes to the development of more repairable and sustainable product architectures, supporting circular economy objectives.
apa_citation: Blanco-Espeleta, E., Pérez-Belis, V., & Bovea, M. D. (2026). Supporting repairability analysis through graph-based visualisation. *Journal of Industrial Ecology*, 30(2), 461–474. https://doi.org/10.1007/s44498-026-00031-1

title: Englishization Beyond the Global North: Dependency, Struggle, and Resistance in Brazilian Management Research
methods: Qualitative study; 55 semi-structured interviews with Brazilian management academics and journal editors conducted in three waves (2020-2023); abductive analysis with constant comparison, line-by-line coding, and axial coding
industry: Higher education / management academia
units: Management academics (young and senior researchers) and journal editors in Brazilian management academia
n_sample: 55 interviews
authors: Boussebaa, M.; Alcadipani, R.; Vianna, F.; Pina e Cunha, M.; Clegg, S.
theories: dependency theory; postcolonial theory
keywords: Brazil; Englishization; Frankenstein papers; Global North; Global South; academic dependency; counter-hegemonic resistance; dependency theory; management research; postcolonial ambivalence; postcolonial theory; strategic compliance
research_question: How are imperatives to publish in English experienced within Brazilian management academia, and how do dynamics of dependency and postcoloniality shape these experiences?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: academic dependency (Alatas); postcolonial ambivalence and mimicry (Bhabha); Global North–South epistemic hierarchies; intra-national institutional divide between elite management schools and public universities
- Process / phases (the paper's three overarching themes): (1) Navigating Englishization — institutional pressures and strategic appropriation by Brazilian journals and evaluation systems; (2) Struggling with Englishization — linguistic barriers and institutional/material constraints producing "Frankenstein" papers; (3) Contesting Englishization — strategic compliance, postcolonial ambivalence, and counter-hegemonic resistance
- Modes of dependency: pressure from international journal rankings; Qualis and SciELO evaluation systems rewarding English-language publication; elite schools' international-publication expectations; Brazilian journals' own adoption of English in pursuit of visibility
- Practices / acts of resistance: perfunctory mimicry "without mastery" via cheap translations; editors' reluctant compliance while criticizing English as colonization; refusing editorial roles in English-only journals; founding Portuguese-only journals (e.g., RECADM, RCD); requesting exclusion from Qualis; withdrawing from doctoral programs requiring English
- Boundary conditions: elite management schools vs. public universities; availability of translation/editing funds; Brazilian currency devaluation; academic seniority and bilingual capital; journals' ranking status on international lists

Drawing on dependency theory and postcolonial theory, the paper theorizes Englishization as both reflective and constitutive of academic dependency: global Anglophone hierarchies and rankings pressure Global South research systems to align with Northern epistemic norms, and domestic institutions — Qualis, SciELO, and Brazilian journals themselves — internalize these standards in pursuit of international visibility. Within this structural terrain, materially constrained academics and editors engage in hybrid, ambivalent practices that simultaneously reproduce and destabilize the dominance of English. Englishization is thus enacted as a contested, uneven process shaped by the interplay of Global North–South inequalities, domestic institutional logics, and constrained local agency.
theoretical_contribution: The study offers a more nuanced, context-sensitive, and theoretically-informed account of Englishization by (1) challenging the assumption that Englishization unfolds uniformly across national contexts and foregrounding intra-national institutional variation (elite management schools vs. public universities); (2) conceptualizing Englishization as both reflective and constitutive of academic dependency, reproduced through domestic institutions' strategic appropriation of Anglo-American norms under structural constraint; and (3) demonstrating that Englishization is actively negotiated through hybrid practices—strategic compliance, postcolonial ambivalence, and counter-hegemonic resistance—rather than being simply adopted or resisted. By bridging dependency and postcolonial theory, the paper advances a politically grounded understanding of Englishization as entangled with enduring Global North–South inequalities.
practical_implication: Business schools, journal editors, and research-evaluation bodies in the Global South should recognize that imposing English-language publication requirements without supporting linguistic and material resources can produce poorly translated "Frankenstein" papers that undermine rather than enhance international visibility. Domestic evaluation systems (e.g., Qualis, SciELO) and local journals should reconsider policies that reward English-language publication as a proxy for quality, and support linguistic diversity and Portuguese-language scholarship as legitimate venues. More broadly, decolonizing management knowledge production requires attending to the intra-national inequalities and material constraints that shape whose scholarship becomes visible in global academic circuits.
limitations: Findings from Brazil may not generalize to other Global South contexts with different colonial histories, economic structures, and academic traditions. The study focused on academics and journal editors but paid limited attention to subfields or subdisciplines within management, and its interpretive qualitative design limits broad generalizability and may reflect self-reporting biases.
future_research: Future studies should investigate Englishization in other Global South countries (e.g., China, former British and French colonies) and use comparative cross-national designs to examine how different colonial legacies and academic traditions shape dependency, struggle, and resistance. Research should also explore disciplinary variation in Englishization within Brazilian and other Global South contexts, use quantitative methods to systematically examine patterns of Englishization and its impact on visibility, and extend analysis to teaching and service domains of academic work.
abstract: This paper advances understanding of Englishization in management research by addressing a key limitation in the existing literature: its predominant focus on the Global North, especially Europe. Drawing on dependency theory and postcolonial theory, we examine how management academics experience and navigate Englishization in Brazil. Based on qualitative research, we found that Brazilian academics, like their European counterparts, faced growing pressures to publish in international journals, effectively compelling publication in English, though such expectations were largely concentrated in elite institutions. We also found that Brazilian journals themselves increasingly adopted English in pursuit of international visibility, thereby intensifying pressure to publish in English and extending Englishization beyond the elite. One consequence was the publication of “Frankenstein” papers—the result of limited English proficiency and financial constraints—which paradoxically risked undermining the visibility Brazilian journals sought to enhance. Yet Englishization was not passively accepted: it was actively negotiated through strategic compliance, postcolonial ambivalence, and counter-hegemonic resistance. These findings contribute to a more nuanced, context-sensitive, and theoretically-informed understanding of Englishization by applying postcolonial and dependency perspectives to highlight its uneven trajectory across and within national contexts and to show how it is entangled with enduring Global North–South inequalities.
apa_citation: Boussebaa, M., Alcadipani, R., Vianna, F., Pina e Cunha, M., & Clegg, S. (2026). Englishization beyond the Global North: Dependency, struggle, and resistance in Brazilian management research. *Academy of Management Learning & Education*, 25(2), 187–205. https://doi.org/10.5465/amle.2023.0245

title: Are Social Media Platforms a Threat to Democracy? An Ecosystem Governance Perspective
methods: Conceptual 'Counterpoint' essay; develops an ecosystem-governance framework that reconceptualizes misinformation, fake news, and polarization on social media platforms as 'ecosystem failures' rather than political-space problems, and derives a four-mechanism reflexive governance architecture (participatory governance, adaptive control, integrity safeguards, intentional value redistribution) through theoretical synthesis of platform ecosystem and stakeholder-governance literatures, illustrated with secondary evidence (e.g., Cambridge Analytica, Zollo et al. 2017, Velichety & Shrivastava 2022).
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Cennamo, C.; Karanovic, J.
theories: ecosystem failures (Jacobides, Cennamo & Gawer 2024); ecosystem theory / ecosystem governance; generativity tension (desired vs. undesired generativity); multi-sided platform / two-sided markets theory; stakeholder governance theory (Bacq & Aguilera 2021)
keywords: democracy; digital; ecosystem failures; ecosystem governance; multi-sided markets; social media platforms
research_question: Are social media platforms inherently threatening democracy, and if so, is the appropriate response to reimagine them as political/deliberative spaces — or is it instead to treat their harms (fake news, misinformation, polarization) as 'ecosystem failures' and redesign platform governance to internalize these negative externalities?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: SMP harms are not defects of commercial nature per se but endogenous 'ecosystem failures' — structural consequences of algorithmic curation, strong network effects with near-zero replication costs, and engagement-based monetization decoupled from editorial standards.
- Key constructs: ecosystem failure (value capture distortion / coordination failure / structural pathology); negative externalities of user attention markets; generativity tension between 'desired' and 'undesired' output; multidimensional value (short-term engagement vs. long-term trust, informational quality, ecosystem health).
- Diagnosis of three failures: (1) fake news as value-capture distortion (virality uncorrelated with truth); (2) misinformation as coordination failure (selective exposure and biased amplification); (3) polarization as structural ecosystem pathology (engagement-driven personalization endogenizing ideological segmentation).
- Proposed mechanisms (reflexive ecosystem governance): participatory governance, adaptive control, integrity safeguards, and intentional value redistribution — operating ex ante by re-pricing visibility and rewards rather than ex post content moderation.
- Boundary conditions / trade-offs: platform heterogeneity (not all SMPs face the same failures); advertiser brand-safety vs. user preferences for controversial content; risk of over- or under-moderation; political capture of moderation authority.

The authors argue that because SMPs are ecosystem architects whose governance design choices shape who sees what and why, misinformation and polarization cannot be corrected by reactive moderation, user education, or by reimagining platforms as public utilities. Instead, changing the structure of the payoff — embedding integrity costs into ranking, replication, and reward rules — realigns private incentives with ecosystem health, so that high-quality engagement dominates low-integrity virality as a design-time rather than police-time outcome.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances a 'reflexive ecosystem governance' framework that reframes platform democratic harms as ecosystem failures rather than political-space failures, and operationalizes Jacobides, Cennamo & Gawer's (2024) ecosystem-failure typology for social media through four governance mechanisms adapted from Bacq and Aguilera's (2021) stakeholder governance theory. In doing so, it distinguishes SMP externalities from traditional-media externalities along three structural dimensions (algorithmic selectivity, near-zero replication cost, engagement–monetization decoupling), and reframes platform governance from incentive-control dyads toward multidimensional value management — what the authors call 'platform governance 2.0'.
practical_implication: Platforms should move from ex post content moderation toward ex ante governance design: risk-adjusted ranking, demotion of low-integrity content, bot-detection and political-ad transparency, participatory algorithm-review panels, intentional redistribution of visibility to public-interest journalists and educators, and algorithmic audits with trusted flaggers. Regulators (e.g., under the EU Digital Services Act) should focus on requiring platforms to identify, assess, and mitigate systemic risks rather than directly adjudicating content, and should resist solutions that effectively make platforms arbiters of truth.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors call for further scholarly work on the future of platform governance and on designing more trustworthy digital ecosystems, including how to distinguish structural incentives from actual content effects across heterogeneous SMP models, how to operationalize reflexive governance mechanisms in practice, and how regulation (e.g., DSA-style systemic-risk reporting) can complement rather than substitute for platform-level ecosystem design.
abstract: Castello, Colleoni, Scherer, and Trittin contend that social media platforms threaten democratic processes by facilitating the spread of misinformation and fostering polarized debates – dynamics that ultimately serve to monetize user attention. We acknowledge the problem but challenge their proposed solution of conceptualizing social media as political spaces. Instead, drawing on ecosystem theory, we propose an 'ecosystem failure' analysis as a more robust analytical framework. Within this perspective, we see these information-related problems as negative externalities that weaken the entire platform ecosystem. These externalities are structurally distinct due to algorithmic curation that selectively amplifies content in ways that reinforce user biases, strong network effects with almost zero replication costs, and engagement-based monetization that decouples production from editorial standards. We therefore advance a reflexive ecosystem governance approach that accounts for broader ecosystem value, shifting the focus beyond short-term economic performance to encompass overall ecosystem health. This approach transforms the management of negative externalities into a core strategic interest for the platform and aligns the incentives of individual actors ex ante, by design, prioritizing risk-adjusted reach and demotion of low-integrity content over ex post moderation. We analyse the mechanisms that align private incentives with democratic resilience and highlight the inherent trade-offs involved.
apa_citation: Cennamo, C., & Karanovic, J. (2026). Are social media platforms a threat to democracy? An ecosystem governance perspective. *Journal of Management Studies*. https://doi.org/10.1111/joms.70044
