title: Constructing Carbon Abatement Cost Curves
methods: Analytical/conceptual model development of a generic combinatorial abatement cost framework; numerical calibration to European cement plants using industry data (ECRA 2022) with sensitivity analyses in the appendices
industry: Cement manufacturing (Portland cement), with discussion of steel, chemicals, power, and transportation
country: European Union (cement producers subject to the EU Emissions Trading System)
time_period: Calibration based on recent industry data, including 2023 average EU ETS carbon price (€85/ton)
units: European cement reference plants and their abatement lever portfolios
n_sample: Nine elementary abatement levers, yielding up to 2^9 = 512 combined levers; 18 cost-efficient combined levers identified
authors: Glenk, G.; Meier, R.; Reichelstein, S.
theories: combinatorial optimization of production decisions; life-cycle costing / cost accounting; marginal abatement cost theory
keywords: capacity investments; carbon emissions; industrial decarbonization; life-cycle costing; marginal abatement cost
research_question: How can firms construct abatement cost curves that accurately capture the cost of alternative carbon dioxide emission reduction targets when elementary abatement levers exhibit interactions, and what do such curves imply for optimal corporate decarbonization pathways under carbon pricing regimes like the EU Emissions Trading System?
mechanism_summary: The paper articulates a cost-accounting mechanism. A firm choosing among a finite set of binary elementary levers (energy efficiency, process changes, input substitutions, fuel switching, carbon capture and sequestration, etc.) faces a combinatorial optimization problem in which the Total Abatement Cost is the life-cycle expenditure of the cheapest combined lever that meets a given emission target. Because the emission and cost effects of levers interact when deployed together, the resulting MAC curve is a step function that is not necessarily monotonically increasing, and the firm's optimal response to a carbon price is the combined lever whose marginal cost crosses the prevailing carbon price. Carbon prices therefore select among only a subset of cost-efficient combined levers, and shifts in prices generate discrete jumps in optimal abatement, investment, and production cost.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces a generic combinatorial model of abatement cost curves that, in contrast to the classical McKinsey-style MAC curves, explicitly accounts for interactions between elementary levers and yields step-function MAC curves that need not be monotonically increasing. By grounding abatement cost in life-cycle costing concepts familiar from management accounting, it bridges environmental economics and cost accounting and provides a tractable framework for deriving firms' optimal abatement responses, the carbon prices that trigger specific technologies, and the minimum lump-sum payments required under carbon contracts for difference. The paper thereby contributes a formal foundation for assessing the credibility of corporate decarbonization pledges and for quantifying the pass-through of carbon prices into product costs.
practical_implication: The framework gives companies and external analysts a planning tool for projecting decarbonization pathways, identifying cost-efficient combinations of abatement levers, and estimating the carbon prices required to make particular technologies (e.g., carbon capture and sequestration) economically viable. Applied to European cement, the model shows that a €85/ton EU ETS price incentivizes roughly a one-third reduction in direct emissions, while €126/ton triggers a 78 percent reduction and €141/ton delivers near-full decarbonization at an additional levelized product cost of only about €16 per ton of cement (roughly 12 percent of the 2023 market price). The approach also allows governments to gauge the minimum lump-sum payment needed to secure bilateral carbon contracts for difference with heavy-emitting firms.
limitations: The calibration is limited to European cement production and to currently available abatement technologies, so the quantitative estimates may not generalize to other industries or future cost trajectories. The model treats the adoption of elementary levers as a static, deterministic binary choice and abstracts from uncertainty in carbon prices and learning-driven improvements in abatement technologies. Residual emissions projections are contingent on current manufacturing and abatement technologies, and the authors note that such forecasts must be qualified by that reference point.
future_research: A promising extension is to model lever adoption as a stochastic, dynamic decision problem that incorporates uncertain learning effects for abatement technologies and volatile carbon prices, allowing firms to value the option of waiting before investing. The framework can also be extended to quantify the effects of alternative accounting rules for CO2 emissions, including combinations of biomass with carbon capture that could yield net-negative cement production. Finally, the authors encourage applying the model to other hard-to-decarbonize, essential industries such as steel, glass, and chemicals.
abstract: Companies across industries face increasing pressure to assess the costs of decarbonizing their operations. This paper develops a generic model for constructing abatement cost curves in connection with carbon dioxide emissions. The resulting abatement cost curves provide a planning tool for companies seeking to project their decarbonization pathways and to determine optimal abatement levels in response to environmental regulations such as carbon pricing. We calibrate our model in the context of European cement producers that are required to obtain emission permits under the European Emissions Trading System. We find that a price of e85 per ton of carbon dioxide, as observed on average in 2023, incentivizes firms to reduce their annual direct emissions by about one-third relative to the status quo. Yet, this incentive increases sharply when prices rise above the benchmark of e100 per ton of carbon dioxide.
apa_citation: Glenk, G., Meier, R., & Reichelstein, S. (2026). Constructing carbon abatement cost curves. *The Accounting Review*, 101(3), 223-255. https://doi.org/10.2308/TAR-2025-0315

title: Joining Decision-Making, Moral Thinking, and Collective Action: Grand Challenges as a Phenomenology of Deliberation
methods: Conceptual article; inter-theoretical integration of the Carnegie School (CS) theory of organization and justification theory (JT) / economies of worth to develop a phenomenological model of situated deliberation linking decision-making with moral reasoning, and to derive a three-phase (detection, diagnosis, design) methodological framework for grand challenge research oriented toward the figure of the 'entrepreneurial observer'.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Grattarola, A.; Gond, J.-P.; Haefliger, S.
theories: Carnegie School theory of organization (Cyert & March 1963; March & Simon 1993; Simon 1997); French pragmatic sociology / economies of worth / justification theory (Boltanski & Thévenot 2006); economics of conventions; phenomenology (as methodological standpoint); robust action (Padgett & Ansell 1993; Ferraro et al. 2015)
keywords: Carnegie School; decision-making; economies of worth; grand challenges; morality; phenomenological models
research_question: How can deliberation be represented phenomenologically? What distinguishes a grand challenge perspective on deliberation? And what are the theoretical and methodological implications of adopting such a perspective for organization and management studies?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: grand challenges are best approached not as a distinct class of coordination problems or as mere empirical contexts, but as a phenomenological perspective that structures how researchers perceive, interpret, and act on processes and systems of deliberation.
- Key constructs: deliberation (situated, evaluative, contestable, trans-institutional decision-making); social systems of deliberation; decision management; satisficing value; situated judgment; entrepreneurial observer.
- Theoretical integration: Carnegie School (bounded rationality, attention, search, composite organizations) is joined with justification theory / economies of worth (normative pluralism, orders of worth, collective construction and critique of action norms) to yield a unified phenomenological model linking decision-making and moral reasoning.
- Properties of deliberative systems: dynamism, distribution, loose coupling, contestability, extensibility — claimed to apply to ordinary and grand challenge problems alike, on a continuum rather than as a categorical divide.
- Methodological framework: three-phase research logic of detection (identifying uncoordinated representations as 'interesting' problems), diagnosis (theorizing why problems are 'important' and articulating desirable futures), and design (specifying realizable paths via explanatory and predictive middle-range theorizing).
- Boundary conditions: the model depends on observer standpoint, socio-economic and scientific commitments, and is framed as a hybrid of CS and JT that acknowledges but does not fully resolve its borrowings from social systems theory (Luhmann).

The phenomenological model treats deliberation as a situated and socially embedded interlocking of decision-making, moral thinking, and contestation through managerial action. By linking situated judgment with search and connecting local deliberative events into networks, observers construe 'social systems of deliberation' whose scale and scope depend on their epistemic objectives rather than on pre-given ontological categories. Adopting the standpoint of the entrepreneurial observer then allows researchers to anchor their inquiry in socio-economic and scientific commitments simultaneously, reconciling the analyst/actor paradox characteristic of engaged grand challenge research.
theoretical_contribution: The article offers three contributions. First, by integrating the Carnegie School and justification theory it develops a novel model of organizational decision-making co-constituted by actors' natural cognitive capacities and sociality and by the normative pluralism and structural complexity of collective action problems. Second, it advances the debate on the significance of grand challenges by shifting the focus from conceptual or empirical categories to the integration of researchers' analytical capacities, societal commitments, and embedding in scholarly fields. Third, it outlines a methodological framework to support this orientation by coordinating diverse theorizing stances and methodological tools through phases of detection, diagnosis, and design.
practical_implication: Researchers and research-adjacent practitioners (e.g., policymakers, managers, advocacy actors) can adopt the standpoint of entrepreneurial observers: anchoring their work in explicit socio-economic and scientific commitments, identifying 'interesting' problems where actors' representations remain uncoordinated, diagnosing them as instances of broader grand challenge horizons, and designing actionable interventions through middle-range, explanatory theorizing that connects local deliberative contexts to global ones (e.g., treating rural water scarcity as a scale model for national climate deliberation).
limitations: The model is a hybrid construction whose conjunction of CS and JT may require further conceptual refinement to serve as a robust reference point for future research, and some theoretical distinctions (e.g., Simon's logical positivism, Boltanski's critical turn, convention theory's structuralist lineage) were not fully addressed. The article also draws on social systems theory (Luhmann) only at an intuitive level and has not assessed whether these borrowings are theoretically warranted, particularly in relation to pragmatist epistemology and emancipatory or performative theorizing, nor systematically engaged with alternative systems accounts of grand challenges.
future_research: Future work should further develop and refine a social systems approach to deliberation, including empirical studies of deliberative decision-making that join situated judgment and search; study 'decision management' as a techne that links means and ends beyond formal organization; pursue middle-range explanatory and predictive theorizing on the structure and dynamics of social systems of deliberation; and exploit the parsimonious social ontology of the model to enable cross-scale comparisons (e.g., local as proxy for global) and computational social-science designs leveraging digital traces and simulation.
abstract: This conceptual article argues that the mutual relevance of grand challenges and organization and management studies is best approached phenomenologically. Rather than constituting objects to be theorized or denoting special empirical contexts, grand challenges structure researchers' attention and shape their interpretations of the processes and systems of deliberation through which collective action is coordinated. From this perspective, grand challenges require researchers to innovate their understandings of deliberation and to ensure that newly generated knowledge is redirected towards management and policymaking. The article integrates the Carnegie School theory of organization with French pragmatic sociology's theory of justification, or economies of worth, to develop a phenomenological model of situated deliberation that links decision-making with moral reasoning. This model highlights deliberation's articulated, evaluative, contestable, and trans-institutional character, as well as its grounding in the cognitive capacities and sociality of actors and observers – regardless of the scale, scope, or stratification of the underlying coordination problems. Building on this framework, the article advocates that grand challenge researchers adopt the standpoint of entrepreneurial observers: actors anchored by socio-economic and scientific commitments who envision the integration of previously disjointed social systems of deliberation to orient collective action.
apa_citation: Grattarola, A., Gond, J.-P., & Haefliger, S. (2026). Joining decision-making, moral thinking, and collective action: Grand challenges as a phenomenology of deliberation. *Journal of Management Studies*. https://doi.org/10.1111/joms.70062

title: Fair Lending in Car Financing: Unintended Racial Consequences of Consumer Financial Protection Bureau Supervision of Dealer Markups
methods: Regression-discontinuity-in-time (RDiT) design around the September 2014 CFPB supervisory highlights, using buyer-dealer-vehicle-lender-day transaction data with local polynomial time trends and vehicle, dealer, and lender fixed effects; complemented by a difference-in-differences robustness strategy and moderation analyses.
industry: Auto retailing and indirect auto lending (banks and credit unions)
units: Individual indirect auto-loan transactions
n_sample: 18,235,808 transactions from 3,183 lenders across 6,239 dealers in 2,643,077 census block groups
authors: He, C.; Ozturk, O. C.; Chintagunta, P. K.
theories: channel pricing; price discrimination in bargaining; unintended consequences of regulation
keywords: Equal Credit Opportunity Act; auto loans; consumer protection; dealer markup; fair lending; price discrimination; public policy; racial gap
research_question: What was the impact of the CFPB's 2014 supervisory highlights on dealer markups, buy rates, total interest rates, total payments, and racial disparities in indirect auto lending, and through what channel-pricing mechanisms did those effects unfold?
mechanism_summary: Because the Bureau's recommendation was voluntary, credit unions disproportionately adopted flat compensation while banks largely did not. The resulting asymmetry induced a spillover in channel pricing: dealers reduced markups on bank loans most when buyers could plausibly also obtain a flat-compensation offer from a credit union, to retain those transactions. Because nonminorities were more likely than minorities to secure credit-union offers, the spillover concentrated markup reductions among nonminorities, widening the racial gap in dealer markups and, combined with higher buy rates that lenders introduced to keep dealer business viable, in total interest rates.
theoretical_contribution: The article advances research on channel pricing and price disparities in bargaining by empirically evaluating an actual—rather than simulated—government intervention that made removal of dealer discretion voluntary for lenders. It shows that voluntary compensation reform can succeed on its target outcome (dealer markups) yet fail to improve average consumer welfare, and introduces a novel policy-induced spillover mechanism whereby zero-markup contracts offered by one lender type (credit unions) alter strategic intermediary pricing on loans from another type (banks), inadvertently amplifying racial disparities.
practical_implication: For policy makers, the findings caution that voluntary financial-channel interventions can be undone by compensating adjustments from lenders and dealers, leaving consumers' total costs unchanged and potentially worsening racial gaps. Regulators should account for interlender competition and heterogeneous lender incentives when designing fair-lending reforms, and should monitor spillovers across loan channels rather than focusing only on the directly targeted markup.
limitations: The identification relies on a quasi-experimental RDiT strategy using observational data, so readers should weigh causal interpretations alongside the robustness and mechanism checks the authors report. The data cover dealer markups up to 2015, so the long-term effects of the intervention—especially after the 2018 congressional disapproval of the CFPB's heightened scrutiny—cannot be assessed. The authors also observe only accepted loan contracts, not the full set of offers presented to each borrower.
future_research: Future work should examine the long-term effects of the CFPB intervention, including the consequences of the 2018 repeal of the Bureau's heightened scrutiny of indirect auto lenders. More broadly, researchers should investigate how voluntary financial-channel policies interact with lender heterogeneity and intermediary pricing in other credit markets, and how spillover mechanisms across lender types shape disparate impacts by race.
abstract: Over 80% of car buyers in the United States obtain a loan through a dealership. Dealers often mark up lender-provided interest rates (buy rates), but consumers cannot distinguish these markups from total rates. In 2014, the Consumer Financial Protection Bureau made an unprecedented public disclosure regarding its supervision of auto lenders, revealing racial disparities in dealer markups disadvantaging minority borrowers. The Bureau strongly recommended—but did not mandate—that lenders providing loans through dealers eliminate dealer discretion in markups by adopting ﬂat dealer compensation per transaction. The authors examine the effectiveness of this intervention in reducing consumer ﬁnancing, car payments, and the racial gap. Using detailed individual transaction-level data and a regression-discontinuity-in-time design, they ﬁnd that dealer markups declined by 5.55 basis points after the intervention, saving $66.60 for a typical loan. However, the intervention also increased buy rates, resulting in no signiﬁcant changes to consumers’ total interest rates and total payments. Dealers experienced a slight increase in ﬁnancing proﬁts but no signiﬁcant change in vehicle proﬁts. Importantly, the racial gap in dealer markups and total interest rates widened; dealer markups decreased for both minorities and nonminorities, but they decreased more for nonminorities. The ﬁndings can illuminate the intended and unintended consequences of such government oversight and provide insights into their underlying mechanisms.
apa_citation: He, C., Ozturk, O. C., & Chintagunta, P. K. (2026). Fair lending in car financing: Unintended racial consequences of Consumer Financial Protection Bureau supervision of dealer markups. *Journal of Marketing Research*, 63(2), 257-277. https://doi.org/10.1177/00222437251352482

title: Coordination and Commitment in International Climate Action: Evidence From Palm Oil
methods: Dynamic empirical framework combining an almost ideal demand system for vegetable oils (iterated linear least squares with weather-shock IVs) and a dynamic discrete-continuous choice model of firm entry and plantation investment (estimated via combined discrete/continuous Euler CCP methods with IVs); model used for counterfactual policy simulations (production taxes, import tariffs, export taxes, CBAM) solved for equilibrium prices, production, consumption, and CO2 emissions.
industry: Palm oil (vegetable oils sector; plantations and mills)
country: Indonesia and Malaysia (supply); EU, China, India, rest of world (demand)
units: Sites (groupings of plantations and mills treated as firms) on supply side; consumer markets on demand side
n_sample: 2050 sites on the supply side; demand panel covers four consumer markets across 29 years
authors: Hsiao, Allan
theories: industry dynamics (Hopenhayn; Ericson-Pakes); leakage and carbon coalitions; time-inconsistency / commitment in environmental regulation; trade and environment theory
keywords: European Union; Indonesia; Malaysia; climate change; environmental regulation; palm oil; trade policy
research_question: How effective is trade policy as a substitute for direct domestic regulation in reducing carbon emissions, and how do the twin challenges of international coordination (across importers) and commitment (over time) shape the emission reductions and welfare costs achievable with import tariffs, export taxes, and a carbon border adjustment mechanism?
mechanism_summary: Emissions depend on dynamic, forward-looking firm investment in mills and plantations in Indonesia and Malaysia, where deforestation — especially of carbon-rich peatlands — releases CO2. Trade policy changes the world prices firms receive, altering both the extensive margin (mill entry) and the intensive margin (hectares deforested). Demand-side leakage (consumption shifting to unregulated importers) and supply-side leakage (production shifting across regions) erode uncoordinated tariffs, while the government's temptation to drop tariffs once emissions are sunk generates a commitment problem: both coordination and commitment are each necessary to achieve the largest emission reductions.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops a new dynamic empirical framework for quantitatively evaluating green trade policy, jointly formalizing and estimating the coordination problem (from leakage to unregulated markets) and the commitment problem (from time-inconsistency once emissions are sunk) in a single industry-equilibrium setting. Methodologically, it combines discrete and continuous Euler conditional-choice-probability techniques to estimate a dynamic discrete-continuous choice model of firm entry and investment without solving it, offering a computationally light strategy that can be applied to other trade-and-environment settings.
practical_implication: For policymakers, EU-led import tariffs on palm oil can reduce emissions by 5.4 Gt at a cost of only $15 per ton of CO2 inclusive of compensating transfers to Indonesia and Malaysia, but only if importers coordinate and commit over the long run; unilateral EU action still yields 1 Gt of abatement at roughly $50 per ton. Export taxes in producing countries achieve the same emission reductions as import tariffs while generating domestic fiscal revenue, and a carbon border adjustment mechanism crediting domestic regulation can encourage producing countries to regulate themselves.
limitations: The author notes that demand estimation relies on annual data, which may attenuate elasticity estimates and thereby understate leakage concerns. The supply model assumes zero exit of plantations and mills (justified by the perennial nature of oil palm and observed low exit rates), and smallholder-mill relationships are treated as vertically integrated because mills extract rents through exclusive contracting. The framework abstracts from substitution between palm oil and fossil fuels in the EU because of binding biofuel blending targets.
future_research: The author closes by asking how international climate action can progress in "an increasingly fragmented and unequal world" and calls for future work grounded in political realities — specifically, analyses that incorporate the political economy of coordination and commitment among heterogeneous, lower-income producing countries facing competing growth imperatives.
abstract: Weak environmental regulation has global consequences. When domestic regulation fails, the international community can target emitters with trade policy. I develop a dynamic empirical framework for evaluating trade policy as a substitute for domestic regulation, and I apply the framework to the market for palm oil, a major driver of deforestation and global CO2 emissions. Relative to business as usual, a domestic production tax of 50% reduces CO2 emissions by 7.4 Gt from 1988 to 2016, amounting to 0.26 Gt annually. Coordinated, committed import tariffs of similar magnitude reduce emissions by 5.4 Gt over the same period. The cost of these import tariffs is only $15 per ton of CO2 , even accounting for compensating transfers that recognize welfare losses for producing countries. Without coordination and commitment, import tariffs have more limited effects. Alternative policies include domestic export taxes, which are fiscally appealing independent of emission concerns, and a carbon border adjustment mechanism, which encourages domestic regulation.
apa_citation: Hsiao, A. (2026). Coordination and commitment in international climate action: Evidence from palm oil. *Econometrica*, 94(1), 1–33. https://doi.org/10.3982/ECTA20608

title: Sustainable HRM in the Public Sector: A Question of Viability or Legitimacy?
methods: Multi-level mixed methods qualitative design combining six in-depth organisational case studies of English local authorities, 49 semi-structured interviews with HR managers, service and procurement managers, and trade unionists, 11 expert interviews, a telephone survey of 72 local authorities, and documentary analysis; thematic analysis using three levels of coding.
industry: UK public sector / local government (with a focus on adult social care and outsourced services)
units: Local authorities (metropolitan boroughs) in England
n_sample: 6 in-depth case-study local authorities; 49 semi-structured case-study interviews; 72 local authorities surveyed; 11 expert interviews
authors: Johnson, Mathew; Mustchin, Stephen; Grimshaw, Damian; Koukiadaki, Aristea
theories: common good HRM; institutional logics; sustainable HRM
keywords: common good HRM; institutional logics; outsourcing; public sector; sustainable HRM
research_question: The study asks: What evidence is there of local authority implementation of sustainable HRM bundles and how do these bundles reflect the competing demands of different stakeholders? How are HRM bundles framed by the institutional logics of viability and legitimacy? And to what extent is there organisational feedback and adaptation of sustainable HRM practices over time?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: logic of viability (resources/capacities to deliver) vs. logic of legitimacy (satisfying social and moral demands for fair and consistent HR practices)
- Practices: living wage accreditation, flexible working, training and career development, collective negotiation with trade unions, socially responsible restructuring (voluntary severance, internal redeployment), ethical procurement charters
- Triggering conditions: approximately 40% real-terms budget cuts to UK local government since 2010, rising service demand, staff shortages in social care, outsourcing pressures, left-wing (Labour) political leadership in deprived areas
- Feedback loops: positive (virtuous cycle) in which viability-enhancing HRM reinforces external legitimacy and vice versa; negative (vicious cycle) in which cost-driven outsourcing erodes socially legitimate employment and generates worker/union resistance
- Emergent outcomes: heterogeneous, ad hoc HRM bundles; a shift from collective "common good" protections toward individual human capital and wellbeing initiatives; selective insourcing and ethical procurement where trade unions and political leaders align

Across the six cases, managers navigate competing institutional logics by assembling heterogeneous bundles that combine higher wages, flexibility, and training with headcount reductions and functional flexibility. Trade-union engagement and strong political leadership (notably at LA3 and LA4) help break the negative feedback loop from cost-driven outsourcing by re-legitimising in-house delivery and ethical procurement. Elsewhere, pragmatic "business case" logic narrows sustainable HRM to improvements above legal minima rather than best-in-class common good standards.
theoretical_contribution: The paper moves beyond ideal-type typologies of sustainable HRM (socially responsible, green, triple bottom line, common good) by revealing the tensions and contradictions within heterogeneous HRM bundles as they are assembled in public-sector settings. It elaborates the dynamic interplay between institutional logics of viability and legitimacy, showing how positive and negative feedback loops operate over time and how public-sector managers are—contra some prior claims—highly sensitive to economic viability pressures alongside legitimacy demands.
practical_implication: For policymakers and managers, the findings underscore that coordinated action is needed across the fragmented HR ecosystem, including subcontracted workers. Authorities should rebuild eroded HR capacities, extend decent standards through procurement and supplier charters, and pair individual practices (living wage, flexible working, training) with sector-wide reforms such as the UK Employment Rights Act (2025) fair pay agreement for social care.
limitations: The sample is limited to six English local authorities that had positively engaged with workforce sustainability, which the authors acknowledge is not representative of the sector; cases that disengaged or faced stronger resistance are absent. Data reliance on interviews and documents may be subject to confirmation bias from comparative "selecting-to-similarity" sampling. The study also does not offer a longitudinal test of whether the observed practices sustain worker wellbeing over the long term.
future_research: Future work could investigate public sector organisations that have not engaged with sustainability HRM strategies, or contexts with strong financial barriers or stakeholder resistance. Longitudinal and temporal-dynamics studies are needed to model fit between HRM approaches and viability/legitimacy logics as market conditions change. Matched-pair public/private comparisons would clarify sector-specific reference points, norms and accreditation schemes, especially as EDI debates evolve.
abstract: Recent research has underlined the growing importance of sustainability in HRM policy and practice, taking into account long‐ term multi‐stakeholder goals. However, few studies have specified the drivers and outcomes of sustainable HRM practices, nor the contradictions that arise when managers attempt to satisfy the demands of both internal and external stakeholders. Drawing on in‐depth case studies of the UK public sector, we distinguish between the institutional sustainability logics of (i) viability (concerned with necessary resources and capacities to deliver) and (ii) legitimacy (concerned with satisfying social and moral demands for fair and consistent HR practices) and explore how these two logics interact through positive and negative feedback loops. Our findings show that across an increasingly complex HRM eco‐system, the tensions between viability and legitimacy are increasingly addressed through a focus on building individual human capital and personal wellbeing rather than protecting job security and the collective 'common good'.
apa_citation: Johnson, M., Mustchin, S., Grimshaw, D., & Koukiadaki, A. (2026). Sustainable HRM in the public sector: A question of viability or legitimacy? *Human Resource Management Journal*. https://doi.org/10.1111/1748-8583.70034

title: Contending with Perceived Legitimacy Tensions: Impact Investing in Pluralistic Institutional Environments
methods: Inductive qualitative theory-building study; multi-year longitudinal field research using semi-structured in-person interviews, field notes, archival data (press articles, LinkedIn pages, websites, video recordings), and follow-up interviews; data analyzed using the Gioia method (first- and second-order codes into aggregate theoretical dimensions)
industry: Early-stage impact ventures across agriculture, clean energy, health care, transportation, cookstoves, fintech, financial services, coffee, mobile technology, food technology, artisan goods, banking, honey/beekeeping, farming supply chain, education/women's empowerment, waste management, grocery/supply chain, essential oils, food and retail
country: Kenya, India, and Mexico (entrepreneurs); Western impact investors (counterparties)
units: Entrepreneurs (plus 16 knowledgeable informants for triangulation)
n_sample: 30 entrepreneurs (10 from each of Kenya, India, and Mexico), 46 total interviews
authors: Jones, J.; Murray, A.
theories: institutional logics (market vs. community logic); institutional pluralism; new venture legitimacy theory
keywords: entrepreneurship; impact investing; institutional pluralism; new venture legitimacy; non-­Western contexts
research_question: How do entrepreneurs in non-Western countries contend with perceived legitimacy tensions that arise when pursuing financial resources from non-local impact investors?
mechanism_summary: Non-Western entrepreneurs embedded in a local community logic encounter a market-based community logic when seeking financial resources from Western impact investors, generating perceived legitimacy tensions between incompatible audience expectations. Entrepreneurs initially anchor themselves to one audience's expectations and attempt to balance, focus, or withdraw in response. Through recursive interactions with investors, entrepreneurs update their perceptions of what legitimate behaviour looks like, which in turn shapes their subsequent legitimation strategies. In many cases, entrepreneurs ultimately commit to their local community—foregoing financial capital—when they perceive that investor expectations would compromise community legitimacy, illustrating entrepreneurs' agency in the legitimation process.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances theory on new venture legitimacy by foregrounding the entrepreneur's (not the investor's) perspective and by showing how non-financial resource providers such as local communities alter the legitimating actions entrepreneurs take toward financial investors. It contributes to the institutional pluralism literature by theorizing how entrepreneurs navigate incompatible market and community logics across non-Western and Western institutional environments, revealing that legitimacy seeking is dynamic, recursive, and may entail opting out of resource acquisition altogether. It further contributes to entrepreneurship research by theorizing non-Western contexts as theoretically distinct rather than a descriptive backdrop, surfacing a community logic as a critical boundary condition on prevailing legitimacy theory.
practical_implication: The findings suggest impact investing has yet to fully realize the value of local communities and may be rendered ineffective if its dominant Western, market-logic framing ignores the community logic central to non-Western contexts. Practically, considering the perspectives of entrepreneurs—and the suggestions they offer—can enable a more mutually beneficial and effective use of impact investing funds in non-Western settings. Powerful actors in the impact investing field (e.g., GIIN members, intermediaries) should adapt benchmarks, due diligence, and engagement practices to the community-logic realities of recipient entrepreneurs.
limitations: The study focuses on entrepreneurs from non-Western contexts, yet non-market logics also exist in Western settings (e.g., artisan communities, maker movements, social enterprises), so findings may generalize only partially beyond the studied contexts. Insights are generalized across three diverse non-Western institutional contexts (Kenya, India, Mexico), which substantiates breadth but may mask country-level differences (e.g., varying legacies of colonization) that could shape entrepreneurs' anchoring decisions. Finally, the sample captures a mix of entrepreneurs with different prior funding outcomes, and these distinct scenarios may differentially shape legitimacy perceptions in ways not fully disentangled in the analyses.
future_research: Researchers could build on the anchoring decisions entrepreneurs make as they seek legitimacy from different audiences, examining Western contexts in which both market and community logics coexist (artisan communities, maker movements, social enterprises). Studies should investigate how structural and cultural legacies of colonization affect entrepreneurs' anchoring decisions at the start of the legitimation process, and how country-level institutional differences shape community-logic strength. Further work can theoretically elaborate on how prior funding experience (successful, unsuccessful, or no prior offers) shapes entrepreneurs' perceived legitimacy tensions and their subsequent legitimating behaviours.
abstract: This study advances our understanding of new venture legitimacy and resource acquisition by broadening the epistemological scope of our theory to be inclusive of non-­Western contexts. We conducted a qualitative study of entrepreneurs from India, Kenya, and Mexico seeking impact investments from Western impact investors to better understand how entrepreneurs in non-­Western contexts contend with perceived legitimacy tensions that arise when pursuing financial resources from non-­local investors. In contrast to the prevailing assumption that non-­Western entrepreneurs are resource-­constrained and adhere to existing strategies to seek legitimacy from financial resource providers, we find that such entrepreneurs perceive their local institutional environment to have important non-­financial resources that impact how they seek legitimacy from financial resource providers. This study offers a novel conceptualization of new venture legitimation by acknowledging non-­Western and Western institutional environments as important considerations in how entrepreneurs navigate legitimacy with both financial and non-­financial audiences over time.
apa_citation: Jones, J., & Murray, A. (2026). Contending with perceived legitimacy tensions: Impact investing in pluralistic institutional environments. *Journal of Management Studies*, 63(1), 232-269. https://doi.org/10.1111/joms.13233

title: Environmental, social and governance impacts of beef production systems: an integrated literature review
methods: Systematic literature review following PRISMA guidelines; integrated ESG impact assessment across 32 indicators on a -5 to +5 scoring rubric, combined with a SWOT analysis; independent author scoring followed by group-consensus reconciliation.
industry: Beef production (agri-food / livestock sector)
country: Global (research concentrated in North America, South America, and Europe)
time_period: Literature published through 2024 (search conducted prior to September 2024)
units: Eight beef production systems: transhumant; pasture-based; silvopasture; landless industrialized; small-scale landless; biodynamic farming; certified organic; regenerative grazing
n_sample: 98 articles included from 408 screened (PRISMA)
authors: Joshi, P.; Chakraborty, A.; Sandhu, S.; Recht, L.; Sandhu, H.
theories: ESG framework; TEEBAgriFood framework
keywords: Beef production systems; ESG impact; Evolution; SWOT analysis; Sustainability
research_question: How have beef production systems evolved over time, and how do eight distinct beef production systems compare in their environmental, social, and governance (ESG) impacts across natural, social, human, produced, and governance capital domains?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the TEEBAgriFood framework, the authors argue that different beef production systems embody distinct configurations of land use, labor, inputs, animal welfare practices, and governance arrangements, which in turn generate divergent externalities across ESG dimensions. Systematic scoring against 32 indicators derived from the literature, followed by SWOT analysis, reveals how traditional, low-input, and regenerative systems tend to deliver stronger ecological and community outcomes while landless industrialized systems achieve productivity and formal corporate governance at the cost of environmental and welfare performance.
theoretical_contribution: The paper offers three contributions: it provides a historical overview of how beef production systems have transitioned over time and their ESG assessment; it outlines pathways towards sustainability-enhancing practices in beef production; and it contributes to the development of an integrated ESG framework adapted to the complexities of agri-food production by operationalizing the TEEBAgriFood approach across 32 indicators and eight production systems.
practical_implication: The ESG scoring and SWOT analysis provide a base for actionable, context-specific policy recommendations targeting the strengths and weaknesses of each system. Policymakers should combine system-specific production-side reforms (e.g., supporting regenerative grazing, addressing certification barriers for organic and biodynamic systems, tightening environmental and welfare standards for landless industrialized operations) with demand-side measures such as public awareness campaigns, dietary guidelines, and incentives for protein diversification. Stakeholders are encouraged to promote transparent governance and collaborative, participatory arrangements to steer the beef sector toward ethical and sustainable practices.
limitations: The review may not fully capture within-system variation driven by geography, management, and scale, and it focuses on the production side, excluding processing, transportation, distribution, and food security/nutrition aspects. Integrated dairy-beef systems are not included, and the study is limited to English-language literature. Equal weighting is applied to all ESG indicators, and methodological choices and system boundaries across primary studies produce uncertainties in reported GHG emissions, so the rankings should be interpreted as indicative rather than absolute.
future_research: Future studies could include dairy-origin beef systems as a separate category and explore alternative weighting schemes and sensitivity analyses to test the robustness of rankings under different assumptions. Additional indicators reflecting agricultural value chain variations could be incorporated, and further research is needed on socially conscious beef production, since social and human indicators remain the least discussed in the existing literature. Biodynamic farming in particular warrants more research attention given its limited evidence base.
abstract: Beef production systems have been evolving for more than a millennium with a primary focus on increasing efficiency. Recently, environmental, social and governance (ESG) concerns have become another key focus due to societal, consumer and regulatory pressures. We identify the temporal evolution of various beef production systems. Using the Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses (PRISMA) guidelines, we assessed the ESG impacts of eight systems across five domains: natural capital; social capital; human capital; produced capital; and governance. This analysis includes 98 articles. Additionally, we conducted a strengths, weaknesses, opportunities and threats (SWOT) analysis. Regenerative grazing ranks high while landless industrialized system ranks low on all performance metrics. Silvopasture and pasture-based systems excel in community engagement. Biodynamic farming, certified organic and regenerative systems support livelihoods and demonstrate robust governance structures with active stakeholder engagement. Landless industrialized systems demonstrate strong corporate governance. This study provides policymakers with insights on promoting sustainable and ethical beef production practices.
apa_citation: Joshi, P., Chakraborty, A., Sandhu, S., Recht, L., & Sandhu, H. (2026). Environmental, social and governance impacts of beef production systems: An integrated literature review. *Journal of Industrial Ecology*, 30(1), 135-150. https://doi.org/10.1007/s44498-026-00011-5

title: Publishing with AMLE: An Invitation to Join the “Big Table” for Pluriversal Conversations
methods: Editorial essay (From the Editors); reflective argumentation supplemented by descriptive bibliometric analysis of AMLE authorship country diversity 2002–2025
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Joy, S.; Ryazanova, O.; Colombo, L. A.; Coraiola, D. M.
theories: decolonial theory; epistemic coloniality; pluriversality
keywords: Global South; big table; contextualized theorizing; decolonizing management knowledge; epistemic coloniality; management learning and education; ontological empathy; periphery; phenomenon-based research; pluriversal conversations
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary: Not reported in paper
theoretical_contribution: The editorial reframes knowledge generation in management learning and education as a "pluriversal conversation" rather than a homogenizing, universalist project anchored in Global North research traditions. Drawing on decolonial and pluriversal scholarship (Banerjee, 2022; Jammulamadaka et al., 2021; Mignolo, 2011), the authors argue that journals like AMLE should treat the global academic community as a pluriverse of coexisting worlds with distinct ontologies and epistemologies, and should cultivate ontological and epistemic equality through phenomenon-based research, contextualized theorizing, and ontological/epistemological grounding of methods. The piece positions AMLE's "big table" as a platform for connecting pluriversal conversations across peripheries and centers, thereby challenging epistemic coloniality in elite management journals.
practical_implication: The editors invite scholars from peripheral academic communities — including those within Global North institutions — to submit work that draws on their lifeworlds, focuses on locally meaningful problems, puzzles, and practices, and engages with context-relevant social science and indigenous knowledge traditions (e.g., buen vivir, swaraj, kgotla). They describe concrete AMLE initiatives including community-specific, stage-wise paper development workshops in emerging research ecosystems, a "Talk-to-Editor" feedback initiative, multilingual abstracts and PDWs, and partnerships with local AOM chapters and academic organizations to build reviewer pools and editorial board representation. Authors, reviewers, and editors are urged to practice ontological empathy and to travel to other knowledge worlds as genuine travelers rather than "academic tourists."
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editorial calls for future AMLE submissions that centre phenomenon-based research on under-studied practices (e.g., rural immersion, land-based learning), contextualized theory-building that draws on disciplines like sociology, anthropology, and political science to fit peripheral settings, and methodological work grounded in diverse ontological and epistemological positions. It encourages studies that investigate dimensions of diversity beyond the dominant gender/race/sexual-identity frame (e.g., urban–rural divide, caste, language), reexamine human–nature relations in light of socio-ecological crises, and generate new forms of evidence beyond corporate, managerial, and curated archival data sources.
abstract: FROM THE EDITORS PUBLISHING WITH AMLE: AN INVITATION TO JOIN THE “BIG TABLE” FOR PLURIVERSAL CONVERSATIONS

title: Social Upgrading in Global Supply Chains: Reframing Corporate Responsibilities
methods: Conceptual/normative philosophical analysis; critique of the 'vertical integration' and 'big buyer control' assumptions in sweatshop ethics, followed by argument for 'constrained influence' and illustrative case discussion of the International Accord and ACT on Living Wages
industry: Apparel, footwear, electronics and other consumer-goods global supply chains (illustrative cases)
country: High-income 'big buyer' countries and low-income manufacturing countries (notably Bangladesh)
time_period: 1980s-present, with emphasis on post-Rana Plaza (2013-2024) transnational industrial agreements
units: Big buyers (branded marketers) and the supplier factories they source from
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Kingston, E.; Carnovale, M.
theories: agonistic CSR; constrained influence; federated corporate social responsibility; structural injustice theory (Young)
keywords: responsibility; social upgrading; supply chain; sweatshop; unbundling
research_question: What moral responsibilities do "big buyers" in high-income countries bear for social upgrading in their global supply chains once we relax the philosophical literature's unrealistic "vertical integration" and "control" assumptions and instead recognize that big buyers exert only a "constrained influence" over working conditions at their suppliers?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on structural-injustice theorists (Young, Müller) and empirical work on global value chains, the authors argue that unbundling severs the simple employer-employee link assumed by vertical integrationists and by "big buyer control" theorists. Because no individual buyer can unilaterally guarantee decent working conditions at independent suppliers, standard arguments that assign (or exonerate) buyers on the basis of direct control misidentify the problem. Yet constrained influence does not dissolve responsibility: buyers retain forward-looking duties to contribute to social upgrading, and the most plausible way to discharge those duties is through federated/agonistic CSR arrangements in which buyers collectively cede power to accountable third parties—exemplified by binding transnational industrial agreements such as the International Accord on Fire and Building Safety in Bangladesh and the ACT on Living Wages initiative.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reframes the philosophical debate on sweatshop ethics by replacing the dominant "vertical integration" and "big buyer control" assumptions with an empirically grounded "constrained influence" assumption, and by showing how this reframing changes the rationale and content of big buyers' responsibilities for social upgrading. It bridges structural-injustice theory with emerging work on federated and agonistic corporate social responsibility to argue that responsibility is discharged through collective, formalized commitments among buyers rather than individual due diligence or consumer activism.
practical_implication: Big buyers should not treat social upgrading as a matter of unilateral auditing or codes of conduct, but should instead join binding multi-employer agreements—such as the International Accord and ACT—that commit them to cede authority to accountable third parties (unions, multi-stakeholder bodies) in setting and enforcing labor standards. Such federated commitments can strengthen rather than weaken domestic labor law enforcement and offer a more realistic route to improving worker rights than either purely market-based or state-centric approaches.
limitations: The article focuses exclusively on the duties of big buyers and brackets the responsibilities of sovereign governments, consumers, and other stakeholders, which it acknowledges limits the scope of its conclusions. Its tentative proposals place little emphasis on the state as a mechanism for resolving the epistemic and collective action problems of supply chains, and it does not fully adjudicate critiques that such federated private governance may still enable big buyers to evade political and legal responsibility. The argument is normative and illustrative rather than based on systematic empirical evaluation of the International Accord or ACT's effects.
future_research: Future work should examine the responsibilities of sovereign governments and other actors (consumers, trade policy-makers, domestic regulators) alongside those of big buyers, and assess how state regulation can best interact with federated buyer commitments. Further scholarship is needed on the design and effectiveness of transnational industrial agreements beyond the International Accord and on how collective wage-setting initiatives such as ACT can navigate trade-offs between efficiency and living-wage goals. The authors also invite deeper exploration of how structural theorists' accounts of responsibility can be operationalized across different consumer-goods sectors.
abstract: Much philosophical literature on sweatshop ethics assumes that the individual branded marketers that sell consumer goods either employ sweatshop workers or can strongly influence the conditions under which those workers labor. This oversimplification misidentifies the rationale for and details of the responsibilities of big buyers for the labor standards in their supply chains. Throughout this article, we illustrate how philosophers’ “vertical integration” and “control” assumptions distort our understanding of the internal dynamics within supply chains. Under the more realistic assumption that big buyers have “constrained influence” over labor conditions in their supply chains, we show that big buyers retain the responsibility to work toward social upgrading goals. However, fulfilling such a responsibility requires big buyers to collectively cede power to third parties in supply chains in formalized and accountable ways. Recent developments in transnational industrial agreements, such as the International Accord, are examples of this commitment.
apa_citation: Kingston, E., & Carnovale, M. (2026). Social upgrading in global supply chains: Reframing corporate responsibilities. *Business Ethics Quarterly*, 1-28. https://doi.org/10.1017/beq.2025.10103

title: Social entrepreneurship mechanisms for systems change: A structuration approach
methods: Conceptual theory-building paper that develops a structuration model of systems change through social entrepreneurship, using the evolution of microfinance as an in-depth illustrative case and additional illustrative cases of social entrepreneurship that have achieved or failed to achieve systems change
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Krlev, G.
theories: connected modalities (interpretative schemes, resources, norms); duality of agency and structure; structuration theory (Giddens)
keywords: Mechanisms; Social entrepreneurship; Structuration theory; Systems change
research_question: How do social entrepreneurs seek to advance systems change, and when do they succeed or fail to achieve it? More specifically, which mechanisms enable social entrepreneurial agency to translate into structural, systemic transformations in society?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on Giddens' structuration theory, the paper argues that agency and structure are recursively constituted through modalities that actors mobilize in practice. Social entrepreneurs drive systems change when they actively combine new interpretative schemes, new resource constellations, and new norms into "connected modalities" rather than relying on a single lever. Such connected modalities simultaneously enable high integrativeness (triggering parallel change across issues and societal levels) and high inclusiveness (activating diverse stakeholders to co-engage), producing a reinforcing interplay between entrepreneurial agency and institutional structure that overcomes the dualism treating structure as a constraint on agency.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces "connected modalities" as the central mechanism through which social entrepreneurship produces systems change, reframing the agency-structure relationship in social entrepreneurship as a productive duality rather than a dualism. By applying Giddens' structuration theory, the model bridges the micro-macro divide in social entrepreneurship research and explains why some social entrepreneurs achieve fundamental transformations while others do not. It further reconceptualizes core but poorly theorized features of social entrepreneurship — its capacity to enhance society's ability to act, its reliance on revolving innovation practices, and its characteristic vulnerability — as consequences of the need to continually generate and align connected modalities with evolving stakeholder constellations.
practical_implication: Support for social entrepreneurship should stop mimicking commercial incubation practices, which focus narrowly on viable business models and financial resource mobilization, because these are insufficient for systems change. Entrepreneurial strategy, incubation, and policy should instead train and support ventures to craft and orchestrate connected modalities — combining new interpretative schemes, resources, and norms — and to engage in political brokerage that aligns diverse stakeholders. Policy makers should favor instruments such as crowdsourcing around broadly defined sustainability challenges over standard pitch competitions and innovation prizes, which select for commercial viability rather than inclusiveness and integrativeness.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Future work could investigate the relationships between different modalities and their self-perpetuating outcomes, including whether there are optimal combinations, sequences, or numbers of modalities to connect and whether particular "packages" of connected modalities characterize specific industries or issue areas. Researchers could also examine softer discursive and normative forms of bricolage as drivers of innovation, and explore the boundary conditions at which tuning connected modalities to stakeholder agendas, or extending integrativeness to additional societal domains, undermines rather than promotes systems change.
abstract: This article advocates for harnessing Giddens' structuration theory to improve our understanding. I develop a structuration model of systems change through social entrepreneurship, which shows that connected modalities, that is, intricate combinations of (1) interpretative schemes, (2) resources, and (3) norms, act as the key mechanisms that enable systemic effects.
apa_citation: Krlev, G. (2026). Social entrepreneurship mechanisms for systems change: A structuration approach. *Journal of Business Venturing*, 41(2), 106561. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbusvent.2025.106561

title: Using Photo-Elicitation to Make Marginalised Voices Heard and Seen in Human Resource Management Research
methods: Methodological observational study presenting two qualitative exemplars of photo-elicitation (PE) in HRM research. Exemplar 1 (participant-driven PE): semi-structured photo-elicit interviews with six Vietnamese women academic leaders in higher education, analysed via Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis (IPA) using a seven-step process (transcription, coding, familiarisation, exploratory notes, experimental statements, personal experiential themes, group experiential themes). Exemplar 2 (researcher-driven PE): semi-structured online interviews with 22 trans and gender-diverse (TGD) individuals in Australia, followed by a photo-elicit open-ended questionnaire (n = 42) for HR/managerial personnel using six researcher-generated metaphorical images paired into three randomised sets with modified SHOWED-style questions; analysed via critical grounded theory (CGT) with first- and second-cycle coding, memo-writing, member-checking, and method triangulation.
industry: Higher education (Vietnam) and cross-industry workplaces with HR/diversity/leadership roles (Australia)
units: Individuals: women academic leaders; trans and gender-diverse employees; HR/diversity/leadership managers
n_sample: Exemplar 1: 6 women academics. Exemplar 2: 22 TGD interviewees plus 42 questionnaire respondents.
authors: Ladwig, R. C.; Phuong, J.
theories: Interpretative Phenomenological Analysis (IPA); critical HRM / critical HRD (humanism paradigm); critical grounded theory; critical theory; visual methods / photo-elicitation (Harper; Warren; Rose)
keywords: critical human resource development; marginalised groups; photo‐elicit interviews; research method; researcher guidance
research_question: How can photo-elicitation be used as a qualitative HRM research method to make marginalised voices heard and seen, and what practical guidance can researchers draw from participant-driven versus researcher-driven PE approaches? Two sub-questions structure the exemplars: "How do women in academic leadership make sense of their experience in a university in Vietnam?" and "How do trans and gender diverse identities influence individual work engagement and personal career development of TGD individuals in relation to the organisational awareness of gender diversity?"
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: Marginalised voices; material and physical practices of work; power dynamics between researcher and participant; embodied, emancipatory knowledge production.
- PE variants: Participant-driven PE (reflexive photography, auto-driving, photovoice — participants generate images) versus researcher-driven PE (researcher preselects or generates images for stimulated response).
- Process / phases: (1) research design aligned with critical theory and humanism; (2) purposive/snowball sampling of marginalised participants; (3) image generation (participant-produced or researcher-generated metaphorical images); (4) photo-elicit interview or open-ended questionnaire; (5) iterative analysis (IPA seven-step for exemplar 1; critical grounded theory with first- and second-cycle coding plus memo-writing and member-checking for exemplar 2); (6) reflexivity throughout.
- Practices: Member-checking; metaphorical image construction; SHOWED-style questioning adapted to questionnaire; method triangulation; positionality disclosure.
- Boundary conditions / challenges: Ethical issues of ownership, authorship, consent, and privacy; risk of tokenism; interpretive subjectivity; emotional labour; resource intensity; limited small samples.
- Emergent outcomes: "Powershift" from disempowered respondent to equitable research partner; surfacing of tacit, embodied, and symbolic knowledge (e.g., "swimming alone" metaphor for women's leadership journey; metaphorical images of dress code, toilet doors, and team inclusion for TGD experiences); authentic inclusion of marginalised voices in HRM research.

The central process is that PE, by introducing images as a "third party" in the research encounter, unlocks embodied and symbolic communication that verbal interviewing alone cannot access, thereby rebalancing power relations and enabling marginalised participants to co-construct meaning about their material and physical work practices. In the Vietnamese exemplar, participant-chosen photos (e.g., motorbikes, rivers, Don Quixote windmills) functioned as metaphorical anchors that prompted deep reflection on gendered leadership constraints; in the Australian exemplar, researcher-generated metaphorical images protected TGD participants' privacy while still eliciting HR/managerial personnel's taken-for-granted assumptions about cisnormativity. Across both, PE operationalises critical HRM/HRD humanism by foregrounding voices that verbal-centric, Global-North methods systematically under-represent.
theoretical_contribution: The paper offers a conceptual contribution to qualitative research methods in HRM by revising and translating PE for HRM scholarship, clarifying the distinction between participant-driven and researcher-driven PE and their differential implications for power dynamics, ethics, and analytic depth. It re-envisions the role of PE in providing holistic insights into the material and physical practices of work, narrowing Kjellstrand and Vince's (2020) noted gap on how to use photos in the OMS research process. By linking PE to critical HRD's humanistic paradigm, the authors position visual methods as an emancipatory tool that challenges meta-narratives of marginalisation in HRM.
practical_implication: HRM researchers are encouraged to adopt PE to access tacit, embodied, and symbolic dimensions of workplace experience — especially for marginalised populations where verbal interviewing may reproduce suppression. The authors provide a practical research-design toolkit (Tables 1-3) covering participant selection, preparation, ethical considerations (consent, ownership, authorship, privacy), data collection structure, analytic frameworks, and reflexivity prompts. For organisations, the exemplars surface actionable insights: Vietnamese higher education institutions should address systemic underrepresentation of women in leadership (7.6% of rectors vs. 50.1% of academics) and the unpaid gendered labour of event organising; Australian employers should improve TGD inclusion through spatial (e.g., toilets, workstations), symbolic (dress code, documentation), and leadership practices.
limitations: Each exemplar faces interpretive challenges: the participant-driven approach raises complex ethical issues of image ownership, joint interpretation, and consent when others appear in photos (leading Jane to describe rather than publish images); the researcher-driven approach introduces researcher subjectivity in image generation, depends on respondents' willingness to engage with metaphors, and is resource-intensive. COVID-19 restrictions forced the Australian study to pivot from photovoice to researcher-generated images. Small sample sizes (6 and 22) limit generalisability, and the online asynchronous questionnaire format precluded follow-up probing.
future_research: The authors call for HRM scholars to experiment with PE and other alternative, non-verbal research methods beyond the Global North's verbal-linguistic norms, to further diversify research engagement and knowledge production. They encourage further work on addressing power dynamics between researcher and participant, refining ethical protocols for visual data (ownership, authorship, consent), extending PE to other marginalised communities, and developing quality indicators for mixed visual-verbal analysis. They also advocate interdisciplinary collaboration to continue narrowing the gap on how visual methods contribute to critical HRM/HRD scholarship.
abstract: Photo‐elicitation (PE) is a qualitative research method that utilises images to obtain a deeper understanding of the perspectives, and beliefs of the research participants. The PE approach can be particularly advantageous for marginalised voices (silenced or underrepresented groups with limited power) by exploring different world views, challenging commonly accepted information, and investigating the material work context. Additionally, it has the potential to impact power dynamics between the researcher and the participants, while promoting collaboration. Consequently, we contend that the use of PE can enhance the representation of marginalised perspectives in human resource management (HRM) and the effectiveness of sustainable transformations in the workplace. To support our assertion, we present our PE research endeavours from a participant‐driven and researcher‐driven PE approach. While there are other instances of PE in HRM studies, this observational study identifies and revises the practical translation of applied method and data analysis supporting researchers in their future PE applications.
apa_citation: Ladwig, R. C., & Phuong, J. (2026). Using photo-elicitation to make marginalised voices heard and seen in human resource management research. *Human Resource Management Journal*. https://doi.org/10.1111/1748-8583.70033

title: Who Benefits from Alternative Data for Credit Scoring? Evidence from Peru
methods: Observational study using merged administrative and proprietary data (credit card applications, loyalty card transaction data, Peruvian administrative credit registry RCC, and Equifax utility payment score RP2). Machine-learning credit scoring algorithms trained separately for applicants with and without credit history; simulation of credit card approval decisions under alternative business objectives; counterfactual comparisons of approval rates with and without retail data.
industry: Consumer credit / retail (credit card issuer owned by a Peruvian multi-sector company)
time_period: January 2021-September 2023 (loyalty and credit registry); credit card applications from June 2022
units: Individual credit card applicants (with and without formal credit history)
n_sample: Applicants grouped into six cohorts (e.g., Group A: 24,899 approved applicants with credit history; Group C: 1,719; Group D: 1,016; Group F: 10,132 no-history rejected applicants also denied elsewhere)
authors: Lee, J. Y.; Yang, J.; Anderson, E. T.
theories: algorithmic decision-making and fairness; distributional consequences of data; information asymmetry in credit markets
keywords: alternative data; consumer finance; credit cards market; financial inclusion
research_question: Can retail transaction data be used as alternative data to construct credit scores that expand credit access for individuals lacking formal credit histories, and who benefits most from incorporating this alternative data into credit scoring decisions?
mechanism_summary: For applicants without a formal credit history, lenders face severe information asymmetry because conventional socioeconomic and utility-based predictors produce highly overlapping risk score distributions for defaulters and non-defaulters. Retail transaction data adds orthogonal behavioral signals (what and how consumers buy) that sharpen the ability to separate low- and high-risk individuals, which expands the set of applicants whose predicted risk falls below approval thresholds. For applicants already in the credit registry, registry data already captures most repayment-relevant information, so retail data produces only marginal reclassification and may even tighten approval when used to identify higher-risk borrowers inside the existing pool.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to four literatures: the distributional consequences of data and algorithmic decision-making in marketing, the use of alternative data for credit scoring (extending Lee, Yang, and Anderson 2025 beyond grocery to multi-domain retail), the emerging literature on nonfinancial firms entering consumer lending, and information-frictions research in credit markets. It reframes marketing data as a nonmarketing asset whose predictive power is concentrated precisely where traditional financial data is missing, and shows that the welfare effect of alternative data depends on which segment of the applicant pool currently suffers from information asymmetry rather than on data volume per se.
practical_implication: For lenders, retail transaction data is most valuable as a "second look" tool for evaluating applicants who would otherwise be rejected due to missing credit histories, and can nearly triple approval rates for no-history applicants (from 15.6% to as high as 47.8%) depending on risk appetite. For policy makers, the results suggest alternative data can advance financial inclusion goals for unbanked populations, but realizing these gains requires attention to long-term consumer welfare, procedural and distributive fairness, strategic manipulation risks, and privacy safeguards around cross-sector data sharing.
limitations: Selection bias remains because the algorithms cannot account for approved applicants who never activated their cards or for rejected applicants who do not appear in the credit registry. The sample is restricted to individuals who voluntarily enrolled in a single firm's loyalty program, limiting generalizability to the broader applicant pool and excluding people entirely invisible to traditional financial data. The analysis relies on simulated rather than randomized field-experimental approval decisions.
future_research: The authors suggest extending analyses to broader pools of potential borrowers beyond loyalty-program members, conducting field experiments that deploy alternative-data scoring algorithms in production to validate downstream behavioral effects, and investigating how alternative data reshapes organizational practices beyond credit scoring, including marketing targeting, customer acquisition strategies, and post-approval customer service and operations for newly included borrowers.
abstract: The World Bank estimates that 1.4 billion individuals worldwide are unbanked, lacking access to credit due to the absence of traditional credit scores. In this article, the authors demonstrate how retail transaction data can be used to construct an alternative credit score, potentially expanding credit access for these individuals. The study utilizes a unique dataset obtained through a partnership with a Peruvian company. The authors merge customer loyalty data and credit card repayment data with administrative records from the Peruvian ﬁnancial system that provide individuals’ detailed ﬁnancial histories. This comprehensive dataset allows the authors to construct credit scores for people both with and without a credit history. Through simulations of credit card approval decisions, they ﬁnd that incorporating retail data increases approval rates for individuals without a credit history, from 16% to between 31% and 48%. In contrast, for those with an established credit history, approval rates remain largely unchanged, at around 88%. The authors investigate why retail data particularly beneﬁts people without a credit history and discuss the broader implications of this credit scoring methodology for consumers, ﬁrms, and policy makers. The ﬁndings highlight the methodology’s potential to transform credit access for millions of previously unbanked individuals.
apa_citation: Lee, J. Y., Yang, J., & Anderson, E. T. (2026). Who benefits from alternative data for credit scoring? Evidence from Peru. *Journal of Marketing Research*, 63(1), 105-126. https://doi.org/10.1177/00222437251360996
