title: From a Portfolio of Journals to a System of Knowledge Production
methods: Editorial essay (From the AOM Editors). Develops a conceptual framework — the knowledge production value cycle — that maps the seven AOM journals onto four quadrants (generation, synthesis, education, application) and illustrates inter-journal linkages with a running example on the venting literature.
industry: na
units: na
n_sample: na
authors: Cronin, Matthew A.; Galvin, Benjamin Martell; George, Elizabeth; Gruber, Marc; Lindebaum, Dirk; Markman, Gideon D.; Miller, C. Chet; Rose, Elizabeth L.; Thatcher, Sherry M. B.; Wood, Geoffrey T.
theories: disciplined imagination (Weick, 1989); full-cycle research (Chatman & Flynn, 2005); knowledge production system / value cycle framework
keywords: AOM journals; application; education; generation; knowledge production system; scholarly impact; synthesis; value cycle
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: The seven AOM journals function not as a portfolio but as an integrated knowledge production system organized into a value cycle of generation, synthesis, education, and application.
- Key constructs: knowledge generation (AMD, AMJ, AMR), knowledge synthesis (AMR, AMC, Annals), education (Annals, AMLE), application (AMP, AMLE).
- Process / phases: discoveries (AMD) seed empirical investigation (AMJ) and unit-theory development (AMR); these feed synthesis (AMC, Annals); synthesized knowledge informs pedagogy (AMLE) and practitioner-facing prescriptions (AMP); application surfaces new puzzles that restart the cycle.
- Boundary conditions: a single paper need not serve all readers; clarity about which quadrant(s) a paper occupies determines for whom its findings are credible, useful, and usable.

The essay argues that scientific impact is amplified when authors recognize how their paper's function relates to other quadrants of the cycle — between-journal hand-offs are themselves part of the value chain, with the output of one journal becoming the input to another. A worked example follows the venting construct from a counterintuitive AMD discovery, through AMJ empirical work and AMR theorizing, into AMC curation and Annals integration, and finally into AMLE pedagogy and AMP practitioner translation.
theoretical_contribution: Reframes the AOM journal portfolio as an interdependent knowledge production system rather than a collection of independent outlets, articulating the distinct epistemic function of each journal within a generation–synthesis–education–application value cycle. Positions inter-journal linkages — not within-paper rigor alone — as the locus of cumulative scholarly impact, and invites authors to write with explicit awareness of how their work feeds and is fed by adjacent quadrants.
practical_implication: Authors, reviewers, and editors should locate each paper within the value cycle and clarify what function it serves, who its audience is, and how its findings can be leveraged by papers in other quadrants. This reframing dissolves the false dichotomy between managerial-application impact and citation-based impact: all four quadrants are necessary, and clarity of function makes findings more usable for researchers, educators, students, and practitioners alike.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editors invite the AOM community to enact the value-cycle perspective in concrete authorship and review practices — making explicit how individual papers connect across journals, integrating AMLE pedagogical lessons into practitioner-oriented AMP work, and using application-phase failures to surface new discoveries that restart the cycle.
abstract: For science to have impact, researchers must generate new knowledge and synthesize it with what is already

From a portfolio of journals to a system of knowledge production. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(1), 11-18. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2025.0123

title: Socialization as a Political Arena: A Multi-Agent Interactionist Perspective to Understand Political Skill and Newcomer Socialization Rates
methods: Four-wave longitudinal field survey of newcomers nested within supervisors at a large Chinese IT corporation; latent growth curve modeling of role clarity, task mastery, and social integration trajectories over the first six months of employment, with two-month intervals between waves; moderated-mediation path analysis using Mplus 8.6 with a sandwich estimator (TYPE = COMPLEX) to handle multilevel nesting and FIML for missing data; Monte Carlo bootstrap with 20,000 replications for indirect and conditional indirect effects; supplemented by two cross-lagged panel studies (each n = 200) for causal inference and discriminant validity, plus interpretive interviews with 40 newcomers.
industry: Information technology — a large IT corporation that recruited campus hires in 2019; supplemental Studies A and B used 200 newcomers each from an IT company and a university alumni network
time_period: Four waves spanning the first six months after newcomer entry — Time 0 (before first day), Time 1 (second month), Time 2 (fourth month), Time 3 (sixth month); 2019 cohort
units: Organizational newcomers (employee-rated IVs/mediators/moderator) nested in their direct supervisors (supervisor-rated adjustment outcomes at T1, T2, T3)
n_sample: 1,197 newcomers nested in 550 supervisors (final response rate 85.1%); two supplemental studies of 200 newcomers each; supplemental interviews with 40 newcomers
authors: Deng, Yingxin; Lin, Weipeng; Song, Yifan; Wang, Mo; Cai, Di; Liu, Jia
theories: interactionist perspective on newcomer socialization rates (Reichers, 1987); multi-agent perspective on socialization (supervisor vs. veteran colleagues); person-by-situation interaction perspective; political skill theory (Ferris et al., 2005, 2007)
keywords: growth rates of adjustment; horizontal social interaction; interaction frequency; interactionist perspective; newcomer socialization; political skill; prosocial climate for newcomers; role clarity; social integration; socialization rates; supervisor; task mastery; vertical social interaction; veteran colleagues
research_question: How and when does newcomers' political skill shape their socialization rates, and through which social-interaction-based mechanism — vertical interactions with one's supervisor versus horizontal interactions with veteran colleagues — does it transmit its effects into different adjustment dynamics (early-entry adjustment states vs. growth rates of role clarity, task mastery, and social integration), conditional on a prosocial climate for newcomers?
mechanism_summary:
- Moderator: Prosocial climate for newcomers (T1, newcomers' psychological perception that insiders care about their needs and adjustment).
- Controls: age, gender, education, Big Five personality, proactive personality, and interaction quality with each agent.

The interactionist perspective holds that interaction frequency is "the primary mechanism through which newcomers are transformed into insiders," and that frequent interactions and rapid socialization arise when newcomer interpersonal characteristics (political skill) and the social environment (insider prosociality) jointly favor adjustment. Politically skilled newcomers can both initiate and prolong interactions with insiders and inspire insiders to interact with them, but vertical and horizontal channels carry different content: supervisor interactions deliver authoritative, structured, holistic information that crystallizes early-entry understanding of roles, tasks, and norms, whereas veteran-colleague interactions supply detailed, diverse, sometimes fragmentary information whose value compounds over time as newcomers integrate it into a sophisticated cognitive schema — producing growth in adjustment rather than immediate states. A prosocial climate amplifies both pathways by making insiders responsive and signaling that interaction returns are high.
theoretical_contribution: The paper integrates two previously disconnected social-interaction-based mechanisms in the political skill literature (vertical vs. horizontal) into a single multi-agent interactionist model and shows that they map onto distinct adjustment dynamics — supervisor interactions to early-entry states and veteran-colleague interactions to growth rates — thereby reconciling disparate views about how political skill operates and overcoming the literature's prior treatment of social contacts as undifferentiated. By identifying prosocial climate for newcomers as a moderator, it advances a person-by-situation account in which newcomer adjustment is a joint function of newcomer social skill and insider prosociality, and extends the political skill nomological network into the underexamined newcomer socialization domain.
practical_implication: Organizations can incorporate political-skill training into newcomer orientation, design mentoring programs and social events that maximize early-entry interaction with supervisors and sustained interaction with veteran colleagues, and recognize the differential roles each agent plays at different stages of adaptation. Because prosocial climate is critical for politically skilled newcomers to socialize effectively, firms should establish formal procedures, anonymous newcomer-feedback systems, and reward structures that motivate supervisors and veterans to attend to newcomer needs and integrate insider prosociality into performance evaluation.
limitations: Adjustment outcomes were rated only by supervisors, so multi-source replication (newcomer, coworker) is needed; cross-lagged supplemental analyses strengthen but do not establish causality, and reverse-direction effects (e.g., better adjustment reducing reliance on insider interactions) cannot be ruled out. All data were collected in China, a high-collectivism, high-power-distance setting where political skill and deference to supervisors may be especially salient, limiting cross-cultural generalizability. Other interpersonal constructs (emotional intelligence, emotional regulation, impression management) were not controlled for, and the conscientiousness measure showed alpha below conventional benchmarks.
future_research: Future studies should replicate the model with multi-source adjustment data and in non-Chinese contexts to test cultural generalizability, control for additional interpersonal-skill confounds (emotional intelligence, emotional regulation, impression management) while parsing variance overlap with proactive personality and Big Five traits, and examine alternative moderators such as psychological safety climate or the level of organizational politics. Researchers should also empirically test the qualitative speculations about why vertical interactions matter for early-entry states and horizontal interactions for growth rates, and explore reciprocal dynamics in which political skill is itself shaped over time through interactions with insiders.
abstract: Integrating two social-interaction-based perspectives (i.e., vertical vs. horizontal social interactions) on political skill, the current research examined how organizational newcomers might leverage their political skill to promote their socialization rates through frequent interactions with different agents of socialization (i.e., supervisor vs. veteran colleagues). Using a four-wave longitudinal study with a sample of 1,197 organizational newcomers nested in 550 supervisors, we found that newcomer political skill had positive indirect effects on growth rates of adjustment outcomes via interaction frequency with veteran colleagues (but not with supervisor), while it had positive indirect effects on early-entry adjustment states via interaction frequency with supervisor (but not with veteran colleagues). Moreover, these effects were stronger when the level of prosocial climate for newcomers was high. Two supplemental studies, each using repeated-measures data from 200 organizational newcomers, provide stronger causal inference of the impact of newcomer political skill that is contingent on prosocial climate for newcomers, and rule out alternative explanations. Furthermore, we conducted supplemental interviews with 40 newcomers to provide an in-depth discussion on the differential roles of vertical (newcomer–supervisor) versus horizontal (newcomer–veteran colleagues) interactions in transmitting newcomer political skill into different adjustment dynamics (i.e., early-entry adjustment states vs. growth rates of adjustment).
apa_citation: Deng, Y., Lin, W., Song, Y., Wang, M., Cai, D., & Liu, J. (2025). Socialization as a political arena: A multi-agent interactionist perspective to understand political skill and newcomer socialization rates. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(1), 108-137. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0669

title: Information-Based Competition: The Case of Rival Owners in Rating Agencies
methods: Pooled OLS regressions of MSCI ESG ratings on rival-MSCI common institutional ownership with industry-year fixed effects and standard errors clustered by industry-year; firm-year panel from 2013-2019 with 12,634 firm-year observations on 2,787 firms; moderation tests for rival/target ESG media controversies and ESG awards; robustness checks include Heckman selection models, firm fixed effects, random effects, alternative industry classifications (FIC-100 vs MSCI IVA), benchmarking against ESG risk incidents and ASSET4 evaluations, quasi-natural experiments around MSCI's 2010 acquisition of KLD and Thomson Reuters' 2009 acquisition of ASSET4, ITCV omitted-variable analysis, and decomposition by dedicated/transient/quasi-indexer investors.
industry: U.S. publicly traded firms across industries (rivals identified using Hoberg-Phillips fixed industry classification FIC-100); rating agency MSCI ESG Research
time_period: 2013-2019 (sample period set by MSCI ESG Ratings Time Series database coverage and availability of all data sources)
units: Firm-year observations of target firms covered by MSCI ESG Research; rival-MSCI common institutional owners with >=1% ownership stakes in both MSCI and the rival firm
n_sample: 12,634 firm-year observations on 2,787 unique firms
authors: DesJardine, Mark R.; Li, Boshuo; Shi, Wei
theories: awareness-motivation-capability framework (Durand, Hawn & Ioannou, 2019); common ownership theory (Connelly et al., 2019); competitive dynamics theory (Chen & Miller, 2015); information intermediary / coverage bias literature (Sharkey et al., 2023); reputation theory (Bundy, Iqbal & Pfarrer, 2021)
keywords: ESG awards; ESG media controversies; ESG ratings; MSCI; common ownership; competitive dynamics; information intermediaries; information-based competition; institutional investors; rating agencies; reputational opportunities; reputational threats
research_question: How and when do competitive forces operating through information intermediaries shape the information that stakeholders receive about a firm — specifically, do firms receive less favorable ESG ratings from rating agencies in which their rivals' institutional investors hold greater ownership stakes, and how is this rating bias amplified or attenuated by reputational threats (rival/target ESG media controversies) and reputational opportunities (rival/target ESG awards)?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: target firm-MSCI CIO, rival/target firm-other agency CIO, target firm-rival CIO, market overlap, size similarity, target/rival firm financial and governance characteristics, ESG compensation/board/regulatory enforcement, industry-year fixed effects.

The theorized mechanism is that institutional investors who simultaneously own significant stakes in MSCI and in firms outside the rating industry have both motive (portfolio returns benefit when their portfolio firms appear better than rivals on ESG) and means (legal "advisor" status, opaque ESG rating processes, executive incentives to placate large investors) to tilt MSCI's ESG ratings against the portfolio firms' rivals. The bias intensifies when reputational stakes are high — rival ESG media controversies create motivation to repair rivals' reputations via ratings, and rival ESG awards heighten raters' receptivity to negative information about less-decorated peers — and weakens when target firms themselves are already drawing reputational scrutiny or have countervailing ESG awards that buffer their ratings.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces information-based competition — competition waged by manufacturing, assembling, and disseminating information that shapes stakeholders' perceptions of other organizations — as a new construct in competitive dynamics research, and adds a dynamic dimension by theorizing the reputational threats and opportunities under which outside actors leverage their influence over information intermediaries. It extends the information-intermediary literature by showing that bias originates not only from rated firms and direct economic exchange partners but also from the rivals' common investors, and contributes to the common-ownership literature by uncovering an indirect channel — boundary-spanning ownership in publicly traded rating agencies — through which common owners profit from competitive intervention.
practical_implication: Firms should manage information flows with intermediaries by disclosing ESG activities transparently in standardized formats, enabling stakeholders and privately owned raters to assess them directly and limiting common-owner influence. Socially responsible investors should use ESG ratings from multiple agencies and account for the ownership/governance structures of those agencies when weighting them. Regulators (notably the SEC) should treat common owners of ESG rating agencies as a category of "unaffiliated third parties" whose conflicts of interest warrant disclosure, internal-control requirements, and ownership-transparency standards.
limitations: The empirical strategy does not lag explanatory variables (because investor influence is theorized to operate during current holding periods), which the authors acknowledge weakens causal claims; endogeneity is mitigated through Heckman selection, ITCV, and quasi-natural experiments rather than fully resolved. The setting is U.S. firms rated by a single agency (MSCI), and although robustness checks against ASSET4 align with the theory, generalizability to non-publicly-traded raters and non-U.S. settings is untested. The two channels of investor influence — direct intervention versus executive pacification of investors — could not be empirically separated.
future_research: Scholars should explore information-based competition along three dimensions: the actor engaging in it (institutional vs. individual investors, formal officeholders inside intermediaries), the type of intermediary (social media platforms like X, online review aggregators like Yelp), and the tie through which influence is exerted (ownership, formal position, advertising). Future work could disentangle whether common-owner influence operates through investor-initiated intervention or executive-initiated pacification, examine information-based competition in non-rating contexts, and probe how multiple competing information sources interact in shaping firm reputations and competitive dynamics.
abstract: This study extends competitive dynamics research by theorizing how and when information about a firm may be shaped by competitive forces operating through information intermediaries. Arguing that ownership bestows investors with influence over information intermediaries from which investors can profit, we suggest that firms receive less favorable ratings from rating agencies in which their rivals' investors have greater ownership. Building on research in this vein, we develop a dynamic theory of information-based competition that suggests the rating discounts a firm receives from rating agencies owned by its rivals' investors are shaped by the reputational threats and opportunities faced by the firm and its rivals, which motivate and enable investors to use information intermediaries to intervene in competitive dynamics. Analyses of environmental, social, and governance (ESG) ratings of 2,787 firms by a leading ESG rating agency support these ideas. Our study introduces the concept of information-based competition and illuminates the conditions under which investors engage in information-based competitive attacks.
apa_citation: DesJardine, M. R., Li, B., & Shi, W. (2025). Information-based competition: The case of rival owners in rating agencies. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(1), 221-256. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0421

title: Analyzing Academy of Management Journal Operations with Artificial Intelligence (2006–2022)
methods: Editorial descriptive analysis using submission-, review-, and scientist-level data from ScholarOne (2006–2022); sentence embeddings (SBERT) used to match rejected AMJ submissions to subsequent publications by their first authors.
industry: na
units: na
n_sample: na
authors: Gruber, Marc
theories: science of science
keywords: Academy of Management Journal; editorial process; machine learning; manuscript submissions; peer review; science of science; sentence embeddings
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: AMJ has experienced strong, sustained growth in submission volume (from ~830/year in Rynes's term to ~1,550/year in Tihanyi's term, totaling 20,003 original manuscripts over 2006–2022) while preserving timely, developmental review.
- Editorial adaptations to manage growth: editorial team size more than doubled (8 to 23 editors); decentralized reviewer assignment from Colquitt's term onward; deputy editor role introduced under Shaw; editorial review board grew from 174 to 285 members and became more internationally diverse.
- Review-process performance: average turnaround under 60 days; review length stable (~1,200 words median); reviewers consistently rate "theoretical contribution" lowest among novelty, theoretical contribution, empirical rigor, and organizational importance; novelty rarely scored high by all three reviewers.
- Post-rejection trajectories: in the 2009 cohort, 47.6% of rejected submissions were matched to a publication elsewhere (using SBERT embeddings of titles and abstracts with similarity >0.7); 18.4% appeared in another FT50 journal; AMJ-published studies received the highest median 5-year citations.

The editorial uses machine-learning techniques applied to ScholarOne digital traces and to a sentence-embedding model (SBERT) to provide a transparent, descriptive look at AMJ's editorial workflow across six editorial teams. The narrative is one of growth managed through editorial team expansion, workflow restructuring, and increasingly global reviewer recruitment, while the journal preserved developmental review as a hallmark of its identity.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial contributes to the "science of science" by demonstrating how machine-learning techniques (SBERT-based matching of rejected submissions to subsequent publications) can be applied to internal editorial archives to track manuscripts beyond the focal journal. It documents the operational evolution of a flagship management journal across six editorial terms and reveals patterns relevant to authors, editors, and scholars studying academic publishing.
practical_implication: Authors should persevere after AMJ rejections — nearly half of rejected 2009 submissions were ultimately published elsewhere, with substantial representation in FT50 journals (Journal of Applied Psychology, Strategic Management Journal, Organization Science, etc.). Authors should attend particularly to theoretical contribution (consistently rated lowest by reviewers) and frame their work's novelty for AMJ's broad readership. Editors at peer journals could perform similar analyses to advance understanding of submission preferences and patterns.
limitations: The current editorial term (2023–2025) was excluded to avoid conflicts of interest, limiting coverage of the most recent developments. The SBERT-based matching of rejected submissions to subsequent publications relies on a similarity threshold (>0.7) that may miss substantially revised manuscripts or those abandoned by their authors; impact analyses are restricted to a five-year citation window using conventional citation counts.
future_research: Advanced analyses are needed to better gauge the value of editors' manuscript choices and developmental feedback. Future work should consider finer-grained citation measures (e.g., effects across management subfields or beyond the discipline), alternative impact measures (MBA curricula, public discourse, doctoral seminar syllabi), and the implications of generative AI for the peer review process and the broader research workflow.
abstract: FROM THE EDITORS ANALYZING ACADEMY OF MANAGEMENT JOURNAL OPERATIONS WITH ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE (2006–2022)
apa_citation: Gruber, M. (2025). Analyzing Academy of Management Journal operations with artificial intelligence (2006–2022). *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(1), 1-10. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2025.4001

title: Bright and Dark Imagining: How Creators Navigate Moral Consequences of Developing Ideas for Artificial Intelligence
methods: Inductive, qualitative study following the Gioia method (Gioia, Corley & Hamilton, 2013) combined with elements of process theorizing (Langley, 1999). Preparatory work included non-participant observation at a three-day AI conference and exploratory interviews with three AI creators and five executives. Main data: 69 semi-structured interviews (~1 hour each, recorded and transcribed verbatim) with 64 AI creators (researchers, scientists, engineers, professors using machine learning) and 5 AI executives across academia and industry. Analysis proceeded in three phases: Phase 0 (grounded constant comparison and contact summary sheets), Phase 1 (open and axial coding by two authors fresh to the context, building a Gioia data structure with first-order codes, second-order themes, and aggregate dimensions), Phase 2 (analytical tables and visual artifacts to compare cases, identify catalyzing surprises, and develop a process model). Member checks during latter interview batches validated insights.
industry: Artificial intelligence development — software engineers, machine learning engineers, research scientists, and professors developing AI tools (e.g., healthcare robots, online shopping recommenders, GPS truck trackers, physician-patient interaction apps, banking fraud detection)
country: Not reported in paper (sample drawn across academia and industry, multiple AI domains)
units: Individual AI creators and executives
n_sample: 69 interviews (64 AI creators and 5 AI executives)
authors: Hagtvedt, Lydia Paine; Harvey, Sarah; Demir-Caliskan, Ozumcan; Hagtvedt, Henrik
theories: constraints and creativity (Acar, Tarakci & van Knippenberg, 2019); construal-level theory (Trope & Liberman, 2010); creativity theory (Amabile, 1983; Woodman, Sawyer & Griffin, 1993); future-making / imagined futures (Thompson & Byrne, 2022); issue-contingent model of ethical decision making (Jones, 1991); moral imagination (Johnson, 1985); sensemaking (Sonenshein, 2007; Weick, 1995)
keywords: artificial intelligence; bright imagining; constraints; creative work; creativity; dark imagining; imagining; moral consequences; moral issues; sensemaking
research_question: Whether, when, and how do creators navigate moral issues arising from their ideas during the course of creative work in a context — AI development — where creative advances are happening rapidly and the potential for widespread harm is heightened?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: imagining (sensemaking projection of an idea's future); bright imagining (positive distal vision, ideas as magical/black-box); dark imagining (negative distal vision, ideas as controllable); moral consequences; surprises as catalysts.
- Catalyzing conditions: surprises during deep technical work (unexpected algorithm outputs while delving into details) trigger bright imagining; surprises when broadening the context (encountering unanticipated effects on humans, testing with users, conversations with outsiders) trigger dark imagining.
- Two parallel processes: (1) Disconnecting through bright imagining — making sense of ideas as magical, constructing positive futures, distancing harms, systematizing safeguards external to the idea, orienting toward novelty, developing ideas without constraints; (2) Integrating through dark imagining — making sense of ideas as controllable, constructing negative futures, moving harms closer, internalizing responsibility, orienting toward usefulness, embedding constraints into idea development.
- Boundary conditions: most creators show a dominant orientation but blend both; broader-context surprises can shift creators from bright to dark imagining over time; the model is bounded by the degree of harm an idea may cause and the maturity of the industry.
- Emergent outcomes: customized product constraints versus generalized safeguards/regulation; transformation of moral issues into creative challenges that shape the technology itself.

The central process is an imaginative, prospective sensemaking activity in which surprises during creative work prompt creators to project ideas into the future, and those projections in turn reshape both how creators engage with moral issues (distancing vs. internalizing) and how they develop ideas (unconstrained novelty pursuit vs. embedded customized constraints). The two pathways are mutually reinforcing — technical surprises sustain bright imagining and unconstrained discovery, while contextual surprises move harms closer, evoke negative emotions, and turn moral concerns into design constraints that fuel further creativity.
theoretical_contribution: The study presents a grounded model of how creators engage with potential distal moral consequences of their ideas during the creative process itself, adding moral consequences to the criteria (beyond novelty and usefulness) by which creators project their ideas. It moves beyond prior "dark side" work that attributed unethical behavior to creators' personal characteristics, instead showing that engaging with moral issues is itself a constructive, creative process catalyzed by experiences during creative work. It also unpacks how morally motivated constraints can become a stimulus that shapes the nature of ideas themselves, extending research on constraints and creativity into morally charged domains.
practical_implication: Managers of AI and other novel-technology development should cultivate awareness of bright and dark imagining and of the catalyzing surprises that encourage each, recognizing that disconnecting and embedding approaches yield different governance solutions (broad regulation vs. customized product constraints). A mix of both is likely needed: product constraints integrated into specific technologies plus broader field-level guidelines. Managers can introduce procedures that remind creators of distal consequences without overemphasizing harm, since this can both head off disasters and spur additional creativity, while overemphasis may hinder the development of breakthrough beneficial products.
limitations: The study cannot reveal whether one approach (disconnecting or integrating) is more effective at reducing harms, and the focus on individual creators leaves the collective dynamics that may facilitate the process in the background. The qualitative data do not yield definitive insights into the personalities or underlying characteristics that lead creators toward pure scientific discovery versus applied work. Generalizability is bounded by the degree of potential harm of an idea and the maturity of the industry; AI was chosen as an extreme case where standards had not yet emerged.
future_research: Future work should investigate how creators develop ideas that are simultaneously novel, useful, and minimally harmful, and how other criteria such as sustainability, aesthetic dimensions, or long-term value enter creators' projections. Scholars could explore the relative effectiveness of customized product constraints versus broader regulation, examine how alternative forms of constraint interact within a domain, and study how power imbalances and collective dynamics shape which form of imagining dominates over time.
abstract: Despite an emerging stream of work on negative behaviors associated with engaging in creativity, research on the consequences of creativity has largely focused on unleashing the proximal success of new ideas. Both approaches overlook the downstream potential for creative ideas to directly cause harm. Through an inductive, qualitative study of individuals creating artificial intelligence technologies, the present study shifts the conversation to how workers navigate potential distal moral consequences of ideas while engaging in creative work. Our study unveils that surprises during creative work catalyze a process of imagining future consequences of ideas, which shapes the way creators engage with moral issues and approach idea development. A key insight of our study is that imagining unfolds in two ways: (1) bright imagining is associated with disconnecting moral issues from idea development, so that creators develop ideas in the relative absence of constraints and moral issues are addressed through systematized safeguards; (2) dark imagining is associated with integrating moral issues into idea development, transforming morally motivated constraints into creative forces with potential to shape the nature of ideas themselves. Our study recasts interacting with moral consequences intertwined with creative ideas as itself a creative, constructive process.
apa_citation: Hagtvedt, L. P., Harvey, S., Demir-Caliskan, O., & Hagtvedt, H. (2025). Bright and dark imagining: How creators navigate moral consequences of developing ideas for artificial intelligence. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(1), 19-49. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0850

title: Breaking Free or Locking In: How Socially Disadvantaged Individuals Achieve or Reject an Aspired Identity in an Entrepreneurial Context
methods: Inductive, qualitative longitudinal field study using Locke (2001) and Strauss & Corbin (1998) grounded-theory analysis; 102 semi-structured interviews conducted in four waves (December 2019; summer 2020; summer 2022; February 2023) with 61 women employees and prospective/actual salon owners at BeautyCo (a pseudonymous Chinese beauty services company), supplemented by interviews with top management, HR managers, and mentors plus newsletters, media articles, and a virtual staff meeting; three authors performed independent open coding, weekly meetings to reach consensus, axial coding, and theoretical coding; theoretical saturation, member checks with five participants, and ruling out alternative explanations (profitability, prior failure, family support, prior work experience).
industry: Beauty and wellness services (skincare, massage); employees of a single multi-salon Chinese beauty services company (BeautyCo, pseudonym) participating in an in-house entrepreneurial program to open employee-owned BeautyCo-branded salons
time_period: December 2019 to February 2023, four waves of interviews
units: Front-line female employees of BeautyCo (beauticians, consultants, managers) who had become, were starting, or were considering becoming salon owners (Laoban)
n_sample: 61 women participants; 102 interviews total
authors: Jiang, Winnie Y.; Zhao-Ding, Amy; Qi, Shelly
theories: emotions in identity and entrepreneurship (Maitlis, 2020; Cardon, Foo, Shepherd & Wiklund, 2012); entrepreneurship-as-emancipation (Rindova, Barry & Ketchen, 2009; Bruton, Ketchen & Ireland, 2013); identity formation / identity work (Ibarra, 1999; Pratt, Rockmann & Kaufmann, 2006; Ashforth & Schinoff, 2016); identity network approach (Ramarajan, 2014; Bataille & Vough, 2022); sensemaking (Maitlis & Christianson, 2014; Maitlis, Vogus & Lawrence, 2013)
keywords: aspired identity; emotional regulation; emotions; entrepreneurship-as-emancipation; existing identities; identity conflict; identity formation; identity network; identity work; inter-identity work; interpersonal sensemaking; retrospective sensemaking; sensemaking; socially disadvantaged individuals
research_question: How do socially disadvantaged individuals navigate the perceived conflict between their existing socially disadvantaged identities and an aspired identity, and why do some successfully acquire the aspired identity to achieve positive self-transformation while others do not?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: An "aspiring event" (BeautyCo's entrepreneurial program) activates the aspired identity (being a business owner / Laoban) and conflicting existing socially disadvantaged identities (rural origin, low education, family obligations as women).
- Initial emotional reactions: Concurrent positive (desire) and negative (concern) emotions whose relative strength is shaped by, and in turn shapes, retrospective sensemaking of prior failure and interpersonal sensemaking.
- Three paths (each a coupled cycle of emotions + identity work + inter-identity work):
  - Elevation path: emotional regulation by separating displayed (positive) and felt (negative) emotions; identity work to suppress conflicting existing identities and strengthen the aspired identity; inter-identity work to neutralize threatening meanings, yielding compatible relations and a realized aspired identity (with worse work-family balance).
  - Experimentation path: emotional regulation by support-seeking and action-taking; identity work to change the meanings of conflicting existing identities; inter-identity work to find bridging meanings producing synergistic relations and a realized aspired identity (with better work-family balance).
  - Entrenchment path: no emotional regulation or transformation; identity work to devalue the aspired identity and strengthen conflicting existing identities; inter-identity work that reinforces threatening meanings, leaving the conflict intact and the aspired identity rejected (return to prior employee role).
- Driving sensemaking factors: retrospective sensemaking of prior failure (less salient / instructive / paralyzing) and interpersonal sensemaking from mentors, employees, and family.

The central process is that aspiring events surface a perceived conflict between existing and aspired identities, generating internal obstacles in the form of negative emotions; whether individuals reach emotional transformation depends on how they regulate emotions and make sense of prior failure and others' reactions, which in turn motivate distinct identity work (suppressing, reframing, or devaluing identities) and inter-identity work (neutralizing or bridging meanings) that either reconstruct identities into compatible or synergistic relations or lock the conflict in place.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to the identity-formation literature by foregrounding how aspired identities are achieved or rejected amid conflicting existing identities, identifying three paths defined by distinct patterns of emotional reactions, identity work, and inter-identity work, and showing how relationships among identities can shift from conflict into compatible or synergistic ties through emotion-regulation tactics. It also contributes to the entrepreneurship-as-emancipation literature by uncovering the emotional and cognitive identity dynamics that explain why socially disadvantaged individuals receiving similar entrepreneurial opportunities diverge in entrepreneurial entry, achievement, and subjective work-family outcomes.
practical_implication: Individuals pursuing aspirational goals should recognize internal obstacles as products of identity conflict, identify the threatening meanings of their existing identities, and use emotion-regulation tactics to either neutralize or reframe those meanings. Entrepreneurial support organizations targeting socially disadvantaged individuals should look beyond resource and knowledge barriers and provide guidance on retrospective sensemaking of prior failure and on regulating negative emotions, thereby helping participants reconstruct conflicting inter-identity relationships into compatible or synergistic ones.
limitations: The study cannot speak to whether or how compatible or synergistic inter-identity relationships might later revert to conflicting ones, since data were not collected over a long enough horizon. The setting features socially constructed disadvantaged identities (rural, low SES, female caregiver) within a Chinese cultural context where being Laoban carries elevated status; internal obstacles rooted in personal rather than social identities, or contexts where entrepreneurship is necessity-driven and not aspirational, may not fit the model.
future_research: Researchers should track inter-identity relationships over longer periods to assess the stability of compatible and synergistic ties and the factors that destabilize them. They should also investigate how individuals overcome internal obstacles arising from personal (rather than social or relational) identities, and what explains varied entrepreneurial outcomes for socially disadvantaged individuals in cultural contexts where entrepreneurship is not aspirational or status-conferring.
abstract: This research examines how individuals achieve or reject an aspired identity when they perceive it as conflicting with some existing identities. We conducted an inductive, qualitative study of 61 socially disadvantaged women in China who were provided an entrepreneurial opportunity. This opportunity activated these women's aspired identity of being business owners as well as their existing identities of being women from a humble background. They perceived these identities as in conflict, which generated internal obstacles to the attainment of their aspired identity. Adopting the identity network approach as our theoretical lens, we revealed three paths through which they navigated the perceived identity conflict—elevation, experimentation, or entrenchment—marked by different patterns of emotions, identity work, and inter-identity work. Further, we found that retrospective sensemaking of past failure and interpersonal sensemaking shaped the paths these women took to achieve or reject the aspired identity. Our study uncovers the emotional and cognitive dynamics that lead individuals to either be emancipated from or remain entrenched by the internal obstacles stemming from their perceived conflict between aspired and existing identities that hinders positive self-transformation, contributing to the literatures on identity formation and entrepreneurship-as-emancipation.
apa_citation: Jiang, W. Y., Zhao-Ding, A., & Qi, S. (2025). Breaking free or locking in: How socially disadvantaged individuals achieve or reject an aspired identity in an entrepreneurial context. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(1), 162-190. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.1104

title: Does Identification Hurt or Help Under Identity Threat? The Exacerbating Role of Identity Centrality on Feeling Offended and the Buffering Role of Coworker Solidarity on Identity-Protection Behaviors
methods: Two two-wave, time-lagged online experiments via Prolific with U.S. full-time/part-time employees. Phase 1 measured trait-like moderators (identity centrality, solidarity with identity, coworker solidarity); one week later Phase 2 randomly assigned participants to high vs. low identity threat scenarios (coworker vignettes) and measured feeling offended and three identity-protection responses (derogation, positive distinctiveness, avoidance). Hypotheses tested with Mplus path analysis (v8.9), Monte Carlo confidence intervals for indirect effects, simple slopes analysis for moderation, plus ANOVA confirmation of manipulation. Discriminant validity supported via CFA and average variance extracted (AVE) tests; supplemental qualitative study A and content-validation study G validate measures.
industry: Mixed; full-time and part-time U.S. employees recruited via Prolific (no industry restriction)
time_period: Study 1: October 2022 (political identity); Study 2: May 2023 (working parent identity)
units: Individual employees who self-identified as Democrats/Republicans (Study 1) or working parents (Study 2)
n_sample: Study 1: 453 (88.5% full-time, 37.3% Republican); Study 2: 637 working parents (86% full-time)
authors: Johnson, Hana Huang; Umphress, Elizabeth; Bates, Jay T.; Parkinson, Shaun M.; Sheppard, Leah D.
theories: Petriglieri's (2011) identity threat framework; cognitive-motivational-relational theory of emotions (Lazarus, 1991; Lazarus & Folkman, 1984); identity theory (Stets & Burke, 2000; Stryker, 1980); self-verification theory (Swann, 1983); social identity theory (Tajfel & Turner, 1986)
keywords: avoidance; coworker solidarity; derogation; feeling offended; identity centrality; identity threat; identity-protection responses; political identity; positive distinctiveness; solidarity; working parent identity
research_question: How and why do different facets of identification — specifically, identity centrality (an inward-focused trait reflecting how important an identity is to one's self-concept) versus solidarity (an outward-focused bond with another person or group) — differentially shape employees' emotional and behavioral responses to identity threats posed by coworkers, and does the referent of solidarity (others sharing the threatened identity vs. coworkers) matter for identity-protection responses?
mechanism_summary:
- Mediator: Feeling offended (composite of "offended," "insulted," "hurt").
- Moderator (first stage): Identity centrality of the threatened identity (10-item validated scale) — exacerbates the threat-to-feeling-offended link.
- Moderator (second stage): Coworker solidarity (Leach et al., 2008) — buffers the feeling-offended-to-protection-response links.
- Controls: solidarity with others sharing the threatened identity, political affiliation (Study 1), gender, child age, coworker identity centrality (Study 2).

Drawing on cognitive-motivational-relational (CMR) theory of emotions, the authors theorize that identity threat from a coworker triggers a primary appraisal in which individuals feel offended — particularly when the threatened identity is highly central to their self-concept and thus more sensitive to value-diminishing events. Feeling offended then prompts a secondary appraisal that mobilizes engagement coping (derogating the source, asserting positive distinctiveness) or disengagement coping (avoidance). Coworker solidarity blurs self-other boundaries, making harmful responses toward coworkers feel self-harming and thereby weakening dysfunctional behavioral reactions; solidarity with others sharing the threatened identity, by contrast, exhibits weaker and less consistent buffering, suggesting the relational referent of solidarity matters for which identity-protection behaviors are dampened.
theoretical_contribution: The paper untangles identity centrality from solidarity — two facets of identification that prior identity-threat research has conflated — and shows they operate at different stages of the threat-response process: centrality exacerbates emotional appraisal (feeling offended), while solidarity moderates behavioral coping. By contrasting solidarity with the threatened-identity in-group versus solidarity with coworkers, the authors reveal that the relational referent of solidarity matters, with coworker solidarity providing the more robust buffer against dysfunctional identity-protection responses. The work also introduces a validated 10-item identity centrality measure that distinguishes centrality from solidarity, and extends Petriglieri's (2011) identity-threat framework empirically by linking CMR-theoretic emotional mediators to derogation, positive distinctiveness, and avoidance outcomes.
practical_implication: Organizations and leaders pursuing diversity and inclusion should recognize that highly central identities make even benign coworker remarks potential triggers of dysfunctional protective behaviors, and that fostering coworker solidarity — through transformational leadership, employee-focused climates, and structured opportunities for cross-identity social connection — may neutralize derogation and avoidance reactions. Beyond affinity groups for shared marginalized identities, leaders should cultivate a superordinate shared identity that subsumes employees' diverse central identities. When designing policies that differentially affect identity groups (e.g., parental-leave changes), leaders should consult focus groups composed of those with high identity centrality to anticipate threat reactions.
limitations: Reliance on self-report measures raises common-method-variance concerns, although Phase 1/Phase 2 temporal separation and experimental manipulation of the IV mitigate this; identity centrality and solidarity-with-identity were highly correlated in Study 1 (CFA/AVE tests support discriminant validity but third-party ratings of dependent variables would strengthen inference). The mediator (feeling offended) was measured contemporaneously with the dependent variables, weakening causal claims about emotion-to-behavior links. Behavioral measures captured intentions in hypothetical scenarios rather than actual workplace behaviors, raising external-validity concerns despite supplemental qualitative evidence (Supplement A) that real workplace identity threats elicit comparable responses. The model assumes identity centrality and solidarity are trait-like, but both may shift over time in response to identity-relevant events.
future_research: Scholars should use experience-sampling methods to capture real-time emotional and behavioral responses to identity threats in naturalistic settings, and measure actual rather than self-reported behaviors via coworker/supervisor reports. Future work should explore the broader range of discrete emotions (e.g., anger when injustice is salient) elicited by different identity threats, treat threat severity as a continuum, and test whether the type of threatened identity (personal, role, or group) shapes which protection responses dominate and whether solidarity with the threatened-identity in-group interacts with coworker solidarity. Researchers should also investigate identity-restructuring responses (beyond identity-protection) and how organizational context — leadership style, collectivist culture, organizational justice — amplifies or dampens identity-threat consequences.
abstract: Research on how identification impacts the experience of identity threat has uncovered mixed findings, which the current work helps resolve. We uncouple two conceptually distinct aspects of identification that research has conflated: identity centrality and solidarity. Identity centrality is focused inward on the extent to which an identity is important and salient to an individual, whereas solidarity is focused outward on the strength of the bond an individual perceives with another person or group. We propose that higher centrality exacerbates reactions to identity threat and ultimately predicts identity-protection responses (e.g., derogating or avoiding the threat source), whereas greater solidarity with coworkers mitigates negative responses stemming from identity threat. We consider two groups with whom individuals might experience solidarity in organizations: (1) others who share the threatened identity and (2) coworkers. We test and find support for our hypotheses in two studies using two-wave, time-lagged online experimental methods investigating political identity (Study 1) and working parent identity (Study 2). Notably, solidarity with coworkers provides a buffering effect while solidarity with others sharing the threatened identity does so to a lesser degree. Our work helps reconcile how and why identity centrality and certain forms of solidarity can differentially influence identity threat reactions.
apa_citation: Johnson, H. H., Umphress, E., Bates, J. T., Parkinson, S. M., & Sheppard, L. D. (2025). Does identification hurt or help under identity threat? The exacerbating role of identity centrality on feeling offended and the buffering role of coworker solidarity on identity-protection behaviors. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(1), 50-80. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0221

title: The Paradox of Spatial and Relational Embeddedness: Tie Reinitiation after a Trust Violation
methods: Continuous-time Weibull regression model for multiple ordered events (event history analysis) of focal firm-shipbroker tie reinitiation, with cluster-robust standard errors at the focal firm-broker dyad level and year fixed effects; main models include trust violation, focal firm-broker collocation, relational embeddedness, and the two trust-violation interaction terms; robustness checks include instrumental-variable estimation (using Diaspora as instrument for collocation), Lee's (1983) two-stage selection model, broker fixed effects, inverse-Gaussian frailty model, charterer fixed effects, dependence controls, alternative distributions (exponential, Gompertz, Cox proportional hazards), continuous geographic-proximity measures, and qualitative triangulation via 14 semi-structured interviews averaging 37 minutes each.
industry: Global dry cargo (dry bulk) shipping; voyage-level brokerage exchanges between a Copenhagen-based shipowner-operator (focal firm) and its shipbroker partners
country: Focal firm headquartered in Copenhagen, Denmark; shipbroker partner firms located worldwide (global dry cargo shipping market)
time_period: 2011-2018 (voyage-level exchanges); supplemented by 14 semi-structured interviews conducted between 2016 and 2023
units: Focal firm-shipbroker dyad-voyage exchanges (3,618 voyage-level exchanges across 235 shipbroker partner firms; 169 brokers were re-tied, yielding 3,383 tie reinitiations); right-censored observations for non-reinitiated brokers
n_sample: 3,618 voyage-level exchanges between the focal firm and 235 shipbroker partner firms
authors: Kumar, Pankaj; Nowinska, Agnieszka; Zaheer, Akbar
theories: brokerage theory / structural holes (Burt, 1992; Stovel & Shaw, 2012); embeddedness theory (Granovetter, 1985; Uzzi, 1997); geography of social networks (ter Wal, 2014; Bell & Zaheer, 2007); interfirm trust theory (Zaheer, McEvily & Perrone, 1998; Mayer, Davis & Schoorman, 1995); tertius gaudens / divided loyalties (Fleming & Waguespack, 2007; Stovel et al., 2011); trust repair / violation literature (Kim, Dirks & Cooper, 2009; Dirks, Lewicki & Zaheer, 2009)
keywords: brokerage; collocation; divided loyalties; dry cargo shipping; embeddedness as a buffer; et tu Brute logic; interfirm trust; relational embeddedness; shipbroking; spatial embeddedness; tie reinitiation; trust violation
research_question: How does embeddedness — both spatial (collocation) and relational (a history of prior dyadic ties) — influence interfirm tie reinitiation when trust is violated, and does embeddedness in brokerage relationships act as a buffer that absorbs the shock of a trust violation, or does it instead aggravate the trust impairment such that embedded ties take proportionately longer to be reinitiated than non-embedded ones?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Broker status (top-10 Lloyd's List ranking), Broker commission, DWT, Vessel type (six categories), Contract type (five categories), Focal firm-charterer collocation, Broker-charterer collocation, Voyage start year fixed effects.

The theorized mechanism is the paradox of embeddedness in brokered settings: while collocation and prior interactional history both enhance trust and routinely foster tie reinitiation, the same elevated expectations make embedded trust unusually brittle once a broker — whose role inherently involves divided loyalties between the focal firm and the charterer — engages in tertius-gaudens behavior. Spatial embeddedness binds managers in multiplex informal relationships generating affect-based interpersonal trust, so a violation feels like betrayal by a friend; relational embeddedness creates bilateral routines and norms whose breach signals expectancy violation and raises fears of future information leakage. In both cases the "et tu Brute" logic dominates the "embeddedness as a buffer" logic, producing a proportionately greater (relative) decline in tie-reinitiation hazard after violation among embedded brokers (a 226% increase in median days to reinitiation for collocated brokers vs. 65% for non-collocated; 127% increase for highly relationally embedded vs. 62% for low).
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to trust and network research by analyzing how embeddedness conditions the trust-violation–tie-persistence relationship in brokered settings, reconciling the "embeddedness as a buffer" and "et tu Brute" perspectives by showing that the broker's divided loyalty inverts the buffering logic and turns embeddedness into a sanctioning amplifier in relative terms. It links trust dynamics to network research on the fragility of brokerage relations and develops a paradoxical view of trust in tie repetition: spatial and relational trust foster repeat ties under normal conditions but lengthen the reinitiation clock disproportionately after a violation. The study also distinguishes the distinct mechanisms by which spatial embeddedness (informal, interpersonal, affect-based) and relational embeddedness (formal, aspatial, routine-based) translate into proportionate sanctioning, with post hoc evidence that spatial embeddedness exerts the more potent penalty.
practical_implication: For shipbrokers and other market intermediaries who depend on repeat business and commissions, the findings imply that maintaining collocated or relationally embedded client relationships requires more than arbitrage value — it demands sharing private market information and tacit recognition of client needs, because trust violations in such close-knit ties trigger sharper relative declines in reinitiation than in arm's-length ones. For client firms, the results show that trust-violating brokers may still be retained for specific routes or cargoes since severing embedded ties is often infeasible, but slowing the speed and frequency of reinitiation acts as a clear sanctioning signal that registers dismay at the violation while preserving commercial flexibility.
limitations: The voyage-level longitudinal database originates from a single focal firm in a single empirical setting (global shipbroking), which sacrifices breadth for depth and limits generalizability to other mediated markets, especially those where brokers and partners directly compete or are in horizontal relationships. The data lack information on all of the charterer's ties via this broker (only those involving the focal firm are observed) and on brokers' ties with other focal firms, so the frailty robustness check must be interpreted with caution. The study also cannot definitively separate trust-based from coordination/communication-cost or balance-theory explanations, though falsification tests with continuous proximity measures and charterer fixed effects support the trust-based mechanism.
future_research: Future work could theorize about the effect of geography on brokerage stability when the broker and its partners stand in horizontal rather than vertical relationships — for example, brokerage structures within networks of strategic alliances — and disentangle trust violation from broker competitive behavior in such settings. The interplay between different types of embeddedness deserves deeper conceptual treatment, building on the paper's post hoc finding that collocated relationally embedded shipbrokers face a more severe reinitiation penalty than non-collocated relationally embedded ones, examining how spatial and relational embeddedness combine and substitute. Extending the framework to other mediated market contexts (real estate, talent agency, financial intermediation) would test the generalizability of the paradox.
abstract: How does embeddedness—spatial and relational—influence interfirm tie reinitiation when trust is violated? The taken-for-granted relationship between prior ties and repeat tie formation becomes complex when trust violations are introduced. Two divergent schools of thought emerge: an "embeddedness as a buffer" logic, wherein embedded partners may be more forgiving, versus an "et tu Brute" logic, wherein embedded partners feel betrayed. We tackle this theoretical tension by examining brokerage relationships. When spatially or relationally embedded, focal firm–broker ties further enhance trust but the ensuing higher expectations also make embedded trust more brittle due to the broker's divided loyalties, resulting in a steeper decline in trust after a violation. Our central hypotheses thus highlight a paradox of embeddedness: while spatial and relational trust foster repeat ties under normal circumstances, it takes proportionately longer for such embedded ties to be reinitiated when trust is violated. We use a unique hand-collected data set in the global dry cargo shipping industry with voyage-level data on 3,618 exchanges between a Copenhagen-based shipowner–operator (the focal firm) and 235 shipbrokers from 2011 to 2018 and a Weibull hazard rate estimator. We find support for the paradox of embeddedness when trust is violated in brokerage relationships.
apa_citation: Kumar, P., Nowinska, A., & Zaheer, A. (2025). The paradox of spatial and relational embeddedness: Tie reinitiation after a trust violation. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(1), 81-107. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.1143

title: A Diversity Signal Set Perspective: Examining Interactive Effects of Diversity Practices on Women and Racialized Non-Leader and Leader Turnover
methods: Two-study mixed-methods design. Study 1 is a longitudinal panel field study using fractional logit regressions with firm and year fixed effects and cluster-robust standard errors on archival data from Vault and the Minority Corporate Counsel Association (MCAA) Law Firm Diversity Database, hand-coding diversity practices into Leslie's (2019) resource, non-discrimination, and accountability typology and predicting one-year-ahead turnover rates of women and racialized associates (non-leaders) and partners (leaders); Tobit fixed-effects models used as robustness checks. Study 2 is a preregistered 2x2x2 between-person experimental vignette study (separate non-leader and leader samples on Prolific) using ANOVA and Mplus path modeling with 5,000 bias-corrected bootstrap resamples to test mediated three-way interaction effects of manipulated resource, non-discrimination, and accountability practices on concerns about unfair treatment, personal relevance, and turnover intention; manipulation checks, confirmatory factor analyses, and supplementary content-validity (Q-sort/ANOVA) and personal-relevance validation samples support the measures.
industry: Study 1: large U.S. law firms (associates and partners); Study 2: women and racialized non-managers and managers across industries recruited via Prolific.
country: Study 1: United States (firms with U.S.-based attorney staff); Study 2: U.S.-employed Prolific participants.
time_period: Study 1: 2010-2018 turnover years (diversity practices coded from 2010-2019 Vault/MCAA surveys); Study 2: data collected for two preregistered experiments (no calendar window reported).
units: Study 1: firm-year observations of law firms (associates and partners as separate models). Study 2: individual women and racialized non-managers (non-leaders) and supervisors (leaders) randomly assigned to one of eight diversity-practice scenarios.
n_sample: Study 1: 280 firms / 1,608 firm-year observations for non-leaders and 270 firms / 1,572 firm-year observations for leaders. Study 2: 650 non-leaders and 661 leaders.
authors: Trzebiatowski, Tiffany; Jiang, Kaifeng; Zhang, Zhen; Eckardt, Rory; Kim, Yeongsu Anthony
theories: Bowen and Ostroff (2004) HRM strength framework; cognitive view of signaling theory / signal set congruence-incongruence (Drover, Wood & Corbett, 2018); diversity initiative effectiveness model (Nishii et al., 2018; Leslie, 2019); role theory (Biddle, 1979, 1986); signaling theory (Spence, 1973; Connelly et al., 2011)
keywords: accountability practices; concerns about unfair treatment; diversity initiative effectiveness; diversity practices; non-discrimination practices; non-leaders and leaders; personal relevance; resource practices; role theory; signal set congruence and incongruence; signaling theory; turnover; women and racialized employees
research_question: Whether and how three categories of diversity practices — resource, non-discrimination, and accountability — interact to shape the turnover of women and racialized non-leaders and leaders, and what role-based psychological mechanisms (concerns about unfair treatment vs. personal relevance) link these signal-set configurations to turnover intentions.
mechanism_summary:
- Manipulated IVs (Study 2): high vs. low resource, non-discrimination, and accountability practices in a 2x2x2 between-person vignette about a fictional company "Ascentira."
- Moderation logic: the focal three-way interaction is theorized as resource practices moderated jointly by the safeguarding signal set (non-discrimination and accountability), with role-based interpretation differing for non-leaders and leaders.
- Controls (Study 1): firm size (log attorneys), international operations dummy, leverage ratio (associate-to-partner), diversity of non-leaders and of leaders (Blau's index), firm and year fixed effects.

The theorized process is that top executives' diversity practices act as signals whose congruence (both safeguarding practices high or both low) lowers uncertainty and triggers heuristic, "more-is-better" processing, while incongruent (mixed) signal sets raise uncertainty and elicit role-based systematic processing. Non-leaders, more removed from signal senders and more vulnerable to "undeserving beneficiary" stereotypes, disproportionately weight mixed signals as negative, so resource practices fail to reduce turnover when the safeguarding set is mixed, with concerns about unfair treatment as the salient micro-level mechanism. Leaders, who hold an implementer role, disproportionately weight low accountability signals as a lack of recognition for their diversity efforts, so resource practices fail to reduce turnover when accountability is low, with personal relevance as the salient micro-level mechanism.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends diversity-initiative effectiveness research and signaling theory by theorizing and testing three-way interactive effects across multiple categories of diversity practices rather than examining practices in isolation or as additive indices. By marrying a cognitive view of signaling (signal set congruence/incongruence) with role theory, it explains why different configurations of diversity practices produce different outcomes for non-leaders versus leaders within women and racialized employee groups, and identifies role-specific mediators (concerns about unfair treatment vs. personal relevance), thereby offering a more nuanced multilevel account of when and for whom diversity practices reduce turnover.
practical_implication: Investing in resource practices alone is insufficient — and potentially counterproductive — for retaining women and racialized employees. To reduce non-leader turnover, organizations must avoid mixed safeguarding signals and pair resource practices with both high non-discrimination and high accountability; if all three cannot be supported, leaders' retention is best protected by emphasizing accountability practices in particular. Managers should recognize that beneficiaries and implementers interpret the same diversity-practice bundle through different lenses (fair-treatment concerns vs. personal relevance), so communication and program design should address both.
limitations: Study 1's law firm setting limits generalizability because law is among the least diverse U.S. industries and law firms have flatter hierarchies than many organizations. The diversity-practice items, although derived from a comprehensive practitioner-informed survey, may not capture every relevant practice (e.g., name-blinding), and Study 2's vignettes simplified each category to a single example. The two mediators are highly correlated and were measured with common-method designs, and the authors did not measure perceived signal uncertainty directly; signal intent was assumed to be substantive and well-intentioned.
future_research: Future studies should test the model with field samples of managers and across more hierarchical and demographically diverse industries, expand the set of diversity practices examined (including AI- and VR-enabled vignettes and eye-tracking of attention to signals), and explore additional mechanisms such as other-oriented relevance and exclusion, as well as reactions of dominant-group non-beneficiaries. Time-separated or experimentally manipulated mediator designs could strengthen causal inference about concerns-about-unfair-treatment and personal-relevance pathways, and scholars could relax the substantive-intent assumption to study signal intent alongside signal quality.
abstract: While organizations often implement multiple diversity practices, our understanding of their interactive effects is limited. Integrating the signal set congruence and incongruence literature with role theory, we theorize three-way interactive effects among diversity practices (resource, non-discrimination, and accountability) on women and racialized non-leader and leader turnover. We examine our model with two studies. In Study 1 (longitudinal panel data from 2010 to 2018; n 5 270–280 law firms), we find higher levels of resource practices decreased turnover rates when both safeguarding practices (non-discrimination and accountability) were high and congruent. Further, resource practices are less useful in reducing non-leaders' turnover rate when safeguarding practices were mixed, whereas they are less useful in curbing leaders' turnover rate when accountability practices were low. We theorize different underlying mechanisms for non-leaders and leaders at the individual level of analysis, finding initial support in an experimental vignette study (Study 2, n 5 650 non-managers and 661 managers). The relationships between the three-way interaction and non-leaders' and leaders' turnover intentions were mediated by concerns about unfair treatment and personal relevance, respectively. By focusing on interaction effects, we help disentangle when and how different configurations of diversity practices may be beneficial versus detrimental.
apa_citation: Trzebiatowski, T., Jiang, K., Zhang, Z., Eckardt, R., & Kim, Y. A. (2025). A diversity signal set perspective: Examining interactive effects of diversity practices on women and racialized non-leader and leader turnover. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(1), 191-220. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1838

title: A Tale of Two Signals: Partner CSR versus CSI and Alliance Formation
methods: Quantitative analysis of dyad-year data on alliance formation among U.S.-listed high-tech firms (1995-2016). Risk set built from all possible dyads between any two high-tech firms with KLD CSR/CSI records in each year; realized alliances identified via SDC Platinum and verified in Factiva. Logistic regression with year fixed effects and robust standard errors clustered at the dyad level. Independent variables aggregated to dyad level by summing partner-level KLD strength (CSR) and concern (CSI) scores across five dimensions (community, diversity, employee relations, environment, product). Moderators: same-CBSA proximity dummy and partners' summed Factiva media-coverage count (scaled by 1/100). Robustness: ASSET4 / RepRisk alternatives, double-entry estimation, restrictive risk set, rare-event logit, two-stage Heckman probit, CSR/CSI mix dummies, and CSR-decoupling controls.
industry: U.S.-listed high-tech firms across 28 four-digit SIC industries (e.g., 2833-2836 pharma, 357x computers, 366x electronics/communications, 38xx instruments, 481x-489x telecom, 737x software/IT services), as defined by Loughran and Ritter (2004)
units: Dyad-year observations (potential firm-firm alliance pairs); within-dyad partners are treated as indistinguishable, so measures are aggregated as sums across the two partners
n_sample: 1,564 unique firms; 740 realized alliances and 2,672,756 non-realized dyad-year observations (N = 2,673,496 in the main models)
authors: Yu, Q.; Cuypers, I. R. P.; Wang, H.
theories: negativity bias / asymmetric processing of positive vs negative signals; signaling theory; spillover mechanism (stakeholder evaluations transferred via affiliation); trust mechanism (CSR/CSI as signals of partner moral character)
keywords: alliance formation; corporate social irresponsibility; corporate social responsibility; high-tech firms; media coverage; moral character; proximity; signaling theory; spillover mechanism; trust mechanism
research_question: How do a potential partner's CSR and CSI affect the likelihood of alliance formation, and — given that positive and negative signals are conceptually distinct — which signaling mechanism (trust vs. spillover) dominates in each case?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls (firm-level summed across the dyad): firm size (log sales), ROA, Tobin's Q, R&D intensity, general alliance experience (5-year count), reputation (Fortune Most Admired). Dyad-level controls: partner-specific prior alliance experience, same industry (4-digit SIC), relative partner CSR, relative partner CSI, and year fixed effects.
- Theoretical mechanisms: (1) Trust mechanism — CSR/CSI signals a partner's moral character to the focal firm, shaping inferences about behavioral trustworthiness in the alliance (endogenous uncertainty, manageable via governance). (2) Spillover mechanism — CSR/CSI signals partner moral character to the focal firm's external stakeholders, who may extend their (positive or negative) evaluations to the focal firm via affiliation (exogenous uncertainty, beyond the firm's direct control).

The central argument is that signal valence determines the dominant mechanism: because firms have limited proactive influence over stakeholder spillovers (exogenous uncertainty) but considerable governance-based capacity to manage behavioral risk (endogenous uncertainty), and because negative signals are processed more intensively than positive ones, the trust mechanism dominates for CSR while the spillover mechanism dominates for CSI. This implies opposite moderation patterns: factors that reduce information asymmetry about partner trustworthiness (proximity, media coverage) should weaken the CSR-alliance link, while the same factors — by amplifying stakeholders' attention to partner misdeeds and the visibility of co-location — should strengthen the (negative) CSI-alliance link. The empirical pattern (negative CSR×proximity, CSR×media; negative CSI×proximity, CSI×media) is consistent with these dual mechanisms.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances signaling theory by providing a nuanced account of how positive and negative signals operate distinctly in strategic decisions: rather than negative signals being mere mirror images of positive ones, partner CSR and CSI work through different dominant mechanisms (trust for CSR, spillover for CSI). It contributes to the alliance literature by introducing partners' social activities as a novel and important antecedent of alliance formation and by linking specific signal valences to the two long-recognized partner-selection concerns — trustworthiness and spillover — thereby clarifying which type of concern each signal primarily addresses.
practical_implication: Managers evaluating potential alliance partners should treat CSR as a primary cue about a partner's likely trustworthiness inside the alliance, and CSI as a primary indicator of reputational spillover risk to the focal firm's stakeholders. Reliance on CSR signals is most useful when alternative information about partner behavior is scarce (e.g., low proximity, low media coverage), whereas vigilance about CSI is especially warranted when partners are co-located or highly visible in the media, because spillover is then amplified.
limitations: The empirical setting is restricted to U.S.-listed high-tech firms, which face heightened information asymmetry and somewhat lower CSI scores than firms in other industries; generalization to other contexts requires further work. The study treats CSR and CSI as separate distinct signals and does not theorize their interplay or asymmetric differences between partners' CSR/CSI levels. Because alliance formation requires mutual agreement and the dyadic partners are indistinguishable, the analysis is conducted at the dyad level and cannot distinguish initiator from receiver effects.
future_research: Scholars should test the framework in other industry contexts, examine the interplay between partner CSR and CSI rather than treating them only as independent signals, and use field or survey data that distinguishes alliance initiators from receivers to study how partner role asymmetries shape signal use. Further work could also extend the framework beyond alliance formation to other alliance outcomes such as governance-mode choice, alliance stability, and innovative or financial performance.
abstract: This study outlines two signaling mechanisms—trust and spillover—through which a potential partner's corporate social responsibility (CSR) and corporate social irresponsibility (CSI) affect alliance formation. Extending a key insight in signaling theory that positive and negative signals are conceptually distinct, we propose that which mechanism is dominant in explaining alliance formation varies between CSR and CSI. Specifically, we argue that the dominant signaling mechanism for CSR is the trust mechanism, through which CSR signals the moral character of a potential partner, which is used by the focal firm to infer the partner's trustworthiness. In contrast, CSI negatively affects alliance formation primarily through a spillover mechanism: CSI signals a potential partner's moral character to a firm's external stakeholders. Stakeholders' negative assessments based on this signal might then spill over to the focal firm if it forms an alliance with that partner. We further identify two contingency factors—namely, proximity and media coverage— that help verify the dominant signaling roles of partner CSR and CSI. Using a sample of alliances formed by high-tech firms between 1995 and 2016, we find support for our predictions.
apa_citation: Yu, Q., Cuypers, I. R. P., & Wang, H. (2025). A tale of two signals: Partner CSR versus CSI and alliance formation. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(1), 138-161. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0862
