title: Putting Distant Futures into Action: How Actors Sustain a Course of Action toward Distant-Future Goals through Path Enactment
methods: In-depth, real-time longitudinal qualitative case study at Arla Foods (2019-2022) combining process research and inductive theory building (Gioia method). Primary data: nonparticipatory observation of 31 online sustainable-packaging strategy meetings (37 hours of recordings, 675 transcribed pages); 16 semi-structured interviews with 10 informants; 4 feedback sessions with senior managers; archival documents (presentations, press releases, annual/CSR reports). Analysis proceeded through four stages: differentiating existing vs. imagined solutions, coding activities into bracketing/narrating/calculating mechanisms, temporal bracketing into three phases (path imagining, path integration, path stretching), and triangulating across phases.
industry: Dairy / sustainable packaging — sustainable packaging workstream within the world's fourth-largest dairy cooperative
country: Denmark (headquarters); operations across 153 countries; farmer-owners across multiple markets
time_period: 2019-2022, with primary real-time observation of strategy meetings concentrated in 2020
units: Sustainable packaging workstream (core team plus Business Development Team, Steering Committee, Sustainability Board, EMT) at Arla Foods
n_sample: 31 observed online strategy meetings (2020-2021); 16 semi-structured interviews with 10 informants; 4 feedback sessions; 9,000+ farmer-owners at the focal company
authors: Feuls, M.; Hernes, T.; Schultz, M.
theories: future-making theory; imagined futures (Beckert); narrative theory (Ricoeur); path concepts (path dependence, path creation, path patterning); performativity of calculation (Callon & Muniesa); process view of time; sensemaking and bracketing (Weick)
keywords: bracketing; calculating; climate change; distant-future goals; future making; grand challenges; imagination; narrating; net-zero; path enactment; realizability; sustainability strategy
research_question: How do actors develop a sustained course of action toward distant-future goals — that is, how can organizations balance imagination and realizability so that abstract distant futures (e.g., 2050 climate goals) are translated into ongoing strategic action through solutions that do not yet exist?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: distant-future goals; existing vs. imagined solutions; paths (multiple interacting paths through time); path enactment as ongoing collective activity.
- Three iterative phases: (1) Path imagining — actors generate solutions and connect them into tentative paths; (2) Path integration — emerging paths are bundled into configurations and aligned with organizational context; (3) Path stretching — selected path configurations are extended further toward the distant-future goal via intermediate steps.
- Three mechanisms operating differently in each phase: Bracketing (selecting and isolating solutions/paths to find common ground); Narrating (connecting disparate solutions, actors, materials, and technologies into meaningful wholes); Calculating (establishing distinctions and assessing relative realizability through numbers and qualitative comparisons).
- Triggering / contextual conditions: Sustainability Board, EMT, Steering Committee meetings, KPIs, brand strategy, and other workstreams' targets channel and constrain path enactment.
- Emergent outcomes: a sustained yet flexible sustainability strategy in which imagined solutions become inscribed as "placeholders" on paths stretched toward 2050 goals.

The process operates as "disciplined imagination": imagination expands the space of future paths during path imagining; path integration narrows that space by selecting realistic configurations aligned with organizational context; and path stretching combines imagination and discipline by extending paths through intermediate steps toward the distant goal. Bracketing forms a basis for narrating broader implications, which in turn enables calculating realizability — so that calculation is not merely numerical but a way of judging the plausibility of complex narratives of the future.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops the concept of path enactment to explain how actors sustain a course of action toward distant-future goals, contributing to research on future making by unpacking how the underexplored mechanisms of bracketing, narrating, and calculating dynamically combine across three phases to balance imagination and realizability. It shifts the field's focus from time horizons (short- vs. long-term) to solutions, showing how the same material solutions can recur in different constellations as actors move through time. It also distinguishes path enactment from prior path concepts (path dependence, path creation, path patterning) by emphasizing an effortful, directional, yet emergent and ongoing process toward the future.
practical_implication: Solution-driven path enactment offers organizations a methodology for integrating sustainability with corporate strategy by extending concerns toward distant-future goals via local experimentation and cross-unit learning. Practitioners pursuing 2050-style ambitions should treat imagined solutions as legitimate placeholders to be iteratively stretched, rather than dismissing them as unrealistic, and should design governance (e.g., Sustainability Boards, KPIs, intermediate milestones) that disciplines imagination without collapsing distant goals into short-term routines.
limitations: The model is grounded in a sequence of meetings dedicated to developing one sustainability strategy in a single organization (Arla Foods' sustainable packaging workstream), which constrains generalizability. The study focuses on a context where overall packaging emissions are relatively small compared to other workstreams, so the dynamics of path enactment under stronger emissions stakes remain to be tested. The authors note that power imbalances and dominant-actor interests can hinder path enactment but do not systematically theorize these conditions.
future_research: Future research adopting a path enactment perspective could examine how multiple paths diverge, converge, or change across organizations, industries, or intersecting grand challenges (climate change, inequality, poverty). Scholars should explore path enactment in different contexts and over longer horizons to test the model's robustness, and investigate how power imbalances and misalignment between corporate and sustainability strategies condition the stretching of paths toward distant futures.
abstract: One of the most significant challenges of pursuing distant-future goals is sustaining a course of action toward a future with solutions that do not yet exist. We address this challenge in a real-time study of a sustainability workstream's collective activities to develop a sustainability strategy toward a net-zero carbon future. We observed how the participants in the core team engaged in an iterative three-phase process that we call path enactment. Path enactment evolved from (a) imagining how solutions could connect into paths to (b) integrating emerging paths into configurations, and then (c) stretching these new path configurations toward the company's distant-future goals. We identified three mechanisms, bracketing, narrating, and calculating, which combined in different ways throughout the phases to enable path enactment. Based on our findings, we develop a process model to explain how these paths become increasingly stretched toward distant-future goals and embedded as a sustained course of action in a sustainability strategy. Our research significantly extends theories of organizational future making by combining imagination and realizability in our model, enriching our understanding of how organizations may put distant-future goals into action.
apa_citation: Feuls, M., Hernes, T., & Schultz, M. (2025). Putting distant futures into action: How actors sustain a course of action toward distant-future goals through path enactment. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(2), 297-325. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0257

title: CEO Political Partisanship and Corporate Misconduct
methods: Panel firm-year analysis of Fortune 500 firms 2010-2018; Poisson regressions of misconduct counts with one- and three-year lags, four-digit SIC industry and year fixed effects, and standard errors clustered by firm; CEO political ideology measured via the Chin et al. (2013) FEC-donations index, with partisanship operationalized as the squared deviation from the political center; difference-in-differences robustness using within-firm CEO turnovers (including exogenous deaths/illness); inverse-document-frequency text analysis of earnings-call Q&A speeches to probe perspective-taking and moral-foundations mechanisms.
industry: Cross-industry Fortune 500 firms (SIC industry fixed effects); regulatory enforcement actions span competition, consumer protection, employment, environmental, financial, government contracting, and health-and-safety violations from the Good Jobs First Violations Tracker
units: Firm-year observations (CEOs of Fortune 500 firms)
n_sample: 498 firms, 831 CEOs, 3,950 firm-year observations
authors: Fewer, T. J.; Tarakci, M.
theories: ideology-as-identity perspective; ideology-as-values perspective; moral foundations theory; rationalization (fraud triangle); social identity theory; upper echelons theory
keywords: CEO political partisanship; Fortune 500; corporate misconduct; ideology-as-identity; ideology-as-values; moral foundations; perspective taking; political ideology; rationalization; upper echelons
research_question: Are firms led by politically partisan CEOs (strongly liberal or strongly conservative) more likely to engage in corporate misconduct than firms led by politically nonpartisan (moderate) CEOs, and does the explanatory power lie in the identity dimension of political ideology rather than its values dimension?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the ideology-as-identity perspective, the authors argue that strong identification with a political group—not the content of liberal or conservative values—predisposes CEOs to rationalize corporate misconduct. Partisan CEOs (a) take the perspectives of out-group stakeholders less, narrowing concern for those harmed by wrongdoing, and (b) experience elevated moral foundations that grant them a sense of moral superiority, freeing them from a felt obligation to police firm conduct or framing transgressions as righteous retribution against "immoral" outsiders. The empirical analysis fails to detect a perspective-taking link to misconduct but supports the moral-foundations mechanism: partisan CEOs use more moral language in earnings calls, and elevated moral language is positively associated with misconduct.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances CEO-based theories of corporate misconduct by introducing political ideology as an antecedent and, more pointedly, by isolating the previously understudied identity dimension (partisanship) from the values dimension. It contributes to research on political ideology in organizations by showing that political identity—not liberal vs. conservative values—drives a non-political organizational outcome (regulatory wrongdoing), thereby integrating an "ideology-as-identity" lens into upper echelons research. It also contributes to the political-science/organization-studies nexus by demonstrating moral foundations as the cognitive bridge through which partisan identities translate into harmful firm behavior.
practical_implication: Boards, regulators, and stakeholders should treat the strength of a CEO's political identification—not just its left-right direction—as a risk factor for corporate misconduct, since strongly partisan CEOs of either ideology are more likely to preside over enforcement-triggering wrongdoing. Governance designs that increase ideological diversity on top management teams and boards, and monitoring practices that resist deference to a CEO's self-perceived moral authority, may attenuate the partisanship-misconduct link. As executives engage more publicly in political discourse and polarization deepens, attention to CEOs' partisan identities offers a timely warning signal for organizational risk.
limitations: The study cannot disentangle whether tracked-but-unobserved value differences predict difficult-to-prosecute forms of misconduct that fall outside the Violations Tracker's scope, and the violations-based proxy depends on detection. Political donations are an unobtrusive but indirect ideology proxy and may also reflect strategic favor-seeking from regulators. The findings come from a U.S. bipartisan Fortune 500 setting, limiting generalization to multiparty or authoritarian contexts and to non-elite organizations. Despite difference-in-differences and exogenous-turnover robustness checks, residual omitted-variable concerns remain.
future_research: Future work should test whether other deep-rooted belief systems (religious, populist) drive misconduct, and how political ideology interacts with pressure and opportunity (e.g., board ideology, stakeholder alignment) to shape misconduct. Researchers should pursue more direct ideology measures (e.g., social-media content analysis, factor-analytic separation of values and identity), dyadic and longer-run mechanism tests of perspective taking and moral foundations, and cross-national designs in multiparty or authoritarian regimes. Studies should also examine whether partisan CEOs alienate ideologically distant stakeholders and whether ideologically homogeneous TMTs and boards function as echo chambers that magnify the partisanship-misconduct effect.
abstract: Are firms led by liberal versus conservative CEOs more likely to engage in corporate misconduct? This study unearths the ideological bases of misconduct by distinguishing the values and identity perspectives of political ideology. Rather than attributing misconduct to liberal or conservative values, we introduce and examine CEO political partisanship—that is, the strength of a CEO's identification with political and ideological groups. We hypothesize and find robust evidence for a positive relationship between CEO political partisanship and corporate misconduct in a sample of Fortune 500 CEOs from 2010 to 2018. Our findings thus contribute to the conversation on the role of political ideology in organizations by unearthing the organizational implications of political identities. As CEOs increasingly engage in political discussions and political divisions grow stronger, our study offers a timely warning about the harmful link between CEO political partisanship and corporate misconduct.
apa_citation: Fewer, T. J., & Tarakci, M. (2025). CEO political partisanship and corporate misconduct. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(2), 357-379. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0909

title: The Fury Beneath the Morphing: A Theory of Defensive Organizing
methods: Longitudinal inductive ethnography over 42 months in a single professional services firm; data sources include 760 hours of nonparticipant observation across leadership team, project team, and oversight group meetings, 214 impromptu real-time interviews, 104 scheduled semi-structured interviews, and corporate documents; analysis combined inductive open/axial coding with a clinical (systems psychodynamic) interpretive approach to infer unconscious defense mechanisms underpinning successive cycles of organizing.
industry: Professional services firm (pseudonym 'Recco'), one of three top firms in its sector
country: G7 country (head office in the country's capital, with offices in its largest cities; specific country not disclosed at firm's request)
time_period: 42 months of fieldwork during a period spanning a corporate change effort and an industry-wide downturn that ended with the firm being divested and its top team replaced
units: Leadership Team (CEO, three division VPs — VPL&B, VPC&I, VPPS — CFO, CHRO, CMO), 23 senior managers participating in cross-divisional project teams, and a later 'Change Oversight Group' (COG)
n_sample: 760 observation hours across 760 meetings (385 leadership team, 231 project team, 144 COG); 214 impromptu and 104 scheduled interviews; 91 meeting minutes, 58 PowerPoint decks, 22 corporate communications, 40 project reports, 5 internal surveys; firm had ~1,600 employees
authors: Fitzsimons, D.; Petriglieri, J. L.; Petriglieri, G.
theories: leadership as social construction; psychodynamic theory of defense mechanisms; social defense theory; systems psychodynamics; theory of organizing (Weick)
keywords: defensive organizing; displacement; enactment; ethnography; idealization; leadership; overwhelming anxiety; projective identification; scapegoating; sensemaking; social defenses; systems psychodynamics
research_question: How does anxiety that leaders cannot consciously regulate shape organizing, and how does organizing affect their anxiety in turn?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: Overwhelming anxiety — the conjunction of shared performance anxiety among executives and the absence of any conscious sharing or processing of it — provoked by falling performance vis-a-vis competitors and the threat of being exposed as underperforming as a leader in relation to one's peers.
- Common defense mechanisms (per cycle, as unconscious organizing principles): (1) idealization, (2) projective identification, (3) displacement, (4) scapegoating.
- Sensemaking enactments per cycle: (1) "the legacy organization is responsible for performance issues" enacted through cautious investment in collaboration; (2) "a collaborative structure will solve performance issues" enacted through contentious investment in collaboration; (3) "collaboration is not working and it is someone else's fault" enacted through feigned collaboration; (4) "collaborative structures are responsible for performance issues" enacted through disinvestment from collaboration.
- Resulting social defenses (organized features per cycle): (1) the Leadership Team as a structure; (2) project management as a process; (3) reciprocal blame as a discourse / "substitute problem"; (4) scapegoating of the COG and ultimately the CEO.
- Constraints that compel transition to next cycle: isolation, embattlement, alienation, and finally collapse.
- Levels of analysis traversed: group → intergroup → organizational → back to group/individual.

The authors argue that overwhelming anxiety, which leaders cannot consciously acknowledge or work through, is unconsciously regulated by coopting the process of organizing into a series of cycles. In each cycle, a shared defense mechanism informs plausible-but-irrational sensemaking and motivates visible enactments that produce an organized feature serving as a social defense. Each social defense diffuses, deflects, or displaces anxiety while bolstering leaders' power, but creates a constraint that erodes its effectiveness, resurfacing anxiety and compelling the next cycle. The arc traces emergence, expansion, consolidation, and collapse of an evolving social defense — an "arc of defensive organizing."
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends Weick's theory of organizing by showing that when emotions cannot be consciously regulated, organizing can shade into a defensive process that defeats its own adaptive purpose, serving instead to mitigate anxiety and prop up established leaders. To social defense theory, the paper provides the first articulation of a social defense's full life cycle, from emergence through consolidation to collapse, and reframes social defenses as products of ongoing organizing rather than as fixed organizational features. It also reveals the political nature of organizing — power is granted to leaders by the very defensive arrangements they produce, even as those arrangements ultimately bring them down.
practical_implication: Leaders championing bold visions of change (collaboration, digital transformation, AI deployment) may unwittingly be staving off anxiety about their own incompetence rather than addressing real organizational problems, condemning the change effort to fail. The alternative is to cultivate "negative capability" — the capacity to recognize, contain, and work through anxiety as data — and to access "holding environments" that help leaders process distress collectively rather than enacting it as defensive organizing. Boards and stakeholders should be alert to mood shifts (e.g., from anxiety to enthusiasm) that occur without corresponding changes in performance circumstances.
limitations: The theory rests on a single 42-month ethnography of one professional services firm, raising standard concerns about generalizability beyond this setting. The interpretive approach precludes counterfactuals: an alternative reading is that Recco's executives were neurotically defensive in general and that their disposition contributed to (rather than was a consequence of) poor performance. The methods do not allow assessment of the mental health or baseline intrapsychic disturbance of the individuals involved.
future_research: Future studies should examine how emotions, especially anxiety, fundamentally shape organizational processes such as change, innovation, and crisis response — generating better meso-level theories linking individual experience to organized processes. Researchers should investigate what replaces social defenses once they collapse (a leadership change initiating a new defensive arc, or genuine adaptive awakening) and what factors hinder or facilitate post-defensive transitions. Psychometric work could probe baseline executive distress as a moderator of defensive organizing, and scholars should attend to mood shifts that occur without changes in circumstances as a signal that systems psychodynamic investigation is warranted.
abstract: Building on a longitudinal ethnography of a firm whose leaders attempted to organize collaboratively to remedy a drop in performance, this paper develops a theory of defensive organizing—a process that diffuses, deflects, and displaces overwhelming anxiety, and bolsters the power of established leaders. A systems psychodynamic approach helped us theorize how leaders' conscious intent to collaborate served as a cover for an unconscious effort to stave off anxiety that they could not share and process. We document the arc of defensive organizing across four cycles that begin between leaders and move across groups until the whole organization becomes mobilized to unconsciously mitigate anxiety. We theorize that a common defense mechanism informs sensemaking and motivates enactments in each cycle, coopting organizing to produce a social defense for psychological and social protection. Defensive organizing consolidates, we argue, when it can shield leaders and members of an organization from anxiety while letting them perform their roles. When it cannot, it collapses, taking the leaders with it. This study extends theory by showing how anxiety can make organizing appear sensible yet fail to be adaptive, and how social defenses evolve over time, keeping leaders' anxiety in check and anxious leaders in charge.
apa_citation: Fitzsimons, D., Petriglieri, J. L., & Petriglieri, G. (2025). The fury beneath the morphing: A theory of defensive organizing. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(2), 409-435. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0564

title: A Window into Coworkers' Worlds: The Relational Outcomes of Learning Vivid, Unintentional, and Nonwork-Related Information about Coworkers
methods: Multimethod design integrating a phenomenological exploration of archival/critical-incident text, two preregistered between-subjects experiments using video/audio Zoom-meeting confederate stimuli (Prolific and undergraduate samples), and two critical-incident surveys of working adults; tests use MANOVA, t-tests, confirmatory factor analysis, and bias-corrected bootstrap path analyses (Mplus) of indirect effects on multiplex relational investment.
industry: Cross-industry working adults; Study 1 confederate scenario set in remote/hybrid office work
units: Adult employees recruited via Prolific Academic, ROI Rocket, and an undergraduate panel; Study 2 reported on established coworker relationships of average 4.68 years
n_sample: Study 1 experiment n=1,244; Study 2 critical-incident survey n=377; supplementary Study A1 experiment n=413 undergraduates; Study A2 survey n=273; phenomenological exploration of 286 critical incidents, 7 interviews, and ~70,000+ archival comments
authors: Hardin, A. E.; Schinoff, B. S.; Byron, K.; Balven, R. M.
theories: availability heuristic; front stage/back stage (Goffman); media richness theory; social penetration theory; social presence theory
keywords: authenticity; coworker relationships; humanness; interpersonal learning; multiplex relational investment; nonwork-related information; perceived unintentionality; remote work; social penetration theory; trustworthiness; vividness
research_question: How do three dimensions of interpersonal learning about a coworker — vividness, perceived unintentionality, and nonwork relatedness — alter how employees perceive that coworker (as authentic, trustworthy, and human) and motivate them to invest in a multiplex (combined personal + professional) relationship with that coworker?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on social penetration theory, the authors argue that how learning happens (vividness, perceived unintentionality) and what is learned (nonwork-related content) jointly shape three relational social judgments. Vivid learning intensifies emotional resonance and approximates real-life human presence; perceived unintentional disclosure functions as an unfiltered backstage glimpse that signals reduced impression management and prompts reciprocal vulnerability; nonwork-related content individuates the coworker beyond their work role. Together these perceptions of greater authenticity, trustworthiness, and humanness motivate employees to broaden and deepen relational investment, sometimes producing a "fast-forward" leap in intimacy that violates social penetration theory's incremental, intentional-disclosure tenet.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends and partially upends social penetration theory by demonstrating that nonwork-related, perceived-unintentional, vivid disclosures can drive non-incremental "leaps" in coworker intimacy, in contrast to the theory's tenet of slow, intentional, escalating disclosure. It contributes to social cognition by showing that within-channel variation in how (vividly, unintentionally) and what (nonwork) employees learn matters more for relational judgments than between-channel media-richness differences. It also opens a new theoretical front on the physical work-from-home environment as a relational affordance, complementing prior office-based research on workspace and relationship development.
practical_implication: Managers should treat work-from-home as a potential relational asset, not only a productivity setting: video meetings from home can reveal authentic, humanizing nonwork glimpses that strengthen coworker investment. Employees should weigh the relational cost of curating blurred or false Zoom backgrounds, since hiding nonwork cues can preclude the vivid, perceived-unintentional learning that motivates coworkers to invest. The findings caution against orchestrated "authenticity" — the relational benefit hinges on the disclosure being seen as unintentional rather than as image management.
limitations: The studies measure only short-term intent or recent investment, leaving open how these effects persist or evolve as relationships repeat such disclosures over time. The authors capture only the perspective of the employee learning about the coworker, not the coworker who is learned about, limiting insight into the dyadic and potentially asymmetric consequences of this learning. The phenomenological exploration also yielded relatively few negatively valenced incidents, suggesting unobserved boundary conditions around when negative unintentional disclosures help versus harm relationships.
future_research: Future work should examine longer-term affective and behavioral consequences of these learning dimensions (e.g., avoidance, ambivalence, ongoing engagement); explore additional learning dimensions such as the source of information (direct observation vs. gossip), the timing/location of learning, and audience size; and investigate contextual antecedents like organizational culture, open office plans, and individual differences (openness, extraversion). Researchers should also adopt dyadic designs (live video recordings, paired interviews) to capture how the person being learned about experiences such disclosures.
abstract: Research suggests that changes in where and how employees work may make it difficult to build meaningful coworker relationships. These same changes, however, may provide a window into their coworkers' nonwork lives—offering vivid and seemingly unintended insights into how their coworkers parent, what their hobbies are, and what their homes look like. In this way, the changing nature of work provides a potential paradox: it can simultaneously increase social distance between coworkers while potentially decreasing that distance by providing a vivid and unfiltered glimpse into their nonwork lives. Extending social penetration theory, we use a multimethod approach including experimental studies and surveys to examine how three dimensions of coworker learning—learning that is (1) more vivid, (2) perceived as unintentional, and (3) about a coworker's nonwork life—lead an employee to view that coworker as more authentic, trustworthy, and human. Moreover, seeing a coworker in these ways increases an employee's actual and intended investment in their personal and professional relationship with that coworker. Our findings contribute to the literatures on work relationships, social cognition, and the physical work environment.
apa_citation: Hardin, A. E., Schinoff, B. S., Byron, K., & Balven, R. M. (2025). A window into coworkers' worlds: The relational outcomes of learning vivid, unintentional, and nonwork-related information about coworkers. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(2), 267-296. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0683

title: Chasing Storms: Temporal Work to Foster Group Engagement under Uncertainty
methods: Ethnographic field study (~1,400 hours) of three U.S. storm chasing teams across five trips, supplemented by 42 semi-structured/informational interviews and archival data; grounded, processual analysis with open coding, vignette construction, and member checks.
industry: Storm chasing teams (recreational, educational, and commercial tour) in North America's Great Plains
units: Storm chasing teams (College Team, Private Team, Commercial Team) plus interview participants
n_sample: Three focal teams; 22 semi-structured interviews and 20 informational interviews (42 interviewees)
authors: Kent, D.; Granqvist, N.
theories: group engagement theory; interaction ritual theory; organizational temporality; temporal work
keywords: ethnography; experienced duration; group engagement; storm chasing; temporal uncertainty; temporal work
research_question: How do groups foster engagement across the idle and busy periods in temporally uncertain work?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: engagement inertia (inertia of expectations vs. inertia of immersion); experienced duration as subjective and modifiable; temporal uncertainty as boundary condition
- Process / phases: idle periods → idle-to-encounter transitions → encounter (busy) periods → encounter-to-idle transitions → return to idle periods
- Practices (forms of temporal work): broadening focus (constructing multiple possible futures; restraining immersion in specific aspects of the future); compressing experienced duration (temporal autonomy, detours/games/socializing, filler tasks); narrowing focus on the present encounter (running commentary, redundant tasks, dismissing forgone targets); extending duration (bringing near future into present; bringing past into present)
- Triggering conditions: unpredictable transitions between idle and busy periods that disrupt the durations needed for mutual immersion
- Emergent outcomes: an experience of temporal control (enabling willing engagement at encounter onset) and an experience of immersive duration (enabling willing disengagement at encounter end)
- Boundary conditions: extreme temporal uncertainty in self-governing teams whose members have stake in core tasks

Across idle and busy periods, groups manage members' shared expectations and experience of duration to foster immersive episodes of engagement. In idle periods, broadening focus and compressing duration produce a sense of temporal control that prevents inertia of expectations; in encounters, narrowing focus and extending duration produce immersive duration that prevents inertia of immersion. The two interdependent transitions are managed as a balancing act between opposing forms of temporal work tailored to upcoming events and members' anticipated reactions.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances a temporally founded view of group engagement, showing that engagement and disengagement are transition processes shaped by the temporal context in which groups encounter their work, not merely by task design or recovery intervals. It also develops a durational perspective for organizational temporality research, recasting duration as a subjective and modifiable experience that members extend or compress through temporal work, and highlighting risks of letting members invest in precise expectations under uncertainty.
practical_implication: Leaders and members in temporally uncertain settings (e.g., emergency response, broadcast journalism, professional services) can foster performance and satisfaction by deliberately broadening expectations, compressing the experience of waiting, narrowing focus during encounters, and extending immersive duration through preparatory work, dramatized transitions, and debriefing rituals.
limitations: The study examines small-group interactions in a setting of extreme temporal uncertainty, so the scale and visibility of temporal work observed may exceed what occurs in less uncertain or more routinized settings. The empirical context (storm chasing) blends serious leisure and work, and findings may require adaptation in settings with more institutionalized durational norms or interorganizational contexts.
future_research: Future work could examine how temporal work manifests in settings with more predictable or routinized transitions, how interdepartmental or interorganizational settings shape durational experiences, how "temporal macro-structures" in large-scale projects influence experienced duration, and how individuals develop "temporal competence" to feel in control and immerse themselves in work despite incomplete control over events.
abstract: Experiences of group engagement have a significant impact on members' performance and satisfaction with work. In temporally uncertain settings, however, unpredictable transitions between idle and busy periods disrupt engagement. Through an ethnographic study of storm chasing teams in Tornado Alley, we investigate how groups foster engagement under uncertainty. We found that while efficient coordination helped teams get to storms, it could frustrate members' engagement with tasks across unpredictable transitions. We develop a process model explaining how groups use temporal work to foster immersive episodes of engagement across transitions that are hard to control or predict. We show that broadening focus and compressing duration foster experiences of temporal control that enable engaging transitions into busy periods of work, whereas narrowing focus and extending duration foster experiences of immersive duration that enable satisfying transitions into idle periods. We develop a temporal perspective to engagement at work, showing how groups set the temporal context within which they encounter work episodes and how this context enables or constrains engagement. We also advance a durational perspective for organizational temporality research, elucidating how experiences of duration represent a key issue to be managed in organizations.
apa_citation: Kent, D., & Granqvist, N. (2025). Chasing storms: Temporal work to foster group engagement under uncertainty. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(2), 380-408. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1316

title: The Psychological Design of Firm Boundaries: Preempting Escalating Commitment through Buy vs. Make
methods: Empirical-mixed design combining (i) OLS and logit regressions on product-level archival data with year-by-season, product-category, brand, product-category-by-brand, and contract-manufacturer fixed effects and two-way clustered standard errors; (ii) two years of qualitative fieldwork (interviews with designers, sourcing teams, and merchandisers); and (iii) a comparative case analysis of fast-fashion brand Zara to test boundary conditions.
industry: High-end fashion (apparel) brands; supplementary case of Zara/Inditex (fast fashion)
country: Multinational holding company; the dominant majority of identified contract manufacturers are located in a single high-cost country (country not disclosed)
time_period: Multi-season product-level archival panel collected during an internal due-diligence process; supplemented by approximately two years of fieldwork (specific calendar dates not reported)
units: Individual products (designs) within 21 fashion brands owned by 'Life Holding Co.,' a diversified multinational financial holding company
n_sample: 249,705 products across 21 brands; 49,646 products with cost data; 40,129 products for the brand with manufacturer identifiers
authors: Keum, D. D.
theories: behavioral theory of escalating commitment; bounded rationality; psychological design of organizations / choice architecture; psychological ownership theory; transaction cost theory
keywords: buy vs. make decisions; cognitive bias; contract manufacturing; escalating commitment; fashion industry; firm boundaries; internal transaction cost; psychological design of organizations; psychological ownership; transaction cost theory
research_question: How do boundedly rational managers use buy vs. make decisions ex ante to weaken psychological and organizational ownership over a product, thereby preempting escalating commitment and lowering the internal transaction cost of timely product termination?
mechanism_summary: Managers anticipate that internal production cultivates strong psychological attachment (control, intimate knowledge, invested time), which fuels endowment-, IKEA-, and sunk-cost-style biases that delay termination of failing products. Because escalating commitment resists ex post debiasing, managers proactively design firm boundaries to create psychological and organizational distance: outsourcing risky and core products via contract manufacturing "blackboxes" development, mobilizes fewer internal stakeholders, and makes failure smaller and more reversible. Firms accept 9–16% higher production costs on trendy outsourced products as the price of preempting escalating commitment.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops a cognitive foundation for transaction cost theory that does not rely on opportunism or asset specificity, recasting buy not as a last-resort response to external market failure but as a debiasing intervention against internal hierarchical failure. By framing buy vs. make as part of the "psychological design of organizations," it bridges TCT, behavioral economics on escalating commitment, and choice architecture, and adds termination cost — alongside production and coordination costs — as a constitutive concern in the theory of the firm.
practical_implication: Managers of innovation-intensive firms should resist the default that core or strategically important products must be produced in-house: when failure risk is high, contract manufacturing — even at meaningfully higher unit cost — can pay for itself by enabling timely exit and preserving capacity for future experimentation. Sourcing decisions should be evaluated jointly on generation benefits and termination costs, with explicit attention to managers' own psychological vulnerabilities.
limitations: The study does not claim causal inference and cannot rule out unobserved product attributes correlated with failure risk. The factoryless, concurrent-sourcing setting of high-end fashion enhances internal validity but limits generalizability to asset-heavy or capability-intensive industries. Psychological ownership is theorized rather than directly measured, and identifying information on contract manufacturers is available for only a subset of brands.
future_research: Future work should employ field and laboratory experiments to manipulate psychological attachment directly and to observe underlying psychological constructs that archival data only proxy. Researchers should examine how managers' own characteristics (regulatory focus, risk preference, overconfidence) interact with cognitive biases to shape buy vs. make choices, and extend the framework to other biases (envy, home bias, homophily, 1/N allocation) and to hybrid governance forms such as alliances and joint ventures across asset-heavy industries.
abstract: Strong ownership of the product development process, while facilitating the generation of new products, can exacerbate escalating commitment and impede timely termination. We examine how managers, with foresight, use buy vs. make decisions to create psychological and organizational distance from a product and preempt escalating commitment. Using detailed product-level data from the fashion industry and its unique features, we show that managers contract manufacture risky products even when they can be readily manufactured in-house, especially core products that are central to a firm's identity and hence more prone to escalating commitment. Our findings highlight cognitive bias as a source of internal transaction cost and bring its preemption to the forefront of buy vs. make decisions. Our study develops a cognitive foundation for transaction cost theory that does not depend on opportunism or asset specificity and highlights the psychological designing of firm boundaries as a strategy for improving the decision environment.
apa_citation: Keum, D. D. (2025). The psychological design of firm boundaries: Preempting escalating commitment through buy vs. make. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(2), 436–463. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0730

title: Sensing the Room: The Role of Atmosphere in Collective Sensemaking
methods: Inductive qualitative micro-ethnographic case study of a single strategy program ('Longreach') at an Australian electronic bank ('E-Bank' pseudonym); real-time process data over 18 months from June 2016 to December 2017 across 12 strategy-making workshops (26 hours of video recordings) plus 26 audio-recorded operational meetings (14 hours), 15 design meetings (10 hours), 8 CEO interviews, 24 serial interviews with 3 E-Bank managers, 16 serial interviews with 2 consultants, 244 prototype/Trello screenshots, 156 photos, and 18 handouts; analyzed in four stages combining open coding of dominant atmospheres, thick second-by-second descriptions of bodily tonality and interaction, identification of sensemaking styles and language use, and recoding for 'dissonant moments' that shifted atmospheres.
industry: Retail/digital banking; strategy-making team developing a new AI-informed savings-spending app
units: One strategic project team (Longreach) of 3 E-Bank managers and 2 consultants, plus the CEO; observed across strategy-making workshops, operational meetings, and design meetings
n_sample: 12 video-recorded strategy-making workshops; 41 additional operational/design meetings; 56 interviews; 1 focal team in 1 organization
authors: Knight, E.; Lok, J.; Jarzabkowski, P.; Wenzel, M.
theories: atmosphere (Bohme; Slaby); cultural affect theory; immanent sensemaking (Heideggerian phenomenology; Sandberg & Tsoukas); practice theory / strategy-as-practice (SAP); relational affect (Slaby)
keywords: atmosphere; bodily tonality; collective sensemaking; dissonant moments; embodied affect; immanent sensemaking; interaction; practice world; relational affect; sensemaking styles; strategy-making workshops
research_question: How is atmosphere implicated in processes of collective (immanent) sensemaking, and to what effect(s)? Specifically, how do bodily tonality and interaction in strategy-making workshops coproduce atmospheres that enable and constrain collective sensemaking styles, and how do these atmospheres shift over time?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: atmosphere (a "possibility space" of affective intensity in a situational encounter); bodily tonality (collective mood-component: facial, vocal, postural cues); interaction (active inter-corporeal dimension: bodily, verbal, gestural patterns between participants); sensemaking style (combination of tonality–interaction entanglement, language use, and interaction patterns).
- Four dominant atmospheres / sensemaking styles: (1) relaxed–calm, respectful–affirmative atmosphere with a "consolidating" sensemaking style (clarifying interpretations through affirmative factual language); (2) pensive–serious, tentative–curious atmosphere with a "considering" style (searching tentatively through explorative language); (3) tense–sharp, argumentative–dismissive atmosphere with a "clashing" style (entrenching interpretations through oppositional language); (4) excited–buoyant, enthusiastic–affirmative atmosphere with a "creating" style (building cumulatively through "yes, and" language).
- Triggering / shifting conditions: "dissonant moments" — brief instances in which one or more participants' facial expressions, body positions, vocal characteristics, or speech content diverge from the dominant atmosphere; these can either accumulate to a tipping point that shifts atmosphere and sensemaking style, or subside while the prevailing style is maintained.
- Practice-world arrangements: the physical workspace (lounge-like layout, artifacts on walls), norms of professionalism, and teleoaffectivity (CEO-imposed deadline) channel and modulate atmospheric dynamics.
- Emergent outcomes: the same issue is interpreted differently across atmospheres; shared understandings, tentative solutions, forced binary choices, or generative possibilities emerge depending on the dominant style.

Immanent sensing of collective bodily tonality and interaction operates as an ongoing affective background that "solidifies" a sensemaking style and makes certain interpretations of a strategic issue more likely than others. Rather than affect being a discrete trigger or response, it is a continuously coproduced "possibility space" in which deliberate sensemaking unfolds. Style shifts only when accumulated dissonant moments cross an atmospheric tipping point, after which the entanglement of tonality and interaction reorganizes around a new dominant atmosphere and a new way of framing the issue.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces atmosphere as a "possibility space" for collective sensemaking, conceptualizing it as the dynamic entanglement of bodily tonality and interaction that affords distinctive sensemaking styles shaping not only how but also what sense is made. This extends emotions research in sensemaking beyond individually felt corporeal experience and beyond the dualistic positive/negative-valence link to generative/integrative orientations, offering a more nuanced affective phenomenology of collective sensemaking. It contributes to strategy-as-practice by identifying "bodies-in-use" as a previously unrecognized strategy "tool of and for feeling," and extends atmosphere research by drawing attention to the transient affective qualities of materiality and the role of normativity and teleoaffectivity in coproducing atmosphere.
practical_implication: Strategy-makers and meeting facilitators should attend not only to spatial design but to the moment-to-moment entanglement of bodily tonality and interaction — "sensing the room" — because the resulting atmosphere shapes which interpretations of a strategic issue become thinkable. Monitoring for dissonant moments (subtle shifts in posture, vocal inflection, facial expression, or speech content) offers a leading indicator of impending atmospheric — and therefore sensemaking — shifts. Leaders cannot fully "engineer" productive atmospheres through aesthetic design alone, but can cultivate practice-world norms (professionalism, teleoaffective commitment) that channel atmospheric dynamics toward more generative sensemaking styles.
limitations: The study relies primarily on video data, which cannot capture all dimensions of affect that escape visible bodily display, and the first-author researcher's first-hand affective impressions are necessarily partial given limited time in the field compared to fully participatory ethnographies. The single-team, single-organization design of one Australian electronic bank limits generalizability and leaves open how atmospheric dynamics differ across industries, cultures, or non-strategy practice worlds. Authority and power dynamics that may shape who disproportionately influences collective tonality and interaction were not the focus of analysis.
future_research: Future work should systematically explore the longitudinal effects of atmospheric dynamics on individuals and groups, including whether and how atmospheres become characteristic of a group over time and shift with the nature of issues a team encounters. Phenomenological-ethnographic methods that incorporate the researcher's own bodily experience could complement video-based approaches. Promising avenues include the relationship between atmosphere and authority (whether some actors disproportionately "set the tone" through affective tonality), and deeper exploration of how the normative and teleoaffective dimensions of practice worlds — beyond materiality — shape atmospheric dynamics.
abstract: Drawing on video recordings, interviews, and first-hand observations of a strategic project team at an electronic bank, we explore the role of atmosphere in collective sensemaking. By analyzing collective entanglements of bodily tonality and interaction, we show how distinctive atmospheres in strategy-making workshops are closely associated with different sensemaking styles that shape not only how but also what sense is constructed. Our findings show that in group settings, participants' immanent sensing of atmospheric dynamics serves as an affective and affecting background that enables and constrains collective sensemaking in relation to an issue. We also find that atmosphere can shift as dissonant moments accumulate and reach an atmospheric tipping point that enables a change in the group's sensemaking style. These dissonant moments manifest as subtle changes in one or more participants' bodily orientation, speech content, or vocal characteristics. The concept of atmosphere enables us to extend emotions research in sensemaking and strategic management by offering a better understanding of the role of embodied affect in collective sensemaking beyond individually felt corporeal experience.
apa_citation: Knight, E., Lok, J., Jarzabkowski, P., & Wenzel, M. (2025). Sensing the room: The role of atmosphere in collective sensemaking. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(2), 326-356. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1389

title: Making a Theoretical Contribution with Qualitative Research
methods: Editorial essay; the AMJ editor team organized a 'freewheeling conversation' identifying common mistakes qualitative authors make when claiming theoretical contributions, then synthesized six pathways illustrated with prior AMJ exemplars and the authors' own studies.
industry: na
units: na
n_sample: na
authors: Rouse, E.; Reinecke, J.; Ravasi, D.; Langley, A.; Grimes, M.; Gruber, M.
theories: abductive reasoning; critical realism; inference-to-best-explanation; process organization studies; theory of theoretical contribution
keywords: abduction; artificial intelligence; induction; process models; qualitative research; retroduction; rhetoric; tensions; theoretical contribution; theorizing
research_question: How can qualitative researchers overcome the challenges of articulating a crisp, generalizable theoretical contribution — rather than just illuminating a fascinating context or surfacing empirical patterns — when submitting to AMJ?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: A theoretical contribution requires articulating new theoretical knowledge positioned in relation to existing theory; qualitative studies often fall short by privileging context, patterns, or visualization over explanation.
- Six pathways:
  - Leverage the Unusual — beyond fascinating settings: ensure the research question is driven by a theoretical puzzle and that constructs travel beyond the specific context.
  - Leverage Inference — beyond patterns: move from describing patterns to explaining mechanisms via induction, abduction, or retroduction (the latter postulating deeper structures that generate observed patterns).
  - Leverage Tensions — beyond opening the black box: surface contradictions, dialectics, paradoxes, or tensions that drive organizational dynamics, potentially combining process and variance perspectives.
  - Leverage Visualizations — beyond boxes and arrows: explicitly theorize the "why" behind arrows, label relationships, and embrace nonlinearity rather than forcing data into neat process diagrams.
  - Leverage Language — beyond hyperbole: substantiate contribution claims through fair, comprehensive engagement with relevant literature; avoid superlatives, straw-man framings, and "old wine in new bottles."
  - Leverage the Moment — beyond emulating past work: adapt, extend, synthesize, or innovate prior theory for transformational contemporary contexts (AI, new data sources), using AI tools as "co-theorists" rather than substitutes for human interpretation.

The editorial frames theorizing as an iterative, ongoing accomplishment in qualitative research, where contribution emerges from coupling rich empirical stories with theoretical stories that generalize beyond the studied setting through inference, attention to underlying tensions, and rigorous rhetorical craft.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial provides a meta-level typology of six pathways through which qualitative researchers can transcend common pitfalls (descriptive context, unexplained patterns, banal process maps, hyperbolic rhetoric, derivative theorizing) to make compelling theoretical contributions. It elevates retroduction alongside induction and abduction as a distinct inference mode, and reframes the process-versus-variance dichotomy as a productive combination through attention to tensions.
practical_implication: The editors offer concrete guidance for qualitative authors submitting to AMJ: drive research questions by theoretical puzzles rather than setting uniqueness; explicitly explain "why" behind observed patterns; label and code the arrows in process models; engage seriously with the most threatening adjacent literatures; and harness generative AI as a co-theorist for pattern detection and theoretical brainstorming while preserving human-led interpretation.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editorial invites qualitative scholars to push theorizing further by reinterpreting, adapting, and synthesizing classical theories for transformational research contexts; expanding visualization toolkits beyond boxes and arrows (e.g., systems-dynamics maps, thick narrative description); and exploring how AI tools can responsibly augment qualitative theorizing without substituting for situated, sociocultural human interpretation.
abstract: FROM THE EDITORS MAKING A THEORETICAL CONTRIBUTION WITH QUALITATIVE RESEARCH

title: Reengaging with the Classics
methods: Editorial essay; the authors surveyed AMJ editors and editorial board members to construct a working definition of a 'management classic,' then illustrate three approaches (revise, repurpose, rediscover) with exemplar AMJ papers and apply them to contemporary domains (AI, large digital tech, geopolitical leadership).
industry: na
units: na
n_sample: na
authors: Bermiss, Y. S.; Farh, C. I. C.; Simons, T.; ter Wal, A. L. J.; von Krogh, G.; Gruber, M.
theories: March and Simon turnover framework; job design theory; social exchange theory; structural contingency theory; transformational leadership theory
keywords: doctoral education; management classics; rediscover; repurpose; revise; theory development; transformational times
research_question: How can scholars effectively draw on management classics to inform developments in the world of today, particularly amid transformational phenomena such as AI, digital platform consolidation, and geopolitical polarization?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Management classics — widely influential works that have shaped theory and practice — remain valuable but are often subject to "institutional forgetting," typified citation, and selective attention; substantive reengagement (rather than ceremonial citation) generates new theoretical and practical insights for transformational times.
- Three pathways for reengagement:
  - Revise: challenge taken-for-granted assumptions and propose substantive changes (e.g., Sine, Mitsuhashi & Kirsch 2006 revising Burns & Stalker 1961 by showing new ventures benefit from mechanistic, not organic, structures).
  - Repurpose: leverage insights from a classic to resolve a problem in an adjacent theory (e.g., Colquitt & Rodell 2011 repurposing Blau's 1964 social exchange theory to reconceive justice-trust relations).
  - Rediscover: identify previously overlooked theoretical claims in a classic and bring them back into focus (e.g., Trevor 2001 rediscovering untested ideas in March & Simon 1958 about individual-level determinants of voluntary turnover).
- Application domains: AI era (job design theory, bounded rationality), large digital tech (Chandler's Strategy and Structure), and political polarization (Burns's transformational leadership).
- Boundary condition / caution: reengagement requires "vigilant reading" that situates classic theory in its political, social, and economic context rather than blindly idealizing or transposing it.

The editorial argues that scholarly progress is cumulative and path-dependent, which causes classic works to be selectively read and "typified" in citation practices; deliberate revising, repurposing, and rediscovering counteract this drift and let foundational theories address new phenomena while clarifying scope conditions.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial offers a typology — revise, repurpose, rediscover — for substantive engagement with management classics, distinguishing it from normative or ceremonial citation. It clarifies how each pathway produces a distinct kind of theoretical contribution (new boundary conditions, novel cross-domain application, recovery of latent claims) and links these pathways to contemporary phenomena (AI, large tech firms, geopolitical leadership) where classics regain explanatory power.
practical_implication: The editors call on scholars, doctoral advisors, and journal reviewers to move beyond ceremonial citation of classics — introducing PhD students to foundational texts, encouraging deep engagement, and proactively suggesting opportunities to reengage with classics in reviews — so that contemporary research on AI, digital platforms, and geopolitical disruption can build on, rather than reinvent, prior insights.
limitations: The authors note that the three approaches they identify are "merely indicative, and not exhaustive"; surely additional ways to reengage with the classics exist beyond the typology offered.
future_research: The editorial invites scholars to reengage with classics across three illustrative domains: (a) the AI era, by extending bounded rationality, job design, and team-effectiveness classics to AI-augmented work; (b) large digital technology firms, by revisiting Chandler's strategy-structure thesis; and (c) leadership amid political polarization and geopolitical tension, by reexamining Burns's transformational leadership in light of values conflict and instability.
abstract: Classics in management offer a wealth of ideas and knowledge—arguably, wealth that is underutilized.
apa_citation: Bermiss, Y. S., Farh, C. I. C., Simons, T., ter Wal, A. L. J., von Krogh, G., & Gruber, M. (2025). Reengaging with the classics. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(3), 465-474. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2025.4003

title: Crossing Technological Boundaries: Brokerage and the Emergence of Innovation Networks
methods: Mixed-methods design, dominantly quantitative. Quantitative core: longitudinal stochastic actor-oriented models (SAOMs) estimated in RSiena on a multivariate two-network panel (mAbs and small-molecule R&D alliance networks) of 453 cancer-therapy firms over 2004-2020 (17 yearly waves), modeling tie formation as the dependent variable with directed ties from client to R&D firm; tests of within-network uniplex closure (GWDSP), cross-network multiplex closure (WW->X), and a 'jumpWWClosure' term capturing mAbs-expertise asymmetric boundary-spanning closure; goodness-of-fit via sienaGOF (Mahalanobis distance) on indegree, outdegree, and triad-census distributions; rare-events logistic regression (King & Zeng 2001) on a 1.18M dyad-year panel as robustness; sienaCompositionChange to handle entry/exit; controls drawn from BioSciDB, USPTO patent records (CPC codes), FDA approvals, geocoded headquarters distance. Qualitative supplement: 14 in-depth interviews with scientists, big-pharma managers, biotech start-ups, regulators, and industry experts (9 informing context/hypotheses; 5 post-results sensemaking).
industry: Biopharmaceutical / cancer therapy R&D alliances; specifically incumbent small-molecule (chemistry-based) and emergent monoclonal-antibody (mAbs) targeted-therapy technologies, with related modalities such as gene therapy
time_period: 2004-2020 (17-year network observation window); 14 interviews with industry experts conducted around the study
units: Interfirm directed R&D alliance ties (client to R&D firm) constituting two co-evolving technology networks (mAbs emergent and small-molecule incumbent)
n_sample: 453 firms; alliance counts grow from 45 mAbs/50 small-molecule ties in 2004 to peaks of 131 mAbs (2016) and 106 small-molecule (2015); 1.18 million dyad-year observations in robustness logistic regression; 14 expert interviews
authors: Brändle, L.; Berger, E. S. C.; Howard, M. D.
theories: Gould and Fernandez brokerage roles; knowledge brokering; structural holes / brokerage theory (Burt); tertius gaudens (Simmel); tertius iungens (Obstfeld); triadic closure / network dynamics theory
keywords: R&D alliances; brokerage; emergent technology; incumbent technology; innovation networks; monoclonal antibodies; multiplex technology closure; small molecules; stochastic actor-oriented models; tertius gaudens; tertius iungens; triadic closure
research_question: Under what circumstances do brokers within and across technology networks facilitate or inhibit collaboration between their unconnected partners?
mechanism_summary: The mechanism rests on the tertius iungens vs. tertius gaudens tension. In uniplex emergent-technology triads, brokers' competitive incentives are weak (all parties already operate in the same technology network), so the dominant logic is uncertainty mitigation: brokers introduce their unconnected emergent-technology partners to one another, fostering triadic closure (H1 supported, b=0.153, p<.05). In multiplex triads — where the broker mediates incumbent-technology ties between two firms that may form an emergent-technology tie — brokers in symmetrically informed dyads still favor closure because cross-technology referrals reduce relational and technological uncertainty (H2 supported, b=2.209, p<.05). However, when one alter has unique mAbs expertise and the other does not, the broker occupies a competitively attractive boundary-spanning position; the tertius gaudens logic dominates and brokers actively block the closure that would expose their information advantage (H3 supported, b=-126.659, p<.001). Findings replicate in rare-events logistic regression on 1.18M dyad-years.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces multiplex technology closure — transitive collaborations spanning incumbent and emergent technology networks — as a new triadic concept that resolves the tertius iungens vs. tertius gaudens tension by jointly considering tie multiplexity and nodal expertise asymmetry. It contributes to (1) collaborative innovation theory by showing that the incumbent technology network exerts a paramount influence on the structure of emergent technology networks; (2) network dynamics research by modeling cross-network influences with simultaneously dependent networks; and (3) brokerage and triadic-closure theory by extending Shipilov and Li's (2012) multiplexity framework to triadic structures defined by technology focus and by demonstrating that brokers actively prevent closure to defend unique access to emergent-technology expertise.
practical_implication: For pharmaceutical and biotech managers, the findings imply that established small-molecule incumbents can leverage existing collaborator triads to access emergent biological technologies such as mAbs, but should anticipate that intermediaries with unique mAbs expertise are unlikely to broker direct introductions. New entrants holding scarce emergent-technology expertise face strategic choices about whether to retain their boundary-spanning rents or grant access. For alliance designers and innovation policymakers, multiplex collaborative architectures across technologies are a central lever for accelerating cross-boundary innovation in domains where competing technologies coexist (e.g., antibody-drug conjugates).
limitations: First, the empirical focus on the joint evolution of two networks (mAbs and small molecules) rather than full coevolution constrains inferences about two-way feedback. Second, generalizability beyond cancer therapy is uncertain because technological trajectories differ across industries; the findings may not transfer to settings with strong dominant designs or to breakthroughs (e.g., CRISPR-Cas) that establish a single dominant technology. Third, the study draws network boundaries based on the technological focus of relationships, leaving open the question of how a technology-community approach defined through relational distance would yield different patterns.
future_research: The authors call for studies that (1) model true coevolution of multiple networks with two-way interdependencies, (2) examine how broker dynamics change when emergent technologies become new dominant standards (e.g., CRISPR-Cas), (3) investigate cyclical phases of technology emergence and their effects on cross-network broker behavior, and (4) explore multiplex technology closure within and across innovation communities defined by relational distance, asking how broker separation strategies play out across community boundaries.
abstract: Firms face elevated levels of uncertainty in collaborations focused on new technologies. While third parties may foster such collaborations by reducing technological and relational uncertainties (i.e., tertius iungens), they might have competitive incentives to prevent such connections (i.e., tertius gaudens). Building on this theoretical tension, this study investigates how brokerage and incumbent technology network structure shape emergent technology networks. We argue that organizations in the position of knowledge brokers tend to facilitate tie formation between partners to help mitigate uncertainty in an emergent technology, yet this tendency is reversed when keeping the separation between partners allows them to secure competitively advantageous positions. We collect longitudinal data on research and development collaborations in the cancer therapy industry, and conduct in-depth interviews with scientists and industry experts. We test our theoretical framework through stochastic actor-oriented modeling by examining the emergence of technology networks after the technological breakthrough of monoclonal antibodies for cancer therapy. Our findings indicate that over time, the role of brokers in fostering or preventing triadic closure across technologies significantly impacts the structure of emergent technology networks.
apa_citation: Brändle, L., Berger, E. S. C., & Howard, M. D. (2025). Crossing technological boundaries: Brokerage and the emergence of innovation networks. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(3), 620-647. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0439

title: The Boundaries of Modern Slavery: The Role of Exemplars in New Category Formation
methods: Qualitative case study with discourse analysis (Fairclough's three-dimensional approach) of consultation submissions, 1,240 UK media articles (2010-2016), 13 expert key informant interviews, parliamentary debates, and bill drafts surrounding the UK Modern Slavery Act 2015.
industry: Multi-sector legal/policy formation around modern slavery (encompassing organized crime, sex work, agriculture, construction, domestic work, fishing, factory labor)
units: Linguistic exemplars in textual data (consultation submissions, media articles, parliamentary debates, bill iterations, key informant interviews)
n_sample: 1,240 UK media articles, 21 oral and 102 written consultation submissions, 40 Public Bill Committee submissions, 19 parliamentary debate transcripts, 13 expert interviews
authors: Caruana, R.; Crane, A.; Ingram, C.
theories: boundary work; category formation theory; exemplar model of categorization
keywords: boundary work; category formation; discourse analysis; exemplars; grand challenges; modern slavery
research_question: How and why do organizational actors deploy category exemplars in discourse surrounding new categories to establish the social and symbolic boundaries of an incipient category, in the absence of an established prototype, as illustrated by the formation of the legal category of modern slavery?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: typifying, familiarizing, and scoping exemplars; symbolic vs. social boundaries of categories
- Triggering conditions: introduction of a new legal category lacking a prototype; competing actor goals (government, NGOs, business, media)
- Process / phases: pre-initiation (2010-2012), initiation (2013), contestation (2014), formalization (2015 onward)
- Practices: typifying exemplars list common attributes to clarify symbolic boundaries and pull in social boundaries; familiarizing exemplars use metaphor/simile/analogy to import meaning from adjacent categories (e.g., organized crime, prostitution) and provisionally establish symbolic boundaries while creating social-boundary ambiguity; scoping exemplars use diagnosis-prognosis-bridging to expand social boundaries by including outlier members (e.g., children, overseas domestic workers)
- Boundary conditions: exemplar success depends on (1) goal alignment with category promoters and (2) stakeholder consensus on the appropriateness of including suggested actors/practices
- Emergent outcomes: deliberate inclusion of children and overseas domestic workers; exclusion of refugees and regular employers (as perpetrators); restriction of prostitution scope to forced prostitution

The process traces how, absent a prototype, different actors discursively construct provisional category boundaries through three distinct linguistic forms of exemplar that operate dynamically across four phases. Familiarizing exemplars (e.g., organized crime, sex work analogies) initially attract attention but risk overinflating social boundaries; typifying exemplars consolidate central category meaning by listing common attributes; scoping exemplars (used mostly by NGOs) deliberately push the social boundary outward to include otherwise excluded members. The success or failure of any exemplar depends on whether it aligns with the goals of category promoters (the UK Government) and whether stakeholders converge on its appropriateness — a dynamic that explains why children and migrant domestic workers were ultimately incorporated as victims, but refugees were not.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends the exemplar model of categorization beyond concrete category members by theorizing exemplars as discursively constituted linguistic representations that actively shape new category boundaries. It contributes (1) a typology of three linguistic forms of exemplar (typifying, familiarizing, scoping); (2) a reconceptualization of exemplar salience as contextually determined by goal alignment and stakeholder consensus rather than being a stable attribute; (3) a dynamic four-phase model linking exemplar deployment to specific social and symbolic boundary effects across category formation.
practical_implication: The findings have policy implications because the success or failure of different actors in deploying persuasive exemplars during category formation can determine which subjects (e.g., children, refugees, migrant workers) are included in or omitted from legal protections — the difference, in the case of modern slavery, between incarceration as a criminal and liberation as a victim. Organizations engaging in new policy or category-formation processes (e.g., on climate change, supply chain modern slavery) should be mindful of the role of exemplars and the windows of opportunity they create.
limitations: The case is distinctive because it concerns a legal category that is hierarchically defined by legislators with a fixed end point (the passage of the Act), rather than a market or organizational category that emerges more organically. Findings are most generalizable to contexts of deliberate category creation involving official category definers (governments, professional associations) and to categories with strong policing of boundaries. The single-case design limits cross-context comparison.
future_research: Future studies could conduct comparative case studies across multiple legal categories, examine how legal categories interact with related market categories (e.g., slave-free products), and extend exemplar-based analysis to other grand challenges such as climate change, biodiversity, migration, and inequality. Research could also explore the temporal redefinition of categories and how exemplars contribute to their endurance over time.
abstract: Modern slavery is one of the most prominent grand challenges facing organizations today. Yet, its precise meaning, and the scope of people and practices it subsumes, remain hotly contested. The recent formation of a legal category of "modern slavery" represents a critical point in establishing clear meanings and boundaries. While existing research on categories has shed light on how such new category meanings and boundaries may be shaped, we lack insight on how this happens in the absence of category prototypes that demarcate the meaning and membership of categories. We turn to category exemplars as an important but, as yet, poorly understood tool influencing category formation and subsequent membership. We conduct a systematic analysis of discourse surrounding the formation of the new category of modern slavery to identify three distinct forms of exemplars (typifying, familiarizing, and scoping), and develop a model that links actors' use of these exemplars to specific boundary effects across four phases of category formation. We also reveal the conditions under which different exemplars were successful in influencing the final category. We thereby contribute to the literature an exemplar-based theorization of new category formation that explains how categories associated with grand challenges like modern slavery are formed.
apa_citation: Caruana, R., Crane, A., & Ingram, C. (2025). The boundaries of modern slavery: The role of exemplars in new category formation. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(3), 507-539. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0210
