title: How Identity Structure Influences Identity Adoption: The Case of Hybrid Entrepreneurs
methods: Inductive, qualitative grounded-theory study based on 82 semi-structured interviews with academic entrepreneurs at eight U.S. universities; analysis combined informant drawings of identity networks, the Twenty Statements Test, and three phases of open/axial/selective coding.
industry: Higher education / academic entrepreneurship (faculty engaged in startups or licensing)
units: Faculty members who had engaged in commercializing their research (started companies and/or licensed inventions)
n_sample: 82 informants
authors: Fenters, V. W.; Balven, R. M.; Ashforth, B. E.; Waldman, D. A.; Siegel, D. S.
theories: identity construction / identity work; intrapersonal identity network theory; multiple identities theory; schema theory
keywords: academic entrepreneurs; entrepreneurial identity; hybrid entrepreneurs; identity adoption; identity structure; intrapersonal identity network
research_question: How does the cognitive structure of individuals' intrapersonal identity network influence the process of adopting a new identity? To answer this, the authors first ask: How do individuals cognitively structure their intrapersonal identity network in the first place?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: intrapersonal identity network; four dimensions (depth, overlap, density, range); three structure types (hierarchical, integrative, web); experienced needs; identity functions; evaluative logics
- Identity structures: hierarchical (high depth), integrative (high overlap), web (high density), with horizontal range informing each
- Adoption paths and needs: proving path (need for upward affirmation; new identity acts as conduit); holism path (need for coherence in a purpose; new identity acts as partner); distributive path (need for action-based resources; new identity serves utilitarian function)
- Evaluative logics for stabilizing/revising adoption: competing logic (proving), converging logic (holism), additive logic (distributive)
- Outcome: reinforcement or revision of the new identity's position in the network

The process model holds that an individual's cognitive structure focuses attention on some dimensions while limiting others, which makes a particular identity-based need salient. Individuals adopt a new identity to serve a function corresponding to that need (conduit, partner, or utilitarian resource provider) and then evaluate the adoption with a logic that matches the function. Whether the new identity is reinforced or revised in the network depends on whether it satisfies the underlying need without unduly threatening other identities, producing a recursive structuring process.
theoretical_contribution: The study identifies three distinct cognitive structures (hierarchical, integrative, web) built from a common set of four dimensions, challenging the long-held assumption that hierarchies of importance are pervasive and showing that high levels of certain dimensions tend to preclude others. It develops a process model linking each structure to a unique adoption path, experienced need, identity function, and evaluative logic, thereby explaining why provisional identities are reinforced or revised. It also reframes hybrid and entrepreneurial identity research by showing that the broader network of identities, not just the academic-entrepreneur dyad, shapes how an entrepreneurial identity emerges and stabilizes.
practical_implication: Assessing a person's identity structure reveals what they treat as opportunities versus threats and which incentives or framings will resonate. Entrepreneurial training programs (for example, NSF I-Corps) are likely to be more effective when tailored to the trainee's structure: emphasizing market validation for hierarchical structures, alignment with a higher purpose for integrative structures, and resource flows for web structures. Managers can use the same insight to design role assignments and rewards that fit employees' structures.
limitations: The sample is restricted to U.S. academic entrepreneurs who voluntarily entered entrepreneurship and were therefore likely to have well-articulated identity sets, which may limit transferability to less voluntary or non-academic contexts. Although the authors believe their tripartite typology and adoption model travel broadly, this generalizability has not been empirically tested beyond the present setting. The study also does not speak to antecedents of structures or to outcomes beyond identity adoption.
future_research: Future work should examine how each identity structure influences thinking, feeling, and acting beyond identity adoption, including links to performance, growth, and well-being; assess transferability of the tripartite typology to other work settings; explore conscious versus unconscious identity adoption; investigate what it means for an identity to "fit" a structure and how individuals tolerate misfit; and identify antecedents (developmental, individual differences, identity motives) that shape which structure emerges.
abstract: Scholars have theorized that individuals have an intrapersonal identity network comprised of multiple identities and the relationships between them. However, it remains unclear how individuals cognitively structure these networks and how such structures influence the adoption of a new identity. This matters because such adoptions facilitate growth and adaptation, and the literature hints that the structure will affect the adoption process. To explore these issues, we conducted an inductive, qualitative study of hybrid entrepreneurs (specifically, academic entrepreneurs). Our findings reveal that individuals combine four dimensions (depth, overlap, density, and range) to create one of three cognitive structures (hierarchical, integrative, or web), and that high levels of one dimension may preclude high levels of certain others. Further, we develop a model of how these structures impact the adoption of a new identity by focusing attention on some dimensions while limiting attention to others. In doing so, they guide individuals into a unique adoption path involving a certain need that identity adoption satisfies by serving a particular function in the network. This function influences the logic used in determining whether the new identity becomes stabilized in the network. Thus, our theory contributes to the literatures on multiple identities and hybrid entrepreneurs.
apa_citation: Fenters, V. W., Balven, R. M., Ashforth, B. E., Waldman, D. A., & Siegel, D. S. (2025). How identity structure influences identity adoption: The case of hybrid entrepreneurs. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(3), 567-597. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0520

title: How Political Context Affects Immigrant Newcomers’ Social Undermining Dynamics and Well-Being at Work
methods: Longitudinal multilevel design with seven survey waves (intake T0, biweekly T1–T6, follow-up T7) over 13 weeks, matched to 2021 German federal election data at the electoral-district level; empirical Bayes estimates of within-person social undermining slopes used in a first-stage moderated-mediation model with bootstrapped indexes of moderated mediation (Hayes 2015) and Johnson–Neyman conditional-effect probing.
industry: Mixed: predominantly finance (19.7%), healthcare (17.8%), and metal (11.8%) industries; participants drawn from 208 companies in Germany’s federally regulated dual vocational training system.
time_period: First 13 weeks of participants’ apprenticeship in 2021, matched with 2021 German federal election data.
units: Apprentices (immigrant and native newcomers) nested in companies and electoral districts.
n_sample: 500 apprentices in 110 electoral districts in the main moderated-mediation analysis (79 immigrants, 421 natives); empirical Bayes slopes estimated from 2,127 biweekly observations within 776 apprentices across 128 districts.
authors: Korman, B. A.; Reinwald, M.; Kunze, F.; Koos, S.
theories: gestalt characteristics theory; relational demography; social identity approach; social mobility strategy
keywords: emotional exhaustion; far-right political support; gestalt characteristics theory; immigrant newcomers; job satisfaction; relational demography; social identity; social mobility; social undermining; well-being
research_question: When and how does the macro-political context outside the organization — specifically regional far-right voter support — shape immigrant newcomers’ trajectories of perceived social undermining by coworkers over time, and how do these trajectories in turn affect their emotional exhaustion and job satisfaction?
mechanism_summary:
- Moderator: Regional far-right political support (2021 AfD vote share at the electoral-district level).
- Mediator: Slope of perceived social undermining by coworkers over T1–T6 (empirical Bayes estimate from a multilevel linear growth model).
- Covariates: Days to complete survey, positive/negative affect, age, gender, monthly salary, prior organizational experience, immigrant proportion in work region, immigrant proportion in apprenticeship type.

Drawing relational demography together with gestalt characteristics theory, the authors theorize that immigrants — a low-status outgroup — read their coworkers’ social undermining over time as a "directional arrow" indicating whether a social mobility strategy into the high-status native ingroup is viable. In high-AfD-vote regions, anti-immigrant sentiment seeps into workplaces, so undermining behaviors converge upward and immigrants’ sensitivity to subtle slights grows, producing increasing undermining trajectories that signal blocked status mobility and erode well-being. In low-AfD regions, immigrants are less threatening to natives, undermining decreases as acceptance grows, and a successful social mobility strategy protects well-being. Native newcomers’ trajectories are insensitive to regional far-right support because their high-status group membership reduces identity uncertainty and sensitivity to negative treatment.
theoretical_contribution: The study contributes to immigrant-workplace, relational-demography, and political-ideology-in-management literatures by (a) integrating gestalt characteristics theory with the social identity approach to give a dynamic, temporally structured account of dissimilar newcomers’ socialization, (b) introducing the macro-political context as a regional moderator that shapes within-organization status hierarchies and the success of immigrants’ social mobility strategies, and (c) demonstrating that political ideology operates not only as an individual-level construct but as a contextual force that defines group permeability and affects underrepresented employees’ well-being.
practical_implication: Organizations located in regions with substantial far-right support — the authors identify a tipping point near 13.0% AfD vote share — should recognize the elevated risk of escalating coworker undermining toward immigrant newcomers and respond with an inclusive climate, customized socialization tactics, leader role modeling, and zero-tolerance enforcement against undermining behaviors. Firms relying on immigrants to address labor shortages have an additional incentive to actively shape societal debates on immigration to create a more favorable external environment.
limitations: The sample is drawn solely from Germany, limiting generalizability to other polarized contexts (though the authors expect similar dynamics in France, Italy, and the U.S.). Immigrants comprised only 15.8% of the final sample, and stronger effects might be expected in settings where immigrants are less underrepresented. The study covers only the first 13 weeks of apprenticeship, focusing on negative interactions and well-being outcomes rather than longer-term performance or turnover.
future_research: Future work could test whether the model holds across countries with rising far-right movements, examine whether the observed effects strengthen in contexts with more balanced immigrant representation, extend the temporal window to longer-term outcomes such as performance and turnover, apply experience sampling to characterize daily interaction features, and broaden the focus from social undermining to positive interaction trajectories — which may be especially salient against an aversive macro context.
abstract: Although the successful labor market integration of immigrants can promote their prosperity and that of their organization, immigrant newcomers often face a difficult socialization process that can jeopardize their well-being. Considering the rising political polarization in many societies, as well as the dynamic nature of the newcomer socialization process, we develop a dynamic and multilevel model of immigrant employees’ socialization experiences over time, which is shaped by far-right voter support in the region where their organization is located. Building on and extending the relational demography literature, we argue that immigrants’ trajectories of social undermining— whether increasing or decreasing—are strongly influenced by levels of regional far-right support. Immigrants’ social undermining trajectories signal to them the feasibility of achieving a high-status identity at work, thereby impacting their well-being. We find support for our model in a unique dataset matching apprentices in Germany (tracked from day one of their apprenticeship over 13 weeks) with German election data. Specifically, we find that immigrants perceive increasing (decreasing) social undermining by their coworkers over time when employed in a region with higher (lower) far-right political support. Perceptions of increasing (decreasing) negative treatment, in turn, negatively (positively) affect employees’ emotional exhaustion and job satisfaction.
apa_citation: Korman, B. A., Reinwald, M., Kunze, F., & Koos, S. (2025). How political context affects immigrant newcomers’ social undermining dynamics and well-being at work. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(3), 540–566. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0303

title: Rolling the Dice: Resolving Demand Uncertainty in Markets with Partial Network Effects
methods: Endogenous treatment effects regression (linear endogenous treatment regression model) with heteroskedasticity-robust standard errors; Levene's test and heteroskedastic linear regression for variance comparison; coarsened exact matching (CEM) for robustness; instrumental variable (MtG events) in treatment equation.
industry: Global board games industry (nondigital tabletop games)
time_period: Board games released 2011-2017; data collected September 2020
units: Board games (with and without collectible components)
n_sample: 19,432 board games (225 with collectible components / CNGs)
authors: Ploog, J. N.; Rietveld, J.
theories: diffusion of innovations; network effects theory
keywords: competition intensity; demand uncertainty; diffusion of innovations; network effects; partial network effects; product novelty; social features
research_question: How does demand uncertainty differentially impact the diffusion of network products versus standalone products that compete in the same market? How do strategies to resolve consumers' perceived uncertainty affect the diffusion of network products compared to standalone products?
mechanism_summary: Network products' value depends on attracting a large user base, generating greater consumer-perceived demand uncertainty than standalone products. The authors theorize that any factor amplifying (alleviating) demand uncertainty will disproportionately harm (help) the diffusion of network products. Empirically, novelty, competition intensity, and hit competition all interact negatively with collectible components on owners; the predicted positive effect of Kickstarter success on network products is reversed, which the authors attribute to a "chasm" between early adopters and mainstream consumers.
theoretical_contribution: The paper relaxes the universality assumption in the network effects literature by introducing markets with partial network effects, demonstrating that nonnetwork factors interact with social features to differentially shape diffusion of network versus standalone products. It also flips the diffusion of innovations literature's typical firm-side perspective on demand uncertainty toward the customer's perspective, showing that drivers of perceived uncertainty have disproportionately negative effects on network products' diffusion.
practical_implication: Managers in markets with partial network effects should weigh whether and when to add social features by considering how product-market decisions (novelty, timing of release, competitive context) interact with social features to shape consumers' perceived uncertainty. Pairing network products with less novel designs and avoiding launches in highly competitive windows can mitigate downside diffusion risk; crowdfunding may not benefit network products and can dampen their diffusion.
limitations: The study relies on archival data from a single, contextualized analog setting (Board Game Geek), which may not generalize to digital product markets. Demand uncertainty is not directly measured, nor is variation in early- versus late-adopter perceptions captured. Crowdfunding is the only frontloading mechanism considered; alternatives such as betas, preorders, crowdsourcing, and influencer partnerships are not examined.
future_research: Future work should replicate findings in other industries—including digital product markets—and use laboratory experiments or demand-uncertainty questionnaires to measure perceived uncertainty more directly. Researchers could also study contexts with truly heterogeneous network effects, settings where firms add network value post-release, two- or multisided platform markets, and the dynamics of user engagement (versus mere adoption) in sustaining network effects.
abstract: It is commonly assumed that when markets are characterized by network effects, this universally affects all competing products. In reality, however, firms often have agency in terms of whether to incorporate social features that have the potential to generate network effects. When this is the case, markets are characterized by partial network effects—some products have network effects, whereas others compete on the basis of a standalone value proposition. In this study, we focus on the differences in demand uncertainty between network products and standalone products competing in such a market. We develop theory predicting how a product's social features interact with other known drivers of demand uncertainty to impact diffusion. We test our arguments in the global board games industry, where board games designed around the collection and trading of "collectible components" compete against traditional board games. Results show that network products exhibit greater variance in diffusion and that their diffusion is disproportionately affected by the degree of product novelty and the intensity and type of competition. Our findings contribute to the literatures on network effects and the diffusion of innovations.
apa_citation: Ploog, J. N., & Rietveld, J. (2025). Rolling the dice: Resolving demand uncertainty in markets with partial network effects. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(3), 598-619. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0133

title: Between War and Peace: How Boredom Shapes the Enactment of Idealized Futures in Extreme Contexts
methods: Inductive qualitative study using grounded-theory analysis (Strauss & Corbin, 1990) of 63 unsolicited personal diaries (kept for 31 months to 9 years) of UN peacekeeping officers, supplemented by 69 interviews, 397 hours of nonparticipant observations on a UNMISS mission in South Sudan (2019), and 1,896 internal/archival documents; three-stage open, axial, and theoretical coding in NVivo across five temporal phases (pre-deployment to post-deployment).
industry: United Nations peacekeeping (UNMISS, MONUSCO, MINUSCA, UNIFIL, MINUSMA, UNFICYP, MINURSO, UNMIK)
country: Active missions in Central African Republic, Democratic Republic of the Congo, and South Sudan; with comparative material from Mali, Lebanon, Cyprus, Western Sahara, Kosovo
time_period: Diaries span 2013-2018 (deployments); fieldwork in South Sudan in 2019; interviews 2018-2021
units: UN peacekeeping officers across hierarchical levels, plus seven additional UN personnel (coordinators, HQ staff)
n_sample: 63 diarists; 69 interviews (62 with diarists + 7 others); 397 hours of nonparticipant observations; 1,896 documents
authors: Rauch, M.
theories: existential boredom; futures and future-making; imaginaries; morality and meaning at work; temporal work
keywords: UN peacekeeping; diaries; existential boredom; extreme contexts; future-making; idealized futures; moral values; temporal orientation
research_question: How do workers who are deeply motivated to enact idealized futures experience and respond to boredom at work? Specifically, how does boredom shape workers' enactment of idealized futures in extreme contexts?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: idealized future (peace) vs. mundane realities of peacekeeping work; existential boredom (lack of meaning) vs. situational/task boredom (lack of activity); morals vs. work practices as the locus of questioning; pragmatic vs. absolute idealized futures.
- Triggering condition: misalignment between peacekeepers' moral aspirations to enact peace and the daily reality of patrolling, waiting, and "standing around," which surfaces existential boredom and prompts workers to question meaning and purpose.
- Two divergent pathways:
  - Boredom-induced adjustments (embracing): situational adjustment of moral values, redefining the meaning of the idealized future, and anchoring temporal orientation in the present reality.
  - Boredom-induced adherence (renouncing): clinging to morals, maintaining the meaning of the idealized future, and anchoring temporal orientation in the distant idealized future.
- Emergent outcomes: peacekeepers who embrace boredom enact a pragmatic idealized future, reframing misalignment and adopting new work practices (e.g., installing radio stations, veterinary camps, female outreach); peacekeepers who renounce boredom enact an absolute idealized future, intensifying misalignment and thwarting the adoption of new work practices.

The process unfolds when highly motivated workers, drawn to peacekeeping by an idealized vision of peace, encounter the protracted, mundane fieldwork of UN missions. The resulting existential boredom is not simply a void but a generative condition: it forces workers to confront the gap between their moral commitments and their daily tasks. Some recalibrate morals and meaning to fit local realities, generating positive emotional energy and pride; others double down on absolute ideals, with morale eroding into frustration and disengagement.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces "idealized futures" — visions of how the world ought to be, grounded in strong moral beliefs — as a distinct construct in the futures literature, shifting attention from imagining or narrating futures to enacting them in mundane work. It shows how morality and meaning underpin future enactment and that workers' temporal orientation (present vs. distant future) is directly tied to their willingness to situationally adjust moral values. For workplace boredom in extreme contexts, the paper distinguishes existential boredom from situational/task boredom and reveals boredom as a double-edged sword that can catalyze, not just undermine, meaning-making and the adoption of new practices.
practical_implication: Organizations pursuing grand visions or idealized futures (peacekeeping, humanitarian, climate) should identify root causes of worker boredom and treat existential boredom as a signal worth engaging with rather than suppressing. Aligning the meaning of work with local realities — rather than insisting on absolute ideals — supports adoption of context-appropriate practices, retention, and worker pride. The findings translate to less extreme settings (e.g., educators experiencing repetitive content) where reframing meaning can restore engagement.
limitations: The study is bounded by its access to diaries, observations, and interviews from UN peacekeepers on their first missions, with individual traits like resilience deliberately not analyzed to protect anonymity. The author did not study how reflections might evolve after returning home, and the sample, though demographically varied, is specific to UN peacekeeping; generalizability to other extreme or non-extreme settings warrants further empirical attention. The paper also does not rigorously analyze organizational retention, relying on indicative patterns.
future_research: Future work should extend the analysis to a broader emotional palette beyond boredom (anger, frustration, contentment, pride) and explore cross-occupational differences in responses to existential and situational boredom in both traditional and less traditional settings. Researchers could connect these findings to disengagement in less extreme contexts, such as employee engagement with corporate social responsibility initiatives, and probe how technology — particularly AI taking over repetitive, well-codified work — reshapes the antecedents and experience of existential boredom.
abstract: To investigate how workers enact idealized futures in extreme contexts, I analyze 63 unsolicited personal diaries of United Nations (UN) peacekeeping officers, as well as interviews, documents, and observational data related to UN peacekeeping missions. My findings reveal how peacekeepers navigate tensions between their idealized aspirations to achieve peace and the often-mundane aspects of their fieldwork. Central to these tensions is the experience of existential boredom, which plays a crucial role in shaping their responses. Peacekeepers respond to existential boredom in two ways. Some embrace boredom and follow a pathway of situational adjustment of their moral values, redefining the meaning of the idealized future, and anchoring the temporal orientation in the present reality. Others renounce boredom and follow a pathway of adherence by clinging to their moral values, maintaining the meaning of the idealized future, and anchoring the temporal orientation in the idealized future. The enactment of either a pragmatic or absolute idealized future, respectively, has important implications for the adoption of new work practices. I contribute to the literatures on futures and boredom by showing how boredom influences the enactment of idealized futures and identifying implications for day-to-day work in extreme contexts.
apa_citation: Rauch, M. (2025). Between war and peace: How boredom shapes the enactment of idealized futures in extreme contexts. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(3), 475-506. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0618

title: It Is Not the Whole Story: Toward a Broader Understanding of Entrepreneurial Ventures’ Symbolic Differentiation
methods: Multilevel random coefficient (hierarchical linear) modeling with random industry intercepts; topic modeling via latent Dirichlet allocation to construct narrative distinctiveness; coarsened exact matching as robustness check.
industry: All UK industries with venture activity (415 industries identified by five-digit SIC codes)
time_period: Ventures incorporated between January 1, 2010 and December 31, 2021
units: Entrepreneurial ventures (UK-headquartered private companies identified as 'ambitious' by Beauhurst)
n_sample: 31,270 ventures
authors: Taeuscher, Karl; Lounsbury, Michael D.
theories: cultural entrepreneurship theory; optimal distinctiveness; signaling theory
keywords: cultural entrepreneurship; entrepreneurial narratives; industry hotness; narrative distinctiveness; patents; quality signals; symbolic differentiation
research_question: How does the possession of quality-signaling resources (specifically patents) shape the degree to which entrepreneurial ventures convey distinctiveness in their entrepreneurial narratives, and under what industry conditions do resource-rich ventures forgo opportunities for symbolic differentiation?
mechanism_summary: The central mechanism is that ventures rich in quality-signaling resources possess strong "narrative ingredients" but their motivation to deploy them depends on external incentives. In cold industries, ventures face high pressure to stand out, so patent-rich ventures leverage their resources to craft distinctive narratives. In hot industries, patent-rich ventures already attract favorable attention via their quality signals and—constrained by bounded rationality—lack incentives to invest in symbolic differentiation, sometimes deliberately emphasizing nondistinctive elements to position themselves as legitimate industry members. The moderation strengthens when patents have broad impact (more valuable signals) and when patents are rare in the industry (more discriminating signals).
theoretical_contribution: The study challenges cultural entrepreneurship theory's prevailing assumption that ventures always aim to present themselves as distinctive as legitimately possible, showing that resource-rich ventures may strategically forgo symbolic differentiation when external incentives are absent. It extends Lounsbury and Glynn's seminal model by introducing entrepreneurs' varying incentives for symbolic differentiation as a mechanism explaining heterogeneity in narrative distinctiveness, and contributes to organizational distinctiveness research by jointly considering firm-level (quality-signaling resources) and industry-level (industry hotness) factors.
practical_implication: Entrepreneurs should recognize that whether to emphasize distinctiveness in venture narratives is contingent on industry conditions—in cold (low-attention) industries, emphasizing distinctiveness around resources like patents is beneficial, but in hot (high-attention) industries with rare patents, the symbolic emphasis matters less because the underlying quality signal already attracts attention. Resource providers evaluating ventures in hot industries should attend to substantive quality signals rather than narrative claims of distinctiveness alone.
limitations: The authors only have access to ventures' narratives at one point in time, preventing analysis of how narratives evolve as patent stocks build up. They cannot directly test the proposed motivational mechanism because their data do not capture how individual entrepreneurs perceive their environment. Their models explain only a moderate share of variance (R² ≈ .26) in narrative distinctiveness, suggesting other unobserved factors shape narrative content.
future_research: Future research could track narrative changes longitudinally as ventures accumulate patents, contextualize arguments to audience-specific differentiation by analyzing private documents like pitch decks, and explore how other quality-signaling resources (e.g., strategic alliances, founder experience) influence narrative distinctiveness. Microlevel process studies could also illuminate the cognitive and motivational processes underlying entrepreneurs' storytelling and symbolic differentiation efforts.
abstract: Entrepreneurial ventures strategically communicate information about themselves to convey their distinctiveness and attract favorable audience attention. This study explores how the possession of quality-signaling resources, such as patents, influences the degree to which entrepreneurial ventures convey distinctiveness in their entrepreneurial narratives. Cultural entrepreneurship research spotlights such resources as the ingredients around which entrepreneurs construct distinctive narratives and proposes that resource-rich ventures will present themselves as particularly distinctive. Challenging this, we argue that ventures rich in quality-signaling resources—while ideally positioned to convey their distinctiveness—will likely forgo this symbolic differentiation opportunity under certain industry conditions due to a lack of external incentives. Our analysis of 31,270 UK-based ventures launched between 2010 and 2021 finds that, compared to patent-poor ventures, patent-rich ventures exhibit higher levels of narrative distinctiveness when situated in industries that receive little attention, but substantially lower levels of narrative distinctiveness when situated in hot industries that attract a lot of attention. In doing so, our study challenges the assumption that entrepreneurial ventures always aim to present themselves as distinctive as legitimately possible, delineates conditions under which this assumption is likely violated, and lays the groundwork for a broader research agenda on organizations’ substantive and symbolic differentiation.
apa_citation: Taeuscher, K., & Lounsbury, M. D. (2025). It is not the whole story: Toward a broader understanding of entrepreneurial ventures’ symbolic differentiation. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(3), 648–668. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0581

title: Falling Off the Unicorn: The Maintenance of Unrealistic Startup Labor Narratives
methods: Inductive qualitative study using 86 in-depth, ethnographic, semi-structured interviews yielding 130 role perspectives, analyzed via three-stage open/axial/selective coding (Gioia methodology).
industry: High-tech startups in software, hardware, manufacturing, and biotechnology
country: United States (East and West Coasts) and Denmark
units: Founders, employees, and venture capital investors of VC-backed high-growth startups
n_sample: 86 interviews representing 130 role perspectives (38 employee, 59 founder, 33 investor)
authors: Canales, R.; Regele, M. D.; Groberg, M.-G.; Eftekhari, N.
theories: entrepreneurial storytelling; labor market dynamics; perpetuation of false narratives; pluralistic ignorance
keywords: entrepreneurial narratives; high-growth startups; labor market; narrative maintenance; startup labor; venture capital
research_question: Why and how are startup labor narratives maintained even as employees' expectations remain unmet? The authors examine how generalized narratives promising autonomy, rapid career growth, and financial rewards persist in VC-backed high-growth startups despite repeated failure to deliver on those promises.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: generalized startup labor narratives (autonomy, rapid career growth, financial windfalls) vs. divergent lived experiences; entrepreneurial narrative revision vs. narrative maintenance; employee agency vs. labor-market entrapment.
- Process / phases: four startup stages structure the dynamic — (1) resource acquisition (opportunity narratives established and labor narratives leveraged), (2) launch (talent attraction and socialization), (3) growth and adaptation (recrafting external pivot narratives), and (4) exit (entrapment in startup labor market and narrative reproduction).
- Mechanisms of maintenance: (1) misaligned incentives between VCs (portfolio diversification + de-diversification of individual ventures), founders, and employees; (2) social pressures of normative optimism, motivated sampling, ageism, and stigma of failure; (3) structural labor-market features producing a parallel startup labor market with restricted mobility back to established firms.
- Emergent outcomes: employees shift from accepting to actively propagating narratives they privately doubt, becoming "entrapped in startup markets and narratives."

The process unfolds when VCs structure investments as portfolios that absorb individual failures while pushing portfolio companies to de-diversify and "swing for the fences." Founders and investors knowingly use ambitious labor narratives to attract talent, while employees, once inside, find themselves in a secondary labor market with limited exit options. Social pressure to remain optimistic, fear of being labeled a failure, ageism, and motivated sampling of positive stories suppress dissent and keep employees publicly endorsing narratives they have learned to distrust.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends entrepreneurial narrative theory by shifting attention from narrative revision (how founders adapt stories for external audiences) to narrative maintenance, showing investors are fully aware of overly optimistic promises and actively sustain them. It identifies employees as active narrative co-producers rather than passive recipients, and links the perpetuation-of-false-narratives literature (pluralistic ignorance, socially constructed valuation) to the specific structural configuration of VC-backed entrepreneurship.
practical_implication: Employees should join broader information networks outside the startup community to form more realistic expectations about salaries, stock packages, and developmental paths. Founders should provide transparency on risks and invest in retention and learning. Investors and policymakers should challenge unfounded narratives, support entrepreneurship education that reflects realistic career trajectories, and improve safety nets for startup employees.
limitations: The study focuses on VC-backed high-growth startups in the U.S. and Denmark across four high-tech industries, so dynamics may differ for bootstrapped or independent startups where investor pressures are weaker. Retrospective interviews may introduce bias, although the authors argue any bias would work against their findings. Comparative outcomes across firm types holding employee ability constant remain unobserved.
future_research: Future work should pursue longitudinal analyses of career, health, and personal outcomes for comparable employees across established firms, VC-backed startups, and independent startups. Additional qualitative work is needed on young firms to understand startup work first-hand, and further research could map the distribution of risks, rewards, benefits, and harms across stakeholders as entrepreneurial ecosystems evolve.
abstract: This study examines the persistence of unrealistic generalized startup labor narratives, which promise autonomy, rapid career growth, and significant financial rewards, despite high failure rates and unmet expectations. Drawing on interviews with founders, employees, and venture capitalists in high-growth tech startups, we explore why and how these narratives endure. Our findings highlight three primary mechanisms driving narrative maintenance: (1) structural misalignments between investor, founder, and employee incentives; (2) social pressures within the entrepreneurial ecosystem that reinforce optimism, and (3) structural constraints in the labor market that restrict employee mobility and career alternatives. Contrary to prior research focusing mainly on founders, we reveal that investors and employees also actively participate in sustaining these narratives despite their divergence from reality. This study contributes to the literature on entrepreneurial storytelling by demonstrating how the structural configuration of high-tech investing creates labor market constraints and social dynamics that foster false narrative maintenance, yielding hidden costs for startup employees while benefiting investors and founders.
apa_citation: Canales, R., Regele, M. D., Groberg, M.-G., & Eftekhari, N. (2025). Falling off the unicorn: The maintenance of unrealistic startup labor narratives. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(4), 760-786. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0013

title: Calm in the Storm: Job Security and Post-Merger Performance in Family versus Nonfamily Firms
methods: Longitudinal panel design with coarsened exact matching (CEM); structural equation modeling with bootstrapping mediation; two-stage residual inclusion (2SRI) for endogeneity; supplementary moderated mediation (critical experiment); 11 semi-structured qualitative interviews for triangulation.
industry: Private (limited) Swedish firms across multiple industries (manufacturing, construction, wholesale/retail, professional services, etc.) coded by SNI codes
country: Sweden (with qualitative interviews across Australia, India, Norway, Sweden, and the United States)
time_period: 2004-2012, with merged firms followed up to five years post-merger
units: Merger dyads (paired merging firms): family-family, family-nonfamily, nonfamily-nonfamily
n_sample: 1,173 mergers (2,346 firms): 115 family-family, 389 family-nonfamily, 669 nonfamily-nonfamily; 11 qualitative interviews
authors: Chirico, Francesco; Hoskisson, Robert E.; Pathak, Seemantini; Baù, Massimo
theories: social identity theory; socioemotional wealth perspective
keywords: family firms; job security; mergers and acquisitions; post-merger performance; social identity theory; socioemotional wealth
research_question: How do owners' social identities (family vs. nonfamily) of the two firms involved in a merger influence employee job security and post-merger performance, and how does industry dissimilarity moderate this relationship?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Differences in firm age, CEO age, sales, employees, prior merger experience, slack, past ROA, environmental dynamism, geographic distance, log of time, plus an endogeneity score from 2SRI using percentage of family firms in the same municipality and industry as instruments.

Family owners share a salient, long-term social identity that fosters in-group recognition between merging family firms, reinforcing homophily-based agreement on strategy and a stewardship orientation toward employees. Two paired family owners therefore offer greater job security, preserving each family's tacit knowledge and identity through their respective employees, which sustains commitment, productivity, and ultimately post-merger performance. In contrast, mergers involving at least one nonfamily firm import diffuse social identities (founder, investor, partner, foreign-controlled) that prioritize short-term efficiency and cost cutting, generating layoffs that erode the combined resource base. Under industry dissimilarity, family-family pairs further benefit because each family can retain autonomy in its own segment while shared identity enables integration — turning unrelated diversification into a complementarity rather than the SEW-predicted liability.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends social identity theory to merger contexts by showing that beyond identity similarity, the *type* of owner identity matters: family-owner identity is uniquely strong and salient compared to other ownership types, generating a "calm in the storm" advantage that resolves the integration-autonomy tension via shared identity plus employee retention. It revises Neckebrouck et al.'s (2018) finding that family firms are worse organizational stewards by showing that in transformative events such as mergers, family firms are simultaneously better organizational stewards (higher job security) and better financial stewards (higher post-merger performance). The critical experiment against the socioemotional wealth perspective overturns the standard SEW prediction by showing family-firm mergers perform best under industry dissimilarity through cross-industry complementarities.
practical_implication: Owners and managers should weigh social identity factors — not only financial fit — when selecting merger partners, as identity compatibility shapes both economic and human outcomes. Family-firm owners are advised to seek other family firms as partners, particularly in unrelated industries where complementarities can be exploited while each family retains autonomy. More generally, leaders should resist using post-merger employee downsizing as an "easy lever," because workforce reduction erodes the merged firm's resource base, demoralizes survivors, and undermines performance.
limitations: The study emphasizes positive aspects of family-firm characteristics and does not fully model negatives such as bifurcation bias, parental altruism, and nepotism, which could attenuate the documented effects. It treats all family firms as possessing a uniformly strong familial social identity, while in reality family firms are heterogeneous in identity strength and form. The Swedish private-firm setting and the inability to exhaustively test all competing mechanisms (e.g., behavioral theories, resource dependence, hubris) constrain generalizability.
future_research: Future studies could replicate the findings in other countries and industries, and examine fine-grained differences in how family-firm partners manage dysfunctional conflicts and integration with nonfamily employees. Researchers could investigate family-firm heterogeneity in the salience of family identity, and compare merger choice and post-merger performance between publicly traded and privately held family firms. Additional work could explore alternative theoretical mechanisms (resource-based view, imitation, hubris) and specific synergy motives behind workforce reduction versus other forms of asset restructuring.
abstract: Building on social identity theory, we theorize and find that in a merger, paired family firms are better able to retain employees and improve post-merger performance compared to other merger pairs. We contribute to social identity theory by theorizing better post-merger performance as mediated by job security for family firm combinations. We also contribute to the job security and merger and acquisition literature by examining how job security and post-merger performance vary based on the paired social identity of owners. In addition to identity similarity, the type of identity also matters in mergers. We argue that family owner social identity similarity fosters greater integration between merging parties while allowing family owner pairs to retain some autonomy through their employees, thereby maximizing post-merger performance. Our data on private Swedish firms, complemented by 11 qualitative interviews across five countries and three continents, confirm that family mergers outperform other merger combinations via job security. In a supplementary critical experiment examining industry dissimilarity, we compare the socioemotional wealth perspective—which emphasizes loss aversion and predicts family firms' unrelated diversification avoidance—to social identity theory. Consistent with social identity theory, our results show that both job security and post-merger performance improve with unrelated family firm mergers.
apa_citation: Chirico, F., Hoskisson, R. E., Pathak, S., & Baù, M. (2025). Calm in the storm: Job security and post-merger performance in family versus nonfamily firms. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(4), 845-869. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0496

title: Threading the Needle of Corporate Activism: How Firms Frame Their Stances on Polarizing Social Issues
methods: Stacked-cohort difference-in-differences panel regression on a firm-month panel of Fortune 500 companies' LGBTQ-related communications (1999-2019). Dependent variable is firms' relative use of collective action frames vs. individual action frames in press releases and tweets, computed from a dictionary of keywords built via human coding plus latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) topic modeling. Treatment is a marriage-equality settlement (state legislative or judicial decision) in the firm's headquarters state, with cohorts constructed for each of the 36 distinct settlement events; control firms are Fortune 500 firms in states without a settlement during the cohort window. Models include firm, cohort, and month-year fixed effects, clustered standard errors at firm-cohort level, and a Heckman (1979) two-stage selection correction (first-stage probit instrumented by industry average elite-network size). Robustness checks include negative binomial counts, contemporaneous ideology measures, exclusion of CEO-attributed text, federal vs. state settlement decomposition, and annual-frequency models.
industry: Fortune 500 companies across all sectors (cross-industry); identification leverages two-digit SIC industry-level political-ideology measures
country: United States (50 states; 36 state-level marriage-equality settlement events between 2004 Massachusetts and 2015 Obergefell v. Hodges)
time_period: 1999-2019 observation window (covering the 2004 Massachusetts settlement through the 2015 Obergefell decision plus four-year lead/lag)
units: Firm-month observations of LGBTQ-related corporate press releases and corporate Twitter posts; dictionary-based frame measures
n_sample: 152 Fortune 500 firms that spoke out on LGBTQ issues; 6,461 press releases and 6,450 tweets analyzed; 6,920 firm-month observations in main regression; first-stage probit on 619,047 firm-months from all Fortune 500 firms
authors: Chung, Sung Hun (Brian); Odziemkowska, Kate; Piazza, Alessandro
theories: collective action frame theory (Snow & Benford); institutional theory (Fligstein & McAdam contestation/settlement); social movement theory; stakeholder theory; strategic framing theory; upper echelons theory
keywords: LGBTQ rights; advocacy organizations; collective action frames; contestation; corporate activism; executive ideology; individual action frames; issue settlement; same-sex marriage; social arrangements; stakeholder alignment; strategic framing
research_question: How do firms strategically frame their stances on polarizing social issues as institutional arrangements evolve from contested to settled, and how is the shift from individual action frames to collective action frames conditioned by the ideological alignment of internal and external stakeholders?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on social movement theory's distinction between collective action frames (diagnostic, prognostic, motivational; injustice and moral outrage) and "individual action frames" (the authors' coinage for business/workplace framings tied to firm-level economic rationales), the paper argues that for-profit firms confronting contested social issues default to lower-risk individual action frames during contestation and only shift to collective action frames after a judicial or legislative settlement legitimizes a position and reduces stakeholder fault lines. Settlements operate by encoding authoritative signals of acceptable conduct and catalyzing attitudinal shifts, lowering the ex post backlash risk of injustice framings. The shift, however, is filtered through ideological alignment with key stakeholders: liberal executives, liberal industries, liberal states, and the presence of LGBTQ-aligned NGOs amplify the post-settlement shift to collective action frames, while conservative executives, industries, or state electorates suppress it.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends institutional theory by showing how firms' rhetorical strategies — not just their structural or practice-level adoptions — reflect and adapt to evolving institutional contestation/settlement cycles, and it advances stakeholder theory by demonstrating that ideological alignment with multiple stakeholder layers (executives, industry peers, state electorates, NGOs) conditions how, not just whether, firms speak out. It also contributes to corporate-activism research by surfacing an irony: "true" activist (collective-action) framing predominantly emerges after social arrangements are already settled, suggesting that "corporate activism" is partly a misnomer — firms tend to enter the fray only after the battle has been won.
practical_implication: For managers, the findings imply that signaling support during periods of active contestation is most safely done through individual action frames anchored in business and workplace rationales, while collective action frames carry meaningful backlash risk until institutional legitimacy is established. For activists, NGOs, and policymakers expecting corporations to be vanguards of change, the analysis cautions that corporate "activist" speech is largely a lagging, contingent response calibrated to executive, industry, and state-level ideology rather than a proactive driver of contested social change.
limitations: The study is restricted to Fortune 500 firms, so smaller firms — which may employ different niche or product-market framing strategies — are not covered. The empirical setting (U.S. same-sex marriage legalization, 2004-2015) was an unusually clean and progressive trajectory of staggered settlements, raising questions about generalizability to settlements moving in conservative directions or settlements that diverge sharply from public opinion (e.g., Dobbs). Identification rests on settlements in the headquarters state, even though stakeholders (employees, customers) may reside elsewhere; constructs such as the motivational dimension of collective action frames partly overlap with prior issue-selling and symbolic-vs-substantive distinctions in management research.
future_research: Future work could probe how stakeholder ideology shapes reactions to firms' framing choices and whether mixed framing produces less coherence or broader appeal across heterogeneous audiences. Researchers could examine settlements that go "against the grain" of public opinion, settlements moving in conservative directions, framing choices of small and private firms, and the welfare consequences of corporations' heavy reliance on individual action frames (business-case framings) for the beneficiaries of prosocial corporate initiatives. Cross-national replications and richer measurement of employee ideology and stakeholder networks are also promising avenues.
abstract: Corporate activism, or the active involvement of business in contested social and political issues, presents strategic challenges for organizations. Despite the risks of stakeholder backlash, corporate activism is on the rise. We suggest that how firms speak out on polarizing social issues may help explain this quandary. Leveraging corporate press releases and Twitter accounts of Fortune 500 companies that spoke on LGBTQ issues between 1999 and 2019, as same-sex marriage progressively became legal across the United States, we find that prior to legalization in their home state, firms default to touting the economic merits of their track record on LGBTQ workplace issues, avoiding contentious debates. Once marriage equality is enacted, firms shift their speech toward activism, advocating for broader societal change. Further, the shift to activism is highly dependent on internal and external stakeholder preferences. Our findings point to an irony: corporate "activism" in pursuit of social change often takes place only after polarizing issues have been settled. This study contributes to the growing literature on corporate activism by shedding light on how firms strategically frame their communications to navigate the complex terrain of stakeholder expectations, and how these framing strategies are shaped by the evolving legal and institutional landscape.
apa_citation: Chung, S. H. (B.), Odziemkowska, K., & Piazza, A. (2025). Threading the needle of corporate activism: How firms frame their stances on polarizing social issues. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(4), 680-706. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0237

title: Compelling Empirics in Quantitative Studies
methods: Editorial advice piece drawing on collective experience as authors and editors, with examples from past AMJ Best Paper and Impact Award finalists and winners; no empirical analysis.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Grégoire, Denis A.; Tröster, Christian; Kotha, Reddi; Zhu, Jing; Farh, Crystal; Roberson, Quinetta; Gruber, Marc
theories: theory-method alignment
keywords: compelling empirics; field studies; longitudinal studies; multi-study designs; quantitative studies; theory-method alignment
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Compelling empirics — data and findings that demand attention and convince readers — play an influential role alongside theoretical contributions in shepherding manuscripts through review and shaping their reception, yet limited guidance exists on how to craft them.
- Pathways toward compelling empirics: (1) study design and storytelling grounded in deep understanding of real-world phenomena; (2) multi-study designs anchored in clear strategic purposes (triangulation, constructive replication, multilevel designs, experimental mediation); (3) combining data from distant sources; (4) field studies that articulate causal pathways and respect temporal ordering; (5) longitudinal studies with theory-driven measurement timing and analytic strategies; (6) presenting results with completeness, clarity, and credibility (the "three C's").
- Threats to compelling empirics: theory–method misalignments (untenable proxies, mechanism claims without empirical observation, level mismatches); the limits of large samples (false positives, trivially small effects); over-reliance on crowdsourced data without quality controls; multi-study designs lacking synergistic support across hypotheses.

This editorial does not present a causal mechanism but develops a set of editorial heuristics for what makes quantitative empirics "sing." Building on recent AMJ editorials and exemplar award-winning papers, the authors argue that compelling empirics begin with theory-to-method alignment — designing data collection and measurement to closely correspond with the focal theoretical constructs — and extend through choices about multi-study architecture, data sourcing, temporal design, and results presentation. Throughout, the authors advise authors to ground research designs in a keen understanding of what takes place in the real world, so that data choices serve theory rather than exist for their own sake.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial consolidates editorial guidance for quantitative authors at AMJ by articulating what "compelling empirics" mean and offering pathways and pitfalls. It complements parallel guidance on theoretical contributions and qualitative methods, and reinforces the principle of theory-to-method alignment as a benchmark for evaluating quantitative work submitted to top management journals.
practical_implication: Authors of quantitative submissions should ground research designs in real-world phenomena, justify data and measurement choices by their fit to theoretical constructs, articulate a clear strategic purpose for multi-study designs, theorize the form of change before designing longitudinal studies, complement statistical significance with discussion of effect magnitude and practical meaning, and ensure that crowdsourced or large-sample data come with explicit quality and attentiveness checks.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editorial encourages authors to consider preregistering analysis plans (even during revision), to develop theories about the form of change before collecting longitudinal data, to use experimental mediation designs that test causal steps directly rather than relying on measured-mediator analyses, and to integrate qualitative observations as fully integrated strategies rather than convenient afterthoughts to quantitative findings.
abstract: FROM THE EDITORS COMPELLING EMPIRICS IN QUANTITATIVE STUDIES
apa_citation: Grégoire, D. A., Tröster, C., Kotha, R., Zhu, J., Farh, C., Roberson, Q., & Gruber, M. (2025). Compelling empirics in quantitative studies. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(4), 669–679. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2025.4004

title: The Roles of Learning and Status Attainment in Successful Newcomer Socialization: Random Assignments to Complex Projects and Early Career Outcomes
methods: Longitudinal archival field study using random assignment of newcomers to projects; mixed-effects linear regression for performance and mediators, repeated-events Cox proportional hazards model for promotion, parametric bootstrap with Monte Carlo simulation (20,000 replications) for moderated mediation.
industry: Aerospace/space industry (private Chinese space firm 'SpeedTech', avionics R&D)
units: Newcomers (employees with <2 years tenure) randomly assigned to project teams
n_sample: 507 newcomers; 7,753 monthly assignment records; 2,558 quarterly monetary reward observations; 799 annual supervisor evaluations
authors: Li, Shihan; Krackhardt, David; Niezink, Nynke M. D.
theories: absorptive capacity; organizational socialization theory; signaling theory; status characteristics theory; task complexity theory
keywords: career outcomes; learning; newcomer socialization; prior same-industry experience; project complexity; random assignment; status attainment
research_question: What features of on-the-job experiences generate early career benefits for newcomers, through what mechanisms do these benefits occur, and who is best positioned to capitalize on these experiences? Specifically, how do project component complexity and coordination complexity influence newcomers’ performance and promotion via learning and status attainment, and how does prior same-industry experience moderate these relationships?
mechanism_summary:
- Moderator: Prior same-industry experience (years worked in the space industry before joining SpeedTech)

Newcomers assigned to projects with higher coordination and component complexity engage in interrelated, varied actions that drive both intra- and inter-personal learning—building technical mastery and a broad knowledge base—and simultaneously increase visibility and signal commitment, competence, and contribution to colleagues, who then accord higher status. Learning translates into higher performance and faster promotion through accumulated human capital, while status confers privileges, resources, and positive evaluation biases that further accelerate career advancement. Prior same-industry experience strengthens these pathways by providing the absorptive capacity needed to extract learning value from complex assignments and serving as a credibility cue colleagues use when attributing collective success.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes three contributions to newcomer socialization research. First, it integrates status attainment into the socialization model, expanding the dominant focus on relationship-building to include integration into the informal status hierarchy as a distinct adjustment milestone. Second, it advances a learning perspective on socialization by demonstrating how the development of technical expertise—not just social norms—mediates the link between early experiences and career outcomes. Third, it extends research on newcomer personal capital by showing that prior same-industry experience operates as a boundary condition rather than only a direct predictor, enabling newcomers to translate complex assignments into competence and recognition.
practical_implication: Organizations should align the complexity of newcomer project assignments with newcomers’ prior industry experience to avoid a “rich get richer” dynamic. Newcomers with substantial same-industry experience are well-suited for complex projects, while inexperienced newcomers may benefit more from simpler assignments paired with systematic training programs and supportive environments that help them build the foundation needed to leverage challenging work later.
limitations: The newsletter-appearance status proxy is highly skewed (73% of quarterly observations are zero) and may underestimate nuance among newcomers who never appeared, allowing only a conservative test of status effects. The mechanisms through which complexity fosters learning and status (e.g., intrapersonal vs. interpersonal learning, recognition of competence vs. effort) are not directly observed. Mediation analyses indicate learning and status account for only 30% and 7% of coordination complexity’s total effect on monetary rewards and promotion respectively, suggesting other unmeasured mechanisms (e.g., intrinsic motivation, meaningfulness) remain.
future_research: Future studies should employ more direct status measures such as peer-evaluated status to overcome proxy limitations. Lab or field experiments could directly test the underlying mechanisms (intra- vs. interpersonal learning; effort- vs. competence-based status attribution). Researchers should also examine contextual and individual moderators—supportive workplace climate, growth-need strength—and the dynamics of project-assignment sequences (e.g., simple-to-complex versus complex-to-simple trajectories) on learning, status attainment, and longer-term career development.
abstract: Newcomers’ early work experiences in an organization can considerably affect their socialization. While much of the literature on this topic has documented how certain organization-wide practices succeed or fail in molding newcomers into “good citizens,” little is known about how differentiated early experiences lead to varied socialization outcomes. To this end, we systematically examine the impact of early project team assignments on newcomers’ career kick-offs. We propose two distinct but complementary mechanisms—learning and status attainment—to explain why some newcomers achieve superior performance and quicker promotions. We theorize that being assigned to complex projects offers newcomers opportunities to build competence and gain social recognition. Leveraging longitudinal archival data from a high-tech company where newcomers were randomly assigned to projects during their first two years, we found that those assigned to more complex projects obtained more professional certifications, reported higher levels of learning, and appeared more frequently in the company’s internal newsletters. These outcomes were associated with higher promotion rates, increased monetary rewards, and better supervisor evaluations. Additionally, we demonstrated that prior same-industry experience amplified the positive effects of project complexity on learning and status attainment. Our findings underscore the pivotal role of early assignments in shaping newcomers’ career development.
apa_citation: Li, S., Krackhardt, D., & Niezink, N. M. D. (2025). The roles of learning and status attainment in successful newcomer socialization: Random assignments to complex projects and early career outcomes. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(4), 787–817. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.1057

title: Uprooting Loneliness: A Theory of Continuity-Breaking Self-Narrative Change
methods: Inductive, interpretivist qualitative study grounded in narrative inquiry. Data collected over 15 months via 90-120 minute semi-structured video interviews with 61 senior executives recruited from an international business school's alumni population. Pilot interviews with 7 executives preceded the main study. Analysis used iterative first-order, axial, and theoretical coding (Locke, 2001; Salda~na, 2009), case summaries, narrative causality (Carton, 2018), and circling between data and the literatures on self-narratives, work loneliness, and attachment. Theoretical saturation was reached at interview 46; data collection stopped at 61.
industry: Not reported in paper
time_period: 15 months of data collection (specific calendar dates not reported); informants reported persistent loneliness experiences of at least two years' duration
units: Senior executives (e.g., CEOs, CFOs, CHROs, CMOs, COOs, partners, presidents, directors) who self-reported sustained or repeated loneliness in their work roles
n_sample: 61 executives in the formal study (28 holding constraining self-narratives, 24 having transitioned to connecting self-narratives, 9 in the midst of change), plus 7 pilot interviewees
authors: Petriglieri, Jennifer Louise; Sheprow, Elizabeth
theories: attachment theory; narrative inquiry; psychodynamic perspective; relational scripts; self-narrative theory; threshold theory
keywords: attachment theory; connecting self-narrative; constraining self-narrative; continuity-breaking self-narrative change; emotional threshold; executives; narrative inquiry; persistent loneliness; relational scripts; self-narratives; self-referential insight; work loneliness
research_question: Why and how do people engage in continuity-breaking self-narrative change related to their work? The authors examine this question in the empirical context of executives experiencing persistent work loneliness.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: self-narratives and their central themes; constraining vs. connecting relational scripts ("If I am self-reliant, then I will be less vulnerable to others who could undermine my success" vs. "If I am open, then I will be more connected to others, and this will aid my success"); deprived vs. enriched relational realities; persistent/painful vs. short-lived/unpleasant loneliness.
- Triggering conditions: an accumulation of distressing loneliness, often catalyzed by a precipitating work event, that pushes informants past an emotional threshold beyond which loneliness becomes intolerable and rhetorical justification and relational compensation can no longer keep it tolerable.
- Process / phases: (1) constraining self-narrative maintained through enactment of self-isolating behavior, rhetorical justification of loneliness as the price of success, and relational compensation outside work; (2) crossing an emotional threshold when loneliness becomes intolerable; (3) self-referential insight that loneliness is partly an agentic construction; (4) translating insight into experimental relational actions, or hitting an impasse driven by fear; (5) oscillation between competing relational scripts; (6) connecting self-narrative in which the alternative script replaces the original and other stories of self are updated.
- Practices: enacting guarded/self-isolating behaviors vs. open/affiliative behaviors; rhetorical justification; relational compensation in nonwork life; gradual experimentation ("almost by stealth"); seeking help; building "circles of confidence."
- Emergent outcomes: continuity-breaking self-narrative change creating "disjuncture between past and present selves"; reconstruction of work realities even when little in the work environment has changed; loneliness moves from persistent and painful to short-lived and unpleasant.

The central process is that persistent loneliness becomes "rooted in the self" via constraining relational scripts that yoke success to self-reliance and invulnerability. Continuity-breaking change is triggered by a coalescence of external precipitating events and an internal emotional shift across a threshold; this distress drives a self-referential insight that loneliness is partly self-constructed, which in turn fuels experimentation with connecting scripts that develop in parallel, compete with, and ultimately supplant the original ones as the central themes of the self-narrative — at which point informants update other stories of self and reconstruct their relational realities at work.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops a theory of continuity-breaking self-narrative change that complements existing work on narrative continuity by showing how people create disjunctions between past and present selves. It contributes to research on work loneliness by theorizing loneliness as partly an agentic construction in which people psychologically invest, and shifts the field's perspective from a static phenomenon to a dynamic process. It expands attachment theory by mapping how relational scripts — the cognitive elements of attachment systems — change through parallel development, competition, and replacement rather than gradual revision, and it advances a contextualized view of relational scripts as domain-specific (e.g., to work) rather than only global or relationship-specific.
practical_implication: Workplace interventions that focus solely on the work environment are unlikely to be sufficient to combat the loneliness epidemic. Organizations should pair opportunities to connect with interventions that help workers question their assumptions about who they need to be and how they need to act in relation to others at work, and create opportunities for self-reflection. Senior executives, in particular, should recognize that the bargains they have made between self-reliance and success can produce persistent loneliness, and that humanizing oneself at work can paradoxically aid leadership effectiveness.
limitations: The findings are drawn from an elite sample of executives recruited from an international business school's alumni population, raising questions about generalizability to non-executives or to workers with job (rather than career) orientations. The method is purposefully subjective and relies on narrative causality, so it cannot establish that the changes described actually occurred objectively as informants recounted them. Data were collected at a single point in time rather than longitudinally, although the authors mitigate this by sampling informants at different stages of the process.
future_research: Future studies could investigate cultural and collective dynamics of continuity-breaking self-narrative change — for example, whether senior executives' updated self-narratives diffuse to surrounding workers, paralleling contagion effects in loneliness. Researchers could examine self-narrative change among workers at different career stages, compare workers with different orientations to work, and explore how loneliness in one life domain affects relational behavior in others. Scholars are also encouraged to examine threshold-crossing levels of other social emotions (anger, shame, envy) as triggers of self-narrative change, and to investigate interindividual variance in whether and when people cross such thresholds.
abstract: Through an inductive study of executives reporting persistent loneliness at work, we examine how problematic work experiences can be rooted in the self through narratives, and the process by which they can be uprooted. In the case of loneliness, we found that relational scripts formed the central theme of executives' self-narratives, which informed how they acted and felt at work. Some executives drew on constraining scripts that portrayed self-isolating behavior as necessary for success. This led them to construct a deprived relational reality that rendered loneliness a persistent feature of their working selves. Our study reveals the process through which people changed this constraining self-narrative. For the executives we studied, it began when people crossed an emotional threshold, past which loneliness became intolerable. This prompted the insight that loneliness was partially an agentic construction, and drove experimentation with connecting scripts that, over time, replaced the constraining ones. As this occurred, people made deliberate efforts to construct more relationally enriching work realities and updated their other stories of self. Building on these findings, we develop a theory of continuity-breaking self-narrative change that reveals how people can create disjunctions between past and present selves.
apa_citation: Petriglieri, J. L., & Sheprow, E. (2025). Uprooting loneliness: A theory of continuity-breaking self-narrative change. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(4), 707-734. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2024.0061
