title: Distributed Knowledge and the Creation of Public Value: Community-Based Organizing and Wildfire Management in Northern California
methods: Longitudinal quantitative design using an original community-year panel of fire prevention plans (FPPs) and fire histories in seven Northern California counties (1998-2018). Identification combines two-way fixed effects (TWFE) difference-in-differences, propensity-score matching, and two-stage least squares (2SLS) instrumental-variable DiD; stakeholder and collaboration variables coded from FPP documents; principal component analysis (PCA) used to classify cross-level collaboration types; robustness checks include alternative stakeholder measures, negative binomial models, and validation with insurance-claims data.
industry: Public-sector and community-based wildfire management (local, state, and federal fire agencies collaborating with self-organizing communities)
units: Community-year observations, where each observation is a yearly community-fire dyad in Del Norte, Humboldt, Siskiyou, Trinity, Shasta, Modoc, and Lassen counties
n_sample: 6,741 community-year observations across 321 communities; 103 unique fire prevention plan observations
authors: Stein, Devin; Minniti, Maria
theories: Hayekian distributed knowledge; Ostrom collective action theory; new institutional economics; new stakeholder theory; polycentric governance; public value theory
keywords: collective action; cross-level collaboration; distributed knowledge; fire prevention plans; local knowledge; noncontractual governance; public value; public-private collaborations; self-organizing communities; stakeholder participation; wildfire management
research_question: How and why do self-organizing communities, and their cross-level collaborations with higher-level public organizations, contribute to the creation of public value beyond what is achievable by public organizations alone? Specifically, how do stakeholder participation and the intensity of cross-level public–private collaborations shape public value outcomes such as wildfire-related property losses?
mechanism_summary: Building on Ostrom's governing principles for collective action and Hayek's notion of distributed local knowledge, the authors argue that self-organizing communities of vested and mutually vulnerable stakeholders can internalize coordination costs because private and public value overlap. Communities accumulate embedded local knowledge through trial-and-error learning, and cross-level collaborations with higher-level public organizations enable that local knowledge to be combined with "global" knowledge (e.g., scientific best practices and economies of scale), producing more contextualized and effective solutions to public problems. Broad stakeholder participation and extensive, active cross-level orchestration reinforce the benefits, though with diminishing returns once coordination costs outpace added knowledge.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances Ostrom-style collective action theory by explaining how and why self-organizing communities create public value alongside higher-level public organizations, rather than merely as substitutes for public governance. It reframes self-organizing communities as a distinct organizational form that functions as a repository of distributed local knowledge, and posits noncontractual cross-level collaborations as a governance mechanism that reduces knowledge (not just resource) constraints. In doing so, it enriches new stakeholder theory and the public–private collaborations literature by showing that the effectiveness of community-level public–private arrangements is contingent on the type and intensity of cross-level engagement.
practical_implication: For policy, the results support distributed, polycentric governance: local self-organizing with active orchestration of higher-level public agencies tends to outperform top-down application of homogenous best practices, with implications for wildfire management and for public education, water, sewage, roads, waste, healthcare, and public safety. For practice, managers and public officials can leverage noncontractual, relational governance mechanisms to mobilize community-based organizing, even when formal contracts are incomplete or infeasible due to high coordination costs.
limitations: The quasi-experimental and 2SLS-DiD design does not fully rule out endogeneity from unobservable reasons some communities face higher fire risk, though the likely direction of bias runs against the findings. Property losses are a limited proxy for public value since they combine public and private structures and do not capture all wildfire impacts. The authors cannot directly measure knowledge or knowledge flows and therefore cannot rule out alternative mechanisms such as improved coordination, incentive alignment, or richer resource environments.
future_research: Future work should test the boundary conditions of the theory in other contexts (e.g., education, water, sanitation, healthcare) and where community engagement or information asymmetries are less pronounced. Scholars could unpack the relative importance and interplay of stakeholder engagement, cross-level collaborations, and subsequent decision-making and resource use, and examine the specific content and mechanisms of knowledge dissemination within self-organizing communities. Finally, researchers could dig into communities' histories and leadership structures to explain the origins and evolution of self-organizing.
abstract: We build on extant theories of collective action and public–private collaborations to unpack the distinctive role self-organizing communities may play alongside other organizations in the creation of public value. We suggest that, when communities selforganize to address a common goal and, in particular, when they collaborate with higher-level public organizations, they discover more and better opportunities to reduce market frictions. Using an original longitudinal dataset of community-level fire prevention plans in Northern California from 1998 to 2018, we find that communities taking local actions and coordinating them with state and federal organizations manage wildfires better and experience fewer property losses than communities that do not organize. The number of stakeholders participating in these collaborations and the extent of the collaborations across hierarchical levels reinforce this effect. We conjecture that one possible mechanism explaining why self-organizing and cross-level collaborations tend to yield positive outcomes may be because they allow stakeholders to benefit from otherwise idle local knowledge. Unpacking the role played by self-organizing communities and their cross-level collaborations is important because it may enable contextualized solutions and prevent the potentially ineffective application of homogenous practices to heterogeneous problems, even in the absence of formal contracts.
apa_citation: Stein, D., & Minniti, M. (2025). Distributed knowledge and the creation of public value: Community-based organizing and wildfire management in Northern California. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(5), 1000-1030. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.1331

title: Temporary Regulations and Institutional Change: Insights from the Brazilian World Cup Experience
methods: Qualitative historical case study with abductive analysis; 52 semi-structured interviews (professionals and fans), 611 newspaper articles (1995-2023), 66 legal/governmental documents, industry reports, FIFA documents, and participant observation at eight matches; open, axial, and abductive coding following Gioia methodology and Langley process approaches to build a process model of institutional change.
industry: Professional football/soccer and beer industries
units: Institutional actors surrounding beer regulation in stadiums (beer producers, stadium security, public administrators, journalists, academics, activists, football fans, legislators)
n_sample: 52 interviews (31 professionals, 21 fans); 611 newspaper articles; 66 legal documents; 8 observed matches
authors: Bitektine, Alex; Dolbec, Pierre-Yann; Martins, Michele; Kruidenier, Stijn
theories: institutional learning; institutional theory; institutional work; practice theory
keywords: counter-normative practice; institutional change; institutional learning; institutional maintenance; institutional work; practice variation; process model; temporary regulations
research_question: How and why can temporary regulatory changes—regulations adopted for a limited time and not expected to continue beyond an established end date—lead to lasting institutional change, even when their initial introduction conflicts with prevailing societal understandings and actors involved have no intent to transform institutions?
mechanism_summary: A temporary regulation creates a bounded time window in which a counter-normative practice can be performed and observed at scale. Because actors expect the practice to revert when the regulation lapses, they tolerate it and engage in institutional maintenance work (training, standardizing, gentrifying participants, discursively framing) to contain anticipated negative outcomes. Successfully containing these outcomes unintendedly generates new societal understandings about the practice's risks and viability—a form of institutional learning distinct from socialization to conformity. Interested actors then leverage these new understandings to challenge the old regulation and push for permanent change, resolving three sequential tensions between regulations, understandings, and practices.
theoretical_contribution: The paper identifies a previously unexplored path of institutional change through temporary regulations, developing a process model in which maintenance work—typically a force for stability—unintendedly drives change. It extends institutional work scholarship by showing how practice deviations can occur even in highly visible, tightly controlled settings when temporary regulations create bounded time periods shielded from maintenance pressures. It also reframes institutional learning as learning about "how things can be done differently," distinct from socialization-based learning, positioning it as a critical precursor to institutional change.
practical_implication: Temporary regulations can be a valuable policy tool for reducing uncertainty, enabling institutional learning, and complementing experimentalist governance approaches in domains facing polycrisis conditions such as climate change, AI, and pandemics. Policymakers can pilot counter-normative arrangements (e.g., emission standards, work-from-home policies) under time-limited rules, observe outcomes, and use accumulated learning to design durable solutions. However, temporary regulations can also be misused to establish states of exception and erode democratic institutions, so safeguards are essential.
limitations: The study builds theory from a single context (beer regulations in Brazilian stadiums around the 2014 World Cup), so transferability requires care. The authors identify five boundary conditions (resistance must be overcome, broad observability, favorable perceived outcomes, generation of novel understandings, and legal/political opportunity for renegotiation) whose generalizability requires empirical validation. The qualitative historical design limits causal inference and depends on retrospective accounts and media sources of varying credibility.
future_research: Future work should explore how temporariness shapes actors' willingness to tolerate counter-normative arrangements, empirically validate the boundary conditions and examine varying durations and visibility levels of temporary regulations, and investigate how "success" is socially constructed when actors lack shared interests, coordination, or power. Researchers should also further develop the underexplored role of institutional learning—beyond maintenance-oriented socialization—in driving institutional change.
abstract: While institutional theory has extensively examined how durable regulations contribute to institutional change, little is known about how temporary regulations—pervasive in modern societies—can transform institutions. This study examines how and why temporary regulations can lead to lasting institutional change by analyzing Brazil’s temporary legalization of beer sales and consumption in stadiums during the 2014 FIFA World Cup. Using extensive qualitative data, we develop a process model explaining how the introduction of a temporary regulation creates an opportunity for society to experience a counter-normative practice and learn from it. During this period, multiple institutional actors—while pursuing objectives far removed from considerations of institutional change—perform various types of institutional work to contain the practice’s expected negative outcomes. This institutional work unintendedly generates new societal understandings about the practice’s risks and consequences, which interested actors then leverage to achieve lasting institutional change. We advance institutional theory by identifying an unexplored path of institutional change through temporary regulations, demonstrating how temporary regulations can enable practice variations and institutional learning, and revealing how institutional maintenance work can drive change. Our insights suggest that temporary regulations can be valuable policy tools for reducing uncertainty and enabling learning about alternative institutional arrangements.
apa_citation: Bitektine, A., Dolbec, P.-Y., Martins, M., & Kruidenier, S. (2025). Temporary regulations and institutional change: Insights from the Brazilian World Cup experience. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(6), 1168-1206. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.1135

title: Bearing, Belittling, or Befriending Doubt: Understanding Doubt’s Generative versus Corrosive Effect on Entrepreneurs’ Early-Stage Idea Validation
methods: Inductive longitudinal qualitative study using a grounded theory approach (Strauss & Corbin, 1990, 1998); weekly follow-up interviews with early-stage entrepreneurs throughout a 10-week incubation program across six consecutive cohorts (2018-2021), complemented by intake interviews, participant observation at trainings and pitches, and archival data (training materials, newsletters, social media); open and axial coding in NVivo with constant comparative method, visual mapping (Langley, 1999), and iteration between emerging categories and theory on goal striving, self-regulation, and attribution to develop a doubt regulation process model.
industry: Mixed early-stage entrepreneurial ventures (art & crafts, medical, film, sustainability, entertainment, architectural design, education, industrial design, AI, IT & fashion, HR & IT, digital transformation, tech, marketing, agriculture, blockchain, healthcare)
units: Individual early-stage entrepreneurs in an incubation program
n_sample: 308 interviews with 35 entrepreneurs; primary sample of 29 entrepreneurs with 5-14 weekly interviews each
authors: Cholakova, Magdalena; Yalcin, Umut; Andriopoulos, Constantine
theories: attribution theory; opportunity evaluation theory; pragmatist philosophy of doubt; self-regulation and goal-striving theory; social cognitive theory
keywords: attribution theory; curtailing orientation; doubt; doubt regulation; early-stage entrepreneurship; goal striving; idea validation; idea-related doubt; mastering orientation; self-desirability doubt; self-feasibility doubt; self-regulation; yearning orientation
research_question: (1) How do different types of doubt emerge in the course of validating one’s entrepreneurial idea and (2) how and why does one’s response to these doubts play a corrosive or a generative role in one’s ability to identify the value of the idea and in sustaining commitment to this goal?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: Entrepreneurs' perceived lack of (or positive) goal progress toward surfacing a proof of concept during idea validation in an incubation program
- Attribution patterns: Internal-unstable-controllable (insufficient effort); internal-stable-uncontrollable (lack of ability); external-stable (task difficulty/others' control)
- Focal doubt types: Idea-related doubts; self-feasibility doubts (about one's abilities); self-desirability doubts (about one's motivation)
- Doubt regulation practices: Embracing (engaging with doubt and working toward improvement); downplaying (dismissing doubt as unimportant); normalizing (treating doubt as "part of the game" and emotionally tolerating it)
- Emergent orientations: Mastering (sustained engagement and skill-stretching); curtailing (rationing of effort and procrastination); yearning (gradual disengagement accompanied by lingering attachment to the goal)

As entrepreneurs iterate through weekly idea validation work, perceptions of goal progress become salient and trigger causal attributions that make different doubts focal. Effort-based attributions direct attention to idea-related doubts, which entrepreneurs embrace through active inquiry, producing refined understanding of the idea's value and a mastering orientation. Ability-based attributions surface self-feasibility doubts that become ruminative and hijack attention from idea work; entrepreneurs normalize these as part of the entrepreneurial experience, eroding progress and yielding a yearning orientation. External attributions lead entrepreneurs to downplay idea doubts and dismiss disconfirming feedback, sustaining self-desirability beliefs but producing a curtailing orientation. Recurring attributions across weekly cycles solidify these feedback loops between doubt regulation and perceived progress.
theoretical_contribution: The paper empirically demonstrates that self-feasibility and self-desirability doubts resurface well after entrepreneurial action has commenced, challenging the assumption in opportunity evaluation and goal-striving research that such doubts are resolved prior to action. Drawing on self-regulation, goal-striving, and attribution theory, it develops a doubt regulation process model that integrates the generative (Locke et al., 2008) and corrosive (McMullen & Shepherd, 2006) perspectives on doubt by specifying the conditions and mechanisms (embracing, downplaying, normalizing) under which doubt plays a generative versus corrosive role. It also exposes the limits to entrepreneurial motivation during idea validation, showing how perceived progress (or its absence) shapes sustained commitment and three distinct orientations—mastering, curtailing, and yearning—toward further effort.
practical_implication: The findings inform the design of interventions in early-stage incubation programs aimed at supporting entrepreneurs' doubt regulation. Interventions that build entrepreneurial self-efficacy by supporting effort-based (internal-controllable) attributions for outcomes, foster growth mindsets, and tailor experimentation programs to entrepreneurs' decision-making characteristics may prevent premature disengagement. Program designers should be aware that both over-confidence (downplaying) and self-defeating rumination (normalizing) can undermine idea validation, and should create conditions that help entrepreneurs embrace rather than avoid or internalize their doubts.
limitations: The study relies on a sample of mostly novice early-stage entrepreneurs drawn from a single European incubation program, which may limit transferability to more experienced founders or different institutional contexts. The triggers for goal progress reflections may be artifacts of milestones set by the incubator, and the authors could not fully observe within-person variation in attributions across settings. The longitudinal weekly-interview design, while rich, entails trade-offs between frequent data collection and depth of reflection in each session.
future_research: Future research should examine whether entrepreneurs who embrace idea doubts still fall prey to classic decision-making biases over longer horizons, and how effective reasoning strategies under uncertainty can support doubt regulation over time. Scholars should investigate the role of psychological ownership reappraisal, inter- and intra-individual comparisons, and emotions accompanying surprise and discovery in triggering and shaping doubt responses. Intervention-based designs in the pre-entry period could explore how doubt regulation approaches can be influenced to prevent premature idea abandonment, and how entrepreneurial self-efficacy and growth mindsets moderate attribution patterns and subsequent doubt regulation.
abstract: Doubt in the early-stage entrepreneurial process is pervasive. However, we argue that our understanding of the types of doubt individuals experience during entrepreneurial action— and how their responses to these doubts affect their ability to determine the value of their ideas and remain committed to this goal—remains incomplete. When entrepreneurs set out to validate their ideas, doubt is typically framed in one of two ways: as corrosive or as generative. In both perspectives, the focus is on idea-related doubts, overlooking the possibility that once entrepreneurial action commences, self-related doubts about one’s abilities or motivation may resurface. Furthermore, while the generative approach provides valuable insights into how entrepreneurs can engage with idea doubts, it tells us little about why some individuals can do so while others may not. To address these questions, we followed early-stage entrepreneurs weekly during an incubation program. We found that they experienced not only idea-related but also self-related doubts. Their responses to these doubts— namely embracing, downplaying, or normalizing them—shaped their ability to make progress toward their goal and influenced their commitment to it. Ultimately, this commitment was underpinned by the emergence of three distinct orientations toward expending further goal-directed effort: mastering, curtailing, or yearning.
apa_citation: Cholakova, M., Yalcin, U., & Andriopoulos, C. (2025). Bearing, belittling, or befriending doubt: Understanding doubt’s generative versus corrosive effect on entrepreneurs’ early-stage idea validation. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(6), 1355-1379. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.1126

title: Constraints, Competencies, or Choices? How Lay Theories of the Gender Gap Impact Diversity Support
methods: Multimethod, intermediate-theory design across five studies: (1a) qualitative content coding of open-ended explanations (Prolific sample and online news comments); (1b) exploratory factor analysis on a 50-item lay-theory battery (CloudResearch); (2) partitioning-around-medoids cluster analysis of locus/controllability attributions; (3) preregistered two-wave correlational survey with regression and indirect-effect (mediation) analyses (Prolific); (4) preregistered experiment manipulating gender gap lay theories with condition contrasts, regression, and indirect-effect tests (CloudResearch).
industry: Working adults across industries (general online-panel samples); supplementary analysis of reader comments from U.S. national newspapers (New York Times, Wall Street Journal, Washington Post)
country: United States (online-panel working adults; U.S. newspapers)
time_period: Newspaper comments 2015-2020; primary data collection circa 2022-2023 (paper accepted 2025)
units: Individual working adults (employees and managers); secondary units: online reader comments on gender-gap news articles
n_sample: Study 1a Sample A n=300 (Prolific) plus Sample B n=779 reader comments from 34 articles; Study 1b n=361 (CloudResearch); Study 2 n=190; Study 3 n=450 (two-wave Prolific); Study 4 n~680 (CloudResearch experimental)
authors: Flynn, Elinor
theories: Weiner's attributional theory of motivation; attribution theory; lay theory perspective; sensemaking
keywords: attribution theory; controllability; dispositional factors; diversity initiative support; diversity resistance; gender gap; gender inequality; lay theories; locus of causality; organizational barriers; perceived responsibility; personal choice
research_question: Do employees form lay theories about why the gender gap in career attainment persists, and if so, how do the locus and controllability attributions implied by these lay theories shape perceived responsibility for the gap and, in turn, support for organizational diversity initiatives?
mechanism_summary:
- Competing predictors / controls: Self-interest (respondent gender) and ideology (political ideology and diversity ideologies) — included as existing predictors against which lay-theory effects are tested for incremental validity, not as moderators
- Comparative condition: Target group of the gap (gender vs. racial; Study 3) used to contrast lay-theory endorsement across inequality domains

Drawing on classic attribution theory (Weiner), Flynn argues that each lay theory carries distinct causal-dimensional implications. The organizational barriers theory locates causes as external and uncontrollable, so women are judged less responsible and diversity initiatives are seen as a legitimate corrective — increasing support. The dispositional factors theory locates causes as internal but not clearly controllable, modestly increasing attributions of responsibility and dampening support. The personal choice theory locates causes as both internal and controllable, maximally increasing perceived responsibility and most strongly undermining diversity initiative support. Because gender gaps are attributed to personal choice more than racial gaps are, this mechanism helps explain resistance that cannot be accounted for by demographics or ideology alone.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reframes individual-level diversity support from a self-interest/ideology account to an attributional-sensemaking account, introducing gender gap lay theories as a distinct and malleable antecedent. It reconciles evidence that women are now equally educated and perceived as equally competent with persistent resistance to diversity policy by showing that inequality is increasingly attributed to personal choice rather than competence, rendering gaps as reflections of preferences rather than inequities needing intervention. It also extends attributional theory in organizations by demonstrating that internal causes vary in perceived controllability (traits vs. choices), and shows that lay theories of inequality differ by target group (gender vs. race).
practical_implication: Organizational leaders seeking to increase diversity initiative support should assess and address employee lay theories about the gender gap, particularly the belief that gaps reflect women’s personal choices. Leader rhetoric and organizational policies (e.g., ideal-worker norms, parental-leave design) shape which lay theories take root, so leaders should frame inequality in ways that surface external and structural causes rather than emphasizing women’s exits or differential preferences. Diversity training should explicitly bridge the gap between lay and academic understandings of how "choices" are constrained.
limitations: Diversity support was measured globally, so the author cannot test whether specific initiative types (e.g., diversity slates vs. leadership training) moderate the lay-theory effect. Qualitative boundaries between categories (e.g., personal choice vs. work-family demands, self-advocacy vs. backlash avoidance) are hard to delineate precisely, and some attributions may reflect barriers rather than dispositions. The Study 4 manipulation failed to reliably shift organizational barriers attributions and produced unexpected positive associations between the dispositional/choice conditions and support after controlling for attributions, suggesting reactance or interaction with individual differences. Controllability effects were also less consistent than theorized across studies.
future_research: Scholars should examine whether specific types of diversity initiatives elicit different lay-theory-driven reactions, and whether ambivalence toward diversity arises from interactions between lay theories and self-interest or diversity ideologies. Future work should study how gender gap lay theories form within organizations, including the roles of leader rhetoric, ideal-worker norms, and observed workplace dynamics in shaping them. Research could also investigate whether highlighting the role of significant others and structural constraints in shaping women’s "choices" can shift attributions, and extend lay-theory analyses to other inequality domains and non-Western cultural contexts where agency is construed differently.
abstract: Resistance to diversity initiatives can undermine their effectiveness. I advance insight into diversity support by identifying lay theories about why the gender gap persists. I identify three gender gap lay theories, which differ in the implied locus and control women have over the gap. These attributions shape women’s perceived responsibility, which, in turn, undermines diversity initiative support. Attributing the gap to organizational barriers is positively associated with diversity support because it implies the causes are external and uncontrollable by women. Attributing the gap to dispositional factors (e.g., traits) is negatively associated with diversity support because it implies the causes are internal to women (but not necessarily controllable). Finally, attributing the gap to women’s personal choices is negatively associated with diversity support because it implies the causes are both internal to and controllable by women; thus, it is most undermining overall. I test these ideas in five multimethod studies, which generally support my theory. I also find gender gaps are attributed to personal choice more than racial gaps are. Overall, this project suggests that, beyond demographics and ideology, sensemaking about the causes of gender inequality and, in particular, the belief that it is due to personal choice, significantly undermines diversity support.
apa_citation: Flynn, E. (2025). Constraints, competencies, or choices? How lay theories of the gender gap impact diversity support. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(6), 1380-1406. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0625

title: Passing the Baton to the 24th Editorial Team
methods: Editorial reflection; no empirical design. Narrative summary of the 23rd AMJ editorial team's operations, innovations, and digital outreach activities over a three-year term.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Gruber, M.
keywords: digital outreach; editorial transition; journal innovations; journal operations; paper development workshops; research methods; transformational era
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary: Not reported in paper
theoretical_contribution: As an editorial reflecting on the 23rd AMJ editorial term, the piece does not make a theoretical contribution in the conventional sense. It documents how the editorial team pursued the theme "Our transformational times and their implications for management and organizations," built out a methods-oriented manuscript portfolio (including new research methods articles designed to advance theory), and launched digital initiatives to extend AMJ's reach and discourse with scholarly audiences. It positions editorial stewardship itself as a strategic activity for shaping what counts as rigorous, relevant management scholarship in an era of rapid technological and methodological change.
practical_implication: The editorial signals to prospective authors that AMJ welcomes studies employing machine-learning methods, novel data sources, and work on sustainability, technology/AI, and inclusion, and that fast, developmental turnaround (close to a 58-day average for fully reviewed first submissions) remains a core service commitment. It also points authors to practical tools developed during the term, including the AMJ Management Research Canvas and a series of editorials on research design, multimethod research, interdisciplinary collaboration, and qualitative and quantitative empirics. Finally, it encourages perseverance and openness to peer feedback, noting that many rejected AMJ submissions ultimately find strong outlets elsewhere.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editorial invites the incoming 24th editorial team, led by Quinetta Roberson, to continue expanding AMJ's global footprint and digital presence, to further integrate new methods (including machine learning) into management scholarship, and to advance work on sustainability, AI and technology, and inclusion as central concerns of the transformational era. It also suggests further attention to regions with historically lower AMJ activity identified through the interactive submission map.
abstract: FROM THE EDITORS PASSING THE BATON TO THE 24TH EDITORIAL TEAM

title: Understanding I-Deals through the Social Ledger Lens: The Role of Trust and Hindrance Networks
methods: Two-study, multisource, time-lagged field design with multilevel path analysis in Mplus; employee-reported i-deals and coworker-nominated trust and hindrance ties at Time 1, supervisor-rated task performance at Time 2; group-mean-centered individual predictors, grand-mean-centered group-level moderators (trust/hindrance network density), Monte Carlo resampling (20,000 draws) for (moderated) indirect effects, and relative weight analysis (RWA) to test negative asymmetry (Hypothesis 3).
industry: Study 1: midsize restaurants (day-shift servers); Study 2: diverse white-collar organizations
country: Study 1: United States (Southwestern city); Study 2: Iran
time_period: Two measurement waves separated by approximately one month (Time 1 employee survey, Time 2 supervisor performance ratings)
units: Employees nested within workgroups reporting to the same supervisor
n_sample: Study 1: 305 employees nested in 57 supervisors/workgroups (groups of 4-15, mean size 6.39); Study 2: 502 employees nested in 101 supervisors
authors: Jahantab, Farid; Anand, Smriti; Vidyarthi, Prajya R.; Erdogan, Berrin
theories: social comparison theory; social exchange theory; social ledger model; social network theory
keywords: hindrance network; i-deals; idiosyncratic deals; negative asymmetry; network centrality; network density; social comparison; social ledger; task performance; trust network
research_question: How does an employee's position in the workgroup's trust and hindrance networks — and the density of those networks — shape the employee's ability to obtain i-deals, and, through i-deals, their task performance? More specifically, is the negative (hindrance) side of the social ledger more strongly related to i-deals than the positive (trust) side, and how do contrasting network densities moderate these effects?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on Labianca and Brass's social ledger model, the authors argue that being central in the trust network increases an employee's confidence, reputation, and access to private information, lowers the leader's perceived social cost of granting differential treatment, and therefore raises the likelihood of obtaining i-deals. Centrality in the hindrance network does the opposite: it signals social liability, dampens coworker and leader willingness to support differentiated treatment, and suppresses i-deals attainment. Because negative ties are rarer and more diagnostic than positive ties, hindrance centrality exerts a stronger effect than trust centrality (negative asymmetry). Integrating social comparison theory, the authors further argue that a central employee's ties stand out more when they contrast with the prevailing network context — trust centrality is amplified when hindrance ties are dense, and hindrance centrality is amplified when trust ties are dense. Via social exchange, i-deals recipients reciprocate with higher task performance, producing indirect effects of both network positions on performance through i-deals.
theoretical_contribution: The study integrates i-deals research with the social network perspective, reframing i-deals as an individual-within-workgroup meso-level phenomenon rather than a purely individual or dyadic one, and identifying the social structure of trust and hindrance ties as a previously overlooked antecedent. It provides rare empirical evidence for the social ledger model's negative-asymmetry proposition by showing that hindrance centrality explains substantially more variance in i-deals than trust centrality. It also advances the social comparison perspective in network research by theorizing and testing how the density of opposing ties (the network context) moderates the effects of individual centrality on valued organizational outcomes.
practical_implication: Managers and HR professionals should recognize that access to i-deals is shaped by employees' informal network positions, not only their individual traits or LMX quality. Because hindrance ties carry outsized consequences, organizations should monitor workgroup climate, train employees to build trust-based ties, and actively minimize avoidance/hindrance ties when composing teams. Managers can also use carefully designed i-deals — coupled with transparent communication to the wider team — to help employees in difficult personal circumstances (e.g., caregiving) move out of hindrance-network positions, while being mindful of contextual pressures against such allocations.
limitations: The time-lagged two-wave design cannot rule out reverse causality between network position and i-deals, and is not a substitute for longitudinal or experimental evidence. The authors could not directly test the social comparison mechanism theorized to underlie the density moderation. The Study 1 hindrance measure may have confounded avoidance with hindrance, and several potentially relevant employee-, leader-, and group-level controls (e.g., personality, LLX, team potency, turnover rate, ties outside the workgroup) were not included.
future_research: Future work should use experimental or genuinely longitudinal designs to establish the causal direction between network centrality and i-deals, and should directly measure the social comparison processes theorized to drive density moderation. Scholars are encouraged to examine additional individual-, leader-, and group-level moderators, other outcomes (employee innovation, creativity, CWB, turnover), and to investigate how social networks develop and how i-deals are dispensed in virtual and multicultural workgroups where interaction norms differ.
abstract: Individually negotiated customized work arrangements or idiosyncratic deals (i-deals) are an increasingly common practice in contemporary organizations. We propose that these deals are created and implemented in the social context of the workgroup and that one’s position in the web of social relationships serves as a strategic advantage or barrier to accessing i-deals. Drawing on the social ledger model, we propose that being central in the trust network is positively associated with i-deals, whereas being central in the hindrance network is negatively associated with i-deals, with indirect effects on employee performance. Further, we predict that i-deals are affected more strongly by centrality in the hindrance network than by centrality in the trust network. Finally, we theorize that trust network density strengthens the negative relationship between hindrance network centrality and i-deals and that hindrance network density strengthens the positive relationship between trust network centrality and i-deals. Results of multilevel modeling and relative importance analyses in two field studies employing data from 305 employees and 57 supervisors in the United States and 502 employees and 101 supervisors in Iran provided support for our model. Implications and future directions for social network and i-deals research are discussed.
apa_citation: Jahantab, F., Anand, S., Vidyarthi, P. R., & Erdogan, B. (2025). Understanding i-deals through the social ledger lens: The role of trust and hindrance networks. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(6), 1207-1234. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0745

title: Diversity Incentives Can Increase Women’s Aspirations to Lead
methods: Preregistered field experiment (Study 1) with female employees at a global telecommunications and engineering firm, randomly assigning employees (and their managers) to a control, diversity-goal-alone, or diversity-goal-and-incentives condition embedded in annual performance-review emails; outcome was a binary self-reported willingness to manage others, analyzed with linear-probability/OLS regressions (intention-to-treat, with robustness checks). Three preregistered online experiments (Studies 2, 3A, 3B) on Prolific and MTurk using incentive-compatible and Likert-scale leadership-aspiration measures, analyzed with t-tests, regression, and mediation/moderation tests of expected managerial support as the proposed mechanism.
industry: Telecommunications and engineering (Study 1); online labor-market participants across occupations (Studies 2, 3A, 3B)
country: European and Latin American offices of a global firm (Study 1); United States (online studies)
time_period: Field experiment launched in December 2022 during the firm's annual performance-review cycle
units: Individual female employees (Study 1); individual women and men recruited online (Studies 2-3B)
n_sample: Study 1 n=2,035 female employees; Studies 2, 3A, 3B total n=2,495; plus an additional OSF mediation study n=925
authors: Kirgios, E. L.; Chang, E. H.
theories: identity threat theory; signaling theory; social role theory
keywords: costly signals; diversity incentives; field experiment; gender diversity goals; identity threat; leadership aspirations; managerial support; signaling theory; women in leadership
research_question: Will organizational gender diversity incentives increase or decrease female employees' leadership aspirations, and through what mechanism?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on economic signaling theory, the authors argue that because diversity incentives are a literally costly organizational commitment, women interpret them as a credible signal that managers will support their advancement. This raises women's expectations of sponsorship, helping them clear the confidence threshold they require before being willing to state leadership aspirations, and thereby increases their willingness to express such aspirations. The effect is stronger when prior signals of managerial support are weak, consistent with the idea that costly positive signals are most informative in environments otherwise read as non-supportive.
theoretical_contribution: The paper shifts consensus on identity-conscious diversity interventions by showing that, contrary to identity-threat and backlash-focused accounts, communicating diversity incentives can increase—not depress—women's leadership aspirations. It brings an economic signaling perspective (emphasizing signal cost) into the diversity-initiatives literature and contributes to work on gender and leadership by foregrounding women as strategic actors who update their aspirations based on supply-side-relevant organizational signals, and by illuminating anticipated managerial sponsorship as a key mediating mechanism.
practical_implication: Organizations seeking to close gender gaps in leadership attainment can raise women's willingness to express leadership aspirations by instituting and communicating diversity incentives, rather than only trying to change women's self-assessments. The authors caution, however, that organizations should be prepared to follow through on diversity commitments and to have advancement structures in place, since insincere commitments or raising aspirations without real opportunities may harm women's belonging and trust.
limitations: The field experiment was conducted in a single, relatively male-dominated engineering firm with low baseline gender diversity, which may amplify the benefits of diversity-incentive signals and limit generalizability to other industries or more gender-balanced firms. The authors did not collect data on actual promotions, so they cannot assess long-run or behavioral outcomes, and they lacked field data on male employees' reactions. They also rely on short-run outcomes and a single operationalization of leadership aspirations.
future_research: Future work should examine heterogeneity across field settings to identify when diversity-incentive effects generalize, study long-run and separating-equilibrium dynamics including whether organizations follow through on commitments, test effects on majority-group (male) employees in the field, and explore demand-side consequences such as whether diversity incentives eventually shift managers' evaluations and promotion decisions. Researchers should also probe when diversity goals alone function as costly signals and how manager gender conditions women's responses to diversity initiatives.
abstract: To boost diversity, organizations are increasingly using “diversity incentives,” or payouts for managers or executives dependent on progress toward a specific diversity goal. Diversity incentives can affect both actors—managers incentivized to meet the goal— and targets—marginalized group members who are the focus of the incentivized goal. Whereas the effects of incentives on actors are well documented, it is unclear how targets will be affected. We examine how gender diversity incentives affect women’s aspirations to lead. On one hand, diversity incentives may generate identity threat and concerns about backlash among women; on the other, they may be viewed as costly signals of organizational support for women’s leadership aspirations. A preregistered field experiment (n 5 2,035) shows that communicating the existence of organizational diversity incentives increases women’s aspirations to lead by 11.3% relative to sharing a goal-free diversity statement and by 11.7% relative to communicating diversity goals alone. We replicate these findings across three preregistered experiments (total n 5 2,495) and provide evidence that diversity incentives increase women’s expectations of receiving sponsorship from their managers, thereby increasing their willingness to state leadership aspirations. Our findings contribute to our understanding of the drivers of female leadership aspirations.
apa_citation: Kirgios, E. L., & Chang, E. H. (2025). Diversity incentives can increase women’s aspirations to lead. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(6), 1328-1354. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2024.0691

title: Emotional Signaling: How Helpers’ Emotional Expressions Affect Attributions of Motives, Relationship Quality, and Reciprocation
methods: Multi-study experimental design: (1) pilot study rating nine discrete emotions on social engagement and frequency in helping; (2) Study 1, preregistered critical incident recall experiment with random assignment to four emotion conditions (pride, contempt, gratitude, sympathy); (3) Study 2, laboratory experiment with video-recorded confederate manipulating the four discrete emotions during a museum case-study helping task, measuring relationship quality and three behavioral reciprocation decisions (raffle tickets, gift-card allocation, subject-pool recommendation); (4) Study 3, preregistered vignette experiment with a control condition, in-basket reward and time-allocation reciprocation tasks, and perceived morality/compassion as alternative mediators. Analyses used path analysis, bias-corrected bootstrapping for indirect effects, confirmatory factor analysis, and hierarchical linear models for manipulation checks.
industry: Mixed (working adults across industries in pilot and Study 1; undergraduate business students in Study 2; working adults in Study 3)
country: United States (Study 1, 2, 3); United States and Canada (pilot study)
units: Individuals recalling or imagining receiving workplace help from a coworker expressing discrete emotions
n_sample: Pilot: 266 working adults; Study 1: 221 working adults (Prolific Academic); Study 2: 86 undergraduate business students; Study 3: 305 working adults (Prolific Academic; 243 excluding control condition)
authors: Lee, Stephen H.; Johnson, Michael D.
theories: attribution theory; emotions as social information (EASI); social engagement-disengagement framework; social exchange theory; social-functional approach to emotions
keywords: attribution of motives; discrete emotional expressions; emotions as social information; instrumental motives; interpersonal helping; prosocial motives; reciprocation; relationship quality; social disengagement; social engagement
research_question: When and how do recipients of interpersonal help attribute different motives to the helper, and how do the helper’s discrete emotional expressions—varying in their socially engaging versus disengaging functions—shape attributions of prosocial versus instrumental motives, relationship quality, and reciprocal responses to help?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the emotions as social information (EASI) framework and Markus and Kitayama’s social engagement–disengagement dimension, the authors theorize that helpers’ discrete emotional expressions provide recipients with salient, accessible social information about the helper’s orientation toward relational harmony or separation. Gratitude and sympathy signal engagement and lead recipients to infer prosocial (other-oriented) motives, whereas pride and contempt signal disengagement and lead recipients to infer instrumental (self-interested) motives. These inferred motives then shape the recipient’s perceived relationship quality and willingness to reciprocate, with only the attribution of prosocial motives consistently mediating downstream relational and reciprocal outcomes.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances social exchange theory by challenging the presumption of reciprocity and showing that reciprocation depends on an interpretive process grounded in social information embedded in the helper’s emotional expressions, rather than solely on the material benefits exchanged. It reintegrates an affective perspective into the workplace helping literature, reconciling prior inconsistent findings on emotionality and valence by introducing the social engagement–disengagement dimension of discrete emotions. It also contributes to attribution theory by identifying discrete emotional expressions as a salient and accessible source of information recipients use to infer prosocial versus instrumental motives when traditional reputational or normative cues are unavailable.
practical_implication: Leaders and employees should recognize that the emotions they express while helping carry significant social information that shapes how recipients attribute motives and reciprocate. Helpers can benefit from deep-acting regulation and perspective-taking to authentically express socially engaging emotions (gratitude, sympathy) that strengthen reciprocal bonds, while leaders can cultivate affective cultures that normalize the expression of engaging emotions during helping. Recipients of help may also benefit from introspectively reappraising emotional signals when maintaining a work relationship is practically valuable.
limitations: First, Study 1’s critical incident design carries a risk of common method bias because focal variables were measured from the same source at the same time, though separation, varied scale formats, and behavioral in-basket decisions in Studies 2 and 3 partially mitigated this concern. Second, the research was conducted primarily in controlled settings and cannot establish the real-world frequency of these emotional expressions during helping. Third, the studies focused on anticipatory, task-related helping, so findings may not generalize to reactive help or to non-task domains such as emotion helping or helping with personal problems.
future_research: Future research should examine broader relational and temporal contexts in which help is exchanged, the role of emotional regulation and its personal costs for helpers, how virtual and text-based communication affects perceptions of helpers’ emotions, and how combinations of multiple emotions are interpreted by recipients. Scholars should also extend the framework to additional discrete emotions (e.g., pity, guilt, disgust, anger) to further develop the social engagement–disengagement dimension, and explore how recipients may reappraise emotional signals to sustain work relationships.
abstract: Research has shown that interpersonal helping offers many social benefits for the helper, yet these social benefits depend on the recipients’ responses. Whether helping is perceived favorably by recipients depends on the underlying motives they attribute to the helper, which raises the questions of when and how recipients may attribute different motives to helping. To explain when and how those receiving help make these attributions, we adopt a social-functional approach to emotions to examine the impact of the social information conveyed through discrete emotional expressions. We draw on the framework of emotions as social information to theorize that the helper’s emotional expressions act as signals of social engagement or disengagement that differentially impact the recipient’s attribution of motives and reactions to help. Across three studies, we find that helpers’ expressions of socially engaging emotions (gratitude and sympathy) lead to greater attributions of prosocial motives, whereas expressions of socially disengaging emotions (pride and contempt) lead to greater attributions of instrumental motives. We find that the effect of these expressed emotions on relationship quality and reciprocation toward the helper is mediated by the recipient’s attribution of prosocial motives but not by the attribution of instrumental motives.
apa_citation: Lee, S. H., & Johnson, M. D. (2025). Emotional signaling: How helpers’ emotional expressions affect attributions of motives, relationship quality, and reciprocation. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(6), 1266-1299. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.0313

title: Autonomous, Yet Interdependent: Designing Interfaces across Routine Clusters
methods: 12-month ethnographic single-case study of the reorganization of agile (Scrum) software development at a medium-sized high-tech manufacturing firm ('Technology Innovation Corporation'). Data comprise ~1,000 pages of fieldnotes and 122 hours of audio from 2-3 days/week of observation, 35 formal interviews (30 during fieldwork plus 5 follow-ups two years later), ~6,300 pages of documents and ~2,000 emails, and ~163,000 digital trace actions from Jira. Inductive process analysis in five steps: identifying routine clusters, constructing an event timeline of reorganization, classifying emerging interdependencies via Thompson's (1967) pooled/sequential/reciprocal typology, inductively coding four interface design practices, and modeling the dynamics linking reorganization, interdependencies, and interface design work across three analytical periods.
industry: High-tech manufacturing (agile software development for industrial machines such as cellphone-display cutters and laser battery-pack welders)
time_period: November 2016 - October 2017 (primary fieldwork), with trace data and follow-up interviews extending to 2021
units: Two agile (Scrum) software development teams ('Team Alpha' and 'Team Beta') embedded in one medium-sized firm ('Technology Innovation Corporation', a pseudonym)
n_sample: 2 teams (~10-12 developers, product owners, and Scrum masters in total) within 1 firm
authors: Mahringer, C. A.; Danner-Schröder, A.
theories: interdependence theory (Thompson 1967); organizational design; practice and process ontology; routine dynamics
keywords: Scrum; agile software development; autonomy; interdependence; interface design; organizational design; pooled, sequential, and reciprocal interdependence; resources; routine clusters; routine dynamics
research_question: The paper asks: (1) How do interdependencies emerge during the reorganization of routine clusters? (2) How does the process of designing interfaces to manage those interdependencies unfold?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering conditions: Reorganization of work into two semi-autonomous routine clusters through replication of routines across teams and the sharing of scarce resources (actors and artifacts such as software code).
- Key constructs / tensions: Autonomy aspiration vs. emergent interdependence; pooled, sequential, and reciprocal interdependencies (Thompson 1967); divisibility vs. indivisibility of shared resources; transitory vs. patterning interventions.
- Practices ("interface design work"): analyzing interdependencies, reconfiguring resources, mitigating interferences, and reconfiguring schedules — differentiated along two dimensions: durability (transitory vs. patterning) and design elements (artifacts vs. timing).
- Process / phases: (1) implementing the second cluster and discovering emergent pooled interdependencies; (2) iterative harmonization that escalates pooled interdependencies into sequential and, under time pressure, reciprocal ones; (3) stabilization of interfaces that allows the clusters to operate relatively autonomously.
- Emergent outcomes: Stabilized interfaces between the clusters and a mutually constitutive relationship between resourcing, patterning, and performing.

Because contemporary organizations operate in flux, interdependencies between routine clusters are not exogenously given but emerge as actors replicate routines and share resources across clusters. These emergent pooled interdependencies trigger "interface design work": actors analyze interdependencies and mitigate interferences through transitory adjustments within routine performances, while reconfiguring resources and reconfiguring schedules produce more durable patterning of interfaces. Harmonizing one interdependence often creates new and more complex ones — pooled interdependencies can escalate into sequential ones when reconfiguration work must precede regular work, and sequential interdependencies compress into reciprocal ones under time pressure. Through iterative cycles of creating and harmonizing interdependencies, routine participants themselves act as endogenous designers who eventually stabilize interfaces, disentangling the clusters.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances routine dynamics, interdependence, and organizational design research by reframing interface design as an endogenous, iterative process of creating and harmonizing emergent interdependencies, performed by routine participants rather than by detached exogenous architects. It uncovers "interface design work" as four distinct practices differentiated along two dimensions — durability (transitory vs. patterning) and design elements (artifacts vs. timing) — and theorizes a mutually constitutive relationship between resourcing, patterning, and performing. Third, it unpacks the role of resources in interface design, showing that sharing scarce resources generates pooled interdependencies that can escalate into sequential and reciprocal forms, such that disentangling routine clusters may paradoxically produce heightened forms of entanglement before stable autonomy is achieved.
practical_implication: Managers reorganizing work into autonomous units (e.g., agile teams, project-based structures) should not treat interface design as a one-off, up-front architectural task: because interdependencies between clusters emerge as routines are performed and resources are shared, routine participants themselves need the latitude and tooling to act as endogenous interface designers. Practically, the study points to four concrete practices — analyzing interdependencies, reconfiguring resources, mitigating interferences, and reconfiguring schedules — that teams can deploy, combining transitory fixes within ongoing routines with more durable patterning of shared artifacts and timing. Managers should also anticipate that sharing scarce, indivisible resources can escalate pooled interdependencies into sequential and reciprocal ones under time pressure, so synchronizing release cadences and reserving slack for joint reconfiguration work is essential to actually realize the autonomy that reorganization is meant to create.
limitations: The authors acknowledge that their theoretical insights rest on a single 12-month ethnographic case study of two agile (Scrum) software development teams in one high-tech manufacturing firm, so the findings are bounded by this particular context. The setting is one in which both teams valued autonomy and operated with high mutual respect, shared scarce (rather than freely copiable) resources, used Scrum routines that combine task decomposition with regular collective-reflection meetings, and involved only two clusters that replicated essentially the same routines. The authors flag each of these features as scope conditions rather than general properties, noting in particular that the absence of power asymmetries and the presence of Scrum-enabled reflection may have shaped how interface design work unfolded in ways that would not necessarily transfer to other settings.
future_research: The authors identify four boundary conditions inviting further work: (a) settings with more than two clusters sharing resources, or with orchestrated resource allocation across multiple clusters, and settings where resource allocation shifts over time; (b) reorganizations in which autonomy is less central, or which involve clusters with entirely different routines or temporal rhythms, potentially collapsing into a single cluster; (c) contexts where routines are not based on Scrum or task decomposition, and where routines afford less collective reflection, to clarify how task decomposition and reflection shape interface design; and (d) cases of lower mutual respect or power asymmetries between clusters, where breakdowns or alternative resolutions might emerge.
abstract: This paper examines the process of designing interfaces between routine clusters in a world in flux in which interdependencies are emergent. We reveal that designing interfaces is an endogenous and iterative process of creating and harmonizing interdependencies. In our ethnographic study of the reorganization of agile software development, actors implemented a second routine cluster, which they envisioned to work autonomously. Because the teams shared resources, however, they created interdependencies across the two routine clusters that challenged their autonomy. Our findings contribute to research on routine dynamics, interdependence, and organizations design. They do so, first, by showing how the dynamics of designing interfaces are driven by the emergent nature of interdependencies, which routine participants may endogenously harmonize through routine performances. Second, our findings detail the practices through which “interface design work” is accomplished. Although some of these practices imply transitory variations in routine performances, others change the patterning of interfaces more sustainably. Third, resources play an important role in designing interfaces because making them readily available may require their reconfiguration. Moreover, the pooled interdependencies that flow from shared resources can escalate into more complex types of interdependence.
apa_citation: Mahringer, C. A., & Danner-Schröder, A. (2025). Autonomous, yet interdependent: Designing interfaces across routine clusters. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(6), 1300-1327. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0853

title: Intermediated Legitimation: How Founders Build New Venture Legitimacy among Make-or-Break Audiences
methods: Six-month inductive, comparative, ethnographic case study of three startups in one Australian seed-funded accelerator ('Equilate'); data include 43 semi-structured real-time interviews (approx. 80 hours) with founders, accelerator mentors, staff, and other startup founders, 30 in-situ observations of mentoring sessions, pitching, and social events (approx. 60 hours), and approximately 335 archival items (newsletters, Twitter posts, blog posts, and media articles); grounded, comparative analysis following DiBenigno and Kellogg (2014) with inductive coding of founders' legitimacy concerns, emotional reactions, venture work, and legitimation outcomes, triangulated across interview, observational, and archival sources.
industry: Technology startups (logistics, entertainment, virtual reality) participating in a seed-funded startup accelerator
time_period: October 2019 to approximately mid-2020 (six-month program and shortly thereafter)
units: Three focal startups (LogiPedia, Flix, ImmerseVR) and their founders in the 2019-2020 cohort of the Equilate accelerator, plus accelerator mentors/staff and other cohort founders
n_sample: 3 focal ventures; 43 interviews; 30 observations; 335 archival items
authors: Neuberger, Isabel; Mattioli, Fabio; Richards, Harriette; Nyilasy, Greg; Tracey, Paul
theories: entrepreneurial ecosystems; organizational legitimacy; social-symbolic work; symbolic interactionism; theory of prospective achievement emotions
keywords: adaptive venture work; founders; make-or-break audiences; new venture legitimation; obedient path; pragmatic path; prospective achievement emotions; rebellious path; relational venture work; social-symbolic work; startup accelerators; venture work
research_question: How do founders seek to build legitimacy for their ventures among make-or-break audiences—resource-holding audiences such as startup accelerators that expect a say in the strategic direction of the venture?
mechanism_summary:
- Make-or-break audience environment: the accelerator imposes expedited achievement pressures (valorizing scale, intense competitive culture) and standardized evaluation metrics (reporting requirements, "investor mindset"), producing founder legitimacy concerns.
- Shared triggering emotion: frustration with Equilate's requirements, experienced by all founders across the three ventures.
- Adaptive venture work (three types): Type 1 accommodation (acquiescing to accelerator demands), Type 2 selective adherence (complying with demands deemed enhancing, rejecting those deemed undermining), Type 3 assertive resistance (openly dismissing demands and repositioning the venture to founders' own understanding).
- Dominant prospective achievement emotions: hopelessness (following accommodation and perceived poor performance), assurance (following selective adherence and perceived progress), anticipatory joy (following assertive resistance and perceived success).
- Relational venture work (three types): Type A symbolic distancing (disassociating the venture from the accelerator's symbolic system), Type B symbolic embedding (leveraging affiliation with the accelerator to build outside relationships), Type C symbolic reuniting (repositioning as compliant after initial rejection).
- Three legitimation pathways: obedient path (accommodation → hopelessness → symbolic distancing → negative evaluation); pragmatic path (selective adherence → assurance → symbolic embedding → positive evaluation); rebellious path (assertive resistance → anticipatory joy → symbolic reuniting → positive evaluation).

The process unfolds as founders encounter an evaluative environment that generates legitimacy concerns and shared frustration, then enact different types of adaptive venture work to position their ventures vis-a-vis Equilate's demands. These positionings elicit distinct prospective achievement emotions tied to perceived venture performance, which in turn shape subsequent relational venture work through which founders distance from, embed in, or reunite with the audience's symbolic system. Counterintuitively, obedient accommodation spirals toward negative legitimacy evaluation, while selective adherence and assertive resistance more often yield positive evaluations from the make-or-break audience.
theoretical_contribution: The paper theorizes three novel new venture legitimation pathways—obedient, pragmatic, and rebellious—rooted in varying degrees of compliance and resistance, and foregrounds emotions (especially prospective achievement emotions) as central to how these paths unfold, challenging prevailing cognitive-rational accounts of legitimation. It conceptualizes "venture work" as a distinct category of social-symbolic work that spans the self work/organization work divide of Lawrence and Phillips (2019), because the venture is both an organization-in-the-making and inseparable from its founders. It also reframes entrepreneurial ecosystem support organizations: value is created through dialogic interaction rather than unidirectional "coachability," shifting attention from founder deficiencies to the relational and emotional dynamics of intermediated legitimation.
practical_implication: Accelerator participation can be emotionally corrosive for founders, and obedient accommodation of mentor demands may backfire rather than secure approval; founders benefit from questioning advice and maintaining conviction in their own understanding of the venture. For accelerator and other ecosystem support organizations, feedback should be a two-way dialogue, standardized performance metrics should be applied with care, and programs should actively monitor founder wellbeing—providing safe spaces, mental-health retreats, and resilience support to address the emotional strain of continuous prospective evaluation.
limitations: The study examines only one accelerator in Australia and three focal ventures, so generalization to other ecosystems (e.g., more mature Silicon Valley programs or emerging Latin American ones) is uncertain. The focal accelerator was new and studying its first cohort, meaning some dynamics may reflect Equilate's own legitimacy-seeking rather than typical accelerator behavior. The three theorized pathways and emotion categories are unlikely to exhaust the space of possible founder responses and emotional experiences.
future_research: Researchers should examine the role of emotions in new venture legitimation outside accelerators—in incubators, science parks, venture capital relationships, and other make-or-break settings—and across diverse ecosystems with different resource endowments. Further work could study more ventures at different stages to uncover additional legitimation pathways and emotional responses, and investigate how the legitimacy of the accelerator itself shapes the legitimacy concerns of participating ventures.
abstract: Many new ventures enter relationships with intermediaries, thereby ceding control to an organization that becomes a make-or-break audience for them. These settings foster intense experiences, suggesting that participating founders are likely to face a distinct set of challenges as they seek to build their venture’s legitimacy. Yet we lack a systematic analysis of new venture legitimation processes in the context of this critical audience type. To build new understanding of these important dynamics, we conducted an ethnographic study of three ventures in an Australian accelerator. Our study reveals three distinct legitimation pathways that ventures may follow when seeking legitimacy from a make-or-break audience—the obedient, pragmatic, and rebellious paths. We find that these pathways are jointly shaped by the expectations of the audience, the emotional experiences of founders, and founders’ reactions to these emotions in the context of perceived venture performance. We contribute to organizational scholarship by identifying a novel set of new venture legitimation pathways that incorporate emotion, conceptualizing “venture work” as a distinct type of social-symbolic work designed to legitimate startups and shedding new light on the role of new venture support organizations in entrepreneurial ecosystems.
apa_citation: Neuberger, I., Mattioli, F., Richards, H., Nyilasy, G., & Tracey, P. (2025). Intermediated legitimation: How founders build new venture legitimacy among make-or-break audiences. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(6), 1235-1265. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.1258

title: Is a picture worth a thousand words? Image usage in ESG reports
methods: Large-sample archival study; deep-learning object detection (Faster R-CNN) plus manual coding to measure image usage in 526 firms' stand-alone ESG reports (2005-2018); OLS regressions of image usage on impression-management proxies and of stakeholder outcomes (CAR, shareholder proposals, ESG awards) on image usage, with industry and year fixed effects.
industry: U.S. public companies across Fama-French 12 industries (incl. sin, dirty, controversial, B2C sectors)
units: Firm-year stand-alone ESG reports
n_sample: 526 unique firms; 2,602 reports in main analysis
authors: Albrecht, Disen Huang; Anantharaman, Divya; Zhao, Keyi
theories: impression management (Elsbach & Sutton, 1992; Merkl-Davies & Brennan, 2007); legitimacy theory (Dowling & Pfeffer, 1975); stakeholder theory (Freeman, 1984)
keywords: ESG reports; aesthetic images; disclosure quality; generic images; greenwashing; specific images; visual impression management
research_question: Do firms use aesthetic images in ESG reports as a tool of visual impression management, and does such image usage actually shape stakeholders' perceptions of corporate ESG performance outside laboratory settings?
mechanism_summary: Grounded in legitimacy theory and the impression-management literature, the paper argues that because ESG claims are hard to substantiate and aesthetic images are processed subconsciously and are hard to cite as evidence in disputes, visual impression management is a low-cost way for firms—especially poor performers in stigmatized industries with thin textual disclosure—to inflate perceived legitimacy. Generic images carry more impression-management content than substantiating specific images. The consequences tests show the embellishment "works" on unsophisticated retail traders (overvaluation and later return reversals) but is seen through by sophisticated experts: ESG award committees reward specific and penalize generic images, and institutional activists extract information from specific images, revealing previously underestimated detection costs.
theoretical_contribution: The study contributes large-scale archival evidence to the ESG-disclosure and impression-management literatures by distinguishing generic images (a presentation/reporting choice) from specific images (reflecting underlying ESG activities), thereby illuminating the decoupling of reporting from activities. It documents the first evidence the authors are aware of that visual impression management can elicit negative reactions from expert audiences, cautioning against over-generalizing prior experimental and small-scale findings to wider market participants.
practical_implication: For regulators and standard-setters, the findings show that aesthetic images—largely left to preparer discretion in ESG frameworks—carry information and can mislead less sophisticated stakeholders, so heavy or generic image usage may warrant scrutiny as a greenwashing signal predictive of future misconduct. Preparers and sophisticated stakeholders should recognize that generic image usage can backfire with expert audiences such as award committees.
limitations: Shareholder-response measures are noisier than in annual-report settings because only the month and year of ESG report publication are known, precluding short-window event studies. Translating experimental insights to archival settings is imperfect, the consequence settings do not perfectly overlap, large-sample tests capture only average effects, and ESG ratings are acknowledged as imperfect proxies for ESG performance. The high empirical correlation between specific and generic images limits inferences about their independent roles.
future_research: The authors invite work using archival, field, and experimental methods to probe the mechanisms and conditions under which visual impression management elicits negative expert reactions, including survey evidence on report producers' choices, better disentangling of bundled ESG information, and study of how generative-AI image creation may cheapen or obscure the information content of "specific" images.
abstract: We collect a novel dataset on images in Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) reports using deep learning and artificial intelligence (AI), to test hypotheses arising from experimental literature that visual impression management enhances audience perception. We further combine AI and manual methods to contrast images that lack information content ("generic images" evoking broad ESG themes) against images with information content ("specific images" depicting firmspecific ESG activities). We hypothesize and find that firms (1) operating in socially problematic industries (e.g., "sinful", polluting, or controversial industries), (2) issuing less extensive textual ESG disclosures, and (3) experiencing poor ESG performance tend to use more images, especially generic images, consistent with strategic motivations. We then examine the impact of image usage on stakeholders, observing unduly enhanced perception in retail traders but seldom in institutional investors. We observe that ESG award committees reward specific images and penalize generic images. Our results show that ESG report visual impression management affects stakeholders in the real world, though its impact can be overcome by attention and expertise.
apa_citation: Albrecht, D. H., Anantharaman, D., & Zhao, K. (2025). Is a picture worth a thousand words? Image usage in ESG reports. *Accounting, Organizations and Society*, *115*, 101616. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aos.2025.101616
