title: Do Nature‐Loving CEOs Make the World Greener?
methods: Archival panel study of Chinese A-share listed firms (firm-year level). OLS linear probability models regressing CDM participation on a CEO childhood-greenspace proxy with industry, year, firm-city, and CEO-hometown fixed effects; standard errors clustered by firm. Validation via earnings-call tone analysis; CEO-turnover, subsample (Tree-Planting vs. Down-to-the-Countryside), cross-sectional, and Trucost-emissions tests.
industry: CDM-eligible industries (15 sectors, e.g., energy, manufacturing, chemicals, metals); Chinese A-share listed firms
time_period: 2006–2011 (main sample); conference-call and emissions validations extend to 2008–2017
units: firm-years (CEOs of listed firms)
n_sample: 1,397 firm-year observations, 377 firms, 421 CEOs, 152 hometown cities
authors: Zhi, Weijia
theories: manager (upper-echelons) effect literature (Bertrand and Schoar, 2003); social ecology / nature-experience research (Dunlap and Heffernan, 1975; Wells and Lekies, 2006; Jensen and Olsen, 2019; Gifford, 2014)
keywords: CEOs; carbon emissions; green investments; nature preference
research_question: Whether—and under what conditions—CEOs’ personal nature-loving preferences (an intrinsic preference for environmental protection) translate into corporate environmental actions, after weighing those preferences against organizational performance pressures and personal career considerations.
mechanism_summary: Building on social ecology research—which holds that childhood appreciation-oriented interactions with nature cultivate a durable adult preference for environmental protection—the paper argues that CEOs imprint this personal preference onto firm policy. Because translating environmental values into corporate action requires reallocating managerial attention and firm resources (at the expense of short-term profitability), the effect surfaces most strongly where CEOs have discretion (greater decision-making power) and where preferences align with local stakeholder interests (high public environmental concern). The childhood-greenspace measure predates careers, mitigating endogeneity, and CEO-turnover tests rule out systematic environmental-preference-based hiring.
theoretical_contribution: First, the paper extends the "manager effect" literature by isolating environmental preference as a distinct CEO trait—operationalized through a novel, childhood-greenspace measure exogenous to later-life professional influences—that is empirically separable from general prosocial tendencies and CEO risk preferences. Second, it extends the CSR literature, which has emphasized external stakeholder pressure, by showing that CEOs as key insiders incorporate personal environmental preferences into corporate decision-making, highlighting the multifaceted determinants of CSR.
practical_implication: The childhood-greenspace measure offers boards and researchers a tractable tool for identifying personal value–based drivers of executives' environmental decisions. Because nature-loving CEOs are associated with greater corporate carbon reduction yet draw no significant market reaction at appointment, the findings suggest such preferences advance environmental and societal welfare without a detectable shareholder-value penalty or premium.
limitations: The setting is China-specific, relying on the hukou system to infer CEOs' childhood hometowns and on hand-collected city greenspace data subject to measurement error (e.g., pre-1986 reporting gaps, addressed via winsorizing and exclusions). The actual-emissions (Trucost) test rests on a small sample (limited Chinese coverage), requiring extension of the sample window to 2006–2017 to gain statistical power. Beyond these, additional limitations are Not reported in paper.
future_research: Not reported in paper.
abstract: This study examines the relation between CEOs’ preferences for environmental protection (“nature-loving preferences”) and corporate environmental actions. Drawing on social ecology research, I develop a proxy for CEOs’ nature-loving preferences based on their childhood exposure to greenspace and validate it using their positive discussions of nature during earnings conference calls. Findings show a significant positive association between CEOs’ nature-loving preferences and their firms’ participation in Clean Development Mechanism projects. This effect remains robust after addressing firm–CEO matching and omitted variable concerns. Cross-sectional tests reveal stronger effects when local environmental demands are high
apa_citation: Zhi, W. (2025). Do nature‐loving CEOs make the world greener? *Journal of Accounting Research*. https://doi.org/10.1111/1475-679x.70033

title: Biodiversity Co-Benefits in Carbon Markets? Evidence from Voluntary Offset Projects
methods: Interrupted time series / event-study panel regressions on an 11-year balanced panel of geocoded offset projects; project, year, country, and registry fixed effects; standard errors clustered at the project level; robustness with BHI/BERI long-difference specifications and land-cover (LULC) change regressions.
industry: Voluntary carbon market (VCM) nature-based offset projects (reforestation, avoided deforestation, peatland/soil carbon); buyers concentrated in energy, aviation, and industrial sectors
country: Global; projects span forty-six countries with concentration in North America, Europe, and Asia, and many in the Global South
time_period: 2000–2023 (registry data); HII analysis 2000–2020; balanced 11-year panel (5 years pre/post establishment)
units: Geocoded voluntary carbon offset projects (project-area boundaries)
n_sample: 1,730 projects in the balanced 11-year panel (of 2,701 geolocated nature-based projects; 29,974 total projects, 419,267 retirement records, 13,664 firms)
authors: Zhou, Zoey Yiyuan; Almond, Douglas
theories: additionality in ecosystem service markets; market for lemons / asymmetric information (Akerlof, 1970; Kotchen & Vogt, 2024)
keywords: additionality; biodiversity; carbon farming; event study; nature-based solutions; voluntary carbon offset
research_question: Do voluntary carbon offset projects that advertise biodiversity co-benefits actually improve local habitat quality, or are these claimed ecological co-benefits unrealized in practice across the voluntary carbon market (VCM)?
mechanism_summary: Using an interrupted time series design that exploits stable, flat pre-treatment HII trends, the authors interpret post-establishment deviations as causal local effects of offset projects. Rather than catalyzing natural regeneration, projects are associated with a prompt ~3.7 percent rise in HII (driven by conversion of structurally diverse shrublands and forests into simplified pasture), so the "win-win" of joint carbon-and-biodiversity gains theorized in ecology is not systematically realized in current VCM practice. The pattern echoes information-friction/"market for lemons" concerns: co-benefit claims and ratings are carbon-centric and not validated against habitat outcomes, leaving investors exposed to unpriced ecological risk.
theoretical_contribution: The paper provides the first systematic, large-scale empirical evidence on the biodiversity (habitat) impacts of the voluntary carbon market, advancing debates on the effectiveness of market-based conservation, additionality, and the financialization of nature. By documenting that stated and realized ecological performance diverge—and that disclosure, certification, and third-party ratings fail to identify better-performing projects—it reframes VCM co-benefit claims as an information-frictions and governance problem in the market for biodiversity-linked environmental assets.
practical_implication: Findings call for stronger biodiversity safeguards within VCM standards, more transparent ecological (habitat) reporting, and third-party verification of co-benefit claims. Investors should demand quantitative disclosure of habitat outcomes alongside carbon metrics, and policymakers can leverage improving, strategically-unbiased satellite-derived measures within MRV systems and frameworks such as the CBD and the Taskforce on Nature-related Financial Disclosures.
limitations: Results reflect outcomes within developer-disclosed project boundaries (not surrounding spillovers) and are restricted to nature-based projects from 2000–2020 with adequate geospatial documentation, which skew newer, larger, and toward Australia—limiting external validity to the full VCM universe. HII proxies human pressure rather than species directly and can produce false negatives, and boundary imprecision plus partly subjective verification may introduce measurement error.
future_research: Longer time series, ground-based verification, and more comprehensive project coverage are needed to validate and extend the findings, since some ecological benefits (e.g., species recolonization, successional recovery) may unfold over decades. The authors also flag general-equilibrium structural models to address a broader set of VCM counterfactuals, including how projects in one area affect project siting elsewhere.
abstract: We compile the largest extant dataset of voluntary carbon offset projects and link them to high-resolution satellite-derived measures of habitat integrity in local ecosystems. Contrary to asserted co-benefits, we find carbon offset projects are associated with a 3.7 percent increase in habitat disturbance, as measured by the Human Influence Index. Land-use analyses show that these projects tend to accelerate the conversion of biodiverse shrublands and less-dense forests into pastures and simplified landscapes, implying reduced habitat integrity and ecological trade-offs. We examine impact heterogeneity by ecosystem condition, certifications, stated co-benefits, protected area overlap, registry, and rating coverage, but still find no evidence of improved habitats. Nor do alternative habitat measures, the Bioclimate Ecosystem Resilience Index and the Biodiversity Habitat Index, show measurable improvement following offset projects. We conclude that voluntary carbon markets have ecological performance gaps and information frictions relevant to both investors and offset market governance.
apa_citation: Zhou, Z. Y., & Almond, D. (2025). Biodiversity co-benefits in carbon markets? Evidence from voluntary offset projects. *SSRN Electronic Journal*. https://doi.org/10.2139/ssrn.5678422

title: Making Sense of Conflicts During Management Consulting Missions: A Values-in-Practice Approach
methods: Qualitative, abductive design; 26 semi-structured interviews yielding 50 contrasted case studies of management consulting missions, coded manually following the Gioia methodology (first-order concepts, second-order themes) and compared to reveal temporal conflict dynamics.
industry: Management consulting (organizational change missions: operational, digital, structural, HR, cultural transformation)
time_period: Long-term missions lasting 6-24+ months; interviews conducted during/after the COVID-19 pandemic (data collected ~2020-2024)
units: Consultant-client relationships (dyadic missions), reported via interviews with consultants and clients
n_sample: 50 case studies drawn from 26 semi-structured interviews with 17 consultants and 9 clients
authors: Bazdah, Myriam; Allard-Poesi, Florence
theories: conflict typology: task/process/relational conflict (Jehn, 1992, 1997; Jehn & Mannix, 2001); sensemaking theory (Weick, 1995); values-in-practice approach (Aadland, 2010; Espedal, 2020; Gehman, 2021)
keywords: Management consulting relationship; Methodological conflict; Methodological practices; Relational conflict; Relational practices; Values; Values conflict
research_question: To what extent do the practices of the consultant and the client generate conflicts and conflicts of values during management consulting missions? How do these conflicts emerge, and what are their consequences for the consultant and/or the client?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: relational practices vs. methodological practices; relational, methodological (process), and values conflicts; "expected values" (trust, benevolence, recognition, respect) vs. uncooperative relational practices; positive vs. negative emotionality.
- Process / phases: four temporal dynamics of the consulting relationship — (1) fluid relational and methodological agreement; (2) methodological (process) disagreement; (3) relational disagreement; (4) methodological disagreement that evolves over time into a values conflict.
- Triggering conditions: a methodological disagreement expressed through relational practices that violate respected norms (e.g., verbal violence during meetings) is reframed by actors as a values conflict.
- Emergent outcomes: low negative emotionality (frustration, distrust) for relational/methodological disagreements; high negative emotionality (stress, anger, exhaustion) for values conflicts, which can lead an actor to quit the mission.

Drawing on a values-in-practice approach (Aadland, 2010) and Jehn's conflict typology, the authors show that values conflicts are not confrontations between two stable value systems but are temporally and dynamically situated in the actors' interactions, always preceded by a methodological disagreement. Through a Weickian sensemaking lens, actors invoke "values"—rather than contextual or psychological explanations—to make sense of practices they perceive as inappropriate and morally accountable, anchoring abstract values in concrete relational practices retrospectively described during interviews.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes three theoretical contributions: it shows that values conflicts are situated and dynamic, arising from relational practices that violate norms of respectful business interaction rather than from clashing value systems; it demonstrates that "values" function as discursive resources participants call upon to make sense of stressful, expectation-disrupting situations; and it complements Jehn's (1992) definition of relational conflict by specifying its source—differing conceptions of and expectations regarding the business relationship.
practical_implication: Consultants should clarify and discuss their intervention mode and methodological practices with clients at the outset, and both parties should articulate their relational expectations and a relational framework to limit deterioration. Because such frameworks are not sufficient, the authors also recommend involving middle management to give consultants and clients space to discuss relational difficulties and to support consultants in managing the emotional work of missions.
limitations: The COVID-19 pandemic prevented the planned observations and data triangulation, so the study relies on interview accounts alone. The research did not capture the consultant's and the client's views of the same mission (no matched dyads), and the authors caution that the effectiveness of their managerial recommendations remains uncertain given the intensity and evolution of disagreements.
future_research: Future work could examine the emotions and emotional work conflicts generate (e.g., non-verbal behavior; Hirschman's exit-voice-loyalty for post-conflict coping), study the consultant–client dyad on the same mission to surface asymmetric conflict perceptions, and extend the analysis to other service relationships such as care settings. Developing a measurement scale for conflicts and their emotions is also proposed as an avenue for quantitative studies.
abstract: This study investigates to what extent and how consultant–client relationships generate conflicts, in particular conflicts of values. Based on 50 case studies of management consulting missions, our research identifies four client–consultant relationship dynamics, of which three are marked by relational, methodological, or values conflicts. Our results: (1) highlight the situated and dynamic nature of values conflicts, which are shown to be associated with the consultant’s or client’s relational practices, as they violate norms of respectful business interactions; (2) demonstrate how values—referring to what practices are and are not desirable from the consultant’s and client’s points of view—are called upon to make sense of situations that are experienced as highly stressful or emotional and that disrupt expectations of a business relationship; and (3) complement Jehn’s (1992) definition of relational conflict by specifying that it can be associated with various conceptions of business relationships. From these results, we suggest two sets of practical recommendations to limit the risk of conflicts and improve both clients’ and consultants’ well-being during consulting projects.
apa_citation: Bazdah, M., & Allard-Poesi, F. (2025). Making sense of conflicts during management consulting missions: A values-in-practice approach. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06211-6

title: Perceived Air Pollution and Innovative Work Behavior: An Empirical Examination Based on Conservation of Resources Theory
methods: Multi-wave (four waves, one-month lags), multi-source survey design with supervisor-subordinate dyads; moderated-mediation model tested via PROCESS macro in SPSS with bootstrapped confidence intervals (10,000 samples); CFA in AMOS-22 for measurement validation; objective air pollution (AQI) and demographics included as controls.
industry: Research and development (R&D) units of 17 companies in heavy engineering, automobiles, pharmaceuticals, and manufacturing
time_period: Four survey waves interspersed with a one-month time lag each (T1-T4)
units: Supervisor-subordinate dyads
n_sample: 506 supervisor-subordinate dyads (response rate 45.5%)
authors: Bhattacharjee, Arindam; Sarkar, Anita
theories: conservation of resources (COR) theory (Hobfoll, 1989, 2001); goal disengagement perspective (Carver & Scheier, 1998)
keywords: COR theory; IWB; India; air pollution; emotional exhaustion; perceived air pollution; supervisor support
research_question: Can an environmental factor like perceived air pollution influence innovative work behavior (IWB), and if so, why (through what mechanism) and when (under what boundary conditions)? The study examines whether emotional exhaustion mediates the perceived air pollution–IWB link and whether supervisor support moderates it.
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Objective air pollution (AQI), subordinate age/gender/education, supervisor age/gender

Drawing on COR theory, the authors argue that perceived air pollution depletes or threatens multiple valued resources (personal health, money, health of loved ones, time, energy, sense of control), precipitating emotional exhaustion; emotionally exhausted employees then withdraw from resource-intensive activities like IWB to conserve remaining resources, a logic reinforced by the goal disengagement perspective. Supervisor support, itself a COR resource, buffers this process: at high supervisor support the positive air pollution–exhaustion link is attenuated (and the negative indirect effect on IWB turns nonsignificant), whereas at low support it is amplified. All five hypotheses were supported.
theoretical_contribution: The study introduces a novel resource-depletion mechanism (emotional exhaustion) linking perceived air pollution to a positive employee performance outcome (IWB), extending a literature that had focused on destructive outcomes such as CWBs and abusive supervision. It identifies a flexible, controllable boundary condition—supervisor support—in contrast to prior static or uncontrollable moderators (trait self-control, cloudiness), and positions perceived air pollution as a novel, organization-external antecedent of IWB, expanding the construct's nomological network.
practical_implication: Organizations should target emotional exhaustion (e.g., positive work reflection, CBT, meditation, humor) and empower supervisors to grant work-from-home arrangements, actively listen, and adjust workloads for employees affected by air pollution. Training should be directed at units with low supervisor support. For policy, governments are urged to treat perceived air pollution as a psychosocial risk factor under occupational health and safety frameworks and to sponsor supervisory training.
limitations: The multi-wave multi-source survey design precludes causal inference, and data were restricted to R&D departments in Delhi NCR, India, limiting generalizability. Only supervisor support was examined as a moderator (omitting coworker/family support), IWB relied on potentially biased supervisory ratings, and the chronically polluted setting raises the possibility of desensitization effects among respondents.
future_research: The authors call for longitudinal designs to strengthen internal validity, replication across polluted and relatively unpolluted cities worldwide to address desensitization, and examination of additional moderators (coworker/family support, wellness programs) and individual differences (e.g., health anxiety). They also suggest exploring whether perceived air pollution triggers green behaviors or turnover intentions, and using more objective IWB measures such as patent disclosures.
abstract: Innovative work behavior is crucial for organizational sustenance and growth, and scholars have uncovered many determinants of the same. However, the majority of predictors are either in the work or organizational domain. It is not known whether environmental factors like perceived air pollution can influence innovative work behavior, and if yes, why and when. Our study aims to address this gap by leveraging the conservation of resources theory, which posits that individuals experiencing stressors try to protect their limited resources by withdrawing from work outcomes. The authors utilized a multi-wave multi-source research design and collected data from 506 supervisor–subordinate dyads working in research and development units of 17 companies in India. We found that emotional exhaustion mediated the negative relationship between perceived air pollution and innovative work behavior, and that this relationship was buffered (accentuated) at high (low) levels of supervisor support. Theoretical and practical implications are discussed.
apa_citation: Bhattacharjee, A., & Sarkar, A. (2025). Perceived air pollution and innovative work behavior: An empirical examination based on conservation of resources theory. *Organization & Environment*, 38(4), 547–571. https://doi.org/10.1177/10860266251350540

title: Green Pressure, Lean Measures: Unveiling Corporate Downsizing Within the European Union Emissions Trading System
journal: Journal of Financial and Quantitative Analysis
methods: Difference-in-differences linear probability models with firm fixed effects, using the Feb. 28, 2017 European Council intervention as a quasi-natural experiment; EU ETS-covered firms matched to uncovered controls via propensity score matching; cross-sectional intra-EU ETS tests on decile-ranked emission intensity; placebo, logit, balanced-panel, and alternative-cutoff robustness checks.
industry: EU ETS-covered emission-intensive industries (stationary installations across sectors such as steel, cement, chemicals, energy)
units: Firms (private and listed) plus matched non-EU ETS control firms
n_sample: 2,337 EU ETS-covered firms (832 listed, 1,505 private) controlling 3,460 installations, ~35% of EU ETS emissions
authors: Boeckx, Jana; Struyfs, Kristof; Torsin, Wouter
keywords: EU ETS; carbon prices; corporate downsizing; emission intensity; financial constraints; private firms
research_question: How do firms covered by the EU ETS respond, in terms of employment, productivity, and emission outcomes, to the increase in carbon costs following the European Council's Feb. 2017 intervention, and which firms internalize these costs through corporate downsizing and downscaling?
mechanism_summary: The intervention sharply raised carbon allowance prices, making EU ETS costs material for the first time. For cash-constrained private firms, higher emission intensity raised the probability of downsizing; this response operated through divesting production assets (downscaling), which simultaneously reduced personnel and emissions (~7.8% emission drop) while raising productivity. Smaller cash-constrained listed firms also downsized, but to reduce operating leverage rather than via divestment, leaving emission output and asset levels unchanged while still improving productivity. Larger listed firms appear to absorb costs through alternative responses (R&D, cost pass-through, hedging, relocation, lobbying).
theoretical_contribution: The study adds climate-policy interventions and associated environmental costs as a new antecedent of corporate downsizing, and contributes to the finance literature on the drivers and outcomes of corporate environmental behavior by showing that firms weigh carbon costs against employment costs. It advances understanding of heterogeneous firm responses to the EU ETS by distinguishing private from listed firms and by formally documenting labor-market consequences across 27 countries, answering prior calls for causal evidence on the EU ETS and labor outcomes.
practical_implication: Policymakers intervening in emissions trading schemes should anticipate that sudden carbon-price increases impose transition costs concentrated in cash-constrained private firms and smaller cash-constrained listed firms, which downsize as emission intensity rises. Targeted instruments such as green loan investment programs, subsidies, or guarantees, and reallocating auction/innovation-fund revenue toward worker retraining, could sustain employment and dampen divestment incentives while still enabling emission reductions and productivity gains, especially for non-leakage sectors that receive fewer free allowances.
limitations: The sample ends in 2020 at a carbon price of €32.57, before prices peaked near €100 in 2023, so the documented employment effects are modest (~3.5% workforce reduction for the most-polluting private firms relative to the least-polluting) and likely understate later transition costs. The matching between high- and low-emission-intensity firms remains imperfect due to limited sample size, and evidence on listed-firm coping mechanisms (R&D, relocation, pass-through) is largely anecdotal from annual reports rather than formally tested.
future_research: Future work could map the skill profiles of jobs lost versus green jobs created through innovation funds to assess distributional consequences, examine voluntary turnover and whether a "cost of environmental distress" drives talent flight between strongly and weakly environmentally performing EU ETS firms, and study the persistence and longer-run magnitude of these job losses as carbon prices continued to surge after the sample period.
abstract: In 2017, the European Union Emissions Trading System underwent a policy intervention that resulted in a surge in carbon prices. Using this setting as a quasi-natural experiment, we focus on employment, productivity, and emission outcomes among covered enterprises. Results show that emission-intensive private firms, particularly those with financial constraints, are more likely to downsize by divesting production assets, reducing both workforce and emissions. Smaller, cash-strapped listed firms are also prone to downsize by decreasing their operating leverage while maintaining emission output and asset levels. Positive productivity outcomes indicate that both private and listed firms become leaner postintervention.
apa_citation: Boeckx, J., Struyfs, K., & Torsin, W. (2025). Green pressure, lean measures: Unveiling corporate downsizing within the European Union Emissions Trading System. *Journal of Financial and Quantitative Analysis*, 60(8), 4091-4130. https://doi.org/10.1017/s0022109025000237

title: Detecting Gender Stereotype Biases Against Women Entrepreneurs in Large Language Models
methods: Explorative multi-study quantitative design using GPT-4 and GPT-4o via the OpenAI API: (1) BSRI short-form trait-attribution prompts (100 trials each); (2) adaptation/expansion of Gupta et al. (2014) stereotype-activation experiment with two-sample t-tests and one-way ANOVA across masculine/feminine/nullified/control conditions; (3) Reddit field study of 68 entrepreneurial-opportunity threads; (4) an OpenAI-style regression-based method asking LLMs to generate logistic-regression coefficients for investor preference; plus five robustness checks across o1, DeepSeek V3, Gemini-2.5 Flash, and Claude Opus 4.
industry: Artificial intelligence / large language models applied to entrepreneurial evaluation; real-world entrepreneurship discussion threads from Reddit
time_period: LLM API responses collected from 2024.7.23 (GPT-4 and GPT-4o)
units: LLM-generated responses (GPT-4, GPT-4o, and additional models); Reddit entrepreneurial-opportunity threads
n_sample: 100-400 trials per condition per model across experiments; 68 Reddit threads (28 feminine-toned, 40 masculine-toned); 100 coefficient trials each for masculinity and femininity
authors: Cao, Xian; Li, Hongfei; Xu, Qingyu; Zhu, Ruoqing
theories: Role Congruity Theory (Eagly & Karau, 2002); Role Theory (Biddle & Thomas, 1966); Social Role Theory (Eagly, 1987a, 1987b)
keywords: Entrepreneurship; Gender stereotype biases; Language learning models (LLMs)
research_question: Does AI, like its human counterparts, hold gender stereotype biases against women entrepreneurs? Specifically, does AI stereotypically associate masculine traits with successful entrepreneurship, undervalue femininity, and consequently rate women entrepreneurs lower?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on Role Congruity Theory, the authors expected LLMs to penalize women entrepreneurs whose communal (feminine-coded) traits conflict with the agentic (masculine-coded) entrepreneurial ideal. Across trait-description, experimental, and field-study tasks the LLMs instead showed balanced or slightly feminine-leaning evaluations, diverging from the theory's predictions. Only when the OpenAI-style regression method probed simulated funding decisions did masculine coefficients dominate feminine ones (e.g., GPT-4 masculinity 0.592 vs. femininity −0.047, p < 0.001), revealing that gender stereotype biases remain latent and emerge selectively under high-stakes evaluative pressure.
theoretical_contribution: The study raises critical questions about the applicability of human-centric frameworks such as Role Congruity Theory when analyzing LLM outputs, showing that LLMs often diverge from the theory's predictions and providing initial evidence that gender stereotype biases manifest differently in AI systems. It thereby argues for reconsidering or adapting human-centric theories to account for AI's distinct evaluative logic, and highlights the necessity of carefully integrating AI-generated data and suitable analytical tools into theory development.
practical_implication: For entrepreneurs and organizations, AI generally offered balanced, less prejudiced evaluations in non-critical domains such as idea refinement and opportunity evaluation, but encoded subtle masculine-favoring biases in funding decisions. The authors call for bias audits before deployment, debiasing techniques during fine-tuning (e.g., counterfactual data augmentation, adversarial training), explainable-AI tools, and auditing or certification frameworks for AI used in accelerators, pitch competitions, and venture-capital screening.
limitations: The study could not fully disentangle the three interrelated sources of bias (historical/systemic, statistical/computational, human cognitive), and convergence across models cannot be attributed definitively to shared training data, alignment strategies, or safety guardrails. Findings derive from focused studies on gender bias and may not generalize to other research questions or domains, and the work is constrained by financial and logistical limitations on data depth and breadth.
future_research: Future work could systematically vary training-data inputs, fine-tuning procedures, and prompt structures to isolate the sources of bias; compare architectures, corpora, and alignment strategies to identify drivers of cross-model convergence; examine whether LLMs preserve baseline neutrality across iterative multi-turn user interactions; and investigate how AI aligns with or diverges from theoretical expectations in fields beyond entrepreneurship.
abstract: We investigate the capability of Artificial Intelligence (AI), specifically Large Language Models (LLMs) like ChatGPT, to exhibit gender stereotype biases against women entrepreneurs—a critical issue as AI becomes more prevalent in entrepreneurial decision-making. Despite optimism about AI's utility, the risk of reinforcing existing gender stereotype biases poses significant ethical challenges. Our explorative research, which builds on prior studies and utilizes a regression-based method demonstrated by OpenAI, analyzes LLM responses to various scenarios. Our findings show that LLMs did not ascribe strong masculine traits to successful entrepreneurs, although some nuances still remained. Furthermore, LLMs did not rate femininity lower in general business evaluations, and explicitly identifying the entrepreneur's sex did not alter these results. These findings seemingly suggest that AI's perspective deviated from the traditional view that entrepreneurship is predominantly a masculine occupation. However, in investment-specific scenarios, LLMs exhibited a notable bias toward masculine traits over feminine traits, aligning with traditional stereotypical views of entrepreneurship. This variability in responses not only deepens our understanding of gender stereotype biases in AI but also underscores the complexity of addressing these biases, thereby contributing to discussions about AI's potential to offer less biased evaluations than human judgments and paving the way for fairer AI-driven business practices. Additionally, these findings highlight the necessity for careful integration of AI-generated data into theoretical development, considering AI's unique information processing capabilities, and the employment of suitable analytical tools to more effectively detect subtle patterns and nuances in AI-generated data.
apa_citation: Cao, X., Li, H., Xu, Q., & Zhu, R. (2025). Detecting gender stereotype biases against women entrepreneurs in large language models. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06216-1

title: Social Sustainability Practice: The Interplay between State and Community Logics
methods: Cross-sectional survey of senior managers; multiple regression with mean-centred state and community (Ubuntu) logic variables and their interaction term; simple-slope plots; Harman's single-factor common-method-bias check; EFA with CR/AVE and HTMT validity diagnostics; normative-pressure robustness check.
industry: Firms across 10 sectors (agriculture, construction, energy, finance, hospitality, IT, manufacturing, mining, services, transport)
time_period: Survey conducted circa 2020-2021 (response rate affected by COVID-19 pandemic); directory accessed 2021
units: Firms (senior managers/directors as respondents)
n_sample: 276 firms (36.8% response rate)
authors: Chindondondo, Sithabile; Reddy, Colin David
theories: institutional logics theory (Thornton et al., 2012; Greenwood et al., 2011); relational ethics / Ubuntu (Mbigi, 2005; Metz, 2013)
keywords: Community; Institutional logics; Relational ethics; State; Sustainability
research_question: How do state and community logics interact to influence firms' social sustainability practices, and under what boundary conditions do state-led regulations become less central or necessary relative to voluntary, community-rooted approaches grounded in relational ethics and Ubuntu?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Multinational status, firm size (employees), firm age, market logic

State logic exerts a positive effect on social sustainability practice that weakens as community logic strengthens: the state-logic × community-logic interaction is significant and negative (b = -0.38, p < 0.001), and a strong community logic sustains high social sustainability practice even when state logic is weak. The authors theorise this substitutive pattern as *functional compensation*—a dynamic recalibration in which organisations lean more heavily on one institutional logic as another becomes less salient, legitimate, or effective. Ubuntu-rooted relational ethics internalise sustainability as a moral imperative embedded in community relationships, reducing reliance on coercive state enforcement; where community logic is weak, state logic compensates by enforcing compliance.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces *functional compensation* as a novel mechanism for navigating institutional complexity, recasting organisational engagement with logics as temporally situated, inverse, and relationally modulated rather than as static blending, compartmentalisation, or hybridisation. By integrating the African philosophy of Ubuntu into institutional logics theory, it repositions community logic—often subordinated in institutional analysis—as a potentially autonomous and even superordinate force, and advances business ethics by foregrounding a relational, culturally embedded conception of moral agency over individualistic, rationalist models.
practical_implication: A one-size-fits-all approach to sustainability regulation is unlikely to succeed across contexts; where relational ethics such as Ubuntu are strong, voluntary, community-based initiatives may outperform coercive regulation, and mandatory rules could include sunset clauses that phase out as relational ethics become institutionalised. Practitioners and management training programs should cultivate a sustainability mindset rooted in solidarity, compassion, and respect, and firms in community-oriented regions should integrate local cultural values into sustainability strategy.
limitations: The study is contextually specific to Zimbabwe, limiting generalisability across other cultural and institutional environments. Its cross-sectional, single-instrument design offers only a temporal snapshot and risks common-method bias (addressed but not eliminated via Harman's test), and the measurement scales—especially for community logic / Ubuntu—remain incipient, with average variance extracted for the social-sustainability and community-logic scales falling below the 0.50 threshold.
future_research: Future work should test whether community logic's substitutability for state intervention holds in other African, Latin American, Asian, and Global North communal settings; employ longitudinal designs to trace how relational logics and community logics themselves strengthen or weaken over time, including with rising economic well-being; examine how relational ethics can be embedded into formal corporate governance structures; and refine measurement by extending the community-logic scale into a validated "relational ethics" scale grounded in relational ontology.
abstract: This study contributes to business ethics by theorising how relational ethics, rooted in community-based values, shape the role of state intervention in driving corporate sustainability behaviour. We introduce the concept of functional compensation to explain how organisations navigate institutional complexity by shifting between state and community logics. Drawing on the African ethic of Ubuntu—which emphasises solidarity, mutual respect, and human dignity—we suggest that community logic reflects a relational ethic rooted in culturally embedded forms of moral agency. Using survey data from 276 firms in Zimbabwe, we find that state logic, centred on regulatory enforcement, drives sustainability compliance when community logic is weak. However, as Ubuntu-based community logic strengthens, it functionally substitutes for state logic, sustaining or even enhancing ethical performance in the absence of formal regulation. By integrating Ubuntu into institutional logics theory, this paper offers a context-sensitive approach to corporate ethics and social sustainability, advancing both theoretical insight and policy relevance in diverse governance environments.
apa_citation: Chindondondo, S., & Reddy, C. D. (2025). Social sustainability practice: The interplay between state and community logics. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06207-2

title: Re-enchanting Consumer Ethics Through Embodied Relationality: An Ethnographic Approach to the Attitude-Behaviour Gap
methods: Ethnographic fieldwork (14 months) with six families; participant observation across planning, shopping, cooking, eating; 12 semi-structured interviews, kitchen and walking-with tours, tasting events, and food diaries; theoretical/latent thematic analysis (Braun & Clarke, 2006) read through practice-theory devices of bodily dispositions, material elements, and embodied sense.
industry: Household food consumption (groceries, cooking, eating; supermarkets and alternative/ethical food outlets)
time_period: Fieldwork lasted fourteen months (ethics approval dated 1 March 2016)
units: Families (households)
n_sample: Six families
authors: Discetti, Roberta; Tocco, Barbara; Gorton, Matthew
theories: embodied relationality (Mandalaki & Fotaki, 2020); practice theory (Bourdieu, 1977, 1990; Reckwitz, 2002; Warde, 2005)
keywords: Embodiment; Family consumption; Food practices
research_question: How do embodied relational processes — the corporeal, sensory, and interdependent aspects of everyday consumption — shape consumer ethics, and how can attending to them cast new light on the attitude-behaviour gap (ABG)?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / lens: "embodied relationality" (Mandalaki & Fotaki, 2020) read through practice theory's three devices — bodily dispositions, material elements, and embodied sense (Bourdieu; Warde).
- Practices (emergent findings): (1) reconciling conflicting embodied states; (2) negotiating togetherness; (3) seeking sensorial familiarity; (4) calibrating attention and engagement.
- Triggering conditions: competing desires/emotions in shared eating (e.g., meat reduction vs. social occasions); childhood/ancestral food memories; sensory familiarity of products and retailers; material affordances of shopping environments (supermarkets vs. alternative outlets).
- Boundary conditions: family unit of analysis; food practices; six self-assessed low-to-high ethical-engagement households.

Across family food practices, ethical (in)action is shown to emerge from pre-reflexive, corporeal states rather than linear belief-to-behaviour reasoning. Familiar foods and retailers afford an "ontological security" (Giddens, 1984) that locks consumers into comfortable-but-constraining routines, while shared sensorial practices generate belonging that is continually renegotiated. Reframing the ABG through these co-present, ambivalent embodied states yields a more nuanced account of why "unsustainable" practices persist than cognitive or attitudinal explanations allow.
theoretical_contribution: The paper offers "embodied relationality" as a framework that surfaces neglected bodily, material, and sensory dimensions in ABG scholarship, and recasts the gap as a *disenchanted construct* — an epistemological problem detached from the embodied nature of social action — rather than a methodological or policy problem to be "bridged." It also extends practice-theory scholarship by responding to Jacobsen and Hansen's (2021) call to reconnect with first-generation theorists' attention to the embodied (not merely socio-material) dimension of practices.
practical_implication: The authors propose multi-sensorial interventions that disrupt un-reflexivity and foster curiosity, address the pre-reflexive dimension of choice, and cultivate embodied belonging within local ecosystems of relationships; they advocate shifting from individual, cognitive, information-giving policies toward systemic interventions and experiential, sensory food education (especially for children).
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors outline four "re-enchantment" avenues: (1) methodological innovations accommodating embodied/pre-reflexive and more-than-human entanglements (e.g., neuro-gastronomy); (2) extending embodied relationality beyond the family to collective and institutional consumption spaces; (3) engaging all senses (touch, taste, smell) to study how familiarity and curiosity develop; and (4) using embodied relational methods to investigate which embodied states are conducive to destabilizing and reconfiguring constellations of practices.
abstract: Most research on the 'attitude-behaviour gap' adopts linear, rational, and individual models, or highlights notions of consumer uncertainty and confusion. We argue that different strands of the debate can benefit from an exploration of embodied relational processes shaping consumer ethics, namely an acknowledgement of the profound corporeal and interdependent nature of all consumption experiences. The aim of this paper is thus to explore the embodied relational aspects of everyday ethical consumption and cast new light on the 'attitude-behaviour gap'. We do so by drawing on embodiment in practice theories and through an ethnographic study with six families in North-East England, including observations, interviews, kitchen and walking-with tours, and participants' diaries. Our findings highlight four embodied relational practices: reconciling competing embodied states; negotiating togetherness; seeking sensorial familiarity; and calibrating attention and engagement. The paper contributes to the existing literature by offering embodied relationality as a theoretical framework to visualise the complex ambivalent embodied state consumers experience in everyday practices. We leverage this to rethink the 'attitude-behaviour gap' as a disenchanted concept, namely detached from the embodied nature of all social action and, as such, limited in its explanatory potential. We conclude the paper by outlining future research and intervention avenues to re-enchant consumer ethics scholarship, namely reconnect it with its embodied and relational nature.
apa_citation: Discetti, R., Tocco, B., & Gorton, M. (2025). Re-enchanting consumer ethics through embodied relationality: An ethnographic approach to the attitude-behaviour gap. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06227-y

title: A ‘Sticky’ Past: The Viscosity of Organizational Memory in Navigating Ethical Change
methods: Single qualitative case study of one Dutch bank, using an abductive approach; data triangulate 54 semi-structured interviews with internal/external documents and real-time observations, analyzed via the Gioia method (first-, second-, and third-order coding).
industry: Banking / financial services (a Dutch incumbent commercial bank, pseudonym 'NorthBank')
time_period: Study period 2018–2021 (focal 'sticky past' is the 2007–2008 financial crisis)
units: Organizational members across hierarchical levels (top, higher, middle management, employees)
n_sample: 54 in-depth interviews (51 respondents), plus 142 documents, 8 observed meetings, and 19 videos
authors: Dudink, Yentl; Taminiau, Yvette; Veenswijk, Marcel; Bencherki, Nicolas
theories: historic corporate social responsibility (Schrempf-Stirling et al., 2016); organizational memory; organizational multi-temporality (Maclean et al., 2023); viscosity / stickiness (Ahmed, 2004; Saldanha, 2005, 2016)
keywords: Historic corporate social responsibility; Organizational memory; Temporality
research_question: How does the viscosity of organizational memory shape the dynamics of ethical transformation in organizations?
mechanism_summary:
- Key construct: *viscosity* — a condition of organizational memory in which a problematic past "persistently resist[s] resolution, detachment or reframing," producing internal friction when change is attempted.
- Process / dimensions (third-order): (1) *emotional stickiness of the past*; (2) *haunting of the past*; (3) *drag and friction*; (4) *the past as a persistent interpretive lens*.
- Triggering condition: a retrospectively problematized "sticky past" (NorthBank's conduct around the 2007–08 financial crisis and government bailout) that members cannot fully forget or reframe.
- Emergent outcomes: ethical change is slowed, made more cautious, and rendered vulnerable to external skepticism — yet the same persistence can act as a *moral intensifier* that strengthens commitment to reform.
- Boundary condition: viscosity is *not intentional* and cannot be pinpointed to a single source; it is "felt in its effects," which limits how far managers can strategically bend organizational history.

The central process is that an irresponsible past does not simply recede but folds back into present organizational life through affective, material, and temporal traces, generating "drag" that resists managerial control. Because remembering here is involuntary rather than a strategic choice, viscosity bounds the interpretive flexibility of history: there is "only so much that can be done to bend it." Paradoxically, the very inability to "move on" repeatedly reminds members why ethical change is needed, so viscosity both constrains transformation and can catalyze it as a moral compass.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances *viscosity* as a distinct theoretical lens for organizational memory, showing that the past persists not only discursively but also affectively, materially, and temporally, thereby highlighting the limits of managerial agency that prior work on strategic remembering/forgetting tends to overlook. It contributes a vocabulary for how organizations "live with, and through, their unresolved and 'sticky' pasts," distinguishing viscosity from "stickiness" and "ghostly" founder metaphors and arguing—against accounts that treat the past as pliable or unilaterally negative—that viscous memory both constrains change and can serve as a moral intensifier.
practical_implication: Practitioners should treat memory and change as embodied rather than purely discursive: managers cannot assume that remembering is intentional or that a problematic past can be erased through strategic reframing. Responding to viscosity calls for patience with members' affective states (shame, guilt, fatigue), cleansing rituals of acknowledgment and mourning, and material/temporal "support structures." Most importantly, managers should recognize viscosity as an ethical compass — persistent grievances about the past signal ethical friction to be confronted, not mere resistance to dismiss.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Not reported in paper
abstract: This paper introduces viscosity as a conceptual lens to examine how organizational memory shapes ethical transformation. Based on a qualitative case study of a Dutch bank seeking to distance itself from a legacy of irresponsibility, we show how the past persists not only in discourse but also through affective, material, and temporal traces. These lingering effects create friction that complicates efforts at ethical renewal. Building on scholarship in organizational memory, historic corporate responsibility, and temporality, we theorize viscosity as the condition through which a problematic past becomes difficult to dislodge—sticking to identities, routines, and interpretations in ways that elude managerial control. Rather than framing ethical change as a matter of strategic reframing or discursive forgetting, viscosity draws attention to how history continues to be felt, inhabited, and negotiated in everyday organizational life. We show that while viscosity can constrain transformation, it may also simultaneously intensify the moral urgency for reform. In doing so, this study contributes to research on organizational memory by offering a vocabulary for understanding how organizations live with, and through, their unresolved and ‘sticky’ pasts.
apa_citation: Dudink, Y., Taminiau, Y., Veenswijk, M., & Bencherki, N. (2025). A ‘sticky’ past: The viscosity of organizational memory in navigating ethical change. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06210-7

title: The Business–Social Paradox of ESG Investing: Responding to Persistent Tensions over Time
methods: Qualitative 32-month longitudinal field study of one group ('Delphi') of institutional investors building an ESG indicator framework; non-participant observation of 26 conference calls and two in-person meetings, 15 interviews, and 27 documents; thematic analysis via constant comparison, temporal bracketing, and Gioia-style coding in NVivo.
industry: Sustainable/ESG investing within financial services (institutional investors: asset owners, asset managers, financial advisors)
country: Western European and mainstream US conventional investment banks (international cross-section)
units: A single voluntary expert working group ('Delphi') developing an ESG investment framework
n_sample: 35 recognized sustainable-investing experts, 10 regularly active; 26 conference calls, 2 meetings, 15 interviews, 27 documents
authors: Dumas, Christel; Louche, Céline; Bednarek, Rebecca
theories: business–social paradox (Battilana et al., 2017; Smith & Besharov, 2019); paradox theory (Smith & Lewis, 2011; Schad et al., 2016); practice lens on paradox (Feldman & Orlikowski, 2011; Jarzabkowski et al., 2013)
keywords: Business–social paradox; ESG investing tools; ESG obstacles; Paradox knots; Paradox multiplicity; Paradox persistence; Paradox responses
research_question: How do ESG investors, in their interactions with one another, respond to and construct persistent tensions over time to develop ESG investing tools?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: The business–social paradox, surfacing as three entangled tensions — measurement (quantitative metrics vs. expert judgment), efficiency (resource allocation vs. unproven materiality), and temporal (past indicators vs. future, short- vs. long-term).
- Paradox responses (practices): Experimenting (amplifying contradictions to spark creative solutions), ordering (structuring and defining the contradictory poles), anchoring (connecting the paradox to established external norms and principles), and shielding (parking and minimising to provide momentary relief).
- Process / phases: Three phases tracked the tool's evolution — Phase 1 "Cautious Discovery" (anchoring-dominant, measurement tension only), Phase 2 "Productive Frustration" (experimenting + shielding added, efficiency tension emerges), Phase 3 "Confident Dynamism" (rapid switching among all four responses, temporal tension surfaces, tensions become knotted).
- Emergent outcomes: Paradox "layered-ness" and "knotted-ness" — actors progressively construct the paradox in more complex ways, while the ESG tool grows from a fragile "strawman" (92 cells) into a comprehensive composite framework (~1,000 cells) combining metrics and narrative.

Over time the group moved from sequential, single responses to rapid, simultaneous combinations of all four strategies. Counterintuitively, members' confidence and comfort grew as the paradox's acknowledged complexity increased, suggesting a "learning spiral." Shielding, conventionally cast as a defensive response, instead created the conditions for sustained engagement and active experimentation, and the everyday "mundane" work of responding to the paradox yielded a more nuanced, ethical measurement tool that embraces rather than resolves contradiction.
theoretical_contribution: The study theorizes ESG investing through a paradox lens for the first time, shifting the focus from cataloguing and resolving obstacles to understanding how actors work through ESG's inherent business–social paradox over time. It contributes to paradox theory by extending "serial constructions of paradox" — showing how paradoxes are constructed in increasingly complex, layered, and knotted ways — and by reframing shielding as a dynamic enabler of long-term engagement rather than a defensive, vicious-cycle-inducing strategy, thereby challenging the binary of active "both/and" versus defensive responses.
practical_implication: A paradox perspective offers practitioners a fresh framework for making more informed, ethical ESG decisions and may alleviate the frustrations they face. Because finance professionals tend to dismiss ESG's business–social paradox, the study highlights the importance of cultivating mindsets to embrace paradox and gaining experience with it — skills often neglected in financial education — to avoid downplaying tensions and developing inappropriate, reductive tools.
limitations: The study focused on meetings and overlooked participants' activities between them, where noteworthy events might occur. The findings derive from a single collaborative, voluntary working group with little top-down control, which the authors note shapes the dynamics they observed.
future_research: Future work could study settings without interruptions to examine subtle, less-active responses, and could test the framework's transferability to other business tools navigating CSR or strategy paradoxes and to more top-down or hierarchical settings. Researchers could further examine the mechanisms driving shifts between response types and elucidate how the "learning spiral" competencies for engaging paradox are cultivated at individual and collective levels.
abstract: Growing support for Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) investing over the past twenty years has not been matched by widespread implementation. Prior research has identified obstacles to ESG investing. However, little attention has been paid to its inherent paradoxical nature, to examine the persistent tensions involved and understand its complicated practical realisation. Studying why barriers to ESG investing endure offers valuable insights into how paradoxes persist over time, particularly when examining how these paradoxes interact with various response strategies. Over 32 months of field work, we collected observations of meetings, interviews and secondary data of institutional investors developing an ESG investment framework. Our analysis dissects the business–social paradox’s interconnected tensions linked to measurement, efficiency, and temporality. We identified four paradox response strategies—experimenting, ordering, anchoring, and shielding—that enabled the group to construct and work through multiple layers of complexity over time. Our findings advance ESG investing literature by theoretically reframing ESG investing into a paradoxical lens and showing how the mundane responses to paradoxical tensions, which shift over time, can support the development of more nuanced ESG tools. They also contribute to paradox theory by emphasizing the role of shielding as a dynamic enabler rather than a defensive strategy and by showing how responses gradually deepen over time.
apa_citation: Dumas, C., Louche, C., & Bednarek, R. (2025). The business–social paradox of ESG investing: Responding to persistent tensions over time. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06217-0

title: What Happens After Whistleblowing? A Systematic Literature Review of the Post-whistleblowing Phase
methods: Systematic literature review (PRISMA) of empirical studies on the post-whistleblowing phase; database search (JSTOR, Emerald, APA PsycInfo, ScienceDirect) plus manual search restricted to 2022 ABDC-listed journals, yielding a final corpus of k = 34 studies synthesised narratively across theory, stakeholders, outcomes, temporality, and methods.
industry: Cross-sector; source studies span public sector, military, financial services, education, and others, across a wide range of professions and occupations
country: Predominantly North America (26 of 34 studies feature US participants; 20 US-only), with smaller representation from the UK, Norway, Ireland, and Asia
time_period: No restriction on publication date; included studies span 1982-2024
units: Peer-reviewed empirical studies on the post-whistleblowing phase (the unit of theoretical interest is the individual whistleblower and other affected stakeholders)
n_sample: k = 34 studies
authors: Edirisinghe, Chamari Lalanthi; Vleugels, Wouter; Flatau-Harrison, Huw; Noblet, Andrew
theories: minority influence theory (Moscovici, 1976); prosocial organizational behavior (POB) model (Dozier & Miceli, 1985; Miceli & Near, 1992); resource dependency theory (Pfeffer & Salancik, 1978)
keywords: Organisational wrongdoing; Post-whistleblowing; Systematic literature review; Whistleblowing
research_question: The review is guided by three questions: (1) what theoretical and methodological paradigms have been used to study the post-whistleblowing phase of the whistleblowing process?; (2) how are different stakeholders impacted during the post-whistleblowing phase?; and (3) how does the post-whistleblowing phase unfold over time (i.e., is the process characterised by different stages and a temporal chain of events)?
mechanism_summary:
- Core constructs: post-whistleblowing as a temporal process unfolding from short-term (immediate aftermath) to long-term outcomes for whistleblowers, wrongdoers, and other stakeholders.
- Influencing factors: organised into personal factors (demographic, personality, individual job-situation characteristics) and environmental factors (organisational, wrongdoing, and country/culture characteristics), with environmental — especially co-worker, supervisor, and top-management support — receiving most attention.
- Outcome families (Vallerand's motivational consequences framework): cognitive, affective, and behavioural outcomes, predominantly negative (e.g., retaliation, dismissal, anxiety) but occasionally positive (e.g., policy reform, peace of mind).
- Dominant theoretical lenses: the prosocial organizational behavior (POB) model, resource dependency theory, and minority influence theory.

The review synthesises a fragmented empirical literature by mapping how organisational responses to disclosure (often retaliatory) interact with personal and environmental factors to produce a temporally evolving chain of outcomes for multiple stakeholders. It argues that proximal short-term outcomes lay the foundation for more severe distal long-term outcomes, and that power dynamics between whistleblower and wrongdoer — theorised via resource dependency and minority influence — shape the trajectory and severity of these consequences.
theoretical_contribution: By focusing on the under-examined post-whistleblowing phase, the review provides a more holistic account of the complete whistleblowing process and integrates evidence on the responses of both whistleblowers and wrongdoers in the post-disclosure phase. It introduces a temporal perspective distinguishing short- and long-term outcomes and the interplay between proximal and distal consequences, and proposes additional theoretical frameworks (e.g., Job Demands-Resources, affective recognition, institutional, social identity, and corporate ethical virtues theories) to better bridge individual- and organisational-level post-whistleblowing experiences.
practical_implication: A systematic assessment of post-disclosure responses generates insights into the legislation and policy needed to safeguard whistleblowers' rights after disclosure, and highlights national and cross-national variation in whistleblower protections. The authors call for strengthened practice-oriented research that enhances whistleblower legitimacy through improved legal protections and underscores corporate governance's role in combating financial crime.
limitations: The reviewed evidence base is dominated by US-based, cross-sectional studies focused almost exclusively on the whistleblower's perspective (31 of 34 studies), introducing geographical, cultural, and stakeholder biases and limiting understanding of wrongdoers and other stakeholders. Considerable variation in how studies define short- and long-term, and the scarcity of longitudinal designs (only four of 34), constrain inferences about the temporal dynamics the review seeks to characterise.
future_research: Future work should adopt longitudinal and temporally precise designs (e.g., panel and diary studies) with consistent reference periods, incorporate multi-source data capturing wrongdoers' and third parties' perspectives, and extend cross-cultural and cross-jurisdictional comparisons on a global scale. The authors set out a structured agenda (Table 3) pairing research questions with frameworks such as resource dependency, minority influence, Job Demands-Resources, corporate ethical virtues, social learning/cognitive, social identity, and institutional theory, and recommend mixed-method approaches and evaluations of the effectiveness of whistleblower protection laws.
abstract: In the wake of a rise in high-profile whistleblowing incidents, there has been a substantial increase in scholarly attention toward understanding the complexities of what happens after people have engaged in the act of whistleblowing. According to the current literature, this phase is often associated with significant harm for both the individuals who ‘blow the whistle’ as well as the organisations in which the wrongdoing has occurred. Given this real-world significance, together with the rapidly growing body of empirical research, a comprehensive review of the post-whistleblowing literature is warranted. The overall aim of the current review is to provide a state-of-the-art assessment of what is known about the post-whistleblowing phase and to identify how future research can advance current research and scholarship on whistleblowing. Specifically, we summarise information about current research trends and dominant theoretical perspectives evident in the post-whistleblowing literature; provide a categorisation of the different types of organisational wrongdoing; discuss the factors influencing, mediating, and moderating post-whistleblowing outcomes, and outline the methodological strengths and weaknesses of prior research. Finally, the current review highlights areas in critical need of development and paves the way for a more holistic understanding of the complete whistleblowing process, including a temporal perspective of the post-whistleblowing phase.
apa_citation: Edirisinghe, C. L., Vleugels, W., Flatau-Harrison, H., & Noblet, A. (2025). What happens after whistleblowing? A systematic literature review of the post-whistleblowing phase. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06214-3

title: Toward a typology of boundaries in crisis management
methods: Conceptual theory development; the authors synthesize the crisis-management and organizational-boundaries literatures to build a typology of four boundary types (physical, social, mental, temporal) crossed with two dimensions (intentional/unintentional), and demonstrate it through a single illustrative case of a weather-related crisis at a summer camp.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Estey, Rachel; Potočnik, Kristina; Skountridaki, Lila
theories: boundary theory / organizational boundaries (Hernes, 2004; Jæger and Pedersen, 2020; Ashforth et al., 2000); complexity theory (Cilliers, 2001); stakeholder theory (Freeman et al., 2010)
keywords: boundary management; crisis management; crisis response; typology of boundaries
research_question: Why do so many organizations fail to respond to crises effectively, and what can be done to improve their crisis response? The paper asks how the concept of organizational boundaries can be used to understand and better prepare for crises in ways that linear, prescriptive crisis-management models have not.
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: organizations are social constructs constituted by interacting and intersecting boundaries; locating, defining, and managing those boundaries yields a more holistic view of the organization that improves crisis decision-making toward a desired outcome.
- Key constructs (boundary definition): a boundary is "a permeable element between two or more concepts or units that supports or impedes flows across heterogeneous spaces," characterized by four core attributes — permeability, ambiguity, multifacetedness, and dynamism — each existing on a continuum.
- Four boundary types: physical (material/regulatory; order and location), social (relational interactions among internal/external stakeholders), mental (understanding, sensemaking, groupthink, mental models), and temporal (time-bound and time-sensitive aspects of a crisis).
- Two dimensions: intentional (consciously planned/managed) vs. unintentional (unplanned byproducts), yielding eight possible boundary configurations.
- Boundary relations: boundaries coexist, intersect, and interact, producing compounding "ripple effects" in which a change to one boundary creates, reinforces, transcends, or dissolves others.
- Boundary conditions: it is impossible to know with certainty how individuals or groups will react in a crisis or which boundaries will come into play; centralized and decentralized decision-making are theorized as occurring simultaneously across all organizational levels.

The central process is that crises perturb an organization's pre-crisis configuration of boundaries (aligned with Hernes' loose/medium/tight boundary configurations), and the reinforcement, transcendence, or dissolution of any one boundary cascades through the others. By making these shifting boundaries identifiable and definable, practitioners can anticipate ripple effects and enact boundary-management actions that steer a non-linear, dispersed crisis response toward a favorable resolution rather than relying on prescriptive stage models.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes three contributions: it imports the boundary-management literature into crisis management to address the limitations of overly rational, linear stage models; it bridges the internal-external divide in most crisis-management frameworks (Bundy et al., 2017) by treating within-organization dynamics and stakeholder/environment interactions through a single boundary lens; and it advances the boundary literature itself by theorizing boundaries and organizations as inseparable, mutually constituting entities, offering a definition centered on what a boundary *is* (permeable element) rather than what it does.
practical_implication: By identifying and locating the physical, social, mental, and temporal boundaries present in a crisis, practitioners can make educated guesses about the ripple effects of boundary changes and enact boundary-management actions (reinforcing, weakening, or dissolving boundaries) to guide the response toward a desired outcome. Tools such as "war gaming" crisis plans and post-crisis reflective practice can surface and resolve boundary tensions before or after a crisis, and managers are advised to treat centralized and decentralized decision-making as operating simultaneously rather than as command-and-control alone.
limitations: The typology is conceptual and is demonstrated only through a single illustrative case of a weather-related crisis at a US summer camp; it has not been empirically tested. The authors note that it is inherently impossible to know with certainty which boundaries will arise in a crisis or how actors will react, and that unintentional boundaries are difficult to predict and manage, constraining the predictive power of the framework.
future_research: The authors call for refining the typology through empirical data collection, including manipulation of specific boundaries toward a set goal and intervention studies; developing an actionable decision-making model to help practitioners identify needed boundary-management actions; examining the long-term implications of boundary shifts (whether boundaries return to pre-crisis states or become a "new normal," and consequences for stakeholder management); and testing whether the typology applies to organizing beyond crisis contexts more generally.
abstract: Why do so many organizations fail to respond to crises effectively, and what can be done to improve their crisis response? We aim to address these questions by developing a typology of boundaries that can help us understand and better prepare for future crises. Building on the existing literature on crisis management and boundaries, our typology seeks to address the critique of current crisis management models, such as these being too prescriptive and placing too much emphasis on assumptions that only the decisions made by the crisis management team (i.e., centralized decisions) will impact the crisis response. We address this critique by elaborating on four categories of boundaries that are relevant to manage crisis response, such as physical, mental, social, and temporal. Examples of these boundaries could include access to properties (physical), interpreting whether a crisis is happening (mental), shifting relationship dynamics (social), and the urgency of response time to a crisis (temporal). Our typology offers a lens to consider crisis management by taking into consideration the complexities and nuances that linear crisis management models have not yet adequately addressed. We argue that by identifying, defining, and understanding different configurations of boundaries, one can better manage crises toward a desired outcome.
apa_citation: Estey, R., Potočnik, K., & Skountridaki, L. (2025). Toward a typology of boundaries in crisis management. *Human Relations*. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187267251391692

title: The Crisis of Dignity at Work: Dehumanization during Digital Transformation, Societal Upheaval, and Workforce Uncertainty
methods: Conceptual guidepost essay; develops a research agenda by synthesizing the authors' prior dignity-at-work research, illustrative media narratives, and public-sentiment analysis of media reports; proposes three pathways forward for management and organizational scholarship.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Gibson, Cristina B.; Groves, Kevin S.; Margolis, Jaclyn; Lusk, Carol; Sakamoto, Kevin
theories: dignity at work (Gibson, Thomason, Margolis, Groves, Gibson & Franczak, 2023); hope theory (Snyder et al., 1991)
keywords: automation and AI; dehumanization; digital transformation; dignity at work; hope theory; rehumanization
research_question: At what cost to society are workforce disruptions justified by the promise that automation and AI can substitute for human work, and what can organizational scholars do to protect dignity during digital transformation? The authors ask how research, policy, and practice can rehumanize work, recognize the dark side of automation and AI, and reinstate hope and dignity.
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Justified by an unsubstantiated promise of AI/automation efficiencies, mass layoffs and devaluation of human capital are eroding dignity at work, producing disposability, disrespect, and alienation.
- Pathway 1 (From Dehumanization to Humanization): recognize and celebrate uniquely human capabilities (e.g., authentic empathy) that AI cannot replicate.
- Pathway 2 (From Ubiquity to Curvilinearity): investigate the potential dark side and diminishing returns of pervasive AI use on human cognitive and emotional capabilities.
- Pathway 3 (From Hopeless and Undignified to Hopeful and Dignified): apply hope theory, transparency, and professional identity refocusing to restore agency, hope, and dignity.

The guidepost theorizes that digital transformation, when framed solely around cost savings and efficiencies, detaches employees from meaning and purpose and signals their expendability, producing humiliation and ego threat. Drawing on the authors' science of dignity at work and on hope theory (Snyder et al., 1991) — which holds that hope develops when perceptions of agency and a pathway are present — they argue that leader development, transparency, and identity refocusing can rehumanize work and re-instill hope across a growing digital divide.
theoretical_contribution: The piece extends the authors' integrative "dignity at work" framework to the context of digital transformation, societal upheaval, and workforce uncertainty, articulating detractors and promoters of dignity as AI and automation diffuse. It introduces hope theory as a lens for restoring agency and dignity and proposes a curvilinear (point-of-diminishing-returns) view of AI use as an empirical research agenda for the field.
practical_implication: Leaders and organizations should adopt humane layoff and dismissal processes, make employees feel valued and their work meaningful, and pursue algorithmic and agent transparency to preserve human agency. Building AI as an integrated capability that creates sustainable value — rather than a bolted-on efficiency play — and supporting professional identity refocusing can protect hope and dignity during transformation.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors call for empirical exploration of three pathways: identifying and harnessing uniquely human capabilities, uncovering the long-term and potentially curvilinear effects of AI use on human cognition, thriving, and the customer experience, and examining how leaders, transparency, hope-building, and professional identity refocusing can cultivate dignity across stages of transformation.
abstract: Today, we witness unprecedented threats to dignity at work on a global scale, precipitated by workforce reductions, demeaning of public sector professionals and their contributions to society, funding withdrawal from important agencies and initiatives, and program closures at such a scale not thought possible until recently. This is putting progress for everything at risk, from environmental justice (access to clean water and air), to programs for the differently-abled and marginalized communities, to basic research on life-saving medical therapies. Many of these disruptions have been justified based on the promise that automation and artificial intelligence (AI) can substitute for the work of humans. In this guidepost, we ask, at what cost to society? And what can organizational scholars do about this? We call for research that rehumanizes work, recognizes the potential dark side of automation and AI, and reinstates hope and dignity during digital transformation.
apa_citation: Gibson, C. B., Groves, K. S., Margolis, J., Lusk, C., & Sakamoto, K. (2025). The crisis of dignity at work: Dehumanization during digital transformation, societal upheaval, and workforce uncertainty. *Academy of Management Discoveries*, 11(4), 479–484. https://doi.org/10.5465/amd.2025.0359
