title: Making Better Uses of Classics? Evaluating Critically the Legacy of Howard R. Bowen’s 1953 Social Responsibilities of the Businessman (1953–2023)
methods: Critical citation context analysis combining analytical induction (Gioia method) and a quantification strategy; manual coding of 435 citation contexts from 320 articles citing SRB, with breadth and depth scores, chi-square tests of cross-period differences, and an ordinal logistic regression on citation depth.
industry: Academic scholarship (320 articles across 73 journals) in business ethics, generalist management, and management-discipline domains
units: Citation contexts (435) nested in articles (320) citing Bowen's 1953 book SRB
n_sample: 435 citation contexts from 320 articles
authors: Gond, Jean-Pascal; Badih, Khodr
theories: analytical induction / Gioia method (Bansal & Corley, 2012; Gioia et al., 2013); citation context analysis (Anderson & Lemken, 2023; Small, 1982); obliteration by incorporation (Merton, 1973); politics of citation (Bermiss et al., 2025; Serenko et al., 2021)
keywords: Business and society; Business ethics; Citation context analysis; Classics; Howard R. Bowen
research_question: For what purpose do scholars use foundational works (RQ1)? Have scholars ignored valuable ideas in foundational works (RQ2)? And to what extent have scholars disregarded the theories and arguments of classics because of their ritualized citation (RQ3)? The paper investigates these questions through the case of Howard R. Bowen's 1953 *Social Responsibilities of the Businessman* (SRB).
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: "arguing" (mobilizing SRB to build or back an argument), "defining" (relying on SRB's "page 6" definition to specify CSR or related concepts), and "landmarking" (using SRB or Bowen to signal the historical/conceptual origin of the field); the tension between formal recognition and informal neglect of a classic.
- Process / analytic layers: open coding of citation contexts into first-order codes, aggregation into second-order themes and three aggregate dimensions (Gioia method); then a quantification layer scoring per-article "breadth" (range of distinct first-order codes) and "depth" (1 = generic mention to 3 = substantive engagement).
- Triggering conditions: a classic's rising citation volume coupled with the "low-paradigm" status of business ethics, which keeps scholars reliant on classics rather than building theory from them.
- Emergent outcomes: landmarking dominates and rises over time (50–80% of citations since the late 1990s) while arguing—the deepest mode—declines; average breadth (1.12) and depth (1.33) stay low, confirming "obliteration by incorporation" of SRB's substance.

Combining citation context analysis with analytical induction lets the authors move beyond bibliometric counts to characterize *what being cited means*: a high citation count does not equal positive or substantive impact. By scoring breadth and depth and relating them to the three induced modes, they show that SRB has become a canonical reference point invoked for legitimacy rather than a source of conceptual renewal, with an ordinal logistic regression confirming that arguing citations are significantly deeper than landmarking or defining ones.
theoretical_contribution: The study makes a threefold contribution: it extends prior insights into the origins of CSR by theorizing the nuanced, paradoxical status of Bowen as a recognized yet neglected founding thinker; it reveals problematic "politics of citation" in business ethics and management, where ritual citation of classics raises ethical concerns about credit, knowledge accumulation, and "reinventing the wheel"; and the induced "arguing–defining–landmarking" triptych lays the ground for theorizing a repertoire of the "manifest" and "latent" functions of how scholars use (and misuse) classical works.
practical_implication: For teaching, the authors encourage reading classic texts such as SRB "against the grain" in relation to contemporary debates (e.g., using its chapters on religion, power, or institutionalization), rather than citing it ritually. For research, scholars can mine SRB's overlooked institutional- and welfare-economics arguments to address current CSR puzzles, and can reuse the paper's research design to assess the appropriation of other foundational works (e.g., Freeman's stakeholder theory or Friedman's 1970 article).
limitations: The citation context analysis follows the standard approach of building a hand-coded sample of papers, which limits scale; the authors note this could be extended beyond hand-coding by combining manual coding with supervised machine learning to analyze thousands of citations. A second limitation is the focus on a single book: although Bowen's case is especially significant for business ethics, the investigation remains a "unique case" of one foundational work.
future_research: Future studies could scale citation context analysis via supervised machine learning to detect subtle trends across disciplines and time. They could also contrast the journeys of multiple foundational works within and beyond business ethics to theorize the dynamics of classical works' neglect and rediscovery, and build a theory of the "manifest" and "latent" functions of citing classics in management theory and the social sciences.
abstract: Problematizing the use of classics as core to the advancement of scholarly fields, we analyze the purpose underlying the citation of SRB (why it is cited) as well as the breadth (what is cited) and the depth (how much is being discussed) of scholarly engagement with this classic. Combining analytical induction, quantification and a critical citation context analysis of 435 citations from 320 articles citing SRB between 1953 and 2023, we identified three main categories of use—arguing, defining, and landmarking—and evaluated the depth and breadth of such scholarly uses of SRB. Our results reveal how these uses differ across disciplinary domains and over time. Our analysis of breadth and depth confirms the continuous superficial engagement with SRB. We rely on this case to conceptualize the paradoxical legacy of Bowen, discuss ethical issues inherent to the politics of citation and evaluate what ‘fair’ uses of classics could be.
apa_citation: Gond, J.-P., & Badih, K. (2025). Making better uses of classics? Evaluating critically the legacy of Howard R. Bowen’s 1953 Social Responsibilities of the Businessman (1953–2023). *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06157-9

title: Guardrails for Human-AI Ecologies: Norm-Based Coordination and Design for Predictability
methods: Conceptual design-theory paper. The authors integrate two kernel theories (predictive processing theory and social norm theory) to derive a design theory comprising four design principles (with subprinciples) for norm-based coordination in human-AI ecologies, illustrated via an autonomous-warehouse running example.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Grisold, Thomas; Berente, Nicholas; Seidel, Stefan
theories: design theory (Gregor et al., 2020); predictive processing theory (Clark, 2013, 2016; Hohwy, 2013; Friston et al., 2017); social norm theory (Hawkins et al., 2019; Lewis, 1969; Ullmann-Margalit, 1977)
keywords: Human-AI ecology; design principles; predictive processing; social norms; unstructured coordination
research_question: How do norms emerge as humans and AI-based agents interact in human-AI ecologies involving unstructured and changing situations, and how can managers and developers design these ecologies so that norm-based coordination remains consistent with essential values while allowing norms to emerge in unspecified situations?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Norms function as shared predictive models that render agents' behavior mutually predictable; "deep norms" (abstract, stable, high-level predictions with high precision) give rise to situated "surface norms" (specific, flexible lower-level predictions), and prediction errors in uncertain coordination scenarios drive surface-norm adjustment, generation, and—when reliable and value-consistent—reinforcement back into deep norms.
- Key constructs: deep norms vs. surface norms; predictive models, precision weights, and prediction errors; structured vs. unstructured coordination; coordinated interaction; low- vs. high-uncertainty coordination scenarios; roles of managers, developers, and agents.
- Design principles: DP1 design for shared norms (deep- and surface-norm encoding); DP2 design for human intervention (outcome monitoring and deep-norm reinforcement); DP3 design for predictability through sensory signals (norm-enactment communication and inference); DP4 design for predictability by enacting norms (surface-norm defaulting, adjustment, generation, reinforcing).
- Boundary conditions: agents must share the same deep norms; predictions diagnosed as deep-norm violations should not trigger surface-norm updates; the framework presumes AI-based agents can be approximated as Bayesian prediction machines, which current machines only partially realize.

The paper integrates predictive processing theory—which treats both human brains and AI learning algorithms as prediction machines that project prior knowledge onto incoming sensory signals and update models in response to prediction errors—with social norm theory, in which norms are conventions providing shared expectations that stabilize interaction. Synthesizing the two, norms become "mutually constraining error minimization processes": deep norms encode the ecology's values as high-precision high-level predictions, while surface norms instantiate them for specific coordination scenarios. When agents face uncertainty and existing predictions fail, prediction errors signal a need to adjust or generate surface norms; successful, value-consistent surface norms can be reinforced into deep norms, whereas large errors implicating deep norms should generally not update the model.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops a design theory for norm-based coordination in human-AI ecologies by integrating predictive processing theory and social norm theory, proposing "design for predictability" as the organizing logic and four design principles (with subprinciples) as actionable design knowledge. It advances a more symmetrical view of human and AI-based agents—both modeled as Bayesian prediction machines—departing from the prevailing information-systems assumption that humans and AI are fundamentally distinct agents requiring constant reconciliation, and it theorizes the bidirectional dynamics between deep and surface norms as a mechanism for value-consistent emergence and governance of norms.
practical_implication: Managers and developers can encode an ecology's values as deep norms and selected surface norms in agents' hierarchical prediction models (e.g., through simulation-based training), design agents to communicate and infer the surface norms they enact via sensory signals, and continuously monitor emergent surface norms to intervene and reinforce desirable ones. The principles also offer mechanisms for embedding regulatory requirements (e.g., EU AI Act principles and post-market monitoring) into the design and ongoing management of high-risk AI ecologies such as autonomous warehouses.
limitations: The design principles are future-oriented and intended to inform inquiry rather than to be directly empirically validated, and they confront an "implementation problem"—current machines are not yet capable of fully implementing predictive processing—and an "update problem"—encoding and communicating new deep and surface norms requires both technical means to change agents' inner workings and means to retrain or inform large numbers of human and AI-based agents, which is challenging. The symmetrical human-AI framing is acknowledged as a broad-brush abstraction that elides important differences, such as the more embodied nature of human cognition.
future_research: Future work can address the implementation and update problems by specifying how the abstracted mechanisms (e.g., reinforcement learning) can be realized across technologies, and can leverage the assumed human-AI symmetry to design overarching interventions—such as environmental manipulations that trigger prediction errors in both agent types simultaneously—relevant to impact-oriented and design-science research. Researchers can also explore how implementing values as deep norms enables simulation to predict when, how, and why surface norms will emerge, and how monitoring emergent norms can inform evolving AI policymaking and regulatory design.
abstract: Human-AI ecologies involve human and AI-based agents that coordinate their interactions in part by following social norms. Social norms, therefore, are important for establishing the guardrails that ensure desirable interactions in a way that is consistent with essential values, such as human safety. Managing human-AI ecologies requires specifying norms to enable coordination in known situations but also allowing for the emergence of norms to enable coordination in unspecified, unstructured situations. We integrate predictive processing theory and social norm theory to explain how existing norms are enacted and reinforced based on agents' predictive models and how new norms emerge as agents update their predictive models in response to prediction errors in uncertain coordination scenarios. Rooted in this perspective, we develop a design theory that emphasizes design for predictability and propose a set of design principles for managers and developers to encode norms to evolve in human-AI ecologies, monitor outcomes, and intervene when necessary.
apa_citation: Grisold, T., Berente, N., & Seidel, S. (2025). Guardrails for human-AI ecologies: Norm-based coordination and design for predictability. *MIS Quarterly, 49*(4), 1239-1266. https://doi.org/10.25300/misq/2025/18058

title: Nexus Effect: Unraveling the Impact of Political Patronage Connections on Corporate Investment
journal: Journal of Financial and Quantitative Analysis
methods: Staggered difference-in-differences exploiting provincial leadership turnover; OLS with firm and year fixed effects, standard errors clustered by firm; 1:1 propensity-score-matched sample; Gardner (2022) two-stage estimator; cross-sectional and channel/efficiency analyses.
industry: Publicly listed A-share firms on the Shanghai and Shenzhen exchanges, excluding financial-industry firms and cross-listed B-share/H-share firms
units: Firm-year observations (firm-matched sample of 10,848 observations); 243 prefectural units across 25 provincial regions; 201 provincial leaders and 2,391 prefectural officials
n_sample: 10,848 firm-year observations (firm-matched sample)
authors: Guo, Ping; Shi, Guifeng; Tian, Gary Gang
theories: relational contracts / patronage and agency in political hierarchies; social identity theory (Akerlof & Kranton, 2000; Chen & Li, 2009)
keywords: China; corporate investment; investment efficiency; political patronage networks; promotion incentives; school ties
research_question: How do patronage networks among politicians — specifically school ties between Chinese prefectural officials and their superior provincial leaders — affect the investment expenditures and investment efficiency of firms located in connected prefectures, and through what mechanism?
mechanism_summary: School ties between prefectural and provincial officials build mutual trust and signal favoritism within the political hierarchy, raising connected officials’ promotion prospects. To demonstrate loyalty and improve advancement odds, these officials push local firms to invest more and channel preferential resources to them, lifting investment expenditures (a DiD-estimated ~29.85% increase). Because officials’ interests diverge from the public’s, this loyalty-driven effort distorts allocation — producing overinvestment, weaker innovation, lower total factor productivity, and negative market reactions — so patronage benefits client and patron officials at the expense of broader economic efficiency.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends research on political networks from direct politician–firm connections to interconnections among politicians, documenting a spillover of patronage onto the micro-economy that is less prone to endogeneity. It reconciles competing views in the patronage literature by showing that promotion incentives and distributive favoritism can co-exist, benefiting both client and patron officials while harming economic efficiency. It also enriches the literature on political influence over corporate investment by identifying patronage ties with higher-ranked leaders as a driver of higher but less efficient investment.
practical_implication: Managers and investors should weigh the broader political factors — including officials’ patronage networks — that shape a firm’s operating environment when making investment decisions, as these networks offer a novel explanation for regional investment imbalances in China and globally. Authorities should recognize that informal personal ties within government, while reducing information asymmetry and agency problems, can distort subordinates’ incentives and produce unbalanced resource allocation.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Not reported in paper
abstract: This study investigates how political patronage influences local firms’ investment decisions in China, focusing on changes in patronage ties resulting from provincial leadership turnover. By examining prefectural officials with connections to their provincial superiors, we find that firms in these regions experience increased investment expenditures, albeit with reduced efficiency. This effect is primarily driven by stronger promotion incentives for local officials, bolstered by favoritism from provincial patrons. While political patronage helps address agency problems within political hierarchies, our findings highlight its adverse economic impact due to misaligned interests between politicians and the public.
apa_citation: Guo, P., Shi, G., & Tian, G. G. (2025). Nexus effect: Unraveling the impact of political patronage connections on corporate investment. *Journal of Financial and Quantitative Analysis*, 60(8), 4035–4064. https://doi.org/10.1017/s0022109025000067

title: Enacting responsible leadership in cross-sector partnerships: A dynamic choreography of power
methods: Single-case study of one cross-sector partnership using a theory-elaboration approach; 2 years of on-site and virtual observation, 42 semi-structured interviews across four partner organizations, and document review, analyzed via process-research guidelines with abductive, iterative coding (power-with as sensitizing concept, expanded to power-over).
industry: Agricultural value chain / 'sweet' supply-chain development in a cross-sector partnership (Fortune 500 company, two international development organizations, and an implementing NGO)
country: 'Pearl', a pseudonymized developing country affected by natural disasters
time_period: 2-year data-collection period spanning the CSP's launch, pivot, and end phases
units: One cross-sector partnership comprising four partnering organizations (pseudonyms Corpo, Dollo, Fundo, NGO)
n_sample: 42 semi-structured interviews with individuals from all four partner organizations
authors: Harvey, Jean-François
theories: Mary Parker Follett's power-with / power-over (Follett, 1924, 1941, 1949); leadership-as-practice (Crevani and Endrissat, 2016; Raelin, 2017); responsible leadership (Maak and Pless, 2006; Pless, 2007)
keywords: cross-sector partnerships; leadership-as-practice; power dynamics; responsible leadership; stakeholder collaboration
research_question: How are power dynamics mobilized and navigated in CSPs to enact responsible leadership (RL) in practice?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: "power-with" (jointly developed, collaborative capacity for coordinated action) vs. "power-over" (more coercive, directive control); responsible leadership reframed as the "ethical orchestration of power."
- Process / phases: three CSP lifecycle phases — Launch ("gaining the spotlight"), Pivot ("disrupting the established order"), End ("meeting project needs") — each posing a distinct situated challenge.
- Practices (seven RL tactics): differentiating the project; garnering high-level support; debunking flawed strategies; linking to partners' broader goals; cornering resistance; tailoring outcomes; mobilizing external momentum.
- Triggering conditions: marked power asymmetries among partners, entrenched stakeholder interests/inertia, and divergent organizational evaluation metrics.
- Emergent outcomes: sustained partnership momentum and concrete results (increased farmer participation, higher incomes, lower reject rates, wider market access).

Responsible leadership in the CSP unfolded as a dynamic choreography of power: NGO actors, despite lacking formal authority, sequenced and recombined collaborative (power-with) and assertive (power-over) tactics in response to evolving stakeholder configurations at each phase. Grounded in Follett's "law of the situation," influence derived not from position but from convening actors around a collectively discerned course of action, with power-over selectively mobilized — through sequencing, signaling, and external accountability — to unblock progress when collaboration alone stalled. The process demonstrates that RL is distributed across interactions rather than residing in any single leader, and that carefully exercised power-over can be facilitative rather than corrosive to stakeholder trust.
theoretical_contribution: The study contributes a practice-based, empirically grounded reconceptualization of RL as the "ethical orchestration of power" — an adaptive, context-sensitive process in which actors blend power-with and power-over tactics to align interests and sustain joint action — rather than a fixed moral disposition or collaborative ideal. It extends RL theory beyond its normative, collaboration-centric roots by showing that assertive, even subtly coercive, action can be compatible with and instrumental to RL in contexts of diffuse authority, and it revives and updates Mary Parker Follett's relational view of power for contemporary cross-sector settings.
practical_implication: Leaders of CSPs should shift their focus from controlling resources and people toward active engagement with stakeholders and "the situation at hand," recognizing when and how to assertively guide action without overt dominance. RL leadership-development programs should train emerging leaders in the fragile balance between collaborative (power-with) and coercive (power-over) tactics through case studies and role-play, and cultivate the foresight to anticipate distinct challenges at a partnership's beginning, middle, and end stages.
limitations: As a single-case study, the findings are well-suited to theory elaboration but limited in generalizability and should be interpreted alongside other evidence; comparative case studies of multiple CSPs could strengthen external validity. The study also did not explore how the intersection of different institutional logics might lead stakeholders to interpret or enact RL differently, necessarily limiting the breadth of its insights.
future_research: Comparative case studies across diverse CSP contexts could reveal common patterns and context-specific variations in RL tactics during shared phases (launch, pivot, conclusion), informing a flexible, context-sensitive typology of RL tactics. Future work could also examine the contextually sensitive boundaries or "threshold" at which power-over remains ethically facilitative, and how differing institutional logics shape divergent enactments of RL.
abstract: How can leaders guide diverse organizations to work together on society’s biggest challenges when power is unevenly distributed? Cross-sector partnerships (CSPs) bring together companies, governments, and nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) to address complex problems, yet their success hinges on how leaders navigate competing interests and shifting power dynamics. This study examines a high-profile CSP involving a Fortune 500 company, two international development organizations, and an NGO. Drawing on Mary Parker Follett’s ideas about “power-with” and “power-over,” I show how NGO leaders combined collaborative and more coercive tactics to move the partnership forward. My analysis reveals that responsible leadership (RL) in CSPs is not a static trait or style but evolves through a dynamic choreography of power as challenges and priorities change. The findings offer practical lessons for leaders seeking to balance ethics, inclusion, and influence in multi-stakeholder collaborations, and they extend theory by reframing RL as a shifting, context-sensitive process rather than a fixed style.
apa_citation: Harvey, J.-F. (2025). Enacting responsible leadership in cross-sector partnerships: A dynamic choreography of power. *Human Relations*. https://doi.org/10.1177/00187267251398388

title: Performing Diversity: Navigating Tensions, Identity Threats, and Self-Instrumentalization in Applicant Diversity Statements
methods: Phenomenological, sequential, non-nested mixed-methods design combining an open-ended survey (Phase 1, 2021) with in-depth semi-structured interviews (Phase 2, 2024); inductive qualitative content analysis of survey data and directed (abductive) content analysis of interview data, with theoretical saturation as the stopping criterion.
industry: Academic job market (faculty and doctoral applicants); Phase 2 focused on Management faculty applicants
country: United States (with some adoption in Canada)
time_period: Phase 1 survey February 2021; Phase 2 interviews October–November 2024 (candidates on the market 2022–2024)
units: Academic job candidates who had written an applicant diversity statement
n_sample: 89 participants total (55 survey respondents in Phase 1; 34 interviewees in Phase 2)
authors: He, Joyce C.; Agarwal, Grusha; Kang, Sonia K.
theories: identity work (Goffman, 1959; Roberts, 2005); instrumental DEI framing (Kaplan, 2020; Georgeac & Rattan, 2022); organizational performativity (Roberson, Avery, & Leigh, 2024)
keywords: applicant diversity statements; diversity initiatives; diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI); organizational performativity; self-instrumentalization; social identity management
research_question: (1) How do applicants manage the self-presentation of their commitment to and contributions toward DEI, and what tensions do they encounter in doing so? (2) Under what conditions do these tensions arise, and what are the downstream consequences of engaging in this practice?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: Ambiguity about (a) what to include in a diversity statement and (b) what "diversity" actually means, amplified by the lack of institutional guidance and the evaluative, high-stakes hiring context.
- Core tensions: *Self-Disclosure Tension* — whether and how much to reveal personal identities and experiences in an instrumental setting; *Framing Tension* — how to package those identities and experiences to balance perceived institutional expectations with authentic self-expression.
- Central process — *self-instrumentalization*: a compelled, public, strategic, and evaluative form of identity work in which applicants must actively enact instrumental DEI framing of their own identities, with agency present but constrained by institutional expectations and power asymmetries.
- Emergent outcomes (threats): *Trivialization Threat* — the fear that one's identity is undermined and reduced to symbolic/token gestures; *Exploitation Threat* — the sense that one's identity is being used or co-opted for institutional gain, prompting discomfort, resentment, and disengagement.
- Boundary condition (preliminary): Non-White applicants more often described Self-Disclosure Tension (external pressure to highlight marginalized identities), while White applicants more often expressed Framing Tension (acknowledging privilege without overclaiming disadvantage).

Ambiguity about evaluators' expectations fuels two self-presentation tensions that reflect a conflict between strategic impression management (aligning with implicit expectations in a competitive market) and the need for authenticity (intensified by the personal and moral nature of identity). When statements are perceived as performative, these tensions amplify into threats of trivialization and exploitation. The paper theorizes self-instrumentalization as a distinct departure from prior DEI-framing work: rather than reacting to being instrumentalized by others, applicants are compelled to instrumentalize themselves, shifting the burden of proving DEI commitment onto individuals without corresponding institutional accountability.
theoretical_contribution: The study offers four contributions: (1) it extends research on instrumental DEI framing by shifting the focus from how individuals react to being instrumentalized by others to how they actively navigate compelled self-instrumentalization; (2) it contributes to the identity work literature by showing that strength-based identity framing—usually assumed to foster empowerment and authenticity—can feel exploitative and trivializing when publicly performed under evaluative pressure, including across multiple intersecting identities; (3) it extends research on organizational performativity by moving the analytical gaze from organizations' DEI motives to the effects of performative practices on the people they aim to include; and (4) it builds on work examining the ambiguity and subjectivity of "diversity" by showing how that uncertainty shapes applicants' self-presentation strategies and experiences of "inclusion."
practical_implication: The findings underscore the unintended psychological and strategic consequences of performative DEI practices that shift the burden of proof onto individuals without institutional accountability, which can generate ambivalence, resistance, and backlash even among those most invested in DEI. The authors argue that diversity statements can be meaningful rather than harmful only with greater organizational accountability and transparency—when inclusion is not merely stated but enacted in ways that support authenticity, autonomy, and mutual accountability.
limitations: The qualitative, sequential mixed-methods design limits the ability to make causal claims and to generalize, and the non-nested design with a roughly three-year temporal gap between phases means temporal shifts and field differences cannot be fully ruled out. Social desirability and identity dynamics may have shaped interview disclosures, and the focus on Management academia in the U.S. (with limited Canadian adoption) further constrains generalizability across disciplines, national contexts, and non-academic sectors.
future_research: Future work could use larger, more diverse samples and quantitative methods to test causal mechanisms, assess generalizability, and explore subgroup and intersectional differences (e.g., race × gender, race × disability). Researchers could examine how evolving political and institutional contexts shape applicant perceptions over time, how these tensions manifest in other evaluative contexts (promotion reviews, award nominations, DEI task forces), and how evaluators interpret and assign value to diversity statements and whether such statements help institutions achieve equity goals.
abstract: These tensions, triggered by ambiguity about the task's purpose and content, produce two identity-related threats: Trivialization Threat—the fear of one's identity being undermined and reduced to token gestures—and Exploitation Threat—the sense that one's identity is being used for institutional gain. By centering the applicant perspective, our study extends research on instrumental DEI framing, identity management, and organizational performativity by highlighting the self-instrumentalization required by applicants navigating ambiguous and evaluative DEI practices. Practically, our findings underscore the unintended consequences of performative DEI practices that shift the burden of proof onto individuals without institutional accountability.
apa_citation: He, J. C., Agarwal, G., & Kang, S. K. (2025). Performing diversity: Navigating tensions, identity threats, and self-instrumentalization in applicant diversity statements. *Academy of Management Discoveries*. https://doi.org/10.5465/amd.2024.0158

title: Second-Order Knowledge Intermediaries and Multi-Country Entrepreneurial Entry into a Nascent Industry
methods: Mixed methods. Stage 1: inductive qualitative analysis of 25 interviews with 18 clean cookstove organizations plus archival data (reports, news, company websites). Stage 2: Poisson pseudo-maximum-likelihood regressions (ppmlhdfe in Stata) on 676 country-year observations with year fixed effects and country-clustered standard errors, testing four hypotheses.
industry: Clean cookstove industry (mission-oriented; addressing health, environmental, and gender challenges of polluting cooking technologies)
country: 113 secondary countries (developing-country contexts where the focal first-order knowledge intermediary was not directly active)
units: Country-year observations (derived from the Global Alliance for Clean Cookstoves partner directory)
n_sample: 1,996 organizations yielding 791 country-year observations; 676 used in the analyzed sample
authors: Jue-Rajasingh, Diana
theories: industry emergence / nascent industries (Moeen & Agarwal, 2017; Agarwal et al., 2025); institutional intermediaries (Armanios et al., 2017); knowledge diffusion across borders (Wormald et al., 2021)
keywords: diffusion of innovation; entrepreneurship; intermediaries; international management
research_question: Can a geographically bound first-order knowledge intermediary (FOKI) support entrepreneurial entry and industry development in countries where it is not directly present, and if so, through what mechanisms and under what conditions?
mechanism_summary: The two-stage process holds that in Stage 1 a FOKI accumulates an industry knowledge base in a few primary countries through carrying, co-creating, capacity building, and convening, and that SOKIs participating in these activities acquire transferable industry knowledge and industry-building capabilities. In Stage 2, motivated SOKIs re-enact those activities in secondary countries, lowering local entrepreneurs' costs of acquiring and developing knowledge and thereby enabling entry. Effectiveness depends on complementarity: a balanced mix of company (knowledge-centric) and nonprofit (actor-centric) SOKIs builds a fuller knowledge base, whereas imbalance and the presence of domestic actors holding redundant knowledge dampen the SOKI-entry relationship.
theoretical_contribution: The study introduces a non-firm-led mechanism of cross-border knowledge diffusion for mission-oriented industries, extending "born global" accounts of industry emergence by showing how a geographically constrained FOKI can indirectly seed distant industries by activating SOKIs. It extends the institutional-intermediaries literature by demonstrating that knowledge intermediaries address technological, demand, ecosystem, and institutional uncertainty—not institutional uncertainty alone—across multiple national contexts, and it illuminates how under-resourced local entrepreneurs access global knowledge through SOKIs acting as knowledge "carriers."
practical_implication: Policymakers, foundations, and development organizations should invest in deliberately building market-based industry knowledge rather than charity-push giveaways, since strategic, concentrated investment in a FOKI in a few hub countries can yield outsized global impact when those hubs host regionally or globally active organizations able to transfer knowledge across borders. FOKI leaders should produce codifiable-yet-adaptable knowledge outputs, select primary countries by the presence of globally active actors, and empower SOKIs as industry ambassadors through capability-building, recognition, and legitimacy.
limitations: As a single-industry study of a sector with decades of prior donor-driven exposure, generalizability is limited and pre-existing conditions are hard to fully control. The data are self-reported from the Alliance's partner directory, raising the possibility of missing, inaccurate, or biased information, and the analysis is correlational, so causality cannot be established given potential endogeneity (e.g., SOKIs selectively entering countries already primed for growth). Multicollinearity among the explanatory variable and moderators may also inflate standard errors.
future_research: Future work could study an industry entirely new to all participating countries to isolate early-stage diffusion from a uniform baseline, and draw on regional, subnational, or administrative (non-self-reported) data for less biased measurement. Quasi-experimental designs such as matched-sample comparisons or instrumental variables could more rigorously test the proposed causal mechanisms, and longer-horizon studies could examine whether SOKIs remain central to building the industry knowledge base after entry accelerates or whether local entrepreneurs take on that role.
abstract: A quantitative analysis of data derived from 1,996 clean cookstove organizations operating across 113 countries (2013–2019) was conducted to assess whether a greater number of SOKIs in secondary countries was associated with increased entrepreneurial entries. Results support the model; more SOKIs were associated with more entrepreneurial entries, and this relationship was weakened when company and nonprofit SOKIs were imbalanced and when more domestic industry actors possessed overlapping knowledge. This study contributes to the literature on industry emergence by introducing a mechanism of non-firm-led, cross-border knowledge diffusion in mission-oriented industries. It also shows how local entrepreneurs in under-resourced contexts can access global knowledge—not through firms or FOKIs but through SOKIs that extend the FOKIs’ work.
apa_citation: Jue-Rajasingh, D. (2025). Second-order knowledge intermediaries and multi-country entrepreneurial entry into a nascent industry. *Organization Science*, 36(6), 2435–2458. https://doi.org/10.1287/orsc.2021.15661

title: Organizing Open Social Innovation For Societal Challenges: Unlocking the Potential of Open Spaces
methods: Single processual case study (MyHumanKit, a French disability-specialized fab lab); chronological narrative and temporal bracketing with interpretative coding of semi-structured interviews and archival sources.
industry: Fabrication laboratory (fab lab) / disability assistive-technology and open social innovation
units: One pioneering case study (MyHumanKit / Bionico Hand) within a French fab lab
n_sample: 1 case (MyHumanKit, MHK)
authors: Kherrazi, Soufiane
theories: open social innovation (Chalmers, 2013; Chesbrough & Di Minin, 2014); orchestration; scaling of social innovation
keywords: disability discrimination; hybrid organizations; open innovation; orchestration; scaling; social innovation
research_question: How can OSI be effectively organized and enabled to tackle grand challenges? Specifically, the paper examines how open spaces — particularly fab labs — enable the orchestration of the OSI process and the scaling of its outcomes.
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: Grand challenges are too complex and cross-sectoral to be solved by a single focal organization, motivating a society-centered "OSI 2.0" mode of organizing that cannot be fully designed a priori.
- Orchestrating mechanisms: (1) (re)framing the idea/project to align stakeholders; (2) resourcing — acquiring key resources and inviting stakeholders via global fab lab networks; (3) community building — setting rules, mobilizing volunteers, and fostering inclusion.
- Scaling mechanisms: (1) integrating (and evolving) across local, European, and international communities; (2) commons sharing under open-source/MIT-charter practices; (3) spinning off the initiative into an autonomous values-driven OSI venture.
- Emergent dynamics: The six mechanisms are interdependent (e.g., resourcing with integrating; reframing with spinning off) and shift across the process — community building early, commons sharing later — so the OSI 2.0 process emerges and evolves rather than being predetermined.

The case shows that fab labs act as hybrid open spaces (the "Middleground") that orchestrate and scale OSI by mobilizing dispersed actors and resources, lowering barriers to participation, and enabling commons-based co-production, ultimately catalyzing emergent, multi-stakeholder social innovation around disability.
theoretical_contribution: The study offers an updated framework for OSI research, providing empirical insight into the cross-sectoral and multi-stakeholder OSI 2.0 perspective and highlighting the emergent, dynamic nature of its processes versus the organization-centric OSI 1.0. It uncovers interdependencies among orchestration and scaling mechanisms that prior literature overlooked, and enriches the literature by exploring the intersection of open innovation and disability — including spinning off as an understudied element of empowerment distinct from business and social venturing contexts.
practical_implication: For social entrepreneurs, fab labs offer platforms to design interventions, build networks, and rapidly prototype to scale impact. Policymakers, philanthropists, and universities should secure fab labs' financial viability and tools through funding and public-private partnerships. Firms and managers can use fab labs for low-volume repair/customization, to empower disabled employees, and to reinforce CSR, while fab managers gain a life-cycle toolkit of mechanisms from (re)framing to spinning off.
limitations: The exploratory research is grounded in a single pioneering case study, which constrains generalizability of the derived framework. The finding that spinning off is a critical element of empowerment is noted as requiring further investigation.
future_research: Future studies could employ multiple case studies to validate the framework derived from these findings. More research on open spaces is needed, and the role of spinning off in social and disability contexts — distinct from business and social venturing — warrants deeper investigation.
abstract: Open Social Innovation (OSI) is gaining increasing attention in discourses among scholars and practitioners as a new approach to addressing grand challenges. However, little is known about how to organize such OSI efforts. To address this gap, this article explores the role of open spaces, especially fabrication laboratories (fab labs), in orchestrating and scaling OSI efforts. Using a qualitative investigation, the findings reveal key mechanisms enabled by fab labs to orchestrate the OSI process and scale its outcomes. Implications for research, society, and practice are then discussed.
apa_citation: Kherrazi, S. (2025). Organizing open social innovation for societal challenges: Unlocking the potential of open spaces. *California Management Review*, 68(1), 76-96. https://doi.org/10.1177/00081256251381959

title: The Empire’s New Clothes: A Review of “Butler to the World: How Britain Became the Servant of Tycoons, Tax Dodgers, Kleptocrats and Criminals” by Oliver Bullough
methods: Book review (narrative critique) of Oliver Bullough's 'Butler to the World,' a journalistic, case-study-based account of the UK's role as a global financial intermediary.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Krammer, Sorin M. S.
theories: organizational justice / moral philosophy (fairness and equality) (Greenberg & Bies, 1992)
keywords: corporate social responsibility; corruption; financial deregulation; illicit financial flows; money laundering; tax havens
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
Not reported in paper

This is a book review and does not develop its own causal mechanism. As a critical reading, it foregrounds the process Bullough describes: Britain's post-imperial reinvention as a global financial "Butler," driven by both voluntary "push" factors (lax regulatory frameworks, minimal enforcement) and exogenous "pull" factors (the financial deregulation pressures of the 1980s and 1990s). Innovations such as London's Eurodollar market and offshoring nodes (e.g., the British Virgin Islands, Gibraltar, Scottish Limited Partnerships) sustain an "ethically gray system," while underfunding and a box-ticking compliance culture leave law enforcement unable to investigate sophisticated financial crime.
theoretical_contribution: The review connects Bullough's journalistic account to business-ethics scholarship, arguing that the UK's "butlering" activities violate two core tenets of moral philosophy — fairness and equality (Greenberg & Bies, 1992) — and that they harm global development and good governance by spurring inequality, corruption, and crime. Krammer positions "butlering" as an under-examined ethical phenomenon warranting more academic scrutiny, calling for ethics research that moves beyond conceptual and descriptive approaches toward practical policy solutions.
practical_implication: The review argues for reform: closing regulatory loopholes, enhancing tax transparency, ensuring effective enforcement, and eliminating inconsistent laws and preferential treatment for certain UK territories. It cautions that such reform is counterbalanced by powerful opposing interests and the UK's economic over-reliance on these activities, and notes that organizations often pair tax-minimizing and secrecy strategies with CSR or greenwashing to deflect societal criticism.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Krammer proposes three directions for ethics scholarship: (1) examine the drawbacks of minimal regulatory oversight and secrecy and the relationship between financial deregulation and societal deficiencies (corruption, crime, inequality); (2) investigate the trade-offs organizations face when combining opportunistic location-specific strategies with CSR or greenwashing; and (3) better leverage civil society and stakeholder mechanisms — including transparency in penalty enforcement and social-media activism — to improve corporate accountability.
abstract: the nation started assuming a new role as a global fixer to

title: Market Demand, Competition for Knowledge Workers, and Impact on Invention: Evidence from Electric Vehicle Technologies
methods: Difference-in-differences OLS on a firm-year patent panel (USPTO/PatentsView, 1981-2000), exploiting the 1990 California ZEV mandate as an exogenous demand shock; firm and year fixed effects, standard errors clustered at the firm level.
industry: Manufacturing firms holding patents in technological domains adjacent to electric-vehicle (EV) CPC subclasses (e.g., chemical, machinery, computer/electronic, plastics/rubber manufacturing); benchmarked against EV firms and control firms
country: United States (shock originates in California via the CARB ZEV mandate)
time_period: 1981-2000 (10 years before and 10 years after the 1990 ZEV mandate)
units: Firm-year observations (patent assignees); also inventor-year panel for mechanism tests
n_sample: 982 affected (adjacent-domain) firms, 4,198 control firms, and 111 EV firms; main firm-year panel of 92,801 observations
authors: Lu, Jino
theories: demand-pull innovation (Schmookler, 1966; Mowery & Rosenberg, 1979); knowledge-worker mobility (Palomeras & Melero, 2010); resource allocation / reallocation of innovation resources
keywords: adjacent technological domains; competition for skilled knowledge workers; electric vehicle technologies; firm invention; market demand; rate and direction of technological progress
research_question: How does an increase in downstream market demand within one technological domain affect the rate and direction of technological progress among firms in adjacent (non-demand-relevant) domains, by reshaping the competitive landscape for skilled knowledge workers?
mechanism_summary: Following an exogenous rise in downstream demand for EV technologies (the 1990 CARB ZEV mandate), EV firms increased EV inventive activity and competed more intensely for skilled knowledge workers, whose short-run supply is inelastic. Firms in adjacent technological domains—whose inventors possessed knowledge transferable to EV technologies—disproportionately lost those inventors to EV firms. Because knowledge workers drive both core technological capabilities and the capacity to diversify, the affected firms subsequently produced fewer inventions, concentrated their decline in core areas, and became less likely to explore new technological areas, with effects strongest for firms in growing domains and for smaller, younger firms.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends demand-pull innovation research by identifying an indirect negative spillover: demand growth in one domain can erode technological progress in adjacent domains through competition for a shared, inelastically-supplied innovation resource (knowledge workers). It bridges the demand-pull and worker-mobility literatures to show how a focal firm can be harmed by market and technological changes in seemingly unrelated industries, and it contributes to research on firms' reallocation of innovation resources by demonstrating that resource losses—not just opportunistic gains—shape which firms can diversify.
practical_implication: Policymakers designing demand-side innovation policies (subsidies, procurement, mandates) should account for indirect spillovers, since boosting one domain can siphon scarce inventors away from other emerging domains such as renewable energy, undermining overall policy efficiency. Firms should monitor the threat of losing knowledge workers to non-product-market competitors operating in technologically related domains and think more broadly about their boundaries and underlying technical capabilities.
limitations: Inferring inventor mobility from changes in patent-assignee information may miss inventors who moved but stopped patenting (false negatives), though the author argues this biases estimates toward underestimation. The findings may also reflect idiosyncratic features of the 1990s emerging-EV setting (a large talent gap, an unanticipated sales mandate), so negative spillovers could be tempered in technology-push contexts or when labor supply is more abundant. EV firms were identified ex post from CARB policy reports rather than by their ex ante diversification decisions.
future_research: The author calls for work on whether negative spillovers affect all firms equally, the short-term versus long-term effects of such spillovers, how firms can mitigate the risk of losing innovation resources and knowledge workers, and how policymakers might mitigate these negative spillovers when designing innovation policy.
abstract: workers—a critical innovation resource whose supply is often inelastic in the short term. Empirically, I test these arguments by exploiting an unexpected environmental policy shock—the zero emission vehicle (ZEV) mandate—which led to an exogenous increase in demand for electric vehicle (EV) technologies. Following the ZEV mandate, I find evidence of increased inventive activities in the EV domain by EV firms. However, firms in adjacent (non-EV) domains were more likely to lose knowledge workers to EV firms following

title: Tech Ethics, Organizational Values, and Ethical Work: Insights from a Qualitative Study of Five UK-Based Digital Technology Businesses
methods: Reflexive (reflective) thematic analysis of 30 semi-structured online interviews; inductive coding in NVivo following Braun & Clarke (2006, 2022); purposive sampling across five firms.
industry: Digital technology / tech sector firms (multi-national internet provider, software companies, digital services provider, start-up tech recruitment)
units: Employees of varying rank (engineers, senior managers/directors, marketing, HR, sales, procurement) across five UK-based technology businesses
n_sample: 30 interviewees (P1-P30); 12 female, 18 male; ages 19-64
authors: Maile, Andrew Jonathan; Brooks, Edward; Gulliford, Liz; Park, Rebecca
theories: espoused vs. enacted organisational values (Bourne et al., 2019); ethical climate / internal reputation (Bates et al., 2024; Harvey et al., 2022); virtue ethics (Neo-Aristotelian) (Robinson et al., 2013; Yuan et al., 2023)
keywords: Organisational moral identity; Organisational values; Tech ethics; Virtue ethics
research_question: Within a broader inquiry into the character strengths most important in the UK digital technology sector, the paper pursues two exploratory questions: (1) Which virtues or character strengths are considered most important for ethical leadership in these tech companies? (2) What organisational practices, both implicit and explicit, cultivate or limit the expression of these virtues in the workplace?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: espoused versus enacted organisational values; internal reputational coherence; moral alignment between personal and organisational values; exemplars vs. anti-exemplars; virtue vs. vice
- Themes (process): (i) "A lot of really, really good shiny examples" — leaders and followers act as lived exemplars of values; (ii) Values should 'talk the walk' — values must authentically articulate and reflect the existing culture; (iii) "You can't have a value if you don't believe in it fully" — personal-organisational value resonance underpins adoption into conduct
- Practices: bringing values into everyday "corridor" conversations; challenging the status quo; questioning and dialogue; embedding values into appraisal so enacting them becomes "part of the job"
- Emergent outcomes: cultivation of epistemic and moral virtues (humility, helpfulness, open-mindedness, curiosity, courage, epistemic generosity); strengthened moral agency; counteracting of vice (hubris, ego)

When organisational values are deeply embedded and enacted—rather than merely espoused—they articulate a tangible culture that guides moral conduct. Through a Neo-Aristotelian virtue-ethical lens, exemplars elicit admiration and emulation of virtues, while value resonance between employees and the firm lets individuals "fully believe" in the values and sustain moral integrity, supporting habituation of virtue and engaged moral agency in an ethically ambiguous sector.
theoretical_contribution: The paper offers a novel virtue-ethical, internally focused account of how organisational values operate at the local level to guide ethical conduct, shifting attention from external regulation and compliance-oriented "tech ethics" toward enacted values and ethical culture. It empirically substantiates conceptual proposals (e.g., de Rooij & de Bruin, 2022) on collective epistemic virtue, showing how exemplars, anti-exemplars, and personal-organisational value alignment foster virtue cultivation and moral agency within technology firms.
practical_implication: Technology firms can encourage moral conduct by selecting values that reflect human (not merely "corporate") virtues, ensuring espoused values are genuinely enacted and authentically reflect lived culture, surfacing both positive exemplars and counter-examples in everyday conversation, and embedding values into meaningful appraisal processes so that enacting them becomes part of the job. Senior leaders should support employees in developing the epistemic, moral, and service-oriented virtues their roles require.
limitations: Participant recruitment relied on organisational gatekeepers, and framing the study around "character strengths," "values," and "leadership development" may have introduced social-desirability, self-deception, and self-confirmation biases, partly mitigated by including negative/contradictory accounts and assuring confidentiality. Researcher bias is possible, addressed via reflexivity, bracketing, and blind peer review. Data were collected (June 2021-March 2022) before the widespread adoption of generative AI/LLMs such as ChatGPT, so the study could not directly address LLM-specific ethical issues.
future_research: The authors note that the ethical questions raised by the recent prevalence of LLMs and generative AI are now a major focus for ongoing research in the field (e.g., Zhao et al., 2025) and invite further work extending their findings to this rapidly evolving context. They also frame their study as putting "empirical meat onto the bones" of conceptual proposals about collective epistemic virtue, inviting further empirical development of virtue-ethical approaches to organisational values.
abstract: The ethical challenges raised by the reach of digital technologies into all aspects of life highlight the moral role of the technology industry in modern society. While there is great potential for good, irresponsible development and commercialisation efforts can result in serious harm. As digital technologies and artificial intelligence become increasingly ubiquitous, technology companies face pressure from government and civil society to take their moral responsibility seriously. This paper takes a novel, virtue-ethical approach to the role and contribution of the values of technology firms. Rather than focusing on regulation, it considers how organisational values might support ethical decisions and moral conduct among employees. The paper presents findings of a qualitative study, which reveals how enacted organisational values within five technology companies support the establishment of an ethical culture that guides moral conduct. Based on our reflective thematic analysis of 30 interviews with UK employees, we identify three themes for the effective use of organisational values and the establishment of an ethical workplace culture: (i) “A lot of really, really good shiny examples”, (ii) Values should ‘talk the walk’, and (iii) “You can’t have a value if you don’t believe in it fully”. According to participant accounts, technology companies can successfully encourage moral conduct through effective use of organisational values.
apa_citation: Maile, A. J., Brooks, E., Gulliford, L., & Park, R. (2025). Tech ethics, organizational values, and ethical work: Insights from a qualitative study of five UK-based digital technology businesses. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06205-4

title: Distinguishing Three Concepts of the Societal Good: The Managerial Case for Adopting the Thomistic Concept of the Common Good
methods: Conceptual / philosophical analysis. The authors trace and disentangle three concepts of the societal good (mutual advantage, common good, collective good) through close reading of Aristotle, Aquinas, and Enlightenment/post-Enlightenment thinkers, then derive firm-level managerial implications.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Mandray, Sara; Koehn, Daryl; Hühn, Matthias P.
theories: Thomistic / Aristotelian common good theory; consequentialism / utilitarianism (Bentham); virtue ethics
keywords: Aquinas; Aristotle; Collective good; Common good; Dignity; Mutual advantage; Personhood; Societal good
research_question: What are the conceptual differences among mutual advantage, the common good, and the collective good as conceptions of the "societal good," and why should business ethicists and managers adopt the Thomistic concept of the common good rather than the prevailing collectivist (consequentialist) alternative when theorizing the firm?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: The "societal good" conflates three historically distinct concepts — Aristotelian *mutual advantage* (τὸ κοινὸν συμφέρον), the Thomistic *common good* (bonum commune), and the modern *collective good* — which posit divergent products, foundations, scales, and anthropologies (Table 1).
- Key constructs: human dignity and personhood; eudaimonia/flourishing; virtue ethics vs. consequentialism; ethics' subordination to (or independence from) politics; charity and justice as the dual foundations of the Thomistic common good.
- Boundary conditions: Aristotelian mutual advantage is realizable only within a polis/nation-state and privileges male citizens, so it lacks safeguards for non-citizens; the collective good, rooted in consequentialism, reduces persons to aggregable utilities and threatens dignity.
- Managerial domains derived: leadership (sympathetic, charitable leadership vs. functional emotional intelligence), strategy (collegial, person-centered deliberation), organization theory (resisting the Weberian "iron cage"), human resources (dignity, inclusion, salary compression), and entrepreneurship (engaged, community-rooted founders).

The paper's central process is conceptual disentanglement: by reading Aristotle and Aquinas in their own terms, the authors show that Aquinas enlarges Aristotle's politically embedded mutual advantage into a charity-grounded common good extending to all of humanity, which subordinates law and politics to the dignity of each person. Because only the common good (bonum commune) — not the polis-bound Aristotelian good or the consequentialist collective good — secures the infinite worth of the human person, the authors argue it offers a superior, dignity-respecting foundation for theorizing the firm and its management.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes a clarifying conceptual taxonomy that separates three frequently conflated notions of the societal good and traces each to its intellectual lineage (Aristotle, Aquinas, Enlightenment consequentialism). It argues that the dominant common-good theory of the firm is an "Aristotelian-Thomist amalgamation" and sketches a "purer" Thomistic conception in which ethics is not subservient to politics, thereby positioning the bonum commune as a virtue-ethics foundation for management theory that better protects human dignity.
practical_implication: The authors translate the Thomistic common good into concrete managerial prescriptions: leaders should practice sympathy and charity (e.g., regular "mini-town halls," collegial weekly deliberation); strategy should integrate stakeholder input through humble, person-centered listening; HR should pursue inclusion, regular and prompt pay, and salary compression to express that the common good is shared; and entrepreneurship should be reframed around engaged, community-serving founders rather than the "Great Man." Organizations should resist rule-bound, system-centric governance that "partitions the soul."
limitations: The authors explicitly bound their scope: "Offering a comprehensive Thomist theory of the firm is beyond the scope of this paper," noting that such a theory is being developed in a separate working paper. The managerial implications are sketched rather than empirically tested, and the argument rests on a normative, religiously grounded (Thomistic/Catholic) framework whose premises may not be shared across all readers.
future_research: The paper points toward developing a comprehensive Thomist theory of the firm (referenced as forthcoming work) and toward elaborating the firm-level mechanisms — leadership tied to subsidiarity, discursive/dialogic decision-making, and person-centered HR — through which the common good can be realized within organizations independently of the political community.
abstract: How exactly individual flourishing and societal flourishing are connected is a central question in social sciences in general and in business ethics in particular. We argue that the main theories of flourishing have developed along a trajectory going from Aristotle to Aquinas and then to the current, prevailing understanding of the relation between individual and societal flourishing. This relationship has been theorized in terms of the pursuit and achievement of the "societal good." This concept of "societal good" includes three distinct concepts: mutual advantage, the common good, and the collective good. In this paper, we meticulously distinguish these concepts, tracing the concept of mutual advantage back to Aristotle, the common good to St. Thomas Aquinas, and the collective good to Enlightenment and post-Enlightenment thinkers. Our failure to be clear about the significant differences among these three concepts means we have not always understood exactly what is at stake in favoring (explicitly or implicitly) one notion over another. We argue that many business ethicists have adopted a collectivist approach, which, in subordinating ethics to politics, poses a significant threat to human dignity and thus to flourishing at both the individual and community level. Other business ethicists have argued for the adoption of an Aristotelian understanding of eudaimonia or flourishing, but that approach, too, may threaten human dignity. Although Aristotle stresses a politically embedded individual who possesses the authoritative agency to achieve his or her own flourishing while pursuing interests shared with other members of the polis, Aristotle's approach may be less effective than that of Aquinas when it comes to safeguarding the worth of each human being in the modern economy and society. Adopting a Thomistic approach to the common good, an approach which does not make ethics subservient to politics, offers a dignity-respecting and community-enhancing way for ethicists to rethink key elements of business management, including leadership, strategy, organization theory, human resources, and entrepreneurship.
apa_citation: Mandray, S., Koehn, D., & Hühn, M. P. (2025). Distinguishing three concepts of the societal good: The managerial case for adopting the Thomistic concept of the common good. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06206-3

title: Social Media and Political Affiliation: How Expressing Hot-Button Opinions Affects Raters’ Assessments of Job Applicants
methods: Online vignette experiment with a 2 × 2 × 2 × 2 between-subjects design (political stance × platform × academic-performance × community/volunteer individuating information); analyzed via MANCOVA and structural equation modeling (lavaan in R), supplemented by a post hoc qualitative applicability check with 79 hiring managers.
industry: Working professionals with hiring experience (cross-industry), recruited via Qualtrics
units: Individual raters (hiring decision makers)
n_sample: 377 raters in the experiment; 79 hiring managers in the qualitative applicability check
authors: Nabity-Grover, Teagen; Matthews, Kevin D.; Roth, Philip L.; Kwon, Youngjin; Thatcher, Jason B.
theories: CARE theory (Leidner & Tona, 2021); political affiliation model (PAM; Roth et al., 2017); similarity-attraction paradigm (Byrne, 1971); social identity theory (Tajfel & Turner, 2004)
keywords: CARE theory; immigration; online experiment; political affiliation; political ideology; social media
research_question: How, and on which social media platform, does viewing an applicant's posts about a hot-button political issue (immigration) affect hiring managers' (raters') evaluations of the applicant's hireability, and does the platform on which the content appears moderate that influence?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Two forms of job-relevant individuating information (academic performance; community/volunteer engagement).

The paper contextualizes the political affiliation model (PAM) and the underlying similarity-attraction paradigm to social media screening: viewing an applicant's hot-button posts triggers an early affective response (identification or disidentification), which shapes perceived similarity, which in turn drives liking, which raises hireability ratings. Drawing on CARE theory, the authors frame disclosure as "showing self" and screening as "knowing others." Empirically, the PAM chain holds (H1a–1d, H2, H3 supported), but platform does not moderate it (H4 unsupported), yielding the novel construct of "hedonic platform generalizability." Strikingly, the job-irrelevant hot-button information swamps the effect of job-relevant individuating information on hireability.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances IS and organizational-behavior research by showing that issue-level political stances (rather than party affiliation) bias hiring decisions through perceived similarity and liking as specified by the PAM, and by extending CARE theory's account of how personal digital data shape human dignity in screening. It introduces the novel construct of "hedonic platform generalizability"—the claim that the nature of the disclosed information, not the hedonic platform on which it appears, drives the influence on off-line organizational decisions.
practical_implication: The authors propose three responses to social-media-screening bias: forgoing information-gathering from hedonic social media, integrating any such information into formalized structured assessments that reduce rater bias, and incorporating the findings into training programs that educate hiring managers on the risks of screening. They also alert applicants—especially younger users and cyberactivists—that hot-button posts can cost career opportunities regardless of platform, recommending private or anonymized accounts.
limitations: Because the study used an online experiment, raters may have altered their answers in ways that did not reflect actual behavior, possibly muffling effects. The study was conducted only in the United States, so results may differ in other political cultures. Only Facebook and Twitter were examined, and the platform was operationalized as a binary variable, permitting only a platform-level (not feature-level) discussion.
future_research: Future work should examine how hot-button posts combine with other disclosed information (e.g., multiple issue stances, faith, party intensity, or more benign content like sports fandom) to shape decisions, and how broader political moods and shocking events amplify or dampen these effects. Researchers should also study the applicant's perspective on screening and further test and bound the proposed "hedonic platform generalizability" concept across additional platforms and decision contexts.
abstract: The platform on which the social media posts appeared (i.e., Facebook or Twitter) did not moderate the influence of viewing the posts on hiring decisions, suggesting hedonic platform generalizability. We explore these findings with a qualitative applicability check conducted using insights drawn from 79 hiring professionals. Taken together, our findings suggest that social media posts about hot-button issues impact hiring managers’ assessments of the hireability of applicants regardless of the platform on which the posts appear.
apa_citation: Nabity-Grover, T., Matthews, K. D., Roth, P. L., Kwon, Y., & Thatcher, J. B. (2025). Social media and political affiliation: How expressing hot-button opinions affects raters’ assessments of job applicants. *Information Systems Research, 36*(4), 2375–2390. https://doi.org/10.1287/isre.2020.0497

title: Ethical Leadership in Climate Action: Navigating National Government Intervention, Financial Constraints, and Corporate Decarbonization
methods: Panel regressions (OLS and Poisson PMLE) of firm-year carbon emissions on financial constraints interacted with national policy and institutional measures, with year, industry, and country fixed effects; two-stage least squares instrumenting policy stringency with staggered carbon-tax adoption; logit models for financing outcomes.
industry: Firms across all sectors except financial services and utilities (Trucost universe; pollution-intensive industries flagged)
units: Firms (firm-year observations)
n_sample: 72,487 firm-year observations (12,749 unique firms)
authors: Ng, Lilian; Wang, Xiaoqiong; Yu, Jing; Zaiats, Nataliya
theories: business ethics; institutional theory (North, 1990; Williamson, 2000; Helmke & Levitsky, 2012; Ioannou & Serafeim, 2012); pollution haven hypothesis; substitution-complementarity perspective
keywords: Climate change; Decarbonization; Environmental regulation; Financial constraints; Government subsidies; MNCs; Outsourcing emissions
research_question: The paper asks two questions: first, do national climate policy instruments (carbon taxes, emissions trading systems, enforcement mechanisms, and subsidies) mitigate the adverse effect of financial constraints on firms' carbon emissions; and second, is the effectiveness of these policy tools shaped by the surrounding formal and informal institutions?
mechanism_summary: The paper adopts a substitution–complementarity perspective from institutional theory: formal or informal institutions may substitute for regulation when existing governance structures or societal norms already induce responsible behavior, or complement regulation by amplifying its legitimacy, monitoring, and enforcement. Regulatory enforcement and carbon pricing relax the constraint–emissions link by raising the expected cost of inaction, whereas subsidies do not. Cross-country heterogeneity in effectiveness is interpreted through whether institutions reinforce or compensate for regulation, and financial constraints are shown to tilt MNCs toward shifting emissions to weaker jurisdictions rather than abating.
theoretical_contribution: The study makes three contributions to the literatures on business ethics, climate governance, and institutional theory. First, it provides the first global firm-level evidence on how financing frictions shape corporate decarbonization under different policy regimes, extending largely U.S.-focused prior work to 51 countries. Second, it extends institutional theory by formalizing and testing a substitution–complementarity framework for how regulation and the institutional environment (formal rules, informal norms, ownership structures) jointly determine whether regulation substitutes for or complements existing governance. Third, it connects firm-level decarbonization to global markets, showing that constrained MNCs shift emissions rather than abate and that constrained high-emitters face tighter access to equity and bond financing unless they decarbonize under stringent regulation.
practical_implication: Ethical leadership in climate action requires more than voluntary pledges; it requires credible regulation that holds firms accountable and aligns financial incentives with environmental responsibility, since firms demonstrating genuine commitment to decarbonization are rewarded with better access to global capital. For policymakers, addressing emissions arbitrage by multinational firms is essential to prevent unfair advantages and safeguard domestic progress; tools such as carbon border adjustments can reduce loopholes, but broader international coordination is necessary to ensure the burdens and benefits of climate action are distributed fairly.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Not reported in paper
abstract: This study investigates how national climate policy instruments interact with financial constraints to shape corporate decarbonization, using data on 12,749 domestic and multinational firms across 51 countries from 2005 to 2022. We show that regulatory enforcement and carbon pricing reduce emissions even when firms face capital scarcity, while targeted subsidies have no measurable effect. The impact of regulation depends on institutional context: ESG disclosure requirements and supportive informal norms such as social trust, individualism, and competitiveness amplify effectiveness, whereas strong shareholder rights or uncertainty-averse cultures substitute for regulation and diminish its marginal role. We also uncover ethical risks in global production. Financially constrained multinationals often reallocate emissions to affiliates in weaker jurisdictions rather than invest in abatement, undermining collective climate goals, while firms subject to stringent regulation gain privileged access to global capital, disadvantaging peers in laxer regimes. By integrating institutional theory with business ethics, our findings show that climate accountability is not only a matter of environmental effectiveness but also of justice and fairness. Well-designed and well-enforced policies, aligned with institutional contexts and coordinated internationally, are essential for ensuring that the burdens and benefits of decarbonization are equitably distributed.
apa_citation: Ng, L., Wang, X., Yu, J., & Zaiats, N. (2025). Ethical leadership in climate action: Navigating national government intervention, financial constraints, and corporate decarbonization. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06195-3
