title: Mapping Heterotopia: Enjoyment as practice in two alternative spaces
methods: Immersive multi-case ethnography of two alternative spaces (a hackerspace and an occupied theatre); over 800 hours of participant/non-participant observation, 29 semi-structured interviews, and organizational documents, analyzed via thematic analysis.
industry: Alternative/activist organizations: a hackerspace (SouthHackSpace) and an occupied former theatre/cinema (OccupyTheatre)
country: France (two spaces in the South of France)
time_period: Three-year ethnographic project begun in 2016 (SouthHackSpace March 2016–June 2018; OccupyTheatre July 2016–May 2017)
units: Two alternative spaces (heterotopias) and their members (hackers and squatters)
n_sample: 29 formal interviews; over 800 hours of observation across two sites
authors: Peiro, Mickael; Aroles, Jeremy; Granter, Edward
theories: Thirding-as-Othering (Soja, 1996); enjoyment as practice (Warner, 1980; Böhm & Batta, 2010); heterotopia (Foucault, 1986)
keywords: alternative space; enjoyment; ethnography; heterotopia
research_question: How do heterotopias give rise to practices of enjoyment through the production of alternative projects? The paper examines the relationship between heterotopia and enjoyment — how heterotopias can be sources of enjoyment while relying on enjoyment as a driving force.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: heterotopia (other spaces that represent, contest and reverse other spaces); enjoyment as a non-calculative practice of othering; the threat of the ephemeral (heterotopias as fleeting, fragile, short-lived).
- Process / phases (three heterotopic processes):
  - Experimenting — enjoyment through illusion: re-appropriating neglected spaces and practices to build an "other than" space within/against the established order.
  - Articulating — enjoyment through otherness: enacting spatial multiplicity so diverse, contrasting identities and practices coexist in one place.
  - Seizing — enjoyment through unsettling/renouncement: framing means as ends; faced with a fragile temporality, actors turn the means they put in place into their own ends.
- Empirical settings: a hackerspace (SouthHackSpace) resisting technological monopolies and an occupied theatre (OccupyTheatre) resisting property speculation and cultural hegemony.

Enjoyment is theorized not as merely symbolic but as a practice that is constitutive of, and fuelled by, heterotopic spaces. Because hackers and squatters organize to occupy and hack spaces and practices, they generate a joyful "illusion" of an alternative order, unify multiplicity without synthesizing it, and incorporate their inherently unsettled nature into how they activate — "seizing fatality as an opportunity." Enjoyment thereby acts as a source of strength and progressive momentum that transcends the pessimism associated with the ephemeral nature of alternative projects.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances understanding of the role of heterotopias in generating alternative projects by examining the coalescence of diverse spaces, practices, temporalities and intentions within one physical space. It foregrounds enjoyment as a driving force that can transcend the pessimism associated with the ephemeral nature of alternative organizations, and consolidates this through a framework of three heterotopic processes (experimenting, articulating, seizing) through which enjoyment, as a practice, is both enacted and generated.
practical_implication: For activists, hackers and organizers of alternative spaces, the study suggests that enjoyment is not a distraction from political goals but a practical resource: positioning enjoyment "as an ethics of life" offers a means of resistance to the politics of despair and sustains involvement even when projects are ephemeral. Such heterotopias carry the potential to instigate change by transforming cultural practices as they permeate public discourse, and their value extends beyond any destabilization of the dominant order their mere existence may cause.
limitations: The authors note that the study of only two alternative spaces does not exhaust the richness and scope of the heterotopology proposed by Foucault (1986), implying limited transferability beyond the two French cases studied.
future_research: The authors encourage further committed, longitudinal and ethnographic study of hackerspaces and occupation movements around the world; research on the relationship between political economy and the formation of heterotopias; investigation of spaces constructed in resistance to established orders (patriarchy, capitalism, imperialism); and deeper examination of the mechanisms of genesis, establishment, decline and dissolution of such spaces ("lifespan" of heterotopias).
abstract: Heterotopias are spaces at the margins that disturb the system from within. They offer a vantage point to explore the affective practices which animate projects existing to challenge the dominant order. In this paper, we are interested in the relationship between heterotopia and enjoyment, specifically the ways in which heterotopias can be sources of enjoyment, while relying on enjoyment as a driving force. Our discussion draws on an immersive ethnography of two alternative projects – the first against urban inequalities and the second against technological dystopia. Data collection involved more than 800 hours of observation, attending dozens of meetings and events, conducting 29 formal interviews and collecting emails and online exchanges. Through our empirical accounts, we identify three types of heterotopic processes that are linked to specific practices of enjoyment: experimenting, articulating and seizing. Placing these in the context of theories of heterotopia, we show how enjoyment acts as a source of strength and potential in the development of alternative imaginaries.
apa_citation: Peiro, M., Aroles, J., & Granter, E. (2025). Mapping heterotopia: Enjoyment as practice in two alternative spaces. *Organization Studies*. https://doi.org/10.1177/01708406251400484

title: Judgmental Bot: Conversational Agents in Online Mental Health Screening
methods: Mixed methods: four randomized online/in-person vignette experiments (Studies 1, 1-robustness, 3a, 3b) testing mediation with Hayes PROCESS / SmartPLS, plus a grounded-theory qualitative interview study (Study 2).
industry: Online mental health screening (healthcare); chatbot vs. human mental health care professional
time_period: Interviews conducted over three days in July 2023; survey experiments contemporaneous
units: Individual users/participants (MTurk, Prolific, Cloud Research panels, and university students)
n_sample: Experiments: 290 (Study 1), 1,105 (robustness), 413 (Study 3a), 293 (Study 3b); interviews: 41 (Study 2)
authors: Raimi, Ryan; Kim, Antino; Ayabakan, Sezgin; Dennis, Alan R.
theories: algorithm aversion; computers are social actors (CASA) paradigm (Nass & Moon, 2000; Nass et al., 1994); judgmentalness / stigma in help-seeking
keywords: Chatbot; construct building; construct validation; conversational agent; experiment; grounded theory; interview; judgmentalness; mental health; mixed methods; stigma; theory development; theory testing
research_question: Why, and with what consequences, do users perceive a text-based chatbot as more or less judgmental than a human mental health care professional during online mental health screening, and what theoretical mechanisms drive that perception?
mechanism_summary: Contrary to the "judgment-free chatbot" assumption, participants consistently perceived the chatbot as more judgmental than an identically behaving human, and this perception reduced use, disclosure, and follow-through (partial-to-full mediation). The qualitative study reframes judgmentalness as being "quick to make misinformed judgments," and the experiments show the gap is driven not by behavior but by users believing the chatbot cannot deeply understand the issue (emotionally and socially) or make them feel heard and validated.
theoretical_contribution: The paper overturns the long-held assumption that chatbots are perceived as nonjudgmental, showing judgmentalness is ascribed to chatbots precisely because they are not human. It reconceptualizes judgmentalness as "quick to form misinformed judgments" rather than moral judgment, and extends the computers-are-social-actors (CASA) paradigm by demonstrating that identical behavior is interpreted differently depending on whether the actor is a human or a chatbot.
practical_implication: Organizations should be cautious about deploying chatbots for mental health screening, favoring contexts that do not depend on deep emotional or social understanding. Designers must address users' underlying beliefs about chatbots — not just behavior — and program agents to display deeper understanding, active listening, and validation, while recognizing emotional support from a machine is still valued though less effective than from a human.
limitations: The experiments relied on artificial vignettes lacking real consequences, while the interview study lacked control and risked researcher bias. Participants were exclusively U.S. adults, limiting generalizability to a single sociopolitical, cultural, and healthcare context, and the work examined only screening (triage), not treatment.
future_research: Future work should test the boundary conditions across contexts, cultures, age groups, and countries, and across settings that differ in their need for emotional and social understanding. Researchers should investigate how trust and privacy concerns shape perceived judgmentalness, how to shift users' beliefs about chatbots, and extend the work to chatbot-assisted treatment.
abstract: Only a fraction of people with mental health issues seek medical care, in part because of fear of judgment, so deploying text-based conversational agents (i.e., chatbots) for mental health screening is often viewed as a way to lower barriers to mental health care. We conducted four experiments and a qualitative study and, contrary to common assumptions, consistently found that participants perceived a text-based chatbot as more judgmental than a human mental health care professional, even though the interactions were identical.
apa_citation: Raimi, R., Kim, A., Ayabakan, S., & Dennis, A. R. (2025). Judgmental bot: Conversational agents in online mental health screening. *MIS Quarterly*, 49(4), 1319-1356. https://doi.org/10.25300/misq/2025/18555

title: Homelessness‐based impact hiring and consumers' contagion concerns
methods: Five between-participants randomized experiments (one in-person behavioral lab choice study; four online), plus pretests, post-tests, and exploratory studies. Analysis via chi-square, binary logistic regression, one-way and 2x2 ANOVAs with planned contrasts, and PROCESS mediation/moderated-mediation models (model 4 and model 8; 5,000 bootstrap samples).
industry: Consumer-behavior experiments using fictitious and actual brands of food and non-food products (e.g., baked goods, ice cream, pizza, massage services, computer monitors)
time_period: Project begun early March 2020 (pilot before pandemic onset); manuscript received 2023, accepted 2025
units: Individual consumers / undergraduate students and online panelists (Prolific, Cloud Research)
n_sample: N = 2,435 across the five main experiments (per the within-paper meta-analysis); broader total including pretests and post-tests
authors: Reich, Brandon J.; Shi, Wanying; Wang, Cindy Xin
theories: Moral Foundations Theory / moral psychology (Graham et al., 2009; Haidt, 2001); corporate social responsibility / benevolent halo (Brown & Dacin, 1997; Chernev & Blair, 2015, 2021); evolutionary psychology of social proof (Sundie et al., 2012; Griskevicius & Kenrick, 2013); psychological contagion and disgust (Rozin et al., 1989; Argo et al., 2006); sociological theories of stigma (Crocker et al., 1998; Phelan et al., 1997)
keywords: contagion; disgust; homelessness; impact hiring; stigma
research_question: Why do consumers respond unfavorably to the products of brands that practice homelessness-based impact hiring (IH) even when they morally support the practice, and can a social proof intervention attenuate that unfavorable response by targeting its underlying contagion mechanism?
mechanism_summary: Although consumers judge homelessness-based IH as at least as morally good as comparison conditions, the unique, pathogen-related stigma of homelessness activates implicit contagion concerns that elicit disgust, which in turn triggers an avoidance response and lowers intentions to buy or consume the brand's products. This disgust pathway can operate independently of moral judgment. The effect is strongest for contagion-relevant products (those involving close, direct, or intimate contact) and attenuated for contagion-irrelevant products. Communicating social proof — signaling that many others safely engage with the product — reassures consumers against contagion risk, reducing disgust and improving intentions, while having little effect in control conditions where contagion is irrelevant.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends the CSR literature by documenting a meaningful exception to the benevolent-halo rule: consumers can respond unfavorably to a brand's products even under otherwise favorable CSR conditions, motivating a more nuanced view that weighs deliberative moral judgment against intrinsic self-protective motives. It advances moral-psychology theory by showing disgust can operate independently of moral reasoning in ethical consumption, departing from social-intuitionist models. It also bridges evolutionary psychology and sociological theories of stigma, demonstrating that a subtle social-proof approach can reduce a contagion-based stigma where overt contact-based approaches may fail.
practical_implication: Practitioners of homelessness-based IH should not take consumer support for granted, since the practice may deter adoption of contagion-relevant products even among morally supportive consumers. By incorporating social proof tactics into marketing communications, brands can communicate the initiative while minimizing its negative impact on consumer response — benefiting society without sacrificing revenue. Social proof may also aid public-policy efforts such as recruiting shelter volunteers or building support for homelessness-friendly policies.
limitations: Experiment 1 observed product choice without examining how consumers physically interact with the product, so disgust was not validated biometrically. The research tested only one intervention (social proof) and does not demonstrate any positive (advantage-conferring) effect of the practice — even contagion-irrelevant products showed no benefit. The authors also acknowledge the COVID-19 pandemic may have heightened disgust sensitivity and thus affected the observed pattern of effects.
future_research: Future work could validate disgust on a biological level through biometric methods (e.g., facial-action coding, salivation) and explore alternative interventions such as opaque versus transparent product or packaging design. Researchers might examine contexts in which employees have no direct contact with the product or consumers, where homelessness-based IH could yield a competitive edge, and further probe potential COVID-related impacts on consumer response.
abstract: Experiments 4 and 5 demonstrate how social proof may enhance the favorability of consumer response by targeting contagion concerns and therefore reducing disgust. Findings suggest that, despite consumer moral support, marketers cannot rely on halo effects from this emerging practice, while also providing theory-driven guidance on overcoming obstacles to its implementation.
apa_citation: Reich, B. J., Shi, W., & Wang, C. X. (2025). Homelessness‐based impact hiring and consumers' contagion concerns. *Journal of Consumer Psychology*. https://doi.org/10.1002/jcpy.70010

title: Earnings Pressure and Firms’ ESG Performance: Do Executive Environmental Experience and MBA Experience Matter?
methods: Firm fixed-effects panel regression on Chinese A-share listed manufacturing firms (2010–2021); robustness via high-dimensional fixed-effects Poisson, instrumental-variable (Lewbel cubic deviation) and Heckman two-stage models; managerial-myopia mediation tested via Baron & Kenny causal-step method.
industry: Manufacturing (29 manufacturing industries)
units: A-share listed manufacturing firms (chairperson/CEO experience coded)
n_sample: 13,046 firm-year observations across 2,122 firms
authors: Ren, Ge; Zeng, Ping; Zhong, Xi
theories: agency theory (Jensen & Meckling, 1976); upper echelons theory (Hambrick & Mason, 1984)
keywords: ESG performance; MBA experience; earnings pressure; environmental experience; sustainability
research_question: How does earnings pressure (the gap between analysts' forecast EPS and a firm's potential EPS) affect firms' ESG performance, and how do executives' environmental experience and MBA experience moderate that relationship?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on agency theory, the authors argue that earnings pressure threatens executives' compensation and job security, leading these risk-averse, self-interested agents to cut ESG activities whose returns are uncertain or long-delayed in order to protect short-term financial performance (H1). Integrating upper echelons theory, they posit that executives' prior experiences shape how they appraise ESG risk–reward: environmentally experienced executives, attuned to sustainability and identifying with nature, perceive ESG as less risky and resist cutting it under pressure (H2a), whereas MBA-trained executives—socialized into pessimistic, self-interested economic assumptions—perceive greater ESG return uncertainty and cut it more aggressively (H2b). Managerial myopia is shown to be the channel through which earnings pressure depresses ESG.
theoretical_contribution: The study identifies earnings pressure (an analyst-driven external force) as a novel antecedent of firms' overall ESG performance, extending a literature that had focused on internal stakeholders (executives, boards, shareholders) and on isolated ESG dimensions rather than its totality. It enriches both agency theory and upper echelons theory by offering a contingent account of how executive experience (environmental vs. MBA) alters the agency problems and opportunistic behavior that earnings pressure provokes.
practical_implication: Boards should reduce or eliminate penalties on executives who miss analysts' short-term forecasts and tie executive compensation to sustainability KPIs to counteract myopia. Firms can improve ESG by selecting executives with environmental backgrounds (and sending managers to Sustainability/Green MBA programs) rather than relying on conventional MBA-trained, finance-focused leaders. Regulators should promote longer-horizon forecasts and stronger ESG disclosure standards.
limitations: The sample is restricted to Chinese listed manufacturing firms, limiting generalizability to services, other emerging economies, or developed-economy governance contexts. Executive experience—especially environmental experience—is measured from publicly available resumes, which may oversimplify the construct, and data limits prevented distinguishing among MBA program types (e.g., Green/Sustainability MBAs).
future_research: Test the hypothesized relationships across other industries (e.g., services) and in developed-economy or multi-country datasets; triangulate resume-based experience measures with qualitative methods such as in-depth interviews; differentiate specialized (Green/Sustainability) MBA programs from conventional ones; and examine whether star versus non-star analysts generate differential earnings-pressure effects on ESG.
abstract: On the basis of agency theory and upper echelons theory, this study examines the moderating role of executive experience in the relationship between earnings pressure and firms’ environmental, social, and governance (ESG) performance. We argue that earnings pressure negatively impacts ESG performance, as it leads executives to prioritize short-term financial gains over ESG activities with uncertain or delayed financial returns. Moreover, we posit that executives with environmental experience are less likely to reduce ESG efforts under earnings pressure, whereas those with MBA backgrounds are more inclined to do so. Using a fixed effects model, we empirically analyze data from Chinese listed manufacturing firms (2010–2021), finding strong support for these hypotheses. This research enhances our understanding of ESG performance under earnings pressure and offers valuable insights for promoting sustainable corporate development.
apa_citation: Ren, G., Zeng, P., & Zhong, X. (2025). Earnings pressure and firms’ ESG performance: Do executive environmental experience and MBA experience matter? *Organization & Environment*, 38(4), 572–598. https://doi.org/10.1177/10860266251353266

title: My Oppressive Past- and Present-Day Vulnerability: Understanding How and When Authoritarian Leadership Adversely Impacts Black Employees at Work
methods: Multi-study design combining a cross-sectional field survey (Study 1, linear regression and PROCESS macro) with an experimental-causal-chain across two vignette experiments (Studies 2 and 3, ANOVA), plus a post hoc single-item validation study.
industry: Various industries (Black working professionals across diverse sectors)
units: Individual Black working adults (employed at least 20 hours per week)
n_sample: Study 1: 328 completed surveys; Studies 2-3: 87 participants (33 in Study 2, 54 in Study 3); post hoc validation: 92
authors: Rice, Darryl B.; Smith, Shona G.; Maxie, Jamila S.; Johnson, Erica
theories: social information processing theory (Salancik & Pfeffer, 1978; Crick & Dodge, 1994)
keywords: Authoritarian leadership; Psychological diversity climate; Thriving at work; Vulnerable employees
research_question: How and when does authoritarian leadership adversely impact Black employees' ability to thrive at work? The paper investigates which dark side of leadership is harmful and socio-culturally relevant to vulnerable employees, focusing on psychological diversity climate as the linking mechanism and psychological attachment as the boundary condition.
mechanism_summary: Drawing on social information processing theory, the authors argue that leadership style is a salient social cue that shapes how employees interpret the work environment. Authoritarian leadership signals to Black employees that there is opposition to their fair and inclusive treatment, a cue made culturally salient by Black employees' history of slavery and systemic oppression in the United States. This cue lowers their evaluations of psychological diversity climate, which in turn hinders their ability to thrive at work. The negative authoritarian-leadership-to-climate relationship (and the downstream indirect effect on thriving) is stronger when Black employees' psychological attachment to the organization is relatively low, yielding a first-stage moderated-mediation model.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes three contributions: it extends the supervisor-leadership-and-vulnerable-employees literature by adding authoritarian leadership as a harmful, socio-culturally relevant style (complementing prior work on the "bright side"); it extends research on factors shaping vulnerable employees' thriving by foregrounding psychological diversity climate; and it shows when psychological attachment moderates a leadership style's effect on employee attitudes. It also affirms social information processing theory as a useful lens for explaining how authoritarian leadership operates and contributes to Black scholarship and business-ethics research on oppressive leadership.
practical_implication: Organizations risk superficial allyship if they issue diversity statements while tolerating authoritarian supervisors without oversight. Because Black employees' thriving is directly linked to psychological diversity climate, organizations should attend to factors that raise or lower that climate and build training and selection/promotion processes that screen out dark-triad-linked authoritarian tendencies while cultivating an ethical-leadership norm celebrated through reward and recognition.
limitations: Study 1 is single-source and cross-sectional, raising common-method-variance and causality concerns, and controls only for organizational tenure, supervisor tenure, sex, and industry. Studies 2 and 3 have small samples (N = 33 and N = 54), rely on experimental vignettes (external-validity concern), and use single-item dependent-variable measures. Convenience sampling of Black working adults may limit generalizability to broader populations.
future_research: Future work could examine how cultural variations within Black communities (region, social class, ethnicity) shape responses to authoritarian leadership, and whether authoritarian leadership similarly undermines other vulnerable groups such as women or refugee employees. Researchers could study additional harmful outcomes (mental health, withdrawal, counterproductive behaviors), test other moderators (e.g., Black employee resilience) and intersectional perspectives, and investigate how colonial legacies and intergenerational transmission of systemic racism operate in the workplace.
abstract: Our research contributes to the literature centered on increasing awareness, and understanding the impact, of supervisory leadership on vulnerable employees. Whereas recent scholars have explained the bright side of leadership that is helpful and socio-culturally relevant to vulnerable employees, we complement these prior studies by explaining why a dark side of leadership is harmful and socio-culturally relevant to vulnerable employees. We draw on insights from Black employees' oppressive past to shed light on their present-day vulnerability in the workplace. Subsequently, we rely on social information processing theory to explain how and when authoritarian leadership impedes Black employees' ability to thrive at work. We argue that authoritarian leadership is a salient workplace cue that conveys to Black employees that there is significant opposition to them being treated inclusively and fairly. Accordingly, we propose that authoritarian leadership should adversely impact Black employees' evaluations of psychological diversity climate. In turn, as Black employees believe that they are not welcomed (i.e., evidenced by their evaluations of psychological diversity climate), their ability to thrive at work becomes hindered. We also explain why this is particularly the case for Black employees with relatively low levels of psychological attachment to the organization. We find support for our model across a combination of field and experimental vignette studies. Theoretical and practical implications are discussed.
apa_citation: Rice, D. B., Smith, S. G., Maxie, J. S., & Johnson, E. (2025). My oppressive past- and present-day vulnerability: Understanding how and when authoritarian leadership adversely impacts Black employees at work. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06202-7

title: Degrowth and Organization Studies
methods: Conceptual essay; inductive theorizing from illustrative past and present micro-instantiations of degrowth (eco-communities, commons, and firms such as Keiunkan, Fairphone, and Radically Open Security) to derive four organizing principles.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Slade Shantz, Angelique; Khorasani, Niki; Toubiana, Madeline
theories: degrowth; doughnut economics (Raworth, 2017); institutional theory (DiMaggio & Powell, 1983)
keywords: degrowth; degrowth organizing; institutional change; post-growth; post-growth organizing; sustainability
research_question: How can organization studies theorize and imagine degrowth—an intentional reduction in material and energy throughput—when growth is so deeply institutionalized, and what organizing principles can be induced from small-scale instantiations to envision what degrowth organizing might look like?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Growth is an institutionalized, taken-for-granted norm (measured via GDP and shareholder value) rather than an inherent or inevitable feature of organizing; degrowth scrutinizes this status rather than rejecting growth outright.
- Key constructs: degrowth micro-instantiations ("small wins"); the space between a social foundation and an ecological ceiling (doughnut economics); growth as a strategic decision rather than a given.
- Organizing principles (induced from past/present examples): resizing organizations and organizing; decelerating tempo; regenerating sufficiency; governing trade-offs.
- Boundary conditions: ventures often remain dependent on growth-rooted systems (funding models, supply chains, legal structures), raising the question of how degrowth organizing sustains itself within, alongside, or against dominant institutions.

The authors theorize inductively: rather than testing variables, they assemble historical and contemporary micro-instantiations of constrained-growth organizing (agrarian commons, Ostrom's managed commons, eco-communities such as Kan Pasqual and Can Masdeu, and firms such as the Keiunkan hotel, Fairphone, and Radically Open Security) and derive four organizing principles from their shared features. The central claim is that organizational designs enabling resizing, decelerating, regenerating sufficiency, and governing trade-offs are more likely to de-emphasize "growthism" and center well-being, sufficiency, and ecological fit, with isomorphism (not only disruptive institutional work) potentially rendering such experiments visible and legitimate over time.
theoretical_contribution: The essay shifts the degrowth conversation from the "why" to the "how" of degrowth organizing, offering four inductively derived organizing principles as a research agenda for organization studies. It reframes growth as a contingent, context-sensitive outcome rather than a normative imperative, and provokes reconsideration of institutional theory (e.g., whether isomorphism developed for growth-driven organizations applies under degrowth), practice theory and measurement, and resource dependence as a theory of power—proposing "a-growth" as an absence of belief that growth is necessarily good.
practical_implication: Managers and organizations can treat growth as a strategic decision (how big should we grow) rather than a default imperative, designing governance "guardrails" that protect both an ecological ceiling and a social foundation. Practices include scaling deep rather than up, slowing product turnover (e.g., durable, repairable design), reclaiming and sharing commons to create sufficiency, and substituting judgment for metric-driven measures of success.
limitations: The paper is a conceptual essay drawing on a non-comprehensive set of illustrative, small-scale micro-instantiations, which the authors acknowledge may "independently seem unrealistic, unscalable or even undesirable" and are offered as conceptual fodder rather than templates to be followed precisely. It does not empirically test the proposed principles or their scalability within growth-oriented institutional environments.
future_research: The authors call for a broad program studying the antecedents, mechanisms, and outcomes of "small wins" rather than grand challenges; how local experiments coalesce into institutional change via isomorphism; how organizations might measure success beyond growth (or whether to measure at all); resource-dependence questions of when sharing reduces individual power but increases collective power; and what governance "guardrails," guardians, and means degrowth organizing requires.
abstract: This essay explores the relevance of degrowth for organization studies, questioning the institutionalization of growth as a taken-for-granted organizational and societal goal. Drawing on historical and contemporary examples, we induce four organizing principles—resizing, decelerating tempo, regenerating sufficiency, and governing trade-offs. These principles reveal how organizations can prioritize ecological sustainability and social well-being over perpetual expansion. We emphasize that degrowth is not about shrinking for its own sake, but about rethinking growth as a contingent, context-sensitive outcome rather than a normative imperative. By highlighting practices that operate within, alongside, or against dominant institutions, we show how degrowth organizing provides empirical grounding for post-growth imaginaries. Our aim is to catalyze a broader research program that considers how organizing can unfold differently within the space between a social foundation and an ecological ceiling.
apa_citation: Slade Shantz, A., Khorasani, N., & Toubiana, M. (2025). Degrowth and organization studies. *Organization Studies*, 46(12), 1759–1778. https://doi.org/10.1177/01708406251362935

title: Climate-Triggered Institutional Price Pressure: Does It Affect Firms’ Cost of Equity?
journal: Journal of Financial and Quantitative Analysis
methods: Empirical asset pricing. Derives a closed-form stock-level climate change price pressure (CCPP) measure within the Koijen & Yogo (2019) demand-based framework; estimates investor demand coefficients by GMM on 13F holdings quarter-by-quarter; runs panel regressions of option-implied cost of equity (Martin-Wagner; Chabi-Yo et al.) on CCPP with year-quarter and SIC2 industry fixed effects and firm-clustered standard errors, plus predictive regressions of future green activities on CCPP.
industry: S&P 500 constituent firms (cross-section of individual U.S. stocks); 13F institutional investors
units: Firm-quarter (S&P 500 stocks); investor-quarter for demand estimation
n_sample: Stock-level CCPP estimated quarterly for each S&P 500 constituent stock; up to ~43,707 firm-quarter exposure observations and ~10,420 distinct 13F investors
authors: Skiadopoulos, George; Xue, Cheng
theories: demand-based asset pricing / demand system approach (Koijen & Yogo, 2019)
keywords: climate change exposures; climate change price pressure; cost of equity; demand-based asset pricing; divestment; environmental profiles; institutional portfolio rebalancing
research_question: Does institutional portfolio rebalancing triggered by firm-level climate change exposures affect firms’ cost of equity; if so, does it affect their future environmental profiles; are physical or transition exposures more important; and which types of institutional investors drive these effects?
mechanism_summary: Within the Koijen-Yogo (2019) demand system, each investor's optimal portfolio weight depends on stock characteristics; adding firm-level climate exposures as a characteristic yields a closed-form CCPP equal to the ownership-weighted sum of demand responses divided by aggregate demand elasticity. Because the market is inelastic, climate-triggered underweighting produces negative CCPP, making such stocks riskier and raising their required returns (cost of equity). Yet because environmental reforms carry large tangible and intangible costs, the roughly 6% cost-of-equity effect is too small to change firms' cost-benefit calculus, so environmental behavior is unaffected.
theoretical_contribution: The paper provides a microfounded, closed-form CCPP channel that links heterogeneous institutional portfolio rebalancing in response to climate exposures to firms' cost of equity, departing from CAPM/homogeneous-investor settings (e.g., Berk & van Binsbergen). By analyzing cost-of-equity and environmental-profile effects within a single unified, demand-based framework using forward-looking option-implied returns and "soft" textual exposure measures, it supplies an economic metric for assessing whether divestment-driven price pressure can promote climate adaptation.
practical_implication: At current divestment levels, climate-triggered price pressure raises firms' equity-financing costs without altering their climate behavior, suggesting investor engagement (voice) may be more effective than divestment for driving corporate reform. Policymakers and investors can use the CCPP metrics and portfolio-sensitivity estimates to target sectors facing more negative CCPP and to check whether investors rebalance in line with their sustainability commitments.
limitations: Identifying how large CCPP must become to influence environmental outcomes is nontrivial because it requires weighing mitigation benefits against hard-to-quantify intangible reform costs (corporate culture, organizational structure, labor relations). The analysis is confined to large, optionable S&P 500 U.S. firms over 2005–2021, and exposure measures derive from earnings-call text, which may not capture all dimensions of climate risk.
future_research: The authors suggest that results could differ if more investors were to divest, implying value in studying counterfactual higher-divestment regimes and the relative efficacy of engagement versus price pressure. Further work could assess how large CCPP must be to shift environmental outcomes and extend the framework beyond large-cap U.S. equities.
abstract: We document that climate-triggered institutional portfolio rebalancing affects S&P 500 firms’ cost of equity through climate change price pressure (CCPP). Using a demand-based asset pricing framework, we estimate firm-level CCPP from physical and transition exposures over 2005–2021. A one-standard-deviation intensification of CCPP raises the cost of equity by up to 6% of its average, with banks and insurers as the main drivers. Yet firms do not subsequently improve environmental performance, indicating that the statistically significant effect of CCPP on cost of equity is ineffective to alter corporate behavior. Our CCPP metrics can help policymakers and investors design targeted environmental strategies.
apa_citation: Skiadopoulos, G., & Xue, C. (2025). Climate-triggered institutional price pressure: Does it affect firms’ cost of equity? *Journal of Financial and Quantitative Analysis*, 1–28. https://doi.org/10.1017/s0022109025102226

title: Can Compassion Appeals Change the World? A Critical Review and Reconceptualization
methods: Critical (integrative) literature review following SPAR-4-SLR; systematic Web of Science keyword search yielding 1,061 results, screened to a final sample of 77 articles (116 empirical studies); abductive coding by three independent coders, theory building toward a conceptual model of compassion blends.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Peer-reviewed empirical journal articles (and the studies they report) on compassion appeals and prosocial behavior
n_sample: 77 articles reporting 116 empirical studies (final review sample)
authors: Valor, Carmen; Antonetti, Paolo; Crisafulli, Benedetta
theories: ethic of care / ethic of responsibility (Gilligan, 1982; Schlaile et al., 2018); mixed emotion specificity (Oh & Tong, 2022); social identity model of collective action (Van Zomeren et al., 2008, 2018)
keywords: Appeals; Global problems; Literature review; Moral foundations; Prosocial behavior
research_question: The review poses three questions about appeal-induced compassion and prosocial behavior: RQ1 — how and under what conditions is compassion elicited by persuasive appeals; RQ2 — why and when are appeals to compassion resisted; and RQ3 — how and why does appeal-induced compassion motivate prosocial helping or transformative behavior.
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Compassion appeals "moralize suffering" via an ethic of care, but their effectiveness depends on three conditions — successful elicitation, the regulation of co-activated distress, and a match between the emotion's action tendency and the prosocial action sought.
- Key constructs: severity and legitimacy of the plight (primary elicitors); identification, affective traits, moral-political beliefs, and contextual cues (four groups of boundary conditions on elicitation); distress as a co-activated, actively regulated emotion.
- Boundary conditions / thresholds: an "area of acceptability" bounded by an elicitation threshold (appeal moving enough to trigger compassion) and a backfiring threshold (distress overcomes compassion), theorized as a nonlinear relationship.
- Prosocial-behavior distinction: prosocial helping (occasional relief, grounded in an ethic of care) versus prosocial transformative behavior (sustained, structural change, grounded in an ethic of responsibility); compassion alone drives helping but is psychologically ill-suited to transformative action.
- Reconceptualization — compassion blends: compassion combined with moral anger ("commitment," desire to restore justice through other-directed redress/punishment) or with guilt ("atonement," desire to restore justice through self-directed self-improvement), drawing on mixed-emotion specificity (Oh & Tong, 2022) and additive appraisal effects.

Synthesizing 116 studies, the authors argue that compassion is an "erratic force" stabilized or undermined by identifiable situational factors, and that its limited power over transformative action arises because the care-based motivation it produces does not supply the appraisals of responsibility, accountability, and moral agency that structural change requires. They theorize that pairing compassion with moral anger or guilt produces complementary appraisals and additive effects (mechanisms M1–M3 in Fig. 3) that can channel motivation from immediate helping toward transformative prosocial behavior.
theoretical_contribution: The review reconciles mixed evidence by (a) mapping the antecedents and moderators of compassion elicitation, (b) introducing the threshold-bounded "area of acceptability" governing when appeals persuade versus backfire, and (c) reconceptualizing compassion appeals around "compassion blends" (atonement and commitment), revealing for the first time the distinct psychological mechanisms linking compassion to helping versus transformative prosocial behavior and bridging an ethic of care with an ethic of responsibility.
practical_implication: Severity and legitimacy of the plight, and receiver characteristics conducive to compassion, guide segmentation, targeting, and campaign design (Table 1). Practitioners should pretest campaigns with discrete-emotion scales to minimize distress and backfiring risk (Fig. 2), and deliberately design appeals that blend compassion with moral anger or guilt when transformative prosocial action is the goal (Figs. 3 and 5 provide a hands-on tool).
limitations: As a critical (rather than systematic-descriptive) review, the goal is theoretical integration and reinterpretation rather than detailed description of past studies, so the proposed model of compassion blends is conceptual and not yet empirically tested. The synthesis is bounded by the heterogeneity and gaps of the reviewed evidence (e.g., few studies measure emotions beyond compassion or distinguish helping from transformative outcomes).
future_research: The authors propose three directions (Table 3): empirically test the conceptual model of how appeal components activate each compassion blend and the conditions moderating helping versus transformative outcomes; extend the model to other compassion blends (e.g., disgust, hope) and to mechanisms beyond additive integration (ambivalence, coexistence); and examine the role of emotion regulation and blend composition on elicitation and backfiring thresholds, drawing on mixed-emotions, emotion-regulation, and coping scholarship.
abstract: Compassion appeals are employed to mobilize prosocial actions that address global grand challenges. However, the evidence of the effectiveness of such appeals is still mixed, with studies using a multitude of persuasion appeals and examining diverse outcomes. To explain the mixed findings, we conduct an integrative, critical review of 77 articles (altogether reporting 116 empirical studies) on the role of compassion appeals in motivating prosocial behavior. This review explains the conditions that determine the relative effectiveness of compassion appeals. First, we identify the appeal components and contextual factors that elicit compassion. Successful elicitation is a prerequisite for the effectiveness of compassion appeals. Second, we reveal that compassion often is elicited with distress, an emotion actively regulated by audiences. Consequently, persuasive compassion appeals are most effective when elicited compassion is maintained within an area of acceptability, demarcated by elicitation and backfiring thresholds. Third, we show that mixed findings are often due to a mismatch between the motivation elicited by compassion and the prosocial action encouraged by the appeal. Compassion motivates the relief of immediate suffering but is not suited to drive sustained commitment toward promoting structural social change. To address this limitation of compassion, we conceptualize the need for compassion blends, mixed emotions where compassion is elicited along with guilt and/or moral anger. We explain why and how compassion blends can motivate transformative prosocial behavior. The results from our critical review inform an agenda for future research and offer actionable insights for the design of effective compassion appeals.
apa_citation: Valor, C., Antonetti, P., & Crisafulli, B. (2025). Can compassion appeals change the world? A critical review and reconceptualization. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06220-5

title: Rethinking Responsibility in the Digital Age: A Narrative Approach
methods: Conceptual research commentary; develops a Ricoeur-inspired narrative theory of responsibility, illustrated through three interpretive case vignettes (plagiarism detection, social media, AI).
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: de Vaujany, François-Xavier; Leclercq-Vandelannoitte, Aurélie; Aroles, Jeremy; Introna, Lucas; Davidson, Scott
theories: Paul Ricoeur's hermeneutic phenomenology and narrative theory (imputation, narrative emplotment, mimesis); sociomateriality / performativity (Orlikowski & Scott, 2008; Introna, 2013)
keywords: Responsibility; Ricoeur; emplotment; imputation; information technologies; narrative events; temporality
research_question: How can responsibility be reconceptualized so that it makes sense of the extended, connective, and posthuman conditions of the digital age—specifically, how do sociomaterial assemblages narratively impute actors as already responsible, and how can actors reflexively take responsibility through narrative emplotment? The traditional, individualistic, human-centered conception "is unable to make sense of the increasingly extended scope of responsibility in the digital age."
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: responsibility is not a fixed attribution but an ongoing "becoming responsible" through imputation enacted in the narrative emplotment of extended sociomaterial events (Ricoeur).
- Key constructs: imputation ("attributing an action to its true author"); sociomateriality / performativity; narrative emplotment via the threefold mimesis of prefiguration, configuration, and refiguration.
- Three narrative emplotments of responsibility: (1) causal responsibility as responsibility *attribution* (past actions, third-party judgment); (2) relational responsibility as responsibility *making* (sociomaterial performativity enacts actors as already responsible); (3) narrative responsibility as responsibility *taking* (individual/communal self-imputation, future-oriented).
- Boundary conditions / target domain: digital technologies characterized by radical connectivity, nonlinear consequences, extended/emergent actions, and posthuman performative agencies.

The paper argues that the traditional model rests on three untenable assumptions—linearity of the world, a single space-time for agency, and the centrality of intentional human action—that digital connectivity subverts. Drawing on Ricoeur's hermeneutic phenomenology, it reframes responsibility as a temporal process in which sociomaterial assemblages "in their performativity... not only actively produce the world... but also actively impute actors as being already responsible." Through prefiguration, configuration, and refiguration, narratives both relationally assign responsibilities to actors/characters and allow actors to reflexively re-narrate their emplotments to "take responsibility, differently," shifting the emphasis from backward-looking attribution toward forward-looking, future-oriented responsibility-taking.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends responsibility theory in information systems by introducing the *imputative* (not merely performative) function of digital sociomaterial assemblages and a Ricoeur-inspired narrative theory of the "becoming" of responsibility. It distinguishes three narrative emplotments—attribution, making, and taking—and proposes a narrative topology of the multidimensional time-space of responsibility, departing from Coeckelbergh's (2023) hermeneutic-responsibility terminology to foreground temporality and sociomaterial performativity.
practical_implication: IS professionals, designers, managers, and policymakers should attend not only to what their systems do but to "what sort of responsibilities, or responsible actors, they are continuously producing." The paper urges constructing counternarratives, value-sensitive and transparent design, and collective, forward-looking responsibility-taking that "speculatively engages with the future on its own terms" rather than treating technological trajectories as inevitable.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors invite the MIS community and society to draw on the proposed narrative topology to reflect on their imputations and to develop "refiguring alternative narratives" of technological futures—elaborating alternative narrative trajectories from past to future to keep open new spaces for responsibility-taking, individually and collectively.
abstract: Researchers, policymakers, and industry are increasingly aware of the urgent risks and threats arising in the digital age. Their awareness of this urgency has led to a rise of interest in responsibility. While this “turn to responsibility” is well-intentioned, an underappreciated problem is that the dominant, centuries-old view of responsibility is not up to this task—it is unable to make sense of the increasingly extended scope of responsibility in the digital age because it is mired in outdated assumptions about causality, agency, and human action. Inspired by Paul Ricoeur’s philosophy, we show the benefits of rethinking responsibility as an ongoing process of becoming responsible—that is, becoming responsible by being imputed through the narrative emplotment of extended sociomaterial events. We illustrate the benefits of this conception for the digital age using vignettes from plagiarism detection, social media, and AI. The paper concludes by proposing a Ricoeur-inspired narrative topology of the multidimensional time-space of responsibility emplotment. This paper calls on the MIS community, and society more broadly, to draw on this topology to reflect on their imputations and take up responsibility, individually and collectively.
apa_citation: de Vaujany, F.-X., Leclercq-Vandelannoitte, A., Aroles, J., Introna, L., & Davidson, S. (2025). Rethinking responsibility in the digital age: A narrative approach. *MIS Quarterly*. https://doi.org/10.25300/misq/2025/17970

title: Girls Help Girls: The Gender Preference of FOF Managers
methods: Panel regressions (logit, probit, LPM) of fund-pair gender matching on FOF manager gender, with year-quarter, fund-family, and fund-category fixed effects and FOF-clustered standard errors; generalized difference-in-differences (DID) exploiting FOF management-team turnover events as quasi-exogenous shocks to gender composition; mechanism tests via subsample exclusions, risk-measure regressions, and performance (benchmark-adjusted return, Sharpe ratio, multi-factor alpha) comparisons of same- vs. opposite-gender pairs.
industry: Asset management / mutual funds (Fund of Funds and their underlying funds)
units: FOF-fund-quarter pairs (511 unique FOFs; 3,480 underlying funds)
n_sample: 48,466 FOF-fund-quarter observations
authors: Wu, Mianzhi; Zhang, Yongjie; Ding, Jing; Cao, Xing; Meng, Xiangtong
theories: homophily / in-group favoritism; statistical discrimination (Arrow, 1971; Phelps, 1972); taste-based discrimination (Becker, 1957)
keywords: Fund of Funds (FOF); Gender preference; Investment decisions
research_question: Do male and female Fund of Funds (FOF) managers exhibit systematic same-gender preferences in their allocation decisions, and—critically, within the context of gender inequality—do women receive support from other women? What mechanisms (statistical vs. taste-based discrimination) underlie any observed gender-based preferences, and do men and women differ in their role in perpetuating gender inequality in asset management?
mechanism_summary: FOF managers display homophily—an in-group preference for funds run by managers of their own gender—that survives extensive controls and a DID identification leveraging quasi-exogenous turnover. The authors interpret this through two discrimination logics: statistical discrimination (gender as a rational proxy for unobserved skill) versus taste-based discrimination (prejudice incurring a financial cost). A performance test discriminates between them: female FOF managers' female-managed picks outperform their male-managed picks (consistent with rational, statistical discrimination), whereas male FOF managers' male-managed picks underperform the female-managed alternatives they hold (consistent with costly taste-based discrimination), revealing similar surface behavior driven by fundamentally different motives.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances research on gender inequality in financial markets by conceptualizing the FOF-underlying-fund relationship as an investor-fundraiser dynamic and showing that men and women play asymmetric roles in perpetuating gender disparities—same-gender preference is taste-based bias for male managers but performance-driven (statistical) for female managers. It contributes to the emerging literature on behavioral biases among FOF managers by identifying gender bias as a new form of bias, and it addresses pervasive endogeneity concerns by exploiting FOF manager-turnover events within a generalized DID design rather than relying on OLS cross-sectional comparisons.
practical_implication: The finding that male FOF managers sacrifice returns to favor male-managed funds shows that taste-based discrimination systematically constrains qualified female fund managers' access to capital and career advancement, raising equity concerns in asset management. By documenting that women's support for women is financially justified rather than blind solidarity, the authors aim to raise awareness of an often-implicit same-gender preference and encourage more conscious efforts toward a more gender-equal financial market.
limitations: Most notably, the analysis excludes FOFs and funds managed by mixed-gender teams because investment decisions cannot be cleanly attributed to a specific gender, leaving the behavior of mixed-gender teams unexamined. The setting is confined to the Chinese FOF market over 2018-2024, and the baseline sample relies substantially on quarterly top-10 holdings disclosures (validated against semiannual/annual complete holdings).
future_research: Future work could explore behavioral differences between mixed-gender and single-gender teams—for example, whether male-female collaboration mitigates in-group favoritism and yields more gender-neutral, returns-driven portfolio choices. Subsequent studies could also use manager characteristics such as age or work experience to infer dominance within mixed-gender teams, enabling comparisons between male- or female-dominated mixed-gender teams and single-gender teams to illuminate internal gender-interaction dynamics.
abstract: The Fund of Funds (FOF) is a specialized form of fund that invests in other mutual funds rather than directly in stocks or bonds. Using disclosures of FOF holdings, we find significant gender-based preferences: female FOF managers prefer to invest in funds managed by females, whereas their male counterparts prefer those managed by males. To mitigate potential endogeneity concerns, we take advantage of management team turnover events in FOFs that induce shifts in gender composition, and apply the generalized difference-in-differences (DID) approach. Results indicate that transitions from all-male to all-female management teams result in an increase in the proportion of investments in female-managed funds, and vice versa. Our mechanism tests reveal that neither social networks overlap between FOF and fund managers nor similarity in risk preferences within the same gender can fully explain the observed effects. Crucially, we find that male FOF managers' gender preferences lack financial justification, raising concerns about potential gender bias (taste-based discrimination), whereas their female counterparts' behavior is consistent with rationally using gender as a proxy for unobserved fund manager characteristics (statistical discrimination).
apa_citation: Wu, M., Zhang, Y., Ding, J., Cao, X., & Meng, X. (2025). Girls help girls: The gender preference of FOF managers. *Journal of Business Ethics*. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10551-025-06225-0

title: Social Media Is a Threat for Democracy! A Political Perspective for Analysing and Diminishing Harm
methods: Conceptual essay developing a political perspective on social media platforms. The authors build the argument in three moves: (1) a critical review of two dominant management lenses on platform governance (market and technology perspectives) and their democratic-theoretical foundations; (2) theoretical synthesis drawing on Habermasian deliberative democracy and the political view of the firm to reconceptualize social media companies as political actors responsible for the public sphere; (3) derivation of four deliberative capacities (transparency and accountability, openness and inclusiveness, conduciveness to argumentation, consequentiality) with illustrative examples and multi-level (platform, regulatory, individual) reform proposals. No empirical data collection or analysis.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Castelló, I.; Colleoni, E.; Scherer, A. G.; Trittin-Ulbrich, H.
theories: deliberative democracy (Habermas); participatory democratic theory; political CSR / political view of the firm (Scherer and Palazzo); public sphere theory; representative democratic theory; surveillance capitalism (Zuboff)
keywords: artificial intelligence; deliberation; democracy; digital platforms; governance; organized immaturity; political view of the firm; public sphere; social media; surveillance capitalism
research_question: Is social media threatening the democratic capacity of our societies, and if so, what should we do about it? The authors ask how management scholarship can re-conceptualize social media platforms — beyond market and technological lenses — as political actors whose governance shapes the deliberative quality of the public sphere.
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Social media platforms are political actors (not merely market intermediaries or technical infrastructures) that produce and regulate a public sphere, and therefore bear responsibility for its deliberative quality.
- Key constructs: Deliberative capacity of the public sphere; four democratic capacities — (1) transparency and accountability, (2) openness and inclusiveness, (3) conduciveness to argumentation, (4) consequentiality.
- Boundary conditions / existing lenses: Market perspective (representative democracy; transparency + accountability) and technology perspective (participatory democracy; openness + inclusion) — each necessary but individually insufficient for a well-functioning public sphere.
- Proposed mechanisms for reform: Platform-level redesign of algorithmic architectures (e.g., diversity-preserving recommendation, sustained engagement with critical issues); regulatory-level frameworks for platform accountability and deliberative governance; individual-level digital literacy and citizenship education.

The political perspective argues that platforms' business models — surveillance-based attention maximization, algorithmic amplification of emotional engagement, and concentrated ownership — systematically erode the conditions for Habermasian rational-critical discourse, producing polarization, hate speech, misinformation, and democratic fragmentation. Market fixes (fact-checking, antitrust, transparency about algorithms) address information quality but not deliberative quality; technological fixes (decentralization, blockchain verification, open-source protocols) address openness and inclusion but not argumentation or democratic will formation. Enhancing deliberative capacity therefore requires adding conduciveness to argumentation (platform affordances that foster reasoned exchange rather than reactive outrage) and consequentiality (mechanisms that translate deliberative outcomes into binding public decisions), jointly enabling social media to support rather than undermine collective will formation.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes a political perspective of social media platforms grounded in deliberative democracy and the political view of the firm (Scherer and Palazzo), complementing and integrating the dominant market and technology perspectives in management scholarship on platform governance. It introduces two novel capacities of the public sphere — conduciveness to argumentation and consequentiality — and theorizes how platform design, ownership, and governance jointly shape these capacities. It also connects debates on platform governance with social evaluations, stakeholder engagement, and the "political role of business" literature, advocating a deeper engagement with democratic theory in management research.
practical_implication: The essay proposes concrete reforms at three levels. At the platform level, companies should redesign recommendation and engagement systems to foster diverse, sustained, and reasoned discourse (e.g., connecting neighbouring discourses instead of reinforcing echo chambers; rewarding extended engagement with critical issues rather than quick emotional reactions). At the regulatory level, governments should develop frameworks (e.g., in the spirit of the EU Digital Services Act) that hold platforms accountable as political actors with public-sphere responsibilities, including deliberative capacity obligations. At the individual level, digital literacy and civic education — supported by governments, schools, libraries, and non-profits — should equip citizens to participate competently in algorithmically mediated public debate.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors call for further theoretical and empirical investigation of the tensions underlying conducive argumentation (polarization, fragmentation, hate speech, misinformation, short-termism) across different platform governance models, including varying degrees of centralization and accountability, and the internal power dynamics of platform organizations. They also urge research on consequentiality — how deliberative outcomes on social media translate into legitimacy changes, governance practices, stakeholder engagement, and institutional change — and on the cognitive and emotional barriers that hinder this translation. Finally, they call for interdisciplinary collaboration among technologists, philosophers, political theorists, and management scholars to develop the ethical, political, and regulatory frameworks needed for democratic integration of social media.
abstract: Social media platforms, once hailed as potential champions of dialogue, have evolved into commodified spaces in which their business models incentivize hate speech, misinformation, polarization, and the political fragmentation of society, benefiting corporate and political elites while eroding democracy. The existing market and technological conceptualizations of social media platforms offer only partial analysis of and solutions to these issues. This paper advances a political perspective grounded in deliberative democracy, arguing that social media companies are political actors responsible for maintaining a well-functioning public sphere. We argue that transforming social media platforms into true facilitators of democratic discourse requires enhancing their deliberative capacity. Beyond the capacities of the public sphere suggested by the market and technology perspectives (1) transparency and accountability and (2) openness and inclusiveness, we propose that fostering deliberative capacity also requires (3) conduciveness to argumentation and (4) consequentiality. We thus contribute to understanding how management theories can explain and address current threats to democracy.
apa_citation: Castelló, I., Colleoni, E., Scherer, A. G., & Trittin-Ulbrich, H. (2025). Social media is a threat for democracy! A political perspective for analysing and diminishing harm. *Journal of Management Studies*. https://doi.org/10.1111/joms.70053

title: Do Women Executives Make Workplaces Safer? Evidence from Workplace Safety Violations
methods: Firm-fixed-effects panel regressions on longitudinal firm-year data; linear probability model for binary DV; mediation analysis via Seo-style product-of-coefficients path decomposition.
industry: US publicly traded firms across industries (S&P 500 / 400 mid-cap / 600 small-cap), breakdown by 2-digit SIC reported
units: firm-year observations
n_sample: 15,635 firm-year observations across 1,188 US firms
authors: Chen, Y.; Ofosu, E.; O'Sullivan, D.; Tang, S.; Zolotoy, L.
theories: critical mass theory; gender role theory; upper echelons theory
keywords: corporate social performance; women's executive representation; workplace safety
research_question: Whether, when, and how the prevalence of women executives in firms' top management influences workplace safety — a fundamental 'do no harm' aspect of corporate social performance.
mechanism_summary:
- Mediator: Employee Workload (first principal component of abnormal production and ln production per employee)
- Controls: board independence and size, CEO-chair duality, firm size, ROA, market-to-book, leverage, turnover, tangibility, capital expenditure, firm fixed effects, year fixed effects

The authors theorize that women executives' communal orientation and greater risk aversion elevate the salience of employee welfare within top management decision-making, discouraging excessive risk-taking and encouraging more deliberative oversight of safety policies, safety climate, and — most directly — employee workload. Because workload decisions cascade from executive priorities, performance targets, and incentive designs, TMTs with critical-mass women's representation are expected to avoid production pressures that push workers beyond safe capacity. The influence of women executives is amplified when structural supports — a gender-diverse board and gender-advocating institutional owners — legitimate and embolden their voice, attenuating the discounting of minority perspectives within the TMT.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reframes research on executive gender by shifting attention from the 'do good' to the 'do no harm' dimension of corporate social performance, showing that demographic composition at the apex is consequential for firms' basic obligation to protect employee welfare. It bridges the largely disconnected literatures on executive gender and board gender diversity by documenting their reinforcing dynamic, and refines theory on institutional ownership by showing that gender-advocating owners can legitimate — rather than substitute for — women executives' influence. By identifying employee workload as a proximal mediating mechanism, it extends the small body of work that opens the 'black box' between women's executive representation and firm outcomes.
practical_implication: Organizations seeking to improve workplace safety have a concrete motivation to recruit, retain, and advance women executives through leadership development, mentorship, and inclusive hiring. Firms with less diverse boards can pursue board-level gender diversity and partnerships with gender-advocating institutional owners to create a reinforcing governance dynamic. Transparent disclosure of workplace safety metrics and diversity initiatives targeting top management can align investor expectations with organizational safety priorities.
limitations: Reliance on US data may limit generalizability to other cultural and institutional contexts with different regulatory enforcement or gender norms. The Violation Tracker proxy captures reported violations and may overlook unreported incidents or latent safety concerns. The workload measure relies on archival proxies (abnormal production, production per employee) rather than employees' self-reported workload, task complexity, or physiological stress indicators.
future_research: Cross-national and cross-cultural studies could uncover additional boundary conditions on how societal, regulatory, and cultural factors shape the gender-safety link. Researchers could examine downstream effects on employee well-being, absenteeism, and long-term firm performance, and explore which specific executive roles (CFO, COO, CHRO) and the alignment between women executives and women middle managers most shape safety outcomes. Employee surveys, qualitative interviews, and third-party safety audits would enrich measurement of both workload and safety.
abstract: In this study, we develop and test theory on whether, when, and how the prevalence of women in firms' top management influences workplace safety – an important 'do no harm' dimension of corporate social performance. Consistent with our theorizing, we find that there is a negative relationship between the prevalence of women executives in firms' top management and workplace safety violations. We show that the relationship is amplified by board gender diversity and ownership by institutional investors who advocate for greater gender diversity in firms' upper echelons. We also show that the relationship is partially mediated by employee workload. Our findings have important implications for the management literature on the gender profile of firms' top management and for the workplace safety literature.
apa_citation: Chen, Y., Ofosu, E., O'Sullivan, D., Tang, S., & Zolotoy, L. (2025). Do women executives make workplaces safer? Evidence from workplace safety violations. *Journal of Management Studies*. https://doi.org/10.1111/joms.70055

title: Firm Growth and Corporate Social Responsibility
methods: Panel data analysis; OLS regressions with firm and year fixed effects; instrumental-variable two-stage models and ITCV tests for endogeneity; moderation analyses
industry: Multi-industry (firms listed in LSEG ESG database)
country: 46 countries (global sample spanning shareholder- and stakeholder-oriented economies)
units: Firm-year observations
n_sample: 51,649 firm-year observations for 4,305 firms
authors: DesJardine, M. R.; Kim, J.; Bansal, P.
theories: attention-based view of the firm; resource-based trade-offs
keywords: business sustainability; corporate social responsibility; financial slack; firm growth; managerial attention
research_question: How does the rate of firm growth affect firms' CSR engagement, and how is this relationship shaped by firms' financial and attentional resources?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the attention-based view of the firm, the authors argue that managerial attention and financial capital are finite resources that both firm growth and CSR draw upon. When growth accelerates, managers must allocate scarce cognitive and financial resources toward coordinating expansion, scaling operations, and meeting short-term performance targets, leaving less bandwidth and liquidity for CSR initiatives that yield intangible, long-term, and difficult-to-measure returns. Because CSR outcomes are distant and peripheral to core business activity, managers under pressure to deliver measurable growth-related outcomes reallocate attention and resources away from CSR. Performance variability amplifies this trade-off by further depleting attentional reservoirs, while financial slack attenuates it by providing a capital cushion that preserves managerial latitude to pursue non-growth initiatives during periods of rapid expansion.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances CSR research by identifying firm growth—one of the most pervasive and central features of organizational life—as a previously underexplored driver of CSR engagement, complementing work on organizational architecture, identity, and institutional drivers. It integrates the attention-based view with CSR theory to foreground the role of managerial attention (not only financial slack) as a scarce input that shapes corporate commitments, and it links the attention-based view with stakeholder governance by showing how growth pressures condition the shareholder-stakeholder trade-off. In doing so, the paper reframes the growth-sustainability debate away from a purely ideological contest and toward a pragmatic, trade-off-based account of managerial resource allocation.
practical_implication: For companies committed to CSR, the authors recommend establishing systems and controls that insulate CSR from fluctuations in managerial attention and financial resources—for instance, CSR departments with independent budgets (via corporate foundations or trusts) and strategic autonomy, analogous to governance structures afforded to R&D. Elevating CSR to an executive leadership position can help integrate it into the firm's core rather than treating it as peripheral. Socially responsible shareholders and stakeholders can also counteract growth pressures by putting forth CSR-related proposals during high-growth periods to redirect managerial attention and resources back toward CSR.
limitations: The study does not directly observe the microlevel managerial processes that link growth rates to CSR decisions, since attention and resource-allocation decisions are hard to capture with archival data. The analysis also takes a relatively static snapshot and is agnostic about the long-run and overall effect of growth on CSR and business sustainability, which may differ from the short-term trade-offs documented.
future_research: Future work should directly observe managers in high-growth firms (e.g., through qualitative or field methods) to examine whether growth-CSR trade-offs are made deliberately or emerge unintentionally. Researchers should also study the longer-term, historical relationship between growth and CSR, including whether growth-focused firms eventually produce game-changing CSR innovations that offset earlier trade-offs. Further theorizing is needed on how managers make attention-related trade-offs in pursuing CSR and on which organizational structures and characteristics shape those decisions.
abstract: This paper examines how the rate of firm growth influences corporate social responsibility (CSR). Periods of rapid growth can place substantial financial and managerial demands on firms, requiring leaders to prioritize the allocation of limited resources toward managing expansion. We propose that when firms grow quickly, managers may limit CSR investments—not because they undervalue CSR, but because growth itself consumes financial and attentional capacity. Using 19 years of data from 4,305 firms across 46 countries, we find that faster firm growth is associated with lower levels of CSR activity. This relationship is moderated by firms’ financial and attentional resources: firms with greater financial slack and managerial bandwidth maintain stronger CSR engagement even while growing rapidly. Overall, our results suggest that the relationship between growth and CSR reflects a pragmatic balancing of competing demands.
apa_citation: DesJardine, M. R., Kim, J., & Bansal, P. (2025). Firm growth and corporate social responsibility. *Journal of Management*. https://doi.org/10.1177/01492063251398785
