title: The Ethics of Global Business, by Denis G. Arnold. Wiley Blackwell, 2023. 161 pp.
methods: Book review; narrative critique of Denis G. Arnold's The Ethics of Global Business (2023)
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Enderle, G.
theories: claim-rights theory; human rights cosmopolitanism
keywords: Base of the Pyramid; Tripartite Framework; corporate social responsibility; global business ethics; human rights; human rights cosmopolitanism; transnational corporations
research_question: Not reported in paper.
mechanism_summary: Not reported in paper.
theoretical_contribution: Not reported in paper.
practical_implication: Enderle emphasizes that Arnold's action-oriented analysis is valuable for practice: TNCs should respect the basic human rights of people impacted at the BoP, ensure minimal health and safety conditions in the workplace, inform employees of workplace hazards during hiring, and pay a weekly wage consistent with basic human dignity. Business ventures engaging Moderate and Extreme Poverty populations should pursue empowerment strategies (assessed against human rights, utility, and capabilities) rather than exploitation strategies. The book is recommended for teaching, research, and training in TNCs.
limitations: Enderle raises four critical remarks about Arnold's book: (1) it does not fully apply Gewirth's five-component structure of claim-rights, leaving imprecision about subjects, objects, respondents, binding nature, and justification of human rights; (2) it conflates "duty" and "responsibility," defining TNCs as "Duty Bearers" rather than "Bearers of Responsibility" and retaining the confusing term CSR; (3) its assessment of the Tripartite Framework is less favorable than warranted, and it omits a philosophical justification grounded in human dignity; and (4) the cited literature is almost exclusively pre-2013 and would benefit from more recent references.
future_research: Enderle suggests that the philosophical justification of human rights in pluralistic societies, the precise division of obligations between states and corporations, and the clarification of responsibility as a three-aspect ethical term (who, for what, towards whom) deserve further exploration. He also notes that the long-term impact of the Tripartite Framework—visible, for instance, in the EU's 2025 Corporate Sustainability Due Diligence Directive—merits continued study, and that the intriguing issues raised in Arnold's book deserve to be further explored and discussed.
abstract: n his book The Ethics of Global Business (2023) Denis Arnold addresses highly important challenges of global business, with a focus on the relations of the powerful transnational corporations (TNCs) to the Base of the Pyramid (BoP)— that is, four billion people below $9.05 per day (107, Figure 7.1)—and the ethical assessment in terms of universal human rights. The author draws on decades of research and his familiarity as a five-year editor of BEQ with a vast part of literature of business ethics. He is also supported by the collaboration of Andrew Valentin and Laura Williams in the last two chapters (7 and 8).
apa_citation: Enderle, G. (2025). The ethics of global business, by Denis G. Arnold. Wiley Blackwell, 2023. 161 pp. *Business Ethics Quarterly*, 35(4), 706-709. https://doi.org/10.1017/beq.2025.10100

title: Magic Will Happen if You Gather Us in One Room: Gender Homophily and Women-Only Networking
methods: Qualitative inductive study combining participant observation, semi-structured interviews, and secondary document analysis; triangulated across data sources with iterative coding to derive meanings of gender homophily and distinguish enforced from agentic homophily.
industry: Male-dominated, competitive industries including banking, finance, corporate law, consulting, media, tech start-ups, pharma, energy, and advertising
country: Primarily Germany and the United Kingdom; participants from multiple countries including Poland, United States, Brazil, China, Kenya, the Netherlands, and Switzerland
units: Women attending corporate-sponsored women-only networking events (junior, mid-career, and senior-level professionals)
n_sample: 40 observed events (120 hours); 75 interviews; 49 informal conversations; 10 member checks; 150 secondary documents
authors: Hein, P.; Diehl, M.-R.; Kreutzer, K.
theories: activist choice homophily; benevolent sexism; gender homophily; gendered organizations theory
keywords: gender homophily; gendered organizations; inequality; meanings; networking; women
research_question: How do women perceive and experience networking at women-only events, and how do contextual factors shape the meanings women attach to gender homophily in such settings?
mechanism_summary: Women internalize gendered organizational structures differently depending on their seniority, which shapes the meanings (status-driven for senior-level, instrumental for mid-career, expressive for junior-level) they attach to networking with other women. When events are designed with enforced homophily — a patronizing, benevolent-sexism frame that treats women as a homogeneous disadvantaged group needing help — these differences in meanings become salient, fostering distancing and preventing women from leveraging homophilic ties. When events permit agentic homophily — granting women autonomy to shape the format and pursue professional outcomes — the differences in meanings recede, enabling collective, business-focused networking that can challenge rather than reproduce gendered organizational structures.
theoretical_contribution: The study reconceptualizes gender homophily as a multifaceted force shaped by the meanings individuals attach to it and by the context in which networking unfolds, rather than as a singular social mechanism. By distinguishing enforced from agentic homophily and uncovering three interlinked meanings (status-driven, instrumental, expressive), the paper extends research on gender homophily, activist choice homophily, and gendered organizations, showing that well-intentioned corporate women-only events can reproduce benevolent sexism while agentic settings enable women to challenge gendered structures.
practical_implication: Organizers should recognize that women-only networking events designed with a patronizing, "fix-the-women" framing can perpetuate benevolent sexism and structural barriers rather than dismantle them. Companies should grant women agency to shape events from the ground up, focus on collective professional outcomes, de-emphasize seniority hierarchies, and consider mixed-gender formats that recognize women's achievements as alternatives that may better address underlying gendered organizational structures.
limitations: The study focused on corporately sponsored events in highly competitive, male-dominated industries in Germany and the UK, limiting generalizability to other industries, female-dominated fields, and non-Western cultural contexts. The authors' egalitarian cultural backgrounds may have caused them to miss subtle forms of benevolent sexism. Intersectional dynamics (race, disability, LGBT+ status, socioeconomic background) did not feature prominently and were not deeply examined.
future_research: Future work should examine women's networking in female-dominated fields, internal organizational networks tracked longitudinally, and events without corporate sponsorship to capture naturally occurring homophilic ties. Longitudinal studies could explore generational and cultural shifts, while cross-cultural comparative work can surface country-specific dynamics. Research on intersectionality and networking among other disadvantaged groups (by race, disability, or LGBT+ status), as well as mixed-gender networking events, would deepen understanding of context-specific homophily dynamics.
abstract: Although networking is critical for career advancement, prior research has primarily examined women’s networking in comparison to men’s, often concluding that it plays a less instrumental role due to gendered organizational structures. Women-only networking events are organized to enable women to leverage gender homophily for mutual support and career advancement. However, the mechanisms of gender homophily in women’s networking are not straightforward. Drawing on observations of women-only networking events, interviews, and secondary data, we identify three meanings women attach to homophilic networking (status-driven, instrumental, and expressive) and show how these meanings shape networking behaviors. In networking contexts characterized by “enforced homophily,” where support is offered under the guise of benevolence, women are more aware of these differences in meanings, which prevent them from leveraging the potential benefits of networking with other women. In contexts characterized by “agentic homophily,” the relevance of these differences is less salient, empowering women to challenge, rather than reproduce, gendered organizational structures.
apa_citation: Hein, P., Diehl, M.-R., & Kreutzer, K. (2025). Magic will happen if you gather us in one room: Gender homophily and women-only networking. *Journal of Management*. https://doi.org/10.1177/01492063251390850

title: Guest Editors’ Introduction: Redefining Organizational Ethics Through the Lens of Life-and-Death
methods: Editorial essay introducing a special issue; conceptual and normative argumentation, synthesis of philosophical, organizational, and posthumanist literatures, and framing of the six articles selected for the special issue
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Pérezts, M.; Fotaki, M.; Shymko, Y.; Islam, G.
theories: ethics of care; existential phenomenology; necropolitics; politics of inclusivity; posthumanism / more-than-human ethics
keywords: death; ethics of care and politics of inclusivity; life; organization and business ethics
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary: The guest editors advance a normative framing rather than a causal model. They argue that organizing is never ethically neutral: routines, metrics, and rhetorics of efficiency distribute livability and non-livability across human and other-than-human actors, shaping who is enabled to flourish and who is "left to die" or consigned to "slow death." They propose that an organizational ethics of life-and-death must hold two poles in tension — the existential givenness of mortality and the political contingency of how lives are organized — and must be grounded in care, relationality, and collective solidarity rather than in shareholder or narrowly stakeholder logics. The essay uses this frame to read the six articles of the special issue as illustrations of how organizational practices, discourses, memes, bodies, and multispecies entanglements materially produce vital or deadly outcomes.
theoretical_contribution: The introduction reframes organizational and business ethics around "life-and-death" as a compound existential, collective, and posthuman category, extending Islam and Greenwood's (2021) call for a substantive (rather than narrow misconduct-focused) business ethics. Drawing on ethics of care, feminist relational thought, necropolitics, and more-than-human theorizing, the authors argue for an ethics that bridges micro-existential experience and macro-political organization, centers vulnerability and interdependence, and extends moral consideration beyond shareholders and even stakeholders to include nonhuman and not-yet-present others. They position this as a programmatic redefinition of the boundaries of business ethics in an era of intersecting ecological, geopolitical, and humanitarian crises.
practical_implication: The editors urge business ethics scholars, organizations, and managers to treat life-and-death stakes as core rather than peripheral to organizational decisions, resisting the sanitizing vocabularies of efficiency, "wellness," and neutral measurement that obscure who lives and who dies as a result of organizing. They call for organizing practices centered on care, relationality, collective solidarity, and multispecies accountability, and for visibilizing (rather than tabooing) death as part of ethical organizational life. Academia itself is implicated and should reflect on how its own infrastructures participate in life-and-death processes.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editors sketch a research agenda organized around three lines of inquiry: principles of care and concern, collective solidarity, and accountability and relationality, including posthuman and multispecies extensions. They invite future work that broadens the geographies, voices, and scopes of the conversation, interrogates how measurement and datafication reconfigure life-and-death stakes, examines the role of academia in (re)producing livability, and develops organizational ethics capable of affirming life in the face of genocide, ecological collapse, and normalized "business as usual" forms of slow death.
abstract: We share the world we live and die in with others, in ways that are organized and disorganized. The authors of this special issue address life-and-death as a compound term, foregrounding the vital and deadly outcomes of (dis)organization and their (business) ethics implications as they play out in the context of growing inequalities and ongoing health, geopolitical, environmental, refugee crises and egregious war crimes. Organizations and organizing can shape such contexts by engaging in the ethics of care and politics of inclusivity, redefining “essential” or “front line” work, managing relationships between bodily health and work, or ethically relating to nonhuman forms of life. Considering the roles of organizations in terms of life-and-death can help scholars redefine organizations and/in/for/with the world by stressing the ethical dimensions of organizing for life which involves human and other-thanhuman relatedness and the obligation of care for all forms of life.
apa_citation: Pérezts, M., Fotaki, M., Shymko, Y., & Islam, G. (2025). Guest editors’ introduction: Redefining organizational ethics through the lens of life-and-death. *Business Ethics Quarterly*, 35(4), 502-524. https://doi.org/10.1017/beq.2025.10098

title: Beyond Dangling Carrots: The Effect of Policy Maker Motives on Their Response to Corporate Political Activity
methods: Conceptual theory-development paper drawing on social influence theory to build a policy-maker-centered framework of CPA; develops propositions about three motive types (instrumental, relational, moral), three CPA types, policy-maker type (politician vs. bureaucrat), and lobbyist intermediation.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Tabasi-Nejad, P.; Deutsch, Y.
theories: corporate political activity (political marketplace view); public service motivation; social influence theory
keywords: bureaucrats; corporate political activity; instrumental motives; lobbyists; moral motives; policy maker motives; politicians; relational motives; social influence theory
research_question: Why would a policy maker be influenced (or not) by a firm's CPA efforts? The authors develop a more comprehensive framework of CPA that centers the motives of the target policy maker rather than treating the policy maker as a solely instrumentally driven actor in an exchange.
mechanism_summary: Drawing on social influence theory, the paper argues that policy makers are driven not only by instrumental (self-interest) motives but also by relational motives (connection, belonging) and moral motives (values, identity). Firms can therefore engage in three corresponding CPA types, and effectiveness depends on the match between CPA type and the policy maker's dominant motive. Mismatches can backfire, as when instrumental inducements trigger moral identity threat in a morally motivated policy maker. Bureaucrats and politicians differ systematically in their motivational profiles and in how they process monitoring, relational salience, and moral outrage, so the same CPA tactic is not equally effective across policy-maker types. Third-party lobbyists enhance CPA by amplifying monitoring capacity, relational ties, and moral credibility, going beyond their currently theorized roles of resource aggregation and access provision.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends CPA theory beyond its exclusive focus on instrumental motives by integrating relational and moral motives, thereby identifying new noninstrumental types of CPA and explaining when CPA may succeed, fail, or even produce negative influence. It introduces policy-maker type (politician vs. bureaucrat) as a core theoretical component of CPA, arguing that bureaucrats constitute a motivationally distinct class of policy maker who have been largely neglected. It also expands the conceptualization of lobbyists from resource aggregators or access providers to key intermediaries who enhance influence determinants, and in doing so extends social influence theory to account for intermediaries and for influencee type.
practical_implication: For firms designing CPA strategies, the framework suggests that "dangling carrots" is often not the most effective tactic and can be counterproductive when policy makers are driven by relational or moral motives. Firms should diagnose the dominant motive profile of the target policy maker and match it with an appropriate CPA type, taking special care when targeting bureaucrats, who tend to differ from politicians in their motivational orientations. Employing third-party lobbyists is valuable not only for access but also for monitoring, relationship building, and enhancing moral credibility.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors invite research on CPA that simultaneously combines multiple influence types (e.g., relational and moral) and on which combinations are more likely to be used and succeed. They encourage empirical work on the relationship between corporate social responsibility and CPA when both speak to moral motives, and on how noninstrumental motives shape outcomes in settings beyond politics (e.g., incentive use in knowledge-sharing communities). They also call for research on how influencee type (e.g., bureaucrat-like, technically oriented stakeholders) shapes the effectiveness of relational influence, and on how intermediaries beyond lobbyists (e.g., lawyers serving activist shareholders) enhance influence in other contexts.
abstract: Management scholars have predominantly theorized corporate political activity (CPA) as an exchange wherein a firm offers resources to a policy maker in return for favorable policy. While the CPA literature has extensively explored what drives firms to engage in CPA, it remains largely silent on the question of why a policy maker would accede to a firm’s attempt at influence. Drawing on social influence theory, we develop a framework that extends current CPA theory and centers a policy maker’s perspective when faced with CPA. We introduce a policy maker’s noninstrumental motives and identify novel types of CPA that appeal to these motives. We also consider the role of the bureaucrat—a heretofore largely ignored but important type of policy maker whose motives differ from those of politicians in key respects that shape whether and how a given policy maker is influenced. Lastly, we examine how third-party lobbyists affect the CPA process, identifying key pathways through which they are likely to enhance a firm’s CPA. By theorizing around the motives of the policy maker faced with CPA, we present a novel policy maker-centered framework that helps us better understand this socially and economically important strategy.
apa_citation: Tabasi-Nejad, P., & Deutsch, Y. (2025). Beyond dangling carrots: The effect of policy maker motives on their response to corporate political activity. *Academy of Management Review*. https://doi.org/10.5465/amr.2023.0033

title: Getting Away with Murder: Legitimizing Unethical Organization Practices via Memes
methods: Inductive qualitative case study of Internet memes legitimizing three lethal Brazilian police operations (Jacarezinho, Vila Cruzeiro, Complexo do Alemão, 2021-2022); collection of 495 Instagram memes (feeds and stories) from police, politicians, and right-wing influencer profiles, and selection of seven most-engaged memes; discourse analysis following Shifman's (2013) and Gal et al.'s (2016) three-dimensional meme framework (form, content, stance), combined with intertextual tracing of cultural artefacts and traditional media references
industry: State policing / public security; social media (Instagram)
time_period: 2021-2022 (one-week collection windows following each of three police operations)
units: Internet memes circulated on Instagram in the aftermath of police massacres in Rio de Janeiro favelas
n_sample: 495 memes collected; 7 memes retained for in-depth discourse analysis (3 from Jacarezinho, 2 from Vila Cruzeiro, 2 from Complexo do Alemão)
authors: Vianna, F.; Alcadipani, R.; Barros, M.; Matarazzo, G.
theories: discursive legitimation; extreme case research; necrocapitalism; necropolitics; state of exception
keywords: ethics of life and death; favelas; legitimation; memes; police
research_question: How do social media memes legitimize organizational actions related to the ethics of life and death? The authors investigate this in the context of rising authoritarianism through the extreme case of Brazilian police massacres in Rio de Janeiro favelas.
mechanism_summary: Drawing on necropolitics and necrocapitalism, the paper argues that state organizations and their supporters use memes as a new legitimation technology that departs from the traditional discourses of normalization and invisibilization. Instead of hiding or routinizing killing, memes render deaths hyper-visible and celebrate them: by ridiculing human-rights critics, framing favela residents as deserving of death, and sanctifying police as rightful executioners, the memetic discourse constructs favelas as "states of exception" where lethal violence is morally warranted. The humorous, rapid, and ephemeral nature of memes short-circuits rational ethical debate and replaces it with superficial, affective engagement, thereby supporting racialized necropolitical regimes within a broader authoritarian drive enabled by social media platforms.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes three contributions to the literature on the ethics of the organization of death. First, it identifies a third legitimation discourse alongside normalization and invisibilization — one that makes death visible and celebrates it, particularly when directed at marginalized groups. Second, it theorizes memes as a distinctive legitimation technology whose speed, humor, and ephemerality void ethical debate and replace it with vacuous engagement, linking social media affordances to the reproduction of authoritarian and necropolitical discourses. Third, it foregrounds the state's law-enforcement apparatus as an expression of racialized colonial inequalities, connecting business ethics to the critical study of state violence and the complicity of Big Tech platforms that allow such content to circulate.
practical_implication: The authors argue that ethical corporations, particularly Big Tech platforms, must account for the memetic content they allow to circulate, since permitting such material makes them complicit in the regulation of death and the terror inflicted on vulnerable populations. They also suggest that social media can be mobilized in the opposite direction — to combat inequalities in the organization of life and death, as seen in the George Floyd case — and call on business ethics practitioners, regulators, and civil society to contest memetic legitimations of state violence rather than treat them as harmless humor.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors call for further research on the organizational ethics of life and death in the post-truth era, particularly on the role of social media platforms and Big Tech complicity in enabling legitimations of killing. They invite studies that examine how memes and other pictorial technologies operate in other authoritarian and necropolitical contexts, and how counter-memes and alternative discourses might contest the celebration of death directed at marginalized and racialized populations.
abstract: Research on the ethics of life and death in organizations has overlooked the growing role of social media in shaping the ethical implications of organizational regulations on death. More specifically, while recent research suggests that memes are increasingly used to influence the reputation and perception of organizational actions, research on their roles in legitimating the organization of death is scant. This paper analyzes Internet memes legitimizing police slaughter in Brazilian favelas. Three primary discourses were identified: denying life worthiness, establishing actors deserving death, and upholding the police organization as executioners of death. This paper contributes to research on the ethics of organizational death in three different ways in a context of political polarization and growing authoritarism. First, it discusses how social media creates a legitimate discourse that makes death visible and gloats over its victims. Second, this discourse is enabled by social media memes, which divert the ethical debate on life and death, replacing it with superficial and transient engagement. Third, we discuss how memes can reinforce the role of the state police enforcement apparatus as an expression of racialized inequalities.
apa_citation: Vianna, F., Alcadipani, R., Barros, M., & Matarazzo, G. (2025). Getting away with murder: Legitimizing unethical organization practices via memes. *Business Ethics Quarterly*, 35(4), 620-651. https://doi.org/10.1017/beq.2025.10094

title: How Do Fieldworkers in Poverty Craft Meaningful Roles to Achieve Social Impact? Female Teachers in Slums in India
methods: Mixed-methods sequential design. Study 1: inductive qualitative study with four rounds of fieldwork (2010-2017) at the Indian NGO Gyan Shala, comprising 131 semi-structured interviews with teachers, former students, siblings, supervisors, curriculum-design staff, and the CEO, analyzed in NVivo via grounded-theory coding (Gioia approach). Study 2: three-wave time-lagged survey of teachers (Time 1, Time 2) and supervisors (Time 2, Time 3) at Gyan Shala's Ahmedabad operations, with translation/back-translation to Gujarati, CFA on an independent Uttar Pradesh sample (n=176), and a moderated-mediation path model estimated in Mplus with bootstrapped indirect effects.
industry: Education / non-governmental organization (NGO) — para-skilled female primary and middle-school teachers at Gyan Shala, an NGO providing low-cost schooling for children in slums.
country: India (Ahmedabad; with curriculum scope across Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh, and Bihar)
time_period: 2010-2017 (Study 1 qualitative fieldwork, four rounds); Study 2 three-wave time-lagged survey at Gyan Shala's Ahmedabad location
units: Individual female fieldworker (teachers), with supervisor ratings nested in supervisors
n_sample: Study 1: 131 semi-structured interviews; Study 2: 175 teachers with complete data across three waves, rated by 33 supervisors (CFA validation sample n=176)
authors: Barkema, Harry G.; Coyle-Shapiro, Jacqueline A-M.; le Grand, Eva M.
theories: background stressors framework (Gump & Matthews, 1999); conservation of resources theory (Hobfoll, 2001); job-crafting theory (Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001); social identification with community (Mael & Ashforth, 1992)
keywords: community identification; female teachers; fieldworkers; job crafting; meaningful work; moral duty; poverty; relational job crafting; social impact; sustainable development goals
research_question: Do fieldworkers in social organizations operating in poverty settings (e.g., female para-teachers in Indian slums) engage in job crafting and, if so, what poverty- and gender-related challenges constrain them and what enablers (e.g., identification with the local community) support them in crafting meaningful work that translates into social impact for users?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: poverty- and gender-related background stressors (organizational dependence, lack of family support) vs. moral duty and community identification as enabling resources; relational job crafting as the discretionary behavior; user-centered social impact as the ultimate outcome.
- Process / phases: Study 1 inductively surfaces challenges and enablers in context (four rounds of fieldwork, grounded-theory coding); Study 2 operationalizes the emergent constructs and tests a moderated-mediation model with three time-lagged waves.
- Practices: teachers stay after class, visit children at home, invite students to their homes on weekends, and expand interactions with parents and community members — relational crafting that happens largely outside the formal workplace, "in the community."
- Triggering conditions: poverty-induced financial worry and household demands deplete energy and resources (per conservation of resources theory; Hobfoll, 2001); moral duty to educate the poor motivates extra-role behavior; perceived organizational contribution to the cause amplifies the moral-duty effect.
- Boundary conditions: community identification buffers the depleting effect of organizational dependence on relational crafting; perceived organizational contribution to the cause moderates the moral-duty pathway; results hold within one NGO, one sector (education), and one country.
- Emergent outcomes: relational job crafting mediates the path to social impact, measured along independent decision-making, ambition, confidence, and social-relationship dimensions valued by users (a Sen/Nussbaum capability-based construction).

The central process is that female fieldworkers in poverty are not simply depleted by their circumstances: although poverty- and gender-related stressors deplete energetic and resource reserves and constrain relational crafting, teachers' identification with the local community in which they live and work supplies a counterbalancing resource that enables them to expand the frequency, quality, and intensity of their social interactions with students and parents. This relational crafting, reinforced by a sense of moral duty that resonates with the organization's perceived contribution to the cause, translates fieldworkers' meaningful work into measurable social impact in terms of what users (school children) have reason to value to be and to do in life.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends job-crafting theory into the extreme, under-studied context of female fieldworkers in poverty and shows that much relational crafting takes place in the local community rather than the formal workplace — broadening the empirical and theoretical scope of where and how crafting happens. It develops and corroborates community identification as a novel enabler that buffers the depleting effects of poverty- and gender-related background stressors, and theorizes moral duty (amplified by perceived organizational contribution) as a distinct driver of relational crafting. The paper also establishes job-crafting theory as a new lens for understanding how social organizations attain SDG-related social impact through their fieldworkers, and offers a user-centered, capability-based measurement approach to social impact in context.
practical_implication: For NGOs, social enterprises, and other organizations addressing SDGs, the findings imply that recruiting fieldworkers from the local community is not merely a cost-saving expedient but a strategic source of relational energy and meaning. Social organizations can boost fieldworkers' meaningful work and social impact by reducing barriers (e.g., flexible scheduling that respects household responsibilities) and bolstering enablers (e.g., visible, credible organizational commitment to the cause that resonates with workers' moral duty). Importantly, NGOs that rely on employee goodwill without demonstrating a parallel organizational commitment to the cause will fall short in their impact.
limitations: The research is conducted in one organization (Gyan Shala), one sector (education), and one country (India), which strengthens internal consistency across qualitative and quantitative evidence but limits external validity. The study focuses inductively on relational job crafting and does not test other crafting forms (e.g., cognitive crafting) that may matter elsewhere. The sample is exclusively female teachers in primary/middle school, so generalization to male fieldworkers, other sectors (e.g., health), or Global-North poverty contexts remains an open empirical question.
future_research: The authors invite research on whether the identified challenges and enablers (especially community identification) generalize to other fieldworkers such as community health workers and youth workers, to male fieldworkers, and to other poverty contexts in the Global South and Global North (e.g., Chicago, Los Angeles, London). They suggest examining additional crafting forms (e.g., cognitive crafting) in other settings, exploring the underlying mechanisms (knowledge, understanding, perspective taking) that link relational crafting to social impact, and continuing to refine context-sensitive, user-centered measures of social impact for NGOs, hybrid organizations, and impact investors.
abstract: Prior research has adopted a job-crafting perspective to explain why employees attempt to craft their roles meaningfully (Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001). We explore this theoretical lens in a new context that is particularly challenging for workers and where it would seem unlikely to apply: poverty. More specifically, we study female teachers in slums in India. We use a mixed-methods approach—first qualitative research, then quantitative research—to contextualize job-crafting theorizing by identifying, conceptualizing, and testing situational challenges and enablers in regard to meaningful work in this context. More specifically, we develop and corroborate new theory suggesting that poverty- and gender-related stressors deplete teachers' energy and resources, limiting relational job crafting, but that teachers' identification with the community helps to counteract this challenge, ultimately increasing their social impact. More fundamentally, we show how job-crafting theorizing, contextualized in a poverty setting, helps to explain how social organizations, through their fieldworkers (e.g., female teachers), create social impact.
apa_citation: Barkema, H. G., Coyle-Shapiro, J. A-M., & le Grand, E. M. (2024). How do fieldworkers in poverty craft meaningful roles to achieve social impact? Female teachers in slums in India. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(1), 232-261. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1244

title: Media Coverage of Firms in the Presence of Multiple Signals: A Configurational Approach
methods: Fuzzy-set qualitative comparative analysis (fsQCA) of 118 firm-year observations supplemented by in-depth case analyses, LIWC text analysis of sustainability reports and press releases, and longitudinal pattern tracing across configurations.
industry: Electric utilities industry (energy utility)
country: International sample with headquarters in multiple countries (English- and local-language media)
units: Firm-year observations of electric utility firms
n_sample: 118 firm-year observations covering 26 firms
authors: Bettinazzi, E. L. M.; Jacqueminet, A.; Neumann, K.; Snoeren, P.
theories: optimal distinctiveness (Deephouse, 1999; Zhao, Fisher, Lounsbury, & Miller, 2017); signaling theory (Connelly, Certo, Ireland, & Reutzel, 2011; Stern, Dukerich, & Zajac, 2014; Vergne, Wernicke, & Brenner, 2018)
keywords: configurational approach; conformity; distinctiveness; environmental sustainability; fsQCA; media coverage; optimal distinctiveness; signal congruence; signal credibility; signaling theory
research_question: How do the media combine the various conformity and distinctiveness signals that firms emit about their environmental behavior to construct an overall representation, and which signal configurations are associated with positive (versus the absence of positive) media coverage?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: Six signal types crossing conformity vs. distinctiveness with three levels of credibility — firm-generated (donations, transformative actions), relationship-generated (associations, interfirm partnerships), and third-party-generated (certifications such as ISO 14001, ratings such as DJSI inclusion).
- Outcome: Presence vs. absence of positive media coverage of firms' environmental behavior (Janis–Fadner-based tenor measure).
- Patterns producing positive coverage: (1) Congruent signalers (congruent conformity or distinctiveness around a credible third-party signal); (2) Balancing signalers (a credible third-party signal paired with a lower-credibility opposing signal); (3) Muddling-through signalers (combinations of lower-credibility signals, granted temporary "benefit of the doubt").
- Patterns producing absence of positive coverage: (4) Minimal signalers (insufficient signaling, no anchor); (5) Credible incongruent signalers (simultaneous presence of opposing highly credible third-party signals that prevent media from anchoring on a dominant message).
- Boundary conditions: Causal complexity, equifinality, and conjunctural causation; underlying perception depends jointly on signal credibility and congruence.

The process by which media form environmental assessments is configurational rather than additive: they triangulate multiple noisy signals through a sensemaking process anchored on the most credible signals available. Incongruence is only problematic when it occurs at the level of highly credible third-party signals because the media cannot identify a dominant message; lower-credibility signals can rebalance a dominant credible message or, in the absence of credible signals, temporarily compensate. Optimal distinctiveness via simultaneous high-credibility conformity and distinctiveness signaling is therefore difficult — the media tend to read it as greenwashing-like incongruence.
theoretical_contribution: The paper cross-fertilizes signaling theory and optimal distinctiveness literature by showing that signal (in)congruence and signal credibility are interdependent drivers of audience evaluation. It identifies signal credibility as a deeper-level determinant of incongruence perception: incongruence at the level of highly credible signals undermines audience approval, whereas balanced or congruent credible anchors enable positive assessments even when lower-credibility opposing signals are present. The paper also reframes optimal distinctiveness as harder to achieve than prior literature suggests because audiences penalize postures they cannot categorize.
practical_implication: Firms should focus on obtaining third-party endorsement of either their conformity (e.g., ISO 14001) or their distinctiveness (e.g., DJSI inclusion), but not both simultaneously, because over-endorsement with opposing credible signals invites media scrutiny and is read as incongruent. Firms without credible third-party recognition can use combinations of lower-credibility signals as a temporary buffer, but must move toward acquiring a credible anchor to sustain positive coverage.
limitations: The findings rest on the assumption that firms have agency in deciding which signals to emit, which may not hold equally across contexts (e.g., financial disclosures vs. marketing campaigns). The setting (environmental sustainability in electric utilities) is characterized by high causal ambiguity and may not generalize to more standardized contexts where signal credibility and congruence may matter less. The interpretation of configurations and patterns is abductive and primarily exploratory.
future_research: Future studies could examine (in)congruence in other behavioral dimensions beyond conformity–distinctiveness and in contexts where highly credible third-party signals are absent or less relevant. Heterogeneity among media (including social media and other arbiters), context ambiguity as a moderator, and the cross-influence between media reactions and other stakeholder reactions (e.g., activists) are promising avenues. Researchers could also disentangle the sensemaking process audiences use when facing incongruent signals of differing credibility.
abstract: Recent management research suggests that firms try to orchestrate conformity and distinctiveness in their behavior to reconcile opposing demands of norm compliance and competitive distinction in order to appear optimally distinct. Whether they succeed or not depends on how audiences perceive the multiple signals they elicit. Extant literature has not fully uncovered how the media combine these different signals to form a representation of firms' behavior and their subsequent reactions to these combinations. We adopt a configurational approach to address this gap in the environmental sustainability context. Drawing on unique proprietary data on 118 cases in the energy utility industry and using fsQCA, we determine that different equifinal signal configurations are associated with positive media coverage regarding environmental behavior or the lack thereof. We then generate five patterns of signals that explain media reactions and disentangle the underlying logics behind them. Our study adds to the research on the multifaceted nature of signal (in)congruence perception by discussing the role of conformity and distinctiveness and highlighting that this perception depends on the signals' credibility. Delivering a balanced orchestration of conformity and distinctiveness signals is possible, but complex, because audiences tend to penalize incongruent postures they cannot associate with a given category.
apa_citation: Bettinazzi, E. L. M., Jacqueminet, A., Neumann, K., & Snoeren, P. (2024). Media coverage of firms in the presence of multiple signals: A configurational approach. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(1), 150–177. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1791

title: Performance Shortfalls, Response Directions, and Belief in the Effectiveness of Responses
methods: Firm-level fixed-effects panel regressions on an unbalanced firm-year panel of publicly listed Chinese firms (2009–2018); independent, moderating, and control variables lagged one year; standard errors clustered at the firm level; shared-belief variables constructed from MD&A documents via a causal-mapping NLP approach (sentence-count of R&D–performance and CSR–performance causal statements); evidence-of-belief measured as three-year rolling correlations between R&D/CSR and ROA based on the firm's own history and on a five-firm matched peer group; Granger causality and AR(1) (xtregar) robustness checks.
industry: Publicly listed firms in China, excluding financial firms and specially treated (ST) firms; further excluding industries (e.g., real estate, residential services) where most firms do not invest substantially in R&D
units: firm-year
n_sample: Unbalanced panel; analytic samples of 12,837 firm-years for R&D-intensity models and 11,495 firm-years for donation-intensity models
authors: Cao, Zhi; Jiang, Feifei; Wang, Donghan
theories: behavioral theory of the firm (Cyert & March, 1963); managerial and social cognition (March & Simon, 1958; Fiske & Taylor, 2021); problemistic search / decision rules (Greve, 2003a; Posen et al., 2018)
keywords: R&D intensity; behavioral theory of the firm; corporate social responsibility; donation intensity; performance shortfalls; problemistic search; shared belief
research_question: Which specific responses do firms adopt when addressing performance shortfalls, once we relax the implicit assumption that all considered responses are equally workable, and how do decision-makers' shared beliefs in the effectiveness of those responses—and the evidence supporting those beliefs—shape the direction of problemistic search?
mechanism_summary: When a firm falls below its aspiration level, decision-makers do not simply apply a single decision rule (proximity, time-horizon, risk-taking) to a set of equally workable responses; rather, they assess whether a particular response is believed to improve performance. A dominant shared belief in R&D effectiveness steepens the positive performance-shortfall–R&D-intensity slope, while a dominant shared belief in CSR effectiveness flattens the otherwise negative performance-shortfall–donation-intensity slope. These shared-belief effects are strongest when accompanied by supportive correlational evidence linking the response to firm performance, because such evidence persuades non-supporters and consolidates the dominant belief. The process thus combines behavioral search with a social-cognitive mechanism in which action–outcome linkages, not just abstract response characteristics, direct search.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends the behavioral theory of the firm by introducing decision-makers' shared beliefs in the effectiveness of specific responses as a determinant of problemistic search directions, complementing the established decision-rule approach that focuses on abstract response characteristics. It enriches managerial-cognition research in strategy by showing that supportive evidence (here, response–performance correlations) can substitute for political maneuvering or power-based coalition-building in producing a dominant shared belief among decision-maker subgroups. It also contributes a behavioral perspective to the CSR literature by explaining when firms expand or contract philanthropic donation intensity in response to performance shortfalls.
practical_implication: Top management teams that wish to redirect search toward a particular response after a performance shortfall (for example, increasing R&D or sustaining CSR investments such as philanthropic donations) should invest in building and documenting a shared belief in that response's effectiveness, ideally supported by concrete correlational evidence linking the response to firm performance. Presenting strong evidence can persuade dissenting subgroups without resorting to power plays or ally-building, and is reflected in the firm's communicative artifacts such as MD&A narratives.
limitations: The study examines only two responses (R&D and philanthropic donations), so generalization to other market and non-market responses requires further work. Data constraints prevented direct observation of the micro-level process by which shared beliefs are formed and how evidence persuades doubters. Reliance on publicly listed Chinese firms means the findings reflect a distinct institutional environment whose national culture and formal institutions may bound generalizability to other contexts.
future_research: Future work should extend the shared-belief framework to additional response types beyond R&D and philanthropic donations, including other market and non-market actions. It should also open up the micro-process by which subgroups of decision-makers with diverging beliefs interact, marshal evidence, and converge on a dominant shared belief. Comparative studies in non-Chinese institutional environments would clarify how the effects of shared beliefs and supporting evidence on problemistic search vary across contexts.
abstract: Problemistic search literature has long sought to understand which responses firms adopt when addressing performance shortfalls. Studies have typically considered certain responses and focused on established decision rules to examine search directions, thereby, implicitly assuming that all responses considered are workable solutions to performance shortfalls. Conversely, we argue that variations in decision-makers' beliefs about the effectiveness of particular responses in improving firm performance play an important role. These beliefs, alongside evidence supporting them, determine which specific responses firms adopt. To test this argument, we focus on two types of search solutions represented by research and development (R&D) intensity and philanthropic donation intensity. Based on 2009–2018 data collected from publicly listed Chinese firms, we find that, when decision-makers agree on the effectiveness of R&D, the positive relationship between performance shortfalls and R&D intensity strengthens; whereas when they agree on the effectiveness of corporate social responsibility (CSR), the negative relationship between performance shortfalls and donation intensity weakens. The effects of shared beliefs on the effectiveness of R&D and CSR are stronger when they are supported by relevant evidence—that is, when there is a stronger correlation between R&D or CSR on the one hand and firm performance on the other.
apa_citation: Cao, Z., Jiang, F., & Wang, D. (2024). Performance shortfalls, response directions, and belief in the effectiveness of responses. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(1), 178–207. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0241

title: Firm–Stakeholder Dialogue and the Media: The Evolution of Stakeholder Evaluations in Different Informational Environments
methods: Quantitative analysis of an original, hand-coded panel of 6,948 stakeholder-day observations capturing source–verb–target triplets from 22,229 media articles about 26 gold mines in 20 countries. Estimated multilevel random coefficient models (RCMs) with mine-level random effects, country and year fixed effects, and a Heckman-style first-stage selection correction (inverse Mills ratio) to test moderation of firm–stakeholder dialogue by information availability, ESG thematic content, and three measures of media reliability (V-Dem government censorship, V-Dem media-critical, and Freedom House press-freedom).
industry: Gold mining (firms publicly listed on the Toronto Stock Exchange operating mines outside the U.S., Canada, and Australia)
country: 20 countries across Africa, Asia, Europe, Latin America, and Oceania (e.g., Romania, Brazil, Eritrea, Bolivia, Greece, Philippines, DRC, Kazakhstan)
units: Firm–stakeholder dyads observed via media-reported interactions; analytic unit is stakeholder-day
n_sample: 2,454 stakeholders and 26 gold mines; 34,745 coded source–verb–target events; 6,948 stakeholder-day observations in the main model
authors: Dorobantu, S.; Henisz, W. J.; Nartey, L. J.
theories: agenda-setting theory of the media (McCombs & Shaw, 1972); impression management; information asymmetry / lemons problem (Akerlof, 1970); stakeholder theory (Freeman, 1984)
keywords: ESG content; firm–stakeholder dialogue; impression management; information availability; information reliability; informational environment; media; stakeholder evaluations
research_question: How does the informational environment — specifically the availability, ESG thematic content, and reliability of media information about a firm — moderate the effect of proactive firm–stakeholder dialogue on the evolution of a stakeholder's evaluation of the firm?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Stakeholder type (political, social, economic), stakeholder location (local, national, international), prior stakeholder evaluations, inclusive stakeholder strategy, media sentiment, country and year fixed effects, inverse Mills ratio from a first-stage selection model

Building on Akerlof's lemons logic and stakeholder theory, the paper argues that firm–stakeholder dialogue and media information are alternative channels for reducing information asymmetries between a firm and its stakeholders. Dialogue most improves stakeholder evaluations precisely when media information is scarce, ESG-focused content is low, and the media is trusted; conversely, when the media is censored or unreliable, stakeholders' distrust spills over to firm-provided information, dampening dialogue's effect. Dense ESG-laden coverage shifts the agenda of firm–stakeholder interactions and crowds out the firm's ability to shape evaluations through proactive dialogue.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances stakeholder, reputation, and impression-management research by theorizing the informational environment as a critical moderator of firm–stakeholder dialogue, shifting the analytic level from the firm to the firm–stakeholder dyad and integrating proactive dialogue with reactive impression management on a single proactive-to-reactive continuum. It also extends attention to nonmarket stakeholders — communities, NGOs, governments, religious leaders — whose evaluations form a key part of a firm's social approval but who are absent from conventional governance mechanisms. Counterintuitively, it shows that low media reliability undermines rather than amplifies the value of direct firm–stakeholder communication, because distrust in the broader information environment spills over.
practical_implication: Managers should match stakeholder strategy to the informational environment: proactive dialogue pays off most when media coverage of the firm is sparse, ESG salience is low, and stakeholders trust the media; under heavy ESG-focused or censored coverage, dialogue alone cannot offset the agenda set by the media and may need to be paired with other responses. Investments in stakeholder dialogue — the basis of a firm's "social license to operate" — are particularly valuable for projects in environments where independent media is weak but where direct, trusted relationships can still be built.
limitations: The study observes only stakeholder evaluations reported in English-language media and therefore relies on a first-stage selection model with an inverse Mills ratio to mitigate sample-selection bias. The empirical setting is restricted to gold-mining firms listed on the Toronto Stock Exchange operating outside the U.S., Canada, and Australia, which improves comparability but limits generalizability to other industries and to interactions not visible to the media. The Rosia Montana fieldwork is presented as illustration rather than as theory-building qualitative data.
future_research: Future work should examine how signaling and impression-management tactics play out in different institutional environments, including under political openness, executive constraints, and varying stakeholder network structures. Scholars should further investigate how the deteriorating reliability of media — including "fake news" and disinformation — reshapes the value of direct firm disclosure, third-party certification, and firm–stakeholder dialogue across industries. Closer attention is needed to how reactive impression management interacts with proactive dialogue along the continuum theorized here.
abstract: We propose that the informational environment in which firm–stakeholder interactions are embedded shapes the effect of firm–stakeholder dialogue on a stakeholder's evaluation of a firm. How much stakeholders know about a firm (information availability), what stakeholders know about a firm (information content), and how much they trust the media that informs them (information reliability) vary across countries, firms, and time. We theorize and assess how these three dimensions of the informational environment shape the effects of firm–stakeholder dialogue on the evolution of a stakeholder's evaluation of a firm. We illustrate our arguments with insights from field interviews conducted to understand stakeholders' evaluations of the Rosia Montana gold mine in Romania, and we analyze them using original, manually coded stakeholder-level data that capture 2,454 stakeholders' evaluations of 26 gold-mining operations around the world.
apa_citation: Dorobantu, S., Henisz, W. J., & Nartey, L. J. (2024). Firm–stakeholder dialogue and the media: The evolution of stakeholder evaluations in different informational environments. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(1), 92–125. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0103

title: Expanding AMJ’S Manuscript Portfolio: Research Methods Articles Designed to Advance Theory and Span Boundaries
methods: Editorial announcement; no empirical analysis. Articulates editorial criteria and introduces incoming methods-focused editors.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Gruber, M.; Bliese, P.
keywords: AMJ editorial policy; boundary spanning; methodological articles; research methods; theory development
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: AMJ is, as of January 1, 2024, expanding its manuscript portfolio to include feature-length methodological articles, on the premise that methods provide foundational grounding for theory development rather than playing a strictly secondary role.
- Two acceptable approaches: (1) manuscripts that illustrate how methodological advances established elsewhere can be applied to advance theory in management topic areas; (2) "best practice" manuscripts that make a compelling case for novel theoretical advancement (the bar for the latter is explicitly set "quite high").
- Key evaluative criteria for AMJ methods articles: clearly advance theory; achieve broad relevance and span boundaries across micro/macro and AOM divisions; provide example hypotheses; clarify connections between novel research practices and theoretical developments; be accessible (avoid heavy reliance on equations or specialized software; provide example code/data where applicable).
- Particular openness to underrepresented submission types — explicitly macro and qualitative methods implications.
- Editorial team additions to handle methods manuscripts: Paul Bliese (deputy editor, research methods), Trevis Certo (quantitative-macro), Anne Smith (qualitative), and Mo Wang (quantitative-micro).

The editorial reframes the role of methods within AMJ from a vehicle for testing theory to a generative source of theoretical advancement. Methodological articles will be evaluated less on technical novelty in isolation and more on the theoretical shortcomings they help address and the breadth of the audience that can use them — explicitly inverting the typical structure of a methods article from "what problem is being solved" to "what theoretical shortcomings can be addressed by the method."
theoretical_contribution: The editorial repositions methodology as a primary engine of theory development at AMJ rather than an instrumental adjunct, articulating evaluative criteria that privilege theoretical payoff, boundary-spanning audience reach, and accessibility. It builds on AMJ's prior history of publishing influential methods articles in the 1990s (e.g., Edwards & Parry, 1993; Miller, Cardinal & Glick, 1997) and seeks to host the next generation of cornerstone methodological contributions.
practical_implication: For prospective authors, the editorial supplies concrete guidelines: frame submissions around the theoretical shortcomings a method addresses (not the methodological problem solved); aim for broad relevance across AOM divisions; provide example hypotheses; relegate equations and code to appendices; and supply reproducible examples and data where possible. For the field, the policy signals AMJ's intent to lower the friction for methods-driven theoretical innovation, with explicit encouragement for macro and qualitative methods submissions.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Not reported in paper
abstract: FROM THE EDITORS EXPANDING AMJ’S MANUSCRIPT PORTFOLIO: RESEARCH METHODS ARTICLES DESIGNED TO ADVANCE THEORY AND SPAN BOUNDARIES

title: Now You See Me: How Status and Categorical Proximity Shape Misconduct Scandalization
methods: Ordered probit regression with standard errors clustered by firm, predicting a three-category ordered dependent variable (no media coverage / moderate coverage / scandalized) from firm status, prevalence of high-status insider and outsider breaches, and their interactions, with marginal-effects analysis and robustness checks (alternative cutoffs, continuous specifications, RIR endogeneity analysis).
industry: Publicly traded U.S. firms across multiple industries (10 of the 12 Fama–French industries represented) that disclosed corporate data breaches
units: Disclosed corporate data breach incidents (firm-breach observations)
n_sample: 224 breaches involving 157 publicly traded U.S. companies
authors: Han, J.-H.; Pollock, T. G.; Graffin, S. D.
theories: category theory (Delmestri & Greenwood, 2016; Han & Pollock, 2021); context theory (Johns, 2006, 2017); media routines perspective (Graf-Vlachy et al., 2020; Shoemaker & Reese, 2013); status theory (Podolny & Phillips, 1996; Sauder et al., 2012)
keywords: categorical proximity; data breaches; media routines; misconduct; newsworthiness; scandalization; status
research_question: Why do only some instances of firm misconduct become scandals, and how do the status and industry category of past transgressors shape whether the focal high-status firm's misconduct is scandalized by the media?
mechanism_summary: The mechanism rests on a media-routines logic: journalists face deadline pressures, limited attention, and reward structures that favor "scoops" with industry-based narrative arcs. High-status focal firms are inherently newsworthy because their data breaches violate normative expectations and carry social significance. When prior high-status transgressors share the focal firm's industry, journalists infer an industry-wide pattern, draw on richer industry-based narratives, and intensify scrutiny—amplifying the status–scandalization link (scrutiny-in-numbers). When prior high-status transgressors are scattered across other industries, no coherent industry storyline emerges, journalists redirect attention outside the focal industry, and the focal firm's status loses its salience (safety-in-numbers), attenuating scandalization.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to the scandalization literature by identifying past transgressors' status and categorical proximity as previously overlooked contextual boundary conditions on the well-established status–scandalization relationship, showing that context can amplify or even nullify the effect of focal-firm status. It also extends status theory by demonstrating that status is relational not only through status leakage but through how other high-status actors—inside and outside the focal category—create evaluative contexts that shape audiences' interpretations, theorizing the intersection of vertical (status) and horizontal (category) dimensions of social evaluation.
practical_implication: Managers of high-status firms should recognize that their status is not invariably a liability after misconduct; when high-status outsiders have committed similar transgressions, scandalization risk is reduced. Conversely, middle- and high-status firms facing prevalent misconduct within their industry should prepare for elevated media scrutiny and consider proactive measures to limit publicity, but the authors caution they are not advising firms on how to "escape" responsibility, only how to anticipate publicity dynamics.
limitations: The study focuses exclusively on data breaches as the misconduct of interest and on industry-based categorization, leaving open whether other misconduct types or alternative categorizations (geographic, customer-based, strategic groups) would yield similar dynamics. Because the design uses archival media-coverage data, the cognitive mechanisms attributed to journalists (pattern perception, narrative construction, attentional allocation) cannot be measured directly.
future_research: Replicate the theory with other forms of misconduct and other audiences (analysts, investors, peer firms) to test boundary conditions; examine alternative category schemes beyond industry; and use experiments or policy-capturing designs to directly test the cognitive microfoundations of journalists' attention allocation and narrative construction.
abstract: Despite the formidable consequences for firms of having their misconduct publicized— and thus scandalized—we know little about why only some misconduct instances become scandals beyond the idea that high-status firms' transgressions are scandalized more often. Focusing on the media's essential role in scandalizing misconduct, we take a media routines perspective to theorize how the status of past transgressors inside and outside the focal transgressor's industry creates different contexts that shape the likelihood of scandalization. We argue that the prevalence of past transgressions by high-status firms within the industry leads journalists to scrutinize the misconduct more, amplifying the effect of the focal firm's status by highlighting its commonalities with past transgressors. Conversely, the prevalence of transgressions by high-status firms outside the industry attenuates the firm status effect on scandalization by directing media attention outside the industry, limiting the information that can be inferred from firm status. Past transgressors' status and their categorical proximity to current transgressors serve as boundary conditions for the scandalizing effect of status. Our contribution lies in elucidating contextual factors that influence how status acts as an antecedent of scandals, and explaining how status and categories feed media routines that influence the likelihood of firm misconduct being scandalized.
apa_citation: Han, J.-H., Pollock, T. G., & Graffin, S. D. (2024). Now you see me: How status and categorical proximity shape misconduct scandalization. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(1), 208–231. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0365

title: When and How Artificial Intelligence Augments Employee Creativity
methods: Mixed methods. Study 1 is a randomized field experiment with a 2 (AI assistance vs. no AI assistance) x 2 (top vs. bottom agents) full-factorial design; 40 sales agents and 3,144 customers were doubly randomized across four groups; outcomes measured via voice recognition / text mining of recorded calls, with OLS regressions clustered at the agent level and causal mediation analysis with 1,000 bootstrap replications. Study 2 is an inductive grounded-theory analysis of 28 semi-structured interviews following the Gioia method.
industry: Telemarketing — outbound sales of credit cards in partnership with a major bank
country: Asia (an unnamed large telemarketing company; authors anonymize the country)
time_period: Calls placed during a single window on a Wednesday at the launch of a new credit-card business line; year not reported in paper
units: Customer-level analysis nested in sales agents; qualitative interviews at the agent level
n_sample: 3,144 customers and 40 sales agents in the field experiment; 28 sales agents in the follow-up semi-structured interviews
authors: Jia, Nan; Luo, Xueming; Fang, Zheng; Liao, Chengcheng
theories: AI–human collaboration (Puranam, 2021; Wilson & Daugherty, 2018); broaden-and-build theory of positive emotions (Fredrickson, 2004); componential theory of employee creativity (Amabile, 1996); conservation of resources (Hobfoll et al., 2000); job characteristic models / job design (Hackman et al., 1975; Elsbach & Hargadon, 2006); skill-biased technological change (Card & DiNardo, 2002)
keywords: AI-augmented employee creativity; AI–human collaboration; artificial intelligence; employee creativity; job design; sequential division of labor; skill-biased technology
research_question: Can AI technology increase the creativity of human employees — thus generating "AI-augmented employee creativity" — when AI and humans collaborate via a sequential division of labor, and under what conditions does this effect hold?
mechanism_summary: A sequential division of labor in which AI handles well-codified lead generation and humans handle higher-level sales persuasion changes the job design of employees by removing repetitive tasks while intensifying exposure to serious, challenging customers. For higher-skilled agents, conserved cognitive resources and increased task complexity translate into new scripts, better moods, higher morale, and a stronger sense of freedom that produce creative outcomes; for lower-skilled agents, the same change raises workload pressure and triggers negative emotions, so creative gains fail to materialize. Causal mediation analysis confirms that the creativity gain is the path through which AI assistance lifts customer purchases.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops the construct of "AI-augmented employee creativity" and identifies job skills as the critical scope condition, showing that the benefits of AI–human sequential division of labor are skill-biased rather than uniform. It bridges AI–human collaboration, job design, and employee-creativity literatures by demonstrating that AI's effect on creativity operates through both cognitive (resource conservation, increased complexity) and psychological (positive emotions, organizational support) channels, and that domain expertise is required to convert these channels into new scripts.
practical_implication: Organizations deploying AI to handle routine work should not assume creativity gains will accrue equally across the workforce — they accrue mainly to higher-skilled employees. To prevent a "double loss" for lower-skilled workers, managers should pair AI adoption with complementary investments such as extra training, knowledge-sharing incentives, and team-oriented culture so that lower-skilled employees can also benefit.
limitations: The field experiment is conducted at a single anonymous telemarketing firm in Asia, which constrains generalizability across industries and national contexts. The authors restricted the AI bot to common protocols rather than dynamic personalization, making the estimates conservative. The qualitative interviews capture employees' subjective accounts and cannot establish causality.
future_research: The authors call for studying more sophisticated forms of AI–human collaboration in which humans and AI directly use or modify each other's work, and for unpacking whether domain knowledge or psychology is the more critical lever for productive AI adoption. They also urge research on managerial and policy designs — training, incentives, culture — that curb the dark side of AI augmentation for lower-skilled employees.
abstract: Can artificial intelligence (AI) assist human employees in increasing employee creativity? Drawing on research on AI–human collaboration, job design, and employee creativity, we examine AI assistance in the form of a sequential division of labor within organizations: in a task, AI handles the initial portion, which is well-codified and repetitive, and employees focus on the subsequent portion, involving higher-level problem-solving. First, we provide causal evidence from a field experiment conducted at a telemarketing company. We find that AI assistance in generating sales leads, on average, increases employees' creativity in answering customers' questions during subsequent sales persuasion. Enhanced creativity leads to increased sales. However, this effect is much more pronounced for higher-skilled employees. Next, we conducted a qualitative study using semi-structured interviews with the employees. We found that AI assistance changes job design by intensifying employees' interactions with more serious customers. This change enables higher-skilled employees to generate innovative scripts and develop positive emotions at work, which are conducive to creativity. By contrast, with AI assistance, lower-skilled employees make limited improvements to scripts and experience negative emotions at work. We conclude that employees can achieve AI-augmented creativity, but this desirable outcome is skill-biased by favoring experts with greater job skills.
apa_citation: Jia, N., Luo, X., Fang, Z., & Liao, C. (2024). When and how artificial intelligence augments employee creativity. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(1), 5–32. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0426

title: Coevolutionary Lock-In in External Search
methods: Longitudinal panel analysis of crowd-period observations from a U.S. crowdsourcing software vendor; fixed effects regressions with crowd-level fixed effects and clustered standard errors predict log-transformed idea variety; idea (dis)similarity measured via bag-of-words cosine distance with NLTK/scikit-learn preprocessing, with Doc2Vec word-embedding robustness check; supplementary OLS and contributor-fixed-effects models test individual-level self-selection and self-adjustment mechanisms.
industry: Crowdsourcing initiatives (suggestion-box style) hosted on the websites of organizations using a U.S. cloud-based crowdsourcing software vendor
country: United States (software vendor; client organizations span the vendor's user base)
time_period: November 2007 to March 2018 (panel periods of eight weeks each)
units: Crowd-period (each crowd is an organization's open call for ideas)
n_sample: 1,218 organizations, 1,435 crowds, 1.44 million ideas from 1.07 million contributors, yielding 11,296 crowd-period observations
authors: Park, Sanghyun; Piezunka, Henning; Dahlander, Linus
theories: coevolutionary lock-in (Burgelman, 2002; Levinthal & Myatt, 1994); microfoundations of organization-individual interaction (Felin, Foss, & Ployhart, 2015); vicarious learning and signaling in open search (Piezunka & Dahlander, 2015, 2019)
keywords: coevolutionary lock-in; crowdsourcing; external search; idea variety; open innovation; selection consistency; self-adjustment; self-selection
research_question: How does an organization's consistency in selecting which crowdsourced ideas to implement shape the variety of ideas that external contributors subsequently submit, and what features of the external-contributor network moderate this coevolutionary process?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: crowd fixed effects, age of crowd, number of active contributors (t and t−1), idea length, degree of idea clustering, share of selected ideas, employee suggestions, number and length of organizational responses, number of votes, alignment between contributor votes and organizational selections

The mechanism is a positive feedback loop on the crowd: when an organization repeatedly selects similar ideas, external contributors infer a narrow taste from that signal, and adapt either by exiting (self-selection) or by submitting closer-to-selected ideas (self-adjustment). The pool of ideas the organization next sees therefore narrows in content variety, which in turn constrains future selection and reinforces the cycle. The effect is stronger when contributors have been exposed to (and socialized into) the signal — i.e., low churn and high network cohesion — and weaker when newcomers arrive or established contributors leave.
theoretical_contribution: The paper recasts external search as a two-way street: organizations not only absorb ideas from externals but also shape, through their selection signals, the very pool of ideas they will later receive. It identifies a coevolutionary lock-in mechanism that explains why open and crowdsourced search — typically framed as broadening — can over time converge to the same narrow solution space as internal search. By specifying self-selection and self-adjustment as the individual-level micro-foundations of this crowd-level convergence and by tying their strength to structural network properties (churn, cohesion), the study bridges micro and macro accounts of how organizations and external contributors jointly produce the information environment for organizational learning.
practical_implication: Managers who want sustained idea variety should treat selection policy as a deliberate design lever rather than a neutral filter. Introducing some ambiguity or noise into selection (less perfectly consistent choices) preserves the perceived openness of the search and keeps contributors exploring broadly; concentrating selection accelerates fit but narrows the solution space, possibly irreversibly. Managers can also dampen lock-in by limiting visibility or interactions among external contributors when broad search is the goal, and should expect the fit-versus-variety trade-off to favor consistency in static environments and ambiguity in dynamic or breakthrough-seeking contexts.
limitations: The evidence is correlational and may be subject to time-varying unobserved confounders despite crowd fixed effects. The individual-level analysis is restricted to contributors who submitted at least one idea, so it likely underestimates the true magnitude of self-selection by missing observers who never submit. The data also do not permit assessment of the performance consequences of coevolutionary lock-in, leaving the welfare implications of the fit-variety trade-off open.
future_research: Field or lab experiments that manipulate selection policy could establish causal effects of selection consistency on idea variety. The authors call for studies of whether the coevolutionary process emerges across other forms of external search — alliances, hackathons, open innovation, informal networks — where contributors' costs of nonselection and incentives to align differ from crowdsourcing. Further work could also examine how performance feedback, payoff asymmetries, and contributor incentives shape the strength and reversibility of lock-in.
abstract: While external search allows organizations to source diverse ideas from people outside the organization, it often generates a narrow set of nondiverse ideas. We theorize that this stems from an interplay between organizations' idea selection and the external generation of ideas: an organization selects ideas shared by external contributors, and the external contributors, who strive to see their ideas selected, use the prior selection to infer what kind of ideas the organization is looking for, and to respond. Contributors whose ideas are misaligned with the organization's selection tend to stop submitting ideas (i.e., self-selection) or adjust the ideas they submit so that they correspond (i.e., self-adjustment), resulting in a less diverse pool of ideas. Our central hypothesis is that the more consistent organizations are in their selection, the stronger the coevolutionary lock-in: organizations with greater selection consistency receive future ideas with lower content variety. We find support for these predictions by combining large-scale network analysis and natural language processing across a large number of organizations that use crowdsourcing. Our findings suggest a reconceptualization of external search as a two-way street: organizations are not simply passive receivers of ideas but send signals that shape the pool of ideas that externals share.
apa_citation: Park, S., Piezunka, H., & Dahlander, L. (2024). Coevolutionary lock-in in external search. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(1), 262–288. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0710
