title: Intra-Individual Conflict and Task Performance in a Multiteam Context: Examining the Structural Elements of Conflict Experience
methods: Mixed-methods empirical design with two studies. Study 1: observational behavioral coding of six live-actor mass-casualty response training exercises with 13 paramedic students, yielding 3,036 15-second temporal units cross-nested in 110 patients and 6 scenarios; analyzed with three-level hierarchical cross-classified mixed models (lme4 R package). Study 2: online mixed-factorial scenario-based vignette experiment with 400 Qualtrics-recruited US workers (2 directions x 2 boundaries; sending vs. receiving between-subject, within- vs. between-team within-subject); analyzed with random coefficient models (nlme R package) and bootstrapped mediation.
industry: Study 1: emergency medical services (paramedic mass-casualty response training); Study 2: working adults across mixed industries (e.g., manufacturing, construction, retail)
time_period: Study 1 data are from mass-casualty exercise event code 1703 (a single day); Study 2 online experiment (date not explicitly reported)
units: Study 1: 3,036 15-second temporal patient-care units cross-nested in 110 patients, 13 paramedics, and 6 scenarios; Study 2: 800 within-subject observations from 400 individual participants
n_sample: Study 1: 3,036 temporal units (13 paramedics; 110 patients; 6 scenarios); Study 2: 400 participants (800 observations)
authors: Park, Semin; Luciano, Margaret M.; Mathieu, John E.; Fenters, Virgil W.
theories: cognitive resource allocation theory (Hirst & Kalmar, 1987; Kanfer & Ackerman, 1989); communication / sender-receiver model (Gerbner, 1956; Lunenberg, 2010; Weingart et al., 2015); intra-individual conflict cognitive process framework (this paper); multiteam systems theory (Luciano, DeChurch, & Mathieu, 2018; Mathieu et al., 2017); self-regulatory process of attentional focus (Beal, Weiss, Barros, & MacDermid, 2005; Kahneman, 1973)
keywords: conflict experience; directions of conflict (sending vs. receiving); intra-individual conflict; microfoundations; multiteam systems; off-task attentional demands; on-task attentional pull; self-regulatory process of attentional focus; structural elements of conflict; task engagement; task performance; team boundaries (within vs. between team)
research_question: How and why do the structural elements of an individual's conflict experiences—the direction of conflict (sending vs. receiving) and the team boundary in which it occurs (within team vs. between team)—shape that individual's task performance in a multiteam context, and what self-regulatory attentional-focus mechanisms account for these differential effects?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls/covariates (Study 1): scenario time, team time, scenario type, team membership dummies, team size, task difficulty, patient priority, cumulative individual conflict (sending and receiving), and collective intra-team and inter-team conflict.

The dual-path attentional-focus mechanism is as follows. The direction of conflict governs which self-regulatory path is engaged: sending conflict mobilizes on-task attentional pull (a sense of control that re-anchors attention on the focal task), while receiving conflict raises off-task attentional demands (a competing pull to address the conflict issue). Team boundaries then modulate the magnitudes of these paths because in-group conflict is perceived as more legitimate and high-priority — strengthening senders' on-task pull within-team but also amplifying receivers' off-task demands within-team. Consequently, sending within-team and receiving between-team conflict are predicted to benefit task performance, whereas receiving within-team conflict is predicted to harm it.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances a novel intra-individual conflict cognitive process framework that resolves long-standing mixed findings in the conflict literature by aligning the level of theory, phenomenon, and method at the intra-individual level. By introducing two structural elements of conflict experiences—directions (sending vs. receiving) and boundaries (within team vs. between team)—and showing that they trigger distinct self-regulatory attentional pathways (on-task pull for senders; off-task demands for receivers), the framework explains when and why conflict helps or hurts individual performance and elevates conflict as a substantive concern in multiteam-systems theory.
practical_implication: Managers and team members should "pick their battles": even helpful conflict for the sender (sending within-team) can disrupt teammates' task engagement by raising their off-task attentional demands, so senders should clarify the urgency of the issue to allow receivers to manage their attention. Repeated targeting of a single individual (cumulative receiving) is particularly damaging to performance and should be monitored. In multiteam settings, leaders should distinguish within- and between-team conflict because the same behavior carries different performance consequences across these boundaries.
limitations: The Study 1 sample, although behaviorally rich (3,036 15-second units), was accumulated over the course of a single day with 13 paramedic students in low-diversity, physically separated component teams, limiting generalizability across longer time frames, organizational contexts, and MTS configurations. The studies do not directly compare behavioral conflict with perceptual measures, leaving the link between conflict behaviors and summary judgments unexamined. Study 2's online vignette could not fully reproduce the unique information gains that may explain the positive receiving between-team effect observed in Study 1.
future_research: Future work should extend the sampling window to weeks or years to assess short- and long-term effects of cumulative conflict (e.g., normalization, toxic dyads), and broaden the structural-elements lens to include emotions, conflict tactics, and content-specificity manipulations. Researchers should also examine boundary conditions tied to individual differences (e.g., agreeableness, conscientiousness), team conflict norms, component team distance, task importance, and levels of inter-team interdependence, ideally with designs that compare behavioral and perceptual conflict and that test the framework in MTS configurations with greater geographic, cultural, and functional diversity.
abstract: In this article, we advance a novel intra-individual conflict cognitive process framework, highlighting two structural elements of conflict experiences: (1) directions (sending vs. receiving) and (2) team boundaries (within team vs. between team). Integrating theorizing on cognitive resource allocation, we explain how and why the effect of individuals' conflict experiences on their task performances hinges upon their self-regulation of attention processes guided by the structural elements of conflict experiences. In Study 1, using data extracted from continuous audio and video streams of paramedics participating in live-actor mass-casualty response exercises, we found that sending within-team conflict and receiving between-team conflict related positively with task performance, whereas receiving within-team conflict related negatively to task performance. In Study 2, we conducted a mixed-design scenario-based experiment to examine the underlying mechanisms that drive such effects. Our findings reveal that on-task attentional pull was an important mechanism for a positive sending within-team conflict–task engagement relationship, whereas off-task attentional demands were driving the negative receiving within-team conflict–task engagement relationship. Collectively, we demonstrate that examining the intra-individual microfoundations of conflict in a multiteam context yields unique insights and enables building new theory on when and why conflict is beneficial or harmful.
apa_citation: Park, S., Luciano, M. M., Mathieu, J. E., & Fenters, V. W. (2024). Intra-individual conflict and task performance in a multiteam context: Examining the structural elements of conflict experience. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(1), 33–60. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0285

title: Coherence within and across Categories: The Dynamic Viability of Product Categories on Kickstarter
methods: Large-scale longitudinal panel of category-days using language-based measures derived from a 750-topic structural topic model (STM) over the Story sections of 425,525 Kickstarter campaigns; generalized least squares (GLS) regression with quadratic and interaction terms, plus robustness checks (negative binomial, hurdle, sensitivity, statistical-power resampling).
industry: Crowdfunding platform (Kickstarter) spanning 15 superordinate sets of categories (art, comics, crafts, dance, design, fashion, film and video, food, games, journalism, music, photography, publishing, technology, theater) regrouping 170 product categories
country: United States–based crowdfunding platform with global campaigns (English-language content)
units: Category-day observations (170 product categories observed on a daily basis)
n_sample: 428,873 category-day observations covering 170 categories and 425,525 campaigns
authors: Soublière, J.-F.; Lo, J. Y.; Rhee, E. Y.
theories: category viability and dynamics (Lo, Fiss, Rhee, & Kennedy, 2020; Wry, Lounsbury, & Glynn, 2011; Grodal, 2018); cultural entrepreneurship (Lounsbury & Glynn, 2001, 2019; Soublière & Lockwood, 2022); optimal distinctiveness (Deephouse, 1999; Zhao, Fisher, Lounsbury, & Miller, 2017)
keywords: Kickstarter; category viability; crowdfunding; cultural entrepreneurship; distinctiveness; heterogeneity; inter-category dynamics; intra-category dynamics; product categories; topic modeling
research_question: How does the viability of a product category shift over time, and specifically, how do intra-category heterogeneity and inter-category distinctiveness — both of which are dynamically reshaped by actors' product-positioning efforts — jointly determine whether a category remains in active use?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Category-level (past performance, established audience base), category-set-level (set fixed effects, number of related categories, external Google Trends interest), and platform-level (Kickstarter popularity, seasonal cycles, post-emergence dummy, post-algorithm-change dummy, potato-salad-incident dummy).

The mechanism rests on two competing benefits that categories provide: classification benefits (low within-category heterogeneity helps actors and audiences group comparable products together) and valuation benefits (greater within-category variety lets actors carve out distinct positions and meet diverse audience preferences). When a focal category is not very distinctive from its neighbors, classification benefits dominate at the extremes and valuation benefits dominate in the middle, yielding the canonical inverted-U. As the focal category becomes more distinctive, classification benefits weaken (the label already signals separation), valuation benefits at the extremes strengthen, and the curve flattens and then flips to a U-shape — in which both very tight and very loose categories outperform middling ones.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reconciles competing strands in the category-dynamics literature (life-cycle vs. fluid views; negative, positive, and inverted-U findings on heterogeneity) by jointly modeling intra-category heterogeneity and inter-category distinctiveness, showing that the heterogeneity–viability relationship is not a single fixed shape but a family of shapes contingent on distinctiveness. It extends Lo et al. (2020) by formalizing the classification/valuation trade-off and demonstrating that what appears "optimal" is dynamically accomplished and may even invert when categories become highly distinctive. It also advances cultural entrepreneurship research by shifting attention from individual product symbolic actions to how those actions, taken together across a classification system, shape the viability of entire categories.
practical_implication: For platform designers and category curators on crowdfunding sites, the results imply that "healthy" levels of within-category variety are not absolute but should be evaluated against how distinctive the category already is from its neighbors. Entrepreneurs choosing where to position their campaigns should recognize that introducing products that increase within-category heterogeneity has different implications depending on the focal category's relation to adjacent categories — sometimes broadening the category helps viability, sometimes sharpening it does.
limitations: The empirical setting is bounded to relatively simple classification systems (Kickstarter's 15 superordinate sets containing 170 categories), and more complex nested systems (e.g., Québec terroir, B Corp) may exhibit different dynamics. The language-based measures capture symbolic positioning but not material aspects of products or the evolution of individual entrepreneurs' claims. Operationalizing viability via the count of new entries is appropriate to crowdfunding but is an imperfect proxy for viability more broadly (which may also reflect popularity, attractiveness, or demand).
future_research: Future studies could test the theorization in more complex classification systems and in non-crowdfunding contexts, using longitudinal or experimental designs to capture actors' propensity to claim or forgo category membership. Other promising avenues include unpacking when specific cultural resources contribute primarily to classification vs. valuation benefits, examining the codes that encourage or discourage variety, integrating material and symbolic dimensions of products, and exploring richer forms of inter-category relatedness beyond "A is not B" (e.g., A as B, A with B, A for B, A against B).
abstract: How does the viability of a product category shift over time? Studies abound on how categories emerge and become established, or fall out of use. Yet, extant research has often examined the evolution of categories one at a time, leaving open the question of how related categories affect a focal category's viability. In contrast, we consider both intra- and inter-category dynamics. Viewing categories as continuously shaped by actors' efforts to position their products, we argue that these efforts alter the coherence that products exhibit not only within a category (a category's heterogeneity), but also across related categories (a category's distinctiveness). We theorize how the interaction between a category's heterogeneity and distinctiveness shapes its subsequent viability. When a focal category's distinctiveness is low, the heterogeneity–viability relationship takes an inverted U-shape. However, as distinctiveness grows, the relationship flattens and eventually flips to a U-shape. We explain this by considering the trade-off between the "classification" and "valuation" benefits that a category affords. We find support for our argument by tracking 170 categories over an 11-year period on Kickstarter, one of the largest crowdfunding platforms. By providing a nuanced understanding of category dynamics, we shed new light onto the fluctuating viability of categories.
apa_citation: Soublière, J.-F., Lo, J. Y., & Rhee, E. Y. (2024). Coherence within and across categories: The dynamic viability of product categories on Kickstarter. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(1), 61–91. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0105

title: How Much Inequity Do You See? Structural Power, Perceptions of Gender and Racial Inequity, and Support for Diversity Initiatives
methods: Ten studies combining seven archival surveys of U.S. federal employees (Studies 1a-1g) analyzed via OLS regressions with organization fixed effects and a Schmidt-Hunter random-effects meta-analysis, two MTurk/Prolific surveys testing organizational identification as mediator (Studies 2a, 2b), one community-sample survey of working adults with serial mediation analysis (Study 3), and one preregistered Prolific experiment manipulating deliberate processing of workplace inequity (Study 4).
industry: U.S. federal government agencies (Studies 1a-1g; 24+ agencies including DoD, NASA, Department of Education) and U.S. full-time working adults across industries (Studies 2a-4)
time_period: Archival surveys 1992-2016; primary data collected during the COVID-19 pandemic
units: Individual employees (supervisors and non-supervisors)
n_sample: 66,523 federal employees across Studies 1a-1g; 947 (Study 2a), 761 (Study 2b), 381 (Study 3), and 726 (Study 4) working adults
authors: To, C.; Sherf, E. N.; Kouchaki, M.
theories: dark side of organizational identification (Caprar et al., 2022; Conroy, Henle, Shore, & Stelman, 2017); organizational identification (Ashforth & Mael, 1989; Caprar, Walker, & Ashforth, 2022); structural power theory (Tost, 2015; Tost & Johnson, 2019)
keywords: diversity initiatives; diversity, equity, and inclusion; gender inequity; managers; organizational identification; perceptions of inequity; racial inequity; structural power
research_question: Why do managers—who occupy positions of structural power on which the success of diversity initiatives depends—often fail to support those initiatives, and under what conditions can their resistance be overcome?
mechanism_summary: Structural power induces a heightened sense of organizational identification because controlling others' resources and outcomes makes the role central to self-concept; identification then motivates managers to view their organization positively, including as more equitable than it is. This identification-induced "motivated blindness" suppresses perceptions of workplace inequity—a precursor that, when absent, removes the impetus to support diversity initiatives. Asking managers to deliberately recall concrete inequity in their own workplace disrupts the heuristic positive view, restores perceptions of inequity, and eliminates the negative association between structural power and support for diversity initiatives.
theoretical_contribution: The paper shifts the explanation of managerial resistance to diversity initiatives away from demographics or ideology toward the structural-power position itself, identifying organizational identification as the proximal psychological barrier. It contributes to structural-power theory by showing that prosocial power effects can fail to emerge not because the powerful are unwilling to act but because they fail to perceive the problem, and contributes to the "dark side" of organizational identification literature by demonstrating a perception-blunting mechanism distinct from the deliberate-rule-bending mechanism previously documented.
practical_implication: Awareness campaigns about societal inequity are insufficient because managers exhibit a "not here" bias: organizations should require managers to deliberately engage with specific examples of inequity within their own workplace, ideally via anonymous reporting channels or aggregate equity dashboards. Reframing what counts as a "good" organization—one that actively acknowledges and combats inequity rather than denies its presence—can realign manager identification with diversity goals.
limitations: The diversity-support measure forces allocation among competing taskforces, which may differ from attitudinal Likert measures and may understate support; primary data were collected during COVID-19 remote work, which could confound identification dynamics either way. The authors do not experimentally manipulate the structural-power-to-identification link, so causal ordering between power and identification cannot be definitively established. Common method variance is a concern in the survey studies despite supplementary analyses.
future_research: Future work could manipulate structural power directly to test causal order with identification, examine whether the "not here" bias is stronger among those who normatively value equity (e.g., political liberals), and explore how remote work, organizational diversity composition, and time/historical context moderate the structural-power-to-inequity-perception link. Researchers could also disentangle whether different forms of discrimination (e.g., stereotype-based, evaluation-based) produce distinct perception patterns among power holders.
abstract: The success of diversity initiatives depends on the support of those in positions of structural power—that is, managers. However, managers often resist such initiatives. Existing academic and practitioner conversations point to managers' demographics or ideology as the source of resistance to diversity initiatives. We propose that such resistance may originate from the managerial position itself. We argue that positions of structural power induce organizational identification, which motivates a view that one's workplace is equitable. Seven archival studies of government employees (Studies 1a–1 g) and three surveys of working adults (Studies 2a, 2b, and 3) suggest those higher in structural power perceive less inequity in their workplace due to higher organizational identification, resulting in lower support for diversity initiatives. Asking managers to deliberate on inequities within their own organization, thus challenging their default positive view, helps managers overcome their identification barrier (Study 4). This research helps shift the foci of resistance toward diversity initiatives from demographics or ideology to one's managerial position and provides practical implications for organizations seeking to support their diversity initiatives.
apa_citation: To, C., Sherf, E. N., & Kouchaki, M. (2024). How much inequity do you see? Structural power, perceptions of gender and racial inequity, and support for diversity initiatives. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(1), 126–149. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0253

title: Left Behind? Understanding the Career Consequences of Collaborator Exits
methods: Discrete-time event history analysis using binary logistic regression with maximum likelihood estimation, on employee-month panel data; competing-risks framework treating organizational exit and vacancy-driven promotion as censored; supplementary employee-dyad-level matched-sample logistic regressions; mediation via Hayes's PROCESS macro with bootstrapped standard errors.
industry: Not-for-profit scientific research organization (quasi-academic research institute)
units: Employee-months for scientific and technical staff in research roles
n_sample: 42,406 employee-months across 1,326 employees
authors: Anderson, T.
theories: career interdependence (Barnett & Miner, 1992); social learning theory (Bandura, 1977); vacancy chains theory (White, 1970)
keywords: career interdependence; collaboration structure; collaborator exits; competency-driven promotion; knowledge workers; vacancy chains
research_question: How do collaborator exits—as opposed to noncollaborator exits—shape the competency-driven promotion of remaining employees in knowledge-intensive organizations, and to what extent does this depend on the relative job grade of the exiting collaborators?
mechanism_summary: When collaborators leave, they vacate "relational vacancies" in the collaboration structure that connected employees can fill by forming new collaborative ties—particularly with colleagues who shared the same departing collaborators. These new ties expose remaining employees to different human capital and stretch projects, accelerating learning and human-capital development that managers recognize through competency-driven promotion. The effect is strongest when the exiting collaborators were in higher job grades (offering richer learning) and when the remaining employee is early in their career, when there is more competency still to be developed.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends career interdependence theory by applying the vacancy-chain perspective to relational structures (the collaboration network) rather than only the job structure, showing that colleague exits create not just job vacancies but multiple relational vacancies with implications for competency-driven—not just vacancy-driven—promotion. It also broadens the literature on collaborator loss beyond star deaths, highlighting positive learning consequences of routine collaborator exits in a bounded organizational setting.
practical_implication: Managers should map collaboration structures to anticipate which employees will be affected by exits and where new collaboration opportunities will arise, and may need to actively facilitate the formation of new collaborative ties—possibly through "collaborator rotation" programs analogous to job rotation—because seminars and infrastructure alone may be insufficient to dislodge employees from over-long collaborative relationships that limit learning.
limitations: Results are correlational rather than causal, and use of journal coauthorship to identify collaborations means non-publishing collaborations go undetected, restricting findings to "successful" research collaborations. Focus on a single organizationally bounded research institute limits generalizability to settings where collaborations span organizational boundaries (e.g., academia) or where competency-driven promotion is uncommon.
future_research: Future work should examine longer collaboration vacancy chains as decaying ties redirect attention to newer collaborators, factors that facilitate or hinder vacancy chains in collaboration structures (e.g., scarcity, external hiring, prior network size), and whether the same dynamics apply to friendship and advice networks or to non-research collaborations such as project-based work in professional service firms.
abstract: Prior research on career interdependence has focused on how colleague exits shape employees' intraorganizational careers through the creation of job vacancies and vacancy-driven promotion. In this paper, I propose that colleague exits can shape employees' careers through the creation of valuable relational "vacancies." Focusing on employees engaged in collaborative research, and drawing on the vacancy chains literature, I argue that colleague exits create new collaboration opportunities for remaining employees that can enhance learning and facilitate competency-driven promotion. Yet, employees will benefit only when these opportunities arise as a result of their own collaborators leaving—that is, employees must lose to gain. Using longitudinal data on employees within a single research organization, I show that collaborator exits increase the likelihood of employees' competency-driven promotion in a way that noncollaborator exits do not. Furthermore, it is the exit of higher-level collaborators that is most beneficial. These findings highlight a different type of career interdependence and the role of collaborator mobility in shaping the intraorganizational careers of knowledge workers.
apa_citation: Anderson, T. (2024). Left behind? Understanding the career consequences of collaborator exits. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(2), 526–553. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0623

title: Public–Private and Private–Private Collaboration as Pathways for Socially Beneficial Innovation: Evidence from Antimicrobial Drug-Development Tasks
methods: Quantitative analysis of 7,388 drug–indication–organization–country–phase pentads (2,496 unique antimicrobial drugs) from the Cortellis database covering 1995–2019, with 736 collaborative drug-development tasks; inverse probability weighting regression adjustment (IPWRA) with multinomial logistic treatment model and logistic (effectiveness) or Poisson (efficiency) outcome models to estimate ATET while addressing endogenous selection into collaboration; robustness checks via IPW and coarsened exact matching (CEM); supplementary patenting analysis; complemented by qualitative insights from six semi-structured interviews with three industry experts (December 2020–February 2023) and text analysis of 176 collaborative drug-development contracts
industry: Antimicrobial drug development (pharmaceutical sector—drugs targeting bacterial, viral, parasitic, and fungal pathogens)
country: 109 countries (39% United States, 8.9% China, remainder scattered globally)
time_period: Drug-development tasks initiated 1995–2019, with progress observed through July 2022
units: Drug–indication–organization–country–phase pentads representing specific drug-development tasks; partner organizations classified as for-profit firms ('private') or public-mission entities ('public': governmental institutions, academic institutions, other nonprofits)
n_sample: 7,388 pentads (2,496 unique drugs; 3,071 unique drug–indication pairs); 736 collaborative tasks of which 367 are public–private and 369 are private–private; supplemented by 6 expert interviews and 176 contracts
authors: Arslan, Birgul; Vasudeva, Gurneeta; Hirsch, Elizabeth B.
theories: collaboration risk comprising cooperation and coordination problems (Das & Teng, 1998; Gulati, Wohlgezogen, & Zhelyazkov, 2012); innovation collaboration literature (Bruce et al., 2019; Madhok, 2002; Williamson, 1991); market failure and public R&D (Cockburn & Henderson, 1998; Mahoney, McGahan, & Pitelis, 2009; Stiglitz & Wallsten, 1999); transaction cost economics and value appropriation (Teece, 1986; Williamson, 1991)
keywords: antimicrobial drug development; collaboration risk; cooperation problems; coordination problems; grand challenges; private–private collaboration; public–private collaboration; socially beneficial innovation; task effectiveness; task efficiency; technological uncertainty
research_question: Whether and when public–private collaboration contributes to the success of socially beneficial innovation tasks, relative not only to firms' solo efforts but also to collaboration involving only private-sector actors, and how cooperation and coordination mechanisms drive any performance differences.
mechanism_summary:
- Theoretical mechanisms (collaboration risk): cooperation problems from relational risk (misaligned social vs. economic incentives, weaker governance provisions in public–private contracts) and coordination problems from operational risk (differences in organizational structures, processes, and special government regulations such as the Buy American Act)
- Estimation: IPWRA addressing endogenous partner selection; ATET estimates compare collaborative outcomes to a synthetic counterfactual of solo development with similar covariate distribution

The central mechanism is that collaboration risk—comprising cooperation problems from relational risk and coordination problems from operational risk—is higher in public–private than in private–private collaboration because public partners pursue social value/knowledge dissemination goals while private firms pursue commercial returns, and public–private contracts lack governance provisions (development milestones, joint patenting, joint steering committees, termination clauses) that private–private contracts use to mitigate these tensions. Heightened cooperation problems primarily depress effectiveness (whether a task moves to the next stage), while coordination problems primarily depress efficiency (time to progress). Technological uncertainty attenuates this gap: in the discovery stage and for novel drug mechanisms, partners' knowledge-creation interests are more aligned and there is greater tolerance for trial-and-error, so public–private collaboration performs comparably to private–private collaboration; in clinical/commercialization stages and for established mechanisms, incentive misalignment and coordination demands amplify the gap.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances the innovation collaboration literature in three primary ways: it offers an explicit counterfactual comparison between public–private and private–private collaboration that prior public–private work typically lacks; it disentangles cooperation and coordination mechanisms—often examined in separate literature streams—by simultaneously modeling task effectiveness and task efficiency outcomes; and it incorporates the contingent role of technological uncertainty (drug-development stage and technological novelty) and uses "holistic triangulation" combining IPWRA estimation with qualitative interview and contractual evidence to elaborate the relational and operational processes underpinning collaboration risk in public–private versus private–private innovation tasks for societal grand challenges.
practical_implication: Public–private collaboration absorbs roughly USD 660 million in additional clinical-phase investment to advance one drug to the next stage relative to private–private collaboration; managers can reduce this performance gap by drafting public–private contracts that include governance provisions presently rare in such arrangements—development milestones (only 22% of public–private vs. 71% of private–private contracts include them), joint patenting, joint steering committees with equal representation, and conflict-resolution procedures—so that cooperation and coordination problems are mitigated even where public-disclosure and global-access requirements remain.
limitations: The cooperation and coordination mechanisms—incentive alignment, governance structures, and operational processes—cannot be observed directly and are inferred from task-level outcomes supplemented by interviews and contractual text analysis. The study examines a single industry (antimicrobial drug development) and does not capture variation across cross-national institutional settings or across different types of public agencies within the same national innovation system. The dyadic-factor models incorporating complementarity, previous ties, and collaboration duration have fewer observations and findings should be interpreted with caution.
future_research: Future studies could examine how cross-national institutional settings and characteristics of public agencies (e.g., centralized vs. decentralized structures such as DARPA's flat organization) affect the alignment of public- and private-sector innovation goals; investigate collaboration outcomes with different types of public partners (governmental, academic, and nonprofit organizations) given supplementary findings that academic partners excel in discovery while nonprofits perform well in clinical stages; and further disentangle the factors leading to task-level cooperation versus coordination problems, including the role of nonprofit organizations in addressing societal grand challenges.
abstract: Although public–private collaboration abounds in addressing societal grand challenges, little is known about its performance relative to private-sector collaboration. Drawing from the innovation collaboration literature, and qualitative insights from interviews and contracts, we argue that collaboration risk impairs task performance of public– private versus private–private collaboration. We attribute this to two collaboration risk mechanisms: cooperation problems arising from misaligned social versus economic incentives, and coordination problems due to differences in organizational governance and processes. We test our hypotheses using the development history of 2,496 antimicrobial drugs from 1995–2019. After accounting for endogenous selection into collaboration, we find that public–private collaboration is less effective than private–private collaboration. However, this performance gap reduces for innovation tasks under high technological uncertainty (i.e., in the discovery stage and for developing new drug action mechanisms). To probe the distinctive cooperation and coordination mechanisms, we also examine innovation task efficiency. Findings reveal a wider gap in task effectiveness but not efficiency between public–private and private–private collaboration, suggesting more significant cooperation than coordination problems. Our theoretical and practical insights pertain to whether, when, and how public–private and private–private collaboration offer viable pathways for socially beneficial innovation tasks.
apa_citation: Arslan, B., Vasudeva, G., & Hirsch, E. B. (2024). Public–private and private–private collaboration as pathways for socially beneficial innovation: Evidence from antimicrobial drug-development tasks. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(2), 554-582. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1260

title: Mobilizing New Sources of Data: Opportunities and Recommendations
methods: Editorial: descriptive review of AMJ publication trends 2017-2023 plus recommendations for authors mobilizing new data sources.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: AMJ published articles
n_sample: 531 articles published in AMJ from 2017 through 2023
authors: Grégoire, Denis A.; Ter Wal, Anne L. J.; Little, Laura M.; Bermiss, Sekou; Kotha, Reddi; Gruber, Marc
theories: Not reported in paper
keywords: big data; data context; data transparency; digital traces; machine learning; new data sources; research methods; theory-method alignment
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: New data sources (wearables, sensors, digital traces, big data, AI/ML outputs) offer untapped opportunities for management research, but mobilizing them requires deliberate attention to context, transparency, and theory–method alignment.
- Key constructs: New data sources; new methods of analysis; data context; data transparency and ethics; theory–method alignment.
- Recommendation #1 — Take data context seriously: Provide rich detail on how, when, where, and why data were compiled; flag representativeness limits and recording artefacts.
- Recommendation #2 — Take data transparency and ethical considerations seriously: Be transparent about access, consent, training data, and trade-offs between causal precision and participant harm.
- Recommendation #3 — Mind the alignment of theory and method: Establish that empirical measures (including ML-derived topics or features) validly map onto theorized constructs.
- Boundary conditions: Recommendations apply across micro and macro, qualitative and quantitative submissions; intensity of concern depends on the type of new data being used.

The editorial argues that the digitization of work and society, the falling cost of field experiments, and the proliferation of digital footprints and AI/blockchain platforms create three converging trends that expand the data palette available to management scholars. Yet "opportunities for mobilizing and interpreting these new data are arising faster than guidance about best practices can be developed," raising the risk that apparent advances rest on "feet of clay." The editors therefore urge authors to make a compelling case for how new data advance theory and to address three recurring failure modes — insufficient data-context description, weak transparency/ethics around access and ML training inputs, and loose alignment between algorithmically derived measures and theorized constructs.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial does not develop a new theory but stakes out an editorial position for AMJ on how to evaluate and improve manuscripts that mobilize novel data sources. It contributes a tripartite framework (data context, transparency/ethics, theory–method alignment) that authors and reviewers can use as evaluative criteria, and it documents the relatively stable distribution of data sources in AMJ from 2017–2023 (43% archival, 21% survey, 18% interview, 14% experimental) as a baseline against which the editors argue substantial untapped potential exists for new-data submissions.
practical_implication: Authors submitting to AMJ are advised to: (1) provide rich descriptive accounts of where data come from, who compiled them, what is captured vs. omitted, and how recording may bias behavior; (2) preregister where feasible, document data-access agreements and ethical review, disclose ML training data and prompt inputs, and weigh experimental control against participant harm; and (3) demonstrate that algorithmic outputs (e.g., LDA topics, feature importance scores) validly represent the theoretical constructs of interest, including via bootstrapping, randomization tests, and sensitivity checks.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editors invite authors to embrace new data sources — wearables and mobile sensing, video and immersive-reality data, GPS and SafeGraph traces, blockchain timestamps, social-media and email corpora, generative-AI experimental conditions, and ML-analyzable unstructured text from filings, patents, and legal documents — and to pair them with new analytical techniques where relevant, supported by AMJ's expanded methodological consulting panel and its broadened editorial methods portfolio.
abstract: FROM THE EDITORS MOBILIZING NEW SOURCES OF DATA: OPPORTUNITIES AND RECOMMENDATIONS
apa_citation: Grégoire, D. A., Ter Wal, A. L. J., Little, L. M., Bermiss, S., Kotha, R., & Gruber, M. (2024). Mobilizing new sources of data: Opportunities and recommendations. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(2), 289–298. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2024.4002

title: How Founder Human Capital and Founding Conditions Shape New Firm Performance: A Study of Necessity Entrepreneurship during Times of Economic Crisis
methods: OLS regression with robust (Huber-White) standard errors clustered by firm; event-history (firm-year) panel analysis combining founder survey data with secondary industry-level data on yearly revenues; tests of three hypotheses including interactions between human capital type and favorable/unfavorable industry conditions; robustness checks using education-only measures and industry-volatility moderation.
industry: 15 NACE Rev. 2 industries including commerce, professional activities, accommodation/food service, manufacturing, construction, health/social work, and other 'mundane' sectors
time_period: Firms founded in 2008 or 2009 (during the Great Recession); survey administered in 2013; up to three years of revenue data per firm
units: Firm-years (necessity entrepreneurs who transitioned from unemployment to self-employment via a Greek government program)
n_sample: 500 founders contributing 1,463 firm-year observations
authors: Gruber, M.; Dencker, J. C.; Nikiforou, A.
theories: Lazear's balanced skills theory (Lazear, 2005); human capital theory (Becker, 1964; Mincer, 1974); liabilities of newness (Stinchcombe, 1965)
keywords: balanced skills theory; economic crisis; founding conditions; general human capital; human capital; industry conditions; necessity entrepreneurship; new firm performance; specific human capital
research_question: How do founders' general versus specific human capital endowments affect the performance of newly launched firms during an economic crisis, and how does this relationship vary across favorable and unfavorable industry founding conditions?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Founder demographics (gender, age, marital status, children), years of education, years of industry-specific experience, entrepreneurial experience, managerial experience, long-term unemployment, self-efficacy, initial investment, partner, target market/customers, alternatives considered, mainland location, industry fixed effects, firm duration

The mechanism reconciles competing predictions from human capital theory. On average, general human capital wins because conceptual breadth, abstract problem-solving, and a wider knowledge repertoire help founders navigate the high uncertainty, ambiguity, and rapidly shifting demand of a crisis—Lazear's "jack-of-all-trades" advantage. But this average masks contingency: in favorable industries, where crisis features are attenuated, specialists' ready-to-use skills, deeper supplier/distributor networks, and customer trust as domain experts let them outperform generalists. In the most unfavorable (sharply declining) industries, the authors predicted generalists would dominate—yet specialists again outperform, apparently because their narrow focus and pattern recognition let them act faster under extreme time pressure even when adaptation is needed. Resilience, the authors conclude, is also a matter of "entrepreneurial luck"—being the right person (with the right human capital) at the right place (industry) at the right time.
theoretical_contribution: The paper resolves a long-standing specialist–generalist debate in entrepreneurship by showing that the value of general versus specific human capital is contingent on industry founding conditions during a crisis, rather than uniformly favoring one type. It urges scholars to move beyond treating economic crises as homogeneous, economy-wide shocks and to instead theorize crises at a granular industry level. It also reframes "resilience" in entrepreneurship as a person–context match contingent on luck, extending necessity-entrepreneurship theory and human capital theory (Becker, 1964; Mincer, 1974; Lazear, 2005).
practical_implication: Policy-makers should expand support for unemployed individuals possessing general human capital who wish to start firms, since current government programs in Austria, Germany, and Greece tend to favor specialists with formal professional qualifications. Necessity entrepreneurs should attend closely to whether their own industry is more or less affected than the average economy, question whether prior knowledge and networks remain meaningful, and be prepared to adjust plans swiftly. Governments should also counteract negative crisis narratives and provide dedicated training on operating businesses under crisis conditions.
limitations: The study examines a single geographic context (Greece) during a specific crisis (the Great Recession), which raises generalizability questions—though the authors note similarities to founders elsewhere. The empirical study benefited from the severity of the crisis, leaving the human capital–performance relationship in strongly booming economic contexts open. Despite extensive controls, results may still be subject to unobserved heterogeneity at the individual (e.g., personality, general mental ability) and firm (e.g., firm type) levels.
future_research: Researchers should extend the study to other settings to assess whether contextual variation matters for entrepreneurial outcomes and investigate how strongly booming industries shape the human capital–new firm performance relationship. Future work should also further integrate notions of crisis into research on necessity entrepreneurship, since dramatic downturns suddenly create large pools of necessity entrepreneurs whose process and performance dynamics remain underexplored.
abstract: Economic crises have profound effects on societies, motivating many individuals to launch their own firms in order to make a living. Although these firms created "out of necessity" possess few resources besides their founder's human capital, the role that this critical endowment plays in establishing a successful firm during a crisis is unclear, as existing knowledge offers diverging predictions about the value of general and specific human capital. We argue that this debate remains unresolved because we lack a holistic understanding of how each human capital type influences performance when founding conditions vary, and aim to reconcile the contrasting claims by considering how "hard" a crisis hits a given industry. Analyzing data collected from 500 founders who created firms in Greece during the Great Recession, combined with data from the Greek Statistical Office, we find that general human capital provides the greatest benefits, on average, during a crisis; yet specific human capital is more valuable in both the most favorable and the most unfavorable industry contexts. These results reveal how the value of human capital in entrepreneurship is contingent on founding conditions, and call into question existing notions of what it means to be resilient in a crisis.
apa_citation: Gruber, M., Dencker, J. C., & Nikiforou, A. (2024). How founder human capital and founding conditions shape new firm performance: A study of necessity entrepreneurship during times of economic crisis. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(2), 382–406. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0405

title: Before Birth: How Provisional Spaces Shape the Localized Emergence of New Organizational Forms
methods: Continuous-time event-history analysis using piecewise-exponential hazard rate models of a community's first founding of a movie theater (any form, nickelodeon-only, or movie house/palace-only). Independent variables are time-discounted (25% per year) cumulative counts of community-specific provisional spaces (all, highbrow, lowbrow), lagged by one year. Models include controls for population, median income, education, foreign-born share, professional workers, dwelling density, university and recreational presence, average movie runtime, annual film supply, and neighboring-community organizational density. Estimated by maximum likelihood in Stata (stpiece) on a split-spell file of year-long intervals; robust standard errors via Huber–White sandwich estimator clustered by community. Robustness checks include alternative discount rates, discrete-time event-history equivalents, and a placebo test on trade schools.
industry: Historical movie exhibition (movie-showing venues — provisional spaces such as opera houses, concert halls, penny arcades, dime museums, burlesque houses, dance halls, airdomes, storefronts; and dedicated movie theaters — nickelodeons, movie houses, movie palaces)
country: United States — Chicago, Illinois (75 officially designated community areas)
time_period: 1896–1927 (from the year movie projectors were introduced in the U.S. market through the beginning of the Hollywood Golden Age triggered by sound films)
units: 75 Chicago community areas (community-year observations in a continuous-time event-history split-spell file); the venue population covers 927 movie-showing venues comprising 286 provisional spaces and 641 dedicated movie theaters (453 nickelodeons, 166 movie houses, 22 movie palaces)
n_sample: 75 communities (events: 49 communities experienced a first nickelodeon founding, 46 a first movie house or palace, 24 experienced neither); 927 movie-showing venues underlie the independent and control variables
authors: Li, Ying; Khessina, Olga M.
theories: categorization and proto-forms (Polos, Hannan, & Carroll, 2002; McKendrick, Jaffee, Carroll, & Khessina, 2003); density dependence and legitimation (Hannan & Carroll, 1992; Hsu & Hannan, 2005); highbrow/lowbrow cultural distinction (Bourdieu, 1984; DiMaggio, 1992); organizational form emergence and ecology (Hannan & Freeman, 1977; Hannan, Polos, & Carroll, 2007; Carroll & Hannan, 2000); spaces in organizational change (Cartel, Boxenbaum, & Aggeri, 2019; Furnari, 2014; Taylor & Spicer, 2007)
keywords: geographic communities; localized opportunity structure; movie theaters; nickelodeon; organizational ecology; organizational form emergence; prebirth stage; proto-form; provisional spaces; spatiotemporal context
research_question: How do prebirth dynamics — that is, market pioneers' experimentation with an innovation in preexisting "provisional spaces" — affect the emergence patterns of the first organizational instances of a new form across geographic communities? In particular, where and when does the first organization dedicated to exploiting an innovation appear, and which type of new form arises locally?
mechanism_summary: The proposed mechanism is that, during the prebirth stage between an innovation's creation and the founding of the first dedicated organization, market pioneers experiment in small-scale, accessible preexisting venues. This experimentation disseminates community-specific knowledge about possible applications and gives rise to a proto-form that potential founders use as a template. Where lowbrow (or highbrow) provisional spaces accumulate, potential founders perceive a corresponding proto-form, accelerating emergence of a same-form dedicated organization (nickelodeon, or movie house/palace) and slowing the dissimilar one. Localized opportunity structure thus arises endogenously from the ecology of provisional spaces within a place.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops a predictive theory of provisional spaces that situates the first organizational instantiation of a new form in a concrete spatiotemporal context, displacing the prevailing "genetics view" that treats the first organization as random variation. It introduces the concept of a proto-form to link prebirth experimentation to community-specific categorization processes, and articulates an ecological perspective on spaces that connects place-level form emergence to populations of small accessible settings within a community.
practical_implication: Managers should recognize that experimenting with novel technology in their existing organizations can seed dedicated competitors that later substitute for them. Entrepreneurs can locate opportunities to found new organizational forms by attending to which preexisting community organizations are experimenting with a novel technology. Policy-makers can accelerate localized emergence of desired new forms by subsidizing, publicizing, and diversifying provisional spaces within their communities.
limitations: The empirical setting is an organizational form that emerged successfully, so generalizability to forms that gestated but failed to materialize is uncertain. The archival design lacks exogenous shocks or matched comparisons, so the documented relationships between provisional spaces and first foundings are correlational rather than causal. The historical, single-city scope (Chicago) and reliance on directories and newspaper advertisements may also miss provisional spaces that left no archival trace.
future_research: Researchers should study contemporary prebirth dynamics ethnographically (e.g., via Technology Transfer Offices) to observe how managers and would-be founders perceive proto-forms in real time. Internet-era data sources — online forums, review sites, discussion boards, and the Wayback Machine — can extend the framework to digitally-mediated emergence. Future work could also examine how factors such as inventor fame, infrastructure, and innovation aesthetics moderate the prebirth-to-emergence link, and explore non-U.S. contexts where culturally distinct provisional spaces (e.g., tea houses, stock exchanges) seeded early film exhibition.
abstract: The literature on evolution of organizational forms has remained largely silent on where the first organizational instance of a new form comes from, treating it as either a given or an outcome of random variation. We challenge this agnostic assumption by putting the first organizational founding into a specific spatiotemporal context and revealing the role of provisional spaces, defined as small-scale, easily accessible settings where market pioneers temporarily experiment with applications of an innovation before dedicated organizations emerge. We theorize that provisional spaces disseminate necessary information about an innovation and enable potential entrepreneurs to envision a new template for organizing. Therefore, geographic communities with a higher number of provisional spaces are more likely to host the first organization embodying a new form sooner than others. Using archival data on all movie-showing venues in Chicago communities, 1896–1927, we find empirical support for our theorizing. Community variance in volume and types of provisional spaces for movie projectors, such as opera houses and penny arcades, led to community-level differences in the emergence of distinct movie theater forms: nickelodeon, movie house, and movie palace. We advance scholarship on organizational form emergence by uncovering the role of provisional spaces in shaping localized opportunity structures.
apa_citation: Li, Y., & Khessina, O. M. (2024). Before birth: How provisional spaces shape the localized emergence of new organizational forms. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(2), 494–525. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0897

title: The Gendered Liability of Venture Novelty
methods: Mixed-methods design combining (1) an archival study of Shark Tank pitches (2009-2019) using independently coded video ratings analyzed via OLS and logistic regressions with bootstrapped conditional indirect effects (PROCESS Model 7); (2) Study 2A, a preregistered 2 (gender) x 2 (venture novelty) between-subjects online crowdfunding experiment with funders from Prolific; and (3) Study 2B, a preregistered 2 x 2 x 2 between-subjects field experiment with private venture funders adding venture gender type as a third factor, tested via OLS regression and moderated serial mediation (PROCESS Model 83).
industry: Early-stage entrepreneurial ventures across diverse sectors (retail trade 36.6%, manufacturing 34.1%, specialty services 5.6%, etc. in Study 1; apparel manufacturing in Study 2A; high-tech, apparel, and health/personal care industries in Study 2B)
country: United States (Shark Tank data); online funders recruited via Prolific (predominantly U.S.-based) for Study 2A; private venture funders recruited from a Germany-based European investment consulting firm for Study 2B
time_period: Study 1 archival data: episodes aired 2009-2019, coded April-May 2022; Study 2A completed November 2022; Study 2B conducted July-August 2022
units: Study 1: 895 entrepreneurial pitches (individuals or teams) coded by independent raters; Study 2A: online funders evaluating one of four venture conditions; Study 2B: private venture funders (e.g., angel investors) evaluating one of eight conditions
n_sample: Study 1 n=895 ventures (754 single-gender units in robustness check); Study 2A n=282 funders; Study 2B n=362 investors
authors: Liao, Z.; Zhang, J. H.; Wang, N.; Bottom, W. P.; Deichmann, D.; Tang, P. M.
theories: role congruity theory (Eagly & Karau, 2002); social role theory (Eagly & Wood, 1999); third-party bias (Abraham, 2020; Correll et al., 2017; Vial, Brescoll, & Dovidio, 2019); venture legitimacy / institutional legitimacy (Suchman, 1995; Zimmerman & Zeitz, 2002; Navis & Glynn, 2011)
keywords: entrepreneurial financing; gender; social approval forecasting; social role theory; third-party bias; venture norm violation judgments; venture novelty
research_question: When are venture capital investors more apt to invoke the anticipated funding preferences of peer investors in ways that disfavor women entrepreneurs, and how do they develop such anticipation? Specifically, does venture novelty activate gendered venture norm violation judgments that translate into unpromising social approval forecasting and reduced funding support for women-led ventures?
mechanism_summary:
- Moderator: Venture novelty (high vs. low); in Study 2B, also venture gender type (male-typed vs. female-typed industry).

Drawing on social role theory and third-party bias, the authors argue that women's gender role violations (being assertive, competitive, self-reliant as entrepreneurs) become particularly salient when paired with venture novelty, because novelty connotes adventurousness and norm-breaking that are at odds with women's prescribed communal role. This intensified perception of gender role violation spills over to harsher venture norm violation judgments, which then signal to investors that other investors will not endorse the venture (low social approval forecasting), ultimately leading investors to withhold funding. The effect is amplified when women lead novel ventures in male-typed industries, where industrial gender typing further exacerbates perceived role incongruity.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends the third-party bias literature by identifying venture novelty as a critical boundary condition that activates third-party gender bias and by showing that decision-makers may use gender as a cue to social legitimacy (not just status or competence) to resolve the third-party inference problem. It contributes to gender-stratification research in entrepreneurial finance by documenting an "entrepreneurial gender dilemma" — the very novelty that drives entrepreneurial success becomes a covert liability for women — and advances venture funding research by showing that beyond economic potential, investors weigh prospective endorsement from peer investors as a social-based criterion in funding decisions.
practical_implication: Crowdfunding platforms and venture capital firms should implement training programs that raise investor awareness of third-party bias and improve gender-neutral portfolio optimization, reducing investors' tendency to imitate the anticipated choices of others. Targeted training should focus on diminishing unfavorable reactions to women-led novel ventures and improving precision in venture potential forecasting irrespective of entrepreneur gender. Policymakers can complement this by reducing industrial and occupational gender segregation through regulatory initiatives and acceleration programs that broaden women entrepreneurs' cross-sector access to resources and mentorship.
limitations: Each study has its own limitations: Shark Tank is an edited TV show with potential sampling and production-induced bias, and its observational nature precluded testing the full serial mediation. The two experiments may raise external validity concerns despite using actual investors as participants, and venture novelty was operationalized using a single item in Study 1. The theorizing took a binary approach to gender that does not capture the experiences of trans and nonbinary entrepreneurs, and the gender-neutral ventures in Study 2A may have constrained the range of novelty variation tested.
future_research: Future work could employ field experiments on real funding platforms to examine social-inference-based mechanisms of gender bias, take a more nuanced (non-binary) gender perspective and extend findings to other underrepresented groups by race, ethnicity, sexual orientation, or religion, and explore systemic organizational and legislative safeguards (e.g., venture capital firm nudges) for reducing barriers to women pioneering novel ventures. Researchers should also further unpack the role of venture gender type and homophily in shaping investors' norm violation judgments on women-led novel ventures.
abstract: To hedge unforeseen risk, investors may prioritize male-led ventures that they anticipate most other investors will prefer, arriving at decisions that are biased against women. Yet, little is known about how investors infer such gendered preferences and when they are particularly likely to do so. Integrating insights from third-party bias research with social role theory, we posit that when women propose novel ventures, investors are more apt to make unpromising social approval forecasting—an anticipation of the extent to which other investors will endorse these ventures—and thus withhold funding support. This is because the intensified gender role violations due to women being entrepreneurial in tandem with being novel lead investors to impose harsher judgments that these ventures violate normative business practices. Our hypotheses receive support from results of three methodologically complementary studies, including an archival study of Shark Tank (2009–2019) coupled with preregistered online and field experiments. By casting light on how venture novelty, a key determining factor of entrepreneurial success, makes third-party bias against women particularly salient, our work identifies a less overt "entrepreneurial gender dilemma" and derives new insights into policy-making designed to help women entrepreneurs surmount financial and social barriers in the innovation-based economy.
apa_citation: Liao, Z., Zhang, J. H., Wang, N., Bottom, W. P., Deichmann, D., & Tang, P. M. (2024). The gendered liability of venture novelty. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(2), 299-330. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1425

title: Erring Professionals as Second Victims: Grappling with Guilt and Identity in the Aftermath of Error
methods: Grounded theory; 68 semi-structured interviews with 56 clinicians across four waves, supplemented by archival data (medical professionalism initiatives, identity-formation practices, government white papers, hospital quality-management reports); inductive three-stage Gioia coding from first-order codes to second-order themes to aggregate dimensions, with member-checking.
industry: Healthcare (medical clinicians working in hospitals)
country: Germany (51 clinicians); 5 had previously worked in German hospitals; 2 worked in hospitals in Asia (China and Malaysia)
time_period: Four interview waves; wave three conducted via Microsoft Teams during the Covid-19 pandemic
units: Individual clinicians (medical professionals)
n_sample: 68 interviews with 56 clinicians (38 men and 18 women); average age 39.88 years
authors: Muethel, Miriam; Ballmann, Christina; Hollensbe, Elaine
theories: identity work (Sveningsson & Alvesson, 2003; Petriglieri, 2011); self-discrepancy theory (Higgins, 1987); sensemaking theory (Weick, 1988; Maitlis & Christianson, 2014); stress-coping (Lazarus & Folkman, 1984)
keywords: control-focused identity work; errors; escape-focused identity work; guilt; identity work; professional identity; second victims; sensemaking; sensemaking assets; sensemaking liabilities
research_question: (a) How do professionals experience and make sense of errors? (b) What is the effect, if any, of errors on professional identity?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: Initial feelings of guilt (intuitive emotional reaction to unintentional harm) versus ultimate feelings of guilt (post-sensemaking judgment of responsibility); sensemaking assets versus sensemaking liabilities; control-focused versus escape-focused identity work; identity thriving versus identity deterioration.
- Triggering conditions: Error situation characterized by severity of harm (no harm, near miss, minor, major) and causality (equivocal vs. unequivocal); professional identity content (helper, service provider, problem-fixer) and centrality.
- Sensegivers / dualities: Five sources—self, supervisors, organization, colleagues, clients—each functioning as either an asset (alleviating guilt via self-compassion, support, learning culture, empathy, forgiveness) or a liability (aggravating guilt via perfectionism, depreciation, blame culture, sneer, accusation).
- Process / phases: (1) Error event; (2) Initial intuitive feelings of guilt; (3) Cognitive sensemaking with stakeholders' input shaping ultimate judgment of responsibility; (4) Identity work strategy adopted; (5) Identity thriving or deterioration outcome.
- Four identity work types (Categories A–D): A) Control-focused behavioral (learn from errors, severe harm with unequivocal causality), B) Control-focused cognitive (reject image of omnipotence, severe harm with equivocal causality), C) Escape-focused behavioral (engage in self-defense, near-miss with equivocal causality), D) Escape-focused cognitive (distance self from professional identity, minor harm with unequivocal causality).
- Emergent outcomes: Professional identity thriving (strengthening or flourishing) when assets dominate, or identity deterioration (diminishment or deprecation) when liabilities dominate.

The process unfolds when professionals unintentionally harm others, triggering guilt as an other-centered, morally laden emotion. The intensity of guilt depends on harm severity, but is then reshaped through a sensemaking process in which the self and four stakeholder groups can either alleviate (assets) or aggravate (liabilities) feelings of guilt. The configuration of error severity, causality, and the asset/liability profile of self and others channels professionals into one of four identity work strategies, which in turn drive whether the professional's future identity thrives or deteriorates.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes three contributions: (1) it elaborates a new perspective on error and trauma theory by introducing "second victims" as professionals traumatized by the unintentional harm they cause others, distinguishing guilt (other-centered) from grief (self-centered); (2) it advances sensemaking theory by demonstrating dualities in sensegiving—how the self and stakeholders can serve as either sensemaking assets or sensemaking liabilities depending on whether they alleviate or aggravate guilt; and (3) it contributes to professional identity theory by identifying four distinct identity work strategies (control-focused behavioral/cognitive and escape-focused behavioral/cognitive) that determine whether errors lead to identity thriving or deterioration.
practical_implication: Supervisors play the most pivotal role in alleviating or aggravating clinicians' feelings of guilt; when supervisors react destructively, no other stakeholder can compensate for this sensemaking liability. Supervisors should therefore be trained to recognize and refrain from destructive behaviors and instead practice active listening, perspective-taking, empathy, and constructive guidance. Organizations should foster supportive learning cultures that allow professionals to make sense of errors rather than fear blame.
limitations: First, some data are retrospective, though triangulation across waves and member-checking enhanced trustworthiness; direct observation would have strengthened the design. Second, the study examines single critical incidents rather than cumulative cycles of errors that may produce more self-destructive identity trajectories over time. Third, the clinician sample saw helping as a moral duty, so findings may differ for self-interested professionals or industries where status-seeking dominates.
future_research: Future research should explore stakeholder reactions over time and across error magnitudes (e.g., why supervisors react empathetically to severe but destructively to minor errors), examine optimal levels of blame, and investigate how multiple sensegiving sources interact within identity networks when they diverge. Longitudinal designs could capture cumulative identity deterioration after repeated errors, and studies in non-medical industries could test whether self-interested "glory seekers" engage in different identity work tactics.
abstract: Applying a grounded theory approach, we investigate how professionals cope with making errors that unintentionally harm others. We purposively sampled medical clinicians to study this phenomenon, as these professionals understand themselves as "enablers of patient health." When unintentionally harming others, clinicians experienced initial feelings of guilt. Triggered by the error experience, these professionals engaged in a cognitive sensemaking process, aiming to determine the root causes of the error and evaluate their level of responsibility. In this process, clinicians considered aspects of themselves and their social environment (i.e., supervisors, organization, colleagues, and clients) as either sensemaking assets or liabilities. The self and others became sensemaking assets when helping to alleviate initial feelings of guilt, and sensemaking liabilities when aggravating them. Depending on the outcome of their sensemaking process, clinicians engaged in control-focused or escape-focused identity work. This identity work, in turn, influenced the thriving or deterioration of their future professional identity.
apa_citation: Muethel, M., Ballmann, C., & Hollensbe, E. (2024). Erring professionals as second victims: Grappling with guilt and identity in the aftermath of error. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(2), 407–436. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1132
