title: An Integrative Model of Hybrid Governance: The Role of Boards in Helping Sustain Organizational Hybridity
methods: Inductive longitudinal comparative case study of five work integration social enterprises (WISEs) with institutionally diverse boards in France; data include 81 semi-structured interviews with field experts, board members, senior managers, and middle managers; archival data (bylaws, board minutes, board packs, annual reports); on-site observations; and follow-up interviews in 2018, 2021, and 2022; within-case analysis followed by cross-case analysis with double-coder validation.
industry: Work integration social enterprises in recycling and temp work sectors
time_period: Founding (1985-1998) through 2007, with follow-up interviews in 2018, 2021, and 2022
units: Five work integration social enterprises (WISEs) with institutionally diverse boards
n_sample: 5 WISEs (RECYCLE, FORWORK, NOWASTE, TEMPO, RECRUIT) with 81 interviews across the cases
authors: Pache, Anne-Claire; Battilana, Julie; Spencer, Channing
theories: attention-based view of the firm (Ocasio, 1997); faultlines theory (Lau & Murnighan, 1998); institutional logics theory (Thornton, Ocasio, & Lounsbury, 2012)
keywords: board governance; faultlines; hybrid organizations; institutional logics; organizational attention; relational leadership; work integration social enterprises
research_question: How can institutionally diverse boards of hybrid organizations effectively help these organizations sustain the pursuit of their multiple goals?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: protective board structure, relational leadership processes, enculturation processes, bridging processes, attentional engagement, faultlines, cognitive and emotional conflicts
- Patterns of attentional engagement: "oscillating attentional engagement" (RECYCLE, FORWORK, NOWASTE) versus "dual-focus attentional engagement" (TEMPO, RECRUIT)
- Structural element: a protective board structure with enshrined rules ensures continuous representation of multiple logics on the board and prevents unintended shifts in the power balance between social and commercial factions
- Process element 1 (Enculturation processes): interventions enacted by a hybrid board leader, both cognitive (agenda-setting, constant reminders, one-on-one phone calls) and emotional (dinners, beneficiary stories, site visits), that reduce cognitive and emotional gaps between institutionally diverse board members and connect them to the dual mission
- Process element 2 (Bridging processes): systematic interventions by a board leader (chair or executive director) with a hybrid profile to foster communication and alignment between the board and senior managers in the decision-making process — preparing meetings with senior managers, inviting senior manager presentations, explaining board decisions back to senior managers
- Boundary condition: a protective board structure is necessary but not sufficient (NOWASTE had it yet still experienced conflict); relational leadership processes are needed to make logics not just available but also accessible and activated
- Emergent outcome: when protective structure combines with both relational processes, decision-makers attain dual-focus attentional engagement to multiple goals without distracting conflicts

The integrative model of hybrid governance shows that institutionally diverse boards can sustain hybrid organizations' attention to multiple goals only when a protective board structure (which makes both logics available by enshrining their representation) is combined with relational leadership processes performed by a hybrid leader. Enculturation processes make logics cognitively accessible and emotionally salient to board members, while bridging processes activate both logics during decision-making by tying the board to senior managers' experience and translating board decisions back to the organization. Together, these mechanisms prevent the cognitive and emotional conflicts that arise from board faultlines and enable boards to fulfill their guardrail role.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops an integrative model of hybrid governance that uncovers how institutionally diverse boards can effectively serve as guardrails of organizational hybridity. It extends research on hybrids by identifying a previously undocumented "dual-focus attentional engagement" pattern alongside the more familiar "oscillating" pattern, shows how the interplay between board structure and relational leadership processes mitigates cognitive and emotional faultline conflicts, and contributes to organizational attention theory by mapping how governance structures and processes shape the availability, accessibility, and activation of multiple institutional logics — including emotion-management as a key, previously underemphasized lever.
practical_implication: The study offers concrete guidance for the boards of hybrid (and increasingly, conventional) organizations seeking to balance social, environmental, and financial goals: (a) enshrine board composition rules in bylaws to lock in continuous representation of competing logics and prevent political drift; (b) appoint hybrid leaders who can perform enculturation and bridging work; and (c) institutionalize practices such as dual-KPI agendas, beneficiary site visits, and senior-manager participation in board meetings to keep both logics accessible and activated in decision-making.
limitations: Generalizability is limited by the qualitative design and the focus on relatively small French WISEs operating under similar regulatory pressures; decision-making power may differ in larger hybrids where senior managers play a larger role. The data did not allow strong claims about the relationship between attentional engagement patterns and organizational performance, nor about whether enculturation and bridging are individually necessary, since no case exhibited only one of the two relational leadership processes.
future_research: Future work should examine how larger hybrids and hybrids combining different sets of logics with varying compatibility maintain dual-focus attention; how legal status and democratic organizational forms shape board structures and their ability to safeguard hybridity; whether the size and composition of boards moderate these dynamics; and how emotional control and power relations interact with cognition to shape how individuals enact institutional logics, leveraging recent work on institutions and emotions.
abstract: Hybrid organizations must sustainably attend to multiple goals embedded in different institutional spheres. Past research has highlighted the value for hybrids in recruiting board members representing different logics to avoid attentional drifts; yet, diverse boards have also been prone to conflicts that occasion attentional lapses, thereby jeopardizing these organizations' pursuit of multiple goals. We draw on a longitudinal comparative case study of five work integration social enterprises with institutionally diverse boards to uncover an integrative model of hybrid governance consisting of a protective board structure and relational leadership processes that, together, prevent distracting cognitive and emotional conflicts and foster attentional engagement of both the board and senior managers to multiple goals. The protective board structure prevents shifts in board composition and ensures the enshrined representation of the organization's logics. Relational leadership processes—which help board members relate to each other, with the organization's mission, and with senior managers—include enculturation processes (i.e., interventions to reduce the cognitive and emotional gap between institutionally diverse board members) and bridging processes (i.e., interventions to facilitate communication and alignment between the board and senior managers in the decision-making process). These findings contribute to research on hybrid organizations, board governance, and organizational attention.
apa_citation: Pache, A.-C., Battilana, J., & Spencer, C. (2024). An integrative model of hybrid governance: The role of boards in helping sustain organizational hybridity. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(2), 437–467. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0966

title: The Publicization of Organizational Misconduct: A Social Structural Approach
methods: Random-effects panel OLS regressions on a panel of 176 U.S. Catholic dioceses tracked at four decade points (1980, 1990, 2000, 2010); 704 diocese-decade observations. Supplementary cross-sectional negative binomial models for time-to-publicization at the offender level (n=2,091). DV: count of first-time misconduct media articles per decade. Predictors: ethnic Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (homogeneity), Catholic share of population (Church embeddedness), density of social/voluntary organizations per capita (social connectedness).
industry: Catholic Church (religious organizations) and U.S. local newspaper media
units: diocese-decade observations (with supplementary offender-level analyses)
n_sample: 704 diocese-decade observations across 176 U.S. dioceses; 2,091 offender-level observations in supplementary analysis
authors: Piazza, Alessandro; Jourdan, Julien
theories: embeddedness perspective (Granovetter, 1985; Marquis & Battilana, 2009); social capital theory (Coleman, 1988; Putnam, 2000); social identity theory (Tajfel & Turner, 1986); theory of scandal (Adut, 2005, 2008)
keywords: community; embeddedness; homogeneity; organizational misconduct; publicization; scandals; social connectedness; social structure; whistleblowing
research_question: How do social-structural features of communities — specifically community homogeneity, the local embeddedness of the offending organization, and community social connectedness — shape the likelihood that organizational misconduct will be publicized (i.e., enter the public domain via media coverage), and thereby give rise to scandal?
mechanism_summary: The mechanism operates through two structural channels. First, homogeneous communities and communities where the offending organization is highly embedded raise the social cost of speaking up: closely-knit ties, shared identity, and material/symbolic dependence on the offending organization mean that disclosing misconduct threatens the ingroup with shame, ostracism, and collective scandal-contamination, deterring informants. Second, social connectedness — bridging social capital created by religious, civic, and recreational associations — enables boundary-spanning rumor diffusion across social groups, so misconduct information escapes its narrow social circle, gains credibility through repetition, and reaches journalists who require multi-source corroboration. Heterogeneous, well-connected, weakly-embedded communities therefore publicize misconduct most readily.
theoretical_contribution: The paper conceptualizes scandals and misconduct publicization as socially embedded phenomena rather than products of external shocks, lone whistleblowers, or investigative journalism alone. It extends scholarship on organizational scandals by showing that community-level social structure — homogeneity, organizational embeddedness, and bridging social capital — independently determines whether wrongdoing surfaces, even after controlling for the actual prevalence and severity of misconduct. By reframing whistleblowing as a collective community-level response, the study bridges the organization-community nexus with literatures on scandal, deviance, and social capital.
practical_implication: Managers and policymakers should appreciate that misconduct detection is not simply a function of internal controls or media vigilance but of the social structure surrounding the organization. Highly community-embedded organizations face heightened risk of undetected internal misconduct; their leaders should adopt more participatory, inclusive governance structures that enable frank exchange of views and external scrutiny. Policymakers concerned with curbing wrongdoing should attend to community-level conditions — particularly the availability of bridging social ties — that enable misconduct to surface.
limitations: The account is correlational, not causal: the true underlying prevalence of misconduct in each diocese is unknown, so hidden heterogeneity in undetected misconduct cannot be fully ruled out. U.S. Census and ARDA data are available only at decade intervals, constraining temporal granularity. The empirical setting (Catholic clergy sex abuse, 1980-2010) largely predates social media and online "citizen journalism," potentially limiting external validity of findings to contemporary digital contexts.
future_research: Future work should examine publicization dynamics in non-geographical communities, especially occupational communities (e.g., Hollywood/Weinstein) and online communities where rumors proliferate. Researchers should explore the conditions under which local scandals escalate to national or international levels and become recognized as societal issues, and should incorporate broader media sources and discovery mechanisms — including social media and citizen journalism — to test the boundary conditions of the social-structural framework.
abstract: Scandals are momentous events with far-reaching consequences for organizations and society, but we still know relatively little about the contextual conditions that enable them. In this paper, we develop and test a theory of the publicization of organizational misconduct—that is, of how an act of wrongdoing by an organization becomes widely publicized, thereby giving rise to a scandal; or receives little coverage, with little or no consequence for the offending organization. We argue that communities have structural features that affect the social cost of making information public as well as its dissemination, shaping the likelihood of organizational misconduct being publicized in the process. We test and find evidence in support of our arguments using data on sex abuse in the U.S. Catholic Church between 1980 and 2010, and exploring the heterogeneity in the publicization of patterns of misconduct by Catholic clergy across dioceses and over time. We conclude by discussing the implications of our findings for the scholarly understanding of organizational misconduct and scandals across settings.
apa_citation: Piazza, A., & Jourdan, J. (2024). The publicization of organizational misconduct: A social structural approach. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(2), 468-493. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1337

title: Echoes from the Past: The Impact of Racial Transference on Leader Selection during Succession
methods: Multi-method package combining: (Study 1) archival binary logistic regression on 186 NCAA Division I men's basketball head-coach appointments (2000-2020) with team fixed effects, testing the interaction of predecessor race and predecessor winning percentage on Black-vs-non-Black successor selection; (Study 2) preregistered 2 (predecessor-candidate racial similarity: yes/no) x 2 (predecessor performance: low/high) between-subjects vignette experiment with 491 full-time hiring professionals (Prolific) evaluating a fictitious VP-of-Marketing candidate, with two-way ANOVA, simple-effects tests, and Hayes (2013) PROCESS bootstrapped (10,000) moderated-mediation models using a Ritter-and-Lord-style false-alarm memory-recognition score as the transference mediator and willingness-to-appoint plus a 3-item leader-potential scale as DVs; (Supplementary Study 1) preregistered experiment (n=684) ruling out leader prototyping and racial entitativity as alternative mechanisms with an Asian/Black/White choice-set DV; (Supplementary Study 2) quasi-experiment with 601 registered voters in southeastern US states using real Georgia senators (Ossoff, Warnock) to test evaluators' familiarity with the predecessor as a boundary condition; (Supplementary Study 3) experiment (n=299) testing predecessor-candidate gender similarity as a boundary condition with an Asian female predecessor.
industry: Sports (NCAA Division I men's basketball — Big Ten, Big East, Big 12, ACC, SEC, Pac-12) for Study 1; cross-industry full-time professionals with hiring/interviewing experience evaluating a marketing-leader vignette for Study 2 and Supplementary Studies 1 and 3; politics (US Senate) for Supplementary Study 2.
time_period: Study 1 archival window 2000-2020; Studies 2 and supplementary studies conducted as cross-sectional online experiments (Supplementary Study 2 referenced 2020-2021 Georgia Senate context).
units: Study 1 unit of analysis: head-coach succession events (186); Studies 2 and supplementary: individual evaluators (full-time hiring professionals or registered voters).
n_sample: Study 1 n=186 (135 White, 51 Black predecessors); Study 2 n=491 (299 White, 101 Black, 70 Asian, 21 biracial/other); Supplementary Study 1 n=684; Supplementary Study 2 n=601; Supplementary Study 3 n=299.
authors: Preston, McKenzie C.; Carton, Andrew M.
theories: attribution theory (Calder, 1977; Kelley & Michela, 1980); inference-based approaches to leadership; leader categorization theory (Lord, Foti, & Phillips, 1982); leader transference (Ritter & Lord, 2007); racial transference (introduced)
keywords: Glass Cliff effect; White Savior effect; leader categorization theory; leader selection; leader succession; leader transference; predecessor performance; prototypes; racial bias; racial transference; stereotypes; trait imputation
research_question: How does racial similarity between a predecessor and a leader candidate bias evaluators' selection decisions during succession, and does the dominant framework — leader categorization theory — adequately explain this bias, or is a distinct mechanism (racial transference) at work?
mechanism_summary:
- Mediator: Racial transference, operationalized as a false-alarm score on a Ritter-and-Lord (2007) memory-recognition task — the probability of erroneously recognizing predecessor traits as candidate traits, capturing evaluators' false generalization of personality traits from a racially similar predecessor to the candidate.
- Ruled-out alternative mechanisms: leader prototyping, racial entitativity, perceived group similarity (Supplementary Study 1).

The mechanism unfolds in two stages. First, racial similarity between a predecessor and candidate triggers trait imputation: because racial similarity is easy to observe and confuses the two leaders in episodic memory, evaluators falsely generalize the predecessor's personality traits to the candidate, even when those traits contradict prevailing racial stereotypes and prototypes. Second, drawing on attribution theory and inference-based approaches to leadership, evaluators infer whether the imputed traits are desirable based on the predecessor's performance — if the predecessor succeeded, the trait is virtuous; if the predecessor failed, the same trait is liability. The conjunction of trait imputation and performance attribution produces a within-race contingent bias: candidates racially similar to high-performing predecessors are favored, while those similar to low-performing predecessors are devalued; candidates racially dissimilar to predecessors are unaffected by predecessor performance.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces racial transference as a distinct, episodic-memory-based racial bias that operates in succession contexts and supplants the categorization mechanisms (stereotyping, prototyping) at the heart of leader categorization theory. By integrating theory on leader transference (Ritter & Lord, 2007), attribution theory, and racial categorization, the authors argue that racial similarity is a necessary precondition for leader transference and that evaluators generalize not only personality traits but also performance expectations between racially similar leaders. This recasts canonical findings such as the Glass Cliff and White Savior effects as artifacts of within-race preference shifts rather than between-race preferences, and predicts that — over the long run — racial transference may slowly increase, rather than decrease, the representation of racial minority leaders, raising an ethical quandary because the mechanism of progress is itself a racial bias.
practical_implication: Organizations seeking to mitigate racial transference can identify evaluators who are unfamiliar with the predecessor (e.g., outsourcing succession decisions to search firms that did not know the predecessor), refrain from invoking the predecessor in candidate-discussion meetings, and add comparing-candidates-to-predecessors as a target of bias training alongside debiasing of stereotypes. Recruiters should be especially cautious not to discount qualified racial-minority candidates when replacing low-performing predecessors of the same race, and policies that diversify the candidate pool (e.g., the Rooney Rule, affirmative action) should be paired with explicit safeguards against transference-based discounting.
limitations: Study 1's sport-industry archival data raise external-validity concerns and lacks randomization for causal inference; the binary DV cannot disentangle assimilation versus contrast biases, and it does not directly observe transference as a mechanism. The studies do not address how legacy effects from founders or distant past leaders interact with racial transference, nor do they fully resolve the role of predecessor and candidate agency in succession politics. The study of leader gender and intersectionality is preliminary, with only one supplementary study examining gender-similarity boundary conditions.
future_research: The authors encourage research on whether prominent founders and distant past leaders create long-standing transference imprints, whether predecessors and candidates can amplify or undermine racial transference (e.g., a predecessor publicly endorsing a racially similar candidate, or a candidate explicitly contrasting with an underperforming predecessor), and whether racial transference spills across leadership roles and organizations or into non-leader succession events. They also call for deeper engagement with leader gender and intersectionality, especially how race-and-gender similarity jointly shape transference between predecessors and candidates.
abstract: The dominant perspective used to understand racial bias in leader selection decisions is leader categorization theory (LCT). Received wisdom is twofold: evaluators prefer White leaders because they possess prototypical leader traits; however, during periods of decline, evaluators prefer racial minority leaders because their stereotypical traits (e.g., warmth) position them to galvanize followers. We argue that whereas LCT is useful for explaining racial bias in nonsuccession contexts, it is insufficient for explaining racial bias during succession. During succession, evaluators focus on the predecessor as the comparison point when evaluating candidates. This causes stereotypes and prototypes to fade from evaluators' minds, only to be replaced by a different racial bias—racial transference—which occurs when evaluators falsely assume that a leader candidate has the same personality traits as a racially similar predecessor, and thus will perform similarly. Consequently, evaluators select (reject) candidates who are racially similar to successful (unsuccessful) predecessors. We find support for these predictions in an archival study, a preregistered experiment, and three supplementary studies. Altogether, theory on racial transference establishes that racial bias during succession is driven by evaluators' tendency to generalize between racially similar predecessors and candidates, even if such generalizations are inconsistent with prevailing stereotypes and prototypes.
apa_citation: Preston, M. C., & Carton, A. M. (2024). Echoes from the past: The impact of racial transference on leader selection during succession. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(2), 331-358. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1031

title: Vicarious Coding: Breaching Computational Opacity in the Digital Era
methods: Eight-month inductive ethnography of a single CNC machine-shop (Formco), February-December 2018, by the first author. Data include 28 semi-structured interviews (with 9 machine operators, 7 managers, 4 top-level managers, 2 customers, 2 salespeople, 3 assistants, and the financial director, lasting 45 minutes to three hours), shadowing across sales/design/programming/machining/fitting departments, photographs, video recordings, and detailed field notes. Analysis followed iterative open coding of 90 fully observed mold-production cases, yielding a contrast between 57 routine button-pushing and 33 deviating cases (9 simple execution errors and 24 complex problems involving programmer help). The 24 complex cases (13 with operator-as-observer, 5 operator-diagnosed/programmer-fixed, 6 operator-fixed) were chronologically ordered to derive a three-stage learning model, then iterated with literatures on representations and vicarious learning.
industry: Manufacturing — a CNC (computer-numeric-control) machine-shop producing molds in aluminum, resin, or wood for packaged-goods, automotive, and sports clients (Formco, a pseudonymous firm of 70 employees with revenues exceeding 7 million EUR in 2018, divided into two business units)
country: France (norms strongly entrenched within the French regulatory context)
time_period: February 2018 to December 2018 (eight months of fieldwork; two to four days per week)
units: Machine operators and programmers at a CNC machine-shop, observed during the production of individual molds; case-level units are 90 mold-production episodes (57 routine, 33 deviating), with 24 complex-problem episodes coded in depth
n_sample: Eight-month ethnography of one firm; 28 interviews with 9 machine operators plus managers, customers, salespeople, and finance director; 90 molds observed end-to-end; 24 complex-problem episodes analyzed in depth
authors: Rostain, Marjolaine; Huising, Ruthanne
theories: computational opacity and technical illiteracy (Burrell, 2016; Kallinikos, Aaltonen, & Marton, 2010, 2013); literature on representations and transformational technologies (Bailey, Leonardi, & Barley, 2012; Leonardi & Bailey, 2008); situated learning and legitimate peripheral participation (Lave & Wenger, 1991); vicarious learning (Bandura, 1977; Manz & Sims, 1981; Myers, 2018)
keywords: CNC machining; computational opacity; digital divide; digital work; ethnography; machine operators; practical computational literacy; programmers; representations; vicarious learning
research_question: How do nonprogrammers — workers who routinely work *from* computationally generated representations rather than write code — breach the computational opacity of those representations and become able to diagnose and fix misalignments between representations and the physical world without depending on programmers?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: digital representations; computational opacity (closed and unpliable representations); practical computational literacy (a layered, three-skill capacity); vicarious learning across occupational groups; misalignment between representations and the physical world.
- Triggering conditions: production episodes in which routine button-pushing is disrupted by programming errors or unanticipated material/tool variation, generating misalignments that exceed the operator's execution-error repertoire and require the programmer to reconceptualize and reprogram the work.
- Process / phases (three accumulated skill levels):
  - Level 1 — Visualizing misalignment between the natural-language tool sheet and the physical entity (mold, machine, tool path, motion), learned by listening to programmers narrate and mimic the machining process and by watching machining simulations.
  - Level 2 — Translating between the natural language of the tool sheet and the symbols, syntax, and block structure of the programming script (e.g., reading T02 as a milling cutter, M06 as "pick the tool," and mapping a tool-sheet line to a code block).
  - Level 3 — Editing the code by deleting/inserting symbols and copy-pasting blocks of script to produce small but consequential changes that realign the representation with the physical entity.
- Practices: shadowing programmers as they "fix problems aloud," running and replaying machining simulations, finger-tracing code lines, mimicking tool motion with hands, and tinkering with copy-paste edits on the shop-floor computer.
- Boundary conditions: misalignment problems that require a computational fix; observation of programmers who narrate their work without protecting their jurisdiction; and access to the programming script that generates the representation. Where transformational technology is itself opaque (e.g., learning algorithms), vicarious coding is "possible but limited."
- Emergent outcomes: machine operators autonomously fix and even improve programming scripts, anticipate and avoid problems, gain momentary autonomy and discretion, and acquire informal status as "smart" workers assigned the most complex molds; programmers' scripts are upgraded and reused, generating short- and long-term organizational benefits.

The process Rostain and Huising theorize is one of vicarious accumulation rather than apprenticeship: machine operators are not granted legitimate peripheral participation in programming work, yet by repeatedly observing and listening to programmers solve misalignment problems aloud they collect "tidbits" of computational symbols, syntax, and structure that accrete, over time, into a practical (functional, problem-focused) literacy. The three skills layer cumulatively — visualizing first, then translating, then editing — and each layer relaxes the closure and unpliability of the representation. The result is a shift from working *from* representations (intimate with the physical, distant from the code) toward fleeting, problem-bounded moments of working *within* them, breaching computational opacity at the source rather than working around it.
theoretical_contribution: The paper foregrounds *computational opacity* — the black-boxing of the coding work that produces representations — as a distinct and increasingly consequential form of organizational opacity, and theorizes *practical computational literacy* (visualize, translate, edit) as a means of breaching it that goes beyond the workaround literature on misalignment between representations and the physical world. By documenting *vicarious coding* — the cross-occupational, observation-based acquisition of programming skill outside legitimate peripheral participation — the paper extends vicarious-learning theory beyond the within-occupation peer setting in which it has typically been studied, and identifies the conditions (misalignment-driven help-seeking, narrated programmer problem-solving, and access to programming scripts) under which a translation-and-experimentation pathway becomes viable for outsiders.
practical_implication: Organizations relying on computationally generated representations can reduce dependence on programmers — and the bottlenecks, delays, and one-off workarounds that come with it — by deliberately creating opportunities for nonprogrammers to observe programmers narrating their problem-solving and by giving them access to the underlying scripts. Managers should recognize that operators who acquire practical computational literacy avoid problems, fix problems faster, and improve the code base for future jobs, while also monitoring the risks of distributed code changes in tightly coupled systems. For occupations confronting algorithmic representations (lawyers, bankers, HR managers, radiologists), the paper suggests that computational literacy may become a complement to substantive domain expertise rather than the exclusive preserve of programmers.
limitations: The findings come from a single eight-month ethnography of one French CNC machine-shop with a clear, codified division of labor and physically co-located programmers and operators, conditions that supported the kinds of narrated problem-solving and script access on which vicarious coding depends; settings without these conditions (e.g., remote programmers, locked scripts, opaque learning algorithms) may sustain less learning. The transformational technology studied is comparatively scrutable code (CAM programming script with discrete tools and block structure), so the pathway documented is "possible but limited" where the underlying logic — for example, machine-learning models — cannot be observed. Practical computational literacy was not formally rewarded by Formco, and the paper observes but does not evaluate the longer-term career or compensation consequences for operators who develop it.
future_research: Future studies could examine the degree and distribution of coding competencies across occupations and their consequences for organizational performance, the conditions under which vicarious coding generates productive versus damaging outcomes in tightly coupled systems, and whether new hybrid roles combining substantive and computational expertise are emerging within professions facing algorithmic opacity (lawyers, bankers, HR managers). Researchers could also investigate whether and how the practical/functional literacy identified here evolves toward more sophisticated computational work, how openness and pliability of representations shape learning potential, and how online resources (StackOverflow, GitHub, video tutorials) substitute for direct co-located observation of programmers.
abstract: Digital representations are ubiquitous in the workplace. Screen displays, forecasts, simulations, indicators, multidimensional models, figures, and images are increasingly central to work of all kinds. Representations are simultaneously transparent and opaque. They contain and reveal information about the organization. Yet, they conceal the computational work used to convert data about the physical world into abstract depictions. Computational opacity is consequential when representations become misaligned with the physical world they depict. We examine how computational opacity can be breached. Drawing on an ethnography of a machine-shop, we show how operators develop practical computational literacy skills—the capacity to visualize and talk about physical objects and processes independent of them; to translate this noncomputational thinking and talking into computational symbols, syntax, structure, and assumptions; and to create computational solutions. Operators develop this skill vicariously, observing programmers as they solve problems. We contribute to understanding how those without programming capacities may decrease their dependence on programmers and increase their capacity to create and alter representations of the physical world.
apa_citation: Rostain, M., & Huising, R. (2024). Vicarious coding: Breaching computational opacity in the digital era. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(2), 359-381. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0150

title: Becoming a Right-Hand Partner: How Lower-Power Employees Heedfully Challenge Organizational Leaders
methods: Inductive grounded theory study (Strauss & Corbin, 1998; Gioia, Corley, & Hamilton, 2013) of how lower-power employees emerge as right-hand partners. Four iterative phases of data collection and analysis with 74 individuals (61 chiefs of staff and 13 of their matched leaders), comprising 44 one-on-one interviews with COSs (averaging 45 minutes), 13 phone interviews with leaders (averaging 23 minutes), and three 90-minute focus groups (17 COS participants). Analysis used in vivo open coding (~250 codes), axial coding to second-order analytical themes, and theoretical coding into aggregate dimensions; member checking with two COSs and one leader confirmed the model.
industry: Diverse — corporate organizations across technology, healthcare, education, financial services, manufacturing, nonprofit, philanthropy, government, entertainment, and professional services
country: United States (corporate sector; not explicitly named in text but inferred from author affiliations and Prime Chief of Staff partnership)
time_period: Not reported in paper (interviews conducted across four iterative phases prior to publication; participants were currently or recently working as COSs within the past year)
units: Lower-power employee–leader dyads — primarily chiefs of staff and their executive principals (CEOs, COOs, Presidents, etc.)
n_sample: 74 individuals (61 chiefs of staff and 13 of their matched leaders)
authors: Barnes, Liza Y.; Lacerenza, Christina N.; Volpone, Sabrina D.
theories: employee voice and dissent-based interacting (Morrison, 2014, 2023); perspective-taking and power (Galinsky, Magee, Inesi, & Gruenfeld, 2006; Galinsky et al., 2014); power in organizations (Magee & Galinsky, 2008; Anderson & Brion, 2014); unequal-power relationships (Schaerer, du Plessis, Yap, & Thau, 2018); upward influence and issue selling (Ashford, Rothbard, Piderit, & Dutton, 1998; DiBenigno, 2020)
keywords: chiefs of staff; heedful challenging; interpersonal learning; lower-power employees; loyalty; perspective-broadening; proxying; right-hand partner; unequal-power relationships; upward influence
research_question: How does a lower-power employee become a right-hand partner and influence a high-power organizational leader?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: heedful challenging; right-hand partner (a lower-power, strategic partner to a powerful leader who possesses inimitable credibility and an ability to discreetly influence the leader); unwavering loyalty; unequal-power relationships
- Process / phases: Phase 1 — Interpersonal learning (acquiring detailed knowledge about the leader through observing publicly and inquiring privately); Phase 2 — Proxying (serving as a deputy or representative of the leader by extending presence and synthesizing information); Phase 3 — Perspective-broadening (challenging the leader by identifying blind spots and illuminating implications of past or intended behavior, conducted in private settings)
- Relational foundation: continuous displays of loyalty enacted before Phase 1 and reinforced throughout each phase, communicating a lack of ulterior motives and alignment with the leader's agenda
- Defining characteristics of the right-hand partner role: strategic partnership (sparring discussions, synchrony, tensility), inimitable credibility, hidden influence
- Drawbacks: one-sided relationship (focus on amplifying the leader, deprioritizing own career), limited recognition (success defined by the success of others, no tangible output)
- Boundary conditions / breakdowns: process can stall at each phase — Phase 1 fails when leaders refuse to invest time; Phase 2 fails when leaders or colleagues resist the COS as a representative; Phase 3 fails when COSs lack confidence or leaders dismiss lower-power input

The process model proposes that right-hand partner emergence unfolds sequentially: lower-power employees first acquire deep knowledge of the leader's intra- and interpersonal processes through observation and probing inquiry, then leverage that knowledge by proxying for the leader in public — gathering ground-level intelligence others would not share with a powerful figure — and finally use the combined understanding of the leader's blind spots and the organization's perspectives to challenge the leader privately, framing critique as perspective-broadening rather than direct attack. Continuous displays of loyalty signal that the challenge is constructive and aligned with the leader's interests, enabling a "yes person" tendency and ingratiation to be replaced by candid, customized, hard-to-deliver feedback that the leader integrates without feeling threatened.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces right-hand partners to management scholarship and articulates a process model of their emergence through heedful challenging that addresses three theoretical gaps. First, it extends theory on unequal-power relationships by showing how lower-power employees can wield genuine upward influence without resorting to ingratiation or self-interested issue selling, defying the prevailing view that asymmetric-power dyads are transactional. Second, it operationalizes Galinsky and colleagues' theorizing on the synergistic effects of power and perspective-taking by demonstrating that what is incompatible within a single individual can be realized within a dyad — with the lower-power employee functioning as the perspective-taking catalyst. Third, it advances the dissent-based interacting literature (employee voice, upward influence, issue selling, person-focused feedback) by specifying when, where, and how to deliver hard-to-deliver content (personal critiques, contrarian views) so that it resonates rather than damages the relationship.
practical_implication: Lower-power employees wishing to voice alternative views to organizational leaders should invest extensive time in learning about the leader, frame dissent through perspective-broadening rather than direct criticism, and deliver such feedback in private to protect the leader's public credibility and signal continued loyalty. Powerful leaders who wish to access diverse perspectives, navigate complexity, or guard against the well-documented overconfidence and perspective-taking deficits of high-power roles should consider hiring a chief of staff and structuring the relationship to permit candid, private challenge. Organizations should recognize and legitimate the largely invisible, behind-the-scenes work of right-hand partners despite the difficulty of measuring its impact on company performance.
limitations: The sampling strategy — asking COSs to introduce their leaders — likely biased the leader sub-sample toward those with positive COS relationships, leaving more dysfunctional dyads underrepresented. The study focuses narrowly on the lived experience of right-hand partners themselves and on the corporate COS context, so it cannot fully characterize how the relationship is experienced or shaped by the leader, by demographic diversity within the dyad, or by other organizational members affected by it. Because power is relative, the authors cannot make claims about the amount of power right-hand partners hold over others, leaving open the possibility of a "dark side" in which a right-hand partner uses inauthentic loyalty to accumulate personal power.
future_research: Future work should examine right-hand partners outside the corporate COS context — including government, military, athletic teams, the arts, ghost writers, and graduate-student–advisor dyads — to test boundary conditions of the heedful challenging process. Additional research could investigate whether having a right-hand partner steers leaders away from unethical behavior, improves operational and firm performance, and reshapes the leader's identity, as well as how demographic homophily (especially gender, given the predominance of women in COS roles versus men in executive roles) shapes right-hand partner emergence. Longitudinal designs could trace how long individuals remain right-hand partners and what propels transitions out of the role, and could probe whether some right-hand partners pursue their own power agendas inauthentically.
abstract: Powerful leaders need to be challenged and pushed to consider uncontemplated perspectives. Research has indicated that employees in lower-power positions are best poised to challenge leaders, because these individuals better understand others' perspectives and have a different lens on day-to-day organizational issues compared to powerful leaders. While several well-known leaders rely on a lower-power employee (i.e., right-hand partner) to amplify their efforts, it is unclear how one becomes a right-hand partner or what this entails. Relying on qualitative data from 74 individuals (61 chiefs of staff and 13 leaders), we develop a process of heedful challenging: the process of presenting alternative perspectives to someone in an individualized manner. In this process, the lower-power employee utilizes their knowledge of the leader and their understanding of how organizational issues are impacting employees to thoughtfully challenge the leader by presenting differing perspectives and illuminating the implications of their behavior. We articulate how one becomes a right-hand partner through this process, present the defining characteristics and drawbacks of being a right-hand partner, and describe instances when this process goes awry. This work contributes to theory about unequal-power relationships and provides insight into how lower-power employees can broaden leader's perspectives.
apa_citation: Barnes, L. Y., Lacerenza, C. N., & Volpone, S. D. (2024). Becoming a right-hand partner: How lower-power employees heedfully challenge organizational leaders. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(3), 704-736. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1390

title: Organizational Ghosts: How “Ghostly Encounters” Enable Former Leaders to Influence Current Organizational Members
methods: Inductive, interpretivist qualitative field study of two merging family firms (Edwards Brothers and Malloy). Eight months of nonparticipant observation (21 days onsite); 43 semi-structured interviews using timeline elicitation; analysis of corporate histories, newsletters, and physical artifacts. Open and focused coding with theoretical coding to develop the organizational ghosts model.
industry: Book printing / book manufacturing
time_period: Field work conducted around the announced merger of Edwards Brothers and Malloy (data collected over an eight-month period prior to and shortly after the merger)
units: Two merging family-business book printers and their current and former members
n_sample: 43 interviews (20 current Edwards Brothers employees, 20 current Malloy employees, two interviews with former Malloy CEO Herb Upton, one with Malloy's first employee)
authors: Bednar, J. S.; Brown, J. A.
theories: legacy motive (Wade-Benzoni, Sondak, & Galinsky, 2010); social ghosts (Gergen, 2000); social identity theory of leadership (Hogg, 2001; Van Knippenberg & Hogg, 2003); stewardship theory (Davis, Schoorman, & Donaldson, 1997)
keywords: collective memory; ghostly encounters; imagined interactions; leader group prototypicality; legacy; organizational ghosts; social identity theory of leadership
research_question: How do specific leaders become embodied group prototypes whose influence endures after their organizational exit, and through what cognitive mechanisms do these "organizational ghosts" continue to shape current leaders and members?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: organizational ghosts (former members who become the ideal prototype of an organization's values and identity); leader group prototypicality; "ghostly encounters" (remembered or imagined cognitive engagements with departed leaders).
- Triggering conditions for becoming a ghost: extensive exposure (long tenure, visible roles) and admiration/adoration grounded in social judgments of extraordinary competence and warmth, leading to the leader being seen as the creator of the organization's values and identity.
- Institutionalization mechanisms: (a) associative learning that links physical and social cues to the leader; (b) perpetuated practices that continue "the way we do things around here"; (c) physical memory work via portraits, plaques, timelines, "Herb Posters," and preserved offices.
- Activation modes: intentional "summoning" by current members and organic "appearances" in the minds of remaining members.
- Emergent encounter types: (1) safeguarding encounters that foster accountability and stewardship; (2) comforting encounters that legitimize decisions and ease fears; (3) devaluing encounters that disparage alternative leadership styles or value systems.
- Boundary conditions: ghosts can "die" if institutionalization weakens or activation ceases; persistence depends on a loyal group of followers willing to keep the ghost alive.

The process unfolds as exceptionally exposed leaders, judged extraordinarily competent and warm, come to embody an organization's prototype. After exit, associative learning, perpetuated practices, and physical memory work institutionalize them so they can be cognitively activated. The resulting remembered or imagined encounters become comparators that current members use to evaluate themselves and others, thereby extending former leaders' influence across the organization.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces "organizational ghosts" and "ghostly encounters" as concepts that bridge the social identity theory of leadership and the literature on the legacy motive. It moves leader group prototypicality from an abstract, ahistorical construct to a historically embedded one, explains how nonfounders can supplant founders as prototypes, and addresses the "problem of reception" by focusing on legatees rather than legators. It also extends Gergen's "social ghosts" by showing how organizational ghosts come to embody the framework for defining morality, making decisions, and navigating change.
practical_implication: Current leaders and HR practitioners should recognize that former leaders continue to operate cognitively in current members' minds and shape decisions, identity, and resistance to change—particularly during transitions like mergers. Successors can leverage ghostly encounters to legitimize change while preserving valued continuities, but should also anticipate that ghosts can be invoked to disparage current leadership when actions diverge from the ghost's perceived values.
limitations: The two organizations were small, multigenerational family businesses with long-tenured employees, which may make ghostly encounters more salient than in other contexts. The authors did not observe Herb and Marty during their tenures, so accounts of how they became prototypes are reconstructed from informants. The merger context may have heightened the visibility of imagined encounters because of organizational loss and bereavement.
future_research: Future studies could examine ghostly encounters in larger, more dispersed, or non-family organizations and in periods of stability rather than upheaval; use longitudinal designs to study "ghosts in the making" prior to a leader's exit; explore negatively valenced ghosts, ghosts crossing organizational boundaries (e.g., Jack Welch's surrogates), and demographic variation (gender, race) in who becomes a ghost; and investigate the relative effectiveness of intentional summonings versus organic appearances and how depersonalized trust enables connection for members who never met the former leader.
abstract: Leadership research has assumed that leader group prototypicality enhances leadership influence during a leader’s tenure. Other leadership research has also assumed that leaders, especially founders, can transform their leadership influence into a legacy that will survive death and departure from the organization and continue to impact followers. However, to understand the historically embedded nature of leadership influence, it is critical to unpack how specific leaders can become group prototypes and influence followers after their departure. We address this opportunity by examining organizational ghosts—former organizational members who become the ideal prototype of an organization’s values and identity. Drawing on qualitative data from an organizational merger, our analysis reveals that admired and adored leaders with broad exposure became embodied organizational prototypes. After organizational exit, they became organizational ghosts, institutionalized by associative learning, perpetuated practices, and physical memory work. When activated—either intentionally or organically—these ghosts had “ghostly encounters” (remembered and imagined) with remaining members that safeguarded the organization, comforted organizational members, and devalued alternative value systems. We introduce the concept of organizational ghosts, explain how leaders can become ghosts, and specify remembered and imagined encounters as mechanisms through which former leaders can have enduring influence in organizations.
apa_citation: Bednar, J. S., & Brown, J. A. (2024). Organizational ghosts: How "ghostly encounters" enable former leaders to influence current organizational members. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(3), 737-766. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0622

title: Responding to Professional Knowledge Disruptions of Unmitigable Uncertainty: The Role of Emotions, Practices, and Moral Duty among COVID-19 Physicians
methods: Inductive embedded case study of one academic medical center (C-Hospital) in Milan, Lombardy; primary data are 81 semi-structured interviews (74 physicians and 7 managers; ~1,100 + 98 pages of transcript), triangulated with internal communications, organizational archival materials (195 items), 273 scientific publications by C-Hospital physicians, and 15 video presentations; analysis followed gradual abstraction with open and axial coding, temporal bracketing into three phases (Langley, 1999), and systematic coding of emotions cross-checked with the Italian LIWC dictionary
industry: Healthcare; an academic medical center (research-intensive 600-bed hospital) converted into a dedicated Covid-19 facility
time_period: First Covid-19 outbreak in Italy, February-June 2020 (interviews conducted starting July 2020 after ethics approval)
units: Hospital physicians from internal medicine, surgical, and emergency/intensive care specialties involved in caring for Covid-19 patients during the first outbreak
n_sample: 74 physicians (~80% of those active during the first outbreak) plus 7 managers; 81 semi-structured interviews total
authors: Compagni, Amelia; Cappellaro, Giulia; Nigam, Amit
theories: epistemic emotions (Morton, 2010; Pekrun & Linnenbrink-Garcia, 2014); moral emotions (Haidt, 2003a); professional moral duty (Abbott, 1983; Freidson, 1994); service orientation in professions (Goode, 1966; Moore, 1970); sociology of professions (Abbott, 1988; Freidson, 2001; Larson, 1977)
keywords: COVID-19; collegial work; epistemic emotions; humanistic work; knowledge disruptions; moral duty; moral emotions; professions; service-oriented practices; unmitigable uncertainty
research_question: When the knowledge base of an entire profession is made uncertain, how do professionals respond, in their workplace setting, to such knowledge disruption? When specialized knowledge fails, what else can professionals rely upon to face such uncertainty?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: knowledge disruption of unmitigable uncertainty; negative epistemic emotions (confusion, fear of the unknown, hopelessness, anguish); positive moral emotions (relief, elevation); professional sense of moral duty (to act, to do good, to do well); service-oriented practices of collegial and humanistic work; failed knowledge-based strategies
- Triggering condition: a societal challenge (Covid-19) that, absent an alternative body of knowledge, makes professionals' knowledge inadequate at all steps of the inferential process (diagnosis, prognosis, treatment) and resists knowledge-based mitigation
- Phases: Phase I — uncertainty about defining the imminent knowledge disruption (confusion -> fear; moral duty to act; initiating service-oriented practices; relief). Phase II — uncertainty in identifying solutions and forecasting outcomes (hopelessness; moral duty to do good; prioritizing service-oriented practices; elevation). Phase III — uncertainty about finding solution to the knowledge disruption (anguish; moral duty to do well; consolidating service-oriented practices in professional work)
- Practices of collegial work: improvising collaborative solutions to common problems; creating peer synergistic relations that deemphasize hierarchy; making clinical decisions based on a collective experiential logic; consolidating cross-specialty professional ties
- Practices of humanistic work: volunteering for mundane tasks for the sake of colleagues and patients; seeing the patient as a person; removing impersonality and connecting empathically with patients and families; keeping empathic communication with patients and relatives in daily practice
- Emergent outcome: service-oriented practices, reinforced by positive moral emotions, become embedded in professionals' day-to-day work even after the acute phase ends

The process model proposes that when uncertainty disrupts every step of the inferential process and cannot be redressed through knowledge-based means or by adapting an alternative body of knowledge, professionals experience a sequence of negative epistemic emotions while a heightened professional sense of moral duty motivates them to engage in service-oriented practices of collegial and humanistic work. These practices, undertaken for the benefit of others rather than to expand or defend specialized knowledge, generate positive moral emotions (relief, elevation) that compensate for negative epistemic emotions and reinforce continued engagement. Repeated positive moral reinforcement leads professionals to consolidate and embed collegial and humanistic practices in their professional work, marking a shift away from a purely cognitive-rational, knowledge-centric model of professionalism toward one in which service orientation is treated as core.
theoretical_contribution: The study theorizes a distinctive category of "knowledge disruptions of unmitigable uncertainty" — characterized by uncertainty that progressively impairs all steps of the professional inferential process and cannot be repaired through traditional knowledge-based strategies, even though no alternative body of knowledge has emerged to threaten jurisdiction. It introduces epistemic emotions (confusion, fear of the unknown, hopelessness, anguish) as a collectively shared dynamic that constitutes such disruptions, and theorizes service-oriented practices of collegial and humanistic work — motivated by professional moral duty and reinforced by positive moral emotions — as a viable alternative response that diverges from the cognitive-calculative responses of knowledge expansion and jurisdictional politics emphasized in prior literature on professions.
practical_implication: For professionals facing complex societal challenges that overwhelm their specialized knowledge, sustaining engagement and producing a viable response may depend less on knowledge expansion or jurisdictional defense than on activating the profession's normative commitments to service. Hospitals and other professional organizations should make space for, and legitimate, collegial cross-specialty collaboration and humanistic patient relations as central rather than ancillary practices, recognizing that they can generate positive moral feedback that helps professionals persist amid uncertainty. The findings imply that effective coordinated responses to grand challenges may emerge when professionals weaken knowledge-based status boundaries and reorient around shared moral duties.
limitations: The setting is highly professionalized — Italian academic-medicine physicians with strong knowledge identification, a codified Hippocratic oath, and immediate visibility of failure (lives lost) — so the strength and specific form of the negative epistemic emotions and the salience of moral duty may not generalize to less professionalized occupations or to professions whose work failures are less visible. Field-access restrictions during the pandemic forced reliance on retrospective interviews rather than direct observation of Steering Committee meetings or wards, raising the possibility of hindsight bias even though the authors argue that interviews offered a uniquely reflective space. The observation period was limited to the first wave, so the durability of consolidated service-oriented practices over time was not directly verified.
future_research: Future work could systematically explore professionals' role in producing responses to societal challenges and the conditions under which knowledge disruptions of unmitigable uncertainty are central features of those challenges. Comparative research in less professionalized occupations (e.g., animal-shelter workers, journalists) and in occupational groups for whom collegial and humanistic work is closer to baseline (e.g., nurses, social workers, teachers) could illuminate how epistemic emotions interact with or are displaced by other emotions such as compassion and empathy. Longitudinal studies should examine whether consolidated service-oriented practices persist over time and whether moving away from a knowledge-focused professionalism erodes or reshapes professional status vis-a-vis other groups.
abstract: Drawing on an in-depth study of physicians facing the Covid-19 pandemic in Italy in 2020, we advance theory on how professionals in their workplace respond to knowledge disruptions associated with complex societal challenges that undermine the adequacy of their knowledge base to solve professional problems. We show that, in the context of uncertainty generated by the knowledge disruption and the inability to mitigate this uncertainty through typical knowledge-based strategies, professionals experience a trail of negative epistemic emotions. Despite these negative epistemic emotions, and motivated by a heightened sense of moral duty, professionals engage in service-oriented practices of collegial and humanistic work that depart from the knowledge-centric practices of their usual work. We detail how the repeated development of positive moral emotions when performing such practices leads professionals to ultimately consolidate and embed service-oriented practices in their professional work. Our study contributes to the literature on professions and organizations by theorizing the distinctive category of knowledge disruptions of unmitigable uncertainty and by uncovering the microlevel dynamics and mechanisms that sustain professionals' responses.
apa_citation: Compagni, A., Cappellaro, G., & Nigam, A. (2024). Responding to professional knowledge disruptions of unmitigable uncertainty: The role of emotions, practices, and moral duty among COVID-19 physicians. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(3), 829-861. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0697

title: Balancing Professional Autonomy and Managerial Goals amid Broad Technology Adoption Pressures: Intraprofessional Segmentation at a Finnish School
methods: Inductive ethnographic case study of a single Finnish school (pseudonym 'Mainland'), with the first author conducting 16 months of fieldwork (~10 hours/week during the 2018-2019 academic year, ~5 hours/week in fall 2019); data include real-time field notes from classroom observations, teachers' meetings, field trips, and school events, 22 ethnographic interviews with Mainland participants, 16 additional interviews with teachers and managers from other Finnish schools for generalizability checks, and archival materials including national core curricula; iterative analysis with open coding, focused coding, analytic induction (Becker, 1998), grounded theory principles (Glaser & Strauss, 1967), and member checks (Lincoln & Guba, 1985)
industry: K-12 and high school education ('teaching school' run privately under a university; grades 1-12, also trains teacher trainees)
time_period: 16-month ethnography spanning the 2018-2019 academic year and fall 2019, situated within the post-2014/2015 Finnish educational policy reforms emphasizing digital technology adoption
units: Teachers (specialized in one or more subjects across grades 1-12) and managers (three principals: basic education, high school, and a head principal) at the school 'Mainland'
n_sample: 31 teachers coded for technological practices (Table 2) plus three principals; 22 ethnographic interviews at Mainland and 16 additional interviews at other Finnish schools for generalizability
authors: Koljonen, Tomi; Chan, Curtis K.
theories: literature on work, technology, and occupations (Barley, 1986; Bailey & Leonardi, 2015; Bechky, 2020); middle-ground approaches to professional autonomy and managerial control (Edmondson et al., 2001; Kellogg, 2022; Anthony, 2021); practice theory (Feldman & Orlikowski, 2011; Schatzki, 2001, 2012); sociology of professions and intraprofessional segments (Abbott, 1988; Bucher & Strauss, 1961; Freidson, 1984)
keywords: broad technology adoption pressures; deepening practices; differentiating practices; formalizing practices; intraprofessional segmentation; managerial goals; occupations and technology; permitting practices; professional autonomy; reinforcing practices
research_question: How is the tension between professional autonomy and managerial interests navigated in an organization facing broad technology adoption pressures—that is, expectations that lack precise prescriptions, concrete success criteria, and formalized sanctions?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: broad technology adoption pressures (here, Finnish national curricula reforms championing "digitalization" and the "digital leap" without specifying technologies, success criteria, or sanctions) that managers nonetheless perceive as consequential for organizational standing
- Key tension: managers' goal of adopting technology vs. teachers' protected professional autonomy in a high-status school
- Two divergent technology-related approaches within one profession: (1) reconfiguring practices = positive technological frames + additive technology use; (2) restraining practices = critical technological frames + substitutional technology use
- Differentiating practices (allowing divergence): permitting practices, including managers' emphatic framing, encouraging discretion, and peers' accepting differentiation
- Reinforcing practices (furthering and stabilizing divergence): formalizing practices (managers allocating technology-related roles and resources, e.g., digital tutors); deepening practices (external engagement by enthusiasts and hiving-off of technology-related tasks from restrainers to reconfigurers); legitimating practices (managers and peers affirming the role specialization)
- Emergent outcome: intraprofessional segmentation as an organizational middle ground that permits some adoption while preserving the autonomy of skeptical professionals; relations between differentiating and reinforcing practices are temporally overlapping, iterative, and recursive

The process model proposes that under broad technology adoption pressures, managers and professionals together generate intraprofessional segmentation through two intertwined bundles of practices. Differentiating practices first allow the open divergence of technological frames and uses within one occupational community: managers emphatically frame technology adoption as important while encouraging professional discretion, and peers explicitly accept the resulting heterogeneity rather than treating it as deviance or conflict. Reinforcing practices then formalize, reorganize, and strengthen this differentiation: managers create specialized technology roles and channel resources to enthusiastic professionals, those professionals take on external engagement and absorb hived-off technology tasks from skeptical colleagues, and both managers and peers legitimate the resulting division of labor. The two bundles run in parallel and feed back on each other, producing a relatively stable workplace settlement in which technological adoption proceeds through some professionals without coercing others.
theoretical_contribution: The study introduces broad technology adoption pressures as a distinct organizational condition (lacking precise prescriptions, success criteria, and formalized sanctions) and theorizes intraprofessional segmentation as a novel middle-ground response that prior literature has overlooked because it focused on the implementation of specific technologies and on cross-occupational rather than within-profession variation. By unpacking differentiating and reinforcing practices, the paper extends the sociology of professions (Abbott, 1988; Bucher & Strauss, 1961) by showing that intraprofessional segments need not be a source of conflict but can be actively permitted, formalized, and legitimated by managers and peers, repositioning intraoccupational variation as a supportive rather than adversarial feature of professional organization.
practical_implication: Managers facing broad pressures to adopt expansive technological categories (e.g., digital transformation, AI) need not pursue uniform adoption across all professionals. Instead, they can permit and reinforce specialization within a single profession—creating dedicated roles and resources for enthusiastic professionals, accepting that others will continue more conservative practices, and legitimating both choices—to achieve meaningful technology uptake without triggering the resistance and demoralization that come with coercive implementation in autonomy-protective settings such as schools, hospitals, and other professional organizations.
limitations: The findings come from a single high-status Finnish "teaching school" run privately under a university, which may make intraprofessional segmentation more available than in lower-status or more tightly regulated organizations and may bound generalizability. The school setting also features less interprofessional competition than hospitals or engineering firms, and the relatively similar status of teachers within the school removed the kind of status-based dynamics that drive hiving-off in other studies. The 16-month observation window cannot speak to whether the apparently functional segmentation remains supportive over the longer term or eventually becomes adversarial.
future_research: Future work could examine the relationship between organizational status and the likelihood of intraprofessional segmentation emerging as a response to broad technology adoption pressures, and study how segmentation evolves where higher- and lower-status professional groups work together on new technologies. Researchers could also follow intraprofessional segmentation longitudinally to see whether it stabilizes, becomes adversarial, or produces broader occupational change such as jurisdictional battles or spin-offs of new occupations. Finally, scholars should pay closer attention to within-profession variance in technology use and to the explicit permitting practices through which such divergence is legitimated.
abstract: Managers often desire to see their organization adopt new technologies but depend on autonomous professionals to use such technologies in practice. In seeking technology adoption, organizations thus must find a middle ground between professional autonomy and managerial goals. Extant scholarship has examined such middle grounds but has focused on adoptions of specific technologies. However, amid broad technology adoption pressures—expectations on organizations lacking precise prescriptions, success criteria, and sanctions—novel middle-ground approaches might emerge. Our 16-month ethnography of a Finnish school indicates that intraprofessional segmentation—specialization of work tasks within a professional community—can allow organizations to navigate technology adoption while balancing professional autonomy and managerial goals. This segmentation supported managers' and certain teachers' enthusiasm about new technologies at work, without undermining other teachers' autonomy. This segmentation emerged, persisted, and intensified through two processes. First, it emerged through differentiating practices, which allowed a divergence of what teachers said and did regarding technology. Second, this segmentation persisted and intensified through reinforcing practices, which furthered differentiation by formalizing, reorganizing, and strengthening it. Our study contributes to scholarship on occupations and technology by showing how intraprofessional segmentation can help organizations align technology with professional work amid broad technology adoption pressures.
apa_citation: Koljonen, T., & Chan, C. K. (2024). Balancing professional autonomy and managerial goals amid broad technology adoption pressures: Intraprofessional segmentation at a Finnish school. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(3), 798-828. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.1093

title: External Disruption Following CEO Succession: How Human and Social Capital Changes May Attract Competitive Attacks
methods: Negative binomial regression with Huber-White robust standard errors and year fixed effects on a panel of 175 unique CEO succession events at S&P 500 firms (2010-2018). PSPED operationalized as Euclidean distance between predecessor and successor on eight binary professional-experience attributes (functional background, military, international, outside-board, founder). Two-stage extended negative binomial models with instrumental variables (ranked PSPED differential within industry; new independent directors) address endogeneity; supplementary Poisson, Heckman two-stage, and matched-sample analyses test robustness. Competitive attacks coded from RavenPack co-mention data verified via Factiva.
industry: S&P 500 firms across multiple industries (multi-industry, identified by four-digit SIC code in Mergent Intellect)
units: CEO succession events at focal firms (firm-year observations in the post-succession year)
n_sample: 175 unique succession events
authors: Lee, E. Y.; Tsai, W.
theories: CEO succession literature (Berns & Klarner, 2017; Shen & Cannella, 2002; Zhang & Rajagopalan, 2010); competitive dynamics (Chen & Miller, 2012; Smith, Grimm, & Gannon, 1992); executive human capital theory (Becker, 1964; Bailey & Helfat, 2003); social capital theory (Nahapiet & Ghoshal, 1998; Adler & Kwon, 2002)
keywords: CEO succession; board chair retention; competitive attacks; external disruption; human capital; insider-outsider successor; predecessor-successor professional experience differential (PSPED); preemption; social capital
research_question: "How do internal disruptions due to human or social capital changes in the CEO position lead to external disruption by attracting competitors' attacks?" The paper investigates how human and social capital changes accompanying CEO succession motivate rivals to launch competitive attacks against the focal firm, and whether retaining the predecessor as board chair attenuates these external disruptions.
mechanism_summary: The mechanism: large PSPED signals strategic discontinuity (different "thought worlds") that creates short-run productivity drag (an exploitable vulnerability) and a longer-run repositioning that threatens rivals (a preemption motive); outsider status signals weak internal social capital, predicting power struggles and infighting (an additional vulnerability). Because human and social capital "mutually influence" one another, change in only one form is less attractive to attackers than change in both. Retaining the predecessor as chair preserves the focal firm's human capital (constraining the successor's strategic discretion) and dampens rivals' incentive to exploit PSPED-driven disruption — but does not similarly buffer social-capital disruption.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops a theory of *external* disruption following CEO succession, extending a literature that has confined succession's disruptive consequences to internal effects (performance, turnover) and largely ignored competitor responses. It introduces the PSPED as a new construct capturing the magnitude of human-capital change, links corporate governance to competitive dynamics by showing that a governance event drives rival attacks, and reframes the practice of retaining the predecessor as board chair from a discretion-limiting or symbolic gesture into a substantive human-capital preservation strategy that wards off competitive attacks.
practical_implication: Boards seeking organizational change through CEO succession should consider candidates who change only the firm's human or its social capital structure, not both — appointing an outsider who is also vastly different from the predecessor maximizes external disruption. When meaningful disruption is necessary, retaining the predecessor as board chair can dampen competitor attacks following a high-PSPED succession by signaling continuity and constraining the successor's discretion to enact disruptive strategic change.
limitations: The study examines only the competitive landscape, leaving other forms of external disruption (regulators, social movements, activist investors) unexplored. The authors cannot directly observe managers' thought processes about whether a focal succession is perceived as an exploitable opportunity or a future threat to preempt. The PSPED measure places equal weight on different professional-experience attributes, ignoring the substantive content of specific functional transitions (e.g., marketing-to-law versus marketing-to-finance).
future_research: Future studies could (a) extend the disruption framework to other external stakeholders (regulators, social movements, ESG-focused activists) and to industry-wide succession waves; (b) qualitatively or experimentally identify the specific cues that lead competitors to assess a CEO succession as an opportunity or a future threat; (c) investigate preemption more broadly as a competitive behavior, given the scarcity of empirical work; and (d) examine whether PSPED's effects vary by the substantive content of professional-experience changes (e.g., functional area combinations, functional vs. industry experience).
abstract: Competitors may perceive a focal firm's CEO succession as creating an exploitable opportunity as well as a future threat to preempt. Drawing from theories of executive human and social capital, we suggest that anticipation of internal disruption stemming from human and social capital changes in a CEO succession may lead to external disruption for the focal firm. In our sample of S&P 500 firms between 2010 and 2018, we find that competitors are more likely to attack the focal firm when the successor's professional experience differs drastically from that of the predecessor and less likely to do so when the successor is an insider. We also find that competitors' attacks following a succession event can be minimized by limiting changes to only one form of capital (i.e., human or social). Moreover, retaining the predecessor as board chair after the succession could be an effective human capital preservation strategy for reducing competitors' attacks when the difference in professional experience between the predecessor and successor is high. However, retaining the predecessor as board chair may not have the same effect when it comes to social capital. Our research highlights the competitive implications of CEO succession, offering new insight into disruption.
apa_citation: Lee, E. Y., & Tsai, W. (2024). External disruption following CEO succession: How human and social capital changes may attract competitive attacks. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(3), 625-647. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0448
