title: Happy Talk: Is Common Diversity Rhetoric Effective Diversity Rhetoric?
methods: Multistudy, multimethod design: Study 1 archival content analysis of Fortune 100 diversity webpages with logistic regression; Studies 2 and 3 online experiments with manipulation of rhetoric type and bootstrapped mediation analyses; Study 4 two-wave time-separated survey with regression-based mediation.
industry: Fortune 100 corporations (Study 1); working adults across industries recruited via Prolific Academic (Studies 2-4)
time_period: Webpages coded summer 2019; diversity rankings gathered fall 2019; experimental and survey data collected for Studies 2-4 (specific dates not reported)
units: Organizations (Study 1); individual leaders/employees (Studies 2-4)
n_sample: 100 organizations / 102 webpages (Study 1); 136 working adults with supervisory experience (Study 2); 503 working adults (Study 3); 893 working adults across two survey waves (Study 4)
authors: Leslie, L. M.; Flynn, E.; Foster-Gimbel, O. A.; Manchester, C. F.
theories: goal-setting theory (Locke & Latham, 2002, 2019); happy talk (Bell & Hartmann, 2007; Bergsieker et al., 2012); persuasion / argument strength (Petty & Cacioppo, 1986); psychology of the self / self-presentation (Banaji & Prentice, 1994; Leary, 2007)
keywords: argument strength; contingent-diversity rhetoric; diversity effort; diversity rhetoric; fear of appearing prejudiced; goal-setting theory; happy talk; value-in-diversity rhetoric
research_question: Does the diversity rhetoric leaders use most commonly (value-in-diversity rhetoric) actually motivate the highest levels of employee diversity effort, or does a less common rhetoric type that acknowledges diversity's challenges (contingent-diversity rhetoric) prove more effective—and what psychological mechanisms explain any such descriptive-prescriptive paradox?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the psychology of the self, leaders fear contingent rhetoric will make them appear prejudiced because it includes subtly negative diversity content, leading them to default to uniformly positive value rhetoric ("happy talk"). Drawing on goal-setting theory, contingent rhetoric uniquely raises perceived diversity-goal difficulty, which signals to employees that personal effort, attention, and persistence are required to achieve a difficult collective goal—counteracting social loafing. Both rhetoric types boost diversity effort via perceived argument strength, but only contingent rhetoric activates the goal-difficulty pathway, producing higher overall effort.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces the new construct of contingent-diversity rhetoric and demonstrates a descriptive-prescriptive paradox: the most common diversity rhetoric is not the most effective. It advances diversity theory by identifying self-presentation concerns (fear of appearing prejudiced) as the mechanism explaining leaders' rhetoric choices, and identifies argument strength and perceived goal difficulty as the dual mechanisms explaining when and why diversity rhetoric motivates employees. Secondary contributions extend self-presentation theory by showing happy talk applies to subtly negative content, and extend goal-setting theory to collective organizational diversity goals.
practical_implication: Leaders can increase employees' diversity effort, and help organizations become more diverse and inclusive, by shifting from value-in-diversity rhetoric to contingent-diversity rhetoric—emphasizing that realizing diversity's benefits requires overcoming its challenges. Because leaders' fears of appearing prejudiced are largely unfounded (contingent rhetoric does not increase perceived leader prejudice), interventions should reassure leaders and educate them on the comparative effectiveness of contingent rhetoric, particularly framing it around achieving important organizational diversity goals.
limitations: Findings may not generalize to all samples or settings; the studies relied largely on U.S. online panel samples and simulated organizational contexts in the experiments, which raises possibilities of demand effects despite design safeguards. Study 1 uses webpage rhetoric and external rankings as proxies rather than direct measures of leader rhetoric and employee effort, and Study 4's correlational design measured rhetoric and mechanisms at the same time wave. The hypothesized mechanisms were largely supported but additional unmeasured mechanisms may also operate.
future_research: Future work should investigate boundary conditions such as whether the relative effectiveness of contingent versus value rhetoric varies by diversity-initiative maturity (new vs. established), the specific rationale invoked (business case, moral case), and additional rhetoric types (e.g., colorblind, challenge-only). Researchers should also examine whether integrating diversity's challenges into related constructs—diversity climates, pro-diversity beliefs, and multicultural ideologies—similarly amplifies their effects on diversity outcomes.
abstract: Despite their prevalence, diversity initiatives do not necessarily motivate employees to facilitate diversity goals. We advance understanding of diversity rhetoric—defined as how leaders talk about diversity and its effects—as a tool for motivating employees to foster diversity and inclusion. Prior work has investigated rhetoric that emphasizes diversity in organizations is necessarily beneficial (value-in-diversity rhetoric), which is puzzling given the reality that diversity can have positive or negative consequences. We introduce the construct of contingent-diversity rhetoric, which emphasizes that diversity is beneficial if its challenges are overcome, and thus captures the reality of diversity's effects. Drawing from the psychology of the self, we theorize that leaders use contingent-diversity rhetoric less commonly than value-in-diversity rhetoric, due to fear of appearing prejudiced. Drawing from the psychology of employee motivation, we theorize that contingent-diversity rhetoric results in more diversity effort among employees than value-in-diversity rhetoric does because contingent rhetoric increases perceptions that diversity goals are difficult to achieve. Four multimethod studies support the proposed descriptive–prescriptive paradox: contingent-diversity rhetoric is descriptively less common, but prescriptively more effective, than value-in-diversity rhetoric. Our research advances theory on fostering diversity and inclusion in organizations and suggests that leaders can increase employees' diversity effort by changing the way they talk about diversity.
apa_citation: Leslie, L. M., Flynn, E., Foster-Gimbel, O. A., & Manchester, C. F. (2024). Happy talk: Is common diversity rhetoric effective diversity rhetoric? *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(3), 595-624. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1402

title: What Makes Research Collaborations Successful? Advice from AMJ Authors
methods: Editorial based on a survey distributed to a randomly picked set of authors of co-authored papers accepted by AMJ between January 1, 2019 and May 16, 2024 (548 contacted, 60 responded, 29 with international collaboration experience), combined with descriptive analysis of authorship patterns across 364 articles and 1,084 authors.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Ravasi, Davide; Zhu, Jing; Wan, William; Dorobantu, Sinziana; Gruber, Marc
theories: research collaboration framework (Katz & Martin, 1997)
keywords: AMJ authors; authorship order; co-authorship; international collaboration; research collaboration; research teams; scholarly partnerships; team science
research_question: What enables collaborative projects published in AMJ to overcome the challenges of research collaboration, and what lessons learned from recent AMJ authors can help prospective co-authors take full advantage of the benefits of joint projects while mitigating their risks?
mechanism_summary:
- People matter (more than project or location): Get to know potential partners first; work with people you trust and get along well with; ensure work styles and ethics fit; choose people equally motivated to complete the project; reflect on your own unique value proposition.
- Set clear expectations early on: Clarify expected outcomes, roles and contributions, leadership and authorship order, and working style/timelines/constraints/availabilities.
- Manage the process: Establish clear schedules, milestones, and deliverables; communicate frequently and openly; make the best of available technologies (Zoom, cloud storage, online docs) while preserving in-person meetings for breakthroughs.
- Ensure shared accountability for data collection and analysis: Enable equal access to data; share contextual knowledge; "trust but verify" in light of elevated concerns about data integrity.
- Be mindful of differences and open minded: Be open to alternative viewpoints and approaches; treat collaboration as an opportunity to question and revisit assumptions; avoid ethnocentrism in international teams.
- Manage strategically your portfolio of collaborations: Focus efforts on big ideas; search for synergies among projects; balance senior and same-stage co-authors.

The editorial draws on responses from a survey of 60 AMJ authors (drawn from 1,084 authors of 364 articles accepted between January 2019 and May 2024) to articulate six bundles of practices that distinguish successful collaborations. Descriptive analysis shows 53% of manuscripts resulted from cross-country collaborations and 41% from cross-continent collaborations, with COVID-19 producing a delayed and lasting decline in international collaborations. The advice synthesizes practitioner wisdom rather than testing a causal model.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial offers a practitioner-oriented synthesis of what makes research collaborations successful in management scholarship, organizing AMJ author wisdom into six interrelated practice bundles spanning partner selection, expectation setting, process management, data accountability, openness to differences, and portfolio strategy. It also documents empirical patterns in AMJ co-authorship over 2019–2024, including the disproportionate and persistent impact of COVID-19 on international collaborations despite the rise of remote-work technologies.
practical_implication: The editorial provides concrete, actionable advice to current and prospective AMJ authors — especially junior scholars — on how to choose collaborators, set expectations about authorship order and roles up front, manage remote and in-person interactions, and curate a balanced portfolio of collaborations. Table 1 summarizes the advice as a usable checklist, and the editors highlight the heightened importance of shared data accountability given growing concerns about research integrity.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Not reported in paper

title: The Outcomes of Cross-Category Career Moves: How Cross-Industry Mobility and Industry Prestige Jointly Impact Executive Compensation
methods: Matched-sample quasi-experimental design using one-to-one (k2k) coarsened exact matching (CEM) on individual-, firm-, and pre-pay covariates; difference-in-differences regressions of log total compensation (TDC1) on industry-changer x post-job-change interaction with three-year pre/post window; moderation by status-leap and status-leak dummies derived from Gallup industry prestige rankings; robustness checks with propensity score matching, full unmatched panel, continuous status-difference measures, executive fixed effects, and industry-relatedness controls (Hoberg-Phillips text-based classification).
industry: S&P 1500 firms across NAICS 2-digit business sectors (Gallup-rated industries)
time_period: Job changes 1999-2014 (compensation observations 1996-2017 for 3-year window; 1994-2019 for 5-year robustness)
units: Voluntary inter-firm executive job changes (CEOs and non-CEOs)
n_sample: 922 matched job changers (461 industry-changers, 461 within-industry movers); 6,323 executive-year observations; status moderation subsample of 232 leapers and 229 leakers
authors: Sabanci, H.; Elvira, M. M.
theories: categorical imperative / category-spanning theory (Zuckerman, 1999; Hsu, Hannan, & Koçak, 2009); category status / industry prestige (Sharkey, 2014; Delmestri & Greenwood, 2016); signaling theory (Spence, 1973); status-attainment theory (Bidwell et al., 2015; Rider & Tan, 2015; Podolny, 2005)
keywords: categorical affiliation; category status; cross-industry mobility; executive compensation; industry prestige; status leak; status leap; voluntary executive mobility
research_question: How does a change in industry impact compensation in executives’ voluntary job mobility, and how do status differences between origin and destination industries jointly moderate the demand- and supply-side mechanisms that govern the cross-category compensation discount?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Executive age, gender, tenure, CEO indicator, prior pay rank, prior compensation, firm ROA, sales, debt, capital expenditures, profit margin, sales growth, board size, board independence, CEO duality, board directorship, network size, and gender/industry diversity; industry-relatedness (Hoberg-Phillips) in alternative-explanation tests.

Demand-side audiences (hiring firms and search intermediaries) discount industry-changers because cross-category moves trigger ambiguity about how an executive’s attributes transfer to a new domain and raise future-commitment concerns about flight risk. Supply-side candidates accept smaller monetary premiums because they ascribe value to acquiring experience in a new industry, expanding networks, and capturing nonpecuniary rewards such as prestige. Industry-status differentials condition both mechanisms: leaks (moves down the status hierarchy) attenuate audience ambiguity/commitment penalties and dampen candidate willingness to accept lower pay, while leaps (moves up the status hierarchy) amplify audience scrutiny and candidate willingness to trade compensation for prestige.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces categorical status as a new contingency in the categorical-affiliation–labor-market-outcomes relationship, joining the vertical (status) and horizontal (category) dimensions of social evaluation. It separates industry-changing from generalist/specialist profiles and unpacks both demand- and supply-side mechanisms behind cross-category career discounts. By conceptualizing industry status as a novel reward form that can substitute for monetary compensation, it advances status-attainment research beyond firm-level status comparisons within a single industry and offers an integrative framework linking category dynamics, status hierarchies, and executive mobility outcomes.
practical_implication: Boards, compensation committees, and executive search firms should recognize that cross-industry hires accept systematically lower pay than within-industry movers, but this discount is contingent on relative industry prestige—status leapers will accept the steepest discounts and status leakers little or none. Executives weighing voluntary moves can use industry prestige as a non-pecuniary reward when negotiating compensation packages, while firms in lower-prestige sectors should expect to pay a premium to attract talent from higher-prestige industries.
limitations: Executive mobility is endogenous and lacks an exogenous shock, so causal interpretation requires caution despite the matching, comparative-trends, and rich-controls strategies the authors employ. The design cannot directly observe recruiter and candidate cognitions, so the authors cannot definitively partition the observed compensation effects between demand-side and supply-side mechanisms. The S&P 1500 setting contains very few women and underrepresented racial groups, precluding analysis of how ascriptive characteristics interact with the proposed mechanisms.
future_research: Future work could examine whether the mechanisms differ across audiences and candidates—particularly for women and ethnic minorities, where ascriptive characteristics may shape recruiters’ assessments of agency and ambiguity. Researchers could exploit experimental designs or natural experiments to isolate demand- versus supply-side contributions to the cross-industry discount, extend the framework to more diverse empirical contexts beyond U.S. S&P 1500 firms, and investigate how industry status interacts with other forms of categorical stigma or with occupational status in shaping labor-market outcomes.
abstract: Identifying executives’ industry affiliation with categorical membership, this study examines how moving to a different industry impacts mobility-compensation outcomes. On the demand side, we propose that audience ambiguity and commitment concerns regarding cross-category moves limit the potential compensation of industry-changing executives. On the supply side, we argue that executives might accept smaller monetary rewards in exchange for acquiring experience in a new domain. Since category status also affects audience evaluations of candidates and candidates’ desire to affiliate with a specific social category, we further hypothesize that both demand and supply mechanisms are moderated by status differences between an executive’s origin and destination industries. Our analysis of voluntary mobility and compensation patterns of S&P 1500 executives supports these arguments: industry-changing executives realize lower compensation than closely matched within-industry movers. As expected, the compressing effect of changing industry on compensation is contingent upon status differences across industries. Executives who transition to higher-status industries face more stringent compensation discounts, while those moving to lower-status industries experience similar compensation variation as within-industry movers. Our study advances category and status-attainment research by incorporating the influence of industry prestige on career outcomes of cross-category moves, while casting light on the individual consequences of executive mobility.
apa_citation: Sabanci, H., & Elvira, M. M. (2024). The outcomes of cross-category career moves: How cross-industry mobility and industry prestige jointly impact executive compensation. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(3), 767-797. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1423

title: Job Insecurity and Well-Being: Integrating Life History and Transactional Stress Theories
methods: Two preregistered studies. Study 1: multisource, five-wave longitudinal field study during COVID-19 in India with bi-weekly surveys over 2.5 months; multilevel (three-level) linear regression with cases nested within employees and supervisors, person-centered time-variant predictors, grand mean-centered moderator and controls; moderated path analysis using bootstrap with 5,000 resamples for moderated indirect effects; lagged and reciprocal-effect specifications. Study 2: preregistered quasi-experiment in India and the United States via Qualtrics Panel manipulating job insecurity vs. job security through guided reflection task; structural equation modeling with bootstrap (5,000 resamples) for mediation.
industry: White-collar mid-level employees in small and medium-sized firms across finance, manufacturing, information technology, health care and social assistance, retail trade, and education sectors
time_period: Study 1: five waves over 2.5 months at the height of the COVID-19 crisis; Study 2: cross-sectional quasi-experiment
units: Study 1: employees nested under supervisors in small/medium-sized firms; Study 2: individual employees recruited through Qualtrics Panel
n_sample: Study 1: 449 employees nested under 147 supervisors (2,207 observations); Study 2: 1,651 employees (818 United States; 833 India)
authors: Sirola, Nina
theories: evolutionary life history theory (Belsky, Steinberg, & Draper, 1991; Ellis & Del Giudice, 2014, 2019); transactional stress theory (Lazarus & Folkman, 1984)
keywords: childhood financial standing; emotional exhaustion; engagement; inequality reproduction; job insecurity; job loss risk; life history theory; sense of control; transactional stress theory; well-being
research_question: How does perceived job insecurity differentially shape well-being (engagement and emotional exhaustion) and downstream job loss risk as a function of employees' childhood financial standing, and through what psychological mechanism (sense of control)? Can job insecurity act as an invisible mechanism that reproduces childhood inequalities in adulthood, even when employees from poorer and wealthier backgrounds occupy comparable jobs and current financial situations?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Employee current financial standing (and its interaction with job insecurity), years of leadership experience, number of employees fired due to crisis, wave

The integrated mechanism predicts that the early-life programming of the stress response system (sensitized by harshness during childhood, proxied by family financial standing) conditions how individuals appraise threat in adulthood. Under salient job insecurity, employees from poorer backgrounds experience a drop in sense of control, producing lower engagement and higher emotional exhaustion; these well-being deficits are then read by supervisors as signs of declining contributions, amplifying job loss risk. Employees from wealthier backgrounds are buffered, and at the very high end of childhood financial standing job insecurity even raises sense of control, producing an energizing response (higher engagement, lower exhaustion) that lowers job loss risk. Differences only emerge when job insecurity is salient, consistent with the conditioned-stress-response logic.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces a novel evolutionary life history perspective to organizational research on job insecurity and well-being, identifying childhood financial standing as a major individual difference that stratifies how job insecurity impacts well-being and, through it, job loss risk — even among employees in comparable current jobs and financial situations. By integrating life history theory with transactional stress theory, it expands the explanatory power of extant job insecurity models and reframes job insecurity as an invisible mechanism that reproduces childhood inequalities. The integration also opens organizational stress research more broadly to life history theory, beyond its prior focus on temporal-discounting behaviors, by showing that conditioned stress responses shape reactions to a key work-related stressor.
practical_implication: The findings highlight a hitherto invisible and likely large-scale cost of job insecurity for public policy: rising precariousness systematically disadvantages employees born into poorer families, undermining performance-relevant well-being and amplifying their job loss risk in ways that conflict with core values of meritocracy and socioeconomic mobility. Within organizations, the supplementary analyses suggest that managerial "opportunity framing" — emphasizing opportunities arising from contextual adversity — can attenuate the problematic effects of perceived job insecurity on engagement among workers from poorer backgrounds, although its buffering effect on emotional exhaustion was not significant.
limitations: The study uses self-reported measures of childhood financial standing as a proxy for childhood environmental harshness, and Study 1 was conducted in the specific context of the COVID-19 crisis among small and medium-sized firms in India, raising questions about generalizability. Although Study 2 replicated effects in India and the United States, broader cross-cultural validation in non-Western contexts is limited, and life history theory has historically relied on relatively elite samples. Multilevel structural equation modeling could not converge for Study 1 given the complexity of the data, and the leader-rated job loss risk measure captures supervisors' willingness to let employees go rather than realized terminations.
future_research: The author calls for broader application of life history theory to other workplace stressors beyond job insecurity, to identify employees who may be disproportionately burdened and to surface attenuating factors. Cross-cultural research outside Western contexts is highlighted as an important next step, given limited data on life history processes in non-Western settings. Further work should examine specific life events that may modify or reinforce the early-life imprint of childhood financial standing, and develop additional managerial and policy-level interventions (beyond opportunity framing) that strengthen sense of control under stress, especially for workers from poorer backgrounds.
abstract: The current research proposes and tests a novel model explaining how job insecurity shapes well-being and has consequences for stratification and inequality. I draw on evolutionary life history theory, which proposes that growing up in a poorer versus wealthier environment impacts the sense of control people feel when exposed to threat in adulthood. I integrate this perspective with transactional stress theory to propose that job insecurity has a disproportionately negative effect on employees from poorer backgrounds, leading to lower engagement and higher emotional exhaustion among such employees, while those from wealthier backgrounds are buffered against these effects. These responses to job insecurity, in turn, amplify job loss risk for employees from poorer backgrounds, regardless of employees' current job or financial situation. A preregistered, multisource, five-wave longitudinal study conducted at the height of the COVID-19 crisis in India found support for these predictions. A follow-up quasi-experiment conducted in India and the United States replicated the effects on engagement and exhaustion. The impact of job insecurity on well-being is stratified and acts as a mechanism that reproduces childhood inequalities.
apa_citation: Sirola, N. (2024). Job insecurity and well-being: Integrating life history and transactional stress theories. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(3), 679-703. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0285

title: When the Symphony Does Jazz: How Resourcefulness Fosters Organizational Resilience during Adversity
methods: Grounded theory, longitudinal comparative case study of two professional orchestras matched on adversity (COVID-19) but differing in initial response; data triangulated from interviews, observations, and archival sources; iterative coding via Nvivo following Strauss & Corbin.
industry: Performing arts (professional orchestras)
units: Two professional orchestras (Fortis and Revenite, pseudonyms); informants include management, staff, musicians, and board members
n_sample: 41 interviews (22 at Fortis, 19 at Revenite); 19.5 hours of meeting observations; 1,095 pages of archival documents
authors: Sonenshein, S.; Nault, K.
theories: crisis management (Pearson & Clair, 1998; Williams et al., 2017); organizational resilience (Sutcliffe & Vogus, 2003; Vogus & Sutcliffe, 2007); regulatory focus theory (Higgins, 1997); resourcefulness (Williams et al., 2021; Sonenshein, 2014)
keywords: adversity orientation; crisis management; organizational identity; organizational resilience; preventative resourcefulness; promotive resourcefulness; regulatory focus; resourcefulness
research_question: How do established organizations use resourcefulness to act resiliently during a crisis?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering conditions: Adversity orientation (provisional vs. categorical) shapes how members perceive uncertainty and time horizons
- Key constructs / tensions: Promotive resourcefulness (opportunity-focused) vs. preventative resourcefulness (worst-case-focused); crisis agency, trust, hopefulness vs. hopelessness
- Practices: Endogenous resource generation through ampliative cycles (generativity, hopefulness) vs. preservational and hopelessness cycles
- Process / phases: (1) initial adversity orientation shapes (2) crisis agency, which channels (3) resourcefulness type, which sustains itself via (4) recursive cycles, ultimately producing (5) expanded vs. lost organizational identity and (6) adversity adjustment; financial, emotional, and operational "updating" can shift trajectories mid-crisis
- Emergent outcomes: Strong adversity adjustment with expanded identity (Fortis) vs. weak adjustment with lost identity (Revenite, until updating triggered a turnaround)
- Boundary conditions: Established (not entrepreneurial) organizations facing industrywide crisis with similar resource stocks

The process model explains organizational resilience as emerging from how members orient to adversity at its onset. A provisional orientation (embracing uncertainty, short-term acting) empowers crisis agency and promotive resourcefulness, which becomes self-reinforcing through cycles that endogenously generate trust, hopefulness, and additional agency, expanding organizational identity and producing resilient acts. A categorical orientation (needing certainty, long-term planning) collapses crisis agency, channels members into preventative resourcefulness focused on survival, and triggers cycles of low trust and hopelessness that erode identity. Critically, financial, emotional, and operational updating can break the preventative cycle and reorient organizations toward promotive resourcefulness even after a poor initial response.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops a process theory of organizational resilience that integrates resourcefulness and crisis management literatures, distinguishing promotive and preventative resourcefulness as qualitatively different mechanisms tied to regulatory focus theory (Higgins, 1997). It theorizes endogenous resource creation through ampliative cycles and the role of organizational identity expansion as both an outcome and a fueling mechanism of sustained resilient action, moving beyond the static "bouncing back" vs. "bouncing forward" framing.
practical_implication: Practitioners often spotlight leadership for resilience, but resourceful actions of employees (including musicians at Revenite) are critical for adversity adjustment. Radical cost-cutting can ensure survival but inflicts employee suffering and forecloses positive adjustment. Managers should embrace short-term action under uncertainty rather than projecting certainty and long-term planning, as a provisional orientation endogenously produces the resources needed to act resiliently.
limitations: The study examines two organizations within a single industry (professional orchestras) facing one crisis (COVID-19), limiting the range of mechanisms observed. The authors did not examine reverse shifts from promotive to preventative resourcefulness, nor moves from resilience back to mere survival or organizational death. Industry-specific dynamics (knowledge diffusion among orchestras) may have shaped the resilience trajectories observed.
future_research: Scholars should examine whether "resilience contagion" operates within and across organizations, whether adversity adjustment is zero-sum across competitors, and what constitutes a suitable time period for studying adversity adjustment. Additional research could investigate shifts from promotive to preventative resourcefulness (the reverse trajectory), how updating functions when it fails to trigger a shift, and whether updating provides a relational pause that resets low-trust environments.
abstract: Using a grounded theory study of two prominent performing arts organizations during the COVID-19 pandemic, we develop theory about how organizations respond to adversity over time. Building on research on resilience, resourcefulness, and crisis management, we induce a process model that unpacks the mechanisms and dynamics that enable organizations to act resiliently. We find that organizations approach adversity using two types of resourcefulness. Promotive resourcefulness focuses on opportunities from adversity, which leads to acting resiliently. We show how promotive resourcefulness becomes sustained over time by endogenously producing resources—crisis agency, trust, and hopefulness—which expands an organization's identity and leads to resilient acts. In contrast, preventative resourcefulness focuses on minimizing worst-case outcomes, which leads to a lost organizational identity and relatively weak adversity adjustment. We find that preventative resourcefulness becomes part of cycles that erode trust, limit crisis agency, and generate hopelessness. Additionally, we explain how financial, emotional, and operational updating can shift preventative to promotive resourcefulness, allowing organizations to act resiliently later in a crisis. Our findings unpack critical mechanisms and processes that explain whether and how organizations act resiliently over time.
apa_citation: Sonenshein, S., & Nault, K. (2024). When the symphony does jazz: How resourcefulness fosters organizational resilience during adversity. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(3), 648-678. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0988

title: Strengthening Theory–Methods–Data Links
methods: Editorial synthesis identifying underutilized methodological and data approaches that can strengthen theory–method–data linkages in management research, drawing on examples from management, economics, sociology, psychology, and medicine.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Bliese, Paul D.; Certo, S. Trevis; Smith, Anne D.; Wang, Mo; Gruber, Marc
theories: theory–method–data alignment framework
keywords: Bayesian methods; computational modeling; construct measurement; difference-in-difference; exogenous events; latent mixture models; longitudinal data; null hypothesis significance testing; observed power; off-diagonal research; outliers; theory-methods-data alignment
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Theory, methods, and data are inexorably intertwined; the foundation of a strong research discipline hinges on the strength of theory–method–data alignment, and innovations in any one element often drive advances in the others.
- Familiar-but-underused approaches: report and interpret observed power; adopt Bayesian techniques as alternatives to NHST; leverage longitudinal (panel) data and exogenous events with designs such as difference-in-difference and interrupted time series for stronger causal inference; revisit constructs and measurement (primary instruments, machine learning, wearable sensors).
- Less familiar but established approaches: incorporate computational modeling to formalize verbal theories and derive predictions for dynamic/complex phenomena; use latent mixture models to theorize about and detect unobserved heterogeneity (subpopulations, intersectionality, boundary conditions).
- Cross-paradigm integration: embrace outliers as theoretically informative rather than trimming them, using qualitative comparative analysis or multiple case study to interpret them; consider "off-diagonal" research — quantitative data with inductive methods (e.g., quantitative grounded theory) and qualitative data with deductive design (e.g., alternate templates, pattern matching).
- Boundary conditions: data and results are often more variable than acknowledged; robust theories require systematically making sense of heterogeneity (in outliers, subpopulations, and observed power).

The editorial advances a chain-of-strength metaphor: any weak link — design, method, or interpretation — threatens the whole research chain, and "brilliant theory that cannot be tested with methods and data might be considered a form of science fiction." The authors organize their suggestions into three buckets (familiar-but-underused tools, methods well established outside AMJ, and qualitative–quantitative integration) and argue that each can sharpen causal inference and theory development by acknowledging variability and heterogeneity rather than smoothing them away.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial provides a programmatic agenda for AMJ authors and reviewers: it explicitly names underutilized approaches (observed power, Bayesian techniques, DD with exogenous events, computational modeling, latent mixture analysis, outlier-as-evidence, off-diagonal designs, modernized measurement) and frames them as concrete levers for strengthening theory–method–data alignment. By complementing Grégoire et al. (2024) on novel data sources, it consolidates a vocabulary for cross-fertilization across micro/macro/qualitative subfields and across cognate disciplines (economics, sociology, psychology, medicine).
practical_implication: Researchers are encouraged to (a) routinely report and discuss observed power and consider Bayesian credibility intervals, (b) exploit longitudinal data and exogenous shocks (e.g., COVID-19, the great recession) for difference-in-difference and interrupted time-series designs, (c) translate verbal theories into computational models before empirical testing, (d) use latent mixture methods to model heterogeneity, (e) treat outliers as theoretical opportunities rather than nuisances, (f) explore quantitative grounded theory and alternate-templates pattern matching, and (g) update construct measurement using primary instruments, machine learning, and digital/wearable sensors.
limitations: The authors explicitly acknowledge that the discussion is "not meant to be comprehensive" and that no individual or group of authors can claim expertise in every method discussed; their collective expertise does not cover the entirety of the methods landscape, and the selection of approaches reflects the editorial team's judgment about what is currently underutilized at AMJ.
future_research: Future management research can enhance the rigor and robustness of causal inference by deploying these diverse approaches in combination — for example, pairing longitudinal exogenous-event designs with computational models, or combining latent mixture analysis with qualitative case work on outliers. The authors call for ongoing dialogue and mutual learning across methodological traditions and disciplines to facilitate adoption of these underutilized approaches.
abstract: FROM THE EDITORS STRENGTHENING THEORY–METHODS–DATA LINKS

title: Tick Off the Gender Diversity Box: Examining the Cross-Level Effects of Women’s Representation in Senior Management
methods: Longitudinal panel analysis (2007-2015) using population-averaged negative binomial regression with generalized estimating equations; Sobel mediation tests; ITCV endogeneity sensitivity analysis; supplementary qualitative interviews with 38 decision-makers
industry: Largest U.S. corporate law firms (legal services)
units: Law firms (firm-year observations)
n_sample: 193 law firms, 1,202 firm-year observations
authors: Dwivedi, P.; Paolella, L.
theories: attention-based view of the firm (Ocasio, 1997); critical mass theory (Kanter, 1977); practice-based attention (Feldman & Orlikowski, 2011)
keywords: attention-based view; cross-level spillover; diversity and hiring committees; gender diversity; internal diversity practices; law firms; women in senior management
research_question: What impact does greater gender diversity at the top have on junior women’s career opportunities, and how does the higher representation of women in senior management impact employment outcomes for women in lower ranks in male-dominated industries?
mechanism_summary:
- Mediator: Engagement with internal diversity practices (averaged 0-3 score across six internal diversity initiatives)
- Moderator: Substantive representation of women on diversity and hiring committees (proportion of women across both committees)
- Controls: Firm prestige, firm size, geographic location (Southern states), associate-partner ratio, women associates who left, formal partnership policy, total job offers made, year dummies

The authors theorize that firms with comparatively higher proportions of senior women than industry peers paradoxically reduce attention to internal diversity practices for three reasons: (1) men perceive an "illusion of progress" and satisficing behavior dampens further effort, (2) firms prioritize externally rewarding diversity targets at the top (e.g., lateral hiring) over uncertain internal practices, and (3) senior women face structural barriers (lack of critical mass, stereotype threat, extra-role costs) that constrain their collective ability to influence diversity practices. This reduced attentional engagement degrades targeted recruitment and bias-mitigating selection processes, leading to fewer job offers to junior women. Substantive women's representation on diversity and hiring committees mitigates this spillover by granting formal authority and creating supportive coalitions.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the attention-based view of the firm by adopting a practice lens (Feldman & Orlikowski, 2011), showing how demographic composition in senior management shapes attentional engagement with diversity through interrelated practices. It contributes to the literature on unintended consequences of diversity initiatives by demonstrating cross-level spillovers from senior gender diversity all the way to entry-level women, and offers a remedy by showing that women's substantive representation on diversity and hiring committees disrupts the negative spillover.
practical_implication: Organizations should pursue a dual strategy: simultaneously increasing women's representation in senior management while appointing a critical mass of women across ranks to diversity and hiring committees. Firms should formally specify diversity tasks in role prescriptions and reward systems for both men and women managers to avoid placing the burden of diversity work disproportionately on women. Firms must avoid treating senior gender diversity as a "tick-the-box" exercise.
limitations: The data are survey-based archival data, which precludes definitive causal claims and does not directly capture the proposed attentional mechanism. The study is limited to large U.S. law firms in a male-dominated industry, raising questions about generalizability to gender-balanced or smaller-firm contexts. Alternative theoretical mechanisms (e.g., moral licensing, threshold effects) were not directly tested.
future_research: Future work should use experimental or quasi-experimental designs (e.g., leveraging European Commission Directive 2022 board quotas) to identify causal effects, examine industries with more balanced gender composition, investigate alternative mitigating conditions involving male allyship and client engagement, and extend the framework to other forms of inequality (racial, LGBTQ+) and other outcomes (pay disparity, turnover).
abstract: In male-dominated industries, organizations face considerable pressure to enhance women’s representation in top leadership roles. Firms respond to this pressure by increasing gender diversity in senior positions, but often fail to achieve a critical mass of senior women at the top. This raises a key question: What impact does greater gender diversity at the top have on junior women’s career opportunities? We develop an attention-based perspective on gender diversity and theorize that firms with relatively more women in senior management compared to their industry peers are likely to allocate fewer attentional resources, time, and effort toward internal diversity practices. This inadvertently hurts the recruitment of women at lower levels. We propose that one way to mitigate these adverse cross-level spillover effects is to ensure women’s substantive representation on committees responsible for overseeing and monitoring the firm’s diversity and hiring-related decision-making processes. We test our contentions and find support for our model using a panel dataset on the largest U.S. law firms. We conduct several supplemental analyses to provide insights into our findings.
apa_citation: Dwivedi, P., & Paolella, L. (2024). Tick off the gender diversity box: Examining the cross-level effects of women’s representation in senior management. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(4), 991-1023. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0506

title: Collaborating on the Edge of Failure: Frame Alignment across Multiple Interaction Arenas in Multi-Stakeholder Partnerships
methods: Inductive, longitudinal process study with a six-year multi-phase data collection (Langley, 1999); 92 interviews (81 formal semi-structured plus 11 informal) with 48 individuals, complemented by participant observations and 3,120 pages of documents and archival records; analysis traced an event chronology, mapped interaction arenas, and coded relational, issue, and process framing within and across arenas to build a process model
industry: German textiles and apparel industry, plus NGOs, trade unions, standards organizations, and government
time_period: 2014-2021 (process traced from partnership inception in early 2014; primary data collection from November 2015 to November 2021)
units: Steering committee members of the German Partnership for Sustainable Textiles and their constituent stakeholder groups
n_sample: 92 interviews with 48 individuals (47 SC members interviewed multiple times; 32 regular stakeholder members) plus 3,120 pages of documents
authors: Grimm, J.; Reinecke, J.
theories: frame alignment in social movements (Snow et al., 1986; Benford & Snow, 2000); interactional framing theory (Dewulf et al., 2009; Gray, Purdy, & Ansari, 2015); two-table problem / level-two negotiations (Colosi, 1983; Putnam, 1988; Sebenius, 2013)
keywords: collaborative governance; frame alignment; frame stretching; interactional framing; multi-stakeholder partnerships; multiparty collaboration; sustainable textiles; two-table problem
research_question: How do multiple interaction arenas affect frame alignment, and how can frame alignment be navigated across multiple interaction arenas in multi-stakeholder partnerships?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: shared interactional frame; two-table problem; red lines; frame break; frame stretching; collaboration on the edge of failure
- Three within-arena framing laminations: relational framing (inquiring, re-evaluating, identifying — shifting attributional orientation from "enemies" to allies); issue framing (extension of interpretive scope from subjective to intersubjective understanding); process framing (reframing the ongoing interaction from antagonistic to collaborative)
- Three cross-arena framing processes: reciprocal frame stretching (through reciprocal concessions between negotiators); integrative frame stretching (through integrating constituents/experts into expert working groups); bilateral frame stretching (through cross-table scanning by representatives)
- Triggering condition: near-collapse / repeated frame breaks at "homecoming" when constituents reject negotiated frames
- Phases: six topic-framing cycles spanning Partnership phase (2014) through Action Plan 1.0, Action Plan 2.0, three re-adjustments, and COVID-related adjustments
- Emergent outcome: deepened commitments to ambitious goals previously rejected, despite waning external pressure

The process model shows that shared frames negotiated in one interaction arena (e.g., the Steering Committee) cannot simply be "transported" to constituent group arenas. Instead, frame alignment requires iterative back-and-forth interactions in which negotiators progressively laminate relational, issue, and process meanings onto a shared frame and then "stretch" that frame across arenas through reciprocal, integrative, and bilateral processes. Collective recognition that the partnership is "on the edge of failure" generates willingness to cross red lines and reach the highest common denominator rather than the lowest. Frame stretching always carries the risk of frame break, but iterative ongoing efforts are what propel collaboration from near-collapse to deepened, more substantive commitment.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to framing theory by conceptualizing frame alignment as an ongoing, iterative, multi-arena process in which frames are not transported but stretched, becoming more "elastic" through cumulative laminations of relational, issue, and process framing. It foregrounds the agency of audiences and the relational dynamics among those who "do" the framing, showing that frames are grounded in interpersonal relationships rather than free-floating cognitive packages. It also contributes to scholarship on multi-stakeholder partnerships and inter-organizational collaboration by unpacking the previously underexplored "two-table problem" and revealing how negotiation dynamics, rather than external pressures alone, sustain collaboration "on the edge of failure."
practical_implication: For conveners and participants of multi-stakeholder partnerships, sustaining collaboration requires deliberate investment in informal relational interactions (e.g., off-table meals, "time outs") that build personal bonds enabling representatives to move beyond antagonistic framings. Negotiators must continuously cultivate buy-in from their "back home" constituents through integrative working groups and cross-table scanning rather than treating partnership agreements as one-shot deals. Conveners should expect non-linear progress: maintaining a partnership requires accepting the risk of operating "on the edge of failure," because the willingness to cross red lines often emerges only when collapse appears imminent.
limitations: The authors could not directly observe Steering Committee meetings due to confidentiality, relying instead on interpretive interviewing that may be subject to hindsight bias and may miss interactional dynamics that ethnographic methods could capture. The study examines only two main interaction arenas (partnership and stakeholder group), leaving the intra-organizational arena (e.g., conflicts between CSR and procurement within firms) outside scope. The Textiles Partnership was government-convened, so the role of government as a convener is a boundary condition limiting generalizability to fully private, non-state-sponsored partnerships.
future_research: Future work should apply close-up observational methods or conversation analysis to capture additional cross-arena framing dynamics. Researchers could extend the model to a third negotiation table inside participants' home organizations, examining how CSR managers function as internal activists who "sell" frames to colleagues. Comparative studies of MSPs with and without government sponsorship and of varying degrees of representative authorization and accountability could illuminate how external context shapes framing strategies. Further work could probe the emotional and identity challenges faced by individuals navigating between arenas and how representatives cope with frame shifts at a personal level while remaining "true" to their constituents.
abstract: One of the greatest challenges of multi-stakeholder partnerships lies in forging a shared understanding and obtaining and sustaining commitment among parties representing different interests and goals. While studies have emphasized the importance of developing shared frames for enabling collaboration and collective action through frame alignment, scant attention has been paid to how stakeholder representatives can attain commitment from their constituents "back home" to frames negotiated on their behalf. Our longitudinal process study explores how participants in the German Partnership for Sustainable Textiles successfully confronted the challenge of aligning frames across multiple interaction arenas, highlighting how failing to tackle this "two-table problem" can risk partnership collapse. Our process model captures how back-and-forth interactions enabled the stretching of shared frames across interaction arenas, thereby propelling the partnership from near collapse to deepened commitments. While stretching frames heightens the risk of frame break, our analysis shows how such iterative ongoing efforts are essential for deepening commitments and advancing collaboration. We thus contribute to framing theory by highlighting how frame alignment can be achieved across multiple interaction arenas by "collaborating on the edge of failure." We further contribute to scholarship on multiparty collaboration by unpacking the multi-table negotiation dynamics that help explain collaborative outcomes.
apa_citation: Grimm, J., & Reinecke, J. (2024). Collaborating on the edge of failure: Frame alignment across multiple interaction arenas in multi-stakeholder partnerships. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(4), 956-990. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0466

title: Change at Last, but When Does Change Last? Preserving Attentional Engagement around Past Failures and Their Lessons
methods: Fixed-effects maximum likelihood nonlinear regression on an unbalanced firm-year panel of all U.S. coal mining firms (1988-2021), estimating depreciation parameters (lambda) for accident experience using the feNmlm function in R; learning-curve specification with firm and year fixed effects.
industry: U.S. coal mining
time_period: 1988-2021 (data 1983-2021; first five years dropped to construct lags)
units: firm-years
n_sample: 28,304 firm-year observations from 5,869 firms
authors: Madsen, P. M.; Desai, V.
theories: attention-based view (Ocasio, 1997, 2011; Nicolini & Korica, 2021); organizational learning theory (Argote et al., 2021; Levitt & March, 1988)
keywords: attention-based view; attentional engagement; culpability; failure complexity; knowledge depreciation; organizational learning; routine relatedness; vigilance
research_question: What characteristics of past organizational failures preserve managerial and employee attention over time, such that the knowledge derived from those failures depreciates more slowly? Why does knowledge from some failures persist longer in organizational structures, routines, and memories than knowledge from others?
mechanism_summary: The authors adapt the attention-based view to argue that failure characteristics that surface associations with other organizational routines repeatedly draw collective attention back to the failed area. Complex failures expose interconnections across diverse routines; culpable failures spread blame and scrutiny that diffuse to other domains; failures involving routines related to the firm's dominant routines remain salient through everyday work. These three forms of association preserve attentional engagement, slow knowledge depreciation, and extend the performance benefits of prior learning.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops theory about attentional engagement and vigilance within the attention-based view, specifying characteristics of past failures that pull attention back rather than allowing it to drift away. It contributes to organizational learning theory by showing that knowledge gleaned from different types of failures depreciates at different rates—answering Argote et al.'s (2021) call for theory about heterogeneity in retention rates—and by linking the ABV more tightly to the temporality of organizational memory.
practical_implication: Managers can extend the lifespan of lessons learned from failures by conducting thorough investigations that surface causal complexity rather than simplifying causal stories, by openly engaging with the firm's culpability rather than externalizing blame, and by explicitly demonstrating how routines involved in past failures relate to dominant routines used elsewhere in the firm. Formal "lessons learned" programs (e.g., NASA's) illustrate this practice.
limitations: The findings concern failure experience and may not generalize to nonfailure (success) experiences. The failures studied are coal mine accidents, so other types of organizational failures may show different depreciation patterns. The authors could not observe firm financial performance (most are not public), the analysis is correlational rather than causal, and learning and depreciation are inferred from experience-performance associations rather than directly observed in routines.
future_research: Future work should apply the theory and the novel feNmlm-based depreciation estimation method to other forms of experience and to other types of organizational failures. The authors encourage examination of additional experiential and organizational factors that may influence depreciation rates and call for richer measurement of organizational knowledge creation and depreciation.
abstract: Although failures and other experiences can capture attention and motivate organizations to learn and improve, this knowledge is not always retained over time, leaving some organizations dangerously prone to repeat the same mistakes repeatedly. We adapt theory on the attention-based view—and specifically on attentional engagement and vigilance—to shed new light on this process. While prior research has examined how competing events may draw attention away, our theory leads us to consider the circumstances that will motivate employees to maintain attention on learning from failure that has already occurred. Our framework examines the conditions that preserve attention to past failures by increasing the perception that related issues exist elsewhere, serving as continuing reminders of or cues about the failure when attention begins to drift away. We find support for related hypotheses involving a failure's complexity, the firm's culpability, and the ongoing use of routines related to those involved in the failure. Our findings contribute to the attention-based view by developing theory about attentional engagement and vigilance and by emphasizing the conditions that can keep attention focused on, rather drawn away from, past failure. We also contribute to efforts to examine knowledge depreciation and forgetting in more depth in organizational learning theory.
apa_citation: Madsen, P. M., & Desai, V. (2024). Change at last, but when does change last? Preserving attentional engagement around past failures and their lessons. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(4), 933-955. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0391

title: Turning Work into a Refuge: Job Crafting as Coping with Personal, Grief-Inducing Events
methods: Inductive, qualitative grounded theory study based on 68 in-depth interviews with employees who experienced bereavement or breakup; line-by-line open coding with iterative theory building
industry: Mixed occupations including analytics director, animator, financial advisor, fire lieutenant, lawyer, project manager, software engineer, teacher
country: Primarily United States (59); also France, Netherlands, Russia
time_period: Average four years since onset of grief-inducing event; some within six months; data collection spanned COVID-19 pandemic period
units: Individual employees who experienced personal grief-inducing events while holding a job
n_sample: 68 interviews
authors: Pletneva, L.
theories: coping theory (Lazarus & Folkman, 1984); grief in organizations; job crafting theory (Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001)
keywords: bereavement; breakup; coping; grief; job crafting; promotion-oriented job crafting; work as refuge
research_question: How, and with what consequences, can work become a refuge from grief-inducing experiences in people's lives?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: Personal grief-inducing event (bereavement or breakup) that causes routine disruption, event-related feelings, and vulnerability
- Unmet needs generated: Need for a mental break, need for emotional balance, need for support
- Facilitating factors: Lack of well-established coping mechanisms; high importance of work; positive prior work experiences
- Job crafting tactics (promotion-oriented):
  - Job expanding (broadening tasks, volunteering, learning new skills)
  - Task fulfillment adjusting (more effort, reprioritizing, inventing new methods)
  - Interactions expanding (more interactions, more social events with coworkers)
  - Interactions enriching (opening up and sharing personal information, deepening interaction quality)
- Mechanisms by which job crafting transforms work into a refuge:
  - Getting distracted from event-related thoughts
  - Experiencing emotional counterpoint to event-generated feelings
  - Feeling supported
- Emergent outcome: Cognitive reframing of work as refuge ("anchor," "crutch," "vacation")
- Consequences (in work and life domains, positive and negative)

Grieving employees become active actors who turn work into a refuge by engaging in promotion-oriented job crafting behavior to address event-generated needs. Through expanding tasks, adjusting task fulfillment, and expanding and enriching interactions, work begins to function as a distraction, an emotional counterpoint, and a source of support. This process eventually leads to a cognitive reframing of work itself, with consequences spanning both domains: positive (professional growth, organizational commitment, avoidance of depression and substance abuse, expanded friendship network, personal growth) and, when job crafting is overly intense and prolonged beyond three months, negative (decreased quality of life and delayed processing of the event).
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes three contributions. First, it returns agency to grieving employees in the literature on grief in organizations by showing they not only find refuge in work passively but can actively turn work into a refuge through job crafting. Second, it expands job crafting theory by introducing a nonwork-related antecedent (personal grief-inducing events) that crosses the work-life boundary, refining the assumption that job crafting is triggered solely by work-related stimuli. Third, it adds balance to job crafting scholarship by uncovering negative consequences of promotion-oriented job crafting (decreased quality of life, delayed event processing) alongside the typically emphasized positive ones.
practical_implication: Employees can use job crafting tactics to cope with grief-inducing events and may even benefit from doing so, but should be aware that intense, prolonged immersion in work can have harmful consequences. Managers can facilitate the work-as-refuge experience by creating positive work environments before crises occur, ensuring employees have agency over their jobs and access to job crafting opportunities, and proactively addressing overly intense and lengthy job crafting that may signal underlying personal struggles.
limitations: The sample focuses on employees whose work was unrelated to the loss event, who returned to stable workplaces, and who believed work helped them cope; this excludes more complex situations where work and the event are entangled or where work was experienced as primarily burdensome. The sample is largely Western (mostly US, with Dutch, French, and Russian participants), so cultural variation is not examined. The study does not deeply analyze how choice of job crafting tactic varies with event context.
future_research: Future work should examine job crafting in non-grief contexts, including stressful but emotionally positive events such as marriage, and explore antecedents of job crafting beyond work-related triggers. Researchers could investigate cultural differences in how work-as-refuge is used, factors affecting the choice between distraction-oriented and processing-oriented job crafting tactics, and the role of remote/hybrid work arrangements. Identity-related factors (integrators vs. segmentors) and additional boundary conditions for negative outcomes also warrant investigation.
abstract: Building on an inductive, qualitative study of employees who experienced grief-inducing events such as bereavement or breakup, this paper explores how and with what consequences work becomes a refuge from grief-inducing experiences in people's lives. Using the results of 68 in-depth interviews, I develop an inductive model that shows that grieving employees not only find refuge in work but can also turn their work into a refuge for themselves. In particular, the model explicates how people turn work into a refuge by engaging in job crafting behavior. I also show perceived consequences of this process that occur in both work and life domains. Such consequences tend to be positive, yet those who work significantly harder also experience negative consequences. This paper advances theorizing on grief in organizations and job crafting.
apa_citation: Pletneva, L. (2024). Turning work into a refuge: Job crafting as coping with personal, grief-inducing events. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(4), 1055-1083. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0604

title: Dirty Heroes? Healthcare Workers’ Experience of Mixed Social Evaluations during the Pandemic
methods: Grounded theory, inductive/abductive qualitative study based on 98 semi-structured remote interviews (60-90 minutes) with healthcare workers, plus a member-checking follow-up survey (n=20) in late 2021/early 2022; two-step coding system with iterative theoretical-memo writing.
industry: Healthcare (hospitals, long-term care, clinics)
country: Primarily United States (n=92), with 6 from South America
time_period: Interviews during the COVID-19 pandemic; member checking late 2021 and early 2022
units: Healthcare workers (nurses, physicians, physician assistants, administrators, other clinicians)
n_sample: 98 interviews; 20 follow-up member-checking respondents
authors: Rapp, Devin J.; Hughey, J. Matthew; Kreiner, Glen E.
theories: dirty work theory (Ashforth & Kreiner, 1999; Hughes, 1951); social evaluations (Helms & Patterson, 2014; Piazza & Augustine, 2022); stigma theory (Goffman, 1963)
keywords: COVID-19 pandemic; dirty heroes; dirty work; healthcare workers; hero-washing; mixed social evaluations; social evaluation events; stigma
research_question: How do stigmatized workers experience, evaluate, and respond to mixed social evaluations (e.g., being treated like a "dirty hero")?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: Social evaluation event (the COVID-19 pandemic) disrupts existing social consensus and provokes widespread reappraisal of healthcare work.
- Evaluative dimensions of work (context-specific): physical (taint via bodily fluids/disease), social (courtesy stigma from contact with infected patients), and moral (lionization as heroes vs. stigmatization as pandemic "coconspirators").
- Spatial-distance dynamic: at high distance, evaluators emphasize moral dimensions ("heroes"); at close distance, physical/social taint becomes salient ("don't get near me").
- Evaluating-the-evaluator criteria: workers appraise each evaluation along proximity, informedness, and authenticity of the source.
- Internalization outcomes: (a) acceptance — supporting supporters (pride/gratitude) or condoning condemners (empathizing/blaming other factors); (b) partial/full rejection — discounting/condemning supporters (insufficient, did-not-motivate, annoyance/anger) or discounting condemners (feeling ostracized, avoidance/concealing).
- Cumulative process: repeated mixed evaluations increase scrutiny, surfacing "hero-washing" when public celebration coexists with private neglect (insufficient PPE, staffing, compensation).

The grounded model shows that healthcare workers were not passive recipients of stigma or praise; they actively interrogated the proximity, informedness, and authenticity of their evaluators, producing internalization outcomes — including discounting supporters and condoning condemners — that prior dirty-work theory had not anticipated. Mixed evaluations operating across three dirty-work dimensions intensified internal-external identity dissonance, and this dissonance, rather than canceling out, compounded the disruption to workers' social selves.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to dirty work and stigma scholarship by introducing "dirty heroes" — occupational members simultaneously stigmatized and celebrated along distinct evaluative dimensions of work — and by theorizing "hero-washing," whereby publicly bestowed honorifics substitute for material support. It advances stigma research by specifying how targets evaluate their evaluators (proximity, informedness, authenticity), demonstrating that stigmatized workers are active agents who can condone condemners and discount supporters. It also introduces "social evaluation events" as an umbrella concept distinct from stigma, moralization, or valorization events because they need not produce evaluations of uniform valence.
practical_implication: Praising an entire workforce can backfire when not paired with concrete material support; idealization at a distance can substitute for needed action and may compound psychological stress. Organizations and managers should align stigma management tactics with the actual stigmatized dimension of work, remove distances, demonstrate informed understanding, and pair recognition with authentic, targeted resources (staffing, PPE, compensation) so that evaluations are perceived as authentic rather than as impression management.
limitations: The authors interviewed only healthcare workers, not the evaluators themselves; the front end of the model is therefore inferred abductively from workers' accounts of how others treated them. The sample is heterogeneous and largely U.S.-based (with a small South American subsample), limiting cross-country generalization, and the moral and regulatory legitimacy of healthcare may bound the findings. Quantitative validation of the proposed criteria and dimension combinations remains untested.
future_research: Future work should dive deeper into hero-washing across other lauded-yet-neglected occupations (teachers, soldiers, mental health professionals, neurodiverse workers) and quantitatively test how varying dimensions of stigma (and their combinations, e.g., physical-social) generate behavioral and adaptive outcomes. Researchers should also explore how the proximity, informedness, and authenticity criteria operate in occupations with different status and legitimacy profiles, and probe boundary conditions linking legitimacy, status, and stigma response.
abstract: The sudden onset of the COVID-19 pandemic ushered in an unprecedented era of public admiration for healthcare workers. Indeed, the title “healthcare heroes” became a ubiquitous moniker for healthcare providers of all stripes during the pandemic, a sentiment reflected in countless advertisements and banners. Paradoxically, these same “healthcare heroes” who were being publicly celebrated for their work in the fight against a novel coronavirus also faced stigma for their work amid the virus and infected patients. Using grounded theory, we document how stigmatized members of an occupation experience and respond to mixed—and even conflicting—social evaluations. We contribute to the literature on stigma and social evaluations more broadly by showing how targets of stigma evaluate their evaluators through nuanced logical and emotional processing and, moreover, that such processing can lead recipients of mixed evaluations toward a number of outcomes not previously theorized. We explore the concept of “dirty heroes,” where workers are celebrated and stigmatized along distinct dimensions of work traditionally studied in dirty work (i.e., physical, social, and moral). Our findings further illustrate how high-legitimacy occupations can be subject to “hero-washing,” whereby workers are publicly celebrated yet privately neglected.
apa_citation: Rapp, D. J., Hughey, J. M., & Kreiner, G. E. (2024). Dirty heroes? Healthcare workers’ experience of mixed social evaluations during the pandemic. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(4), 1124–1157. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0502
