title: Bricks without Straw: Overcoming Resource Limitations to Architect Ecosystem Leadership
methods: Nine-year longitudinal qualitative case study of Xiaomi within Google's Android ecosystem (2014-2023). Data corpus combines 112 semi-structured interviews (90 plus 22 follow-ups with Xiaomi managers and employees, suppliers, OEMs, complementors, consumers/Mi fans, and industry analysts), 18 visits and participant observations at Xiaomi headquarters, R&D office, customer experience stores, MIUI Beijing club, plus competitor sites (Huawei, Foxconn, Tencent, Oppo), 6 focus groups, and over 3,000 pages of archival/secondary data. Inductive analysis followed Gioia methodology with four steps: developing chronologies, temporal bracketing into three phases, open and axial coding of microprocesses, and aggregation into a process model.
industry: Smartphone industry / Android ecosystem (software user interface, smartphone OEM, smart home and IoT complementary devices)
country: China (with global market expansion, including India)
time_period: 2010-2023 (data collection from June 2014 to March 2023; covering Xiaomi's evolution from 2010 founding through 2022 financials)
units: Single revelatory case (Xiaomi) and its interactions with key Android ecosystem constituents (suppliers, OEMs, complementors, focal firm Google, end users)
n_sample: 1 focal firm (Xiaomi); 112 semi-structured interviews; 18 site visits; 6 focus groups; 2,179 secondary documents
authors: Shi, X.; Liang, X.; Ansari, S.
theories: Simmel's dyad/triad sociology (Simmel 1950); cultural entrepreneurship and entrepreneurial vision (Lounsbury & Glynn 2019; Logue & Grimes 2022); demand-side perspective in strategy (Priem 2007; Aversa et al. 2021); ecosystem theory (Adner 2017; Jacobides et al. 2018; Kapoor 2018); identity movements (Rao & Dutta 2018); resource-based view (Wernerfelt 1984; Barney 1991)
keywords: complementor strategy; demand-side strategy; ecosystem leadership; emancipatory vision; foothold resource; identity movement; orchestrator vision; pioneering vision; resource-disadvantaged firms
research_question: How does a resource-disadvantaged complementor architect ecosystem leadership? Specifically, how can peripheral complementors lacking critical supply-side resources establish themselves as ecosystem leaders by orchestrating demand-side mobilization and supply-side resource coalescence over time?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: foothold resource, identity movement, emancipatory vision, pioneering vision, orchestrator vision, second-tier complementor, first-tier complementor, focal orchestrator, dyadic vs. triadic microprocesses
- Process / phases: Phase I — Building a demand-side community by leveraging mundane resources (challenge: high entry barriers; opportunity: underserved user niche; role R1: second-tier complementor); Phase II — Building supply-side legitimacy without a track record (challenge: difficulty forming dyadic supplier relationships; opportunity: high growth potential; role R2: first-tier complementor); Phase III — Creating a derivative ecosystem by orchestrating value creation (challenge: diminishing advantage from supply-side homogeneity; opportunity: supply- and demand-side diversification; role R3: focal orchestrator while remaining a first-tier complementor)
- Persuasion mechanisms: (1) Crafting an emancipatory vision around a foothold resource via dyadic microprocesses of cultivating a community of users to democratize innovation and evoking emotions to generate affiliation; (2) Crafting a pioneering vision around material and social proof via triadic microprocesses of leveraging demand-side support and providing social proof from high-status connections; (3) Crafting an orchestrator vision around demand- and supply-side as material proof via incubating fledgling firms, evoking emotions with second-tier complementors, and combining supply- and demand-side synergies
- Triggering conditions: open ecosystem (Android), large potential user base, presence of underserved users, ability to generate demand-side synergies among complementary offerings
- Emergent outcomes: structural transformation of ecosystem, ascent from peripheral complementor to focal orchestrator, derivative ecosystem creation

The process unfolds through three sequential phases in which Xiaomi progressively reframes its vision and role in the Android ecosystem. A resource-disadvantaged firm begins by identifying a "foothold resource" (a customizable user interface) that channels the energies of underserved users into an identity movement, generating affective bonds and a fan community. It then leverages this engaged demand-side community as triadic "material proof" of demand-pull to persuade reluctant suppliers to provide critical supply-side resources, gaining supply-side legitimacy. Finally, the firm orchestrates a derivative ecosystem by incubating fledgling firms and combining supply- and demand-side synergies, transforming itself from peripheral complementor to focal orchestrator without disrupting the primary ecosystem—a mutualistic "rising tide lifts all boats" approach.
theoretical_contribution: The paper integrates the supply-side resource-based view with the demand-side perspective in strategy by showing how peripheral firms create value through mundane "foothold resources" that catalyze identity movements among underserved users. It introduces a sociological model of how resource-disadvantaged complementors—not just resource-endowed "star" complementors—can reshape ecosystem structure by combining emotionally evocative visions with substantive material proof. It extends ecosystem theory from a structural to a sociological understanding of how peripheral firms transform ecosystem structure through compelling visions and dedicated user communities, advancing a mutualistic rather than disruptive logic of ecosystem leadership.
practical_implication: Managers of resource-limited firms can build sense of community around their products and create buzz by engaging users through social media, forums, events, and influencer partnerships, turning customers into "fans" with emotional affiliation. Incubating small firms in exchange for minority equity stakes offers an alternative to traditional corporate venture capital, blending financial returns with strategic alignment, and creating equity and complementarity dependencies that reduce opportunism while enabling diversification and growth.
limitations: Xiaomi joined Android's ecosystem during its emergent phase when Google was actively pursuing growth in China, giving Xiaomi more latitude to shape the ecosystem; the model may not generalize to mature ecosystems. The study is externally oriented (firm and ecosystem) and does not examine the internal microprocesses of how a start-up complementor develops integrative capabilities. The research mainly considers a commercial ecosystem and may not apply to highly regulated markets where government policies significantly shape ecosystem development.
future_research: Future studies can examine how start-ups gain advantageous positions in mature ecosystems where openings to shape ecosystem structure are narrower. Researchers can investigate the internal microprocesses through which start-up complementors develop integrative capabilities. Future work can illuminate how firms navigate the institutional context, particularly in regulated markets and countries like China where government wields considerable influence, and as firms scale and attract greater regulatory and societal scrutiny.
abstract: While scholars have explored how focal firms harness demand-side value through ecosystem development, less emphasis has been placed on understanding how peripheral complementors become competitive. Unlike focal firms, complementors can seldom set value extraction rules. How can complementors lacking critical supply-side resources establish themselves as ecosystem leaders? We conducted a longitudinal analysis of Xiaomi, which began life as a smartphone software complementor within the Android ecosystem but ultimately gained architectural control by orchestrating the creation of a derivative ecosystem. We elucidate how these resource-disadvantaged firms may begin with fueling an "identity movement" among underserved users centered around a pivotal "foothold resource." This movement serves as their entry point into an ecosystem and enables them to build a demand-side community. They then employ "demand pull" as concrete evidence to substantiate their vision, coalesce supply-side resources, and ultimately become an orchestrator. While conventional strategy research emphasizes supply-side resources as drivers of value creation, we explore the synergy between demand-side mobilization and supply-side orchestration. Finally, we illustrate how firms lacking resources craft compelling visions in conjunction with substantive actions to engage evolving target audiences. This approach allows them to secure more advantageous positions within the ecosystem, thereby transforming its structure over time.
apa_citation: Shi, X., Liang, X., & Ansari, S. (2024). Bricks without straw: Overcoming resource limitations to architect ecosystem leadership. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(4), 1084-1123. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1440

title: The Social Structure of Insiders and Outsiders: Toward a Network Community Perspective on Firm Performance
methods: OLS regressions with firm and year fixed effects (standard errors clustered at firm level) on VC firm-year panel data; community detection via clique percolation method (CPM, k=4) over one-year and three-year trailing-window syndication networks; tests for inverted U-shape following Haans, Pieters, & He (2016); robustness via Arellano-Bond/system-GMM dynamic panels, coarsened exact matching, IPO-rate alternative DV, principal component composite of centrality measures, and outlier checks (winsorization, trimming).
industry: Venture capital (VC) syndication networks; VC firms investing in 26,484 portfolio companies
time_period: VC investments 2003-2016; portfolio exits tracked through 2018
units: VC firm-year observations (parent-firm level)
n_sample: 15,767 firm-year observations from 8,245 unique domestic VC firms (10,609 firm-years from 3,087 firms in fixed-effects models)
authors: Wu, X.; Adbi, A.; Mahmood, I. P.
theories: institutional theory (Khanna & Palepu, 1997; North, 1990); network community perspective; social network theory (connections, positions, cliques); structural folds (Vedres & Stark, 2010); structural holes (Burt, 1992)
keywords: clique percolation method; insiders and outsiders; institutional development; interorganizational relationships; network communities; syndication networks; venture capital
research_question: How do a focal firm's network community affiliations enhance firm performance, and what factors might accentuate or diminish the benefits of those affiliations? In particular, the paper asks whether community insiders outperform outsiders, whether multiple insiders face diseconomies, and how institutional development moderates these relationships.
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Degree centrality, betweenness centrality, eigenvector centrality, Burt's network constraint, prior experience (cumulative invested capital), prior success (cumulative successful exits); firm and year fixed effects

The mechanism rests on the argument that network communities — overlapping clusters of densely interconnected firms detected via the clique percolation method — provide insiders with information-acquisition and resource-exchange advantages, especially through indirect connections (partners' partners) that supply third-party referrals, trust, and reciprocity. Multiple insiders enjoy familiar access to diverse resource bases but face attentional and inertial costs of maintaining many community affiliations, producing an inverted U-shape between community count and performance. Well-developed institutions substitute for community-based exchange by providing market intermediaries, dampening insider advantages and shifting the inverted-U apex to fewer affiliations. Mechanism tests reveal that the benefit comes from indirect rather than direct community ties.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces a network community perspective on firm performance that complements existing connection, position, and clique perspectives by capturing intermediate-level cohesive structures with overlapping membership. It shows that connections and centrality alone are insufficient — being part of a community is what generates performance advantages — and that the benefits of network communities operate through indirect ties rather than direct syndication partners. By integrating social network and institutional perspectives, the study reveals a substitutive relationship between network communities and institutional development, identifying boundary conditions under which network-based advantages diminish.
practical_implication: Managers should prioritize deepening connections that lead to network community affiliations rather than building superficial connections, while exercising caution about over-affiliating with multiple communities, which can cause information overload and conflicts of interest. With advances in community-detection technologies (e.g., CPM), managers can develop a broader understanding of their network landscape. Governments in less-developed institutional contexts can incentivize community outsiders to become insiders (e.g., through public sponsorship of certain industries) to foster more equitable economic growth.
limitations: The study focuses on a single country (China), raising generalizability concerns, and the coinvestment partnerships in China tend to be short-lived, which may shift the relative importance of outsiders, insiders, and multiple insiders. The orientation and stability of partnerships may differ in other settings, and informal institutions are not directly modeled. Identification of network community boundaries depends on parametric choices (clique size k, trailing window n) that involve theoretical and methodological trade-offs.
future_research: Future studies can explore the performance effects of network communities in more- versus less-developed economies, investigate how informal institutions interact with formal institutions and network communities, and examine network configurations that lead to the emergence and persistence of multiple insiders. Researchers can combine CPM with exponential random graph models or relational event models to study the coevolution of insiders and outsiders, and analyze how bridging ties operate within, outside, and across community boundaries.
abstract: Management literature on how interorganizational relationships influence firm performance has emphasized the importance of connections, positions, and cliques. We advance this literature by examining how network communities (membership in cohesive network structures) shape the performance of venture capital (VC) firms. We propose that community insiders affiliated with at least one network community will outperform outsiders. We also explicate the conditions under which the advantages of network community affiliations are likely to be muted. Specifically, we argue for the potential diseconomies of network community affiliations and the possibility of a substitutive relationship between network communities and institutional development. Leveraging recent advances in community-detection technology and longitudinal data on VC firms in China, we find support for our theoretical arguments. Analysis of mechanisms underlying our theoretical arguments reveals that the value of community affiliations comes from indirect connections within network communities. By integrating social network and institutional perspectives, this study highlights not only the promises but also the limits of relying on network community affiliations.
apa_citation: Wu, X., Adbi, A., & Mahmood, I. P. (2024). The social structure of insiders and outsiders: Toward a network community perspective on firm performance. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(4), 903-932. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0925

title: A Theory of Host Country Sentiments: An Illustration in Cross-Border Acquisitions
methods: Logit regression analysis on 241 cross-border acquisition announcements; sentiment analysis using Stanford CoreNLP (lexicon-based Loughran-McDonald dictionary for deal sentiment, machine learning for acquirer and inward investment sentiment) on three corpora (9,533 + 30,120 + 9,741 documents); robustness checks include Heckman two-stage selection model, machine learning robustness test, shared corpora weighting, and impact threshold of confounding variable.
industry: Cross-border acquisitions across multiple industries (manufacturing, service, trade, financial, natural resources, high-tech, materials, telecommunications, hotel/real estate)
units: Cross-border acquisition announcements by Chinese firms targeting U.S. firms
n_sample: 241 acquisition announcements
authors: Yiu, D. W.; Wan, W. P.; Chen, K. X.; Tian, X.
theories: Durkheim's theory of collective effervescence; institutional theory; ritual interactionist theory; sense-making theory; social constructionist theory; theory of host country sentiments
keywords: cross-border acquisitions; emerging market firms; host country sentiments; legitimacy; sentiment analysis; sentiment dynamics
research_question: How do host country sentiments — socially constructed and collectively shared attitudes and emotional dispositions among host country stakeholders toward foreign entities — and their dynamics (volatility, wavelength, augmentation) influence the likelihood of cross-border acquisition deal completion?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Acquirer characteristics (size, public status, diversification, SOE status, acquisition experience, name with U.S. identity, subsidiary acquirer); target characteristics (public status, subsidiary); deal characteristics (same industry, minority ownership, financial acquisition); country-level variables (public opinion about China, political animosity toward China, frequency of inward acquisitions); year and industry dummies

The theory posits a three-stage mechanism: (1) sentiment arousal — a foreign entity's contentious strategic action in a value-laden host country stimulates stakeholders' sentiments and engagement in discourse; (2) sentiment competence — host country sentiments are mobilized via systems of discourses (mass and social media) underpinning rhythmic entrainment that gives rise to collective conscience; (3) sentiment resonance — host country stakeholders impose social judgment legitimizing or de-legitimizing the foreign entity's action. Sentiment dynamics (volatility, wavelength, augmentation) capture granular temporal patterns that exert heterogeneous effects on legitimation outcomes.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces a novel general theory of host country sentiments that elevates sentiment-based social institutions in international and strategic management research. It synthesizes ritual interactionist, social constructionist, sense-making, and institutional perspectives by integrating situational, interactional, motivational, and cognitive elements of social sentiments. The conceptual differentiation of sentiment structures (deal, acquirer, inward investments) and sentiment dynamics (volatility, wavelength, augmentation) advances understanding of granular, time-dynamic effects of social sentiments on MNE legitimation outcomes.
practical_implication: For acquirers from emerging markets like China, cross-border acquisitions easily turn into a public issue, and recognizing/managing the legitimacy of such acquisitions can make or break a planned deal. Firms contemplating acquisitions in developed economies should pay greater attention to host country sentiments, leverage sentiment analysis tools to monitor stakeholder discourse, and proactively manage stakeholders' social sentiments in systems of discourses facilitated by online media.
limitations: The empirical context is limited to Chinese firm acquisitions of U.S. targets between 2013 and 2016, raising questions about generalizability to other country dyads with different bilateral relationships. The effects of host country sentiment volatility on deal completion produced inconclusive results requiring further investigation. Additionally, sentiment analysis relies on shared corpora that may have classification limitations, particularly for the dictionary approach where the subject of each sentence cannot be as easily identified.
future_research: Future research can extend the theory using different country dyads (e.g., Korea, India as acquirers in the United States) to differentiate how host country sentiments affect inward acquisition outcomes from different home countries. The theory is generalizable to other salient inter-country events such as IPOs, foreign CEO appointments, MNE HR policies, and inter-country tensions. Future efforts can also investigate the inconclusive effects of host country sentiment volatility and explore cross-lag interactions between host country sentiment types.
abstract: Capitalizing on massive sentiment diffusion and mobilization aided by mass media and social media nowadays, we introduce a general theory of host country sentiments to illuminate the granular, context-situated, and time dynamic power of social sentiments. We posit that host country is a value-laden context wherein a foreign entity's strategic action stimulates host country stakeholders' social sentiments and engagement in systems of discourses ("sentiment arousal"), mobilizes social sentiments toward the foreign entity ("sentiment competence"), and legitimizes social sentiments and the strategic action of the foreign entity ("sentiment resonance"). To test our theory, we employ a context of inward acquisitions by emerging market firms in an advanced economy, and postulate that host country sentiments toward objects of the inward acquisition (deal, acquirer, and inward investments from home country) will influence the likelihood of deal completion, an indication of host country legitimacy. We further highlight the patterns of host country sentiment dynamics, and showcase how sentiment volatility, wavelength, and augmentation exert heterogeneous effects on deal completion. Overall, we advocate a theory of host country sentiments in international management research that elucidates the interplay among host country sentiments, systems of social discourses, and multinational enterprise strategic outcomes in a host country.
apa_citation: Yiu, D. W., Wan, W. P., Chen, K. X., & Tian, X. (2024). A theory of host country sentiments: An illustration in cross-border acquisitions. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(4), 1024-1054. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0239

title: The AMJ Management Research Canvas: A Tool for Conducting and Reporting Empirical Research
methods: Editorial synthesis of editorial experience and prior AMJ guidance into a nine-element 'canvas' framework for conceptualizing, conducting, and reporting empirical management research.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Dorobantu, Sinziana; Gruber, Marc; Ravasi, Davide; Wellman, Ned
theories: canvas-based approach; research design framework
keywords: boundary conditions; contributions; empirical research; puzzle; research canvas; research design; research question; research setting; theoretical constructs
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: All successful empirical management research projects share nine core elements that must "fit" together for the project to be cohesive — the puzzle, the audience and prior research, the research question, the theoretical constructs and relationships, the research setting, the research design and analysis, the empirical findings, the contributions, and the boundary conditions and limitations.
- The nucleus: The "puzzle" — a broad theoretical or phenomenological conundrum that preoccupies a community of management scholars — anchors every other element of the canvas.
- Four parts: (1) framing (audience/prior research + research question), (2) theoretical framework (constructs and their relationships), (3) empirical methodology (research setting + research design and analysis), (4) conclusions (empirical findings + contributions + boundary conditions/limitations).
- Boundary conditions: The canvas is not a rigid template; ontological and epistemological assumptions influence which elements are emphasized (e.g., setting matters more for qualitative studies than for controlled experiments). Iteration across elements is encouraged rather than linear progression.

The editorial argues that although the nine core elements are generally well-known, they are not always explicitly considered during research design and manuscript preparation, leading to submissions that fail to meet AMJ's publication bar. The canvas serves as a "checklist" and a visual mapping tool to help authors — especially less experienced ones — conceptualize, develop, and revise empirical research projects systematically. It complements earlier AMJ editorials (e.g., Colquitt & George, 2011) by integrating their guidance into a single framework.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes a synthesizing framework — the AMJ Management Research Canvas — that aggregates and operationalizes prior AMJ editorial guidance into a nine-element template applicable across micro/macro and qualitative/quantitative research traditions. By offering a list of guiding questions for each element (Table 1), the canvas provides scholars with a systematic tool for moving from initial research idea to fully developed research project, while preserving flexibility for different methodological approaches.
practical_implication: Authors — particularly less experienced ones — are encouraged to use the canvas as both a planning tool (mapping out the nine elements before designing a study, possibly on a poster-size printout with sticky notes) and a checklist when preparing manuscripts for submission. The canvas can also facilitate preregistration where appropriate, and can be used in collaborative settings (author teams, PhD supervision) to align on the structure of a research project.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Not reported in paper
abstract: For more information see: Dorobantu, S., Gruber, M., Ravasi, D., & Wellman, N., 2024. From the EditorsThe AMJ Management Research Canvas: A Tool for Conducting and Reporting Empirical Research. Academy of Management Journal, 67: 1163–1174.
apa_citation: Dorobantu, S., Gruber, M., Ravasi, D., & Wellman, N. (2024). The AMJ management research canvas: A tool for conducting and reporting empirical research. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(5), 1163-1174. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2024.4005

title: Credentials or Chemistry? Entrepreneur Gender and Cofounder Selection
methods: Multi-study mixed-design empirical-quantitative paper combining (a) Study 1 — archival text-analytic study of 9,306 user profiles scraped from the Y Combinator Cofounder Matching platform (January 2022), using LIWC dictionaries to measure interpersonal attraction, resource seeking, and interdependent self-construal, and two-limit Tobit regressions with country and functional-responsibility fixed effects plus mediation tests via the R 'mediation' package with 10,000 bootstrapped draws; (b) Study 2 — pre-registered online experiment with 945 entrepreneur respondents on Prolific Academic randomly assigned to high (n=459) or low (n=486) legitimacy conditions, using a 12-task choice-based conjoint analysis (CBC, 11,340 decisions) administered via Sawtooth Software with hierarchical Bayesian multinomial logit estimation of attribute importance scores, OLS regressions for moderation tests; (c) Study 3 — survey and audio data from a U.S. Midwest incubator program covering 78 entrepreneurs and 171 potential cofounders (242 ties from 3,136 possible dyads), with interdependent self-construal measured via LIWC dictionary applied to transcribed pitch audio, dyadic composite measures of resource seeking and interpersonal attraction, mixed-effects regressions with entrepreneur and cofounder random effects plus function and industry fixed effects, and supplemental OLS/logistic regressions linking selection strategies to investor ratings of viability and 1-year venture survival.
industry: Technology / startup-stage entrepreneurial ventures (online cofounder-matching platform users, online entrepreneurial respondents, and incubator participants across various startup industries)
country: United States primarily, with international participation: Study 1 spans 127 countries via Y Combinator's global platform; Study 2 restricted to United Kingdom or United States respondents on Prolific Academic; Study 3 conducted at a prestigious incubator program in the Midwest United States
time_period: Study 1 archival data scraped as of January 2022; Study 2 pre-registered online experiment (timing not specified); Study 3 spans incubator program cohort plus 1-year post-formation venture survival follow-up
units: Individual entrepreneurs (Studies 1 and 2) and entrepreneur–potential cofounder dyads (Study 3, with team-level aggregation for venture-outcome supplemental analyses)
n_sample: Study 1: 9,306 Y Combinator profiles; Study 2: 945 Prolific Academic participants completing 11,340 conjoint decisions (459 high-legitimacy, 486 low-legitimacy); Study 3: 249 individuals (78 entrepreneurs, 171 potential cofounders) yielding 242 formed ties out of 3,136 possible dyads, with 60 of 78 teams responding to the 1-year venture-stability survey
authors: Gray, S. M.; Howell, T.; Strassman, J.; Yamamoto, K.
theories: entrepreneurial networking and tie formation (Hallen, Davis, & Murray, 2020; Brands et al., 2022; Vershinina et al., 2020); gender role / social role theory (Eagly & Karau, 2002; Eagly & Wood, 2012); interdependent self-construal (Cross, Bacon, & Morris, 2000; Cross & Madson, 1997; Cross et al., 2011); legitimacy in new ventures (Zott & Huy, 2007)
keywords: cofounder selection; entrepreneur gender; entrepreneurial networking; founding teams; interdependent self-construal; interpersonal attraction; legitimacy; person-situation interactionist model; resource seeking; tie formation
research_question: How, when, and why do women entrepreneurs differ from men entrepreneurs in how they choose cofounders?
mechanism_summary:
- Mediator: Interdependent self-construal (the tendency to define one's self in terms of close relationships)
- Moderator: Legitimacy (the extent to which a venture and founder are deemed appropriate by external evaluators), operationalized via founder-related signals (top-ranked university, terminal degree, "big five" tech experience, prior entrepreneurial experience) in Study 1, randomly assigned high/low conditions in Study 2, and a composite of organizational achievements plus years of entrepreneurial experience in Study 3.

The mechanism the authors propose is a person-situation interactionist model: socialization into communal-versus-agentic gender roles produces, on average, higher interdependent self-construals in women than in men; higher interdependent self-construal heightens the value placed on relational cues (similarity, liking, familiarity) and dampens preference for instrumental, resource-bearing ties, channeling women toward an interpersonal attraction selection strategy and away from resource seeking. However, because individuals with high interdependent self-construals are also more attuned to external expectations and more responsive to mismatches with situational norms, they recalibrate when legitimacy is low — increasing resource seeking and decreasing interpersonal attraction to meet investors' expectations. This makes women's networking adaptable across high- and low-legitimacy contexts, while men's resource-seeking orientation remains relatively constant.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reconciles competing predictions from the entrepreneurial networking literature (women should resource-seek to counter investor bias) and the gender in networking literature (women should prioritize interpersonal attraction for relational stability) by introducing a person-situation interactionist model in which interdependent self-construal mediates gender's effect on cofounder selection and legitimacy moderates this mediated relationship. It empirically validates a mechanism — interdependent self-construal — that prior reviews (Brands et al., 2022) had flagged as a promising but underused explanation for gender differences in tie formation, and reframes interdependent self-construal as a source of behavioral adaptability rather than only relational motivation.
practical_implication: Entrepreneurs should reflect on their implicit cofounder-selection preferences and the downstream consequences of those preferences for venture funding and venture stability; for example, an entrepreneur who defaults to resource seeking might benefit from shifting toward interpersonal attraction once legitimacy is established. Accelerators and incubators can measure participants' interdependent self-construal and tailor advice toward the selection strategy each entrepreneur is likely to overlook, helping form teams that balance investor appeal with internal cohesion.
limitations: The studies analyze cofounder selection conditional on the entrepreneur seeking cofounders and do not capture solo-founding decisions. They focus on cofounder selection rather than later "joiner" hiring, leaving open how founder gender and self-construal carry over into employee selection. The analyses also center the focal entrepreneur's preferences without modeling how potential cofounders evaluate the entrepreneur or founding team, and Study 3's 60-team venture-survival analysis relies on a modest sample.
future_research: Researchers should examine whether gender differences extend to the choice between cofounding and remaining a solo founder, and whether founder gender shapes subsequent selection of startup joiners (employees) given the path-dependent nature of early hiring decisions. Future work should also adopt a dyadic or systemic lens that incorporates how potential cofounders and joiners assess entrepreneurs and founding teams (including team gender composition), and could probe whether women founders become more resource-seeking as employees accumulate and relational needs are increasingly satisfied internally.
abstract: Research on entrepreneurial networking suggests that women may be more likely than men to select cofounders based on their resources (e.g., knowledge, skills, and experience), as a means to counteract investor bias. In contrast, research on gender in networking suggests that women may be more likely than men to select cofounders based on interpersonal attraction (e.g., liking, trustworthiness, and familiarity), because it aligns with their preference for stable and harmonious relationships. In this paper, we reconcile this tension and ask: "How, when, and why do women differ from men in how they choose cofounders?" We propose that women entrepreneurs generally prioritize interpersonal attraction and de-prioritize resources because doing so aligns with their interdependent self-construal, but these relationships are mitigated when legitimacy is low, such that women entrepreneurs decrease their use of interpersonal attraction and increase their focus on resources as a means to bolster legitimacy. We find support for our framework across three studies. Our findings reconcile a key tension in the literature and highlight how interdependent self-construals enable women entrepreneurs to flexibly adjust their networking approach to cofounder selection by prioritizing interpersonal attraction when legitimacy is high and by increasing their use of resource seeking when legitimacy is low.
apa_citation: Gray, S. M., Howell, T., Strassman, J., & Yamamoto, K. (2024). Credentials or chemistry? Entrepreneur gender and cofounder selection. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(5), 1302-1330. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0640

title: Revving Up or Backing Down? Cross-Level Effects of Firm-Level Tournaments on Employees’ Competitive Actions
methods: Conditional fixed-effects Poisson QML estimation on rider-lap panel data; supplementary OLS linear probability models on rider-sector overtake opportunities; coarsened exact matching robustness checks; six sets of fixed effects (rider, rider rank, team rank, lap, racetrack, year).
industry: Professional motorcycle racing (MotoGP championship)
country: International (races across roughly 16-19 countries per season)
units: Rider-lap observations (riders nested in teams)
n_sample: 104,606 rider-lap observations involving 125 distinct riders
authors: Hallila, P.; Frankort, H. T. W.; Aversa, P.
theories: career-concerns theory; common-enemy effects; individual reputational incentives; resource-based view; tournament theory
keywords: MotoGP; career concerns; common-enemy effects; competitive actions; cross-level effects; reputational incentives; tournament theory
research_question: How do a firm’s competitive threats and opportunities in a firm-level tournament affect the internal and external competitive actions of its employees, who themselves strive for career-related rewards in a nested individual-level tournament?
mechanism_summary: The proposed mechanism centers on individual reputational incentives: employees anticipate that (prospective) employers' assessments of their ability incorporate the firm's tournament standing, so employees modulate their behavior to dissociate from potential losses and associate with potential gains. Under firm-level competitive threat, internal competitive actions become reputationally costly (employees risk being seen as blameworthy if the firm loses standing) whereas external competitive actions help fend off the threat. Under firm-level competitive opportunities, both internal and external competitive actions help the employee claim credit for the firm's improved standing. These effects intensify when potential movements are unexpected — when threats come from resource-disadvantaged underdogs or opportunities arise against resource-advantaged favorites.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances tournament theory by demonstrating that the locus and intensity of employees' competitive actions respond not only to the individual-level career tournament (the prevailing focus of the literature) but also to the dynamics of the firm-level tournament in which it is nested. It also extends the common-enemy literature by introducing vertical differentiation among competing firms (threats from below vs. opportunities against above) and by accounting for individual career concerns that allow employees to engage in both internal and external competitive actions simultaneously.
practical_implication: Managers can shape employees' competitive behaviors by strategically framing the firm's tournament position — emphasizing competitive threats from underdogs to redirect employees away from internal rivalry and toward external competition, or highlighting opportunities against favorites to mobilize both internal and external competitive efforts. Understanding cross-level reputational dynamics may help organizations harness or temper destructive intra-firm competition during periods of firm-level pressure.
limitations: MotoGP is an unusually transparent setting with formal rules, observable competitive actions, homogeneous roles, and clear separation of collaboration and competition; these features may not generalize to traditional organizations where reputations form more slowly, roles vary, regulations shift, and collaboration and competition coexist. Competitive actions in MotoGP take a single observable form (overtakes) and may not capture the broader, more nuanced range of competitive behaviors in conventional firms.
future_research: Subsequent studies should examine cross-level tournament effects in settings where competitive threats and opportunities are cognitively appraised rather than objectively shared, where collaboration and competition are interdependent, and where regulations are less stable. Researchers might also explore how the position of a firm-level tournament within a hierarchy of tournaments shapes employee incentives, and probe heterogeneity in employee responses tied to contractual conditions, role differences, and the lateral vs. vertical targeting of internal competitive actions.
abstract: The tournament literature has typically traced employees’ competitive actions to characteristics of individual-level career tournaments. Yet, such individual-level tournaments usually transcend firms that themselves compete in a firm-level tournament. We study the cross-level implications of a firm-level tournament for the competitive actions that constituent employees undertake against other individuals internal and external to their firm. We propose a theory of individual reputational incentives, which predicts that a firm’s competitive threats decrease its employees’ internal competitive actions yet increase their external competitive actions, while a firm’s competitive opportunities increase employees’ internal and external competitive actions. The theory also predicts that these effects are largest when a firm faces potential unexpected losses or gains in its standing, such as when the firm experiences competitive threats from resource-disadvantaged firms, or competitive opportunities against resource-advantaged firms. In panel data on the population of motorcycle riders competing in MotoGP from 2004 to 2020, we examine these hypotheses using overtakes to measure riders’ internal and external competitive actions. Our findings reveal how riders adjust their internal and external overtakes based on their team’s competitive threats and opportunities, and on the relative resource endowments of the teams supplying such threats or opportunities.
apa_citation: Hallila, P., Frankort, H. T. W., & Aversa, P. (2024). Revving up or backing down? Cross-level effects of firm-level tournaments on employees’ competitive actions. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(5), 1331–1358. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0946

title: Faking It with the Boss’s Jokes? Leader Humor Quantity, Follower Surface Acting, and Power Distance
methods: Three studies testing a moderated-mediation model in which leader humor quantity (LH quantity) increases follower surface acting and indirectly worsens follower emotional exhaustion and job satisfaction, with follower power distance moderating the first stage. Study 1 is a one-week field experiment with 88 leader-follower dyads at organizations in Southern China, randomly assigning leaders enrolled in a leadership course to a LH quantity intervention or control condition with follower-reported outcomes (Time 2 surveys) and ANOVA plus Mplus 8.5 single-level regression with robust standard errors and bias-corrected bootstrapping (10,000 resamples) for indirect effects. Study 2 is a pre-registered 2 (LH quantity: low vs. high) x 2 (power distance: low vs. high) between-subjects laboratory experiment in 49 focus groups (n = 198 students/employees in the U.S.) led by a trained professional actor-moderator delivering scripted puns and power-distance norms, analyzed via cluster-robust regression accounting for nested groups. Study 3 is a pre-registered three-wave correlational field study at a Southern China technology firm (n = 126 employees) combining employee surveys (Times 1 and 2, separated by ~1 week) with company pulse-survey records (Time 3) for outcomes; analyses use Mplus 8.5 path models (Hayes Models 4 and 7), CFAs, controls for leader humor quality, LMX, charismatic leadership, positive affect, demographics, and 10,000 bootstrap resamples. Supplemental mini meta-analyses across studies, plus exploratory tests of LH quantity x LH quality interactions, mediation, and additive models.
industry: Mixed organizations of Chinese part-time professional-degree managers and their direct subordinates (Study 1); university bookstore focus-group context with student and local-employee participants in the northeastern United States (Study 2); a single technology firm with a project-based structure based in Southern China (Study 3).
country: Study 1: Southern China; Study 2: northeastern United States; Study 3: Southern China
time_period: Study 1: one workweek (Time 1 leader manipulation, Time 2 follower outcomes one week later); Study 2: ~30-minute in-person experimental session; Study 3: three waves spanning approximately two weeks (Time 1, Time 2 ~5 workdays later, Time 3 ~1 week after Time 2).
units: Leader-follower dyads (Study 1); individual focus-group participants nested in 49 groups led by a focus-group leader (Study 2); employees reporting on focal managers within a single firm (Study 3).
n_sample: Study 1: 88 leader-follower dyads (42 control, 46 LH quantity); Study 2: 198 participants across 49 focus groups; Study 3: 126 employees with complete data across all three time points.
authors: Hu, Xiaoran; Parke, Michael R.; Peterson, Randall S.; Simon, Grace M.
theories: emotion regulation (Diefendorff & Gosserand, 2003; Elfenbein, 2007); emotional labor theory (Grandey, 2000; Grandey & Gabriel, 2015; Morris & Feldman, 1996); experience of power and social hierarchy (Magee & Galinsky, 2008); leader humor research (Cooper, 2005; Cooper, Kong, & Crossley, 2018); power distance values (Hofstede, 1980; Kirkman et al., 2009)
keywords: emotion regulation; emotional exhaustion; emotional labor; field experiment; follower surface acting; job satisfaction; laboratory experiment; leader humor quality; leader humor quantity; leader-follower relationships; multi-wave field study; power distance
research_question: Why and when does leader humor quantity (LH quantity) — irrespective of its quality — increase follower surface acting and, through this emotion-regulation pathway, impair follower emotional exhaustion and job satisfaction; and how does follower power distance moderate these effects?
mechanism_summary:
- Mediator: Follower surface acting — masking, faking, or suppressing felt emotions to display positive emotions (amusement, happiness, interest) toward the leader.
- Moderator: Follower power distance — the extent to which followers accept unequal power distribution between leaders and followers (first-stage moderator on LH quantity to surface acting).
- Controls: Leader humor quality, follower positive affect, deep acting, leader-member exchange (LMX), charismatic leadership, follower/leader demographics.

Drawing on emotional labor theory and the experience of power, the authors argue that leader humor signals a strong positive display rule (laughing, smiling, amusement) that followers — as lower-power actors dependent on leaders for resources and acceptance — feel obliged to meet. Because humor is unexpected and individualized, frequent expression creates frequent discrepancies between followers' naturally occurring affect and the expected display, making surface acting (rather than deep acting) the available response-focused regulation strategy. Surface acting then drains cognitive resources and produces inauthenticity, increasing exhaustion and reducing satisfaction. The effect is amplified for high-power-distance followers because (a) compliance with leader display standards is more instrumental to their goals and (b) leader humor more sharply violates their counter-normative expectation that leaders maintain social distance.
theoretical_contribution: The paper challenges the dominant positive view of leader humor on follower well-being by theoretically and empirically distinguishing leader humor quantity from leader humor quality, identifying surface acting as a novel emotion-regulation mechanism through which expressing humor frequently can backfire, and positioning follower power distance as a key moderator of when LH quantity is most damaging. Beyond leader humor research, the work contributes to emotional labor theory by showing that a positively intended leader behavior — not just negative interpersonal events — can elevate surface acting, and to the broader humor literature by adding "how much" and "to whom" as risks alongside the traditional "what kind."
practical_implication: Leaders should scale back the frequency of humor expression and adopt a "less is more" approach — focusing on fewer, higher-reward humor attempts to capture the benefits of humor quality while avoiding the emotional-labor costs of humor quantity, especially because followers are often surface-acting positive feedback that masks the harm. Managers should also calibrate humor to follower power-distance values: high-power-distance followers are more likely to fake positive responses and bear well-being costs, so managers can use existing surveys (e.g., Kirkman et al., 2009 scale) to gauge follower values before expressing humor.
limitations: Sample sizes were modest in Study 1 (n = 88) and Study 3 (n = 126), reducing power to detect direct effects of LH quantity on well-being and possibly affecting measure reliability; the Dorfman-Howell (1988) power distance measure had Cronbach's alpha below the 0.70 threshold in Studies 1 and 3, although within the range reported in prior work. Study 3 was correlational, could not rule out nesting effects given anonymized data, and relied on employees as the source for all model variables, raising common-method-bias concerns despite time separation and supplemental tests.
future_research: Future studies should examine other boundary conditions (e.g., perceived appropriateness of leader humor, follower's own sense of humor, group humor norms, humor styles such as self-enhancing vs. self-defeating) and additional mechanisms beyond surface acting and positive affect — such as follower appreciation of humor attempts — to reconcile the joint effects of LH quantity and LH quality. Within-person/event-level designs that track multiple humor episodes throughout a day, larger and cross-cultural samples with stronger power-distance measures, and disentangling leader power from follower power distance would help specify the optimal ratio of high- versus low-quality humor and identify factors (e.g., leaders explicitly acknowledging display rules) that mitigate the harms of LH quantity for high-power-distance followers.
abstract: Most scholarly work concludes that leader humor positively affects emotional outcomes for followers. However, two interrelated issues with past research make this view incomplete: (a) studies often conflate the act of expressing humor with the humor’s effectiveness, and (b) existing research overlooks follower emotion regulation. In addressing these issues, we generate and test new theory that challenges the theoretical consensus, highlighting why and when leader humor expression has emotional costs for followers. Specifically, we theorize that leader humor quantity, irrespective of its quality, puts pressure on followers to engage in “surface acting” to fake or exaggerate positive emotions. We further propose that these surface acting responses are more likely to occur when followers hold high power distance values, such that followers more willingly comply with expectations to display positive emotions in response to leader humor expression. This increase in surface acting then leads to more emotional exhaustion and less job satisfaction for followers. Results from three studies—incorporating a field experiment, a laboratory experiment, and a multi-wave field study—provide support for these hypotheses. We close with a discussion of how our findings provide a unique counterpoint regarding the effects of leader humor on follower emotional outcomes.
apa_citation: Hu, X., Parke, M. R., Peterson, R. S., & Simon, G. M. (2024). Faking it with the boss's jokes? Leader humor quantity, follower surface acting, and power distance. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(5), 1175-1206. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0195

title: Hiding and Seeking Knowledge-Providing Ties from Rivals: A Strategic Perspective on Network Perceptions
methods: Mixed methods: two between-subjects online experiments (Experiment 1 N=248; Experiment 2 N=259 with 2x2 design) testing rivalry-induction and hiding/seeking intentions and behaviors; plus a two-wave field study using cognitive social network data from 73 employees of a chemical company headquarters, analyzed with MRQAP (dyadic level) and OLS / negative binomial regression (individual level for career outcomes).
industry: Online experiments: working adults across various industries (e.g., 13% health care, 12% education); field study: a leading global chemical company (Taiwan-Japan joint venture) supplying agrochemicals, textile, food, pharmaceutics, metals, construction, and electronics products.
country: United States (online experimental panels) and Asia (field study; Taiwan-Japan international joint venture).
time_period: Field-study network data collected in two waves one year apart, with archival career-outcome data drawn three and four years after the wave-2 survey.
units: Individual employees and dyads (i.e., perceiver-target pairs within the same organization); reduced to 69 individuals for the longitudinal career-outcome regressions after attrition.
n_sample: Experiment 1: 248 participants; Experiment 2: 259 participants; field study: 73 employees (5,329 dyads = 73*73 - 73), reduced to 69 individuals for career-outcome models.
authors: Kilduff, Martin; Wang, Kun; Lee, Sun Young; Tsai, Wenpin; Chuang, You-Ta; Tsai, Fu-Sheng
theories: cognitive social network theory; social psychology of rivalry; structurally equivalent rivalry theory
keywords: career outcomes; cognitive social networks; errors of omission; knowledge hiding; knowledge-providing ties; network perceptions; rivalry; structural equivalence
research_question: Do individuals in organizations hide their knowledge-providing ties from those they perceive as rivals, and do they actively seek to detect the knowledge-providing ties of those rivals — and does success in this hiding and seeking translate into career advantage? More foundationally, does the perception of structural equivalence engender rivalry and motivate action toward perceived rivals?
mechanism_summary: The proposed mechanism extends structurally equivalent rivalry theory: when ego perceives a coworker to share many of the same knowledge providers, this triggers rivalrous feelings of competitive threat. Rivalry then motivates two complementary forms of strategic network manipulation — minimizing one's own errors of omission with respect to the rival's knowledge ties (seeking) and maximizing the rival's errors of omission with respect to one's own ties (hiding). Hiding success is hypothesized to confer career benefits because protected knowledge sources support innovative work, while seeking success is hypothesized to provide informational advantage through awareness of rivals' resources. The experiments validate the assumption that perceived structural equivalence ignites rivalry and prompts action; the field study shows these processes operate in a real organization even after controlling for actual structural equivalence, common friendship ties, and direct knowledge-sharing ties.
theoretical_contribution: The paper provides the first empirical test of two foundational but previously untested assumptions of structural-equivalence research: that perceived structural equivalence engenders rivalry, and that individuals take strategic action toward those perceived as structural equivalents. It bridges the social-psychological literature on dyadic rivalry with the network literature on structural equivalence, and contributes a new perspective on cognitive social networks by showing that errors in network perception are not merely cognitive limitations but partly the outcome of strategic deception (hiding) and detection (seeking) in pursuit of competitive advantage.
practical_implication: Encouraging individual-level competition for valued goals such as salary increases or promotions can have the unintended consequence of stifling the cooperation organizations require, because rivalrous employees devote effort to concealing their knowledge sources and probing those of others. Managers may need to make knowledge-sharing relationships more transparent and to design incentives that reward collaboration over the maintenance of exclusive knowledge ties, while balancing the motivational benefits of competition against the corrosive effects of rivalry.
limitations: The field-study sample is relatively small (73 employees, 69 retained for individual-level career outcomes), limiting statistical power particularly for the conservative two-tailed career-outcome tests. The study captures hiding and seeking outcomes through observed network-perception data rather than directly observing tactics in real time, and the three- to four-year lag between independent variables and career outcomes introduces noise that may obscure effects. Mutual rivalry was rare in the empirical context, so the seeking-trumps-hiding interaction result should be interpreted with caution.
future_research: Future work could establish the causal chain from perceived structural equivalence through rivalry to hiding and seeking using longitudinal observational designs, identify the specific tactics individuals use to conceal knowledge providers and to detect rivals' contacts, and examine whether individual gains from hiding and seeking damage broader organizational productivity. Researchers could also investigate boundary conditions in non-competitive settings (e.g., mentorship dyads, multiplex relationships) and extend the framework to firm-level effects of false-positive and false-negative network perceptions.
abstract: Rivalry is endemic in society and organizations, fueling competitive intentions and behaviors. According to social network theory, rivalry emerges among people who, like siblings, have many of the same connections to others. For this structurally equivalent rivalry to have its effects, the individual must see the other person as a rival. We ask whether, in the context of competition, people seek to identify the knowledge providers of their rivals while striving to hide their own knowledge providers from perceived rivals. We conducted two experiments that showed, for the first time, that structural equivalence does induce feelings of rivalry and does lead people to take action with respect to perceived rivals, namely to hide and seek knowledge providers. Our analysis of timeseparated social network and outcome data from all 73 employees in the headquarters of a chemical company found support for these patterns of hiding and seeking in relation to perceived rivals. We also found limited evidence that career outcomes may be influenced by individuals' success in hiding and seeking. Bringing together research on rivalry and network cognition, we provide a new approach to the strategic deployment of deception and detection in social networks.
apa_citation: Kilduff, M., Wang, K., Lee, S. Y., Tsai, W., Chuang, Y.-T., & Tsai, F.-S. (2024). Hiding and seeking knowledge-providing ties from rivals: A strategic perspective on network perceptions. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(5), 1207-1233. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0091

title: Constructing Envelopes: How Institutional Custodians Can Tame Disruptive Algorithms
methods: Inductive grounded-theory qualitative analysis combining 1,512 newspaper articles, 1,185 pages of nine U.S. Congressional hearing transcripts, 39 semi-structured interviews with 29 actors, and a secondary dataset of 90,322 newspaper articles; coding via Gioia methodology with temporal bracketing into three phases.
industry: U.S. securities trading (financial markets)
units: Custodian and noncustodian actors (guardians, reformers, regulators, high-frequency traders, challengers) responding to high-frequency trading
n_sample: 140 individuals identified as publicly responding to high-frequency trading; 39 interviews with 29 individuals
authors: Marti, E.; Lawrence, T. B.; Steele, C. W. J.
theories: envelope theory; institutional custodianship; social dynamics of algorithms
keywords: U.S. securities trading; disruptive algorithms; envelopes; fairness; high-frequency trading; institutional custodians
research_question: How do custodians construct envelopes around algorithms that threaten to disrupt valued elements of organizational fields?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: institutional custodians vs. disruptive algorithms; values, governance, and practices of the field vs. impacts, inputs/outputs, and usage of algorithms; "envelope" as a boundary delimiting interactions
- Process / phases: Phase 1 (2009–2010, normative layer — guardians couple values to algorithmic impacts); Phase 2 (2010–2014, governance layer — reformers couple governance to algorithmic inputs/outputs); Phase 3 (2014–2016, practice layer — regulators couple legitimate practices to algorithmic usage)
- Triggering conditions: attention-increasing events (Aleynikov arrest 2009, Flash Crash 2010, publication of Flash Boys 2014) that heightened anxiety and fueled political contests
- Practices: rhetorical work (guardians), diagnostic and showcasing work (reformers via IEX, whistleblowing), enforcement actions and rule-making (regulators)
- Emergent outcomes: a three-layered envelope that transformed high-frequency trading from a disruptive threat into "an unproblematic part of the field"
- Boundary conditions: tightly bounded field; relatively passive algorithm-developing firms; well-connected custodians with clear roles

The process model shows custodians constructing envelopes through a "conjunctive" rather than linear sequence: each layer coupled a field element to an algorithmic aspect, with established layers motivating and resourcing subsequent custodians. Different custodian types drew on heterogeneous resources — guardians on prominence, reformers on technical expertise, regulators on formal authority — and their cumulative work delimited the algorithm's interactions with the field without any single actor needing to fully understand the algorithm's inner workings.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to research on the social dynamics of algorithms by generating empirically grounded process theory of how envelopes around algorithms are constructed, showing actors can work around algorithmic opacity by leveraging field-specific expertise rather than mastering code. It contributes to institutional custodianship research by identifying envelope construction as a third custodial response — distinct from buffering institutions and intervening in threats — that allows custodians to steer institutional change when threats make change unavoidable.
practical_implication: The findings suggest that actors lacking deep technological expertise or access to proprietary algorithms can still meaningfully influence the algorithmic transformation of society by coupling familiar field elements (values, governance arrangements, practices) to specific aspects of algorithms (their impacts, inputs/outputs, usage). Custodians can thereby tame disruptive algorithms without halting their adoption.
limitations: The high-frequency trading firms played a relatively passive role during the studied period, leaving open how proponents of algorithms work to counteract envelope construction. The field of U.S. securities trading is also relatively tightly bounded, limiting generalizability to algorithms that span multiple, less well-bounded fields (e.g., Google search algorithms).
future_research: Future work should explore how proponents of algorithms actively resist or counter custodial efforts, and how envelopes can be constructed around algorithms that traverse multiple fields or that disrupt less well-bounded fields. Researchers can also examine how exogenous shocks and adaptive algorithm development trigger new cycles of envelope-maintenance work.
abstract: The infusion of algorithms into organizational fields—accelerated by advances in artificial intelligence—can have disruptive effects that trigger defensive responses. One important response involves establishing a boundary around an algorithm to delimit its interactions with its environment—in engineering terms, constructing an "envelope." Yet, we know little about the process through which such envelopes are constructed. We address this issue by exploring how institutional custodians construct envelopes around disruptive algorithms. We empirically examine custodians' responses to the high-frequency trading algorithms that disrupted the field of U.S. securities trading, focusing on the years 2009–2016. Our inductive analysis shows that custodians created an envelope with interconnected normative, governance, and practice "layers" that jointly constrained high-frequency trading. Each layer emerged as custodians "coupled" one element of the field (e.g., its values) to one aspect of the disruptive algorithms (e.g., their impacts). Our study contributes to research on the social dynamics of algorithms by generating novel theory of how envelopes around algorithms are constructed, and to research on institutional custodianship by highlighting the constructing of envelopes as a custodial response to a wide range of threats—including, but not restricted to, disruptive algorithms.
apa_citation: Marti, E., Lawrence, T. B., & Steele, C. W. J. (2024). Constructing envelopes: How institutional custodians can tame disruptive algorithms. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(5), 1273–1301. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1343

title: Avoidance and Aggression in Stakeholder Engagement: The Impact of CEO Paranoia and Paranoia-Relevant Cues
methods: Longitudinal panel of 925 CEOs in 774 S&P 1500 firms over 2010-2017 (3,823 firm-year observations); CEO paranoia operationalized via a newly developed and validated content-analytic dictionary applied to earnings call transcripts (six components: anxiety, distrust, fear, obsession, suspicion, threat); Tobit regression for left-censored lobbying breadth and negative binomial regression for count-valued competitive actions, with two-digit SIC and year fixed effects, Heckman-style selection correction (inverse Mills ratio), robustness-of-inference-to-replacement (RIR), and two-stage residual inclusion (2SRI) endogeneity checks.
industry: S&P 1500 firms (multi-industry, with two-digit SIC dummies)
units: CEO-firm-year observations from earnings calls
n_sample: 3,823 firm-year observations covering 925 CEOs across 774 firms
authors: Ridge, Jason W.; Hill, Aaron D.; Ingram, Amy; Kolomeitsev, Sergei; Worrell, Dan L.
theories: psychology of paranoia and safety behaviors (Chan & McAllister, 2014; Freeman et al., 2001, 2002); trait activation theory (Tett & Burnett, 2003; Tett & Guterman, 2000); upper echelons theory (Hambrick & Mason, 1984)
keywords: CEO paranoia; aggression; avoidance; competitive actions; content analysis; lobbying breadth; paranoia-relevant cues; regulatory rulings; rival attacks; stakeholder engagement; trait activation; upper echelons theory
research_question: How does CEO paranoia affect firms' engagement with powerful external stakeholders (government and competitors), and how do paranoia-relevant cues (regulatory rulings and rival attacks) activate the trait to shift CEOs from avoidance toward more aggressive engagement?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Firm size, market performance (Tobin's Q), tax aggressiveness, governmental subsidies and contracts, lobbying expenditures, blockholder ownership, TMT power, CEO compensation and pay sensitivity, perceived political and nonpolitical risk, inverse Mills ratio, year and two-digit SIC dummies.

Drawing on upper echelons theory and trait-activation logic, the paper argues that CEO paranoia — anchored in hypervigilance, rumination, self-as-target bias, and sinister attribution bias — manifests primarily in avoidance safety behaviors that reduce engagement with external stakeholders perceived as threatening. When trait-relevant cues (severe regulatory rulings against the firm; rival attacks on the firm) signal that avoidance has failed to provide protection, the trait activates an alternative response: aggression, defined as direct engagement intended to deter further targeting. Empirically, paranoia is negatively associated with lobbying breadth (avoidance of government), and this relationship flips toward aggression as regulatory rulings become more severe; the predicted negative direct effect on competitive actions is not supported, but the activation interaction with rival attacks is positive and significant.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends upper echelons theory by integrating trait activation, showing that CEO traits — typically treated as stable predictors of stable outcomes — manifest differently when triggered by trait-relevant cues, opening a more dynamic view of how CEOs engage stakeholders over time. It introduces CEO paranoia as a previously overlooked but consequential trait in a literature dominated by self-assured, attention-seeking executive characterizations, and theorizes how paranoia produces stakeholder-specific patterns of avoidance and aggression. Methodologically, it develops and validates the first content-analytic measure of paranoia in management research, derived from psychology and applicable beyond CEOs to other linguistic populations.
practical_implication: The findings suggest that CEO paranoia has tangible firm-level consequences — for example, a one-standard-deviation increase in CEO paranoia is associated with roughly a 7% decrease in lobbying breadth, and small lobbying changes can carry meaningful tax-rate and contracting consequences. Boards, advisors, and shareholders should recognize that paranoid CEOs may avoid information exchange with regulators yet swing aggressively when targeted, while also potentially fostering bright-side outcomes such as more prudent risk management, supply-chain diversification, and verification practices.
limitations: The paper focuses only on two stakeholder types (government and competitors), so generalization to other stakeholders (boards, suppliers, investors, partners) is uncertain, and CEO paranoia is shown to operate differently across different stakeholders, suggesting context-specific contingencies the study cannot fully unpack. The negative direct effect of paranoia on competitive actions is not supported, indicating boundary conditions on the avoidance argument. The empirical setting is the relatively stable 2010-2017 economic period within S&P 1500 earnings-call discourse, raising concerns about extension to post-pandemic or other firm-level contexts; CEO selection and other endogeneity concerns are addressed but cannot be fully eliminated.
future_research: The authors call for studies extending CEO paranoia to other stakeholder relationships (cooperative-strategy partners, acquisitions, investors, boards, suppliers, buyers), examining tournament-theory and structural-interdependence dynamics within TMTs, and exploring how paranoia interacts with agency-theoretic accounts of executive decision-making. They encourage further validation of the content-analytic paranoia measure across different settings and populations, exploration of "bright" versus "dark" sides of subclinical paranoia (e.g., risk-management benefits vs. missed partnerships), and broader application of trait-activation logic to other CEO traits whose outcomes may also be cue-dependent rather than uniformly stable.
abstract: We develop and test theory regarding the effect of CEO paranoia, defined as stable tendencies toward suspicion, feelings of ill will or resentment, mistrust, and belief in external control or influence, on firm stakeholder engagement. We theorize that because CEOs higher in paranoia have tendencies toward hypervigilance and the biases of self-as-target and sinister attributions they typically avoid engagement with external stakeholders. Further, we argue that CEOs higher in paranoia are subject to paranoid activation when confronted with trait-relevant cues that confirm suspicions of being targeted by a malevolent external entity (here, regulatory rulings or rival attacks), thus eliciting a shift away from avoidance to a more aggressive engagement with those stakeholders. To do so, we develop a content-analytic measure of CEO paranoia following both theory and evidence in psychology and methodological best practices, finding evidence that broadly supports our premise. In total, our study draws attention to how the manifestation of CEO paranoia changes the way CEOs engage stakeholders over time, contributing to understanding in multiple ways.
apa_citation: Ridge, J. W., Hill, A. D., Ingram, A., Kolomeitsev, S., & Worrell, D. L. (2024). Avoidance and aggression in stakeholder engagement: The impact of CEO paranoia and paranoia-relevant cues. *Academy of Management Journal*, 67(5), 1392-1415. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1432
