title: Fostering Entrepreneurship in Nonmetro Areas: Ecosystem Architects as Conduits of Knowledge Spillovers
methods: Quasi-experimental field intervention with panel-data econometric analysis. The intervention was a two-day in-person gathering (Sept 5-6, 2019, Waseca, MN) of 116 entrepreneurial ecosystem architects (EEAs) from nonmetro Minnesota counties (37 of 84 nonmetro counties had at least one participating EEA). The unit of observation is county-month, with monthly business-registration counts from the Minnesota Secretary of State spanning ~5 years pre-intervention through March 2020. Main models are linear county fixed-effects (FE) panel models with monthly period FE, a linear time trend, cluster-robust standard errors clustered on county, and a 12-month lagged registrations control. Robustness checks include FE negative binomial models, panel GLM with log link, and an instrumental-variable specification using county centroid distance to the venue as an instrument for participation. Tables 1-3 and ancillary surveys/interviews characterize EEA roles and post-intervention beliefs.
industry: Mixed nonmetro economies (healthcare, waste management, utilities/renewable energy, educational services, manufacturing, services); EEAs work in nonprofits, local/county/city government, business or cooperative, college/university, and foundations
time_period: Five years prior to intervention through March 2020 (intervention held September 5-6, 2019)
units: Nonmetro counties in Minnesota (county-month panel); intervention attendees were individual entrepreneurial ecosystem architects (EEAs)
n_sample: 84 nonmetro counties (37 participant, 47 non-participant; Mahnomen excluded); 116 EEAs attended the conference; 5,572-6,175 county-month observations in regression models
authors: Dutta, Sunasir; Forbes, Daniel P.
theories: entrepreneurial ecosystems perspective; institutional theory / institutional work; knowledge spillover theory of entrepreneurship; social capital theory; social trust theory
keywords: bridging ties; business registrations; community improvement associations; economic inequality; ecosystem architects; entrepreneurial ecosystems; institutional work; knowledge spillovers; nonmetropolitan areas; regional entrepreneurship; social capital; social trust; tacit knowledge
research_question: How do locally rooted "entrepreneurial ecosystem architects" (EEAs) in less-endowed nonmetropolitan regions catalyze entrepreneurial activity, and how is their efficacy as conduits of voluntary, horizontal knowledge spillovers moderated by community-level social capital and social trust?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on institutional theory and the knowledge spillover theory of entrepreneurship, the authors argue that EEAs perform institutional maintenance work that catalyzes voluntary, horizontal knowledge spillovers within their communities. Because tacit knowledge transmission decays with geographic distance, EEAs in dispersed nonmetro counties typically lack access to nonredundant, cross-context knowledge. A temporary in-person gathering supplies that nonlocal tacit knowledge through cross-county "bridging" ties, which EEAs then channel to nascent entrepreneurs back home, "nudging" them past the registration threshold. This conduit effect is amplified where preexisting voluntary associations provide trusted, organized channels for dissemination, and dampened where high economic inequality erodes the generalized social trust required for entrepreneurs to act on advice from non-intimates. Empirically, participating counties registered roughly 10% more monthly foundings (about 1.8 additional registrations/month) than non-participants, with a one-SD increase in community improvement associations boosting the marginal effect by 23% and a two-percentage-point higher Gini wiping it out.
theoretical_contribution: First, the paper integrates institutional theory and knowledge spillover theory by recasting EEAs as individual-level conduits of voluntary, horizontal knowledge spillovers — augmenting a knowledge-spillover tradition historically focused on technical knowledge from established firms and universities. Second, by drawing on adjacent sociological theories of social capital and social trust, it specifies community-level moderators (civic associations, economic inequality) that determine whether spillovers are absorbed, addressing the "human dimension" of place that knowledge-spillover scholars have flagged but underdeveloped. Third, it advances a model of "bridging" institutional work that helps deeply embedded local actors in sparse, scattered communities escape the double-edged sword of localism without breaking the local ties that enable trust.
practical_implication: For regional entrepreneurship policy, the study illustrates that comparatively inexpensive "investments in spillover conduits" — informal temporary gatherings that help dispersed EEAs build cross-geographic bridging ties — can yield near-term increases in business registrations, particularly in resource-scarce nonmetro areas. However, policymakers should expect heterogeneous payoffs: communities with denser civic and improvement associations are better positioned to absorb the imported tacit knowledge, while communities with high economic inequality may absorb little of the benefit, suggesting that pairing such convenings with complementary investments in social capital and trust is necessary to translate knowledge access into entrepreneurial activity.
limitations: The study is confined to a single U.S. state (Minnesota), so generalizability across different regional, national, and institutional contexts is unverified. It is a quasi-experiment with first-come, first-served (rather than randomized) participation, which the authors mitigate but cannot fully eliminate through robustness checks and an instrumental-variable strategy. The dependent variable captures only one type of firm-formation event (business registration) and the study lacks individual-level evidence from entrepreneurs themselves linking specific EEA actions to specific registrations. Finally, social trust is proxied indirectly via economic inequality; trust has additional drivers the design does not measure.
future_research: Future work could systematically compare EEAs' work and influence across metro vs. nonmetro contexts and across different institutional regimes; trace at the individual level how EEAs' knowledge transmissions advance specific ventures from idea to registration; theorize and test additional drivers of community social trust beyond inequality; and examine richer temporal and spatial dynamics, including potential cross-county spillovers from neighboring counties' participation as time passes. Researchers might also clarify how EEAs shape the quality and composition of new ventures, and how EEAs themselves are reshaped by the ventures they support.
abstract: Prevailing theories hold that regional entrepreneurial activity is a function of each region's preexisting local resource endowments. But these theories do not explain entrepreneurial activity in regions that possess few of the requisite endowments, such as nonmetropolitan areas, which often lack many of the resources conducive to entrepreneurship. Drawing on institutional theory and knowledge spillover theory, we theorize that individuals engaged in institutional work play a key role in catalyzing entrepreneurial activity within such regions by serving as conduits of voluntary, horizontal knowledge spillovers. We propose that their ability to do so is enhanced when they access nonlocal tacit knowledge from others working in similar contexts. We further propose that, consistent with sociological theories of social capital and trust, local socioeconomic conditions can constrain the absorption of such knowledge and thereby local venturing in their home communities. Empirically, we study these propositions through a field intervention: an in-person gathering fostering tacit knowledge spillovers among individual entrepreneurial ecosystem architects from nonmetro counties in a Midwestern U.S. state. We conducted additional tests of alternative specifications, identification, and behavioral impacts. Taken together, these arguments and findings deepen our theoretical understanding of an important pathway in the development of regional entrepreneurial ecosystems.
apa_citation: Dutta, S., & Forbes, D. P. (2024). Fostering entrepreneurship in nonmetro areas: Ecosystem architects as conduits of knowledge spillovers. *Academy of Management Journal*, 68(4), 735-759. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.1249

title: Painting a Clear Picture while Seeing the Big Picture: When and Why Leaders Overcome the Trade-Off Between Concreteness and Scale
methods: Multimethod design. Study 1: archival analysis of 303 publicly traded firms using hierarchical OLS regression, with CEO speech patterns from quarterly earnings call transcripts (Factiva), hierarchy measures from Compustat/ExecuComp/The Official Board, and organizational change measured via R&D, acquisitions, and property sales. Study 2: preregistered 1x2 between-subjects online experiment (Prolific) measuring dispositional concreteness and manipulating hierarchy, with moderated mediation analysis.
industry: Publicly traded firms across eight SIC categories (construction, manufacturing, transportation/utilities, wholesale, retail, finance, services); Study 2 uses full-time employees with direct reports.
country: United States (Study 2 restricted to U.S. nationals living in the U.S.; Study 1 firms drawn from Compustat/ExecuComp, predominantly U.S.)
time_period: Study 1 core data set 2014 to 2018; Study 2 conducted during the COVID-19 delta variant wave.
units: CEOs of publicly traded firms (Study 1); online participants role-playing CEO of a fictional company (Study 2).
n_sample: 303 CEOs (Study 1); 403 participants (Study 2).
authors: Carton, A. M.; Knowlton, K.; Coutifaris, C. G. V.; Kundro, T. G.; Boysen, A. P.
theories: attention-based view (Ocasio, 1997); construal level theory (Wiesenfeld, Reyt, Brockner, & Trope, 2017; Trope & Liberman, 2003, 2010); organizational hierarchy (Bunderson, van der Vegt, Cantimur, & Rink, 2016)
keywords: CEO cognition; abstract orientation; attention; concrete orientation; construal level theory; large-scale change; organizational hierarchy; vision communication
research_question: When and why do leaders with a concrete cognitive orientation — who naturally focus on short-term, narrow details — overcome that predisposition and instead spearhead large-scale organizational change? Specifically, how does organizational hierarchy interact with leader concreteness to shape attention toward large-scale goals and, in turn, organizational change?
mechanism_summary:
- Mediator: CEO attention to large-scale (long-term and broad-scope) goals.
- Moderator: Organizational hierarchical strength (number of management layers and concentration of authority; five operationalizations in Study 1, manipulated in Study 2).

The decoupling mechanism runs as follows: leaders with a concrete orientation normally co-attend to short-term, narrow problems because concreteness and small-scale focus tend to covary in cognition. When such leaders helm organizations with strong hierarchies, however, structural distance from day-to-day operations and a wide span of control redirect their finite attention toward long-term, organization-wide problems. Because concrete thinkers can calibrate attentional shifts in discernible increments, they translate this redirected attention into pursuit of large-scale change (R&D, acquisitions, property sales). Abstract-oriented leaders, lacking the same precise sense of timeline and scope, are not similarly affected by hierarchy.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances construal level theory by introducing the notion of cognitive decoupling — showing that concreteness of thought and scale of attention, long presumed to covary, can be deliberately reconfigured by organizational structure. It builds a bridge between macro-organizational theory (organizational hierarchy) and organizational psychology (attention and construal) using attention as a linking pin, and it challenges person–job fit assumptions by showing that the leaders with the greatest change potential are initially misaligned with the top-leader role until structure redirects their attention.
practical_implication: Boards selecting CEOs should evaluate person–job fit not just on individual cognitive style but on the interaction between style and organizational hierarchy: concrete-thinking leaders are better positioned to drive substantial change in strongly hierarchical organizations, while flat organizations may need a different selection logic. Executive search firms that already assess leader concreteness should also encourage clients to account for hierarchical structure when making hiring and promotion decisions.
limitations: Study 1 lacks complete panel data, preventing fine-grained tracking of changes over time, and data for The Official Board variables were collected in a different year than Compustat/ExecuComp data, raising temporal-equivocality concerns. The concreteness measure captures speech rather than thought directly. Study 2's predicted interaction on change itself was not significant (only on the attention mediator), and most participants lacked CEO experience, limiting generalization to the Study 1 population.
future_research: Future scholars can examine how powerfully and consistently cognitive decoupling can occur across contexts, and integrate the present findings on attention redirection with prior research on how concreteness aids collective-action mobilization during change implementation. Researchers can also more systematically account for the evolving interplay between hierarchy and concreteness as the change process moves from enactment through implementation phases.
abstract: One of the most effective ways leaders can promote change is by generating visions with image-based rhetoric ("make children smile"). By conjuring visual snapshots of the future, leaders paint a portrait of what their organizations can one day achieve. It would thus stand to reason that leaders who naturally think and speak in terms of picture-like detail (a "concrete orientation") would promote more organizational change than those who are inclined to think vaguely (an "abstract orientation"). Yet, research has established that individuals with a concrete orientation tend to focus on short-term, narrow details (e.g., small features of a single product) rather than long-term visions requiring the coordinated effort of all employees. To determine how and when concrete-thinking leaders induce large-scale change, we integrate theory on attention, organizational hierarchy, and construal. We predict that leaders who have a concrete orientation will promote change by redirecting their attention toward long-term visions of the future, if their organizations have strong, rather than weak, hierarchies. By contrast, hierarchical strength will have no such effect on leaders with an abstract orientation. We test these predictions in an archival study of CEOs and then examine the attention-based process that helps explain this effect in a preregistered experiment.
apa_citation: Carton, A. M., Knowlton, K., Coutifaris, C. G. V., Kundro, T. G., & Boysen, A. P. (2023). Painting a clear picture while seeing the big picture: When and why leaders overcome the trade-off between concreteness and scale. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(1), 43–66. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.1019

title: The Role of Discernment and Modulation in Enacting Occupational Values: How Career Advising Professionals Navigate Tensions with Clients
methods: Two-phase qualitative study: (Phase 1) 18-month ethnography of an undergraduate business-school career-advice office at 'Ipsum University,' including 51 observational sessions, 37 participant-observation sessions (junior-adviser role), and 33 ethnographic interviews with advisers and students; (Phase 2) 48 semi-structured interviews with business-school career advisers at other ranked U.S. undergraduate business schools. Data analyzed inductively with grounded-theory coding.
industry: Undergraduate business-school career advising (university career services)
time_period: Phase 1 ethnography conducted over approximately one and a half years; Phase 2 interviews recruited from Poets and Quants' 2018 list of 88 ranked U.S. undergraduate business schools
units: Career advisers (individual professionals) and their interactions with student clients
n_sample: 88 observation/participant-observation sessions (~107 hours) plus 81 interviews (33 in Phase 1 with 6 advisers and 22 students; 48 in Phase 2 with advisers from 48 different schools)
authors: Chan, C. K.; Hedden, L. N.
theories: occupational values (Van Maanen & Barley; Kraatz et al.); relational perspective on occupations and professions; sociology of professions and jurisdictional control (Abbott)
keywords: career advising; discernment; intraclientele heterogeneity; jurisdictional control; modulation; occupational values; relational perspective on occupations; value-magnifying; value-masking; value-moderating
research_question: How can expert professionals enact their occupational values without jeopardizing their jurisdictional control when working with powerful clients who hold incongruent values?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: occupational values (purpose, individualized fit) vs. client values (wealth, prestige, social approval); jurisdictional control as the legitimacy and autonomy with which experts hold their task domain.
- Triggering conditions: skewed but heterogeneous clientele, advisers' inability to refuse clients, and downstream threats to jurisdictional control (negative student feedback to deans, school-ranking surveys, parental complaints).
- Discernment: advisers notice cues in the interaction (a student's declared career commitment, type of questions, openness to exploration) to infer whether the client's values are incongruent, unclear, or congruent.
- Modulation — three value-enactment approaches: (a) value-masking when values are discerned as incongruent — impartial career-attainment help, encouraging future visits, exclusively pragmatic resources; (b) value-moderating when values are unclear — gentle reframing, raising open questions, using neutral resources; (c) value-magnifying when values are congruent — undermining extrinsic pressure, encouraging alternative career paths, drawing on values-rich resources.
- Emergent outcome: experts uphold jurisdictional control while still enacting occupational values whenever the client situation permits; negative cases occur when discernment or modulation fails.

Across one ethnographic site and 48 interviews at other U.S. business schools, advisers navigated relational tensions not by abandoning their values nor by overtly resisting incongruent clients, but by reading each student's enacted values and adjusting how visibly and strongly to enact their own. The process unifies covert concealment, low-risk moderation, and rare overt enactment within a single mixed strategy for protecting jurisdictional control amid client power asymmetry.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to the relational perspective on occupations and professions by theorizing discernment and modulation as previously overlooked mechanisms through which experts navigate value tensions with clients. It reframes the field's "compliance-or-resistance" dichotomy as a continuum that includes situated masking, moderating, and magnifying, and shows that recognizing intraclientele heterogeneity is itself a strategic resource — overturning the common assumption that an occupation's clientele is monolithic. It also identifies a middle ground (value-moderating) between overt value enactment and outright value abstention that prior work on concealment has not articulated.
practical_implication: For professionals whose clients have grown more powerful (career advisers, lawyers, physicians, therapists, consultants), the study suggests cultivating diagnostic skill at reading client values and a repertoire of variably visible value-enactment practices, rather than committing wholesale to either accommodation or resistance. Occupational training that emphasizes relational skills and a service-oriented disposition toward heterogeneous clients can help experts protect their jurisdiction without surrendering their professional purpose.
limitations: Findings derive from one ethnographic site and an interview sample of business-school career advisers at U.S. ranked undergraduate institutions, so the specific value tensions (purpose/fit vs. wealth/prestige/social approval) may not generalize beyond similar advice-giving contexts. The authors propose two boundary conditions: discernment and modulation likely require relatively lengthy client interactions and occupational socialization that emphasizes relational, service-oriented skills; occupations with brief, scripted, or adversarial client encounters may not show the same mechanisms.
future_research: Future work could examine variation across occupations in how experts read and exploit intraclientele heterogeneity, including whether experts actively cultivate (rather than merely discern) client diversity, and whether individual-level differences in discernment ability shape outcomes. Studies of occupations where negative cases dominate could reveal when sustained value enactment with unreceptive clients threatens an occupation's overall survival, and additional contexts could test the proposed boundary conditions on interaction length and occupational socialization.
abstract: Enacting occupational values is vital to expert professionals' solidarity and sense of purpose. Yet, many professionals face audiences in their relational contexts—especially powerful clients—who can hold incongruent values and may threaten professionals' jurisdictional control. How can experts enact their values without jeopardizing their jurisdictional control amid clients holding incongruent values? We examine career advisers in undergraduate business schools, whose occupational values often contrasted with values common among their student clients. Through an ethnography of one school's career advisers, combined with interviews of such advisers throughout the United States, we find that advisers navigated interactions by discerning student values and accordingly modulating their value-enactment practices through masking, moderating, or magnifying their values. This allowed advisers to uphold their jurisdictional control when facing students exhibiting incongruent values, while enacting their values with students exhibiting unclear or congruent values. We contribute to the relational perspective on occupations and professions by positing how discernment and modulation help experts navigate relational tensions by recognizing and drawing on intraclientele heterogeneity, unpacking how professionals might not entirely resist or change amid incongruence but instead pursue a more mixed approach, and highlighting when and how experts mask or moderate rather than overtly enact their values.
apa_citation: Chan, C. K., & Hedden, L. N. (2023). The role of discernment and modulation in enacting occupational values: How career advising professionals navigate tensions with clients. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(1), 276–305. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1014

title: Valuing Spanners: Why Category Nesting and Expertise Matter
methods: Mixed-methods design combining a longitudinal correlational analysis of 29,033 venture capital deals (1994-2017) using Heckman two-stage selection models with year fixed effects and double clustering (Study 1), and two preregistered experimental studies with mediation analysis using PROCESS macro (Studies 2 and 3); Study 3 uses a 2x2 between-subjects design with concurrent double-randomization to manipulate the mediator (congruence) and test causality.
industry: Venture capital deals across multiple industries (with a focus on finance and information technology in Studies 2 and 3)
country: Worldwide, predominantly North America (88.2%), Europe (9.5%), and East Asia (1.4%); Study 2 used Hong Kong undergraduate students; Study 3 used U.S. Prolific participants
time_period: 1994 to 2017 (with analysis covering 2000-2017 due to six-year rolling windows)
units: Venture capital funding deals (Study 1); individual participants evaluating start-up profiles (Studies 2 and 3)
n_sample: 29,033 VC deals (Study 1, with 4,932 investors and 15,811 companies); 307 undergraduate students (Study 2); 396 online participants (Study 3)
authors: Cudennec, A.; Durand, R.
theories: categorization theory; expertise and cognitive entrenchment; information processing perspective
keywords: category nesting; category spanning; congruence; expertise; information processing; optimal distinctiveness; valuation; venture capital
research_question: How does category nesting (subordinate vs. basic category spanning) and audience expertise jointly affect investors' valuation of category-spanning firms, and to what extent does perceived inter-category congruence mediate this relationship?
mechanism_summary: The central mechanism is information processing: subordinate categories are more specific but less distinctive than basic categories, so finding congruence among spanned subcategories demands greater cognitive effort. Average investors fail to perceive harmony among subcategory cues and therefore devalue subcategory spanners relative to basic-category spanners. Expertise increases the ability to discriminate subcategories within one's knowledge domain and to perceive their complementarity as congruent, which attenuates and can reverse the subcategory-spanning penalty for expert evaluators.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to three streams. First, it advances the optimal distinctiveness literature by showing that category nesting and audience information-processing capacity are essential dimensions of an organization's positioning. Second, it reframes congruence in market categorization from an evaluation of similarity to preexisting types into a meaningful correspondence among categorical cues across nested levels, recalibrating the supposed benefits of categorical purism (subcategory spanners may have been miscounted as purists in prior studies). Third, it documents how expertise differentially modulates valuation penalties at subordinate versus basic levels of nesting, resolving prior "mixed results" on expertise effects on valuations of deviations from categorical standards.
practical_implication: Identifying with subcategories can be a rewarding differentiation strategy, but only if entrepreneurs target investors whose expertise lies within the focal domain. Founders should pay attention not only to market structure and category nesting but also to their target audience's degree of expertise in order to maximize valuation. Category spanning is best understood as a tool for achieving simultaneous conformity and distinctiveness through congruent informational cues.
limitations: The industrial category structure used is relatively simple and does not account for spanning across subordinate categories belonging to different basic categories or for highly normative categorical boundaries that could trigger moral sanctions. The study focuses on VC investors as a single audience type; other audiences (consumers, crowdfunding users, media, regulators) may evaluate categorical combinations differently. The expertise measure relies on objective knowledge tests in experimental settings rather than real-world expert samples or direct manipulation of expertise.
future_research: Scholars could replicate the framework with more complex nested category associations, test the theory under highly normative categorical boundaries, examine other audiences (consumers, media, legal bodies, crowdfunding users), and ensure exogeneity of expertise through real-life expert samples or direct manipulation. Alternative or complementary mechanisms such as flexibility, framing (concrete "how" vs. abstract "why"), interest, conscientiousness, or intelligence deserve further scrutiny, as does whether more expert investors actually make better valuation decisions.
abstract: Organizations need to both differentiate themselves while conforming to their audiences' expectations. To meet this demand, organizations may span different categories. However, valuing spanners is challenging for audiences. We contend that spanners' valuation depends on category nesting, as the congruence of informational cues varies between basic categories and subcategories. Furthermore, we expect that more expert audiences find spanners to be more congruent (and hence, more valuable) at a subordinate level than at a basic level of categorization. We test our hypotheses using a mixedmethods design in the context of venture capital investments. We analyze observational data on more than 29,000 venture capital deals and develop two experimental studies. Our findings support our hypotheses that subcategory spanning lowers valuation, and that this effect is attenuated as investors' expertise increases. Our experimental studies further show that congruence is a causal mechanism explaining these effects. Our findings have important implications for research on organizational conformity and optimal distinctiveness, categorization in markets from an information processing perspective, and the impact of expertise on valuation.
apa_citation: Cudennec, A., & Durand, R. (2023). Valuing spanners: Why category nesting and expertise matter. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(1), 335-365. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0042

title: Gains and Losses: Week-To-Week Changes in Leader–Follower Relationships
methods: Two within-person experience-sampling studies with weekly surveys; hierarchical linear modeling and polynomial regression with response surface methodology; multilevel CFAs; critical incidents coding in Study 2.
industry: Mixed (Study 1: employed adults from a university survey center; Study 2: sales company)
time_period: Study 1: 10 weekly Friday surveys; Study 2: 8 weekly Friday surveys
units: Employees nested in weekly observations (leader–follower dyads)
n_sample: Study 1: 73 individuals, 464 weekly observations; Study 2: 613 longer-tenured (2,136 obs) plus 145 newly hired (324 obs)
authors: Dimotakis, N.; Lambert, L. S.; Fu, S. Q.; Boulamatsi, A.; Smith, T. A.; Runnalls, B.; Corner, A. J.; Tepper, B. J.; Maurer, T.
theories: Approach–avoidance / positive–negative affect frameworks; Leader–member exchange (LMX) theory; Social comparison / referent-standard theory; Social exchange theory
keywords: LMX; critical incidents; gains and losses; leader–member exchange; negative affect; positive affect; social comparison; week-to-week change
research_question: How do week-to-week changes (gains and losses) in leader–member exchange (LMX), evaluated against the follower's own prior-week LMX as a referent standard, relate to followers' positive and negative affect, and what within-week incidents and prior states explain such LMX change?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on social exchange and LMX theory, the authors argue that followers evaluate their LMX by comparing the current week to the most proximal and available referent — their own previous week — and that the resulting gains or losses generate affective responses through approach (PA) and avoidance (NA) systems. Losses are theorized to weigh more heavily than gains; stable-high LMX yields higher PA and lower NA than stable-low LMX; and weekly incidents drive LMX change with positive incidents amplified by relevance and prior LMX, while negative incidents are unconditionally aversive.
theoretical_contribution: The paper relaxes LMX theory's long-standing stability assumption and shows that even mature LMX dyads exhibit meaningful week-to-week fluctuation, with gains tied to PA and losses tied to NA in an asymmetric pattern. It distinguishes differentiation (between-dyad variance, which grows with relationship maturity) from stability (within-dyad variance, which does not shrink with maturity), and integrates referent-comparison logic into social-exchange theorizing on hierarchical relationships.
practical_implication: Leaders should not treat established LMX relationships as static: maintaining relationships matters as much as cultivating them, because week-to-week deterioration generates negative affect even in otherwise high-quality dyads. Low-quality relationships can be improved but more slowly, since prior LMX moderates the impact of positive incidents; negative incidents, by contrast, hurt regardless of context, so leaders should guard against them and "cushion" unavoidable declines.
limitations: The studies examined only one leadership construct (LMX), one temporal frame (week), one referent (prior self), and one main outcome family (affect), all from a single (follower) source. Common-method concerns remain despite the time-separated, nonlinear design. The newcomer subsample had a low within-individual response rate, limiting tests of change dynamics in newly formed relationships.
future_research: Future work should integrate other referents (self vs. others, episode vs. average), other time frames, and other leadership constructs; examine antecedents of sustained upward or downward LMX trends and "crossing-over" between in- and out-groups; investigate person- and situation-level moderators (e.g., neuroticism, power distance) of sensitivity to gains and losses; and study leader-side reactions to perceived LMX changes for a more complete dyadic view.
abstract: Although the importance of leader–follower relationships in the workplace has been widely acknowledged, the impact of changes in such relationships has not been adequately examined in the literature. Leader–member exchange (LMX) theory and research have suggested that leader–follower relationships develop, mature, and then stabilize. Integrating recent work that has found important and impactful within-individual variations in what was thought to be relatively unvarying relationships, we theorize that leader–follower relationships (like other linkage types), are not only differentiated (varying between people) but also fluctuate across time periods (varying within people). We also discuss how people can understand their relationships better by comparing them to a relevant and proximal referent; this underlines how prior levels of LMX serve as a lens by which followers evaluate their current LMX. Across two studies, we discuss how comparing this week's LMX to last week's LMX can signal improvement (i.e., gains) or deterioration (i.e., losses) in the relationship, with differential implications for positive and negative affect. We also provide additional insights into LMX fluctuations by contrasting within- and between-individual variance in established and newly founded relationships, and by examining those incidents that are more likely to influence LMX in a given week.
apa_citation: Dimotakis, N., Lambert, L. S., Fu, S. Q., Boulamatsi, A., Smith, T. A., Runnalls, B., Corner, A. J., Tepper, B. J., & Maurer, T. (2023). Gains and losses: Week-to-week changes in leader–follower relationships. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(1), 248–275. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1100

title: Receiving Service from a Person with a Disability: Stereotypes, Perceptions of Corporate Social Responsibility, and the Opportunity for Increased Corporate Reputation
methods: Multistudy, multimethod design. Study 1: field survey of 317 supermarket customers in Lithuania with regression and PROCESS-macro moderated-mediation. Study 2: online experiment using a Solomon four-group design with video vignettes (n=562 after exclusions), tested with PROCESS-macro mediation and moderation.
industry: Retail (supermarket/grocery chain)
units: Customers receiving service from cashiers with and without disabilities
n_sample: 317 (Study 1 field study); 562 (Study 2 online experiment, after attention and manipulation checks)
authors: Dwertmann, David J. G.; Goštautaitė, Bernadeta; Kazlauskaitė, Ruta; Bučiūnienė, Ilona
theories: signaling theory; stereotype content model; stigma-by-association; valuation-by-association
keywords: corporate reputation; corporate social responsibility; disability; signaling theory; stereotypes; valuation by association
research_question: Does customer experience of receiving service from an employee with a disability improve customer-based corporate reputation, and if so, through what mechanism (CSR perceptions) and under what boundary conditions (contact quality, CSR motive attributions)?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on signaling theory, the authors argue that hiring people with disabilities in customer-facing roles signals organizational concern for social well-being and is interpreted as a costly, sincere CSR act, which in turn boosts corporate reputation. Stereotype-disconfirming positive contact with employees with disabilities updates customers' competence (and to a lesser extent warmth) stereotypes via causal reasoning. Through valuation by association, the positive evaluation of the stigmatized employee transfers to the employing organization, raising its reputation — but only when customers attribute sincere (not egoistic) motives to the hiring.
theoretical_contribution: The paper provides the first multimethod empirical evidence linking employment of people with disabilities to better corporate reputation through CSR perceptions, refining stigma-by-association theory by demonstrating positive "valuation by association" at the organizational level. It connects disability research with signaling theory and the stereotype content model, and identifies CSR motive attributions as a critical boundary condition that determines whether disability-inclusive hiring is interpreted as reputational asset or liability.
practical_implication: Managers should view employment of people with disabilities in customer-facing roles as an opportunity rather than a reputational threat: such employment signals CSR and improves corporate reputation. Organizations should communicate sincere, value-driven motives for inclusive hiring rather than emphasizing tax incentives or self-interest, and should ensure good disability–job fit through accommodations, onboarding, and training so that service contact is satisfactory.
limitations: Both studies were conducted in Lithuania with one supermarket chain, limiting generalizability across industries and national contexts. Social desirability is a concern in disability research, although the authors mitigate it via unobtrusive measures, control variables, and the Solomon four-group design. CSR and corporate reputation correlate highly (.71–.79) in Study 2, raising discriminant-validity questions despite supportive CFA results.
future_research: Future studies should test whether positive contact effects on stereotypes generalize across disabilities or remain disability-specific, investigate effects in different industries and countries, and disentangle the distinct roles of disability visibility vs. customer awareness of the disability. Researchers should also probe the CSR–corporate reputation distinction further given the high observed correlation.
abstract: Whereas advocates point to benefits of employing people with disabilities for organizations, employers’ concern over negative customer reactions is still a barrier to the employment of people with disabilities in service occupations. We contribute to this discussion and the management literature on disability by examining the effects of receiving service from employees with a hearing disability and employees who use a wheelchair on corporate reputation. Based on signaling theory, stereotypes, and valuation-by-association logic, we argue and find in a multistudy, multimethod approach that employing people with disabilities can be perceived as corporate social responsibility and leads to better corporate reputation. A field study with 317 customers of a large international supermarket chain in Lithuania demonstrates higher ratings of corporate reputation for customers receiving service from an employee with a hearing disability than for customers receiving service from an employee without disabilities. In an online experiment using a Solomon four-group design, we utilize video vignettes to test our model with corporate social responsibility perceptions as a mediator. Together, our findings show that managers’ concerns about how biased customers might respond to service employees with disabilities are likely unfounded and highlight—in addition to an ethical case for inclusion—the potential for organizations to benefit from employing of people with disabilities, as it leads to favorable reputation effects for organizations.
apa_citation: Dwertmann, D. J. G., Goštautaitė, B., Kazlauskaitė, R., & Bučiūnienė, I. (2023). Receiving service from a person with a disability: Stereotypes, perceptions of corporate social responsibility, and the opportunity for increased corporate reputation. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(1), 133–163. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0084

title: From the Editors—An Innovative Journal during Transformational Times: Embarking on the 23rd Editorial Term
methods: Editorial essay; no empirical methodology. The new editor-in-chief articulates strategic priorities, describes editorial team composition, and outlines workflow changes for the 23rd editorial term (2023-2025).
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Gruber, M.
theories: Not reported in paper
keywords: DEI; artificial intelligence; editorial agenda; methodological innovation; sustainability; transformational times
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
- Strategic priority 1: Encourage management research that increases our understanding of transformational times (technological, societal, ecological change).
- Strategic priority 2: Embrace new data sources and key methodological advances at AMJ (including AI, machine learning, natural language processing).
- Strategic priority 3: Experiment with innovative ways of engaging with prospective authors, the Academy, and other key stakeholders (podcasts, video series, social media forums, joint PDWs with sister journals).
- Structural changes: third deputy editor position added (micro, macro, qualitative); newly created panel of Consulting Methodological Experts; annual DEI award; expanded digital outreach.

The editorial lays out a three-part agenda for AMJ's 23rd editorial term (2023-2025) under editor-in-chief Marc Gruber. It frames the contemporary context as transformational — driven by AI, hybrid work, sustainability imperatives, and DEI commitments — and positions AMJ to publish cornerstone studies on these emerging realities while preserving rigor. The piece also describes the editorial team's composition (22 members from eight countries, balanced micro/macro/qualitative, gender-balanced) and the revised manuscript workflow.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial argues that "transformational times" call for management scholars to revisit boundary conditions and causal mechanisms rather than anchor on past theories — citing George (2016) on the field's tendency to default to inherited frameworks. It positions AMJ as a venue where new seminal studies can recast existing theories and, where necessary, develop entirely new ones to explain rapidly evolving organizational realities (AI, sustainability transitions, DEI, hybrid work).
practical_implication: The editorial signals to prospective authors that AMJ welcomes submissions addressing transformational phenomena (AI, sustainability, DEI, new work arrangements) and is open to novel data sources, less-common empirical methods, and unconventional theoretical foundations. It also advertises new author-facing supports: a Consulting Methodological Experts panel, monthly podcasts, video series, joint paper development workshops, and a turnaround target of 60 days or less for first decisions.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editorial encourages submissions that recast existing theories to fit transformational conditions or develop entirely new theories where current frameworks lack explanatory power. It flags AI-enabled "instant research paradigms," sustainability transitions, DEI scholarship, and the changing nature of work as priority areas, and announces a forthcoming Special Research Forum call on transformational times.
abstract: Not reported in paper

title: Does Power Protect Female Moral Objectors? How and When Moral Objectors’ Gender, Power, and Use of Organizational Frames Influence Perceived Self-Control and Experienced Retaliation
methods: Multi-study mixed-methods design dominated by experiments: Study 1 logistic regression on archival U.S. Merit Systems Protection Board survey (n=33,715); Study 2 critical incident technique with 2x2 between-subjects experimental design (n=219); Studies 3 and 4 preregistered between-subjects randomized experiments with 2x2x2 designs (n=399; n=359) testing mediation by perceived self-control via moderated path analysis with bootstrap samples.
industry: U.S. federal agencies (Study 1: EPA, DOJ, NASA, etc.); cross-industry working adults recruited via TurkPrime/MTurk (Studies 2-4)
time_period: Study 1 survey conducted in 2007; Studies 2-4 are experiments without dated archival windows
units: Individual employees and online experimental participants evaluating moral objectors
n_sample: Study 1: 33,715; Study 2: 219; Study 3: 399; Study 4: 359
authors: Kundro, T. G.; Rothbard, N. P.
theories: expectancy violation theory (Jussim, Coleman, & Lerch, 1987); gender role theory (Eagly & Karau, 2002); power role theory (French, Raven & Cartwright, 1959; Tost, 2015)
keywords: expectancy violation; gender; moral objection; organizational frame; power; retaliation; self-control
research_question: Does structural power protect female moral objectors from retaliation in the same way it protects men, and under what conditions (i.e., how a moral objection is framed) can higher-powered female moral objectors avoid retaliation?
mechanism_summary:
- Mediator: Observers' perceptions of the moral objector's self-control

Drawing on power role theories, gender role theories, and expectancy violation theory, the authors argue that moral objection is an agentic act that observers expect from higher-power actors but not from women. When higher-powered women morally object using a standard frame, they violate salient gender role expectations, are perceived as lower in self-control (impulsive and selfishly imposing their morality on the group), and face retaliation. Reframing the same objection in organizational/communal language signals other-oriented benefit to the group, neutralizes the gender role violation, restores self-control perceptions, and reduces retaliation across gender and power levels.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to gender and power research by systematically showing that power does not automatically protect female moral objectors—instead, moral objection framing functions as a more effective antidote because organizational frames do not violate gender role expectations. It extends threat-based accounts of moral objection by establishing perceived self-control as a key mediating mechanism explaining retaliation, and it contributes to organizational ethics theory by demonstrating that, counter to prior assumptions, power does not shield all moral objectors—particularly women—from retaliation.
practical_implication: Organizations should encourage all moral objectors, not just women, to use organizational rather than standard frames, since organizationally framed objections face less retaliation across the board. Managers can train employees in framing, debias observers' tendency to view female moral objectors as lower in self-control through perspective-taking workshops, and publicly highlight the communal benefits of moral objections to reduce their perceived agentic threat.
limitations: Hypothesis 1 (main effect of power on retaliation) was not supported in Studies 2 and 4, though the interaction effects were consistent. The studies tested framing only at average and high power levels, leaving extremely low-power positions unexamined, and three of four studies relied on MTurk samples. Retaliation was treated as a unitary construct rather than its multiple forms, and the objected-to behavior was not itself gendered.
future_research: Future work should investigate framing effects at lower organizational levels, examine multiple forms of retaliation, test whether organizational framing helps women objecting to gendered topics like sexual harassment or gender equity, and explore alternative non-other-oriented frames. Researchers should also study other downstream consequences of competence perceptions, such as promotion and investment opportunities for female moral objectors.
abstract: Organizational scholars have called upon higher-power individuals to serve as moral objectors to combat unethical behavior at work because it is assumed they will face less retaliation. However, research has painted an unclear picture of whether power protects women in the same way as it does men. In this paper, we draw on two distinct role theories (i.e., power role theories and gender role theories) as well as expectancy violation theory, theorizing and finding that female moral objectors benefit less from power than male moral objectors because they are viewed as lower in self-control. We further investigate an alternative remedy, or organizational frame, that may mitigate retaliation against higher-powered female moral objectors, finding that using this frame increases perceptions of self-control and reduces retaliation. We test and find support for our theory across four studies, including an archival study (n 5 33,715), a critical incident technique experiment, and two preregistered experiments testing our intervention.
apa_citation: Kundro, T. G., & Rothbard, N. P. (2023). Does power protect female moral objectors? How and when moral objectors’ gender, power, and use of organizational frames influence perceived self-control and experienced retaliation. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(1), 306-334. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1383

title: Losing Control: The Uncertain Management of Concealable Stigmas When Work and Social Media Collide
methods: Inductive qualitative case study at a multinational car manufacturer's Latin American subsidiary; 480 hours of participant observation plus 20 semi-structured interviews with 18 gay male employees; iterative coding in NVivo with constant comparison and member checks.
industry: Automotive manufacturing (multinational car manufacturer subsidiary)
country: Latin America (Latin American subsidiary; Brazil implied via context)
time_period: October 2018 to January 2019 (four months of in-loco fieldwork)
units: Gay male employees at Globalcar (pseudonym)
n_sample: 18 gay male employees (20 formal interviews; two interviewees were interviewed twice)
authors: Lauriano, Lucas Amaral; Coacci, Thiago
theories: concealable stigma management (Goffman, 1963; Jones & King, 2014; Ragins, 2008); context collapse (Davis & Jurgenson, 2014; Marwick & Boyd, 2011); professionalism as heteronormativity (Rumens & Kerfoot, 2009)
keywords: LGBTQIA+; concealable stigmas; context collapse; disclosure dilemma; gay male employees; heteronormativity; professionalism; social network sites
research_question: How does the context collapse caused by social network sites (SNSs) impact employees' management of concealable stigmas?
mechanism_summary:
- Types of context collapse: bottom-up context collisions at work (spontaneous, taken-for-granted online–offline interactions between employees) and top-down context collusions at work (managers' intentional stimuli to use SNSs for work-related purposes)
- Disclosure dilemma: "How can I be a professional online and keep SNSs as safe spaces?"
- Tensions: professionalism understood as heteronormativity vs. SNSs as safe spaces; default publicness of SNSs vs. need to compartmentalize audiences
- Response patterns (employees with concealable stigmas):
  - Mirroring (dominant): replicating face-to-face disclosure levels on SNSs, either toward concealing or toward revealing — characterized by reproducing face-to-face professionalism as heteronormativity on SNSs and constant effort to maintain online safe spaces
  - Online destigmatization efforts (minor): strategic use of SNSs to normalize and differentiate being gay at work
  - Collapse denial (outlier): treating online and face-to-face as independent contexts, producing mismatched disclosure behaviors

The process documents how SNSs collapse previously separable audiences, generating uncertainty about who sees what and when. Gay male employees lose the relative control over disclosure that prior literature assumed they possessed in face-to-face microinteractions. To cope, most fall back on what they already know — face-to-face heteronormative scripts — and attempt to mirror these online, even though SNSs' default publicness, peers' unsolicited tagging, and managers' work-related demands repeatedly break that mirroring and force renewed boundary work. A minority leverages SNSs as activist platforms, while a denial case illustrates the costs of treating the two contexts as separate.
theoretical_contribution: The paper sets boundary conditions on the literature that has portrayed concealable-stigma disclosure as a relatively controlled individual decision, showing that under context collapse this process becomes uncertain and employees lose control over who sees their stigma-related information and when. It nuances the assumption of SNSs as safe spaces by documenting the continuous monitoring and behavioral adjustment required to keep them so, especially when work invades them. It also challenges the assumption in context-collapse-at-work research that the phenomenon affects all employees similarly, surfacing unintended negative impacts of context collapse on employees with concealable stigmas.
practical_implication: Organizations should recognize the repercussions of spontaneous and incentivized online interactions for employees with concealable stigmas — antidiscriminatory policies and HR training should explicitly cover online behavior. Managers should be aware that incentivizing SNS use for work-related purposes can be disproportionately burdensome for LGBTQIA+ employees. Identifying employees already engaging in online destigmatization efforts and involving them in diversity committees can help organizations develop more responsive practices, as Globalcar did after the study.
limitations: The study focuses on gay male employees in one Latin American automotive subsidiary, so patterns may not generalize to other stigmatized groups or industries. The masculine, heteronormative automotive setting may amplify the dynamics observed. Access constraints and the topic's sensitivity meant that nine potential informants declined to participate, which both validates the topic's salience and may bias the sample toward employees willing to discuss it.
future_research: Future work should extend the model to lesbian, bisexual, and transgender employees and examine intersections with race, religion, class, and culture. Researchers could study specific organizational practices such as the role of social media in hiring, the impact of COVID-19 on SNS-mediated work interactions, and longitudinal feedback loops between disclosure and outcomes. Comparative work across different SNS affordances (e.g., Facebook vs. Reddit vs. anonymous platforms) could deepen understanding of how platform features shape stigma management.
abstract: Scholars have typically portrayed employees' management of concealable stigmas in face-to-face encounters in which social groups are easily separable (e.g., friends, family, and coworkers). This analytical predisposition overlooks the possible roles of social network sites (SNSs) such as Facebook and Instagram. These online platforms enable a cohabitation of different audiences—that is, a context collapse that allows a growing, invisible audience to easily access information about one's stigma. In our qualitative analysis in a Latin American organization, we develop a model that documents the everyday dynamics of context collapse among gay male employees. In a disclosure dilemma, employees are uncertain about how to be a professional online and simultaneously keep SNSs as platforms where they can show more relaxed versions of themselves. As a response, most employees adopt mirroring, and attempt to reflect their face-to-face disclosure levels on SNSs. Some employees engage in destigmatization efforts online, and as an outlier case we mapped an employee in collapse denial. Our study questions the established idea of disclosure as a relatively controlled process in microinteractions. We also nuance the assumption of SNSs as safe spaces and show the unintended impacts of context collapse on the stigmatized.
apa_citation: Lauriano, L. A., & Coacci, T. (2023). Losing control: The uncertain management of concealable stigmas when work and social media collide. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(1), 222–247. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0586

title: When Do Novel Business Models Lead to High Performance? A Configurational Approach to Value Drivers, Competitive Strategy, and Firm Environment
methods: Fuzzy set qualitative comparative analysis (fsQCA) of two samples of publicly traded Internet-enabled firms, with calibrated set-membership thresholds and standard sufficiency/necessity analyses (consistency threshold 0.80, frequency threshold 2).
industry: Publicly traded Internet-enabled firms (e-businesses) in the IT sector
country: Firms listed on the five largest stock exchanges in North America and Europe (Euronext, Frankfurt, London, NASDAQ, NYSE)
units: firms
n_sample: Sample 1 (1999): 125 firms; Sample 2 (2014): 169 firms
authors: Leppänen, P.; George, G.; Alexy, O.
theories: configuration theory (Meyer, Tsui, & Hinings, 1993); neo-configurational perspective (Misangyi et al., 2017); transaction design view of business models (Amit & Zott, 2001)
keywords: business model design; competition; complementarity; differentiation; efficiency; firm performance; fuzzy set qualitative comparative analysis; lock-in; low-cost strategy; novelty
research_question: When and how does novel business model design lead to high firm performance, given the interdependence between business model value drivers (novelty, efficiency, lock-in, complementarity), competitive strategy (differentiation, low-cost), and firm environment (competition, firm size, technological maturity)?
mechanism_summary: The paper develops a configurational mechanism in which novelty primarily drives value creation ("growing the pie") but is insufficient on its own to deliver high firm performance because it does little for value capture. Performance arises only when novelty co-occurs with at least one value-capture-oriented driver (efficiency, lock-in, or complementarity) and a coherent competitive strategy, and the right configuration depends on firm size and whether the enabling technology is new or mature. fsQCA reveals equifinal high-performing configurations and shows novelty as necessary for very high performance only in new-technology contexts.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends transaction-design and business-model-innovation research by showing that novelty is neither sufficient nor universally necessary for high performance; rather, it works through interdependent combinations with other value drivers, strategy, and environmental contingencies. By taking a neo-configurational lens, the authors uncover equifinality across novelty-centric, novelty-enhanced, novelty-neutral, novelty-averse, and novelty-phobic configurations, and demonstrate that novelty and efficiency can act as either complements or substitutes depending on strategy and competition.
practical_implication: Managers should avoid a "novelty trap": novel business model design pays off only when it is connected to clear value-capture mechanisms (efficiency, lock-in, or complementarity) and to a coherent differentiation or low-cost strategy in competitive industries. As environments mature, firms should re-evaluate their business model design choices and ensure that any novel configuration remains worth more than the sum of its parts.
limitations: Researcher choices about which conditions to include constrain fsQCA model complexity, potentially omitting relevant firm- and industry-level controls. The method's inferential power and out-of-sample generalizability are still debated, and the sampling strategy may introduce survival bias. The exclusive focus on Internet-enabled IT-sector firms may limit how the findings travel to less innovation-friendly or more capital-intensive industries.
future_research: Future work could deploy content analysis or NLP on IPO prospectuses and annual reports to scale up business model coding and enable longitudinal designs across industries. Researchers could also examine how business model configurations evolve via vicarious learning or institutional pressure, and whether Red Queen dynamics or causal ambiguity explain why firms converge externally yet diverge in performance.
abstract: The strategy literature has viewed novel business model design as a universally positive antecedent of high performance in entrepreneurial ventures. Not only have scholars emphasized novelty as a necessity, but they have almost considered it a sufficient condition for performance. Challenging this assumption, we hypothesize that novelty can produce high performance only in combination with specific configurations that feature other value drivers, such as efficiency, lock-in, and complementarity, which help firms not only create value but also capture more of it. Fuzzy set qualitative comparative analysis of two samples of Internet-enabled firms largely supports our hypotheses. We find that novelty alone is insufficient for high performance, even when it appears as a necessary condition for very high performance in new technological environments. Our results highlight how novelty combines effectively with other value drivers (in particular with efficiency) and strategies (in particular with differentiation) contingent on the intensity of competition, firm size, and firms’ technological environment. Our study contributes to literature streams on value creation, business model design, and innovation.
apa_citation: Leppänen, P., George, G., & Alexy, O. (2023). When do novel business models lead to high performance? A configurational approach to value drivers, competitive strategy, and firm environment. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(1), 164–194. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0969

title: Polishing the Gilt Edge: Elite Category Endurance and Symbolic Boundaries in U.S. Luxury Hotels, 1790–2015
methods: Inductive, longitudinal historical case study combining grounded-theory qualitative analysis of archival texts, 25 semi-structured interviews, and ~120 hours of observations with semiotic chain analysis and LDA topic modeling of ~16,000 pages of press and trade-publication text spanning 1790–2015.
industry: U.S. hotel industry, focusing on the luxury hotel category vis-à-vis nonluxury (upscale, midscale, economy) tiers
time_period: 1790–2015 (225 years), with archival press coverage from 1853–2015
units: Luxury hotel category-level discourse drawn from popular and trade press, industry rating systems, books by hoteliers/historians, performance data, expert interviews, and on-site observations
n_sample: ~16,000 pages of archival text; 25 semi-structured interviews; one dozen on-site visits; ~120 hours of conference observation; LDA model with 24 topics (13 used in findings)
authors: Lockwood, Christi; Glynn, Mary Ann; Giorgi, Simona
theories: category theory (Durand & Paolella, 2013; Hsu & Grodal, 2021); cultural consonance (Giorgi, Maoret, & Zajac, 2019); semiotic code analysis (Granqvist, Grodal, & Woolley, 2013; Weber et al., 2008); symbolic boundaries (Bourdieu, 1984; Lamont & Molnár, 2002)
keywords: cultural consonance; elite category endurance; elite exclusivity; luxury hotels; oppositional appropriation; semiotic code; social partitioning; symbolic boundary; temporal translation
research_question: How does an elite market category endure over the very long term in the face of recurrent industry- and society-driven threats to its status? Specifically, how is the symbolic boundary that distinguishes the elite from the nonelite discursively constructed, settled, disrupted, and redefined across historical periods?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: elite exclusivity vs. cultural consonance; symbolic boundary as a temporary "settlement" encoded in a semiotic code of oppositions (connotations and denotations) between luxury and nonluxury.
- Process / phases: three historical periods — Grand Hotel Period (1790s–1920s, luxury = opulence), Chain Hotel Period (1930s–mid-1990s, luxury = cosmopolitanism), Lifestyle Hotel Period (late 1990s–2015, luxury = originality) — each demarcated by symbolic boundary settlement followed by disruption.
- Practices (three discursive mechanisms): social partitioning (drawing the boundary around an idealized elite clientele), oppositional appropriation (selectively absorbing elements from the nonelite category), and temporal translation (reinterpreting the category's own past for the present).
- Triggering conditions: industry shifts (e.g., chain hotels, lifestyle hotels) and societal sociocultural movements (modern progressive, artisanal) that heighten the tension between exclusivity and consonance.
- Emergent outcomes: a new symbolic boundary settlement and a new semiotic code that re-partitions luxury from nonluxury, sustaining elite category endurance.

Across all three periods the three mechanisms interlock — one is "primary" at category emergence (social partitioning), growth (oppositional appropriation), or maturity (temporal translation), while the other two play supporting roles. The process resembles a punctuated equilibrium of long stable settlements separated by brief disruption-and-redefinition phases, with a notable correspondence between the primary settlement mechanism and the source of the next disruption.
theoretical_contribution: The paper recasts elite category endurance as an active, ongoing cultural accomplishment rather than an inertial property of status hierarchies, showing how symbolic boundaries adapt by holding elite exclusivity and cultural consonance in productive tension. It identifies three novel, interlocking discursive mechanisms — social partitioning, oppositional appropriation, and temporal translation — and exposes a periodicity in which each mechanism takes the lead at a different phase of the category's evolution. It also bridges debates between impermeable and porous category boundaries by showing that elite symbolic boundaries oscillate between sharp definition in stable times and greater fluidity under threat.
practical_implication: Managers of elite categories and luxury brands should monitor shifts in broader societal tastes and values, not just competitive moves, because cultural consonance is as crucial to enduring eliteness as exclusivity. Sustaining elite status calls for organizational ambidexterity: preserving continuity with the category's storied past while selectively absorbing nonelite practices and reinterpreting tradition to align with contemporary values.
limitations: The study is based on a single category (luxury) within a single industry (U.S. hotels), which constrains generalizability across sectors and national settings. The analysis takes a broad-brush, category-level approach to public discourse and does not deeply track individual organizations, specific actors, or membership-level boundary dynamics.
future_research: The authors call for replication of the symbolic boundary settlement–disruption framework in other cultural-industry contexts (restaurants, retail, watches, wine, haute couture, "masstige" brands) and in non-cultural or credence-good settings. They also encourage cross-national comparisons of how class structures shape boundary dynamics and finer-grained tracking of which actors and organizations come to influence elite category discourse over time.
abstract: We conducted an inductive study of discourse associated with the U.S. luxury hotel category, from its beginnings in 1790 through 2015, to explain how cultural processes contribute to elite category endurance. Analyzing data from historical archives, interviews, and observations, we use both qualitative and quantitative methods to reveal how the endurance of an elite category is an ongoing cultural accomplishment that occurs via periodic redefinition of its "symbolic boundary"—that is, the set of meanings and practices that distinguish the elite from nonelite. Modeling the boundary in terms of semiotic codes of opposition, we find that the encoding of the symbolic boundary serves as a temporary settlement that conveys both the category's elite exclusivity and its cultural consonance with broader societal beliefs, tastes, and practices. Over time, however, with industry and societal changes, tensions erupt in the balance between the needs for exclusivity and consonance, propelling a redefinition of what it means to be elite. Our research extends current theorizations of elite category endurance by revealing how the elite symbolic boundary is neither permanent nor unassailable; rather, we show how it changes to enable long-term elite category endurance.
apa_citation: Lockwood, C., Glynn, M. A., & Giorgi, S. (2023). Polishing the gilt edge: Elite category endurance and symbolic boundaries in U.S. luxury hotels, 1790–2015. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(1), 9–42. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0201

title: Balancing “Protective Disguise” with “Harmonious Advocacy”: Social Venture Legitimation in Authoritarian Contexts
methods: In-depth, single-case qualitative study using process research (Langley, 1999) and Gioia-style open coding; 45 semi-structured interviews across four rounds, 93 media texts, 12 public speeches/interviews, and field-visit observations, with theory-building through process-tracing.
industry: Disability-rights social venture (registered as NGO, positioned as accessibility consultancy) and its key audiences (government, corporate employers, nondisabled employees, NGOs)
time_period: Organizational history 2011-2020; primary fieldwork during two visits in February and April 2018
units: One focal social venture ("Amal," pseudonym) plus actors in its audience environment
n_sample: 45 interviews; 93 media texts; 12 public speeches/interviews; field observations from two visits
authors: Neuberger, I.; Kroezen, J.; Tracey, P.
theories: optimal distinctiveness (Deephouse, 1999; Zhao et al., 2017); organizational legitimacy (Suchman, 1995; Deephouse et al., 2017; Suddaby, Bitektine, & Haack, 2017); social venture legitimation (Ruebottom, 2013; Nicholls, 2010)
keywords: authoritarian contexts; disability inclusion; harmonious advocacy; optimal assimilation; protective disguise; social venture legitimation
research_question: "How do new social ventures gain legitimacy in authoritarian contexts?"
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: protective disguise vs. harmonious advocacy; political vs. cultural manifestations of authoritarianism; "fitting in" vs. advocating for beneficiaries
- Legitimation challenges: (a) political manifestation — social activism perceived as threat to national interests; (b) cultural manifestation — entrenched beliefs and practices that devalue the human rights of marginalized groups
- Protective disguise practices: (1) cloaking social mission under rhetorical alignment with regime goals (economic prosperity, national stability, tourism); (2) camouflaging social action through an innocuous organizational design (positioning as a commercial accessibility consultancy rather than a human-rights NGO)
- Harmonious advocacy practices: (3) experiential construction of social problems (community days, blindfolded simulations, ambassador stories that build empathy); (4) reactive campaigning for social mission (leveraging conducive events such as the 2017 disability law and the "Year of People with Disabilities," while explicitly shielding government from criticism)
- Emergent outcomes: venture perceived as nonthreatening, then as worthy of support; growing audience backing
- Boundary conditions: continuous balancing required; ventures may need to pivot back toward disguise during crackdowns and forward toward advocacy during conducive periods

The process Amal followed shows that legitimation in authoritarian contexts is underpinned by a continuous balancing of disguise and advocacy that the authors label "optimal assimilation." Disguise is necessary because confrontation triggers regime aggression; advocacy is necessary because pure disguise prevents the venture from acting on its mission. Disguise lays the platform that makes later, carefully calibrated advocacy possible, and the two strategies are continuously rebalanced in response to political-cultural dynamics.
theoretical_contribution: The paper builds a process model of social-venture legitimation in authoritarian contexts that identifies "protective disguise" and "harmonious advocacy" as the two overarching strategies and "optimal assimilation" as the governing principle — a counterpart to "optimal distinctiveness" in democratic contexts. It addresses the democratic-society bias in organizational-legitimacy research by showing that core assumptions (e.g., the evaluative benefits of "standing out," the four-state progression toward "accepted") do not transfer to authoritarian regimes, where ventures occupy a liminal zone between "debated" and "proper" and political elites uniquely bestow or withdraw legitimacy.
practical_implication: Social ventures operating in authoritarian regimes should resist transplanting democratic-context playbooks: confrontational framing, hero/villain rhetoric, and overt rights-based campaigning can endanger founders and trigger forced closure. Founders should instead cloak their mission in language that aligns with regime priorities, adopt an innocuous organizational form (e.g., a service-providing consultancy), and use empathy-building experiential activities plus reactive campaigning tied to government-initiated events to advance their cause without attracting unwanted political attention.
limitations: The study rests on a single case in one authoritarian context (post-revolutionary Egypt), focused on a relatively less politically contentious issue (disability inclusion); the findings may differ for ventures tackling more sensitive issues (e.g., free speech, LGBTQ rights) or operating in other authoritarian or hybrid regimes (China, Russia, Saudi Arabia, Albania, Nigeria). The authors also flag that whether "optimal assimilation" is the morally best path is a normative question they cannot answer.
future_research: Comparative work across authoritarian and hybrid regimes is needed to test whether the disguise-advocacy balance generalizes. Future studies should examine more politically and culturally contentious causes, explore how a commercial vs. social orientation shapes legitimation under different regimes, and investigate the state's role as a uniquely powerful audience whose interdependency with other audiences shapes legitimacy judgments — even in democracies.
abstract: This paper seeks to advance understanding of how new social ventures can gain legitimacy in authoritarian contexts. Through a study of a new disability rights organization in post-revolutionary Egypt, we theorize how authoritarianism poses distinct challenges for social ventures that require different legitimation strategies than those commonly reported in the literature. Specifically, we use our case study to build a theoretical model that suggests social ventures need to achieve “optimal assimilation” by balancing “protective disguise” with “harmonious advocacy.” By explicitly theorizing social venture legitimation in authoritarian contexts, we advance the budding literature on social venture legitimation that has so far predominantly considered legitimation in more democratic contexts. Moreover, our study shows that organizational legitimacy may need to be conceptualized differently when examining social ventures—and indeed other forms of organization—in authoritarian regimes.
apa_citation: Neuberger, I., Kroezen, J., & Tracey, P. (2023). Balancing “protective disguise” with “harmonious advocacy”: Social venture legitimation in authoritarian contexts. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(1), 67-101. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0517
