title: Toward a Configural Theory of Job Demands and Resources
methods: Three cross-sectional and time-lagged survey studies of employees; fuzzy set qualitative comparative analysis (fsQCA) supplemented by OLS regression of configuration membership scores on outcomes.
industry: Diverse occupations (MTurk workers; MBA alumni and a major technology company; Prolific Academic online panel)
time_period: Study 2 collected during the COVID-19 pandemic; calibration data drawn from the past 10 years of published management research
units: Full-time employees
n_sample: 199 (Study 1); 124 (Study 2); 412 (Study 3)
authors: Ong, W. J.; Johnson, M. D.
theories: Job demands–resources (JDR) model; bad-is-stronger-than-good principle; demand–control–support model; person-centered/configural theory
keywords: autonomy; configurations; emotional demands; engagement; exhaustion; fuzzy set qualitative comparative analysis; job demands–resources; person-centered approach; social support; workload
research_question: Which configurations of job demands and resources are sufficient to produce the presence and absence of exhaustion and engagement (and other strain outcomes), and do these configurations exhibit equifinality and causal asymmetry that variable-centered models cannot detect?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the demand–control–support extension of JDR theory and the "bad-is-stronger-than-good" cognition literature, the authors theorize that several qualitatively different configurations of high demands and low resources can be equally sufficient to produce exhaustion (equifinality), and that the configurations producing an outcome are not the inverse of those producing its absence (causal asymmetry). They predict that high-workload/low-autonomy and high-workload/low-social-support combinations are each sufficient for exhaustion, that some configurations of multiple high demands cannot be buffered by any resource, and that there are more pathways to negative than positive outcomes.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reinterprets the JDR model in configural terms, replacing the additive/multiplicative variable-centered logic with set-theoretic propositions about equifinal and causally asymmetric configurations of demands and resources. It shows that certain demands (high workload combined with emotional demands) cannot be buffered by any resource, that the absence of social support can itself function as a demand, and that there are more configurations sufficient for exhaustion than for engagement, instantiating a "bad-is-stronger-than-good" pattern within JDR theory.
practical_implication: Managers should diagnose employees' overall configurations of job demands and resources rather than intervening on any single demand or resource in isolation: simply lowering workload will not help an employee whose exhaustion comes from low social support, and adding social support will not help when high workload combines with high emotional demands. Because exhaustion has multiple sufficient pathways but its absence has none, prevention is more tractable than cure.
limitations: Studies 1 and 2 measured demands, resources, and outcomes at the same time from the same source, raising common-method concerns (addressed in Study 3 with time-lagged measurement). The observational design leaves the causal ordering of JDRs and outcomes open, and samples were drawn primarily from the United States, which may limit cross-cultural generalizability. The set of demands and resources examined was finite and did not include nonwork antecedents of strain.
future_research: The authors call for longitudinal designs that test lagged effects between JDRs and outcomes, replications in non-U.S. and especially collectivistic cultural contexts to test whether social support and autonomy play different buffering roles, and extensions to additional outcomes such as performance. They also suggest examining antecedents of configuration membership (e.g., personality traits, organizational and national institutions) and identifying configuration-specific mediating mechanisms.
abstract: Job demands–resources research has largely adopted a variable-centered approach to test main and interactive effects of demands and resources on employee outcomes. However, this approach cannot detect distinct configurations of job demands and resources that may lead to the same outcomes, and the many multiplicative models attempting to do so have received little support. We take a person-centered theoretical approach to examine configurations of demands and resources that are sufficient to produce exhaustion, engagement, and other strain outcomes. Using fuzzy set qualitative comparative analysis across three studies, we find three equifinal configurations sufficient to produce exhaustion: (a) high workload and emotional demands, (b) low social support, and (c) high workload and low autonomy. We also find evidence for causal asymmetry in both exhaustion and engagement. Our person-centered approach yields more theoretically consistent results compared to a variable-centered approach. The findings highlight that certain job demands may only be buffered by specific resources, and that certain configurations of demands cannot be buffered at all.
apa_citation: Ong, W. J., & Johnson, M. D. (2023). Toward a configural theory of job demands and resources. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(1), 195–221. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0493

title: The Impact of Communist Ideology on the Patenting Activity of Chinese Firms
methods: Panel analysis of 1,298 non-state-owned Chinese listed manufacturing firms (5,731 firm-year observations) over 2000-2017. Random-effects negative binomial regression for patent applications (count outcome) and logit model with standard errors clustered by firm for patent infringement (binary outcome). Propensity score matching (PSM) addresses endogeneity of Party membership; robustness checks include coarsened exact matching (CEM), entropy balancing, full-sample estimation, alternative measures, and impact-threshold-of-confounding-variable analysis.
industry: Non-state-owned manufacturing firms (industry grouping based on three-digit classification by China Securities Regulatory Commission)
units: Firm-year observations of listed non-SOEs on the Shanghai and Shenzhen Stock Exchanges
n_sample: 1,298 firms; 5,731 matched firm-year observations (9,262 in full sample)
authors: Xu, Dean; Zhou, Kevin Zheng; Chen, Shihua
theories: Imprinting theory (Marquis & Tilcsik, 2013; Stinchcombe, 1965); Political ideology in business research; Upper echelons theory (Hambrick, 2007; Hambrick & Mason, 1984)
keywords: Chinese Communist Party; Maoist communist ideology; board chairs; ideological decay; non-state-owned enterprises; patent infringement; patenting activity; secondhand ideological imprinting
research_question: Why does Maoist communist ideology continue to affect the patenting behavior of Chinese Communist Party members—including younger members who never directly experienced the Maoist era—and through what specific imprinting mechanism does this ideological influence persist amid decades of decoupling between Party policy and Maoist doctrine?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Founder chair, government/SOE experience, overseas experience, chair ownership, R&D intensity, state shares, firm size, firm age, ROA, industry competition (1 − HHI), city-level GDP growth, Internet development, provincial legal development, year and industry dummies

Drawing on imprinting theory, the authors argue that older Party members carry firsthand Maoist imprints from the politically sensitive 1960s–1970s, while younger Party members—who joined under Dengism—acquire Maoist orientations only indirectly, through "secondhand imprinting" by older Party-member peers in their elite social and role category. Maoist beliefs (anti-private-property, anti-IPR, devaluation of scientific work and intellectuals) translate into lower patent application volumes and higher patent infringement propensity. Higher education and younger age weaken this effect (ideological decay), but a high regional presence of older Party-member directors reactivates Maoist influence among younger chairs (offsetting age-based decay), though it does not offset education-based decay.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends imprinting theory by empirically demonstrating secondhand imprinting in a setting where the original imprinting environment has disappeared—showing that older Party-member directors in the same regional elite category pass Maoist ideological imprints onto younger Party-member board chairs who never experienced the Maoist era directly. It contributes to the political ideology literature by modeling the interplay between ideological decay (via age, education) and ideological persistence (via secondhand imprinting) within a single integrated framework, departing from the Western liberal/conservative dichotomy that dominates extant work.
practical_implication: When assessing Chinese non-SOEs as innovation partners or rivals, observers should weigh board chairs' ideological background (CCP membership) alongside their age, education, and the demographic composition of older Party-member directors in their regional social network—because firms led by Party-member chairs file fewer patents, more often violate other firms' patent rights, and may instead diversify into multiple industries as an alternative growth strategy. Education in particular appears to robustly counteract Maoist ideological influence on innovation.
limitations: The study isolates Maoist ideological elements, but these are intermixed with broader Chinese cultural features (e.g., Confucian skepticism of individualism and IPR; historically modest cultural emphasis on science and technology) that also bear on innovation activity. The authors caution against overgeneralizing the ideology effect to all corporate decisions, noting that when evidence is unambiguous Party members often act pragmatically (e.g., founding private firms after SOE collapse).
future_research: Cross-national comparisons of how different versions of communist ideology shape firm behavior in China, Cuba, Vietnam, and former Soviet Bloc countries would test the generalizability of the secondhand-imprinting mechanism. Future work could also examine how a board chair's home province, prior SOE/planned-economy experience, or other contextual moderators interact with political ideology to shape innovation orientations.
abstract: The Chinese Communist Party began decoupling its policies and practices from Maoist communist ideology more than four decades ago. Yet, why does Maoism continue to have an impact on the behavior of Party members? In this study, we argue that, although the influence of Maoist ideology has become weaker among younger Party members and Party members with higher educational attainment, such ideological decay is countered by a process of secondhand ideological imprinting. Based on data from 1,298 nonstate-owned Chinese listed firms for 2000–2017, we find that firms with Party-member board chairs file fewer patent applications and are more likely to commit patent infringement. These effects are weaker if a board chair is younger or has higher educational attainment. Importantly, the moderating effect of young age is reduced as the presence of older Party-member corporate directors in a region becomes more prominent. However, the moderating effect of education appears to be unaffected by the presence of older Party-member directors. These findings generate fresh insights on the dynamics of ideological decay and persistence.
apa_citation: Xu, D., Zhou, K. Z., & Chen, S. (2023). The impact of communist ideology on the patenting activity of Chinese firms. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(1), 102–132. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0810

title: Does Job Satisfaction Rise and Fall with the Economy? Cross-Sectional, Longitudinal, and Experimental Evidence That Job Satisfaction Increases during Recessions
methods: Three studies: (1) OLS regressions on repeated cross-sectional GSS data with national and state unemployment rates, standard errors clustered by year (and by state-year); (2) hierarchical/multilevel linear models with autoregressive residuals and individual fixed effects on longitudinal BHPS panel data; (3) randomized between-subjects experiment manipulating economic conditions via newspaper articles, with bootstrapped mediation analysis (lavaan).
industry: Cross-industry working adults (16/17-category NAICS classifications used as controls)
time_period: Study 1: GSS waves 1974–2016 (data over four decades); Study 2: BHPS/Understanding Society 1991–2013; Study 3: online experiment (article adapted from 2021)
units: Individual employed adults (survey respondents and experiment participants)
n_sample: Study 1: 23,335 respondents; Study 2: 12,859 respondents; Study 3: 512 participants
authors: Bianchi, Emily C.; Martin, Chris C.; Li, Ren
theories: Cornell model / frame-of-reference theory of job satisfaction (Smith, Kendall, & Hulin, 1969); impressionable years hypothesis; investment model (Rusbult & Farrell, 1983); system justification / compensatory control (Kay et al., 2009)
keywords: economic conditions; job satisfaction; recessions; salience of alternative jobs; sense-making; unemployment rate
research_question: Do macroeconomic conditions outside the workplace shape how satisfied people are with their jobs? Specifically, the authors ask whether job satisfaction rises during recessions (when unemployment is high) and falls during booms, and whether this relationship is driven by the reduced salience of alternative jobs.
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Age, age squared, gender, income, linear time trend; industry and occupation; tenure (Study 3); state dummies (Study 1 state models).

When job alternatives are plentiful and salient, jobs are judged against higher reference standards and comparisons surface their negative features, depressing satisfaction (the Cornell frame-of-reference and investment models). During recessions, scarce and unreachable alternatives become less salient, so people focus on the positive features of their existing jobs and use system-justifying, compensatory-control strategies to view their situation more favorably. Study 3's mediation confirms that worse economic conditions lower the salience of alternatives, which in turn raises job satisfaction.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends job-satisfaction research—which has focused almost exclusively on situational conditions inside the workplace—by showing that contextual conditions outside organizations also shape workplace attitudes. It contributes to work on the psychological effects of recessions and provides theoretical and empirical support for the idea that adverse societal events can promote positive (not just disengaging) sense-making processes that bolster workplace attitudes.
practical_implication: Organizations may benefit from acknowledging and addressing external economic events rather than ignoring them. Because employees seem driven to view their jobs favorably during downturns, efforts to raise satisfaction may be well-received then, and hiring during recessions may yield workers who rate their jobs more highly. Conversely, firms should attend more to satisfaction during booms, when salient alternatives make dissatisfaction costlier and turnover easier.
limitations: The magnitude of the effects is relatively small, plausibly because countervailing forces—greater workplace stress and job insecurity during recessions—depress satisfaction at the same time the identified sense-making processes elevate it. The first two studies are correlational; only the experiment establishes causality and tests the mediator.
future_research: Future work could simultaneously capture the competing forces that elevate and depress satisfaction during downturns, and examine when the elevating factors dominate. Researchers could also test moderation by personality (e.g., need for cognitive closure) or demographics (recessions are unevenly experienced, e.g., low-wage workers), and explore additional psychological mechanisms such as tempered expectations or gratitude, and why alternatives undermine satisfaction.
abstract: While recessions have many adverse consequences for individuals and organizations, we propose that they have positive implications for job satisfaction. We argue that during bad economic times, people will be less attuned to other possible jobs, and more likely to see their own jobs favorably. We find support for these predictions across three studies. Study 1 (n 5 23,335) utilizes a large cross-sectional survey of American adults collected over four decades and finds that job satisfaction increases during recessions and declines during booms. Study 2 (n 5 12,859) replicates this result using a large longitudinal survey of British adults and finds that job satisfaction rises and falls with the unemployment rate even within the same people. Finally, Study 3 (n 5 512) uses an experimental design and finds that the relationship between economic conditions and job satisfaction is mediated by the reduced salience of alternative jobs. While scholars have long recognized that job satisfaction is affected by situational features inside organizations, our findings suggest that conditions outside the workplace can also influence how people think about and evaluate their jobs.
apa_citation: Bianchi, E. C., Martin, C. C., & Li, R. (2023). Does job satisfaction rise and fall with the economy? Cross-sectional, longitudinal, and experimental evidence that job satisfaction increases during recessions. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(2), 688–709. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0405

title: Do I Dare? The Psychodynamics of Anticipated Image Risk, Leader-Identity Endorsement, and Leader Emergence
methods: Multistage mixed-methods design across four studies (plus supplementary studies B and C): a longitudinal field survey of MBA consulting teams (Study 1), a quantitative experiment manipulating lay theories (Study B), an exploratory qualitative survey (Study 2), a multisource field survey of employee-supervisor dyads (Study 3), and a podcast-based experiment (Study 4). Quantitative analyses use multivariate regression and PROCESS macro moderated-mediation models with bootstrapped indirect effects; qualitative data thematically coded.
industry: Study 1: multidisciplinary MBA student consulting teams addressing business challenges; later studies span a variety of industries (online/MTurk workers, Air Force cadets, employee-supervisor dyads)
time_period: Study 1 data collected in 2017; project published 2023
units: Individuals (within consulting teams, dyads, and online/virtual worker samples)
n_sample: Study 1: 411 individuals in 83 teams; Study 2: 327 workers (plus 119 cadets); Study 3: 377 employee-supervisor pairs; Study 4: 448 virtual workers
authors: Lee Cunningham, Julia; Sonday, Laura; Ashford, Susan J.
theories: identity work (Brown, 2015, 2017; Caza, Vough, & Puranik, 2018); lay theories / implicit theories (Dweck, 2006); psychological/ego defense (Cramer, 2000; Freud, 1936)
keywords: anticipated image risk; identity work; lay theories of leadership ability; leader-identity endorsement; leadership emergence; psychodynamic defense
research_question: Why don't more people internalize a leader identity and emerge as leaders? Specifically, how does anticipated image risk—individuals' belief that leading might harm how others see them—reduce leader-identity endorsement and thereby leadership emergence, and how do lay (fixed vs. malleable) theories of leadership ability moderate this process?
mechanism_summary: When individuals anticipate that leading will damage their image, ego/self-protection prompts a psychodynamic form of identity work in which they decline to endorse "leader" as a self-descriptor ("that's just not who I am"), neutralizing the threat while avoiding seeing themselves as lacking courage. Reduced leader-identity endorsement in turn lowers leadership emergence. A fixed lay theory of leadership ability strengthens this negative path because reputational damage is seen as permanent and growth as impossible, whereas a malleable view buffers it. The qualitative study specifies three concrete image concerns (bossy, unqualified, different) underlying the construct, with fear of seeming unqualified the most debilitating.
theoretical_contribution: The paper shifts leader-identity scholarship from a lasting "once and for all" construction toward situational, fluid endorsement, and introduces a rarely invoked ego-defense (psychodynamic) explanation for why people deny a leader identity. It advances the concept of metaperception—how people think others think of them—as an underexplored driver of identity, and elaborates image risk from a molar construct into three specific reputational concerns (seeming bossy, unqualified, different).
practical_implication: Organizations seeking more leadership at all ranks should reckon with negative, outdated stereotypes of leaders as aggressive or domineering and foster a consensus that leadership is independent of formal title. They should also promote a malleable view of leadership ability—reinforcing that leadership is a learnable skill and praising attempts to lead—to help employees circumvent the ego-defense response that suppresses leader-identity endorsement and emergence.
limitations: The studies emphasize within-person perceptions over social context, leaving out other context-specific variables (e.g., perceived organizational support, hierarchy) that may shape image risk and endorsement. Study 1 used single-source survey measures vulnerable to common method variance, and support for the lay-theory moderation hypotheses (H5, H6) was mixed across studies—significant in Studies 1 and B but not when image risk was manipulated and lay theories measured in Study 4.
future_research: Scholars could further test the moderating role of lay theories across varied, multisource settings to clarify when this mindset matters, and probe additional context variables (e.g., organizational support, hierarchy) shaping image risk. Researchers might examine the full range of anticipated reactions (negative, neutral, positive), whether people over- or accurately estimate social backlash, and why fear of seeming unqualified is the most debilitating concern.
abstract: Although many organizations value leadership across levels, individuals are reluctant to step up and lead. We explore how anticipated image risk (i.e., individuals' beliefs that the act of leading might harm their image with others) and lay beliefs about leadership ability (i.e., that this ability is fixed versus malleable) may diminish individuals' endorsement of a leader identity and thus their leadership emergence. Across MBA consulting teams, supervisor–employee dyads, and virtual workers, we find that lay theories of leadership ability moderate the negative relationship between anticipated image risk in leadership and leader-identity endorsement, which affects subsequent leadership emergence (Study 1). Using qualitative survey data, Study 2 identifies three specific image concerns that individuals associated with leading: seeming bossy, seeming unqualified, and seeming different from one's peers. Study 3 tests whether a newly developed measure of three specific image risks similarly predicts leader-identity endorsement and emergence. Last, in Study 4 we experimentally manipulate the anticipation of image risk to show its causal impact on leader-identity endorsement and emergence.
apa_citation: Lee Cunningham, J., Sonday, L., & Ashford, S. J. (2023). Do I dare? The psychodynamics of anticipated image risk, leader-identity endorsement, and leader emergence. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(2), 374–401. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.1258

title: Leveraging Knowledge Diversity in Hierarchically Differentiated Teams: The Critical Role of Hierarchy Stability
methods: Multilevel (mixed-effects) modeling of cross-sectional field survey data with random intercepts for organization; three-way (moderated-moderation) interaction tested via Dawson and Richter's slope-difference procedure; moderated mediation tested via nonparametric bootstrapping (10,000 draws); supplementary dyadic round-robin analysis.
industry: Work teams across 13 industries (e.g., information technology, hospitality, health care, finance, manufacturing, government)
time_period: All data collected within a three-month period
units: Intact work teams (data from team members and supervisors)
n_sample: 156 teams comprising 995 individuals from 110 organizations
authors: Gray, Steven M.; Bunderson, J. Stuart; van der Vegt, Gerben S.; Rink, Floor; Gedik, Yeliz
theories: hierarchy stability; status hierarchy / status characteristics
keywords: hierarchy stability; knowledge diversity; status hierarchy; team information sharing; team performance
research_question: When and how does a team's status hierarchy constrict versus amplify the relationship between knowledge diversity and team information sharing (and, in turn, team performance), and does hierarchy stability determine which effect prevails?
mechanism_summary: The paper argues that status hierarchy has two countervailing effects on diverse teams: a constricting effect (high-status members' perspectives dominate, low-status members feel inhibited) and an amplifying effect (hierarchy offers status-enhancement incentives for sharing unique knowledge). Drawing on the concept of hierarchy stability, the authors propose that stable hierarchies activate four constricting dynamics—greater dominance of one perspective, reduced accountability of high-status members, increased personal risk to low-status members, and weakened status-enhancement incentives—so that diversity's benefit to information sharing (and downstream performance) is weakest when status differences are large and stable. The empirical three-way interaction supports this: knowledge diversity was negatively related to information sharing when status hierarchy and stability were both high, and only marginally positive when hierarchy was high but unstable.
theoretical_contribution: The study integrates the historically separate diversity (horizontal differentiation) and hierarchy (vertical differentiation) literatures, showing that the information-sharing and performance benefits of knowledge diversity cannot be understood without accounting for status hierarchy and its stability. It extends hierarchy-stability research—previously focused on homogeneous teams, where stability is beneficial—by demonstrating that stability can instead be detrimental in knowledge-diverse teams with steep hierarchies.
practical_implication: Managers of knowledge-diverse teams should ask, in order, how important diverse-knowledge exchange is, whether status differences exist, and whether the status hierarchy is stable. Where status differences are unavoidable, managers should deliberately destabilize the hierarchy—rotating leadership, changing roles, refreshing membership, and structuring interactions to require all members' input—to keep diverse knowledge flowing.
limitations: Hierarchy stability was measured via team-member perceptions rather than directly observed changes in the hierarchy. The temporally separated but cross-sectional field design cannot establish causality or rule out reverse effects of information sharing on hierarchy. The aggregated team-level information-sharing measure cannot decompose high- versus low-status sharing or unique versus common information content (partially addressed by a supplementary dyadic analysis that still did not capture actual content exchanged).
future_research: Future work should test the correspondence between perceived and actual hierarchy stability and whether they predict different outcomes, use (field) experiments or longitudinal designs to probe the reciprocal information-sharing/hierarchy relationship, examine why stability effects appear stronger in homogeneous than diverse teams, and capture the actual content of information exchanged across diversity, hierarchy, and stability conditions.
abstract: Past research has been equivocal about the information sharing and performance effects of knowledge diversity in teams. In an attempt to resolve this equivocality, scholars have suggested that status hierarchy may play a role, proposing that status differences can constrict team-level information sharing in diverse teams by privileging the knowledge of higher-status members and discounting the knowledge of lower-status members. Although there is some evidence to support this notion, there is also evidence to suggest that status differences may amplify information sharing in diverse teams by offering status enhancement incentives to members who share unique knowledge. In this paper, we reconcile these different predictions by suggesting that the effects of status hierarchy on the relationship between knowledge diversity and team information sharing will depend on the stability of the hierarchy. Using a diverse sample of 156 teams across 110 organizations, we found that status differences constricted information sharing in knowledge-diverse teams when hierarchy stability was high and amplified information sharing in knowledge-diverse teams when hierarchy stability was low. These information sharing effects, in turn, affected team performance. Our study highlights how hierarchy stability is critical for understanding whether status differences constrict or amplify information sharing in knowledge-diverse teams.
apa_citation: Gray, S. M., Bunderson, J. S., van der Vegt, G. S., Rink, F., & Gedik, Y. (2023). Leveraging knowledge diversity in hierarchically differentiated teams: The critical role of hierarchy stability. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(2), 462–488. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1136

title: Mixed Messages: Crisis Communication–Dismissal (In)Coherence and Shareholder Trust Following Misconduct
methods: Mixed methods. Study 1: fuzzy-set qualitative comparative analysis (fsQCA) of 51 financial-misconduct cases, with cumulative abnormal returns (CAR) calibrated as outcome sets. Study 2: policy-capturing survey of investors analyzed with hierarchical linear modeling (HLM).
industry: S&P 1500 firms that misstated financial statements (financial restatements due to accounting irregularities); plus active individual investors
time_period: Study 1 restatements announced 1997–2012; Study 2 survey of investors
units: Study 1: firm-level financial-misconduct cases (CEO/CFO dismissals); Study 2: individual investor respondents
n_sample: Study 1: 51 cases; Study 2: 349 respondents
authors: Hersel, Matt C.; Gangloff, K. Ashley; Shropshire, Christine
theories: signaling/informational cues; strategic coherence lens
keywords: coherence; crisis communication; crisis management strategy; executive dismissal; financial misconduct; shareholder trust repair
research_question: How does a firm's communication about its transgression influence shareholder reactions to its dismissal of an executive following financial misconduct? Specifically, how does the (in)coherence between post-transgression crisis communication and executive dismissal shape shareholder trust repair?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: crisis management strategy coherence (degree to which communication and corrective action fit together logically and consistently); (in)coherence as the focal driver of shareholder trust repair.
- Communicative elements (causal conditions): blame acceptance, severity minimization, first-person disclosure.
- Corrective action (causal condition): type of executive dismissal (CEO vs. CFO).
- Emergent configurations: four "recipes" — e.g., major violation + CEO dismissal and isolated reporting error + CFO dismissal yield favorable reactions; major violation + CFO dismissal and ambiguous problem + unexpected dismissal yield unfavorable reactions.
- Outcomes: cumulative abnormal returns (Study 1); perceived (in)coherence, expected recidivism, and likelihood of increasing/reducing holdings (Study 2).

Shareholders jointly evaluate what a firm says about its misconduct, how it says it, and what it does (executive dismissal), and respond to the coherence among these tactics rather than to any tactic in isolation. When the dismissal logically resolves the problem described in the firm's communication, the strategy reads as coherent and trustworthy; mismatched "mixed messages" signal incoherence, raising expectations of recidivism and prompting divestment. Study 2 shows perceived (in)coherence statistically mediates the interaction of communicative sentiment and dismissal type on shareholders' intended holdings.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends crisis-management and corporate-governance research by theorizing the firm's overarching crisis management *strategy*—and its coherence—as a higher-order construct and important unit of analysis, rather than evaluating communication and dismissal tactics in isolation. Adopting a strategic coherence lens and a neo-configurational (fsQCA) approach, it explains previously inconsistent findings about market reactions to isolated tactics by showing that tactics combine in causally complex, equifinal ways to help or hinder shareholder trust repair.
practical_implication: When restoring shareholder trust after a transgression, firms should avoid sending mixed messages and instead craft a coherent crisis management strategy that deliberately coordinates communications with corrective actions. For example, dismissing the CFO after the CEO has personally accepted blame can lead shareholders to question whether the action resolved the problem; the exemplar cases offer a roadmap for aligning words and deeds.
limitations: Study 1 could not directly measure shareholders' perceptions of (in)coherence and used a relatively small sample with a 90-day dismissal window that created right-censoring. Study 2 tested only simplified versions of select configurations and, as a survey-based quasi-experiment, is susceptible to priming effects and respondent biases; the two studies are designed to counterbalance each other's limitations.
future_research: Researchers could generalize the (in)coherence framework to other stakeholder groups (e.g., employees) and other misconduct types (e.g., consumer-safety or environmental issues), and examine executive *retention* and other corrective actions (e.g., changing reporting or governance practices) as elements of crisis management strategy. Further work could explore sequencing, path dependencies, and contextual factors (severity, magnitude, firm/industry preconceptions) that shape overall coherence.
abstract: We explore how firms’ post-transgression crisis communication and executive dismissals jointly influence shareholder trust following financial misconduct. We argue the coherence of a firm’s crisis management strategy—the degree to which its elements fit together consistently and logically—plays an important but previously unconsidered role in shareholder trust repair. We utilize multiple methods to abductively develop and test our theory. First, we conduct a qualitative comparative analysis of 51 cases of financial misconduct among S&P 1500 firms that disclosed a misstatement via press release and dismissed either the CEO or CFO within 90 days of the disclosure. Analyzing aspects of these firms’ crisis communication and the type of dismissal executed, this study reveals four configurations that highlight the influence of crisis management (in)coherence on shareholder trust. Second, a policy-capturing study examines the underlying mechanisms that drive shareholders’ perceptions and intended behaviors relative to manipulated crisis management strategies in a controlled setting. Together, findings from these studies indicate that shareholder trust following misconduct depends in part on the (in)coherence between what firms say about their misconduct, how they communicate that information, and what they do to resolve the problem.
apa_citation: Hersel, M. C., Gangloff, K. A., & Shropshire, C. (2023). Mixed messages: Crisis communication–dismissal (in)coherence and shareholder trust following misconduct. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(2), 638–666. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0275

title: Appraising and Reacting to Perceived Pay For Performance: Leader Competence and Warmth as Critical Contingencies
methods: Two three-wave, multi-source survey studies of leader–employee dyads (employee-rated predictors/appraisals, supervisor-rated task performance); confirmatory factor analysis and moderated-mediation path models in Mplus with bias-corrected bootstrapping (1,000 replications).
industry: Diverse industries (e.g., finance, information technology, education, manufacturing, hotel, consumer goods); pay raises / merit pay (PFP-I) as focal pay form
time_period: Three-wave panels (one-month lags in Study 1; two-week lags in Study 2)
units: Matched leader–employee dyads
n_sample: Study 1: 247 dyads from 234 organizations; Study 2: 192 dyads
authors: Kong, Dejun Tony; Park, Sanghee; Peng, Jian
theories: Park and Sturman's (2022) PFP perception framework; role engagement theory (Kahn, 1990); transactional theory of stress (Lazarus & Folkman, 1984)
keywords: challenge appraisal; leader competence; leader warmth; pay for performance; perceived PFP-I; task performance; threat appraisal; work engagement; work withdrawal
research_question: How can organizations and leaders make employees engage in, rather than withdraw from, work—and thus perform well—under pay for individual performance (PFP-I)? Specifically, how do employees appraise and react to perceived PFP-I, and how do leader competence and warmth shape these appraisals and reactions?
mechanism_summary: Integrating transactional theory of stress and role engagement theory within Park and Sturman's PFP perception framework, the paper treats PFP-I as a job stressor whose meaning is not fixed but appraised. When the leader is competent—signaling reliable evaluation and task-related resources—employees appraise perceived PFP-I as a challenge, creating a positive psychological condition (meaningfulness, availability) that fuels work engagement and higher performance. When the leader is warm—signaling fairness and socioemotional support—employees are less likely to appraise perceived PFP-I as a threat, reducing work withdrawal and protecting performance. The two countervailing pathways operate concurrently, so employees can have clear or ambivalent reactions depending on the leader's competence–warmth profile.
theoretical_contribution: The research makes a threefold contribution to compensation research: (1) it stresses employees' active role in perceiving, appraising, and reacting to PFP-I, showing perceived PFP-I varies across employees and drives motivational mechanisms for in-role performance; (2) it extends Park and Sturman's (2022) PFP perception framework and integrates leadership and motivation research by positioning leader competence and warmth as fundamental moderators of appraisals and role (dis)engagement; and (3) it foregrounds employees' role (dis)engagement—especially work withdrawal as distinct from (not the opposite of) engagement—as a neglected motivational mechanism that helps reconcile the mixed evidence on the incentive effect of PFP.
practical_implication: Designing a sound PFP system is necessary but not sufficient; organizations and leaders must understand how employees perceive and appraise PFP. Because employees care about leaders' competence and warmth when appraising perceived PFP-I, leaders should develop and visibly communicate both characteristics—e.g., accurate performance evaluation and clear paths to goals (competence) and supportive, considerate behaviors and positive emotions such as gratitude (warmth)—to channel PFP toward engagement rather than withdrawal and to prevent harmful outcomes (performance decrements, ill-being).
limitations: Both studies were conducted in a single, collectivistic culture (China), raising generalizability questions across individualistic cultures. The samples skewed relatively young, presumably because younger employees were more willing to complete the online surveys, so it remains untested whether the findings replicate in samples comprising mostly older employees. Although Study 2 added many controls and explored predictors of perceived PFP-I, the research did not have a theoretical focus on the antecedents of perceived PFP-I.
future_research: Future work could examine nonleader contingencies (e.g., job design features such as autonomy and feedback) that may similarly shape employees' challenge and threat appraisals of perceived PFP-I. Researchers could further probe how employee individual differences moderate reactions to perceived PFP-I (rather than to pay design characteristics), and could more directly theorize and test the antecedents of perceived PFP-I within the broader PFP environment.
abstract: While pay for performance (PFP) is widely adopted in organizations to boost employee performance, the empirical evidence on its incentive effect has been mixed. Based on Park and Sturman's PFP perception framework, we integrated two motivation theories—transactional theory of stress and role engagement theory—to delineate two countervailing mechanisms that explain how individuals appraise and react to perceived PFP. Our research considers employees' perception of PFP-I (perceived PFP based on individual performance) and highlights leader competence and warmth as critical contingencies. We propose that perceived PFP-I is appraised by employees as a challenge when leader competence is higher, which leads to employees' work engagement and enhanced task performance, but is appraised as a threat when leader warmth is lower, which leads to employees' work withdrawal and decreased task performance. In two three-wave survey studies, we found support for our model. The present research advances our knowledge regarding the incentive effect of PFP-I by emphasizing the importance of perceived PFP-I and proposing a new way of seeing employee motivation and performance ensuing from perceived PFP-I through the lenses of stress and role (dis)engagement. Our findings provide insights into how organizations and leaders can effectively utilize PFP to motivate employees.
apa_citation: Kong, D. T., Park, S., & Peng, J. (2023). Appraising and reacting to perceived pay for performance: Leader competence and warmth as critical contingencies. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(2), 402–431. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0209

title: Recognizing and Utilizing Novel Research Opportunities with Artificial Intelligence
methods: Editorial (From the Editors). Develops a four-cell typology of machine-learning research strategies for theory building and testing in AMJ, organized by phenomenon continuity (continuous vs. discontinuous) and theoretical coherence (fragmentation vs. coherence).
industry: Not reported in paper
units: na
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: von Krogh, Georg; Roberson, Quinetta; Gruber, Marc
theories: Not reported in paper
keywords: artificial intelligence; build and test theory; machine learning; predictions; research opportunities
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: As a subset of AI, machine learning offers management scholars new research opportunities by enlarging their methodological toolbox to leverage vast and varied data, with both benefits (statistical flexibility) and challenges (access, bias, cost).
- Key constructs: The editorial advances "a typology for conducting ML research in ways that build and test theory," distinguishing strategies by the continuity of the phenomenon investigated and its theoretical coherence.
- Typology (Table 1): (1) predictive selection — finding stable predictors across alternative theories for continuous phenomena under theoretical fragmentation; (2) predictive refinement — refining theory by training and evaluating models on new but similar data under theoretical coherence; (3) formative discovery — patterns in discontinuous data that give rise to novel (alternative) theory; (4) reductive discovery — patterns that show the limit to generalizability of existing theory.
- Recent advancements: federated learning, transfer learning, explainable AI, and AutoML are reviewed as tools that help scholars deal with access, bias, and cost challenges.

The editorial argues that machine learning has become a powerful means of discovering patterns in massive amounts of data and making predictions, and that its value to management scholarship is realized only when ML strategies are matched to the state of theorizing about a phenomenon. The four-cell typology guides authors in selecting an ML strategy whose epistemic purpose — predicting, refining, discovering, or bounding theory — fits the phenomenon's data continuity and theoretical coherence, consistent with AMJ's mission to test, extend, or build management theory.
theoretical_contribution: The piece offers a typology of four machine-learning research strategies (predictive selection, predictive refinement, formative discovery, reductive discovery) for building and testing theory, mapping each onto a 2x2 of phenomenon continuity and theoretical coherence. It reframes ML not as a mere predictive tool but as a means of advancing scholarly understanding of management phenomena.
practical_implication: Authors targeting AMJ with ML-based submissions should demonstrate deep engagement with their data, attend carefully to feature engineering, reflect on how bias may influence parameter choices and interpretation, share their code, and develop a comprehensive data management plan that safeguards data security and privacy.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editors encourage authors to explore ML research opportunities within AMJ, approaching research questions with a portfolio of strategies including formative and reductive discovery, predictive selection, and predictive refinement, in order to transform management research and practice.
abstract: AI should not ossify a creative mind but instead

title: Lay Theories of Instrumental Relations: Explaining Individual Differences in Dispositional Similarity-Attraction
methods: Three studies. Field Study: longitudinal network survey of executive MBA students (three time points), dyadic logit models with three-way (ego-alter-section) clustered standard errors. Experiment 1: preregistered between-subjects fictitious-stranger design, logistic regression and SEM-based moderated mediation (bootstrapped). Experiment 2: in-class dyadic-task experiment manipulating fixed vs. growth theory, OLS regression with dyad-clustered errors. Also develops and validates a 5-item LIR scale.
industry: Financial services and technology (Field Study executive MBA cohort); online/student working populations (experiments)
country: United States (Field Study, Experiment 2); United Kingdom or United States (Experiment 1)
time_period: Field Study August–December 2019; Experiment 1 March 2021; Experiment 2 conducted before Experiment 1 (date not reported)
units: Individuals (working professionals / MBA students) and their dyadic pairings
n_sample: Field Study: 111 individuals (2,756 ties across 6,062 dyads); Experiment 1: 492; Experiment 2: 174 MBA students
authors: Kuwabara, Ko; Cao, Jiyin; Cho, Soomin; Ingram, Paul
theories: lay theories / implicit theories (Dweck, 2000; Carr, Rattan, & Dweck, 2012); lay theories of instrumental networking (Kuwabara, Hildebrand, & Zou, 2018); similarity-attraction paradigm (Byrne, 1961; Montoya & Horton, 2013)
keywords: deep-level diversity; dispositional similarity-attraction; expected compatibility; fixed versus growth theory; homophily; instrumental ties; lay theories of instrumental relations
research_question: Are some individuals more likely than others to choose or get along with people who have different values, attitudes, and personality traits, and why? Specifically, how do lay theories of instrumental relations (LIR) — beliefs about whether interpersonal compatibility is fixed (natural chemistry) versus malleable (effort) — moderate dispositional similarity-attraction in partner choice and partner satisfaction?
mechanism_summary: People with a fixed theory believe relationships form on natural compatibility ("like pieces of a puzzle"), so they treat dispositional similarity as a quick diagnostic of compatibility and avoid dissimilar partners; people with a growth theory believe compatibility is cultivated through effort ("like muscles"), so they are more receptive to dissimilar others. Because lay theories are self-fulfilling, fixed theorists derive satisfaction from effortless clicking while growth theorists tolerate the effort of working with dissimilar partners. The result is a mediated-moderation model: dispositional similarity-attraction is stronger for fixed theorists, and (for partner choice) this is channeled through expected compatibility.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances a novel account of individual differences in dispositional (deep-level) similarity-attraction, departing from prior explanations centered on stereotyping and social categorization of surface-level differences by invoking *compatibility* as the key mechanism. It extends lay-theory research from individuals, intimate relations, and episodic exchanges into ongoing workplace instrumental relations, introducing LIR as both a theoretical construct and a validated measure. It also speaks to the problem of agency in networks, framing LIR as a basis of human agency in how people form and shape instrumental ties rather than treating homophily as a universal.
practical_implication: Measuring or changing employees' lay theories may offer a less heavy-handed route to building diverse yet cohesive workplaces — cultivating growth theories to promote cultural and interpersonal adaptability rather than screening employees for cultural fit. Because lay theories are simple and can be taught "on the spot," brief interventions may help employees value dissimilar partners. The authors caution that growth theories are a barrier-lowering tool, not a silver bullet, and warn against a "false growth mindset" that blames individuals and masks systemic barriers.
limitations: Samples were drawn from students or online populations, limiting generalizability, and the student samples are not large by current standards for testing moderation, warranting caution. Experiment 2 was limited by class size to two treatment groups (no neutral control), so firm conclusions about the relative effects of fixed versus growth manipulations cannot be drawn. The Field Study did not involve experimental treatment and conflates partner choice with partner satisfaction, and the studies capture relatively short-term interactions, raising questions about long-term effects.
future_research: Future work should test whether LIR effects endure over time and shape relationships over longer horizons, and whether LIR affect other relational outcomes such as network multiplexity. More research is needed on the antecedents of lay theories — why some people become fixed rather than growth theorists — including how organizational culture and diversity climate shape employees' LIR. Studies could also examine LIR across non-instrumental relations and the limits of how much deep-level dissimilarity growth theories can overcome.
abstract: The growing diversity in today's workplace requires engaging with people who not only look different but also think different. Yet, research on workplace relations has treated similarity-attraction as a human universal and paid limited attention to individual differences in who respects or tolerates different views and values, and why. We address this gap by examining how lay theories people hold about instrumental relations affect dispositional similarity-attraction. Because people who hold a fixed (versus growth) theory of instrumental relations believe that relationships form primarily on the basis of natural compatibility (versus effort), they should be particularly prone to similarity-attraction on the basis of dispositional similarity in values, attitudes, and personality traits. To test our arguments, we first develop a lay theories of instrumental relations scale, which we use to demonstrate that holding a growth theory decreases (moderates) people's tendency to avoid dispositionally dissimilar partners in a naturally occurring network (Field Study) and a dyadic task (Experiment 1). Finally (Experiment 2), we manipulate lay theories to show that inducing growth theories increases people's satisfaction with a task partner who is dispositionally dissimilar. We conclude by discussing theoretical and practical implications for building diverse yet cohesive workplaces.
apa_citation: Kuwabara, K., Cao, J., Cho, S., & Ingram, P. (2023). Lay theories of instrumental relations: Explaining individual differences in dispositional similarity-attraction. *Academy of Management Journal, 66*(2), 667–687. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0770

title: The Relational Dynamics of Issue-Selling: Enacting Different Genres for Dealing with Discontent
methods: Inductive, longitudinal multiple-case field study; cross-case comparison of issue-selling trajectories using video-recorded meetings, observations, informal conversations, and semi-structured interviews, transcribed and coded for episodes.
industry: Manufacturing / product innovation (precision instruments, high-end consumer goods, machining centers); product development across mechanical, design, and software/electrical engineering
country: Europe (German-speaking; firms not individually located; one firm owned by sales organizations in the United States and Europe; project funded by the Swiss Commission for Technology and Innovation)
time_period: Two-year research project; each case followed over 7–12 months, with follow-up interviews 12–15 months after observations
units: Issue-selling episodes between issue-sellers and -recipients within six manufacturing companies
n_sample: Six companies; 108 participants observed
authors: Lauche, Kristina; Erez, Miriam
theories: genre theory (Downing, 1997); issue-selling theory (Dutton & Ashford, 1993); relationality (Emirbayer, 1997)
keywords: discontent; issue-selling; perspective-taking; strategic renewal
research_question: How do issue-sellers and -recipients jointly shape how discontent is enacted or circumnavigated over time? The study investigates how less powerful actors pursue strategic-change initiatives and whether discontent serves as a motivational driver or a hampering force for issue-selling.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: discontent (cognitive disagreement paired with emotional commitment to an issue tied to one's values or professional identity); perspective-taking; relational history (power distance, prior conflicts, quality of previous encounters)
- Process / phases: framing → mobilizing → pursuing, with recipients' responses shaping each trajectory
- Genres (emergent patterns): (a) productive confrontation — framing as far-future opportunity, mobilizing by explicating/openly challenging, pursuing by escalating concerns, recipients acknowledging; (b) avoiding escalation — framing as near-future technical renewal, mobilizing by foreshadowing, pursuing by implicit fighting, recipients deferring; (c) collective moaning — framing as avoiding past errors, mobilizing by indirect alluding/generic complaints, pursuing by appeasing, recipients leaving open
- Boundary conditions: low power distance and constructive relational history enable productive confrontation; high power distance and fear yield avoiding escalation; accumulated frustration and pseudo-egalitarian distance yield collective moaning

The authors theorize issue-selling as a relational, collectively enacted process rather than an individual or dyadic act. Whether discontent energizes or paralyzes change depends not only on the seller's moves but on the jointly enacted genre and the recipient's response: supportive acknowledgment fosters agency and productive confrontation, whereas double-bind or indifferent responses foster fear, frustration, and collective helplessness. Reframing DeCelles et al.'s (2020) fight/flight/freeze responses as collective patterns, the genres function as an organization's shared repertoire for raising and responding to change.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to the issue-selling literature by showing that discontent can be a motivational force for change—conditional on a relational history of collective problem-solving and shared repertoires of open conflict resolution—rather than merely a fear-inducing inhibitor. It conceptualizes issue-selling as genres: collectively shared, recognizable patterns of framing, mobilizing, and pursuing that sellers and recipients jointly enact and reenact, demonstrating that the success of issue-selling cannot be understood from the seller's perspective alone. Collective moaning is identified as a previously unreported phenomenon.
practical_implication: Issue-sellers can diagnose their context: those in low-power-distance settings are best served by framing issues as future opportunities, articulating explicitly, and daring to escalate; those facing high power distance should "read the wind," engage more actively in perspective-taking, and avoid self-depreciation. Managers should recognize that their reactions shape outcomes—acknowledging concerns and following up with clear decisions retains valuable staff, whereas deferring and leaving things open alienates issue-sellers. Facilitators can help teams focus on the shared object and address implicit fighting and moaning.
limitations: Given the inductive design, the researchers could not know in advance how trajectories would unfold or what to look for, which they compensated for by returning to rich video recordings. Interviews focused on issue-sellers, so the subjective experience of recipients—who also play a key role—was underexamined. Spreading data collection across several companies strengthens generalization but means empirical differences between companies can have diverse causes, since the firms differed in innovation practices, scope, team composition, and senior-management roles.
future_research: Future work should include recipients' subjective experience and emotional involvement in issue-selling trajectories. Researchers should examine whether the selling strategies and responses of the three genres are always empirically confounded, and test in experimental or intervention studies whether more successful strategies and responses can improve collective outcomes (e.g., counteracting moaning by explicating content and resource issues). Because the study focused on new product development, future research should further investigate the temporal dynamics of issue-selling in different contexts.
abstract: Actors without formal power can initiate and shape organizational change through issue-selling as a means to address their concerns about organizational practices. The literature has emphasized the need for perspective-taking and found discontent to be associated with inhibiting fear. Based on a longitudinal field study of middle managers trying to instigate strategic renewal in six different manufacturing companies, we find that discontent can also energize issue-selling, particularly if sellers and -recipients also engage in perspective-taking. We identify three different genres of issue-selling and enacting discontent that are jointly shaped by sellers and -recipients: (a) productive confrontation, (b) avoiding escalation, and (c) collective moaning. In a process model of issue-selling as framing, mobilizing, and pursuing, we illustrate how the genres form part of an organization's collective repertoire of initiating and responding to change. Our findings have implications for pursuing change, as well as for managers seeking to create a constructive environment for expressing discontent.
apa_citation: Lauche, K., & Erez, M. (2023). The relational dynamics of issue-selling: Enacting different genres for dealing with discontent. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(2), 553–577. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1484

title: The Role of Attribution in Learning from Performance Feedback: Behavioral Perspective on the Choice between Alliances and Acquisitions
methods: Event-history (survival) analysis estimating the hazard rate of a firm switching from an alliance to an acquisition, using a Cox model with firm fixed effects and standard errors clustered on firms; alliance performance measured by cumulative abnormal returns (CAR) relative to a social aspiration level via a spline; robustness via linear probability, conditional logit/multinomial logit, alternative aspiration levels, and alternative samples.
industry: Cross-industry; equity alliances/joint ventures and acquisitions by large U.S. corporations (SDC Joint Ventures and Alliances and Mergers and Acquisitions databases; COMPUSTAT/CRSP)
units: Firm-quarter observations (firms whose most recent transaction was an alliance, at risk of switching to an acquisition)
n_sample: 13,535 firm-quarter observations (39,570 before filtering); 4,017 alliance-to-acquisition switches; models estimated on n = 26,005
authors: Lee, Jaemin; Lee, Joon Mahn; Kim, Ji-Yub (Jay)
theories: attribution theory (Weiner, 1979, 1986; Sedikides & Alicke, 2012); performance feedback theory / behavioral theory of the firm (Cyert & March, 1963; Greve, 2003b); self-serving attribution (Mezulis et al., 2004; Shepperd et al., 2008)
keywords: acquisitions; alliances; aspiration level; attribution; diffusion of responsibility; governance mode choice; performance feedback; self-serving attribution
research_question: How does the causal attribution of past alliance performance influence a firm's subsequent choice between acquisitions and alliances, and how do three attribution-relevant factors—the diffusion of responsibility, the perceived capability of partners, and the ambiguity of performance information—moderate the effect of alliance performance feedback on that choice?
mechanism_summary: The paper integrates attribution theory into the four-step performance feedback process (evaluation, attribution, search, change). Because managers make self-serving attributions—blaming partners or external events for failure and crediting themselves for success—poor alliance performance does not deter future alliances, and strong performance does not encourage switching, as much as classic theory predicts. The three moderators shift how strongly self-serving attribution manifests: more partners and higher performance variability amplify external attribution of failure (weakening the push toward acquisitions), while highly capable partners make blaming them less credible (strengthening the push toward acquisitions after failure).
theoretical_contribution: The study is the first to explicitly integrate the attribution process into performance feedback theory, showing that a firm's response to prior performance is significantly shaped by how managers attribute that performance and arguing that core predictions of performance feedback theory may require revision. By identifying three attribution-influencing moderators, it uncovers the theoretical mechanisms by which attribution operates and helps explain enduring inconsistent findings on whether failure triggers strategic change. It also extends corporate strategy research by providing the first behavioral account of the alliance-versus-acquisition choice, departing from economic and agency perspectives.
practical_implication: Governance-mode decisions (alliances vs. acquisitions) are not purely rational or opportunistic but can be distorted by managerial heuristics and biases such as self-serving attribution. Managers and stakeholders should be aware that conditions enabling blame-shifting—many alliance partners, highly variable performance histories, or weakly capable partners—can suppress learning from alliance failure and bias subsequent governance choices, warranting more deliberate causal analysis of performance outcomes.
limitations: The study examines only the choice between acquisitions and alliances and cannot observe internal development (organic growth), which typically goes unannounced. Its performance measure (CAR), though most suitable here, may not generalize to other metrics. The theory focuses on the focal firm's attribution and does not integrate the partner firm's view, and although results are robust across alternative aspiration-level specifications, slack resources may also influence how attributions are formed.
future_research: Future work could study settings where organic growth or other performance metrics can be reliably measured and tightly linked to specific actions, more fully integrate partners' views using matching or dyadic methods, examine how attribution processes differ across alternative aspiration-level specifications, and explore how attribution and slack search jointly shape other types of organizational change such as managerial succession or foreign-market entry mode.
abstract: The causal attribution of performance has not been explicitly considered in the performance feedback literature, despite its potential value in learning from prior performance. In this study, we develop a theory concerning the attribution in learning from performance feedback and explore how the attribution of past alliance performance can influence a firm's choice between future acquisitions and alliances. We also examine the mechanisms by which attribution manifests by exploring how three theoretical factors, known to influence attribution, can moderate the relationship—the diffusion of responsibility, the perceived capability of partners, and the ambiguity of performance information. We find strong evidence supporting our predictions. This study contributes to the performance feedback literature by integrating attribution with performance feedback theory. It also extends research on corporate strategy by providing a behavioral account of the choice between acquisitions and alliances.
apa_citation: Lee, J., Lee, J. M., & Kim, J.-Y. (J.). (2023). The role of attribution in learning from performance feedback: Behavioral perspective on the choice between alliances and acquisitions. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(2), 578–603. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1293

title: With Head and Heart: How Emotions Shape Paradox Navigation in Veterinary Work
methods: Inductive grounded-theory study (Strauss & Corbin, 1990; Gioia et al., 2013); semi-structured interviews (n=63 veterinarians, 93 total interviews), ~239 hours of nonparticipant observation, and archival data, analyzed iteratively to build a four-dimension process model.
industry: Veterinary medicine (private veterinary clinics)
time_period: Data collected 2009–2015, with follow-ups and additional interviews in 2019
units: Individual veterinarians navigating the business–care paradox
n_sample: 63 veterinarians (93 interviews; ~239 observation hours; 79 emotion-laden instances across 53 vets in 12 clinics)
authors: Pradies, Camille
theories: emotional labor (Hochschild, 1983); paradox theory; social emotions (Zietsma & Toubiana, 2018)
keywords: business–care paradox; emotional labor; emotions; intuition; microfoundations; paradox navigation; salience
research_question: How do emotions shape the ways in which individuals navigate paradox? The study examines this through veterinarians confronting the business–care paradox—the opposing yet interrelated business and care goals embedded in their work.
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: The business–care paradox, made salient each time a client interaction pits profitability against the care of a pet.
- Phase 1 — Emotional salience of paradox: Social bonds (perceived deservingness of pet owners) surface social emotions (empathy, compassion, guilt), creating a personal connection to paradoxical tensions and an impetus to consider both sides.
- Phase 2 — Flexible emotional labor between demands: Vets intuit "room to maneuver" and either justify downplaying or justify following emotions, opting for cost or care; organizational billing norms influence the direction.
- Phase 3 — Emotional traces following response: Decisions leave emotional fatigue (focus on cost) or emotional energy (focus on care) that linger after the consultation.
- Phase 4 — Ongoing learning from emotions: Traces drive vets to construct "workable barriers" that block fatigue while preserving exceptions, enabling learning over repeated exposure.
- Influencing factors / boundary conditions: Social bond between vet and client; perceived strictness of organizational billing norms.

Pradies theorizes paradox navigation as an emotionally fueled process rather than a purely cognitive or rational act. Emotions make paradox personally felt (not merely "seen" cognitively), and intuition—a swift, unconscious process—drives flexible iteration between competing demands. The emotional traces left by each response accumulate into learning, so that over time individuals adjust their workable barriers to balance energy and fatigue while sustaining engagement with both poles.
theoretical_contribution: The study enriches the microfoundations of paradox theory by explaining how emotions shape both the salience of paradox and individuals' responses to it. It theorizes an affective route to salience (driven by a desire to sustain social bonds), positions intuition as the key driver of flexible responses, and extends Smith's (2014) dynamic-equilibrium model to show that learning to navigate paradox entails balancing emotional traces, not just responses. More broadly it answers calls to address the dominant rational focus of paradox scholarship by foregrounding emotionality alongside rationality.
practical_implication: Practitioners benefit from being attuned to their emotions when confronting paradoxical tensions, since learning to navigate paradox involves reaching a dynamic equilibrium between emotional traces (energy and fatigue), and building a "workable barrier" should be a conscious act. Management educators should highlight emotional awareness and intelligence as key skills in paradox navigation.
limitations: As with other qualitative studies, the single French veterinary context raises questions about generalizability, though the dynamics may transfer to other professional settings where occupational values are pitted against business imperatives. Findings are likely most applicable to organizational members with high autonomy. It is also difficult to verify whether consultations categorized as lacking social emotions were truly devoid of emotion or whether emotions were repressed.
future_research: Future work could examine how varying levels of emotional intensity produce different levels of salience over time, explore emotions and paradox at the organizational level (e.g., via multiple case studies of emotion norms), and investigate how individual backgrounds, work orientations, or affective commitments shape emotional experiences of paradox.
abstract: Emotions are part of everyday life, and paradox scholars have long acknowledged that navigating paradox—that is, opposite, interrelated, and enduring tensions—is inherently emotional. Yet, how such emotions shape the ways in which individuals navigate paradoxes remains unclear. Drawing on an in-depth qualitative study of 63 veterinarians facing the business–care paradox, I develop an emotionally informed model of how individuals navigate paradox at work. I find that emotions: (a) make paradox salient by surfacing a personal connection to paradoxical tensions, (b) trigger flexible emotional labor while responding to paradox, and (c) leave emotional traces that foster ongoing learning. In so doing, I surface how emotions shape the way paradoxes are recognized and experienced. My model invites paradox scholars to consider the centrality of emotions in paradox navigation, thus moving beyond emotions as something to be avoided and instead embracing their generative potential. Foregrounding emotions offers rich theoretical opportunities for future work.
apa_citation: Pradies, C. (2023). With head and heart: How emotions shape paradox navigation in veterinary work. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(2), 521–552. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0633
