title: Inside-Out, Outside-In, or All-in-One? The Role of Network Sequencing in the Elaboration of Ideas
methods: Mixed but dominantly quantitative: exploratory interviews (40 R&D scientists/engineers, 30 R&D managers) inform construct development, followed by hypothesis-testing on a multi-survey design. Three surveys of scientists/engineers, managers, and directors; network data via name-generator/-interpreter and alter–alter matrix questions; a scenario-based question (NASA technology-readiness stages) measures timing of network mobilization. An inside-out network sequencing (IONS) index combines dispersion and consistency components. Ordered probit models predict the ordinal innovation rating, standard errors clustered by rank, IV and moderators mean-centered; robustness via Poisson model on an alternative count DV.
industry: Corporate R&D in a large, technology-intensive, Fortune-500 multinational firm (anonymized as 'Neptune') spanning diverse product markets and technologies
country: Multinational firm with R&D sites in Asia, Europe, and North America (single firm, anonymized)
time_period: Survey wave with innovation performance ratings obtained six months after the survey (data circa 2020; collection year not explicitly stated)
units: Individual R&D scientists and engineers and their ego networks of advice/support ties
n_sample: 301 R&D scientists and engineers (final analytic sample; 61% response rate, 48% of the original population)
authors: Ter Wal, Anne L. J.; Criscuolo, Paola; Salter, Ammon
theories: network mobilization / timing effects (Mannucci & Perry-Smith, 2021; Perry-Smith & Mannucci, 2017); network theory of innovation / structural holes (Burt, 2004); social circles (Dunbar, 1998; Kadushin, 1968); social cohesion around relationships (Reagans & McEvily, 2003; Tortoriello et al., 2012)
keywords: all-in-one network mobilization; idea elaboration; inner-circle ties; innovation performance; inside-out network sequencing; outer-circle ties; outside-in network sequencing; shared third-party ties
research_question: How does the sequence in which R&D scientists and engineers mobilize feedback and support from colleagues for the elaboration of novel ideas affect their innovation performance? In particular, does mobilizing inner-circle ties before outer-circle ones ("inside-out" sequencing) outperform "outside-in" or "all-in-one" approaches, and is this advantage stronger in less supportive work environments?
mechanism_summary: Inner-circle ties (those sharing many third-party connections with the idea creator) bring greater commitment and information redundancy, so they patiently digest ill-defined, ill-structured early-stage ideas, supply candid constructive feedback, and surface logical inconsistencies before creators grow wedded to flawed directions. Exposing immature ideas to outer-circle ties too early risks dismissive reactions and "false negatives," whereas these nonredundant outsiders become valuable later, once ideas are robust enough to withstand external scrutiny and to validate fit with the wider organization. Sequencing inner-before-outer therefore lets individuals secure early feedback while shielding fragile ideas, mitigating the dual risk of dropping good ideas or persisting with bad ones. The contrast between circles—and hence the payoff to careful sequencing—sharpens in unsupportive contexts where outer-circle skepticism is most damaging.
theoretical_contribution: The study introduces "network sequencing" as a temporal, behavioral dimension of networks distinct from network structure and position, showing that *when* individuals involve which contacts—not just *whom* they are connected to—explains differences in innovation performance over and above network constraint. It connects to and extends research on strategic networking behaviors by demonstrating that creative individuals outperform peers by making "savvy choices of who to involve when." Third, it shows that network mechanisms such as feedback and support are time- and alter-dependent and may backfire if mobilized at the wrong time, unpacking idea elaboration into substages in which strong/inner and weak/outer ties play different roles.
practical_implication: Because network sequencing matters yet many individuals make poor mobilization choices, organizations should inform and train R&D talent to deliberately sequence their networks—seeking early input from committed inner-circle ties to nurture ideas in "safe spaces" before exposing them to outer-circle scrutiny, while avoiding cleaving too closely to the inner circle and engaging outer-circle ties once ideas mature. Such training is especially valuable in units lacking innovation support, where engaging outsiders too early is costly; careful management of slack, autonomy/accountability balance, cannibalization concerns, and supportive leadership can each reduce the risk of squandering creative talent.
limitations: The study is based on a single organization with relatively homogeneous HR practices, limiting generalizability, and it examines only intra-organizational networks. The scenario-based measure of mobilization timing captures expected rather than actual behavior and cannot establish whether the reported approach generalizes across different ideas, contexts, or situations. The innovation-performance measure has a short (single-year) time horizon, and inner/outer-circle ties are operationalized structurally (shared third parties) without directly observing tie strength; the paper also does not explore why sequencing differences arise.
future_research: Future work could examine network sequencing in other organizational contexts and across organizational boundaries, and use more direct methods (e.g., diary-based monitoring) to capture how individuals seek feedback for different ideas. Researchers could disentangle tie strength from social cohesion in sequencing effects, study how idea radicalness and longer-horizon (radical) innovation call for different sequencing strategies, and investigate antecedents of sequencing choices such as confidence, political skill, personality, and cognition.
abstract: The structure of advice and support networks within organizations has a profound impact on the elaboration of novel ideas. We explore how the sequence in which individuals expose ideas to their network contacts affects their innovation performance. We argue that, during idea elaboration, inside-out network sequencing—that is, mobilizing input and support from inner-circle ties before outer-circle ones—yields an innovation performance advantage over outside-in network sequencing and all-in-one mobilization of network contacts. Inside-out network sequencing generates valuable early feedback and support from inner-circle ties that actively engage with ill-defined, ill-structured, and uncertain ideas, and delays exposure to outer-circle ties until ideas can better withstand criticism from beyond the social circle where they emerged. We further contend that the benefits of inside-out network sequencing are amplified in environments that lack support for innovation. Using an analysis of survey data and archival innovation performance records for 301 research and development scientists and engineers in a large multinational firm, we find support for our predictions.
apa_citation: Ter Wal, A. L. J., Criscuolo, P., & Salter, A. (2023). Inside-out, outside-in, or all-in-one? The role of network sequencing in the elaboration of ideas. *Academy of Management Journal, 66*(2), 432–461. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1231

title: Pay Transparency as a Moving Target: A Multistep Model of Pay Compression, I-Deals, and Collectivist Shared Values
methods: Two studies. Study 1: preregistered 2x2 between-subjects online business-simulation experiment (managerial agency in pay compression). Study 2: multisource, firm-level field study analyzed with multilevel structural equation modeling (2-2-1-2 MSEM) in Mplus, with Monte Carlo tests of (conditional) serial indirect effects.
industry: Medical device distribution firms (Study 2); managers recruited via Prolific (Study 1)
country: China (Study 2); United States and United Kingdom (Study 1)
time_period: Endogenous variables measured over the past year; two-phase data collection
units: Firms (with employer and employee survey + archival pay data); individual managers in Study 1
n_sample: Study 1: 117 managers (final); Study 2: 645 employees in 111 firms
authors: Wong, Man-Nok; Cheng, Bonnie Hayden; Lam, Leon Wai-Yuan; Bamberger, Peter A.
theories: compensating wage differentials (CWD) theory (Rosen, 1974, 1986); social comparison theory (Gibbons & Buunk, 1999); transparency-privacy model (Bernstein, 2017)
keywords: collectivist shared values; compensating wage differentials; i-deals; pay transparency; transparency-privacy model; variable pay compression
research_question: Whether, how, and when does firm-level pay transparency become a "moving target" — that is, through what multistep process does transparency lead managers and employees to shift remuneration from more observable forms (variable pay) to less observable ones (nonmonetary i-deals), and under what conditions is this process amplified?
mechanism_summary: Building on Bernstein's (2017) transparency-privacy model, the authors argue that making pay more observable pressures managers to avoid controversy over visible pay differentials by compressing variable pay. Consistent with compensating wage differentials theory, this compression motivates employees to seek redress through less observable, particularistic rewards (i-deals), and those requests are in turn granted — thereby "hiding" reward dispersion in discreet forms. Drawing on social comparison theory, firms with more collectivist shared values heighten the salience of observability and social monitoring, amplifying the compression-to-i-deal-request stage of the serial indirect effect. The field study supported Hypotheses 1–4 and 6 (and 5b), while Hypotheses 5a was not supported.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends Bernstein's (2017) transparency-privacy theory from the individual to the firm level, explicating a previously undescribed multistep mechanism by which transparency indirectly (rather than intentionally) produces hiding of reward differentials. It contributes to i-deals research by identifying pay compression as a firm-level antecedent of i-deal requests and grants and by isolating collectivist shared values as a boundary condition that counterintuitively amplifies the transparency–privacy paradox.
practical_implication: Because pay transparency can trigger unintended pay compression and a downstream rise in nonmonetary i-deal grants, leaders should anticipate these externalities — e.g., emphasizing to managers that justifiable pay differences benefit their own performance, and monitoring i-deal allocation so hidden rewards are not used as a "back door" for gender- and ethnicity-based disparities. Effects are especially pronounced in collectivist contexts, where managers should make pay policies more transparent and procedurally clear.
limitations: Study 1's experimental design cannot rule out reciprocal causation between compression and transparency, and its short-term managerial role may not generalize to real-life decisions. Study 2 used field data from small firms in a single industry (medical device distribution) and a single occupation (salespeople) in one country, limiting generalizability to larger firms, other industries, and contexts where vertical/tournament pay dynamics matter.
future_research: Future work should test the causal link between pay compression and i-deals via experimental or quasi-experimental designs, examine pay compression as a mechanism linking transparency to outcomes like collective performance and human-capital sorting, and pursue cross-industry and cross-cultural samples. Researchers could also unpack the dominant mechanism behind the collectivist amplification effect and study pay "noticeability" at the individual level.
abstract: Drawing from research on the transparency-privacy dilemma in management, we theorize that firm-level pay transparency elicits a multistep process involving managers and employees that shifts the dispersion in remuneration from more to less observable forms, thus making pay transparency a “moving target.” We posit a serial indirect effect of pay transparency on firm-level rates of i-deal grants (a less observable form of remuneration) via variable pay compression and heightened rates of employee i-deal requests, with this indirect effect amplified in firms characterized by collectivist shared values. First examining the role of managerial agency and collectivist shared values in the pay transparency–compression relationship in a simulation-based experiment, we test the overall model in a multisource field study using a sample of 111 medical device distribution firms. Our findings demonstrate that: (a) firm-level pay transparency is predictive of greater pay compression, (b) firm-level rates of i-deal grants are largely explained by this pay compression via its effects on employee i-deal requests, and (c) this sequential effect is amplified in firms with more collectivist shared values. Accordingly, we explicate how transparency triggers unintentional hiding, and suggest that accompanying more transparent pay may be an increased reliance upon rewards that, by their very nature, are less transparent.
apa_citation: Wong, M.-N., Cheng, B. H., Lam, L. W.-Y., & Bamberger, P. A. (2023). Pay transparency as a moving target: A multistep model of pay compression, i-deals, and collectivist shared values. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(2), 489–520. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1831

title: Learning Down to Train Up: Mentors Are More Effective When They Value Insights from Below
methods: Five studies: three survey studies (Study 1 cross-sectional; Study 2A correlational; Study 2B two-wave pre-registered longitudinal) testing the downward-learning -> engagement link via OLS/Tobit/Poisson regression and mediation; a field study (Study 3) linking mentors' learning direction to archival mentee outcomes via multilevel ordinal logistic and OLS regressions; and an online experiment (Study 4) manipulating learning direction, with multilevel mediation and moderated-mediation analyses. Confirmatory factor analyses establish discriminant validity of the new scale.
industry: Working professionals/supervisors across diverse industries (Prolific Academic samples); software engineers and data scientists mentoring novice coders at an online programming boot camp (field study)
units: Individual mentors (and matched mentees), via Prolific Academic surveys/experiments and a field setting
n_sample: Study 1 n=499; Study 2A n=776; Study 2B n=400 (364 at Time 2); Study 3 = 278 mentor-mentee pairs (50 matched mentors); Study 4 = 439 mentors and 295 mentees
authors: Zhang, Ting; Wang, Dan J.; Galinsky, Adam D.
theories: growth mindset; learning orientation; social hierarchy and power
keywords: downward learning; learning direction; learning orientation; mentor engagement; mentoring effectiveness; social hierarchy
research_question: What motivates potential mentors to engage with and be effective for their mentees? The paper asks whether mentors' "learning direction"—specifically the belief that those lower in a hierarchy are valuable sources of knowledge—predicts mentor engagement and mentoring effectiveness, above and beyond related constructs like learning orientation and growth mindset.
mechanism_summary: Believing that people below oneself in a social hierarchy hold valuable knowledge reframes mentoring from a costly obligation into a two-way learning opportunity. This reframing raises mentors' engagement (the time and effort they invest), which in turn improves mentees' learning outcomes and their perceptions of advice and support. Because power tends to reduce perspective-taking, the downward-learning manipulation has its strongest effects for mentors high in positional power.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces "learning direction" as a new construct that shifts learning from a task-related construct (learning orientation) to a relational one—who one believes one can learn from across a hierarchy. It extends work on learning orientation and growth mindset (which focus on the self) by showing that downward learning predicts mentor engagement and effectiveness independent of those constructs, and integrates research on social hierarchy, mentoring, and advice to explain what makes mentors effective.
practical_implication: Organizations typically motivate mentors by emphasizing benefits to mentees, which is insufficient because mentorship still feels like a costly obligation. Instead, they should highlight the learning value mentees offer to mentors. The research identifies an easy-to-implement intervention: having mentors reflect on times they learned from someone below them in a hierarchy shifts their learning direction and boosts engagement, especially for those in positions of power.
limitations: In the field study (Study 3), although mentor-mentee matches were based only on scheduling availability, the authors acknowledge the relationship between mentors' downward learning and student outcomes could partly run in reverse, with mentee performance shaping mentors' reported learning direction. The field sample of matched mentors was small and predominantly male, motivating the controlled, gender-diverse experiment in Study 4.
future_research: Future work should examine downward learning beyond mentoring (e.g., how leaders' downward learning affects their own and their team's productivity and innovation), and explore optimal learning directions across career stages and roles. Researchers are also encouraged to study the conversational-level process through which downward learning improves mentee outcomes—disentangling content of knowledge exchanged from relational rapport—and to extend learning direction to nontraditional sources of learning.
abstract: Although mentorship is vital for individual success, potential mentors often view it as a costly burden. To understand what motivates mentors to overcome this barrier and more fully engage with their mentees, we introduce a new construct, “learning direction,” which captures the beliefs people have about which individuals within a hierarchy— upward, lateral, or downward—are valuable sources of knowledge. Although most mentors believe knowledge resides higher up in hierarchies, the current research demonstrates mentors are more engaged and effective when they value insights from below. Our first three studies found that downward learning predicted mentor engagement (Study 1) because downward learning-directed mentors viewed mentoring as a worthwhile opportunity for their own learning (Studies 2A and 2B). A field study examined downstream consequences on mentees’ learning outcomes, finding that downward learning-directed mentors trained more hireable mentees (Study 3). A final experiment established causality while also demonstrating that learning direction is open to intervention: reflecting on a downward learning experience increased mentor engagement, which enhanced the mentorship experience for their mentees (Study 4). We extend theories on learning orientation to learning direction and demonstrate that beliefs about the hierarchical sources of learning are critical to successful mentoring.
apa_citation: Zhang, T., Wang, D. J., & Galinsky, A. D. (2023). Learning down to train up: Mentors are more effective when they value insights from below. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(2), 604–637. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0430

title: May I Please Go the Extra Mile? Citizenship Communication Strategies and Their Effect on Individual Initiative OCB, Work–Family Conflict, and Partner Satisfaction
methods: Three studies. Study 1: inductive qualitative interviews (constant comparative analysis) with married couples to identify communication strategies. Study 2: multi-source (employee + partner) cross-sectional survey, hierarchical linear regression and relative weights analysis. Study 3: between-subjects experimental vignette study, ANOVA with Tukey's HSD.
industry: Knowledge-work occupations (e.g., management, sales, professional services, government, attorney, financial planner)
units: Married/partnered employees and their partners (couples)
n_sample: Study 1: 22 individuals (11 couples), expanded by 6; Study 2: 192 employee–partner pairs; Study 3: 922 participants
authors: Bolino, Mark C.; Flores, Marisa L.; Kelemen, Thomas K.; Bisel, Ryan S.
theories: dual concern theory (Pruitt & Rubin, 1986; Beitler, Scherer, & Zapf, 2018); social exchange perspective on OCB (Organ, 1990)
keywords: citizenship communication strategies; dual concern theory; individual initiative; organizational citizenship behavior; partner satisfaction; work–family conflict
research_question: How do employees communicate with their partner at home when engaging in individual initiative OCB, and how do these citizenship communication strategies affect the employee's OCB, their work–family conflict, and their partner's satisfaction?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on dual concern theory from the conflict-management literature, the authors argue that self-concerned strategies emphasize the employee's own career goals and the rewards of OCB, whereas other-concerned strategies prioritize accommodating the partner. Self-concerned strategies should facilitate OCB but strain home life, while other-concerned strategies should reduce work–family conflict and raise partner satisfaction. Empirically, the trade-off is asymmetric: invoking prior conversations and projecting payoffs raise OCB (and invoking prior conversations raises work–family conflict; projecting payoffs lowers partner satisfaction), while seeking permission and providing early notice raise partner satisfaction but do not reduce work–family conflict and (for seeking permission) lower OCB.
theoretical_contribution: By identifying the *content* of partner communication specific to OCB, the paper extends partner-communication and work–family research—previously focused on how communication is enacted (e.g., demand–withdraw, assertive–passive)—into the citizenship domain. It demonstrates that there is no single communication strategy beneficial for both work (OCB) and home (reduced conflict, higher partner satisfaction), and shows that employees and their partners conceptualize OCB as involuntary for achieving family goals, deepening the literature on felt pressure to engage in citizenship.
practical_implication: Organizations and managers should recognize that "extra-role" behavior is often experienced as non-discretionary by employees and their partners, raising justice concerns. Employees can strategically match communication to their priorities: emphasizing seeking permission and providing early notice to protect home life, or projecting payoffs and invoking prior conversations to enable OCB—while being mindful of each strategy's costs. Organizations should encourage, but not compel, good citizenship.
limitations: The Study 1 qualitative sample was relatively young, well educated, predominantly White, and seemingly happily married, possibly surfacing mainly constructive strategies and limiting transferability. Study 2 data were cross-sectional, precluding causal inference, and OCB was partner-rated rather than fully objective. The research focused on one resource-consuming OCB type (individual initiative) and on one-way (employee) communication rather than dyadic exchange.
future_research: Scholars could examine dysfunctional or post-hoc communication strategies, collect reciprocal dyadic data using the actor–partner interdependence model, and study how OCB may generate work–family enrichment rather than only conflict. Additional work could investigate antecedents of citizenship communication (e.g., personality, organizational norms) and whether these strategies extend to other OCB types and targets such as coworkers and managers.
abstract: Organizational citizenship behavior (OCB) contributes to organizational effectiveness, but it can also create a burden for employees and their partners. In three studies, we examine how employees communicate with their partner about engaging in OCB and how this affects employees' OCB, their work–family conflict, and their partner's satisfaction. In Study 1, we use qualitative data to identify five ways employees communicate with their partner in order to engage in individual initiative OCB (e.g., staying late at work, volunteering for special assignments). In Study 2, using dual-concern theory, we hypothesize that self-concerned citizenship communication strategies will not only increase OCB, but also increase work–family conflict, and we predict that other-concerned communication strategies will reduce work–family conflict and increase partner satisfaction. Using survey data from employees and their partners, these hypotheses were partially supported. Finally, in Study 3, we use experimental vignette methodology to provide causal evidence that citizenship communication strategies influence partner satisfaction. Together, these studies increase our understanding of how employees communicate with their partner about OCB, indicate that there is no single communication strategy that has positive implications for both OCB and home life, and suggest that employees and their partners tend to think of OCB as involuntary for achieving family goals.
apa_citation: Bolino, M. C., Flores, M. L., Kelemen, T. K., & Bisel, R. S. (2023). May I please go the extra mile? Citizenship communication strategies and their effect on individual initiative OCB, work–family conflict, and partner satisfaction. *Academy of Management Journal, 66*(3), 894–925. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0581

title: Double Weaving: A Bottom-Up Process of Connecting Locations and Scales to Mitigate Grand Challenges
methods: Inductive, longitudinal single-case study of the nonprofit CINI; participatory fieldwork plus 40 years of archival records; over 1,200 documents (~9,000 pages), 225 days of participant observation, 42 open-ended semi-structured individual interviews and 17 group interviews; Gioia-style first-/second-/third-order coding (24 first-order categories, 8 second-order themes, 4 aggregate dimensions), visual mapping, narratives, and triangulation.
industry: Nonprofit / NGO sector addressing child malnutrition (health, nutrition, education, protection)
country: India (West Bengal and Jharkhand; CINI headquartered near Kolkata)
units: Single organization (Child In Need Institute, CINI) and the actors it connects across locations and scales
n_sample: 1 organization (longitudinal case); ~5 million beneficiaries in 2013 scaling to over 7 million by end of fieldwork
authors: Chatterjee, Arijit; Ghosh, Anjan; Leca, Bernard
theories: grand challenges / wicked problems (Ferraro, Etzion, & Gehman, 2015; George et al., 2016); human geography of space, locations, and scales (Lefebvre, 1974/1991; Marston et al., 2005; Delaney & Leitner, 1997); process / inductive theory building (Langley, 1999; Gioia, Corley, & Hamilton, 2013); scaling social innovation (Westley et al., 2014; Bansal, Kim, & Wood, 2018)
keywords: child malnutrition; double weaving; grand challenges; grassroots organization; locations and scales; scalar asymmetry; scaling; spatial turn
research_question: How can a grassroots organization work within and across locations and scales to mitigate a grand challenge? The study uses a spatial lens that treats space as both horizontal (locations) and vertical (scales), asking how a resource-poor local organization can connect dispersed actors and resources to address a system-wide, "wicked" problem.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / dimensions: Scaling out, scaling up, scaling down (three directional components) plus reinforcing (a process driver) — the four broad activity types that comprise double weaving.
- Process / phases: Scaling out (local intervening, disseminating) → scaling up (synchronizing, strategic linking) → scaling down (collective capacity building, anchoring) → reinforcing (diagnosing, consolidating), iterating recursively through multiple cycles.
- Triggering conditions: Grassroots interventions lack financial resources and access to policy-making, which triggers a need to scale up to higher-scale actors; successful scaling up builds on credible disseminated outcomes of local interventions.
- Boundary conditions: A cooperative case with no contestation/conflict; norms of freely shared knowledge; and a long, nested four-decade scaling path from community to government.

Double weaving is theorized as a multidirectional, recursive process in which a focal organization "weaves" actors and resources horizontally across locations and vertically across scales at the same time, analogous to two fabrics woven and interwoven for strength. Reinforcing (continuous diagnosing of root causes and consolidating of what works while discarding what fails) drives and sustains scaling out, up, and down, giving the process continuity and durability. Rather than seeking shared consensus among all stakeholders, the organization weaves actors around specific problems on which agreement can be more easily managed, navigating scalar asymmetries of power and contextual familiarity.
theoretical_contribution: The study contributes to grand-challenges research by showing that identifying a grand challenge and its root causes is inseparable from engaging with it, and by treating diagnosing and mitigating as concomitant rather than sequential. It advances the "spatial turn" in organizational research by developing the under-theorized scalar (vertical) dimension alongside locations (horizontal), and extends scaling research—which has treated scaling as a unidirectional process—by theorizing scaling down as a complement to scaling out and up and by foregrounding scalar asymmetry.
practical_implication: For grassroots organizations with limited resources, the case offers lessons on scaling social impact: pair local interventions with disseminating credible outcomes to attract higher-scale actors, synchronize with the mandates and expectations of those actors (rather than lecturing them), and use scaling down to channel resources and policy into superior grassroots interventions. Lower-scale actors can exploit asymmetries—using higher-scale actors' resources and policy access while supplying the local contextual familiarity those actors lack.
limitations: The authors flag three boundary conditions of a single-case study. First, the case features collaboration and cooperation without the contestation or conflict often inherent in grand challenges. Second, CINI members freely shared knowledge, whereas many settings involve closely guarded ideas and inter-firm rivalry. Third, the nested, four-decade scaling path highlights how slow the process can be and may not generalize to contexts with faster, politically accountable pathways.
future_research: Future work could compare less emotionally intense and vivid issues (e.g., preventable diseases such as malaria) with child malnutrition; examine the conditions under which knowledge diffuses widely given the tension between sparse networks for idea brokering and dense networks for implementation; and study grand challenges from a higher-scale actor's perspective. Researchers could also examine more closely how actors exploit scalar asymmetries across scales.
abstract: Grand challenges are system-wide problems. While top-down approaches to mitigating grand challenges may have potentially far-reaching impact, such initiatives from the higher scales can fail because of limited knowledge of ground realities. At the same time, resilient local efforts to address societal challenges can have limited reach. How can a grassroots organization work across locations and scales to address a grand challenge? We report the findings of a longitudinal case study of the Child In Need Institute, a nonprofit organization that successfully scaled its impact to reach over seven million beneficiaries. Based on 40 years of archival data and four years of intensive fieldwork, our findings reveal “double weaving” as a recursive process of diagnosing and addressing problems by connecting actors and resources across locations and scales. The double weaving process gives researchers a framework to explore the complex interplay between locations and scales that is necessary to address grand challenges. Our study contributes to research on grand challenges and scaling, advancing the spatial turn in organizational studies by developing the scalar dimension, and expanding research that has primarily focused on locations.
apa_citation: Chatterjee, A., Ghosh, A., & Leca, B. (2023). Double weaving: A bottom-up process of connecting locations and scales to mitigate grand challenges. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(3), 797–828. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1348

title: Identity Multiplicity and the Formation of Professional Network Ties
methods: Logit regression on dyad-level data (26,234 dyads) from 401 part-time MBA students across three cohorts; standard errors clustered simultaneously by ego and alter; KHB mediation tests; LSA of identity-map text to operationalize identity homophily; cohort fixed effects.
industry: Subjects employed across finance (35%), healthcare/pharmaceuticals (10%), technology (8%), and consulting (7%); context is a part-time MBA program
time_period: Three MBA cohorts entering summer 2020, fall 2020, and fall 2021; network survey at end of first semester
units: Dyads (pairs of classmates within a section)
n_sample: 401 subjects; 26,234 dyads (13,082 for reciprocated-tie models)
authors: Ingram, Paul
theories: homophily / value homophily (McPherson, Smith-Lovin, & Cook, 2001); identity theory (Stryker & Burke, 2000); individuation (Maslach, 1974; Kang & Bodenhausen, 2015); social identity theory (Tajfel, 1981; Tajfel & Turner, 1986)
keywords: homophily; identity homophily; identity multiplicity; individuation; professional network ties; racial difference
research_question: How does identity multiplicity—the extent to which personal identities draw on multiple elements—predict the incidence of professional network ties, and does it particularly promote ties between individuals of different races?
mechanism_summary: Identity multiplicity makes a person harder to categorize, which prompts interlocutors to individuate them—perceiving them as a unique person rather than a category representative—and individuation drives attraction, engagement, and the initiation of ties. Simultaneously, more identity elements raise the odds of sharing salient elements with an alter (identity homophily), creating bases of attraction and interaction. KHB tests indicate individuation and identity homophily jointly mediate ~82% of the identity-multiplicity effect (full mediation). Because individuation reduces negative racial stereotyping, identity multiplicity is especially potent for forging interracial ties—what the author labels the "Grunitzky effect."
theoretical_contribution: The paper brings personal identity directly into network-formation theory, advancing the "bring the individual back in" agenda by treating self-articulated identity (not demographic proxies) as a determinant of ties. It introduces "identity homophily," extending value homophily beyond race and gender to any self-identified element, and theorizes individuation as an original, tested mechanism linking identity to tie formation and to the transcendence of racial categories.
practical_implication: Individuals who want richer, more diverse professional networks should cultivate identity multiplicity—e.g., by joining multiple affinity groups rather than focusing on one, and by experimenting with and refining authentic identity narratives. Organizations can leverage existing identity-based affinity groups to build cross-demographic ties that enhance creativity, decision-making, collaboration, and inclusion.
limitations: The data do not capture off-campus socializing, so the exact location where each tie formed is unobserved (though the author argues this does not threaten the hypotheses). The reverse-causality concern—that ties reshape identity salience—is addressed through several robustness analyses but cannot be fully ruled out, particularly over longer time periods. The setting is a single part-time MBA program, and data collection occurred under COVID-19 constraints.
future_research: Future work could analyze the structure of identity maps (e.g., path distance or eigenvector centrality of elements) to study how the importance and interconnection of identity elements shape networking. Researchers could examine networking agency and goal achievement as a function of identity multiplicity, apply text analysis of large identity corpora (e.g., college essays) to study identity homophily at scale, and explore implications for tie efficacy and overall network structure (e.g., modularity).
abstract: How does personal identity multiplicity—a self-perception with more elements—help an individual connect with others? I argue that identity multiplicity increases the likelihood of establishing professional network ties through two mechanisms: individuation, or the tendency of a potential network partner to view one as an individual as opposed to a representative of a category; and identity homophily, or the attraction and interaction opportunities that emerge as a function of shared identity elements with a potential partner. The benefits of identity multiplicity are particularly strong for promoting interracial ties as individuation reduces racial stereotyping. I find support for these ideas in an analysis of the creation of network ties of mid-career professionals.
apa_citation: Ingram, P. (2023). Identity multiplicity and the formation of professional network ties. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(3), 720–743. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0230

title: Structuring the Start-Up: How Coordination Emerges in Start-Ups through Learning Sequencing
methods: Inductive, longitudinal multiple-case study of five early-stage start-ups followed for 22 months; data from 52 participant-observation visits (240+ hours), 96 semistructured interviews across three rounds, and archival sources; within-case and cross-case analysis with replication logic and member checks.
industry: Early-stage digital start-ups (all building a digital platform, or a digital brand in one case)
country: Norway (start-ups recruited from coworking spaces; authors at NHH Norwegian School of Economics and BI Norwegian Business School)
units: Start-ups (firms)
n_sample: 5 start-ups
authors: Jones, Marius; Schou, Peter Kalum
theories: emergent coordination (Okhuysen & Bechky, 2009); organizational learning / learning styles (Bingham & Davis, 2012; Miner, Bassof, & Moorman, 2001)
keywords: coordination; emergent coordination; learning sequencing; organizational learning; start-ups
research_question: The paper asks: (a) How do start-ups develop coordination over time? and (b) How does successful coordination affect organizational performance?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: Early-stage start-ups lack the institutionalized scaffolding (roles, routines, rules, plans) that the emergent-coordination literature assumes is already present, so they must learn to coordinate from a rudimentary starting point.
- Process / phases (the "best practice" learning sequence): (1) *Anticipating coordination* (anticipatory learning) — foreseeing coordination needs before crises emerge; (2) *Steal with pride* (vicarious learning) — importing ready-made frameworks and practices from peers and competitors; (3) *Experimental implementation* (experimental learning) — minimal roll-outs, then scaling mechanisms that prove functional; (4) *Combine and simplify* (trial-and-error learning) — layering and simplifying mechanisms to fit the start-up and avoid over-formalization.
- Key constructs (coordination mechanisms scored per case): rules and plans, routines, and roles (Okhuysen & Bechky, 2009).
- Emergent outcomes: Coordination mechanisms that improve performance, indexed by market growth, received funding, and employee job satisfaction.
- Boundary conditions: Learning styles complement and prompt one another (path-dependent sequencing); start-ups may restart the sequence at junctures such as rapid scaling or pivots.

Across the five cases, the high performer (Freya) executed the full anticipatory→vicarious→experimental→trial-and-error sequence, while lower performers stalled by relying on improvised, unstructured solutions or by failing to combine and simplify. The central process insight is that, lacking pre-existing scaffolds, start-ups must learn coordination *deliberately*: each learning style makes a distinct contribution, and styles feed into one another (e.g., anticipatory learning motivates vicarious search; vicarious learning supplies frameworks for experimentation). Coordination is thus reframed as a state reached through learning rather than something that emerges from institutionalized scaffolds.
theoretical_contribution: The paper's core contribution is a processual theoretical framework explaining how start-ups learn to coordinate by sequencing complementary learning styles, addressing a literature on start-up coordination that has been dominated by static, top-down "blueprint" explanations. It extends the emergent-coordination literature by showing how coordination emerges from an immature state with underdeveloped routines and roles—through deliberate learning rather than from pre-existing scaffolds—and adds to the organizational learning literature by demonstrating how different learning styles interact in learning sequences, with one style compensating for shortcomings in another.
practical_implication: Start-ups should not fear having clear roles, rules, plans, and routines and should deliberately seek to implement them rather than staying unstructured. Entrepreneurs are advised to anticipate coordination needs early, learn from peers, and continuously combine and simplify coordination mechanisms to fit their current situation while avoiding bureaucratic rigidity.
limitations: As an inductive multiple-case study, the work is limited in its knowledge claims, especially regarding performance outcomes, and the authors cannot fully rule out alternative explanations (e.g., market volatility, team-formation effects, founder experience). Because only the high performer restarted its learning sequence within the study window, the authors' ability to theorize iterative sequences and recovery from coordination crises is constrained, and the data are limited on how start-ups recover from such crises.
future_research: The authors call for larger-scale, quantitative work to test whether the learning styles are additive and sequential (does order matter, or can styles be reordered?) and whether the findings replicate in a larger population of start-ups. They also encourage research on how specific learning styles contribute to distinct aspects of coordination, how start-ups restart learning sequences as they scale, and how start-ups recover from coordination crises.
abstract: To succeed in growing and scaling their organization, start-ups must establish roles, routines, rules, and plans that coordinate organizational activities. However, early-stage start-ups often lack such coordination mechanisms. Through a longitudinal qualitative multiple-case study of five start-ups, we develop a theoretical framework for how start-ups develop and improve coordination over time. We find that start-ups establish coordination through a learning sequence consisting of four distinct learning styles. To develop coordination successfully, start-ups anticipate coordination problems before they escalate, steal ideas and frameworks from others, experimentally implement coordination, and combine and simplify coordination mechanisms. By providing a processual understanding of how start-ups develop coordination, we contribute to the literature on coordination in start-ups, which has tended toward static explanations. We also add to the literatures on emergent coordination and organizational learning, as we highlight the role of deliberate learning in developing coordination, and how different learning styles link together to create learning sequences.
apa_citation: Jones, M., & Schou, P. K. (2023). Structuring the start-up: How coordination emerges in start-ups through learning sequencing. *Academy of Management Journal, 66*(3), 859–893. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0149

title: Gaining Organizational Adoption: Strategically Pacing the Position of Digital Innovations
methods: Two-year ethnographic field study (inductive, grounded process research) of 54 firms in a digital health accelerator; data include participant observation, 150 interviews, and firm-level documents, coded temporally to induct a process model.
industry: Digital health (care management, diagnostics, patient education, treatment, wellness)
units: Entrepreneurial firms participating in a digital health accelerator (pseudonym 'Cure')
n_sample: 54 entrepreneurial firms (58 new use cases)
authors: Karp, Rebecca
theories: disruptive innovation theory; effectuation / customer-led innovation; jurisdiction and occupational expertise; literature on pacing in entrepreneurial action
keywords: digital innovations; disruptive innovation; embedded approach; entrepreneurship; market-centric approach; new use cases; organizational adoption; strategic pacing
research_question: Given that adoption decisions inside customer organizations involve a plurality of members with divergent views rather than a single executive, how do entrepreneurial firms gain organizational adoption for their (often disruptive) digital innovations?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: "new use cases" (extensions that expand an innovation's relevance); the single-versus-multiparty difference in organizational adoption; the tension between revealing value-adding disruptive functionality and threatening members' expertise, roles, and jurisdiction.
- Two approaches: an *embedded approach* (deep, on-site engagement with customers to co-develop use cases) versus a *market-centric approach* (scanning competitors and the market, demonstrating expertise, magnifying claims, comprehensively rolling out functionality).
- Practices of strategic pacing (embedded firms only): (i) concealing disruptive functionality, (ii) restraining claims that an innovation displaces work, and (iii) shifting the velocity of deployment based on impact to members' expertise.
- Triggering conditions: deep engagement surfaces how an innovation threatens members' jurisdiction, prompting firms to withhold or slow disruptive elements; threats from *outside* a focal business unit require concealing functionality, whereas threats from *within* a unit can be managed by restraining claims alone.
- Emergent outcome: firms using the embedded approach with strategic pacing generally gained adoption (paid pilots); market-centric firms sparked executive interest but generally failed to gain adoption.

Across 54 firms, identifying unmet customer needs was necessary but insufficient for adoption. The embedded approach gave entrepreneurs contextual insight into how diverse organizational members would receive an innovation, enabling them to strategically pace deployment—temporarily concealing disruptive, value-adding features and restraining claims that the innovation would automate or replace members' work—so as to appease audiences whose expertise or jurisdiction the innovation threatened. The digital nature of the innovations made this selective, reversible "switching on and off" of functionality feasible.
theoretical_contribution: The core contribution is a theoretically grounded process model explaining how entrepreneurial firms gain organizational adoption for digital innovations through strategic pacing, extending research that typically picks up after the adoption decision or assumes adoption is made by a few individuals. It reconceives pacing as a *strategic* tool (slowing the introduction of disruptive elements to manage internal stakeholders) rather than only a reflective device for managing market uncertainty, and shows that threats to members' expertise and jurisdiction from outside versus inside a focal business unit call for different tactics (concealing functionality vs. positioning alone).
practical_implication: Entrepreneurs seeking organizational adoption should engage deeply with customers not merely to identify unmet needs but to gain contextual awareness of how diverse organizational members will react, and should strategically pace deployment—building disruptive functionality robustly but withholding or slowly releasing it, and positioning innovations as complements or aids—until affected members can demonstrate expertise in new ways.
limitations: The study examines digital health and does not test whether the observations generalize to other contexts (e.g., financial services or fields further afield), nor whether strategic pacing works for non-digital innovations whose features cannot be flexibly turned off. It also does not address the long-term effects of an embedded process and strategic pacing.
future_research: Future work could examine how these dynamics transition over time as firms gain scale or market dominance and may shift from an embedded to a market-centric process, whether an embedded process and strategic pacing enable other forms of entrepreneurial growth such as funding, and how entrepreneurs foster adoption of disruptive innovations in other regulatory and industry settings.
abstract: Scholars have long suggested that, to foster adoption for their innovations, entrepreneurs should engage with customers to better understand their unmet needs. Yet, customers frequently reside in organizations, and organizational members may not be aligned in their views regarding the benefits an innovation offers. Decision-making within organizations is rarely under the purview of a single executive and various members can wield influence. Given this plurality, how do entrepreneurial firms gain organizational adoption for their innovations? I explore how 54 entrepreneurial firms, all participating in a digital health accelerator, attempted to gain organizational adoption for their innovations. I show that, when entrepreneurs pursued an embedded process, they developed ways to expand the relevance of their innovations for new customers through new use cases, but strategically paced and withheld disruptive aspects of their innovations to appease different audiences within customer organizations. Firms that leveraged a market-centric process also expanded the relevance of their innovations. Despite sparking the interest of executives, firms using a market-centric approach did not gain adoption. By unpacking the process by which some entrepreneurs discovered how to strategically pace the deployment of their digital innovations, I contribute a mechanism that explains how digital innovations gain organizational adoption.
apa_citation: Karp, R. (2023). Gaining organizational adoption: Strategically pacing the position of digital innovations. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(3), 773–796. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0548

title: Opening Up AMJ’s Research Methods Repertoire
methods: Editorial essay; the five methods experts each selected a research approach deserving more attention, illustrating it with published examples, resources, and evaluation criteria.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: na
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Langley, Ann; Bell, Emma; Bliese, Paul; LeBaron, Curtis; Gruber, Marc
theories: performativity of methods; process and temporality in organization studies
keywords: microethnography; multilevel theorizing; researching forward; visual methods
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: AMJ's mission welcomes all empirical methods, yet some valuable methodologies remain underrepresented in its pages; the 23rd editorial team signals openness by adding methods experts and inviting innovative submissions.
- Visual methods: moving beyond words and numbers to study visible, material features of organization through images, video, object interviewing, photo-elicitation, semiotics, and quantitative content analysis.
- Microethnography ("video ethnography"): capturing meaning-making in situ through detailed examination of "small" activities, drawing on conversation analysis, context analysis, and participant observation.
- Incorporating time into quantitative analyses: moving from treating time as a control toward trajectory- and growth-model thinking that theorizes change over time.
- Multilevel theorizing: routinely testing and reporting when within-level effects differ from between-level (emergent or incremental) effects.
- Researching forward: engaged, real-time, forward-looking inquiry (field experiments, action research, collaborative and inside-out research) that helps practitioners "live forward" rather than only explaining the past.

These five illustrations are a "selected sampling of possibilities." Underpinning them is a performative view of method: what we know cannot be separated from how we come to know it, so widening the variety of methods we draw on enriches the knowledge and theory the field can generate.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial advances a performative understanding of research methods—rejecting the conventional assumption that method choice merely flows from a fixed ontological and epistemological stance, and instead positing that methods help constitute reality. On this view, broadening AMJ's methods repertoire is itself a route to richer organizational knowledge and theory.
practical_implication: Authors are encouraged to submit work drawing on less-established methods, with assurance that the editorial team will recruit qualified reviewers to judge it on its merits. Researchers using participative, action-research, or inside-out approaches are asked to be transparent about their relationships with research sites and how those relationships may shape findings.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editors invite greater use of visual analysis (qualitative and quantitative), microethnographic study of micro-level interaction, trajectory-based and emergent-effect modeling that exploits existing nested data, and forward-looking engaged methods such as field experiments and collaborative inquiry, hoping to see these better represented in AMJ.

title: Betwixt and Between: National and Organizational Identification of Host Country Managers Working in MNE Subsidiaries
methods: Cross-sectional single-source survey of host country managers in domestic firms vs. MNE subsidiaries in Korea. Latent profile analysis (LPA) classifies respondents into four dual-identity profiles; three-step BCH covariate analysis tests profile-membership hypotheses (H1-H2), supplemented by propensity score matching; treatment effect regression addresses nonrandom assignment for continuous-DV hypotheses (H3-H6); Heckman selection regression tests the interaction effect on acculturative stress (H7). Cluster-correlated robust variance estimates account for nonindependence within firms.
industry: Large multinational and domestic firms across 21 Korean Standard Industrial Classification industries (manufacturing as reference category)
country: Republic of Korea (South Korea); MNE subsidiaries headquartered in 11 parent countries
units: Individual managers (Korean nationals) nested in firms
n_sample: 843 managers (219 in 19 domestic firms; 624 in 60 MNE subsidiaries); H7 model uses 624 MNE-subsidiary managers
authors: Lee, Khan-Pyo; Kim, Minyoung; You, Chi-Yeon
theories: acculturation profile model (Berry, 1997; Berry et al., 2006); liability of foreignness (Zaheer, 1995); social identity theory (Tajfel & Turner, 1986; Hogg, 2016; Hornsey, 2008)
keywords: MNE subsidiaries; acculturative stress; dual identities; host country managers; identity conflict; liability of foreignness; national identification; organizational identification
research_question: How do the dual identity patterns of host country managers working in foreign MNE subsidiaries—in terms of the interrelationship and relative strength of their national and organizational identities—differ from those of domestic firm managers, and how do these distinctive patterns relate to the identity-related (acculturative) stress they experience within the foreign-owned firm?
mechanism_summary: The paper argues that because host country managers' national and organizational identities cross-cut each other (whereas domestic managers' identities are nested), foreign ownership and control heighten the salience of national identity and pose distinctiveness/value threats to it. These threats promote subgroup (national) identification and inhibit superordinate (organizational) identification, making the national profile more likely and the integrated/organizational profiles less likely than for domestic managers. Strong national plus weak organizational identification then exposes managers to greater acculturative stress; high organizational identification neutralizes the positive national-identification–stress link, while more positive perceived career prospects attenuate the foreignness effects.
theoretical_contribution: The study contributes to international management in three ways: it uses a counterfactual framework to show how host country managers' dual-identity patterns differ from domestic managers' (rather than studying host country managers in isolation); it identifies an internal, human-resource source of the liability of foreignness (low organizational identification and acculturative stress) overlooked by prior work focused on external-environment sources; and it shows that host country managers' persistent strong national identity implies the MNE is a multicultural "mosaic" rather than a "melting pot," so imposing a common corporate identity may backfire.
practical_implication: Rather than substituting host country managers with parent country nationals, MNEs should adopt measures that raise host country managers' organizational identification and reduce acculturative stress—notably well-developed global talent-management programs that provide clear, credible career development paths across the worldwide organization. A dual identity approach that acknowledges distinct national affiliate identities while cultivating a common ingroup identity is recommended over a "one-company" global identity approach.
limitations: The theoretical premise that nationality is a subjectively important identity may not hold where perceived "nation-ness" is low or in less-developed host countries, so the main thesis may not generalize beyond a relatively developed, collectivist setting like Korea. The single-source, cross-sectional data likely deflate the interaction effect (common method variance) and make it difficult to verify the causal direction between identification patterns and acculturative stress.
future_research: Future work should explore how perceived nation-ness and sociocultural divides shape attitudes toward foreignness, and test whether the main thesis holds in more individualistic and in considerably less-developed host countries. Longitudinal designs collecting identification and acculturative-stress data at different time points are needed to address common method variance and establish causality in MNE subsidiary contexts.
abstract: This study investigates the patterns of dual identities of host country managers working in multinational enterprise (MNE) subsidiaries and how these patterns relate to their feelings of identity-related stress within the foreign-owned firms. Drawing on the acculturation literature, we classify the patterns of dual identities based on membership in nation and organization into four identity profiles: (a) diffuse (weak national and organizational identification), (b) national (strong national and weak organizational identification), (c) organizational (weak national and strong organizational identification), and (d) integrated (strong national and organizational identification). We submit that perceived conflict between the dual identities renders MNEs' host country managers less likely to present the integrated and the organizational identity profiles and more likely to present the national identity profile than domestic firm managers. MNEs' host country managers also identify more strongly with their nation and less strongly with their organization than domestic firm managers do. The combination of strong national identification and weak organizational identification among MNEs' host country managers exposes them to greater acculturative stress within foreign organizations. The results of an analysis of a sample of 843 Korean managers working in 19 Korean firms and 60 MNE subsidiaries in Korea corroborate our hypotheses.
apa_citation: Lee, K.-P., Kim, M., & You, C.-Y. (2023). Betwixt and between: National and organizational identification of host country managers working in MNE subsidiaries. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(3), 744–772. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1810

title: On the Status Shocks of Tournament Rituals: How Ritual Enactment Affects Productivity, Input Provision, and Performance
methods: Quantitative causal-inference design using difference-in-differences (DID) with player, season, player-by-season, and game fixed effects, plus a regression discontinuity design (RDD) and coarsened exact matching as robustness checks; standard errors clustered by player-by-season.
industry: Professional sports (men's professional basketball, NBA)
country: United States (NBA; league also includes one Canadian franchise)
units: Player-game observations; treatment = All-Star Game participants, control = 'Almost All-Stars'
n_sample: 33,507 game-level observations from 1,676 player-seasons (734 treatment, 942 control)
authors: Maoret, Massimo; Marchesini, Giacomo; Ertug, Gokhan
theories: interaction ritual theory (Collins, 2004); status / signaling theory
keywords: emotional energy; ritual enactment; status shocks; tournament rituals
research_question: Beyond the well-established informational "signaling" pathway, is there another process through which a status shock causally affects winners' subsequent performance? The authors ask whether participating in the ritualistic conferment of a status-boosting award ("ritual enactment") increases tournament winners' productivity and the inputs they receive, and how durable that effect is.
mechanism_summary: Drawing on Collins's (2004) interaction ritual theory, the authors argue that physically enacting a prestige-conferring ritual charges winners with emotional energy, which raises their drive to convert inputs into outputs (productivity) and, by creating an EE gap relative to non-participating input providers, attracts greater deference and resources (more touches/usage) from teammates. Because EE is transitory, the performance gain decays over subsequent games. The NBA setting separates ritual enactment from signaling because the All-Star announcement and the game occur weeks apart and coaches/teammates already have little uncertainty about player quality.
theoretical_contribution: The paper's primary contribution is introducing "ritual enactment" as a novel, symbolic/social process—distinct from informational signaling—through which status shocks causally affect performance. It shows status shocks affect not just audiences' perception of output quality but the production of output itself (input acquisition and the transformation of inputs into outputs), and it distinguishes status "shocks" from self-reinforcing status "stocks," demonstrating that the ritual-enactment advantage is of limited duration.
practical_implication: Managers can use ceremonial, prestige-conferring events (not merely announcing winners or paying incentives) to generate motivational and performative boosts among employees, and could organize smaller-scale tournament-ritual-like events within teams or organizations. Because such events may reallocate resources via shifting deference, managers should monitor whether the resulting distribution stays fair and effective for organizational goals.
limitations: The authors cannot measure emotional energy directly, so EE is inferred rather than observed (they provide indirect support via in-ritual exposure). Because the chosen input providers (coaches and teammates) have low uncertainty about player quality, the design isolates ritual enactment but precludes a direct comparison of the magnitude and duration of signaling versus ritual enactment within the same setting.
future_research: Future work could compare the relative magnitude and duration of signaling versus ritual enactment in settings where audiences hold quality uncertainty, examine how formal/informal social structures (e.g., hierarchy, ideology) condition ritual-enactment effects, and study the interaction between status stocks and status shocks. Scholars could also investigate which ritual characteristics (formal vs. informal, public vs. private, field-level vs. organizational) and individual differences amplify or dampen the effect, and whether the framework extends to rituals routinely enacted within organizations.
abstract: We propose a novel process through which status shocks may enhance performance. Specifically, we theorize that, when status shocks include a ritualistic conferment of social prestige—such as in the case of “tournament rituals”—participating in that ritual enactment may increase tournament winners’ productivity and improve the inputs they receive, thereby improving their overall performance. We also consider the duration of that performance improvement, finding a decay that is consistent with our theorized mechanisms that are based on emotional energy. Our study shows that status shocks may carry not only informational value, as signals of quality, but also symbolic and social value that change the behavior of individuals who receive these shocks and of the input providers with whom they interact. We employ difference-in-differences and regression discontinuity designs on game-level data from the National Basketball Association to provide causal evidence for our hypotheses.
apa_citation: Maoret, M., Marchesini, G., & Ertug, G. (2023). On the status shocks of tournament rituals: How ritual enactment affects productivity, input provision, and performance. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(3), 926–952. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0585

title: I Don’t Need a Degree, I’ve Got Abs: Influencer Warmth and Competence, Communication Mode, and Stakeholder Engagement on Social Media
methods: Quantitative observational study; six months of scraped Instagram data on 488 fitness/nutrition influencers (2,928 influencer-month observations) analyzed with Hausman–Taylor random effects regression and Wald tests comparing standardized coefficients; LIWC content analysis and rater-coded image classification for cue measures.
industry: Fitness and nutrition industry influencers (experience-good services) on Instagram
units: Social media entrepreneurs (influencers); influencer-month observations
n_sample: 488 influencers; 2,928 influencer-month observations
authors: Roccapriore, Ashley Y.; Pollock, Timothy G.
theories: customer engagement (van Doorn et al., 2010); multimodal communication / semiotics (Messaris, 1997; Meyer et al., 2018); warmth and competence (Fiske, Cuddy, & Glick, 2007; Abele & Wojciszke, 2014)
keywords: multimodal communication; social judgments; social media influencers; stakeholder engagement; warmth and competence
research_question: (a) Do images and words vary in their relative influence on stimulating different levels of stakeholder engagement, and (b) do warmth and competence cues vary in their relative influence on these outcomes? The paper asks how communication mode and content shape behaviors that differ in the cognitive effort they require.
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Post/reply/comment word counts, posting activity, profile age, traditional credentials, rater-coded attractiveness and fitness, video and deleted posts, gender, race

Because images are processed rapidly and unmediated and offer heuristic "proof" (iconicity, indexicality), they exert more influence on the low-effort decision to follow, while words' syntactic determinacy lets them convey the specific arguments and trust cues needed for the higher-effort decision to interact. Parallel logic applies to content: competence cues credibly signal underlying ability and matter most for following an experience-good provider, whereas warmth cues build the liking, trust, and authenticity that drive cognitively effortful positive interaction. Statistical-significance patterns supported H1b and H2a; the relative effect-size hypotheses (H1a, H2b) were not significant due to wide confidence intervals.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances multimodal communication research by separating communication mode from content—conflated in prior work—and showing that a mode's or cue's effectiveness depends on the cognitive effort the targeted engagement behavior requires. It extends the "Big Two" social-judgment literature by demonstrating that the long-assumed primacy of warmth over competence is conditional: competence cues dominate low-effort engagement, warmth cues dominate high-effort engagement. It also extends entrepreneur–stakeholder engagement research from one-to-one dyads to one-to-many online interactions.
practical_implication: Influencers and entrepreneurs should use images and videos demonstrating competence to grow their following, and deploy both image- and word-based warmth cues to stimulate higher-level engagement such as positive interactions. They should avoid self-focused rhetoric and general cheerleading, instead using other-directed language; posts need not be long, but regular, attentive interaction is crucial for cultivating online relationships and monetization opportunities.
limitations: Findings may not generalize beyond the visual, business-to-consumer fitness and nutrition context, and may differ in B2B settings or where visual evidence is less dramatic. Because data covered a fixed window with small monthly variation in followership and predictors, the authors could not compare within-influencer changes over time. Some images contain words, and Instagram's proprietary algorithm is an unobserved factor only partially addressed by the Hausman–Taylor design.
future_research: Longitudinal designs tracking entrepreneurs from founding onward could reveal how engagement processes evolve as followerships grow. More granular image-based measures and machine-learning approaches could better capture mixed image-text content. Scholars should also examine how image-based competence cues affect investor decisions in computer-mediated settings such as crowdfunding.
abstract: In this study we consider whether (a) image- and word-based communication modes and (b) warmth and competence cues vary in their relative influence on different levels of stakeholder engagement on social media. Specifically, we explore social media fitness influencers’ abilities to attract followers and get followers to positively interact with them via posts and comments. We theorize that differences in the ways each communication mode is processed, and differences in how competence and warmth cues are perceived, will lead to different relative effects on lower- and higher-engagement behaviors. Using the social media platform Instagram, we followed 488 social media entrepreneurs in the fitness and nutrition industry for six months, and found that images have a positive relationship with less cognitively effortful engagement (following) whereas words do not have a significant relationship, and words have a stronger relationship than images with more cognitively effortful engagement (positive interactions). We also found that competence cues have a stronger positive relationship than warmth cues with the number of followers, and warmth cues have a positive relationship with positive interactions, whereas competence cues do not. Our findings have implications for research on multimodal communication, social judgments, and entrepreneur–stakeholder engagement.
apa_citation: Roccapriore, A. Y., & Pollock, T. G. (2023). I don’t need a degree, I’ve got abs: Influencer warmth and competence, communication mode, and stakeholder engagement on social media. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(3), 979–1006. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1546

title: Creative Jolts: Exploring How Entrepreneurs Let Go of Ideas During Creative Revision
methods: Inductive multiple-case study (Eisenhardt, 1989); longitudinal one-year tracking of 12 early-stage entrepreneurs via 85 semi-structured personal interviews, 42 phone interviews, and 376 mobile-app entries; experience-sampling-inspired data collection; multistep grounded analysis with temporal narratives, LIWC/PANAS-X affect coding, and cross-case comparison.
industry: Early-stage ventures across diverse domains (online travel, fitness, education, fundraising, food delivery, etc.) based in London coworking spaces
time_period: Approximately one year of observation per venture (six to 13 months); fieldwork spanning 2016–2017
units: Early-stage entrepreneurs (founders/cofounders who were primary inventors of the venture idea)
n_sample: 12 entrepreneurs; six experienced creative jolts, yielding seven jolt episodes analyzed
authors: Toivonen, Tuukka; Idoko, Onyaglanu; Jha, Harsh Kumar; Harvey, Sarah
theories: assumptive worlds (Parkes, 1975; Janoff-Bulman, 1989); idea journey / creative revision (Perry-Smith & Mannucci, 2017); jolts and shocks / unfolding model (Lee & Mitchell, 1994); sensemaking (Weick, 1993)
keywords: assumptive worlds; autonomous creators; creative jolt; creative revision; entrepreneurs; idea journey; letting go of ideas; trigger feedback
research_question: How do creators let go of ideas during creative revision (in order to allow new ideas to emerge and develop)? The paper studies how autonomous creators—early-stage entrepreneurs—navigate the disruptive episodes through which they relinquish ideas.
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: *Trigger feedback* — negative, subtractive, core-directed feedback from a credible source (trusted/high-status mentor) that is perceived as threatening the idea or the venture's core mission.
- Key constructs / tensions: the *creative jolt* (an episode of cognitive and emotional upheaval); *assumptive worlds* that must be broken down before letting go; the persistency paradox between flexible revision and rigid commitment.
- Process / phases: (1) *existential crisis* — shattered assumptions plus a heightened emotional state, branching into *existential dread* (threat to idea) or *existential awakening* (threat to mission); (2) *reappraisal* — sensemaking via *reaching out* (diversifying through new networks) or *reaching in* (reflecting on core values with close peers); (3) *resolution* — discarding core aspects and reorienting via *recreation* (a fundamentally new idea) or *restoration* (return to the original mission).
- Emergent outcomes: a dramatic, generative shift onto a new idea trajectory, accompanied by emotional repair.

Creative jolts loosen creators' attachment to ideas by breaking down the assumptive worlds underpinning them, opening a "gap in meaning" that motivates a sensemaking search through which a new idea emerges. Negative affect (dread) and emotional ambivalence (awakening) supply the motivation, while reaching out and reaching in supply the cognitive raw material for reconstruction—so that the remains of the old idea define the opening into which the new one grows.
theoretical_contribution: The paper conceptualizes the previously unexamined *destructive* counterpart of idea generation—how letting go of ideas unfolds through creative revision—and introduces the creative jolt as a distinct, affectively charged, multi-phase episode that pushes ideas onto radically new trajectories. It shifts research attention from how ideas are generated and selected toward the processes by which "openings" for new ideas are constructed, arguing that ideas alone are not enough and that commitment/rigidity can paradoxically enable radical change once shaken.
practical_implication: Autonomous creators and the mentors, accelerators, and coworking communities supporting them should recognize that highly critical, core-directed feedback can trigger an agonizing but generative period rather than mere failure. Supporting creators through the emotional upheaval—via opportunities to reach out to diverse networks and reach in to core values—can help them let go of unworkable ideas and reorient productively.
limitations: The analysis deliberately focused on cases where creators let go of ideas, so it cannot establish whether letting go improved outcomes or whether persistence would have succeeded. The study is confined to autonomous creators (individual founders or primary cofounders), so collective- or team-level jolt dynamics could not be elaborated. Findings rely partly on retrospective interviewing, mitigated but not eliminated by the longitudinal, near-real-time design.
future_research: Future work should map creators' emotional journeys more completely and examine how upheaval is prompted across different creators and contexts (e.g., organizational settings where support is withdrawn after a project is greenlit). Scholars could probe the nature of assumptive worlds and how creators emotionally prepare for their destruction, whether jolts can arise from positive triggers, and how jolts unfold at the team or collective level.
abstract: Creative revision involves sharing nascent ideas and soliciting feedback. This process has been portrayed as providing instrumental feedback that enables autonomous creators to shape and progress their ideas in a mostly positive, developmental way. However, creative revision also entails destruction—letting go of some ideas to make way for the new. Research has suggested that abandoning some aspects of ideas will be affectively challenging for creators, but has yet to elaborate how they navigate that challenge. In a longitudinal qualitative study of entrepreneurial ventures over a one-year period, we uncovered a novel phenomenon: the creative jolt—an episode of cognitive and emotional upheaval through which creators let go of their original ideas and allowed new ones to emerge, so that their ideas shifted in dramatic ways. We elaborate the processes through which creative jolts shape the trajectories of creative ideas. Our study captures the rollercoaster of heightened affect and cognitive disorientation experienced by autonomous creators as they try to bring nascent ideas to life.
apa_citation: Toivonen, T., Idoko, O., Jha, H. K., & Harvey, S. (2023). Creative jolts: Exploring how entrepreneurs let go of ideas during creative revision. *Academy of Management Journal, 66*(3), 829–858. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1054
