title: Rekindling the Fire and Stoking the Flames: How and When Workplace Interpersonal Capitalization Facilitates Pride and Knowledge Sharing at Work
methods: Two studies. Study 1: source- and time-separated two-wave field survey with round-robin reports, analyzed via multilevel path analysis (Mplus 8.3, MLR estimator, parametric bootstrapping). Study 2: 10-day experience sampling field experiment with a daily randomized capitalization manipulation, analyzed via multilevel path analysis with random slopes.
industry: Study 1: a privately owned mechanical-gear manufacturing company; Study 2: working professionals across diverse industries and occupations (e.g., nurse, chef, pharmacist, HR advisor)
time_period: Study 1: two survey waves one month apart; Study 2: two daily surveys across 10 consecutive workdays
units: Individual employees (disclosers)
n_sample: Study 1: 448 focal disclosers matched to 1,989 round-robin knowledge-sharing reports across 44 teams; Study 2: 105 participants providing 787 paired daily observations
authors: Watkins, Trevor; Kleshinski, Catherine E.; Longmire, Natalie H.; He, Wei
theories: attribution theory; social-functionalist model of emotions; theory on capitalization
keywords: effort attribution; knowledge sharing; perceived coworker responsiveness; pride; social-functionalist model of emotions; workplace interpersonal capitalization
research_question: How and when does workplace interpersonal capitalization (sharing personal work-related positive events with coworkers) serve the instrumental function of spurring disclosers' knowledge sharing? The paper asks whether capitalization is not only socioemotional but also instrumental, with pride as the linking mechanism, effort attribution as a first-stage boundary condition, and perceived coworker responsiveness as a second-stage boundary condition.
mechanism_summary: Integrating capitalization theory with the social-functionalist model of emotions, the authors argue that disclosing a positive work event "rekindles the fire" of past achievement, drawing attention to the self and activating self-enhancement that elicits pride—but only when the event is attributed to one's own effort, a marker of work-goal progress. Pride in turn motivates disclosers to "stoke the flames" by sharing knowledge, a prototypical prestige-seeking behavior through which individuals convert valued achievements into higher social status. This prestige-seeking pathway holds only when disclosers perceive coworkers as responsive, because responsiveness signals that knowledge sharing will be rewarded with status. The full model is a two-stage moderated mediation in which the indirect effect is strongest when both effort attribution and responsiveness are high.
theoretical_contribution: The paper broadens the concept of capitalization by theorizing its instrumental potential, challenging the long-standing assumption that capitalization is a purely socioemotional process and that the characteristics of the disclosed event are inconsequential. By positioning effort attribution as a boundary condition, it shows that only effort-attributed events activate the instrumental process. It also contributes to the social-functionalist model of emotions by qualifying the assumed evolutionary, universal pride–knowledge sharing link with the boundary condition of responsiveness, demonstrating that the link is not as unbounded as previously theorized.
practical_implication: Because sharing good news at work can indirectly promote knowledge sharing, organizational leaders—especially in knowledge-intensive and innovation-oriented organizations—should encourage employees to share positive events (e.g., in meetings or via workspace design) and coach employees to respond responsively when they are targets of capitalization. Leaders should be cautious, however, since capitalization can backfire through unfavorable social comparison or undermining, and overly controlling reactions (e.g., financial rewards) can reduce intrinsic motivation.
limitations: Study 1 is correlational and cannot fully establish causality; it is set in a single manufacturing organization in China, limiting generalizability, and cannot fully assess whether a given team member was both a capitalization and a knowledge-sharing target. The model does not explain why or when disclosers come to perceive responsiveness in coworkers, and the authors did not directly theorize the instrumental downsides of workplace interpersonal capitalization.
future_research: Future work could examine other characteristics of the disclosed positive event beyond effort attribution, explore the "half-life" of capitalization-elicited pride over time, develop theory on the antecedents of perceived responsiveness, and test whether the instrumental capitalization process generalizes to non-work contexts such as romantic relationships, religious groups, schools, or families. Researchers are also encouraged to study the potential costs and net effects of sharing good news at work.
abstract: Sharing positive events with others is a phenomenon referred to as “capitalization.” Extant theory on capitalization considers the process of disclosing positive events as socioemotional in nature. However, individuals capitalize in the context of workplace relationships that serve instrumental functions, pointing to the possibility that the process of capitalization may at times be instrumental as well. In this paper, we integrate theory on capitalization with the social-functionalist model of emotions to develop a model that explains how workplace interpersonal capitalization serves the instrumental function of spurring knowledge sharing. We identify pride as a linking mechanism between workplace interpersonal capitalization and knowledge sharing, and we argue that experiencing pride is contingent on employees disclosing positive events that they attribute to their own efforts. We also identify perceived coworker responsiveness as a boundary condition qualifying the association between pride and knowledge sharing. A sourceand time-separated field study and an experience sampling field experiment with a daily intervention provide support for our hypothesized model. Overall, our work broadens the concept of capitalization and contributes to the social-functionalist model of emotions.
apa_citation: Watkins, T., Kleshinski, C. E., Longmire, N. H., & He, W. (2023). Rekindling the fire and stoking the flames: How and when workplace interpersonal capitalization facilitates pride and knowledge sharing at work. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(3), 953–978. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1022

title: “Distinctive from What? And for Whom?” Deep Learning-Based Product Distinctiveness, Social Structure, and Third-Party Certifications
methods: Longitudinal panel of artist-years; convolutional neural network (CaffeNet/AlexNet) extracts 4,096-dimensional feature vectors of artworks, with cosine distance to three reference points (prior competitors, current competitors, past self); negative binomial regression (NBREG, Stata MP 15.1) with robust standard errors, artist and year fixed effects, one-year lags, and supply-/demand-side status moderators.
industry: Fine art (modern-art painting and drawing); third-party certification via galleries and salons
country: Europe (works/exhibitions catalogued in the Database of Modern Exhibitions)
units: Artists (artist-year observations); 12,193 artworks analyzed
n_sample: 153 artists
authors: Banerjee, Mitali; Cole, Benjamin M.; Ingram, Paul
theories: optimal distinctiveness theory (Deephouse, 1999; Zhao et al., 2017); reference-point and categorization perspectives (Bourdieu, 1983; Cattani et al., 2014); social structure / status theory (Zuckerman, 2017; Podolny, 1993)
keywords: deep learning; optimal distinctiveness; product distinctiveness; reference points; social structure; supply-side and demand-side status; third-party certifications
research_question: How do a producer's distinctiveness and its position in social structure jointly shape the third-party certifications it receives? The paper centers on two questions: distinctive compared to what reference point (prior competitors, current competitors, or one's own past), and for whom — i.e., how supply-side (peer) and demand-side (audience) status moderate the rewards to distinctiveness.
mechanism_summary: The paper theorizes that distinctiveness from each reference point is rewarded by certifiers up to a point, but a producer's autonomy to differentiate is constrained by two "pulls" of social structure. Supply-side peer pressure to conform is non-monotonic: high- and low-status artists are rewarded for distinctiveness from current competitors, while middle-status artists are penalized (a U-shaped pattern). Demand-side market status licenses distinctiveness from one's own past yet penalizes distinctiveness from current competitors, because high-status producers risk alienating the peers and audiences on whom their legitimacy depends.
theoretical_contribution: The study refines optimal-distinctiveness and status research by specifying which reference point matters and for whom, and by being the first (to the authors' knowledge) to empirically test Zuckerman's (2017) distinction between supply-side and demand-side conformity pressures. It also advances method and theory in aesthetic/sensorial markets by using a deep-learning measure of whole-product distinctiveness that does not rely on predefined categories, making previously unfalsifiable arguments (e.g., Bourdieu, 1983) empirically testable.
practical_implication: Innovators competing for certifications should choose their referent for differentiation strategically given their status: high-market-status producers are better off differentiating from their own past work, while those without such status (e.g., new entrants) benefit more from differentiating from current competitors, and middle-status peers are better off converging closer to their contemporaries.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The authors note the framework and deep-learning approach can be extended beyond fine art to other visually driven industries (fashion, architecture, advertising) and, via tools for sound and text, to music, film, and publishing; distance can also be computed to any theoretically interesting reference point (e.g., a particular movement or artist), and image- and text-recognition techniques could be combined to study how distinctiveness across modalities jointly affects certification.
abstract: How do producers’ distinctiveness and social structure influence third-party certifications? We argue that producers compete against prior and current competitors, and against their past selves. In the context of 153 artists active during a key period of the emergence of modern art (1905–1916), we utilize a convolutional neural network used in computer vision to extract feature vectors of artworks, and measure quantitative distance of these artists’ works from canonical reference points. We find that artists are rewarded for distinctiveness from prior and current competitors and their past selves (up to a point). However, artists’ autonomy to differentiate themselves depends on their position in the social structure, which we divide into supply-side artist-to-artist networks, and demand-side artist-to-gallerist networks. Artists with high or low supply-side status receive higher rewards for distinctiveness from current competitors than do artists with middle supply-side status. Artists with higher demand-side status receive higher rewards for distinctiveness from their own past, but lower rewards for distinctiveness from current competitors. These results show that peers strive to constrain each other to conform to positions of gravity within product space, and that market audiences deploy either higher or lower constraints on a producer’s identity depending on the reference point.
apa_citation: Banerjee, M., Cole, B. M., & Ingram, P. (2023). “Distinctive from what? And for whom?” Deep learning-based product distinctiveness, social structure, and third-party certifications. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(4), 1016–1041. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0175

title: A Spillover Model of Dreams and Work Behavior: How Dream Meaning Ascription Promotes Awe and Employee Resilience
methods: Three studies: (1) a morning-of field study of business-school alumni; (2) a single-day morning-afternoon study; (3) a two-week experience sampling study. Hypotheses tested via structural equation / path analysis (Studies 1-2) and multilevel path models in Mplus 8.4 with group- and grand-mean centering and bootstrapped indirect effects (Study 3).
industry: Full-time employees across occupations (no single focal industry); Study 1 used business-school alumni
time_period: Single-week (Study 1), five-week recruitment (Study 2), and two-week daily (Study 3) windows; year not reported in paper
units: Working individuals (employees / business-school alumni)
n_sample: Study 1: 119 alumni; Study 2: 63 employees; Study 3: 411 employees retained for multilevel analysis
authors: Belinda, Casher D.; Christian, Michael S.
theories: appraisal theories of emotion; appraisal-tendency theory; dream-spillover model
keywords: appraisal-tendency theory; awe; dream meaning ascription; dreams; epistemic curiosity; goal progress; resilience; sleep
research_question: How and for whom do dreams spill over to shape daily work behavior? Specifically, the authors ask how ascribing meaning to recalled dreams elicits waking awe that promotes employee resilience and goal progress throughout the workday, and whether trait epistemic curiosity moderates the awe-resilience link.
mechanism_summary: Integrating research on dream meaning ascription with appraisal theories of emotion, the authors argue that forming the belief that a dream carries meaning triggers core awe appraisals—perceptions of vastness and a need for cognitive accommodation—giving rise to awe. Through an appraisal-tendency mechanism, awe induces a "small-self" frame that makes subsequent work stressors feel less threatening, increasing resilience and downstream goal progress. Because awe requires expanding one's existing ways of knowing, its benefits accrue mainly to employees high in epistemic curiosity.
theoretical_contribution: The paper augments the organizational sleep literature by delineating a psychological (rather than purely biophysiological) pathway—dream meaning ascription—through which sleep spills over into waking behavior. It advances research on emotions by positioning awe as a linchpin between dreams and behavior, and on resilience by showing that resilience can result from emotions occurring prior to stressful encounters, challenging the directional assumption of transactional appraisal theories that appraisal precedes emotion.
practical_implication: Because roughly 40% of working people recall dreams on a given morning and tend to make sense of them, managers and employees benefit from treating sleep as a meaning-laden process. Low-effort practices such as keeping a dream journal or imagery-rehearsal techniques can elicit meaningful dreams and awe; organizations can also cultivate awe directly (e.g., biophilic job design, leadership storytelling).
limitations: Convenience sampling in the time-separated studies (Studies 2-3) may have increased attrition, and analyses rely primarily on self-report data. The time-separated designs lacked a third measurement period, and dream meaning ascription was measured with single items; focusing on dream-recall days created potential selection bias addressed through multiple robustness checks.
future_research: Future work could combine biophysiological and dream-spillover perspectives—e.g., examining dreams as a moderator that offsets or exaggerates the effects of sleep deprivation, or parsing other aspects of sleep. Researchers might use probability sampling, objective and other-report outcome measures, and additional measurement periods, and examine how work outcomes vary solely as a function of dream recall.
abstract: Sleep and employee behavior are linked. While the dominant explanations for this link involve physiology and cognitive resources, we offer a different perspective. We suggest that dreams, or psychological experiences during sleep, spill over to affect employee behavior. This spillover effect, we argue, results from the interplay between dreaming, meaning creation, and emotion. Taking a morning-of perspective, we theorize that recalling and ascribing meaning to dream experiences elicits awe—an epistemic emotion produced by appraisals of vastness and a need for cognitive accommodation. In turn, because awe reduces individuals’ focus on themselves and their concerns, we argue that experiencing awe upon awakening increases employee resilience, and ultimately goal progress, throughout the workday. However, because awe entails a felt need to expand one’s existing ways of knowing, employees may vary in their receptivity to awe. We therefore argue that the link between awe and resilience hinges on trait epistemic curiosity, or employees’ inherent desire to seek and acquire new knowledge. Across three studies—including a morning-of field study, a single-day morning–afternoon study, and a two-week experience sampling study—we find that ascribing positive meaning to dreams elicits awe, which in turn promotes resilience, and ultimately goal progress, throughout the workday.
apa_citation: Belinda, C. D., & Christian, M. S. (2023). A spillover model of dreams and work behavior: How dream meaning ascription promotes awe and employee resilience. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(4), 1152–1182. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0377

title: Communication Miscalibration: The Price Leaders Pay for Not Sharing Enough
methods: Four preregistered studies (n = 1,812) combining archival, survey, and experimental methods: Study 1a content-codes 2,717 qualitative comments from 360-degree leadership assessments (chi-square); Study 1b surveys a nationally representative U.S. sample (one-sample t-test); Study 2 is a between-subjects experiment with a multicategorical mediation (PROCESS Macro Model 4); Study 3 uses polynomial regression and response-surface analysis with block-variable mediation.
industry: General working population / employed adults across diverse organizational contexts (not industry-specific)
units: Individual employees / followers evaluating their leaders' communication
n_sample: n = 1,812 across four studies (Study 1a: 2,717 comments on 924 leaders; Study 1b: 354; Study 2: 312; Study 3: 222)
authors: Flynn, Francis J.; Lide, Chelsea R.
theories: curse of knowledge; empathic concern / social judgment; prosocial signaling
keywords: communication calibration; downward communication; empathic concern; leadership ability; over-communication; under-communication
research_question: How does the perceived quantity of a leader's downward communication—specifically the (mis)calibration between how much employees perceive and prefer their leader to communicate—shape followers' evaluations of that leader's ability, and why is under-communication penalized more severely than over-communication?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on social judgment and helping-behavior research, the authors argue that employees read communication quantity as a prosocial signal of a leader's empathic concern. Because of the "curse of knowledge," leaders systematically underestimate how much their followers want and are therefore seen as under-communicating more often than over-communicating. Under-communication sends a stronger negative signal—that the leader neither helped nor even engaged the employee—whereas over-communication signals a misguided but well-intentioned effort. Consequently, perceived under-communication lowers perceived empathy, which in turn lowers perceived leadership ability, producing an asymmetric penalty.
theoretical_contribution: The paper shows that the trade-offs of communication miscalibration are asymmetric in leadership evaluations: under-communication "looms larger" than over-communication and is judged as a more significant failing, so not all forms of miscalibration are equal. It documents a robust perceptual bias whereby leaders are seen as under-communicating far more often than over-communicating, and it identifies perceived empathic concern as the psychological mechanism linking communication quantity to leadership evaluations—remedying prior downward-communication research that offered no account of how much communication is "the right amount."
practical_implication: Leaders cannot trust their intuition when calibrating the quantity of downward communication; they should adopt independent mechanisms—soliciting employees' idiosyncratic preferences, establishing routine rather than ad hoc communication patterns, and "pretesting" messages for calibration. In most cases leaders should err toward communicating more, accepting the relatively minor risk of being seen as over-communicating to send a stronger signal of empathy and concern.
limitations: The samples were primarily American, so the findings cannot account for cultural differences (e.g., high-context or high-power-distance cultures) that might shape judgments of communication calibration. The theorizing and measures focus exclusively on leadership evaluations as the outcome, leaving aside other objectives such as supporting employee autonomy, and the studies isolate communication quantity while not modeling the qualitative dimension (content, style, delivery) of communication.
future_research: Future work should examine how the quantitative and qualitative dimensions of leader communication interrelate and which matters more in particular cases, including whether qualitative miscalibration signals incompetence rather than low empathy. Researchers should also test whether under-communication can function as deliberate "tough love" that serves employees' developmental or autonomy needs, and how communication expectations vary with hierarchical distance between leaders and direct versus indirect reports.
abstract: Leaders may be seen by their followers as miscalibrating the quantity of their communication—sharing too much or too little. We propose that leaders are more likely to be seen as under-communicating than over-communicating, even though under-communication is more heavily penalized. In Study 1a, we examine 2,717 qualitative comments from archived leadership assessments and find that leaders are nearly 10 times as likely to be criticized for under-communicating than for over-communicating. In Study 1b, we obtain further evidence of this bias using a representative sample of U.S. adults. In Study 2, we manipulate communication (mis)calibration, showing that leaders who under-communicate are viewed as less qualified for a leadership role because they are viewed as less empathic. In Study 3, we use separate measures of employee perceptions of their manager's communication as well as their preferences. When there is a lack of congruence between perceived and preferred communication, employees judge their leaders as lacking empathy and, in turn, leadership ability.
apa_citation: Flynn, F. J., & Lide, C. R. (2023). Communication miscalibration: The price leaders pay for not sharing enough. *Academy of Management Journal, 66*(4), 1102–1122. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0245

title: The Technological Entrainment of Moral Issues: The Case of Genomic Data Markets
methods: Inductive process / grounded theory case study of the emerging U.S. genomic (DNA) data market; data from 55 semi-structured interviews (57 people), 19 days of field observation, and 914 archival documents, analyzed through open coding, narrative construction, and process modeling.
industry: Genomic (DNA) data exchange market (health care, biomedical research, genealogy, forensics)
time_period: Data collected October 2016 to May 2022; archival documents span 2008-2022
units: Organizations and actors (clusters) engaged in genomic data exchange in a single emerging digital market
n_sample: 55 interviews with 57 people, 224 hours of field observation, 914 archival documents
authors: Gray, Barbara; Briscoe, Forrest; Diaz Ferraro, Celeste
theories: cultural and institutional entrepreneurship; technological framing; theory of morally contentious markets
keywords: digital markets; exchange rules; genomic data; institutional entrepreneurship; moral issues; morally contentious markets; technological entrainment
research_question: What role does technology play in shaping the rules of exchange in digital markets fraught with moral conflicts? The authors investigate this in the emerging U.S. market for human genomic data, where exchange generates moral conflicts over privacy and ownership.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: technological entrainment of moral issues; moral conflicts over privacy and ownership of personal (genomic) data; exchange rules; technological framing (designers encoding values into technologies); competing actor clusters (Right-to-Science, Trusted Brokers, Walled Gardens).
- Process / phases: a four-step entrainment process involving the progressive synchronization of (1) framings, (2) technical policies, (3) technologies/software, (4) exchange platforms, and ultimately market participant behaviors.
- Triggering conditions: entrepreneurial technology-development "moves" by actors seeking to advance their preferred exchange rules in an emergent digital market.
- Emergent outcomes: particular moral stances become "baked into" technological designs, which then dictate the rules participants must follow; a technologically mediated coercive isomorphism that locks in some moral stances and forecloses others.

Through inductive grounded theorizing, the authors show that as actors develop technical policies, software, and exchange platforms, their preferred moral stances on privacy and ownership become progressively synchronized and embedded in the material technology itself. Once these interlocking technologies are widely distributed, they impose de facto exchange rules behind the scenes, rendering discursive or rhetorical contestation ineffectual and making the entrained moral order path-dependent and hard to reverse.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces "technological entrainment of moral issues" to explain how the material (not merely symbolic) aspects of technology processes constitute the moral order of digital markets. It extends theories of morally contentious markets and cultural/institutional entrepreneurship by showing technology as a constitutive force—rather than only a cultural resource imbued with meaning—through which exchange rules and moral legitimacy are determined, and it surfaces exchange-platform organizations and technology developers as central (if less visible) moral entrepreneurs.
practical_implication: Because exchange rules are set implicitly through upstream technology development, policy attention and early regulatory action should focus on the exchange-technology-development process before institutionalization, opening it to broader, inclusive, multiparty societal dialogue rather than leaving it to actors with vested interests and technical capabilities. Civil society and collective-action efforts (e.g., data trusts) could champion alternative exchange technologies that protect collective privacy and ownership interests.
limitations: The study focuses on a single emerging market (U.S. genomic data) and could observe only the Right-to-Science and Trusted Brokers clusters in detail; the secretive "Walled Gardens" cluster (e.g., direct-to-consumer testing firms like 23andMe) was excluded from the analysis because of limited access to their exchange technology and its development.
future_research: Future work could compare technology-centered entrainment with sociocultural models of moral and institutional entrepreneurship, examine how the two relate, combine, or compete to produce market- or field-level settlements, and explore whether entrainment constitutes a fourth form of moral-controversy settlement. Researchers could also investigate the role of platform organizations, the legitimacy and exclusionary implications of converting moral questions into technical ones, and the dynamics of Walled Gardens in market evolution.
abstract: This research considers the role of technology in shaping the moral foundations of emerging digital markets. Through an inductive process study, we examine the development of the market for genomic (DNA) data—an emergent digital market plagued by moral conflicts over privacy and ownership. Drawing on interview, archival, and observational data, we analyze how rules for exchanging genomic data emerged, and how entrepreneurial technology-development moves influenced those rules. Our findings reveal a four-step process, which we call the technological entrainment of moral issues, involving the progressive synchronization of framings, technical policies, technologies (software), exchange platforms, and market participant behaviors. This process baked particular moral stances on privacy and ownership into technological designs, which then dictated the rules market participants were required to follow to engage in exchange. We conclude that when markets are digital, technology processes central to exchange end up controlling many moral aspects of exchange. Our study contributes to theories of morally contentious markets and cultural or institutional entrepreneurship by illuminating how those theories can incorporate material (not only symbolic) aspects of technology processes and better explain the emerging moral order within the rapidly expanding realm of digital markets.
apa_citation: Gray, B., Briscoe, F., & Diaz Ferraro, C. (2023). The technological entrainment of moral issues: The case of genomic data markets. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(4), 1123-1151. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1202

title: Signaling Legitimacy: Why Mixed-Gender Coalitions Outperform Single-Gender Coalitions in Advocating for Gender Equity
methods: Three experiments (a field-based experiment plus two fully randomized online experiments, Study 3 pre-registered with stimulus sampling) manipulating coalition gender composition; analysis of variance (ANOVA) for main and interaction effects and bootstrapped mediation tests in Stata 16.1.
industry: Working adults / managers across organizations (general workforce, not industry-restricted)
time_period: Study 1 November 2018; Study 2 March 2019; Study 3 August 2021
units: Individual managers / participants assessing advocacy coalitions
n_sample: Study 1: 707 managers; Study 2: 974 participants; Study 3: 1,553 responses
authors: Hussain, Insiya; Tangirala, Subrahmaniam; Sherf, Elad N.
theories: legitimacy theory (Suddaby, Bitektine, & Haack, 2017; Suchman, 1995); signaling theory (Spence, 1973)
keywords: coalition legitimacy; gender equity advocacy; issue legitimacy; legitimacy; mixed-gender coalitions; signaling
research_question: Does the gender composition of an employee advocacy coalition affect managers' support for gender equity issues, and why are mixed-gender coalitions more persuasive than women-only or men-only coalitions?
mechanism_summary: The paper integrates signaling theory and legitimacy theory to argue that a coalition's gender composition signals two distinct forms of legitimacy. The presence of women signals coalition legitimacy (the messengers have material stake and are the appropriate spokespersons), while the presence of men signals issue legitimacy (the issue is strategically important rather than a niche "women's" concern). Because women-only coalitions convey the former but not the latter and men-only coalitions the reverse, only mixed-gender coalitions signal both, making them uniquely effective at winning managerial endorsement for gender equity issues.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends signaling and legitimacy theories by distinguishing messenger legitimacy (coalition legitimacy) from message legitimacy (issue legitimacy) and showing that the characteristics conferring one need not confer the other, so advocacy coalitions must be composed to convey both simultaneously. It contributes to research on employee coalitions by specifying who should be part of them, and to work on men's allyship by moving beyond individual confrontation of sexism to the collective, coalition-based context.
practical_implication: Organizations and employees seeking to advance gender equity should deliberately recruit mixed-gender coalitions rather than relying on gender-homophilous (women-only) groups, because including men alongside women helps the coalition signal both that it is a credible messenger and that the issue is strategically important to the business.
limitations: The studies were experimental and thus limited by their simulated nature despite using immersive paradigms. The threshold of male representation required for a coalition to appear sufficiently "mixed" remains an open empirical question, and the main effect of coalition composition on managerial support was not significant across studies because the two mediators operated in opposing (suppressing) directions.
future_research: Future studies could use qualitative comparative case study approaches to examine how coalition gender composition shapes legitimacy and support over time, investigate the threshold at which a social group is viewed as properly represented rather than tokenistic, and explore additional boundary conditions of the theory.
abstract: Employees often engage in collective grassroots efforts to bring about gender equity in the workplace. Such coalition-based advocacy is largely driven by women, which has led to debate about whether men's involvement as allies can help. Integrating literatures on signaling and legitimacy, we propose that the demographic composition of a gender equity advocacy coalition matters: men-only groups lack "coalition legitimacy," or the perception that they are the "right" spokespersons for gender equity issues, whereas women-only groups struggle to convey "issue legitimacy," or the perception that gender equity is of strategic importance within business organizations. By contrast, mixed-gender coalitions signal both forms of legitimacy, and are thus uniquely effective. We demonstrate these effects over three studies: managers reporting on advocacy coalitions comprising their colleagues (Study 1), an audio-based study showing that the proposed effects are unique for gender equity issues (Study 2), and a stimulus sampling study involving multiple policy proposals (Study 3). Our findings highlight the different forms of legitimacy that women and men bring to the table when selling gender equity issues. We discuss implications for who should be recruited to gender equity advocacy coalitions, and why.
apa_citation: Hussain, I., Tangirala, S., & Sherf, E. N. (2023). Signaling legitimacy: Why mixed-gender coalitions outperform single-gender coalitions in advocating for gender equity. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(4), 1233–1262. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0174

title: Corporate Proximity and Product Market Reentry: The Role of Corporate Headquarters in Business Unit Response to Product Failure
methods: Discrete event history analysis (complementary log-log model) of business unit–product market–year observations in the U.S. medical device industry; mediated moderation analyses using executive career-path data and text analysis of annual reports.
industry: Medical device industry (FDA-regulated)
units: Business unit–product market–year observations (229 reentries, 157 business units, 686 product markets)
n_sample: 4,760 business unit–product market–year observations
authors: Kim, Cheon Mok (John); Cunningham, Colleen; Joseph, John
theories: attention-based view; behavioral theory of the firm; strategic human capital; theories of search
keywords: business unit search; corporate attention; corporate headquarters; corporate proximity; organizational learning; product failure; product market reentry; vertical linkages
research_question: Whether and how a corporate office shapes business unit search following product failure in multiunit hierarchical organizations—specifically, how the hierarchical, geographic, and cognitive proximity of business units to the corporate headquarters conditions a unit's likelihood of reentering a product market after a prior product failure.
mechanism_summary: The paper draws on the behavioral theory of the firm, theories of search, strategic human capital, and the attention-based view to argue that variation in failure response stems partly from how proximate business units are to corporate headquarters. Corporate proximity improves a unit's local search process through two channels—vertical linkages that transfer corporate resources and assistance, and corporate attention directed at the unit's problem—thereby increasing the likelihood the unit persists and reenters the failed product market. These effects are amplified when the product market is established and when failures are technical and severe, conditions that increase the corporate office's ability and motivation to support problem-solving.
theoretical_contribution: The study contributes to the behavioral theory of the firm and organizational learning by drawing renewed attention to corporate proximity as a determinant of business unit response to failure, theorizing and empirically demonstrating two operative mechanisms (vertical linkages and corporate attention). It advances the corporate headquarters literature by treating proximity as a multidimensional construct (beyond geographic distance) and by showing corporate influence as a contingent phenomenon shaped by failure characteristics. It also contributes to the literature on determinants of persistence in product entry by positioning corporate proximity as a driver of persistence.
practical_implication: Understanding how structural variation shapes reentry is of value to managers making decisions about proximity via structural design, location, and managerial hiring across units. Although executives rarely relocate corporate headquarters or units, they may channel and redirect attention through changes to structure or cognitive ties, and may configure proximity levers to support performance-enhancing reentry following failure.
limitations: Because the authors only have access to annual data on entry and exit, they cannot capture more granular reentry events (e.g., reentry in the same year as exit) that may also reflect learning. While product failures may cause exits, they do not always do so, so the authors cannot claim exits were caused by a preceding failure in every case (though such "false positives" make the test more conservative). The empirical setting is a single industry over a historical window.
future_research: Future research could examine conditions under which the effects of hierarchical, geographic, and cognitive proximity might diverge (e.g., successful exploration may require close hierarchical but distant cognitive proximity). More work is needed on the performance consequences of proximity's effects on failure response, how and when firms should adjust proximity choices, and how different configurations of proximity levers can create performance-enhancing levels of reentry.
abstract: Understanding how organizations respond to failure is important to management research, yet prior studies have offered contrasting findings for whether, in a multiunit hierarchical organization, a corporate office improves business unit search following product failure. To better understand how a corporate office affects business unit search, we focus on the role of corporate proximity (hierarchical, geographic, and cognitive) between the corporate office and constituent units. We argue that corporate proximity improves a business unit’s local search process through two mechanisms—vertical linkages and corporate attention—that positively condition the likelihood of persisting; that is, reentering a product market after having experienced a prior product failure in that market. We find support for our theory using data on reentry in the U.S. medical device industry following exit from the market due to product failure. We also explore how age of the product market and characteristics of the failure—cause and severity— further moderate corporate proximity’s role in business unit reentry. Overall, our study offers a better understanding of how complex organizations respond to failure, thereby contributing to literatures on search, corporate headquarters, and product entry.
apa_citation: Kim, C. M. (J.), Cunningham, C., & Joseph, J. (2023). Corporate proximity and product market reentry: The role of corporate headquarters in business unit response to product failure. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(4), 1209–1232. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0295

title: The Benefits and Burdens of Work Moralization on Creativity
methods: Mixed quantitative design: two field surveys (employee self-reports plus supervisor creativity ratings) analyzed with path analysis in lavaan, and a preregistered online experiment manipulating work moralization and value congruence, with indirect effects tested via bootstrapped PROCESS models.
industry: Environmental management organization (Study 1); sanitation plant (Study 2); online working adults designing parks (Study 3 experiment)
time_period: Field data collected in 2019; preregistered online experiment
units: Employees (Studies 1-2, with supervisor ratings of creativity in Study 2); working adults (Study 3 experiment)
n_sample: 134 employees (Study 1); 590 employee-supervisor dyads (Study 2); 379 working adults (Study 3)
authors: Kundro, Timothy G.
theories: regulatory focus theory; value congruence
keywords: cognitive flexibility; creativity; moral rumination; regulatory focus theory; value congruence; work moralization
research_question: How does work moralization—the degree to which individuals integrate moral considerations into their work tasks—influence employee creativity, and under what conditions (value congruence) is its effect a benefit versus a burden?
mechanism_summary: Grounded in regulatory focus theory, work moralization is theorized as a double-edged sword: it can trigger prevention-focused moral rumination that drains the cognitive resources creativity requires, while simultaneously prompting promotion-focused cognitive flexibility that enables creative associations. Value congruence is the critical moderator that tips the balance—when employees perceive their values align with the organization's, they are least likely to ruminate and most likely to become cognitively flexible, so work moralization yields higher creativity; when values are misaligned, the burden side dominates.
theoretical_contribution: The paper challenges the largely held assumption that moralizing work generally harms creativity, showing instead that work moralization can—and often does—positively influence creativity through offsetting cognitive pathways. It contributes to regulatory focus theory by demonstrating that morality is itself a key predictor of promotion- and prevention-focused cognitions, and it introduces a conceptualization of work moralization that advances research on organizational ethics.
practical_implication: Organizations should not assume that encouraging employees to view their work in moral terms will dampen creativity. Because value congruence determines whether moralization sparks cognitive flexibility or rumination, cultivating alignment between employee and organizational values can help employees who moralize their work translate that orientation into creative, rather than draining, cognitive outcomes.
limitations: The author notes that the field studies could not establish causal inference, motivating the preregistered experiment as stronger causal evidence. Further evidence is needed to investigate the effects of work moralization on creativity and the role of the proposed moderator across additional contexts.
future_research: Future work should further investigate when and why work moralization benefits versus burdens creativity, examine additional moderators and boundary conditions beyond value congruence, and extend the model across varied organizational contexts and types of work.
abstract: Although morality has long been considered an important and integral element of organizational life for employees, research has suggested that moral considerations reduce creativity. However, evidence has hinted that morality may not always be a burden and instead could spark creativity. I develop a model that investigates how work moralization—or the degree to which individuals integrate moral considerations into their work tasks—influences employee creativity. Drawing on regulatory focus theory, I argue that work moralization can lead to prevention-focused cognitions (i.e., moral rumination), which impair and redirect the cognitive resources needed for creativity. However, I also posit that work moralization can lead to promotion-focused cognitions (i.e., cognitive flexibility), leading employees to adopt cognitive approaches that increase creativity. Moreover, integrating research on value congruence, I suggest that work moralizers are the least likely to ruminate—and the most likely to become cognitively flexible—when they perceive that their values are aligned with those of their organization, which ultimately fosters higher levels of creativity. I test my theoretical model with two field studies and a preregistered experiment. This research challenges the consensus that moral considerations generally have a negative impact on creativity, and contributes to research on organizational ethics and creativity.
apa_citation: Kundro, T. G. (2023). The benefits and burdens of work moralization on creativity. *Academy of Management Journal, 66*(4), 1183–1208. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0273

title: The Glass Wall and the Gendered Evaluation of Role Expansion in Freelancing Careers
methods: Three studies. Study 1A: archival panel/event-history of K-pop songwriter careers, Cox proportional hazard regression of the hazard of a new release with year, role, and songwriter fixed effects. Study 1B: 2x2 mixed-design survey experiment of K-pop songwriters, OLS with participant fixed effects and bootstrapped moderated-mediation. Study 2: 2x2 between-subjects resume experiment of U.S. film workers, OLS with heteroscedasticity-robust errors and bootstrapped moderated-mediation.
industry: Creative freelancing: K-pop (Korean popular music) songwriting (Studies 1A, 1B) and U.S. film production (Study 2)
time_period: Study 1A songs written January 2000-September 2012 (debut cohort January 2003-September 2012); Studies 1B and 2 survey timing not reported
units: Songwriters (archival careers), K-pop songwriter survey participants, and general-population participants evaluating film-worker resumes
n_sample: Study 1A: 8,168 songwriters who debuted (6,401 male, 1,767 female); Study 1B: 78 K-pop songwriters (156 participant-scenario observations); Study 2: 313 U.S. participants (of 400 recruited from Prolific Academic)
authors: Lee, Yonghoon G.; Koval, Christy Zhou; Lee, S. Susie
theories: career progression paradox (O'Mahony & Bechky, 2006); gender stereotypes / gender bias; generalist discount (Zuckerman et al., 2003); social role theory (Eagly, 1987)
keywords: freelancing careers; gendered evaluation; generalist discount; glass wall; nonstandard work; perceived agency; progressive role expansion; role expansion
research_question: How is gender bias manifested in freelancing careers that advance outside formal organizations, and specifically, does progressive role expansion (starting as a specialist and later expanding into new work roles) yield the same career benefits for women as for men, or do women face a "glass wall"?
mechanism_summary: The paper argues that progressive role expansion can signal agency—taking control of one's career—but that gender stereotypes act as an evaluative anchor on how that expansion is read. Because women are stereotyped as communal, emotional, and lacking self-control, their role expansion is attributed to situational pressures or impulsiveness rather than purposeful career-crafting, so it is perceived as less agentic than men's. Reduced perceived agency in turn lowers perceived competence and commitment—the cues clients use to award work—producing an invisible "glass wall" that blocks women from converting role expansion into new opportunities. The effect is conditioned by situational ambiguity, which is endemic to freelancing.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces the "glass wall"—an invisible barrier disadvantaging women who pursue lateral role expansion within professional careers—as a complement to the glass ceiling, glass cliff, and glass escalator that operate inside organizational hierarchies, extending gender-bias theory to nonstandard, organization-free work. It identifies the gendered evaluation of role expansion via perceived agency as the underlying mechanism, and contributes to the generalist-discount/advantage debate by showing that gender stereotypes anchor whether generalism is read as competent agency or as erratic situational drift.
practical_implication: Freelancers (especially women) may mitigate the bias by presenting their practice under an incorporated business name to shift client attention from demographic cues to work portfolios. Clients are cautioned that stereotype-tainted evaluations lead to suboptimal hiring and that female generalists may be an undervalued labor pool; policymakers could accredit licensing schemes that let women credibly signal agentic investment in acquiring new work roles.
limitations: The empirical setting is confined to high-skill creative freelancing (music and film), so generalizability to low-skill platform work (e.g., Uber, Mechanical Turk) and to part-time or transitional freelancing remains untested. Both focal industries are male-dominated, which heightens the salience of gender and may inflate the observed bias, leaving open whether the glass wall appears in female-dominated industries.
future_research: Future work should test whether the glass wall extends beyond high-skill creative freelancing to low-skill gig work and to traditional careers being reformed toward greater worker autonomy, and whether it appears in female-dominated industries. Scholars are also encouraged to examine additional parallel paths to the glass wall—including demand-side algorithmic matching and supply-side self-selection into specialization—and how they may reinforce one another.
abstract: People pursuing careers outside organizations often face a career progression paradox: they must develop expertise to find new work opportunities, but such expertise is largely acquired through accumulating work experience to which they seldom have access. Research has proposed that progressive role expansion—starting one's career specialized in a single work role and later expanding into new work roles—would be helpful to navigate this challenge. However, we propose that when women engage in lateral role expansion within professional careers, they face a "glass wall"—an invisible barrier that limits their work opportunities due to a gendered evaluation of role expansion. Specifically, role expansion by female (vs. male) freelancers is seen as less agentic, and such difference undermines female freelancers' perceived competence and commitment. Three studies using different methods (archival data and experiment), industries (music and film), and samples (Korean and American) show that female (vs. male) freelancers who expand into new work roles are seen as less agentic and have less chance of finding new work opportunities. Although women who pursue careers outside of organizations may become unshackled from gender biases embedded within traditional organizational hierarchies, we suggest that they still face obstacles in the new forms of work.
apa_citation: Lee, Y. G., Koval, C. Z., & Lee, S. S. (2023). The glass wall and the gendered evaluation of role expansion in freelancing careers. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(4), 1042–1070. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1176

title: How Organizations Influence Interpersonal Trust Repair: The Case of a French Antiterrorist Unit
methods: Inductive, qualitative grounded-theory study of a single organization (GIGN); three data-collection strategies — 42 semi-structured interviews plus follow-ups, semi-overt participant observation, and archival data; open/axial/theoretical coding to build a process model.
industry: Elite antiterrorist / paramilitary special-forces unit (Groupe d'Intervention de la Gendarmerie Nationale, GIGN)
time_period: Fieldwork from 2014, with interviews and follow-up conversations spanning roughly four years
units: GIGN operatives (officers and noncommissioned officers), candidates, instructors, and one psychiatrist
n_sample: 42 formal semi-structured interviews (17 officers, 25 NCOs) plus participant observation of ~50 candidates and 15 instructors (~360 hours) and archival documents
authors: Sala, Gabriel R.; Pratt, Michael G.
theories: interpersonal trust repair (Kim, Dirks, & Cooper, 2009; Kramer & Lewicki, 2010); organizational responses to wrongdoing and reinstatement (Stewart & Brown, 2014; McCarter & Caza, 2010; Frey, 2022); third-party / 'third wave' trust (Dirks & de Jong, 2021)
keywords: interpersonal trust repair; organizational practices; organizational wrongdoers; reintegration; trust templates; trust violation rules
research_question: What role, if any, does an organization play in facilitating interpersonal trust repair among its members — that is, how might an organization broker the relationship between a wrongdoer and the person(s) whose trust has been violated? The authors examine this in an elite antiterrorism organization, the GIGN.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: trust as "capital" eroded by mistakes; the dilemma that high-stakes interdependent work both requires trust and makes it fragile; trust violations versus trust repair.
- Organizational practices: communicating trust standards through selection and socialization; conducting debriefing sessions about violations; using practice, drills, and simulations; team-leader assessments of trust repair; and role assignments on missions.
- Core mechanisms (the "why"): (a) establishing trust violation rules based on trust standards, (b) detecting violations and assigning blame, (c) providing a trust repair template and opportunities, and (d) rendering and communicating an organizational trust judgment (first by the mission team leader, then by all team leaders).
- Emergent outcomes: full, partial, or failed interpersonal trust repair, with downstream consequences for whether and how violators are reintegrated into mission teams.
- Boundary conditions: the "mesh size" (tightness) of trust violation rules and the rigidity of the repair template vary with the organization's task requirements (e.g., how consequential, complex, and interdependent the work is).

The paper theorizes a "Big O" (organizational) process model in which the organization, not just the trustor–trustee dyad, structures interpersonal trust repair. Because all GIGN mistakes are treated as trust violations and repaired through a single standardized process that mirrors selection and socialization (fix character before competence), the organization can repair and reintegrate scarce expert operatives relatively quickly and unambiguously, signaling restored trust to future teammates via mission assignments.
theoretical_contribution: The study develops a process model of how organizations influence interpersonal trust repair, contributing to the "third wave" of trust research that looks beyond the trustor–trustee dyad to third parties such as the organization. It shows that what is usually treated as a dyadic, idiosyncratic phenomenon can in fact be collective and standardized, and it bridges the trust-repair literature with research on how organizations deal with wrongdoers (discipline, reinstatement, reintegration).
practical_implication: Coworkers are unlikely to fully trust reinstated violators on the strength of an organizational "stamp of approval" alone; managers should attend to reintegration, not just punishment. The authors suggest that shared agreement on what counts as a violation and how it is repaired, transparency in detection and the remediation process, and trust in the organization and its practices are conditions that make organizationally directed trust repair effective.
limitations: As a theory-elaborating qualitative study of a single antiterrorist unit, transferability and generalizability are constrained; findings apply most directly to similar elite, paramilitary, or project-team organizations that must retain experts after trust violations. The sensitive nature of the missions prevented the researchers from attending violation-detection and blame-assignment meetings or tracking specific incidents in real time, and the studied organization already had functional trust standards, rules, and templates in place.
future_research: Future work could track individuals longitudinally to identify additional ways organizations become involved at different points in the repair process, follow a comparable organization to see how trust standards, rules, and templates form, and examine cases where organizational attempts to repair interpersonal trust fail. Researchers might also probe boundary conditions such as the "mesh size" of violation rules and the rigidity of repair templates across contexts.
abstract: Organizations that increasingly rely on interdependent teams, such as project teams, often imply work that is important, improvisational, and interdependent. However, these are the very same task conditions where trust is most fragile and in need of repair. As a result, organizations may wish to intervene in repairing interpersonal trust among members. Through an inductive qualitative study of a French antiterrorist unit, we move beyond dyadic treatments of interpersonal trust repair to theorize a model that elucidates the organizational practices that can be used to guide members through the trust repair process. We also induce core mechanisms, such as establishing trust violation rules and providing trust templates, that explain why these organizational practices work to repair interpersonal trust. We suggest how this organizationally directed trust repair process can lead to full, partial, or failed trust repair for trust violators, with consequences on whether and how these violators may be reintegrated into the organization. We conclude by discussing the contributions of our research on trust repair, as well as on how organizations treat organizational wrongdoers, and suggest several avenues for future research.
apa_citation: Sala, G. R., & Pratt, M. G. (2023). How organizations influence interpersonal trust repair: The case of a French antiterrorist unit. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(4), 1263–1293. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1093

title: “We Can’t Compete on Human Rights”: Creating Market-Protected Spaces to Institutionalize the Emerging Logic of Responsible Management
methods: Inductive, abductive qualitative case study of the global apparel industry post-Rana Plaza; at least two semi-structured interviews per firm (CSR and purchasing managers), documentary evidence, and 63 industry events, analyzed via iterative NVivo and table coding (Gioia approach).
industry: Apparel/garment retailing (fast fashion, branded retailers, department stores, supermarkets, sports/outdoor)
country: Germany and the United Kingdom (with transnational field-level data)
units: Lead-firm apparel MNCs plus field-level stakeholders (governments, NGOs, unions, multi-stakeholder initiatives)
n_sample: 20 apparel retailers and brands (drawn from a larger project on 79 leading apparel MNCs), plus 40 formal and 33 informal stakeholder interviews
authors: Schuessler, Elke S.; Lohmeyer, Nora; Ashwin, Sarah
theories: institutional logics (Thornton, Ocasio, & Lounsbury, 2012); institutional work (Lawrence, Suddaby, & Leca, 2009)
keywords: corporate social responsibility; global supply chains; institutional logics; institutional work; labor standards; market logic; responsible management
research_question: To what extent can multinational corporations address societal challenges through voluntary CSR, and how can firm- and field-level actors overcome the systemic constraints of the dominant market logic to institutionalize the emerging logic of responsible management?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: tension between the emerging responsible management logic and the highly institutionalized market logic; the "market-protected space" as an institutionally bound space that suspends the dominance of the market logic on selected issues.
- Approaches identified: (a) prioritizing the market logic, (b) accommodating responsible management within the market logic, and (c) prioritizing responsible management in market-protected spaces.
- Institutional work (process): restraining the jurisdiction of the market logic; infusing the responsible management logic with nonmarket elements; maintaining market-protected spaces against resistance.
- Boundary conditions: construction relies on pre-institutionalized market-constraining actors (e.g., trade unions) to act as legal representatives of rights holders; spaces are fragile, contested, and require continuous maintenance.

Through three interlocking forms of institutional work, firm- and field-level actors jointly construct a binding institutional infrastructure that brackets the market logic on selected issues (e.g., fire/building safety, living wages) so that responsible management can be prioritized rather than merely accommodated. The authors theorize this as a multilevel mechanism for institutionalizing an emerging logic within a field dominated by a highly institutionalized rival logic, framed as cyclical embedding and disembedding of the market (Polanyi) rather than linear progress.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to institutional theory by specifying an alternative way to address tensions between conflicting institutional logics when one logic (the market) is highly institutionalized and dominant. It advances the concept of a "market-protected space" held in place by a binding institutional infrastructure as a distinct alternative to voluntary CSR, and theorizes the multilevel institutional work required to construct and maintain such spaces, informing debates on the limits of CSR.
practical_implication: Binding sectoral agreements—rather than the proliferation of small-scale voluntary projects—can restrain the market in ways that materially benefit peripheral stakeholders such as workers. Firms must align internal incentives (e.g., purchasing managers' KPIs) with responsible management demands, and policy-makers should strengthen market-restraining actors and provide binding regulation, since catastrophic events alone do not shift dominant logics.
limitations: The study examines only one issue (labor standards) in one industry (apparel), so the observed dynamics may be context-specific and not immediately transferable to other grand challenges. The market-protected spaces studied were temporary and fragile, leaving open how the institutional work can scale and stabilize beyond these spaces.
future_research: The authors call for comparative studies of market-protected spaces across grand challenges (e.g., environmental issues), examining which stakeholders can become key allies in restraining the market. Further work could analyze the multilevel political struggles around creating and maintaining such spaces from an open-polity perspective, how practices diffuse from these spaces into organizations, and why firms adopt different approaches cross-nationally.
abstract: To what extent are multinational corporations able to address societal challenges through voluntary corporate social responsibility (CSR)? Based on an in-depth qualitative study of how apparel multinational corporations have addressed labor standards violations since the deadly 2013 Rana Plaza factory collapse, we show how CSR managers navigate the tension between the emerging responsible management logic and the highly institutionalized market logic, revealing how some go beyond accommodating responsible management within the market by prioritizing responsible management in market-protected spaces. We theorize the construction of market-protected spaces as a multilevel mechanism for institutionalizing an emerging logic in the context of a field dominated by the market logic via three forms of institutional work: restraining the jurisdiction of the market logic, infusing the responsible management logic with nonmarket elements, and maintaining market-protected spaces against resistance. A “marketprotected space” is an institutionally bound space that suspends the dominance of the market logic on selected issues based on a binding institutional infrastructure that allows prioritizing responsible management—unlike voluntary CSR, which still prioritizes the market logic. The concept of a market-protected space maps a path for policy-makers, managers, and other actors interested in institutionalizing responsible management in the global economy.
apa_citation: Schuessler, E. S., Lohmeyer, N., & Ashwin, S. (2023). “We can’t compete on human rights”: Creating market-protected spaces to institutionalize the emerging logic of responsible management. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(4), 1071–1101. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1614

title: Publishing Multimethod Research in AMJ: A Review and Best-Practice Recommendations
methods: Editorial (From the Editors). Structured review of all 238 articles published in AMJ between 2020 and the present, classifying the 60 multimethod papers into five design archetypes, followed by editor-observed pitfalls and best-practice recommendations.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Wellman, Ned; Tröster, Christian; Grimes, Matthew; Roberson, Quinetta; Rink, Floor; Gruber, Marc
theories: Methodological fit (Edmondson & McManus, 2007); Scientific reasoning (deduction vs. induction)
keywords: deductive reasoning; inductive reasoning; methodological fit; methodological triangulation; multimethod research; theoretical contribution
research_question: How can authors capitalize on the benefits of multimethod research—the use of more than one methodology in a single research project—while surmounting its limitations and meeting AMJ's publishing standards? The editorial asks what archetypes of multimethod work currently appear in AMJ, what pitfalls undermine such submissions, and how authors can enhance the boldness and rigor of multimethod designs.
mechanism_summary:
- Building blocks: two forms of scientific reasoning, deduction (applying established principles via syllogistic argument to derive testable conclusions) and induction (moving from empirical experience toward theory), which methods orient toward but rarely conform to exclusively.
- Five archetypes (from a review of 60 multimethod papers): methodological triangulation for hypothesis testing (45/60, 75%), methodological triangulation for theory development (2/60, 3%), test and explore (5/60, 8%), explore and test (5/60, 8%), and full research cycle (3/60, 5%).
- Common pitfalls: (1) poor justification for mixing methods, (2) poor "methodological fit" between a method and the maturity of the relevant theory, and (3) poor theoretical complementarity between studies.
- Recommendations: employ less common archetypes, explain why and how methods are combined, ensure theoretical and operational alignment, and use appendices/online supplements thoughtfully.

The editorial argues that more methods are not inherently better: value arises only when multiple methods are integrated so the end product exceeds the sum of its qualitative and quantitative parts. Because the field is heavily skewed toward triangulation for hypothesis testing, the authors contend that groundbreaking theoretical contributions are more likely to emerge from the rarer inductive–deductive combinations (test and explore, explore and test, full research cycle), and that fit between method and theory maturity governs whether a multimethod design pays off.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial provides a field-level taxonomy of five multimethod design archetypes in AMJ and reframes the value of multimethod work around theoretical contribution rather than methodological multiplicity. It positions "methodological fit" and "theoretical complementarity" as the conditions under which combining methods generates new theory, rather than redundant or fishing-expedition findings.
practical_implication: Authors are advised to justify their choice of methods early, align studies theoretically and operationally (including explicit aggregation logic for meso designs), "storyboard" projects around rising action, climax, and falling action, partner with coauthors of complementary methodological expertise, and move non-central material to appendices or online supplements.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editorial encourages authors to adopt the less common inductive–deductive archetypes (test and explore, explore and test, and the full research cycle) to increase the theoretical boldness of their submissions, noting these designs are underused partly because few researchers possess expertise in both inductive and deductive methods.
abstract: methods rarely conform exclusively to the precepts
apa_citation: Wellman, N., Tröster, C., Grimes, M., Roberson, Q., Rink, F., & Gruber, M. (2023). Publishing multimethod research in AMJ: A review and best-practice recommendations. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(4), 1007–1015. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.4004

title: When People Build Networks That Hurt Their Performance: Structural Holes, Cognitive Style, and the Unintended Consequences of Person–Network Fit
methods: Randomized longitudinal field experiment over a 2.5-year project in one business unit; a full-day executive-education treatment provided exogenous opportunities to form bridging ties. Two-stage analysis using panel regressions with individual random effects, and an instrumental-variable approach (treatment as instrument for network constraint) to identify performance effects. Cognitive style measured with the Kirton adaption–innovation inventory; innovative performance rated by supervisors at four time points.
industry: Semiconductor design and manufacturing (a business unit that designs, develops, and produces prototypes of innovative semiconductor components)
time_period: 2.5-year research project; networks and performance measured at four time points spanning roughly 13 months before to 7 months after the treatment
units: Employees of a single business unit of a global semiconductor company
n_sample: 172–187 employees tracked across waves; 74 invited to the treatment and 102 to the control group
authors: Carnabuci, Gianluca; Quintane, Eric
theories: adaption–innovation theory of cognitive style (Kirton); complementary versus supplementary person–network fit; social capital theory; structural holes / brokerage theory (Burt)
keywords: adaptors and innovators; cognitive style; innovative performance; network brokerage; person–network fit; structural holes
research_question: Do people build the network structure in which they perform best? Specifically, are adaptors more likely than are innovators to build bridging relationships across structural holes, even though brokerage benefits adaptors' performance more than innovators'?
mechanism_summary: The paper advances a two-stage person–network fit model. People perform best in networks that "complement" (combine productively with) their cognitive style, but they tend to build networks that "supplement" (align with and reinforce) their cognitive style. Because adaptors prefer well-defined, consensus-based, stable interactions, they avoid forming the bridging ties across structural holes that would most benefit their idea-implementation strengths; innovators, who gain little from brokerage, nonetheless keep building ever-new bridging relations. When complementary and supplementary fit are misaligned, people build networks that detract from rather than enhance their performance.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the cognitive-microfoundations stream of network theory by showing that cognitive style shapes the brokerage–performance relationship in a more complex, counterintuitive way than prior contingency accounts: it governs not only who benefits from structural holes but also who builds them, and the two are misaligned. By integrating network formation and network performance within a single explanatory framework, it explains why large numbers of employees build networks that are not good for their performance, contributing to both structural-holes and person–environment fit research.
practical_implication: Organizations investing in network-building interventions should tailor them to employees' cognitive styles rather than encouraging brokerage uniformly. Adaptors should be helped to bridge structural holes (which they avoid but benefit from), whereas innovators are better served by deepening cohesive networks optimized for idea implementation; even a single day of executive education can shift employees' network positions.
limitations: Evidence is from a single business unit of one semiconductor company in Italy, which may limit generalizability across settings and industries. Individual-level fixed effects could not be modeled because treatment varied between rather than within employees, so the design relies on random assignment and instrumental-variable estimation; noncompliance and always-takers required an intention-to-treat correction.
future_research: Future work could test the two-stage complementary-versus-supplementary fit model in other organizational and cultural settings and with other dimensions of person–network fit, and examine additional cognitive or dispositional traits that drive how people build networks misaligned with their own performance.
abstract: Extant research shows that individual cognitive style affects whether employees benefit most from a brokering or a dense network. But do people build the network structure in which they perform best? We address this question by advancing a novel two-stage explanatory model that explicitly disentangles the network formation process from its performance effects. We hypothesize that "adaptors" (i.e., individuals inclined to focus on implementable solutions through commonly accepted and well-defined approaches) perform best when their network spans structural holes. Yet, these same individuals systematically forego opportunities to build relations across structural holes. By contrast, "innovators" (i.e., individuals inclined to focus on envisioning creative solutions that break away with established approaches) draw no or even negative performance returns from structural holes. Nevertheless, their inclination is to build ever-new bridging relations. We test and find support for this counterintuitive hypothesis through a randomized longitudinal field experiment enabling us to disentangle empirically both stages of our theorized process model. Our findings help illuminate why people may build networks that hurt their performance, shed a new light on the role of individual cognitive style in shaping network advantage, and bear concrete implications for organizations aiming to leverage networks to enhance employees' performance.
apa_citation: Carnabuci, G., & Quintane, E. (2023). When people build networks that hurt their performance: Structural holes, cognitive style, and the unintended consequences of person–network fit. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(5), 1360–1383. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1227
