title: On the Plurality and Politics of Ally Work: Liberalism and Self, Relational, and Organizational Ally Work
methods: Multi-study, pre-registered survey design. Study 1 developed and validated a 23-item ally work scale (EFA, CFA, convergent/discriminant validity) across MTurk, Prolific, and Lucid samples. Studies 2-4 tested hypotheses using hierarchical linear regression with interaction terms; Study 4 was time-separated to address common method bias.
industry: Working adults across a diverse array of industries and occupations (mix of for-profit, plus undergraduate business students in Study 2a)
units: Individual employees (and undergraduate business students)
n_sample: Hypothesis-testing studies total n = 1,076 working adults (Studies 2-4); plus additional scale-development samples in Study 1
authors: Dang, Carolyn T.; Joshi, Aparna
theories: perceived affordance theory (Gibson, 1979); self-classification / tripartite self theory (Brewer & Chen, 2007); theory of political ideology as motivated cognition (Jost et al., 2009)
keywords: ally work; diversity and inclusion; extra-role behavior; motivated cognition; perceived affordances; political ideology
research_question: What motivates employees in positions of relative social advantage to engage in ally work, and how does political ideology—filtered through contextual affordances—shape the distinct self, relational, and organizational modalities through which ally work is enacted within organizations?
mechanism_summary: The paper theorizes that political ideology operates as motivated cognition: a liberal (vs. conservative) ideology functions as a "higher-order want" and "perceptual filter" that leads advantaged employees to "see" their organizations as arenas of marginalization where progress requires challenging the status quo, motivating engagement across all three ally-work modalities. Perceived affordances act as a "push" that narrows liberals' choice set toward modality-specific action. Empirically, liberalism showed a consistent positive main effect on all three modalities, and perceived organizational liberalism amplified liberals' organizational ally work; however, the personal-accountability and colleague-diversity moderation hypotheses were not supported, and personal accountability instead promoted self work among more conservative-leaning employees.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances a pluralistic tripartite framework that disaggregates ally work into self, relational, and organizational modalities operating at different levels of the social self, and develops a validated measure of these constructs. It identifies political ideology-as-motivated-cognition as a general motivational mechanism for ally work that is portable across multiple intersecting social categories, moving beyond prior identity-specific or social-identity-based accounts. By introducing perceived affordances, it shows how organizational context conditions—and sometimes reverses—the behavioral expression of ideology.
practical_implication: Managers can encourage allyship by creating affordances tailored to specific modalities (e.g., signaling organizational openness to structural change to elicit organizational ally work). Because self work is relatively accessible and self-focused, affording personal accountability can engage even conservative-leaning or politically ambivalent employees in self-directed change. Where employees resist organizational ally work, leaders are advised to move cautiously and use subtler messaging.
limitations: Ally work was assessed only from the focal employee's self-reports, raising questions about whether liberals are "aspirational allies" whose self-reported ally work aligns with observers' reports. The colleague-diversity hypothesis yielded discrepant patterns, suggesting unmeasured moderators (e.g., task interdependence) the data could not test. Several samples were drawn from online panels and student participants, and the design is largely cross-sectional within the U.S. liberal–conservative context.
future_research: Future work should examine how organizational factors such as leadership and culture might offset exogenous political influences on discretionary diversity behaviors, and study ally work within specific identity relations and from beneficiaries' perspectives. Researchers could investigate when it is useful to combine versus distinguish the three modalities, the strong self–organizational correlation, and the political ramifications and potential polarization effects that ally work may produce.
abstract: We theorize that ally work (actions by employees belonging to advantaged social groups to support employees belonging to disadvantaged social groups) can be organized into three modalities: self ally work that is oriented toward developing skills and capabilities, relational ally work that is focused on building positive work relationships, and organizational ally work aimed at changing organizational practices to support disadvantaged coworkers. Drawing from the theory of political ideology as motivated cognition, across multiple pre-registered field studies we found that employees who were more liberal-leaning (vs. conservative-leaning) reported higher engagement in all three modalities. We also examined whether contextual affordances amplified the liberalism effect. Specifically, we tested whether personal accountability for diversity, colleague diversity, and organizational ideology directed liberals toward greater engagement in self, relational, and organizational work, respectively. As hypothesized, more liberal-leaning employees reported higher engagement in organizational ally work in more ideologically liberal organizations. However, the hypothesized effects of personal accountability were not supported. Notably, personal accountability had a positive effect on self ally work among more conservative-leaning employees. The moderating effects of colleague diversity were also mixed across studies. Highlighting both the plurality and politics of ally work, we discuss theoretical and practical implications of our findings.
apa_citation: Dang, C. T., & Joshi, A. (2023). On the plurality and politics of ally work: Liberalism and self, relational, and organizational ally work. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(5), 1554–1585. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0999

title: Positioning Research on Novel Phenomena: The Winding Road From Periphery to Core
methods: Editorial (From the Editors); develops a core–periphery conceptual framework and a four-scenario typology to guide authors in positioning research on novel phenomena.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Dencker, John C.; Gruber, Marc; Miller, Toyah; Rouse, Elizabeth D.; von Krogh, Georg
theories: core-periphery knowledge structures (Lyles & Schwenk, 2000; Borgatti & Everett, 2000); legitimate distinctiveness / optimal distinctiveness (Navis & Glynn, 2011)
keywords: core-periphery structure; incremental novelty; legitimacy; method alignment; novel phenomena; novelty; positioning research; radical novelty; theory elaboration
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: A key priority of the 2023–2025 AMJ editorial term is to publish research on novel phenomena — "regularities that are unexpected, that challenge existing knowledge ... and that are relevant to scientific discourse" — yet such research is risky because all novelty starts at the periphery and must be connected to, and legitimized through, a field's core conventional knowledge.
- Key constructs: Core vs. periphery of a field's knowledge structure; novelty and legitimacy; positioning; the review process as a legitimacy-conferring interaction between authors, reviewers, and editors.
- Typology of four scenarios (Figure 1), defined by whether the peripheral/novel element resides only in the phenomenon or also in the theory and/or methods:
  - Incremental Novelty: phenomenon is peripheral; theory and methods are core. Strategy — justify the importance of the novel phenomenon and show how knowledge advances significantly.
  - Method Alignment: phenomenon and methods are peripheral; theory is core. Strategy — justify why the novel method is needed, connect it to core methods used in other fields, and show it fulfills a need core methods cannot.
  - Theory Elaboration: phenomenon and theory are peripheral; methods are core. Strategy — make a compelling case for the inadequacy of the core theory and a clear connection to core theories, especially when the novel theory recombines existing concepts.
  - Radical Novelty: phenomenon, theory, and methods all peripheral. Strategy — mitigate complexity by using core elements where possible and weigh the trade-off between parsimony and transparency.
- Boundary conditions: Complexity and risk rise as more elements move to the periphery; radical novelty is the most rewarding but the riskiest, and a single article may be unable to communicate all novel elements parsimoniously.

The editorial advances a normative, programmatic argument grounded in core–periphery knowledge structures: a field's core represents conventional, legitimate knowledge while the periphery is unconventional and only partly legitimate, and "all novel phenomena are peripheral, yet not all peripheral phenomena are novel." Because the core "defines what is feasible" and supplies selection criteria, authors must anchor novel elements to it, since "the core can serve as a legitimacy anchor" that is comprehensible and acceptable to the field. Positioning therefore involves striking a balance between the novel and the conventional and persuading reviewers and editors — who confer legitimacy through the review process — that the novelty makes an important, well-connected contribution.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial does not test a theory but stakes out an editorial position and a generative framework: it situates research on novel phenomena within a core–periphery structure and derives a four-scenario typology (incremental novelty, method alignment, theory elaboration, radical novelty) with scenario-specific positioning strategies. Its central idea is that novel phenomena are peripheral ideas that must be connected to a core conventional knowledge set and legitimized through interactions between authors, reviewers, and editors, offering authors and gatekeepers a shared vocabulary for diagnosing where a study's novelty lies and how to legitimize it.
practical_implication: Authors are advised to identify precisely what is novel relative to the extant literature, validate the importance of the novel aspect, and connect peripheral elements (phenomenon, theory, methods) to core conventional knowledge as anchoring points. The editors recommend tailoring strategy to the scenario, considering a "portfolio approach" that mixes peripheral and core projects (especially for junior scholars facing promotion), and weighing whether all three dimensions need to be novel — including the option of separating theory from empirics or targeting a better-fit outlet such as AMR or Academy of Management Discoveries. Reviewers and editors are urged to guard against institutional gatekeeping that goes beyond legitimate critique of logic and ideas.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: As editors, the team commits to seeking out and developing research on novel phenomena during transformational times, to watching for institutional gatekeeping, and to minimizing the risk inherent in studying novelty. They encourage authors to engage in such research despite low base rates of publication success, anticipating that artificial intelligence (von Krogh et al., 2023) and attention to transformational times (Gruber, 2023) can help authors recognize and position novel research opportunities.
abstract: FROM THE EDITORS POSITIONING RESEARCH ON NOVEL PHENOMENA: THE WINDING ROAD FROM PERIPHERY TO CORE
apa_citation: Dencker, J. C., Gruber, M., Miller, T., Rouse, E. D., & von Krogh, G. (2023). Positioning research on novel phenomena: The winding road from periphery to core. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(5), 1295–1302. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.4005

title: Hierarchical Team Structures Limit Joint Gain in Interteam Negotiations: The Role of Information Elaboration and Value-Claiming Behavior
methods: Four experimental negotiation studies (between-teams designs) with interactive team-on-team negotiations; manipulation checks via one-way ANOVA, and mediation/serial-mediation tested with PROCESS bootstrapping; Study 4 uses a moderation-of-process design manipulating information elaboration.
industry: Business negotiation simulations completed by MBA and undergraduate business students
units: Negotiating teams nested within team-on-team negotiation groups
n_sample: Study 1: 318 MBA students; Study 2: 990 undergraduates; Study 3: 368 undergraduates (164 teams); Study 4: 623 undergraduates (282 teams)
authors: Doyle, Sarah P.; Chung, Seunghoo; Lount, Robert B., Jr.; Swaab, Roderick I.; Rathjens, Jake
theories: fixed-pie bias in negotiation (De Dreu, 2003); information elaboration / information processing in teams (Van Knippenberg et al., 2004); intrateam hierarchy (Greer et al., 2018; Magee & Galinsky, 2008)
keywords: fixed-pie bias; hierarchical team structures; information elaboration; interteam negotiation; joint gain; value-claiming behavior
research_question: When and why do formal hierarchical structures within negotiation teams shape a team's ability to maximize joint gain in negotiations between teams that have integrative potential?
mechanism_summary: Formal intrateam hierarchy restricts the free flow and exchange of information within teams, reducing intrateam information elaboration. Lower elaboration reinforces the "fixed-pie" assumption that one party's gain is the other's loss, which fosters competitive value-claiming behavior between teams and ultimately lowers joint gain. Study 4's manipulation of information elaboration shows that providing elaboration instructions attenuates the harmful effect of hierarchy, supporting the proposed causal role of the elaboration-then-value-claiming pathway.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends team-negotiation research—which had focused predominantly on the benefits of teams over solo negotiators and treated teams as monolithic—by theorizing how within-team hierarchy undermines intrateam information elaboration and interteam joint gain. It bridges the literatures on intrateam hierarchy and interteam collaboration, showing that formal hierarchies can harm value creation across teams (not just within them), and contributes to work on hierarchical dynamics in teams and on multiteam systems by identifying when and why teams leave valuable resources unused.
practical_implication: Contrary to the common advice from negotiation-training organizations that teams should appoint a single leader with decision-making authority, simply assigning members to leader and follower roles (or allowing self-selection) can inhibit information elaboration, promote competitive behavior between teams, and leave resources unused. Teams with leaders should deliberately take time to engage in information elaboration; egalitarian teams do not perform worse than competitive hierarchical counterparts, offering reassurance for sending more egalitarian teams to the table.
limitations: The studies used MBA and undergraduate student samples in simulated business negotiations, raising generalizability concerns relative to more-complex, hostility-laden real-world negotiations. Effects of hierarchical differentiation may be contingent on the leader's negotiation expertise. The team sizes studied were small, and findings may not extend to larger teams, to status (rather than formal) hierarchies, or to negotiations lacking integrative potential.
future_research: Future work could leverage real-world samples or more difficult-to-resolve negotiations with pre-existing hostilities, examine how leader expertise and leadership styles condition the effects, and study informal status hierarchies and alternative hierarchical shapes in larger teams. Researchers could also measure the fixed-pie bias more directly and examine how hierarchically structured teams perform across tasks with different coordination needs, including purely distributive (single-issue) settings.
abstract: Although teams of negotiators are widely assumed to be better at unlocking integrative solutions compared to individual negotiators, the interteam negotiation context is characterized by unique challenges that can make effective collaboration between teams difficult. We extend our theoretical understanding of interteam negotiations by offering novel insights about when and why teams realize their potential in integrative negotiations. Specifically, we propose a theoretical model that explains how hierarchical team structures reduce information elaboration within teams, which reinforces "fixed-pie" assumptions that prompt the reliance on value-claiming behaviors between teams and lower high-quality outcomes such as the joint gain achieved. Across four studies, each involving interactive team-on-team negotiations, we provide support for the hypothesized effects of formal intrateam hierarchies on joint gain, and test a useful intervention to mitigate the harmful effects of hierarchically structured teams at the negotiation table. Contributions to the literatures on team negotiations, interteam collaboration, and hierarchical differences within teams are discussed.
apa_citation: Doyle, S. P., Chung, S., Lount, R. B., Jr., Swaab, R. I., & Rathjens, J. (2023). Hierarchical team structures limit joint gain in interteam negotiations: The role of information elaboration and value-claiming behavior. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(5), 1586–1616. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1381

title: Signaling Diversity Debt: Startup Gender Composition and the Gender Gap in Joiners’ Interest
methods: Two studies. Study 1: field data from a job-matching mobile app; linear probability models (LPM) with robust standard errors clustered by job advertisement and job seeker, plus job-level fixed effects. Study 2: preregistered 2x2 (job seeker gender x diversity debt signal) between-subjects online experiment with mediation analysis (bootstrapped indirect effects).
industry: Tech/startup employers; jobs dominated by IT/engineering (39%) and marketing (21%) in Study 1; a simulated 'ScaleIT' startup profile in Study 2
country: Netherlands (Study 1 field data); United States (Study 2 experiment)
units: Study 1: job seekers' interest decisions on job advertisements. Study 2: individual experiment participants (joiners)
n_sample: Study 1: 522,451 decisions by 8,340 job seekers across 1,978 job ads from 311 startups. Study 2: 597 participants
authors: Engel, Yuval; Lewis, Trey; Cardon, Melissa S.; Hentschel, Tanja
theories: identity threat (Kroeper et al., 2022; Murphy et al., 2007); lack of fit model (Heilman, 1983, 2012); signaling of employer information (Cable & Turban, 2001); tokenism (Kanter, 1977a, 1977b)
keywords: diversity debt; gender gap in interest; identity threat concerns; joiners; lack of fit; startup gender composition; tokenism
research_question: Why are women underrepresented among startup "joiners" (nonfounder employees), and how does information about a startup's existing gender composition—especially signals of "diversity debt"—shape the gender gap in joiners' interest in applying?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the lack of fit model, the paper argues that the masculine gender-typing of startups leads women joiners to form more negative fit expectations and lower interest than men (H1). Job seekers update these expectations using organizational gender composition as a signal, so a higher proportion of women raises women's (but not men's) interest (H2). At the extreme—when a skewed composition signals diversity debt (token representation)—tokenism logic predicts it triggers identity threat concerns in women, which mediate the link to lower interest, reproducing underrepresentation in a self-reinforcing vicious cycle (H3).
theoretical_contribution: The paper integrates a gender lens into research on "joiners," bringing together the lack of fit model, tokenism, and identity threat to explain the self-reinforcing role of organizational gender composition in women's startup underrepresentation. It extends the prior focus on founder–employee gender congruence by showing that organization-level gender composition—a signal of past hiring behavior—is a meaningful informational cue, and positions women's identity threat concerns as the primary mechanism linking diversity debt signals to lower interest, thereby reconciling mixed prior evidence on supply- and demand-side drivers of gender sorting.
practical_implication: Startups have an active role in shaping their own recruitment pipelines rather than attributing women's underrepresentation to a "pipeline problem." Because signaling diversity debt deters women through identity threat concerns, firms could counteract it with "identity safe cues"—for example, sincere text that explicitly recognizes the current lack of gender diversity alongside a concrete commitment to change, rather than generic diversity statements that can backfire.
limitations: Study 1 captures only initial expressions of interest, not whether applicants and companies reached a final employment agreement, and the reported gender composition was holistic (not broken down by job level) and of unverified accuracy. The Study 2 diversity debt manipulation (5% women) was deliberately strong, raising a possible (though mitigated) risk of demand effects.
future_research: Future work could test "identity safe cues" and other interventions to break diversity debt's self-reinforcing cycle, examine whether hiring women early indirectly improves later recruitment of women via signaling, and jointly study the perspectives of job seekers and startups (e.g., given evidence that startups prefer to hire male employees).
abstract: Women are underrepresented in startups, but research on “joiners”—nonfounder employees attracted to startup work—offers limited explanations for why such underrepresentation occurs and how it persists. We argue that, even among joiners, women are less interested than men in applying for startup jobs and that this gender gap is associated with differential reactions to information about the gender composition of prospective employers. We analyze unique field data obtained from a job-matching mobile-application platform for startups, finding the hypothesized gender gap and showing that women, but not men, are sensitive to information about organizational gender composition, especially for startups signaling “diversity debt”—that is, no or only a token representation of women. A preregistered experiment further reveals that women’s identity threat concerns mediate these effects. Gender disparities in startups are reproduced in a vicious cycle as existing underrepresentation deters women from applying. Our findings have implications for research and practice concerning joiners, the underrepresentation of women in entrepreneurship, and startups’ ability to shape the gender composition of their applicant pools.
apa_citation: Engel, Y., Lewis, T., Cardon, M. S., & Hentschel, T. (2023). Signaling diversity debt: Startup gender composition and the gender gap in joiners' interest. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(5), 1469–1500. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1197

title: Free to Be Me? Evolving Gender Expression and the Dynamic Interplay between Authenticity and the Desire to Be Accepted at Work
methods: Inductive narrative analysis (narrative inquiry) building theory from 100 semi-structured interviews with 25 transgender employees across four waves; iterative grounded-theory-style coding between literature and emergent themes.
industry: Wide range of occupations and sectors (construction, health care, education, consulting, creative industries) across public, private, and nonprofit organizations
time_period: Two-year period, four interview points at six-month intervals
units: Transgender employees (eight transwomen, seven transmen, eight genderqueer, two agender) undergoing a gender transition while employed
n_sample: 25 participants; 100 interviews total
authors: Hennekam, Sophie; Ladge, Jamie J.
theories: authenticity (Hewlin et al., 2020); gender performativity (Butler, 2009); identity transitions (Caza et al., 2018; George et al., 2022)
keywords: authenticity; gender expression; gender performativity; identity transitions; tempered gender expression; transgender employees
research_question: "How does authenticity evolve over time during a gender identity transition within a work context?" The study asks how transgender employees express, revise, and stabilize an authentic gender at work as they undergo a gender transition.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: a dynamic interplay between the desire to express a gender that feels authentic and the desire to feel accepted by others at work; "tempered gender expression" (softening typical masculine/feminine manifestations to balance acceptance with feeling "authentic enough").
- Process / phases: an evolving, nonlinear identity-transition journey in which psychological, physical, relational, and behavioral movements progress and regress at different speeds.
- Practices: a trial-and-error approach to gender performativity through which individuals learn what feels authentic as they perceive others' reactions.
- Emergent outcomes: an "authentic enough" gender expression treated as a compromise rather than a stable endpoint; authenticity reconceived as an ongoing continuum.

Authenticity is theorized not as alignment with an ex ante core self but as a relational, socially constructed process activated through feedback from workplace interactions. Transgender employees temper their gender expression in response to perceived gender norms, learning iteratively which expressions feel authentic and elicit acceptance, so that even after gaining acceptance the process of self-evaluation continues.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes three contributions to the authenticity and identity-transition literatures: (1) it introduces "tempered gender expression," challenging the assumption that individuals always strive for full authentic self-expression; (2) it positions authenticity as an ongoing trial-and-error continuum rather than a binary state with a clear endpoint; and (3) it shows that identity transitions evolve in a less linear fashion than prior research suggests, with interrelated movements that advance and set back.
practical_implication: Organizations should not expect transgender employees to know in advance how they wish to express their gender, and should handle these sensitive transitions with adequate support. Raising awareness, training managers, and establishing clear guidelines are key to improving the transition experiences of transgender employees, especially given that many HR departments lacked proper training or knowledge.
limitations: The study took place only in the Netherlands, making generalization to countries with different legislation, perceptions, and attitudes difficult. The sample is limited by the challenge of recruiting individuals willing to commit over time and was drawn via LGBT social-media communities, which may introduce bias. The authors also could not fully capture how other social identities (age, class, race, sexual orientation) intersect with gender identity.
future_research: Future work could replicate the study across national contexts with distinct cultural and historical origins, examine how transition experiences vary by intersecting identities such as race and class, and explore the temporal dynamics of how gender expressions and others' reactions evolve, including how gender expression continues to evolve after a transition is seemingly "over."
abstract: This study examines how the gender expression of transgender individuals evolves as they transition in the context of work. We draw from interviews with 25 transgender employees conducted at four points in time over a two-year period as they initiated, performed, and continued their gender transition. Contributing to the literature on authenticity and identity transitions, our findings challenge the assumptions that individuals know how to express an authentic self and that authenticity has an endpoint by pointing to the evolving and relational nature of authenticity that involves a trial-and-error approach in which transgender individuals learn to become authentic as they engage in various forms of gender performativity. Further, we position authenticity as a continuum, as individuals may temper their gender expression to elicit acceptance and express a gender that feels "authentic enough" as they contend with the impact of prevailing gender norms and expectations in the workplace. Finally, the findings suggest that the identity transition process evolves in a nonlinear way and involves a dynamic interplay between the desire to express one's gender in a way that feels authentic and the desire to feel accepted by others.
apa_citation: Hennekam, S., & Ladge, J. J. (2023). Free to be me? Evolving gender expression and the dynamic interplay between authenticity and the desire to be accepted at work. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(5), 1529-1553. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1308

title: Wearing Your Worth at Work: The Consequences of Employees’ Daily Clothing Choices
methods: Mixed-methods within-person design: a set of within-person experimental studies (Study 1A/1B) plus a 10-day experience-sampling field study (Study 2) analyzed via multilevel random-coefficient path modeling with a Bayesian estimator in Mplus 8.3.
industry: Online retail, online service, brand design, and information technology firms
units: Full-time professional employees (daily-level observations nested within individuals)
n_sample: 808 daily observations from 84 participants (Study 2 field study); 65 (Study 1A) and 123 (Study 1B) in the experiments
authors: Kim, Joseph K.; Holtz, Brian C.; Vogel, Ryan M.
theories: enclothed cognition theory (Adam & Galinsky, 2012); sociometer theory (Leary & Baumeister, 2000; Leary, 2005)
keywords: dress aesthetics; dress conformity; dress uniqueness; enclothed cognition; goal progress; social avoidance; sociometer theory; state self-esteem; work clothing
research_question: Whether, why, and when employees’ daily clothing choices have implications for their daily state self-esteem and subsequent task and relational work behaviors.
mechanism_summary: Drawing on enclothed cognition theory, the authors argue that the clothes employees wear "embody" symbolic meanings that activate associated psychological processes. Integrating sociometer theory, those meanings (attractiveness, belongingness, distinctiveness) carry relational value that the sociometer registers, raising or lowering state self-esteem on a given day. Heightened state self-esteem then promotes motivated task behavior (goal progress) and reduces relational withdrawal (social avoidance). Because the sociometer is socially attuned, the conformity effect on self-esteem is amplified on days with more interpersonal interaction, when conformity to organizational dress norms is more readily compared and observed.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends enclothed cognition theory from specific, universally meaningful garments (e.g., a doctor's lab coat) to ordinary, day-to-day work clothing applicable to employees in virtually any workplace, and adopts an actor-centric, within-person lens rather than the observer-centric view dominant in the organizational dress literature. It extends sociometer theory by showing that controllable daily choices—not just external events or stable physical attributes—drive state self-esteem, and by providing empirical support for the idea that both fitting in (belongingness) and standing out (distinctiveness) can foster self-esteem.
practical_implication: Investing modest morning effort to dress in clothing that is aesthetically pleasing and unique—and, when interactions are expected, conforming to organizational norms—can meaningfully improve how employees feel about themselves and behave at work. Managers can offer soft guidance and organizationally branded items that help employees fit in while leaving room for self-expression, rather than imposing rigid dress codes.
limitations: All Study 2 measures were self-reported, raising common-method-variance concerns, though the authors temporally separated variables, lagged mediators/outcomes, and group-mean centered within-individual variables to mitigate this. The field-study effects were somewhat small in magnitude, and the study did not use objective indicators of dress or behavior.
future_research: Future studies could assess work behavior with objective indicators (e.g., sales) or other-source reports, and rate attire through a more objective lens (e.g., trained coders) to further reduce common-method-variance concerns. Researchers could examine the dimensionality of clothing with greater specificity, including how particular or personalized clothing attributes convey relational value differently across organizational and cultural contexts (a possible congruence effect).
abstract: Do the clothes worn to work impact employees’ thoughts and behaviors? Despite the universal necessity of wearing clothes and the fact that employees make decisions about this daily, organizational scholars have not yet addressed this question. We integrate sociometer and enclothed cognition theories to propose that aspects of clothing—their aesthetics, conformity, and uniqueness—hold symbolic meanings that have implications for employees’ state self-esteem and subsequent task and relational behaviors (i.e., goal progress, social avoidance). We first provide evidence for the nature of the symbolic meanings associated with these three dimensions of work clothing in a set of within-person experimental studies. The results of a 10-day field study of employees from four organizations generally supported our predictions, showing that daily clothing aesthetics and uniqueness had effects on state self-esteem and downstream behavioral consequences. The effects of daily clothing conformity emerged under the condition of greater interaction frequency with others in the workplace. Our manuscript contributes to both major theories from which we draw, and further offers theoretical and practical contributions to the literature on organizational clothing.
apa_citation: Kim, J. K., Holtz, B. C., & Vogel, R. M. (2023). Wearing your worth at work: The consequences of employees’ daily clothing choices. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(5), 1411–1437. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1358

title: Tempering Temperance? A Contingency Approach to Social Movements’ Entry Deterrence in Scottish Whisky Distilling, 1823–1921
methods: Historical longitudinal panel of county-year whisky distillery foundings; multiple-imputation negative binomial regression with clustered standard errors, era and region dummies, and interaction terms.
industry: Scottish whisky distilling industry
units: County-year observations of whisky distillery foundings
n_sample: 2,744 county-year observations
authors: Lander, Michel W.; Roulet, Thomas J.; Heugens, Pursey P. M. A. R.
theories: cognitive legitimacy / organizational ecology (Hannan & Carroll, 1992; Suchman, 1995); social movement theory (private vs. public politics; Reid & Toffel, 2009)
keywords: cognitive legitimacy; contested industries; countermobilization; entry deterrence; private and public politics; social movements
research_question: How does social movement opposition affect entrepreneurial entry in contested industries, and how does the effectiveness of movements' contestation depend on the fit between their private/public politics strategies, their mass/elite mobilization tactics, and external contingencies (cognitive legitimacy and incumbent countermobilization)?
mechanism_summary: Movements pursuing private politics deter entry by getting the public to internalize a negative frame, so a critical mass of supporters raises the social cost of entry; movements pursuing public politics deter entry by enlisting political elites who can signal forthcoming restrictive regulation. These effects are contingent on context: ancestral populations endow the industry with cognitive legitimacy, while incumbent countermobilization in the national legislative arena blunts elite-driven public politics — and, unexpectedly, can even spur further entry.
theoretical_contribution: The paper builds a contingency theory of social movements' entry deterrence in established-but-contested industries, distinguishing the constituencies (mass vs. elite) that make private versus public politics strategies effective. It theorizes and tests two external contingencies — ancestral-population cognitive legitimacy and incumbent countermobilization — showing that contestation is a two-way, coevolutionary process whose outcomes depend on the internal fit between strategy and tactics and on engagement with these external conditions.
practical_implication: For activists, the effective tactic depends on strategy: mass mobilization matters most for private politics, while enlisting political elites matters most for public politics, and elite leverage is strongest where an industry is historically well-anchored. For incumbents in contested industries, national-level countermobilization can defend sociopolitical legitimacy and may even encourage new entry by making the regulatory environment appear less hostile.
limitations: The historical data preclude fixed-effects estimation and limit the ability to capture how movements adapted their framing or deployed specific tactics (petitions, boycotts, townhall meetings) over a long period. The authors also could not systematically capture the active lobbying behind incumbent countermobilization or countermobilization efforts aimed specifically at private-politics framing.
future_research: Future work could develop finer-grained understanding of contestation strategies and their supporting constituencies, theorizing about the fit between new combinations of internal and external contingencies. Scholars could adopt a more granular historical approach to trace how movement and countermobilization dynamics, including specific tactical repertoires, unfold over prolonged periods.
abstract: What makes social movements successfully deter entry in contested industries? We develop a contingency framework explaining how movements' success depends on the internal fit between their private and public politics strategies with the tactics of mass and elite mobilization. We also highlight the importance of how these tactics fit with external conditions like the cognitive legitimacy of the industry and industry countermobilization. When movements rely on a private politics strategy to condemn an industry in the eyes of the public, social movement mass will be decisive. Alternatively, when movements use a public politics strategy to push for regulatory intervention, mobilization of elites is crucial. We develop our understanding of external contingency factors by exploring how cognitive legitimacy residuals from local ancestral populations affect both mass-driven private politics and elite-driven public politics, and how nationallevel industry countermobilization efforts affect elite-driven public politics strategies. We test these ideas in a historical study of the Scottish whisky distilling industry during the rise of temperance movements (1823–1921). We contribute to the social movements literature by showing how movements' entry deterrence in contested industries depends on the internal fit between their strategies and mobilization tactics, as well as on their engagement with external contingencies.
apa_citation: Lander, M. W., Roulet, T. J., & Heugens, P. P. M. A. R. (2023). Tempering temperance? A contingency approach to social movements’ entry deterrence in Scottish whisky distilling, 1823–1921. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(5), 1384–1410. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1411

title: The Award Goes To… Someone Else: A Natural Quasi-Experiment Examining the Impact of Performance Awards on Nominees’ Workplace Collaboration
methods: Two-study quantitative design at one Chinese company. Study 1 is a longitudinal natural quasi-experiment using 160,830 archival collaboration records of 494 employees over eight months (two months before to six months after a 2016 award announcement), estimated via difference-in-differences (DID) and difference-in-differences-in-differences (DDD) OLS regressions with extensive fixed effects (hour-of-day, received-date, sender, receiver). Study 2 is a multisource field study matching post-2022-ceremony employee survey data (598 respondents, 7- and 5-point Likert scales) with 34,911 archival collaboration records over two months, testing mediation via multiple regression and indirect-effect analysis.
industry: Single technology/software company (largest market share in its industry; research and sales departments studied, plus 27 local offices)
time_period: Study 1 collaboration records Nov 26, 2015–Jul 26, 2016 (award announced Jan 26, 2016); Study 2 survey after the Jan 23, 2022 award ceremony with collaboration records for the following two months
units: Individual employees (and their dyadic collaboration records) classified as winners, nonwinner nominees, or non-nominees
n_sample: Study 1: 494 employees / 160,830 collaboration records (103 nominated, 30 winners); Study 2: 598 survey respondents (81 winners, 109 nonwinner nominees, 408 non-nominees) / 34,911 collaboration records
authors: Liao, Hui; Feng, Qiang; Zhu, Li; Guan, Olivia Zhishuang
theories: affective events theory (Weiss & Cropanzano, 1996); functional theory of counterfactual thinking (Roese, 1997; Roese & Epstude, 2017); social comparison theory (Festinger, 1954)
keywords: collaboration responsiveness; counterfactual thinking; employee awards; negative affect; nonwinner nominees; social comparison; structural proximity
research_question: How does being nominated for but failing to receive an award affect "nonwinner nominees'" collaboration responsiveness to colleagues—both to winners in particular and to others in general—compared with non-nominees, and through what mediating mechanisms, boundary conditions, and temporal dynamics?
mechanism_summary: Integrating social comparison theory with the functional theory of counterfactual thinking, the authors argue that a "near-miss" experience triggers two opposing forces in nonwinner nominees. Causal-inference counterfactual thinking ("if I had performed a little better, I would have won") motivates them to raise collaboration responsiveness to improve future winning chances, while the contrast effect generates negative affect that depresses responsiveness, especially toward winners. Because negative affect is short-lived but motivation is durable, the net effect is reduced responsiveness to winners in the short run (amplified by structural proximity) but elevated responsiveness to others in general in the long run.
theoretical_contribution: The paper makes a finer distinction within nonrecipients—separating nonwinner nominees from non-nominees—and shows that those who come closer to a desired outcome behave differently than those who do not, extending social comparison theory and the star-performer literature (e.g., how "losing stars" can harm "winning stars"). It introduces collaboration responsiveness as a novel outcome to the employee awards literature, identifies a dual mediating mechanism (causal-inference counterfactual thinking and negative affect) that holds beyond competing mechanisms such as role modeling, rivalry, and benign/malicious envy, establishes structural proximity as a boundary condition, and uses a longitudinal design to reveal the temporal dynamics of awards effects.
practical_implication: Because awards programs act as a double-edged sword in the short term but yield net-positive long-run collaboration effects, managers should actively promote collaboration between nonwinner nominees and winners—especially immediately after announcements and especially when the two are structurally proximate—to protect winners' performance and retention. Managers can also help nonwinner nominees curb episodic negative affect by showing appreciation for their contributions and providing supervisory support, encouragement, feedback, and resources, lessening frustration and disappointment.
limitations: The study examines only one facet of collaboration (responsiveness to received requests) and does not test other facets such as initiating collaborations, value contributed, or tie persistence. Data were collected in China, a highly collectivistic culture, which may limit generalizability to individualistic or non-collaboration-valuing contexts, and the awards combined social recognition, feedback, and financial incentives. The proposed mediators explain a modest share of effects, leaving room for additional mechanisms, and the DID framework cannot rule out individual differences that change after the announcement; the temporal turning-point results are exploratory.
future_research: The authors call for examining additional facets and outcomes of collaboration (e.g., initiating requests, performance, withdrawal, unethical behaviors), additional boundary conditions (e.g., cultural context, award type including purely symbolic awards), and additional mediators (e.g., rivalry, perceived fairness/attributions). They also encourage replicating the quasi-experimental design to test the generalizability of the exploratory temporal pattern, studying "surprise" awards whose nominees are not notified, and exploring employee collaboration during the nomination stage.
abstract: Employee awards programs are prevalent in organizations. This research examines a group of employees who have received relatively less attention in the employee awards literature: those who are nominated for but fail to receive an award. Integrating social comparison theory with the functional theory of counterfactual thinking, we argue that nonwinner nominees go through complex motivational states and affective experiences, which influence their collaboration responsiveness to colleagues. We conducted a field quasi-experiment using 160,830 collaboration records of 494 employees over eight months (Study 1) and a multisource study using survey data and 34,911 collaborative records of 598 employees over two months (Study 2). The results revealed that, compared to non-nominees, nonwinner nominees have lower collaboration responsiveness to winners following the award announcement, especially when they have a higher structural proximity to winners. However, in the long run, nonwinner nominees have higher collaboration responsiveness to others in general. Furthermore, being nominated for but failing to receive an award simultaneously induces nonwinner nominees to experience higher causal-inference counterfactual thinking and negative affect, which exert opposing impacts on nonwinner nominees’ collaboration responsiveness. This research advances the understanding of the impact, influencing mechanisms, boundary conditions, and temporal dynamics of employee awards on workplace collaboration.
apa_citation: Liao, H., Feng, Q., Zhu, L., & Guan, O. Z. (2023). The award goes to… someone else: A natural quasi-experiment examining the impact of performance awards on nominees’ workplace collaboration. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(5), 1303-1333. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0662

title: Resource Idling and Capability Erosion
methods: Longitudinal archival panel of Texas oil drillers (Drillinginfo, RigData, Texas Railroad Commission, EIA). Firm capabilities estimated as residual intrinsic drilling speed from a project-level OLS first stage. H1 tested with firm fixed-effects regressions plus treatment-effect analysis (propensity score matching, inverse probability weighting, doubly robust estimation); H2-H4 tested with firm fixed-effects logit models of rig idling, standard errors clustered by firm.
industry: Onshore oil and gas drilling (drilling contractors operating rig fleets)
units: Drillers, rigs; firm-year level (capability erosion) and rig-month level (idling decisions)
n_sample: 102 drillers and 1,396 rigs; 301,536 rig-month observations in the idling logit models
authors: Ross, Jan-Michael; Li, Toby X.; Hawk, Ashton; Reuer, Jeffrey J.
theories: real options theory; resource-based view
keywords: automation; capability erosion; demand uncertainty; human capital; real options; resource idling; sunk costs
research_question: Why would some firms persist with continued operations when facing unfavorable economic conditions? Specifically, how does anticipated capability erosion from temporarily idling resources function as a sunk cost that shapes firms' decisions to idle versus continue operations under demand uncertainty?
mechanism_summary: The paper integrates the resource-based view with real options theory to argue that temporarily idling resources erodes existing capabilities through reduced utilization, organizational forgetting, layoffs, and the breaking of coordinated routines. Because this erosion is a sunk cost that cannot be costlessly reversed upon reactivation, forward-looking firms treat idling like outright exit and rationally persist ("hysteresis") under demand uncertainty rather than idle. The dissuading effect of uncertainty on idling is amplified for firms with superior capabilities (which face larger strategic sunk costs) and for firms reliant on human capital rather than automation (whose capabilities are more vulnerable to erosion). All four hypotheses receive empirical support.
theoretical_contribution: The study introduces capability erosion as a previously unacknowledged form of sunk cost relevant to firms' investment decisions under uncertainty, thereby extending the resource-based view's attention from capability building toward the maturity stage of the capability lifecycle. By explicating how capability erosion operates as a sunk cost within a real options framework, it demonstrates a theoretical synergy between the resource-based view and real options theory and identifies a new, internally generated source of hysteresis. It also contributes to the resource reconfiguration literature by introducing temporary contraction as a reversible deviation from a current configuration.
practical_implication: What seems to be an operational decision to navigate cyclical markets (idling resources) can be highly strategic, because it influences a firm's existing capabilities and its ability to benefit from future growth opportunities. Managers, especially at firms with superior capabilities or heavy reliance on human capital, should weigh anticipated capability erosion and reactivation costs against short-term cash-flow savings before idling resources in a downturn.
limitations: The study is limited to a single industry with usage-specific resources and firms exposed to the same environmental shocks (oil price movements). The data cannot directly observe capability buildup in other value-chain activities (e.g., R&D) or other idling-related factors such as union contracts and labor relations dynamics. The authors note the context may be conservative, as predictions could be even stronger in more knowledge-intensive industries.
future_research: Future work could examine resource idling in other cyclical industries (mining, aircraft, shipping), study different forms of idleness and erosion of newly built rather than established capabilities, and explore exogenous events offering varying managerial discretion (pandemics, disasters, political crises, wars). Researchers could also incorporate capabilities into real options analyses of hysteresis, develop more refined measures of automation and complementary knowledge assets, and study initiatives that curate resources and enhance recovery from erosion.
abstract: Why would some firms persist with continued operations when facing unfavorable economic conditions? Although prior studies have investigated the roles of uncertainty and sunk costs as sources of inertia, an unacknowledged type of sunk cost associated with temporary suspensions of operations is related to the erosion of existing capabilities. Building on the resource-based view and real options theory, we argue that resource idling contributes to capability erosion and that the anticipated capability loss motivates firms to refrain from idling their resources under demand uncertainty in the first place. The negative effects of uncertainty on resource idling are likely to be particularly strong for firms with superior capabilities and for those having a greater reliance on human capital. Using data on oil drilling contractors in Texas, the empirical evidence lends support to our theoretical arguments. Our insights suggest that resource idling shapes the development path of capabilities and risks jeopardizing firms' competitive advantages. The seemingly operational decision of temporarily idling resources can therefore be quite strategic for a firm, and "hysteresis," or inertia in continuing operations, can preserve firms' capabilities.
apa_citation: Ross, J.-M., Li, T. X., Hawk, A., & Reuer, J. J. (2023). Resource idling and capability erosion. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(5), 1334–1359. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1050

title: Reaching for the Stars: How Gender Influences the Formation of High-Status Collaboration Ties
methods: Binary logistic regression on a matched-sample, dyadic (star-nonstar-year) panel with firm and year fixed effects and standard errors clustered at the star-nonstar dyad level; nearest-neighbor matching of female to male nonstars on tenure, prior patents, and competence area.
industry: Pharmaceutical research and development (R&D) laboratories
country: United States (American R&D departments of the 42 largest pharmaceutical companies)
units: Star-nonstar scientist dyad-years (first-time collaboration risk set)
n_sample: 2.04 million female nonstar observations matched to an equal number of male nonstar observations
authors: Rua-Gomez, Carla; Carnabuci, Gianluca; Goossen, Martin C.
theories: Blau's structural theory of proximity (Blau, 1952, 1977); social capital theory (Lin, 2002; Oldroyd & Morris, 2012); status expectation theory (Foschi, 2000; Ridgeway & Smith-Lovin, 1999)
keywords: collaboration ties; gender; geographic proximity; high-status connections; network proximity; social capital; star scientists; third-party ties
research_question: How do two structural factors—geographic proximity and network proximity—differently affect men's versus women's chances of forming high-status collaboration ties with star scientists, and how does the star colleague's own gender condition these effects?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on status expectation theory and Blau's structural argument about spatial proximity, the authors argue that colocation rewards stereotypically male agentic self-promotion, so women profit less than men from being geographically near a star (H1, supported). Network proximity instead lets a third party convey stereotype-congruent, person-specific endorsements that override gender bias, so women benefit more than men from third-party ties (H3, supported). Because female stars share female nonstars' minority status yet also face a "favoritism threat," the star's gender amplifies the network-proximity advantage for female nonstars while leaving the colocation disadvantage persistent regardless of star gender.
theoretical_contribution: The paper moves beyond the established finding that women have reduced access to high-status social capital to specify the structural mechanisms that amplify or reduce that disadvantage, showing that men and women rely on different pathways (colocation vs. third-party ties) into high-status networks. It advances understanding of how status heterophily shapes tie formation by integrating social-psychological accounts of gender stereotyping into Blau's structural theory, and reframes the debate on high-status women from whether they help other women toward the conditions under which they do so.
practical_implication: Organizations should pair sensitization to agency-stereotype bias in face-to-face settings with interventions that deliberately build third-party (sponsorship) ties between low-status women and high-status colleagues, since such ties are an especially powerful lever when women's competence is hard to signal. The increasing geographic dispersion of knowledge work may itself narrow the gender gap in high-status connections.
limitations: The nonexperimental design precludes strong causal claims; proximity may follow rather than precede collaboration intentions, and matched nonstars may differ on unobserved dimensions. EPO patent data may miss collaborations that never result in a patent filing, risking false negatives in the tie-formation measure, and the study does not examine gendered differences in failed collaborations.
future_research: The authors call for field or lab experiments to establish causation, studies of how the theorized effects change over time as the share of (star) women rises and organizations grow more geographically dispersed, and research on how virtual collaboration technologies and remote work shape men's and women's ability to signal competence and form high-status ties.
abstract: Extant research has shown that it is harder for women than for men to form high-status connections in the workplace. Extending this line of research, we examine how two structural factors—geographic and network proximity—affect men's and women's chances of forming high-status connections. Using data on the formation of collaboration ties with star scientists within the research and development laboratories of the 42 largest pharmaceutical companies between 1985 and 2010, we show that women who are geographically colocated with a "star" colleague are less likely to form a tie with that colleague compared to male peers who are similarly colocated, and that this difference persists irrespective of the star's gender. Conversely, women benefit more than men do from network proximity, as indicated by the presence of common third-party ties, and this difference widens if the star colleague is also a woman. By illuminating how geographic and network proximity affect the chances of forming high-status connections differently for women than for men, our study goes beyond the notion that women have reduced access to workplace social capital and expands consideration to the structural factors that underpin—that is, amplify or reduce—that disadvantage.
apa_citation: Rua-Gomez, C., Carnabuci, G., & Goossen, M. C. (2023). Reaching for the stars: How gender influences the formation of high-status collaboration ties. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(5), 1501–1528. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0218

title: Distilling Authenticity: Materiality and Narratives in Canadian Distilleries’ Authenticity Work
methods: Inductive grounded qualitative study of the Canadian whisky industry; 92 semi-structured interviews with distillery representatives and audience members plus in situ observations and archival documents, analyzed via constant comparison.
industry: Canadian whisky industry (incumbent and micro-distilleries)
units: Distilleries (incumbents and micro-distilleries) and their audience members (critics, bloggers, restaurateurs, retailers, connoisseurs)
n_sample: 92 semi-structured interviews across distilleries plus archival and observational data
authors: Voronov, Maxim; Foster, William M.; Patriotta, Gerardo; Weber, Klaus
theories: authenticity as an organizational resource; materiality and narratives in organizations; prefigurative materiality (Schatzki) / practice theory
keywords: anchoring; authenticity; authenticity claims; authenticity work; experiential closeness; materiality; narratives; reinforcement
research_question: How do organizations’ material endowments enable and constrain their authenticity claims, and how does the interplay of materiality and narratives shape the authenticity work through which producers craft and sustain believable authenticity claims?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: authenticity work; authenticity claims (combinations of narratives and material artifacts); material endowments (product features, key people, architectural design); the tension between tradition-based and originality-based bases of authenticity.
- Process / mechanisms: *Anchoring* — central product features tie producers to a prefigured base of authenticity, making some claims natural and others requiring greater narrative effort; *Reinforcement* — other material endowments (human embodiments, architectural design) bolster the integrity and persuasiveness of claims.
- Triggering conditions: differential material endowments (e.g., incumbents’ access to aged liquid vs. micro-distilleries’ lack thereof) prefigure which authenticity claims are credible.
- Emergent outcomes: relatively stable authenticity profiles; experiential closeness between producers and audiences that heightens perceived authenticity; authenticity as a competitive resource or liability.

Materiality is prefigurative: rather than being a blank canvas, an organization’s material endowments inform the selection and audience reception of authenticity narratives. Producers exercise agency by skillfully (re)combining narratives and materiality to overcome the constraints of anchoring or to reinforce an existing base of authenticity, conferring plasticity to authenticity claims and generating audience buy-in.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends understanding of authenticity as a valued organizational resource by identifying anchoring and reinforcement as two mechanisms through which materiality both constrains and facilitates authenticity narratives. It theorizes materiality as prefigurative—shaping which claims are credible rather than merely amplifying chosen claims—and surfaces audience members’ experiential closeness as a route through which material artifacts enhance perceived authenticity. It reframes resource advantage as a matter of how organizations use endowments in authenticity work rather than mere ownership.
practical_implication: For producers in industries where authenticity matters, competitive advantage depends less on owning prestigious material endowments than on skillfully combining materiality and narratives to construct credible claims. Incumbents anchored to tradition may need to demonstrate innovation, while newer entrants can leverage approachable makers and architectural design to build experiential closeness; material endowments can be a resource or a liability depending on the authenticity work performed.
limitations: The study relied on audience members’ accounts of what they deem authentic and deferred to their subjective understandings of how materiality matters, building on an audience-centric view of authenticity. It focuses on a single, tradition-rich industry (Canadian whisky) where a product’s perceived authenticity is tightly tied to that of its producer, which may limit transferability.
future_research: Future research could examine how the challenge of balancing different bases of authenticity plays out across industries, and settings where material bases differ or where the product–organization tie is looser (e.g., high-technology or sharing-economy contexts). Scholars could also study how the meaning of material artifacts is negotiated in situ and how authenticity work and an organization’s primary authenticity claim change over time in response to internal and external factors.
abstract: Authenticity is increasingly seen as a source of competitive advantage in many industries. Accordingly, authenticity work, the organizational efforts to develop and sustain believable authenticity claims, has emerged as an important organizational practice. We examined the interplay of materiality and narratives underpinning producers’ authenticity work in the context of incumbent and micro-distilleries operating in the Canadian whisky industry. We found that producers’ material endowments, especially central product features, anchored which authenticity claims they could credibly narrate. Other material endowments, such as key people and architectural design, were used to reinforce the integrity of authenticity claims. Our study extends our understanding of authenticity as a valued organizational resource. First, we identify two mechanisms, anchoring and reinforcement, through which materiality both constrains and facilitates organizations’ authenticity narratives. Second, our research brings to the fore how audience members’ experiential closeness to producers colors their perceptions of authenticity, and we show how material artifacts can enhance such closeness. Third, our findings highlight the competitive value of authenticity by unpacking how producers’ material endowments may constitute a resource or a liability.
apa_citation: Voronov, M., Foster, W. M., Patriotta, G., & Weber, K. (2023). Distilling authenticity: Materiality and narratives in Canadian distilleries’ authenticity work. *Academy of Management Journal, 66*(5), 1438–1468. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0017

title: Triggers, Traps, and Disconnect: How Governance Obstacles Hinder Progress on Grand Challenges
methods: In-depth interpretive longitudinal case study combining ethnographic observation, semi-structured interviews, and archival documents; ~500 hours of observation, 30 interviews, and extensive documentary material analyzed in three iterative steps (open coding, axial coding, theoretical abstraction) to develop a process model.
industry: Multistakeholder water-health partnership spanning nonprofit, mining, agriculture, government (local, state, federal), academic, and consultancy organizations
units: One multistakeholder initiative (WaterHealthOrg) and its 25 partner organizations
n_sample: 1 case study; 30 semi-structured interviews; ~500 hours of observation; multiple archival documents
authors: Couture, F.; Jarzabkowski, P.; Lê, J. K.
theories: grand challenges (Ferraro, Etzion, & Gehman, 2015; George et al., 2016); multistakeholder governance (Dentoni et al., 2018; Provan & Kenis, 2008); path dependence theory (Sydow, Schreyögg, & Koch, 2009, 2020)
keywords: broadening practices; collective response; containing practices; governance obstacles; governance traps; grand challenges; multistakeholder governance; path dependence; water health
research_question: How are multistakeholder governance obstacles addressed in practice? What are the implications of this for the collective’s ability to mitigate grand challenges?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering conditions: Socioenvironmental triggers (e.g., contamination events, fish deaths, coral bleaching, regulatory debates) that shift the salience of the grand challenge and surface interpretive, structural, and relational governance obstacles.
- Key practices: Broadening practices (stretching the scope by including new activities to incorporate shifting interpretations) and Containing practices (counteracting broadening by limiting scope and keeping focus on agreed activities).
- Process / phases: Three sequential cycles — Developing a collective response (Cycle 1), Protecting a collective response (Cycle 2), Leveraging a collective response (Cycle 3) — each generating positive feedback that narrows the group’s remit.
- Emergent outcomes: Governance traps (D1–Dn) — cumulative lock-in dynamics whereby participants struggle to generate knowledge, develop activities, or deviate from the established response path.
- Boundary conditions: Tool-centric collective responses (e.g., the report card) and tightly coupled multistakeholder initiatives in shifting, evaluative grand challenges.

The process model explains how repeated cycles of trigger → obstacle → broadening/containing practice → narrowed remit produce a cumulative, self-reinforcing path-dependent dynamic. As actors become increasingly skilled at taming governance obstacles to keep the group cohesive, they progressively decouple the collective response from the grand challenge it was intended to mitigate — even external shocks that ought to break the path end up reinforcing it.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces the concept of governance traps to explain why multistakeholder initiatives addressing grand challenges so often produce unintended consequences, and theorizes the cumulative, self-reinforcing nature of these traps by connecting them to path-dependence theory. It extends path-dependence work by showing that exogenous shocks, rather than breaking a response path, can reinforce it in interorganizational settings, and nuances optimistic accounts of multistakeholder governance as a panacea for wicked problems.
practical_implication: For policymakers, communities affected by grand challenges, and organizational theorists designing interventions, the study highlights that consolidating a multistakeholder collective response — even successfully — risks decoupling the response from the underlying problem. Practices such as exchanging knowledge with external stakeholders, encouraging activities that deviate from the established path, and critically examining whether the response still fits the evolving challenge may help avoid governance traps.
limitations: The study is bounded by its single-case design focused on one grand challenge (degrading water health in the Great Barrier Reef) and one tightly affiliated multistakeholder initiative whose collective response revolved around a specific commensuration tool (the report card). The cyclical, path-dependent dynamics observed may unfold differently in more loosely affiliated collectives, social movements, online platforms, or initiatives with different membership compositions.
future_research: Future research should examine whether the disconnect documented here also emerges in collective responses to other grand challenges (e.g., the COVID-19 pandemic, catastrophic disasters, climate-related field-configuring events), study longer-term implications of participant selection for multistakeholder initiatives, and test whether commensuration strategies, collective tools, and information practices enable or hinder progress on grand challenges. Comparative work on more loosely affiliated collectives could clarify boundary conditions for the self-reinforcing dynamics.
abstract: In this paper, we adopt a multistakeholder governance perspective to study how people collectively respond to a grand challenge. Specifically, we show how working through governance obstacles—that is, coordinating and collaborating challenges arising from a multistakeholder governance approach to responding to grand challenges—can erode actors’ ability to mitigate these wicked problems. We illustrate this process through an in-depth case study of WaterHealthOrg, a multistakeholder initiative established to address degrading water health in Australia’s critical Great Barrier Reef region. Our findings reveal how, in an effort to avoid group paralysis or dissolution, actors employ specific practices to address governance obstacles. By doing so, actors set off a cumulative self-reinforcing process, driving them to consolidate rather than critically reflect on and adapt their collective response. Drawing on these insights, we develop a conceptual process model of how efforts to manage multistakeholder governance obstacles can generate governance traps that shape participants’ ability to collectively respond and, ultimately, mitigate grand challenges.
apa_citation: Couture, F., Jarzabkowski, P., & Lê, J. K. (2023). Triggers, traps, and disconnect: How governance obstacles hinder progress on grand challenges. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(6), 1651-1680. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1716
