title: Navigating the Paradox of Promise through the Construction of Meaningful Career Narratives
methods: Inductive qualitative study using a methodological bricolage of grounded theory (iterative open and axial coding, constant comparison) and narrative analysis to develop a theory of imprints and reprinting from retrospective career-narrative interviews; data collection iterated with analysis, with theoretical memos and composite-narrative induction.
industry: Design (Eames Office — a prominent mid-20th-century design firm producing furniture, films, exhibitions, and product/toy design)
time_period: Informants worked at Eames between 1957 and 1978; interviews conducted retrospectively in informants' late career/early retirement
units: Former employees of the Eames Office
n_sample: 33 semi-structured interviews with former Eames employees, supplemented by 7 documentary interviews; informants identified from 188 archived Library of Congress timesheets
authors: Fetzer, G. T.; Harrison, S. H.; Rouse, E. D.
theories: career narratives and sensemaking (McAdams, 1993; Ibarra & Barbulescu, 2010; Weick, 1995); imprinting theory (Marquis & Tilcsik, 2013; Higgins, 2005; Simsek, Fox, & Heavey, 2015); meaningful work (Rosso, Dekas, & Wrzesniewski, 2010; Pratt & Ashforth, 2003); mentoring theory (Kram, 1983; Ragins & Verbos, 2017)
keywords: achievement; belongingness; career meaningfulness; career narratives; imprinting; meaningful work; paradox of promise; prominent mentor; reprinting; self-expression; skills-dominant imprint; values-dominant imprint
research_question: How do individuals who experience the paradox of promise construct a meaningful career narrative? That is, how do people who began their career with a prominent mentor retrospectively make sense of their association with that mentor while still authoring an agentic, personally meaningful career story?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: Paradox of promise — the tension between association with and differentiation from a prominent mentor as individuals retrospectively craft career narratives.
- Imprints (Early Career): Two narrative emphases of the same formative experience — (a) values-dominant imprints (the "know-why," guiding beliefs absorbed from Eames) and (b) skills-dominant imprints (the "know-how," concrete techniques and prestige).
- Reprinting practices (Mid-to-Late Career): Three narrative strategies for interpreting subsequent career experiences in light of the imprint — (a) embracing values, (b) contrasting values, and (c) supplanting values.
- Composite narratives (Outcomes): Three overarching sources of career meaningfulness arise from imprint–reprinting fits — belongingness (values-dominant + embracing), self-expression (skills-dominant + contrasting), and achievement (skills-dominant + supplanting).
- Boundary conditions: Equifinality (multiple narrative routes to meaningfulness) but with coherence constraints — the imprint shapes which reprinting practices yield a plausible story; each meaningfulness path is also circumscribed (trade-offs in belonging vs. individuality, freedom vs. economic viability, etc.).

The process explains how individuals navigate the paradox of promise as an intrapersonal narrative accomplishment: a formative experience with a prominent mentor produces an imprint with a particular emphasis, and individuals use reprinting practices to weave later career episodes into a coherent overarching narrative that yields one of three sources of career meaningfulness. Imprinting is reframed as an interpretive — not merely structural — phenomenon: imprints can be (re)authored, edited, and expressed over time, and meaningfulness is constructed across the whole sweep of a career rather than only in transcendent moments.
theoretical_contribution: The paper offers three contributions. First, it introduces the paradox of promise and shows that promising starting points create both upsides (basking in reflected glory) and downsides (being overshadowed), and that individuals can find meaningfulness in three patterns (belongingness, self-expression, achievement) — not just optimal distinctiveness. Second, it brings a novel interpretive perspective to imprinting by introducing reprinting: imprints are not just stable social facts inferred from the environment but narrative accomplishments that individuals (re)interpret to extract meaning over time. Third, it extends theories of meaningful work by showing how small career episodes are aggregated through narrative into an overall sense of career meaningfulness over the whole sweep of a career.
practical_implication: For individuals navigating the paradox of promise, there is equifinality but also critical trade-offs — people cannot "have it all," and reflecting on what one values and is willing to give up matters. Thinking of one's career as a story (with protagonists, tensions, plot twists, beginnings, and endings) provides flexibility to reframe past experiences. For organizations and leaders, recognizing the different imprints employees bring (values vs. skills emphases) and offering collective narrative resources during socialization may help newcomers' reprinting in their new organization.
limitations: The interpretivist stance is treated as a feature, but the authors acknowledge limits: findings rely on elicited, retrospective narratives focused on Eames, so they cannot draw conclusions about objective career facets and informants may have over-emphasized Eames-related episodes. Inductive design limits classic generalizability (though it highlights transferability), and findings generalize most clearly to creative careers. Self-enhancing storytelling motivations cannot be ruled out, although informants appeared quite self-aware about external evaluation of their work.
future_research: Future work should examine how individuals navigate the paradox of promise in other organizations and occupations (including settings without a clear focal actor, such as high-status consulting firms), explore "tainted" imprints from infamous or disgraced mentors using stigma research, follow cohorts longitudinally to capture how association and differentiation evolve across career stages, build a typology of experiences that offer opportunities for reprinting (e.g., integrating emotions, awe, or "bad is stronger than good" effects), and explore meaningfulness as circumscribed by attention to trade-offs and synergies between different sources of work meaningfulness.
abstract: Working with a prominent mentor can offer many benefits to one’s career: mentors provide skills, resources, and values that leave a lasting imprint. Yet, these promising starting points also present a puzzle as people make sense of their careers further on: they must acknowledge their association with their prominent mentor, without being overshadowed by them. We refer to this tension as the paradox of promise. Through a qualitative study of former employees at the Eames Office, we examine how individuals navigate the paradox of promise as they construct retrospective career narratives. We find that individuals narrate their formative experience as imprints, but with two distinct emphases—values-dominant imprints versus skills-dominant imprints. Individuals then narrate their later career experiences by reprinting, reinforcing the existing meaning or finding new meaning in relation to their imprint; we induced three reprinting practices: (a) embracing values, (b) contrasting values, and (c) supplanting values. Using imprints and reprinting, former Eames employees crafted overarching sources of career meaningfulness: belongingness narratives, emphasizing collaboration and contribution with others; self-expression narratives, emphasizing authenticity and freedom; and achievement narratives, emphasizing expertise and accomplishment. Our study contributes to interpretive perspectives of career success and mentor relationships, and how meaningfulness is constructed over the career.
apa_citation: Fetzer, G. T., Harrison, S. H., & Rouse, E. D. (2023). Navigating the paradox of promise through the construction of meaningful career narratives. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(6), 1896-1928. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0292

title: From Scarcity to Abundance: Scholars and Scholarship in an Age of Generative Artificial Intelligence
methods: Editorial essay; conceptual scenario planning along two uncertainty dimensions (systems transparency × societal regulation) yielding a 2x2 of four futures for management scholarship.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Grimes, Matthew; von Krogh, Georg; Feuerriegel, Stefan; Rink, Floor; Gruber, Marc
theories: know-what / know-how distinction (Kilduff, Mehra, & Dunn, 2011); knowledge production process (Bartunek, Rynes, & Daft, 2001; Van De Ven & Johnson, 2006); scenario planning
keywords: generative AI; knowledge production; large language models; scenario planning; scholarship; societal regulation; systems transparency
research_question: The editorial poses three questions about generative AI in management scholarship: how might it be used to increase the quality and quantity of interesting scholarship; why should we caution its usage; and what does it mean to be a "scholar" and to be a journal that publishes "scholarship" when the field is flooded with manuscripts meeting the highest human-mediated standards for practical importance, theoretical intrigue, and methodological rigor.
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Generative AI shifts management scholarship from a regime of scarce knowledge production to one of potential abundance, with implications across the full value chain of scholarship.
- Key constructs: knowledge synthesis, knowledge development, knowledge evaluation, and knowledge translation as the four stages of the scholarly process that generative AI can both augment and threaten.
- Tensions: promise (efficiency, rigor, accessibility, lower barriers to entry) versus peril (AI hallucinations, "deep research fakes," opacity, fabricated citations, false negatives/positives in peer review).
- Uncertainty dimensions: systems transparency (clarity and comprehensibility of AI data and methods) and societal regulation (extent of governmental restriction on AI development and use).
- Scenario 2x2: combinations of low/high systems transparency and low/high societal regulation yield four futures for the academic profession — ranging from wide adoption with questionable credibility (Scenario 1) to inspectable AI with strict controls reshaping the reward system toward verification and impact (Scenario 4).

The editors argue that the academic profession is currently structured around the presumed scarcity of rigorous scholarly knowledge production — journals derive status from exclusivity, and tenure-track careers depend on signaling scarce "know-what" and "know-how." Generative AI threatens to lower the barriers to that "know-what" and "know-how," which would compress the gap between top and bottom performers and flood journals with manuscripts that meet baseline rigor. The profession's response will be conditioned by two exogenous uncertainties — how transparent the underlying systems become and how strongly societies regulate them — and the editors trace four scenarios that follow from those uncertainties.
theoretical_contribution: The editorial reframes the impact of generative AI on the management profession as a transition from scarcity to abundance, and articulates a scenario-planning framework along two uncertainty dimensions (systems transparency and societal regulation) for thinking about that transition. In doing so, it problematizes the exclusivity-based status hierarchy of management journals and suggests that the long-run distinctive value of the profession may lie in knowledge verification, oversight, and translation rather than in production alone.
practical_implication: PhD programs and journals should prepare scholars not only to use generative AI but, more critically, to evaluate algorithmic knowledge production — including training in the risks of large language models, the ethics of transparent usage, and methodological competencies for ensuring scholarly integrity. Journals should move beyond good-faith assumptions toward evolving governance such as AI training for authors and reviewers, specialized review protocols, AI-assisted verification systems, and periodic audits.
limitations: As an editorial, the piece is normative and speculative rather than empirical; it does not test its scenarios and is explicit that its intention is to expose uncertainties rather than codify a profession-wide response. The two-uncertainty framing necessarily abstracts away from other forces shaping AI adoption, and the scenarios are illustrative rather than exhaustive.
future_research: The editors invite management and organizational scholars to investigate the technological and organizational steps that would reduce the identified risks of generative AI use in scholarship, and more broadly to study the changing distinctive value of the academic profession in a world of abundant management knowledge — including how journals should adapt review protocols, training, and reward systems in each of the four scenarios.
abstract: FROM THE EDITORS FROM SCARCITY TO ABUNDANCE: SCHOLARS AND SCHOLARSHIP IN AN AGE OF GENERATIVE ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE
apa_citation: Grimes, M., von Krogh, G., Feuerriegel, S., Rink, F., & Gruber, M. (2023). From scarcity to abundance: Scholars and scholarship in an age of generative artificial intelligence. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(6), 1617-1624. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2023.4006

title: Confronting the Contested Past: Sensemaking and Rhetorical History in the Reconstruction of Organizational Identity
methods: Inductive, qualitative single-case study of a fintech venture (DigiCo, pseudonym). 46 in-depth interviews (44 with leaders/employees, 2 with consultants) triangulated with extensive archival data (internal texts, web pages, 77 Glassdoor reviews, 22 CEO replies, internal management presentations, 5 employee statement pictures, 7 press releases, 1,250 press articles, 36 industry reports, 1,046 TrustPilot reviews). Analyzed using Gioia-style first-order coding, axial coding to second-order themes, aggregate theoretical dimensions, and temporal bracketing.
industry: Fintech / short-term consumer lending
units: Single organization (DigiCo, pseudonym); interviews with leaders and employees
n_sample: 1 firm; 46 in-depth interviews (20 leaders, 24 members, 2 consultants)
authors: Hampel, Christian E.; Dalpiaz, Elena
theories: Ricoeur's identity as idem vs. ipse (Ricoeur, 1991, 1992); organizational identity theory (Gioia, Patvardhan, Hamilton, & Corley, 2013; Pratt, Schultz, Ashforth, & Ravasi, 2016); rhetorical history (Suddaby, Foster, & Quinn Trank, 2010, 2016); sensemaking and sensegiving (Gioia & Chittipeddi, 1991; Maitlis & Christianson, 2014)
keywords: emotions; identity discontinuity; organizational identity; rhetorical history; sensegiving; sensemaking; temporal identity complexity; temporal synergizing
research_question: How do leaders and members reconstruct the organizational identity when members contest the organization's past in conflicting ways?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: Accumulation of memory cues from external sources (media, public awareness) and internal experiences (reformed and persisting practices) that keep the contested past salient.
- Key constructs / tensions: "Temporal identity complexity" — different member groups develop conflicting cognitive and emotional understandings in which (a) a "bad past" taints the identity in the present, (b) a "good past" dwarfs the identity in the present, and (c) the contested past frustrates identity in the future.
- Leaders' sensegiving strategies ("temporal synergizing"):
  - Transfusing the conflicting past into the present — articulating how a tempered interpretation of the conflicting past benefits the present (past–present).
  - Grafting the future with the conflicting past — integrating key experiences from both interpretations to re-envision future identity enactment (past–future).
  - Defusing present events vis-à-vis the conflicting past — construing current incidents that resemble past violations as evidence of effective identity enactment (past–present).
- Emergent outcomes: Generative sensemaking by members, replacement of resignation and shame with pride and achievement, and reaffirmation of identity claims by capitalizing on identity discontinuity (Ricoeur's identity as ipse) rather than continuity (idem).

The process model shows that when members hold conflicting interpretations of the same period of the past, the standard approach of construing temporal continuity backfires because creating continuity with one view alienates the other and creating continuity with all conflicting views is internally incoherent. Leaders instead succeed by recombining the conflicting past — invoking perceived inconsistencies in past identity enactment to construe improved enactment in the present and future, thereby restoring members' belief in the organization's identity.
theoretical_contribution: The paper shifts the understanding of how leaders create temporal coherence to reaffirm organizational identity from "construing continuity" to "capitalizing on identity discontinuity" — invoking past inconsistencies to demonstrate strengthened identity enactment in the present and future. It articulates "temporal identity complexity" as a novel identity threat in which members develop conflicting cognitive and emotional understandings of the past, and theorizes "temporal synergizing" (transfusing, grafting, defusing) as the sensegiving process by which leaders recombine conflicting pasts into a synergistic basis for present and future identity claims. It also cautions rhetorical-history scholarship against dissociating organizations from problematic pasts or privileging one external stakeholder group, since both approaches can ignite internal identity challenges.
practical_implication: Leaders confronting internal contestation of the organization's past should not rely on selective remembering or on siding with one group's view, because this perpetuates conflict and alienates other members. Instead, they should engage comprehensively with the conflicting memory cues by transfusing, grafting, and defusing — articulating how inconsistencies in past identity enactment have strengthened the organization and inform an improved future. Leaders should also recognize that internal identity contestation may be obscured by simpler external "villain" narratives in the media, and design sensegiving that addresses the distinct emotions (shame, sadness, resignation) underlying each group's view.
limitations: Leaders at DigiCo drove the sensegiving and members provided input but not the formal messaging, leaving open how members themselves engage in sensegiving about identity through less visible channels. The case organization could not remove its negative external image, so the findings privilege contexts in which external image continues to cue a conflicting past. The study examines discursive sensegiving and does not investigate how leaders mobilize organizational practices and materiality (e.g., artifacts that cue the past) to reconstruct identity.
future_research: Future work could examine how members rather than leaders engage in sensegiving about identity through informal channels, and how external image affects temporal identity complexity when leaders cannot reshape that image. Researchers could also study how leaders use materiality and artifacts (versus discourse) to reconstruct contested identities, and how different sensegiving strategies might construe identity significance through a dialectic between idem (continuity) and ipse (interrupted continuity). Further work could investigate emotions of different valence underpinning temporal identity complexity, and situations in which both internal and external stakeholders hold conflicting interpretations of the past.
abstract: This study explores how organizations experience and respond to identity challenges that arise due to conflicting interpretations of their past. Drawing on a case study of a fintech venture, we offer a process model that illuminates the unfolding of "temporal identity complexity," a sensemaking process that involves different members developing conflicting understandings of how the past undermines the organizational identity. Our model also reveals how leaders can restore members' beliefs in the organizational identity through "temporal synergizing," a sensegiving process that recombines conflicting interpretations of the past to support desired identity claims in the present and future. In contrast with prior research that has emphasized the need to construe a sense of identity continuity over time, we show how organizations can instead capitalize on perceived discontinuity in their past to reaffirm their identity. We discuss this and other contributions to research on organizational identity, focusing on its threads on sensemaking and rhetorical history. This includes exploring the important role that temporality and emotions play in organizational identity reconstruction.
apa_citation: Hampel, C. E., & Dalpiaz, E. (2023). Confronting the contested past: Sensemaking and rhetorical history in the reconstruction of organizational identity. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(6), 1711–1740. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1132

title: Advance ’Em to Attract ’Em: How Promotions Influence Applications in Internal Talent Markets
methods: Negative binomial regression on job-posting-level archival panel data with extensive controls and fixed effects (job type, business unit, U.S. region, year); robustness checks with two-year promotion rates, decomposed numerator/denominator, binary operationalizations, manager-mover subsamples, and in-group/lateral-move comparisons. Complemented by 30 semi-structured manager interviews across four firms, iteratively coded to identify mechanisms.
industry: Health services (primary site HealthCo); supplementary interviews at a vehicle manufacturer, a global beauty products firm, and a European manufacturing products firm
country: United States (HealthCo); supplementary interviews in U.S. and Europe
time_period: 2013–2017 (five-year archival window); application data 2014–2017; interviews conducted at four firms
units: Internal job postings; supplementary unit: manager interviews
n_sample: 9,896 internal job postings; 96,712 internal applications; 3,431 hiring managers; 30 semi-structured manager interviews
authors: Keller, JR; Dlugos, Kathryn
theories: career sponsorship (Seibert, Kraimer, & Liden, 2001); signaling theory (Connelly, Certo, Ireland, & Reutzel, 2011); talent hoarding / employee guarding (Dineen, Ling, & Soltis, 2011; Gardner, Munyon, Hom, & Griffeth, 2018)
keywords: career sponsorship; internal mobility; internal talent markets; job applications; promotions; signaling theory; talent hoarding
research_question: Do signals about a manager’s willingness to support subordinates’ career advancement — operationalized as the manager’s recent out-group promotion rate — shape the quantity, quality (high-performer share), and functional diversity of internal applications submitted to that manager’s open jobs in an internal talent market?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on signaling theory, the authors argue that prospective internal applicants face incomplete information about whether an open job will position them for future advancement, so they rely on observable signals — chiefly the manager’s track record of getting subordinates promoted out to other parts of the firm. Career sponsorship and talent hoarding produce variation across managers in subordinates’ advancement; out-group promotions become a public, credible signal of a manager’s willingness to support subordinates, which raises the perceived attractiveness of the manager’s open jobs and thus the quantity, quality, and functional diversity of inbound applications.
theoretical_contribution: The paper integrates the career sponsorship and talent hoarding literatures into research on internal talent markets and applies signaling theory to a within-firm recruiting context, showing that managers are not only "exporters" but also "importers" of internal talent — their behavior toward current subordinates shapes whom they can attract next. It extends the recruiting literature, which has largely neglected internal applications, by identifying a manager-level signal (subordinate promotion track record) that influences applicant pool size, quality, and functional diversity.
practical_implication: HR functions should educate managers that hoarding talent reduces their long-run ability to attract internal applicants, while encouraging subordinates’ advancement enlarges and improves the future applicant pool. Firms can incentivize managers for facilitating internal mobility, and HR business partners can broker backfill pipelines so reluctant managers feel less exposed when they let strong subordinates go.
limitations: The quantitative study is set in a single U.S.-based health services firm, so generalizability requires replication. The archival data do not directly measure employee perceptions, sponsorship, or hoarding behaviors, and the relatively short observation window precludes examining how subordinates perform after being promoted. The independent variable is a ratio, which can be sensitive to denominator dispersion, and the design isolates a main effect without weighing it against other application drivers.
future_research: Pairing archival data with surveys would clarify how employees perceive managers’ reputations and weigh promotion signals against other job attributes. Researchers could examine longer observation windows that incorporate the subsequent performance of promoted subordinates, test boundary conditions (e.g., smaller firms with greater promotion visibility), and explore when managers benefit from supporting subordinates’ external advancement.
abstract: Organizations increasingly turn to internal talent markets to facilitate employees’ movement to new jobs within the firm. A key assumption in academic and practitioner accounts of these markets is that they are effective largely because employees are free to apply and be hired into open internal jobs. We draw on the career sponsorship and talent hoarding literatures to question this assumption, highlighting how individual managers facilitate (and hinder) mobility within these markets. We integrate signaling theory to extend this work, positing that signals of managers’ willingness to support subordinates’ advancement shapes which opportunities employees throughout the firm choose to pursue. Specifically, we argue that open jobs reporting to managers who have secured more promotions for their subordinates will be seen as particularly attractive, generating more internal applications. Our analysis of 96,712 internal applications submitted to 9,896 jobs over a five-year period within a large organization reveals that managers whose subordinates are more frequently promoted subsequently attract more, better, and more functionally diverse internal applicants for their open jobs. We complement these results with qualitative evidence from 30 interviews with managers across four organizations. We conclude by discussing the implications of our findings for the broader functioning of internal talent markets.
apa_citation: Keller, J. R., & Dlugos, K. (2023). Advance ’em to attract ’em: How promotions influence applications in internal talent markets. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(6), 1831–1859. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1174

title: Discerning Saints: Moralization of Intrinsic Motivation and Selective Prosociality at Work
methods: Mixed-methods: a multilevel team-level field survey at a Latin American financial institution analyzed with mixed multilevel regression (n = 784 employees nested in k = 185 teams) plus three online studies including exploratory and confirmatory factor analyses for scale development (Studies 2a and 2b on MTurk/CloudResearch, n = 245 and n = 243) and a preregistered, well-powered, two-condition between-subjects experiment with simulated teammate voice profiles (Study 2c, n = 1,245), testing moderation and moderated-mediation models via simple-slopes, polynomial regression/response surface, and indirect-effect analyses.
industry: Financial services (one large Latin American financial institution) plus online experimental samples recruited through Amazon MTurk via the CloudResearch-approved participant pool
country: Latin America (field study) and United States (online studies)
units: Employees nested in project teams reporting to a common supervisor (field study); individual online participants assigned to simulated three-person teams (Study 2c)
n_sample: Study 1: 784 employees in 185 teams; Studies 2a/2b: 245 and 243 online participants; Study 2c: 1,245 online participants
authors: Kwon, M.; Cunningham, J. L.; Jachimowicz, J. M.
theories: moral psychology and moral emotions (Horberg, Oveis, & Keltner, 2011; Tracy & Robins, 2004); moralization theory (Haidt, 2001; Rhee, Schein, & Bastian, 2019; Rozin, 1999; Rozin, Markwith, & Stoess, 1997; Skitka, 2010; Skitka, Hanson, Morgan, & Wisneski, 2021); social projection theory (Cho & Knowles, 2013; Ross, Greene, & House, 1977; Weiss, Forstmann, & Burgmer, 2021)
keywords: intrinsic motivation; moral judgment; moralization; moralization of intrinsic motivation (MOIM); organizational citizenship behavior (OCB-I); pride and elevation; prosocial behavior; selective prosociality; teammates
research_question: Does employees’ own intrinsic motivation lead them to moralize intrinsic motivation — that is, to associate it with superior morality — and, if so, does this moralization produce selectivity in whom they help, such that more intrinsically motivated employees direct more citizenship behavior toward teammates they perceive as also being intrinsically motivated and less toward those they perceive as not?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Extrinsic motivation, prosocial motivation, team size, dispersion of teammates’ intrinsic motivation, age, gender, tenure (field study); extrinsic motivation, expected reciprocity (Study 2c).

Drawing on moralization theory and social projection theory, the authors argue that intrinsically motivated employees experience positive moral emotions (pride and elevation) that amplify intrinsic motivation into a moral attribute. As MOIM rises, employees project this moral standard onto coworkers and use teammates’ intrinsic motivation as a cue for moral worth, helping more those they perceive as more intrinsically motivated (and therefore more moral) and withdrawing help from those they perceive as less intrinsically motivated. The moderated-mediation pattern is replicated across naturally occurring (Study 1) and experimentally manipulated (Study 2c) variations in teammate intrinsic motivation and across self-reported and behavioral helping measures.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends work-motivation research by distinguishing the experience of intrinsic motivation from its valuation, introducing and validating MOIM as a novel construct and measure. It applies a moralization lens to organizational behavior, arguing that intrinsic motivation can be imbued with moral significance and thereby become a source of interpersonal moral judgment. By taking a dyadic, interpersonal perspective on intrinsic motivation and showing that moralization functions as an exclusionary mechanism in helping decisions, it complicates the prevailing view of intrinsically motivated employees as uniformly good organizational citizens and surfaces a potential source of inequality for employees who lack intrinsic motivation.
practical_implication: Leaders and organizations should recognize that endorsing intrinsic motivation can have unintended consequences through its moralization, including value-incongruence conflicts and reduced support for employees who do not display intrinsic motivation. Employees who lack intrinsic motivation may be judged as less moral and receive less help from colleagues, which could constrain their organizational advancement; managers should be cautious about narratives that elevate intrinsic motivation into a moral signal, and employees should be aware that performing intrinsic motivation can become a form of emotional labor.
limitations: The field study cannot compare effects of actual versus perceived intrinsic motivation, and the data do not capture how quickly perceptions of others’ intrinsic motivation form or whether they are malleable. In Study 2c, the manipulation of low intrinsic motivation used negatively valenced language (e.g., "I don't like the topic of the study"), which may have signaled extrinsic rather than purely lacking intrinsic motivation. The samples (Latin America, United States) leave open whether results generalize to other cultural contexts, particularly tight cultures or contexts where intrinsic motivation may be negatively moralized.
future_research: Future studies could compare actual and perceived intrinsic motivation simultaneously, examine how perceptions of others’ motivation form and update over time, and explore whether and when extrinsic motivation also becomes moralized (e.g., in finance-sector contexts). Boundary conditions tied to cross-cultural settings — including tight cultures and contexts where intrinsic motivation may be negatively valued — warrant investigation, as do other consequences of MOIM such as dehumanization, conflict, resource allocation, and evaluation asymmetries between employees of differing intrinsic motivation.
abstract: Intrinsic motivation has received widespread attention as a predictor of positive work outcomes, including employees’ prosocial behavior. We offer a more nuanced view by proposing that intrinsic motivation does not uniformly increase prosocial behavior toward all others. Specifically, we argue that employees with higher intrinsic motivation are more likely to value intrinsic motivation and associate it with having higher morality (i.e., they moralize it). When employees moralize intrinsic motivation, they perceive others with higher intrinsic motivation as being more moral and thus engage in more prosocial behavior toward those others, and judge others who are less intrinsically motivated as less moral and thereby engage in less prosocial behaviors toward them. We provide empirical support for our theoretical model across a large-scale, team-level field study in a Latin American financial institution (n 5 784, k 5 185) and a set of three online studies, including a preregistered experiment (n 5 245, 243, and 1,245), where we develop a measure of the moralization of intrinsic motivation and provide both causal and mediating evidence. This research complicates our understanding of intrinsic motivation by revealing how its moralization may at times dim the positive light of intrinsic motivation itself.
apa_citation: Kwon, M., Cunningham, J. L., & Jachimowicz, J. M. (2023). Discerning saints: Moralization of intrinsic motivation and selective prosociality at work. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(6), 1625–1650. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1761

title: Mother’s Reentry: A Relative Contribution Perspective of Dual-Earner Parents’ Roles, Resources, and Outcomes
methods: Multistage, multisource dyadic field survey of mother–significant-other couples at three time points (one week before reentry, week of reentry, one week after). Actor–partner interdependence model (APIM) estimated via structural equation modeling with full information maximum likelihood (FIML) in Mplus 8.4; indirect effects tested via bias-corrected bootstrap confidence intervals.
industry: Mixed industries (healthcare, education, finance, science/engineering, retail, manufacturing, IT, social services, etc.) — mothers recruited via pregnancy-related blogs
units: Dual-earner mother–significant-other dyads transitioning back to work after maternity leave
n_sample: 297 U.S.-based dyads (246 mothers and 180 significant others)
authors: Little, L. M.; Masterson, C. R.
theories: conservation of resources theory (Hobfoll, 1989; Hobfoll et al., 2018); crossover model (Westman, 2001); relative contribution model (Kozlowski & Klein, 2000; Mathieu et al., 2017)
keywords: conservation of resources theory; crossover; dual-earner couples; family-work conflict; home stress; interpersonal deviance; maternity leave reentry; perceived organizational support; relative contribution model
research_question: How does perceived organizational support (POS) shape dual-earner parents’ home stress and downstream workplace outcomes (family–work conflict and interpersonal deviance) during the mother’s post-maternity-leave reentry, and through what direct, indirect, and shared-stressor crossover mechanisms — given the mother’s role as the couple’s critical member — does each parent’s POS influence their partner’s home stress?
mechanism_summary: Integrating conservation of resources (COR) theory, the crossover model, and the relative contribution model (RCM), the authors theorize that POS is a boundary-spanning resource whose esteem, status, and positive emotions spill into the home domain and reduce home stress. Because dual-earner parents share the home, stress crosses over directly (emotional contagion) and indirectly (via partner support). Crucially, RCM positions the mother as the couple’s critical member during reentry, so her POS directly attenuates both parents’ home stress (shared-stressor mechanism), which in turn shapes both parents’ FWC and interpersonal deviance at work.
theoretical_contribution: The paper offers a new theoretical lens on the shared-stressor crossover mechanism by integrating the relative contribution model with COR theory: it shows that during shared stressful family events the critical member’s resources — not the focal individual’s alone — drive both partners’ stress. This reframes COR’s standard interpretation of valuable resources by asking *whose* resources matter, and extends crossover research by demonstrating that mothers’ POS shapes significant others’ home stress, FWC, and interpersonal deviance even when significant others’ own POS does not.
practical_implication: Organizations should treat post-maternity-leave reentry as a critical, dyadic family transition rather than a “mother’s issue” and proactively engender POS for returning mothers — e.g., showing appreciation for their goals, checking in on well-being, providing schedule control, and signaling that motherhood will not damage career opportunities. Because the critical family member’s POS spills over to her partner’s stress and workplace behavior, supporting mothers yields collateral benefits for significant others’ workplaces.
limitations: All participants were U.S.-based, so country-level policy and cultural variation in maternity leave was not modeled, and part-time versus full-time status post-leave was not measured. Childcare arrangements were not assessed. Mothers’ contributions may be inflated because the comparison occurred immediately after leave. The mother-deviance scale showed below-cutoff reliability (α = .59), likely because mothers’ deviance was almost exclusively limited to “making fun of someone at work,” reflecting heightened scrutiny on returning mothers.
future_research: Future studies should test the model in different national/cultural contexts, contrast full-time and part-time returners, incorporate childcare arrangements, and examine longer-term consequences across stages of parenting. The COR + RCM integration could extend to other teams where a critical member’s resources may disproportionately shape collective outcomes (e.g., academic teams, medical teams), and to other shared stressors (e.g., COVID-19 caregiving demands) where the locus of the critical member may shift.
abstract: The reentry period after maternity leave can be a stressful but important transition for mothers, their significant others, and their organizations. In the present study, we integrate conservation of resources theory, the crossover model, and the relative contribution model to understand the influence of perceived organizational support during reentry on both mothers’ and their significant others’ home stress and, ultimately, workplace experiences. Beyond actor effects, we investigate the relationship between each parent’s perceived organizational support on their partner’s home stress via three crossover mechanisms: (a) direct, (b) indirect, (c) shared stressor. Notably, we offer a theoretically novel lens through which to view the shared-stressor mechanism. Drawing upon the relative contribution model, we conceptualize dual-earner parents as a team and argue that because mothers are the critical members during this period, their resources will directly influence both parents’ home stress. Findings yielded by this multistage, multisource field study demonstrate the importance of supporting mothers as critical members of the family during reentry, as organizational support that is perceived as inadequate directly affects home stress and indirectly influences workplace experiences that can impede successful transitions and threaten career outcomes for both parents.
apa_citation: Little, L. M., & Masterson, C. R. (2023). Mother’s reentry: A relative contribution perspective of dual-earner parents’ roles, resources, and outcomes. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(6), 1741-1767. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1344

title: The Experimental Hand: How Platform-Based Experimentation Reconfigures Worker Autonomy
methods: Longitudinal qualitative inductive study of one large digital labor platform (pseudonym QuickHire). Data combine primary discussion-board archives (2004-2020), firsthand observational data as registered worker and client (2014-2020), platform announcements, blog posts, help articles, and Terms of Service. Analysis used open coding, descriptive memos, temporal bracketing, and Acker's (2006) regime concept to identify three successive experimentation regimes and theorize the experimental hand.
industry: Digital labor platform specializing in high-skilled remote contract work (software development, data science, research, marketing, web/graphic design)
country: Global (platform users registered from over 150 countries; workers globally distributed and never met in person)
units: One digital labor platform (QuickHire) and the workers, clients, and announcements on it
n_sample: 678 discussion board topics tagged with experiment/test labels plus random sampling of over 300 additional discussion board topics; six years of firsthand registered-user observation
authors: Rahman, H. A.; Weiss, T.; Karunakaran, A.
theories: exit, voice, and loyalty (Hirschman, 1970); inequality regimes (Acker, 2006); organizational management of experimentation (Marres & Stark, 2020; Kellogg, 2022); worker autonomy (Mazmanian, Orlikowski, & Yates, 2013; Kellogg, 2022)
keywords: concealed experimentation regime; digital labor platforms; experimental hand; explicit experimentation regime; less powerful workers; normalization; platform-based experimentation; unbounded experimentation regime; voice efficacy; worker autonomy
research_question: How does platform-based experimentation affect the autonomy of less powerful workers, especially when those workers lack the relational opportunities that workers in conventional bureaucratic organizations have traditionally relied on to preserve their autonomy?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: worker autonomy decomposed into (1) extent of control over work activities and (2) voice efficacy; less powerful workers vs. platform; relational opportunities curtailed by digital, distributed work.
- Process / phases: three successive experimentation regimes — Explicit (2007-2013: voluntary participation, episodic frequency, narrow scope) -> Concealed (2014-2016: assigned, episodic, narrow) -> Unbounded (2017-2020: assigned, continuous, expansive).
- Triggering conditions: digital platform infrastructure enabling cheap, real-time, large-scale experimentation; Terms of Service reserving the right to modify the site at any time; fragmented worker experiences impeding collective sensemaking.
- Emergent outcomes: workers' degree of autonomy first increases (high voice efficacy, enhanced control), then diminishes (low voice efficacy, reduced control), then becomes normalized (resigned acceptance; workers describe themselves as "guinea pigs" and "lab rats").
- Boundary conditions: applies to less powerful workers in digital platform settings with limited social organization; may differ where unions, coalitions, or higher worker collective action exist (e.g., ride-hailing).

The process operates as an "experimental hand": initial explicit, opt-in experimentation elicits worker consent and sets baseline expectations that experimentation is participatory and beneficial; the platform then leverages that consent to deploy concealed experiments without notification, producing fragmented experiences that inhibit collective pushback; finally, unbounded continuous experiments normalize autonomy loss as workers come to expect and accept it as "business as usual." Successive regime shifts thus reconfigure workers' autonomy and produce consent to its own erosion without provoking exit or collective resistance.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops the "experimental hand" as a new theoretical construct that captures how successive changes in platform-based experimentation regimes reconfigure worker autonomy over time and normalize its diminishment. It extends scholarship on worker autonomy beyond conventional bureaucratic organizations by showing that even high-status, high-tenure platform workers cannot prevent accretive autonomy loss because platforms do not endow them with stable positions in an organizational hierarchy. The study also advances the literature on the organizational management of experimentation by foregrounding the "social effects of experimentation" — the experiences and consequences faced by experimental subjects — rather than the design and efficacy concerns of organizations.
practical_implication: Organizations should attend to the social effects of experimentation by establishing internal ethics, audit, and compliance boards comprised of diverse low- and high-power actors to oversee experimentation. External oversight units and third-party certification bodies (e.g., fair-work monitoring) can balance organizations' and workers' rights, open up relational opportunities between low- and high-power stakeholders, and provide credible avenues for workers to push back when experimentation infringes on welfare.
limitations: The study relies on less powerful workers' public discussion-board experiences and the platform's public statements; it does not access internal platform data, which platforms tend to curate for researchers to protect their image. It is bounded to one large digital labor platform that accrued considerable market power, focuses on settings where experimentation is central to optimizing platform features, and may not generalize to platforms with less market power or to settings with higher worker social organization.
future_research: Future research should examine how higher degrees of worker social organization (unionization, coalitions, collective action) shape the implementation of the experimental hand, conduct cross-case comparisons of experimentation regimes across platforms with varying market power, and study whether the experimental hand generalizes to conventional organizations increasingly using algorithmic management and data analytics. Researchers should also broaden the study of social effects beyond autonomy to encompass worker anxiety, well-being, sense of self, and livelihoods, and examine experimentation managers and developers as well as subjects.
abstract: We examine how and when platform-based experimentation influences worker autonomy, especially in contexts where workers do not have access to the same relational opportunities that workers in conventional bureaucratic organizations have traditionally relied on to preserve their autonomy. By analyzing longitudinal qualitative data from one of the world's largest digital labor platforms, we found that the platform implemented three experimentation regimes—explicit, concealed, and unbounded—that reconfigured workers' autonomy in unexpected ways. We theorize the introduction of and successive changes in platform-based experimentation as constitutive of the "experimental hand." Our model of the experimental hand captures how successive changes in platform-based experimentation regimes reconfigure workers' degree of autonomy: workers first experienced increased autonomy, followed by diminished autonomy, and finally workers normalized their diminished autonomy as a "business as usual" aspect of work life on the platform. Whereas prior research has primarily examined the design and efficacy of experiments from the perspective of organizations, our study builds new theory on the social effects of experimentation, capturing the implications faced by workers.
apa_citation: Rahman, H. A., Weiss, T., & Karunakaran, A. (2023). The experimental hand: How platform-based experimentation reconfigures worker autonomy. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(6), 1803-1830. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.0638

title: Who Is Leaving and Why? The Dynamics of High-Quality Human Capital Outflows
methods: Longitudinal panel of administrative store-month records analyzed with a panel vector autoregression (PVAR) model with three lags; impulse response functions decomposed via 2,000 Monte Carlo simulations to estimate cumulative dynamic effects of exit events on subsequent unit-level voluntary turnover.
industry: Retail (large national U.S. retailer)
units: Store-months from 1,620 stores (Nall = 29,358 unit-months used in PVAR; 35,640 total observations)
n_sample: 1,620 stores
authors: Sajjadiani, Sima; Kammeyer-Mueller, John; Benson, Alan
theories: context-emergent turnover (CET) theory (Nyberg & Ployhart, 2013); psychological contract theory (Morrison & Robinson, 1997); signaling theory; social comparison theory; turnover event theory (Laulié & Morgeson, 2021)
keywords: dismissals; high performers; human capital outflows; layoff announcements; panel vector autoregression; turnover event theory; voluntary turnover
research_question: How do different types of employee exit events—dismissals, layoff announcements, and voluntary turnover—differentially affect subsequent unit-level voluntary turnover, and how is this dynamic shaped by the quality (performance) of the human capital leaving the work unit?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on turnover event theory, the authors argue that exit events trigger a disruption-then-recovery phase whose strength is a function of the event's novelty, disruption, and criticality. Layoff announcements signal an uncontrollable, psychological-contract-violating threat, producing strong and immediate spillover quits; voluntary turnover signals viable alternatives and produces moderate but longer-lasting contagion; dismissals largely remove disruptive workers and only weakly (and briefly) depress subsequent quits. The PVAR estimates show these dynamic effects are concentrated within performance strata—high-performer exits beget high-performer quits and low-performer exits beget low-performer quits—revealing a similarity-based "multiplier" structure that individual-level turnover models cannot detect.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends turnover event theory by jointly modeling dismissals, layoff announcements, and voluntary turnover as endogenous, coevolving elements of a dynamic system rather than as independent outcomes, and by separating layoff announcements from layoff events themselves. It generates and tests TET-grounded predictions about how novelty, disruption, and criticality differentiate the magnitude and duration of subsequent voluntary turnover, and it shows that "turnover begets turnover" is not uniform but stratified by performance similarity—a mechanism prior collective-turnover work had not articulated.
practical_implication: Managers should treat layoff announcements as the single most potent driver of subsequent voluntary turnover, especially when high performers are among those being laid off, and weigh the announcement decision accordingly. Because the consequences of departures multiply among similar workers, organizations underestimate the true human capital cost of losing high performers; retention investments in high performers therefore yield compounding returns. Dismissals targeted at clearly poor performers can be a net stabilizer of the unit.
limitations: Findings are drawn from a single large U.S. retailer with relatively low task complexity and modest human capital investment, which may attenuate the magnitude of disruption effects; generalizability to higher-complexity or higher-tenure settings is uncertain. Human capital quality is operationalized via annual supervisor performance ratings, available only for employees with at least one year of tenure, and the authors lacked objective performance measures or a measure of perceived dismissal fairness. Other plausible sources of heterogeneity (job level, full- vs. part-time status) could not be modeled.
future_research: Replicate the dynamic framework in occupations with higher task complexity, longer training, and greater human capital investment, where event novelty, disruption, and criticality should be larger. Use more objective performance metrics and direct measures of event novelty, disruption, and criticality to test the theorized mechanism more cleanly. Explore why exit events spill over within performance strata—e.g., the role of social-network homophily, social comparison, and signaling among similar workers.
abstract: This study proposes a unified, dynamic framework based on turnover event theory to evaluate the effects of dismissals, layoff announcements, and voluntary turnover on subsequent work-unit voluntary turnover. Applying our approach to 1,620 retail stores over 22 months, we show that modeling exit events as a dynamic and interdependent system adds to our ability to predict subsequent human capital outflow. Dismissals had the weakest total effect on subsequent voluntary turnover, layoff announcements had the strongest and most immediate effects, and voluntary turnovers had moderate but lasting effects. We also find that these three exit reasons each exhibit a distinct pattern of subsequent turnover intensity and longevity. Based on characteristics of work units in our setting, our results correspond to a cumulative worker-level average effect of 0.17 quits following dismissals, 0.23 quits following quits, and 2.2 quits following a layoff. We find that these "multiplier" effects are concentrated among workers of similar performance: high performer exits beget high performer quits, just as low-performer exits beget low-performer quits. Our findings suggest analyses offering individual-level estimates of turnover will generally underestimate the broader, work-unit level consequences of individual exit events on similar workers.
apa_citation: Sajjadiani, S., Kammeyer-Mueller, J., & Benson, A. (2023). Who is leaving and why? The dynamics of high-quality human capital outflows. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(6), 1929–1953. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.1327

title: “Can I Sell You Avocadoes and Talk to You About Contraception?” “Well, It Depends Which Comes First”: Anchor Roles and Asymmetric Boundaries
methods: Inductive qualitative grounded-theory study using 53 one-on-one interviews and 10 focus groups across four waves (2016-2021) with 73 Tanzanian peer education agents; supplemented by clinic-staff and HealthOrg interviews, on-site observations, and 29 internal documents. Coded for boundary permeability and flexibility on 216 interrole-boundary instances using a Gioia-style first-order/second-order/aggregate-dimension structure with axial coding.
industry: Self-employed petty trading and subsistence farming combined with a volunteer community role as peer education agents (PEAs) providing family planning counseling for an INGO (HealthOrg)
country: Tanzania (Dar es Salaam, Mara, Mbeya, Morogoro, Mwanza, Njombe, and other regions)
units: Individual Tanzanian peer education agents who simultaneously held a self-employment work role and a family-planning community role
n_sample: 73 PEA volunteers (53 one-on-one interviews of 42 participants; 10 focus groups with 40 participants; 9 overlapping); 216 coded interrole-boundary occurrences for 24 participants
authors: Shulist, P.; Rivera-Santos, M.; Kistruck, G. M.; Nguni, W.
theories: boundary theory (Ashforth, Kreiner, & Fugate, 2000; Nippert-Eng, 1996); role theory (Biddle, 1979, 1986; Katz & Kahn, 1978); work-family/work-work boundary literature (Allen, Cho, & Meier, 2014; Dumas & Sanchez-Burks, 2015)
keywords: Global South; Tanzania; anchor role; asymmetric boundaries; interrole boundaries; multirole interactions; peer education agents; role contrasts; role theory; work-community roles
research_question: What type of interrole boundary do individuals in the Global South construct between contrasting work and community roles, given that role contrasts predict strong boundaries while the dense networks and poverty of Global South contexts predict weak ones?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: Role contrasts (script leeway, knowledge intensity, other- vs. self-interested orientation); boundary strength (permeability and flexibility); the novel concept of "anchor role" — the role that initially sets the stage for a specific social interaction.
- Triggering conditions: A multirole interaction is triggered when role holders have both the ability (dense, overlapping social networks; co-located work and community roles) and the motivation (poverty, time scarcity, care for the community role) to bring two roles together with the same person.
- Practices: When anchored in the work (self-employment) role at one's place of business, role holders enacted permeable, flexible boundaries — freely surfacing the PEA role during work interactions. When anchored in the PEA role (e.g., at the clinic or during community education), role holders enacted strong, narrow boundaries and refrained from work-related behaviors.
- Emergent outcomes: Asymmetric (in)compatibility between two highly contrasted roles, producing relationally embedded asymmetric boundaries within the same relationship — strong when anchored in the community role, weak when anchored in the work role.
- Boundary conditions: Asymmetry emerges when (a) role holders have ability and motivation for multirole interactions and (b) role contrasts produce divergent rather than symmetric (in)compatibility between the two roles.

The process logic departs from existing boundary theory by showing that highly contrasted roles need not be uniformly incompatible. A socioeconomic backdrop of poverty, time scarcity, and dense overlapping networks creates a constant integrative pressure to combine roles, but whether that pressure yields a weak boundary depends on the anchor role: when the anchor role's expectations are relatively simple and self-interested (work), bringing in the other-interested community role creates limited expectation violation; when the anchor role is highly scripted, knowledge-intensive, and other-interested (the PEA role), bringing in work-related behaviors triggers norm violations, confusion, and doubt, leading to a strong boundary.
theoretical_contribution: The paper contributes to role theory and boundary-setting by (1) showing that role contrasts do not always render two roles mutually incompatible — contrasts can produce *asymmetric* (in)compatibility; (2) introducing the construct of the "anchor role" and showing that it has a direct, substantial influence on whether an interaction stays single-role or becomes multirole; and (3) theorizing that relationally embedded asymmetric boundaries are not unique to the Global South but extend to work-family (especially family business), work-work (e.g., nurse-manager), and friendship-coworker pairings whenever ability, motivation, and divergent role contrasts coexist.
practical_implication: The findings highlight that combining community and work roles imposes substantial tensions on individuals living in poverty that international NGOs often overlook, because INGOs focus on their donor agreements and ignore that time spent on volunteer roles directly subtracts from livelihood income. Designers of community-health and similar volunteer programs should seek positive spillovers between the community and work roles (e.g., selecting volunteers whose work has some alignment with the community role) and avoid scaling community programs without acknowledging interrole tensions.
limitations: Connections to participants were made through the partner INGO HealthOrg, which may have introduced selection biases despite efforts to diversify by gender, location, and experience. The three observed role contrasts all worked to constrain the PEA role rather than the self-employment role, leaving open the possibility that in other pairings contrasts could constrain the work role instead. The strength of dyadic relationships was not directly measured and may moderate the magnitude of asymmetry, and the study does not theorize how specific role contrasts differentially impact permeability versus flexibility.
future_research: Future work should investigate the magnitude (not just existence) of asymmetric boundaries, including how strong dyadic relationships might attenuate or amplify them; examine settings with "competing" role contrasts that point to opposing anchor-role asymmetries (e.g., a financial planner volunteering at an animal shelter); explore whether ambiguity about which role anchors an interaction produces distinct boundary dynamics; and theorize how specific role contrasts (e.g., knowledge intensity, professionalism) selectively impact the "when" versus "where" dimensions of boundary flexibility.
abstract: Role theory generally predicts that when the demands and norms of two roles are highly contrasted, individuals will construct a strong boundary to separate the roles. However, such predictions are grounded primarily in the Global North, emphasizing role pairings including “work–family” and hybrid “work–work.” Comparatively, the Global South is characterized by a lack of public services that creates a highly contrasted, highly salient, and yet understudied role pairing—“work–community.” Additionally, the socioeconomic features of the Global South (e.g., dense and overlapping community networks, financial poverty) call into question whether existing predictions surrounding boundary strength are likely to hold. We conducted a qualitative study of 73 Tanzanian participants who had both a self-employed work role and a family planning counselor community role. We found that highly contrasted roles can be simultaneously perceived as both incompatible and compatible. Specifically, the boundaries we observed were neither uniformly strong nor weak, but rather of asymmetric strength: strong when a social interaction was anchored in the community role, but weak when anchored in the work role. The specific role contrasts we identify, and the importance of role anchoring we observe, have important implications for role theory and boundary-setting more broadly.
apa_citation: Shulist, P., Rivera-Santos, M., Kistruck, G. M., & Nguni, W. (2023). “Can I sell you avocadoes and talk to you about contraception?” “Well, it depends which comes first”: Anchor roles and asymmetric boundaries. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(6), 1768-1802. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1821

title: Calling Oneself and Others In: Brokering Identities in Diversity Training
methods: Inductive qualitative study combining ethnography and grounded theory ("methodological bricolage") over 18 months at a large private Midwestern university's diversity training program. Data: ~75 hours of ethnographic observation, 41 semi-structured interviews with 36 participants (ICT staff, consultant, experienced/beginner/student peer trainers), and trainer documentation (guides, slides, handouts). Open and focused coding with two coders, axial coding for relationships, member checks, and peer debriefing.
industry: Higher education (organization-wide diversity training program at a large private Midwestern university)
units: Diversity trainers (volunteer staff, faculty, and student peer trainers) and ICT program staff/consultant
n_sample: 36 participants across 41 semi-structured interviews (plus ~75 hours of observation)
authors: Sugiyama, Keimei; Ladge, Jamie J.; Bilimoria, Diana
theories: cultural brokerage (Jang, 2017; Press, 1969); identity work (Snow & Anderson, 1987; Caza, Vough, & Puranik, 2018); self-efficacy (Bandura, 1977, 1989)
keywords: brokering identities; brokering self-efficacy; calling in; cultural brokerage; diversity training; identity work; social justice-oriented training
research_question: How do trainers draw from their own identities to bridge across differences in demographic and cultural identities within social justice-oriented training contexts?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: trainer identity awareness, brokering identities, diversity repertoire, brokering self-efficacy
- Process phases: (1) Calling oneself in — marshaling identity resources and managing personal risks; (2) Modeling identity work — exemplifying brokering for trainees; (3) Calling others in — accommodating others' identities and encouraging perspective-taking
- Triggering conditions: emotionally charged moments, trainee resistance, offensive remarks, intercultural and demographic differences in the training room
- Emergent outcomes: enriched diversity repertoire, enhanced brokering self-efficacy, refined identity awareness extending beyond the training context
- Boundary conditions: trainers' own privileged/marginalized identities shape personal risks managed; intersectional identities differentially expose trainers

Trainers approach diversity training as cultural brokers who deliberately deploy their own privileged and marginalized identities to bridge across differences. The process is reciprocal and recursive: calling oneself in (drawing on personal stories while regulating triggers and vulnerabilities) is mirrored by calling others in (accommodating trainees' starting points and inviting perspective-taking), with modeling identity work as the connective tissue. Repeated practice builds a stockpile of stories and tactics (diversity repertoire) that strengthens brokering self-efficacy, which feeds back into sharpened identity awareness — explaining why trainers persist despite emotional costs and continue brokering beyond the training room.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reframes diversity trainers as cultural brokers whose identity work — not just their static identity characteristics — drives the training process, contributing to diversity training research that has previously treated trainer identities as categorical inputs to which trainees react. It elaborates identity work theory by showing that identities can be intentionally used to create identity-implicating experiences for others (not only to repair or secure one's own self-concept), and extends cultural brokerage by foregrounding what brokers themselves gain: brokering self-efficacy and refined identity awareness that sustain effort across cycles.
practical_implication: Organizations should recognize that calling in (versus calling out) requires modeling identity work and gives well-meaning participants an example to follow when they lack self-efficacy to engage across differences. Diversity trainers — especially underrepresented employees who bear disproportionate emotional labor — need ongoing debrief or affinity-group support and tangible organizational rewards rather than relying on volunteer goodwill.
limitations: The study is grounded in a single social justice-oriented training program at one large private Midwestern university, which constrains generalizability to other training designs, sectors, and national contexts. The first author's dual role as researcher and active trainer enabled deep ethnographic access but also introduces interpretive proximity that the authors mitigated through member checks, peer debriefing, and triangulation rather than eliminate.
future_research: Future work could examine how trainees draw on the trainer's example in their own brokering, how possible selves operate in diversity training, and how intersectional differences across trainers' identities shape the personal risks they manage and the identity-implicating experiences they create. Researchers could also study cultural brokering in adjacent diversity-oriented initiatives such as mentoring, coaching, and hiring, and follow when individuals engage and disengage from brokering across different roles.
abstract: Diversity training is a common initiative in organizations, yet also the focus of much debate. Legislative attempts have been made to eliminate diversity training, while research has presented mixed findings and contradictions. Although much is known about designs, pedagogies, and trainee outcomes of diversity training, we know far less about the diversity trainer, who bears the brunt of delivering training amid existing controversies. In this study, we examine the diversity trainer’s experience, bringing to light the important use of the trainer’s own identities in navigating the emotionally charged minefield of educating about bias and bridging across cultural and demographic differences. Through this qualitative investigation, we develop a grounded model that illustrates the process of brokering identities, a form of identity work diversity trainers engage in that involves their deliberate efforts to call themselves and others in during diversity training. We highlight the brokering self-efficacy trainers gain from their identity work, which helps to explain why diversity trainers continue to engage in the process of brokering identities despite the challenges and controversies they may face.
apa_citation: Sugiyama, K., Ladge, J. J., & Bilimoria, D. (2023). Calling oneself and others in: Brokering identities in diversity training. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(6), 1681–1710. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1579

title: Seeing the Forest and the Trees: Exploring the Impact of Inter- and Intra-Entrepreneurial Ecosystem Embeddedness on New Venture Creation
methods: Longitudinal panel analysis of 5,016 MSA-year observations (1994-2016) using linear models with multiple-way fixed effects (Stata reghdfe with state and year fixed effects; robust standard errors). Structural embeddedness operationalized as directional in-degree ego network density of inter-MSA VC firm investment ties; cultural embeddedness operationalized as corporate density and religiosity. Hypotheses 2-3 tested via interaction terms with the squared structural embeddedness term; robustness via fixed-effect Poisson, multilevel modeling, 2SLS using media coverage as instrument, alternative embeddedness measures, and sample splits at the inflection point.
industry: Cross-industry regional entrepreneurial ecosystems operationalized as U.S. metropolitan statistical areas (MSAs); inter-EE ties based on independent U.S. venture capital firm investments.
time_period: 1994-2016 (with 1990-1994 used to construct initial five-year moving-window network measures)
units: Metropolitan statistical areas (MSAs) as proxies for entrepreneurial ecosystems; 272 MSAs across 23 years
n_sample: 5,016 MSA-year observations
authors: Xu, L.; Yang, S.; Liu, Y.; Newbert, S. L.; Boal, K.
theories: double embeddedness (Baker & Faulkner, 2009); entrepreneurial ecosystems (Stam, 2015; Spigel, 2017; Wurth, Stam, & Spigel, 2022); social network theory / structural embeddedness (Granovetter, 1985; Uzzi, 1997, 1999; Gulati, 1998)
keywords: corporate density; cultural embeddedness; double embeddedness; entrepreneurial ecosystems; inter-EE network; intra-EE; new venture creation; religiosity; structural embeddedness; venture capital mobility
research_question: Whether, how, and why does an entrepreneurial ecosystem’s position within the larger network of EEs — together with its internal cultural features — shape EE-level new venture creation? Specifically, how do inter-EE structural embeddedness and intra-EE cultural embeddedness (corporate density, religiosity) jointly determine regional new venture creation?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Network size, elite and second-tier universities, regional unemployment rate, regional income per capita, region heat (VC-backed IPOs), state and year fixed effects.

The central mechanism is double embeddedness. As an EE becomes more structurally embedded in the inter-EE network, it gains access to valuable, tacit inflowing resources (knowledge, referrals, business models), increasing new venture creation up to an optimal point; beyond that point, inflows become redundant and competition rises, depressing new venture creation — yielding an inverted-U relationship. Corporate density steepens this curve because public companies act as resource integrators that magnify the value of inflowing resources via spawning and spillovers, then constrain novelty at high embeddedness. Religiosity flattens the curve because intra-EE social interaction creates localized trust and access to private (faith-based) resources, reducing reliance on inflowing resources at both low and high levels of structural embeddedness.
theoretical_contribution: First, the paper applies a double embeddedness framework to EE research, showing that EE-level new venture creation depends not only on internal EE characteristics but also on the inter-EE network of ties — directly answering Wurth et al.’s (2022) call to study the “forest” alongside the “trees.” Second, it advances embeddedness theory by modeling a nonmonotonic, inverted-U effect of structural embeddedness moderated by two dimensions of cultural embeddedness, integrating prior linear/independent treatments into a unified contingent model and identifying a “Goldilocks” zone of inter-EE ties. Third, by integrating network and entrepreneurship literatures it provides a contextualized network view that scholars can apply to other regional-level entrepreneurial outcomes.
practical_implication: Policy-makers seeking to stimulate regional entrepreneurship should justify policies that attract extra-regional VC investment — especially in EEs with many public companies, where the benefits of inflowing resources are magnified, and to a lesser degree in highly religious EEs where such benefits are muted. Policy-makers should monitor returns to external VC and cap incentives to avoid over-embedding their EEs beyond the optimal level. The best policy considers both the forest (inter-EE ties) and the trees (intra-EE features) and identifies the EE-specific combination of factors that maximizes new venture creation.
limitations: The study operationalizes EEs as MSAs, which is one of several possible conceptualizations (e.g., small towns, incubators, university systems, technology hubs), so the results are influenced by this choice. It relies on VC firm mobility to capture inter-EE ties, even though other resource flows (board members, migrants, musicians, etc.) may also matter. Despite a 2SLS analysis using media coverage as an instrument, reverse causality between new venture creation and VC inflows cannot be ruled out entirely. The findings rest on U.S. data and an assumption of geographic inertia in entrepreneurs’ location choice, limiting generalizability.
future_research: Future work should define and measure EEs in alternative ways (small towns, incubators, university systems, technology hubs) to test whether the inter- and intra-EE effects on new venture creation hold in other contexts. Scholars should explore novel types of inter-EE ties (e.g., flows of board members, migrants, knowledge, organizational practices) and richer data sets to assess whether different resource flows generate distinct effects. Researchers should examine how the documented network-based mechanisms operate in other countries, at other levels of analysis, and for other regional outcomes (product development, scaling, customer reach), and should investigate cross-EE founding decisions.
abstract: Research on entrepreneurial ecosystems (EEs) has tended to focus on the role that characteristics internal to the EE play in determining EE-level outcomes. Notwithstanding the insights that academics, policy-makers, and entrepreneurs have gleaned from these studies, prior research has yet to explain whether, how, and why these outcomes might also be impacted by an EE’s position within the larger network of EEs. Given broad acceptance for the important role that networks play in facilitating entrepreneurship at the firm level, we contend that adopting a network-based view of EEs may also help predict and explain aggregate entrepreneurial outcomes at the EE level. Specifically, we adopt a double embeddedness lens to examine the impact of both inter-EE (i.e., structural embeddedness) and intra-EE (i.e., cultural embeddedness) factors on EE-level new venture creation. Using a longitudinal sample of regional data in the United States from 1994 to 2016, we develop and test hypotheses where the relationship between structural embeddedness and new venture creation follows an inverted “U” shape that is itself moderated by cultural embeddedness. We conclude by discussing how these findings inform theory and practice in this area.
apa_citation: Xu, L., Yang, S., Liu, Y., Newbert, S. L., & Boal, K. (2023). Seeing the forest and the trees: Exploring the impact of inter- and intra-entrepreneurial ecosystem embeddedness on new venture creation. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(6), 1954-1982. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0791
