title: The Effects of Between-Group Pay Dispersion
methods: Two multiwave field studies plus a supplementary qualitative study. Study 1: multilevel path analysis (Mplus 8.3, MLR estimator, TYPE=TWOLEVEL with province-mean centering) on store-level objective pay data plus multisource surveys (employees, group leaders, store managers, archives); BGPD and WGPD measured via coefficient of variation. Study 2: branch-level path-analytic model on two-wave perceptual survey data with construct-level BGPD/WGPD measures; controls for pay communication, pay secrecy, interactional justice, and within- vs. between-group comparison tendencies. Qualitative study: thematic coding of interviews/focus groups with managers and group leaders to triangulate the explained-WGPD mechanism.
industry: Retail stores (Study 1; sales and support workgroups in a children's products, education, and entertainment chain) and bank branches (Study 2; service and marketing frontline groups in a large state-owned bank)
country: China (Study 1: 18 provinces of mainland China; Study 2: one Eastern Chinese city)
time_period: Study 1: January-February 2018 (objective pay and turnover from January 2018; multisource surveys in February 2018; performance over first half of 2018). Study 2: two-wave survey collected in January 2022.
units: Stores (Study 1) and bank branches (Study 2) as the focal unit of analysis; individual employees, group leaders, and store managers aggregated to the store/branch level
n_sample: Study 1: 205 stores comprising 4,234 employees, 1,899 group leaders, and 303 store managers. Study 2: 123 bank branches. Qualitative supplement: managers and group leaders interviewed at the focal retail company.
authors: Zhang, Z.; He, W.; Park, T.-Y.; Xing, Z.; Wu, X.
theories: distributive justice climate (Bowen, Gilliland, & Folger, 1999; Colquitt, 2001); explained pay dispersion theory (Shaw et al., 2002; Trevor, Reilly, & Gerhart, 2012); multiple social comparison theory (Brockner & Wiesenfeld, 1996; Festinger, 1954; Goodman, 1974); social identity and self-categorization theory (Turner, 1975; Turner, Hogg, Oakes, Reicher, & Wetherell, 1987); strategic HRM/workforce differentiation (Becker, Huselid, & Beatty, 2009; Lepak & Snell, 2002; Lepak et al., 2007)
keywords: between-group pay dispersion; distributive justice climate; horizontal pay dispersion; intergroup helping; multiple social comparison; organizational performance; social identity; voluntary turnover; within-group pay dispersion
research_question: When and how does between-group pay dispersion (BGPD) — pay-level differences between employees at the same job level but in different functional job groups — affect employees' shared distributive justice perceptions and, ultimately, organizational performance? Specifically, how does the size of explained within-group pay dispersion (WGPD) moderate the relationship between BGPD and the distributive justice climate, and how is this interaction transmitted through intergroup helping and voluntary turnover rates?
mechanism_summary: The process draws on multiple social comparison and self-categorization theories: employees compare both within-group (proximal in-group referents) and between-group (out-group referents) pay. Because BGPD reflects ambiguous job-value differences, employees use the legitimacy of explained WGPD as an external cue when judging BGPD's fairness. When BGPD and explained WGPD align (both large signal meritocracy/differentiation; both small signal harmony/collaboration), a "strong situation" emerges and a high distributive justice climate forms. That climate then increases intergroup helping and reduces voluntary turnover, lifting organizational performance.
theoretical_contribution: The paper refines horizontal pay dispersion theory by decomposing it into a job-based (BGPD) and a person-based (WGPD) form, and shows that the desirable effects of "explained" WGPD spill over to make BGPD perceived as fair when the two are aligned. It extends multiple social comparison and self-categorization theories from the individual level to a collective, group-to-group comparison process and validates a nested pay-comparison mechanism in which in-group (WGPD) cues frame the interpretation of out-group (BGPD) cues. It also contributes to strategic HRM and compensation literature by clarifying that workforce differentiation (large BGPD) and workforce harmony (small BGPD) can each be desirable when consistently aligned with WGPD and the organization's strategic intent.
practical_implication: Organizations should design pay structures with a uniform strategic orientation: increasing BGPD (to reflect job-related inputs) is fair and performance-enhancing only when each group also operates a dispersed, person-based WGPD that legitimately rewards employee KSAOs, tenure, and performance. Senior executives should communicate strategic intent to middle managers and align job-based fixed pay with performance-based variable pay. Managing BGPD legitimately may also help address gender pay gaps that arise from occupational segregation between groups.
limitations: Both studies were conducted in large Chinese firms with relatively explainable WGPD, which limits generalizability to small firms, contexts where WGPD is illegitimate, and individualistic cultures with different distributive norms. The collective-level WGPD measure (averaging group WGPD scores) may be sensitive to skewed within-store WGPD distributions. The Study 1 design is cross-sectional and cannot establish whether justice perceptions cause performance or vice versa.
future_research: Future work should test the model in small-sized companies and in Western/individualistic cultures, operationalize legitimate versus illegitimate WGPD separately following Trevor et al. (2012), and develop alternative collective-level WGPD measures robust to within-unit skewness. Researchers should also extend the theory to cross-functional or heterogeneous-job groups (e.g., top management teams, medical teams) where within-group differences themselves reflect job-function differences, and use longitudinal or experimental designs to establish causality. Pay-transparency settings offer another avenue: how BGPD effects change before and after pay disclosure.
abstract: Although allocating resources (e.g., pay) between different job groups is a critical strategic decision, relevant research is limited. Focusing on the form of pay allocation across job groups, we consider the organizational consequences of between-group pay dispersion (BGPD). Building on the multiple social comparison literature, we propose that employees construe both large and small BGPD as fair when it is aligned with the size of explained within-group pay dispersion (WGPD). We also propose that the interaction effect of BGPD and WGPD on the distributive justice climate be extended to organizational performance via intergroup helping and employee stability (voluntary turnover rates). We present supportive evidence of the hypotheses using two multiwave studies (Study 1: 205 retail stores located in China and comprising 4,234 employees, 1,899 group leaders, and 303 senior managers, featuring objective pay data; Study 2: 123 bank branches of a large state-owned bank in China) and a supplementary qualitative study.
apa_citation: Zhang, Z., He, W., Park, T.-Y., Xing, Z., & Wu, X. (2023). The effects of between-group pay dispersion. *Academy of Management Journal*, 66(6), 1860-1895. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2021.0799

title: Longing for the Road Not Taken: The Affective and Behavioral Consequences of Forgone Identity Dwelling
methods: Three studies. Study 1: multi-wave, multi-source field study (four time periods, employee-coworker dyads) testing a moderated serial-mediation model via structural equation modeling in Mplus with bootstrapped indirect effects. Studies 2a and 2b: online randomized experiments using an experimental-causal-chain approach with writing-reflection manipulations, analyzed via ANOVA/ANCOVA.
industry: Employees across many occupations (e.g., social worker, truck driver, architect, nurse, engineer, accountant); occupationally diverse, not industry-specific
units: Employee-coworker dyads (Study 1); full-time individual employees (Studies 2a, 2b)
n_sample: 316 dyads (Study 1); 183 (Study 2a); 174 (Study 2b)
authors: Burgess, Rachel; Colquitt, Jason A.; Long, Erin C.
theories: cognitive-motivational-relational theory (Lazarus, 1991); identity construction / identity work (Ashforth & Schinoff, 2016; Alvesson & Willmott, 2002)
keywords: forgone identity dwelling; helping behavior; identity construction; job crafting; locus of control; longing; withdrawal behavior
research_question: Do employees continue to dwell on forgone professional identities—the occupational "roads not taken"—long after the identity construction process should have rendered them moot, and if so, what are the affective (longing) and behavioral (withdrawal, helping, job crafting) consequences of such dwelling?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on Lazarus's (1991) cognitive-motivational-relational theory, the authors argue that dwelling on a forgone professional identity triggers primary and secondary appraisals that produce longing—a secondary emotion blending sadness and happiness. The sadness component creates an avoidance action tendency (more withdrawal, less helping because the employee is distracted from the here and now), while the happiness/approach component motivates job crafting to recover unfulfilled values from the forgone path. Because internal-locus employees believe they can shape their own work lives, longing more strongly translates into job crafting for them, which in turn circulates back to reduce withdrawal and increase helping (a serial indirect effect). The field study and two experimental-causal-chain studies converge on these linkages.
theoretical_contribution: The paper challenges the "forward progress" assumption in identity construction theory by showing that "identity echoes" from forgone professional identities continue to shape employee reactions after a current work identity is established, representing a lingering incoherence such models assume is resolved. It extends Obodaru's (2012, 2017) work on alternative selves by introducing identity dwelling as the mechanism through which forgone identities exert influence, and it foregrounds an affective construct—longing—to complement the predominantly cognitive and qualitative identity construction literature, addressing its noted "methodological myopia."
practical_implication: Managers should recognize that employees increasingly carry forgone professional identities and dwell on "what could have been," and should surface these through mentoring, coaching, or developmental conversations. They can then craft work contexts—providing flexibility, autonomy, and delegating tasks aligned with employees' forgone identities—to channel longing toward beneficial job crafting. Locus-of-control training that cultivates a more internal mindset may further encourage positive behavioral reactions to forgone identities.
limitations: Participants may have faced multiple forks in the road and thus multiple forgone identities, but only the most salient was assessed, so findings may miss nuance. The forgone identity dwelling and longing measures were created for this study and need continued use to establish broader construct validity. There may also be reciprocal causation in the dwelling–longing relationship, since cognition and emotion are highly integrated.
future_research: Researchers could examine dwelling on other selves (past, ideal, ought selves) and the cognitive—not just affective—consequences of identity dwelling, drawing on work on repetitive thought. They should probe reciprocal causality between longing and job crafting (e.g., virtuous cycles versus disenchantment) using cross-lagged panel data, and consider how identification strength, occupational prestige, and domain-specific locus of control shape who dwells on forgone identities.
abstract: The literature on identity construction has argued that employees work to create coherent and distinctive identities that encapsulate individual characteristics and professional and organizational memberships. Models of identity construction have suggested that a past career decision—choosing one professional “road” while leaving behind a “road not taken”—should lose its salience over time. We challenged that consensus by introducing the construct of forgone identity dwelling—thinking about and reflecting on a professional path that could have been. Our field and experimental studies showed that employees did dwell on forgone professional identities, even when the decision events that resulted in them were years in the past. Drawing on cognitive-motivational-relational theory, we showed that forgone identity dwelling resulted in longing—a yearning for something missing. Such longing created a “double-edged sword” for the behavioral consequences of forgone identity dwelling. On the one hand, longing was associated with increased withdrawal behavior and decreased helping behavior because employees were distracted from the here and now. On the other hand, longing was associated with job crafting that in turn reduced those reactions—particularly for employees with an internal locus of control. We discuss the implications of our work for models of identity construction.
apa_citation: Burgess, R., Colquitt, J. A., & Long, E. C. (2022). Longing for the road not taken: The affective and behavioral consequences of forgone identity dwelling. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(1), 93–118. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0746

title: Applying Coleman’s Boat in Management Research: Opportunities and Challenges in Bridging Macro and Micro Theory
methods: Conceptual 'From the Editors' essay; develops editorial guidance by using Coleman's (1990) boat as an organizing framework and illustrating three theoretical pitfalls with published exemplars, not empirical data.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Cowen, Amanda P.; Rink, Floor; Cuypers, Ilya R. P.; Grégoire, Denis A.; Weller, Ingo
theories: Coleman’s boat / macro–micro mechanisms framework (Coleman, 1990; Hedström & Ylikoski, 2010); microfoundations (Felin, Foss, & Ployhart, 2015)
keywords: Coleman’s boat; aggregation; macro–micro bridging; microfoundations; multilevel theory; situational mechanisms; transformational mechanisms
research_question: What recurrent theoretical challenges prevent management researchers from successfully bridging the macro and micro domains, and how can Coleman's (1990) "boat" framework help authors specify the situational, action-formation, and transformational mechanisms that connect levels of analysis?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Coleman's boat organizes macro–micro linkages into four arrows — macro-level associations (Arrow 4), situational mechanisms (Arrow 1, macro-to-micro), action-formation mechanisms (Arrow 2, within micro), and transformational mechanisms (Arrow 3, micro-to-macro) — and sound cross-level theory must engage the situational and transformational arrows rather than rely on multilevel methods alone.
- Pitfall 1 — Multilevel methods versus multilevel theory: using a mix of macro and micro variables (or clustered errors/fixed effects) as the only "bridge" between levels without theorizing why the methods are needed.
- Pitfall 2 — Misapplication of micro theory: borrowing psychological constructs (e.g., social identity, imprinting, narcissism, anthropomorphizing) without attending to their central assumptions, trait–state distinctions, or whether they translate to the organizational level.
- Pitfall 3 — Insufficiently nuanced contextualization of micro theory: testing micro processes in abstract or experimental settings too detached from the macro organizational context that shapes them (Arrow 1).
- Pitfall 4 — Neglect of aggregation mechanisms: failing to specify the micro-to-macro aggregation logic (Arrow 3), despite equifinality and the non-additive nature of how individual behavior "sums up" to macro outcomes.

The essay's central process claim is that empirically combining macro and micro variables is insufficient: authors must theoretically articulate the situational mechanisms by which macro context shapes individual affect, behavior, choices, and cognition, and the transformational/aggregation mechanisms by which micro-level dynamics produce macro-level outcomes. Using Coleman's boat as an organizing device, the editors argue that explicating these mechanisms — and treating aggregation as an integral, equifinality-aware part of theorizing — is what turns boundary-spanning empirics into robust, integrated cross-level theory.
theoretical_contribution: The essay extends the use of Coleman's (1990) boat beyond merely motivating microfoundations research, showing it can guide bidirectional bridging (using macro perspectives to contextualize micro processes, and modeling how micro phenomena aggregate to macro outcomes) and can diagnose the specific theoretical elements — situational and transformational mechanisms — needed to connect levels. It synthesizes recurring cross-level pitfalls editors observe at AMJ into an actionable framework for building integrated macro–micro theory.
practical_implication: For authors and reviewers, the piece offers concrete guidance: do not substitute sophisticated multilevel methods for multilevel theory; engage the central assumptions of borrowed micro theories; contextualize micro processes within macro realities; and make aggregation logic explicit. The editors also suggest that realizing the promise of boundary-spanning work may require new approaches to doctoral training and macro–micro coauthor collaborations.
limitations: The intention is explicitly not to catalogue limitations of macro or micro research, and the essay focuses primarily on mechanistic cause–effect cross-level relationships, acknowledging that other cross-level forms (composition, compilation, clustering, mutual dependencies) receive less treatment.
future_research: The editors encourage authors to make aggregation an integral part of their theorizing, to extend (rather than merely apply) micro theories at the macro level, and to cross-fertilize the micro literature by introducing new contingency factors and collective/organizational outcomes; they also call for theorizing that connects meso-level dynamics to micro and macro levels.
abstract: Not reported in paper
apa_citation: Cowen, A. P., Rink, F., Cuypers, I. R. P., Grégoire, D. A., & Weller, I. (2022). Applying Coleman’s boat in management research: Opportunities and challenges in bridging macro and micro theory. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(1), 1–10. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.4001

title: Here Comes the Sun: The Impact of Incidental Contextual Factors on Entrepreneurial Resource Acquisition
methods: Mixed-methods, archival-primary. Study 1: binary logistic regression of Demo Day funding on interday change in sunshine across 1,335 startups (171 Demo Days), errors clustered at the cohort level, with city/sector/accelerator fixed effects and probit/LPM-OLS robustness. Study 2: randomized between-subjects mood-induction experiment (n=92) with three-step, Sobel, and bootstrap mediation tests.
industry: Early-stage / seed-stage technology startups graduating from accelerators (industry fixed effects across sectors)
country: Europe (~a dozen major cities); experiment used a US West-Coast accelerator pitch with online participants
units: Startups (Study 1); individual prospective investors / participants (Study 2)
n_sample: 1,335 startups across 171 Demo Days (archival); 92 participants (experiment)
authors: Dushnitsky, Gary; Sarkar, Sayan
theories: affect-as-information theory (Schwarz & Clore, 1983; Schwarz, 2012)
keywords: affect-as-information theory; entrepreneurial resource acquisition; incidental contextual factors; investor mood; seed-stage investment; sunshine; uncertainty
research_question: What explains seed-stage investment patterns, and what factors affect investors' decisions to fund highly uncertain startups? The paper asks specifically whether incidental contextual factors outside the investor–startup pair — namely a daily change in sunshine on Demo Day — systematically shape seed-stage resource acquisition.
mechanism_summary: Affect-as-information theory holds that people confuse incidental feelings (e.g., a sunshine-induced good mood) with integral feelings about the focal task, so that under intense uncertainty an investor's mood "tips the balance" toward more favorable subjective probability assessments of a startup. A sunnier Demo Day lifts investors' mood, which raises their evaluation of an otherwise opaque seed-stage venture and increases the likelihood of funding; the effect attenuates as uncertainty falls (mature startups, high-human-capital founders) because more objective information then displaces mood as the input to judgment.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces a third class of explanation for seed-stage investment patterns — incidental contextual factors situated outside the investor–startup pair — to complement the two dominant streams that locate variation in startup characteristics or investor traits. It demonstrates that funding-outcome variation can emerge even across a homogeneous pool of investors and startups, simply because some pairs are randomly exposed to contextual shocks, and it imports affect-as-information theory into entrepreneurship to specify mood as the mechanism, bounded by the level of uncertainty.
practical_implication: Rather than ignoring incidental contextual factors, investors and accelerators should heed their effect, since transient conditions such as weather accrue in a foreseeable way on funding decisions. While one cannot control the sun, accelerators could design Demo Days (e.g., refreshment choices or "walking" Demo Days) to manage investors' mood, and scholars/practitioners should collect data on the context in which pairs are situated, not only on startups and investors.
limitations: The arguments are calibrated to seed-stage investment and its intense uncertainty, so the affect-as-information logic need not extend to later-stage deals where a VC committee already knows the startup. The archival analysis captures funding outcomes for hundreds of accelerator graduates but lacks detailed data on the individual investors who attended, and the outcome is measured only as a binary funding variable.
future_research: Future work could trace individual investors' movements to directly map how incidental contextual factors shape their decisions, and examine whether daily sunshine operates through entrepreneurs as well as investors. It could also test generalizability to other geographies and other contextual factors (e.g., choice of refreshment), and explore richer outcome measures such as premoney valuation.
abstract: This paper focuses on investment decisions in nascent, seed-stage startups, where information is scarce and uncertainty is intense. Entrepreneurship literature has shown that investment is a function of the investor–startup pair; some studies have investigated startup characteristics, while others have focused on investor traits. We complement extant work by studying factors beyond the investor–startup pair; namely, incidental contextual factors such as changes in the physical environment. Drawing on affect-as-information theory, we conjecture that, in the face of intense uncertainty, sunnier days may affect investors' mood and result in a greater likelihood of investment. Archival and experimental analyses inform our predictions. The former utilizes proprietary data on 1,335 startups graduating from European accelerators. Every accelerator holds a "Demo Day," where startups pitch to investors. We find that graduating on a sunnier day increases the likelihood of investment. This effect is stronger under intense uncertainty, where (a) startups are nascent, or (b) founders have limited human capital. An experimental study where prospective investors evaluate a Demo Day pitch yields further insights. We find a positive sunshine–investment association, and show that the association is mediated by investors' mood. Our findings complement extant work and contribute insights regarding seed-stage investment decisions.
apa_citation: Dushnitsky, G., & Sarkar, S. (2022). Here comes the sun: The impact of incidental contextual factors on entrepreneurial resource acquisition. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(1), 66–92. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0128

title: “I Go Here…But I Don’t Necessarily Belong”: The Process of Transgressor Reintegration in Organizations
methods: Longitudinal, inductive, grounded-theory case study (Glaser & Strauss, 1965; Strauss & Corbin, 1998) of cadet transgressors at a U.S. military service academy; iterative open and axial coding of interviews plus Langley (1999) process narratives, supplemented by surveys, journals, and informal observation.
industry: Military service academy (officer training; strict honor code forbidding lying, cheating, or stealing)
units: Cadet transgressors who committed honor-code violations, plus their mentors and honor officers
n_sample: 23 cadets, 10 mentors, and 4 honor officers (111 interviews)
authors: Frey, Erin L.
theories: integration / membership and belonging (O’Reilly, Caldwell, & Barnett, 1989; Leary, 2010); liminality (Turner, 1967; Ibarra & Obodaru, 2016); organizational socialization (Van Maanen & Schein, 1978); reintegration (Goodstein, Butterfield, Pfarrer, & Wicks, 2014)
keywords: belonging; institutional belonging; integration; liminality; membership; relational belonging; transgressor reintegration
research_question: How do retained transgressors—organizational members who violated core values and so became "structural insiders but moral outsiders"—attempt to reintegrate and come to feel like full insiders again, and what makes them feel more, or less, reintegrated?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: A transgression decouples membership and belonging, throwing the retained transgressor into liminality (structural insider, moral outsider) and producing precarious membership and precarious belonging.
- Restoration attempts (membership): Compliance behaviors aimed at obtaining institutional endorsements to feel secure in perceptions of membership.
- Restoration attempts (belonging): Investment in relationships aimed at strengthening relational belonging, while institutional belonging depends on endorsements from institutional actors.
- Emergent outcome: Belonging splits into relational belonging and institutional belonging; both, plus restored membership, are necessary for high reintegration.
- Boundary condition: Because belonging restoration depends on positive perceived interactions with many actors, transgressors often restore membership but not belonging, so high reintegration is rare (only 3 of 23 cadets).

Frey advances a process-with-variance model in which reintegration is an intrapsychic, perceptual process driven by transgressors’ own interpretations of interactions rather than by objective, triangulated behaviors. Transgressors use distinct logics—compliance to restore membership versus relational investment to restore belonging—and because lack of restoration in even one element (membership, relational belonging, or institutional belonging) blocks full reintegration, feeling highly reintegrated is difficult to achieve even in resource-intensive programs.
theoretical_contribution: The study conceptualizes transgressor reintegration from the transgressor’s own perspective, foregrounding its liminal starting point and the driving forces (restoring membership and belonging) rather than merely listing features of reintegration. It recasts integration from a static construct into a dynamic, perceptual, and negotiated process that can be formed, threatened, modified, and regained, and it differentiates belonging into relational and institutional types that are complements, not substitutes—offering new leverage for research on socialization, undersocialization, belonging, and nested relational phenomena.
practical_implication: To give employees a genuine second chance, managers must support both restoring perceptions of membership (e.g., bestowing institutional endorsements such as desirable assignments, promotions, and access to perks) and fostering belonging (e.g., coaching peers and supervisors on how to interact with and affirm the transgressor). Managers should also balance transgressor affirmation against good-standing members’ justice perceptions to avoid backlash.
limitations: Pre-violation integration data are retrospective because first interviews occurred after violations, so cadets’ recollections of initial integration may be inaccurate or colored by present feelings. The author could not collect data from transgressors’ interaction partners (personal acquaintances or institutional actors), leaving open whether transgressors’ perceptions are accurate, and the data are not fine-grained enough to definitively map specific actors to specific types of belonging.
future_research: Survey-based quantitative work could test whether transgressors’ recollections of pre-transgression integration are accurate and whether their perceptions of others’ treatment generally align with reality. Researchers could more systematically identify which types or features of organizational actors foster relational versus institutional belonging, and examine how the observed dynamics differ in organizations with little or no formal structure around transgressor reintegration.
abstract: When organizational members violate important organizational standards, they may face termination, or they may instead be retained by the organization and given a second chance. Retained transgressors experience the tension of liminality: they maintain their affiliation to the organization, making them structural insiders, but they have committed a transgression, making them moral outsiders. How might transgressors attempt to reintegrate and feel like full organizational insiders once again? And what makes transgressors feel more, or less, reintegrated? Previous work has studied reintegration from victims’ or third-parties’ perspectives, but little is known about transgressor reintegration. To build theory on transgressor reintegration, I studied transgressors at a military service academy. Through waves of qualitative data collection and inductive analyses, I find that transgressions threaten transgressors’ integration, leading transgressors to feel precarious in their perceptions of membership and their feelings of belonging. Transgressors attempt to restore both elements, but use distinct approaches for each. Because belonging restoration requires positive interactions with many organizational actors, transgressors can—and, in my data, frequently do—experience restoration of membership but not belonging. Therefore, it may be relatively rare for transgressors to feel highly reintegrated following transgressions, even in organizations that devote considerable resources to reintegration.
apa_citation: Frey, E. L. (2022). “I go here…but I don’t necessarily belong”: The process of transgressor reintegration in organizations. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(1), 119–157. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0251

title: Joining Forces: How Can Founding Members’ Prior Experience Variety and Shared Experience Increase Startup Survival?
methods: Quantitative; discrete-time hazard model of startup failure estimated with a linear probability model (and complementary log-log robustness), standard errors clustered by industry, on panel data from the U.S. Census LEHD employer-employee linked dataset.
industry: Five three-digit NAICS technological manufacturing industries (fabricated metal product; machinery; computer and electronic product; electrical equipment, appliance, and component; transportation equipment manufacturing)
time_period: Startups created 2000-2006; survival observed through 2008 (LEHD coverage from 1990)
units: Startups (and startup-year observations); founding teams of at least two employees
n_sample: ≈6,000 startups and ≈31,000 startup-year observations (rounded per U.S. Census rules)
authors: Honoré, Florence
theories: organizational knowledge theory (Grant, 1996; Nelson & Winter, 1982; Nonaka, 1994); social cognition theory (Hayes-Roth, 1977; Ward, 2004)
keywords: founding teams; knowledge combination; knowledge transfer; routines; shared experience; spinouts; startup survival; within-founder experience variety
research_question: How can founding teams combine two distinct experience-based advantages—within-founder experience variety and founders’ shared experience from the same industry incumbent—to reduce the likelihood of startup failure, thereby reconciling the experience-variety and spinout literatures?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on social cognition theory and organizational knowledge theory, the paper argues that founders who frequently changed jobs accumulate diverse, loosely connected knowledge components that fuel innovation but are costly to integrate, so adding such founders alone raises failure risk (H1b supported). Founders who jointly leave the same incumbent transfer a shared knowledge base and well-oiled routines that lower failure risk (H2 supported). When the two combine, shared routines supply common ground that lets the team integrate the variety founders’ components, so the interaction is negative—startups are least likely to fail when within-founder variety is paired with across-team shared experience (H3 supported).
theoretical_contribution: The study bridges the experience-variety and spinout literatures by integrating knowledge-combination and knowledge-transfer mechanisms under a single organizational-knowledge-and-routines framework, introducing the construct of within-founder experience variety (variety encapsulated in a single founder’s career rather than spread across the team). It shows that the level at which human-capital resources are measured matters: complementarities emerge only when variety is concentrated within individual founders and the shared experience is specifically tied to a common industry incumbent, refining prior work that relied on teams’ total number of prior experiences or any industry experience.
practical_implication: Founders who frequently changed jobs before founding should team up with cofounders who jointly transition from the same industry incumbent; variety founders lower failure risk only when at least part of the team supplies shared knowledge and routines. Practically, founders with a variety of experiences are associated with lower startup failure when other founders share at least two years of prior work in the same industry incumbent, helping resource-constrained teams assemble complementary human capital.
limitations: The findings derive from U.S. employer–employee LEHD data covering technological manufacturing startup survival from 2000 to 2008, so implications should be treated with caution in other institutional and economic contexts. The LEHD excludes pre-incorporation, pre-hiring, and self-employment stages, so average survival is likely overstated and implications apply only to "advanced" startups. Mutual selection among team members cannot be fully ruled out, and the data do not capture each member’s functional role or non-employment shared work relationships.
future_research: Future work could model the mutual selection among all team members using newer techniques such as many-sided matching models to better isolate selection effects on startup performance. Building new surveys (e.g., with the U.S. Census Bureau) that span all entrepreneurial stages from idea inception to first hires would broaden generalizability, and linking team members’ occupations and broader forms of shared work relationships could reveal additional human-capital complementarities within founding teams.
abstract: In extant work, two arguments have emerged on the relationship between founders’ experience and startup survival. First, founders’ diverse experiences should foster startup survival because the combination of various experience-based knowledge components drives the needed innovation. Second, founders’ shared experience in an incumbent firm in the industry of the startup should positively relate to startup survival because of the transfer of shared knowledge and routines. This paper reconciles both streams of research by proposing that startups can combine both types of experience-based advantages when founding teams are composed of founders who individually possess a variety of firm experiences and other founders who jointly transition from the same industry incumbent. Using a sample of startups in technological manufacturing industries, I test the direct effect of the two types of experiences and their complementarity in decreasing the likelihood of startup failure. The results show that such complementarities exist so that startups are less likely to fail when their founding teams combine within-founder experience variety and across-team shared experience. I discuss the implications of these findings for startup survival and founding team literature streams.
apa_citation: Honoré, F. (2022). Joining forces: How can founding members’ prior experience variety and shared experience increase startup survival? *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(1), 248–272. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.1386

title: There Is a Time to Be Creative: The Alignment between Chronotype and Time of Day
methods: Three within-person field studies testing a moderated-mediation (synchrony) model: a quasi-experimental repeated-measures field study (morning vs. late-afternoon sessions), a day-reconstruction study across the workweek, and a one-day experience sampling study. Multilevel (Level-1 occasions nested in Level-2 persons) analyses estimated in Mplus 8.2 with grand-mean-centered cross-level moderator (chronotype) and conditional indirect-effects models for mediation.
industry: Studies 1 & 3: employees from companies in diverse industries (Study 1: mechanical engineering, health care, education, public administration, etc.); Study 2: full-time employees in creative jobs (e.g., architects, designers, artists, creative directors).
country: Not reported in paper (Studies 1–2 recruited via students approaching their personal network and companies 'in the region'; Study 3 recruited via Amazon's Mechanical Turk).
time_period: Not reported in paper (within-day designs; data collection year not stated). Measurement windows: morning vs. late-afternoon sessions (Studies 1 and 3) and performance episodes across the workday from 6 a.m. to 9 p.m. (Study 2).
units: Individual employees (Level 2) with repeated measurement occasions / performance episodes (Level 1).
n_sample: Study 1: N = 260 employees (520 occasions); Study 2: N = 238 employees (849 days, 2,716 performance episodes); Study 3: N = 319 employees (638 matched occasions).
authors: Kühnel, Jana; Bledow, Ronald; Kiefer, Markus
theories: broaden-and-build theory (Fredrickson, 2013); embodied cognition perspective; two-process model of sleep regulation (Borbély, 1982)
keywords: chronotype; circadian rhythm; creative self-efficacy; creativity; embodied cognition; positive mood; synchrony effect; time of day
research_question: Does within-day fluctuation in employees’ creativity depend on the alignment between their chronotype and the time of day (a "synchrony effect"), and do positive mood and creative self-efficacy operate as the affective and cognitive mechanisms transmitting this chronobiological influence on creativity?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on Borbély's two-process model of sleep regulation and an embodied cognition perspective, the paper argues that when the time of day matches a person's chronotype, the executive functions that enable creativity are most accessible, allowing the person to form remote associations and recombine elements in novel ways. This synchrony state is theorized to elevate both positive mood (an affective pathway, via the circadian reward-motivation system) and creative self-efficacy (a cognitive pathway, as awareness that one can access one's creative potential), which in turn promote creativity. Empirically, chronotype reliably moderated the time-of-day effect on creativity, but positive mood mediated only in Studies 2 and 3 and creative self-efficacy mediated for earlier (not later) chronotypes, indicating the mediating processes—especially for late chronotypes—are more complex than a simple parallel-mediation model implies.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends creativity research by showing that within-person variation in creativity and its cognitive and affective antecedents are grounded in the current state of the body, calling for theories of creativity to attend to the mind–body interface from an embodied cognition framework. It contributes to the two-process model of sleep regulation by expanding its scope to creativity in a high-stakes work context and specifying affective (positive mood) and cognitive (creative self-efficacy) mechanisms, and shows that chronobiological drivers explain within-day fluctuations in work performance over and above day-specific sleep quality and duration.
practical_implication: Managers should recognize that employees' ability to be creative is not a time-invariant trait but varies over the day and differs across individuals' chronotypes. Organizations should give employees autonomy to schedule creativity-demanding tasks for their state of synchrony (shielding that time from low-value meetings) while assigning routine tasks to other times; employees also need awareness of their own chronobiological preferences to make "wise" scheduling decisions.
limitations: Creativity was operationalized via an objective alternative uses test and self-rated creativity, and the self-report measures may be subject to social-desirability and recollection biases (Study 2 used day reconstruction). Extreme chronotypes were underrepresented because start- and end-of-work times had to be set as participation requirements, yielding a conservative test, and Studies 1 and 3 took only two daily "snapshots" of creativity rather than continuously monitoring its temporal dynamics. The studies also neglected the social environment in which employees are creative.
future_research: Future work should disentangle and directly measure the executive functions assumed to underlie the synchrony effect, examine why late chronotypes' creativity depends less on positive mood (e.g., links to thinking/decision-making styles and right-hemisphere processing), and capture the temporal dynamics of creativity across the entire workday without relying on employee recollection. Researchers should also extend measurement to earlier and later times of day, integrate the joint impact of the synchrony effect and sleep, and study how embodied processes interact with the social context.
abstract: We examine the influence of chronobiological processes on creativity, specifically the influence of a person’s chronotype. Chronotype refers to the setting of a person’s biological clock that gives rise to a distinctive pattern of sleep habits and preferred diurnal activity. We propose a synchrony effect and predict that people are creative when the external clock is aligned with their internal, biological clock. According to our model, positive mood and creative self-efficacy act as affective and cognitive mechanisms of this synchrony effect. We present three studies that test our theorizing: a quasi-experimental field study with 260 employees, a day-reconstruction study with 238 employees, and a one-day experience sampling study with 319 employees. Across the studies, we find that chronotype moderates the effect of time of day on creativity. Overall, late chronotypes were more creative in the late afternoon and early chronotypes tended to be more creative in the morning. The alignment between chronotype and time of day also gave rise to positive mood and creative self-efficacy; however, the studies provide only partial support for the hypothesis that positive mood acts as a mediating mechanism. We discuss the implications of these findings against the background of an embodied cognition perspective on creativity.
apa_citation: Kühnel, J., Bledow, R., & Kiefer, M. (2022). There is a time to be creative: The alignment between chronotype and time of day. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(1), 218–247. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0020

title: The Distinct Effects of Wealth- and CSR-Oriented Shareholder Unrest on CEO Career Outcomes: A New Lens on Settling Up and Executive Job Demands
methods: Longitudinal observational panel of S&P 1500 firms (2005-2016). Generalized estimating equations (GEEs) predict incumbent CEO pay change, dismissal, and voluntary departure; OLS predicts new CEO pay. Firm-level clustered robust standard errors, industry and year fixed effects; 2SLS instrumental-variable and ITCV robustness checks for endogeneity.
industry: Standard and Poor's 1500 public corporations across industries (4-digit GICS), excluding interim CEOs
units: Firm-year observations (incumbent CEO models) and CEO succession events (new CEO pay model)
n_sample: 11,539 firm-years (pay change); 11,818/11,819 (dismissal); 10,085 (voluntary departure); 1,479 succession events (new CEO pay)
authors: Lee, Michelle K.; Gupta, Abhinav; Hambrick, Donald C.
theories: executive job demands theory (Hambrick, Finkelstein, & Mooney, 2005); settling up (Fama, 1980; Wowak, Hambrick, & Henderson, 2011)
keywords: CEO career outcomes; CEO compensation; CEO dismissal; corporate social responsibility; executive job demands; settling up; shareholder activism; shareholder unrest
research_question: How do two distinct forms of shareholder unrest—wealth-oriented and CSR-oriented—differentially affect CEOs' career outcomes (incumbent pay adjustments, dismissal, voluntary departure, and new-CEO pay), and which form is more consequential for boards versus CEOs themselves?
mechanism_summary: The paper positions settling up and executive job demands as "two sides of the same coin." Under settling up, boards make post hoc pay and retention decisions after observing shareholder unrest, sanctioning CEOs blamed for unrest on their watch; because CSR-oriented unrest arises in a domain where alternative performance metrics are few and noisy, it conveys more distinct information and so should provoke stronger board sanctions. Under executive job demands, both forms of unrest add quantitative and qualitative load that pushes incumbents to voluntarily depart and forces firms to pay successors a premium. Empirically, boards sanctioned CSR-oriented unrest (pay cuts and dismissal) but not wealth-oriented unrest, whereas CEOs voluntarily departed in response to wealth-oriented unrest but not CSR-oriented unrest—revealing a divergence in the indicators boards and CEOs weight. New-CEO pay rose equally with both forms of pre-succession unrest.
theoretical_contribution: The study elevates "shareholder activism" (the binary filing of a resolution) to "shareholder unrest"—a continuous, multifaceted construct capturing the scale and gravity of dissatisfaction—and develops a novel index to measure it. It advances settling-up theory by showing boards weight non-financial, CSR-related information more heavily than wealth-related information when its incremental informativeness is high, and it extends executive job demands theory by surfacing a board–CEO divergence in attended indicators and a predecessor–successor linkage in which inherited unrest buoys new-CEO pay.
practical_implication: Boards appear highly attentive to CSR-oriented shareholder dissatisfaction and willing to penalize CEOs for it through reduced pay and dismissal, so executives and directors should treat CSR-oriented unrest as strongly informative rather than background noise. Firms hiring into unrest-laden contexts should anticipate paying compensating premiums to attract qualified successors.
limitations: The design is observational, so causal inference rests on instrumental-variable (2SLS) and impact-threshold (ITCV) robustness checks rather than experimental control. The theorized informativeness and job-demands mechanisms are inferred rather than directly measured, and the sample is limited to large U.S. public corporations (S&P 1500), bounding generalizability.
future_research: Scholars could examine whether other publicly registered stakeholder assessments—media criticism, boycotts, Yelp/Glassdoor ratings—affect CEO labor-market outcomes, and whether CSR-oriented unrest becomes less informative as CSR metrics improve. Promising directions include longer-term career consequences of unrest, effects on directors themselves, and spillover effects on the CEOs of non-targeted neighboring firms.
abstract: Conceptualizing shareholder unrest as the aggregate scale and gravity of shareholder dissatisfaction with company practices, we examine the effects of two distinct types of shareholder unrest—wealth-oriented and corporate social responsibility (CSR)-oriented unrest—on CEOs’ career outcomes. Drawing on the concept of settling up, we hypothesize that CEOs are negatively sanctioned for both types of unrest in terms of reduced pay and increased likelihood of dismissal. Drawing on executive job demands theory, we argue that incumbent CEOs feel burdened by both types of unrest, leading them to voluntarily depart, and that new CEOs of unrest-laden firms receive larger pay packages as compensation for dealing with these inherited job demands. Overarching all these expectations, we argue that the effects of CSR-oriented unrest will be greater than the effects of wealth-oriented unrest, as CSR-oriented unrest occurs in a domain where alternative performance indicators are few and noisy, causing CSR-oriented unrest to be seemingly more meaningful. Testing our ideas on a longitudinal sample of U.S. corporations, we find considerable but not total support for our hypotheses, which we then interpret and discuss.
apa_citation: Lee, M. K., Gupta, A., & Hambrick, D. C. (2022). The distinct effects of wealth- and CSR-oriented shareholder unrest on CEO career outcomes: A new lens on settling up and executive job demands. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(1), 186–217. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1346

title: How Global Leaders Gain Power through Downward Deference and Reduction of Social Distance
methods: Inductive field study at a single multinational firm ("WorldTech"); primary qualitative data from 115 semi-structured interviews coded with the Gioia methodology in NVivo, triangulated with archival/secondary data; supplementary quantitative analysis on a random subset of 61 leaders using nonparametric Mann-Whitney U tests.
industry: Technology consulting (high-tech), single firm
country: United States (HQ); leaders based across 13 countries (Australia, Brazil, China, France, Germany, Italy, Japan, Korea, Mexico, Russia, Singapore, Spain, United States)
units: Top global leaders (interview informants); leader-level archival records
n_sample: 115 interviewed leaders; archival subset of 61 leaders
authors: Neeley, Tsedal; Reiche, B. Sebastian
theories: French and Raven's bases of power (1959); social distance theory of power (Magee & Smith, 2013); transformative learning theory (Mezirow, 1978, 1991)
keywords: downward deference; global leadership; positional power; social distance; yielding to subordinates' expertise
research_question: The paper examines three interrelated questions: (a) why, and under which conditions, do those with positional power defer to lower-ranking individuals; (b) what behaviors do they adopt to practice downward deference; and (c) what role does social distance play in enacting downward deference?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: Leaders hold higher positional power but recognize they have limited expertise, networks, and influence relative to local subordinates in nonnative markets.
- Antecedent (correlate): Deep previous experience in foreign contexts — total time spent abroad and cultural distance/novelty of those experiences — prompts learning and adaptation.
- Behavioral dimension 1 — Reducing social distance: seeking connection, earning trust, adjacent collaboration with subordinates.
- Behavioral dimension 2 — Yielding to subordinates' expertise: privileging subordinates' judgment, transferring influence, conforming to local hierarchical expectations.
- Emergent outcomes: Higher job performance ratings and more progressions into executive ranks for leaders who enacted downward deference.

Drawing on the social distance theory of power and transformative learning theory, the authors inductively develop a framework in which leaders who confront a salient expertise/knowledge deficit in culturally distant settings learn to lower their own position to become equal with subordinates. By reducing perceived social distance and ceding influence to local experts, leaders compensate for their diminished empathic accuracy and reliance on subordinate knowledge, which in turn supports goal accomplishment and their own career advancement.
theoretical_contribution: The study breaks from traditional social-psychology theories that predict the powerful expect upward-flowing deference and resist deferring downward, specifying when and why high-power actors instead defer to lower-ranking subordinates. It shows that positional power varies in its propensity to create social distance, that deep nonnative experience is a necessary condition for downward deference, and that downward deference can be both instrumentally (power-maintaining) and communally motivated — extending theory on power, social distance, international experience, and leadership.
practical_implication: Downward deference behaviors may be valuable to assess and promote in global organizations' talent selection, internal promotion, and development decisions. Offering developmental assignments in unfamiliar, nonnative settings can give future global leaders the deep experiences that enable them to learn and apply downward deference, and such deference may itself serve as a selection criterion for leadership roles.
limitations: The data capture only the leaders' perspective and not subordinates', so it is unknown how recipients perceived and responded to downward deference. The leaders represent an extreme case (highly senior, ~22-year tenures, within four levels of the CEO), limiting generalizability, and the design cannot establish causality between downward deference and the observed performance and promotion outcomes.
future_research: Future work should study subordinates' experience of downward deference, replicate the phenomenon with less senior leaders and at multiple hierarchical levels, and use experimental or systematically varied designs to establish causality and map the construct's nomological network. A further promising direction is the role of gender — whether women who practice downward deference face backlash — and how downward deference unfolds across more versus less masculine industries.
abstract: We theorize about how people with positional power enact “downward deference”—a practice of lowering oneself to be equal to lower-power workers—based on a study of 115 top global leaders at a large U.S. company. These leaders were charged with advancing organizational goals in foreign markets. We find that some leaders enacted downward deference when they recognized that they had less expertise, networks, and influence relative to their local subordinates. This manifested in two ways: (a) attempts to reduce social distance, which involved seeking connection, earning trust, and participating in adjacent collaboration with local subordinates, and (b) yielding to subordinates’ expertise by privileging their judgment, transferring influence to them, and conforming to their local hierarchical expectations. Our supplementary quantitative analyses showed that previous experiences in foreign cultures, both in terms of total time spent abroad and exposure to cultures that were distant from their own, correlated with adopting downward deference. Those leaders also had higher job performance ratings, and were promoted to higher executive levels over time, compared to their counterparts who did not practice downward deference. Our study expands our understanding of positional power, social distance, and the conditions under which actors practice downward deference.
apa_citation: Neeley, T., & Reiche, B. S. (2022). How global leaders gain power through downward deference and reduction of social distance. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(1), 11–34. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0531

title: What Changes after Women Enter Top Management Teams? A Gender-Based Model of Strategic Renewal
methods: Dynamic ordinary least squares (OLS) models on an unbalanced firm-year panel; first-differenced explanatory variables with industry fixed effects; TMT cognitions measured via computer-based content analysis (LIWC) of letters to shareholders; instrumental-variable and subsample (Chow test) approaches for mediation and moderation; SEM robustness checks.
industry: Multinational firms across multiple industries (food/beverages/tobacco, chemicals, pharmaceuticals, metals, electronics, machinery, automotive, software, telecommunications, etc.); European market leaders by sales
country: Firms headquartered in 20 OECD countries; about a third from the United States, the remainder largely European Union-based
units: Firm-years (top management team appointments)
n_sample: 1,911 firm-year observations on 163 firms; 2,771 TMT appointments, of which 276 (10%) female
authors: Post, Corinne; Lokshin, Boris; Boone, Christophe
theories: social identity theory (Riordan, 2000); strategic renewal (Agarwal & Helfat, 2009); upper echelons theory (Hambrick, 2007; Carpenter, Geletkanycz, & Sanders, 2004)
keywords: R&D; TMT change orientation; TMT cognitions; TMT risk-taking propensity; female TMT appointments; mergers and acquisitions; strategic renewal; top management teams; upper echelons theory
research_question: How and when do female (versus male) appointments into top management teams cause firms to change their approach to knowledge-related strategic renewal? The paper asks what changes after women enter TMTs, moving the female-TMT–firm-outcome literature from descriptive ("What is the link?") to explanatory ("How does the linkage occur?") by specifying time-dependent, within-firm mechanisms.
mechanism_summary: Female appointments act as catalysts: because female executives' life experiences and accession paths to the C-suite favor transformative, change-oriented values while their heightened scrutiny and career-derailment risks favor caution, their entry (but not men's) raises TMT change orientation and lowers TMT risk-taking propensity. These cognitive shifts then redirect renewal from buying (M&A) toward building (R&D). Integration conditions (female incumbency, small cohorts) determine whether the unique values of female appointees are voiced, heard, and incorporated, drawing on social identity theory's logic that similar in-group members are more readily integrated.
theoretical_contribution: The study makes three contributions to research on women in upper echelons, upper echelons theory (UET), and strategic renewal. First, it establishes shifts in TMT cognitions as a dynamic, within-firm mechanism linking female TMT appointments to strategic change, replacing static demographic-proxy models with a temporal model of antecedents of cognition. Second, it reconciles the apparent contradiction between women's higher risk aversion and their greater propensity for change by showing the two operate on distinct cognitive channels (risk-taking propensity → M&A; change orientation → R&D). Third, it extends UET and strategic-renewal research by identifying TMT integration as a boundary condition and offering a new temporal understanding of critical mass (combining the flow of new female appointees with the stock of female incumbents).
practical_implication: CEOs and boards should not treat appointing women as "pixie-dust" that automatically yields favorable outcomes; female appointees reshape strategy only when integrated into the TMT. Diversifying TMTs more substantially, attending to female incumbency, avoiding tokenism, and ensuring receptive team-leader gender beliefs make it more likely that female appointees' contributions are incorporated into real-time strategic decisions and that renewal shifts (more R&D, fewer M&A) materialize.
limitations: The model offers two non-exclusive explanations for cognition shifts (direct value transmission versus diversity-driven team-process change) that the data cannot disentangle. Threshold (diminishing-returns) estimates rest on small samples because female appointments to TMTs with existing female incumbents are rare, so they must be interpreted with caution. The study isolates only one appointee characteristic (gender), and its use of letters to shareholders as a proxy for TMT cognitions cannot fully parcel out impression-management content.
future_research: Future work could identify other dynamic models linking female entry into upper echelons to firm decisions via cognition shifts — for example, female appointments' effects on TMT moral sensitivities and corporate social responsibility, or on TMT affective tone. Researchers could probe the processes through which shifts occur (e.g., whether stereotypical risk ascriptions versus actual gender differences drive change), extend the reasoning to other appointee dimensions (race, age, national origin, social class), develop additional moderators (managerial discretion, sociocultural context, demographic fault lines), and disentangle cognition from impression management in shareholder letters.
abstract: The question of what changes when women enter upper-echelons teams has long frustrated upper echelons and gender researchers. We build on the dynamic strategic renewal literature, combine it with upper echelons theory insights, and integrate knowledge about female executives’ career strategies to theorize how and when female appointments into top management teams (TMTs) cause firms to change their approach to knowledge-related strategic renewal. In doing so, we reconcile the tension among extant mediating processes invoked to explain how female TMT representation might affect strategic decisions: change orientation and risk-taking propensity. Estimating a dynamic ordinary least squares model on panel data from 163 multinationals, we find that following female (but not male) TMT appointments, TMT cognitions shift, becoming more change oriented and less risk seeking. Subsequently, these TMT cognitive shifts cause a decrease in mergers and acquisitions and an increase in research and development. Our model of female TMT appointments as catalysts that cause shifts in TMT cognitions, which, in turn, redirect knowledge-related strategic renewal from a buying to a building approach, is a novel effort at advancing research on women at upper echelons to examine time-dependent, within-firm mechanisms linking women in upper echelons and firm outcomes.
apa_citation: Post, C., Lokshin, B., & Boone, C. (2022). What changes after women enter top management teams? A gender-based model of strategic renewal. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(1), 273–303. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.1039

title: OMG! My Boss Just Friended Me: How Evaluations of Colleagues’ Disclosure, Gender, and Rank Shape Personal/Professional Boundary Blurring Online
methods: Multi-study design: Study 1 archival analysis of a nationally representative Pew sample (correlations); Study 2 survey of employees across U.S. industries (scale correlations, EFA); Studies 3 and 4 are 2x2x3 between-subjects experiments manipulating disclosure, gender, and rank, analyzed with ANOVA / logistic regression and PROCESS bootstrapped mediation.
industry: Cross-industry; Study 2 sampled retail/wholesale, health care/social assistance, and leisure/hospitality
time_period: Study 1 uses Pew Research Center (2014) internet-use survey data; experimental studies fielded via MTurk/Lucid (online panels)
units: Working adults / employees (evaluating colleague friend-request scenarios)
n_sample: Study 1 n=586; Study 2 n=513; Study 3 n=614; Study 4 n=740
authors: Rothbard, Nancy P.; Ramarajan, Lakshmi; Ollier-Malaterre, Ariane; Lee, Serenity S.
theories: relational boundary-blurring framework; social cognition / person perception (warmth-competence); status characteristics (gender role expectations; symmetric vs. asymmetric rank)
keywords: boundary management; gender; online social networks; rank; relational boundary blurring; self-disclosure; warmth
research_question: How do employees’ evaluations of a colleague’s self-disclosure, gender, and rank — characteristics that convey warmth — shape their decisions to blur the personal/professional boundary by connecting as friends with that colleague online? The paper shifts boundary management from the focal employee to the relational partner, asking with whom employees choose to blur the boundary.
mechanism_summary: Drawing on social cognition and person perception research, the authors argue that people evaluate others first along warmth, and that more self-disclosure, female gender, and symmetric (peer) rank each signal warmth and benevolent intent, lowering the perceived relational risk of granting a colleague visible, permanent, less-tailored access to one's personal life online. Warmth thus mediates the disclosure effect consistently, while gender and rank operate more conditionally. The three-way interaction reflects that disclosure most strongly improves connection odds for female bosses, whose hierarchical status otherwise violates gender-role expectations, whereas male bosses of asymmetric rank remain least likely to be connected with regardless of disclosure.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces a relational boundary-blurring framework that reorients boundary management theory from the characteristics of the focal employee to evaluations of the relational partner, showing that boundary blurring is decided relationship by relationship rather than uniformly within a domain. It extends social cognition and status-characteristics research by demonstrating that warmth-conveying cues (disclosure, gender, rank) are not equivalent in their effects on boundary blurring, and that gender and rank are less consistently mediated by warmth than disclosure is. It also theorizes online social networks as an extreme case that amplifies the visibility, permanence, and relational risks of boundary blurring.
practical_implication: Employees should recognize that online connection decisions may rest on stereotypes about colleagues' benevolence (especially regarding gender and rank) and deliberate more carefully about the risks and rewards of connecting; moderate, noncontroversial disclosure may suffice to signal warmth. Bosses should recognize that subordinates are often hesitant to connect and avoid initiating requests that create discomfort. Because reinforcing gender-role stereotypes can place disclosing female bosses at greater risk, organizations should avoid one-size-fits-all policies and pursue training and safeguards that protect privacy without perpetuating gender discrimination in workplace social connection.
limitations: Studies 1 and 2 are correlational and not designed to test the hypotheses. Study 3 maximized control using fictitious profiles but lacked realism, while Study 4 used an adapted critical-incident recall design that gained external validity but introduced recall error and could not firmly establish that disclosure precedes the connection decision. Both experiments relied on single-item dependent measures and a shortened warmth scale in Study 3, and the main effect of gender varied across studies.
future_research: Future work should examine the amount and type of online versus offline disclosure more extensively, including appropriate versus politicized or stigmatized content and the role of privacy settings, and further establish the temporal precedence of real-world disclosure. Scholars should investigate other status characteristics such as race and their interaction with gender and rank, the persistence of gender inequalities on OSNs (e.g., whether connections help or harm disclosing female bosses), downstream consequences of online boundary blurring for performance and offline relationships, and extensions to other platforms such as Twitter, Instagram, and WeChat.
abstract: We propose and test a relational boundary-blurring framework, examining how employees’ evaluations of colleagues’ characteristics drive their decisions to connect with colleagues as friends online. We use a multi-method approach across four studies to investigate how self-disclosure of personal information, gender, and rank shape warmth evaluations of colleagues and subsequent boundary blurring decisions on online social networks such as Facebook. Study 1, a large archival study using a nationally representative sample, finds that connecting as friends with colleagues online is prevalent. Study 2, examining employees across several industries, shows that people experience connecting as friends with colleagues online as boundary blurring. Two experimental studies (Studies 3 and 4) ascertain that employees are more likely to connect as friends online with colleagues who engage in more (vs. less) self-disclosure and are less likely to connect with bosses (vs. peers). Further, self-disclosure, gender, and rank interact, such that employees are more likely to connect with female bosses who disclose more, compared to those who disclose less, and compared to male bosses, regardless of self-disclosure. Our work contributes to boundary management research by demonstrating that employees’ decisions to blur the personal/professional boundary online crucially depends on whom they are blurring the boundary with.
apa_citation: Rothbard, N. P., Ramarajan, L., Ollier-Malaterre, A., & Lee, S. S. (2022). OMG! My boss just friended me: How evaluations of colleagues’ disclosure, gender, and rank shape personal/professional boundary blurring online. *Academy of Management Journal, 65*(1), 35–65. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.0755

title: Regulating Top Managers’ Emotions during Strategy Making: Nokia’s Socially Distributed Approach Enabling Radical Change from Mobile Phones to Networks in 2007–2013
methods: Theory-elaborating qualitative single-case study of Nokia (2007-2013); inductive grounded analysis of interviews, real-time documents, and archival materials using open coding, second-order themes, and aggregate dimensions (Gioia-style data structures) to build a process model.
industry: Mobile phones / smartphones and telecommunications networks (Nokia)
country: Finland (headquartered), listed on the New York Stock Exchange
units: Top managers, board members, strategy specialists, advisors, and middle managers within a single firm (Nokia)
n_sample: 121 in-depth interviews (plus ~300 informal discussions, 60 follow-up emails, and real-time documents)
authors: Vuori, Timo O.; Huy, Quy N.
theories: appraisal theories of emotion (Lazarus, 1991); cognitive-processual perspective on strategy making (Kaplan, 2008; Ocasio et al., 2018); emotion regulation (Gross, 1998)
keywords: appraisal theories of emotion; cognitive-processual perspective; emotion regulation; radical strategic change; socially distributed emotion regulation; strategy making; top managers
research_question: (a) Whether and how are strategic decision makers’ emotions regulated during strategy making in large organizations? (b) How does this regulation influence the strategy-making process?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: A strategic situation (a major threat or disruptive change) triggers intense emotional reactions among top managers, activating action tendencies that can produce myopic, rigid, and biased behavior and strategic inertia.
- Structural affordances (regulating groups): Governance structures (board of directors → power to dictate norms/behaviors), advisory structures (analytical teams/consultants → impartial, specialized cognitive capacity), and boundary-spanning structures (potential partner firms → capacity to increase the salience/vividness of options).
- Three regulation mechanisms: (1) increasing top managers’ impulse control (containing initial emotions via formal practices and psychological safety), (2) adding emotionally neutral cognitive capacity (enabling data-informed reappraisals), and (3) increasing the mental vividness of options (amplifying emotional reactions toward specific options).
- Enabling condition: A shared appreciation of emotion regulation (born of the negative experience of emotional inertia in Period 1) makes top managers react favorably to others’ regulation efforts.
- Emergent outcome: A recursive loop in which contained initial emotions → wider/deeper search → data-informed reappraisals → gradually changing emotions → still deeper search, ultimately enabling a radical strategic choice the managers initially disdained.

Across two periods, unregulated emotional reactions (denial, avoidance of the iPhone/Android threat) drove strategic inertia in Period 1, whereas in Period 2 various organizational groups, coordinated through formal structures, distributed the work of regulating top managers’ emotions so that those emotions changed qualitatively over weeks and months. Because the process is socially distributed, each group performs a sub-task with compartmentalized information, and the overall emotion-regulating effect emerges from their joint, complementary efforts rather than from any single leader’s individual emotional intelligence.
theoretical_contribution: The paper enriches work on emotions and strategy making by theorizing emotion regulation as socially distributed across organizational groups and enabled by formal organizational structures, transcending the individual-level limitations that dominate prior emotion-regulation research. It shows how emotion regulation unfolds cumulatively over time (rather than as a one-off episode), and extends the cognitive-processual perspective by demonstrating how emotions shape the framing of strategic issues and how containing initial emotions can later support more adaptive, data-informed reappraisals.
practical_implication: Management teams and boards should recognize that their governance functions naturally arouse emotions that influence strategy making. The authors recommend three practices: create processes that contain initial emotional reactions by letting executives study challenges in "digestible parts"; require executives to form data-informed reappraisals by generating and analyzing multiple strategic options (using consultants and outside advisors); and concretize options through interactions with potential partners, while nurturing patience as emotions and thinking evolve gradually.
limitations: The model is context-dependent: the arguments assume emotions have fairly universal influences on biology and cognition, yet context-specific factors may shape how emotions translate into behavior and whether they can be regulated. Nokia was headquartered in Finland and listed on the NYSE, so its board could actively influence top managers; in other contexts boards may face more constraints. The study also focuses on the emotion-regulation roles of boards, analytical teams, and potential partner firms within a single firm.
future_research: Future work should examine how structural arrangements (e.g., industry norms, ownership structure) shape the occurrence and effectiveness of socially distributed emotion regulation, and how various structural positions elicit the emotions of different groups and influence their emotion-regulation behaviors. Researchers should also explore whether and how organizational groups beyond boards, analytical teams, and partner firms contribute to emotion regulation, and how the timing, sequencing, and rhythm of regulation actions affect strategic outcomes.
abstract: Strategy making can be emotional for decision makers, especially when facing a major threat or a disruptive change. Yet, we know little about whether and how strategic decision makers’ emotions are regulated and how such regulation influences strategy making. Based on a longitudinal study of Nokia from 2007 to 2013, we develop a process model of socially distributed emotion regulation. This model shows how various organizational groups help regulate top managers’ emotions. Top managers contain their initial emotional reactions to strategic options thanks to activities performed by groups with power over top managers. This enables top managers to form data-informed reappraisals of strategic options, contributing to gradual changes in their emotions. The reappraisal process is aided by diverse groups performing distinct roles. Top managers’ revised emotions, in turn, enable them to form new, iterative data-informed reappraisals and ultimately enable radical strategic change. Our study contributes to research on emotions and strategy making by showing how socially distributed emotion regulation operates during strategy making and influences its outcomes. We contribute to the cognitive perspective on strategy by showing how cognition and emotion interact over time during strategy making.
apa_citation: Vuori, T. O., & Huy, Q. N. (2022). Regulating top managers’ emotions during strategy making: Nokia’s socially distributed approach enabling radical change from mobile phones to networks in 2007–2013. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(1), 331–361. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0865
