title: Glue or Gasoline? The Role of Interorganizational Linkages in the Occurrence and Spillover of Competitive Wars
methods: Longitudinal panel design using conditional (city-pair market and period) fixed-effects logistic regressions predicting the binary onset of a price war in a market-period, with interaction terms between interorganizational linkages and incumbents-at-war-in-other-markets; robustness via alternative thresholds, two-way clustering, network-based alliance-clique measures, and dedicated/transient investor and strong/weak alliance subsample analyses.
industry: U.S. domestic airline industry (price wars across city-pair geographic markets)
time_period: Second quarter of 1991 to fourth quarter of 2010 (79 periods)
units: Market-period observations (city-pair routes by quarter)
n_sample: 112,210 market-period observations across 2,066 markets (4,086 price wars identified)
authors: Yu, Tieying; Guo, Wei; Gimeno, Javier; Zhang, Yu
theories: competitive dynamics (Chen, 1996); multimarket competition / mutual forbearance (Edwards, 1955; Gimeno, 1999); social embeddedness perspective (Granovetter, 1985; Gulati, 1998)
keywords: common analyst ties; common ownership ties; competitive wars; interorganizational linkages; multimarket competition; social embeddedness; strategic alliances; war spillover
research_question: How do direct interorganizational linkages (strategic alliances) and indirect linkages (common ownership ties and common analyst ties) influence both the occurrence of competitive wars within a market and their spillover into a focal market once wars have broken out elsewhere?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the social embeddedness perspective, the paper argues that direct alliances suppress war onset because repeated interaction builds trust and resource interdependence, while indirect ties suppress onset because impartial common third-parties (institutional investors, analysts) mediate conflicts and can sanction deviating firms via resource holdings or socioeconomic influence. Once a war erupts in related markets, however, the mechanisms diverge: alliance partners' aligned, complementary operations plus war-induced distrust make spillover more direct and severe (alliances become gasoline), whereas non-partisan third-parties, whose own interests are harmed by spreading wars, intensify their mediating and sanctioning roles (indirect ties become stronger glue). All five focal hypotheses were supported in the airline data.
theoretical_contribution: The study advances competitive dynamics research by being, to the authors' knowledge, the first to systematically examine the occurrence and spillover of competitive wars as a distinct phenomenon from day-to-day rivalry. It extends social embeddedness theory by identifying the onset of a competitive war as a critical situational trigger that can transform direct linkages from constraining competition (glue) into a catalyst for escalation (gasoline), and by foregrounding the distinct mechanisms—trust/interdependence versus third-party mediation/sanctioning—through which direct and indirect ties operate. It also provides initial empirical evidence for the previously untested assumption underlying the multimarket mutual forbearance hypothesis that competitive interactions spread across markets when forbearance breaks down, and introduces common analyst ties as a novel form of embeddedness shaping interfirm competition.
practical_implication: For managers and policymakers, the findings suggest that interorganizational linkages have asymmetric, state-dependent effects on competitive escalation: common ownership and common analyst ties consistently dampen the spread of destructive price wars, whereas strategic alliances—while preventing wars from starting—can accelerate their contagion once fighting begins. The authors note that when competition escalates into catastrophic warfare, common ownership ties can help prevent the situation from worsening, which is beneficial for the industry as a whole, implying alliance partners should anticipate heightened spillover risk during wartime.
limitations: The arguments carry boundary conditions: the theory applies most cleanly to industries with low differentiation and to markets without competing alliance cliques (where high alliance density may not lower rivalry), and the glue-gasoline story fits best alliances aimed at resource sharing such as airline code-sharing. The study examines only lose-lose wars detrimental to performance (e.g., price wars), not value-enhancing competitive wars, and analyzes spillover only across geographic—not product—markets. It also could not directly test whether passively managed index-fund ownership weakens common-ownership effects because the sample ends in 2010.
future_research: Future work could examine how different alliance types and purposes shape war occurrence and spillover differently, study value-enhancing (e.g., marketing or product-development) wars whose dynamics differ from lose-lose wars, and extend the analysis to spillover across product markets. The authors also encourage borrowing from behavioral perspectives (prospect theory, the behavioral theory of the firm, the psychology of rivalry) and strategic-communication lenses, investigating the within-war process of how rivals actually interact, and exploring how competitive wars reshape subsequent cooperative and alliance relationships between rivals.
abstract: Despite their prevalence and significance, competitive wars have received limited attention in the strategy literature. Our knowledge of how interorganizational linkages influence competitive wars is particularly lacking. Drawing on the social embeddedness perspective, we argue that both direct linkages (i.e., strategic alliances) and indirect linkages (i.e., common ownership ties and common analyst ties) reduce the likelihood of war, thereby functioning as the glue that binds firms together. Yet once competitive wars are launched in related markets, indirect linkages through common third-parties may continuously function as glue, reducing the likelihood of war spillover, whereas direct linkages, such as strategic alliances, may facilitate the spillover of competitive wars, akin to adding gasoline to a fire. Using data from the U.S. domestic airline industry between 1991 and 2010, our empirical evidence offers strong support for our predictions.
apa_citation: Yu, T., Guo, W., Gimeno, J., & Zhang, Y. (2022). Glue or gasoline? The role of interorganizational linkages in the occurrence and spillover of competitive wars. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(1), 304–330. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0525

title: Addressing Performance Tensions in Multiteam Systems: Balancing Informal Mechanisms of Coordination within and between Teams
methods: Multisource, multimethod field study of 44 multiteam systems (295 teams, 930 members) completing an 11-week engineering task; interpersonal interactions captured via wearable Bluetooth sensors; hypotheses tested with polynomial regression and response surface analysis using clustered standard errors, plus a block-variable path model for the indirect effect.
industry: Undergraduate engineering education (laboratory course building Rube Goldberg machines)
time_period: 11-week course; interactions assessed during Weeks 5-7, conflict in Week 8, performance in Week 11
units: Multiteam systems, component teams, and individual members
n_sample: 44 MTSs composed of 295 teams and 930 members (hypothesis tests on 268 teams)
authors: Ziegert, Jonathan C.; Knight, Andrew P.; Resick, Christian J.; Graham, Katrina A.
theories: coordination theory (Okhuysen & Bechky, 2009); multiteam systems theory (Mathieu, Marks, & Zaccaro, 2001); team boundary spanning (Ancona, 1990; Marrone, 2010)
keywords: informal coordination; interpersonal interactions; intrateam and interteam interactions; multiteam systems; team boundary spanning; team conflict
research_question: How do informal coordination mechanisms—specifically, the balance of MTS members' intrateam versus interteam interpersonal interactions—influence component team and system-level effectiveness, and through what process do imbalances translate into performance consequences?
mechanism_summary:
- Mediator: Team conflict (Jehn & Mannix's 9-item measure; channels the team-level imbalance effect to team performance)
- Controls: Team familiarity, team size, MTS size; system models also control average team familiarity, team performance, and team conflict

Drawing on the team boundary spanning principle that teams must strike "a balance between internal and external activities" (Choi, 2002), the authors argue that interteam interactions are necessary to coordinate across component teams but generate role overload, divergent task understandings, and intrateam conflict when not matched by corresponding intrateam interactions. At the team level, imbalance (interteam exceeding intrateam) raises team conflict (H1), which in turn depresses component team performance (H2). At the system level, balanced interpersonal interactions serve a coordination function that enables the MTS to integrate adjustments locally, so incongruence in either direction undermines system performance (H3). All three hypotheses were supported.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends MTS theory by showing that informal coordination mechanisms—long viewed pessimistically as unworkable in large, distributed systems—can in fact facilitate MTS effectiveness when intrateam and interteam interactions are considered jointly rather than as independent, additive factors. By integrating MTS and boundary spanning literatures, it identifies the balance of interpersonal interactions as a coordination mechanism, names team conflict as the intermediary that transmits team-level imbalance to performance, and demonstrates the distinct, sometimes countervailing, effects of coordination at the team versus system levels of analysis.
practical_implication: Managers and members of MTSs should consciously manage with whom and how much they interact internally versus externally: interteam interactions must be coupled with at least a similar level of intrateam interactions so that externally acquired information is integrated within the team. Post hoc evidence further suggests focusing external interactions on directly interdependent (adjacent) teams, and that concentrating boundary spanning in a subset of members may benefit team (though not system) performance. Social network and interaction-tracking tools can help monitor and rebalance informal interactions.
limitations: The sample comprised U.S. engineering students completing a standardized sequential-interdependence task, which limits generalizability to organizational, cross-cultural, and other MTS structures, and the system-level sample of 44 limits statistical power. Interaction was operationalized only as time in physical proximity, so the quality and content of interactions and the presumed system-level knowledge-transfer mechanism were not directly measured. Causality could not be established, and conflict measured after the action phase leaves open recursive conflict–interaction dynamics.
future_research: The authors call for studying the intersection of formal and informal coordination mechanisms (including semi-structured forms) in MTSs, examining informal coordination across the full MTS lifecycle and transition phases, and directly measuring system-level mediating mechanisms such as knowledge transfer and interteam conflict. They also encourage examining the absolute volume of interactions, the adjacent/nonadjacent foci distinction, and the use of computational modeling or experiments to identify causal effects in other cultural and organizational contexts and larger samples.
abstract: Due to their distinctive features, multiteam systems (MTSs) face significant coordination challenges—both within component teams and across the larger system. Despite the benefits of informal mechanisms of coordination for knowledge-based work, there is considerable ambiguity regarding their effects in MTSs. To resolve this ambiguity, we build and test theory about how interpersonal interactions among MTS members serve as an informal coordination mechanism that facilitates team and system functioning. Integrating MTS research with insights from the team boundary spanning literature, we argue that the degree to which MTS members balance their interactions with members of their own component team (i.e., intrateam interactions) and with the members of other teams in the system (i.e., interteam interactions) shapes team- and system-level performance. The findings of a multimethod study of 44 MTSs composed of 295 teams and 930 members show that as interteam interactions exceed intrateam interactions, team conflict arises and detracts from component team performance. At the system level, a balance between intrateam and interteam interactions enhances system success. Our findings advance understanding of MTSs by highlighting how informal coordination mechanisms enable MTSs to overcome their coordination challenges and address the unique performance tension between component teams and the larger system.
apa_citation: Ziegert, J. C., Knight, A. P., Resick, C. J., & Graham, K. A. (2022). Addressing performance tensions in multiteam systems: Balancing informal mechanisms of coordination within and between teams. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(1), 158–185. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1043

title: Top Management Team Experiential Variety, Competitive Repertoires, and Firm Performance: Examining the Law of Requisite Variety in the 3D Printing Industry (1986–2017)
methods: Hand-collected unbalanced longitudinal panel of 3D printer manufacturers (1986–2017); structured content analysis of >17,500 articles to code competitive actions; Shannon-entropy complexity and displacement-vector consistency calibrated against top performers; random-effects (plus fixed-effects and hybrid) panel models with robust standard errors; indirect effects via Monte Carlo and SEM.
industry: Commercial 3D printing (additive manufacturing) industry
units: Firm-years (commercial 3D printer manufacturers and their TMTs)
n_sample: 245 firm-years across 23 firms (21–23 firms; performance models 161–183 firm-years)
authors: Fox, Brian C.; Simsek, Zeki; Heavey, Ciaran
theories: competitive dynamics (Chen & Miller, 2012); law of requisite variety (Ashby, 1956; Boisot & McKelvey, 2011; Poulis & Poulis, 2016); upper echelons theory (Hambrick & Mason, 1984)
keywords: calibration; competitive repertoires; firm performance; law of requisite variety; repertoire complexity; repertoire consistency; top management team experiential variety; upper echelons
research_question: Does top management team (TMT) industry experience variety shape firm performance indirectly, by enabling firms to calibrate the complexity and consistency of their competitive repertoires to the external variety embodied in top performers' actions, as predicted by the law of requisite variety (LRV)?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the LRV, the authors argue that experientially varied TMTs possess a richer reserve of cognitive categories and behavioral routines, reducing both "routinized" (over-consistent, under-complex) and "headless chicken" (over-complex, under-consistent) competitive misfires. This allows such firms to calibrate the complexity and consistency of their repertoires to the external variety signaled by contemporaneous top performers, which in turn improves performance. The variety → performance relationship is fully indirect: TMT experiential variety operates through repertoire calibration rather than directly, consistent with the LRV's claim that system performance depends on matching internal to external variety.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces and operationalizes "calibrated competitive repertoires," shifting the LRV from an undefined theoretical-maximum notion of external variety to a measurable standard anchored in top-performing competitors, and extends the law beyond complexity to encompass repertoire consistency (a temporal component). It provides one of the first direct empirical tests of the LRV's performance thesis, modeling the full chain from TMT experiential variety through calibration to performance, and shows that "more is better" is too simplistic—what matters is matching, not maximizing, variety. For upper echelons theory it reframes attention from why executives act to how managerial perceptions produce calibration misfires.
practical_implication: Firms should not pursue maximal complexity or rigid consistency in competitive action, but should calibrate both to the moving standard set by industry top performers; either under- or over-shooting that standard degrades performance. Because calibration is hard to achieve, assembling top management teams with a broad spread of qualitatively different prior industry experiences gives firms the cognitive breadth needed to track shifting external variety and avoid competitive inertia or scattershot responses.
limitations: The overall sample is relatively small, which constrains the power to detect firm-performance effects and to jointly examine earlier and later life-cycle stages. The single-industry design buys less-confounded testing at the expense of cross-industry generalizability, and other TMT experience characteristics (e.g., functional or educational diversity) could yield similar predictions. ROA is a summary financial proxy for adaptation, and the authors cannot directly observe TMTs discriminating among stimuli and constructing their action repertoires.
future_research: Examine whether an inherent trade-off exists between calibrating complexity and calibrating consistency, and which is more valuable when firms are forced to choose. Identify the behavioral routines and rigidities (beyond cognition) that drive miscalibration, and study how different TMT diversity types produce distinct forms of miscalibration—how firms miscalibrate may matter as much as how much. Finer-grained, more micro-level decision-making data could parse the perceptual and selection processes the theory posits.
abstract: This study develops and tests a thesis derived from the law of requisite variety. We contend that the greater the experiential variety of a top management team, the more likely it is that the complexity and consistency of the firm’s competitive repertoire will be calibrated to relevant external variety. In addition, for firms that achieve such calibrated repertoires, we expect that their financial performance will be superior to that of their peers. We then integrate these arguments and examine whether top management team experiential variety indirectly, through calibrated repertoires, contributes to firm performance. Analyzing hand-collected data for firms operating in the 3D printing industry over the past three decades (1986–2017), we find support for the overall thesis and associated hypotheses. The discussion section elaborates on the study’s contributions, limitations, and future research potential.
apa_citation: Fox, B. C., Simsek, Z., & Heavey, C. (2022). Top management team experiential variety, competitive repertoires, and firm performance: Examining the law of requisite variety in the 3D printing industry (1986–2017). *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(2), 545–576. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0734

title: Identity–Society (Mis)Alignment and the Instrumentalization of Firm Creation: Creative Destruction and Creative Reconstruction
methods: Inductive qualitative study in the interpretive tradition; semistructured interviews with 49 founders at 43 firms in Taiwan's cultural-and-creative industry, analyzed via three-stage Gioia-style coding (inductive then abductive), constant comparison, and stacking comparable cases until theoretical saturation.
industry: Cultural products and services industry ("cultural and creative" / wen chuang ventures); mainly fashion and textile sector
units: New-venture founders (individuals; six founding teams interviewed)
n_sample: 49 founders at 43 firms (final sample, after dropping three firms)
authors: Foy, Shirah; Gruber, Marc
theories: Schumpeterian theory of creative destruction (Schumpeter, 1934); founder identity theory (Fauchart & Gruber, 2011); identity structure / social structure (Marcia, 1993; Côté, 1996a, 1996b; Giddens, 1984)
keywords: creative destruction; creative reconstruction; founder identity; identity–society (mis)alignment; new venture creation; social ventures; sociocultural context
research_question: How does the alignment, or misalignment, between an entrepreneur’s identity and perceived surrounding sociocultural context influence new venture creation?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: founder *identity structure* (tradition-, inner-, image-/discovery-directed) juxtaposed against perceived *social structure* (traditional, industrial, postmodern); (mis)alignment is the (in)congruence between the forces driving the two.
- Intermediate psychological outcomes: alignment yields a sense of societal *reinforcement* (emotional, cognitive, social); misalignment generates *tensions* (low to high) reflecting dissatisfaction with the perceived "rules of the game."
- Venture-outcome dimensions: (1) the *sociopolitical dimension* embedded in the venture (absent → dominant, i.e., whether it challenges the sociocultural status quo) and (2) *temporal orientation* (inventing the future vs. restoring the past).
- Emergent typology: six venture types mapping founders onto the two outcome dimensions, including high-tension misaligned founders who build social-mission ventures.

The paper theorizes a micro-level mechanism: when founders perceive misalignment between their identity structure and their surrounding social structures, the resulting tensions push them—via a logic of appropriateness rather than purely rational choice—to instrumentalize firm creation as a vehicle to change society, embedding a sociopolitical dimension oriented either toward a new future (creative destruction of the sociocultural environment) or toward restoring a valued past (creative reconstruction). Aligned founders, experiencing reinforcement, instead venture within the prevailing rules of the game and challenge only the economic status quo.
theoretical_contribution: The study specifies founder identity–society (mis)alignment as a driving mechanism in entrepreneurship that produces qualitatively different venture types, including social ventures, thereby relaxing the implicit assumption that founders’ identities are congruent with their sociocultural context. It extends Schumpeter’s theory of creative destruction beyond the economic domain to the sociocultural environment and introduces the complementary concept of *creative reconstruction*, in which entrepreneurs seek to re-establish elements of a past sociocultural environment. It also contributes to research on organizational emergence by showing early-stage strategic choices follow a logic of appropriateness rather than rational, choice-based decision-making.
practical_implication: The (mis)alignment lens can help founders better understand themselves and prospective partners when forming venture teams, since divergence in identity structures or perceptions of the sociocultural environment shapes the tensions that arise. Recognizing that social-mission ventures often originate in identity-based tensions (rather than a deliberate aim to become "social entrepreneurs") clarifies the motivational roots of socially oriented venturing.
limitations: The study mainly observed ventures created by single individuals (with only a few two-founder teams whose members had similar identities), precluding analysis of how founders with divergent identity structures co-create ventures and the tensions that arise between them. Not all misaligned founders acted on their tension to the same degree; in-depth analysis of why three outlier founders disengaged was outside the study’s scope, and other factors such as self-efficacy and personality were not examined.
future_research: Longitudinal designs could examine the dynamics of identity–society alignment over time and the role of identity-structure shifts, since founders recounted life events that moved them between identity structures. Researchers could also drill into specific identity-structure × social-structure configurations (nine pure permutations) and investigate which levels of sociocultural context (immediate surroundings, industry, nation, global region, global society) are most salient for reinforcement or tension.
abstract: Research on founder identity has significantly advanced our understanding of entrepreneurship and related literatures. By departing from the widely held—though often implicit—assumption that culture defines the parameters of identity formation, this paper investigates how the alignment, or misalignment, between a founder’s identity and their perceived sociocultural context influences new firm creation. We engage in an in-depth qualitative study of 49 founders in Taiwan that reveals how founders’ identity structures can be (mis)aligned with their perceived social structures, and how the reinforcement or tensions generated by (mis)alignment can fuel the creation of fundamentally different types of ventures—including those that challenge the perceived sociocultural status quo with a social mission intended to push society toward a new equilibrium. Our theory of founder identity–society (mis)alignment is therefore able to specify a key mechanism linking the founder’s identity with perceived sociocultural context. Furthermore, we advance the influential Schumpeterian theory of creative destruction beyond the purely economic context to address the broader sociocultural environment and to incorporate the concept of creative reconstruction, in which entrepreneurs seek to restore elements of society past.
apa_citation: Foy, S., & Gruber, M. (2022). Identity–society (mis)alignment and the instrumentalization of firm creation: Creative destruction and creative reconstruction. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(2), 479–515. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0754

title: Strategic Nepotism in Family Director Appointments: Evidence from Family Business Groups in South Korea
methods: Archival panel study of South Korean family business groups; pooled probit and multinomial probit models (603 board appointment events) plus discrete-time event history analysis using random effects probit models (756 firm-year observations); predictors lagged one year; robust standard errors clustered by firm.
industry: Publicly traded firms affiliated with family-controlled business groups (chaebol); private and financial-sector firms excluded
units: Board appointment events / firm-years
n_sample: 603 board appointment events (pooled probit); 756 firm-year observations (event history); 33 business groups; 155 firms
authors: Jeong, Seung-Hwan; Kim, Heechun; Kim, Hicheon
theories: nepotism mixed gamble (Firfiray et al., 2018; Gomez-Mejia et al., 2018); socioemotional wealth (SEW) perspective (Gomez-Mejia et al., 2007)
keywords: board composition; director appointments; family business groups; family firms; intergenerational deterioration; socioemotional wealth; strategic nepotism
research_question: What determines and drives family director appointments in family business groups — that is, where and when do controlling families appoint family members (versus nonfamily insiders) to boards, and how does this "strategic nepotism" vary across family generations?
mechanism_summary: Building on the SEW perspective and the "nepotism mixed gamble," the paper argues that controlling families proactively shape the long-run trade-off between SEW and financial wealth by deliberately routing family heirs to board positions that build human, reputational, and social capital (resource-building nepotism: high-performance, high-reputation, highly interlocked firms) while steering them away from positions that would taint those resources (stigma-avoiding nepotism: financially distressed firms and boards already crowded with family directors). Because founder-specific advantages erode across generations ("intergenerational deterioration"), later-generation members most need such grooming, so strategic nepotism is predicted — and found — to concentrate on them. Empirically, intragroup centrality (H3a), financial-distress avoidance (H4a), and avoidance of family-crowded boards (H5a) are supported; market performance (H1a) is only marginally supported and firm reputation (H2a) is not, but the generational tests show market performance (H1b), reputation (H2b), and distress avoidance (H4b) operate more strongly for later-generation members.
theoretical_contribution: The study introduces "strategic nepotism" to extend the SEW/nepotism-mixed-gamble literature by recasting controlling families as proactive actors who manage the endogenous process of family director selection rather than passive beneficiaries of nepotism. It opens the "black box" of how family involvement arises, brings the SEW perspective into business-group research, reframes family business groups as internal labor markets for grooming family heirs, and supplies a previously neglected SEW-based account of director selection in contexts where family principals dominate.
practical_implication: Families controlling businesses are advised to approach board appointments and family members' career paths strategically and dynamically — especially for later generations — since deliberately nurtured heirs are more likely to develop into qualified executives with firm-specific human and social capital, helping reconcile tensions between SEW and financial wealth.
limitations: The study relies on archival data, so it infers underlying motives (e.g., stigma avoidance) from observed patterns rather than measuring them directly, and real-world motives may be more complex (e.g., avoiding intra-family conflict or dividing labor among kin). Because the data are drawn solely from South Korea, the generalizability of the theory and findings to family firms and business groups in other institutional and cultural contexts is an open question.
future_research: Future work could use surveys, interviews, and ethnographic observation to uncover controlling families' fine-grained motives and intentions, and test the theory cross-nationally to establish institutional and cultural boundary conditions. Scholars could also extend strategic nepotism to multi-business family firms (e.g., assigning kin to favorable business units), and examine whether strategic nepotism actually yields the intended downstream outcomes — improved managerial resources and more positive stakeholder evaluations.
abstract: Controlling families of family firms strive to preserve their socioemotional wealth (SEW) through family involvement in company leadership. Prior research points to a potential trade-off of family involvement, as underqualified and underprepared family members’ involvement would bring SEW gains at the expense of financial wealth. We examine how controlling families seek to shape this trade-off to be in their favor by systematically appointing family members to board positions favorable for building managerial competencies through what we call “strategic” nepotism. We find that compared to nonfamily insiders, family members are preferentially appointed to prestigious firms that provide opportunities for cultivating knowledge, skills, and reputation in a protected organizational environment. We also find that controlling family members are kept away from stigmatizing appointments that would undermine their managerial resource-building efforts. Consistent with an effort to overcome intergenerational deterioration of managerial talent and skills, we find that strategic nepotism is concentrated on later-rather than earlier-generation family members.
apa_citation: Jeong, S.-H., Kim, H., & Kim, H. (2022). Strategic nepotism in family director appointments: Evidence from family business groups in South Korea. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(2), 656–682. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.1418

title: The Fragility of Experts: A Moderated-Mediation Model of Expertise, Expert Identity Threat, and Overprecision
methods: Multimethod design: four pre-registered laboratory experiments (Studies 1-4) using 2x2 / 3x2 mixed factorial designs, with mediation estimated via Hayes's PROCESS macro (Model 4) and bootstrapped confidence intervals; plus two archival studies (Study 5: difference-in-differences OLS on MLB ball-strike calls with umpire/game fixed effects; Study 6: regressions on Duke CFO survey forecasts).
industry: Experimental tasks (baseball play prediction; startup-pitch investment prediction); real-world settings: Major League Baseball umpiring and corporate finance (CFO market forecasts).
country: Lab subjects from a U.S. university behavioral lab; Study 6 uses a panel of 2,000-3,000 U.S. CFOs per quarter; baseball clips drawn from a non-U.S. professional league.
time_period: MLB ejection games sampled between 2014 and 2018; CFO survey forecasts charted 2004-2019.
units: Individuals (lab participants; CFOs); umpire ball-strike calls / games.
n_sample: Four lab experiments with usable samples of 142, 173, 104, and 110 participants (191/224/135/140 recruited); Study 5: 816 games (408 ejection + 408 matched control); Study 6: 61 quarterly CFO survey observations.
authors: Kang, Sanghoon; Kim, Jerry W.
theories: identity threat (Aquino & Douglas, 2003; Marr & Thau, 2014; Steele, Spencer, & Aronson, 2002); overconfidence / overprecision (Moore & Healy, 2008); self-affirmation theory (Steele, 1988; Cohen & Sherman, 2014)
keywords: disconfirming feedback; expert identity threat; expertise; overconfidence; overprecision; self-affirmation
research_question: When and why do experts make sound versus unsound decisions following negative performance feedback? Specifically, the paper asks whether disconfirming feedback leads experts (more than novices) to "double down" and become overprecise in their judgments, whether perceived expert identity threat mediates this effect, and how the feedback source and self-affirmation moderate it.
mechanism_summary: Negative performance feedback poses a salient threat to people's expert identity; because experts' self-concept is grounded in superior knowledge and competence, they experience a more acute threat than novices and respond by reaffirming self-worth—boosting confidence and issuing narrower, overly precise predictions. This identity-threat mechanism is amplified when feedback comes from credible sources (other experts or actual outcomes) and attenuated when experts self-affirm core values beforehand, which restores global self-integrity and removes the need to assert expert identity through overconfidence.
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces expert identity threat as a motivational (not merely cognitive or task-based) factor explaining when experts exhibit sound versus distorted decision-making, integrating identity-threat and self-affirmation theory into the literature on expertise and overconfidence. It reframes overconfidence as a motivated, identity-protective response to threatened valued identities rather than only a heuristic bias or strategic response to external incentives, and shows that performance feedback—long viewed as a debiasing tool—can paradoxically worsen expert judgment.
practical_implication: Managers and organizations that rely on experts (e.g., professional service firms, hospitals) should recognize that bluntly confronting experts with their errors can backfire by triggering identity-protective overprecision. Framing feedback as constructive learning opportunities, deploying self-affirmation interventions that reaffirm individual and organizational values, and cultivating psychological safety can mitigate identity threat and reduce experts' tendency to double down. Monetary incentives, by contrast, did not curb the bias.
limitations: The prediction tasks studied were genuinely difficult with low accuracy rates (often under 40%, and under 20% in the investment task), so a ceiling effect means the findings may not generalize to high-accuracy, routine judgment tasks—an important boundary condition. Providing identical negative feedback to all participants regardless of actual performance limits external validity and raises the possibility of demotivation/frustration artifacts, though identity threat fully mediated the effect. The study examined only one defensive response (overprecision) among many possible forms of defensiveness.
future_research: Future work could extend the identity-threat model to other forms of identity threat (e.g., status loss, rivalry, race- or gender-based stereotype threat) and to inequality within organizations, and could test the model with other defensive behaviors such as rejection, denigration, discounting, derogation, or reframing. Researchers could also examine how the correctness of feedback interacts with identity concerns, particularly for tasks with higher baseline accuracy.
abstract: Experts play a crucial role in modern organizations, but evidence regarding the soundness and reliability of their decision-making is mixed and often contradictory. We develop and test a moderated-mediation model of expert decision-making linking expertise, identity threat, and overprecision to understand when and why experts offer overly precise judgments, and how they can cope with disconfirming feedback. We find support for this model in a series of lab experiments which show that (a) experts are more likely than novices to double down and produce overly precise predictions following disconfirming feedback, (b) this feedback-induced overprecision by experts is mediated by perceived level of expert identity threat, (c) the source of the feedback matters for identity threat and overprecision, and (d) self-affirmation attenuates identity threat and reduces overprecision. We supplement these experimental findings by investigating experts' response to disconfirming feedback in two real-world settings: Major League Baseball umpiring and chief financial officer predictions of stock market returns. Our model and results show that feedback can harm expert decision-making by leading experts to be overly precise in their judgment, challenging existing notions on the ability of expert decision-makers, and providing insight into when and why experts should be relied upon in organizational decisions.
apa_citation: Kang, S., & Kim, J. W. (2022). The fragility of experts: A moderated-mediation model of expertise, expert identity threat, and overprecision. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(2), 577-605. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0899

title: No Change Is an Island: How Interferences between Change Initiatives Evoke Inconsistencies That Undermine Implementation
methods: Longitudinal, real-time inductive/abductive single-case study of a multinational technology firm ('TechCorp'); 94 semi-structured interviews with 65 informants over three years, 1,684 open survey responses, 29 corporate documents, 53 further archival items, and 84 hours of field observations; analyzed via Gioia-style open coding to a data structure of first-order concepts, second-order themes, and aggregate dimensions.
industry: Business-to-business technology manufacturing (engineering-dominant multinational firm undergoing 'servitization')
time_period: Three years of real-time data collection spanning two concurrent change initiatives (Project Foundation and Project Rebranding)
units: One multinational technology firm ('TechCorp'); informants spanning top managers, middle managers, change agents, and employees
n_sample: 94 interviews with 65 informants; 1,684 open survey responses
authors: Kanitz, Rouven; Huy, Quy Nguyen; Backmann, Julia; Hoegl, Martin
theories: appraisal theories of emotion (Ellsworth & Scherer, 2003); consistency theory / cognitive dissonance (Festinger, 1957; Gawronski & Brannon, 2019); emotional contagion / social sharing of emotion (Elfenbein, 2014; Rimé, 2009); sensemaking (Weick, 1995)
keywords: change initiatives; collective emotions; inconsistency judgments; organizational change; sensemaking; strategic change implementation
research_question: How do interferences between change initiatives emerge? And what are the mechanisms linking those interferences to change outcomes?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: Interfering organizational change initiatives — two quasi-concurrent strategic initiatives (Project Foundation, an internally focused culture/process change, and Project Rebranding) whose separate, quasi-autonomous execution generated friction rather than fit.
- Key constructs / judgments: Perceived inconsistencies between initiatives, coded as cognitive (lack of agreement in content), procedural (lack of agreement in required actions), and normative (lack of agreement in norms and values).
- Emotional reactions: Three shared emotional processes — uncertainty-related (confusion, worry), moral-related (indignation, shock), and detachment-related (annoyance, apathy).
- Social sharing activities: Challenging, reproaching, and distancing — recursive sharing that spreads cues and fosters collective emotions, amplified by internal social media.
- Top managers’ responses: Ambiguous top-down communication and restricted bottom-up communication that fueled rather than calmed negativity.
- Emergent outcome: Collective (dis)engagement from change initiatives, producing underperformance in speed, costs, and impact.

Employees socially construct the meaning of concurrent initiatives using consistency criteria; when initiatives are judged inconsistent, this triggers negative emotions that are shared and converge into collective emotions. Top managers’ responses act as triggers that amplify or dampen this sharing, and the resulting collective (dis)engagement — not abstract cognition alone — drives change outcomes through a recursive, self-reinforcing process operating over time.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends theories of navigating large-scale change by developing a consistency perspective on interacting change initiatives, identifying inconsistency judgments (cognitive, normative, procedural) as a previously overlooked socio-psychological mechanism through which one change project interferes with another. It also contributes to the literature on emotions and organizational change by showing how perceptions of inconsistency trigger emotional reactions and how social sharing and top managers’ responses amplify the emergence of collective emotions during change.
practical_implication: Top managers should anticipate that strategic change carried out through multiple initiatives can breed perceptions of inconsistency, and should respond to recipients’ emotional cues with purposeful sensegiving (sensebreaking, sense specification) rather than restricting dialogue. Establishing a centralized transformation team to monitor cross-initiative interactions and address both task- and emotion-related issues can attenuate inconsistency perceptions and sustain momentum.
limitations: The study has the usual limitations of a single case study, so its insights need validation in other settings. The authors cannot entirely rule out that the observed outcomes were also driven by other factors, such as the cultural context of the studied firm.
future_research: The authors invite research testing whether similar inconsistency judgments arise when a change is perceived as inconsistent with an organization’s history or identity (rather than another change), and on hypocrisy between espoused values and leaders’ cues. They also call for studies of "enabling interactions" in strategic initiative portfolios, a narrative/rhetorical lens on the temporal interplay of framing and retelling, and the dynamics of positive, future-oriented emotions (e.g., hope, optimism) — with social media as a contextual factor.
abstract: Organizational change research has concentrated on the challenges of implementing isolated changes, paying little attention to the interactions among concurrent change initiatives. Our longitudinal real-time study of a multinational technology firm examines how two corporate change initiatives interfered with each other. The interfering initiatives provoked inconsistency judgments (cognitive, normative, and procedural) and the emergence of collective emotions that undermined change performance. Top managers’ responses fueled the sharing of inconsistency judgments and emotions that fed into a recursive process that, over time, provoked emotional uncertainty, elicited moral emotions, and eroded emotional attachment to change. Our process model reveals inconsistency judgments as a previously overlooked socio-psychological mechanism underpinning interferences between change initiatives. We reveal the limitations of examining organizational change in terms of isolated initiatives and call for research that considers the dynamics between change initiatives.
apa_citation: Kanitz, R., Huy, Q. N., Backmann, J., & Hoegl, M. (2022). No change is an island: How interferences between change initiatives evoke inconsistencies that undermine implementation. *Academy of Management Journal, 65*(2), 683–710. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0413

title: Big Fish versus Big Pond? Entrepreneurs, Established Firms, and Antecedents of Tie Formation
methods: Mixed methods, primarily quantitative: dyad-year conditional logit and random-effects logit analyses of developer-publisher tie formation, supplemented by 30 in-depth interviews and robustness checks (rare-events logit, two-sided matching).
industry: Video game development and publishing (PlayStation 2 games)
country: Global; firms predominantly in Japan (31%), the United States (29%), and the United Kingdom (16%)
units: Developer-publisher dyad-years (entrepreneurial developers and established publishers)
n_sample: 367 developers and 170 publishers; up to 74,628 dyads; 1,416 observed ties; 30 interviews
authors: Katila, Riitta; Piezunka, Henning; Reineke, Philipp; Eisenhardt, Kathleen M.
theories: resource dependence theory (Pfeffer & Salancik, 2003)
keywords: big fish big pond; competition; development help; entrepreneurs; established firms; market access; resource dependence theory; resource needs; resource uncertainty; tie formation
research_question: What conditions lead an entrepreneur to become a big fish in a small pond (prioritizing development help) versus a small fish in a big pond (prioritizing market access) when forming a tie with an established firm? Specifically, how do anticipated development help and market access drive tie formation, and how are these weightings moderated by an entrepreneur's evolving resource needs and competition-induced resource uncertainty?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Prior collaboration, in-house development, quality difference, geographic distance, same country, established firm size/experience/portfolio size, entrepreneur size, year fixed effects

Resource dependence drives entrepreneurs and established firms to form ties to satisfy mutual resource needs: entrepreneurs supply novel ideas but lack development resources and market access, which established firms control. Because personalized development help is rationed in rank order across the established firm's portfolio of prospective peers, while market access is scalable, entrepreneurs face the "big fish, big pond" trade-off. The paper argues resource dependence is dynamic: as entrepreneurs gain experience their needs shift from internal (development) to external (market) resources (H3), and as competitive intensity rises the value of personalized, differentiating development help increases (H4), so the relative weighting of the two resources at tie formation shifts accordingly.
theoretical_contribution: The study gives resource dependence theory a dynamic element it has lacked, showing that both resource needs (organizational dynamism) and resource uncertainty (environmental dynamism) evolve over time and reshape partner choice. It spotlights the neglected role of prospective peers as a source of competition at the *ex ante* tie-formation stage, demonstrating that partner choice is not simply "find the biggest pond" and that the partner with the best resources is not always the most sought-after.
practical_implication: Resource-constrained, inexperienced entrepreneurs can attract partners and stand out by emphasizing their relative standing among an established firm's prospective peers, seeking "big fish" ties that offer personalized development help. Small, up-and-coming established firms or platforms lacking market access can compete for partners by offering personalized development help, differentiating themselves from larger, more rigid players.
limitations: The conditional logit specification cannot report developer-level (e.g., resource needs) or industry-level (e.g., resource uncertainty) variables that are invariant across a developer's partner choices in a year. Realized ties were rare, requiring rare-events and matching robustness checks. The findings are bounded by conditions specific to "markets of know-how," including limited scalability of established-firm resources, heterogeneity of established firms, and the unpredictability of breakthrough ideas.
future_research: The "big fish, big pond" framework could inform ecosystem and platform scholarship on which platforms attract complementors and which funding partners start-ups prefer, and whether established firms develop reputations as desirable partners. Studying the effectiveness and outcomes of formed ties (not just their formation), and how accumulated dyadic choices reshape alliance portfolios and industry networks, are promising directions.
abstract: Entrepreneurial and established firms form collaborative relationships to commercialize products. Through such ties, entrepreneurs seek (a) development help to hone ideas into marketable products and (b) access to markets. In most cases, entrepreneurs face a trade-off: they can be a big fish in a small pond, getting more attention and development help from a smaller firm with less market access, or a small fish in a big pond, getting less attention and help from a larger firm with more market access. Our study investigates what goes into choosing between these options. Drawing from resource dependence theory and an empirical study of tie formation between developers and publishers of PlayStation 2 video games, we develop and test a framework that identifies the key decision variables and focuses on two moderators—resource need evolution and resource uncertainty related to competition—that explain whether a big fish (more development help) or a big pond (more market access) drives tie formation. Our findings point to prospective peers as one of the significant decision criteria at tie formation and highlight the dynamic nature of resource dependence. Altogether, the results give resource dependence theory a dynamic element it has lacked in the past.
apa_citation: Katila, R., Piezunka, H., Reineke, P., & Eisenhardt, K. M. (2022). Big fish versus big pond? Entrepreneurs, established firms, and antecedents of tie formation. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(2), 427–452. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.1197

title: Which Ideas for Change Are Endorsed? How Agentic and Communal Voice Affects Endorsement Differently for Men and for Women
methods: Three primary studies plus a post hoc experiment. Study 1: event-contingent field design with 169 managers; OLS regression and PROCESS-macro mediation with bootstrapped CIs. Studies 2-3: 2x2 between-subjects vignette/audio experiments (MTurk; N = 299 and N = 309) manipulating speaker gender and voice content (Study 3 adds language use); CFA, ANOVA contrasts, and moderated-mediation.
industry: Working adults across a range of industries (e.g., information technology, education, engineering, health care; Studies 2-3 framed in a financial services context)
time_period: Study 1 used an event-contingent design in which managers recalled a subordinate's idea from within the last two weeks; no calendar collection dates reported
units: Individuals (managers/employees evaluating a coworker's or subordinate's idea for change)
n_sample: Study 1: 169 managers; Study 2: 299; Study 3: 309; post hoc experiment: not reported in paper
authors: McClean, Elizabeth J.; Kim, Sijun; Martinez, Tomas
theories: expectancy violations theory (Jussim, Coleman, & Lerch, 1987); gender role / status characteristics theory; social role theory (Eagly, 1987)
keywords: agentic voice; benevolence; communal voice; competence; expectancy violations theory; gender stereotypes; idea endorsement; positive counter-stereotypical voice; voice
research_question: How does the gender of a speaker, combined with the content and presentation style of the idea they raise, affect whether that idea for change is endorsed—and through which attributional mechanisms (competence vs. benevolence) does this effect operate for women versus for men?
mechanism_summary: Grounded in expectancy violations theory (EVT) and social role theory, the paper argues that behaviors violating gender expectations draw heightened perceptual attention, and that *positive* violations are evaluated more favorably via the augmentation principle. When women voice agentically (counter-stereotypical), evaluators credit unanticipated competence; when men voice communally (counter-stereotypical), evaluators credit unanticipated benevolence. These attributions, in turn, raise the likelihood of endorsement, but the operative dimension of trustworthiness differs by gender. Support was consistent for women (competence pathway) across all studies; for men, the benevolence pathway emerged only in Study 3 when both content and language use jointly signaled communality.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reconceptualizes voice endorsement as jointly contingent on "who" (speaker gender) and "what/how" (idea content and presentation), rather than treating these factors in isolation, creating a framework that maps the two fundamental dimensions of behavioral content—agency and communality—onto gender stereotypes. It identifies a new attributional mechanism—evaluators' inferences about the speaker's competence and benevolence—linking voice to endorsement, and shows the operative dimension differs by gender. It also advances EVT by testing the augmentation process and reconciling gender role theory with EVT by clarifying that positive versus negative violations drive divergent (benefit vs. backlash) outcomes.
practical_implication: Employees should take a holistic approach—considering who they are (especially gender), what they say (content), and how they say it (presentation style) in tandem—to raise the likelihood their idea is endorsed, because identical content or delivery is evaluated differently depending on the speaker's gender. The findings offer "optimism" to women and men who defy gender stereotypes positively: women voicing agentically and men voicing communally may be especially likely to have such counter-stereotypical ideas endorsed.
limitations: Study 1 relied on managers recalling and categorizing a subordinate's voice event (critical incident technique), so the evaluator's perception of content—rather than the subordinate's intended content—is what was captured, and language use could not be controlled in that study. Studies 2 and 3 used vignette experiments, which have been criticized for limited external validity, though this design maximized control over content and language manipulations.
future_research: Future scholars should examine counter-stereotypical voice for other categories of employees (e.g., ethnic minorities, sexual orientations, cultural backgrounds) and intersectional stereotypes such as how women of color are evaluated when voicing agentically. Researchers are also encouraged to study the extent to which an idea positively or negatively violates expectancies (rather than taking valence as given), additional content–language combinations, and the interaction of promotive versus prohibitive voice with agency and communality.
abstract: This paper explores how gender affects idea endorsement. We depart from the existing approach, which considers the effect of gender and other factors in isolation, and propose that endorsement depends on who suggests which ideas and in what ways. Drawing upon expectancy violations theory, we argue that "positive counter-stereotypical voice"—conceptualized as an idea that signals desirable traits stereotypically associated with the opposite gender—is more likely to be endorsed compared to "positive yet stereotypical voice" (i.e., that signals desirable traits consistent with stereotypical expectations of the speaker's gender). We argue that communal voice for men and agentic voice for women constitute positive counter-stereotypical voice, whereas agentic voice for men and communal voice for women constitute positive stereotypical voice. Then, we examine why positive counter-stereotypical voice results in positive evaluations—endorsement—by arguing that it affects attributions of competence and benevolence, albeit differently for men and for women. We test our hypotheses across three studies—one field and two experimental. Combined, we contribute to both the voice and gender literatures and shed new light on our understanding of how and why women and men get their ideas endorsed.
apa_citation: McClean, E. J., Kim, S., & Martinez, T. (2022). Which ideas for change are endorsed? How agentic and communal voice affects endorsement differently for men and for women. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(2), 634–655. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0492

title: How Other- and Self-Compassion Reduce Burnout through Resource Replenishment
methods: Two-study generalization-and-extension design. Study 1: three-wave longitudinal field survey (one year apart) analyzed with multilevel path analysis in Mplus 8.2 (Type=Complex, grand-mean centered). Study 2: 10-day within-person randomized compassion-intervention experiential sampling study analyzed with multilevel path analysis and parametric-bootstrap mediation (Monte Carlo, 20,000 replications).
industry: Social service provision (Study 1); undergraduate business education (Study 2)
time_period: Three annual waves, one year apart (Study 1); 10 consecutive business days (Study 2)
units: Social service employees (Study 1); undergraduate business students, day-level observations (Study 2)
n_sample: 130 social service providers (Study 1); 100 students / 702 day-level observations (Study 2)
authors: Schabram, Kira; Heng, Yu Tse
theories: conservation of resources theory (Hobfoll, 1989; Halbesleben et al., 2014; Hobfoll et al., 2018); self-determination theory (secondary)
keywords: burnout; compassion; conservation of resources; cynicism; exhaustion; inefficacy; resource replenishment; self-compassion
research_question: "When can compassionate action reduce an actor's own burnout?" The paper asks whether and how employees can act compassionately toward themselves or others to generate the specific resources depleted by each dimension of burnout (exhaustion, cynicism, inefficacy) and thereby recover.
mechanism_summary: Grounded in conservation of resources theory's primacy-of-resource-loss and resource-investment principles, the paper argues that burnout persists because early depletion erodes the very resources needed to combat further demands, so recovery requires agentic, resource-generating action. Compassion is most restorative when its particular expression generates the resource that fits the depleted dimension: self-compassion builds self-control to remedy exhaustion, other-compassion builds belonging to remedy cynicism, and both can build self-esteem to remedy inefficacy. Across the field survey (direct effects) and the diary study (indirect effects through resources), self-compassion reduced exhaustion and other-compassion reduced cynicism, while effects on inefficacy were more varied, supporting a compassion–burnout "fit" logic rather than a uniform benefit of compassion.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances burnout research by treating recovery as an agentic act the sufferer can initiate—shifting focus from what others do to the burnt-out employee to what employees can do for themselves—and by retaining all three burnout dimensions, surfacing robust cynicism effects that exhaustion-only models miss. It imports self-compassion from psychology and distinguishes it from other-compassion, showing their consequences differ by resource generated and dimension remedied. For COR theory, it provides proof-of-concept for under-tested dynamics (resource caravans, equifinality, multifinality) by modeling three resource categories simultaneously.
practical_implication: Compassion can help address the workplace burnout epidemic, but the calculus is not straightforward—not all compassion combats all burnout, and benefits depend on matching the compassion expression to the depleted dimension. Because the studies found only indirect effects when compassion was prompted by instruction, reaping benefits likely requires ongoing investment in assessing burnt-out employees' specific needs and matching appropriate recovery strategies; employers can use the validated self- and other-compassion measures to track fluctuations.
limitations: The generalization-and-extension approach varied multiple elements at once (sample, time frame, voluntary vs. directed compassion), making it difficult to attribute inconsistent direct/indirect findings to result instability versus design choices. Both samples were largely women and of equal status (coworkers or fellow students), and both studies relied on self-reports; in Study 2, a morning measurement of self-control inadvertently time-separated it from the night-time resource measures.
future_research: Future work should replicate inconsistent results by varying only one element at a time, extend to male-dominated professions and to leader–subordinate (unequal-status) interactions, and use controlled experiments with objective or multisource measures. The authors call for incorporating compassion recipients to study interpersonal/dyadic resource crossover, probing whether compassion can serve as a prophylactic, examining order effects across burnout dimensions and partial-burnout profiles, and pursuing field experiments and clinical trials to test COR-based interventions.
abstract: The average employee feels burnt out, a multidimensional state of depletion likely to persist without intervention. In this paper, we consider compassion as an agentic action by which employees may replenish their own depleted resources and thereby recover. We draw on conservation of resources theory to examine the resource-generating power of two distinct expressions of compassion (selfand other-directed) on three dimensions of burnout (exhaustion, cynicism, inefficacy). Utilizing two complementary designs—a longitudinal field survey of 130 social service providers and an experiential sampling methodology with 100 business students across 10 days—we find a complex pattern of results indicating that both compassion expressions have the potential to generate salutogenic resources (self-control, belonging, self-esteem) that replenish different dimensions of burnout.
apa_citation: Schabram, K., & Heng, Y. T. (2022). How other- and self-compassion reduce burnout through resource replenishment. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(2), 453–478. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0493

title: “I” Am Affirmed, but Are “We”? Social Identity Processes Influencing Refugees’ Work Initiative and Community Embeddedness
methods: Three-wave time-lagged field survey (two months); two-level (individual + supervisory-unit) structural equation models estimated in Mplus 8.3 with FIML; moderated-mediation tests via Monte Carlo confidence intervals.
industry: Manufacturing (nine companies)
country: A large country at the nexus of the Asian and European continents (Syrian refugee employees)
time_period: Three administrations over two months (post-2011 Syrian civil war context)
units: Syrian refugee employees nested in supervisory units within nine organizations
n_sample: 389 refugee employees in 88 supervisory units
authors: Schaubroeck, John M.; Demirtas, Ozgur; Peng, Ann C.; Pei, Dong
theories: self-categorization theory (Turner, 1985); social identity perspective (Hogg, 2003); social identity theory (Tajfel & Turner, 1979)
keywords: community embeddedness; diversity climate; organizational identification; perceived insider status; perceived societal stigma; refugees; self-categorization theory; work initiative
research_question: How do social identity processes—specifically the salience of the refugee social identity—shape whether refugee employees' perceptions that they are personally treated as insiders translate, through organizational identification, into work initiative and community embeddedness in the host society? When does personal affirmation at work fail to promote engagement?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on self-categorization theory, the authors argue that perceiving high insider status affirms refugees' personal identity and thereby builds organizational identification, which serves as a transitional/bridging identity that supplies the security and confidence to take the interpersonal risks involved in work initiative and in embedding within the broader community. However, an unfavorable diversity climate raises the "category fit" between refugees and nonrefugees, rendering the refugee social identity more salient; per the functional antagonism between social and personal identity salience, a salient refugee identity weakens the effect of personal affirmation (insider status) on identification. This salience-amplifying effect of poor diversity climate operates only among refugees who perceive high societal stigmatization of refugees (high category accessibility), so the indirect effects are weakest when diversity climate is low and perceived stigma is high.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends diversity-climate theory by showing that diversity climate operates as a collective signal that conditions whether individualized, personal-identity-affirming treatment (perceived insider status) builds organizational identification—reframing diversity climate beyond a simple positive perception of the organization toward a determinant of identity salience and collective sensemaking. It also extends work on organizational identification as a bridging/transitional identity (Rogers et al., 2017) from ex-inmates to employed refugees and, potentially, to other stigmatized and marginalized groups, applying self-categorization theory's accessibility-and-fit logic to organizational engagement outcomes.
practical_implication: Employers can harness refugees' human capital by promoting policies that signal fair treatment and inclusion of all employees and by ensuring individuals feel informed and included, which strengthens identification and downstream initiative and community engagement. Because perceived insider status is a scarce, status-based resource that managers cannot grant to everyone, fostering a favorable shared diversity climate—and educating nonrefugee employees against stigmatizing refugees—is an important complementary lever, especially where the broader climate is unfavorable.
limitations: Generalizing beyond Syrian refugees depends on how their sensemaking is shared with other refugee populations, and the sample comprised refugees employed in established businesses working routinely with nonrefugees, distinguishing them from the larger population of unemployed or camp-confined refugees. The design does not establish causality, relying on theory and temporally separated measures (multi-week lags) to infer causal order; the predicted main effect of diversity climate on identification was not supported, possibly owing to the relatively small number of work units.
future_research: Future work could develop theory on how nonrefugee employees' perceptions and behaviors interact with refugees' in ways that support or impede refugee thriving, and compare nonrefugees with refugees to isolate the transitional-identity mechanism. Researchers might examine whether specific personal affirmations (e.g., positive feedback, merit raises, promotions) show weaker identification effects under unfavorable diversity climate and high stigma, and test whether the diversity-climate-by-insider-status process generalizes across different refugee populations and other stigmatized groups.
abstract: Facilitating refugees’ transitions to host country society is of interest to their host countries and municipalities, employers within those countries, and the refugees themselves. We develop and test a model of how social identity processes, as outlined in selfcategorization theory, influence how perceiving that one is treated as an insider encourages behaviors reflecting social engagement with host country nationals, both within and outside of work. In a sample of 389 Syrian refugee employees in 88 supervisory units, perceived insider status was indirectly related to work initiative and community embeddedness through organizational identification. These indirect effects were moderated by diversity climate and perceived stigmatization of refugees in the broader society. Perceived insider status had its weakest effect on identification, and was not related indirectly to the outcomes when diversity climate was lower and perceived stigma was higher. We discuss the implications for theory development and practice concerning how social identity salience can inhibit personal affirmations at work from encouraging members of marginalized groups to demonstrate a deeper commitment to the organization and society.
apa_citation: Schaubroeck, J. M., Demirtas, O., Peng, A. C., & Pei, D. (2022). “I” am affirmed, but are “we”? Social identity processes influencing refugees’ work initiative and community embeddedness. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(2), 403–426. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0033

title: Pumping the Brakes: Examining the Impact of CEO Political Ideology Divergence on Firm Responses
methods: Quantitative panel study of S&P 500 firms; generalized estimating equation (GEE) models with a Gaussian distribution, identity link, and exchangeable correlation; CEO liberalism measured from 10-year donation records; divergence as absolute difference from president-level national political climate; moderation tests for vested stock options and regulated industry; robustness via alternative climate measures, DWH endogeneity tests, and supplemental outcomes.
industry: Standard & Poor's 500 firms across industries (regulated and non-regulated); R&D intensity limited to firms below 1
time_period: CEOs hired 2004-2010 and followed through 2017 (DV window 2005-2017)
units: Firm-year observations (CEOs at S&P 500 firms)
n_sample: 1,242 firm-year observations (table models report n = 1,244 for R&D intensity and n = 1,216 for retained earnings)
authors: Semadeni, Matthew; Chin, M. K.; Krause, Ryan
theories: political psychology of ideology and threat (Thorisdottir & Jost, 2011); threat response theory (Staw, Sandelands, & Dutton, 1981); upper echelons theory (Hambrick & Mason, 1984; Hambrick, 2007)
keywords: CEO political ideology; R&D intensity; ideology divergence; industry regulation; national political climate; retained earnings; threat response; upper echelons; vested stock options
research_question: How does divergence between the national political climate and a CEO's own political ideology — rather than the CEO's ideology in isolation — affect strategic firm decisions, and how do CEO vested stock options and industry regulation condition that effect?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on upper echelons theory and threat response theory, the authors argue that when the national political climate diverges from a CEO's deeply held political ideology, the CEO perceives a threat to their beliefs and responds defensively by retreating to the known and familiar — curtailing forward-looking, uncertain R&D investment (H1) and accumulating internally controllable retained earnings (H2). Because vested stock options tie the CEO's personal wealth to firm outcomes (an "endowment effect"), they heighten sensitivity to the threat and strengthen both responses (H3, H4). Industry regulation, by reducing managerial discretion and providing stability, both lowers the perceived threat and constrains the CEO's ability to respond, weakening the responses (H5, H6). GEE models on S&P 500 firms support all six hypotheses.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the CEO political ideology literature by shifting attention from a CEO's absolute ideology to the divergence between that ideology and the national political climate, thereby capturing the causal-belief (not merely value) dimension of ideology whose effects flip with the political environment. It also extends threat response theory from realized threats (e.g., resource scarcity, competition) to a purely perceived threat experienced only by those discerning the divergence, and identifies CEO wealth and managerial discretion (via regulation) as boundary conditions on when a perceived threat translates into firm action.
practical_implication: Boards should recognize that a CEO may alter firm decisions — cutting R&D and hoarding earnings — when the political climate diverges from the CEO's ideology, creating a potential agency problem, especially where CEO and board ideologies converge. Because vested stock options intensify these responses, boards should weigh this form of compensation carefully, and ensure CEO decisions to cut R&D or build retained earnings are justified to stakeholders who may not perceive the same threat.
limitations: The study is set in the United States' long-established two-party system, so the divergence construct and theory may not generalize directly to multi-party parliamentary systems. The sample comprises large S&P 500 firms with multinational operations, leaving open whether the effects hold for smaller or mid-sized firms embedded in more regional or local political environments. Ideology is characterized along a single liberal–conservative spectrum, whereas political attitudes are increasingly viewed along separate economic and social dimensions.
future_research: Scholars could examine whether convergence between CEO ideology and the national climate generates the opposite of threat — opportunity or (over)optimism — and test for asymmetric effects of convergence versus divergence. Research could extend the theory to other countries with multi-party systems and to regional or local political environments, and decompose divergence along separate social and economic ideological dimensions to see whether effects differ across them.
abstract: Research on CEO political ideology has demonstrated the construct’s impact on many firm outcomes. Yet, political ideology does not exist in a vacuum; beyond representing a CEO’s values, political ideology also affects the CEO’s perception of the political environment. It remains unclear how a difference between the national political climate and the CEO’s own political ideology might affect strategic decision-making. Applying upper echelons and threat response perspectives, we propose that divergence between the national political climate and the CEO’s own ideology provokes a threat response, leading CEOs to cut research and development spending and increase retained earnings. We then examine two boundary conditions for this relationship. First, we propose that CEO wealth tied to the firm in the form of vested stock options will increase the perceived threat of ideology divergence, strengthening the threat response. Second, we propose that industry regulation will dampen the threat perceived from divergence, as well as hindering the CEO’s ability to respond to the threat at all, because of greater stability and lower discretion, respectively. We test these predictions on a sample of Standard & Poor’s 500 CEOs from 2004 to 2017 and find support for our hypothesized relationships.
apa_citation: Semadeni, M., Chin, M. K., & Krause, R. (2022). Pumping the brakes: Examining the impact of CEO political ideology divergence on firm responses. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(2), 516–544. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1131
