title: From the Editors—Insights on How We Try to Show Empathy, Respect, and Inclusion in AMJ
methods: Editorial (From the Editors essay): the AMJ editorial team articulates practices and considerations for showing empathy, respect, and inclusion across reviewer, associate-editor, and author roles.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Umphress, Elizabeth E.; Rink, Floor; Muir (Zapata), Cindy P.; Hideg, Ivona
theories: Not reported in paper
keywords: belongingness; empathy; inclusion; language barriers; peer review; respect; sample preference; uniqueness
research_question: How can the AMJ community—reviewers, associate editors, and authors—demonstrate empathy, respect, and inclusion throughout the manuscript review process in order to maintain and develop an open, inclusive academic community, especially in the wake of disruptive worldwide events?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Feeling included rests on satisfying both belongingness and uniqueness; treating colleagues with empathy and respect during review interactions helps establish an inclusive environment and can enhance the inclusion of ideas and scholars.
- Key constructs: Empathy (other-oriented feelings congruent with another's perceived welfare); respect (regard for the feelings, wishes, and unique qualities of others); inclusion (balancing belongingness and uniqueness).
- Reviewer considerations: Preventing positive cultural sample preference (e.g., reflexive U.S./Western-sample demands); overcoming language barriers for non-native English speakers; respecting differences in research norms, and monetary and temporal resources; being open to ideas from the review team.
- Associate-editor considerations: Assembling diverse, well-equipped review teams; communicating editorial decisions and "high-risk" revisions with empathy and respect; minimizing revision rounds; clarifying what a decision means for the specific journal.
- Author considerations: Respecting human-research-participant rights and institutional review processes; referring to prior scholarship respectfully; granting reviewers and editors the benefit of the doubt.

The editors argue that small, everyday acts of empathy and respect—not only large-scale structural change—can enhance inclusion within the scholarly community. Drawing on the belongingness/uniqueness account of inclusion, they reason that socially supported and respected scholars reciprocate by treating others with empathy, which builds trust, affirms unique strengths, and creates a more open academic community. They apply this logic concretely to the reviewer, associate-editor, and author roles, surfacing recurring inclusion tensions (sample bias, language, differential resources, decision framing) and recommending empathetic, developmental practices to mitigate them.
theoretical_contribution: The essay is not a theory-building piece; it stakes out an editorial position for AMJ, translating research on belongingness, uniqueness, empathy, respect, and organizational justice into a set of inclusion-oriented norms for the peer-review process. Its contribution is normative and practical: it makes visible the often-implicit ways empathy, respect, and inclusion (or their absence) operate across review roles and frames small interpersonal acts as a complement to larger structural reform in building an inclusive scholarly community.
practical_implication: Reviewers are urged to avoid reflexively demanding U.S./Western replications or devaluing research from other cultural contexts, to focus on theory and empirics rather than minor grammar of non-native English authors, to be sensitive to authors' monetary and temporal resource constraints, and to write developmental, respectful reviews. Associate editors are urged to assemble diverse review teams, explain decisions and "high-risk" revisions clearly, and minimize revision rounds. Authors are urged to protect participants, cite prior work respectfully, and extend reviewers the benefit of the doubt.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editors hope these reflections—together with prior and forthcoming discussions of inclusion in the publication process—will prompt continued conversation about demonstrating empathy, respect, and inclusion toward fellow scholars, and will help expand access to authors and reviewers and address research questions that remain less examined in the literature.
abstract: Not reported in paper
apa_citation: Umphress, E. E., Rink, F., Muir (Zapata), C. P., & Hideg, I. (2022). From the editors—Insights on how we try to show empathy, respect, and inclusion in AMJ. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(2), 363–370. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.4002

title: The Past Is Prologue? Venture-Capital Syndicates’ Collaborative Experience and Start-Up Exits
methods: Longitudinal observational study of Crunchbase data; competing-risks proportional-hazards (Cox-type) models of time-to-exit, with Heckman selection correction (inverse Mills ratio) and inverse-probability-of-treatment weighting (IPTW) to address endogeneity; supplemented by linear regression of acquisition-target market-segment similarity.
industry: Technology-sector start-ups across 444 Crunchbase market segments (e.g., software, enterprise software, analytics, e-commerce, mobile, clean technology, semiconductors) backed by venture-capital and angel investors
units: VC-backed start-ups (and their first-round VC syndicates)
n_sample: 10,879 U.S.-based VC-backed start-ups
authors: Wang, Dan; Cox Pahnke, Emily; McDonald, Rory M.
theories: interorganizational networks; relational embeddedness / paradox of embeddedness (Granovetter, 1985; Uzzi, 1997); social capital theory
keywords: acquisition; broadcast success; focused success; initial public offering; interorganizational collaboration; relational embeddedness; start-up exits; venture-capital syndicates
research_question: When do prior collaborations between organizations breed collaborative success? Specifically, how does the relational embeddedness of a start-up's first-round VC syndicate—the extent to which its members have previously coinvested together—shape whether the start-up achieves a "focused" success (acquisition) versus a "broadcast" success (IPO)?
mechanism_summary: The authors argue that higher relational embeddedness among a syndicate's VCs builds shared interpretive schema, trust, and coordination efficiencies that channel specialized, tacit knowledge into the start-up, positioning it for an acquisition—a focused success appraised by domain-specific experts. Lower embeddedness exposes the start-up to more diverse, nonredundant knowledge and broader social ties, encouraging strategies that appeal to multiple market segments and thus an IPO—a broadcast success determined by diverse appraisers and requiring complex coordination. The same diversity that aids broadcast success, however, can impair coordination, which is why less-embedded syndicates are also associated with higher start-up failure.
theoretical_contribution: The paper offers a resolution to the "paradox of embeddedness" by reframing it as a contingency on the type of collective success: rather than embeddedness being uniformly beneficial or harmful, higher embeddedness promotes focused successes while lower embeddedness promotes broadcast successes. It introduces a typology of focused versus broadcast success distinguished by their appraisers, complexity, and prominence, and extends entrepreneurship research by treating investors' prior collaborative experience—and the distinction between exit pathways—as an underappreciated driver of start-up outcomes rather than collapsing exits into a single performance variable.
practical_implication: Founders can read a prospective VC's relational embeddedness (its history of coinvestment partners and exit types) as a signal of the likely path ahead: highly embedded, acquisition-oriented syndicates suit founders aiming to be acquired, whereas a more diverse, less-embedded investor set better supports an IPO ambition—though at higher risk of failure. VCs seeking more IPO "home runs" might deliberately diversify their coinvestors, while those seeking steady acquisition returns might embed in a stable network of like-minded co-investors.
limitations: The measure of collaborative experience captures only first-round investors, omitting later-round investors who also shape trajectories. The study cannot observe how VC partners and founders reach consensus on which exit to pursue, nor does it measure the financial value (acquisition premiums, IPO pricing) created by exited firms. As a macro-level study, it theorizes cognitive mechanisms (identity, coordination, shared interpretive schema) that are not directly observed in the data, and the exclusion restriction for its instrument cannot be explicitly tested.
future_research: Future work could measure ultimate financial returns using acquisition premiums and IPO pricing as outcomes, and examine settings where different types of success are tightly coupled or nonexclusive (e.g., dissertation-committee placements, critical-acclaim-plus-box-office collaborations) rather than mutually exclusive. Micro-level experimental research could better isolate how cognitive factors such as identity and shared schema drive different kinds of success, and further studies could probe how network structure interacts with informational context across diverse appraisal environments.
abstract: Past research has produced contradictory insights into how prior collaboration between organizations—their relational embeddedness—impacts collective collaborative performance. We theorize that the effect of relational embeddedness on collaborative success is contingent on the type of success under consideration, and we develop a typology of two kinds of success. We test our hypotheses using data from Crunchbase on a sample of almost 11,000 U.S. start-ups backed by venture-capital (VC) firms, using the VCs’ previous collaborative experience to predict the type of success that the start-ups will experience. Our findings indicate that as prior collaborative experience within a group of VCs increases, a jointly funded start-up is more likely to exit by acquisition (which we call a focused success); with less prior experience among the group of VCs, a jointly funded start-up is more likely to exit by initial public offering (a broadcast success). Our results deepen understanding of the connections between organizational performance and collaboration networks, contributing to entrepreneurship research on the role of investors in technology ventures.
apa_citation: Wang, D., Cox Pahnke, E., & McDonald, R. M. (2022). The past is prologue? Venture-capital syndicates’ collaborative experience and start-up exits. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(2), 371–402. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1312

title: Too Much on the Plate? How Executive Job Demands Harm Firm Innovation and Reduce Share of Exploratory Innovations
methods: Cross-sectional survey of top management teams; Heckman two-stage sample selection correction followed by second-stage OLS regressions with industry and ownership fixed effects; moderated (interaction) models testing 10 hypotheses.
industry: High-tech industries (manufacturing, transportation, oil processing, software development); mostly small and medium-sized, owner/family-controlled firms
country: China (firms located in 43 districts of Suzhou, Jiangsu Province)
time_period: Survey conducted in 2015; performance-challenge measured over 2013-2014; a 2019 validation survey of 117 executives supplemented measurement
units: Firms (TMT-level survey responses from up to six top executives per firm)
n_sample: 243 firms (final analytic sample after listwise deletion; 412 initial targets, 404 participating)
authors: Zhu, David H.; Jia, Liangding; Li, Fei
theories: executive job demands theory (Hambrick, Finkelstein, & Mooney, 2005); job demands–resources model (Bakker & Demerouti, 2007); upper echelons theory (Hambrick & Mason, 1984; Hambrick, 2007)
keywords: TMT variety; executive job demands; exploitative innovations; exploratory innovations; organizational climate; overall innovation
research_question: How do executive job demands—a fundamental attribute of top executives' objective job environment rather than their cognition or characteristics—influence a firm's overall innovation and its share of exploratory (versus exploitative) innovations, and which features of the executive job environment (TMT variety in gender/age/tenure, controlling climate, innovative climate, employees' education) moderate these effects?
mechanism_summary: Executive job demands consume top executives' limited cognitive and emotional resources, forcing them to attend selectively to familiar information, rely on heuristics (e.g., the availability heuristic), weaken long-term orientation, lower risk-taking propensity, and centralize decision-making—reducing their ability to identify innovation opportunities and integrate knowledge. Because exploratory innovation requires divergent thinking, longer horizons, and greater risk, job demands harm exploration more than exploitation, lowering the exploratory share. TMT variety supplies cognitive diversity that buffers both effects; a controlling climate frees cognitive resources (helping overall innovation) but reinforces exploitation (worsening the exploratory share); educated employees compensate cognitively (helping overall innovation) yet are rewarded for exploitation (worsening the exploratory share).
theoretical_contribution: The study shifts innovation research from executives' cognition and individual attributes toward their objective job environment, identifying executive job demands as a novel and important determinant of firm innovation. It advances strategic-leadership/upper-echelons research by examining the previously untested boundary conditions and applicability of executive job demands theory to innovation, and—by showing systematic effects on overall innovation and the exploratory share—offers what the authors describe as the first piece of direct empirical evidence of the validity of executive job demands theory. It also extends the broader job demands literature to the most influential group of organizational members, top executives.
practical_implication: Executives should be acutely aware that high job demands can damage firm innovation, especially exploratory innovation. Firms can counteract these effects through levers in the executive job environment: cultivating TMT variety in gender, age, and tenure to buffer both overall innovation and the exploratory share; recognizing that a controlling climate and a highly educated workforce help overall innovation but can crowd out exploration; and attending to how the design of the executive job environment shapes the exploration-exploitation balance.
limitations: The one-wave (cross-sectional) survey design limits strong causal inference. Evidence on two moderators was inconsistent: the innovative-climate hypotheses (7 and 8) received no support, and the significance of the innovative-climate and employees'-education moderators shifted depending on whether TMT demographic controls were included, so those moderating findings warrant cautious interpretation. The sample comprised small and medium-sized firms in China, constraining generalizability to other firm sizes and national contexts.
future_research: Future studies could systematically examine why different indicators of executive job demands (e.g., task challenge appeared most influential) have differential effects on strategic decisions, and assess generalizability across countries with stronger or weaker institutional environments and differing cultural individualism. Researchers could explore CEO–TMT interface dynamics (e.g., patterns of dependence between the CEO and other executives, harmony-oriented social norms) as conditioning factors, and extend the framework to other strategic consequences of executive job demands such as competitive behavior, mergers and acquisitions, and other risk-taking decisions.
abstract: Building on psychological research on job demands and executive job demands theory, we explain why executive job demands negatively influence a firm’s overall innovation and shift the balance of innovative activities toward a larger share of exploitative innovations at the expense of exploratory innovations, leading to a smaller share of innovations that are exploratory. In addition, we explain how variety in executives’ gender, age, and tenure and an innovative climate weaken the negative effects of job demands on both overall innovation and the share of exploratory innovations. Our theory suggests that a controlling climate and employees’ education weaken the negative effect of job demands on overall innovation but exacerbate the negative effect of job demands on the share of exploratory innovations. Using surveys collected on-site from 243 Chinese firms, we find support for five of our 10 hypotheses and marginal support for three additional hypotheses but no support for the two moderating effects of innovative climate. This study shifts innovation scholars’ attention away from executive cognition and characteristics to their job environment attributes. It also develops executive job demands theory by examining its boundaries and applicability to the domain of innovation management.
apa_citation: Zhu, D. H., Jia, L., & Li, F. (2022). Too much on the plate? How executive job demands harm firm innovation and reduce share of exploratory innovations. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(2), 606–633. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0334

title: Prosocial Occupations, Work Autonomy, and the Origins of the Social Class Pay Gap
methods: Two cross-sectional observational studies using nationally representative U.S. survey data (General Social Survey 1972-2018; NLSY97). Occupational characteristics from O*NET and BLS matched to respondents' occupations. OLS multiple regression with survey-year/region fixed effects and a bootstrapped dual-path mediation (path analysis) model.
industry: All occupations in the U.S. economy (occupational characteristics from O*NET and BLS); GSS uses 2010 Census classification yielding 539 occupations
time_period: GSS waves 1972-2018; NLSY97 cohort first surveyed 1997, occupations measured in 2017
units: Individual survey respondents (working adults) matched to occupation-level characteristics
n_sample: GSS analytic sample ~50,204 (regression models N≈22,484); NLSY97 analytic samples 5,042-5,322
authors: Fang, Ray Tsai; Tilcsik, András
theories: devaluation theory (England, 1992; England, Allison, & Wu, 2007); models of self (independent vs. interdependent) (Markus & Kitayama, 2010); sociocultural perspective on social class (Bourdieu, 1979; Stephens & Townsend, 2013)
keywords: occupational segregation; occupational sorting; prosocial occupations; social class pay gap; social mobility; work autonomy
research_question: Why do people from lower-class origins earn less than people from upper-class origins even when they are similar in education and occupational prestige? The paper asks whether class-based sorting into occupations that differ in work autonomy versus prosocial orientation helps explain this residual social class pay gap.
mechanism_summary: Drawing on a sociocultural perspective, the authors argue that childhood resource abundance fosters an independent model of self that draws upper-class people toward high-autonomy occupations, while resource scarcity fosters an interdependent model of self that orients lower-class people toward prosocial occupations; this two-sided supply- and demand-side sorting, combined with the wage premium autonomous occupations command over prosocial ones (devaluation of communal work plus willingness to sacrifice pay for meaningful work), reproduces the class pay gap above and beyond education and prestige.
theoretical_contribution: The study fuses the sociocultural perspective on social class with research on occupational wage inequality to develop and test a theory of class reproduction through occupational sorting, identifying an unexplored path—differential attainment of autonomous versus prosocial occupations—by which class origin shapes income. It extends research on social class, occupational segregation, and wage inequality by moving class-based autonomy/prosociality differences from non-work behavioral settings into labor markets, examining nearly all occupations in the economy rather than only access to a narrow set of prestigious jobs.
practical_implication: Compensation managers can curb the class pay gap by avoiding overpaying for autonomy and underpaying for prosociality—e.g., using job evaluation of compensable factors and adding accountability and transparency to pay decisions. Managers can limit class-based occupational segregation by prioritizing task performance and structured, job-analysis-based selection. Business schools and collective-voice institutions such as unions are also identified as broader levers.
limitations: Findings may be subject to omitted-variable bias from unmeasured occupational characteristics, and the authors could not directly control for individual cognitive ability. The occupation-level data lack the granularity to capture intra-occupational variation (e.g., where prosocial-titled jobs diverge in practice from their prosocial promise), and the two data sources differ in available controls and sampled populations.
future_research: Future work could establish the extent to which additional variables (e.g., cognitive ability, social capital, personality) explain the class pay gap, and examine whether the proportion of upper- versus lower-class incumbents shapes an occupation's own characteristics over time. The authors also call for studying intra-occupational variation, such as how the gap between an occupation's prosocial promise and its day-to-day realities affects job seekers from different class origins.
abstract: Despite decades of research on social mobility and wage disparities, it remains a puzzle why people from lower-class families earn less than people from upper-class families even when similar in education and occupational prestige. Taking a sociocultural perspective on social class, we argue that a key contributor to the class pay gap is that people from upper-class origins tend to work in occupations with greater autonomy, whereas their lower-class counterparts tend to work in occupations that are more prosocial. We further propose that autonomous occupations pay better than prosocial occupations. Across two distinct nationally representative samples in the United States, we find that people with upper-class (vs. lower-class) parents are more likely to work in autonomous occupations, but less likely to work in prosocial occupations, even when controlling for education, occupational prestige, and other potential confounds. This pattern of occupational sorting explains a substantial portion of the class pay gap. Our study extends the literatures on social class, occupational segregation, and social mobility, and joins an important scholarly conversation that has, until recently, taken place outside the field of management.
apa_citation: Fang, R. T., & Tilcsik, A. (2022). Prosocial occupations, work autonomy, and the origins of the social class pay gap. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(3), 903–929. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1564

title: Investing In Communities: Forging New Ground in Corporate Community Codevelopment through Relational and Psychological Pathways
methods: Longitudinal inductive grounded-theory ethnography over three years; participant observation, semistructured interviews, informal narratives, reflection journals, and archival documents, analyzed through open coding and the constant comparative method to build a process model.
industry: Mining, oil and gas, banking, retail, professional services, and transportation
country: Australia (remote Indigenous communities in four regions)
time_period: Programs launched in 2001; focal fieldwork over three years
units: 35 corporate-community investment programs; corporate secondees, Indigenous partners, Indigenous leaders, corporate leaders, and a nonprofit broker (Bridge)
n_sample: 35 programs funded by 11 corporations; 1,176 hours of observation, 63 interviews, 51 narratives, 35 reflections; 70 primary participants
authors: Gibson, Cristina B.
theories: collaborative value creation in cross-sector partnerships (Austin & Seitanidi, 2012); identity theory (Ashforth, 2007; Ramarajan, 2014); self-determination theory (Gagne & Deci, 2005; Ryan & Deci, 2000); stakeholder theory
keywords: communal advocacy; community development; corporate community codevelopment; corporate community investment; cross-sector partnerships; dignity; identity synergy; perspective-taking; reciprocating respect; self-determination
research_question: "How does the implementation of CCI contribute to mutual development in corporations and communities?" The study seeks to resolve the tension between the managerial focus on returns that characterizes the corporate community investment (CCI) literature and the capacity-building emphasis advocated by community development scholars.
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering conditions: deep, direct, and ongoing corporate involvement enabled by local embeddedness of corporate secondees and the intentional early exit of the nonprofit broker (Bridge) once programs were underway.
- Relational processes: mutual perspective-taking, reciprocating respect, and communal advocacy (the "connective tissue" between corporations and communities).
- Psychological pathways: identity synergy, dignity, and self-determination (autonomy, competence, relatedness) for Indigenous partners; intercultural competence, meaningfulness, and thriving for corporate secondees.
- Behavioral changes (intermediate): technical/professional skills, proactivity, and strategic capability in communities; intercultural adjustment, prosocial behavior, and informed strategic decisions in corporations.
- Emergent outcomes (longer term): community-wide uplift and social impact (economic participation, educational attainment, community safety), plus reputational and productivity gains and feedback into firms' social-impact strategies.

As corporations and communities collaborated over an extended period, three relational processes each commonly cascaded into specific psychological gains and then behavioral changes among individuals, communities, and corporations. For example, communal advocacy fostered self-determination, which enabled strategic capability; mutual perspective-taking facilitated identity synergy and intercultural competence. These pathways exhibited equifinality and sometimes broadened beyond direct participants—a "cascade of mutual transformation"—shifting CCI from an arm's-length influx of funding toward self-sustaining codevelopment.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops an evidence-based theory of corporate community codevelopment that integrates the management literature on CCI with community development and cross-sector partnerships, identifying the relational and psychological mechanisms that connect investment to lasting behavioral change. It extends self-determination theory by tracing how communal advocacy meets autonomy and competence needs, contributes to community development theory by foregrounding identity synergy (distinct from identity holism or enhancement) and dignity, and documents a previously undocumented mechanism by which cross-sector partnerships produce transformative, cascading change across racial and ethnic lines.
practical_implication: Funding alone is an unreliable CCI strategy; meaningful impact requires deep, ongoing relationships that build local control and autonomy. Practitioners can identify the behavioral outcome of greatest interest and work backward through the pathways—e.g., mutual perspective-taking for skill development, reciprocated respect for proactivity, communal advocacy for strategic capability—using participatory processes such as community visioning, storytelling, and action planning that center community ownership.
limitations: Formal financial analysis was not undertaken because of an insufficient sample size, so business-value claims rest on self-reported gains in loyalty, performance, reputation, and productivity. The approach is unlikely to apply to all types of community development—following Ebrahim and Rangan's contingency framework, simpler change theories (e.g., emergency relief) may warrant a focus on activities rather than longer-term impact—and the findings emerge from a specific Indigenous Australian context.
future_research: Future work should capture each element of the model using longitudinal and quasi-experimental designs that compare codevelopment with more traditional CCI modes and varying program durations. Additional priorities include measuring intermediate community impacts (e.g., strategic capabilities, cultural-heritage maintenance) with multifaceted indicators, examining mutuality in inputs and activities (not just relational processes), and exploring how Bridge's initial brokerage role can be transferred to other settings.
abstract: Within management scholarship and practice, corporate community investments are heralded as an important form of corporate social responsibility. Yet, community development scholars have engaged in discourse and debate regarding corporate community investment, surmising that in some instances it does more harm than good. Where management research has not focused—and can substantially contribute to both theory and practice—is on how to implement corporate community investment for mutual gain. Integrating the fields of management and community development, I provide an evidence-based theory for understanding corporate community codevelopment, addressing relational and psychological pathways for behavioral change. This model emerged from a longitudinal qualitative ethnographic study of corporate-community investment programs, involving 1,176 hours of observations, 63 interviews, as well as narratives and reflections from participants representing 11 large corporations, at-risk remote Indigenous Australian communities, and a nonprofit organization. The 35 programs in focus involved initiatives such as family income management, educational trusts, literacy programs, and nutritional and substance abuse campaigns. The results in competency-building and community development have exceeded the expectations of all parties. Implications for theory, research, policy, and practice in management and community development are included.
apa_citation: Gibson, C. B. (2022). Investing in communities: Forging new ground in corporate community codevelopment through relational and psychological pathways. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(3), 930–957. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1664

title: The Policy–People Gap: Decision-Makers Choose Policies That Favor Different Applicants than They Select When Making Individual Decisions
methods: Series of 16 preregistered survey experiments (4 main + 15 supplementary studies; total N = 10,883) using within- and between-subjects designs; chi-square and McNemar tests of choice, OLS regression, confirmatory factor analysis, PROCESS (moderated) mediation with percentile bootstrap, and an internal random-effects meta-analysis.
industry: College admissions and workplace hiring (technology occupations); samples of admissions officers, technology-industry employees, management faculty, and lay people
country: United States (Study 2 also included United Kingdom-based participants)
time_period: Cross-sectional vignette experiments; not a longitudinal panel
units: Individual decision-makers (admissions officers, hiring-experienced employees, lay participants) making selection choices
n_sample: 16 preregistered studies with 10,883 participants total (four main studies n = 2,698)
authors: Munguia Gomez, David M.; Levine, Emma E.
theories: construal-level theory (Trope & Liberman, 2010); microjustice and macrojustice (Brickman, Folger, Goode, & Schul, 1981)
keywords: college admissions; construal level; fairness; hiring; macrojustice; microjustice; policy–people gap; selection decisions
research_question: Why do decision-makers choose selection policies that favor lower-scoring, disadvantaged applicants yet select higher-scoring, advantaged applicants when making the corresponding individual decisions, and how can this "policy–people gap" be mitigated?
mechanism_summary: Policies are construed at a higher (more abstract) level than individual decisions. Drawing on construal-level theory and the microjustice/macrojustice distinction, the authors theorize that the abstractness of policy decisions shifts decision-makers away from a microjustice standard (what is fair to specific individuals, given their merit) toward a macrojustice standard (what is fair in the aggregate, e.g., equality across groups). Because microjustice favors the higher-achieving applicant and macrojustice favors the disadvantaged applicant, the same person makes systematically divergent choices across the two framings—even when consequences are identical. An intervention asking decision-makers to prioritize their institution's macrojustice standard roughly halved the gap by shifting individual decisions, while leaving policy decisions (already macrojustice-motivated) unchanged.
theoretical_contribution: The paper identifies and explains a novel, robust "policy–people gap" and offers a fresh account of long-noted principle–implementation gaps: rather than failing to think through implementation or yielding to temptation, decision-makers earnestly pursue fairness in both decisions but rely on different standards of fairness that shift with construal level. It imports the microjustice/macrojustice constructs into organizational selection contexts, develops validated scales to measure them, and links justice scholarship to construal-level theory, preference reversals, and the psychology of policymaking.
practical_implication: Organizations seeking to align espoused selection policies with the individual decisions those policies are meant to guide can deploy a simple, transparent intervention: have decision-makers learn about both standards of fairness and explicitly prioritize the organization's macrojustice standard when making individual choices. This low-touch step reduced the gap by about half and increased selection of disadvantaged applicants, though it did not change decision-makers' underlying beliefs about which choice is fair.
limitations: The findings rest on vignette experiments that maximize experimental control but are low in external validity, and policies were operationalized narrowly (information held constant across policy and individual framings). The studies measured reliance on microjustice/macrojustice but did not directly measure construal level, and they sampled only a subset of possible macrojustice and microjustice principles (e.g., egalitarian and meritocratic ones), leaving open whether the gap generalizes beyond these specific principles.
future_research: Future work should examine the policy–people gap in more naturalistic, higher-external-validity settings, directly test whether construal level is the operative mechanism, and study macrojustice and microjustice across a broader range of contexts (e.g., aid and helping decisions where microjustice may align with need). Researchers are also encouraged to probe how personal characteristics—beyond the education effect observed here—shape the magnitude of the gap.
abstract: This work documents a contemporary organizational problem—a gap between selection policies and individual selection decisions—and suggests one intervention to address it. In college admissions and workplace hiring contexts, we find that decision-makers are more likely to favor disadvantaged applicants over applicants with objectively higher achievements when choosing between selection policies than choosing between individual applicants. We document this policy–people gap among admissions officers, working professionals, and lay people using both within-subject and between-subject designs and across a range of stimuli. We find that the gap is driven in part by shifting standards of fairness across the two types of decisions. When choosing between individuals, compared to choosing between policies, decision-makers are more likely to prioritize what is fair to individuals (a microjustice standard of fairness) over what is fair in the aggregate (a macrojustice standard of fairness). As a result, an intervention that has decision-makers prioritize the same standard of fairness across the decisions mitigates the policy–people gap. This research helps us understand why decision-makers’ choices so frequently violate espoused organizational policies and suggests one way to increase the representation of disadvantaged groups in organizations.
apa_citation: Munguia Gomez, D. M., & Levine, E. E. (2022). The policy–people gap: Decision-makers choose policies that favor different applicants than they select when making individual decisions. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(3), 842–869. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1740

title: From Sheltered to Included: The Emancipation of Disabled Workers from Benevolent Marginalization
methods: Six-year longitudinal, theoretically sensitized process case study of a single German sheltered workshop; participant observation (72 days), 60 formal interviews plus 17 follow-ups and 88 informal conversations, and 350 archival documents; Gioia-style grounded inductive analysis with first-order categories, second-order themes, and three aggregate dimensions.
industry: Sheltered workshop providing vocational rehabilitation / employment for disabled workers (metal processing, assembly, packaging, catering, cleaning, gardening/landscaping)
units: One large sheltered workshop (disabled workers and staff)
n_sample: 60 formal interviews, 17 follow-ups, 88 informal conversations; 72 observation days; 350 documents
authors: Hein, Patricia; Ansari, Shaz
theories: conscientization (Freire, 1970); identity work (Snow & Anderson, 1987); micro-/macro-emancipation (Huault, Perret, & Spicer, 2014); organizational identity / identity regulation (Alvesson & Willmott, 2002; Kreiner et al., 2015)
keywords: benevolent marginalization; disability; emancipation; identity regulation; identity work; insider activists; organizational identity
research_question: How may benevolently marginalized individuals whose self-concept is tightly regulated by an organizational identity collectively achieve emancipation from that infantilizing identity?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: benevolent marginalization; the "filter of paternalistic benevolence"; a regulating organizational identity ("shelter") vs. an alternative "inclusion" discourse; spoiled/infantilizing identities; the double-edged sword whereby benevolent marginalization makes resistance harder yet is more amenable to non-threatening change.
- Process / phases: (1) Maintaining identity constraints — staff and workers reproduce a paternalistic organizational identity and maintain workers' prescribed "in need of protection" identity; (2) Mobilization of internal activists — placatory measures unexpectedly yield positive lived experiences and "moments of epiphany," an initial group of workers draws on alternative inclusion conceptions to co-construct a self-determining self-concept, then reframes the discourse reassuringly (via "safe spaces" and enabling measures) to overcome remaining workers' resistance; (3) Driving organizational identity change — a critical mass of self-determining workers clashes with the paternalistic identity, prompting both staff and workers to reshape the workshop's identity and structures toward inclusion.
- Triggering conditions: external criticism (UN CRPD activism, media coverage), placatory and enabling measures, confidence-building experiences, redefinition of powerful actors from guardians to allies.
- Emergent outcomes: a self-determining worker identity (~90% of employees) and a reshaped, participative organizational identity that accommodates both inclusion and those who still prefer protection.

Emancipation from benevolent marginalization is theorized as a collaborative, gradual process of "conscientization" (Freire, 1970) rather than confrontation: marginalized actors work *with* rather than against those in power, leveraging differing priorities among powerful actors to recast them as allies through "joint labor." Insider activists animate currents from external emancipatory discourses inside a protectionist organization, frame them as non-threatening identity opportunities, and mobilize collective identity work that ultimately revises the regulating organizational identity.
theoretical_contribution: The study contributes to the organizational identity literature on three fronts. First, it shows how benevolently marginalized individuals can remove or loosen identity constraints by collaborating with powerful actors to change "who we are," offering a more tempered view of regulating identities and a form of emancipation between micro- and macro-emancipation. Second, it illuminates benevolent (rather than overt) marginalization, where subjugation occurs subtly and in solidarity with those in power, so that emancipation is a collaborative process of confidence building, mutual consciousness raising, and sensitization. Third, it foregrounds insider activists who let currents from external discourses permeate a paternalistic organization by reframing those discourses as identity opportunities.
practical_implication: Benevolent marginalization is harder to tackle through legislation than overt discrimination, so practitioners should dismantle protectionist assumptions, shift from "telling" to "listening," and have those in power act as allies offering nondidactic support. Organizations should create enabling mechanisms and "safe spaces," tailor inclusion experiences to individuals' differing impairments and preferences (including the preference to be protected), and educate employers to view disabled workers as valuable employees—"no decision should be made about them without them."
limitations: The findings derive from a single German sheltered workshop, which constrains generalizability across other forms of benevolent marginalization and institutional contexts. The boundary between internal and external activists is not always distinct, and dividing respondents into nondisabled staff and disabled workers is somewhat simplistic given that a few staff also had disabilities, pointing to fluid disability boundaries.
future_research: Examine other forms of benevolent marginalization (e.g., the infantilizing treatment of older adults in care homes exposed by Covid-19 lockdowns) to surface obstacles to emancipation; study activists who are simultaneously insiders and outsiders; and adopt an intersectionality lens, since people define themselves not only through disability but also ethnicity, caste, gender, or age, all of which may compound oppression.
abstract: While there have been several studies on overt forms of marginalization, few have examined benevolent marginalization, where people may unquestioningly participate in their own paternalistic subjugation by following a prescribed identity. How might such individuals end up achieving emancipation from an infantilizing identity? To address this puzzle, we conducted a longitudinal study of a German sheltered workshop, an organization providing employment for disabled people. We observed that workers with disability initially maintained a regulating organizational identity based on paternalistic segregation. However, over time, they constructed their own self-determining identity and coproduced a change in the workshop’s identity from “shelter” to “inclusion.” First, we show that in order to coconstruct their preferred self-concept, benevolently marginalized individuals need to gain the support of those in power, who change their role from a guardian to an ally. Second, while achieving liberation from overt marginalization is likely to involve confrontation with one’s oppressors, we suggest that achieving emancipation from benevolent marginalization is a collaborative process of mutual consciousness raising and sensitization. Third, while changes of identity may occur after exposure to alternative discourses, often involving the contentious performances of external activists, we show how insider activists mobilize collective action for change within a protectionist organization.
apa_citation: Hein, P., & Ansari, S. (2022). From sheltered to included: The emancipation of disabled workers from benevolent marginalization. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(3), 749–783. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1689

title: “Am I Next?” The Spillover Effects of Mega-Threats on Avoidant Behaviors at Work
methods: Two field-survey studies of racial-minority vs. White employees exposed to real mega-threats. Study 1 is a pre-registered cross-sectional online field study; Study 2 is a three-phase pre/post panel. Hypotheses tested with bootstrapped serial-mediation and SEM path analysis (lavaan, 1,000 resamples), plus a moderated-mediation test of identity-discussion safety.
industry: Working adults across diverse industries and occupations (online panels; employed at least part-time / 20+ hours per week), not restricted to one sector
time_period: Study 1: March 2021 (Atlanta-area spa shootings). Study 2: Phase 1 May 2019; Phase 2 October 2019; Phase 3 May 2020 (George Floyd)
units: Individual employees (racial-minority vs. White comparison groups)
n_sample: Study 1: 321 (181 Asian, 140 White). Study 2: Phase 1 n=403; Phase 2 n=170; Phase 3 n=112
authors: Leigh, Angelica; Melwani, Shimul
theories: conservation of resources theory (Hobfoll, 1989); embodied cognition (Barsalou, 1999; Freeman, 2017); social identity and self-categorization theory (Tajfel & Turner, 1985; Hogg & Terry, 2000); theory of racialized organizations (Ray, 2019)
keywords: avoidant work behaviors; embodied threat; mega-threats; psychological safety of identity-based discussions; racialized organizations; threat suppression
research_question: How and why does exposure to "mega-threats"—negative, identity-relevant, heavily publicized societal events—influence the work behaviors of racial-minority employees who share an identity with the events' victims, and under what organizational conditions are those effects attenuated?
mechanism_summary: When a mega-threat harms a member of one's identity group, observers become "vicarious victims" and, through embodied cognition, experience "embodied threat"—a heightened appraisal that they could personally encounter identity-based harm ("I could be next"). Because organizations are racialized structures that constrain the agency and emotional expression of racial minorities, this threat is suppressed at work rather than expressed. Threat suppression is effortful and depletes psychological resources, so (per conservation of resources theory) employees enact protective avoidance—withdrawing from tasks and disengaging from colleagues. When employees perceive that identity-based discussions are psychologically safe, the need to suppress is reduced, interrupting the deleterious chain.
theoretical_contribution: The paper centers race in organizational theory by extending Ray's (2019) theory of racialized organizations to explain how macro societal events spill across organizational boundaries to shape individual behavior. It introduces and validates the construct of "embodied threat," distinct from identity threat and stereotype threat in that it concerns the fear of personally encountering identity-based physical harm, including death. It also advances work at the intersection of diversity and psychological safety by identifying the psychological safety of identity-based discussions as a distinct, identity-relevant form of safety.
practical_implication: Managers should recognize that mega-threats can cause multitudes of minority employees to suppress threat and withdraw at once, and should increase the psychological safety of identity-based discussions (e.g., through open, honest manager–subordinate relationships and sustained—not merely reactionary—diversity commitments). Organizations can scrutinize racialized norms and routines, and offer flexibility to step away from work after a group-relevant mega-threat so employees can replenish depleted resources.
limitations: The studies rely on online research samples and self-reported measures, raising concerns about common method variance and external validity, and required newly developed scales. All studies centered on mega-threats involving racial-minority victims in the United States, and were constrained to the specific events that occurred during the research period; the Phase 3 (George Floyd) social-engagement effect lost significance after controls, possibly due to COVID-era disruption of workplace interaction.
future_research: Scholars could use experience-sampling or experimental methods to trace the duration and causal structure of mega-threat effects, and examine mega-threats involving other identities (e.g., religious or organizational) and contexts outside the United States. Further work could clarify how mega-threats vary in severity, reach, and cumulative effect, how employees form beliefs about the safety of identity-based discussions, and how mega-threats implicating dominant-group identities differ in their effects.
abstract: “Mega-threats”—negative, identity-relevant societal events that receive significant media attention—are frequent occurrences in society, yet the influence of these events on employees remains unclear. We draw on the theory of racialized organizations to explain the process whereby exposure to mega-threats leads to heightened avoidant work behaviors for racial minority employees. We theorize and find—across two studies centered upon various mega-threats, including a mass shooting targeting Asian Americans and police killings of Black civilians—that event observers who share identities with mega-threat victims become vicarious victims, which triggers an experience of “embodied threat,” an appraisal of the increased likelihood of personally encountering identity-based harm. The experience of embodied threat coupled with the racialized nature of organizational structures, which limits the agency of racial minorities, then compels employees to engage in threat suppression. Furthermore, we find that threat suppression consumes psychological resources, leading to heightened avoidant work behaviors, or higher work withdrawal and lower social engagement, but, when the psychological safety of identity-based discussions is high, it attenuates this effect. Altogether, our paper advances research on mega-threats and race in organizations, and yields practical insights that can assist managers in reducing the detrimental effects of mega-threats on employees.
apa_citation: Leigh, A., & Melwani, S. (2022). “Am I next?” The spillover effects of mega-threats on avoidant behaviors at work. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(3), 720–748. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1657

title: “Invisible” Discrimination: Divergent Outcomes for the Nonprototypicality of Black Women
methods: Multimethod design across eight studies: six between- and within-subjects vignette experiments (Studies 1-4b) testing race/gender manipulations on prototypicality, believability, warmth, pity/contempt, and awarded financial remedy, analyzed with t-tests, ANOVA, and SPSS PROCESS/MEMORE bootstrapped (serial) mediation; plus two archival studies (5a, 5b) of EEOC discrimination charges using binary logistic regression (believability) and linear regression (financial remedy) with covariates.
industry: Workplace discrimination claimants and observers; archival EEOC employment-discrimination charges across industries (coded for industry masculinity)
time_period: EEOC gender-discrimination charges filed 2011-2016; racial-discrimination charges filed 2010-2017
units: Individual participants (online convenience samples) and individual EEOC discrimination charges
n_sample: Experiments: 402 (S1), 285 (S2a), 297 (S2b), 80 + 161 (S3), 201 (S4a), 191 (S4b); Archival: 3,566 gender charges (S5a) and 99,533 racial charges (S5b) with closure outcomes
authors: Ponce de Leon, Rebecca; Rosette, Ashleigh Shelby
theories: behaviors from intergroup affect and stereotypes (BIAS) map (Cuddy et al., 2007, 2008); intersectional invisibility hypothesis (Purdie-Vaughns & Eibach, 2008); intersectional theory (Crenshaw, 1989); stereotype content model (Fiske et al., 2002)
keywords: Black women; believability; discrimination; financial remedy; intersectional invisibility; nonprototypicality; stereotype content; warmth
research_question: Why do people tend to exclude Black women from considerations of gender and racial discrimination, and what are the positive and negative implications of this relative "invisibility" for the believability of their discrimination claims and the financial remedy they are awarded?
mechanism_summary: Black women's dual-subordinated identities make them categorically nonprototypical of both "women" (prototyped by White women) and "Black people" (prototyped by Black men), which depresses the believability of their gender and racial discrimination claims. At the attribute level, the BIAS map predicts that warmth governs helping behavior: because gender-discrimination victims are stereotyped as warm and racial-discrimination victims as cold, Black women's intermediate warmth (lower than White women, higher than Black men) elicits less pity than White women but less contempt than Black men. These divergent emotional reactions translate into less financial remedy than White women in gender cases but more financial remedy than Black men in racial cases — so the same nonprototypicality is simultaneously a disadvantage and an advantage depending on the outcome and the axis of discrimination.
theoretical_contribution: By integrating the intersectional invisibility hypothesis with the BIAS map, the paper distinguishes categorical prototypicality (which drives recognition-based outcomes like believability) from prototypical attributes (which drive support-based outcomes like financial remedy), showing that victim recognition does not necessarily translate into victim support. This integration explains when multiply subordinated individuals experience double jeopardy versus intersectional advantage, reconciling two perspectives prior research has tended to pit against one another and extending intersectional theory into organizationally relevant discrimination contexts.
practical_implication: Initiatives to combat discrimination may inadvertently exclude Black women because their claims are categorically less believable; allies (particularly White allies), accountability-based diversity programs (e.g., diversity committees, mentoring), and tailored efforts addressing intersectional experiences are needed. The authors further argue for structural and legal solutions — recognizing intersectional discrimination claims (per the 2006 EEOC guidance) and amending Title VII to cover combinations of protected bases — so Black women are not forced into single-axis comparisons to White women or Black men.
limitations: Experimental samples were largely homogeneous online convenience samples relying on vignettes, limiting diversity of populations and stimuli. The archival EEOC data lacked variables such as claimants' organizational position or salary that likely affect financial remedy, and the correlational mediation models cannot provide an explicit causal test of categorical prototypicality versus prototypical attributes or rule out alternative explanations such as broader racism and sexism.
future_research: Future work should manipulate the proposed mediators and test the framework among more diverse populations and with a wider array of stimuli, and examine factors such as status and salary that may shape financial remedy. The principles should also be tested in other multiply subordinated groups (e.g., Asian women and men), whose distinct warmth stereotypes may yield different prototypicality and treatment patterns.
abstract: By integrating the intersectional invisibility hypothesis with the behaviors from intergroup affect and stereotypes map framework, we examine the extent to which Black women’s dual-subordinated identities render them nonprototypical victims of discrimination, relative to White women and Black men, and the corresponding consequences. We predicted that Black women’s categorical nonprototypicality would reduce the believability of their discrimination claims, but that their nonprototypical attributes would lead to divergent treatment, depending on the type of discrimination alleged. Our predictions were supported across six experimental studies (Studies 1–4b). Specifically, Black women’s gender and racial discrimination claims were believed less compared to those made by White women and Black men, respectively. Moreover, after they alleged discrimination, Black women received less financial remedy versus White women, but more financial remedy versus Black men. Mediation testing revealed that the mechanisms underlying the believability and treatment of Black women were their nonprototypical categorization and attributes. Using discrimination data from the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission, Studies 5a and 5b replicated the effects observed on believability and financial remedy. By focusing on nonprototypicality at both categorical and attribute levels, we thus help to disentangle when Black women’s intersectional invisibility may result in either intersectional advantages or disadvantages.
apa_citation: Ponce de Leon, R., & Rosette, A. S. (2022). “Invisible” discrimination: Divergent outcomes for the nonprototypicality of Black women. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(3), 784–812. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1623

title: Living Up to the Hype: How New Ventures Manage the Resource and Liability of Future-Oriented Visions within the Nascent Market of Impact Investing
methods: Inductive, longitudinal, comparative multiple-case study (Eisenhardt, 1989) of three new ventures; >decade-long observation; 42 interviews (~64 hours), site visits, local-event attendance, archival documents, and media coverage; cross-case analysis using replication logic.
industry: Impact investing / social finance; social stock exchanges (SSEs)
time_period: 2007–2018 (from first SSE claims in 2007 through launch in 2013 and first five years of trading)
units: New ventures (social stock exchanges): SSX, IIX, SVX
n_sample: 3 ventures (42 interviews, ~64 hours; 5 site visits; 12 local events; >1000 pages of documents)
authors: Logue, Danielle; Grimes, Matthew
theories: imagined futures (Beckert, 2013); social proof (Rao, Greve, & Davis, 2001; Cialdini, 1993); sociology of expectations (Borup, Brown, Konrad, & Van Lente, 2006; van Lente, 2012)
keywords: entrepreneurial flexibility; field organizing; hype; impact investing; nascent markets; social proof; sociology of expectations
research_question: How do entrepreneurs in nascent markets engage with and manage hype so as to retain temporal and categorical flexibility? The study asks how new ventures convert the short-term cultural resource of hype into sustained opportunity while managing its long-term relational liabilities.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: Hype as a short-term unbounded cultural resource versus a long-term relational liability; temporal flexibility versus categorical flexibility.
- Field-organizing practices: Cultural practices (motivating future state, locating the opportunity, role claiming) and relational practices (configuring boundaries, smoothing exchange, local network brokerage).
- Emergent mechanism — social proof: Practices generate third-party endorsement (varying in salience) and engagement (varying in proximity), yielding salient vs. proximate social proof.
- Boundary conditions / outcomes: Salient social proof increases categorical flexibility but reduces temporal flexibility; proximate social proof increases temporal flexibility but reduces categorical flexibility.

Across the three SSEs (London's SSX, Singapore's IIX, Toronto's SVX), the ventures' differing cultural and relational field-organizing efforts produced distinct forms of social proof. That social proof, rather than scarce material proof (e.g., listings or share-price metrics), is what allowed stakeholders' disappointment to be deferred and shaped the categorical and temporal flexibility each venture retained while attempting to "live up to the hype." The model thus theorizes how entrepreneurs actively stimulate social proof rather than passively receiving it through herding.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops an emergent theory of hype management, theorizing hype at the collective level as a vision of a possible future that is simultaneously a short-term cultural resource and a long-term relational liability, distinct from venture-level promises. It challenges and extends research on social proof by showing that entrepreneurs' own field-organizing efforts can directly generate social proof (rather than it merely emerging from imitative or herding behavior), and by distinguishing salient from proximate social proof and their differential effects on categorical and temporal flexibility. It contributes to scholarship on entrepreneurship in nascent markets, the sociology of expectations, and the realization of distant futures.
practical_implication: For entrepreneurs in hyped nascent markets, the study reveals at least two pathways to navigate hype's relational liabilities: developing salient social proof to gain the categorical flexibility to pivot toward more feasible, less distant possibilities (as with IIX), or producing proximate social proof through deep stakeholder engagement to gain the temporal flexibility to make incremental progress toward the original distant vision (as with SVX). Managing hype through field organizing, rather than only revised storytelling, lengthens and broadens the runway available to deliver on collective visions.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: Future research could investigate how ventures attempt to produce different types of social proof at the same time or over time—oscillating between salient and proximate forms—as a way to gain different forms of required flexibility along the entrepreneurial journey, and how such proof types interact alongside material proof as complements rather than substitutes.
abstract: Hype is a collective vision and promise of a possible future, around which attention, excitement, and expectations increase over time. Within nascent markets, hype can thus serve as a cultural resource by which entrepreneurs might encourage greater early stakeholder support and resources. Yet, as hype-driven support couples with mounting temporal and categorical expectations, this can also limit ventures' flexibility during the entrepreneurial process. Drawing on an inductive, longitudinal, and comparative study of three new ventures within the much-hyped nascent market of impact investing, we develop an emergent theory of hype management, illustrating the field organizing practices that give rise to different forms of social proof, thereby allowing new ventures sufficient flexibility to convert hype into a sustained entrepreneurial opportunity. Our account contributes directly to contemporary public conversations of hype by revealing how entrepreneurs might engage with, and indeed live up to, the hype surrounding nascent markets without succumbing to the deceit and disappointment typically associated with hype. Beyond this, our findings extend existing scholarship on entrepreneurship within nascent markets, the sociology of expectations, and the realization of distant futures.
apa_citation: Logue, D., & Grimes, M. (2022). Living up to the hype: How new ventures manage the resource and liability of future-oriented visions within the nascent market of impact investing. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(3), 1055–1082. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1583

title: Remaking Capitalism: The Strength of Weak Legislation in Mobilizing B Corporation Certification
methods: Quasi-experimental staggered-adoption difference-in-differences exploiting variation in the timing of benefit corporation legislation (BCL) across U.S. states. State-level analysis uses two-way fixed-effects Poisson regression with robust standard errors clustered by state (incidence rate ratios); firm-level recertification analysis uses discrete-time survival (hazard) models with a complementary log-log specification, clustered by firm.
industry: Certified B Corporations and benefit corporations across all sectors (e.g., manufacturing, services, wholesale/retail, agriculture); ESG norms measured from MSCI KLD data on publicly traded firms
time_period: State-level panel 2008-2018; firm-level certification/decertification events May 2007 through November 6, 2019
units: State-years (50 states + DC) for initial certification; firm-level certification spells for recertification
n_sample: BCL enacted in 36 U.S. jurisdictions; state-year regressions use 564/528 observations across 44-47 states; firm-level dataset of U.S. B Corporations (data validation added 430 observations for 326 companies)
authors: Lucas, David S.; Grimes, Matthew G.; Gehman, Joel
theories: institutional theory / decoupling (Edelman, 1992; Bromley & Powell, 2012); organizational values (Kraatz, Flores, & Chandler, 2020; Selznick, 1957); sociology of law (Dobbin & Sutton, 1998; Edelman & Suchman, 1997)
keywords: B Corporation certification; benefit corporation legislation; corporate sustainability norms; material authentication; organizational values; remaking capitalism; sociology of law; values authentication
research_question: How does the enactment of weak sustainability legislation (benefit corporation legislation) affect companies' adoption and retention of related, albeit voluntary, B Corporation certification, and under what regional institutional conditions are these effects amplified or attenuated?
mechanism_summary: The authors argue that weak, broad sustainability legislation paradoxically strengthens demand for rigorous voluntary certification. Weak legislation enables low-cost, low-scrutiny affiliation (e.g., the benefit corporation legal form), heightening the risk that non-sustainable firms counterfeit field membership; genuinely values-driven firms respond by seeking strong certification for values authentication. Separately, as the scope of legislation grows, it expands future enforcement opportunities and noncompliance risk, prompting already-certified firms to recertify for material authentication. Regional sustainability norms condition these effects: the values-authentication effect is amplified where norms are weak ("dirty"), whereas the material-authentication effect is amplified where norms are strong ("clean").
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces a systemic, governance-strength perspective on intersecting reforms, parsing legislation into distinct dimensions of strength (unambiguous requirements and strict enforcement) and scope (breadth of regulative requirements) rather than treating legislation as uniformly strong and mandatory. It shows that institutional incompatibility between distributed, imperfectly aligned governance efforts can be generative—even weak legislation can mobilize systems change by triggering an authentication imperative—thereby challenging the assumption that reform efforts must be strong, robust, and aligned to drive change.
practical_implication: For advocates seeking to remake capitalism, "weak" legislation is not necessarily a failure: even ambiguous, lax benefit corporation statutes can catalyze adoption and retention of rigorous certifications like B Corp. Movement organizations such as B Lab can treat legislation and certification as complementary levers, recognizing that legal scaffolding—however imperfect—can mobilize values-driven firms while regional sustainability norms shape where each lever works best.
limitations: The staggered-adoption difference-in-differences design relies on strong assumptions (e.g., quasi-random policy timing), and the proposed values- and material-authentication mechanisms are inferred from differential effects rather than directly measured. The setting is confined to U.S. B Corps and benefit corporation legislation, and some firm-level timing (e.g., exact decertification dates and variable recertification timelines) is imperfectly observed, requiring "certification spell" coding choices.
future_research: Future work could examine intersecting weak and strong reforms in other empirical contexts and governance domains to assess the generalizability of the authentication mechanisms, and could more directly measure the values- and material-authentication processes posited here. Researchers might also explore how additional forms of distributed, overlapping governance interact—reinforcing or undermining broader systems-change efforts—beyond the legislation–certification pairing studied here.
abstract: Myriad cross-sector initiatives seek to remake capitalism into a more just, sustainable, and inclusive system. But how do these distributed efforts—which often vary in strength—interact? To answer this question, we attend to the interaction between weak and strong governance reforms. Drawing on longstanding research on organizational values and the sociology of law, we theorize how the enactment of weak and broad sustainability legislation is likely to increase pressure on values-driven businesses to pursue both values authentication and material authentication by way of strong third-party certification. We test our conceptual model by examining the effects of the frequently criticized benefit corporation legislation passed in 36 U.S. jurisdictions on the related B Corporation certification. We find that new certifications and recertifications both increase in jurisdictions with such legislation and these effects are amplified or attenuated depending on corporate sustainability norms in the region. Taken together, our findings contribute to the intensifying societal conversation regarding the prospects for remaking capitalism, illustrating how even weak legislation can contribute to systems change not only by encouraging incremental sustainability reforms within a field but also by triggering an authentication imperative that mobilizes values-driven businesses to pursue rigorous certifications.
apa_citation: Lucas, D. S., Grimes, M. G., & Gehman, J. (2022). Remaking capitalism: The strength of weak legislation in mobilizing B Corporation certification. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(3), 958–987. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1688

title: Upward Mobility, the Cleft Habitus, and Speaking Up: How Class Transitions Relate to Individual and Organizational Antecedents of Voice
methods: Three-study multi-method design: (1) three-wave lagged field survey (path analysis, CFA, bootstrapped mediation); (2) preregistered archival regression analysis of the Wisconsin Longitudinal Study (interaction and conditional indirect effects); (3) preregistered two-condition between-subjects vignette experiment (t-tests, logistic regression, bootstrapped path models).
industry: Study 1: products division of a large prestigious technology company; Study 2: 1957 Wisconsin high-school graduates across diverse occupations; Study 3: online panel participants (Prolific)
country: United States (Studies 1-2); online panel, predominantly U.S. (Study 3)
time_period: Study 1: three one-week-lagged survey waves; Study 2: 1957-1992 longitudinal waves; Study 3: cross-sectional experiment
units: individual employees / evaluators
n_sample: Study 1: 229 of 490; Study 2: 4,166 (WLS cohort of 10,317); Study 3: 351 of 400
authors: Martin, Sean R.; Harrison, Spencer H.
theories: cleft habitus (Bourdieu, 1999; Friedman, 2016); dual pathways model of voice (Tangirala & Ramanujam, 2012); self-efficacy theory (Bandura, 1977, 1997)
keywords: cleft habitus; cultural fit; employee voice; managerial voice solicitation; perceived competence; self-efficacy; social class; upward mobility
research_question: Do the prior lower-class experiences of the upwardly mobile imprint them with a reduced sense of their own abilities and result in less voice, or does upward transition correspond to a high general sense of efficacy that facilitates voice? And do managers view the upwardly mobile as equally valuable sources for input as employees from higher-class backgrounds, or do they provide more opportunities for input to those with elite pedigrees?
mechanism_summary: The paper integrates the "cleft habitus" idea (that class transitions partially rewrite, rather than fully imprint, one's dispositions) with the dual-pathways model of voice. Along the individual pathway, upward mobility coincides with a high internal sense of self-efficacy that motivates the upwardly mobile to overcome fears and speak up. Along the organizational pathway, lingering signals of a lower-class origin lead managers to perceive the upwardly mobile as less competent and worse cultural fits, so managers solicit their input less often. These two pathways operate in opposing directions, such that the upwardly mobile jump over barriers to voice while managers lower the barriers for those from more privileged backgrounds.
theoretical_contribution: The work changes how management theory accounts for class background: rather than imprinting an enduring low sense of initiative, the self-views of the upwardly mobile better reflect their current class position, evidencing high self-efficacy. It extends voice research by shifting attention from how managers respond to or recognize voice toward how they actively *solicit* it, showing that managers use socially significant cues like class signals when deciding whose input to seek — a previously unexamined source of inequity. It also demonstrates that the individual and organizational antecedents of voice, rarely studied jointly, can compete rather than complement one another.
practical_implication: Firms should treat evidence of an upward trajectory as a positive signal of efficacy and other desirable qualities when screening employees or composing teams. Managers should consciously counteract the tendency to rely on elite cultural signals as proxies for quality and make a deliberate effort to solicit input from people who reached the same destination from less privileged origins, helping rebalance disparities in the opportunity to contribute.
limitations: Studies 1 and 2 used correlational designs and cannot establish causal habitus or efficacy change; efficacy spirals leave open whether high-efficacy lower-class individuals are simply the ones who become upwardly mobile. Voice in Studies 1-2 was self-reported, and Study 3's vignette experiment, while causally informative, sacrifices ecological validity. The Study 1 offsetting competing mediations (yielding a nonsignificant total effect) occurred within a single prestigious organization and may not generalize across contexts.
future_research: Future work could disentangle the causal direction between upward mobility and efficacy via performance/efficacy spirals, examine downward mobility, and test whether the competing-pathways dynamic extends to diversity dimensions beyond social class. Researchers could also study what topics people from different class backgrounds speak up about, use more direct (non-self-report) voice measures, and assess managerial solicitation in more ecologically valid settings and in contexts beyond the workplace.
abstract: This research explores the relationship between upward mobility and voice. We build hypotheses and find evidence that rather than being imprinted with a lower sense of efficacy, the upwardly mobile possess a high internal sense of efficacy and are likely to speak up. However, this positive pathway to voice for the upwardly mobile is offset by managers being more inclined to solicit voice from those who come from, and have remained in, higher social class positions. We test our hypotheses in three studies: a field survey, a preregistered analysis of an archival dataset, and a preregistered experiment. This work provides evidence that the internal self-views long associated with those from lower social class backgrounds may not adequately describe the upwardly mobile. Contrary to having a persistent low sense of their abilities, we find that the upwardly mobile espouse high efficacy and do speak up but that managers appear less likely to provide them with equal opportunities for voice, instead seeking it from employees from more elite backgrounds. This work also extends theories of employee voice by showing how managers' decisions about whose input to solicit are influenced by employees' socially significant characteristics in ways that could lead to systematic disadvantages.
apa_citation: Martin, S. R., & Harrison, S. H. (2022). Upward mobility, the cleft habitus, and speaking up: How class transitions relate to individual and organizational antecedents of voice. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(3), 813-841. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1550
