title: To Catch a Predator: The Lived Experience of Extreme Practices
methods: Three-year phenomenological ethnography (observant participation) of a single U.K. volunteer pedophile-hunting team ('Dark Knight'), drawing on 831 pages of field notes from 36 months of fieldwork plus a ~600,000-word digital archive of the team's chatroom conversations; abductive interpretive analysis of motives, practices, and authored identities.
industry: Online volunteer vigilante community (pedophile-hunting team)
time_period: 36 months of fieldwork from July 2018; chatroom data spanning three years (coded window October 2018-April 2020)
units: One ~15-strong hunting team ('Dark Knight')
n_sample: 831 pages of field notes; ~600,000-word chatroom archive (356,799 coded words across 54 Facebook Messenger rooms)
authors: de Rond, Mark; Lok, Jaco; Marrison, Adrian
theories: organizational institutionalism (Creed et al., 2020; Zietsma et al., 2019); phenomenology (Heidegger, 1962); practice theory (Schatzki, 2002; Nicolini, 2012); teleoaffectivity (Schatzki, 1997)
keywords: emotions; extreme practices; lived experience; pedophile hunting; phenomenology; practice theory; vigilantism; way-of-being
research_question: Why do pedophile hunters persist with harmful, extreme, and difficult-to-justify practices, and how can a phenomenological account of "lived experience" — rather than dispositional social-psychological models — explain their use of extreme practices? More broadly, how can organization research theorize the role of lived experience in social action so as to engage epistemically closed communities?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs: "way-of-being" (Heidegger), "lifeworld," teleoaffectivity, and the layered affectivity of "lived practice"
- Multiple ways-of-being identified: pragmatic, moralistic, narcissistic, and traumatic — with "heroic being" inferred as a fifth way-of-being constituted in the other four
- Identity pairs (authoring self / authoring the other): Protectors of Children vs. Police as Impotent; Community Cleansers vs. Predators as Evil Monsters; Respected Professionals vs. Other Teams as Narcissistic Amateurs; Trauma Survivors vs. Children as Innocents to Be Saved
- Triggering conditions: societal anxiety over CSE, distrust of authorities, and social media lowering the threshold for collective action
- Emergent outcome: complex layered affectivity that insulates hunters against external moral criticism and sustains commitment to extreme practices

The team's phenomenological lifeworld is constituted in its network of practices (decoying, doorknocking, livestreaming). Engaging these practices attunes members to multiple collective ways-of-being that disclose "what is mattering" and bring distinctive thoughts and emotions to the fore. As hunters author identities for themselves and others through the chatroom, these ways-of-being suffuse their practices with a layered affectivity that renders otherwise "puzzling practices" self-evident to them — explaining their persistence and their imperviousness to outside concern.
theoretical_contribution: The study develops the phenomenological concept of "way-of-being" as a conceptual vocabulary for theorizing lived experience in social action, offering a phenomenological alternative to social-psychological models that essentialize vigilantes through fixed dispositional traits. It advances emotions research in organizational institutionalism and practice theory by specifying how multiple, layered ways-of-being carry the affective complexity of "lived practice," and contributes to cross-disciplinary vigilantism research by foregrounding experience over disposition and countering psychological essentialization.
practical_implication: By making hunters' lived experience legible, the study offers police a more empathic basis for constructive mutual engagement rather than distancing, helping mitigate hunting's harmful effects on unconvicted suspects and their families. It also suggests a pathway toward increasing hunters' moral reflexivity, and a more general route for bridging the polarizing, existential differences that characterize epistemically closed, social-media-enabled communities.
limitations: The findings derive from a single U.K. hunting team studied phenomenologically, which necessarily limits the generalizability of the account. The interpretations are explicitly value-laden and rest on the ethnographer's preunderstanding, and they inevitably belie the many idiosyncrasies that characterize the lifeworlds studied.
future_research: The authors position their findings as a starting point for developing deeper understanding of the lived experience of epistemically closed, radicalized online communities more generally, and for further developing the concept of lived experience so that organization research can meaningfully contribute to reducing the harmful effects of polarization.
abstract: Across several countries, ever-growing societal alarm about the threat of online child sexual exploitation has provoked a controversial civic response: volunteer pedophile hunting teams that expose predators in livestreamed confrontations. Their practices have generated strong criticisms from a police force unsure how to engage them because they lack an empathic understanding of hunters’ lived experience. Through a three-year phenomenological ethnography of a U.K. hunting team, we advance efforts across organization research to theorize the role of lived experience in social action. Specifically, we deploy the phenomenological concept “way-of-being” to explain hunters’ use of extreme practices. Our interpretive account shows how the multiple ways-of-being that characterize the team’s lifeworld suffuse their practices with a complex layered affectivity that is constitutive of the commitment necessary for their persistence. This offers a phenomenological alternative to social psychological models of motivations for vigilantism while also advancing emotions research in organizational institutionalism and practice theory. Practically, our study contributes to the policing challenge of mitigating hunting’s harmful effects by facilitating more constructive mutual engagement. This offers a possible pathway for addressing the broader challenge posed by epistemically closed, social media–enabled communities that act out their concerns in ways that disregard our common humanity.
apa_citation: de Rond, M., Lok, J., & Marrison, A. (2022). To catch a predator: The lived experience of extreme practices. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(3), 870–902. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1492

title: When Conscientious Employees Meet Intelligent Machines: An Integrative Approach Inspired by Complementarity Theory and Role Theory
methods: Three studies: (1) a 10-workday experience-sampling field study with multilevel path analysis (Mplus); (2) a pre-registered field experiment with random assignment and path analysis; (3) a pre-registered online experiment with path analysis. Moderated-mediation (conditional indirect effects) tested via Monte Carlo confidence intervals.
industry: Technology/regulatory-compliance services (Study 1); hotel service (Study 2); mixed jobs and industries (Study 3)
units: Working adults (employees / online participants)
n_sample: 681 total across three studies (Study 1: 114; Study 2: 162; Study 3: 405)
authors: Tang, Pok Man; Koopman, Joel; McClean, Shawn T.; Zhang, Jack H.; Li, Chi Hon; De Cremer, David; Lu, Yizhen; Ng, Chin Tung Stewart
theories: complementarity theory (Carson, 1969; Kiesler, 1983); role theory (Kahn, Wolfe, Quinn, Snoek, & Rosenthal, 1964)
keywords: algorithms; artificial intelligence; complementarity theory; conscientiousness; intelligent machines; orderliness; robots; role ambiguity; role breadth self-efficacy; role theory; task performance
research_question: Does the long-standing consensus that conscientious (specifically, orderly) employees are most effective with workplace technology still hold when employees work with autonomous intelligent machines, and for whom do the benefits versus costs of intelligent machines accrue?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on complementarity theory, the authors argue that because intelligent machines autonomously create order and structure workflows, they form a non-complementary mismatch with orderly employees, who prefer to direct work themselves and are therefore less likely to view these machines as credible. Through a role-theory lens, this mismatch weakens orderly employees' role breadth self-efficacy and heightens their role ambiguity, which in turn shape task performance. The negative indirect effect via role ambiguity was supported across all three studies (stronger at higher orderliness), whereas the role breadth self-efficacy path was supported only in Study 3.
theoretical_contribution: The paper refines the established consensus on conscientiousness by showing it requires contextualization to the type of technology employed: orderly employees may experience reduced efficacy and heightened ambiguity with intelligent machines. It identifies orderliness as the moderator answering "for whom" intelligent machines help versus hurt, extends complementarity theory beyond human-to-human interactions to human-machine interactions, and returns role theory to the study of how modern technology reshapes role perceptions.
practical_implication: Managers may wish not to emphasize conscientiousness when recruiting for jobs involving intelligent machines, and to pair such machines with employees lower in conscientiousness (orderliness) where possible, since these employees may be better positioned to benefit. The authors also suggest including work-sample tasks pairing candidates with intelligent machines during selection.
limitations: Studies 1 and 2 each drew on a single organization in Eastern cultural contexts, limiting generalizability, and Study 3 relied on an online sample. Study 1 used shortened scales and coworker performance ratings, and several hypotheses (the role breadth self-efficacy path) went unsupported in Studies 1 and 2, with the authors noting moderating effects are harder to detect in field settings.
future_research: Future work should identify boundary conditions such as the nature of the job and whether intelligent machines offer high-quality or user-friendly output, develop ways to classify the richness of the user experience with intelligent machines, and examine how the employee-machine working relationship varies across machine types, jobs, and industries, including via a trait-activation lens.
abstract: Over the past century, conscientiousness has become seen as the preeminent trait for predicting performance. This consensus is due in part to these employees’ ability to work with traditional 20th-century technology. Such pairings balance the systematic nature of conscientious employees with the technology’s need for user input and direction to perform tasks—resulting in a complementary match. However, the 21st century has seen the incorporation of intelligent machines (e.g., artificial intelligence, robots, and algorithms) into employees’ jobs. Unlike traditional technology, these new machines are equipped with the capability to make decisions autonomously. Thus, their nature overlaps with the orderliness subdimension of conscientious employees—resulting in a non-complementary mismatch. This calls into question whether the consensus about conscientious employees’ effectiveness with 20th-century technology applies to 21st-century jobs. Integrating complementarity and role theory, we refine this consensus. Across three studies using distinct samples (an experience sampling study, a field experiment, and an online experiment from working adults in Malaysia, Taiwan, and the United States), each focused on a different type of intelligent machine, we show not only that using intelligent machines has benefits and consequences, but, importantly, that conscientious (i.e., orderly) employees are less likely to benefit from working with them.
apa_citation: Tang, P. M., Koopman, J., McClean, S. T., Zhang, J. H., Li, C. H., De Cremer, D., Lu, Y., & Ng, C. T. S. (2022). When conscientious employees meet intelligent machines: An integrative approach inspired by complementarity theory and role theory. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(3), 1019–1054. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1516

title: The Impostor Phenomenon Revisited: Examining the Relationship between Workplace Impostor Thoughts and Interpersonal Effectiveness at Work
methods: Multi-study constructive replication (n = 3,603) combining field and lab data: Study 1, two-wave multisource survey of investment-advisory employees with hierarchical linear modeling; Study 2, multisource field study of physicians-in-training in a standardized-patient simulation with video-coded mediator and multilevel SEM; Studies 3A and 3B, two pre-registered yoked-design online experiments with full-time employees analyzed via regression and structural equation modeling. Includes a seven-sample scale-development effort (n = 1,059) validating a five-item workplace impostor thoughts measure.
industry: Investment advisory (finance); medical training (physicians-in-training); and two cross-industry online panels of full-time employees (recruited via Prolific)
time_period: Field studies use two-time-period (Time 1 / Time 2 about two months apart) designs; online experiments are single-session; not otherwise dated in the paper
units: Individual employees (impostor thoughts and self-esteem self-rated; interpersonal effectiveness other-rated by supervisors, standardized patients, or yoked evaluators)
n_sample: 3,603 total across four studies (Study 1: 155; Study 2: 70, with 45 video-coded; Study 3A: 264 final; Study 3B: 597 final), plus yoked evaluator samples
authors: Tewfik, B. A.
theories: contingencies of self-worth (Crocker & Wolfe, 2001; James, 1890); impostor phenomenon theory (Clance & Imes, 1978); sociometer / self-esteem theory
keywords: contingencies of self-worth; domain-switching; impostor phenomenon; interpersonal effectiveness; other-focused orientation; self-esteem; self-handicapping; workplace impostor thoughts
research_question: Are workplace impostor thoughts — the belief that others overestimate one's competence at work — associated with greater (not just lesser) other-perceived interpersonal effectiveness, and if so, through what mechanism, and does that interpersonal benefit come at the expense of competence-related outcomes?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on contingencies-of-self-worth theory, the paper argues that workplace impostor thoughts threaten the self-esteem of people who stake their self-worth on competence, prompting a "domain-switching" response: rather than turning inward, they shift effort toward the interpersonal domain by adopting a more other-focused orientation (attending to, asking questions of, and showing care for others). Because perceivers reward these verbal, paraverbal, and nonverbal behaviors, the other-focused orientation translates into higher other-rated interpersonal effectiveness. A competing self-handicapping pathway is theorized but is not found to harm interpersonal effectiveness. The positive indirect effect through other-focused orientation held across all four studies, even though a significant total effect appeared only in the two field studies.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reconceptualizes the impostor phenomenon — typically treated as an affective, uniformly harmful experience — as a sociocognitive construct ("workplace impostor thoughts"), recovering a "forgotten" conceptualization and supplying a validated measure. It identifies other-focused orientation as the previously missing mechanism that explains why such thoughts can have an interpersonal upside, integrating contingencies-of-self-worth theory with impostor-phenomenon theory to show that threatened self-esteem can prompt people to turn outward rather than inward. In doing so it resolves a latent tension in prior literature between assertions that the phenomenon damages relationships and passing hints that impostors are seen as socially adept and likable.
practical_implication: The findings caution against blanket practitioner advice to "overcome" or "get rid of" impostor feelings: because the same thoughts can foster an other-focused orientation that others reward, guidance should be more nuanced and attend to the workplace context. Interventions such as cognitive reappraisal may help individuals down-regulate the negative emotional undertones while preserving potential interpersonal benefits, but managers should weigh the phenomenon's net effect — especially the degree to which a role is socially embedded — before deciding whether "overcoming" advice is appropriate.
limitations: The supplementary competence-related null effects (no significant harm to performance or selection) should not be overgeneralized, as the studies were not designed primarily to detect such downstream effects, and the competence-forward interview setting in the experiments may have constrained both the interpersonal upside (no significant total effect in Studies 3A and 3B) and the ability to observe negative spillovers. Self-esteem did not significantly mediate the path to other-focused orientation in Study 3B (only a serial path through other-directed positive affect did), and several measures relied on single sources or small subsamples (e.g., 45 coded videos in Study 2).
future_research: Future work could map boundary conditions on the interpersonal upside — for example, whether power attenuates it (greater power increases self-focus) and whether the degree of a job's social embeddedness amplifies or constrains domain-switching. Scholars could examine gender as a moderator of effects rather than as an antecedent, search for additional alternative or competing mechanisms beyond those measured (self-handicapping, self-denigration, self-promotion), and consider the phenomenon's simultaneous "net" effects across interpersonal, competence-related, and well-being outcomes.
abstract: Prevailing wisdom paints the impostor phenomenon as detrimental. I seek to rebalance the existing conversation around this phenomenon by highlighting that it may also have interpersonal benefits. To identify these benefits, I revisit seminal theorizing to advance the construct of workplace impostor thoughts which I define as the belief that others overestimate one's competence at work. Incorporating theory on contingencies of self-worth, I present an integrative model that outlines why such thoughts may be positively associated with other-perceived interpersonal effectiveness and why they may not be. I test my theory across four studies (n 5 3,603) that feature survey, video, and pre-registered experimental data. I find that employees who more frequently have such thoughts are evaluated as more interpersonally effective because they adopt a more other-focused orientation. I do not find that this interpersonal benefit comes at the expense of competence-related outcomes (i.e., performance, selection)—a point I revisit in my future directions. When examining my theorized competing pathway, I find that, whereas workplace impostor thoughts do encourage those who have them to self-handicap—consistent with prevailing wisdom—such thoughts do not operate through self-handicapping to harm other-perceived interpersonal effectiveness. I conclude by situating my findings alongside prior work.
apa_citation: Tewfik, B. A. (2022). The impostor phenomenon revisited: Examining the relationship between workplace impostor thoughts and interpersonal effectiveness at work. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(3), 988–1018. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.1627

title: From the Editors—Joining Societal Conversations on Management and Organizations
methods: Editorial (From the Editors essay) introducing a Special Research Forum: the 22nd AMJ editorial team frames the SRF's aims, describes the submission and review process (144 submissions, 129 fully reviewed), and introduces the 12 accepted articles.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Tihanyi, Laszlo; Howard-Grenville, Jennifer; DeCelles, Katherine A.
theories: Not reported in paper
keywords: Special Research Forum; business schools; impact; relevance; social issues; societal conversations; theory-driven research
research_question: How can AMJ and management scholars more fully join ongoing conversations in broader society about management and organizations—publishing high-quality empirical research on socially important, often-neglected problems—without abandoning the journal's emphasis on theory-driven contributions?
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Management departments are well-positioned to connect business schools to social issues because the discipline is problem- and practice-driven and draws on diverse disciplinary foundations; a Special Research Forum (SRF) can channel rigorous empirical work toward pressing societal debates.
- Diagnosis: Societally relevant topics (socioeconomic status and class, diversity and inclusion, community impact, cross-border workforce movement, national conflicts) have historically been hard to publish in leading management journals due to an emphasis on novelty and theory-driven contributions.
- Tension addressed: Whether AMJ is the right outlet given its theory-driven emphasis; the editors argue that exploring phenomena without testing or developing theory will not yield the progress society requires, so societal relevance and theoretical contribution are complementary rather than opposed.
- Process / mechanism: A call for papers (issued end of 2019, deadline November 30, 2020) yielded 144 submissions; 129 were fully reviewed by the editorial board and team (with 172 board members volunteering during the Covid-19 pandemic), resulting in 12 articles introduced by their assigned editors.

This editorial advances the position that AMJ can increase its relevance and credibility by inviting authors to join societal conversations while preserving rigorous, theory-driven empirical standards. The editors reason that dominant management theories born decades ago increasingly struggle to explain contemporary social complexity, so new theory developed around socially important problems can enrich cross-disciplinary conversations and "move societies forward." The SRF operationalizes this by showcasing empirical studies on neglected, high-stakes topics and having assigned editors narrate each article's contribution and societal relevance.
theoretical_contribution: The piece is an editorial statement of position rather than a theory-building contribution. Its contribution is programmatic: it argues that joining societal conversations and developing or testing theory are complementary, not competing, goals for AMJ, and it reframes the journal's mission to legitimize rigorous empirical research on socially significant, previously underrepresented problems—while reaffirming that phenomenon-driven work should still advance theory.
practical_implication: The editors signal to authors—including scholars in adjacent fields—that AMJ welcomes rigorous empirical work on socially important problems such as class, diversity and inclusion, community impact, and the organizational effects of societal events. They encourage business schools and management departments to broaden their mission by asking multilevel questions and drawing on diverse methodologies, and they make the SRF articles open access for a period to help authors, affected groups, and communities engage more productively.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editors hope the SRF signals to researchers in other fields studying similar problems that AMJ welcomes their work, and that the articles will encourage prospective authors to pursue underresearched topics that matter to broader society—continuing the conversation by developing new theories and applying existing theories to neglected societal problems.
abstract: Not reported in paper

title: How Work Orientation Impacts Objective Career Outcomes via Managerial (Mis)perceptions
methods: Two studies. Study 1: regression analyses of archival, longitudinal Wisconsin Longitudinal Study (WLS) data, with coarsened exact matching (CEM) and Heckman selection robustness checks. Study 2: preregistered between-subjects online experiment (job vs. calling vs. control video-vignette conditions) with PROCESS-macro bootstrapped parallel mediation.
industry: Mixed (13 industry categories in WLS, Study 1); full-time employees who regularly interact with coworkers (Study 2 online sample)
time_period: 2004 and 2011 WLS survey waves (work orientation measured 2004; 1992 wages as control); Study 2 experiment data via Prolific
units: Individuals (WLS respondents; online experiment participants acting as managers)
n_sample: 1,077 (Study 1 main model); 372 (Study 2 experiment)
authors: Cho, Yuna; Jiang, Winnie Y.
theories: cognitive biases (Kahneman, 2011); signaling theory (Spence, 1973; Connelly, Certo, Ireland, & Reutzel, 2011); theory of reciprocity (Falk & Fischbacher, 2006; Gouldner, 1960)
keywords: calling orientation; halo effect; job orientation; managerial perceptions; objective career outcomes; signaling; work orientation
research_question: How does an employee's work orientation (calling vs. job) relate to their objective career outcomes (income, raises, bonuses, promotion), and does this relationship operate through an external pathway in which managers (mis)perceive calling-oriented employees as higher performing and more organizationally committed?
mechanism_summary: Because calling- and job-oriented employees emit different observable workplace signals, managers draw inferences (per signaling theory) and, through common cognitive biases (e.g., availability bias, fallacy of association), misperceive calling-oriented employees as better performers and more committed to the organization—even when objective performance and commitment are held constant. Per the theory of reciprocity, managers then repay these favorable perceptions with greater financial rewards and stronger promotion endorsement. Study 1 shows calling-oriented WLS respondents earn higher wages; Study 2 experimentally demonstrates the perceptual mediation and a halo effect (calling-oriented "Sam" is also seen as more passionate, intrinsically motivated, and positive despite expert-validated objective equivalence).
theoretical_contribution: The paper redirects the meaning-of-work and work-orientation literatures away from their exclusive focus on the internal pathway (how orientation shapes one's own effort and performance) by theorizing and demonstrating an external, organizational pathway operating through managers' misperceptions of performance and commitment. It also conceptually and empirically differentiates calling orientation from passion, and uncovers a novel way passion itself may be (mis)perceived when physical manifestations of passion are held constant.
practical_implication: Managers should recognize that their judgments of subordinates rest partly on employees' verbal expressions and visible behaviors and may be inconsistent with employees' actual psychological and behavioral states, yet still drive consequential outcomes like income and promotion. Job-oriented employees, even when objectively comparable in performance and commitment, may earn less and receive weaker promotion support than calling-oriented peers.
limitations: Both studies have empirical limits: Study 1's WLS categorical work-orientation measure cannot capture intensity, and the data lack precise measures of job performance or organizational commitment, while work orientation was measured only in 2004 (limiting causal claims about reverse causality). Study 2's manipulation bundled four behavioral indicators at once (precluding isolation of any single indicator), may not have fully held passion constant, relied on short one-off video vignettes rather than repeated real-life interactions, and used a control condition that may have under-supplied performance/passion cues.
future_research: Future work should collect richer data on performance and commitment, isolate which behavioral signals drive perceptions, and disentangle a possible "calling orientation premium" from a "job orientation penalty." Scholars are encouraged to study why the bias-to-reward asymmetry arises, why organizational commitment mediates bonuses but not raises/promotions, the antecedents/consequences of interpersonal congruence of passion, the neglected career orientation, and whether rising income reshapes work orientation over time (including crowding-out effects).
abstract: Views differ on whether individuals with a calling orientation toward work (i.e., seeing work as personally fulfilling and contributing to a better world) enjoy more favorable objective career outcomes, such as higher income and chance of promotion, versus those with a job orientation (i.e., seeing work as a means to a financial end). We suggest that the impasse is partially due to prior research's exclusive focus on how work orientation affects one's effort and subsequent job performance. Drawing on theories of signaling, cognitive biases, and reciprocity, we propose that calling-oriented employees enjoy better objective career outcomes than job-oriented employees via an external pathway: managers misperceive employees' calling orientation as evidence of better performance and stronger organizational commitment. In Study 1—analyses of the Wisconsin Longitudinal Study—we find support for the main effect, and in Study 2—an online experiment—we constructively replicate this effect and find evidence for our predicted explanatory mechanisms. Furthermore, observing a calling-oriented employee prompts managers to perceive them more favorably in other domains, creating a halo effect. Our research sheds light on how individuals' subjective view of the meaning of work influences their objective career success, highlighting workplace signals and managerial perceptions as important mechanisms.
apa_citation: Cho, Y., & Jiang, W. Y. (2022). How work orientation impacts objective career outcomes via managerial (mis)perceptions. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(4), 1353–1382. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0841

title: Who Will Stay When Crisis Strikes? Venture Capitalist Threat-Defiant Learning Response to Ventures in Crisis
methods: Quantitatively dominant mixed-methods design. Conditional fixed-effects logit (clogit, venture/lawsuit fixed effects, robust SEs) on 435 VC follow-on investment decisions, supplemented by seven semi-structured post hoc interviews with experienced VCs.
industry: Venture capital firms and their venture investees (entrepreneurial ventures financed by traditional VCs)
country: United States (federal civil lawsuits from the Federal Judicial Center; VentureXpert-listed ventures)
time_period: Ventures first-funded 2006-2009; lawsuits tracked over the 2006-2018 period
units: VC firm decisions on whether to make a follow-on investment in a venture following a lawsuit
n_sample: 435 VC investment decisions in 105 ventures involved in lawsuits; plus 7 VC interviews
authors: Welbourne Eleazar, Miranda J.; Park, Haemin Dennis
theories: defiance as a strategic response to institutional pressures (Oliver, 1991); experiential / organizational learning from crisis (Levinthal & March, 1981; Smith & Elliott, 2007); relational view of crises and post-traumatic growth (Kahn, Barton, & Fellows, 2013); threat-rigidity hypothesis (Staw, Sandelands, & Dutton, 1981)
keywords: crisis; experiential learning; follow-on investment; interfirm relationships; threat rigidity; threat-defiant learning; venture capital; venture lawsuits
research_question: How does learning from prior experience affect a firm's decision whether to continue an interfirm relationship when the other firm faces a crisis? Empirically: what forms of VC experience make a VC threat defiant and likely to make a follow-on investment in a venture facing the crisis of a lawsuit, rather than responding rigidly and abandoning it?
mechanism_summary: Crises act as indirect threats that normally provoke threat-rigidity (conservative, standardized responses and relationship abandonment). The authors theorize "threat-defiant learning": through experience with three relational systems — similar interfirm relationships, the focal venture, and the VC's own prior lawsuits — VCs gain confidence in their ability to control and withstand the crisis, enabling them to dismiss, challenge, or attack the threat (Oliver's defiance) and continue investing. The negativity of the VC's own crisis experience is a boundary condition: defendant experience erodes identification and confidence, whereas settlement experience builds it.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops and labels the concept of threat-defiant learning, integrating the threat-rigidity hypothesis with the organizational-learning-from-crisis and relational (post-traumatic growth) literatures to explain how firms resist rigidity in the face of indirect crises affecting a partner firm. It extends learning-from-crisis theory beyond a firm's own future crisis management to firms' responses to external, indirect threats, and contributes to entrepreneurship by showing how distinct forms of VC experience — and the negativity of that experience — shape follow-on investment in ventures in crisis.
practical_implication: VCs should recognize whether their decision to continue or abandon a ventureship in crisis reflects a rigid versus defiant response, since poor management of a relationship in crisis can impair a firm's ability to attract future partners. Entrepreneurs are alerted to investigate VCs' crisis and lawsuit histories and to build deeper relationships with experienced VCs, prioritizing those less likely to abandon the venture when crisis strikes.
limitations: The authors could not assess every aspect of VC experience and lawsuit characteristics and did not model possible interactions between VC experience and venture/lawsuit attributes. Learning was not measured directly; only its behavioral effects were captured. The qualitative post hoc analysis was limited, and the study relied on federal (not state) lawsuit data due to data-availability constraints.
future_research: Future work could analyze additional types of VC experience, specific lawsuit outcomes and amounts at issue, and their interactions; replicate the findings in state courts as recordkeeping improves; use richer qualitative designs to find direct evidence of the theorized learning; study other venture crises beyond lawsuits; and examine the conditions under which a defiance response outperforms a rigid one, including for direct crises and how rigidity/defiance affects ventures' subsequent success.
abstract: Firms often depend on relationships with other firms to access important resources. When a crisis strikes a firm, the continuation of those relationships can be jeopardized, as many indirectly affected firms will leave the relationship. This abandonment can be particularly harmful if the crisis-struck firm is dependent on the relationship for valuable resources. However, some firms choose to continue their relationship with the crisis-struck firm. We consider what makes those firms resist the temptation to respond rigidly and abandon the crisis-struck firm. We contend that firms that defy such indirect threats do so as a result of threat-defiant learning, which stems from confidence gained from their prior relational experiences. We test this concept in the context of venture capital firms' (VCs) investments after venture crises in the form of venture involvement in a lawsuit. Studying 435 VCs' decisions on whether to continue to invest in 105 ventures experiencing lawsuits, we find support for threat-defiant learning. This study contributes to the literature on crisis, threat rigidity, and entrepreneurship by showing how firms can resist threat rigidity in interfirm relationships, specifically how VCs learn from experience to defy threats posed by venture lawsuits and make post-lawsuit follow-on investments.
apa_citation: Welbourne Eleazar, M. J., & Park, H. D. (2022). Who will stay when crisis strikes? Venture capitalist threat-defiant learning response to ventures in crisis. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(4), 1218–1239. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1402

title: The Analogical Construction of Stigma as a Moral Dualism: The Case of the Fossil Fuel Divestment Movement
methods: Qualitative longitudinal discourse analysis of the global fossil fuel divestment movement; inductive four-stage coding (NVivo) of 342 activist texts, 648 media articles, and additional stakeholder texts, plus 13 interviews, building a process model of analogical stigmatization.
industry: Fossil fuel industry (target of stigmatization); climate activists / fossil fuel divestment movement (the stigmatizers)
country: Global movement; activist interviews in the United Kingdom, United States, and Germany
units: Discursive texts (press releases, speeches, news media, internal guides), interviews, and material artifacts produced by activists and stakeholders
n_sample: 342 activist texts; 648 media articles; 39 celebrity/opinion-leader texts; 107 divesting-organization texts; 47 policy-actor texts; 38 financial-industry texts; 13 interviews
authors: Ferns, George; Lambert, Aliette; Günther, Maik
theories: analogical reasoning (Gentner, 2005; Cornelissen, Holt, & Zundel, 2011); institutional theory / institutional entrepreneurship (Lawrence, Phillips, & Hardy, 1999); labeling theory (Ashforth & Humphrey, 1995); organizational stigma theory (Devers, Dewett, Mishina, & Belsito, 2009)
keywords: analogy; climate activists; discourse analysis; fossil fuel divestment; moral dualism; organizational stigma; stigmatization
research_question: How do actors strategically use analogy to stigmatize an organizational category? The study foregrounds the under-examined "stigmatizers' perspective," asking how fringe actors shift meanings of an organizational category from "normal" to "fundamentally flawed."
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: *Moral dualism* — a "clear-cut and decisive contrast" between a moral ingroup (stigmatizers) and an immoral outgroup (the stigmatized target), with "no middle ground."
- Two analogical forms: *Deep* analogies (e.g., South African apartheid) generate emotive power and moralize the ingroup; *surface* analogies (e.g., tobacco) generate causal power and amplify the target's deviance.
- Process / phases: (1) *Initial framing* — problem articulation and solution proposal; (2) *Establishing ingroup morality* — virtue transfer and affective association (deep analogies); (3) *Amplifying outgroup deviance* — instituting commonalities and reinforcing negative traits (surface analogies); (4) *Stigma uptake and dispersion* — proximal adoption and distanced enactment by stakeholders.
- Emergent outcome: a self-reinforcing moral dualism that disperses stigmatizing associations across diverse stakeholder audiences (faith-based, healthcare, policy, financial actors).

Stigmatization is theorized as a discursive, relational process: activists do not "start from scratch" but appropriate already-stigmatized meanings from analogical source domains, simultaneously moralizing themselves and demonizing the target. Deep analogies transfer affective, virtue-laden meaning (justice, political struggle, morality) to build a moral "we," while surface analogies build intuitive cause-and-effect links (disease, death, deception) that frame the fossil fuel industry as existentially flawed. The interdependence of the two analogical forms supplies both an emotional and a logical imperative that drives—and spreads—stigmatizing action.
theoretical_contribution: The paper's central contribution is to show that stigmatization is contingent on constructing a moral dualism in which stigmatizers actively build their own moral superiority alongside the target's deviance—an inversion of stigma research that has focused on the stigmatized rather than the stigmatizers. It extends analogy scholarship (notably Glaser et al., 2016) by distinguishing deep and surface analogies and showing how they work interdependently to amplify inferential power, and it reframes stigma as potentially *empowering* for fringe actors who use it to loosen an incumbent's grip on a field-level grand challenge.
practical_implication: The moral-dualism model offers fringe actors a transferable playbook: to stigmatize an incumbent, they must clearly delineate a moralized ingroup and a vilified outgroup, and "borrow" meanings from already-stigmatized analogical domains. The authors suggest the approach could help address grand challenges (e.g., stigmatizing high-carbon industries such as beef or shipping) and may illuminate why movements like Occupy Wall Street relatively failed to stigmatize the financial sector—because they did less work to establish their own morality.
limitations: Because the focus was on stigmatizers rather than the stigmatized, the fossil fuel industry's reactions to and defenses against stigmatization were not extensively analyzed. The study took a broad view of "analogy" rather than dissecting specific tropes (metaphor, synecdoche, irony), and although visual/material artifacts were incorporated where relevant, multimodality (videos, images, social media) was not the analytic focus.
future_research: The authors call for research on how incumbent actors defend against stigmatization by contesting fringe actors' analogical construction of morality; on the specific forms of analogy (metaphor, synecdoche, irony) used throughout stigmatization efforts; and on multimodal analogical work (videos and images), especially on social media. They also suggest the moral-dualism lens may extend to other social evaluations and to delegitimation work more broadly.
abstract: We explore the global fossil fuel divestment movement to show how climate activists worked to stigmatize the fossil fuel industry using analogy. In doing so, we develop a model that illustrates how constructing a “moral dualism” is central to stigmatizing an organizational category. This involves concurrently establishing “stigmatizers” (ingroup) as morally superior and amplifying the deviancy of the fossil fuel industry (outgroup), both in relation to analogical contexts. Stigmatizers strategically employed two types of analogy: “deep” and “surface.” Deep analogies produce emotive power, facilitating the moralization of the ingroup through the transfer of affective meanings from a source context to a target domain. Surface analogies generate causal power to inform wider audiences of the target’s deviance through association with already-stigmatized organizational categories. Analogical power underpinning the morally dualistic nature of stigmatization can therefore empower fringe actors to stigmatize an incumbent as they appropriate meanings from analogical source domains.
apa_citation: Ferns, G., Lambert, A., & Günther, M. (2022). The analogical construction of stigma as a moral dualism: The case of the fossil fuel divestment movement. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(4), 1383–1415. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.0615

title: The Use of Strategic Noise in Reactive Impression Management: How Do Market Reactions Matter?
methods: Archival event study; zero-inflated negative binomial regressions of reactive positive and negative strategic-noise counts on the magnitude (absolute CAR[-1,0]) and direction of the M&A stock-market reaction, with firm-clustered robust standard errors and year dummies; negative binomial models as robustness checks.
industry: Mergers and acquisitions (acquiring firms) across industries, drawn from the SDC M&A database
units: M&A deals (acquiring-firm announcements as focal events)
n_sample: 7,575 M&A deals (3,746 with negative and 3,829 with positive stock market reactions)
authors: Jin, Jing; Li, Haiyang; Hoskisson, Robert E.
theories: expectancy violations theory (Burgoon, 1993, 2016); strategic noise / impression management literature (Graffin et al., 2011, 2016)
keywords: cumulative abnormal return; expectancy violations theory; mergers and acquisitions; reactive impression management; stock market reaction; strategic noise
research_question: How do firms use positive and negative strategic noise for *reactive* impression management once the initial stock market reaction to a focal event has become publicly known? Specifically, how do the direction (positive vs. negative) and magnitude (absolute value) of that market reaction shape the amount of reactive positive and negative strategic noise a firm subsequently releases?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on expectancy violations theory, the authors argue that the publicly observed market reaction signals the direction and magnitude of stakeholders' expectancy violation, which firms then exploit. When the reaction is negative but small, firms release more positive strategic noise to offset and possibly flip the violation (H1a); when the negative reaction is substantial, offsetting is futile, so firms disgorge negative news together to convert it into a negative expectancy confirmation and seed future positive surprises ("big bath," H1b). When the reaction is positive but small, firms amplify it with positive noise (H2a); when it is substantial, firms "hide" unrelated negative news under the positive reaction to avoid creating separate future negative violations (H2b). All four hypotheses are supported.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the strategic-noise and impression-management literatures—previously focused almost exclusively on *anticipatory* IM—by being the first to systematically theorize and demonstrate strategic noise as a *reactive* IM tactic. By integrating expectancy violations theory, it shows that positive and negative strategic noise play distinct roles that depend jointly on the direction and magnitude of an already-realized expectancy violation, revealing that the offsetting role of positive noise is limited in reactive IM and that firms may deploy both anticipatory-style (big-bath, hiding) motives reactively when violations are substantial.
practical_implication: For executives releasing news around M&A announcements, the magnitude of the market reaction matters more than its direction in shaping effective reactive IM. Firms can dampen the strategic effect of negative events by using the hiding effect (under a significant positive reaction) or the big-bath effect (under a significant negative reaction), and should calibrate the type and amount of subsequent news to the observed reaction rather than treating positive and negative reactions symmetrically.
limitations: The study treats each news release independently and does not model relationships or consistency among different pieces of news (or between the noise and the M&A itself). The same news type may be positive for one firm or audience and negative for another, introducing classification ambiguity. The authors also do not test whether firms following the proposed IM strategies actually achieve better short- or long-term outcomes (e.g., media sentiment, deal completion).
future_research: Future work could examine whether inconsistency between confounding events and the focal decision affects IM effectiveness, whether the same news is interpreted differently across firms and audience groups, and whether firms adhering to these strategies outperform deviators on outcomes such as media-coverage sentiment, M&A completion likelihood, and deal duration. Researchers could also explore how industry/competitive spillovers shape a focal firm's choice of positive versus negative strategic noise.
abstract: Management scholars have argued and demonstrated that firms use strategic noise as an anticipatory form of impression management to minimize the effect of a potential negative reaction to an event of interest. In this study, we contribute to the impression management literature by exploring how both positive and negative strategic noise may intercede in the process of reactive impression management. We argue that in reactive impression management, since firms already know the initial market reaction to a focal event, they can “strategically” release subsequent positive or negative strategic noise depending upon the direction and magnitude of the initial market reaction to the focal event. Using a sample of 7,575 mergers and acquisitions from 2001 to 2015 that represent our focal events, we find strong evidence to support our arguments.
apa_citation: Jin, J., Li, H., & Hoskisson, R. E. (2022). The use of strategic noise in reactive impression management: How do market reactions matter? *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(4), 1303–1326. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.1054

title: Network Stability: The Role of Geography and Brokerage Structure Inequity
methods: Discrete-time survival analysis of non-repeated events (brokerage triad decay) using a complementary log-log estimator on a 21-year unbalanced panel; robustness via Weibull, frailty, PPML with high-dimensional fixed effects, and two-stage Heckman selection corrections.
industry: Global biopharmaceutical industry (SICs 2833-2836)
units: Brokerage alliance triads (broker-alter-alter); 14,829 unique triads
n_sample: 53,377 triad-year observations, 260 broker firms and 517 alter firms
authors: Kumar, Pankaj; Zaheer, Akbar
theories: cognitive categorization of rivals (Porac et al., 1995); equity theory / inequity aversion (Fehr & Schmidt, 1999; Cook & Emerson, 1978); structural hole theory (Burt, 1992)
keywords: brokerage stability; geographic distance; interfirm alliances; knowledge recombination; network decay; structural holes; structural inequity
research_question: How does brokerage structure inequity affect the stability of brokerage triads, and how does geographic distance contingently influence the effects of structural inequity on the stability of brokerage triads?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Alter-alter and broker-alter knowledge/status/size/patent differences, past ties, ease of recombination, same-country and same-SIC dyads, broker's lack of constraint; broker, broker-industry, and broker-country fixed effects.

Alters perceive the structural inequity in knowledge recombination opportunity favoring the broker as an unfair "cost," which provides motivation to terminate the tie and thereby destabilizes the brokerage triad (H1, supported: hazard ratio 1.12). Geographic distance operates as a sociocognitive prism: when the broker is geographically distant, alters are less likely to cognitively categorize it as a rival and become more tolerant of inequity, attenuating the destabilizing effect and prolonging brokerage (H2, supported: negative interaction).
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends structural hole theory by shifting from a broker-centric to an altercentric account of network persistence, locating the source of brokerage decay in alters' perceived structural inequity in knowledge recombination opportunity rather than alter-alter rivalry. It introduces geography as a sociocognitive moderator—the "shadow of the broker"—showing that spatial distance heterogeneously alters alters' tolerance for inequity through rival categorization, thereby integrating the spatial embeddedness of ties into theories of network evolution and decay.
practical_implication: Broker firms seeking to sustain advantageous brokerage positions are better off forming spatially distant ties and using frequent communication and procedurally fair alliance-management policies to manage geographically proximate, more rivalrous relationships. Alter-firm managers who wish to strengthen their bargaining position should make their distant collaborations more visible internally to stay alert to brokerage benefits they may be ceding.
limitations: Brokerage in a knowledge-intensive industry may differ in degree from non-knowledge-based contexts, potentially weakening the theorizing there. The study captures "what opportunity a broker has" but data limitations preclude directly testing "how the broker behaves," and the authors cannot observe precisely which triad partner initiated termination, relying instead on theorized and post hoc proxies for alter agency.
future_research: Future work could test the broker-behavior boundary condition, explore how structurally equivalent alters or status- and proximity-based reference groups shape inequity perceptions and referent-discrepancy effects, and examine different decay outcomes (tie disintegration, disintermediation, closure) to distinguish their antecedents and consequences.
abstract: While the outcomes of brokerage have been extensively investigated, the issue of when and why brokerage structures persist or decay has attracted relatively little research interest. Taking the perspective of the disconnected firms to whom the broker is tied, the "alters," we argue that structurally induced "inequity" in opportunity, or the broker's potential recombination of a larger share of alters' knowledge than vice versa, exerts a destabilizing effect on brokerage persistence. Furthermore, geographic distance, via its role in the cognitive classification of rivals, enhances alters' tolerance for inequity and contingently weakens the destabilizing effect of structural inequity in knowledge recombination opportunity that benefits the broker. Specifically, we postulate about the sociocognitive prismatic nature of geography in that geographic distance makes it less likely that alter firms see the broker as a rival and are hence less sensitive to inequity. We test our hypotheses with 260 broker and 517 alter firms in the global biopharmaceutical industry from 1985 to 2005, yielding 53,377 triad-year observations using a complementary log-log discrete time survival estimator. Our results support the hypotheses, highlighting the interplay between structural inequity and geographic distance in explaining brokerage decay.
apa_citation: Kumar, P., & Zaheer, A. (2022). Network stability: The role of geography and brokerage structure inequity. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(4), 1139-1168. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0903

title: Forming Entrepreneurial Teams: Mixing Business and Friendship to Create Transactive Memory Systems for Enhanced Success
methods: Three field studies: two observational (Study 1: OLS/Poisson regression and ANOVA on Kickstarter crowdfunding data; Study 2: logistic/Cox regression and Monte Carlo mediation on time-lagged competition data) and one preregistered 2x2 field experiment (Study 3: randomized controlled trial with moderated-mediation via PROCESS).
industry: Technology-based new ventures (Study 1 Kickstarter technology projects; Study 2 technology entrepreneurship competition; Study 3 university entrepreneurship courses)
time_period: Study 1: April–December 2014; Study 2: 2016 and 2017 competition cycles; Study 3: 2018–2019 academic year
units: Founding/new venture teams
n_sample: Study 1: 206 teams; Study 2: 242 teams (631 entrepreneurs); Study 3: 94 teams (287 participants)
authors: Lazar, Moran; Miron-Spektor, Ella; Chen, Gilad; Goldfarb, Brent; Erez, Miriam; Agarwal, Rajshree
theories: resource scarcity and bounded rationality; team-learning theory; transactive memory systems (Wegner, 1987; Ren & Argote, 2011)
keywords: dual formation strategy; early entrepreneurial success; entrepreneurial team formation; interpersonal-attraction strategy; resource-seeking strategy; team learning; transactive memory systems
research_question: How and why do the initial strategies that founders use to form new venture teams—interpersonal attraction, resource-seeking, or a dual combination of both—shape early entrepreneurial success, and what learning mechanism explains this effect?
mechanism_summary: Forming a team with attention to both relational closeness and complementary expertise from the onset lets founders leverage each strategy's selection criteria in their earliest interactions, so that familiar cofounders who also bring complementary knowledge can quickly establish role structure, trust, and coordination. This accelerates the emergence of stronger transactive memory systems, which in turn enable the team to match members to tasks, integrate diverse expertise, and navigate critical early milestones. Because the dual strategy is valuable, rare, and difficult to imitate (if not adopted at the onset), it provides a source of early competitive advantage, with transactive memory systems mediating the link between formation strategy and success.
theoretical_contribution: The paper integrates the macro entrepreneurship literature on team formation with the micro teams-and-learning literature to identify transactive memory systems as a novel mechanism through which initial formation strategies drive early entrepreneurial success. It extends entrepreneurial team formation research by theorizing and testing the value, rarity, and inimitability of a dual strategy, and contributes to the team-learning literature by reconciling mixed findings on prior familiarity—showing that familiarity aids transactive memory systems only when members also bring different expertise.
practical_implication: Aspiring founders should attend to team assembly early, deliberately overcoming the tendency to search only relationally or only instrumentally, and proactively seek cofounders who offer both close relations and complementary skills. Entrepreneurial education programs, accelerators, and cofounder-matching platforms should teach partner-selection processes alongside business-model training, and investors should prioritize teams that combine close relationships with diverse knowledge.
limitations: In Study 3, the dual strategy affected success only indirectly through transactive memory systems (not directly as in Studies 1 and 2), possibly because experimental assignment constrained variance relative to naturally formed teams. Study 1's sample could only include teams that voluntarily disclosed their formation strategy on their campaign, raising potential selection bias and the possibility that disclosed strategies differ from actually employed ones.
future_research: Future work could disentangle self- versus facilitated selection and separate selection from training effects in entrepreneurial programs; examine other team processes (e.g., shared mental models) and external-stakeholder dynamics alongside transactive memory systems; and test whether dual strategies applied within each member versus across different members differentially accelerate transactive memory system emergence. Researchers might also explore the antecedents of formation strategies and their long-term effects at later growth stages.
abstract: Successfully navigating critical uncertainties during the incipient stages of new ventures requires these ventures to develop learning systems, and building the right team may be key in this process. Drawing on prior work indicating that entrepreneurial teams form using either an interpersonal-attraction strategy (relationships with similar others in a close network) or a resource-seeking strategy (instrumental focus on complementary skills), we theorize that a dual formation strategy, although challenging to execute, is critical for early performance. Using dual formation strategies from the onset fosters the development of stronger transactive memory systems, because close relationships facilitate smooth coordination among founders specializing in complementary tasks. Transactive memory systems thus mediate the relationship between formation strategies and early entrepreneurial success. Findings from two field observational studies and a field intervention study support our theory: teams formed based on a dual strategy raised greater seed funding on Kickstarter—a leading crowdfunding platform—(Study 1), were more successful in a prestigious entrepreneurial competition (Study 2), and gained more profits from selling their initial products (Study 3). Our research advances knowledge on entrepreneurial team formation and offers practical recommendations to facilitate this process at such nascent, but critical, stages.
apa_citation: Lazar, M., Miron-Spektor, E., Chen, G., Goldfarb, B., Erez, M., & Agarwal, R. (2022). Forming entrepreneurial teams: Mixing business and friendship to create transactive memory systems for enhanced success. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(4), 1110–1138. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0393

title: Ritual Work and the Formation of a Shared Sense of Meaningfulness
methods: Inductive single-case field study (21 months at 'FitCo'); grounded-theory/abductive analysis (constant comparison, open and theoretical coding) of 147 semistructured interviews, 121 archival documents, and participant/nonparticipant observation, integrated into a three-step process model.
industry: Athletic footwear and apparel (global brand, revenues over $2 billion annually)
units: Single organization ('FitCo', pseudonym); informants = senior leaders (incl. current and two former CEOs), members, an industry analyst, and an advertising-agency member
n_sample: 147 semistructured interviews; 121 documents; observation 2–3 days/week over 21 months
authors: Lepisto, Douglas A.
theories: Selznick's institutionalism (Selznick, 1957); interaction ritual chain theory (Collins, 1993, 2004); meaningfulness / meaningful work (Pratt & Ashforth, 2003; Rosso et al., 2010)
keywords: emotional energy; interaction ritual; leadership; meaningful work; meaningfulness; ritual work; shared feeling
research_question: How does a shared sense of meaningfulness toward an organization's work and activities emerge and form from members' shared experiences, and what actions, actors, and resources trigger and propel that formation?
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition (Step 1 — Proliferating emotional energy): leaders perform *ritual work* — comprising *material production* (creating space, buying objects, hiring instructors), *mobilizing assembly*, and *inclusion practices* — that creates and scales an *organizational interaction ritual* ("Liven," a communal workout) with barriers to outsiders, shared attention, and shared mood.
- Process / phases (Step 2 — Sharing emotional energy): members display and talk about emotional energy through talk and gesture, growing participation and producing a *shared feeling of meaningfulness*.
- Emergent outcome (Step 3 — Representing shared feeling): leaders articulate significance and connect words to the shared feeling, yielding a *shared representation of meaningfulness*.
- Boundary conditions: physical coassembly is possible; leaders do not initially trigger formation through deliberate top–down communication; the focal activity resonates with broader cultural scripts and is conducive to rhythmic synchronization.

Effective interaction rituals "pump participants up with emotional energy"; ritual work assembles members and propels the flow of bodies through these situations, so that personal emotional energy, once shared, constitutes a collective feeling that leaders subsequently endow with symbolic representation. The model recasts leaders as "gatherers" rather than only rhetoricians, and treats meaningfulness as emerging from felt experience before it is cognitively articulated.
theoretical_contribution: The paper expands understanding of the *triggers* of meaningfulness by showing formation can be triggered unintentionally through ritual work, in contrast to prior theory emphasizing deliberate top–down rhetoric and visioning. It identifies new *cultural mechanisms* residing at the level of situations — materiality and inclusion practices that assemble members and facilitate their flow through interaction rituals. It advances the *role of emotions*, offering a correction to predominantly cognitive views by showing meaningfulness has an emotional component (a shared feeling) that can precede and justify its cognitive representation.
practical_implication: Organizations seeking to cultivate a shared sense that "what we do" is meaningful can do so by investing in ritual work — providing spaces, objects, and people, and using mobilizing assembly and inclusion practices to draw the maximum number of members into a recurring, activity-centered interaction ritual — rather than relying first on leaders' communication. Starting with ritual (rather than cheap words) may make later claims of purpose feel authentic rather than opportunistic, though it carries financial and opportunity costs.
limitations: As a single deviant case at "FitCo," the theory generalizes to organizational settings with similar features (e.g., leaders who do not initially communicate to trigger formation; members able to physically assemble) and may hold less relevance for distributed or virtual organizations where physical coassembly cannot occur frequently. The author cannot definitively rule out that the emotional energy observed was simply the hormonal effect of intense exercise, though other research finds non-exercise rituals also produce emotional energy.
future_research: Future work should examine whether and how a shared sense of meaningfulness forms in virtual/distributed settings; whether exercise versus other activities is more or less effective at synchronizing members; how other forms of energy (e.g., adrenaline, caffeine) boost or inhibit ritual outcomes; the tradeoffs of starting with communication versus ritual work; and the conditions under which forming meaningfulness through ritual work generates feelings of exclusion or marginalization.
abstract: Drawing from a 21-month field study of an athletic footwear and apparel company, I build theory on how a shared sense of meaningfulness forms through interaction ritual and emotion. I find that a shared sense of meaningfulness is composed of a shared feeling and a shared representation that are constructed sequentially. Leaders trigger and propel formation through a set of actions I refer to as ritual work that proliferates emotional energy across the population of members. Members subsequently share their feelings of emotional energy through talk and gesture to develop a shared feeling of meaningfulness. Leaders then articulate words and connect these words to shared feelings to produce a shared representation of meaningfulness. I integrate these findings into a three-step process model. This model contributes to our understanding of the triggers, cultural mechanisms, and role of emotions in the formation of meaningfulness in organizations.
apa_citation: Lepisto, D. A. (2022). Ritual work and the formation of a shared sense of meaningfulness. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(4), 1327–1352. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.0854

title: The Push and Pull of Attaining CEO Celebrity: A Media Routines Perspective
methods: Longitudinal panel-based ordered probit regression (xtoprobit, robust SEs clustered by CEO, Stata 16.1) on a modified state-based sample of newly appointed S&P 1500 CEOs; novel multimedia ordinal CEO celebrity index combining 11 volume-of-attention indicators with LIWC-based positive-valence (emotional resonance) scores; marginal-effects analyses, ITCV robustness checks.
industry: S&P 1500 public corporations across industries (2-digit GICS); media coverage measured from newspapers, magazines, broadcast, and online (Wikipedia) sources
time_period: CEOs appointed 2006–2010; celebrity measured per year in office through 2014 (1,450 CEO-year observations)
units: Newly appointed CEOs (CEO-year observations)
n_sample: 244 distinct CEOs; 1,450 CEO-year observations
authors: Lovelace, Jeffrey B.; Bundy, Jonathan; Pollock, Timothy G.; Hambrick, Donald C.
theories: CEO celebrity / social approval assets (Hayward, Rindova, & Pollock, 2004; Rindova, Pollock, & Hayward, 2006); media routines theory (Shoemaker & Reese, 2013); romance of leadership (Meindl, Ehrlich, & Dukerich, 1985); upper echelons theory (Hambrick & Mason, 1984)
keywords: CEO celebrity; celebrity attainment; demographic atypicality; media routines; push–pull theory; self-promotion; strategic nonconformity
research_question: Why do some CEOs become celebrities while others with seemingly equal accomplishments do not? Specifically, what "pull" factors (firm and CEO distinctiveness) and "push" tactics (CEO self-promotion) — operating through journalists' media routines — drive a CEO's degree of celebrity attainment (B-list and, beyond that, A-list)?
mechanism_summary:
- Controls: Firm size, firm performance (industry-adjusted ROA, TSR), firm/CEO presuccession visibility, total press releases, total tweets, CEO tenure, year and industry dummies.

Theorizing from media routines, the authors argue journalists — facing time, information, and audience constraints — "pull" distinctive CEOs (nonconformist firms, demographically atypical leaders) into heroic narratives because they make compelling, newsworthy stories, while CEOs can "push" themselves into coverage by supplying information subsidies and self-promotion that ease journalists' production. Ordered probit results support H1 (strategic nonconformity), H2 (demographic atypicality), and H3 (self-promotion) as positive predictors of celebrity; H5 is supported (self-promotion amplifies atypicality's effect, especially for the A-list) but H4 (self-promotion × nonconformity) is not.
theoretical_contribution: The paper shifts CEO celebrity research from consequences to antecedents, introducing a media-routines-grounded "push–pull theory" that distinguishes who CEOs are (demographic atypicality) from what they do (strategic nonconformity and self-promotion). It reconceptualizes celebrity as an ordinal construct (noncelebrity, B-list, A-list) rather than a binary, and contributes a comprehensive multimedia, valence-weighted ordinal measure spanning newspaper, magazine, broadcast, and online sources.
practical_implication: For CEOs aspiring to celebrity, distinctive personal attributes matter more than self-promotion or strategic actions alone, and combining high self-promotion with demographic atypicality is especially potent for reaching the A-list. Boards should calibrate vigilance to a CEO's self-promotion and distinctiveness, given celebrity's documented risks (complacency, hubris, backlash) for firms.
limitations: The sample is restricted to newly appointed CEOs, excluding longer-serving incumbents who were already celebrities. Modified state-based sampling (oversampling WSJ-covered CEOs) means the sample may differ from a random one, biasing effect magnitudes, so the authors interpret only sign and significance, not effect sizes. The celebrity measure equally weights newspaper, magazine, broadcast, and Internet sources, and the nonconformity measure treats actions exceeding or lagging industry norms equivalently.
future_research: Investigate the motivational mechanisms (e.g., narcissism versus altruistic or firm-promoting motives) driving CEO self-promotion, and how self-promotion shapes susceptibility to backlash when strategic efforts fail. Use the ordinal measure to study trajectories or "arcs" of celebrity — whether levels are attained gradually or abruptly and how celebrity persists or decays. Examine whether more specialized or business-oriented media, or directional (aggressive vs. conservative) nonconformity, yield different interactive effects.
abstract: Why do some CEOs become celebrities, while others with seemingly equal accomplishments do not? After two decades of research, far more is known about the substantial consequences of CEO celebrity than about its determinants. Drawing on the media routines literature, we develop and test a “push–pull theory” of CEO celebrity attainment, arguing and finding that (a) journalists pull certain CEOs into the limelight, particularly those whose firms are nonconformists within their industries and who themselves are demographically atypical; (b) CEOs, through various self-promotion tactics, can push themselves and their stories into public view; and (c) these push tactics are particularly beneficial for helping atypical CEOs achieve celebrity. We conceptualize CEO celebrity as an ordinal construct with discrete gradations, which we refer to as noncelebrities, B-list celebrities, and A-list celebrities. We test our theory using a longitudinal sample of CEOs, and develop a novel, ordinal measure of CEO celebrity that encompasses a broad array of media (newspaper, broadcast, magazine, and online), allowing us to set forth and test a more nuanced theory about different levels of celebrity attainment.
apa_citation: Lovelace, J. B., Bundy, J., Pollock, T. G., & Hambrick, D. C. (2022). The push and pull of attaining CEO celebrity: A media routines perspective. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(4), 1169–1191. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0435

title: “Who Are You Going to Call?” Network Activation in Creative Idea Generation and Elaboration
methods: Three between-subjects laboratory/field experiments (plus a field preliminary study); planned-contrast ANOVA, Poisson and OLS regression, consensual assessment technique for rating creativity, and PROCESS-macro moderated-mediation tests.
industry: Behavioral-lab participant pools at a European business school; a live-action role-playing (LARP) nonprofit, GRV Italia (Italy)
units: Individuals (creators) conversing with or activating their network ties
n_sample: Study 1: 93; Study 2 preliminary field study: 74; Study 2 main: 221; Study 3: 217
authors: Mannucci, Pier Vittorio; Perry-Smith, Jill E.
theories: cognitive/activated networks (Carley, 1986; Krackhardt, 1987, 1990; Smith et al., 2012); decision-making under information/choice overload (Simon, 1955; Tversky & Kahneman, 1974); social network theory of creativity (Perry-Smith & Mannucci, 2017); strength-of-weak-ties perspective (Granovetter, 1973; Baer, 2010)
keywords: creativity; idea elaboration; idea generation; idea journey; network activation; network size; perceived risk; strong ties; tie strength; weak ties
research_question: Which types of ties (weak or strong) are helpful in different phases of the idea journey (generation vs. elaboration), and—given this—what helps or hinders individuals from activating the appropriate ties in each phase and switching ties between phases?
mechanism_summary: Weak ties supply non-redundant knowledge and cognitive flexibility that aid idea generation, whereas strong ties supply constructive feedback and support ("building," yes-anding) that aid idea elaboration. Because networks are cognitive representations subject to limited mental capacity, large networks induce cognitive narrowing and anchoring: creators disproportionately call to mind salient strong ties and stick to prior choices, so they activate fewer weak ties and switch less—especially when creativity feels risky. Failing to switch produces two errors: over-relying on strong ties yields false positives (pursuing uncreative ideas), and sticking to weak ties yields false negatives (abandoning creative ideas).
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops and experimentally tests a theory of network *activation* across phases of the idea journey, shifting the creativity-and-networks debate from the static configuration of ties to which ties creators cognitively call to mind and whether they switch ties as phases change. It supplies a causal test distinguishing weak- versus strong-tie benefits by phase, and explains the long-standing mixed findings on network size by showing that large networks paradoxically impede optimal activation through cognitive narrowing amplified by perceived risk.
practical_implication: Creators and managers should treat tie selection as phase-specific: deliberately consult diverse weak ties when generating ideas and trusted strong ties (especially "inner-circle" confidants) when elaborating, and consciously switch contacts between phases. Because large networks and high perceived risk push people toward over-relying on strong ties, organizations that lower the perceived risk of creativity (e.g., cultures of "plussing"/yes-anding) can help creators activate and switch ties more optimally.
limitations: The experimental design isolates causality but cannot reproduce the complexity, duration, and richer social dynamics of real organizational creative projects, and it captured only a single, clear-cut generation-to-elaboration transition rather than repeated iterations. The studies inferred but did not directly measure the underlying cognitive processes, used multiple studies rather than direct replication, and drew on subject pools predominantly composed of women.
future_research: Future work could use neuroimaging or other methods to directly capture the cognitive mechanisms of tie activation, adopt longitudinal designs to study repeated iterations and blurred phase boundaries, and examine how alter characteristics (e.g., status), indirect ties, and the broader network of contacts shape activation. Scholars are also encouraged to test whether the effects differ in predominantly male settings and to use qualitative methods to further probe conversation dynamics across the idea journey.
abstract: Considering creativity as a journey beyond idea generation, scholars have theorized that different ties are beneficial in different phases. As individuals usually possess different types of ties, selecting the optimal ties in each phase and changing ties as needed are central activities for creative success. We identify the types of ties (weak or strong) that are helpful in idea generation and idea elaboration and, given this understanding, whether individuals activate ties in each phase accordingly. In an experimental study of individuals conversing with their ties, we provide evidence of the causal effects of weak and strong ties on idea generation and idea elaboration. We also find that individuals do not always activate ties optimally and identify network size and risk as barriers. Our results in a series of studies reveal that individuals with large networks, despite providing more opportunity to activate both strong and weak ties, activate fewer weak ties and are less likely to switch ties across phases than individuals with smaller networks, particularly when creativity is perceived as a high-risk endeavor. Finally, we find that activating the wrong ties leads to either dropping creative ideas or pursuing uncreative ones.
apa_citation: Mannucci, P. V., & Perry-Smith, J. E. (2022). “Who are you going to call?” Network activation in creative idea generation and elaboration. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(4), 1192–1217. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0333
