title: Paradox Peers: A Relational Approach to Navigating a Business–Society Paradox
methods: Inductive, grounded-theory qualitative study (Gioia methodology). 40 in-person/phone interviews with corporate grantmakers from 32 large U.S. firms plus observations of 12 monthly peer group meetings; coded in ATLAS.ti through first-order codes, second-order themes, and aggregate dimensions, with member checks and triangulation.
industry: Corporate philanthropy / CSR (grantmakers overseeing corporate giving at Fortune 500–scale firms)
time_period: Interviews late 2014–2016 (plus 2020 follow-up member checks); 12 meetings observed 2015–2016
units: Individual corporate grantmakers (and their peer group meetings)
n_sample: 40 grantmaker informants from 32 firms; 12 observed group meetings
authors: Pamphile, Vontrese Deeds
theories: occupational communities (Van Maanen & Barley, 1984); organizational paradox theory (Smith & Lewis, 2011; Schad, Lewis, Raisch, & Smith, 2016); paradox mindset (Miron-Spektor et al., 2018); relational sociology (Emirbayer, 1997)
keywords: business–society paradox; corporate philanthropy; corporate social responsibility; navigating paradox; occupations; organizational paradox; paradox peers; relational mechanisms
research_question: How do individuals navigate paradox that is core to their occupational role but not a dominant organizational concern for senior leaders? The study examines this through corporate grantmakers who must jointly pursue business and social objectives even when firm leaders prioritize economic goals and treat social impact as peripheral.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: A business–society paradox (persistent, interdependent yet contradictory social and economic objectives) that is core to grantmakers' occupational mandate but dismissed or minimized by profit-focused leaders.
- Triggering condition: Grantmakers occupy a relatively low-power, internally isolated position lacking interactional partners and resource-rich leaders who appreciate the paradox.
- Enabling relationship: "Paradox peers"—ongoing, cooperative, noncompetitive ties to grantmakers at other organizations facing a similar paradox, built and strengthened through repeated interactions (e.g., monthly meetings).
- Relational mechanisms (process): (1) connecting with one another to relieve tension; (2) collectively protecting the paradox (keeping both elements legitimate rather than letting one dominate); (3) collaboratively brainstorming responses.
- Emergent outcome: Working through paradox by recalling peer interactions to remain energized, motivated, and committed to both social and business objectives back at the home organization.

Drawing on an inductive grounded-theory analysis, the study theorizes a peer-based model in which relationships with paradox peers, operating through the three relational mechanisms, sustain individuals' ability to work through a business–society paradox internally. The process is fundamentally relational and collective: lower-power individuals build a sense of efficacy and resilience through cross-organizational peer interaction even without leader support, reinforcing both the relationships and continued engagement with paradox in a positive cycle.
theoretical_contribution: The paper advances a more relational approach to paradox theory, unpacking how and why interactions with external peers enable individuals to navigate paradox—shifting the field beyond individualized cognitive/behavioral accounts and beyond intra-organizational dynamics. It extends the paradox-mindset construct to include a relational, collective dimension; foregrounds the role of lower-power relational partners (rather than high-power leaders); and bridges paradox theory with research on occupations by showing that cross-organizational occupational communities supply relational resources for living with persistent tensions.
practical_implication: Firms that staff CSR, philanthropy, ethics, sustainability, or diversity roles may shift business–society paradoxes from the organizational level to the occupational level, leaving individuals to absorb persistent tensions; without organizational engagement this can stall or reverse social impact. The findings suggest that cross-organizational peer networks and professional associations are valuable supports that help such individuals sustain commitment to social objectives, and that organizations should attend to these positions and relationships.
limitations: The referral-chain sampling in one geographic location with an active peer group likely over-sampled connected grantmakers rather than isolated ones, and all informants worked for large Fortune 500 firms, so the model may not transfer to smaller organizations with scarce philanthropic resources. The study did not capture whether some peer connections matter more than others, nor negative/destructive peer dynamics, and generalizability beyond corporate philanthropy to other competing-demand occupations remains to be tested.
future_research: Researchers could examine variation in how strongly individuals connect with paradox peers (including isolated individuals), whether mature versus still-professionalizing occupations display different relational mechanisms, and the factors that prevent destructive cycles while enabling positive relational dynamics. Further work could explore how occupational groups move social issues from the periphery to the core of the firm, and whether actors lacking a clear occupational community can form paradox-peer ties.
abstract: Despite advancements in our understanding of organizational paradox, we know little about how individuals navigate paradox when tensions are core to one's occupational role but not a dominant concern for senior leaders. This study explores how grantmakers working in corporate philanthropy navigate a business–society paradox when their leaders prioritize business over social objectives at the organization level. By analyzing interview data from grantmakers, alongside observations of their group meetings, I induce a peer-based model of navigating paradox. The findings reveal the important supportive role of relationships with "paradox peers"—ongoing, cooperative connections to individuals external to one's organization but facing similar paradoxical challenges. I detail three relational mechanisms for engaging paradox that together facilitate the ability of individuals to navigate paradox in their home organizations: connecting with others to relieve tension, collectively protecting the paradox, and collaboratively brainstorming responses. My findings shift paradox scholarship toward a more relational understanding of navigating paradoxes, exploring how engaging tensions outside of one's organizational boundaries can support the navigation of paradox internally.
apa_citation: Pamphile, V. D. (2022). Paradox peers: A relational approach to navigating a business–society paradox. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(4), 1274–1302. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0616

title: Stranger Leaders: A Theory of Marginal Leaders’ Conception of Learning in Organizations
methods: Inductive, qualitative theory-building (Glaser & Strauss, 1967; Strauss & Corbin, 1998) using semistructured interviews, complemented by a systems psychodynamic interpretive approach; line-by-line, axial, and theoretical coding with constant comparison and member-check group discussions; data collected and analyzed in parallel over two years to theoretical saturation.
industry: Multinational companies across professional services, finance and insurance (22), chemical and precision manufacturing (14), IT and telecommunications (10), retailing and FMCG (8), traditional and renewable energy (7), biotech and pharmaceuticals (5), construction and transportation (3)
time_period: Data collected and analyzed in parallel over two years (cross-sectional)
units: Senior learning and development (L&D) executives (marginal leaders)
n_sample: 69 senior L&D executives in 69 multinational companies
authors: Petriglieri, Gianpiero; Peshkam, Annie
theories: leader identity construction (DeRue & Ashford, 2010; Ibarra et al., 2014); management of paradox/dualities (Smith & Lewis, 2011; Schad et al., 2016); marginality / the stranger (Simmel, 1908/1950; Park, 1928); systems psychodynamics (Petriglieri & Petriglieri, 2020)
keywords: conception of learning; duality of learning; leader identity; management of dualities; marginal leaders; paradox; systems psychodynamics
research_question: How do marginal leaders navigate defining dualities in organizations? The authors study senior L&D executives as an "extreme case" of marginal leaders who hold a formal leadership role yet lack an established template for their leader identity and face conflicting (instrumental vs. humanistic) prescriptions for their function.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: the "defining duality" of learning (instrumental vs. humanistic views of the function); leadership conceived as an identity rather than a position; marginality / "stranger" status (insiders with loose ties, caught between principles).
- Process / phases: the "conception of learning" — finding a place in relation to significant counterparts, taking a stance on learning, and building learning spaces — through which marginal leaders craft a leader identity.
- Trajectories (emergent leader identities): custodian (instrumental view, leave the margins), challenger (humanistic view, leave the margins), and connector (holds the duality as a paradox, leads from the margins).
- Systems psychodynamic underpinnings: splitting and projection (projecting the instrumental facet onto established leaders and the humanistic facet onto learners) drive the custodian/challenger bifurcation; integration and relatedness underpin the connector trajectory.

The conception of learning is the process through which leadership "comes to life and becomes organized": by relating to significant counterparts and taking a stance on why and what learning counts, marginal leaders settle existential angst into a viable leader identity and translate it into a strategy for organizing learning spaces. Custodians and challengers reduce ambiguity by splitting the duality and grounding identity in a single pole, whereas connectors resist splitting and integrate both poles, treating the duality as a paradox to be held rather than a conflict to be resolved.
theoretical_contribution: The study describes the predicament of marginal leaders — a boundary region between emerging and established leadership — and surfaces existential and strategic layers of leader identity work that lie beneath and beyond the interpersonal/social layer scholars know best. It theorizes the "conception of learning" as the link between leaders' becoming and organizing, and argues the instrumental–humanistic duality may sit at the core of all leaders' identities, thereby bridging the leader-identity literature to the literature on the management of paradoxes.
practical_implication: The work informs the management-learning conversation on the purpose, value, and practice of learning, and the relationship between L&D executives and academic providers of executive education. It advises the proliferating cadre of marginal leaders (heads of ethics, diversity, well-being, learning, etc.) to recast marginality as a positive, creative experience — keeping a healthy distance from the organization while sustaining connections with diverse internal and external groups — since holding the instrumental–humanistic duality requires embracing rather than escaping marginality.
limitations: The sample, though spanning many industries and locations, is a theoretical (not representative) one, so generalizability must be treated cautiously. The cross-sectional design precludes inferences about how leaders' views shift over time, and the authors did not collect data on personality differences or stages of adult development. Interviews better mirror informants' internalized reality than their social acts, limiting insight into the tactics leaders use to influence others and build consensus.
future_research: Further research is needed to determine the conditions under which the theorizing is most informative and the prevalence of different trajectories across leadership functions. Longitudinal research could examine whether the same person takes different trajectories at different times, organizations, or in relation to different populations, and ethnographic methods within a smaller subset of organizations could add nuance about how these interpretations are enacted or denied.
abstract: Not all marginal leaders took the same trajectory toward firm ground for their identities. Some left the margins to lead, embracing either an instrumental or a humanistic view of their function. Others learned to lead from the margins, casting that duality as a paradox. Taking a systems psychodynamic approach to examine marginal leaders’ trajectories through a defining duality, this study reveals the interplay between existential and strategic layers of leader identity construction. Theorizing the conception of learning as the process through which leadership comes to life and becomes organized, the study expands and bridges the literatures on leader identity and on the management of dualities.
apa_citation: Petriglieri, G., & Peshkam, A. (2022). Stranger leaders: A theory of marginal leaders’ conception of learning in organizations. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(4), 1240–1273. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0162

title: How Do Investors Evaluate Past Entrepreneurial Failure? Unpacking Failure Due to Lack of Skill versus Bad Luck
methods: Three online framed-field experiments (Harrison & List, 2004) in an equity-crowdfunding setting, using a randomized between-subjects design; OLS regressions with robust standard errors estimating investment attractiveness, amount invested, and expected investment, plus a U.K. replication.
industry: Equity crowdfunding (early-stage entrepreneurial finance); a single anonymized real crowdfunding venture seeking £350,000 for 20% equity
country: United States (Study 1, MTurk) and United Kingdom (Studies 2 and 3, Prolific; plus a U.K. replication of Study 1)
time_period: Experiments and cues developed in 2019 (before the COVID-19 pandemic)
units: Prospective investors (survey respondents prescreened for prior investment experience)
n_sample: Final analytic samples of 269 (Study 1, from 627 recruited), 140 (Study 2), and 130 (Study 3) respondents
authors: Zunino, Diego; Dushnitsky, Gary; van Praag, Mirjam
theories: Bayesian inference vs. failure aversion; experimental capitalism (Kerr, Nanda, & Rhodes-Kropf, 2014); resource-acquisition theory; skill-and-luck framework (Schumpeter, 1942; Liu & De Rond, 2016)
keywords: entrepreneurial failure; equity crowdfunding; failure aversion; investor evaluation; luck; rational inference; serial entrepreneurs; skill
research_question: How do investors evaluate entrepreneurs who have previously experienced failure, and does information about entrepreneurial skill moderate the negative evaluation of past failure? Specifically, are investors "failure averse" (categorically discounting anyone who failed) or do they make "rational inferences," looking beyond past outcomes to incorporate credible skill cues?
mechanism_summary: The paper builds on a skill-and-luck framework: success requires both skill and luck jointly, whereas failure can arise from lack of skill (mistakes), bad luck (misfortune), or both. Past failure is therefore a *noisy* rather than a *negative* cue of skill. Absent additional information, investors discount failed entrepreneurs (Hypothesis 1). A credible, third-party skill cue (inclusion in Forbes's "30 Under 30" list) resolves the ambiguity about the root cause of failure, allowing Bayesian-like inference of high skill and mitigating the failure discount (Hypothesis 2, supported for investment amount and expected investment). The alternative view that investors are behaviorally "failure averse" and ignore skill cues (Hypothesis 3) finds no support. Study 3 shows that a credible *luck* cue (an E. coli outbreak) does not similarly restore funding, because luck is inherently less diagnostic of skill.
theoretical_contribution: The study challenges the implicit symmetry assumption in the entrepreneurial resource-acquisition literature — that if success signals skill, failure signals its absence — by recasting past failure as a noisy (not negative) cue whose informational value depends on the cues available at the time of investment. It contributes to the crowdfunding literature (crowders make rational inferences rather than being failure averse), foregrounds the under-studied role of misfortune/luck in resource acquisition, and informs the experimental-capitalism literature by identifying a micro-level mechanism (non-aversion to failed founders) that sustains continued entrepreneurial experimentation.
practical_implication: At the seed stage, entrepreneurs may be reluctant to disclose past failures, but the findings show disclosure is not detrimental when paired with an adequate, credible cue of skill. Crowdfunding platform owners have an opportunity to increase transaction volumes by including serial entrepreneurs and introducing mechanisms for reporting credible skill cues.
limitations: The study adopts a binary view of entrepreneurial skill (above/below a threshold) and operationalizes the skill cue with a single proxy (Forbes's "30 Under 30"); moderate-credibility cues may not undo the failure effect. The context is equity crowdfunding with prospective (not always actual) investors, and the experiments focus on two facets — past outcomes and skill cues — among many that matter to serial entrepreneurs.
future_research: Future work could examine whether the interplay between previous-outcome cues and skill cues decays as a venture matures and clearer performance cues emerge; study other sources of negative sentiment (e.g., founder gender or race) and whether credible skill cues dissipate them; test generalizability to other investor types (e.g., venture capitalists); and revisit the role of learning and luck, alternative skill-cue credibility levels, and additional facets such as venture longevity or number of entrepreneurial attempts.
abstract: Research has shown that most ventures fail, yet there has been limited work on investors' views of entrepreneurs who have failed in the past. We address this gap and call attention to an innate asymmetry between past failure and success. This asymmetry arises because success requires skill and luck jointly, whereas failure materializes due to either lack of skill (mistakes) or bad luck (misfortune). We ask: Are investors "failure-averse" and discount a failed entrepreneur even in the presence of additional information about entrepreneurial skill? Or do they make "rational inferences" in light of the additional skill information and proceed to fund the new startup? To test whether investors are failure-averse or engage in rational inference, we use experiments in the context of equity crowdfunding. The results suggest that prospective crowdfunding investors rationally integrate informational cues regarding past outcomes and entrepreneurial skill.
apa_citation: Zunino, D., Dushnitsky, G., & van Praag, M. (2022). How do investors evaluate past entrepreneurial failure? Unpacking failure due to lack of skill versus bad luck. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(4), 1083–1109. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.0579

title: A Trojan Horse Inside the Gates? Knowledge Spillovers During Patent Litigation
methods: Longitudinal observational study of an unbalanced firm-therapeutic class-year panel. Conditional fixed-effects (Poisson) count models for novel drug approvals, with firm, therapeutic-class, and year fixed effects. Coarsened exact matching (CEM) addresses selection; two-stage Heckman models and an alternate citation-count DV provide robustness checks.
industry: U.S. pharmaceutical industry (domestic and foreign firms operating in the U.S. market, covered in Pharmaprojects); patents span 14 therapeutic classes
units: Firm-therapeutic class-year observations (accused vs. non-litigated pharmaceutical firms)
n_sample: 22,486 firm-therapeutic-class-year observations for 169 firms (60 with a litigation event, 109 without); >3,000 patent litigation cases; CEM sample 4,705 observations / 359 firms
authors: Awate, Kiran S.; Makhija, Mona V.
theories: appropriability regimes (Teece, 1986; Cohen, Nelson, & Walsh, 2000); organizational and vicarious learning (Hamel, 1991; Makhija & Ganesh, 1997; Posen & Chen, 2013); recombinant search / knowledge recombination view of innovation (Fleming, 2001; Katila & Ahuja, 2002; Fleming & Sorenson, 2004)
keywords: appropriability regime; innovation; knowledge heterogeneity; knowledge recency; knowledge spillovers; organizational learning; patent litigation; patent scope
research_question: Does the knowledge that a patent-holding firm is compelled to disclose during patent infringement litigation spill over to the accused firm and enhance its subsequent novel innovation? And do attributes of the litigated patent's knowledge—its recency, heterogeneity, and scope—strengthen this effect?
mechanism_summary: The argument rests on the recombinant view of innovation: novel inventions arise from recombining knowledge components, but the path to successful recombination is largely tacit and invisible in the patent itself. Litigation's disclosure rules (discovery, depositions, expert testimony) force the patent-holding firm to reveal procedural knowledge, rejected hypotheses, and causal linkages beyond the patent, creating unusually close colocation and proprietary spillovers to an accused firm that—operating in the same technological space—has both the intent and capacity to learn from them and apply them to its own subsequent innovation.
theoretical_contribution: The study reveals an unforeseen, paradoxical consequence of a core appropriability mechanism: patent litigation, whose purpose is to curtail spillovers and protect proprietary knowledge, can itself generate valuable knowledge spillovers that strengthen a competitor. It is the first to demonstrate that litigation-driven spillovers enhance accused firms' novel innovation, and it sheds light on the micro-mechanisms (disclosure of recent, heterogeneous, and broad-scope knowledge) through which such learning occurs, highlighting the "double-edged sword" and the limits of strong appropriability regimes.
practical_implication: Patent-holding firms should recognize that litigation entails a trade-off: defending the most valuable patents requires divulging additional proprietary, often procedural, knowledge that can strengthen rivals, so the litigation decision should weigh this risk of unintended spillover. The findings also raise—without endorsing—the strategic question of whether an accused firm's ability to learn from spillovers could help offset the costs of being litigated.
limitations: The spillover channel is theorized and supported by qualitative interviews and case evidence but is not directly observed in the panel; the analysis holds the technological class constant, so disease-area or baseline-technology differences are not examined; and the study assumes the patent-holding firm spills knowledge unintentionally rather than strategically managing disclosure.
future_research: Future work could examine litigation involving contested patents of both firms (so spillovers accrue to each party), whether litigation outcomes (winning vs. losing) shape learning, and the trajectory of subsequent innovation in litigated versus non-litigated domains. Scholars could also study weaker-appropriability-regime industries, take a longitudinal view of whether patent-holding firms learn to limit spillovers over time, and revisit the assumption that disclosure is unintentional.
abstract: While patent litigation is an important appropriability mechanism for protecting firms’ proprietary knowledge, through the litigation process, valuable knowledge may unintentionally spill over from firms defending their patents to those they accuse of patent infringement. We examine whether such spillover subsequently enhances the innovation of accused firms by analyzing over 3,000 patent litigation cases from 1998 through 2015 in the U.S. pharmaceutical industry. We find that firms accused of infringement have higher levels of innovation following litigation relative to other similar firms. Furthermore, litigation of patents that build on recent and heterogeneous knowledge and are characterized by greater scope more strongly enhance the accused firms’ subsequent innovation. These findings support the argument that patent litigation can facilitate knowledge spillovers.
apa_citation: Awate, K. S., & Makhija, M. V. (2022). A Trojan horse inside the gates? Knowledge spillovers during patent litigation. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(5), 1747–1769. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.1181

title: Surging Underdogs and Slumping Favorites: How Recent Streaks and Future Expectations Drive Competitive Transgressions
methods: Multi-method, four studies. Study 1: field survey of fantasy football managers, OLS regression with interaction/simple-slopes. Study 2: 18 seasons of archival English Premier League data, Poisson regression with team/year/week fixed effects and clustered SEs. Studies 3-4: preregistered 2x2 (winning vs. losing streak x underdog vs. favorite) between-participants vignette/behavioral experiments analyzed with two-way ANOVA and PROCESS moderated/serial mediation (bootstrapped CIs).
industry: Fantasy football leagues (NFL), professional soccer (English Premier League), public-defender trial law, and a partisan trivia competition
units: Individual competitors (managers, defenders, partisans); team-game observations in Study 2
n_sample: Study 1: 1,059 fantasy football managers; Study 2: 12,694 team-game observations (6,840 contests over 18 seasons); Study 3: 332 public defenders; Study 4: 372 working adults (Democrats/Republicans)
authors: Doyle, Sarah P.; Pettit, Nathan C.; Kim, Sijun; To, Christopher; Lount, Robert B., Jr.
theories: event segmentation theory (EST); mental time travel; psychological momentum
keywords: anxiety; competitive transgressions; excitement; mental time travel; outcome uncertainty; underdog versus favorite standing; winning and losing streaks
research_question: How do an actor's recent performance trajectory (winning vs. losing streak) and future performance expectations (underdog vs. favorite standing) jointly shape the cognitive and affective reactions that drive competitive transgressions? The paper asks when and why competitors who are embedded in a sequence of related contests are willing to depart from accepted rules of conduct.
mechanism_summary: The paper integrates research on underdogs/favorites with work on winning/losing streaks through a temporally embedded model grounded in mental time travel and event segmentation theory (EST). Streaks create continuous "cognitive dots" that let competitors mentally traverse from past to projected future, generating psychological momentum. When these streak-based projections clash with one's standing (a winning streak as an underdog, or a losing streak as a favorite), the mismatch raises outcome uncertainty. This uncertainty makes alternative futures vivid and elicits high-arousal emotions — excitement for underdogs anticipating an unexpected win, anxiety for favorites fearing an unexpected loss — which crowd out moral considerations and precipitate transgressions.
theoretical_contribution: The paper develops a temporally embedded model of competition that connects past competitive outcomes and future expectations, which prior work largely studied in isolation, showing that any single contest is encoded within a larger "event schemata." It deepens understanding of underdog/favorite psychology by shifting attention from motivation to cognitive (outcome uncertainty) and affective (excitement, anxiety) mechanisms, and identifies two previously undocumented competitive conditions — favorites on losing streaks and underdogs on winning streaks — that generate enough uncertainty to trigger transgressive behavior.
practical_implication: Managers should be cautious about introducing or sustaining workplace competitions, because under certain configurations of past performance and standing their deleterious effects can outweigh motivational benefits. Common exhortations to "put the past behind them" or "not get ahead of ourselves" are not equally useful in all situations; managers should attend to the interplay between an actor's competitive past and future expectations, intervening especially when situations arouse uncertainty, excitement, or anxiety.
limitations: Study 1 is subject to common-method bias because all variables were assessed by the same rater at the same time. The theory operationalizes competitive standing as binary (one underdog, one favorite), which does not capture competitions with many players or varying degrees of underdog/favorite status. The focus on streaks as the sole element of the competitive past does not fully represent the broader claim that "the past matters," since non-coherent past outcomes may not shape mental projections in the same way.
future_research: Future work should move beyond the binary conceptualization of standing to examine whether the psychology and behavior of underdogs and favorites differ by the magnitude of standing or the number of competitors. Researchers could explore other factors beyond streaks — such as rivalry-based wins/losses or carryover from prior seasons — that contribute to momentum and mental projection. Scholars should also study conditions that promote good (rather than bad) competitive behavior, and collect multi-wave, multi-source data to address common-method concerns.
abstract: Any single competition is rarely a "one-off" event and instead is often part of a larger sequence of related competitions. Thus, we contend that in order to better understand people's competitive experiences, we must take a more holistic view, where their experiences and behaviors in the present are a function of their past and expected future outcomes. This research expands the temporal lens of competition by examining how past outcomes (i.e., winning vs. losing streak) and future expectations (i.e., underdog vs. favorite standing) collectively influence an actor's cognitive and affective reactions to a competition, with implications for their willingness to transgress. Studies 1 (fantasy football managers) and 2 (the English Premiere League teams) show that streaks and underdog vs. favorite standing interact to predict competitive transgressions: winning streaks increase transgressions for underdogs, and losing streaks increase transgressions for favorites. Studies 3 (public defenders) and 4 (Democrats and Republicans) experimentally manipulate streaks and standing and unpack the cognitive (i.e., outcome uncertainty) and affective (i.e., excitement for underdogs, and anxiety for favorites) mechanisms that precipitate these transgressions. Theoretical implications for the competition literature, as well as managerial insights, are discussed.
apa_citation: Doyle, S. P., Pettit, N. C., Kim, S., To, C., & Lount, R. B., Jr. (2022). Surging underdogs and slumping favorites: How recent streaks and future expectations drive competitive transgressions. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(5), 1507-1540. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1008

title: From the Editors—That’s Important, Interesting, and Generative: Winners of the AMJ 2021 Best Paper Award and 2022 Research Impact Award
methods: Editorial (From the Editors). Reflective essay built around interviews with the authors of two award-winning AMJ articles, distilling themes about how important, interesting, and generative management research is conceived and conducted.
industry: Not reported in paper
units: Not reported in paper
n_sample: Not reported in paper
authors: Howard-Grenville, Jennifer; Vasudeva, Gurneeta; Yiu, Daphne W.
theories: Not reported in paper
keywords: generative research; grand challenges; important research; interesting research; societal relevance
research_question: Not reported in paper
mechanism_summary:
- Core argument: Important and interesting research need not be trade-offs; problem- or phenomenon-driven curiosity can motivate and sustain research that is both societally relevant and theoretically rigorous.
- Motivation: Each award-winning paper started a new conversation by asking fundamentally new questions about an understudied phenomenon (religiosity and the gender wage gap; basic science on diseases of the poor) rather than joining a mature theoretical conversation.
- Process: A surprising main effect pushed the authors toward greater methodological rigor, transparency, and theory building, often using extensive international data.
- Counterintuitive commonality: Both studies illuminated universal principles and global commonalities (rather than differences), making global sampling powerful for theoretical and empirical contribution.
- Generativity: Publication is the beginning, not the end, of a generative, cocreated line of inquiry sustained by authors, audiences, editors, and reviewers.

The editorial distills, in the authors' own words, how curiosity about important yet understudied phenomena, combined with global data and methodological transparency, can produce research that is generative of further impact and study.
theoretical_contribution: As a From-the-Editors essay, the piece does not advance a formal theory but articulates a programmatic stance for AMJ: that "phenomenon-driven, theoretically novel, rigorous, and societally important" research can connect management to grand challenges and societal conversations without sacrificing theoretical and empirical rigor.
practical_implication: The editors encourage management scholars to be confident and courageous in pursuing problem-driven questions of societal importance, to leverage multidisciplinary methods and global data (noting about 48% of recent AMJ studies use non-U.S. samples for theory testing), and to trust that editors and reviewers will support bold, generative work.
limitations: Not reported in paper
future_research: The editors invite authors to look for and generate data from contexts that might reveal surprising commonalities and differences, and to pursue generative research that prompts and propels further novel and important inquiry on societally relevant topics.
abstract: Not reported in paper
apa_citation: Howard-Grenville, J., Vasudeva, G., & Yiu, D. W. (2022). From the editors—That's important, interesting, and generative: Winners of the AMJ 2021 Best Paper Award and 2022 Research Impact Award. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(5), 1417–1423. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2022.4005

title: Enabling Rapid Financial Response to Disasters: Knotting and Reknotting Multiple Paradoxes in Interorganizational Systems
methods: Longitudinal global qualitative (multi-sited ethnographic) study of three multi-country disaster-liquidity risk pools; 135 interviews with 160 individuals, 31 field-configuring-event observations, and 285 documents; inductive NVivo coding iterated with paradox theory.
industry: Disaster-liquidity insurance delivered through multi-country risk-pooling schemes (pools), spanning member governments, network administrative organizations, development/humanitarian actors, and global reinsurance market actors
country: Multi-country: participants across Latin America, the Caribbean, Africa, the South Pacific, the United Kingdom, Europe, and the United States; insured members are low-income countries
time_period: Data collected 2016-2020 (retrospective coverage of pre-2016 processes); pools span 2008-2020
units: Three main multi-country risk pools (interorganizational systems) and their constituent actors
n_sample: 135 audiotaped interviews with 160 individuals; 31 observed field-configuring events; 285 documents
authors: Jarzabkowski, Paula; Bednarek, Rebecca; Chalkias, Konstantinos; Cacciatori, Eugenia
theories: dynamic equilibrium (Smith & Lewis, 2011); paradox knots (Sheep, Fairhurst, & Khazanchi, 2017); paradox theory (Smith & Lewis, 2011; Schad et al., 2016)
keywords: disaster response; dynamic equilibrium; grand challenges; interorganizational systems; market-based solutions; paradox; paradox knotting
research_question: How does the knotting of multiple paradoxes enable, or undermine, dynamic equilibrium within interorganizational systems? The authors ask how coexisting paradoxes are entangled, shape each other, and are managed by actors interacting within an interorganizational system.
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: three knotted paradoxes — market–development (Smith & Besharov, 2019), part–whole (Poole & Van de Ven, 1989), and temporal short–long-term (Slawinski & Bansal, 2015) — co-occurring as an inseparably entangled "paradox knot."
- Process / phases: an iterative cycle between equilibrium (paradoxes balanced and knotted) and disequilibrium (knot unravels), interrupted by "critical incidents" (annual renewals; disasters).
- Knotting practices: four interactions (training on the solution, reviewing/refining the solution, collaborating across stakeholders, communicating the solution) that maintain mutually reinforcing balance.
- Triggering conditions: "clustering" of the short-term and part poles from two different paradoxes, which de-emphasizes the long-term and whole poles and unravels the knot, generating disequilibrium.
- Reknotting practices: six interactions binding the market pole, and separately the development pole, to the clustered short-term and part poles (e.g., running scenarios, developing new products, funding countries' projects, working around product triggers).
- Emergent outcomes: reknotting indirectly restores balance across all three paradoxes, reinstates equilibrium, and strengthens the interorganizational solution over time.

The central process is that organizational actors knot multiple paradoxes into a mutually reinforcing balance to keep the interorganizational system viable; when a critical incident causes specific poles to cluster, the knot unravels into disequilibrium; rather than reasserting the neglected pole, actors reknot the dominant poles to a third paradox's poles, which indirectly rebalances the original paradoxes. Iterating between knotting and reknotting is a generative dynamic that not only restores equilibrium but refines and embeds new initiatives that strengthen the solution.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends the concept of paradox knots (Sheep et al., 2017) from discursive framing to the interactional realm by specifying the social-interaction processes through which knotting and reknotting occur. It introduces "clustering" of individual poles as an explanatory mechanism for how paradox knots unravel into disequilibrium, and theorizes "reknotting across specific poles" as a fundamentally new response to paradox (distinct from reasserting the suppressed pole). It further characterizes disequilibrium concretely and positions the iterative cycle of equilibrium–disequilibrium as a generative dynamic for sustaining interorganizational systems addressing grand challenges.
practical_implication: The study shows that governments of different countries can work together, and with development organizations, to find disaster-response solutions that satisfy both their own partial interests and wider regional ones. Inevitable tensions and lapses into disequilibrium need not destroy such interorganizational solutions; rather, solutions can be incrementally refined and strengthened through constant interactions to knot and reknot paradoxes as actors learn more about the challenges and how their shared purpose can evolve.
limitations: The longitudinal study, based on intensive personal engagement, documented the dynamics of paradox knotting and disequilibrium only over the relatively short period of the authors' engagement. Not all evolutionary paths may be as benign as those observed, and the study examined a context with three specific entangled paradoxes (market–development, part–whole, temporal), so findings may not generalize to other configurations of paradox multiplicity.
future_research: Future research might examine whether the specific clustering of short-term and partial interests is a feature of interorganizational systems where actors can privilege immediate over collective long-term interests, and study paradox knotting within varied types of interorganizational systems (e.g., networks around a central organization, project-based alliances, supply chains) that may exhibit different dynamics. Longer-term historical studies could reveal how these dynamics expand, shift, or reverse, and how variation in paradox multiplicity shapes knotting and its relationship with (dis)equilibrium, including within (not only across) organizations.
abstract: While market-based solutions are increasingly being proposed to address major societal and development issues, they are also often considered antithetical to issues such as climate change, poverty alleviation, and disaster response. In particular, the interorganizational systems involved in such market solutions give rise to multiple contradictory tensions, known as paradoxes. We, therefore, adopt a paradox lens to explain the dynamics through which different actors within these systems navigate the contradictions that are generated. Drawing on a global qualitative study of multi-country risk pools that provide rapid capital in the immediate aftermath of a disaster, we advance paradox theory by showing how organizational actors' interactions (a) maintain equilibrium by generating mutually reinforcing balance among paradoxes, while (b) the clustering of poles from different paradoxes generates disequilibrium, and (c) the reknotting of poles from different paradoxes restores equilibrium. As our process framework shows, these dynamics form an iterative cycle between equilibrium and disequilibrium that is essential in enhancing the promise of market-based solutions to address development issues, which in our study refers to increasing the rapid availability of capital to respond to disasters such as hurricanes, earthquakes, and droughts.
apa_citation: Jarzabkowski, P., Bednarek, R., Chalkias, K., & Cacciatori, E. (2022). Enabling rapid financial response to disasters: Knotting and reknotting multiple paradoxes in interorganizational systems. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(5), 1477–1506. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0745

title: When Do Outside CEOs Underperform? From a CEO-Centric to a Stakeholder-Centric Perspective of Post-Succession Performance
methods: Archival panel study of CEO appointments; two-stage Heckman-style selection (first-stage probit predicting succession) with second-stage generalized least squares (GLS) regression, clustered robust standard errors at the firm level, year fixed effects, and inverse Mills ratio control; Python-based textual sentiment analysis of press coverage (Harvard IV-4 dictionary) to construct the negative sentiment measure.
industry: S&P 500 and S&P 400 MidCap publicly traded firms spanning 55 two-digit SIC industries
units: CEO appointment events (882 firms; 318 outside-CEO and 957 inside-CEO appointments)
n_sample: 1,275 CEO appointments
authors: Keil, Thomas; Lavie, Dovev; Pavicevic, Stevo
theories: experiential learning theory (Kolb, 1984; Quinones, Ford, & Teachout, 1995); negative transfer learning (Ellis, 1965; Dokko, Wilk, & Rothbard, 2009); social cognition / negativity bias (Fiske & Taylor, 1991; Rozin & Royzman, 2001); stakeholder theory (Dorobantu, Henisz, & Nartey, 2017)
keywords: CEO succession; corporate misfit; executive experience; negative transfer learning; outside CEO; post-succession performance; stakeholder sentiment
research_question: Why do outside CEOs underperform relative to inside CEOs, and what drives the substantial heterogeneity in firm performance following CEO appointments? The paper asks whether established CEO-centric explanations (executive experience, corporate fit) or a newly proposed stakeholder-centric explanation (stakeholders' negative sentiment toward the appointment) better account for post-succession performance differences.
mechanism_summary: The authors juxtapose three theoretical mechanisms. From experiential learning theory, longer and broader executive experience should improve decision-making, but inside CEOs leverage familiarity and social embeddedness to convert experience into performance more effectively than outsiders. From negative transfer learning, corporate misfit leads CEOs to misapply "recipes" from dissimilar firms, harming performance—more so for outsiders who cannot diagnose contextual differences. From social cognition (negativity bias), stakeholders' negative sentiment toward an appointment escalates from scrutiny to withdrawn support, organizational resistance, and reputational damage, depressing performance—again more acutely for outsiders who lack embedded ties. Empirically, experience effects are null, while corporate misfit and negative sentiment significantly reduce performance and are amplified for outside CEOs.
theoretical_contribution: The study's primary contribution is introducing a stakeholder-centric theoretical perspective to CEO succession—conceptualizing stakeholders' negative sentiment as a socio-cognitive bias that is a previously overlooked yet powerful driver of post-succession performance and of inside-versus-outside performance differences. Its secondary contribution extends CEO-centric perspectives by importing negative transfer learning into the succession domain to theorize "corporate misfit" between a CEO's background and the firm's characteristics, showing misfit harms all CEOs but more so outsiders. Together, juxtaposing multiple lenses reveals that experience—long the dominant explanation—does not explain performance heterogeneity, whereas misfit and sentiment do.
practical_implication: Boards are advised that appointing an outside CEO can pay off regardless of the length or breadth of the candidate's experience, as long as the outsider's corporate background fits the firm's industry, age, and size and stakeholders' negative sentiment is mitigated. Boards should treat the succession process and stakeholder reactions as mattering at least as much as selection criteria, proactively managing stakeholder integration and sentiment for incoming CEOs.
limitations: Reliance on archival data prevented observing the actual post-appointment decisions CEOs make, and the keyword-based sentiment measure could not isolate when sentiment was tied to objective CEO skills (though experience and fit were controlled). The inferred negative-sentiment measure captures sentiment level but not its origin, and the methods could not trace the temporal dynamics of how sentiment evolves after appointment.
future_research: Future work could use surveys to identify the origins of negative sentiment and qualitative methods to study its temporal dynamics and the proposed micro-mechanisms (scrutiny, deprived support, resistance, reputational damage). Scholars might examine counteractions (e.g., image management, public relations) that CEOs and boards can take to mitigate sentiment, identify additional boundary conditions, use task-based rather than time-based experience measures, and extend the experience/misfit logic to other TMT members.
abstract: How does the appointment of an outside CEO affect the hiring firm's performance? Prior research has reported that outside CEOs tend to underperform compared to inside CEOs, with high performance variance. Extending CEO-centric perspectives, we predict that experiential learning enhances post-succession performance, while negative transfer learning undermines it. We then offer a novel, stakeholder-centric theoretical perspective, conjecturing that stakeholders' negative sentiment toward the CEO appointment undermines post-succession performance. We further conjecture that outside CEOs are less effective in leveraging their executive experience and suffer more from negative transfer and negative sentiment when compared to inside CEOs, who can leverage their familiarity and social embeddedness in the firm, which explains why outside CEOs may underperform. Analyzing the appointments of CEOs in U.S. public firms, we find that counter to expectations, the length and breadth of their executive experience do not explain either post-succession performance or the performance differences between outside CEOs and inside CEOs. Rather, the misfit between CEOs' corporate backgrounds and their firms' characteristics and the negative sentiment surrounding their appointments explain performance differences and the underperformance of outside CEOs. Accordingly, our study directs attention to the important yet previously understudied reactions of stakeholders to CEO appointments.
apa_citation: Keil, T., Lavie, D., & Pavicevic, S. (2022). When do outside CEOs underperform? From a CEO-centric to a stakeholder-centric perspective of post-succession performance. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(5), 1424–1449. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.1087

title: Going Viral or Growing Like an Oak Tree? Towards Sustainable Local Development Through Entrepreneurship
methods: Inductive, processual ethnography of two entrepreneurship-nurturing organizations in Detroit (22 months of fieldwork, July 2012–April 2014; 148 meetings/events observed, 67 interviews) plus six years of archival data (620 news articles, social media, follow-up interviews) tracing growth and contributions of all 27 incubated ventures; two-stage analysis building a process model linking resourcing mode, spatiotemporal scale of growth, and local contributions.
industry: Early-stage entrepreneurial ventures developed in a business accelerator (ACCEL) and an alternative business incubator (GREEN); sectors include software, e-commerce, solar energy, food, video production, and waste-tire repurposing
time_period: Fieldwork July 2012–April 2014; venture growth traced through ~2020 (archival data collected until June 2020)
units: Nascent ventures (and the two nurturing organizations that incubated them)
n_sample: 4 ventures studied in depth via ethnography; 27 ventures total tracked via archival data
authors: Kim, Suntae; Kim, Anna
theories: entrepreneurial bricolage (Baker & Nelson, 2005; Di Domenico, Haugh, & Tracey, 2010); entrepreneurial resourcing (Feldman, 2004; Clough, Fang, Vissa, & Wu, 2019); process theory of organizing (Langley & Tsoukas, 2010); time–space / scale lens (Bansal, Kim, & Wood, 2018)
keywords: entrepreneurial bricolage; entrepreneurial resourcing; local bricolage; scaling deep; scaling up; sustainable local development; time–space lens; venture growth
research_question: How are different resourcing modes and the scale of venture growth related to the sustainable development of impoverished locales? The study problematizes the prevalent assumption that venture-capital-backed, high-growth entrepreneurship is the most viable path to poverty alleviation, asking what it is about ventures that "deviate from the successful template" on resourcing and scale that makes them more promising in economically challenged places.
mechanism_summary:
- Modes of resourcing (the primary driver): *Financing* (securing external venture capital/equity) at ACCEL vs. *local bricolage* (making do by recombining locally available resources) at GREEN.
- Spatiotemporal scale of venture growth (the focal construct): *Scaling up* — "the process whereby an organization pursues fast expansion" (quick, broad geographic coverage) vs. *scaling deep* — "a process whereby an organization pursues enduring growth anchored to its original location" (slow, locally rooted).
- Process / phases (idea-development ethnography): Open coding → identifying "time–space idea codes" → analyzing key interaction scenes that infuse spatiotemporal orientations → tracing actual venture growth and contributions via archival data.
- Triggering conditions: At ACCEL the imperative to win investor funding bred a "pressure for speed" (the time value of money / "speed trap"), which translated into a "spatial mandate for expansion"; at GREEN the focus on repurposing local assets deepened ventures' spatial anchors to Detroit and extended their temporal horizon.
- Emergent outcomes (contributions): Scaling-up ventures produced explosive but impermanent local impact (jobs and value that left Detroit through relocation/acquisition); scaling-deep ventures produced enduring, locally customized contributions to Detroit's economic, social, and environmental problems, with complementarity between the two types.

The paper theorizes a self-reinforcing coupling between resourcing and scale: financing places a venture in the rigid timeframe of venture capital, where speed begets a mandate for geographic expansion and pivoting toward universal, readily exploitable demands, which in turn requires still more financing—locking the venture onto a scaling-up trajectory. Local bricolage, by contrast, confines a venture's spatial scope to a particular place and requires slow accumulation of social capital and deep relationships, locking it onto a scaling-deep trajectory whose growth is "inextricably tied to the region's restoration." Because sustainable local development requires contributions that stay local across generations, the authors argue that vigorous revitalization requires the cohabitation of ventures growing at diverse spatiotemporal scales.
theoretical_contribution: The study's central innovation is to bring the time–space (scale) lens into entrepreneurship, reconceptualizing scaling not as rapid growth in size but as growth that "unfolds at a particular spatiotemporal scale," thereby distinguishing scaling up from a previously undertheorized scaling deep. It extends entrepreneurial bricolage from the individual to the place-based community level ("local bricolage"), showing bricolage is a complementary alternative—not a marginal substitute—to venture capital, and it advances venture-growth research by foregrounding "how" (not just "how much") ventures grow. It also surfaces an inward, identity-based microfoundation of bricolage: founders' deep reflection on their own "deep root" enables them to perceive what local resources can be repurposed.
practical_implication: The findings caution policymakers and ecosystem builders that the prevalent focus on infusing venture capital can obscure a more important need—nurturing entrepreneurial imagination to unlock the latent value of existing local assets in seemingly "barren" landscapes. Scaling-deep ventures can do the critical work of lifting impoverished locales toward self-reliance and can capture and redistribute wealth created by scaling-up ventures, so revitalization strategies should support both types of ventures rather than betting exclusively on high-growth "gazelles."
limitations: The authors acknowledge it is reasonable to ask whether the scaling-up dynamics they observe stem from the peculiarity of venture capital specifically, rather than from financing as a general mode of resourcing (they argue financing's risk-return logic generally intensifies pressure for quick growth). To preserve focus, they also chose not to examine other salient patterns in the data—most notably the dynamics between incubating contexts and participating entrepreneurs (the relative roles of treatment vs. selection effects).
future_research: The authors call for research into alternative venture-financing vehicles—such as "patient capital" and "slow money"—and how they shape invested ventures' scale of growth and local impact; into how individual entrepreneurs' varied responses to incubating-context influence imprint different growth patterns; and into how ventures growing at different scales build connections and create synergies, and what kinds of local ecosystems facilitate such complementary collaborations.
abstract: Nurturing venture capital–backed, high-growth entrepreneurship has been strongly promoted as an effective means to achieve local development in impoverished places. Yet, growing evidence has suggested that, despite its notable successes in resource-rich regions, this approach creates limited impact in economically challenged locales. We address this conundrum by calling into question the crux of high-growth entrepreneurship—the pursuit of quick scaling through venture capital financing. Our field research in two entrepreneurship-nurturing organizations in Detroit reveals important heterogeneity in resourcing modes and venture growth in time and space. Specifically, we find that ventures developed through different modes of resourcing (financing vs. local bricolage) grow at different spatiotemporal scales (scaling up toward fast geographical expansion vs. scaling deep toward locally anchored endurance), and consequently generate distinctive yet complementary contributions to their depleted place of origin. Unlike scaling-up ventures whose local impact was explosive yet short-lived, scaling-deep ventures created jobs, products or services, and spillover effects that stayed local and addressed specific local problems. Building on these findings, we challenge the exclusive pursuit of high-growth entrepreneurship for poverty alleviation and suggest that entrepreneurship-driven local development requires cohabitation of ventures growing at varying scales.
apa_citation: Kim, S., & Kim, A. (2022). Going viral or growing like an oak tree? Towards sustainable local development through entrepreneurship. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(5), 1709–1746. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.0041

title: Overcoming Conflict Between Symmetric Occupations: How “Creatives” and “Suits” Use Gender Ordering in Advertising
methods: Inductive, interpretive multi-sited ethnography pooling two independent projects: 16 months of participant observation at five U.S. advertising agencies plus 116 semi-structured interviews; iterative inductive coding of 113 cross-occupational conflict instances by cross-gender type, conflict type, management practice, and which occupation acquiesced.
industry: Advertising (U.S. advertising agencies); focal occupations are creatives (copywriters, designers, art/creative directors) and account practitioners (“suits”)
time_period: Fieldwork 2011–2017; interview data collection years 2012–2014 (Project 1) and 2013–2017 (Project 2)
units: Cross-occupational creative–account dyads and conflict episodes within agencies
n_sample: 116 interviews and participant observation at 5 agencies; 113 coded conflict instances
authors: Koppman, Sharon; Bechky, Beth A.; Cohen, Andrew C.
theories: gender status beliefs / expectation states (Ridgeway, 2011); gendered organizations (Acker, 1990; West & Zimmerman, 1987); theory of professions and jurisdictional conflict (Abbott, 1988)
keywords: essentialist gender roles and stereotypes; gender ordering; horizontal occupational gender segregation; jurisdictional conflict; scut work; symmetric occupations
research_question: How do members of horizontally gender-segregated, symmetric (same-level) occupations manage jurisdictional conflict when relative rank is ambiguous and no formal hierarchy orders them? Specifically, how do female-dominated account practitioners and male-dominated creatives in U.S. advertising agencies overcome occupational conflict?
mechanism_summary:
- Key constructs / tensions: "gender ordering" (enacting essentialist gender roles/stereotypes that import an informal gender status hierarchy into cross-occupational relationships); jurisdictional conflict over legitimacy, authority, and knowledge claims between symmetric occupations; "scut work" (low-status, menial, emotionally taxing work that does not draw on occupational knowledge).
- Sites of conflict: agency–client interactions (legitimacy), revisions (authority), and concept development (knowledge).
- Practices (female account–male creative pairs): protecting (mediating, buffering) to resolve legitimacy conflict; nurturing (conceptual, physical) to resolve authority conflict; counseling/preparing to resolve knowledge conflict.
- Comparison conditions: minority cross-gender pairs (male account–female creatives) use gender ordering via "salesman"/"men are good at math" framings but without performing scut work; same-gender pairs use "occupational entrenchment" (dominance or reasoning), which usually fails to resolve conflict.
- Emergent outcome: creatives acquiesce; women secure cooperation but cede status.

Through gender ordering, cross-gender pairs convert competition between similarly ranked occupations into a hierarchical relationship by enacting taken-for-granted gender status beliefs (Ridgeway, 2011), so women cede status to men in exchange for cooperation. Because gender carries shared expectations agreed upon by both groups, it supplies an alternative informal hierarchy that resolves disagreements over whose work is more valuable, who may direct work, and whose knowledge is most relevant. For women, however, eliciting this cooperation requires performing emotionally taxing scut work (mothering, buffering, bringing food) that men in the same occupation do not perform, reproducing the gender hierarchy within the everyday functioning of the organization.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends the literature on managing occupational conflict beyond asymmetric occupations to show how symmetric, horizontally gender-segregated occupations resolve jurisdictional conflict through gender ordering. It reconceptualizes the role of demography in occupational conflict: whereas race-, nationality-, or age-based differences aligned with occupational differences exacerbate conflict, shared status beliefs about gender can instead facilitate cross-occupational cooperation. It also identifies cross-occupational relationships as a previously unrecognized source of women’s disadvantage, since gender ordering embeds the gender hierarchy through women’s scut work.
practical_implication: The findings surface a hidden cost borne by women in mixed-gender, interdependent professional work: securing cooperation often requires low-status, emotionally taxing scut work that does not advance their occupational claims or status. Organizations and managers should recognize that informal "getting along" practices that keep work moving can simultaneously entrench women’s disadvantage, and should attend to how coordination burdens are distributed across gender-segregated occupations.
limitations: Most coded examples are drawn from interviews rather than direct observation of paired interactions, and the study lacks dyadic-level data with both members of each dyad, limiting triangulation of situations and dynamics. The studied occupations are uniformly white, college educated, and U.S. born, which constrains the ability to observe demographic similarity-based mechanisms and limits generalizability.
future_research: Future work should examine gender ordering in other symmetric, gender-segregated occupational contexts (e.g., engineering, architecture, television) to assess how it operates elsewhere, and should ideally collect dyadic-level data with interviews from both members of a dyad to triangulate descriptions of situations and dynamics.
abstract: In knowledge-based organizations, conflict among interdependent occupations can be exacerbated by the absence of a clear hierarchical ordering of these occupations within the organization. Moreover, given women’s inroads into some traditionally maledominated occupations but not others, these workplaces are increasingly horizontally gender segregated. In this paper, we study how members of these symmetric and segregated occupations manage conflict in U.S. advertising agencies through the case of relationships between “creatives” (copywriters, designers, and creative directors) and “suits” or account practitioners (account executives, strategists, and managers). Creatives and suits are at the same organizational level in their agencies. While creatives are primarily men, suits, traditionally also men, are now primarily women. Drawing on participant observation in five different U.S. advertising agencies and over 100 interviews, we show how creatives and account practitioners use gender ordering to overcome jurisdictional conflict. These practices are grounded in enacting essentialist gender differences that transform symmetric occupational relationships into hierarchical ones by embedding the gender hierarchy. We find that, while gender ordering helps women and men in cross-occupational pairs get work done, it also reinforces women’s disadvantage, because, for women, it involves low-status and emotionally taxing scut work that it does not involve for men.
apa_citation: Koppman, S., Bechky, B. A., & Cohen, A. C. (2022). Overcoming conflict between symmetric occupations: How “creatives” and “suits” use gender ordering in advertising. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(5), 1623–1651. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0806

title: The Highs and Lows of Hierarchy in Multiteam Systems
methods: Experimental laboratory study (hierarchical vs. egalitarian manipulation via the LDX simulation), longitudinal over three multiteam-system performance episodes (T1-T3). Multilevel path analysis with full-information maximum likelihood in Mplus 7; mediation and serial mediation via Monte Carlo simulations (20,000 replications); index of moderated mediation; pairwise comparisons in Stata 13.1.
industry: Laboratory simulation (Leadership Development Exercise, a military-like target-detection game) staffed by undergraduate students fulfilling business-course requirements
time_period: Cohort research design spanning five years; each multiteam system observed over three performance episodes (T1, T2, T3)
units: Multiteam systems (each composed of three component teams; phenomena aggregated to the multiteam-system level)
n_sample: 76 multiteam systems (n = 1,060 individuals; 228 component teams); 224 usable multiteam-system performance episodes
authors: Matusik, James G.; Mitchell, Rebecca L.; Hays, Nicholas A.; Fath, Sean; Hollenbeck, John R.
theories: compensatory control and the psychological advantages of hierarchy (Friesen et al., 2014); ego/cognitive depletion theory (Hagger et al., 2010); functions and dysfunctions of hierarchy in groups (Anderson & Brown, 2010; Greer et al., 2018); multiteam system theory / linkage attributes (Zaccaro, Marks, & DeChurch, 2012); organizational routines (Feldman, 2000; Feldman & Pentland, 2003)
keywords: cognitive depletion; egalitarianism; hierarchical arrangement; hierarchy; horizontal coordination; multiteam system learning; multiteam systems; performance episodes
research_question: How, why, and when do different hierarchical arrangements (hierarchy vs. egalitarianism) affect multiteam system operations? The paper asks whether hierarchy is simply beneficial or detrimental, or whether it simultaneously facilitates and impedes multiteam system success through distinct mechanisms (horizontal coordination and member cognitive depletion) that evolve over performance episodes.
mechanism_summary: Hierarchy elicits competition, power struggles, and conflict, leading component teams to focus resources and information inward, which lowers horizontal coordination and thereby impedes learning (a coordination "barrier"). Simultaneously, hierarchy provides structure, order, predictability, and cognitive fluency, reducing the mental effort of navigating the complex multiteam-system environment, which lowers cognitive depletion and thereby facilitates learning (a cognitive "boon"). Because routines become self-reinforcing while members build schemas and mental models over time, the coordination disadvantage of hierarchy persists across episodes whereas its cognitive-depletion advantage wanes once members no longer need hierarchy as a cognitive "crutch."
theoretical_contribution: The study qualifies the dominant assumption in the multiteam-system literature that hierarchy (a strong, centralized leadership team) is superior, by importing the intra-team insight that hierarchy is both functional and dysfunctional and showing it operates at the inter-team level. It elucidates *why* hierarchy matters—egalitarianism aids horizontal coordination while hierarchy attenuates cognitive depletion—and, through a longitudinal design, *when* these opposing effects hold, demonstrating that the cognitive benefit decays while the coordination cost endures over performance episodes.
practical_implication: Because organizations are gravitating toward interdependent systems of teams and more egalitarian structures, managers should match the hierarchical arrangement to task demands: egalitarian designs may be preferable when component-team process interdependence is high (e.g., obstetric labor-and-delivery systems), even if initially more depleting, whereas the cognitive benefits of hierarchy may outweigh egalitarianism's coordination advantage when interdependence is lower (pooled or sequential) or teams are temporally limited (e.g., ad hoc teams).
limitations: The authors examined only power-based hierarchies, not status hierarchies, so generalization to status arrangements is untested. The research used undergraduate students in a laboratory military-like simulation, raising external-validity concerns, and cognitive depletion was measured before (not during) task execution. The multiteam systems also had relatively stable membership and tasks with multi-week gaps between episodes, which may not represent more fluid or back-to-back real-world multiteam systems.
future_research: Future work should investigate status hierarchies in multiteam systems to test whether the present theorizing generalizes beyond power. Researchers should also manipulate the level of task interdependence among component teams—rarely varied despite its theoretical importance—and study multiteam systems with more fluid membership, more variable tasks, and shorter (e.g., back-to-back) intervals between performance episodes to assess boundary conditions on the coordination and cognitive-depletion effects.
abstract: Multiteam system research operates under the assumption that multiteam systems should be structured hierarchically, or consist of component teams nested under a formal, centralized leadership team that oversees and orchestrates system activities. Although multiteam system research has certainly provided evidence suggestive of the merits of more hierarchical (vs. egalitarian) structures, we take a more balanced view and argue that hierarchy both facilitates and impedes multiteam system success. Using an experimental study and a sample of 76 multiteam systems observed over three performance episodes, we provide evidence that structural hierarchy (a) facilitates multiteam system learning by mitigating cognitive depletion among members, but (b) undermines multiteam system learning via its deleterious effects on horizontal coordination. Critically, however, we find that the benefits of hierarchy (i.e., reduced cognitive depletion) wane over performance episodes. This investigation underscores the important role that different hierarchical arrangements play in multiteam system functioning. Although a majority of multiteam system researchers have utilized hierarchical structures—which indeed have their virtues—we illustrate that egalitarian structures have key advantages as well.
apa_citation: Matusik, J. G., Mitchell, R. L., Hays, N. A., Fath, S., & Hollenbeck, J. R. (2022). The highs and lows of hierarchy in multiteam systems. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(5), 1571–1592. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0369

title: How Leaders with Divergent Visions Generate Novel Strategy: Navigating the Paradox of Preservation and Modernization in Swiss Watchmaking
methods: Qualitative historical case study (nested case study design) of SMH during the Swiss quartz crisis; abductive analysis of 147 semi-structured interviews, 119 archival interviews, archival documents, and field observations, using temporal bracketing to induce a process model.
industry: Swiss watchmaking (SMH / Swatch Group; Blancpain, Omega, Breguet, Swatch brands)
units: Two senior leaders (Nicolas G. Hayek and Jean-Claude Biver) and the leader dyad they formed at SMH
n_sample: 147 semi-structured interviews (plus 92 + 27 archival interviews)
authors: Raffaelli, Ryan; DeJordy, Rich; McDonald, Rory M.
theories: co-leadership / leadership duos (Alvarez & Svejenova, 2005); organizational ambidexterity; paradox theory (Smith & Lewis, 2011; Smith & Tushman, 2005)
keywords: affective trust; leader dyads; novel strategy; preservation–modernization paradox; strategic paradox; technological discontinuity
research_question: How do leaders with divergent visions for their organization come together to create a novel strategy? Specifically, the paper unpacks how and why a leader dyad integrates seemingly inconsistent strategies—preservation versus modernization—to produce a new, generative approach in the wake of a technological discontinuity.
mechanism_summary:
- Triggering condition: a technological discontinuity (the quartz crisis) to which two leaders respond with divergent visions—industrial modernization (Hayek) and craft preservation (Biver).
- Core tension: a "preservation–modernization paradox" surfaced when SMH acquired Blancpain, manifest as organizational inconsistencies in core technologies, economic models, and time orientation of practices.
- Individual practices: redefining personal goals and subjugating egos (willingness to moderate narcissistic/self-absorbed behavior) as precursors to joint engagement.
- Relational exchanges: cultivating affective trust and interacting frequently (daily private phone calls before teams could weigh in).
- Structural boundaries: carving out shared territory (Biver on SMH board / Omega; Hayek on Breguet committee) and synthesizing skills (each acquiring capabilities initially associated with the other).
- Emergent outcome: a sustained paradox that generated a novel "both/and" strategy and ongoing strategic reorientation at SMH.

The authors induce a recursive process model in which two leaders with opposing visions locate the paradox in their *dyadic relationship* rather than in a single leader or the whole top team. Individual-level practices (goal redefinition, ego subjugation) enable relational exchanges (affective trust, frequent interaction), which are enacted through structural boundaries (shared territory, skill synthesis). These mutually reinforcing mechanisms allow the dyad to embrace and sustain contradictory preservation and modernization demands, productively generating new strategy and continual organizational renewal rather than the destructive infighting prior work would predict.
theoretical_contribution: The study relocates the "locus of paradox" from the singular senior leader or top management team (leader-centric and team-centric models) to the *relationship within a leader dyad*, providing an empirical illustration of a previously largely theoretical "relational" conception of paradox. It specifies three mutually reinforcing classes of leader-dyad mechanisms (individual practices, relational exchanges, structural boundaries) and identifies, defines, and elaborates a generative and generalizable category—the preservation–modernization paradox—reframing the strategic question from "preserve or modernize" to "which elements to preserve while modernizing."
practical_implication: Senior teams whose members hold opposing visions can generate novel strategy if leaders redefine personal goals, subjugate their egos, build affective trust through frequent private interaction, and carve out shared territory that lets them swap positions and synthesize skills. Leaders facing a technological discontinuity need not commit to a single either/or response; sustaining the tension between heritage and modernization can itself be profoundly generative.
limitations: As case-based qualitative research, the findings face scope conditions tied to the empirical setting: the preservation perspective stems from Swiss watchmaking's rich history and a craft orientation characteristic of creative and artistic industries, so the paradox may be less salient where tradition is less bound up in the technology itself. The discontinuity studied was technological, and applicability to other forms of change (e.g., radical environmental change) is uncertain. The study period ended in 2001.
future_research: Researchers could seek other antecedent conditions that enable a convergence of visions (e.g., dyadic psychological safety) and probe the interdependence of affective trust and frequent interaction. Promising directions include mapping how leaders with paradoxical visions reshape industries and fields over time, testing the model in settings where tradition is less entangled with the technology, and examining how confronting and sustaining paradox operates when discontinuity is precipitated by radical environmental change (e.g., COVID-19) rather than a new technology.
abstract: How do leaders with divergent visions for their organization come together to create a novel strategy? This paper employs paradox as a lens to investigate how leader dyads can integrate opposing strategies to produce a new, generative approach. Drawing on a qualitative historical case study of Switzerland's largest watch company during the quartz crisis in Swiss watchmaking, we induce a process model from the activities of two leaders whose relationship embodied the tensions and strategic contradictions of preserving the past and modernizing for the future. The model specifies a set of individual, relational, and structural mechanisms by which leaders productively engage with a "preservation–modernization paradox" to facilitate novel strategy in the wake of a discontinuity. We interpret our findings in terms of the demands of navigating the management and outcomes of strategic paradoxes. While tracing the theoretical and practical implications of our model and our findings, we address leadership conundrums characteristic of organizations confronting paradox.
apa_citation: Raffaelli, R., DeJordy, R., & McDonald, R. M. (2022). How leaders with divergent visions generate novel strategy: Navigating the paradox of preservation and modernization in Swiss watchmaking. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(5), 1593–1622. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2018.0764
