title: Different Roots, Different Fruits: Gender-Based Differences in Cultural Narratives about Perceived Discrimination Produce Divergent Psychological Consequences
methods: Five studies of working adults: Study 1 open-ended coded survey; Study 2 preregistered ranking/rating survey (Mann-Whitney U, Wilcoxon, t-tests); Study 3 preregistered vignette experiment (ANOVA with contrast coding); Studies 4a/4b survey replications using OLS regression with interaction terms and Hayes PROCESS model 8 mediation.
industry: Working adults across a wide variety of industries (Western/U.S. context); no single sector targeted
units: Individual working adults recruited via Prolific Academic and a Qualtrics panel
n_sample: Study 1: 414; Study 2: 733; Study 3: 377; Studies 4a/4b: 800 and 738 (combined 1,538)
authors: Tost, Leigh Plunkett; Hardin, Ashley E.; Roberson, Jacob W.; Gino, Francesca
theories: narrative identity theory (Hammack, 2008; McAdams, 2011); role congruity theory (Eagly & Karau, 2002)
keywords: belonging; cultural narratives; gestalt well-being; patriarchal narrative; perceived gender discrimination; reverse gender discrimination; self-efficacy
research_question: Do women and men differ in their understandings of the nature of discrimination against people of their gender, and do these different understandings lead to differences in workplace-related consequences for women and men?
mechanism_summary: The authors argue that women and men appeal to different dominant cultural narratives to make sense of perceived gender discrimination: women interpret it through a patriarchal narrative (derogation of abilities, ascribed domestic roles, sexual objectification), whereas men interpret it through a reverse-gender-discrimination narrative (diversity/affirmative-action efforts that favor women). Because the patriarchal narrative impugns competence while the reverse-discrimination narrative does not, perceived discrimination lowers self-efficacy only among women, while lowering belonging among both genders. These cognitions in turn reduce gestalt well-being, with a stronger (more consistent) effect for women because two mechanisms operate (self-efficacy and belonging) rather than one (belonging).
theoretical_contribution: The paper introduces a theoretical framework explaining when and why the psychological effects of perceived gender discrimination diverge between advantaged and disadvantaged groups, addressing the long-noted absence of a well-supported explanation (and the mixed support for the rejection-identification/pervasiveness account). Its second contribution is importing the construct of cultural narratives into the conversation on perceived discrimination, showing that understanding outcomes requires attending not just to the extent of perceived discrimination but to the substantive meaning individuals assign to it.
practical_implication: Leaders who understand the dominant cultural narratives operating in their organization can better identify and eliminate managerial behaviors that elicit perceived gender discrimination; because narratives vary, leaders should gather qualitative data rather than assume which narrative dominates. Interventions should bolster both men's and women's sense of belonging while additionally bolstering self-efficacy among women.
limitations: Study 1's sample was too small to examine how non-dominant narratives varied across response types (personal, observed, hypothetical). Study 3, though experimental, relied on lower-stakes vignettes, and Studies 4a/4b are subject to common method bias (mitigated by the dichotomous, nonsubjective gender-interaction design). The work focuses on people identifying as women or men and excludes trans/nonbinary individuals, and statistical power was insufficient to confidently detect race/ethnicity moderation.
future_research: Future work should probe why the gestalt well-being effect for men was inconsistent across Studies 4a/4b, examine the cultural narratives that nonbinary and transgender individuals adopt, and extend the framework to other forms of discrimination (race, religion, age, disability, LGBTQ) and to intersectional combinations. Larger, more highly powered samples are needed to examine race/ethnicity differences, and intervention studies could test the effectiveness of narrative-aware managerial training.
abstract: We further argue that these effects contribute to a reduction of well-being among members of both genders, with the negative effect on well-being being more pronounced among women than among men. We examine these predictions in five studies of working adults. We discuss the implications for research on perceptions of discrimination and for organizations seeking to reduce the negative consequences of perceptions of discrimination.
apa_citation: Tost, L. P., Hardin, A. E., Roberson, J. W., & Gino, F. (2022). Different roots, different fruits: Gender-based differences in cultural narratives about perceived discrimination produce divergent psychological consequences. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(6), 1804–1834. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2020.0339

title: The Downside of CFO Function-Based Language Incongruity
methods: Archival quantitative study. Negative binomial regression (count DV, overdispersion) of the number of debt-contract covenants on CFO function-based language incongruity, with interaction terms for two moderators, 4-digit SIC industry, year, loan-purpose, and lead-bank fixed effects, and standard errors clustered by firm. CFO/CEO language attributes (optimism, risk-taking, promotion focus, future focus) measured from conference-call transcripts via LIWC and combined into a PCA index. Robustness via heteroskedasticity-based instrumental variables, curvilinearity tests, and supplementary moderator analyses.
industry: U.S. public firms (non-financial) that signed bank debt contracts; lenders are commercial banks (lead arrangers)
units: Loan deals (debt contracts) nested within borrowing firms
n_sample: 7,649 deals across 1,915 firms
authors: Um, Cyril Taewoong; Guo, Shiau-Ling; Lumineau, Fabrice; Shi, Wei; Song, Ruixiang
theories: expectancy violations theory; role congruity theory; transaction cost / exchange hazards perspective
keywords: CFO; contract design; debt contract covenants; function-based language incongruity; perceived exchange hazards; role congruity theory; within-role incongruity
research_question: (a) How does role-incongruent language influence the observer's perceived hazard, and (b) how is that relationship moderated by factors that influence the interpretation of incongruity? Specifically, how does a borrowing firm's CFO exhibiting language incongruent with function-based role expectations shape the perceived exchange hazards of the lending bank and thereby the design (number of covenants) of debt contracts?
mechanism_summary: CFOs are culturally expected to be conservative "watchdogs"/"naysayers"; when a CFO's public language exhibits optimism, risk-taking, promotion focus, or future focus, observers (banks) perceive incongruity with the function-based role. Drawing on role congruity and expectancy-violations logic, this within-role incongruity raises banks' perceived exchange hazards, leading them to add more covenants to control risk. Because the violation is interpreted, the effect is weakened when the CEO speaks similarly (the incongruity is read as the organization's broader social norm) and when positive media sentiment makes observers attribute the incongruity less negatively.
theoretical_contribution: The study extends role congruity theory by introducing within-role, function-based language incongruity as a previously overlooked source of the role incongruity effect, moving beyond the demography-based, between-role focus of prior work and showing language as an underexplored trigger. It contributes to the interorganizational contracting literature by foregrounding individuals (specifically the CFO's linguistic attributes) as antecedents of contract design, and advances understanding of the perceptual, microfoundational dimension linking perceived exchange hazards to governance choices.
practical_implication: Boards selecting executives should weigh not only candidates' characteristics but also stakeholders' expectations of the role and how the candidate is perceived; firms can reduce partner-perceived exchange hazards by choosing executives who speak congruently with role expectations and by managing strategic communication when executives address external audiences. Executives should understand their role expectations and use congruent language to avoid triggering perceived hazards; bankers are an important, often-overlooked stakeholder audience.
limitations: The authors do not directly measure whether banks hold different expectation levels or tolerance for CFO role incongruity, so surveys or field studies could capture that variation. Findings are drawn from U.S. bank debt contracting and may not generalize to other contract types or contexts where no ideal characteristic is associated with a given role. The study links incongruent language to more covenants but cannot establish performance implications—whether projects financed under more-covenant contracts ultimately perform better or worse is unclear.
future_research: Future work could investigate why the influence of banks' executive expectations varies across firms and how that variation shapes strategic decisions, and whether role congruity theory of the executive applies in other settings and contract types. Researchers could examine how the individual linguistic attributes differ across industries or contexts, the interplay between perceived counterparty behaviors and the contract drafter's own attributes, and the conditions under which covenant levels translate into better or worse loan-project performance.
abstract: The prior literature on role congruity theory has revolved around demographic-based expectations, emphasizing role incongruity derived from a mismatch between prescriptive expectations of distinct roles. In this study, we depart from this traditional focus on between-role incongruity and explore an alternative source of role incongruity by examining how language can trigger the within-role incongruity of function-based expectations. Through an analysis of conference call transcripts and contracts for 7,649 deals during 2003–2018, we show that the incongruity of function-based expectations manifested through the language of the CFO leads banks to employ more debt contract covenants. This takes place because such incongruity increases banks’ perceived hazards. In addition, by investigating the moderating effects of corresponding CEO language and media sentiment, we show how the social context and sentiment toward the firm weaken this incongruity effect. We discuss the theoretical implications of our study for future research on the sources of role incongruity and the antecedents of contract design.
apa_citation: Um, C. T., Guo, S.-L., Lumineau, F., Shi, W., & Song, R. (2022). The downside of CFO function-based language incongruity. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(6), 1984-2013. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.0943

title: The Social Aftershocks of Voice: An Investigation of Employees’ Affective and Interpersonal Reactions after Speaking Up
methods: Multimethod, three studies — an experimental pilot (MANOVA), a 3-week daily experience-sampling study of employee–coworker dyads analyzed with multilevel path analysis in Mplus and parametric-bootstrap indirect effects, and a preregistered between-subjects experiment analyzed with PROCESS bootstrapped mediation.
industry: Broad range of industries (e.g., computers/information systems; education, training, or library; finance, insurance, real estate, or public policy; healthcare) for the field study; commuter-airline scenario for the experiment
time_period: Three consecutive work weeks of daily surveys (Study 1); cross-sectional experiments (pilot and Study 2)
units: Individual voicers (employees), plus employee–coworker dyads in the field study
n_sample: Pilot: 219 undergraduates; Study 1: 179 employee–coworker dyads (2,103 dyad-day observations); Study 2: 358 full-time employees
authors: Welsh, David T.; Outlaw, Ryan; Newton, Daniel W.; Baer, Michael D.
theories: appraisal theory of emotion; broaden-and-build theory (Fredrickson, 2001); cognitive-motivational-relational theory (Lazarus, 1991)
keywords: anxiety; interpersonal avoidance; interpersonal citizenship behavior; pride; prohibitive voice; promotive voice; voice reception
research_question: How does speaking up shape the voicer's own emotions and subsequent social behavior — specifically, when does voice generate pride versus anxiety, how do these emotions affect interpersonal avoidance and downstream interpersonal citizenship behavior, and how does a recipient's reception of the voice alter these appraisal processes?
mechanism_summary: Grounded in cognitive-motivational-relational (appraisal) theory, the affective process is modeled as a decision tree: promotive voice is appraised as a beneficial, credit-worthy event that elicits pride, whose expansive action tendency reduces social withdrawal; prohibitive voice is appraised as an uncertain social threat that elicits anxiety, whose restrictive action tendency increases withdrawal. Because pride broadens and anxiety narrows social engagement, the two voice types exert opposite serial indirect effects on interpersonal citizenship behavior. A recipient's response operates as a relational lever that can redirect the appraisal down a different branch, intensifying or dampening the dominant emotion.
theoretical_contribution: The paper resolves a standing tension in the voice literature — whether speaking up makes voicers feel accomplished or anxious — by showing that promotive and prohibitive voice branch into distinct emotions (pride vs. anxiety) rather than a single mixed affective state, using episodic within-person theory rather than retrospective between-person assessment. It reveals that one discretionary behavior (prohibitive voice) can inhibit another (interpersonal citizenship behavior) through anxiety-driven avoidance, extending the nomological network of voice. It also extends cognitive-motivational-relational theory by identifying recipients as relational agents who can "hijack" the appraisal process and redirect which emotion is elicited.
practical_implication: Organizations benefit from promotive voice not only through the ideas communicated but indirectly via increased interpersonal citizenship after speaking up, so managers need not respond enthusiastically to every idea — avoiding a negative reception is what matters, since both positive and no reception preserved voicer pride. Rather than suppressing prohibitive voice (which risks missing critical problems), managers should respond constructively and in a positively framed way to defuse the anxiety it provokes.
limitations: The authors focused on constructive voice (promotive and prohibitive per Liang et al., 2012), so other forms such as supportive, defensive, and destructive voice were not examined and may elicit different appraisal processes. The framework targets the impact of voice events rather than the anticipation of voicing, leaving pre-voice affect (e.g., anticipatory pride or hope) unexamined. The studies capture short-term, same-day affective and behavioral consequences rather than cumulative or long-term dynamics.
future_research: Researchers could apply the affective framework to other voice types (supportive, defensive, destructive) to see how they shape voicers' emotions and behavior, and examine the pre-voice phase, including the role of hope and anticipatory appraisals. Longitudinal growth models could trace how discrete voice events accumulate to shape employees' overall propensity to voice and the potential overlap between avoidance and silence over time.
abstract: We draw on cognitive-motivational-relational theory to build a theoretical model that outlines how speaking up affects voicers’ emotions and subsequent social behavior. Across three studies—an experimental pilot study, a daily within-person study of employee–coworker dyads, and a preregistered experiment—we test our proposal that promotive voice elicits pride due to a sense of social accomplishment, whereas prohibitive voice elicits anxiety due to a sense of social uncertainty. We demonstrate that these feelings of pride and anxiety have diverging effects on voicers’ tendency to withdraw from social interaction during the rest of the day. In turn, these diverging effects on voicers’ interpersonal avoidance influence voicers’ daily interpersonal citizenship behaviors. We further propose that recipients of voice have the potential to “hijack” voicers’ affective appraisals in a manner that can amplify or attenuate their emotional reactions and subsequent social behavior. Our results disentangle the complex experience of speaking up and provide novel insights into how voicers and organizations can maximize the benefits of voice while minimizing its harmful social side effects.
apa_citation: Welsh, D. T., Outlaw, R., Newton, D. W., & Baer, M. D. (2022). The social aftershocks of voice: An investigation of employees’ affective and interpersonal reactions after speaking up. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(6), 2034–2057. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1187

title: Leader Perfectionism—Friend or Foe of Employee Creativity? Locus of Control as a Key Contingency
methods: Two studies: (1) a one-way between-subjects lab experiment manipulating leader perfectionism (no/moderate/extreme) analyzed with GLM, orthogonal polynomial contrasts, and PROCESS conditional indirect effects; (2) a two-time-point, multisource field survey analyzed with multilevel (two-level) path analysis in Mplus 7.4.
industry: Study 1: undergraduate students (lab); Study 2: research and development teams at four high-technology firms
time_period: Study 2: two survey waves six weeks apart
units: Study 1: individual student participants; Study 2: leader–subordinate dyads (subordinate-rated perfectionism, locus of control, engagement, exhaustion; leader-rated creativity)
n_sample: Study 1: 191 undergraduates; Study 2: 102 leaders and 503 subordinates (final matched sample)
authors: Xu, Linna; Liu, Zhi; Ji, Ming; Dong, Yuntao; Wu, Chia-Huei
theories: locus of control (Rotter, 1966); self-regulation theory (Carver & Scheier, 1981; Klein, 1989; Baumeister, 1998)
keywords: emotional exhaustion; employee creativity; engagement; leader perfectionism; locus of control; self-regulation
research_question: Does a leader's perfectionism toward employees foster or hinder employee creativity, and through what self-regulatory mechanisms (engagement and emotional exhaustion), for whom (employee locus of control), and to what degree (linear vs. curvilinear effects of perfectionism level)?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on self-regulation theory, the authors argue that leader perfectionism creates a discrepancy between current and desired work outcomes that drives employees to regulate their efforts (raising engagement) while also depleting personal resources (raising emotional exhaustion). Whether these states translate into higher or lower creativity depends on employees' locus of control: internals, who believe they control outcomes, convert perfectionistic challenge into engagement (with diminishing returns at extreme levels) while being buffered from exhaustion; externals, who attribute outcomes to external forces, experience leader perfectionism mainly as a stressor that escalates emotional exhaustion and undermines creativity. The predicted negative downturn for internals at extreme perfectionism was only partially borne out across the two studies.
theoretical_contribution: The paper reconciles conflicting predictions about leader perfectionism and employee creativity by showing it is a double-edged sword whose effect runs through two opposing self-regulatory states (engagement and emotional exhaustion) contingent on employees' locus of control and the level of perfectionism. It extends the perfectionism literature beyond self-oriented perfectionism by theorizing leader (other-oriented) perfectionism as an interpersonal leader characteristic, and extends the locus of control literature by demonstrating the motivational limit of internal locus of control when perfectionism becomes extreme.
practical_implication: Leaders should manage their level of perfectionism, recognizing it as a double-edged sword: even for internals the favorable effect weakens or vanishes when perfectionism is extreme, so leaders should avoid standards far beyond employees' capabilities and tolerate inevitable errors. Leaders should monitor employees—especially externals—for exhaustion and provide resources, timely positive feedback, and instrumental and socioemotional support, while encouraging trial-and-error and constructive negative feedback to sustain creativity.
limitations: Because locus of control was measured rather than manipulated, causal inference about the moderator is limited. In the field study, leaders rated subordinates' creativity, raising potential rating bias (the authors ran a supplementary experiment suggesting perfectionism did not systematically bias ratings). The two studies did not fully replicate each other—particularly the indirect effect on externals' creativity via emotional exhaustion—and both were conducted in China, a high-power-distance culture, which may limit cross-cultural generalizability.
future_research: Future work could manipulate locus of control to strengthen causal inference and identify interventions that shape employees' sense of control; obtain objective or coworker/customer ratings of creativity to mitigate leader rating bias; examine temporal components, contextual factors, and research settings to explain cross-study inconsistencies; test whether employees in low-power-distance cultures react more negatively to extreme leader perfectionism; and study the effects of leaders' self-oriented perfectionism on employees.
abstract: This research aims to explain whether leader perfectionism toward employees fosters or hinders employee creativity. From a self-regulation perspective, we theorize that depending on employees' locus of control, leader perfectionism can influence two regulatory states of employees (i.e., engagement and emotional exhaustion) linearly or curvilinearly, which in turn affect their creativity in opposite directions. In a lab experiment and a multisource, multiwave field study, we found that for internals, leader perfectionism had a curvilinear effect on their engagement (but no effect on emotional exhaustion) and subsequent creativity such that the effect was positive but became weaker when leader perfectionism was extreme. By contrast, we found partial support across the two studies that for externals, leader perfectionism had a positive effect on their emotional exhaustion (but no effect on engagement), which undermined their creativity. We discuss the theoretical contributions of this research and its practical implications for organizations.

title: Fast or Slow: How Temporal Work Design Shapes Experienced Passage of Time and Job Performance
methods: Multi-study quantitative design: two between-subjects MTurk experiments (Studies 1a, 1b), a naturalistic field study in a garment factory (Study 2), a multilevel organizational field study of knowledge workers and supervisors (Study 3), and a three-wave time-lagged online survey (Study 4). Analyses use one-way ANOVA, CFA, and bootstrapped/Bayesian mediation in Mplus 8.0.
industry: MTurk working adults (Studies 1a, 1b, 4); Chinese garment-factory sewing machinists and support workers (Study 2); knowledge workers at an international logistics company (Study 3)
units: individual employees (with supervisor-matched dyads in Studies 2 and 3)
n_sample: Study 1a = 116; Study 1b = 118; Study 2 = 96; Study 3 = 211 employees / 34 supervisors; Study 4 = 270
authors: Zhao, Helen H.; Deng, Hong; Chen, Rocky Peng; Parker, Sharon K.; Zhang, Wei
theories: attentional gate theory (Zakay & Block, 1995; Matthews & Meck, 2016; Walsh, 2003); work design / job characteristics model (Hackman & Oldham, 1976; Parker, 2014)
keywords: attentional gate; experienced passage of time; job performance; task segmentation; temporal predictability; temporal work design; work design
research_question: Is it possible to alter employees' experienced passage of time via work design—and, if it is possible, are there any performance benefits of doing so?
mechanism_summary: Drawing on the attentional-gate perspective, the paper argues that temporal and non-temporal information compete for the same neural resources, so when work design directs attention away from temporal cues, employees process the passage of time less and therefore experience time as passing faster. Temporal predictability removes uncertain "fore-periods" that draw attention to time, and task segmentation inserts categorically different markers that restore diminishing task attention before it drifts to temporal cues. The resulting faster, more pleasant time experience is treated as an intermediate work experience that, in turn, improves job performance.
theoretical_contribution: The paper extends work design theory beyond enrichment-based mechanisms (the job characteristics model and its relational and motivational extensions) by introducing two attention-based temporal work characteristics—temporal predictability and task segmentation—that shape outcomes not by enriching jobs but by redirecting attention away from temporal cues. It establishes experienced passage of time as a previously neglected intermediate work experience linking work design to performance, and connects work design research to the emerging management literature on time.
practical_implication: Managers can design work time, not just work content, to help employees stay focused: eliminate or reduce uncertain wait times (e.g., giving idle "standby" workers other tasks), design better-scheduled meetings, and punctuate the workday with categorically different temporal markers/breaks timed to when attention wanes. Because these levers are flexible and cheap relative to job enrichment, they are especially valuable for simple, standardized, routinized jobs that are hard to enrich, but segmentation should be customized and used cautiously for complex jobs.
limitations: The authors identify only two temporal work characteristics rather than an exhaustive set, and they do not examine boundary conditions (e.g., individual differences such as mindfulness, or whether segmentation is self-initiated versus externally imposed). Although the design attempts to establish the temporal precedence of experienced passage of time over performance, reverse causality—performance shaping experienced time—cannot be fully ruled out. The two experiments (Studies 1a, 1b) are relatively weak in external validity.
future_research: Future work should explore additional temporal work characteristics that alter experienced passage of time (e.g., unusual schedules such as night shifts for ER doctors or air traffic controllers, including chronotype differences), investigate boundary conditions and moderators such as mindfulness and whether segmentation is self-initiated or imposed, and further disentangle the causal ordering between experienced passage of time and job performance.
abstract: Experienced passage of time, the extent to which employees perceive the passage of work time as being fast or slow, is a fundamental aspect of work experience. We identify two novel temporal work design characteristics that can speed up employees' experienced passage of time: temporal predictability and task segmentation. Jobs with high temporal predictability do not make employees go through uncertain wait times before embarking on their next task. High task segmentation occurs when a large chunk of work time is segmented by categorically different temporal markers. We tested a model in which temporal predictability and task segmentation affect experienced passage of time, which in turn influences job performance, with five studies: two experiments that established the internal validity of temporal predictability and task segmentation (Studies 1a and 1b), a naturalistic field study in a factory that investigated the natural consequences of distinct temporal work design (Study 2), an organizational field study that constructively replicated the model using a sample of knowledge workers and their supervisors (Study 3), and an online survey in which we connected our model with the broader work design literature (Study 4). Altogether, the studies support a new temporal approach to work design.
apa_citation: Zhao, H. H., Deng, H., Chen, R. P., Parker, S. K., & Zhang, W. (2022). Fast or slow: How temporal work design shapes experienced passage of time and job performance. *Academy of Management Journal*, 65(6), 2014–2033. https://doi.org/10.5465/amj.2019.1110
